More than 300,000 sign petition asking Trudeau to revoke Elon Musk's Canadian citizenship. Is it even possible?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/more-than-300-000-sign-petition-asking-trudeau/docview/3171932130/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=More+than+300%2C000+sign+petition+asking+Trudeau+to+revoke+Elon+Musk%27s+Canadian+citizenship.+Is+it+even+possible%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A recent petition to the House of Commons calling on Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to strip U.S. tech mogul and senior White House adviser Elon Musk of his Canadian citizenship is on track to become the most-signed House e-petition in history.
More than 300,000 Canadians have signed the document as of time of writing, not even a week after it first opened for signatures on Feb. 20. The petition will close on June 20, after which it can be presented to the House of Commons.
But for all its popularity, immigration lawyers say the document is unlikely to have any effect on Musk's Canadian citizenship.
"The only way that citizenship can be revoked is through fraud or misrepresentation in the application itself," explained immigration lawyer Daniel Kingwell, principle lawyer and founder of Toronto's Kingwell Immigration Law.
"The bottom line is, once you're a citizen, you're one of us. And whatever you do ... it can't lead to citizenship revocation," he said.
Petition calls for revocation of Elon Musk's Canadian citizenship
The e-petition, started by British Columbia-based author Qualia Reed and sponsored by NDP MP Charlie Angus, called on Trudeau to "revoke Elon Musk's dual citizenship status, and revoke his Canadian passport effective immediately."
"Elon Musk has engaged in activities that go against the national interest of Canada," the document argued.
"He has used his wealth and power to influence our elections," it claimed, adding that: "He has now become a member of a foreign government that is attempting to erase Canadian sovereignty" and "the attempts of Elon Musk to attack Canadian sovereignty must be addressed."
In response, Musk wrote: "Canada is not a real country" in a now-deleted post to X.
Olivier Duhaime, a spokesperson for the House of Commons, said that if a petition receives at least 500 valid signatures by the time it closes, the Clerk of Petitions will issue a certificate to the MP that authorized its online publication.
"It can then be presented to the House during one of its sittings by any Member," they explained. "The Standing Orders of the House of Commons require the government to respond to every petition presented to the House within 45 calendar days."
As of time of writing, the petition has attracted more than 301,000 signatures, with the majority - over 113,000 signees - coming from Ontario.
According to Duhaime, the most-signed House e-petition in Canadian history garnered 387,487 signatures over a one month period in late 2023. It called on the House of Commons to hold a vote of no-confidence in the federal Liberal government and, if won, a federal election 45 days after the vote.
Is it possible to get one's Canadian citizenship revoked?
The only way one can get their Canadian citizenship revoked under the Citizenship Act is to either renounce it themselves or commit "false representation or fraud or by knowingly concealing material circumstances" while obtaining, retaining, resuming or renouncing their citizenship, a spokesperson for Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC) told the Star.
For example, during the application process, some permanent residents might exaggerate the amount of time they've lived in Canada to fulfil citizenship requirements, Kingwell continued. Some may fail to disclose criminal charges or convictions that could bar them from citizenship. If this is discovered, it could lead to citizenship revocation after the fact.
Elon Musk's mother, Maye Musk, was born in Regina. As a result, he automatically received Canadian citizenship by descent - despite being born in South Africa, Raj Sharma, an immigration lawyer and managing partner at Calgary firm Stewart Sharma Harsanyi, added.
"The Government does not take the revocation of citizenship lightly," the IRCC spokesperson said. "The Citizenship Act provides numerous steps to ensure fairness has been integrated into the process. This includes the Federal Court being the decision maker for citizenship revocation cases unless the person subject to revocation requests that the Minister act as the decision maker."
They could not comment on Musk's specific case, citing privacy legislation.
According to Kingwell, it was "very, very rare" for Canadians to have their citizenship revoked until the Stephen Harper government expanded the grounds for revocation. It's still relatively rare today - there were seven revocations in 2021 and 25 in 2022, he said.
"The short answer is, right now, there's no legal path to the revocation" of Musk's citizenship, Kingwell finished. "You can say whatever you want and (you'll be) judged in the court of public opinion, but it's not contrary to citizenship law in Canada."
CAPTION: Elon Musk arrives to speak at the Conservative Political Action Conference, CPAC, at the Gaylord National Resort & Convention Center, on Feb. 20, 2025, in Oxon Hill, Md.
CREDIT: Kevin Jiang

Document 2 of 500

Etobicoke businesses sue city, Toronto councillor over Bloor Street bike lanes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/etobicoke-businesses-sue-city-toronto-councillor/docview/3171483862/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Etobicoke+businesses+sue+city%2C+Toronto+councillor+over+Bloor+Street+bike+lanes&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: More than 40 Etobicoke business owners have joined in a lawsuit against the City of Toronto, its transportation manager and Coun. Amber Morley over the dedicated bike lanes installed along Bloor Street West in 2023 and 2024.
The group is seeking $10 million from the city for "negligence and nuisance." It's suing Barbara Gray, general manager, transportation services, and Morley (Ward 3 Etobicoke-Lakeshore) for "misfeasance of public office," according to the statement of claim, filed in Ontario Superior Court on Friday.
The businesses, including restaurants, retailers and service operators, among others, are also seeking an injunction requiring that the bike lanes be removed and the streets returned to their prior state.
The statement of claim has not been tested in court. It typically takes weeks or months for defendants in a lawsuit to respond with a statement of defence.
Neither Morley nor the city responded to requests from the Star for comment.
Sam Pappas, owner of the Crooked Cue, and a participant in the lawsuit, said business is down between 20 and 60 per cent at some establishments, because congestion caused by the bike lanes is driving customers away.
"We're just fighting for our community," said Pappas, adding that there is a feeling that the city's consultations were performative, not authentic.
He said the lawsuit isn't just about bike lanes, it's about letting city hall know it can't ignore what local neighbourhoods think.
"This is a shot across the bow, saying 'we're not going to take it.' "
The lawsuit alleges that the city's planning, consultation and implementation were "willfully lacking in candour, frankness and impartiality" and breached the duty of care the city owed them.
The statement of claim also accuses the city of manipulating and/or misrepresenting data, bicycle counts and the public consultation process that took place.
According to the statement of claim, Morley misrepresented the nature and extent of public consultations, failed to conduct adequate door-to-door consultations with businesses, and gave preferential treatment to CycleTO, a pro-cycling group.
The statement alleges Gray allowed herself to be unlawfully lobbied by CycleTO; and that overall, the city, Morley and Gray showed "reckless disregard, high-handed and egregious treatment of said businesses."
Michael Longfield, executive director of CycleTO, said the lanes were approved by city council before Morley was elected.
"The city is pretty deliberate in how it plans out its cycling structure," said Longfield.
He said his organization wasn't lobbying Gray.
"I understand not everyone is in favour of the project, but I think there is a difference between people being consulted and their opinion ultimately being the one that prevails," Longfield said.
Premier Doug Ford took a personal interest in the issue late last year, resulting in provincial legislation restricting new bike lanes and setting the stage for the removal of the bike lanes on Bloor Street West.
CycleTO is seeking a provincial injunction and mounting a Charter challenge to prevent that from happening.
The lawsuit also accuses the city of failing to properly monitor the outcomes of the bike lanes, including their impact on emergency response times.
The Bloor Street West Complete Street Extension was a two-part project, begun in 2023 and completed in 2024, running from Runnymede Road to Resurrection Road, linking Bloor West Village to the Kingsway neighbourhood.
The businesses say the bike lanes have proven to be "an unmitigated disaster," resulting in traffic congestion, a reduction in emergency response times and "erosion of the neighbourhood character," a decline in property standards and a loss of business and foot traffic.
"The Plaintiffs plead that the foregoing harms were a foreseeable consequence of the Bike Lanes Extension, and that the Defendants nonetheless dogmatically pursued the project for improper purposes entirely, disconnected from the purposes of the project, their statutory grant of authority, or their duty of care," according to the lawsuit.
Cody MacRae, founder of the community group Balance on Bloor, said the bike lanes have resulted in more cars taking shortcuts through residential neighbourhoods, creating a hazard for children playing outdoors.
"They're poorly designed bike lanes," said MacRae, adding that Morley's local leadership on the issue was lacking.
"This community has been dismissed and ignored for more than a year."
CAPTION: A cyclist rides in the bike lane along eastbound Bloor West in Etobicoke last fall.
CREDIT: Francine Kopun

Subject: Injunctions; Transportation services; Litigation; Duty of care

Document 3 of 500

5 things we learned about Mark Carney from the Liberal leadership debates

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/5-things-we-learned-about-mark-carney-liberal/docview/3171483036/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=5+things+we+learned+about+Mark+Carney+from+the+Liberal+leadership+debates&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In the end, it was a friendly affair.
Liberal leadership candidates used their podiums to introduce themselves to party members during these week's debates, but they also shielded Mark Carney, the race's perceived front-runner, from devastating blows that could have helped Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives attack the party during what is expected to be a spring election campaign.
Only Karina Gould, the former Government House leader and cabinet minister, seemed to want to draw Carney into a debate, and even then, it was a pretty tame affair.
Here are a few takeaways from Carney's performance:
Carney's French
It's laborious, painful to listen to, and harder to watch. It may not matter but Monday's French-language performance was a warning that Carney needs to double down on his language studies unless he wants Bloc Québécois Leader Yves-François Blanchet and Poilievre to make mincemeat out of him during a campaign debate.
Freeland is a good friend
Carney, I'm told, was concerned about how Chrystia Freeland would approach him in the debate. The two are long-time friends, and he is the godfather to her youngest child. But he didn't need to worry. It was he who suggested Freeland didn't have the chops for the current task of defending Canada from Donald Trump, telling the audience that while Trump might have been after Canada's market share when Freeland was renegotiating the North American Free Trade Agreement, now the American president is after "our country."
Freeland came to Carney's defence at least three times on Monday. She ensured he could clearly get the words supply management out of his mouth when he told the audience that culture, water and Canada's protection for dairy, eggs, and poultry would never be traded away, an important policy stance for some ridings in Quebec, Ontario and Atlantic Canada.
Later, Freeland re-asked the French moderator's question so Carney could tell the audience he planned to scrap the consumer carbon price.
She also ensured that the audience understood that when he said everyone agreed with Hamas ("Nous sommes d'accord avec Hamas") that he meant to say that everyone on stage agreed on Hamas - that the terrorist group has no place in any discussion about the future of Gaza.
But Freeland wasn't the only candidate to lend Carney a helping hand.
Frank Baylis helped clarify Carney's misspeak when he said Poilievre wanted to cut Radio-Canada - as the Conservative leader has said he wants to defund the CBC, not the French arm of the public broadcaster. Baylis suggested that scrapping the CBC would impact Radio-Canada, as the two share infrastructure (which is true).
Tuesday, there were no lifelines for Carney. But he didn't need them either.
Had Freeland concluded, as many in her own camp have, that she won't win and her best play now is courting a key post in a Carney cabinet?
Carney's key policies
Carney repeated that he's laser focused on the economy, on growing wages faster than inflation, and pledged to be a more hands on manager than Prime Minister Justin Trudeau. Among his promises: a middle-income tax cut, scrapping the GST on the purchase of new homes, eliminating the consumer price on carbon. He said he'll keep the Liberals' child care, dental care, and pharmacare programs. He wants to increase competition in the grocery, broadband, and transportation sectors, and suggested he'd continue with the Liberals' firearms buyback program, and "reinforce" Criminal Code provisions for sexual abuse. He demonstrated a keen interest in artificial intelligence, and sees Canadian investments there as key to finding government efficiencies and combating cybersecurity and information warfare.
Carney is open to new pipelines
Carney also suggested it is "possible" a Liberal government under his watch builds a new pipeline. There could be an Energy East-type project, he said, bringing Alberta's oil to the Maritimes, but he suggested it would have to be built to displace American imports. Trump had changed the calculations, he suggested. But a new pipeline would only get built, if it is in the interest of the entire country and the provinces would get a veto. "Quebec, Ontario, Manitoba, Saskatchewan must be in agreement," he said.
Bill 21
Carney appears to be breaking rank with Trudeau's firm opposition to Bill 21. The Quebec legislation bars wearing religious symbols by people in some public sector jobs, such as teachers and judges - and the current federal government argues the law breaches the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms. When the candidates were asked about that Monday, Carney responded, "There must be reasonable accommodations." Then he said he would "respect absolutely the tradition of laicïté" - often described as the separation between church and state - "here in Quebec." Does that mean a Carney-led government would not intervene in the challenge of the Quebec law at the Supreme Court? When asked, his team sidestepped the question, as well as what he meant specifically when he said he supports "reasonable accommodations."
The term "reasonable accommodation" refers to the majority accommodating religious minorities, namely Muslims, Sikhs and Jews. Back in 2008, Quebec's Bouchard-Taylor Commission recommended a limited ban on wearing religious symbols for people with "coercive" powers of the state, such as police officers, judges and prosecutors. It discussed the accommodating of religious minorities through a new policy of "interculturalism" that would reconcile ethnic diversity with the promotion of francophone identity. Later, the Parti Quebecois and the Coalition Avenir Quebec (CAQ) governments proposed ways to promote the French language while treating religious minorities as second-class citizens, all in the name of reasonable accommodation.
Poilievre's Conservatives have spoken from both sides of their mouths on this issue. During their leadership debates in 2022, Poilievre said he would intervene at the Supreme Court to fight Bill 21. But after he won, his Quebec lieutenant said a Poilievre-led government would not in fact intervene. When the Supreme Court hearing was announced last month, Poilievre's office refused to say where he stood on the issue. Despite weeks of requests for Poilievre's office to clarify his stance on Bill 21, it has refused to respond. Now Carney has some explaining to do, too.
CAPTION: Liberal Party of Canada leadership candidate Mark Carney speaks during the English-language Liberal Leadership debate in Montreal on Feb. 25, 2025.U.S. President Donald Trump and the Canadian economy dominated the discussion as Liberal leadership candidates faced off in the first of two debates. The French debate will be followed by an English debate. Former finance minister Chrystia Freeland, former central banker Mark Carney, former House leader Karina Gould and Montreal businessman and former MP Frank Baylis are vying to replace Prime Minister Justin Trudeau. (Feb. 25, 2025 / The Canadian Press)
CREDIT: Althia Raj

Document 4 of 500

Doug Ford's Tories poised to win another majority, poll suggests

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-tories-poised-win-another-majority/docview/3171354686/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+Tories+poised+to+win+another+majority%2C+poll+suggests&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives are poised to win a third straight majority in Thursday's provincial election, a new poll suggests.
But the Abacus Data survey for the Star indicates Bonnie Crombie's Liberals could leapfrog Marit Stiles' New Democrats into second place forming Official Opposition.
In the final poll of the campaign, Ford's Tories were at 46 per cent support, well ahead of Crombie's Liberals at 28 per cent, Stiles's New Democrats at 16 per cent and Mike Schreiner's Greens at five per cent.
"His gamble at calling an early election well before he had to appears to have worked and achieved the objectives they probably set for themselves," Abacus president David Coletto said Wednesday.
Abacus surveyed 1,000 Ontarians on Monday and Tuesday using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 3.1 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
Coletto said the trend lines are bleak for the NDP, which has been the official opposition to Ford's Tories in the legislature since 2018.
"If the Liberals are ahead of the New Democrats by 13 (percentage points) and they don't form the official opposition, I find that kind of an incredible outcome," said the veteran pollster.
"I think if the NDP do perform this poorly they will have a hard time holding onto official opposition (status) and the Liberals should be able to get ahead of them," he said, noting the poll suggests the Grits are stronger than the NDP in Toronto and Ottawa.
"There's evidence that the Liberals can probably gain some seats. How many, though, remains to be seen. But the NDP probably are feeling that with these kind of numbers, they're going to lose a lot in Toronto, in London, in Windsor and in Ottawa."
However, because the New Democratic vote is traditionally more efficient than the Liberals, being concentrated in select regions, it is possible the NDP finish behind them in popular vote and still win more seats in the 124-member house.
That happened in 2022 when the Liberals won just eight seats with 23.9 per cent of the popular vote and the New Democrats took 31 ridings with 23.7 per cent. Ford's Tories won 83 seats with 40.8 per cent of the vote in the last election. The Greens won one with six per cent and there was one Independent elected in 2022.
The PC leader triggered the election 15 months early, framing it as a referendum on who could best deal with U.S. President Donald Trump's threatened 25 per cent tariffs that would hurt an Ontario economy heavily dependent on American trade.
When respondents were asked for their key ballot question in this campaign, the poll suggested Ford's arguments about Trump and affordability appear to be resonating.
Fully 65 per cent who had an opinion on the matter believed the Tories were the best party "to deal with the impact of Donald Trump," compared to 21 per cent for the Liberals, 11 per cent for the NDP and four per cent for the Greens.
On which party "will keep the cost of living as low as possible," the Tories led with 43 per cent to 25 per cent for the Liberals, 14 per cent for the NDP and eight per cent for the Greens.
On which party "will best manage the provincial economy," the Tories were at 54 per cent, the Liberals at 23 per cent, the NDP at 11 per cent and the Greens at four per cent.
On which party "will improve the health-care system," the Liberals, who have made that the cornerstone of their campaign, were at 46 per cent, the Tories were at 25 per cent, the NDP at 23 per cent and the Greens at four per cent.
On which party "will do the most to make housing more affordable," the NDP and the Tories were tied at 37 per cent apiece with the Liberals at 20 per cent and the Greens at one per cent.
And on which "will improve the education system," 73 per cent said the Liberals, 23 per cent the Tories, four per cent the NDP and one per cent the Greens.
CAPTION: Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford responds to questions from reporters during a campaign stop on Tuesday.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie

Doug Ford's interest in an Oakville development + judge slams Milton jail guards

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-interest-oakville-development-judge/docview/3171354684/se-2?accountid=15090

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+interest+in+an+Oakville+development+%2B+judge+slams+Milton+jail+guards&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Good morning. This is the Wednesday, Feb. 26 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on the Ford government overriding local planning, the rise in units exempt from rent control, and another judge slamming Milton jail guards' treatment of prisoners.

The former Ford government staffer at the heart of the Greenbelt scandal has been accused of promising to help developer for moneyOne quarter of Toronto's condos are now exempt from rent control

Here's a recap.Newfoundland and Labrador Premier Andrew Furey has resigned, citing challenges dealing with the "erratic" U.S president.More than 40 Etobicoke business owners are suing the city over Bloor Street bike lanes.Sidewalk still clogged with snow? Here's what to do - and what could see you fined $360.A Toronto court heard new details about ex-Olympian turned accused international drug lord Ryan Wedding.Doug Ford's son-in-law is facing Toronto police misconduct allegations of insubordination and sharing confidential information.A woman's search for her missing husband has come to a tragic end. Here's the latest.A Toronto man was found guilty of murdering his partner. Here's what the jury wasn't told.A judge suggested in 2023 a 52-year-old man was "flirting" when he sent a 15-year-old girl explicit porn videos. The Ontario Court of Appeal saw things differently.POV
As Ford asks for your vote for a third time, consider this: each of the crises he promised to fix has gotten worse.
CLOSE UP
ETOBICOKE: For nearly two weeks now, 90-year-old Sheila Morris had been trapped inside her Toronto home by a wall of snow that was plowed from her street and dumped at the base of her driveway. Her son, pictured here, said that in addition to missed appointments, the most pressing issue for his mom is making sure she can vote tomorrow.

Thank you for reading. You can reach me and the First Up team at firstup@thestar.ca. I will see you back here tomorrow.

Michael has been sending daily photographs to Toronto's 311 service but they have yet to clear the mass of snow.The eleven condo towers the province has proposed for the development of Oakville's "transit-oriented community" near the GO station in Midtown.

Document 6 of 500

Four more years of Ford will bring regret for decades

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/four-more-years-ford-will-bring-regret-decades/docview/3171235191/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Four+more+years+of+Ford+will+bring+regret+for+decades&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Driving around the GTA last weekend, I saw blue, red, orange and green signs for the candidates vying for MPP along the streets. Some of the blue PC signs were paired with posters declaring, "Canada is not for sale."
Canada's not for sale, but we've seen that parts of Ontario are on the clearance rack. Premier Doug Ford was willing to sell away the Greenbelt and Ontario Place for the best price. When pushed at last week's debate, Ford defended himself by saying, "I was, then I wasn't, then I went ahead" and cancelled the sale of the Greenbelt to developers.
C'mon, Doug.
Even when he does something where I can see reason, his approach is reactionary and without a well-thought-out plan. The Progressive Conservative leader is also shutting down safe injection sites. I can fully understand why residents don't want people with addictions in their neighbourhoods, near schools or parks. The plan is to open Homelessness and Addictions Recovery Treatment (HART) sites, but they won't offer consumption services, safe supply of drugs or needle exchange programs. So, what's the rest of the plan? We don't want people dying on our sidewalks from overdoses.
Thursday is Ontario's provincial election, and I'm worried. We are an apathetic bunch, and apathy has never changed the status quo. Last election, back in 2022, saw the lowest voter turnout in Ontario's history - just 44 per cent. In 2025, only six per cent of eligible voters cast their ballots during the three days of advance voting.
People across the province have been complaining about health care, food prices, lack of affordable housing, education, traffic congestion and more in the GTA.
If you want to change the state of things, this is your time to get to the polls and vote.
Ford says he called a snap election to secure a stronger mandate to fight U.S. President Donald Trump's threatened tariffs and talk of annexation. Cool, cool. I get that.
But if Ford hasn't used his current mandate to make life better for Ontarians by hiring more family doctors, building affordable housing, easing traffic congestion (maybe without a tunnel under Highway 401 - the price tag could be upwards of $100 billion), increasing transit and eliminating transit deserts (I see you, northeast Scarborough), keeping small town emergency rooms open, and all the other things we've asked for that have been ignored, why should we believe him? What's more: why should we re-elect him?
The PC government has committed to increasing health care funding (with no specific figures or details on allocation), removing certain bike lanes in Toronto due to complaints about traffic congestion, investing billions of dollars in unspecified spending and lowering the minimum price of alcohol - whether or not anyone asked him to.
That's all fine, but for the past two years and change, the Ford government has had plenty of opportunities to follow through on their promises to "Protect Ontario." But they simply haven't.
I will never tell anyone who to vote for. Vote your values and for people who won't actively harm you or your community. If we want to see Ontario prosper, the values of political parties and their leaders need to align with our values. They need to commit to long-term, sustainable and necessary change.
But when our leaders offer nonsense initiatives with no details attached to appease us and focus on the wrong priorities, we all lose.
For many of us, that's not enough to motivate us to vote. Apathy - and the idea that someone else will save us - gets in our way. We find reasons not to demand answers, not to read platforms, and not to learn where candidates stand. We put on blinders and ignore the chaos around us. We shrug our shoulders - then spend the next few years suffering under elected officials who do everything except what we actually want and need.
If that's what you want, stay home Thursday and ignore what's happening. But if you want real change, go and vote - because I promise you, if you sit this election out, we'll all pay for it for years to come.
Shellene Drakes-Tull is a Toronto-based storyteller, educator and the founder of Sweet Lime Communications. She is a freelance contributing columnist for the Star.
CREDIT: Shellene Drakes-Tull OPINION Shellene Drakes-Tull is a Toronto-based storyteller, educator and the founder of Sweet Lime Communications. She is a freelance contributing columnist for the Star.;

Document 7 of 500

One quarter of Toronto's condos now exempt from rent control as rents continue to soar

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/one-quarter-torontos-condos-now-exempt-rent/docview/3171235107/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=One+quarter+of+Toronto%27s+condos+now+exempt+from+rent+control+as+rents+continue+to+soar&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If it feels harder now to find a rent-controlled apartment in Ontario than just a few years ago, that's because it is.
In the six years since the Doug Ford government scrapped rent controls for new apartment buildings in 2018, about 26,870 purpose-built rental units in the Toronto area have been built unfettered by rent control, a Star analysis of Canada Mortgage and Housing Corp. (CMHC) data has found.
Some 127,907 new condo units would also be exempt if rented out.
That translates to eight per cent of the Toronto area's purpose-built rental stock and 25 per cent of the condo market without rent controls.
During the same period, the average monthly rent for purpose-built apartments spiked 35 per cent in the Toronto area to $1,852 in from $1,370, with condo unit rents rising 23 per cent to $2,758 from $2,235.
These prices are the average paid by all tenants, including those who signed leases years ago, and the average rental listing price, which reflects what tenants would pay if they moved to a new home, is often higher.
The Ford government removed rent control for new units Nov. 15, 2018 - just months after taking office.
They described the move as a bid to stimulate development, and "give people more choice and bring down housing costs" while protecting existing tenants from the removal of rent control.
While the Ford government framed the rent control exemption as a step toward housing affordability, critics say it makes life for tenants more expensive and precarious.
The date-based exemption is "a stealthy way for governments to eliminate rent control over time," a 2024 report by the Canadian Centre for Policy Alternatives (CCPA) said.
Ricardo Tranjan, political economist and senior researcher with CCPA who co-wrote the report, said governments who see rent control as a "problem" tend to make date-based exemptions because doing so is easier, legally, than removing rent controls across the board.
Previously, Mike Harris's Progressive Conservative government introduced a date-based exemption so rent control no longer applied to units built after November 1991.
It wasn't until 2017 that Kathleen Wynne's Liberal government extended rent control to all units.
Tranjan says the exemption incentivizes developers while worsening affordability in the province.
He added that much of today's housing debates focus - directly or indirectly - on affordability, while most government policy is focused squarely on increasing supply.
The result has been an influx of units that are new, expensive and non-rent-controlled, he said.
"If ... all you're doing is providing incentive for private developers to add new units to the market that are priced high and not rent-controlled," said Tranjan, "how can you possibly (suggest) those policies are contributing to housing affordability?"
That idea, he says, comes from "filtering," the process of higher-income tenants moving into new units and leaving more affordable supply to lower-income tenants.
Tranjan said it's based on the same "flawed logic" of trickle-down economics.
Evictions are 'skyrocketing'Even if filtering were effective, there would be more direct ways of improving housing affordability, Tranjan said, such as imposing rent control on all units, reinstating vacancy control (which applies rent regulations to a unit even after a tenant moves out), and building more nonmarket, non-profit housing.
Dania Majid, a lawyer at the Advocacy Centre for Tenants Ontario (ACTO), said evictions are "skyrocketing" as wages trail rent prices.
The Landlord and Tenant Board held a record 106,000 hearings in 2024, she added.
The date-based exemption creates instability for renters, Majid explained, leaving tenants in post-2018 units with no security of tenure.
Once their lease ends, a tenant's rent can rise by any amount and they can be "economically evicted," and often don't exercise their rights for fear of reprisal, Majid said.
As rent becomes less affordable and options fewer, tenants also sacrifice necessities such as medicine or food just to pay rent Majid said, with food banks becoming "an informal rent subsidy."
Tranjan said there's also a "huge debate" about whether or not removing rent controls actually leads to more housing starts.
He highlighted a 2020 CMHC report on the impact of rent control policies on rental markets, which analyzed data from 1971 to 2019 and found "no significant evidence" that starts for purpose-built rentals were lower in markets with rent control than in markets without rent control.
Significant increase in rental starts and condo startsStill, there's been a significant increase in rental starts and condo starts in recent years, though some say it's not enough to meet demand.
CMHC data shows the number of rental starts in the Toronto area from 2019 to 2024 was 160 per cent higher than during the six years prior, while condo starts increased 22 per cent.
Frank Clayton, senior research fellow at Toronto Metropolitan University's Centre for Urban Research and Land Development, believes the removal of rent controls has had a positive impact on starts, but said there are many factors at play, including incentives from the federal government.
The Apartment Construction Loan Program introduced by CMHC in 2017 made a significant impact on rental starts, contributing to more than 50,000 units nationwide, Clayton said.
Another major incentive was Ottawa's removal of goods and services tax on purpose-built rentals in 2023, he added, and the provincial government removing its portion of harmonized sales tax.
Condo market in plummetingRising rent prices and decreasing interest rates have also led to more starts, Clayton said.
Additionally, some builders who bought land for condo development are turning to purpose-built rental apartments as an alternative because the condo market is plummeting, he said.
The Ontario Liberals say they would "phase-in" rent control, similar to Manitoba.
The Ontario New Democrats, meanwhile, have pledged to get rid of the date-based exemption if elected, to reinstate vacancy control and to end above-guideline rent increases for rent-controlled units.
CAPTION: Average rents paid by Toronto area tenants in purpose-built rentals increased by about 35 per cent since the loosening of rent controls by the province in 2018, CMHC data shows.
CREDIT: Manuela Vega

Document 8 of 500

Vote for change

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/vote-change/docview/3171234831/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Vote+for+change&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford was elected premier seven years ago on a promise to address our multiple and overlapping crises. The cost of housing was skyrocketing, family doctors were highly coveted but rarely found, schools were crumbling. Ford reasonably argued that the Liberals had had their chance and failed. He vowed to do better. Now, as Ford and his Progressive Conservatives ask for your vote for a third time, consider this: each of the crises he promised to fix has gotten worse.
Under Ford's leadership, population growth continues to outpace housing supply, driving ever upward the cost of homes and rent. Many young people can't afford to live in the city where they work. Many Ontarians are fleeing the province for more affordable locales.
Meanwhile, the homeless population is rising and food bank demand continues to soar. Ford recognizes the problem. He promised to build 1.5 million new homes and created a task force to advise him on how to do it. Then he ignored most of its recommendations.
Or consider the family doctor crisis that has tormented so many Ontarians and further strained an already overburdened health-care system. Ford's health ministry said last year that this was "not a major concern." Then, under political pressure, the government finally released something of a plan.
But compare it to the more robust and detailed proposals of any other major party, and you'd be forgiven for thinking the PCs were still not majorly concerned.
On education, Ford deserves credit for making record investments in our K-12 system. Nevertheless, after seven years, our schools are falling apart. Some 84 per cent of Toronto public schools are in substandard condition, according to the Financial Accountability Office. Meanwhile, amid persistent staffing shortages, boards are sometimes bringing in untrained replacement teachers to try to manage badly overcrowded and under-resourced classrooms.
All this has not come cheap.
The Ford government has increased the province's debt by $116 billion, running multibillion-dollar deficits every year and earning the ire of conservative groups such as the Fraser Institute in the process. Some of this spending, such as on education and infrastructure, was needed. But far too much has been spent on cynical politicking: More than $3 billion for the $200 cheques the province sent to every adult, including very rich ones. More than $600 million to get beer and wine in corner stores one year early. Nearly $200 million for an early election, which Ford said was necessary so he could get a clear mandate to deal with U.S. President Donald Trump. Never mind that he already had a clear mandate, especially to deal with the crises in housing, health care and education that have worsened during his tenure.
Ford is not without redeeming qualities. He connects with people. Many seem to trust him as safe hands during tumultuous times. He has professionalized his office after a shambolic first phase of his premiership, one marked by outrageous statements, senseless policy lurches and shocking cronyism. He has often worked effectively with the federal government, despite political differences, making significant and much-needed investments in infrastructure. His willingness to change his mind, as he did on the Greenbelt - and, it seems, on Trump - is laudable, even if we'd rather he showed more discernment in the first place.
And to be sure, the PCs have faced significant headwinds. The pandemic and the persistent high interest rates and supply-chain disruptions that resulted would have made transformative progress difficult for any government. But in hard times, Ford and his PCs have too often chosen easy political gimmicks, showing themselves to be not as serious as the challenges we face.
Fortunately, Ontarians have three credible alternatives, and a vote for any would be a well-spent vote for change. The Liberals, NDP and Greens have all put forward a slate of serious proposals that, if implemented, would at least put a dent in the defining challenges of the moment. Each understands the need to invest urgently to build more homes, recruit more doctors and do more to restore the province's education system.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, for instance, has at times proved a worthy adversary for Ford. An effective critic of the government's record, she seems to get under the PC leader's skin. When she became Liberal leader, having spent a decade as mayor of Mississauga, she took over a party in the wilderness. She deserves credit, if the polls are any indication, for bringing it back into the political mainstream. Her party's platform contains many sensible, moderate proposals that seem suited to the political moment, though she would do well to be more forthright about how she's going to pay for them. Efficiencies alone would not cover billions in new spending, particularly if the Liberals were to follow through with their proposed deep tax cuts.
Marit Stiles's NDP has made a persuasive case that there can be no solution to the province's cost-of-living crisis without active government, including robust social programs. The party's platform is full of bold ideas worthy of consideration, though again, the gap between proposed new revenues and proposed new spending is troubling, as is the plan to increase the capital gains tax in this moment of Trump-induced investment uncertainty. Stiles is an articulate, authentic and likable politician. But two years into her tenure as NDP leader, she's still struggling to get the attention of Ontarians. The province would be better off if she could find a way to change that.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner is a chipper wonk whose enthusiasm for policy is infectious. His platform offers perhaps the most nuanced approach to the housing crisis, recognizing a role for both market and government solutions. Urged to run for the Liberal leadership in 2023, Schreiner admirably chose instead the slow and doubtless frustrating work of building the Green caucus one seat at a time.
As comforting as continuity may seem, what Ontario needs is change. Liberals, NDP, Greens: take your pick. A vote for any is a vote for a better Ontario.
CREDIT: ;

Document 9 of 500

It's not his record, and it's not his platform. But there's a reason Doug Ford keeps winning

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/not-his-record-platform-theres-reason-doug-ford/docview/3171234743/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=It%27s+not+his+record%2C+and+it%27s+not+his+platform.+But+there%27s+a+reason+Doug+Ford+keeps+winning&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When Doug Ford first entered politics, there were good reasons to be skeptical of his prospects.
He was an outsider who had hitched himself to the Icarus-like rise of his brother Rob, the late former mayor, but he was cool and gruff where his younger sibling was folksy and all too human.
"Rob, but without the goofy charm" did not seem like it would have great appeal beyond the Etobicoke family heartland. Indeed, it didn't during the 2014 Toronto municipal elections, when Doug Ford lost his bid for the mayoralty.
But a decade later, Ford is in his seventh year as Premier of Ontario and seems likely to win a third majority government on Thursday - all while his personal popularity, according to polls, hovers in the mid-30s, the lowest rating of any premier.
What sorcery is this? How has Ford managed to buck the trend that has seen incumbent governments defeated across the democratic world as the cost of living has soared?
It's not as if his record is unblemished. As is the case with any government that has been around for a while, Ford's has had its scandals. And it's not his steady hand at the wheel: Ford spent billions on a giveaway to taxpayers before the budget had even been balanced, and there are long-running funding problems in areas of provincial responsibility such as health care and education. Even before he rebranded himself as Ontario's Defender-in-Chief amid U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff threats, Ford was expected to call an early vote to lock in a third majority because, well, he could.
How has he pulled this off? For a time, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau was a magnet for voter outrage, and Ford was happy to oppose the country's least-popular Liberal. But even with that advantage neutralized, Ford still benefits from circumstance. Specifically, he is governing amid a disengaged electorate. In fact, he might just be the ideal politician for the low-information age.
Consider some of his recent policies. The PC leader barrelled ahead with plans to put beer and cocktails in corner stores and gas stations, one of his major pre-election moves. It's more convenient for voters, sure, but at a stupidly high cost: if Ford had simply let existing deals with major brewers expire, the same thing could have been done in 18 months with no penalty. Instead, Ford's early change comes at the cost of hundreds of millions of public dollars.
Then there are the $200 cheques. Everyone likes free money, but at a time when the government was unable to balance its budget, these rebates were a wildly irresponsible bit of fiscal management, the provincial equivalent of a cash advance on a credit card just to buy some snacks.
How about the spa at Ontario Place? One can debate the merits of putting a bunch of pricey hot tubs and saunas on a piece of land that has been underused for many years, but a lot of people will like the end product. And many of those people won't even realize that Ontario handed a sweetheart deal to an Austrian spa developer to make it happen (for reasons that remain unclear).
The gamble that Ford and his advisers keep making is that most voters are paying attention to politics only at surface level. That $200 cheque was handy, and you can use it to get beer more easily. Columns such as this one argue the downsides of those policies, but fewer people are reading mainstream news now, and Canadian sources have been wiped from Facebook. If all you need to worry about are the broad strokes of a policy, it's much easier to sell as a win.
How little is Doug Ford sweating the details? He didn't release a platform until three days before election day, and when he did, it wasn't even costed. There was a time when this would have been considered a disqualifying offence. What kind of party leader would go to the public without a plan for what kind of shape the government's finances would be in?
Well, the same one who pulled the same trick in his first election campaign and became premier then. This isn't the first time Ford hasn't needed to worry about publicizing the finer points of his policy proposals. It's just that now he has perfected the art.
CAPTION: Progressive Conservative leader Doug Ford dons a "Canada: Not for Sale" toque at Queen's Park in Toronto on Jan. 28, 2025.
CREDIT: Scott Stinson, Contributor

Document 10 of 500

Proportional representation is the way to go Ontario

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/proportional-representation-is-way-go-ontario/docview/3171234596/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Proportional+representation+is+the+way+to+go+Ontario&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontarians, it's time for proportional representationOntario polls suggesting victory for Doug Ford with only days to go before provincial vote, Feb. 24
When speaking with Star political columnist Martin Regg Cohn during an interview on The Dais, at the Toronto Metropolitan University, he said: "We all know that one of the reasons the Tories are in power now is because the Liberals and the New Democrats are tearing each other down."
I was pleased to hear the NDP's Marit Stiles, Ontario's official opposition leader, voice her strong support for proportional representation.
Proportional representation is crucial for fostering a more inclusive democracy, not only in Ontario but across Canada, as it ensures that all parties, and by extension a vast majority of voters, have a genuine voice in the decision-making process. In today's political climate, it is vital we do not hand over a majority mandate to a party supported by a minority of voters.
The adoption of proportional representation would enhance the democratic framework of our provinces and our country. It would provide equitable representation, which is essential for the legitimacy and accountability of our political institutions. I encourage ongoing discussions around this important topic and hope that more politicians, especially those with a large platform like Marit Stiles, will continue to advocate for a system that truly reflects the will of the people.
Sara Crane, Chatsworth, Ont.
Ontario lacking visionary plan for its energy future'We are going in the wrong direction': Why last year saw Ontario burn a record amount of natural gas, Feb. 24
Thank you to the Toronto Star for three recent op-eds on the failure of the Doug Ford provincial government to address the health, housing and education crises in Ontario.
There's a fourth crisis they are failing to address, and that is the need for an affordable and sustainable energy future for our province.
Despite the cost of solar electricity falling rapidly, the Ford government has set up barriers to new solar projects. These barriers favour new gas and nuclear projects that are very costly, polluting and, in the case of nuclear, far off in the future.
We could greatly reduce our carbon emissions, improve our health and save billions of dollars by giving solar projects the opportunity to bid competitively for Ontario's electricity contracts. The government is currently subsidizing Hydro upward of $7 billion per year and is planning to expand the most costly sources of power generation.
This costly path is wrong for Ontario.
Lyn Adamson, Toronto
Ontario's rural voters: Are you really better off?
I'm still scratching my head when looking at the polling information and rural Ontario's love affair with Doug Ford.
Health care seems to be the number one issue with voters and rural Ontario has probably been hit the hardest with cuts and closures. I would challenge rural Ontarians to ask themselves: What have Ford's years in power done for your lives? Do you have better health care? A better education system? Do you have a family doctor? What about infrastructure? Can you afford rent?
But hey, at least you don't have to buy a licence sticker and you can get beer at the variety store.
Mike Walker, Washago, Ont.
Wayne Gretzky way offside when it comes to patriotismCanadian fans irked by Wayne Gretzky's affiliations with Trump amid tensions, Feb. 22
Millions of Canadians have adored Wayne Gretzky during and after his illustrious hockey career. Sadly, it appears our great country is no longer held dear to him. He attended Donald Trump's election night party in Mar-a-Lago sporting a MAGA hat.
In the 4 Nations Face-off championship Thursday night in Boston against Team USA, he was our honourary captain. Unlike his American counterpart, who showed up in a U.S. jersey, Gretzky sported a suit with not a trace of Canadiana. As he walked by the U.S. team he flashed a thumbs-up to them.
I have watched the Washington Capitals' Alex Ovechkin inch closer to Gretzky's career goal-scoring record and dreaded Wayne losing it. Now I can't wait for him to lose it.
Sadly, it seems Wayne is no longer one of us.
David Maki, Toronto
Vitriol directed at Gretzky is deserved
I strongly disagree Wayne Gretzky should not be chastised for his support of Trump.
When popular sports figures choose to publicly endorse political figures they can affect the outcome of elections. They are implicitly endorsing the platforms, beliefs and behaviours of those politicians. When those platforms, beliefs and behaviours are self-serving, corrupt and immoral, those popular sports figures should face the unrestrained ire of responsible voters.
Given the recent irrational assault by Trump on Canada, Canadian hockey players in the NHL should keep their opinions to themselves. Play the game, earn your millions of American dollars .
G. Evans, Burlington
Not all Americans share Trump's disdain for Canada
Recently, several letters from Americans apologizing for Trump's words and actions have been published. It is comforting to know not everyone south of the border supports him. I would like to thank those who have written, and strongly encourage them to continue their letter writing to their representatives, senators, governors and other leaders.
And a reminder to my fellow Canadians: although donations to U.S. political parties are not legal, we can donate to organizations such as the Southern Poverty Law Center.
Alice Dearden, Toronto
Fellow Canadians, let's stand up and be heard ... for onceSinger of Canadian anthem at 4 Nations Face-Off changes lyric to protest Trump's 51st state remarks, Feb. 20
To all those who chastise Canadian hockey fans who love Canada enough to take one of the few opportunities to show our displeasure about the lack of respect being shown to Canada by the American president (a criminally indicted felon, compulsive liar and narcissistic bully), I say boo .
The American people voted Trump in and it's time for them to know their vote has consequences, for them and their leader, who shows no love or respect to neighbours and allies.
To my fellow Canadians, please get off your high horse, grow a backbone and tell it like it is. That we are mad and will take every opportunity to let the U.S. know how we feel as proud and patriotic Canadians.
Respect has to be earned.
Barbara Jean-Venne, Mississauga
CAPTION: A letter writer says the time has come for Ontario to implement proportional representation.

Document 11 of 500

No matter who wins, make your vote count

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/no-matter-who-wins-make-your-vote-count/docview/3171234536/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=No+matter+who+wins%2C+make+your+vote+count&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A vote is a terrible thing to waste.
Which is why today's column is a concise voter's guide to making your vote matter more on election day Thursday.
Politicians keep telling us that campaigns matter. Judging by the dismal 44 per cent turnout in the last provincial election, most Ontarians disagree - and are disengaged.
In my previous life as a foreign correspondent, I spent 11 years in Asia and the Middle East writing about people who were willing to die for democracy.
So when I hear people here at home complain about local democracy, I feel a kind of post-democratic stress disorder. Giving up on democracy is giving in to apathy, yes, but it is more than that.
It is not just a dereliction of duty; it is a delegation of authority - empowering others while disenfranchising you. Refusing to choose is itself a decision to let others decide for you.
That's not to say choosing is easy. For it is harder than ever to discern the differences among political parties, personalities and policies.
So let me try to explain why campaigns count - and how every vote can count in its own way.
What about comparing policies?
If you listen to NDP Leader Marit Stiles and Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, the election is all about hallway health care, doctor shortages, housing shortfalls, affordability and a scandal-plagued Progressive Conservative government. If you heed PC Leader Doug Ford, it's all about affordability, fending off Donald Trump's tariffs and defending the economy - the better to bankroll health care and education.
The problem for Crombie and Stiles is that health care rarely rises to the level of a vote-determining issue in Canadian politics. Polls consistently show that Ontarians believe Ford's Tories are best-placed to tackle economic issues, including Trump's tariffs - the decisive ballot question for most voters.
Election platforms were once a big deal in Canadian politics, but the days of fully costed plans are past. The big hole in the latest PC platform is Ford's promise to dig a tunnel under the 401, which is buried deep in the document without a price tag (experts estimate it easily would cost more than $100 billion).
New Democrats have countered on traffic congestion by vowing to remove all tolls from Highway 407 - but won't disclose the multibillion-dollar cost. Liberals have also promised billions of dollars in tax cuts without saying how they'd make up for lost revenues.
And so, in the age of endless social media feeds and short attention spans, every platform looks like a meme with one recurring theme - pocketbook politics. Now, all three major parties are awkwardly aligned on tax cuts, toll cuts and fee cuts while magically boosting government services and supports.
The leaders held back their putative platforms until the campaign's final days, leaving voters less time to read up because none of it adds up. It is the banality and vanity of small differences.
So what's the campaign really about?
This election is less a policy debate and more a personality contest. Ford has grown on Ontarians, partly because he has grown up - he's less bombastic than before and more folksy; less chippy and more chipper.
That said, if you do the math, the surveys show a theoretical majority of voters are opposed to Ford's Tories. So why does he keep winning?
Historically, Ford's opponents couldn't agree on the opposition party best placed to displace him. Centrist and left-leaning voters split their votes among rival "progressive" parties - Liberals, New Democrats and Greens - allowing Tories to come up the middle.
How can votes count more?
Electoral reform won't happen any time soon in Ford's Ontario. Rival parties won't form a common front or come together because they are too tribal. The only way to catch the front-runner is for progressive voters to back the horse with the best chance of catching up.
New Democrats keep insisting only they have what it takes, because they won more seats in the last two elections.
All they needed was more Liberal voters swinging behind them.
Yet that's precisely what happened in 2018 and 2022, when voters put the Liberal dynasty in the penalty box after 15 years in power. Voters swung over to the NDP, yet the result was two missed opportunities for the opposition.
Even when the stars were in alignment for New Democrats, they hit a ceiling. They won the consolation prize of "official Opposition" (with extra legislative funding), while the Liberals languished. But what does the NDP have to show for two terms of supposedly holding the Tories to account?
Today, Ford is coasting to re-election as premier, notwithstanding his blunders on housing, health care and education. Meanwhile, the Liberals have leapfrogged ahead of the NDP as their brand rebounds.
No matter how many times New Democrats introduce Stiles as "the next premier of Ontario," it is a delusion wrapped in an illusion inside an election. Unable to defeat Ford in 2018, 2022 and now 2025, how many more chances does the NDP deserve?
The Liberals, like it or not, have a fighting chance - if not now, then in future. Since becoming leader, Crombie has not exactly caught fire but she is catching up to Ford after running a credible campaign, hovering at 30 per cent in most polls compared to 20 per cent or less for the NDP.
Her party is strongest in the GTA and Ottawa, but weak elsewhere. The Liberal party she took over in late 2023 is starting far behind in the seat count.
And yet the fact that her Liberals are weak in many rural ridings and the southwest is not a reason to abandon them, but all the more reason to shore up their base - so they can rebuild for the future. For unlike the NDP, Liberals are not condemned to the purgatory of perpetual opposition.
In truth, Ontario politics today has more to do with geography than ideology, with each party relying on a regional power base and erratic pockets of support. The NDP that once boasted about being the conscience of parliament acts more like a boutique party that has become a boon for Ford's Tories.
I don't expect diehard New Democrats to defect, even if their cause is doomed. It's their democratic right to elect another right-wing government by default.
But when interested voters ask me who can defeat Ford's Tories, I tell them that every election offers a choice - even if they don't like the options. It's a hard question worth asking, even if you don't like the answer.
This election is not about who will be premier, for that is preordained by Ford's cynically clever timing. It's about the race for second place - and who can do a better job of holding Ford's feet to the fire after the last seven years of lukewarm opposition that laid the groundwork for another four years of PC government.
That's democracy.
CREDIT: Martin Regg Cohn OPINION

Subject: Provincial elections; Political campaigns; Scandals; Democracy; Tax cuts; Voters; Politics; Voter behavior; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Tax cuts Tariffs

Document 12 of 500

Mark Carney and Pierre Poilievre both say they'll get more homes built - a lot more. Here's what they're missing

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/mark-carney-pierre-poilievre-both-say-theyll-get/docview/3171234454/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Mark+Carney+and+Pierre+Poilievre+both+say+they%27ll+get+more+homes+built+-+a+lot+more.+Here%27s+what+they%27re+missing&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Mark Carney, the perceived front-runner to replace Justin Trudeau as Liberal party leader and prime minister, has made homebuilding a priority is his leadership bid. Specifically, he's promised to "catalyze enormous private investment to build millions of new homes for younger Canadians" and remove regulatory barriers that slow the construction of new housing. Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre has also promised to cut red tape to boost housing construction with his "Building Homes, Not Bureaucracy" plan.
Of course, Canada has a housing affordability problem, as millions of Canadians can attest, and regulations, fees and density restrictions imposed on housing developers have undoubtedly increased construction costs and driven up housing prices. So mitigating these impediments would help increase the supply of new housing units in Canada.
But if policymakers, including the next prime minister (whoever that may be) want to substantially increase housing affordability, they must also enact policies - tax reform, spending reductions - that improve the savings and consumption decisions of households and governments. It's not just a red tape problem.
Consider this. According to a 2023 Canadian Mortgage and Housing Corporation (CMHC) report, to restore housing affordability Canada must build an estimated 3.5 million additional housing units - beyond the 18.2 million units it projects will exist - by 2030.
But building 3.5 million housing units will require a massive amount of financing. How much?
According to an analysis of "affordable" housing construction in Toronto, it costs between $472,000 and $520,000 per housing unit (obviously homebuilding in Toronto is often more expensive than in other parts of Canada, but these estimates exclude land purchase costs they're still useful). Based on the lower-cost estimate ($472,000), total construction costs for 3.5 million housing units will reach $1.65 trillion from 2025 to 2030 or around $330 billion per year.
Clearly, for this scale of building, we'll need an increased supply of financial capital. Canada's gross savings (essentially, the savings used to fund investments) was $695 billion in 2023, so Canadians would need to increase their annual savings by almost 50 per cent (above the 2023 total) to finance 3.5 million additional housing units. That's not a realistic goal. Moreover, given the Trudeau government's accelerated rates of immigration in 2023 and 2024, the CMHC's 3.5 million number is likely now too low to achieve "affordability" so the price tag would be even higher.
So, if domestic savings won't adequately finance the mass scale of homebuilding envisaged by Carney, Poilievre and others, where will the capital come from?
Don't look abroad. It's highly unlikely that inflows of foreign capital would help fill the savings gap to any significant extent - in Canada and other wealthy countries, domestic savings remain the main source of financing for domestic investments. To spur domestic savings, policymakers might also look to the Bank of Canada and monetary policy. But while raising interest rates would encourage increased domestic savings, it would also choke off demand for construction loans, which would slow housing construction. Higher mortgage rates would also exacerbate the affordability problem for many current and prospective homeowners.
In reality, any serious effort to increase housing affordability in Canada must include fundamental changes in fiscal policy to promote increased savings and reduced consumption by both households and governments. For example, the federal government and provincial governments should increase sales tax rates and reduce taxes on investment income including real estate. And beginning this budget season, governments should reduce spending to tackle deficits, particularly in Ontario, British Columbia and at the federal level. By reducing deficit spending, governments would free up savings for additional investment in residential housing construction.
We've heard no such policy proposals from those vying to be Canada's next prime minister. Until we do, Canadians should be skeptical about the easy promises of politicians - all of political stripes - to solve Canada's housing affordability problem.
CAPTION: A file photo of new builds of townhomes in Pickering.
CREDIT: Steven Globerman, Contributor

Document 13 of 500

Doug Ford has fallen short. Vote for change

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-has-fallen-short-vote-change/docview/3171234451/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+has+fallen+short.+Vote+for+change&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford was elected premier seven years ago on a promise to address our multiple and overlapping crises. The cost of housing was skyrocketing, family doctors were highly coveted but rarely found, schools were crumbling. Ford reasonably argued that the Liberals had had their chance and failed. He vowed to do better. Now, as Ford and his Progressive Conservatives ask for your vote for a third time, consider this: each of the crises he promised to fix has gotten worse.
Under Ford's leadership, population growth continues to outpace housing supply, driving ever upward the cost of homes and rent. Many young people can't afford to live in the city where they work. Many Ontarians are fleeing the province for more affordable locales. Meanwhile, the homeless population is rising and food-bank demand continues to soar. Ford recognizes the problem. He promised to build 1.5 million new homes and created a task force to advise him on how to do it. Then he ignored most of its recommendations.
Or consider the family-doctor crisis that has tormented so many Ontarians and further strained an already overburdened health-care system. Ford's health ministry said last year that this was "not a major concern." Then, under political pressure, the government finally released something of a plan. But compare it to the more robust and detailed proposals of any other major party, and you'd be forgiven for thinking the PCs were still not majorly concerned.
On education, Ford deserves credit for making record investments in our K-12 system. Nevertheless, after seven years, our schools are falling apart. Some 84 per cent of Toronto public schools are in substandard condition, according to the Financial Accountability Office. Meanwhile, amid persistent staffing shortages, boards are sometimes bringing in untrained replacement teachers to try to manage badly overcrowded and under-resourced classrooms.
All this has not come cheap. The Ford government has increased the province's debt by more than $116 billion, running multibillion-dollar deficits every year and earning the ire of conservative groups such as the Fraser Institute in the process. Some of this spending, such as on education and infrastructure, was needed. But far too much has been spent on cynical politicking: More than $3 billion for the $200 cheques the province sent to every adult, including very rich ones. More than $600 million to get beer and wine in corner stores one year early. Nearly $200 million for an early election, which Ford said was necessary so he could get a clear mandate to deal with U.S. President Donald Trump. Never mind that he already had a clear mandate, especially to deal with the crises in housing, health care and education that have worsened during his tenure.
Ford is not without redeeming qualities. He connects with people. Many seem to trust him as safe hands during tumultuous times. He has professionalized his office after a shambolic first phase of his premiership, one marked by outrageous statements, senseless policy lurches and shocking cronyism. He has often worked effectively with the federal government, despite political differences, making significant and much-needed investments in infrastructure. His willingness to change his mind, as he did on the Greenbelt - and, it seems, on Trump - is laudable, even if we'd rather he showed more discernment in the first place.
And to be sure, the PCs have faced significant headwinds. The pandemic and the persistent high interest rates and supply-chain disruptions that resulted would have made transformative progress difficult for any government.
But in hard times, Ford and his PCs have too often chosen easy political gimmicks, showing themselves to be not as serious as the challenges we face.
Fortunately, Ontarians have three credible alternatives, and a vote for any would be a well-spent vote for change. The Liberals, NDP and Greens have all put forward a slate of serious proposals that, if implemented, would at least put a dent in the defining challenges of the moment. Each understands the need to invest urgently to build more homes, recruit more doctors and do more to restore the province's education system.
Liberal leader Bonnie Crombie, for instance, has at times proved a worthy adversary for Ford. An effective critic of the government's record, she seems to get under the PC leader's skin. When she became Liberal leader, having spent a decade as mayor of Mississauga, she took over a party in the wilderness. She deserves credit, if the polls are any indication, for bringing it back into the political mainstream. Her party's platform contains many sensible, moderate proposals that seem suited to the political moment, though she would do well to be more forthright about how she's going to pay for them. Efficiencies alone would not cover billions in new spending, particularly if the Liberals were to follow through with their proposed deep tax cuts.
Marit Stiles's NDP has made a persuasive case that there can be no solution to the province's cost-of-living crisis without active government, including robust social programs. The party's platform is full of bold ideas worthy of consideration, though again, the gap between proposed new revenues and proposed new spending is troubling, as is the plan to increase the capital gains tax in this moment of Trump-induced investment uncertainty. Stiles is an articulate, authentic and likable politician. But two years into her tenure as NDP leader, she's still struggling to get the attention of Ontarians. The province would be better off if she could find a way to change that.
Green leader Mike Schreiner is a chipper wonk whose enthusiasm for policy is infectious. His platform offers perhaps the most nuanced approach to the housing crisis, recognizing a role for both market and government solutions. Urged to run for the Liberal leadership in 2023, Schreiner admirably chose instead the slow and doubtless frustrating work of building the Green caucus one seat at a time.
As comforting as continuity may seem, what Ontario needs is change.
CAPTION: Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles, Ontario PC Party Leader Doug Ford, Ontario Liberal Party Leader Bonnie Crombie and Ontario Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner at the CBC Broadcast Centre in Toronto on Monday, Feb. 17, 2025. THE CANADIAN PRESS/Chris Young
CREDIT: Star Editorial Board
