Document 14 of 500

Crombie touts Carney's experience: Provincial leader says former BoC governor best bet to stand up to Trump

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/crombie-touts-carneys-experience/docview/3171232987/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Crombie+touts+Carney%27s+experience%3A+Provincial+leader+says+former+BoC+governor+best+bet+to+stand+up+to+Trump&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Bonnie Crombie is touting her decision to endorse federal Liberal leadership candidate Mark Carney with just two days until Ontario's election.
"This is the right moment in time, and I think he is the right leader for this moment," the Ontario Liberal leader said Tuesday in Toronto when asked if the move - first disclosed by the Star - was a distraction from the provincial campaign.
Crombie, who trails Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford in public opinion polls, implied that Carney is best placed to handle tariff threats from the U.S. president as a former governor of the Bank of Canada and Bank of England.
"He's a leader that can stand up to Donald Trump and speak with some authority and experience," she added of Carney, who is widely considered the front-runner in the March 9 contest to succeed Prime Minister Justin Trudeau.
Polls suggest Carney is narrowing a popularity gap long enjoyed by federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre.
"But he's also a leader who will get the basics right for people, and that's my focus here in Ontario today," Crombie said, highlighting her plan to get every Ontarian a family doctor, cut taxes and oppose Ford's push for a multibillion-dollar "fantasy" tunnel under Highway 401.
Throughout the provincial campaign, Ford's argument to voters has been that he is better qualified than Crombie or NDP Leader Marit Stiles to handle Trump's tariff threat, promising billions in supports to offset damage to Ontario's trade-dependent economy.
In Ottawa, Ford said he stands ready to order U.S. beer, wine and spirits off LCBO shelves immediately should Trump's first tranche of tariffs take effect March 4 as scheduled.
"There's 3,600 products from 35 states, so there's going to be a lot of upset Americans that they can't ship their booze up to Ontario," Ford, who is seeking a third term as premier, told reporters at a Porter Airlines hangar.
He also pledged to bar U.S. companies from Ontario contracts and to put unspecified retaliatory tariffs on the province's electricity exports to U.S. states.
Ford referenced the change in federal polling numbers showing Poilievre's lead over the Liberals apparently shrinking, particularly if Carney becomes leader.
"There's probably 1,000 different reasons why the gap is closed and that's going to be up to the federal parties to figure that out," he said.
Crombie acknowledged last week in a meeting with the Star's editorial board that Carney's rise in popularity has been helpful to her party, which had been hindered by Trudeau's unpopularity.
"There's a lot of interaction between the teams," she said Tuesday, speaking of Carney supporters and her own. "He supported me in leadership as well."
After the Star reported the news Tuesday, Carney reacted on social media.
"Thank you @BonnieCrombie - for your support, for your strong advocacy to help Ontarians get better access to a family doctor, and for putting economic growth first," he tweeted.
"Wishing you the best this Thursday, and let's keep building!"
Last June, Carney held a Bay Street fundraiser for Crombie and the two are to meet before Thursday's Ontario vote "to discuss the economy and health care," a Liberal source said, speaking confidentially in order to discuss internal deliberations.
Stiles was in London on Tuesday, where her party holds all three city ridings, reminding voters of her plan to help families deal with the rising costs of living with help that includes monthly grocery rebates and more stringent rent control.
"Your vote has real power," she said in a statement.
"As premier, I will fight against rising costs to help families get ahead."
CAPTION: Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, shown at a campaign event in Toronto Tuesday, suggested that federal Liberal leadership candidate Mark Carney is best placed to handle tariff threats from the U.S.Chris Young THE CANADIAN PRESS
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson, Robert Benzie and Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Document 15 of 500

Businesses file suit over Bloor St. W. bike lanes: Group seeking $10 million from city for 'negligence and nuisance,' says congestion driving customers away

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/businesses-file-suit-over-bloor-st-w-bike-lanes/docview/3171232964/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Businesses+file+suit+over+Bloor+St.+W.+bike+lanes%3A+Group+seeking+%2410+million+from+city+for+%27negligence+and+nuisance%2C%27+says+congestion+driving+customers+away&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: More than 40 Etobicoke business owners have joined in a lawsuit against the City of Toronto, its transportation manager and a city councillor over the dedicated bike lanes installed along Bloor Street West in 2023 and 2024.
The group is seeking $10 million from the city for "negligence and nuisance." It's suing Barbara Gray, general manager, transportation services, and Morley (Ward 3 Etobicoke-Lakeshore) for "misfeasance of public office," according to the statement of claim, filed in Ontario Superior Court on Friday. Coun. Amber Morley is also named in the suit.
The businesses, including restaurants, retailers and service operators, among others, are also seeking an injunction requiring that the bike lanes be removed and the streets returned to their prior state.
The statement of claim has not been tested in court. It typically takes weeks or months for defendants in a lawsuit to respond with a statement of defence.
Neither Morley nor the city responded to requests from the Star for comment.
Sam Pappas, owner of the Crooked Cue, and a participant in the lawsuit, said business is down between 20 and 60 per cent at some establishments, because congestion caused by the bike lanes is driving customers away.
"We're just fighting for our community," said Pappas, adding that there is a feeling that the city's consultations were performative, not authentic.
He said the lawsuit isn't just about bike lanes, it's about letting city hall know it can't ignore what local neighbourhoods think. "This is a shot across the bow, saying 'we're not going to take it.' "
The lawsuit alleges that the city's planning, consultation and implementation were "wilfully lacking in candour, frankness and impartiality" and breached the duty of care the city owed them.
The statement of claim also accuses the city of manipulating and/or misrepresenting data, bicycle counts and the public consultation process that took place.
According to the statement of claim, Morley misrepresented the nature and extent of public consultations, failed to conduct adequate door-to-door consultations with businesses, and gave preferential treatment to CycleTO, a pro-cycling group.
The statement alleges Gray allowed herself to be unlawfully lobbied by CycleTO; and that overall, the city, Morley and Gray showed "reckless disregard, high-handed and egregious treatment of said businesses."
Michael Longfield, executive director of CycleTO, said the lanes were approved by city council before Morley was elected.
"The city is pretty deliberate in how it plans out its cycling structure," said Longfield.
He said his organization wasn't lobbying Gray.
"I understand not everyone is in favour of the project, but I think there is a difference between people being consulted and their opinion ultimately being the one that prevails," Longfield said.
Premier Doug Ford took a personal interest in the issue late last year, resulting in provincial legislation restricting new bike lanes and setting the stage for the removal of the bike lanes on Bloor Street West.
CycleTO is seeking a provincial injunction and mounting a Charter challenge to prevent that from happening.
The lawsuit also accuses the city of failing to properly monitor the outcomes of the bike lanes, including their impact on emergency response times.
The Bloor Street West Complete Street Extension was a two-part project, begun in 2023 and completed in 2024, running from Runnymede Road to Resurrection Road, linking Bloor West Village to the Kingsway neighbourhood.
The businesses say the bike lanes have proven to be "an unmitigated disaster," resulting in traffic congestion, a reduction in emergency response times and "erosion of the neighbourhood character," a decline in property standards and a loss of business and foot traffic.
"The Plaintiffs plead that the foregoing harms were a foreseeable consequence of the Bike Lanes Extension, and that the Defendants nonetheless dogmatically pursued the project for improper purposes entirely, disconnected from the purposes of the project, their statutory grant of authority, or their duty of care," according to the lawsuit.
Cody MacRae, founder of the community group Balance on Bloor, said the bike lanes have resulted in more cars taking shortcuts through residential neighbourhoods, creating a hazard for children playing outdoors.
"They're poorly designed bike lanes," said MacRae, adding that Morley's local leadership on the issue was lacking.
"This community has been dismissed and ignored for more than a year."
CAPTION: Etobicoke businesses say bike lanes along Bloor Street West are eating into emergency response times, eroding neighbourhood character and cutting business and foot traffic.Michelle Mengsu Chang Toronto Star file photo
CREDIT: Francine Kopun Senior Writer

Document 16 of 500

Did Ford actually deliver on health care?: With election day nearing, experts examine how effective the province has been in three key areas

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/did-ford-actually-deliver-on-health-care/docview/3171232945/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Did+Ford+actually+deliver+on+health+care%3F%3A+With+election+day+nearing%2C+experts+examine+how+effective+the+province+has+been+in+three+key+areas&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Health care remains a top concern for many voters as they head to the polls to vote for Ontario's next government.
Ending "hallway health care" and improving the provincial health-care system were big promises in the Progressive Conservative Party's campaign in 2018.
How effective has the PC government been at delivering better health care for Ontarians since they came to power?
With the COVID-19 pandemic diverting many resources to the crisis, Sara Allin, associate professor of health policy at the University of Toronto, said there is a lack of data to properly assess the government's health-care investments overall.
"That in itself is a concern, something that I would see as a sort of failure of governance, of leadership, of a system," Allin said.
"We need to be able to show (the status of the health system) to the public and we're not seeing that."
With election day coming Thursday, the Star spoke with health-care experts and examined the dollars spent to get a better look at the government's recent record in three areas: long-term care, mental health and addictions, and hospital infrastructure.
Long-term care
In 2018, the PC government promised to create 15,000 long-term-care beds in five years and 30,000 beds over 10 years, so as to reduce hospital overcrowding and wait-lists by freeing up beds taken by "alternate level of care" patients.
In the 2024 provincial budget, the province reported that $6.4 billion has been invested in long-term care since 2019. The budget added an investment of $155 million in new funds for this year. More than 18,000 beds are "open, under construction, or have approval to start construction," while plans call for 58,000 new and upgraded beds by 2028.
When the pandemic hit, the government invested heavily in long-term-care facilities and personal support workers, with $2 billion in new funding from 2020 to 2022.
But despite these investments, the 2024 efficiency report by the Ontario Hospitals Association reported the number of alternate level of care (ALC) patients "reached a record high" in January 2024.
The Ontario Long-Term Care Association's 2025 pre-budget submission to the government states 48,000 Ontario seniors are currently on the wait-list for long-term care, with most waiting well over a year for placement.
"Ontario's long-term-care sector is at a critical juncture, with OLTCA member homes concerned about their ability to meet the capacity and care needs of their current and future residents," the report states.
Mental health and addictions
Mental health and addictions is an area where the provincial government has "not kept up in terms of the cost" of services given increased need and inflation, said Canadian Mental Health Association Ontario CEO Camille Quenneville.
"We still see the impact in terms of people who since the pandemic, are sicker than they were before, the clients' needs are greater," she said, adding Ontario loses seven people a day to opioid overdoses, numbers she calls "alarming."
Building on its 2018 campaign promise, the PC government announced its"Roadmap to Wellness" plan in 2020 to invest $1.9 billion over 10 years in mental health, addictions and housing supports, matched by the federal government for a total $3.8 billion.
Between 2018 and 2024, over $500 million was invested as part of "Roadmap to Wellness."
The 2024 budget pledged $396 million in new funding over three years to expand mental health and addictions recovery and treatment services. But despite the number of funded programs, the 2024 performance audit of Ontario's Opioid Strategy stated there has been "limited evaluation of the strategy's impacts and outcomes."
The Canadian Institute of Health Information also showed Ontario had no data on the median wait time for community mental health counselling between 2023 and 2024. "We are so far behind in what the rest of the health-care system takes for granted in terms of not only ongoing stable funding, but in terms of having the metrics to be able to deliver and understand the impact of the services they're delivering," Quenneville said.
Hospital infrastructure
As a key part of reducing surgical backlogs and hospital wait times, the PCs announced in 2018 a $27-billion investment in hospital infrastructure over 10 years.
The government has since spent more than $8.8 billion on hospital infrastructure, adding more than 3,500 hospital beds across Ontario.
In addition, during the pandemic, the PC government invested heavily in hospital repairs, beds, virtual care options and relieving hospitals' financial pressures with roughly $7.5 billion between 2020 and 2022.
Staffing metrics and hospital workforces have increased - although not evenly across the province - through hospital efforts and government funding and policy initiatives.
But Ontario Health data obtained by the Star's Kenyon Wallace shows that between 2021-22 and October 2024, patients across Ontario spent more time in EDs compared with the previous 13 years.
The Canadian Institute of Health Information examined clinical wait times for two different surgeries and found while Ontario was behind most provinces on meeting the 48-hour benchmark for hip fracture surgeries, Ontario was leading the country on the 26-week benchmark for joint replacements.
"There's a celebrating of a lot of the investments, but there's not a lot of this acknowledgment of what the data are showing," Allin said.
Allin said the provincial government needs to work alongside health-care stakeholders to implement change. "If they're not able to do that then these indicators will never improve."
CAPTION: The Cortellucci Vaughan Hospital, which opened in 2021. Since 2018, when the Progressive Conservatives announced a $27-billion investment in hospital infrastructure over 10 years, the government has spent more than $8.8 billion, adding more than 3,500 beds.R.J. JohnstonToronto Star file photo
CREDIT: Joy SpearChief-Morris Toronto Star

Document 17 of 500

A lingering housing crisis: Despite pledge to build, province lags on starts

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/lingering-housing-crisis/docview/3171232776/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+lingering+housing+crisis%3A+Despite+pledge+to+build%2C+province+lags+on+starts&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Today, the Star looks at why our housing crisis has gotten so bad, an issue selected by 38 per cent of respondents.
Ontario's housing affordability crisis persists more than two years after the last provincial election.
While the average price of a home in Ontario has dropped since the pandemic peaked in 2022 - as has the national average - the province still has a housing market that is unaffordable for many people.
The economic strain caused by the pandemic prompted the Bank of Canada to hike interest rates, which hurt housing affordability across the country. Now, the income required to pay for housing costs remains high but is highest in Ontario and B.C. To address the issue, provincial governments have pledged to rapidly build more supply but Ontario lags when it comes to housing starts.
"The issue in Ontario is we've gotten so unaffordable that having slight decreases in prices doesn't address the problem," said Mike Moffatt, economist and senior director of the think tank Smart Prosperity Institute. "We have such a large hole to fill that we're looking at a decade or more before we return to any kind of reasonable standard of affordability."
How did prices get
so high so fast?
The real estate market is hypersensitive to the Bank of Canada's interest rate changes. When interest rates were at their lowest during the 2022 provincial election, it was cheaper for borrowers to take out mortgages spurring a real estate feeding frenzy - buyers and investors piled into the market causing prices to surge. Toronto, the province's hottest market, saw its average home price reach $1.33 million in February 2022.
But once the Bank of Canada began to quickly hike its key lending rate to cool the rising inflation that followed the pandemic, it dampened the housing market. Sales activity dropped and remained sluggish in 2023 and 2024.
The central bank recently switched course. With inflation under control, it began its interest rate cut cycle in June 2024 taking its key rate to three per cent from five per cent.
That has had an impact on housing prices. In the last two years, the average price of a home in Ontario was $964,500 in June 2022 (the last provincial election), then dropped to $850,000 by the end of 2024, according to the Canadian Real Estate Association.
However, in June 2018 when Premier Doug Ford first took office, the average price of an Ontario home was $598,700 representing a 42 per cent increase in seven years. Across Canada, home prices also increased during the same time period but at a rate of 29 per cent, and the national average price for a home is $150,000 less compared with Ontario.
In Toronto, covering homeownership expenses - mortgage cost, property tax, maintenance - requires more than 75 per cent of a median household's income, which is "still far exceeding" the 30 per cent threshold that defines housing affordability, according to an RBC report.
Vancouver's homeownership expenses are the highest in Canada, requiring at 96.7 per cent of median income, and Edmonton's are the least expensive at 33.6 per cent. Nationally, the number is 58.4 per cent.
What about rentals?
In 2024, average asking rents in Canada decreased for the first time since the pandemic with the largest decreases seen in Toronto and Vancouver. That was caused in part by lower interest rates, which allowed some renters to buy property, and lower immigration levels, which reduced demand.
But since the 2018 Ontario election, rent for a two-bedroom apartment in the province is up around 40 per cent, averaging $1,758 in October 2024. The province's rent increase in seven years is the same as the national average, but nationally, a two-bedroom unit averages $1,447 - a $300 decrease, according to Canada Mortgage and Housing Corp. (CMHC).
"Ontario's rents are still considerably higher than the national average and all other provinces except B.C.," said Ricardo Tranjan, senior researcher with the Canadian Centre for Policy Alternatives.
Why is there not enough supply?
Building more housing stock is necessary to alleviate upward price pressure on housing, experts say.
"Policymakers can create significant measures to get at the core issue which is that there hasn't been enough supply for the strong demand which was exacerbated during the pandemic," said Robert Hogue, assistant chief economist at RBC. That's why the Ontario government announced a goal of building 1.5 million homes by 2031, which means around 150,000 homes need to be built every year.
This year, the province will fall 5,800 homes short of its 92,300 goal. Current economic factors and aftershocks of the pandemic have led to a slowdown in construction starts across Canada. But Ontario seems to have a harder time getting homes built compared to other provinces, said Moffatt. In 2024, Ontario registered 74,326 housing starts - almost 5,000 fewer than in 2017, according to CMHC.
Over the last six years, Ontario has ranked in the bottom half of provinces in terms of homebuilding per capita.
"Examining Canada's 100 largest communities paints a stark picture of Ontario's inability to build homes," Moffatt said in a November 2024 report, adding only three communities, Pickering, Oakville, and Kitchener, had housing starts per capita in the top 20. British Columbia had eight communities crack the top 20, while Quebec had four.
There are a number of reasons why Ontario falls behind other provinces, Moffatt said, which include higher municipal and development charges, restrictive zoning prohibiting densification, and municipal permitting delays.
But at the end of the day, "Municipalities are creatures of the province. The buck stops with the province," he added.
CREDIT: Clarrie Feinstein Toronto Star

Subject: Mortgages; Provinces; Provincial elections; Interest rates; Affordable housing; Housing starts; Pandemics; Housing prices; Home ownership

Business indexing term: Subject: Mortgages Interest rates Affordable housing Housing starts Housing prices

Document 18 of 500

If we give Doug Ford four more years, we'll regret it long after he's gone

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/if-we-give-doug-ford-four-more-years-well-regret/docview/3171231807/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=If+we+give+Doug+Ford+four+more+years%2C+we%27ll+regret+it+long+after+he%27s+gone&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Driving around the GTA this weekend, I saw blue, red, orange and green signs for the candidates vying for MPP along the streets. Some of the blue PC signs were paired with posters declaring, "Canada is not for sale."
Canada's not for sale, but we've seen that parts of Ontario are on the clearance rack. Premier Doug Ford was willing to sell away the Greenbelt and Ontario Place for the best price. When pushed at last week's debate, Ford defended himself by saying, "I was, then I wasn't then I went ahead" and cancelled the sale of the Greenbelt to developers.
C'mon, Doug.
Even when he does something where I can see reason, his approach is reactionary and without a well-thought-out plan. The Progressive Conservative leader is also shutting down safe injection sites. I can fully understand why residents don't want people with addictions in their neighbourhoods, near schools or parks. The plan is to open Homelessness and Addictions Recovery Treatment (HART) sites, but they won't offer consumption services, safe supply of drugs or needle exchange programs. So, what's the rest of the plan? We don't want people dying on our sidewalks from overdoses.
Tomorrow is Ontario's provincial election, and I'm worried. We are an apathetic bunch, and apathy has never changed the status quo. Last election, back in 2022, saw the lowest voter turnout in Ontario's history - just 44 per cent. In 2025, only six per cent of eligible voters cast their ballots during the three days of advance voting.
People across the province have been complaining about healthcare, food prices, lack of affordable housing, education, traffic congestion and more in the GTA.
If you want to change the state of things, this is your time to get to the polls and vote.
Premier Ford says he called a snap election to secure a stronger mandate to fight U.S. president Donald Trump's threatened tariffs and talk of annexation. Cool, cool. I get that.
But if Ford hasn't used his current mandate to make life better for Ontarians by hiring more family doctors, building affordable housing, easing traffic congestion (maybe without a tunnel under the 401-the price tag could be upwards of $100 billion), increasing transit and eliminating transit deserts (I see you, northeast Scarborough), keeping small town emergency rooms open, and all the other things we've asked for that have been ignored, why should we believe him? What's more: why should we re-elect him?
The PC government has committed to increasing healthcare funding (with no specific figures or details on allocation), removing certain bike lanes in Toronto due to complaints about traffic congestion, investing billions of dollars in unspecified spending, and lowering the minimum price of alcohol - whether or not anyone asked him to.
That's all fine, but for the last two years and change, the Ford government has had plenty of opportunities to follow through on their promises to "Protect Ontario." But they simply haven't.
I will never tell anyone who to vote for. Vote your values and for people who won't actively harm you or your community. If we want to see Ontario prosper, the values of political parties and their leaders need to align with our values. They need to commit to long-term, sustainable, and necessary change.
But when our leaders offer nonsense initiatives with no details attached to appease us and focus on the wrong priorities, we all lose.
For many of us, that's not enough to motivate us to vote. Apathy - and the idea that someone else will save us - gets in our way. We find reasons not to demand answers, not to read platforms, and not to learn where candidates stand. We put on blinders and ignore the chaos around us. We shrug our shoulders - then spend the next few years suffering under elected officials who do everything except what we actually want and need.
If that's what you want, stay home tomorrow and ignore what's happening. But if you want real change, go and vote - because I promise you, if you sit this election out, we'll all pay for it for years to come.
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford walks towards the offices of Lt.-Gov. Edith Dumont before requesting to dissolve the provincial parliament and hold elections, at Queens Park, the provincial legislature, in Toronto, Tuesday, Jan. 28, 2025.
CREDIT: Shellene Drakes-Tull

Subject: Provincial elections; Apathy; Affordable housing; Traffic congestion; Elections

Business indexing term: Subject: Affordable housing

Location: Canada

Title: If we give Doug Ford four more years, we'll regret it long after he's gone

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2025

Publication date: Feb 26, 2025

Section: opinion/contributors

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto

Country of publication: Canada

Letters: Ontario polls suggesting victory for Doug Ford with only days to go before provincial vote, Feb. 24 'We are going in the wrong direction': Why last year saw Ontario burn a record amount of natural gas, Feb. 24 Canadian fans irked by Wayne Gretzky's affiliations with Trump amid tensions, Feb. 22 Singer of Canadian anthem at 4 Nations Face-Off changes lyric to protest Trump's 51st state remarks, Feb. 20

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3171231725/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Ontario+polls+suggesting+victory+for+Doug+Ford+with+only+days+to+go+before+provincial+vote%2C+Feb.+24+%27We+are+going+in+the+wrong+direction%27%3A+Why+last+year+saw+Ontario+burn+a+record+amount+of+natural+gas%2C+Feb.+24+Canadian+fans+irked+by+Wayne+Gretzky%27s+affiliations+with+Trump+amid+tensions%2C+Feb.+22+Singer+of+Canadian+anthem+at+4+Nations+Face-Off+changes+lyric+to+protest+Trump%27s+51st+state+remarks%2C+Feb.+20&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's time for proportional representation
Ontario polls suggesting victory for Doug Ford with only days to go before provincial vote, Feb. 24
When speaking with Star political columnist Martin Regg Cohn during an interview on The Dais, at the Toronto Metropolitan University, he said: "We all know that one of the reasons the Tories are in power now is because the Liberals and the New Democrats are tearing each other down."
I was pleased to hear the NDP's Marit Stiles voice her strong support for proportional representation.
Proportional representation is crucial for fostering a more inclusive democracy, not only in Ontario but across Canada, as it ensures that all parties, and by extension a vast majority of voters, have a genuine voice in the decision-making process. In today's political climate, it is vital we do not hand over a majority mandate to a party supported by a minority of voters.
The adoption of proportional representation would enhance the democratic framework of our provinces and our country. It would provide equitable representation, which is essential for the legitimacy and accountability of our political institutions. I encourage ongoing discussions around this important topic and hope that more politicians, especially those with a large platform like Stiles, will continue to advocate for a system that truly reflects the will of the people.
Sara Crane, Chatsworth, Ont.
Ontario lacking visionary plan for its energy future
'We are going in the wrong direction': Why last year saw Ontario burn a record amount of natural gas, Feb. 24
Thank you to the Toronto Star for three recent opeds on the failure of Doug Ford's government to address the health, housing and education crises in Ontario.
There's a fourth crisis they are failing to address, and that is the need for an affordable and sustainable energy future for our province.
Despite the cost of solar electricity falling rapidly, the Ford government has set up barriers to new solar projects. These barriers favour new gas and nuclear projects that are very costly, polluting and, in the case of nuclear, far off in the future.
We could greatly reduce our carbon emissions, improve our health and save billions of dollars by giving solar projects the opportunity to bid competitively for Ontario's electricity contracts. The government is currently subsidizing hydro upward of $7 billion per year and is planning to expand the most costly sources of power generation. This costly path is wrong for Ontario.
Lyn Adamson, Toronto
Are rural voters really better off?
I'm still scratching my head when looking at the polling information and rural Ontario's love affair with Doug Ford.
Health care seems to be the No. 1 issue with voters and rural Ontario has probably been hit the hardest with cuts and closures. I would challenge rural Ontarians to ask themselves: What have Ford's years in power done for your lives? Do you have better health care? A better education system? Do you have a family doctor? What about infrastructure? Can you afford rent?
Mike Walker, Washago, Ont.
The Great One
is way offside
Canadian fans irked by Wayne Gretzky's affiliations with Trump amid tensions, Feb. 22
Millions of Canadians have adored Wayne Gretzky during and after his illustrious hockey career. Sadly, it appears our great country is no longer held dear to him. He attended Donald Trump's election night party in Mar-a-Lago sporting a MAGA hat.
In the 4 Nations Face-Off championship last Thursday night in Boston against Team USA, he was our honorary captain. Unlike his American counterpart, who showed up in a U.S. jersey, Gretzky sported a suit with not a trace of Canadiana. As he walked by the U.S. team he flashed a thumbs-up to them. I have watched the Washington Capitals' Alex Ovechkin inch closer to Gretzky's career goal-scoring record and dreaded Wayne losing it. Now I can't wait for him to lose it.
Sadly, it seems Wayne is no longer one of us.
David Maki, Toronto
Gretzky vitriol
is well deserved
I strongly disagree that Wayne Gretzky shouldn't be chastised for his support of Donald Trump. When popular sports figures choose to publicly endorse political figures they can affect the outcome of elections. They are implicitly endorsing the platforms, beliefs and behaviours of those politicians. When those platforms, beliefs and behaviours are self-serving, corrupt and immoral, those popular sports figures should face the unrestrained ire of responsible voters.
Given the recent irrational assault by Trump on Canada, Canadian hockey players in the NHL should keep their opinions to themselves.
G. Evans, Burlington
Appreciation for all the letters from Americans
Recently, several letters from Americans apologizing for Donald Trump's words and actions have been published. It is comforting to know not everyone south of the border supports him. I would like to thank those who have written, and strongly encourage them to continue their letter writing to their representatives, senators, governors and other leaders.
And a reminder to my fellow Canadians: although donations to U.S. political parties are not legal, we can donate to organizations such as the Southern Poverty Law Center.
Alice Dearden, Toronto
Canadians, let's stand up and be heard ... for once
Singer of Canadian anthem at 4 Nations Face-Off changes lyric to protest Trump's 51st state remarks, Feb. 20
To all those who chastise Canadian hockey fans who love Canada enough to take one of the few opportunities to show our displeasure about the lack of respect being shown to Canada by the U.S. president (a criminally indicted felon, compulsive liar and narcissistic bully), I say boo.
The American people voted Donald Trump in and it's time for them to know their vote has consequences, for them and their leader, who shows no respect to neighbours and allies.
To my fellow Canadians, please get off your high horse, grow a backbone and tell it like it is. We are mad and will take every opportunity to let the U.S. know how we feel as proud and patriotic Canadians. Respect has to be earned.
Barbara Jean-Venne,
Mississauga

Document 20 of 500

Oakville councillors blindsided as Ford government steps in with plan for developers' stalled condo project

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/oakville-councillors-blindsided-as-ford/docview/3171231721/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Oakville+councillors+blindsided+as+Ford+government+steps+in+with+plan+for+developers%27+stalled+condo+project&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's government is pushing for a controversial condo-tower development in Oakville that would benefit developers while disregarding the town's vision for the land.
The development would see 11 condo towers, ranging from 46 to 59 storeys, built in an area roughly the size of the Rogers Centre.
After Oakville did not approve the original building applications filed by the developers, the Ontario government stepped in and proposed a similar complex be built through a provincial initiative that can override local planning.
Oakville council members say they were blindsided by the province's proposal, which a staff report warns advances developers' interests while offering few benefits to the community.
Distrikt Developments had applied to build the thicket of towers across four parcels of land, one of which is owned by a company co-directed by Christopher Bratty. Bratty is a developer who sat at Table 3 at Ford's daughter's September 2022 wedding, according to a copy of the reception's seating plan.
The province's proposal mirrors Distrikt's blueprints - except it would allow slightly more units. The proposed transit-oriented community, or TOC, would include more than 6,900 condos, mostly one-bedrooms.
For some, reminders of the Greenbelt scandal
Oakville council unanimously voted last month not to endorse the province's proposal, which town staff say disregards the province's own objectives of transit-oriented communities. The plan includes no provision for affordable housing units, and it's silent on if or how the developers will contribute to any new infrastructure at the nearby Oakville GO station.
"Overall, the TOC proposal appears as a private development proposal with very little to no community benefit for either the town or the province," Oakville planning staff told the province in a December 2024 letter.
For many in Oakville, the situation unfolding there is an unsettling echo of the Greenbelt scandal, where Ford government decisions disproportionately benefitting certain developers were foisted onto local communities.
Neither the premier's office nor the ministry of infrastructure responded to the Star's questions. At the time the province advanced its proposal, an Infrastructure Ontario spokesperson told the Oakville Beaver that the town's GO station is the second busiest in the network and the province wants to transform nearby under-used properties into complete communities with numerous transportation options that could alleviate road congestion.
The province's proposal raises troubling questions about who gets to decide how communities are reshaped - and whose needs are prioritized.
As the province tries to tackle the housing shortages, the housing minister declared that "municipalities know best where and what types of homes are needed to address the unprecedented population growth we've seen."
But critics say the province is taking away Oakville government's decision-making.
"The bigger picture here is they are consistently overruling duly elected councils, suspending good planning rules to reward their developer buddies with ultra-high densities in compact clusters of super high towers," said Victor Doyle, an urban planner widely credited as the architect of Ontario's Greenbelt.
The four parcels sit roughly 300 metres away from the Oakville GO Station.
They make up just 5 per cent of what Oakville's planners have dubbed "Midtown," a 103-hectare belt of underutilized commercial and industrial land just south of the QEW.
Since 2017, Oakville staff have eyed it for redevelopment.
Under Oakville's plans, Midtown would become a vibrant mix-used community, an urban mosaic of residential, commercial and civic buildings complemented by open public spaces. It would have a variety of housing options and building heights catering to different residents' needs including affordable housing and purpose-built rental.
Distrikt aspired to build 11 towers with more than 6,800 condo units on the four parcels of land, according to its applications to the municipality.
"Locating density on underutilized lands next to existing transit infrastructure to meet population growth targets makes sense and is widely supported by planning professionals and Provincial policy direction," said Emil Toma, Distrikt's president, in an email response on behalf of Distrikt and Bratty.
From 2020 to 2024, Distrikt-affiliated companies spent more than $120 million buying the four plots of land, according to property records reviewed by the Star.
Toma is registered as a director for each of the corporations that own the land.
One of those companies is Cross Realty, a joint-venture Toma has with developer Christopher Bratty.
Bratty and his wife were guests at the wedding of one of Premier Ford's daughters in September 2022, according to a seating chart obtained by the Star. The premier had said the developer guests were "friends of the Ford family, and in some cases have been for decades," according to the office of the integrity commissioner.
Bratty and Toma joined forces in 2023 to secure the last of the four land parcels. According to corporate records, Bratty is not a director of any of the companies that own the other three properties.
Bratty and Toma have been active donors to Ontario's Progressive Conservatives since Ford became the leader. Between 2018 and 2024, Toma and Bratty contributed more than $21,000 to the PC party and associations. Of that, Toma donated $3,500 in total directly to the PC party's electoral district association in Oakville in 2020, 2022 and 2023.
While names match, the Star cannot independently verify they are the same people. A Star reporter sent the donation records to the developers and they did not dispute them.
Distrikt has hired Amir Remtulla to lobby the Ontario government for intensification in Oakville's Midtown area.
Remtulla worked as chief of staff to the late Rob Ford, the premier's brother, from 2011 to 2012. The Star previously reported that Remtulla was hired to lobby the provincial government on behalf of developers including TACC Developments, headed by Silvio De Gasperis. De Gasperis was one of the landowners whose properties were to be removed from the Greenbelt before the plan was reversed. Remtulla's lobbying was to be on issues related to "environment" and "housing"; his registration did not mention the Greenbelt.
Records show Remtulla had lobbied the provincial government on behalf of Distrikt with targets including premier Ford's office, the ministry of infrastructure and housing minister's office from 2022 to 2024. The lobbying goals, the records show, were "Intensification and future growth potential of the Oakville Midtown urban growth and major transit station area."
Remtulla did not respond to the Star's questions.
More condo units under Ontario's plan
It's unclear who first suggested Distrikt's lands could be developed as a transit-oriented community.
Oakville Mayor Rob Burton told a council meeting that "Distrikt was approached by the province." In an interview with the Star, Burton would not elaborate on his statement except to say it was accurate and he stood by it.
Distrikt had appealed its development applications to build the condos to the Ontario Land Tribunal (OLT) after it said Oakville failed to make a decision on the applications within the required time.
In November 2024, the province revealed its own proposal for Distrikt's lands to be developed as a transit-oriented community. In the meantime, the appeal to the OLT has been put on hold.
The Ontario government's proposed TOC development wasn't just what the developers had asked for in its original applications. It was more.
The TOC would bring the total number of residential units from the Distrikt-proposed 6,829 to 6,908.
Ultimately, Burton said, "council got dealt out of the planning process for this application because the province decided we were taking too long in public consultation."
Province says daycare, YMCA part of plan
Under the province's transit-oriented community proposal, some 11,600 residents would be expected to live just on the 5-hectare site - almost half of the population Oakville has planned for the 103-hectare Midtown.
The TOC proposal significantly exceeds the province's own density targets. According to an Oakville council motion, the proposal would add 2,350 people and jobs per hectare, which is 10 times the province's minimum requirement.
"It does make sense to intensify around the transit hub and in underutilized land. We totally agree with that. What we don't agree with is this hyper density proposal where they've done a backroom deal with one developer to give them actually taller and more populated buildings than they even applied for in the first place," said resident George Niblock, a representative of the grassroots organization We Love Oakville.
Toma, president of Distrikt, said that there have been many public engagement meetings about their development applications and they were "generally in line with" the town's initial draft plan for the area. Toma said the town has been "very involved" in the TOC process from the beginning.
A report justifying the TOC proposal prepared on behalf of Distrikt says that the proposed Oakville TOC would provide not just new housing but also privately owned but publicly accessible open spaces, office and retail spaces, as well as community amenities including a daycare and a YMCA.
Does Ontario's plan meet its own objectives?
When Ford's government passed the Transit-Oriented Communities Act in July 2020, it was intended to increase housing supply, create jobs, build complete communities, and offset the cost of station construction.
But the proposed condo development fails to meet these objectives, according to Oakville's planning staff.
In a December 2024 letter to the ministry of infrastructure, staff pointed out that the current version of the proposal does not require affordable housing in any of the 11 planned towers. It also does not include sufficient space for retail office and commercial use, and the parkland will be insufficient for the number of residents.
"You can't just plop 11 high story buildings on a couple of pieces of land and expect that to function over time. You need all the community centres, the parks, the libraries, the schools, the infrastructure," said Doyle, an urban planner.
In its letter, Oakville staff say the province failed to explain how the developer will offset the cost of new station infrastructure - or even what, if any, new infrastructure is being proposed.
"There's nothing out there that benefits the community, nor does it really advance the objective of transit ... it doesn't address the additional congestion they will drive," Councillor Janet Haslett-Theall said.
Oakville GO is part of the Lakeshore West Line GO Expansion project, one of the province's largest infrastructure investments. Introducing a TOC to Midtown Oakville can optimize investment and will offer more Ontarians access to housing, quality employment, services, and recreational opportunities, according to a third-party report the province is using to support its proposed development.
In an email, Distrikt's Toma also defended the proposal, saying it "will provide new housing, privately-owned publicly accessible open spaces (POPS), office and retail spaces, as well as public amenities directly next to the busiest GO station, other than Union Station, in all of Ontario. "
The Oakville staff report says the traffic volumes from the TOC will exceed the capacity of the already-congested roads, could block buses from getting in and out of the Oakville GO Station and impact GO transit operations.
Oakville casts vote, but will it matter?
On Jan. 27, hundreds of residents filled the chambers where Oakville council was expected to take an official stance on the province's plan for Distrikt lands.
The vote on the council was unanimous: Oakville does not endorse the TOC plan.
"Satisfying the desire of one is not good planning," Councillor Haslett-Theall, who brought forward the motion, said at the council meeting.
The motion called the TOC proposal a dangerous precedent that prioritizes mega-density at the expense of balanced and sustainable development. The proposal also disregards the town's own plans for the area, the motion said, despite that Oakville continues to meet provincial housing goals.
Oakville is one of 29 Ontario municipalities that have been assigned housing targets by the province through the More Homes Built Faster Act. Oakville was expected to add 33,000 additional units in 10 years. The town reached 80 per cent of its housing construction targets in 2023 and was rewarded with $7 million from the province last November.
For years, Oakville has been charting its own path. On Feb. 18, Oakville passed its official plan amendment for the Midtown area, which includes the four properties owned by Distrikt.
Oakville's plan "will result in needed housing sooner than the impractical, unrealistic and over-ambitious TOC proposal ever could," the mayor told the council.
The amendment sets the maximum residential density and tower height threshold much lower than what the province put forward for the transit-oriented community. It would see the Distrikt land add 4,181 people and 1,314 jobs. The province's TOC proposal would bring more than 11,000 people and 555 new jobs to the same area.
Council has requested Ontario's Ministry of Municipal Affairs and Housing to approve its official plan amendment. Even if it does, the province could still go ahead with the proposed TOC, which is not required to conform with the town's adopted policies.
Transit-oriented communities are typically authorized through a minister's zoning order (MZO), a blunt provincial tool that bypasses municipal planning authorities and is unappealable.
The province would not say if an MZO is being considered for the proposed Oakville TOC.
CAPTION: Critics of the development proposal say Premier Doug Ford's government is imposing a condo tower plan that is not in Oakville's interest.Developer Chris Bratty, shown here in a 2017 photo, co-directs a company that owns one of the four parcels of land that is now subject to a development proposal by the province.Amir Remtulla lobbied the provincial government for intensification in Oakville's Midtown area, on behalf of Distrikt.Oakville Mayor Rob Burton, seen here at a recent transit announcement, said the province approached the developers with the idea to proceed with plans under the transit-oriented community program.The 11 condo towers the province has proposed for the development of Oakville's "transit-oriented community" near the GO station in Midtown.OAKVILLE TOC Oakville council voted unanimously to adopt the Midtown official plan amendment on Feb. 18 2025. Document 21 of 500

Four things we learned about Carney from debates

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/four-things-we-learned-about-carney-debates/docview/3171231061/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Four+things+we+learned+about+Carney+from+debates&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In the end, it was a friendly affair.
Liberal leadership candidates used their podiums to introduce themselves to party members during these week's debates, but they also shielded Mark Carney, the race's perceived front-runner, from devastating blows that could have helped Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives attack the party during what is expected to be a spring election campaign.
Only Karina Gould, the former Government House leader and cabinet minister, seemed to want to draw Carney into a debate, and even then, it was a pretty tame affair.
Here are a few takeaways from Carney's performance:
Carney's French
It's laborious, painful to listen to, and harder to watch. It may not matter but Monday's French-language performance was a warning that Carney needs to double down on his language studies unless he wants Bloc Québécois Leader Yves-François Blanchet and Poilievre to make mincemeat out of him during a campaign debate.
Freeland is a good friend
Carney, I'm told, was concerned about how Chrystia Freeland would approach him in the debate. The two are long-time friends, and he is the godfather to her youngest child. But he didn't need to worry. It was he who suggested Freeland didn't have the chops for the current task of defending Canada from Donald Trump, telling the audience that while Trump might have been after Canada's market share when Freeland was renegotiating the North American Free Trade Agreement, now the American president is after "our country."
Freeland came to Carney's defence at least three times on Monday. She ensured he could clearly get the words supply management out of his mouth when he told the audience that culture, water and Canada's protection for dairy, eggs, and poultry would never be traded away, an important policy stance for some ridings in Quebec, Ontario and Atlantic Canada.
Later, Freeland re-asked the French moderator's question so Carney could tell the audience he planned to scrap the consumer carbon price.
She also ensured that the audience understood that when he said everyone agreed with Hamas ("Nous sommes d'accord avec Hamas") that he meant to say that everyone on stage agreed on Hamas - that the terrorist group has no place in any discussion about the future of Gaza.
But Freeland wasn't the only candidate to lend Carney a helping hand.
Frank Baylis helped clarify Carney's misspeak when he said Poilievre wanted to cut Radio-Canada - as the Conservative leader has said he wants to defund the CBC, not the French arm of the public broadcaster. Baylis suggested that scrapping the CBC would impact Radio-Canada, as the two share infrastructure (which is true).
Tuesday, there were no lifelines for Carney. But he didn't need them either.
Had Freeland concluded, as many in her own camp have, that she won't win and her best play now is courting a key post in a Carney cabinet?
Carney's key policies include possibly new pipelines
Carney repeated that he's laser focused on the economy, on growing wages faster than inflation, and pledged to be a more hands on manager than Prime Minister Justin Trudeau. Among his promises: a middle-income tax cut, scrapping the GST on the purchase of new homes, eliminating the consumer price on carbon. He said he'll keep the Liberals' child care, dental care and pharmacare programs. He wants to increase competition in the grocery, broadband and transportation sectors, and suggested he'd continue with the Liberals' firearms buyback program, and "reinforce" Criminal Code provisions for sexual abuse. He demonstrated a keen interest in artificial intelligence, and sees Canadian investments there as key to finding government efficiencies and combating cybersecurity and information warfare.
Carney also suggested it is "possible" a Liberal government under his watch builds a new pipeline. There could be an Energy East-type project, he said, bringing Alberta's oil to the Maritimes, but he suggested it would have to be built to displace American imports. Trump had changed the calculations, he suggested. But a new pipeline would only get built, if it is in the interest of the entire country and the provinces would get a veto. "Quebec, Ontario, Manitoba, Saskatchewan must be in agreement," he said.
Bill 21
Carney appears to be breaking rank with Trudeau's firm opposition to Bill 21. The Quebec legislation bars wearing religious symbols by people in some public sector jobs, such as teachers and judges - and the current federal government argues the law breaches the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms. When the candidates were asked about that Monday, Carney responded, "There must be reasonable accommodations." Then he said he would "respect absolutely the tradition of laicïté" - often described as the separation between church and state - "here in Quebec."
Does that mean a Carney-led government would not intervene in the challenge of the Quebec law at the Supreme Court? When asked, his team sidestepped the question, as well as what he meant specifically when he said he supports "reasonable accommodations."
The term "reasonable accommodation" refers to the majority accommodating religious minorities, namely Muslims, Sikhs and Jews. Back in 2008, Quebec's Bouchard-Taylor Commission recommended a limited ban on wearing religious symbols for people with "coercive" powers of the state, such as police officers, judges and prosecutors. It discussed the accommodating of religious minorities through a new policy of "interculturalism" that would reconcile ethnic diversity with the promotion of francophone identity.
Later, the Parti Quebecois and the Coalition Avenir Quebec (CAQ) governments proposed ways to promote the French language while treating religious minorities as second-class citizens, all in the name of reasonable accommodation.
Poilievre's Conservatives have spoken from both sides of their mouths on this issue. During their leadership debates in 2022, Poilievre said he would intervene at the Supreme Court to fight Bill 21. But after he won, his Quebec lieutenant said a Poilievre-led government would not in fact intervene. When the Supreme Court hearing was announced last month, Poilievre's office refused to say where he stood on the issue.
Despite weeks of requests for Poilievre's office to clarify his stance on Bill 21, it has refused to respond. Now Carney has some explaining to do, too.
CREDIT: Althia raj OPINION

Subject: Political campaigns; Charter of Rights-Canada; Religion; Audiences; Minority & ethnic groups; Debates; Elections; Information warfare; Political leadership

Document 22 of 500

Ford's son-in-law faces police misconduct claims: Officer allegedly sent confidential information to wife

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-son-law-faces-police-misconduct-claims/docview/3171230547/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+son-in-law+faces+police+misconduct+claims%3A+Officer+allegedly+sent+confidential+information+to+wife&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford's son-in-law is facing more than a dozen police misconduct allegations including that he forwarded dozens of emails detailing his policing duties - including one regarding a sexual assault investigation - from his work account to his wife.
Staff Sgt. Ernest (Dave) Haynes faces 15 non-criminal charges under the Police Services Act for discreditable conduct, insubordination and breach of confidence, according to notices of hearings provided by the tribunal. None of the allegations have been proven.
His wife, Ford's daughter Krista Ford Haynes, is identified only as "KF" in the documents provided by the tribunal.
The charges relate to Haynes allegedly expressing his opinion on policing matters, unsolicited, over email and social media, making posts on his personal Instagram in his service uniform and sharing confidential matters with his wife.
Haynes' lawyer did not respond to a voicemail or email requesting comment. Haynes did not respond to the Star's request for comment in a direct message to his Instagram account. Ford Haynes did not respond to an email or direct message request for comment.
In a GoFundMe campaign to raise money for outside legal counsel, Ford Haynes said her husband "has been facing some serious allegations as a result of him trying to be transparent about a safe working environment." She goes on to say her husband was "sent home on unpaid leave" for seven months over the course of four years "for asking for more information about the ethicacy of a vaccine being forced upon it's members (sic)."
Haynes' latest lawyer Bath-Sheba van den Berg, previously represented members of the so-called Freedom Convoy at the Public Order Emergency Commission inquiry into the use of emergency measures during the pandemic.
The Toronto Police Association, which represents officers, typically provide legal counsel to their members at the tribunal, but officers can choose their external counsel at their own cost.
Haynes remains a staff sergeant - a supervisory role within the service - earning more than $150,000 in 2023, according to the province's salary disclosure list. Haynes is not suspended and is on approved leave, a Toronto police spokesperson confirmed in an email.
The charges against Haynes are grouped into five separate notices of hearing.
The first relates to an allegation Haynes sent an unsolicited mass email to his colleagues in December 2023. Haynes is alleged to have sent the email to everyone in central and south Etobicoke's 22 Division, where he was assigned, as well as some members of 31 Division in North York - a total of 246 people.
"In the email, you expressed your personal opinion relating to policing matters; you were critical of some of the Service's initiatives and direction taken in certain areas. This undermined the Chief's and Command's position," the notice of hearing says.
The second incident relates to a sexual assault investigation.
Also in December 2023, Haynes allegedly sent an email to senior officers, copying the sexual assault complainant. "The email contained confidential police information including previous internal email correspondence relating to the matter," the notice of hearing said.
The nature of the comments was not disclosed.
The same email was forwarded to his wife and also a law student, none of whom, along with the complainant, were authorized to receive it, the notice alleges.
The third set of charges relates to a missing persons case.
Again in December 2023, the service alleges Haynes emailed a sergeant and staff sergeant at the London Police Service after he learned about comments an officer from that service made about a missing person who had been located and found wanted under warrant in London. The nature of those comments was not disclosed.
It is then alleged Haynes forwarded that email to his wife.
The fourth group of charges relates to Haynes' Instagram account - a space where he recently posted he started to "humanize the badge." He has frequently shared critical views of public officials, COVID-19 mandates and the media while also posting content like workout videos. The allegations relating to his social media relate to several specific posts.
In one from February 2024, Haynes is alleged to have disclosed police business by discussing a complaint made against him.
In others, it's alleged Haynes can be seen in his police uniform on what is a personal account, which the service says is contrary to its social media procedure.
The final complaint contains allegations Haynes sent 79 emails from his work account to his wife between November 2020 and February 2024, the majority of which "had some direct connection" to his official policing duties.
There is no indication in the tribunal documents that email correspondence was shared with any of Haynes' other family members, including the premier. When asked if the emails were ever shared with Ford, a spokesperson for the PC Party simply responded: "No."
Haynes is next scheduled to be before the tribunal in April.
CREDIT: Jennifer Pagliaro Toronto Star

Document 24 of 500

How to make a difference in an Ontario election that may change nothing: My guide for voters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/how-make-difference-ontario-election-that-may/docview/3171483547/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+to+make+a+difference+in+an+Ontario+election+that+may+change+nothing%3A+My+guide+for+voters&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A vote is a terrible thing to waste.
Which is why today's column is a concise voter's guide to making your vote matter more on election day this Thursday.
Politicians keep telling us that campaigns matter. Judging by the dismal 44 per cent turnout in the last provincial election, most Ontarians disagree - and are disengaged.
In my previous life as a foreign correspondent, I spent 11 years in Asia and the Middle East writing about people who were willing to die for democracy.
So when I hear people here at home complain about local democracy, I feel a kind of post-democratic stress disorder. Giving up on democracy is giving in to apathy, yes, but it is more than that.
It is not just a dereliction of duty, it is a delegation of authority - empowering others while disenfranchising you. Refusing to choose is itself a decision to let others decide for you.
That's not to say choosing is easy. For it is harder than ever to discern the differences among political parties, personalities and policies.
So let me try to explain why campaigns count - and how every vote can count in its own way.
What about comparing policies?
If you listen to NDP Leader Marit Stiles and Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, the election is all about hallway health care, doctor's shortages, housing shortfalls, affordability and a scandal-plagued Progressive Conservative government. If you heed PC Leader Doug Ford, it's all about affordability, fending off Donald Trump's tariffs and defending the economy - the better to bankroll health care and education.
The problem for Crombie and Stiles is that health care rarely rises to the level of a vote-determining issue in Canadian politics. Polls consistently show that Ontarians believe Ford's Tories are best-placed to tackle economic issues, including Trump's tariffs - the decisive ballot question for most voters.
Election platforms were once a big deal in Canadian politics, but the days of fully-costed plans are past. The big hole in the latest PC platform is Ford's promise to dig a tunnel under the 401, which is buried deep in the document without a price tag (experts estimate it easily would cost more than $100 billion).
New Democrats have countered on traffic congestion by vowing to remove all tolls from Highway 407 - but won't disclose the multibillion-dollar cost. Liberals have also promised billions of dollars in tax cuts without saying how they'd make up for lost revenues.
And so, in the age of endless social media feeds and short attention spans, every platform looks like a meme with one recurring theme - pocketbook politics. Now, all three major parties are awkwardly aligned on tax cuts, toll cuts and fee cuts while magically boosting government services and supports.
The leaders held back their putative platforms until the campaign's final days, leaving voters less time to read up because none of it adds up. It is the banality and vanity of small differences.
So what's the campaign really about?
This election is less a policy debate and more a personality contest. Ford has grown on Ontarians, partly because he has grown up - he's less bombastic than before and more folksy; less chippy and more chipper.
That said, if you do the math, the surveys show a theoretical majority of voters are opposed to Ford's Tories. So why does he keep winning?
Historically, Ford's opponents couldn't agree on the opposition party best placed to displace him. Centrist and left-leaning voters split their votes among rival "progressive" parties - Liberals, New Democrats and Greens - allowing Tories to come up the middle.
How can votes count more?
Electoral reform won't happen anytime soon in Ford's Ontario. Rival parties won't form a common front or come together because they are too tribal.
The only way to catch the front-runner is for progressive voters to back the horse with the best chance of catching up.
New Democrats keep insisting only they have what it takes, because they won more seats in the last two elections.
All they needed was more Liberal voters swinging behind them.
Yet that's precisely what happened in 2018 and 2022, when voters put the Liberal dynasty in the penalty box after 15 years in power. Voters swung over to the NDP, yet the result was two missed opportunities for the opposition.
Even when the stars were in alignment for New Democrats, they hit a ceiling. They won the consolation prize of "Official Opposition" (with extra legislative funding), while the Liberals languished. But what does the NDP have to show for two terms of supposedly holding the Tories to account?
Today, Ford is coasting to re-election as premier, notwithstanding his blunders on housing, health care and education. Meanwhile, the Liberals have leapfrogged ahead of the NDP as their brand rebounds.
No matter how many times New Democrats introduce Stiles as "the next premier of Ontario," it is a delusion wrapped in an illusion inside an election. Unable to defeat Ford in 2018, 2022 and now 2025, how many more chances does the NDP deserve?
The Liberals, like it or not, have a fighting chance - if not now, then in future. Since becoming leader, Crombie has not exactly caught fire but she is catching up to Ford after running a credible campaign, hovering at 30 per cent in most polls compared to 20 per cent or less for the NDP.
Her party is strongest in the GTA and Ottawa, but weak elsewhere. The Liberal party she took over in late 2023 is starting far behind in the seat count.
And yet the fact that her Liberals are weak in many rural ridings and the southwest is not a reason to abandon them, but all the more reason to shore up their base - so they can rebuild for the future. For unlike the NDP, Liberals are not condemned to the purgatory of perpetual opposition.
In truth, Ontario politics today has more to do with geography than ideology, with each party relying on a regional power base and erratic pockets of support. The NDP that once boasted about being the conscience of parliament acts more like a boutique party that has become a boon for Ford's Tories.
I don't expect diehard New Democrats to defect, even if their cause is doomed. It's their democratic right to elect another right-wing government by default.
But when interested voters ask me who can defeat Ford's Tories, I tell them that every election offers a choice - even if they don't like the options. It's a hard question worth asking, even if you don't like the answer.
This election is not about who will be premier, for that is preordained by Ford's cynically clever timing. It's about the race for second place - and who can do a better job of holding Ford's feet to the fire after the last seven years of lukewarm opposition that laid the groundwork for another four years of PC government.
That's democracy.
CAPTION: An Ontario voter make his way to an advance polling station in Toronto, on Feb. 20, 2025.
CREDIT: Martin Regg Cohn

Subject: Provincial elections; Political campaigns; Scandals; Democracy; Tax cuts; Voters; Politics; Voter behavior; Elections; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Tax cuts Tariffs

Title: How to make a difference in an Ontario election that may change nothing: My guide for voters
