Document 25 of 500

5 things I learned about Mark Carney from the Liberal leadership debate

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/5-things-i-learned-about-mark-carney-liberal/docview/3171483200/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=5+things+I+learned+about+Mark+Carney+from+the+Liberal+leadership+debate&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In the end, it was a friendly affair.
Liberal leadership candidates used their podiums to introduce themselves to party members during Monday night's French-language debate, but they also sought to shield Mark Carney, the race's perceived front-runner, from devastating blows that could have helped Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives attack the party during what is expected to be a spring election campaign.
It was also Carney's first big test. Here are a few takeaways:
Carney's French
It's laborious, painful to listen to, and harder to watch. Will it matter? Voters may not care, but Monday's performance was a warning that Carney needs to double down on his French; if he can't, Bloc Québécois Leader Yves-François Blanchet and Poilievre will make mincemeat of him during a campaign debate. His French was also a handicap when it came to simply getting his points across. Debate moderator Pierre Jobin was clearly frustrated at times, trying to hurry him along and even interjecting once to translate "20 seconds" into English.
Freeland is a good friend
Carney, I'm told, was concerned about how Chrystia Freeland would approach him in the debate. The two are long-time friends, and he is the godfather to her youngest child. But he didn't need to worry. It was he who suggested Freeland didn't have the chops for the current task of defending Canada from Donald Trump. He told the audience that while Trump might have been after Canada's market share when Freeland was renegotiating the North American Free Trade Agreement, now the American president is after "our country." He left hanging the suggestion that that required a different set of skills.
Freeland actually came to Carney's defence at least three times. She ensured he could clearly get the words "gestion d'offre" - supply management - out of his mouth when he told the audience that culture, water and Canada's protection for dairy and poultry would never be traded away, an important policy stance for some ridings in Quebec, Ontario and Atlantic Canada.
Later, Freeland re-asked the moderator's question so Carney could tell the audience he planned to scrap the consumer carbon price.
She also ensured that the audience understood that when he said everyone agreed with Hamas ("Nous sommes d'accord avec Hamas") that he meant to say that everyone on stage agreed on Hamas - that the terrorist group has no place in any discussion about the future of Gaza.
But Freeland wasn't the only candidate to lend Carney a helping hand.
Frank Baylis helped clarify Carney's misspeak when he said Poilievre wanted to cut Radio-Canada - as the Conservative leader has said he wants to defund the CBC, not the French arm of the public broadcaster. Baylis suggested that scrapping the CBC would impact Radio-Canada, as the two share infrastructure (which is true).
Freeland may have concluded, as many in her own camp have, that she cannot win. Perhaps, she's decided her best play is courting a key post in a Carney cabinet. Regardless, she did him many favours on Monday, instead of kneecapping him as she did Prime Minister Justin Trudeau just before Christmas by quitting on the day she was to deliver the fiscal update and calling him fiscally irresponsible.
Carney's key policies
Carney said he would introduce a middle-income tax cut, scrap the HST for new homes, and keep the child care and dental care programs already in place. (He did not mention pharmacare.) He wants to increase competition in the grocery and broadband sectors. He will continue with the Liberals' firearms buyback program, and "reinforce" Criminal Code provisions for sexual abuse. On pipelines, he said it was "possible" that there would be an Energy East type project, bringing Alberta's oil to the Maritimes, but he suggested it would have to be built to displace American imports, noting that Quebec imports 70 per cent of its oil from the U.S. "Everything has changed," Carney said. He added that a new pipeline could get built if it is in the interest of the entire country - but "Quebec, Ontario, Manitoba, Saskatchewan must be in agreement."
Carney is cautious
While the other leadership candidates enthusiastically supported the high-speed rail project the Liberal government announced last week, Carney was muted. He noted he wasn't consulted, hadn't seen the numbers, and offered that while it was "probably very important for the economy," it would have to be a public-private project - "probably, if the project is correct, probably the government will support a bit the project."
Carney also demonstrated how cautious he is when he was asked how much groceries cost for a family of four in Quebec. Carney did not venture a guess. Freeland and Karina Gould guessed about $250, the anchor said the price was closer to $300.
Bill 21
Is Carney breaking with the Liberal stance under Trudeau on Bill 21? The Quebec legislation bars wearing religious symbols by people in some public sector jobs, such as teachers and judges - and the current federal government argues the law breaches the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms. When the candidates were asked about that, Carney responded, "There must be reasonable accommodations." Then he said he would "respect absolutely the tradition of laicïté" - often described as the separation between church and state - "here in Quebec." Does that mean a Carney-led government would not intervene in the challenge of the Quebec law at the Supreme Court? His team did not respond to a request for comment.
The term "reasonable accommodation" refers to the majority accommodating religious minorities, namely Muslims, Sikhs and Jews. Back in 2008, Quebec's Bouchard-Taylor Commission recommended a limited ban on wearing religious symbols for people with "coercive" powers of the state, such as police officers, judges and prosecutors. It discussed the accommodating of religious minorities through a new policy of "interculturalism" that would reconcile ethnic diversity with the promotion of francophone identity. Later, the Parti Quebecois and the Coalition Avenir Quebec (CAQ) governments proposed ways to promote the French language while treating religious minorities as second-class citizens, all in the name of reasonable accommodation.
Poilievre's Conservatives have spoken from both sides of their mouths on this issue. During their leadership debates in 2022, Poilievre said he would intervene at the Supreme Court to fight Bill 21. But after he won, his Quebec lieutenant said a Poilievre-led government would not in fact intervene. When the Supreme Court hearing was announced last month, Poilievre's office refused to say where he stood on the issue. Despite weeks of requests for Poilievre's office to clarify his stance on Bill 21, it has refused to respond. Now Carney has some explaining to do, too.
CAPTION: Mark Carney speaks during a Liberal leadership debate in Montreal on Feb. 24, 2025.
CREDIT: Althia Raj

Subject: Political campaigns; Leadership; Charter of Rights-Canada; Religion; Audiences; Candidates; Minority & ethnic groups; Elections

Title: 5 things I learned about Mark Carney from the Liberal leadership debate

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Source type: Blog, Podcast, or Website

Language of publication: English

Document 26 of 500

I fact checked what Doug Ford said for a week during the Ontario election. Here's how many false claims he made

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/i-fact-checked-what-doug-ford-said-week-during/docview/3171483061/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=I+fact+checked+what+Doug+Ford+said+for+a+week+during+the+Ontario+election.+Here%27s+how+many+false+claims+he+made&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The campaign of Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford has been remarkable in several ways, for a conservative provincial leader - touting record investments in health care and education, a slew of union endorsements, and spending a good chunk of his time speaking about how he'd defend Ontario from the president of the United States.
But one thing has remained consistent, dating back to his time as a Toronto city councillor: A tendency to make sweeping, off-the-cuff remarks denigrating the previous government's record and overstating his own accomplishments.
The Star committed to fact checking the three leading party leaders' statements over a seven-day period. First up was Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, followed by New Democratic Party Leader Marit Stiles.
Initially, the Star wasn't planning to include Ford's remarks during the leaders' debate because the fact checks for the other leaders had already been completed by then. But because Ford didn't hold any media availabilities for four days, we have included statements he made during the debate.
In 20 instances over the course of seven days, Ford made seven statements that were true, seven that were a stretch and six that were false.
It was often tough to verify claims Ford made because hard data to back his claims was either not publicly available or the only source was the government's own numbers. The Star did not include these statements in our analysis.
Several of Ford's claims that proved to be a stretch or outright false were not prepared statements, but spontaneous responses to reporters' questions at news conferences. In one case, it appeared to be a slip of a tongue, but our job was to analyze what the leaders said, not what they meant to say.
Several statements that had some semblance of truth were ultimately characterized as false or a stretch because of Ford's propensity for hyperbole.
Here's what we found.
CAPTION: Ford Factcheck Mainart

Ontario election promise tracker: From Trump tariffs to health care, here's what every provincial party leader is pledging

Document 28 of 500

Is Mark Carney's French good enough for a would-be Prime Minister?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/is-mark-carneys-french-good-enough-would-be-prime/docview/3170918003/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+Mark+Carney%27s+French+good+enough+for+a+would-be+Prime+Minister%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It wasn't a debate. It was an audition for the French-speaking role of a Prime Minister who will soon have to face two fluent French speakers in a real debate: Bloc Québécois leader Yves-François Blanchet and Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre.
That's how the performances of the four aspiring Liberal Party leaders were measured by those who watched the friendly exchange. And on that count, only Montreal-born Frank Baylis, who was educated in French in Toronto, earned a passing grade.
Not that Karina Gould, Chrystia Freeland, and Mark Carney couldn't deliver their messages in French. They each did so with varying degrees of ease. They certainly deserve an "E" for effort - more than the leaders of Ontario's four provincial parties, who didn't even bother showing up for last week's French debate.
But this was an interview for the job of leading a country with two official languages. Is a high level of fluency an absolute prerequisite? No. Stephen Harper spoke French with some ease but stumbled at times. But for a Liberal leader who needs Quebec to win nationally, it matters a great deal.
All four could likely qualify for government management positions, which require a minimum Canadian Language Benchmark level of seven. That means they must be able to converse fluently and handle everyday or work-related situations, understand the main ideas in complex conversations, presentations, and media broadcasts, and comprehend moderately complex texts like newspaper articles, reports, and books.
But a political debate, where sparks are expected to fly, is an entirely different dynamic - especially in the House of Commons, where cameras and soundbites shape public perception. That's where judgments are passed, and opponents are unforgiving. The Conservatives reacted in real time on social media when Carney, struggling to interject on the Middle East, stumbled and appeared to say he "agreed with Hamas." That prompted Freeland to immediately clarify, "We don't agree with Hamas," as Carney quickly corrected himself.
Speaking a second language can sometimes lead to embarrassing moments. Take, for example, the wife of French President Charles de Gaulle. At a luncheon with the American ambassador following her husband's retirement, she was asked what she was most looking forward to in the years ahead. She thought for a moment before boldly announcing, "a penis." A startled hush fell over the table - until the former president leaned over and gently corrected her: "My dear, I think it's pronounced 'happiness'."
Ironically, Mark Carney didn't get into that kind of trouble. The moderator did - calling Chrystia Freeland "Madame Carney," prompting an awkward joke from both about being close friends but not that close. More notably, when the moderator told Carney he only had vingt secondes to answer a question, his mumbled delivery led Carney to ask him to repeat himself. The moderator switched to English: "20 seconds." That was both unnecessary and, frankly, unfair.
Will any of this matter in the end? I doubt it.
When Carney slips, Quebecers might feel empathetic embarrassment rather than schadenfreude - the pleasure taken in another's misfortune. They'll appreciate the effort.
They didn't take kindly to another leadership candidate's dismissive attitude toward French. "I was born in Montreal. I am a Quebecer. I understand Quebecers, and I am bilingual," Kevin O'Leary told Ezra Levant in 2017. "I speak the language of English. I'm an English Quebecer. I also speak the language of jobs and economic fiscal policy. They know I have their back. I'm not worried that I don't speak French or Italian or Chinese." He went on to argue that Quebecers would give him "flexibility" because economic issues mattered more than language. They didn't.
But these are unprecedented times. Francophones may be less concerned about Carney's French proficiency than his ability to debate Donald Trump. When Radio-Canada's Patrice Roy recently interviewed him, some viewers commented that French doesn't do him justice - that in English, he is far more convincing than Pierre Poilievre.
That's why, for most Liberals - and eventually for Canadian voters - the English debate will matter far more.
CAPTION: Mark Carney, former Governor of the Bank of Canada, speaks during a press conference following the French language Liberal Leadership Debate at Mels studios in Montreal, Canada on February 24, 2025.
CREDIT: Eric Blais Contributor

Subject: Language; Conferences; Political leadership; Prime ministers

Location: Montreal Quebec Canada; Canada

People: Carney, Mark; Poilievre, Pierre; Freeland, Chrystia

Company / organization: Name: Hamas; NAICS: 813940

Document 29 of 500

Mark Carney is 'a leader that can stand up to Donald Trump,' Bonnie Crombie says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/mark-carney-is-leader-that-can-stand-up-donald/docview/3170917945/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Mark+Carney+is+%27a+leader+that+can+stand+up+to+Donald+Trump%2C%27+Bonnie+Crombie+says&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Bonnie Crombie is touting her decision to endorse federal Liberal leadership candidate Mark Carney with just two days until Ontario's election.
"This is the right moment in time, and I think he is the right leader for this moment," the Ontario Liberal leader said Tuesday in Toronto when asked if the move - first disclosed by the Star - was a distraction from the provincial campaign.
Crombie, who trails Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford in public opinion polls, implied that Carney is best placed to handle tariff threats from the U.S. president as a former governor of the Bank of Canada and Bank of England.
"He's a leader that can stand up to Donald Trump and speak with some authority and experience," she added of Carney, who is widely considered the front-runner in the March 9 contest to succeed Prime Minister Justin Trudeau.
Polls suggest Carney is narrowing a popularity gap long enjoyed by federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre.
"But he's also a leader who will get the basics right for people, and that's my focus here in Ontario today," Crombie said, highlighting her plan to get everyone in Ontario a family doctor, cut taxes and oppose Ford's push for a multibillion-dollar "fantasy" tunnel under Highway 401.
Throughout the provincial campaign, Ford's argument to voters has been that he is better qualified than Crombie or NDP Leader Marit Stiles to handle Trump's tariff threat, promising billions in supports to offset damage to Ontario's trade-dependent economy.
In Ottawa, Ford said he stands ready to order U.S. beer, wine and spirits off LCBO shelves immediately should Trump's first tranche of tariffs take effect March 4 as scheduled.
"There's 3,600 products from 35 states, so there's going to be a lot of upset Americans that they can't ship their booze up to Ontario," Ford, who is seeking a third term as premier, told reporters at a Porter Airlines hangar.
He also pledged to bar U.S. companies from Ontario contracts and to put unspecified retaliatory tariffs on the province's electricity exports to U.S. states.
Ford referenced the change in federal polling numbers showing Poilievre's lead over the Liberals apparently shrinking, particularly if Carney becomes leader.
"There's probably 1,000 different reasons why the gap is closed and that's going to be up to the federal parties to figure that out," he said.
Crombie acknowledged last week in a meeting with the Star's editorial board that Carney's rise in popularity has been helpful to her party, which had been hindered by Trudeau's unpopularity.
"There's a lot of interaction between the teams," she said Tuesday, speaking of Carney supporters and her own. "He supported me in leadership as well."
After the Star reported the news Tuesday, Carney reacted on social media.
"Thank you @BonnieCrombie - for your support, for your strong advocacy to help Ontarians get better access to a family doctor, and for putting economic growth first," he tweeted.
"Wishing you the best this Thursday, and let's keep building!"
Last June, Carney held a Bay Street fundraiser for Crombie and the two are to meet before Thursday's Ontario vote "to discuss the economy and health care," a Liberal source said, speaking confidentially in order to discuss internal deliberations.
Stiles was in London on Tuesday, where her party holds all three city ridings, reminding voters of her plan to help families deal with the rising costs of living with help that includes monthly grocery rebates and more stringent rent control.
"Your vote has real power," she said in a statement. "As premier, I will fight against rising costs to help families get ahead."
CAPTION: Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie poses for a photo with community volunteers as she makes a campaign stop in Toronto on Feb. 25, 2025.
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson, Robert Benzie, Kristin Rushowy

Document 30 of 500

Ford's 'get it done' slogan rings hollow

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-get-done-slogan-rings-hollow/docview/3170797266/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+%27get+it+done%27+slogan+rings+hollow&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Buried in the budget documents debated by Mayor Olivia Chow and Toronto city council this month is a revelation that the Ontario Works (OW) program is not working in Ontario's capital city.
And Premier Doug Ford's government is to blame.
The $1.4-billion Toronto Employment and Social Services department, funded by the province and tasked with managing cases and giving out funds through various provincial welfare programs like OW and the Ontario Disability Support Program, has historically been relatively efficient. They responded to applications for support programs within their target of four business days about 77 per cent of the time in 2023.
In 2024, however, that number plunged to just 40 per cent, their budget submission reports. The drop came at a time when the number of applications to OW was rising fast, with the city's average monthly OW caseload growing 20 per cent over the past year, and new applications rose to an average of 6,500 per month, up from 5,125 in 2023 and 4,040 in 2022.
This all translates to more desperate people waiting longer for money they need to - maybe - stay in their homes and put food on the table. (I say maybe because the combined monthly allowance for basic needs and shelter for a single person on OW is just $733, unchanged since 2018.)
The cause? In 2023, the city says, the Ford government began implementing a new "centralized intake" process for OW applications, with an online form and an automated verification process. It has not gone well. The city budget notes the "final phase" of the centralized intake implementation will come this year, and hopes it will improve things, but it seems pretty skeptical.
"The province's claim - that (centralized intake) would simplify the OW application process for clients, reduce administration, and allow caseworkers more time to support clients - has yet to produce the intended administrative efficiencies," the budget document reads.
That's very diplomatic language. Appropriate, I guess, for a city budget document. But as a columnist, I don't need to be so diplomatic, so let me rephrase: The Ford government royally screwed up. Again.
More than anything, these kinds of screw-ups have been the hallmark of the Ford government. They've used "get it done" as a slogan at times, but the ugly truth is that they've never proven to be very adept at doing things.
Screwing up a program needed by the province's most needy is just one of many past and ongoing examples.
The province has also messed up an intended reform to the various tribunals that adjudicate disputes. Tribunals Watch Ontario, a non-partisan group monitoring the changes the Ford government implemented in 2019, reports that a tenant seeking a hearing with the Landlord and Tenant Board waited an average of 277 days in 2024, compared to just 40 days in 2018.
They managed to make things seven times worse in less than seven years.
And on the housing file, Ford's attempts to compel Toronto and other municipalities to lower fees charged to developers and speed up approvals have also been remarkably messy - and delivered more bad outcomes than good ones.
A series of housing bills have given Toronto city hall a serious case of whiplash.
In 2022 and 2023, the Ford government announced in rapid succession changes that would significantly lower the city's revenue from development charges, then that it would order an audit of city hall's finances to prove Toronto didn't actually need that revenue, then that it would be partly walking back its changes to the development charge system, then that the partly completed audit was cancelled.
Meanwhile, other provincial changes to housing legislation have also seen quixotic outcomes and have done little to help Ontario build new housing. Prescribed tough deadlines for municipalities to deal with planning applications have resulted in Toronto's planning department just automatically rejecting complex developments it believes can't be processed within the deadline to avoid financial penalties.
And other changes to laws about heritage protection have resulted in the city putting in extra hours to review 3,885 listed heritage properties before a 2027 deadline to see if they are, in fact, heritage properties. No one is entirely sure what the point is.
This provincial election has seen a lot of criticism levelled at some of Ford's big ideas. And while many of Ford's ideas absolutely deserve criticism - like the ungodly expensive Highway 401 tunnel or the private spa on public land at Ontario Place - I suspect a lot of voters appreciate that he at least has some ideas to offer in response to real problems. The other parties, too focused on tearing down Ford's plans, have not done nearly enough to articulate their own better ideas.
And they definitely have not done enough to highlight this government's messy, broken and downright embarrassing record when it comes to actually running the day-to-day of government.
With election day on Thursday, it's feeling like a missed opportunity. There's much more to governing than snappy slogans and big ideas, but this premier has been able to get by without much else.
CAPTION: The ugly truth is Doug Ford's government has never proven to be very adept at day-to-day governing, writes Matt Elliott.Nick Lachance Toronto Star
CREDIT: Matt Elliott OPINION

Subject: Local elections; Budgets; Deadlines

Business indexing term: Subject: Budgets

Title: Ford's 'get it done' slogan rings hollow

Document 31 of 500

Ford vows $40B in spending to offset U.S. tariffs: Tory leader admits province will continue to bleed red ink for foreseeable future amid trade war

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-vows-40b-spending-offset-u-s-tariffs/docview/3170778914/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+vows+%2440B+in+spending+to+offset+U.S.+tariffs%3A+Tory+leader+admits+province+will+continue+to+bleed+red+ink+for+foreseeable+future+amid+trade+war&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Donald Trump's looming tariffs mean Ontario must continue to bleed red ink for the foreseeable future, admits Doug Ford.
As Ford's Progressive Conservatives unveiled their platform - a 32-page manifesto that is not fully costed and pledges $40 billion in new spending - he conceded deficits are here to stay.
"If the tariffs hit us, yes, we won't be able to balance because - just similar to the pandemic - we're going to invest into the people," the PC leader told reporters Monday at a North York painters' union centre.
The U.S. president is threatening 25 per cent tariffs on all Canadian products next month, which would be harmful to Ontario's trade-dependent economy.
Since being elected in 2018, the Tories, who governed during the global COVID-19 pandemic, have run massive budget deficits.
Under Ford, the provincial debt has soared by $116 billion to $429 billion, the largest debt of any subnational jurisdiction in the world.
But asked whether he was disrespecting taxpayers by not releasing a costed platform before Thursday's provincial election, the Tory chief was defensive.
"Not at all. We're pretty clear that if these tariffs come, our investments going to be over $40 billion no matter if it's loans or other investments we have for people and businesses to make sure they're secure here if Trump's tariffs come and hit us," he said.
"We've been very fiscally responsible. We're prudent fiscal managers with the taxpayers' money."
But NDP Leader Marit Stiles said Ford's platform is "not worth the paper it's written on."
"For the third election in a row, Doug Ford's making the same empty promises. People know he's failed to deliver for years - just look at the cost of rent or groceries," said Stiles.
"Let's put the waste, corruption and false promises of the Ford years in the rearview mirror, and let's elect an NDP government on your side."
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said she's "never seen" a platform "spend so much and do so little."
"This is a platform for lobbyists and insiders. What has he done to make life more affordable for people," said Crombie in Toronto-St. Paul's, where she was joined by local candidate Stephanie Smyth, a prominent former CP24 anchor.
"He hasn't costed the fantasy tunnel and he doesn't tell us how he's going to pay for it either," she said, referring to Ford's proposed Mississauga-Scarborough tunnel under Highway 401, which could cost $150 billion and is buried on Page 8 of the PC platform without any price tag.
Despite no feasibility study on the scheme, the Tories have vowed to move forward on the tunnel.
But it was not included in the "highways and roads" section of the PC election booklet.
Instead, there was 38-word sentence touting the project underneath a longer entry on how the Tories would "return sanity to bike lanes" by removing them from Bloor Street West, Yonge Street and University Avenue, which was finalized last December.
One new item in the manifesto was the elimination of the minimum price for beer, wine and spirits.
That hearkens to Ford's 2018 "buck-a-beer" election pledge that fell flat because brewers could not afford to produce and sell beer for that price.
Currently, wine must sell for no less than $8.10 for a 750 ml bottle, while beer cannot be sold for less than $2.82 per litre - or $1.41 for a 500 ml tallboy can - and spirits, such as vodka and gin, must sell for at least $31.15 a 750 ml bottle.
Ford, who does not drink alcohol, said he was eliminating the floor price to give people a break.
"They don't do it in Alberta. They don't do it in Quebec. So why do we have to have the base? Let's reduce the cost of that. Put more money back into people's pockets again," he said.
But the LCBO, the Crown-owned booze monopoly, announced Monday beer taxes would increase by about 4.4 per cent schedule on April 1.
Earlier in Ottawa, Crombie was focused on what she sees as the ballot question: finding a family doctor for the 2.5 million Ontarians who don't have one.
"People are telling me that they're afraid to get sick ... they know that our hospitals are overflowing and that they're underfunded and the wait times are enormous," the Liberal leader said, blasting Ford's claim that patients with minor medical problems are swamping emergency rooms instead of going to walk-in clinics.
"I don't know what choice people have," said Crombie, who has pledged to ensure every Ontarian has a family doctor within four years - promises that Ford and Stiles have also made.
"It's not their fault, Doug, it's yours."
At his platform launch, Ford insisted he would improve access to doctors, which is why he hired Dr. Jane Philpott, a former federal Liberal minister, as his health guru.
"It just goes to show, I don't care what political colour you come from, she's well renowned around the entire country, and in four years, we're going to connect ... every single person (with a physician)," he said.
Speaking to the Star editorial board, Stiles said the doctors' shortage "is taking a big toll on Ontarians" and impacting the economy.
"Employers that I talk to - in everything from tech to auto - will tell you that what's making it difficult for them to attract more workers here ... is the fact that they can't ensure that their workers are going to have a home they can afford, that they can't guarantee they're going to get a school for their kids, or a family doctor," she said.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner, meanwhile, was in Bracebridge rallying support for Parry Sound-Muskoka candidate Matt Richter who is trying to unseat Tory Graydon Smith.
"Community members of all political stripes are getting behind Matt because they know that he will be the best advocate for them at Queen's Park," said Schreiner.
"I know from knocking on thousands of doors here that people are ready for change. Electing Matt is the only way to stop another Conservative seat and make that change happen."
CAPTION: Under Doug Ford, the provincial debt has soared by $116 billion to $429 billion, the largest debt of any subnational jurisdiction in the world.Chris YoungThe CanadianPress
CREDIT: Robert Benzie, Rob Ferguson and Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Subject: Provincial elections; Jurisdiction; Budget deficits; Roads & highways; Tariffs; Economic policy

Title: Ford vows $40B in spending to offset U.S. tariffs:   Tory leader admits province will continue to bleed red ink for foreseeable future amid trade war

Document 32 of 500

Rollback puts 5,000 affordable homes at risk: Ford adjusted mandates on eve of early election call

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/rollback-puts-5-000-affordable-homes-at-risk/docview/3170778910/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Rollback+puts+5%2C000+affordable+homes+at+risk%3A+Ford+adjusted+mandates+on+eve+of+early+election+call&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When the City of Toronto allowed landowners to build housing on nearly 70 workplace-designated land parcels, it mandated that a set percentage must be built as affordable housing. But when the change was sent to the Ontario government for approval, the affordability requirements were made optional.
A series of decisions signed on Jan. 27 and published Jan. 28 - the same day Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford requested a dissolution of the legislature and called an early election - replaced wording stating that developments "will secure" a certain amount of affordable housing with new phrasing that says builders will instead be "encouraged to include" a certain number of affordable homes.
The alterations have raised alarm at the municipal level, with city staff estimating that roughly 5,000 expected affordable homes - including rentals and owned units - could be at risk.
"The previously secured number of affordable units that would be built on these lands may no longer be secured," staff said, listing nearly 70 addresses in 18 areas affected by the change.
The province has not commented on why the affordability requirement was made optional, with the ministry of municipal affairs and housing declining to comment due to the election and the Progressive Conservative party campaign office failing to respond to questions.
The chair of city hall's housing committee, Parkdale-High Park Coun. Gord Perks, was caught off guard by the change. "We just lost the potential for 5,000 units of affordable housing in a housing crisis for no apparent reason," he said. "This is a very big setback for our affordable housing plans."
When land designated for workplaces is converted to allow housing, it immediately becomes more valuable, Perks said. "We want a good chunk of that profit reinvested for social purposes and building affordable housing," he said. "Then suddenly, out of nowhere, the province took a whole bunch of them and just said we're removing that public benefit."
The property conversions city hall had put to the province for approval include parts of Golden Mile - a stretch of Eglinton Avenue in Scarborough lined with strip malls and parking lots, expected to see dozens of new towers in the decades ahead - as well as the site of a garden centre and restaurant near Scarborough City Centre and land with an existing shopping market in Liberty Village.
The affordable housing provisions included, for example, a site where any new condos would have to contain at least five per cent affordable rentals, or seven per cent affordable owned units, with the affordability provision in place for at least 99 years. The amount of space required to be set aside could also rise with time - for example, a site where the affordable rental quota would rise by 1.5 per cent per year if certain application milestones weren't reached.
The province has not rejected all of the city's efforts to enshrine this kind of affordable mandate within its planning laws. Perks pointed to other land conversion proposals that have been approved by Queen's Park with the city's affordable housing requirements remaining intact, such as a conversion in his ward approved without any modifications in December 2023. That change secured 56 affordable rental homes in the Junction-Wallace Emerson area, he said.
Asked about the provincial move, Mayor Olivia Chow in a brief statement highlighted the city's existing tax waivers and incentives for affordable housing, and said Queen's Park's choices "do not assist in the city's goal." Chow's office declined an interview, and directed the inquiry to Coun. Perks.
Ontario's housing ministry declined to comment on the changes, citing the ongoing campaign that began just after it issued its decisions. "This means the Ontario government has entered caretaker mode until the completion of the next general election," the ministry wrote, suggesting the Star "may wish to resubmit" questions about the decision after voting day.
The modifications to make the affordable housing requirements optional were made on multiple Official Plan Amendments previously approved by Toronto city council and submitted to the province in 2023. Toronto's Official Plan guides how the city grows, laying out what can be built on various plots of land. The modifications made on Jan. 27 were signed by the Ontario Housing Ministry's Assistant Deputy Minister Sean Fraser, a senior bureaucrat within the government.
The land affected is owned by a long list of companies and individuals, property records show, including many numbered companies, the provincial transit agency Metrolinx, investment groups and real estate trusts such as Choice Properties REIT, financial institutions such as the Bank of Nova Scotia and Desjardins, food producers, and companies working in construction and development such as Torgan Construction and Trojan Gate Developments.
The city's push for more affordable housing on the converted lands comes as a growing number of Toronto households - not only low-income households, but many mid-income earners - have struggled with their bills. As of January, rental.ca data shows the average one-bedroom apartment listing in Toronto cost $2,464 per month. That's lower than last year, but still only affordable - using national criteria - to a household earning at least $99,000 per year.
Some landowners, as part of the submission process to city hall where they made their case for land conversions, argued that pieces of employment land were prime territory for homes, especially as the city looks to encourage growth around transit. In one letter to city hall as part of that process, in mid-2023, the owner of an acre of Scarborough land that houses a Sheridan Nurseries garden centre and a Canbe Foods restaurant noted its proximity to the future Scarborough Subway extension, and suggested that closeness made the site ideal for "much needed housing."
But as city hall put forward sites where it agreed with a proposed conversion, a staff report also noted the importance of ensuring affordability. "As the urgency around the provision of affordable housing has grown, so too has the expectation from Council and local communities that affordable housing be included in new developments," former chief planner Gregg Lintern wrote.
City staff, in a new statement, said its affordable housing minimums - along with rules about minimum amounts of working space - "formed the basis" for council agreeing to make the land changes. Without those mandates, staff say it's likely the city will "achieve less," as it faces negotiations over each site.
The city cannot appeal the decision, with the provincial move considered final.
To Perks, the changes are frustrating and shrouded in mystery, with no clear indication of why they were made. "To just have the rug pulled out from under us, at the eleventh hour before an election, is really mind boggling," he said.
CREDIT: Victoria Gibson Toronto Star

Subject: Landowners; Local elections; Councils; Rentals; Affordable housing; Housing needs; Housing policy

Business indexing term: Subject: Affordable housing

Location: Ontario Canada; Canada

People: Chow, Olivia

Title: Rollback puts 5,000 affordable homes at risk:   Ford adjusted mandates on eve of early election call

Publication title: Toronto Star; Toronto, Ont.

First page: A.1

Publication year: 2025

Publication date: Feb 25, 2025

Document 33 of 500

I discovered the secret strategy behind Donald Trump's tariff plan

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/i-discovered-secret-strategy-behind-donald-trumps/docview/3170778596/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=I+discovered+the+secret+strategy+behind+Donald+Trump%27s+tariff+plan&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: MACOMB COUNTY, MICH. - Something people tend to forget about U.S. President Donald Trump, because of his success and his volume and lizard-like ability to slither onto every screen, is that he is not a very smart man. He has animal cunning, sure, and absolutely no scruples; he believes in nothing but victory, publicity and himself. But he isn't intellectual, like JD Vance, or analytical, like Kamala Harris. He is, instead, simple. Like a virus or a stone shank, he isn't complicated, but he is dangerous, and he has no capacity to care about anyone or anything but himself.
Earlier this month, I spent several days driving around the suburbs of Detroit talking to auto industry experts, politicians and ordinary people about Trump's threat to impose a 25 per cent tariff Canadian imports - a plan Trump told reporters Monday would "going forward on time, on schedule March 4." I wanted to get a sense of how Americans are feeling about an issue that has become existential in our country. Tariffs, and Trump's broader taunting about making Canada the 51st state, have upended Canadian politics. They've overshadowed the Ontario election. They even, somehow, became the central through-line in a hockey tournament that rapidly went from kind of a jape to the most geopolitically weighty sporting event this country has seen since 1972.
It was impossible not to conclude from my conversations, in places like Sterling Heights, Detroit, East Lansing and more, that there is no grand strategy behind Trump's tariff plan. This isn't 4-D Chess. It isn't even tic-tac-toe. It's just a trade war launched by a man who doesn't believe that any game can ever be anything but zero-sum. But what really struck me, in conversations with Democrats, Republicans, academics and business leaders, was that, in Michigan car country at least, Trump's tariffs plans are almost as terrifying and controversial as they are here.
That's notable, at least in part, because Michigan autoworkers and the communities they call home essentially made Trump president. He won Macomb County, home to massive GM, Stellantis and Ford plants, as well 57,000 auto jobs, by almost 50,000 votes in 2016. He won Michigan, and thus the election, by less than 14,000. After losing the state to Joe Biden in 2020, Trump again took Michigan - and the presidency - in 2024, with a big helping hand from Macomb County, just outside Detroit, where union halls dot most every strip mall and single car plants can stretch across multiple blocks.
Michigan autoworkers, in other words, are exactly the kind blue-collar, working class, largely white voters that made Trump's victories possible. They are also the ones who stand to lose most if Trump carries through on just about any version of his threat to impose tariffs on Canadian goods. "We're an interconnected system," said Michael Radtke, a city councillor in Sterling Heights, the second largest municipality in Macomb County. "Tariffs, the type that the idiotic president is encouraging, wouldn't even harm (the auto industry). It would end it."
Radtke is a Democrat. He doesn't like Trump (in case you couldn't tell). But opposition to Trump's tariff plan isn't really a partisan issue in Michigan. Andrea Bitely, a longtime Republican strategist who now runs her own communications firm in Lansing, told me she's terrified of what a trade war would do to her state. "We're going to see the auto industry grind a halt," she told me, "and that is a huge tax base of the state of Michigan."
That threat isn't theoretical, either. A few days before I met with Radtke, Joe Loparco, the co-president of AGS Automotive, a Canadian auto-parts manufacturer that runs plants on both sides of the Ontario/Michigan border, met with city officials in Sterling Heights. He told them, bluntly, that "if the tariffs go into play, they're going to have to lay off hundreds of employees," Radtke said. "They'll do it immediately." (Loparco himself confirmed as much when I reached him last week.)
"I'll be frank, the president of United States seems to be toying with people's lives," Radtke said. "And if there's a strategy behind this, someone should clue me in."
The reality, of course, is that there is no strategy. There is only Trump's single, simple bedrock belief: that if other people lose he wins.
Trump first threatened to impose a 25 per cent tariff on all products coming over the border from Mexico and Canada in January. The day before those tariffs were set to come into force, in early February, he announced a 30-day reprieve, leaving the auto industry in a state of anticipatory chaos. Since then, he has launched new or expanded tariffs on steel and aluminum and promised a separate round of 25 per cent tariffs on all auto imports. The original, blanket tariffs, meanwhile, are now set to come into force next week, based on what Trump said in Washington Monday.
If Trump doesn't agree to some kind of peace on trade, the Canadian economy will suffer, no doubt. But despite what Trump might believe, the American economy won't win just because that happens. This isn't a Happy Meal. He doesn't get more fries just because we get less. If the auto tariffs actually come into force, there will be fewer fries for everyone.
Much has been made since Trump first lobbed the tariff threat of the fact that Canada is far more dependent on the U.S. than the U.S. is on Canada. And that is true. Seventy-seven per cent of Canadian exports went to the U.S. in 2023, according to Scotiabank, compared to just 18 per cent of American exports going the other way. So yes, on a national level, a trade war hurts us more than it hurts them. But that pain would not be evenly distributed across the United States. It would, in fact, be highly concentrated, mostly in manufacturing states near the Canadian border, especially Michigan.
Michigan is to Canada, on a slightly lesser scale, what Canada is to the United States. Forty-two per cent of Michigan's exports go north, according to the Office of the U.S. Trade Representative. More than 700,000 Canadians visited the state in 2023. Perhaps more tangibly, 60 years after Canada and the U.S. signed the first Auto-Pact, the car industries in Michigan and Ontario are almost inextricably intertwined. The average component installed in a car or truck in Michigan or Ontario crosses the border multiple times - I had experts tell me the average is anywhere from six to 10 crossings - before finding a home in a finished product.
But all of that exchange - which lowers prices, increases competition and supports jobs - is only possible because of free trade. Throw up new, sustained tariffs that thicken the border and huge parts of the business don't make sense anymore. Just the threat of tariffs could already be hurting investment, according to Jason Miller, who chairs the department of supply chain management at Michigan State University. Delayed investments mean less money in the economy now and fewer jobs down the road. It means that the people in places like Macomb County, the ones who, perhaps more than anyone constituency, helped get Donald Trump elected, will be the ones most hurt by Trump's tariff.
For Bitely, that may, in the long run, be, if not a good thing, then at least a necessary one. She has Republican friends and colleagues who are still all in on the President's agenda, even as it threatens the well-being of their state. "It's really hard to break through (to them)," she said. "It's really, really, really hard. And honestly, I said this already once, but you have to show them. They may have to feel pain to show that tariffs are actually taxes, and that we're going be the ones impacted."
As for Canada, Bitely thinks our country has no choice but to fight back. "The only thing that works on bully," she said, "is bullying them back."
On my last day in Michigan, I drove to the site of the new Gordie Howe Bridge, on the outskirts or Detroit's historic downtown. It was a cold, cloudy day, just before sunset. The 2.5 km, double-sail span, a $9 billion investment in the future of Ontario/Michigan trade, stretched out of sight into the clouds over the Detroit River.
The bridge, which is set to open this fall, is a massive, physical reminder, that for all the talk of alternative markets and Canada First, we are inexorably and permanently bound to the United States. And nowhere is that more obvious than in Michigan. The low-rise, blue-collar suburbs of Detroit I spent those days driving through aren't competing with Ontario communities like Oshawa and Windsor so much as they are co-operating with them as part of a single, unified industry.
Canada can, and should, find new partners. We must assert our sovereignty, our independence, our culture. But we cannot change our geography. We cannot delink from the United States. We will all be poorer in every way if Donald Trump forces us to try.
CAPTION: Michael Radtke is a city councillor in Sterling Heights, Michigan, in the heart of American car country. Sterling Heights helped deliver the White House to Donald Trump. Few communities in America will be more devastated if Trump goes ahead with his plan to impose tariffs on Canadian goods.Inside the Stellantis' Sterling Heights Assembly Plant in Michigan.Donald Trump addresses a crowd of auto workers on Sept. 27, 2023, at Drake Enterprises in Clinton Township, Michigan. (Katy Kildee/The Detroit News/TNS)
CREDIT: Richard Warnica

Subject: Trade disputes; International trade; Presidents; Exports; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Trade disputes International trade Exports Tariffs

Location: United States--US; Macomb County Michigan; Michigan; Canada; Detroit Michigan

People: Trump, Donald J

Title: I discovered the secret strategy behind Donald Trump's tariff plan

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2025

Publication date: Feb 25, 2025

Section: opinion/star-columnists

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

Source type: Blog, Podcast, or Website

Language of publication: English

Document type: News

ProQuest document ID: 3170778596

Document URL: https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/i-discovered-secret-strategy-behind-donald-trumps/docview/3170778596/se-2?accountid=15090

Copyright: Copyright 2025 Toronto Star Newspapers Limited. All Rights Reserved.

Last updated: 2025-02-25

Database: Canadian Newsstream Collection

Document 34 of 500

Car country confusion: Trump's tariff strategy terrifies Michigan's autoworkers, too

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/car-country-confusion/docview/3170771607/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Car+country+confusion%3A+Trump%27s+tariff+strategy+terrifies+Michigan%27s+autoworkers%2C+too&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

"It's really, really, really hard. And honestly, I said this already once, but you have to show them. They may have to feel pain to show that tariffs are actually taxes, and that we're going be the ones impacted."
As for Canada, Bitely thinks our country has no choice but to fight back. "The only thing that works on bully," she said, "is bullying them back."
On my last day in Michigan, I drove to the site of the new Gordie Howe Bridge, on the outskirts or Detroit's historic downtown. It was a cold, cloudy day, just before sunset. The 2.5 km, double-sail span, a $9 billion investment in the future of Ontario/Michigan trade, stretched out of sight into the clouds over the Detroit River.
The bridge, which is set to open this fall, is a massive, physical reminder, that for all the talk of alternative markets and Canada First, we are inexorably and permanently bound to the United States. And nowhere is that more obvious than in Michigan. The lowrise, blue-collar suburbs of Detroit I spent those days driving through aren't competing with Ontario communities like Oshawa and Windsor so much as they are co-operating with them as part of a single, unified industry.
Canada can, and should, find new partners. We must assert our sovereignty, our independence, our culture. But we cannot change our geography. We cannot delink from the United States. We will all be poorer in every way if Donald Trump forces us to try.
Richard Warnica is a Toronto-based business feature writer for the Star.
CAPTION: Michael Radtke is a city councillor in Sterling Heights, Mich., in the heart of American car country. Sterling Heights helped deliver the White House to Donald Trump. Yet few communities in America will be more devastated if Trump goes ahead with his plan to impose tariffs on Canadian goods.Dax Melmer
CREDIT: Richard Warnica Toronto Star Richard Warnica is a Toronto-based business feature writer for the Star.;

Document 35 of 500

Erasing Canada's past while trying to be patriotic

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/erasing-canadas-past-while-trying-be-patriotic/docview/3170771223/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Erasing+Canada%27s+past+while+trying+to+be+patriotic&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I haven't checked, but I wonder if someone forgot to change the calendar on the wall of the Toronto District School Board (TDSB) headquarters. Over there, it still seems to be 2021 or 2022.
Back then it was all the rage to rename schools, streets, public squares, even whole universities, to erase the names of problematic historical figures. Statues came toppling down or were covered up, all the better to hide the images of men (almost always men) whose legacy we now find unacceptable. Dundas, Ryerson, Macdonald - names once honoured in the land were blackened.
There were an awful lot of assumptions behind that. One was that Canada, its very existence, could be taken for granted. Canadians could afford to subject our history to the most searching examination, even at the cost of tearing down supposed heroes. The task at hand was to purge the sins of the past in order, it was thought, to forge a fairer future.
But here we are in 2025, not just a different year but a different world. Canada is threatened as never before in modern times and a wave of patriotism is sweeping the nation. It's all about "Buy Canadian" and waving the flag. Suddenly, we realize we have something to lose - our country, warts and all.
And yet ... here comes the TDSB with a very 2022-style plan to rename three of its schools and scrub off any reminder of historical figures it says are connected to "systems of oppression." It plans to remove the names of Henry Dundas, Egerton Ryerson and Sir John A. Macdonald because of the "potential impact" they may have on students and staff.
How does this fit with the new mood of Canadian patriotism? Not very well, I think. Can you really condemn whole swaths of Canadian history - or really all of it if you listen to some of the more strident advocates of "anti-colonialism" - while trying to foster pride in the country that is the outcome of that history? Maybe, but it sure isn't easy.
There's also the thorny issue of whether all this cancelling makes any sense. This has been widely debated, but briefly: Henry Dundas was an 18th-century abolitionist whose modern-day critics think didn't push hard enough to end slavery, so his name is off what used to be Yonge-Dundas Square and will now be called by the African term "Sankofa."
Egerton Ryerson was an early Ontario educator who is blamed for inspiring residential schools, even though he died decades before they were introduced. The institution that used to bear his name is now Toronto Metropolitan University (a name I'm warming up to).
And, of course, John A. Macdonald was Canada's founding prime minister whose long record includes some very bad treatment of Indigenous people. But Macdonald's role in uniting a disparate group of colonies into this thing we call Canada should still be honoured. As the late Richard Gwyn, a legendary Star columnist and biographer of Sir John A., once observed: "No Macdonald, no Canada."
Statues of Macdonald have been taken down in half a dozen cities, but surely it's time for a pause and rethink on such moves given the current context. It's not just conservatives (including Pierre Poilievre, who quoted Sir John A.'s ringing defence of Canada against the American threat a century and a half ago as part of his big "Canada First" speech a couple of weekends ago) who are saying that. Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie makes the same point.
She told Chris Selley of the National Post that the TDSB should not go ahead with renaming those schools. "Trump's trade war reminds us why Canadian pride matters," she said. "Our history isn't perfect, but we should learn it - not rewrite it. (Doug) Ford dodges tough conversations. I won't. I'm proud of Canada." Crombie also says someone needs to figure out what to do with the statue of Macdonald at Queen's Park that's been hidden from view behind plywood panels since 2020. "Make a decision and deal with it."
Crombie is right. It must be possible to come to terms with past wrongs without sweeping away Canada's history and the people who made it. Especially now, when Canadians are realizing more than ever the value of what they have. The TDSB ought to read the room, check the calendar, and find another path.
ANDREW PHILLIPS IS A TORONTO-BASED STAFF COLUMNIST FOR THE STAR'S OPINION PAGE. REACH HIM AT APHILLIPS@THESTAR.CA
CAPTION: A statue of Sir John A. Macdonald is pulled down in Hamilton in 2021. Can you condemn whole swaths of Canadian history while trying to foster pride in the country that is the outcomeof that history? Maybe, but it isn't easy, writes Andrew Phillips.Barry Gray The Hamilton Spectator file photo

CREDIT: Andrew Phillips Toronto Star OPINION ANDREW PHILLIPS IS A TORONTO-BASED STAFF COLUMNIST FOR THE STAR'S OPINION PAGE. REACH HIM AT APHILLIPS@THESTAR.CA;

Document 36 of 500

5,000 affordable homes at risk: Ford government rolled back mandates for dozens of Toronto properties on election call eve

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/5-000-affordable-homes-at-risk-ford-government/docview/3170771025/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=5%2C000+affordable+homes+at+risk%3A+Ford+government+rolled+back+mandates+for+dozens+of+Toronto+properties+on+election+call+eve&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When the City of Toronto allowed landowners to build housing on nearly 70 workplace-designated land parcels, it mandated that a set percentage must be built as affordable housing. But when the change was sent to the Ontario government for approval, the affordability requirements were made optional.
A series of decisions signed on Jan. 27 and published Jan. 28 - the same day PC leader Doug Ford requested a dissolution of the legislature and sparked an early election - replaced city wording stating that developments "will secure" a certain amount of affordable housing with new phrasing that says builders will instead be "encouraged to include" a certain number of affordable homes.
The alterations have raised alarm at the municipal level, with city staff estimating that roughly 5,000 expected affordable homes - including rentals and owned units - could be at risk. "The previously secured number of affordable units that would be built on these lands may no longer be secured," staff said, listing nearly 70 addresses in 18 areas impacted by the change.
The province has not commented on why the affordability requirement was made optional, with the ministry of municipal affairs and housing declining to comment due to the election and the Progressive Conservative party campaign office failing to respond to questions.
The chair of city hall's housing committee, Parkdale-High Park Coun. Gord Perks, was caught off guard by the change. "We just lost the potential for 5,000 units of affordable housing in a housing crisis for no apparent reason," he said. "This is a very big setback for our affordable housing plans."
When land designated for workplaces is converted to allow housing, it immediately becomes more valuable, Perks said. "We want a good chunk of that profit reinvested for social purposes and building affordable housing," he said. "Then suddenly, out of nowhere, the province took a whole bunch of them and just said we're removing that public benefit."
The property conversions city hall had put to the province for approval include parts of Golden Mile - a stretch of Eglinton Avenue in Scarborough lined with strip malls and parking lots, expected to see dozens of new towers in the decades ahead - as well as the site of a garden centre and restaurant near Scarborough City Centre and land with an existing shopping market in Liberty Village.
The affordable housing provisions included, for example, a site where any new condos would have to contain at least five per cent affordable rentals, or seven per cent affordable owned units, with the affordability provision in place for at least 99 years. The amount of space required to be set aside could also rise with time - for example, a site where the affordable rental quota would rise by 1.5 per cent per year if certain application milestones weren't reached.
The province has not rejected all of the city's efforts to enshrine this kind of affordable mandate within its planning laws. Perks pointed to other land conversion proposals that have been approved by Queen's Park with the city's affordable housing requirements remaining intact, such as a conversion in his ward approved without any modifications in December 2023. That change secured 56 affordable rental homes in the Junction-Wallace Emerson area, he said.
Asked about the provincial move, Mayor Olivia Chow in a brief statement highlighted the city's existing tax waivers and incentives for affordable housing, and said Queen's Park's choices "do not assist in the city's goal." Chow's office declined an interview, and directed the inquiry to Coun. Perks.
Ontario's housing ministry declined to comment on the changes, citing the ongoing campaign that began just after it issued its decisions. "This means the Ontario government has entered caretaker mode until the completion of the next general election," the ministry wrote, suggesting the Star "may wish to resubmit" questions about the decision after voting day.
The modifications to make the affordable housing requirements optional were made on multiple Official Plan Amendments previously approved by Toronto city council and submitted to the province in 2023. Toronto's Official Plan guides how the city grows, laying out what can be built on various plots of land. The modifications made on Jan. 27 were signed by the Ontario Housing Ministry's Assistant Deputy Minister Sean Fraser, a senior bureaucrat within the government.
The land affected is owned by a long list of companies and individuals, property records show, including many numbered companies, the provincial transit agency Metrolinx, investment groups and real estate trusts such as Choice Properties REIT, financial institutions such as the Bank of Nova Scotia and Desjardins, food producers, and companies working in construction and development such as Torgan Construction and Trojan Gate Developments.
The city's push for more affordable housing on the converted lands comes as a growing number of Toronto households - not only low-income households, but many midincome earners - have struggled with their bills. As of January, rental.ca data shows the average one-bedroom apartment listing in Toronto cost $2,464 per month. That's lower than last year, but still only affordable - using national criteria - to a household earning at least $99,000 per year.
Some landowners, as part of the submission process to city hall where they made their case for land conversions, argued that pieces of employment land were prime territory for homes, especially as the city looks to encourage growth around transit. In one letter to city hall as part of that process, in mid-2023, the owner of an acre of Scarborough land that houses a Sheridan Nurseries garden centre and a Canbe Foods restaurant noted its proximity to the future Scarborough Subway extension, and suggested that closeness made the site ideal for "much needed housing."
But as city hall put forward sites where it agreed with a proposed conversion, a staff report also noted the importance of ensuring affordability. "As the urgency around the provision of affordable housing has grown, so too has the expectation from Council and local communities that affordable housing be included in new developments," former chief planner Gregg Lintern wrote.
City staff, in a new statement, said its affordable housing minimums - along with rules about minimum amounts of working space - "formed the basis" for council agreeing to make the land changes. Without those mandates, staff say it's likely the city will "achieve less," as it faces negotiations over each site.
The city cannot appeal the decision, with the provincial move considered final.
To Perks, the changes are frustrating and shrouded in mystery, with no clear indication of why they were made. "To just have the rug pulled out from under us, at the eleventh hour before an election, is really mind boggling," he said.
CAPTION: pdf of decisionThis property in Liberty Village is among dozens where city hall consented to convert former employment land and allow housing growth. While city hall's planning changes included minimum amounts of affordable housing, a provincial decision loosened the rules, saying instead that developers are"encouraged to include" the units.Andrew Francis Wallace/Toronto Star
CREDIT: Victoria Gibson

Full text: We should follow Singapore's
(bike lane) path
'A crisis of management and inadequate planning': Report on Toronto congestion calls for removal of bike lanes, CaféTO patios and on-street parking, Feb. 20
I find it comical the Board of Trade compares Toronto to Singapore and other cities to argue we should have higher construction fees, yet criticizes Toronto's aims to create complete streets.
They also fail to mention Singapore was voted the third-most bike-friendly city in the world, and that their "car-lite vision" for the city aims to reduce car dependency and instead promote other forms of transportation, including cycling.
I agree we should follow in Singapore's footsteps, but we should adopt their entire philosophy when it comes to clearing up traffic by making it easier for people to get out of their cars.
Barbi Lazarus, Toronto
Gretzky is a friend to Trump,
not Canadians
Canadian fans irked by Wayne Gretzky's affiliations with Trump amid tensions, Feb. 22
Wayne Gretzky's participation in the final 4 Nations Face-Off championship game was distasteful.
He came out on the American side, waved to the American players and couldn't even show his Canadian colours by putting on the red and white.
There are many Canadian former hockey greats who could have been called upon. Gretzky is a friend of Donald Trump and therefore not a friend of Canada. He and his glory days should be allowed to fade away.
Laura Fehr, Mississauga

fair and square
Canada triumphs over U.S. in intense 4 Nations Face-Off final with tensions high,

The outcome of the close game between the star-studded Canadian and American hockey teams in the 4 Nations Face-Off finale was decided by the superstars on Canada's team.

With regard to the booing of the anthems at hockey games, the NHL should simply adopt the policy of just playing the anthem of the home team.
That is, in the Canadian arenas it's just "O Canada." In American arenas, it's their anthem. Playing both is rather silly, since a large number of the players are from other countries anyway. This is the nastiest of public mischief.
No one will be reading the hundreds of thousands of responses that obedient employees send to Musk. His trifling abuse of everyone in federal employ is beyond the pale. It is textbook mischief.
Hugh McKechnie, Sudbury
This U.S. family supports our Canadian friends
My family has often come to Canada for vacation and we have always been delighted with the beauty of the country and hospitality of Canadians.
Most Americans are embarrassed and angry about Donald Trump's idiotic statements and actions. I well understand that Canadians do not want to contribute to the economy of a government that has been so ignorant and callous.
However, I and many people I have talked with want to support Canada and are planning to vacation there this summer. I would ask on behalf of my family and other Americans who come to Canada, to recognize our time there is because we love your nation. So please, do not project onto us the animosity Trump and those supporting him so deserve.
Doug Rasmussen,

Please Canadians, stand your ground. Stand up to the self-anointed "King."
You have more support over here than you know.
Kay Smith, Houston, Texas
Please vote, keep Ford in check
No one cares about the Ontario election. Was that Doug Ford's plan? Feb. 23
To my fellow Ontarians, please get out and vote and stop Premier Doug Ford from at least getting a huge majority.
This is the second year walk-in clinics in Oshawa, Courtice and Whitby have shortened their hours because of Ministry of Health cuts. How much strain does this put on area hospitals? Ford's $3-billion vote buying effort could have done so much to alleviate this problem.
