Document 38 of 500

Doug Ford says he has big plans to 'get it done' in Ontario. His track record in Toronto tells a different story

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-says-he-has-big-plans-get-done-ontario/docview/3170771001/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+says+he+has+big+plans+to+%27get+it+done%27+in+Ontario.+His+track+record+in+Toronto+tells+a+different+story&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Buried in the budget documents debated by Mayor Olivia Chow and Toronto city council earlier this month is a revelation that the Ontario Works (OW) program is not working in Ontario's capital city.
And Premier Doug Ford's government is to blame.
The $1.4 billion Toronto Employment and Social Services department, funded by the province and tasked with managing cases and giving out funds through various provincial welfare programs like OW and the Ontario Disability Support Program, has historically been relatively efficient. They responded to applications for support programs within their target of four business days about 77 per cent of the time in 2023.
In 2024, however, that number plunged to just 40 per cent, their budget submission reports. The drop came at a time when the number of applications to OW was rising fast, with the city's average monthly OW caseload growing 20 per cent over the last year, and new applications rose to an average of 6,500 per month, up from 5,125 in 2023 and 4,040 in 2022.
This all translates to more desperate people waiting longer for money they need to - maybe - stay in their homes and put food on the table. (I say maybe because the combined monthly allowance for basic needs and shelter for a single person on OW is just $733, unchanged since 2018.)
The cause? In 2023, the city says, the Ford government began implementing a new "centralized intake" process for OW applications, with an online form and an automated verification process. It has not gone well. The city budget notes the "final phase" of the centralized intake implementation will come this year, and hopes it will improve things, but it seems pretty skeptical.
"The province's claim - that [centralized intake] would simplify the OW application process for clients, reduce administration, and allow caseworkers more time to support clients - has yet to produce the intended administrative efficiencies," the city budget document reads.
That's very diplomatic language. Appropriate, I guess, for a city budget document. But as a columnist, I don't need to be so diplomatic, so let me rephrase: the Ford government royally screwed up. Again.
More than anything, these kinds of screw-ups have been the hallmark of the Ford government. They've used "get it done" as a slogan at times, but the ugly truth is that they've never proven to be very adept at doing things.
Screwing up a program needed by the province's most needy is just one of many past and ongoing examples.
The province has also messed up an intended reform to the various tribunals that adjudicate disputes. Tribunals Watch Ontario, a non-partisan group monitoring the changes the Ford government implemented in 2019, reports that a tenant seeking a hearing with the Landlord and Tenant Board waited an average of 277 days in 2024, compared to just 40 days in 2018.
They managed to make things seven times worse in less than seven years.
And on the housing file, Ford's attempts to compel Toronto and other municipalities to lower fees charged to developers and speed up approvals have also been remarkably messy - and delivered more bad outcomes than good ones.
A series of housing bills have given Toronto city hall a serious case of whiplash. In 2022 and 2023, the Ford government announced in rapid succession changes that would significantly lower the city's revenue from development charges, then that it would order an audit of city hall's finances to prove Toronto didn't actually need that revenue, then that it would be partly walking back its changes to the development charge system, then that the partly completed audit was cancelled.
Meanwhile, other provincial changes to housing legislation have also seen quixotic outcomes and have done little to help Ontario build new housing. Prescribed tough deadlines for municipalities to deal with planning applications have resulted in Toronto's planning department just automatically rejecting complex developments it believes can't be processed within the deadline to avoid financial penalties.
And other changes to laws about heritage protection have resulted in the city putting in extra hours to review 3,885 listed heritage properties before a 2027 deadline to see if they are, in fact, heritage properties. No one is entirely sure what the point is.
This provincial election has seen a lot of criticism levelled at some of Ford's big ideas. And while many of Ford's ideas absolutely deserve criticism - like the ungodly expensive Highway 401 tunnel or the private spa on public land at Ontario Place - I suspect a lot of voters appreciate that he at least has some ideas to offer in response to real problems. The other parties, too focused on tearing down Ford's plans, have not done nearly enough to articulate their own better ideas.
And they definitely have not done enough to highlight this government's messy, broken and downright embarrassing record when it comes to actually running the day-to-day of government.
With election day on Thursday, it's feeling like a missed opportunity. There's much more to governing than snappy slogans and big ideas, but this premier has been able to get by without much else.
CAPTION: Doug Ford's government has never proven to be very adept at day-to-day governing, writes Matt Elliott.
CREDIT: Matt Elliott

Document 39 of 500

Bonnie Crombie endorses Mark Carney as federal Liberal leader

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/bonnie-crombie-endorses-mark-carney-as-federal/docview/3170770992/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Bonnie+Crombie+endorses+Mark+Carney+as+federal+Liberal+leader&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Bonnie Crombie is endorsing Mark Carney to be federal Liberal leader.
As first reported by the Star, the Ontario Liberal chief said Carney has her backing to be the next prime minister.
Carney, the former governor of the Bank of Canada and the Bank of England, is the front-runner to succeed Prime Minister Justin Trudeau in the March 9 leadership contest.
"Liberals in Ontario and across Canada are feeling a spark right now. A confidence that we can stand united and meet this critical moment in history - together," Crombie said in a statement.
"Staring down the threat of Donald Trump's tariffs, it is more clear than ever that we need leaders who have the right priorities," she said, referring to the U.S. president's threatened 25 per cent levies on Canadian goods.
"Leaders who understand how to get the basics right for people, how to shore up and diversify our economy and how to protect and improve the public health-care system that we all take pride in and deeply rely on every day," added Crombie.
"I believe Mark Carney is that kind of leader. I'm proud to offer him my support to be the next leader of the Liberal Party of Canada and I look forward to working with him in the days and months ahead to make sure we do the best for the people of Ontario and Canada."
After the Star reported the news early Tuesday, Carney reacted on social media.
"Thank you @BonnieCrombie - for your support, for your strong advocacy to help Ontarians get better access to a family doctor, and for putting economic growth first," he tweeted.
"Wishing you the best this Thursday, and let's keep building!"
Last June, Carney held a Bay Street fundraiser for Crombie and the two are to meet before Thursday's Ontario election "to discuss the economy and health care," a Liberal source said, speaking confidentially in order to discuss internal deliberations.
At the Star's editorial board on Friday, the provincial Liberal leader, whose own polling had been hindered by Trudeau's unpopularity, confirmed Carney's rise has been "helpful" to her candidates.
"I think the renewal of leadership is also helpful. I mean, people want to engage at the doors. 'Do you think Carney will win on one ballot?' They hear that we're Liberals and they want to engage," said Crombie.
"Before the renewal, they didn't want to engage so much," she admitted.
Her endorsement came after Carney debated rivals Chrystia Freeland, Karina Gould and Frank Baylis Monday in Montreal in French.
The English-language debate is there Tuesday night.
At the Star's editorial board Monday, provincial NDP Leader Marit Stiles was asked about the travails of the federal New Democrats.
With federal NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh languishing in the national polls, some provincial New Democrats have privately expressed concern that might have an impact on the official opposition party at Queen's Park.
But Stiles said that Crombie's move to the right of former Liberal premier Kathleen Wynne has helped her party because there's still widespread support for left-leaning politics.
"I push back a little on that," she said. "I don't see that at all. A lot of what Crombie and the Liberals have been trying to do is take a lot of our policy and they are running on that."
Stile predicted progressive voters will see through that on Thursday and reject Crombie's move to the centre.
"What I'm finding is that a lot of previous Liberal voters here in Ontario are not interested in that."
While polls have suggested Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives have benefitted from a halo effect from Pierre Poilievre's federal Conservatives in the past, the provincial Tories say Carney's surge in public opinion surveys has changed that.
Ford's PCs now believe they can win more Ontario ridings Thursday than Poilievre's party will win here in a federal election expected in spring.
CAPTION: Bonnie Crombie (left) is endorsing Mark Carney for the Liberal leadership.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie, Kristin Rushowy

Document 41 of 500

The TDSB is set to condemn swaths of Canadian history at exactly the wrong moment

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/tdsb-is-set-condemn-swaths-canadian-history-at/docview/3170770902/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+TDSB+is+set+to+condemn+swaths+of+Canadian+history+at+exactly+the+wrong+moment&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I haven't checked, but I wonder if someone forgot to change the calendar on the wall of the Toronto District School Board (TDSB) headquarters. Over there it still seems to be 2021 or 2022.
Back then it was all the rage to rename schools, streets, public squares, even whole universities, to erase the names of problematic historical figures. Statues came toppling down or were covered up, all the better to hide the images of men (almost always men) whose legacy we now find unacceptable. Dundas, Ryerson, Macdonald - names once honoured in the land were blackened.
There were an awful lot of assumptions behind that. One was that Canada, its very existence, could be taken for granted. Canadians could afford to subject our history to the most searching examination, even at the cost of tearing down supposed heroes. The task at hand was to purge the sins of the past in order, it was thought, to forge a fairer future.
But here we are in 2025, not just a different year but a different world. Canada is threatened as never before in modern times and a wave of patriotism is sweeping the nation. It's all about "Buy Canadian" and waving the flag. Suddenly, we realize we have something to lose - our country, warts and all.
And yet ... here comes the TDSB with a very 2022-style plan to rename three of its schools and scrub off any reminder of historical figures it says are connected to "systems of oppression." It plans to remove the names of Henry Dundas, Egerton Ryerson and Sir John A. Macdonald because of the "potential impact" they may have on students and staff.
How does this fit with the new mood of Canadian patriotism? Not very well, I think. Can you really condemn whole swaths of Canadian history - or really all of it if you listen to some of the more strident advocates of "anti-colonialism" - while trying to foster pride in the country that is the outcome of that history? Maybe, but it sure isn't easy.
There's also the thorny issue of whether all this cancelling makes any sense. This has been widely debated, but briefly: Henry Dundas was an 18th-century abolitionist whose modern-day critics think didn't push hard enough to end slavery, so his name is off what used to be Yonge-Dundas Square and will now be called by the African term "Sankofa."
Egerton Ryerson was an early Ontario educator who is blamed for inspiring residential schools, even though he died decades before they were introduced. The institution that used to bear his name is now Toronto Metropolitan University (a name I'm warming up to).
And, of course, John A. Macdonald was Canada's founding prime minister whose long record includes some very bad treatment of Indigenous people. But Macdonald's role in uniting a disparate group of colonies into this thing we call Canada should still be honoured. As the late Richard Gwyn, a legendary Star columnist and biographer of Sir John A., once observed: "No Macdonald, no Canada."
Statues of Macdonald have been taken down in half a dozen cities, but surely it's time for a pause and rethink on such moves given the current context. It's not just conservatives (including Pierre Poilievre, who quoted Sir John A.'s ringing defence of Canada against the American threat a century and a half ago as part of his big "Canada First" speech a couple of weekends ago) who are saying that. Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie makes the same point.
She told Chris Selley of the National Post that the TDSB should not go ahead with renaming those schools. "Trump's trade war reminds us why Canadian pride matters," she said. "Our history isn't perfect, but we should learn it - not rewrite it. (Doug) Ford dodges tough conversations. I won't. I'm proud of Canada." Crombie also says someone needs to figure out what to do with the statue of Macdonald at Queen's Park that's been hidden from view behind plywood panels since 2020. "Make a decision and deal with it."
Crombie is right. It must be possible to come to terms with past wrongs without sweeping away Canada's history and the people who made it. Especially now, when Canadians are realizing more than ever the value of what they have. The TDSB ought to read the room, check the calendar, and find another path.
CAPTION: A statue of Sir John A. Macdonald is pulled down in Gore Park, Hamilton in 2021.The Toronto District School Board said it is going to rename three schools, including Sir. John A. MacDonald Collegiate Institute.
CREDIT: Andrew Phillips

Full text: Toronto should follow Singapore's (bike lane) path'A crisis of management and inadequate planning': Report on Toronto congestion calls for removal of bike lanes, CaféTO patios and on-street parking, Feb. 20
I find it comical the Board of Trade compares Toronto to Singapore and other cities to argue we should have higher construction fees, yet criticizes Toronto's aims to create complete streets.
They also fail to mention Singapore was voted the third-most bike-friendly city in the world, and that their 'car-lite vision' for the city aims to reduce car dependency and instead promote other forms of transportation, including cycling.
I agree we should follow in Singapore's footsteps, but we should adopt their entire philosophy when it comes to clearing up traffic by making it easier for people to get out of their cars.
Barbi Lazarus, Toronto
Gretzky did no favours to CanadiansCanadian fans irked by Wayne Gretzky's affiliations with Trump amid tensions, Feb. 22
Wayne Gretzky's participation in the final 4 Nations Face-Off championship game was distasteful.
He came out on the American side, waved to the American players and couldn't even show his Canadian colours by putting on the red and white. Surely, he has an old jersey hanging in a closet somewhere.
There are many Canadian former hockey greats that could have been called upon. Wayne Gretzky is a friend of Trump and therefore not a friend of Canada.
He and his glory days should be allowed to fade away.
Laura Fehr, Mississauga
Team Canada won fair and square. Deal with it TrumpCanada triumphs over US in intense 4 Nations Face-Off final with tensions high, Feb. 20
The outcome of the close game between the star-studded Canadian and American hockey teams in the 4 Nations Face-Off finale was decided by the superstars on Canada's team.
It was a brilliant victory in a tough, exciting match.
Hopefully Donald Trump doesn't spoil the memory of the event by blaming American immigrants, the diversity program or too much inclusiveness on the loss. Or was it that the referees were against the Americans?
Let's hope the loss doesn't mean more tariffs on Canada and the outcome is accepted with dignity.
Carl Hager, Gatineau, Que.
There's a solution to booing of the anthemsAmerican fans lightly boo 'O Canada' before 4 Nations Face-Off title game, Feb. 20
With regard to the booing of the anthems at the hockey games, the NHL should simply adopt the policy of just playing the anthem of the host team.
That is, in the Canadian arenas it's just O Canada.
In the American arenas it's their anthem.
Playing both is rather silly since a large number of the players are from other countries anyway.
And why should they, or any of us for that matter, have to listen to the American anthem while playing in Canada?
Fraser Steele, Toronto
Booing doesn't just target Trump, but also those who voted for him
There has been a recent spate of opinions suggesting we shouldn't boo the American anthem because our beef is with Trump, not the American people.
Just one question: How do you think Trump got into the White House?
Because American people voted for him.
They are as much to blame as Trump himself.
David Lovgren, Scarborough
Musk targeting U.S. workers is a disgraceMusk gives all federal workers 48 hours to explain what they did last week or face consequences, Feb. 22
The Musk-Trump assault against those employed by the American federal government has hit a new low.
The unique feature of this attack on every person in government service is its absurdity. Musk requires a five point account of what everyone did last week. "Failure to respond will be taken as a resignation."
This is the nastiest of public mischief.
No one will be reading the hundreds of thousands of responses that obedient employees send to Elon Musk. His trifling abuse of everyone in federal employ is beyond the pale. It is textbook mischief.
Are Americans starting to wonder about the president they elected?
Hugh McKechnie, Sudbury, Ont.
This American family wholeheartedly supports our Canadian friends
My family has often come to Canada for vacation and we have always been delighted with the beauty of the country and hospitality of Canadians.
Most Americans are embarrassed and angry about Trump's idiotic statements and actions, not just towards Canada, but across many fronts. I well understand that Canadians do not want to contribute to the economy of a government that has been so ignorant and callous.
However, I and many people I have talked with want to support Canada and are planning to vacation there this summer. I would ask on behalf of my family and other Americans who come to Canada, to recognize our time there is because we love your nation.
So please, do not project onto us the animosity Trump and those supporting him so deserve.
Doug Rasmussen, East Peoria, Ill.
Canadians have support south of the border
As an American and Texan it is difficult to wake up and hear or read about what new abomination Trump has wrought.
Low-information conspiracy minded individuals elected this monstrosity. They thought electing a reality show entertainer would mean he would be able to do what he did on television. No matter that nearly every business he had went bankrupt.
Please Canadians, stand your ground. Stand up to the self anointed 'King.'
You have more support over here than you know.
Kay Smith, Houston, Tex.
To my fellow Ontarians: Please vote and keep Ford in checkNo one cares about the Ontario election. Was that Doug Ford's plan? Feb. 23
To my fellow Ontarians, please get out and vote and stop Premier Doug Ford from at least getting a huge majority.
Ask yourself, in the past seven years, are you any better off? I don't think many people will say yes.
This is the second year walk-in clinics in Oshawa, Courtice and Whitby have shortened their hours because of Ministry of Health cuts. How much strain does this put on area hospitals?
Ford's $3-billion vote buying effort could have done so much to alleviate this problem, and others in this province.
Vote for change.
Patricia Holland, Oshawa
Canada must take leadership role when it comes to children and nutritionToronto city council has a chance to do something about this shameful new statistic, Feb. 11
A recent article in the Star outlined the importance of child nutrition, in the context of the need for a universal student food plan in Toronto.
While Canadians are rightly concerned about child nutrition at home, the situation globally is worse.
As someone who has worked with children in a Haitian orphanage, I have seen firsthand the devastating effects of malnutrition. In the past, Canada has shown itself to be a leader in the fight against child hunger and malnutrition. That leadership is even more crucial today as conflict, climate change and high food prices are precipitating a global hunger crisis.
Canada will have an opportunity to demonstrate much-needed global leadership at the Nutrition For Growth Summit, which will take place in Paris in late March. Every child deserves a future. They need a healthy start in life to succeed, which means access to plentiful nutritious food throughout their childhood.
For this reason, the Canadian government needs to commit to nutrition-specific funding in Paris next month.
Sharon Graham, Toronto
CAPTION: A letter writer says Toronto would be wise to look to Singapore for answers when it comes to alleviating gridlock.

Document 43 of 500

Doug Ford pledges $40B in new spending to offset Donald Trump's threatened tariffs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-pledges-40b-new-spending-offset-donald/docview/3170240764/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+pledges+%2440B+in+new+spending+to+offset+Donald+Trump%27s+threatened+tariffs&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Donald Trump's looming tariffs mean Ontario must continue to bleed red ink for the foreseeable future, admits Doug Ford.
As Ford's Progressive Conservatives unveiled their platform - a 32-page manifesto that is not fully costed and pledges $40 billion in new spending - he conceded deficits are here to stay.
"If the tariffs hit us, yes, we won't be able to balance because - just similar to the pandemic - we're going to invest into the people," the PC leader told reporters Monday at a North York painters' union centre.
The U.S. president is threatening 25 per cent tariffs on all Canadian products next month, which would be harmful to Ontario's trade-dependent economy.
Since being elected in 2018, the Tories, who governed during the global COVID-19 pandemic, have run massive budget deficits.
Under Ford, the provincial debt has soared by $116 billion to $429 billion, the largest debt of any subnational jurisdiction in the world.
But asked whether he was disrespecting taxpayers by not releasing a costed platform before Thursday's provincial election, the Tory chief was defensive.
"Not at all. We're pretty clear that if these tariffs come, our investments going to be over $40 billion no matter if it's loans or other investments we have for people and businesses to make sure they're secure here if Trump's tariffs come and hit us," he said.
"We've been very fiscally responsible. We're prudent fiscal managers with the taxpayers' money."
But NDP Leader Marit Stiles said Ford's platform is "not worth the paper it's written on."
"For the third election in a row, Doug Ford's making the same empty promises. People know he's failed to deliver for years - just look at the cost of rent or groceries," said Stiles.
"Let's put the waste, corruption and false promises of the Ford years in the rearview mirror, and let's elect an NDP government on your side."
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said she's "never seen" a platform "spend so much and do so little."
"This is a platform for lobbyists and insiders. What has he done to make life more affordable for people," Crombie in Toronto-St. Paul's, where she was joined by local candidate Stephanie Smyth, a prominent former CP24 anchor.
"He hasn't costed the fantasy tunnel and he doesn't tell us how he's going to pay for it either," she said, referring to Ford's proposed Mississauga-Scarborough tunnel under Highway 401, which could cost $150 billion and is buried on page eight of the PC platform without any price tag.
Despite no feasibility study on the scheme, the Tories have vowed to moved forward on the tunnel.
But it was not included in the "highways and roads" section of the PC election booklet.
Instead, there was 38-word sentence touting the project underneath a longer entry on how the Tories would "return sanity to bike lanes" by removing them from Bloor Street West, Yonge Street and University Avenue, which was finalized last December.
One new item in the manifesto was the elimination of the minimum price for beer, wine and spirits.
That hearkens to Ford's 2018 "buck-a-beer" election pledge that fell flat because brewers could not afford to produce and sell beer for that price.
Currently, wine must sell for no less than $8.10 for a 750 ml bottle, while beer cannot be sold for less than $2.82 per litre - or $1.41 for a 500 ml tallboy can - and spirits, such as vodka and gin, must sell for at least $31.15 a 750 ml bottle.
Ford, who does not drink alcohol, said he was eliminating the floor price to give people a break.
"They don't do it in Alberta. They don't do it in Quebec. So why do we have to have the base? Let's reduce the cost of that. Put more money back into people's pockets again," he said.
But the LCBO, the Crown-owned booze monopoly, announced Monday beer taxes would increase by about 4.4 per cent on schedule on April 1.
Earlier in Ottawa, Crombie was focused on what she sees as the ballot question: finding a family doctor for the 2.5 million Ontarians who don't have one.
"People are telling me that they're afraid to get sick ... they know that our hospitals are overflowing and that they're underfunded and the wait times are enormous," the Liberal leader said, blasting Ford's claim that patients with minor medical problems are swamping emergency rooms instead of going to walk-in clinics.
"I don't know what choice people have," said Crombie, who has pledged to ensure every Ontarian has a family doctor within four years - promises that Ford and Stiles have also made.
"It's not their fault, Doug, it's yours."
At his platform launch, Ford insisted he would improve access to doctors, which is why he hired Dr. Jane Philpott, a former federal Liberal minister, as his health guru.
"It just goes to show, I don't care what political colour you come from, she's well renowned around the entire country, and in four years, we're going to connect ... every single person (with a physician)," he said.
Speaking to the Star editorial board, Stiles said the doctors' shortage "is taking a big toll on Ontarians" and impacting the economy.
"Employers that I talk to - in everything from tech to auto - will tell you that what's making it difficult for them to attract more workers here ... is the fact that they can't ensure that their workers are going to have a home they can afford, that they can't guarantee they're going to get a school for their kids, or a family doctor," she said.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner, meanwhile, was in Bracebridge rallying support for Parry Sound-Muskoka candidate Matt Richter who is trying to unseat Tory Graydon Smith.
"Community members of all political stripes are getting behind Matt because they know that he will be the best advocate for them at Queen's Park," said Schreiner.
"I know from knocking on thousands of doors here that people are ready for change. Electing Matt is the only way to stop another Conservative seat and make that change happen."
CAPTION: Ontario PC Leader Doug Ford makes an announcement in Toronto on Feb. 24, 2025.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie, Rob Ferguson, Kristin Rushowy

Subject: Political campaigns; Provincial elections; Beer; Roads & highways; Tariffs

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+polls+suggesting+victory+for+Doug+Ford+with+only+days+to+go+before+provincial+vote&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With the Ontario election campaign underway, the Star has a poll tracker so you can follow all the twists and turns ahead of election day on Feb. 27.
We've got the numbers from the top pollsters in one place, with handy charts, and a rolling, last-four-poll average of all the results.
Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives are well ahead of the second-place Liberals with just days to go before Ontarians head to the polls.

In Ontario's last election, in 2022, Ford's PCs took 40.8 per cent of the popular vote across the province, with the Liberals earning 23.9 per cent and the NDP taking 23.7 per cent. That led Ford to his second straight majority government, with 83 seats. Despite finishing narrowly behind the Liberals in the popular vote, the NDP took 31 seats and the Liberals just eight.
The Star is compiling polling for the 2025 Ontario election from polling firms that received a grade of A or A+ from 338Canada during the 2022 Ontario election. 338Canada is an independent media organization that created a statistical model of electoral projections based on opinion polls, electoral history, and demographic data.
These polling firms use a variety of polling methods, including online surveys and Smart Interactive Voice Response (IVR) systems. As online polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, margins listed here are for a comparable random sample of this size.
Percentage breakdowns are shares of committed or leaning voters, in line with industry conventions.

Document 47 of 500

Liberal leadership debates: 3 key things to watch for during Monday and Tuesday's showdowns

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/liberal-leadership-debates-3-key-things-watch/docview/3169998420/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberal+leadership+debates%3A+3+key+things+to+watch+for+during+Monday+and+Tuesday%27s+showdowns&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Less than two weeks before federal Liberals choose Canada's next prime minister, four candidates to replace Justin Trudeau are set to square off in a pair of debates that could help decide who leads the governing party into a coming federal election.
It's widely seen as a key moment in a condensed race, 49 days after Trudeau suspended Parliament and announced he will resign on March 9 when his party chooses its next leader.
First, on Monday at 8 p.m. EST, the candidates will gather in a Montreal film studio for a French debate that will test how perceived anglophone frontrunners handle themselves in the language of a province crucial to the party's hopes to remain in power.
Then, on Tuesday at 8 p.m. EST, comes the English debate, when contenders will appear in that same studio to pitch themselves to a likely broader audience of Liberals and curious politicos across the country.
For Greg MacEachern, a veteran Liberal strategist and principal at KAN Strategies, the moment is a chance for the Liberals to showcase their offering for a general election that could come in a matter of weeks. But it also comes with risk.
"It doesn't matter the party, but occasionally leadership debates can really show division, or sow division," MacEachern said.
Here are three things to watch for in the Liberal leadership debates.
Leurs français
In a race dominated by anglophone candidates, Monday's French debate is expected to be a critical test of linguistic ability for a party with a political base and deep history in Québec.
"There will be a lot of rankings and reviews of the candidates' French," said Dan Arnold, a seasoned Liberal campaign adviser who worked as director of research and advertising in Trudeau's Prime Minister's Office (PMO).
Arnold said those rankings will matter, since French-dominated Québec is a "key part of the Liberal winning coalition," so "you want to make sure you connect with Quebecers."
But it also matters to Liberals outside Québec, Arnold said, since they understand that whomever they choose as leader must be attractive to Québec voters.
Arnold recalled how, in the 2006 Liberal leadership contest, a newspaper judged the candidate he worked for - former Ontario Liberal education minister Gerard Kennedy - to have poor French.
"It really just deep-sixed his campaign, because people were like, 'well, he can't speak French well enough, so he can't win in Québec,'" Arnold said.
Vandana Kattar, a Liberal campaign organizer who also worked in Trudeau's PMO, said she'll be watching for "the quality of language ability" from the candidates - whether they are at ease and appear conversational, or get thrown off by a technical word or phrase.
While most of the candidates have already demonstrated varying degrees of French ability, with former Liberal MP Frank Baylis the sole contender from the province of Québec, competence in the official language has come up as an issue. Before the party disqualified him, Ottawa-area MP Chandra Arya rejected the assertion that the prime minister should be proficient in French and English.
Former Paul Martin-era MP Ruby Dhalla twice asked for a "personal translator" for the French debate, but she was also disqualified from the race on Friday.
Fireworks
For Kattar and others, another big thing to watch for is whether - or to what extent - the candidates attack each other during the debates.
"My sense is that everybody's probably just going to pile on (Mark) Carney," she said, referring to the former central banker running for the job.
Carney has emerged as the perceived frontrunner, after his campaign raked in more than $1.75 million in donations by Feb. 9, according to Elections Canada returns - more than all the other candidates' reported donations combined. Scores of MPs and cabinet ministers in the Trudeau government have also endorsed the former Bank of Canada and Bank of England governor.
Arnold said he'll be watching especially for how former finance minister Chrystia Freeland pursues Carney, given that he appears to be outpacing her in the race.
"She kind of needs to knock him off his game," Arnold said. "She has to make the case that she's more electable than Mark Carney. And one way to do that is for him to stumble in the debate."
Arnold added that former House leader Karina Gould might also be "more aggressive than she would otherwise be" - especially if she sees a real path to victory in the contest.
But Liberal strategists are also aware that friction between the candidates could be clipped and used by their political opponents if it is particularly cutting. For example, if one candidate lands a real blow against a rival that wins the contest, that exchange could be fodder for the Conservatives in the coming general election.
That's even more of a concern in a shortened race like this one, with opposition parties signalling they intend to topple the Liberal minority government and trigger an election with Parliament set to return with a new prime minister on March 24, MacEachern said.
"It is very much within your prerogative and your right to pursue a tear-down campaign, but I think Liberals will be looking at that through the lens of: how is this really helping Canada, and how is this helping our cause?" he said.
The final boss
That cause, of prolonging their party's hold in office, will face its true challenge in that coming general election. Polls show their main rival in that contest will be Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives.
That looming match-up means Liberals watching this week's leadership debates will also consider how the candidates might perform as the party's standard-bearer in the next federal election, Kattar said.
"(The Conservatives) are so good on their feet, they're so good at (communications), they're so good at driving message. And if you're knocked off your message, how good are you to snap back?"
Arnold added that he is curious for any "preview" of how the candidates would take on Poilievre, and how they might try to "define him" for Canadian voters.
For MacEachern, the candidates - especially the frontrunners - should treat the debates as a chance to send a message to Liberals, and also to the public in general.
And that means aiming attacks at Conservatives, rather than each other.
"It's a big opportunity not to waste," he said.
CAPTION: Liberal leadership candidates, from top left, clockwise, Chrystia Freeland, Mark Carney, Frank Baylis and Karina Gould.
CREDIT: Alex Ballingall

Subject: Candidates; Voters; Anglophones; Political leadership; Prime ministers; Debates; Political advertising; Elections

Location: Canada

People: Carney, Mark; Gould, Karina; Freeland, Chrystia

Document 48 of 500

A guide to the Ontario election's most dramatic ridings to watch. Is yours one of them?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/guide-ontario-elections-most-dramatic-ridings/docview/3169998283/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+guide+to+the+Ontario+election%27s+most+dramatic+ridings+to+watch.+Is+yours+one+of+them%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There are 124 ridings in Ontario and each is represented by one MPP in the legislature. But some ridings have higher stakes and more drama than others.
Here are 10 to watch across the province when the ballots are tallied in the Feb. 27 Ontario election.
Mississauga East-Cooksville
This is ground zero for Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie in her bid to return the party to prominence following devastating defeats in the last two elections.
After 15 months of leading her small caucus of nine MPPs from the sidelines, she is counting on voters who elected her three times as mayor of Mississauga to give her a seat in the legislature.
While the sprawling city has been a stronghold for Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives, the riding has no incumbent after MPP Kaleed Rasheed, a former Tory, decided not to run again.
He was a cabinet minister but was ejected from the PCs to sit as an Independent after giving the integrity commissioner wrong information about a 2020 trip to Las Vegas with associates of Ford and a Greenbelt developer.
Running for the PCs is Silvia Gualtieri, an insurance executive and mother-in-law of Brampton Mayor Patrick Brown, who was Ford's predecessor as party leader. Gualtieri is also the sister of Mississauga-Lakeshore Conservative MPP Rudy Cuzzetto.
Crombie and Brown have had plenty of tangles in Peel politics, upping the ante on this race.
The NDP is running renters' advocate Alex Venuto and the Green candidate is David Zeni, a math and science teacher.
Eglinton-Lawrence
There was plenty of drama in this key PC-held Toronto riding well before election day.
Two-term incumbent Conservative Robin Martin decided not to run again, raising Liberal hopes.
Then, as the nomination deadline closed, Liberal candidate Vince Gasparro got an unexpected boost. New Democrat hopeful Natasha Doyle-Merrick backed out to support him, hoping to avoid an anti-Ford vote split that could keep the area Conservative.
"The race in Eglinton-Lawrence is pivotal," she said.
That move set the stage for a two-way race between Gasparro, a finance executive who served as principal secretary to former Toronto mayor John Tory, and Conservative candidate Michelle Cooper.
She is executive director of the PC Ontario Fund, the party's fundraising arm.
"Even the other party has recognized that this is a Bonnie versus Doug race," Crombie's campaign told the Star, speaking of the NDP.
New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles was less enthusiastic, acknowledging she did not know her candidate was going to drop out and saying she is "disappointed" voters will not have an NDP option.
The Green candidate is Leah Tysoe.
Hamilton Centre
In a bid to retake this downtown riding from Independent MPP Sarah Jama, the New Democrats nominated Dr. Robin Lennox, a family physician.
She's an assistant clinical professor at McMaster University's medical school who also provides care to homeless people and others facing substance use challenges.
Jama, who was ejected from the NDP caucus in October 2023 for insubordination, had tried to get back into the party but her application was rejected, angering some on the riding association's executive.
While Lennox has the party's backing, some in the NDP are frustrated at Jama's ouster. She has a dedicated following as an Independent and has won the endorsements of the city's labour council and a major Steelworkers union local.
The Liberals have nominated Eileen Walker, a former social worker and justice of the peace.
Sarah Bokhari, who ran in the riding in 2022, is the Progressive Conservative candidate and Lucia Iannantuono, an electrical engineer, is running for the Greens.
This riding is important to the NDP. Former party leader Andrea Horwath represented it for years before leaving provincial politics to run successfully for mayor of the steel city in 2022.
Etobicoke-Lakeshore
The Liberals are making a determined effort here after coming within 842 votes of Conservative incumbent Christine Hogarth in 2022.
Liberal candidate Lee Fairclough, a former president of St. Mary's Hospital in Kitchener and until recently a senior vice-president at the Centre for Addiction and Mental Health in Toronto, is making a second run at the riding.
She is campaigning heavily on health-care issues.
The NDP candidate is Rozhen Asrani, a product manager in the health-care technology sector who has served as the president of the Mimico Residents Association. The Green Party candidate is Sean Garcia (McClocklin).
York South-Weston
Michael Ford's decision not to run again for the Progressive Conservatives after one term at Queen's Park has thrown this race wide open.
The New Democrats are hoping former one-term MPP Faisal Hassan can make a comeback in the riding that was Liberal for years until the party's 2018 collapse.
Ford, nephew of the PC leader, wrested the riding from Hassan in the 2022 provincial election and quickly found himself in cabinet, but took a leave of absence for health reasons last fall.
Stepping in to run for the Tories is Mohamed Firin, a former member of the Premier's Council on Equality of Opportunity.
Daniel Di Giorgio, a Toronto Catholic school board trustee, is running for the Liberals. The Green candidate is Lilian Barrera.
Haldimand-Norfolk
Bobbi Ann Brady won this riding as an Independent in 2022 after longtime Tory MPP Toby Barrett retired - and threw his full support to her. This election, he's been putting up signs and knocking on doors once again.
The PCs, who placed second in the last election, have recruited Norfolk County Mayor Amy Martin as their candidate in a bid to regain the riding that includes Port Dover, a Lake Erie fishing and beach town famous for fried perch dinners.
Martin is on an unpaid leave of absence for the campaign.
It's tough for Independent candidates to win in Ontario politics, which makes this an interesting test for both Brady and Ford.
The Liberals are running Vandan Patel, the NDP Erica Englert and the Green Party candidate is Anna Massinen.
Oshawa
Voters have their choice of MPPs - the one who's been at Queen's Park for 11 years, and her PC predecessor.
New Democrat Jennifer French, a teacher, is facing a challenge from former MPP Jerry Ouellette, who held the riding from 1995 until French defeated him by more than 7,000 votes in 2014.
The Conservatives sense an opening in Oshawa after French won by a narrow margin of 757 votes over the Conservatives in 2022 as the governing party made a determined push for labour votes, taking NDP ridings in Hamilton, Windsor and Timmins.
Liberal candidate Viresh Bansal has come under fire for anti-Sikh and homophobic comments, which Crombie has condemned. After several days of controversy, the Liberals parted ways with Bansal on Friday. Nicholas Sirgool is running for the Greens.
Ottawa Centre
Former city councillor Catherine McKenney, who placed a strong second in the city's 2022 mayoralty race, is taking a run at this downtown riding for the New Democrats.
The opening came when NDP incumbent Joel Harden, who won by a huge margin in 2022, left provincial politics to run for the New Democrats in the next federal election, expected soon.
In the mayoralty contest, McKenney outperformed the winner, Mark Sutcliffe, in downtown wards.
She faces Liberal candidate Thomas Simpson, a former public servant and vice-president of the Canadian National Institute for the Blind. Canadian Armed Forces veteran Scott Healey is carrying the PC banner and Simon Beckett is running for the Green Party.
Parkdale-High Park
The departure of New Democrat MPP Bhutila Karpoche for federal politics puts this riding, with its mix of neighbourhoods and incomes, in play.
It has seen close races between the NDP and Liberals in the past.
Alexa Gilmour, a United Church minister and mother of five active in social causes, is running for the New Democrats.
The Liberal candidate is Nadia Guerrera, an educator who ran for the party in neighbouring York South-Weston in the 2022 election.
Queen's Park political staffer Justine Teplycky, a former radio reporter and director of communications to Paul Calandra in the Ministry of Municipal Affairs and Housing, is on the ballot for the Conservatives.
Running for the Greens is Anna Gorka, an architect who specializes in sustainable design and has lived in Parkdale since 1982.
Sault Ste. Marie
With incumbent Conservative Ross Romano leaving electoral politics for personal reasons, this previously Liberal riding has become a political battleground.
One of the biggest local issues has been a doctor shortage after a major medical clinic cut thousands of patients loose last spring because their physicians retired or moved on.
Stiles made a point of launching the NDP's health-care platform here and Crombie has also been in the border city to campaign for the Liberals. Ford had to cancel a planned stop Tuesday after a crash at Pearson International Airport resulted in flight delays and cancellations, but he is planning to visit.
The New Democrats are running city Coun. Lisa Vezeau Allen, while the Liberal candidate is small business owner Gurwinder Dusanjh.
Romano is the campaign manager for Conservative hopeful Chris Scott, a former senior political staffer at the legislature who has previously run Romano's campaigns.
The Green candidate is Jaycob Jacques, a student and the party's youth co-chair.
CAPTION: Liberal candidate Vince Gasparro and Progressive Conservative candidate Michelle Cooper are running in the riding of Eglinton-Lawrence.
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson

Subject: Nominations; Conservatism; Provincial elections; Political appointments; Presidents; Leaves of absence; Legislatures; Voters; Homeless people; Elections; Political parties

Document 49 of 500

Ontario election: Don't be fooled by proponents of for-profit health care. It always leads to higher costs and decreased care

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-election-dont-be-fooled-proponents-profit/docview/3169997947/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+election%3A+Don%27t+be+fooled+by+proponents+of+for-profit+health+care.+It+always+leads+to+higher+costs+and+decreased+care&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: After a slow start, the major players in the Ontario election have finally released their game plans for improving primary care. As a family doctor, who is regularly asked if I'm taking on new patients (and grudgingly must decline), I was relieved to see that every party has committed to at least some of what is needed.
Partial solutions, however, are not enough. Especially when some of the largest corporations in Canada are starting to turn the primary care crisis into profit.
First, the good news.
We've known for a long time that expanding team-based primary care makes sense, not only to improve access for patients but to ensure higher quality of care, and to provide better conditions for the health care professionals working in them. Each of the major parties in Ontario have committed to making this the cornerstone of expansion, including a $1.8 billion commitment by the incumbent Conservatives, $4.05 billion by the NDP and $3.1 billion promised by the Liberals. The Association of Family Health Teams of Ontario recommends just over $4 billion of investments over five year, over-and-above any possible changes to family doctor remuneration.
Each party has also promised several other measures, including reducing the nearly 20 hours of paperwork a week family doctors are saddled with and incentives to bring doctors who have left primary care, back into the fold.
The scope of the problem and delayed action are reflected by the substantial but necessary commitments required to reimagine care. Many of the building blocks already in-place, including existing models of team-based Family Health Teams and Community Health Centers. Expanding them and ensuring they work in harmony is no small task.
This is also very achievable. In fact, other countries with high-performing primary care have shown us the way. They've made sure care is technologically integrated, socially accountable, is place-based much like schools and school boards, and available after-hours. In fact, they've been so successful that walk-in clinics are almost non-existent - imagine that! We need to follow.
The urgency for this type of change is only heighted by external threats. Plans to expand "patient first" care are coming up against the increasing privatization of primary care from unexpected sources: grocers and telecom companies like Loblaw and Telus and lesser known private-equity investment firms.
Defenders of corporatizing primary care often say that if care is publicly funded, it doesn't really matter whether or not the profit-motive is at play.
Evidence and experience tell us otherwise.
For-profit care, even when publicly funded, leads to higher costs and worse patient outcomes. For-profit clinics are incentivized to prioritize people who are wealthier and healthy, less expensive to treat, and provide larger profit-margins. In for-profit settings, patients are more likely to be asked to pay out-of-pocket for ancillary or "uninsured" services.
Sometimes, that publicly funded care is inappropriately made contingent on paying these out-of-pocket fees. In fact, in Ontario, we've very recently seen many of these trends play-out in both for-profit cataract surgery, as well as long-term care. In its most extreme cases, publicly funded for-profit care can even be enabled by political corruption, as is being investigated in Alberta and has been previously alleged in Ontario.
These differences matter, not only because of the scale and scope of the primary care crisis, but because choices being made today can set the course for the next few decades. Aspiring premiers must lead with funding commitments conditioned on not-for-profit care, with the necessary support to spread-and-scale team-based care as quickly as possible.
To ensure the sustainable system of a reimagined system of care, leaders must also lead with Ontario's regulatory power to prevent for-profit primary care from becoming the new normal.
Like any winning strategy, our next government's game plan needs to combine a potent offence, with dependable defence.
CAPTION: "For-profit care, even when publicly funded, leads tohigher costsandworse patient outcomes," writes Dr. Danyaal Raza.
CREDIT: Danyaal Raza Contributor

Subject: Patients; Primary care; Teams; Clinical outcomes

Ontario election: What you need to know about voting on election day

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-election-what-you-need-know-about-voting/docview/3169997616/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+election%3A+What+you+need+to+know+about+voting+on+election+day&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: On Thursday, Ontarians will head to the polls to decide who will lead the country's most populated province.
Though voters have been able to cast their vote early, day-of voting remains a vital and rich tradition for those seeking to make their voices heard at the ballot box.
Here's everything you need to know about voting in-person on election day in Ontario:
When do the polls open and close?
Polls will open at 9 a.m. and close at 9 p.m. on Feb. 27, per Elections Ontario. Between those hours, you can vote in-person at one of the province's more than 7,000 voting locations, which are commonly housed at schools, places of worship and community centres, among others.
Where can I vote?
Elections Ontario has an online tool that allows voters to find their electoral district and election day voting location by postal code.
If, for example, you live at the Scotiabank Arena, you'll be voting at St. Andrew's Centre and electing a representative for Spadina-Fort York.
Those without a permanent address can use the "place where you have returned to most often to eat or sleep in the past five weeks" as an address to vote.
Do I need ID to vote?
Yes. Elections Ontario requires you to show identification at your polling place, but it doesn't necessarily need to include a photo of you and it can be shown on a mobile device.
If you've been sent a voter information card in the mail, your ID can be anything from a birth certificate to a health card to a student card from one of the province's universities, among many others.
If you haven't been issued a voter information card, your piece of ID must have both your full name and your home address. This can include an Ontario driver's license, insurance statement, a child tax benefit statement or a school transcript, per Elections Ontario.
Will I have to wait in line to vote?
Elections Ontario did not say whether they expect to see lines on election night.
But Ontario hasn't had a winter election since the 1920s, so it's possible that some of the inclement weather that has walloped the province this month could contribute to and difficulty parking or getting to the polls.
(In the 1926 election, which took place on Dec. 1, the Star reported that some voters "felt it too cold to brave the near-zero temperatures" to vote; voter turnout that year was still over 64 per cent, however, compared to the 44 per cent and 56 per cent figures in 2022 and 2018, respectively.)
When will we know who won the election?
In the last Ontario election, most networks called the election by 9:30 p.m. on election night. When Ontarians went to the polls in 2014 and 2018, networks were likewise able to call the election results before most of the province went to bed.
While it's yet unclear whether this election will be as quick to project, recent polls gave Ford a commanding lead over Bonnie Crombie's Liberals and Marit Stiles' New Democrats.
CAPTION: Voters in Ontario can vote in-person on Feb. 27 from 9 a.m. to 9 p.m. at one of more than 7,000 voting locations across the province.
CREDIT: Hayden Godfrey

Subject: Voters; Voter behavior; Elections

Document 51 of 500

It looks like we're going to have a Poilievre-Carney race. Here's what it will come down to

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/looks-like-were-going-have-poilievre-carney-race/docview/3169997479/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=It+looks+like+we%27re+going+to+have+a+Poilievre-Carney+race.+Here%27s+what+it+will+come+down+to&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A few assumptions to start.
Mark Carney will handily win the federal Liberal party's nomination, become Prime Minister, and march - more or less straightaway - into Rideau Hall to ask the Governor General to call an election.
During the contest that follows, U.S. President Donald Trump will not suddenly decide to take his foot off the gas. He will continue to bully, intimidate, and harass Canada. He will impose more tariffs, dangle new threats, and heighten economic pressure. He will continue his campaign of extranational humiliation.
In other words, the current crisis will not just persist - it will escalate.
So, make no mistake: this crisis is not the backdrop, it will define the stakes, the strategy, and the ultimate outcome of the election. And with it, three fundamental realities will emerge - each shaping the battle ahead.
First, is the exceedingly obvious point that this election will not be about Justin Trudeau's record, nor will it be a referendum on the carbon tax. The Liberal brand, battered and bruised as it may be, may just well escape a judgment of its record as the election becomes a genuine fight over the future of the country.
Second, is a more counterintuitive point: this contest won't be decided on policy.
At his "Canada First" rally last weekend, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre quipped that Mark Carney would simply "plagiarize" all his policies before the election. It is a clever way to salt the earth for Carney's policy agenda in the coming campaign, to be sure, but it was also a tacit admission of something deeper.
The economic challenges facing Canada are so glaring - so long overdue - that a broad policy consensus has already taken shape. One drawn closer by the fact that Carney, Harper's appointee as governor of the Bank of Canada, carries a distinctly fiscally conservative pedigree.
Yes, Poilievre and Carney will try to outflank each other on the margins. But on the most urgent issues - meeting our NATO commitment, diversifying our trading relationships, eliminating interprovincial trade barriers, tackling productivity stagnation - their objectives will be strikingly similar.
Their execution will of course be wildly different. But the point is this election won't be won or lost on the ideological extremes. This fight isn't about the destination - it's a fight over who gets to drive the country there.
Third, and most importantly, is the fact the ballot question has already been set in stone.
As I argued in Ontario's election, this federal race won't hinge on who is best suited to "negotiate" with Trump. Canadians see through that. No one is "best suited" to deal with an irrational, transactional, and unpredictable actor.
Instead, the ballot question will be: Who is best able to protect Canadians from the harm - economic, psychological, and geopolitical - that Trump's presidency has already inflicted and will continue to bring?
This is no ordinary ballot question. And this will be no ordinary race.
With no incumbency disadvantage, or advantage, and no radical ideological divide in the eyes of most voters, this race will come down to political intangibles.
Who has the leadership qualities to reassure Canadians?
Who exudes confidence, strength, and control?
Who makes us believe that together we can get through this as Canadians?
Who convinces voters they can handle Trump's economic aggression without making things worse?
All the while making it clear that they are on the side of hard working, everyday Canadians who are finding life more and more difficult.
The stakes of this race are unlike anything in recent history. The political ground is shifting beneath our feet. There is no playbook for what's ahead.
But as Canadians narrow their focus on self-preservation in the wake of Donald Trump's return, so too must Carney and Poilievre narrow their strategies around that singular fear. In the end, this election will be a referendum on one thing: who can make Canadians feel safest in a world growing more volatile by the day.
And it will be won by the leader who best embodies that safety and galvanizes Canadians to hold the line against the coming chaos.
CAPTION: Pierre Poilievre, left, and Mark Carney.
CREDIT: Jaime Watt

Subject: Political campaigns; Leadership; Canadians; Referendums; Voters

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

Location: Canada

People: Carney, Mark; Trump, Donald J; Poilievre, Pierre

Title: It looks like we're going to have a Poilievre-Carney race. Here's what it will come down to

Document 52 of 500

'We are going in the wrong direction': Why last year saw Ontario burn a record amount of natural gas

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/we-are-going-wrong-direction-why-last-year-saw/docview/3169997476/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27We+are+going+in+the+wrong+direction%27%3A+Why+last+year+saw+Ontario+burn+a+record+amount+of+natural+gas&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario has never burned so much natural gas to make electricity, firing up the polluting plants more often than at any time since the first ones were built in the 1990s.
According to year-end data from the Independent Electricity Systems Operator, the gas plants produced 24.6 terawatt hours of electricity in 2024 - a nearly 30 per cent jump from the previous year.
This ramp up of natural gas plants means there's been a significant increase in local air pollution as well as far more planet-warming carbon emissions - quadruple the amount produced when Doug Ford was first elected premier.
As a result, Ontario's highly touted clean energy advantage is eroding. The electricity grid, which was 96 per cent carbon-free in 2017, is now only 84 per cent green.
"We are going in the wrong direction," said Jack Gibbons, chair of the Ontario Clean Air Alliance. "Doug Ford's plan to ramp up the pollution from our dirty gas plants is bad for our health, bad for our climate and bad for our pocketbooks."
Emissions are projected to continue to rise as Ontario's reliance on gas plants increases until they make up 25 per cent of all energy produced in 2030, according to the IESO.
In response to questions, Ministry of Energy and Electrification spokesperson Natasha Demetriades said: "Ontario continues to have one of the cleanest electricity systems in the world," and it projects the grid will be "near-zero emissions" by 2050.
Ontario has more than two dozen gas plants, most of which were constructed in the early 2000s and 2010s to replace coal. Their use previously peaked at 22.2 terawatt hours in 2012, two years before the last coal plant was shut. The construction of renewables - wind turbines and solar farms - allowed the province to use less gas in subsequent years.
But Ford halted all construction of renewable energy when he was elected in 2018, even paying $231 million to cancel 758 green energy projects, some of which were partially built.
And with nuclear reactors at Bruce and Darlington now offline for refurbishment, the province has had to lean on gas more than ever before, forcing gas plants that were only supposed to run a few hours a day to run nearly 24/7.
This has serious health implications for those who live near the plants, which includes millions of people in the GTA, where three major plants are located. The plants emit toxic ozone, nitrogen dioxides and particulate matter that have been linked to the development of childhood asthma.
They are also major sources of carbon emissions, which exacerbate the climate crisis and increase the likelihood of extreme weather disasters like flooding and forest fires.
The Port Lands gas plant has the dubious title of being the largest source of carbon emissions in the city.
"It's not just a climate issue," said Keith Brooks, programs director at Environmental Defence. "Almost all of Ontario's gas is imported. Most of it is fracked gas from Pennsylvania and Ohio. Do we want to be spending all that money importing gas from the United States right now, when we're entering into this trade war and talking about tariffs?"
Electricity demand in Ontario has been flat for more than 20 years. But new technologies like electric vehicles and heat pumps mean the province will need 75 per cent more power by 2050, according to IESO projections.
This anticipated surge prompted the Progressive Conservative government to announce the first construction of new nuclear plants in 30 years, with small "modular" reactors planned for Darlington, an expansion of Bruce and a new full-scale nuclear plant in Wesleyville.
While nuclear plants have the ability to produce large amounts of emissions-free energy, lengthy construction times - often taking longer than a decade - and consistent cost overruns, have prompted critics to question a return to the technology when renewables are so much cheaper and quicker to build.
At the same time, the province put out a call for new gas plants and battery storage as a stopgap measure to meet peak demand on hot summer days when people crank up their air conditioners. Local opposition stymied several projects and the province was only able to get half as much gas generation as it wanted.
Meanwhile, renewable power has been on a tear, with wind and solar power projected to nearly triple worldwide by 2030. With solar prices having dropped by more than half in a single year, renewables are now cheaper than any other form of energy.
In Europe, the Russian invasion of Ukraine has accelerated the transition away from natural gas, which had spiked in price, and led to record-breaking construction of wind and solar.
A recent report by Pembina Institute, a clean energy think tank, made the case that green energy is needed to ensure long term prosperity and independence for Ontario.
"Grids around the world have managed to achieve up to 70 per cent of energy supplied from wind and solar and have done so reliably and affordably," said Scott MacDougall, one of the report's authors, citing Texas, California, Denmark and Germany as grids that are leading the world in renewable deployment.
Ontario, which once led the nation in renewable construction, has been stalled at about 12-14 per cent wind and solar since 2018.
"Ontario has a long way to go," said MacDougall. "I don't think it wants to lose its clean energy edge."
In 2023, Ford's Conservative government reversed its opposition to renewables and announced that the province would start rolling procurement of green power. A year later, however, then-energy minister Steven Lecce quietly altered the bidding criteria, opening the door for new gas plants to be built.
Reliance on gas isn't just a climate and cost concern, it's also worrying from an energy security perspective, said Rachel Doran, vice-president of policy and strategy at Clean Energy Canada.
"We're in a moment in time where there's a heightened awareness about how much we've been dependent on trade via the U.S. Natural gas gets shipped across borders and you need more of it every time you fire up a natural gas power plant," she said.
"Whereas once you've installed wind, solar and batteries, these are operating, producing energy domestically and increasing our energy security."
CAPTION: The rise in natural gas usage has serious health implications for those who live near the plants, which includes millions of people in the GTA, where three major plants are located, including the Port Lands.
CREDIT: Marco Chown Oved

Full text: Yes, Canada should be advertising to Americans right now. But here's who Doug Ford's U.S. ads really serve, Feb. 20
I have long complained about Ontario Conservative leader Doug Ford using our tax dollars to promote his party. His "It's all here" TV adverts are true. Ford's obvious corruption is all here. His assumption that we're idiots who can't see through his attempts to sway voters even before the election was called.

First Doug Ford said he was glad that Trump won the election. Then he said he called the upcoming election because he needed a strong mandate to fight Trump's tariffs. Which one is it? You can't have it both ways.
Gary Slippoy, Toronto
Vote to save Canada from oligarchy
This last week of this election campaign should be focused on Ontario Conservative leader Doug Ford showing signs of being a Trump lap-dog. Ford admitted that he was happy to see Donald Trump win the 2024 election. Was Ford's trip to D.C. really just to kiss the ring? Was he there to solicit support from the Musk/Trump regime?
Federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre (who had otherwise been remaining silent) admitted immediately following the Musk/Trump cancellation of USAID that he is in favour of Canada greatly reducing foreign aid.
The most important issue we are facing is the very real risk that Ford and Poilievre could become Canadian versions of the right-wing authoritarian regimes now prevailing in the U.S. and in other countries around the world. To save Ontario and Canada we must recognize these risks. We must use strategic voting to unite the centre/left. Brian Monrad, Toronto
Few consumer goods are made in Canada
As a result of U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff war, there has been a renewed call to buy goods made in Canada. While a noble and worthy cause, it is most unfortunate that most of our everyday consumer goods are no longer made in Canada. Take clothing for example. Up until the early '80s virtually all of our clothing from socks and underwear to outerwear was manufactured in Canada. If you check your current clothing labels you will be hard pressed to find anything manufactured in Canada. Canadians love hockey, but most hockey stick manufacturers now produce these goods offshore.
Corporate entities in the pursuit of cheap labour and increasing profits moved a huge amount of our manufactured products offshore. None of these increased profits were passed on to Canadian workers which is why the buying power of the average worker is still stuck in the 1970s. Jeff Rubin in his book, "The Expendables: How the Middle Class Got Screwed by Globalization" says it best: "What's good for shareholders isn't good for workers."
Speaking of tariffs, how about putting significant tariffs on all offshore products that used to be made in Canada? And to help even more how about significant tax breaks for Canadian manufacturers? In the names of both cheapness and corporate greed we have sold our manufacturing soul.
William Nicholson, Toronto
Canadian businesses should take pre-emptive action
There is a solution to the Trump tariffs: Have the producers of steel, cars, aluminum, pharmaceuticals, and other products stop producing for three weeks. Once products stop flowing into the U.S., customers will force a resolution. The Unions and employers will need to agree to stop production.
Mark Borkowski, Toronto
Don't buy any F-35 fighter jets

If we wanted to emphasize the message, we could invite China and Russia to submit bids for our next fighter fleet. But a serious source for our next fighters would be Sweden. Sweden's jets are capable, flexible and they're designed for a northern climate.
Paul Kern, Toronto

The more one hears about the dangerous, destabilizing rhetoric of U.S. President Donald Trump and his ilk, the more one appreciates the genius of those who have written cautionary tales such as Sir William Golding's "Lord of the Flies." The chaos and erosion of humanity that occurred in the book is unfolding before our eyes.

Based on the blatant, numerous lies that U.S. President Donald Trump is saying about Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy and his positive comments about Russia and its President Vladimir Putin, one has to wonder what damaging personal information Putin has on Trump. Unbelievable! Tell people Canada is the 51st state enough times and they will believe it is true.

The "F-word," as the Star and many others are fond of referring to it, was drained of its power decades ago by the singer Joe McDonald, of Country Joe and the Fish fame, when he sang, "Gimme an F; gimme a U; gimme a C; gimme a K"... etc. It was a hit in the '60s and took the steam out of the word then. The word which has become commonplace on TV and in daily usage today. That you and other self-styled censors continue to give it strength is a mystery to me.
