Document 54 of 500

Race to PM's office will come down to confidence

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/race-pms-office-will-come-down-confidence/docview/3169997395/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Race+to+PM%27s+office+will+come+down+to+confidence&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A few assumptions to start.
Mark Carney will handily win the federal Liberal party's nomination, become prime minister, and march - more or less straightaway - into Rideau Hall to ask the Governor General to call an election.
During the contest that follows, U.S. President Donald Trump will not suddenly decide to take his foot off the gas. He will continue to bully, intimidate, and harass Canada. He will impose more tariffs, dangle new threats, and heighten economic pressure. He will continue his campaign of extranational humiliation.
In other words, the current crisis will not just persist - it will escalate.
So, make no mistake: this crisis is not the backdrop, it will define the stakes, the strategy, and the ultimate outcome of the election. And with it, three fundamental realities will emerge - each shaping the battle ahead.
First, is the exceedingly obvious point that this election will not be about Justin Trudeau's record, nor will it be a referendum on the carbon tax. The Liberal brand, battered and bruised as it may be, may just well escape a judgment of its record as the election becomes a genuine fight over the future of the country.
Second, is a more counterintuitive point: this contest won't be decided on policy.
At his "Canada First" rally last weekend, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre quipped that Mark Carney would simply "plagiarize" all his policies before the election. It is a clever way to salt the earth for Carney's policy agenda in the coming campaign, to be sure, but it was also a tacit admission of something deeper.
The economic challenges facing Canada are so glaring - so long overdue - that a broad policy consensus has already taken shape. One drawn closer by the fact that Carney, Harper's appointee as governor of the Bank of Canada, carries a distinctly fiscally conservative pedigree.
Yes, Poilievre and Carney will try to outflank each other on the margins. But on the most urgent issues - meeting our NATO commitment, diversifying our trading relationships, eliminating interprovincial trade barriers, tackling productivity stagnation - their objectives will be strikingly similar.
Their execution will of course be wildly different. But the point is this election won't be won or lost on the ideological extremes. This fight isn't about the destination - it's a fight over who gets to drive the country there.
Third, and most importantly, is the fact the ballot question has already been set in stone.
As I argued in Ontario's election, this federal race won't hinge on who is best suited to "negotiate" with Trump. Canadians see through that. No one is "best suited" to deal with an irrational, transactional, and unpredictable actor.
Instead, the ballot question will be: Who is best able to protect Canadians from the harm - economic, psychological, and geopolitical - that Trump's presidency has already inflicted and will continue to bring?
This is no ordinary ballot question. And this will be no ordinary race.
With no incumbency disadvantage, or advantage, and no radical ideological divide in the eyes of most voters, this race will come down to political intangibles.
Who has the leadership qualities to reassure Canadians?
Who exudes confidence, strength, and control?
Who makes us believe that together we can get through this as Canadians?
Who convinces voters they can handle Trump's economic aggression without making things worse?
All the while making it clear that they are on the side of hard working, everyday Canadians who are finding life more and more difficult.
The stakes of this race are unlike anything in recent history. The political ground is shifting beneath our feet. There is no playbook for what's ahead.
But as Canadians narrow their focus on self-preservation in the wake of Donald Trump's return, so too must Carney and Poilievre narrow their strategies around that singular fear. In the end, this election will be a referendum on one thing: who can make Canadians feel safest in a world growing more volatile by the day.
And it will be won by the leader who best embodies that safety and galvanizes Canadians to hold the line against the coming chaos.
Jaime Watt is the executive chairman of Navigator Ltd. and a Conservative
strategist. He is a freelance columnist for the Star.
CREDIT: Jaime Watt OPINION Jaime Watt is the executive chairman of Navigator Ltd. and a Conservative strategist. He is a freelance columnist for the Star.;

Document 55 of 500

Ford's popularity persists despite blunders

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-popularity-persists-despite-blunders/docview/3169989950/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+popularity+persists+despite+blunders&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Police bodyguards move into position, eyeing the sea of hard hats packed inside the carpenters' hall.
Hundreds of burly men and a few fearless women, all looking for work, have parked their pickup trucks outside.
It is just after 8 a.m. and the campaign team is worried.
Can Doug Ford get through?
No, this is not an election protest pitting the Progressive Conservative leader against his adversaries in the union movement. Far from a campaign confrontation, it is a coronation at a labour-run training facility.
Welcome to the unlikely marriage of convenience between right-wing Tories and unionized workers who once looked left. Ahead of Thursday's election, an unfathomable love affair between Ford's Conservatives and the construction trades has turned Ontario politics upside down.
Why does Ford's popularity persist, despite the contradictions? Why is the PC leader winning so many union endorsements despite freezing the minimum wage after becoming premier, rolling back labour laws and overriding Charter protections for strikers?
The good news for the PC campaign is that Ford's failures don't seem to count against him in this union hall or across the province. The Tories are polling sky high, presaging an election landslide for a politician who bears the baggage of nearly seven years in power - notably a beer boondoggle and a Greenbelt imbroglio that sparked an ongoing police probe.
What happened? The campaign trail offers clues.
When Ford won the 2018 election, he reflexively badmouthed "union bosses" as corrupt thugs.
Today, an influential unionist is about to endorse his bid to be re-elected as premier - if only Ford can get through the overflowing parking lot in this training facility off Highway 400.
The leader arrives in his converted campaign RV - no media allowed aboard, so no need for a chartered media bus.
Unfazed by the -12 C morning chill, Ford emerges in a dark windbreaker and enters the sprawling hall through a side door.
It is showtime. Cue the PC campaign theme song with its throbbing beat: "Who's gonna fight for you?" goes the chorus. And then, in cheerful harmony, "Together, we can rise above it all."
The cornball lyrics announce Ford's arrival everywhere he goes, a campaign incantation that warms up the crowd by lionizing the leader. A beaming Ford claps back at the adoring audience, then turns his back on them to face the cameras.
Now the hard hats form a human backdrop - props for a photo-op. Almost all of the 250 men and women are hoisting the same placard, handed out by the campaign, proclaiming: "Protect Workers" (with "Ontario PC" on the obverse).
As the cameras roll, Ford is flanked by Jason Rowe, executive secretary of the Carpenters' Regional Council. He has come to repay a political debt to a PC government that has doled out big money for his members.
"Good morning, brothers and sisters," Rowe begins with the traditional union greeting now familiar to Ford.
Ontario's carpenters "would not be where we are without you," Rowe gushes, in a nod to generous government funding. "For the first time in our history, the carpenters' union is endorsing Doug Ford and the PC Party."
There was a time, decades ago, that the Tory-blue PC dynasty was known as "The Big Blue Machine." Now, it has been reincarnated as the Big Blue-Collar Machine.
Ford has made inroads that could not have been imagined when he first won power in 2018, a populist blowhard who blew hot and cold with the lunch-bucket crowd. Today, he boasts about eating the lunch of the rival New Democratic Party which, lest we forget, was founded in 1961 as the voice of the labour movement.
Today's union endorsement is one of more than a dozen for Ford. All that said, not all unions have abandoned the NDP for the PC fold - mostly just the private sector building trades and the occasional Unifor local who depend heavily on government infrastructure investments and training funding.
For these union leaders, every quid pro quo counts. And in politics, perception counts.
"It's a true honour to have an endorsement, Jason," Ford intones with practised humility.
He recounts how his government invested billions in skills and apprenticeship training that benefits the building trades. He doesn't mention starving colleges of funding for the sophisticated skills training that is critical to economic growth since becoming premier.
And then Ford pivots, per the Teleprompter, to the pretext that prompted him to trigger an absurdly early election in the middle of winter: Talk of tariffs from U.S. President Donald Trump.
"With Trump's tariffs threatening millions of workers across Ontario, we'll do whatever it takes to protect Ontario workers," he says gravely.
Behind him, a towering PC banner has been erected that proclaims, "CANADA IS NOT FOR SALE."
His pitch delivered, Ford closes with a prayerful invocation: "May God bless the people of Ontario."
Ford's stump speech is short and bittersweet, but he is all smiles wading through the waiting crowd, clasping outstretched hands and posing for a long line of selfies. Campaign organizers struggle to clear a path to his RV for the next stop in Burlington and then Milton, as the sappy harmony of the campaign theme song fills the hall once again with its hypnotic loop: "Who's gonna fight for you?"
The chorus is a far cry from the call to arms of the 2022 PC campaign theme song, which promised, repeatedly: "Get it done, get it done ... we won't stop, we won't ever fold."
While that song is long forgotten, Ford's "Get it done" slogan still offers fodder for his critics, who point out that most of his promises remain undone. Hallway medicine endures, a doctor shortage persists and housing shortfalls are worse.
Never mind the failed promise of 2022, the faux premise of campaign 2025 - that only Ford can fight Trump's tariffs - is melting like the winter snows. At every step, the PC leader has been trying to have it both ways and the contradictions are catching up.
He publicly condemned the president's tariff plan, but was caught on an open mic saying he "100 per cent" wanted Trump to win the election, notwithstanding the Republican tariff platform.
He boasted about sitting down with Trump to deliver a tough message, man to man, but never got closer than a couple of White House aides who retorted that Canada's premiers should take the president at his word about becoming the 51st state.
Yet the polling suggests voters aren't fussed by Ford's fumbles and failures, because they aren't fond of the alternatives.
He caught a break in the 2022 election when Ontarians gave him high marks for his overall handling of the pandemic, and handed out low marks to the other party leaders.
Back then, Ford gave me a brief interview on the campaign trail saying that he'd "evolved" since winning power in 2018 and governing through the hardship of COVID: "You evolve into your role," he told me at the time.
Some of Ford's critics were incredulous, but also oblivious. In truth, almost all politicians evolve and also revert, as Ford surely does, but he proved unique in his ability to apologize, shrug his shoulders and move on - and up.
All these years later, he has moved even higher up in the polls, despite messing up on the job. He is also facing new opponents - NDP Leader Marit Stiles and Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie - who are better campaigners than their predecessors, but are often too busy fighting it out with each other to duke it out with the Tories.
It's not just that Ford is profiting from the split among progressive voters that benefits his Progressive Conservatives. Every public opinion poll shows affordability and the economy preoccupy Ontarians, thanks to Trump's tariff threat dominating the news.
Who do voters believe is best able to deliver on the economic concerns? For whatever reason, it's the Tories every time - the Liberals less so and New Democrats even less.
Which is why Ford's Tories triggered this early election on a phoney premise of pushing back against Trump.
Never mind that any response to a bilateral trade war would be almost entirely federal.
Unlike his rivals, Ford has not granted an interview to me or other journalists on the provincial campaign trail so far and he has been ducking reporters for days. But later, on the day that I watch Ford in action, on the eve of his Friday return trip to Washington, he holds a news conference lest he be accused of playing peek-a-boo politics.
The hostile questions from reporters keep coming - about his missteps, broken promises and triggering an unnecessary election 15 months ahead of schedule.
But Ford dodges every bullet by pivoting back to the economy, preying on the voter anxieties that work in his favour: "Nothing moves without a strong economy - nothing moves in our health care and our education (sector) ... without a strong economy," he repeats, burnishing the PC brand.
And then, his takeaway line, almost an echo of the lyrics in his campaign theme song:
"We're going to focus on the economy and fight Donald Trump's tariffs every step of the way. That's what this election is about."
No matter that the election day result won't affect Trump's tariffs. Ford is winning the day, for instead of a campaign about his own performance, he has somehow turned this into a vote against Donald Trump.
On the campaign trail, as in the union parking lot, Doug Ford is getting through.
CREDIT: Martin Regg Cohn OPINION

Subject: Political campaigns; Polls & surveys; Voters; Politics; Union leadership; Public opinion surveys; Tariffs; Endorsements

Document 56 of 500

For-profit health care is not worth the cost

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/profit-health-care-is-not-worth-cost/docview/3169989931/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=For-profit+health+care+is+not+worth+the+cost&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: After a slow start, the major players in the Ontario election have finally released their game plans for improving primary care. As a family doctor, who is regularly asked if I'm taking on new patients (and grudgingly must decline), I was relieved to see that every party has committed to at least some of what is needed.
Partial solutions, however, are not enough. Especially when some of the largest corporations in Canada are starting to turn the primary care crisis into profit.
First, the good news.
We've known for a long time that expanding team-based primary care makes sense, not only to improve access for patients but to ensure higher quality of care, and to provide better conditions for the health care professionals working in them.
Each of the major parties in Ontario have committed to making this the cornerstone of expansion, including a $1.8 billion commitment by the incumbent Conservatives, $4.05 billion by the NDP and $3.1 billion promised by the Liberals. The Association of Family Health Teams of Ontario recommends just over $4 billion of investments over five years, over-and-above any possible changes to family doctor remuneration.
Each party has also promised several other measures, including reducing the nearly 20 hours of paperwork a week family doctors are saddled with and incentives to bring doctors who have left primary care, back into the fold.
The scope of the problem and delayed action are reflected by the substantial but necessary commitments required to reimagine care. Many of the building blocks already in-place, including existing models of team-based Family Health Teams and Community Health Centers. Expanding them and ensuring they work in harmony is no small task.
This is also very achievable. In fact, other countries with high-performing primary care have shown us the way. They've made sure care is technologically integrated, socially accountable, is place-based much like schools and school boards, and available after-hours. In fact, they've been so successful that walk-in clinics are almost non-existent - imagine that! We need to follow.
The urgency for this type of change is only heighted by external threats. Plans to expand "patient first" care are coming up against the increasing privatization of primary care from unexpected sources: grocers and telecom companies like Loblaw and Telus and lesser known private-equity investment firms.
Defenders of corporatizing primary care often say that if care is publicly funded, it doesn't really matter whether or not the profit-motive is at play.
Evidence and experience tell us otherwise.
For-profit care, even when publicly funded, leads to higher costs and worse patient outcomes. For-profit clinics are incentivized to prioritize people who are wealthier and healthy, less expensive to treat, and provide larger profit-margins. In for-profit settings, patients are more likely to be asked to pay out-of-pocket for ancillary or "uninsured" services.
Sometimes, that publicly funded care is inappropriately made contingent on paying these out-of-pocket fees.
In fact, in Ontario, we've very recently seen many of these trends play-out in both for-profit cataract surgery, as well as long-term care. In its most extreme cases, publicly funded for-profit care can even be enabled by political corruption, as is being investigated in Alberta and has been previously alleged in Ontario.
These differences matter, not only because of the scale and scope of the primary care crisis, but because choices being made today can set the course for the next few decades.
Aspiring premiers must lead with funding commitments conditioned on not-for-profit care, with the necessary support to spread-and-scale team-based care as quickly as possible.
To ensure the sustainable system of a reimagined system of care, leaders must also lead with Ontario's regulatory power to prevent for-profit primary care from becoming the new normal.
Like any winning strategy, our next government's game plan needs to combine a potent offence, with dependable defence.
Dr. Danyaal Raza is a family physician at St. Michael's Hospital, Unity Health Toronto and past chair of Canadian Doctors for Medicare.
CREDIT: Danyaal Raza Contributor Dr. Danyaal Raza is a family physician at St. Michael's Hospital, Unity Health Toronto and past chair of Canadian Doctors for Medicare.;

Canadians love hockey, but most hockey stick manufacturers now produce them offshore.
Corporate entities in the pursuit of cheap labour and increasing profits moved a huge amount of our manufactured products offshore. None of these increased profits were passed on to Canadian workers which is why the buying power of the average worker is still stuck in the 1970s. Jeff Rubin in his book, "The Expendables: How the Middle Class Got Screwed by Globalization" says it best: "What's good for shareholders isn't good for workers."

Document 58 of 500

'We are going in the wrong direction': Why last year saw Ontario burn a record amount of natural gas

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/we-are-going-wrong-direction/docview/3169989190/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27We+are+going+in+the+wrong+direction%27%3A+Why+last+year+saw+Ontario+burn+a+record+amount+of+natural+gas&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario has never burned so much natural gas to make electricity, firing up the polluting plants more often than at any time since the first ones were built in the 1990s.
According to year-end data from the Independent Electricity Systems Operator, the gas plants produced 24.6 terawatt hours of electricity in 2024 - a nearly 30 per cent jump from the previous year.
This ramp up of natural gas plants means there's been a significant increase in local air pollution as well as far more planet-warming carbon emissions - quadruple the amount produced when Doug Ford was first elected premier.
As a result, Ontario's highly touted clean energy advantage is eroding. The electricity grid, which was 96 per cent carbon-free in 2017, is now only 84 per cent green.
"We are going in the wrong direction," said Jack Gibbons, chair of the Ontario Clean Air Alliance. "Doug Ford's plan to ramp up the pollution from our dirty gas plants is bad for our health, bad for our climate and bad for our pocketbooks."
Emissions are projected to continue to rise as Ontario's reliance on gas plants increases until they make up 25 per cent of all energy produced in 2030, according to the IESO.
In response to questions, Ministry of Energy and Electrification spokesperson Natasha Demetriades said: "Ontario continues to have one of the cleanest electricity systems in the world," and it projects the grid will be "near-zero emissions" by 2050.
Ontario has more than two dozen gas plants, most of which were constructed in the early 2000s and 2010s to replace coal. Their use previously peaked at 22.2 terawatt hours in 2012, two years before the last coal plant was shut. The construction of renewables - wind turbines and solar farms - allowed the province to use less gas in subsequent years.
But Ford halted all construction of renewable energy when he was elected in 2018, even paying $231 million to cancel 758 green energy projects, some of which were partially built.
And with nuclear reactors at Bruce and Darlington now offline for refurbishment, the province has had to lean on gas more than ever before, forcing gas plants that were only supposed to run a few hours a day to run nearly 24/7.
This has serious health implications for those who live near the plants, which includes millions of people in the GTA, where three major plants are located. The plants emit toxic ozone, nitrogen dioxides and particulate matter that have been linked to the development of childhood asthma.
They are also major sources of carbon emissions, which exacerbate the climate crisis and increase the likelihood of extreme weather disasters like flooding and forest fires.
The Port Lands gas plant has the dubious title of being the largest source of carbon emissions in the city.
"It's not just a climate issue," said Keith Brooks, programs director at Environmental Defence. "Almost all of Ontario's gas is imported. Most of it is fracked gas from Pennsylvania and Ohio. Do we want to be spending all that money importing gas from the United States right now, when we're entering into this trade war and talking about tariffs?"
Electricity demand in Ontario has been flat for more than 20 years. But new technologies like electric vehicles and heat pumps mean the province will need 75 per cent more power by 2050, according to IESO projections.
This anticipated surge prompted the Progressive Conservative government to announce the first construction of new nuclear plants in 30 years, with small "modular" reactors planned for Darlington, an expansion of Bruce and a new full-scale nuclear plant in Wesleyville.
While nuclear plants have the ability to produce large amounts of emissions-free energy, lengthy construction times - often taking longer than a decade - and consistent cost overruns, have prompted critics to question a return to the technology when renewables are so much cheaper and quicker to build.
At the same time, the province put out a call for new gas plants and battery storage as a stopgap measure to meet peak demand on hot summer days when people crank up their air conditioners. Local opposition stymied several projects and the province was only able to get half as much gas generation as it wanted.
Meanwhile, renewable power has been on a tear, with wind and solar power projected to nearly triple worldwide by 2030. With solar prices having dropped by more than half in a single year, renewables are now cheaper than any other form of energy.
In Europe, the Russian invasion of Ukraine has accelerated the transition away from natural gas, which had spiked in price, and led to record-breaking construction of wind and solar.
A recent report by Pembina Institute, a clean energy think tank, made the case that green energy is needed to ensure long term prosperity and independence for Ontario.
"Grids around the world have managed to achieve up to 70 per cent of energy supplied from wind and solar and have done so reliably and affordably," said Scott MacDougall, one of the report's authors, citing Texas, California, Denmark and Germany as grids that are leading the world in renewable deployment.
Ontario, which once led the nation in renewable construction, has been stalled at about 12-14 per cent wind and solar since 2018.
"Ontario has a long way to go," said MacDougall. "I don't think it wants to lose its clean energy edge."
In 2023, Ford's Conservative government reversed its opposition to renewables and announced that the province would start rolling procurement of green power. A year later, however, then-energy minister Steven Lecce quietly altered the bidding criteria, opening the door for new gas plants to be built.
Reliance on gas isn't just a climate and cost concern, it's also worrying from an energy security perspective, said Rachel Doran, vice-president of policy and strategy at Clean Energy Canada.
"We're in a moment in time where there's a heightened awareness about how much we've been dependent on trade via the U.S. Natural gas gets shipped across borders and you need more of it every time you fire up a natural gas power plant," she said.
"Whereas once you've installed wind, solar and batteries, these are operating, producing energy domestically and increasing our energy security."
CAPTION: The rise in natural gas usage has serious health implications for those who live near the plants, which includes millions of people in the GTA, where three major plants are located, including the Port Lands.Richard Lautens Toronto Star file photo
CREDIT: Marco Chown Oved Climate Change Reporter

Subject: Alternative energy sources; Coal-fired power plants; Emissions; Clean technology; Climate change; Industrial plant emissions; Nuclear power plants; Natural gas; Electricity generation

Business indexing term: Subject: Coal-fired power plants Nuclear power plants

Location: United States--US; Ontario Canada; Canada

Title: 'We are going in the wrong direction':   Why last year saw Ontario burn a record amount of natural gas

Document 59 of 500

Clothes make the candidate: Party leaders rely on visual cues to get messages across

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/clothes-make-candidate/docview/3169989182/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Clothes+make+the+candidate%3A+Party+leaders+rely+on+visual+cues+to+get+messages+across&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In the whirlwind campaign leading up to the Ontario election on Feb. 27, the provincial party leaders have had a very short time to make a swift yet lasting impression.
That means visual imagery, often consumed without context on social media, has been an important tool to convey ideas, and ideals.
Clothing is one of the quickest ways to send a message, and has played a starring role. Witness Doug Ford's Canada Is Not for Sale ball cap and toque, followed by Bonnie Crombie's parody response on a hat: Real Leaders Fix Healthcare.
When he's not playing up his "Captain Canada" moniker by wearing a hockey jersey ahead of Thursday's big game, or sporting hard hats and union hoodies at campaign stops, Premier Ford leans into a generic "businessman" uniform of navy suit, white shirt and solid tie.
"The Everyman basic look is a shtick," said Janice Meredith, a Toronto personal style expert and TV personality. "He totally embraces it."
Ford wants to look like a guy regular folks could have a beer with. Not too fancy, not too fashionable. Unlike, say Mark Carney, recently accused of wearing $2,000 Zegna sneakers on the federal Liberal leadership campaign trail, or federal NDP leader Jagmeet Singh, known for his sharp suiting and once lambasted for carrying a Versace tote bag.
Ford's solid ties are an effective strategy in this regard. "Paired with a plain white shirt, a solid tie can look cheap," said Tracy Richardson, an Ontario image consultant who often preps professionals for on-camera appearances. "It draws the eye too much."
Male politicians are fond of matching their tie to their party's colour, as Green party leader Mike Schreiner did with a sage tie at the final leadership debate. "You can point out your party affiliation with a more subtle pattern, say with green in it," said Richardson. "You don't have to ram the message home."
NDP leader Marit Stiles also went for green at the debate, choosing a deep shade for her pantsuit, perhaps because an orange suit would be ... a lot. It also meant she and red-clad Crombie, who look somewhat similar, could be easily differentiated.
"Stiles tends to wear softer, more natural colours with warm undertones," said Meredith. "Say a cream blouse instead of a white one, a softer shell rather than a collared blouse."
Stiles tends to leave her shirts untucked, which Meredith noted is a way to make tailoring more relatable. It's a less corporate look that ties in with her party's platform and ideals. Stiles is the only candidate on TikTok, and overall her social media image is curated to be warm, smiling and approachable, punctuated with splashes of bright orange in the form of a coat or scarf.
Crombie is known for her monochromatic approach, especially in signature red in her role as provincial Liberal leader. The sleek scarlet pantsuit she wore for the Family Day debate was a prime example, complete with a red top and burgundy pumps.
"She tends to a bolder blue red, or a true red, with white-white blouses," said Meredith. "She has had a long career in politics to work out her dressing."
It appears Crombie has streamlined her closet somewhat for the campaign trail. In 2017, Mississauga News was granted a look inside the then-mayor's closet. The article described a "sea of business attire, including her signature look, knee-length Jackie O style sheath dresses and jacket ensembles."
"I don't really wear casual clothes," Crombie said in the interview. She added that she got used to wearing a blazer when she was an MP, as the House of Commons dress code states arms should always be covered. "How I present myself is part of my image and my brand. I would describe my style as classic, elegant, traditional and timeless - my hemline is always at the knee."
For women in politics, dressing decisions are much more complicated. The old saying about women dancing - that we have to do all the same moves but backwards while wearing heels - applies here too.
Men have fewer clothing options, said Meredith, but "women are judged far more critically than men when it comes to their appearance." Too fashionable and you are criticized, too frumpy and you also risk disapproval.
There are also practical considerations to dressing for the camera. For instance, "you can't wear a pencil skirt on a couch for an interview," said Meredith. "You have to be aware of how a mic pack will make your blouse hang, or your dress buckle and gape."
You want to stand out, but not too much. "You can't wear anything that distracts, you can't show cleavage," said Richardson. "That is why the default for women in politics now is the pantsuit."
It's like America Ferrera's monologue in the "Barbie" movie: "You can't be too this, or too that," said Meredith. "Women's lives are full of contradictions, especially in how we are allowed to present ourselves."
We all make instant judgments about people based on how they present themselves. There are a lot of issues at stake in this election - we'll find out on Thursday just how effectively the leaders have communicated their messages.
CAPTION: Ontario Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner, Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, Ontario PC Leader Doug Ford, and Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles before the Ontario Leaders' debate on Family Day.Chris YoungThe Canadian Press
CREDIT: Leanne Delap Special to the Star

Document 60 of 500

What to know about election day

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/what-know-about-election-day/docview/3169989165/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=What+to+know+about+election+day&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: On Thursday, Ontarians will head to the polls to decide who will lead the country's most populated province.
Though voters have been able to cast their vote early, day-of voting remains a vital and rich tradition for those seeking to make their voices heard at the ballot box.
Here's everything you need to know about voting in-person on election day.
When do the polls
open and close?
Polls will open at 9 a.m. and close at 9 p.m. on Feb. 27, per Elections Ontario. Between those hours, you can vote in-person at one of the province's more than 7,000 voting locations, which are commonly housed at schools, places of worship and community centres, among others.
Where can I vote?
Elections Ontario has an online tool that allows voters to find their electoral district and election day voting location by postal code.
If, for example, you live at the Scotiabank Arena, you'll be voting at St. Andrew's Centre and electing a representative for Spadina-Fort York.
Those without a permanent address can use the "place where you have returned to most often to eat or sleep in the past five weeks" as an address to vote.
Do I need ID to vote?
Yes. Elections Ontario requires you to show identification at your polling place, but it doesn't necessarily need to include a photo of you and it can be shown on a mobile device.
If you've been sent a voter information card in the mail, your ID can be anything from a birth certificate to a health card to a student card from one of the province's universities, among many others.
If you haven't been issued a voter information card, your piece of ID must have both your full name and your home address. This can include an Ontario driver's license, insurance statement, a child tax benefit statement or a school transcript, per Elections Ontario.
Will I have to wait in line to vote?
Elections Ontario did not say whether they expect to see lines on election night.
But Ontario hasn't had a winter election since the 1920s, so it's possible that some of the inclement weather that has walloped the province this month could contribute to difficulty parking or getting to the polls.
(In the 1926 election, which took place on Dec. 1, the Star reported that some voters "felt it too cold to brave the near-zero temperatures" to vote; voter turnout that year was still over 64 per cent, however, compared with the 44 per cent and 56 per cent figures in 2022 and 2018, respectively.)
When will we know who
won the election?
In the last Ontario election, most networks called the election by 9:30 p.m. on election night. When Ontarians went to the polls in 2014 and 2018, networks were likewise able to call the election results before most of the province went to bed.
While it's yet unclear whether this election will be as quick to project, recent polls gave Ford a commanding lead over Bonnie Crombie's Liberals and Marit Stiles' New Democrats.
CAPTION: Ontarians can vote in-person on Thursday from 9 a.m. to 9 p.m. at one of more than 7,000 voting locations across the province.Chris DonovanTHE CANADIANPRESS file photo
CREDIT: Hayden Godfrey Toronto Star

Subject: Voters; Voter behavior; Elections

Title: What to know about election day

Publication title: Toronto Star; Toronto, Ont.

First page: A.6

Publication year: 2025

Publication date: Feb 24, 2025

Section: News

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto, Ont.

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

ISSN: 03190781

Source type: Newspaper

Language of publication: English

Document 61 of 500

A worsening reality puts patients at risk: Cancer patient spent 40 hours in the emergency department waiting for treatment. It was the most dangerous place she could be

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/worsening-reality-puts-patients-at-risk/docview/3169989059/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+worsening+reality+puts+patients+at+risk%3A+Cancer+patient+spent+40+hours+in+the+emergency+department+waiting+for+treatment.+It+was+the+most+dangerous+place+she+could+be&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Christina Shehata was told she had a life-threatening condition soon after she arrived at Kingston General Hospital's emergency department.
The 51-year-old with metastatic breast cancer had a high fever, and tests revealed she lacked a type of white blood cell that fights infection, putting her at further risk.
Diagnosed with febrile neutropenia, a medical emergency for cancer patients, Christina quickly received IV antibiotics and was told she needed to be admitted.
But no in-patient beds were available. Instead, Christina remained in the emergency department. It would take 40 hours for her to get moved to an in-patient bed.
"I was terrified for her," said her husband, Dr. Adam Shehata, a family physician.
He said Christina received appropriate care in the ED but waiting near other patients put her at high risk for an infection.
"The area she was in was essentially a row of hospital gurneys separated only by curtains with sick patients on either side of her," Adam said. "Her risk of infection was so high and there was nothing I could do to protect her."
Across Ontario, patients are waiting hours - sometimes days - in EDs, a problem that has gotten substantially worse over the last three years, according to provincial data obtained by the Star. In many hospitals, patients waiting in the ED are being treated in hallways, storage areas and other unconventional spaces due to a lack of beds.
This kind of "hallway medicine" frustrates patients but also risks their health and puts further stress on hospital staff struggling to cope in crowded conditions.
Health-care issues, including the family doctor shortage and hallway medicine, have emerged as key issues in the Feb. 27 snap election. During his 2018 campaign, Doug Ford promised to end hallway health care, making the pledge a key part of the Progressive Conservative platform.
Four months after getting elected, Ford stated, "One patient treated in a hallway is one patient too many," and said it was "unacceptable" for patients to wait hours to see a doctor or to get care in hospital hallways.
"Since then, it's gone from bad to worse," said Dr. Raghu Venugopal, an emergency physician in Toronto. "What bad hallway medicine was has only deteriorated to chair- and waiting-room medicine."
Venugopal cites a long list of conditions he had to treat in hospital chairs, including a brain bleed, a broken spine, a bowel obstruction and a heart attack.
Research shows patients who "board" for hours in the ED, particularly older patients, can face a higher risk of death.
"It isn't just that patients get treated in hallways," said Dr. Andrew Petrosoniak, an emergency physician and trauma team leader at St. Michael's Hospital. "Patients who require hospital-level care, their risk of death goes up the longer they're in the emergency department. You can debate the percentages, but there is a not insignificant increase in mortality."
A report by Ontario Health, not previously made public, shows that between April 2022 and October 2024, wait times for patients needing an in-patient bed at the 90th percentile ranged from roughly 32 hours to just over 40 hours. This means 90 per cent of patients stayed in the ED for these lengths of time or less but 10 per cent stayed longer. Pre-pandemic, wait times for an in-patient bed at the 90th percentile ranged roughly from 20 to 30 hours.
Experts say a combination of factors are leading to extended ED waits: an insufficient number of hospital beds to admit new patients; a lack of long-term-care and home-care options, meaning patients who no longer need acute care have to remain in hospital; people relying on EDs due to a shortage of family doctors and difficulties getting diagnostic imaging and specialist care; and ongoing staffing shortages that limit how many patients hospitals can treat quickly.
In recent years, hospitals have seen a rise in the number of very sick patients coming to the ED. These more complex patients, many with a combination of chronic illnesses, require more time to stabilize, assess and treat, which leads to longer wait times for other patients.
"We're seeing more and more frail elderly, and the complexity of the cases that we're seeing is that much greater," said Ottawa-based emergency physician Dr. James Worrall, who noted that Ontario's population of people over 65 has increased dramatically in the past 20 years.
Since 2018, the Ford government has added 3,500 new hospital beds and has said 3,000 more are in the pipeline, tied to 50 new hospital projects. The province has said it has also made significant investments in long-term care. Before the election, the PCs pledged a $1.4 billion investment to connect every Ontarian to primary care by 2029.
In a statement, the Ministry of Health noted the province's "ED landscape and patient demographic have evolved over the years in particular from the pandemic experience," an issue "not unique to Ontario."
Adam, who took Christina to Kingston General Hospital on Feb. 9, said he does not blame the hospital or its staff for her long wait. He said doctors and nurses provided excellent care and understood the couple's worries, but said they couldn't move her due to capacity issues.
During the overnight hours, it got so loud that hospital staff gave Christina earplugs. Earlier that evening, Adam said, nearby patients were ill and coughing and a patient next to Christina had to use a commode.
"The stench was everywhere," said Adam, who was told no isolation rooms were available. "Everyone can understand why they wouldn't want their sick wife, who doesn't have the ability to fight off infection, beside someone who is defecating on one side and coughing on the other."
After more than 24 hours, Adam said Christina was moved out of the area with curtained-off gurneys and into a room with a door that closed. Though still in the ED, Adam said the new space offered more privacy and protection.
In a statement, Kingston Health Sciences Centre (KHSC) said it has experienced a "sustained increase in patient volumes and continues to operate at high capacity" like many hospitals in Canada. It said this is primarily due to "population growth, as well as a growing number of patients with more complex needs than ever before."
KHSC said its teams are "committed to providing timely, high-quality, safe care" and that "rooms and care space allocation" is based on "several factors, including patient acuity and specific medical needs."
Dr. Candace McNaughton, an emergency medicine physician at Sunnybrook hospital, said the challenges Ontario EDs face are more significant now than before COVID. She said individual hospitals and their staff are "doing everything they can with the limited resources they have," but it's not enough.
"Most of my health encounters start with, 'I am so sorry for the wait,' " she said.
Recent research led by McNaughton showed that since 2020, more adults are leaving Ontario EDs without being seen by a physician. The study, published in December, found these patients face a 14 per cent higher risk of death or hospitalization within seven days when compared to patients who left the ED without being seen by a physician before 2020.
McNaughton, a scientist at Sunnybrook Research Institute, said the study raises a "concerning signal" that should prompt system-wide policy interventions.
"There's something wrong with the system; people are not doing as well as they did pre-2020," she said.
After three days, Christina left the hospital following antibiotic treatment and she's now well enough to resume cancer therapy. She said she hopes her experience will highlight long-standing system challenges and push politicians to act.
"It was ridiculous what I experienced," Christina said. "People lining the hallways. People behind these flimsy curtains. People not able to get seen by a doctor or get admitted right away.
"It shouldn't be this way."
CREDIT: Megan Ogilvie and Kenyon Wallace Toronto Star

Subject: Hospitals; Patients; Emergency medical care; Medicine; Health care access; Health care policy

Document 62 of 500

What to watch for in Liberal leadership debates: Hopefuls square off tonight in French in first of two clashes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/what-watch-liberal-leadership-debates/docview/3169989004/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=What+to+watch+for+in+Liberal+leadership+debates%3A+Hopefuls+square+off+tonight+in+French+in+first+of+two+clashes&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Less than two weeks before federal Liberals choose Canada's next prime minister, four candidates to replace Justin Trudeau are set to square off in a pair of debates that could help decide who leads the governing party into a coming federal election.
It's widely seen as a key moment in a condensed race, 49 days after Trudeau suspended Parliament and announced he will resign on March 9 when his party chooses its next leader.
First, on Monday at 8 p.m., the candidates - Mark Carney, Chrystia Freeland, Karina Gould and Frank Baylis - will gather in a Montreal film studio for a French debate that will test how anglophone front-runners handle themselves in the language of a province crucial to the party's hopes to remain in power.
Then, on Tuesday at 8 p.m., comes the English debate, when contenders will appear in that same studio to pitch themselves to a likely broader audience of Liberals and curious politicos across the country.
For Greg MacEachern, a veteran Liberal strategist and principal at KAN Strategies, the moment is a chance for the party to showcase its offering for a general election that could come in a matter of weeks. But it also comes with risk.
"Occasionally leadership debates can really show division, or sow division," MacEachern said.
Here are three things to watch for in the Liberal leadership debates.
The French test
In a race dominated by anglophone candidates, Monday's French debate is expected to be a critical test of linguistic ability for a party with a political base and deep history in Québec.
"There will be a lot of rankings and reviews of the candidates' French," said Dan Arnold, a seasoned Liberal campaign adviser who worked as director of research and advertising in Trudeau's Prime Minister's Office (PMO).
Arnold said those rankings will matter, since French-dominated Québec is a "key part of the Liberal winning coalition," so "you want to make sure you connect with Quebecers."
But it also matters to Liberals outside Québec, Arnold said, since they understand that whomever they choose as leader must be attractive to Québec voters.
Arnold recalled how, in the 2006 Liberal leadership contest, a newspaper judged the candidate he worked for - former Ontario Liberal education minister Gerard Kennedy - to have poor French.
"It really just deep-sixed his campaign, because people were like, 'well, he can't speak French well enough, so he can't win in Québec,' " Arnold said.
Vandana Kattar, a Liberal campaign organizer who also worked in Trudeau's PMO, said she'll be watching for "the quality of language ability" from the candidates - whether they are at ease and appear conversational, or get thrown off by a technical word or phrase.
While most of the candidates have already demonstrated varying degrees of French ability, with former Liberal MP Baylis the sole contender from the province of Québec, competence in the official language has come up as an issue. Before the party disqualified him, Ottawa-area MP Chandra Arya rejected the assertion that the prime minister should be proficient in French and English.
Former Paul Martin-era MP Ruby Dhalla twice asked for a "personal translator" for the French debate, but she was also disqualified from the race on Friday.
Fireworks
For Kattar and others, another big thing to watch for is whether - or to what extent - the candidates attack each other during the debates.
"My sense is that everybody's probably just going to pile on Carney," she said, referring to the former central banker running for the job.
Carney has emerged as the perceived front-runner, after his campaign raked in more than $1.75 million in donations by Feb. 9, according to Elections Canada returns - more than all the other candidates' reported donations combined. Scores of MPs and cabinet ministers in the Trudeau government have also endorsed the former Bank of Canada and Bank of England governor.
Arnold said he'll be watching especially for how Freeland, the former finance minister, pursues Carney, given that he appears to be outpacing her in the race.
"She kind of needs to knock him off his game," Arnold said. "She has to make the case that she's more electable than Mark Carney. And one way to do that is for him to stumble in the debate."
Arnold added that Gould, the former House leader, might also be "more aggressive than she would otherwise be" - especially if she sees a real path to victory in the contest.
But Liberal strategists are also aware that friction between the candidates could be clipped and used by their political opponents if it is particularly cutting. For example, if one candidate lands a real blow against a rival that wins the contest, that exchange could be fodder for the Conservatives in the coming general election.
That's even more of a concern in a shortened race like this one, with opposition parties signalling they intend to topple the Liberal minority government and trigger an election with Parliament set to return with a new prime minister on March 24, MacEachern said.
"It is very much within your prerogative and your right to pursue a teardown campaign, but I think Liberals will be looking at that through the lens of: how is this really helping Canada, and how is this helping our cause?" he said.
The final boss
That cause, of prolonging their party's hold in office, will face its true challenge in that coming general election.
Polls show their main rival in that contest will be Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives.
That looming matchup means Liberals watching this week's leadership debates will also consider how the candidates might perform as the party's standard-bearer in the next federal election, Kattar said.
"(The Conservatives) are so good on their feet, they're so good at (communications), they're so good at driving message. And if you're knocked off your message, how good are you to snap back?"
Arnold added that he is curious for any "preview" of how the candidates would take on Poilievre, and how they might try to "define him" for Canadian voters.
For MacEachern, the candidates - especially the front-runners - should treat the debates as a chance to send a message to Liberals, and also to the public in general.
And that means aiming attacks at Conservatives, rather than each other.
"It's a big opportunity not to waste," he said.
CREDIT: Alex Ballingall Deputy Toronto Star

Subject: Candidates; Voters; Political leadership; Debates; Political advertising; Elections

Document 63 of 500

No one cares about the Ontario election. Was that Doug Ford's plan?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/no-one-cares-about-ontario-election-was-that-doug/docview/3169792164/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=No+one+cares+about+the+Ontario+election.+Was+that+Doug+Ford%27s+plan%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In a recurring feature, Susan Delacourt, a small-l liberal, and Matt Gurney, a small-c conservative, bring their different perspectives - and shared commitment to civil disagreement - to the political debates of the moment.
Susan Delacourt: I don't know if you've noticed, Matt, but we are days away from an Ontario election. While it's dangerous to make predictions in politics these days, you want to take a guess at what we might see when all the votes are tallied Thursday night?
Matt Gurney: You know, I'll get to your question in a moment. But I have to share something first. Readers will have guessed how Susan and I do these. We message back and forth, clean up the text and then send it to the editors. Do you know what happened, Susan, between you sending the first message and me replying with this one? It's very funny.
SD: Given how many things you do in a day, I'm not sure I'd even hazard a guess.
MG: Ha. Well, I'll just tell you. I saw your message and decided I'd rather type on my computer than my phone. So I went to my office. As I was heading there, I checked the mail slot at the side of my house. And therein I found ... a $200 cheque from the government of Ontario! I'd been wondering where my pre-election loot was.
SD: So does that have you feeling all warm about Doug Ford's chances in this election?
MG: It has me slightly closer to a new PS5. My feelings about Ford's chances haven't changed much in a long time. He's cruising to a majority. Perhaps an even larger one than he has. I don't think any poll has suggested anything else in a long time, and the recent announcement by the Liberals that they're pivoting to focus on the NDP is, despite Bonnie's Crombie's valiant effort to deny the blindingly obvious, a sign that the opposition parties know it's a fight for second. What can I say? If I reincarnate, in my next life, I want opponents like the ones Doug has faced over the years.
SD: Forgive me, but what's a PS5?
MG: A PlayStation 5. I find the bribe cheques so odious and craven that I'm insisting on spending the money on something ridiculous and unnecessary. I'll make a charity donation of my own money, but I'm taking what Ford is sending me and wasting it on a frill.
SD: It wasn't so long ago that Chrystia Freeland made a fiery exit from Justin Trudeau's cabinet decrying "costly political gimmicks," and I don't get the sense she's been rewarded for that statement of principle. But that's a story for another day - another contest, also creeping up quickly on us.
Based on what is mainly anecdotal evidence, I do think that at least some Ontario voters are not keen to reward Ford for calling this election. Donald Trump also took some steam out of Ford's Captain Canada campaign when he granted the 30-day reprieve on some tariffs. So I do think Ford has probably had to work harder than he expected in this election to justify it happening.
MG: Honestly, I don't see any sign that Ford is working particularly hard. He's not hesitating to take long breaks from campaigning to jet down to Washington and accomplish absolutely squat while he's there. This has been one of the strangest elections I've ever observed or covered, to the extent that I'm covering it. I don't think I have heard anyone discussing it out there in the real world even once. I know it's a busy news cycle on a lot of fronts, but it seems to me like the opposition parties are trying and failing to get anyone to notice them, Ford is trying to play act as Captain Canada, and another PC majority looms.
SD: I'll tell you why I don't like the trail this has blazed for the federal election. You're right that Ford has mailed a bit of it in, especially when it comes to giving detailed, properly costed platform proposals. He and his candidates haven't been all that great at participating in debates. I can see how all parties, but especially federal Conservatives, would see this as a new standard in what one can get away with when it comes to campaigning.
MG: The last few Ontario elections have been like this, though. I'm not saying there haven't been important issues. The last election was in the immediate aftermath of the pandemic and the provincial government's total abandonment of Ottawa during the convoy. There were lots of important issues. But the electorate seems massively checked out. That's probably something we can't really explore the depths of in this format. But I think it's something we should be aware of and probably be concerned about. What happens if you throw an election and no one shows up?
SD: It's a very good question. I'll be watching those turnout numbers this week. It was less than 44 per cent in the 2022 election - an all-time low. I would not be surprised if this one sets a new apathy record. I do think it's interesting how we're being told to expect a change election federally, while Ontario voters seem pretty content with more of the same. Perhaps all the chaos we're seeing south of the Canada-U.S. border is making Canadians a little more nervous about rampant change. But that's just a theory.
MG: It could be. My own theory is that it's closer to "I don't have the spare energy for Ontario politics. Doug is fine." Whatever it is, seems to be working out as the PC leader intended. Thanks for the PS5.
SD: If the race is for second place, as you suggested, I will also be watching for clues about how Ontarians are feeling about the Liberals and New Democrats, federally. Some of the latest polls suggest that the Liberals, especially under prospective leader Mark Carney, are benefiting from a collapse of the NDP's support. Should the Ontario NDP place third on Thursday, there will be speculation about whether the federal NDP will be in any hurry to have a snap election anytime soon.
MG: Yeah, there'll obviously be some lessons to glean from the provincial result that'll bear on the federal race. Frankly, if the provincial NDP totally tanks and federal polls keep showing Jagmeet Singh heading in the same direction, whether or not we have an election is probably going to stop being anything the federal NDP have a say in. If you're Carney, why wouldn't you pull the trigger?
SD: A spring filled with snap elections - just the thing for a nervous nation. It gives us lots to write about, though.
CAPTION: Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford speaks to reporters, accompanied by other Council of the Federation members, at the Mayflower Hotel in Washington, on Feb. 12, 2025. T
CREDIT: Susan Delacourt, Matt Gurney

Document 64 of 500

Trump's tariffs shine a light on our vulnerability and why Ontario needs more to sell, not just more markets

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/trumps-tariffs-shine-light-on-our-vulnerability/docview/3169792162/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trump%27s+tariffs+shine+a+light+on+our+vulnerability+and+why+Ontario+needs+more+to+sell%2C+not+just+more+markets&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: At his campaign kickoff, Progressive Conservative leader Doug Ford made it clear: this election is about the economy.
He's right, of course, but the question is, what kind of economy is Ontario building?
The province cannot afford to rely on outdated strategies that leave it vulnerable to global trade disruptions.
The looming economic turbulence, particularly from U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff threats, demands a new approach, one that prioritizes resilience, sovereignty, and innovation.
The old model of attracting foreign branch plants with government subsidies is not enough.
Ontario needs a serious, long-term economic strategy that puts its own companies and workers first.
For too long, Canada has been dependent on exporting raw materials while relying on foreign multinationals for high-value industries.
This has left the economy exposed to the whims of global markets, resulting in poor terms of trade and limited economic security.
If Ontario wants to be truly competitive, it must shift from being a supplier of low-margin commodities to a producer of high-value goods and services, like intellectual property and digital services.
That means strengthening the province's fastest-growing companies and ensuring that Ontario is not just participating in the global economy but shaping it.
More bluntly, we need to be producing more of the 21st-century products the world needs.
Ontario is already home to world-class innovation.
Our cybersecurity firms protect critical digital infrastructure. Our health-tech companies are improving global health care systems. Our cleantech sector is commercializing research that reduces emissions and creates high-paying jobs.
These are just some of the industries that will define the future, but they need better support to grow, scale, and stay in Canada.
Without a clear strategy to expand these businesses globally, Ontario risks watching them get snapped up by foreign interests and losing out on the economic benefits of their success.
Governments in every major innovation hub play an active role in supporting domestic industry, and Ontario should be no different.
Entrepreneurs need better access to growth capital to ensure that promising businesses have the funding to scale and compete globally.
The provincial government must also take strategic procurement seriously, prioritizing Ontario-made technologies and services instead of relying on foreign firms.
Every dollar spent on local innovation strengthens the economy and keeps public money working within the province.
Education and workforce development are equally critical. Ontario's talent shortage in AI, cybersecurity, and advanced manufacturing is a major barrier to growth.
More investment in education, training, and talent attraction is needed to ensure the province has the skilled workforce required for an economy of the future.
Economic security also means having a real industrial strategy. In an increasingly unpredictable global economy, trade relationships are fragile, and tariffs are expected to rise.
Former Bank of Canada governor Stephen Poloz has warned that protectionism is on the rise worldwide. Ontario cannot afford to be naive.
The province needs a clear plan that ensures its economic resilience and reduces its dependence on foreign multinationals for key technologies and services.
This requires more than vague commitments - it demands a deliberate effort to expand high-value sectors and ensure Ontario companies can win in global markets.
And Ontario's economy does not exist in isolation. When Ontario businesses succeed, all of Canada benefits. That's why eliminating interprovincial trade barriers and creating a more co-ordinated national strategy is important.
In recent weeks, political leaders have spoken about a "Team Canada" approach to economic challenges. It is the right instinct, but words alone are not enough.
What's needed is action - concrete policies that support Canadian companies and allow them to thrive in an increasingly competitive world.
These solutions address old economy challenges, and while they must be tackled, they are not enough on their own.
The bigger question is: what does the world need that we can build and sell? Access to markets is important, but the real focus should be on creating new, high-value products and services that position Ontario as an indispensable player in the global economy.
The challenge isn't just about reducing trade barriers - it's about ensuring we have something unique and competitive to offer.
This election isn't just about navigating the latest trade dispute - it's about ensuring Ontario's economy is built to withstand whatever comes next.
We can't lurch from crisis to crisis, reacting to every shift in global politics. We need a long-term strategy that makes Ontario stronger, more competitive, and less vulnerable to forces beyond our control.
Prosperity doesn't happen by accident. It comes from bold leadership, smart investment, and a commitment to building industries that will define the future.
Ontario has the talent, the innovation, and the ambition to lead. What it needs now is a government willing to back that vision with action.
The question in this election isn't just who will manage the next four years - it's who has a plan to make Ontario strong for the next 40.
CAPTION: Ontario is home to world-class innovation in areas such as cybersecurity, writes Marcella Arthur, Mike Andrade and Skaidra Puodziunas. But they need support to grow, scale, and stay in Canada.
CREDIT: Marcella Arthur, Mike Andrade and Skaidra Puodziunas Contributors

Subject: Cybersecurity; Political campaigns; Global economy; Global health; Tariffs

Document 65 of 500

She waited 40 hours in the ER, surrounded by sick patients. It was the most dangerous place she could be - and a worsening reality in Ontario

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/she-waited-40-hours-er-surrounded-sick-patients/docview/3169792149/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=She+waited+40+hours+in+the+ER%2C+surrounded+by+sick+patients.+It+was+the+most+dangerous+place+she+could+be+-+and+a+worsening+reality+in+Ontario&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Christina Shehata was told she had a life-threatening condition soon after she arrived at Kingston General Hospital's emergency department.
The 51-year-old with metastatic breast cancer had a high fever, and tests revealed she lacked a type of white blood cell that fights infection, putting her at further risk.
Diagnosed with febrile neutropenia, a medical emergency for cancer patients, Christina quickly received IV antibiotics and was told she needed to be admitted.
But no in-patient beds were available. Instead, Christina remained in the emergency department. It would take 40 hours for her to get moved to an inpatient bed.
"I was terrified for her," said her husband, Dr. Adam Shehata, a family physician.
He said Christina received appropriate care in the ED but waiting near other patients put her at high risk for an infection.
"The area she was in was essentially a row of hospital gurneys separated only by curtains with sick patients on either side of her," Adam said. "Her risk of infection was so high and there was nothing I could do to protect her."
Across Ontario, patients are waiting hours - sometimes days - in EDs, a problem that has gotten substantially worse over the last three years, according to provincial data obtained by the Star.
In many hospitals, patients waiting in the ED are being treated in hallways, storage areas and other unconventional spaces due to a lack of beds elsewhere in the hospital. This kind of "hallway medicine" frustrates patients but also risks their health and puts further stress on hospital staff struggling to cope in crowded conditions.
Health-care issues, including the family doctor shortage and hallway medicine, have emerged as key issues in the Feb. 27 snap election. During his 2018 campaign, Doug Ford promised to end hallway health care, making the pledge a key part of the Progressive Conservative platform.
Four months after getting elected, Ford stated, "One patient treated in a hallway is one patient too many," and said it was "unacceptable" for patients to wait hours to see a doctor or to get care in hospital hallways.
"Since then, it's gone from bad to worse," said Dr. Raghu Venugopal, an emergency physician in Toronto. "What bad hallway medicine was has only deteriorated to chair- and waiting-room medicine."
Venugopal cites a long list of conditions he had to treat in hospital chairs, including a brain bleed, a broken spine, a bowel obstruction and a heart attack.
Research shows patients who "board" for hours in the ED, particularly older patients, can face a higher risk of death.
"It isn't just that patients get treated in hallways," said Dr. Andrew Petrosoniak, an emergency physician and trauma team leader at St. Michael's Hospital. "Patients who require hospital-level care, their risk of death goes up the longer they're in the emergency department. You can debate the percentages, but there is a not insignificant increase in mortality."
A report by Ontario Health, not previously made public, shows that between April 2022 and October 2024, wait times for patients needing an in-patient bed at the 90th percentile ranged from roughly 32 hours to just over 40 hours. This means 90 per cent of patients stayed in the ED for these lengths of time or less but 10 per cent stayed longer. Pre-pandemic, wait times for an in-patient bed at the 90th percentile ranged roughly from 20 to 30 hours.
Experts say a combination of factors are leading to extended ED waits: an insufficient number of hospital beds to admit new patients; a lack of long-term-care and home-care options, meaning patients who no longer need acute care have to remain in hospital; people relying on EDs due to a shortage of family doctors and difficulties getting diagnostic imaging and specialist care; and ongoing staffing shortages that limit how many patients hospitals can treat quickly.
In recent years, hospitals have seen a rise in the number of very sick patients coming to the ED. These more complex patients, many with a combination of chronic illnesses, require more time to stabilize, assess and treat, which leads to longer wait times for other patients.
"We're seeing more and more frail elderly, and the complexity of the cases that we're seeing is that much greater," said Ottawa-based emergency physician Dr. James Worrall, who noted that Ontario's population of people over 65 has increased dramatically in the past 20 years.
Since 2018, the Ford government has added 3,500 new hospital beds and has said 3,000 more are in the pipeline, tied to 50 new hospital projects. The province has said it has also made significant investments in long-term care. Before the election, the PCs pledged a $1.4 billion investment to connect every Ontarian to primary care by 2029.
In a statement, the Ministry of Health noted the province's "ED landscape and patient demographic have evolved over the years in particular from the pandemic experience," an issue "not unique to Ontario."
Adam, who took Christina to Kingston General Hospital on Feb. 9, said he does not blame the hospital or its staff for her long wait. He said doctors and nurses provided excellent care and understood the couple's worries, but said they couldn't move her due to capacity issues.
During the overnight hours, it got so loud that hospital staff gave Christina earplugs. Earlier that evening, Adam said, nearby patients were ill and coughing and a patient next to Christina had to use a commode.
"The stench was everywhere," said Adam, who was told no isolation rooms were available. "Everyone can understand why they wouldn't want their sick wife, who doesn't have the ability to fight off infection, beside someone who is defecating on one side and coughing on the other."
After more than 24 hours, Adam said Christina was moved out of the area with curtained-off gurneys and into a room with a door that closed. Though still in the ED, Adam said the new space offered more privacy and protection.
In a statement, Kingston Health Sciences Centre (KHSC) said it has experienced a "sustained increase in patient volumes and continues to operate at high capacity" like many hospitals in Canada. It said this is primarily due to "population growth, as well as a growing number of patients with more complex needs than ever before."
KHSC said its teams are "committed to providing timely, high-quality, safe care" and that "rooms and care space allocation" is based on "several factors, including patient acuity and specific medical needs."
Dr. Candace McNaughton, an emergency medicine physician at Sunnybrook hospital, said the challenges Ontario EDs face are more significant now than before COVID. She said individual hospitals and their staff are "doing everything they can with the limited resources they have," but it's not enough.
"Most of my health encounters start with, 'I am so sorry for the wait,' " she said.
Recent research led by McNaughton showed that since 2020, more adults are leaving Ontario EDs without being seen by a physician. The study, published in December, found these patients face a 14 per cent higher risk of death or hospitalization within seven days when compared to patients who left the ED without being seen by a physician before 2020.
McNaughton, a scientist at Sunnybrook Research Institute, said the study raises a "concerning signal" that should prompt system-wide policy interventions.
"There's something wrong with the system; people are not doing as well as they did pre-2020," she said.
After three days, Christina left the hospital following antibiotic treatment and she's now well enough to resume cancer therapy. She said she hopes her experience will highlight longstanding system challenges and push politicians to act.
"It was ridiculous what I experienced," Christina said. "People lining the hallways. People behind these flimsy curtains. People not able to get seen by a doctor or get admitted right away.
"It shouldn't be this way."
CAPTION: Christina Shehata was only separated from other sick patients by curtains during her hours-long ER wait."We're seeing more and more frail elderly and the complexity of the cases that we're seeing is that much greater," said Ottawa-based emergency physician Dr. James Worrall.Christina and Adam Shehata. Christina has metastatic breast cancer and went to Kingston General for a life-threatening condition. She got the appropriate medical care but waited 40 hours in the ER before being admitted to hospital for an inpatient bed.Christina and Adam Shehata. Christina has metastatic breast cancer and went to Kingston General for a life-threatening condition. She got the appropriate medical care but waited 40 hours in the ER before being admitted to hospital for an inpatient bed.
CREDIT: Megan Ogilvie, Kenyon Wallace
