Document 66 of 500

So many NIMBYs have blocked healthy changes to Toronto. Does the city have too much democracy?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/so-many-nimbys-have-blocked-healthy-changes/docview/3169792124/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=So+many+NIMBYs+have+blocked+healthy+changes+to+Toronto.+Does+the+city+have+too+much+democracy%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I fantasize about starting a Toronto residents' association that is simply for things.
A new residential building bringing new neighbours? Sure.
A shop deep within the neighbourhood? Plan a welcome party.
I harbour this fantasy as a way to counteract the decades-long chorus of "no" heard from such groups, frustratingly coupled with demands for more consultation when there's already been lots of that. Group members often volunteer willingly for photos with their arms crossed and serious looks near whatever it is they are opposing - so much it's become a cliche.
The problem with being against so much is it's hard to know what they're for. As with any critic, the best ones tell us what's good as much as critiquing the bad.
Constant opposition also risks being a "chicken little" if new things and change always mean the sky is falling. The status quo has left too many out of the prosperity and good life Toronto could provide to more people, so the city must change and grow for its own good.
Two recent examples come to mind.
One was an omnibus item council considered at its February meeting that included a pilot project to permit sixplexes in the Scarborough North ward and tweaks making it easier to increase density on major roads - the "avenues." It's something that's been in the works for a long time. It passed, but as my colleague Matt Elliott points out in his excellent newsletter City Hall Watcher, councillors were inundated with letters from residents associations complaining about lack of consultation when there were already 25 formal consultation meetings and an online survey.
No amount of consultation will ever be enough, it seems, if the conclusion and consensus isn't what residents groups want.
Another example is from a few months ago when some residents associations launched what amounted to a fear-mongering campaign in opposition to allowing small scale commercial shops and services to operate within neighbourhoods. I'll refer to Elliott again, who noted the sad irony of all this in his recent Star column: in the face of Donald Trump's tariffs council voted to support a "buy local" campaign, but just two months before denied a big opportunity to do just that when they acquiesced to the resident group demands.
Even resistance to Trump can be thwarted by NIMBYism.
One thing to keep in mind is that Toronto city staff already do a herculean amount of consultations, whether bike lanes or zoning changes, but because people don't pay attention to municipal governance as much as other levels, it's easy to infer this is not the case.
It does feel odd to argue that there's too much talking and consultation at this particular time in history, when democratic norms and institutions are shrinking and being undermined here and abroad. Locally, consider how the destruction of the Ontario Science Centre and Ontario Place has been rammed through and a foreign spa company picked in secrecy despite considerable public opposition.
Here, calls for any consultation are welcome.
However, like a lot of things, there's a right amount of democratic process. Too much and it leads to paralysis and a kind of antidemocracy where healthy change is blocked. This spans the political spectrum, with the progressive side often as resistant to change as the conservative side. U.S. Democrats are currently going through a period of soul searching on this very thing since their devastating loss to Trump in the fall, a cautionary tale for us in Toronto and Ontario.
One Democratic strategist recently pointed out that people are leaving "blue state" California while "red state" Texas is attracting people, partly because of a dramatic difference in housing prices. Residents in expensive and liberal California cities like Los Angeles and San Francisco have waged legendary anti-housing battles, while it's easier to build in Texas.
Similarly, in his new book "Stuck: How the Privileged and the Propertied Broke the Engine of American Opportunity," Yoni Appelbaum argues, in part, by prohibiting housing through policy manoeuvres like tighter zoning and classic neighbourhood opposition, progressives have frozen the American dream.
Surely that dream, such as it was, has been undermined by multiple forces, but that this conversation is happening in various places, critiquing decades of neighbourhood and city opposition to change, is something to watch.
Of course, not every residents' association is the same or against all change. And there are people working in the YIMBY (Yes In My Back Yard) movement as well as Toronto's More Neighbours group, advocating for housing, but neighbourhood-based residents' organizations have the ear - and often the vote - of local councillors like no other.
Here's a challenge to residents' associations: tell us what you're for, and do it often, and maybe more people might listen to the most important things you're against.
CAPTION: Residents' groups in the city are against so much, it's tough to know what they stand for.
CREDIT: Shawn Micallef

Subject: Political campaigns; Neighborhoods; Zoning ordinances

Location: California; Texas; United States--US

Document 67 of 500

Allan Gregg: Doug Ford has a shot at becoming Ontario's greatest premier. Will he squander the chance?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/allan-gregg-doug-ford-has-shot-at-becoming/docview/3169738080/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Allan+Gregg%3A+Doug+Ford+has+a+shot+at+becoming+Ontario%27s+greatest+premier.+Will+he+squander+the+chance%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Typically, calling an early election that appears to be both unnecessary and opportunist does not go well for an incumbent. Both David Peterson and Justin Trudeau were ahead in the polls when they voluntarily shortened their terms: Peterson went down to outright defeat in 1990 while Trudeau returned a status quo Parliament that looked pretty much like it did before he called the election in 2021.
So far, it appears that Doug Ford and his Progressive Conservatives are about to avoid this fate.
Most polls continue to report a double-digit lead, although with some tightening in the race between the PCs and Liberals, paralleled by a modest deterioration in NDP support. But these changes all appear to be small, predictable and unrelated to events in the campaign itself.
To add to this inertia, between the snow and the lack of enthusiasm for any of the available options, the odds are that voter turnout could also reach an all-time low as well.
So, unless something earthshattering happens over the next week, it looks like we are in the midst of an election about nothing, where nothing happened.
Ford should win his third - and perhaps largest - majority government. The NDP will lose their Official Opposition status to the Liberals, who will probably pick up seats, predominately in Toronto. And if both of these patterns occur, Crombie will most likely win her riding in Mississauga and take her seat in the legislature, while Stiles will lose hers and exit Queen's Park.
But just because nothing much happened during the campaign itself, does not mean that this will hold true once it is over.
What about the "mandate" Doug Ford sought to protect Ontario's interests against the threat of American tariffs by ensuring that his term will extend over the full length of President Trump's?
During the campaign, this focus was clearly opportunistic and clever because it allowed him both to run against a more unpopular opponent, who wasn't even on the ballot (ie Trump), and at the same time, play into his brand strength as a "straight talking, no-nonsense tough guy". It also was extremely cynical and performative.
You don't need to be a constitutional scholar to know that a provincial Premier has as much authority and jurisdiction to make a meaningful difference in international affairs as the Prime Minister has to deliver health care services in Keswick, Ontario.
So, unless he enjoys barking at the moon, it's unlikely that Doug Ford's next term will be consumed with dialogue and negotiations with Donald Trump.
That said, there is little question that between his regular appearances on U.S. cable news, his electoral focus on a major threat to the nation and chairing the Council of the Federation, he has been able to elevate his stature as the senior-most Premier in the country.
Might he use this new status to play a larger role in federal-provincial relations and assume a "unifier" role in Canadian federalism; one who brokers differences between the provinces and Ottawa and even the provinces themselves, in the same tradition as Leslie Frost and Bill Davis?
Its hard to tell. Doug Ford is a very different kind of politician, and his behaviour is very difficult to predict. For all his elbows-up, partisan bluster, he put aside what should have been an antagonistic relationship and showed a surprising willingness to compromise and co-operate with Prime Minister Trudeau during the pandemic. In the same way, once she was elected, he laid down his combative gloves and was able to strike a fairly civil and constructive relationship with Toronto Mayor, Olivia Chow.
So maybe.
But then again, he just might revert to the old bombastic ways of his first six-and-a-half years in office and advocate for idiosyncratic measures which signal to his broad coalition that only he understands and can protect their interests.
If this comes to pass, we can probably expect four more years of one-off initiatives: those that make it easier for commuters to drive from the suburbs to their desired destination or for city dwellers to avoid playing roller derby with bicyclists; developer-friendly incursions into green spaces to accommodate more urban sprawl; tax relief and the relaxation of regulatory measures that appeal to small business owners and proprietors; edicts to municipal governments that make it more difficult to increase urban density, to the benefit of the single-family home owner; and grandiose, pipe-dream schemes (like building a 60-kilometre tunnel under the 401). So, initiatives that represent magical thinking more than true vision.
This Ford Redux might happen ... but I don't think so.
What is different now, compared to his first two terms, is the question of Ford's legacy.
No matter what you might think of him, if he forms a third majority government, Doug Ford will become the most successful provincial politician in Ontario history. And for a guy from a family who has been sold short and made the brunt of Toronto elite jokes all his life, this just might be a big deal.
In his next term, there will be no real need to pander to his base (largely at the expense of his distractors), so maybe Ford will see the next four years as his opportunity not just to be Ontario' s most successful Premier but Ontario's greatest Premier.
Maybe he will recruit and assemble a team of experts who will revamp professional credentialization and recruit the physicians, nurses and heath care workers necessary to revitalize a system badly in need of repair. Once resourced, he might then turn his attention to real innovation in structural change and adopt a plan like Jane Philpott has proposed where all Ontarians will have automatic access to community-based facilities, staffed by multi-disciplinary health care professionals.
Next, he could task Ontario's 15 research universities to convene with the Ontario Board of Trade and Chamber of Commerce to craft a blue-print to prioritize the province's economic strengths and weaknesses in order to revitalize our industrial base so we could compete more effectively in the global market place.
He might then reach out to urban planners and Ontario's mayors and challenge them to design a world class, mixed transportation system for our cities that is sustainable and practical and designed both to alleviate congestion and facilitate mobility. While they were still in the same room, he could bring in the construction industry to devise a plan to reduce development charges, rejig density requirements and build more modular housing. With these in hand, he could then go to the federal government and challenge them to find ways to tap capital markets to fund those plans.
And finally, to give Ontario a central role in the new world AI economy and to make sure that Indigenous Peoples in the province receive their rightful share of the riches that would flow from that place, he could sit down with Ontario' s First Nations, mining and transportation leaders and announce his intention to become the world leader in critical minerals by providing the infrastructure and leadership to develop the Ring of Fire.
Now that would be some mandate.
So, what will be get - Doug Ford the unifying force in Canadian federalism; the idiosyncratic appeaser; or the legacy seeker?
It's hard to know.
But because this has been an election about nothing, where nothing happened, should he win a third majority, the one thing we do know is that there are no guardrails and Doug Ford's mandate for the next four years will be whatever he wants it be.
Whether a source of fear or optimism, this election will have left him unbridled and unleashed.
CAPTION: Ontario Progressive Conservative Party Leader Doug Ford speaks during the Ontario election debate in North Bay on Friday February 14, 2025.
CREDIT: Allan Gregg

Subject: Federalism; Political campaigns; Federal provincial relations; Political leadership; Prime ministers

People: Trump, Donald J

Document 68 of 500

Poilievre pivots from 'broken' to 'Canada first'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/poilievre-pivots-broken-canada-first/docview/3169738070/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Poilievre+pivots+from+%27broken%27+to+%27Canada+first%27&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Are the federal Conservatives flailing?
In the span of a week, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre has gone from his previous description of Canada as a country where "everything is broken" to basking in a sea of red and white, and an election-style speech asking supporters, staff and MPs: "Who's ready to put Canada First?"
Days later, however, Poilievre was tacking further to the right. He held a press conference in Toronto Thursday in which he offered no comment about U.S. President Donald Trump's sellout of Ukraine (and he wasn't asked). He doubled down on the "broken" and the "radical woke" culture-war narrative that poisoned politics in the United States; peppered legitimate criticisms about his potential Liberal challenger with gross exaggerations, and took questions from only pre-approved media outlets, such as Rebel News and True North, and none from the mainstream English press.
The narrowing of public opinion polls - an enormous reversal of an 18-month trend that had Conservatives leading by more than 20 per cent to now single digits - have Conservatives spooked. Several polls suggest that former Bank of Canada governor and current Liberal leadership front-runner Mark Carney would be a threat to Poilievre's efforts to become prime minister. A Carney-led Liberal party, according to the latest Leger poll, would be statistically tied in public opinion, and lead in Ontario, Atlantic Canada and Quebec which would, hypothetically, translate into a Liberal minority government.
It's no wonder Poilievre is so focused on Carney. On Thursday alone, he mentioned Carney's name 48 times during his 27-minute press conference.
But what's happened over the past few days is the public expression of the pulling and pushing of differing poles within the Conservative tent.
On the one hand, there is the desire to respond to the threat of the moment: to Trump, his 51st-state musings, and his menacing tariffs. That was the Poilievre on display on Saturday, Feb. 15. Showing off his pride, Poilievre quoted from Conservative prime minister John A. Macdonald's famous speech in the Commons where he lobbied for more funding for the Pacific railway, a nation-building project that would tie Halifax to the Pacific Ocean.
"Conservatives will fight ... to give us a great, a united, a rich, an improving, a developing Canada instead of making us a tributary to American laws, American railways, American bondage, and American tolls," Poilievre said, to the cheering crowd. (Nevermind that the Conservatives currently don't seem fond of investing billions of dollars into a newly-announced high-speed-rail project that would tie Quebec City to Toronto.)
Poilievre noted last Saturday that "sometimes it does take a threat to remind us what we have, what we could lose." He spoke of building the country up, and noted policies he has long championed - some of which recall Trump's own promises - more oil and gas development, lower taxes, scrapping environmental assessment rules and slashing spending on bureaucrats, consultants and foreign aid.
But on Trump, Poilievre was specific. He called on Canada to respond to the threat with "strength."
"If they put tariffs on our steel and aluminum, I will put tariffs on their steel and aluminum. If they hit us with generalized tariffs, we will respond dollar for dollar," he said, echoing the position of Liberal leadership candidates.
A trade war, Poilievre predicted, would weaken both the U.S. and Canada. But then he offered another way out of the (Trump-made) crisis: closer integration with Canada's southern neighbour.
"Option two," Poilievre said, "we trade even more. And both our workers make more. Our consumers pay less. The resulting GDP boost allows Canada to further strengthen its border. And up our military spending to two per cent of GDP faster," he said, to large cheers from the crowd.
That option did not find a fan in former Conservative prime minister Stephen Harper. Writing in the Globe and Mail this week, Harper said, "our focus now should not be on pursuing a deeper economic and security partnership. At this stage, whatever comes next from the President, Canada must avoid further dependency on the United States."
But while Harper was telling Poilievre that he needed to adopt a different tact to respond to the American economic threat, the Conservative leader appears to have concluded he cannot win on a ballot question that involves negotiating with Trump.
Instead, Poilievre pivoted back to his old strategy, ensuring the next election is fought on Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's record. His message was retooled for the moment. "Imagine if they (the Liberals) did this much damage before the tariffs, how dangerous they would be after tariffs?"
That strategy has already been so successful it led to Trudeau's resignation.
If he needs to pretend that Carney was Trudeau's puppet master over the past five years, he will.
Tying NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh to the unpopular Trudeau government sunk that party's poll numbers.
Now, the Conservatives hope it will demolish Carney, too.
CAPTION: Conservative Party Leader Pierre Poilievre speaks in Ottawa at a Canada First rally on Feb. 15. The narrowing of public opinion polls have Conservatives spooked. Several polls suggest Liberal leadership front-runner Mark Carney would be statistically tied in public opinion, and lead in Ontario, Atlantic Canada and Quebec.Andrej Ivanov Getty Images
CREDIT: Althia raj OPINION

Document 69 of 500

From Doug Ford's 'Captain Canada' jersey to Bonnie Crombie's red suit: Why what Ontario election candidates wear matters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-captain-canada-jersey-bonnie-crombies/docview/3169738028/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=From+Doug+Ford%27s+%27Captain+Canada%27+jersey+to+Bonnie+Crombie%27s+red+suit%3A+Why+what+Ontario+election+candidates+wear+matters&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In the whirlwind campaign leading up to the Ontario election on Feb. 27, the provincial party leaders have had a very short time to make a swift yet lasting impression.
That means visual imagery, often consumed without context on social media, has been an important tool to convey ideas, and ideals.
Clothing is one of the quickest ways to send a message, and has played a starring role. Witness Doug Ford's Canada Is Not for Sale ball cap and toque, followed by Bonnie Crombie's parody response on a trucker hat: Real Leaders Fix Healthcare.
When he's not playing up his "Captain Canada" moniker by wearing a hockey jersey ahead of Thursday's big game, or sporting hard hats and union hoodies at campaign stops, Premier Ford leans into a generic "businessman" uniform of navy suit, white shirt and solid tie.
"The Everyman basic look is a shtick," said Janice Meredith, a Toronto personal style expert and TV personality. "He totally embraces it."
Ford wants to look like a guy regular folks could have a beer with. Not too fancy, not too fashionable. Unlike, say Mark Carney, recently accused of wearing $2,000 Zegna sneakers on the federal Liberal leadership campaign trail, or federal NDP leader Jagmeet Singh, known for his sharp suiting and once lambasted for carrying a Versace tote bag.
Ford's solid ties are an effective strategy in this regard. "Paired with a plain white shirt, a solid tie can look cheap," said Tracy Richardson, an Ontario image consultant who often preps professionals for on-camera appearances. "It draws the eye too much."
Male politicians are fond of matching their tie to their party's colour, as Green party leader Mike Schreiner did with a sage tie at the final leadership debate. "You can point out your party affiliation with a more subtle pattern, say with green in it," said Richardson. "You don't have to ram the message home."
NDP leader Marit Stiles also went for green at the debate, choosing a deep shade for her pantsuit, perhaps because an orange suit would be ... a lot. It also meant she and red-clad Crombie, who look somewhat similar, could be easily differentiated.
"Stiles tends to wear softer, more natural colours with warm undertones," said Meredith. "Say a cream blouse instead of a white one, a softer shell rather than a collared blouse."
Stiles tends to leave her shirts untucked, which Meredith noted is a way to make tailoring more relatable. It's a less corporate look that ties in with her party's platform and ideals. Stiles is the only candidate on TikTok, and overall her social media image is curated to be warm, smiling and approachable, punctuated with splashes of bright orange in the form of a coat or scarf.
Crombie is known for her monochromatic approach, especially in signature red in her role as provincial Liberal leader. The sleek scarlet pantsuit she wore for the Family Day debate was a prime example, complete with a red top and burgundy pumps.
"She tends to a bolder blue red, or a true red, with white-white blouses," said Meredith. "She has had a long career in politics to work out her dressing."
It appears Crombie has streamlined her closet somewhat for the campaign trail. In 2017, Mississauga News was granted a look inside the then-mayor's closet. The article described a "sea of business attire, including her signature look, knee-length Jackie O style sheath dresses and jacket ensembles."
"I don't really wear casual clothes," Crombie said in the interview. She added that she got used to wearing a blazer when she was an MP, as the House of Commons dress code states arms should always be covered. "How I present myself is part of my image and my brand. I would describe my style as classic, elegant, traditional and timeless - my hemline is always at the knee."
For women in politics, dressing decisions are much more complicated. The old saying about women dancing - that we have to do all the same moves but backwards while wearing heels - applies here too.
Men have fewer clothing options, said Meredith, but "women are judged far more critically than men when it comes to their appearance." Too fashionable and you are criticized, too frumpy and you also risk disapproval.
There are also practical considerations to dressing for the camera. For instance, "you can't wear a pencil skirt on a couch for an interview," said Meredith. "You have to be aware of how a mic pack will make your blouse hang, or your dress buckle and gape."
You want to stand out, but not too much. "You can't wear anything that distracts, you can't show cleavage," said Richardson. "That is why the default for women in politics now is the pantsuit."
It's like America Ferrera's monologue in the "Barbie" movie: "You can't be too this, or too that," said Meredith. "Women's lives are full of contradictions, especially in how we are allowed to present ourselves."
We all make instant judgments about people based on how they present themselves. There are a lot of issues at stake in this election - we'll find out on Thursday just how effectively the leaders have communicated their messages.
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford and Liberal party leader Bonnie Crombie both wore slogan hats in January.Doug Ford steps off his campaign bus in Milton, on Feb. 20, 2025.The party leaders at the Ontario Leaders' debate on Feb. 17, 2025Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie at Queen's Park on Dec. 20, 2023.Ontario Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner, Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, Ontario PC Leader Doug Ford, and Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles before the Ontario Leaders' debate on Feb. 17, 2025.Marit Stiles speaks in Scarborough on Feb. 1, 2025.
CREDIT: Leanne Delap

Document 70 of 500

Premiers get some support in tariff battle: U.S. governors raise concerns in meeting with key Trump aide

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/premiers-get-some-support-tariff-battle/docview/3169738022/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Premiers+get+some+support+in+tariff+battle%3A+U.S.+governors+raise+concerns+in+meeting+with+key+Trump+aide&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In an encouraging sign for Canada, concerns about the damaging effects of U.S. President Donald Trump's threatened tariffs were raised by state governors at the White House.
The chair of the bipartisan National Governors Association said Saturday he discussed potential problems with a top Trump aide known for having the president's ear - deputy chief of staff for policy Stephen Miller.
"My point was, whatever you're doing ... whether it's a master negotiating strategy or we're just stabbing ourselves a bunch of times, get it done quickly here so we get the stability we need for business and investment," Colorado Gov. Jared Polis, a Democrat, told reporters.
"I think he took that message back. He first defended their strategy ... but then he did understand that I was asking about making sure we get it done quickly."
The "stabbing" reference reflects Polis's fears that 25 per cent tariffs on goods exported to the U.S. from Canada and Mexico would invite retaliatory tariffs, raising prices and putting jobs at risk.
"Nobody should have to go without Kentucky bourbon," quipped that state's governor, Andy Beshear, a Democrat.
Views on Trump's tariff plan appear to be divided along party lines, however.
Two Republican governors interviewed by the Star said they back it as a way to attract more manufacturing.
"I support President Trump and what he's doing to get manufacturing, get jobs back to the U.S. and he's using this as a tool," said Oklahoma Gov. Kevin Stitt, vice-chair of the governors association.
"If there's a lever that we're using right now for negotiating purposes, we stand behind the president," he added, echoing comments from North Dakota Gov. Kelly Armstrong.
"We want to reset and make sure we don't turn into just a service industry country," the Republican said.
The meeting with Trump, cabinet secretaries and senior staff took place Friday morning as governors of U.S. states and territories gathered for the weekend at a downtown hotel to discuss bilateral issues of national importance and mutual concern.
Polis said tariffs came up in "many conversations" as governors in small groups rotated around tables with various members of the new administration.
With less than a week until Ontarians vote in Thursday's snap election, Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford attended the governors' convention to press his case against tariffs that would damage economies on both sides of the border and put millions of jobs at risk.
He was joined by Nova Scotia Premier Tim Houston, who said governors tend to lend a sympathetic ear and have "a lot of respect for the (Canada-U. S.) relationship."
"But, you know ... the president is the president."
Ford said being in tariff limbo is difficult, with Trump levies slated to take effect next month after being delayed at least once - and lingering questions as to whether that will happen again.
"We need certainty. A lot of companies are wavering back and forth," Ford said Friday before leaving for weekend campaign stops in Thunder Bay and elsewhere in northern Ontario.
"The president is a tough person to nail down, to say the least," added a frustrated Ford, who called an election that will cost $189 million 15 months ahead of the scheduled June 2026 vote.
He is attempting to frame the ballot question as who is best to deal with Trump tariffs, but been criticized for hypocrisy by NDP Leader Marit Stiles and Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie given he was recently caught in a hot mic gaffe acknowledging he "100 per cent" wanted Trump to win a second term despite his tariff threats.
In an example of the uncertainty caused by the tariff threats, auto giant Stellantis is "pausing" work on the next generation of Jeep Compass slated to be built at the company's Brampton plant beginning next fall. The plant employs 2,200.
Polis agreed with Ford that uncertainty over tariffs is already hurting business on both sides of the border.
"The premiers have a very receptive and supportive audience with most governors," he said.
"I hope that whatever we're doing here, we figure it out in a few months because it's a freeze on investment, whether its factories in Canada or factories in the United States," Polis added.
"If nobody knows what the trade regime will look like, it delays business decision-making."
Polis stressed a renewed free trade agreement between the U.S., Canada and Mexico is "absolutely crucial with the geopolitical competition we face with China and across the world."
"We're excited to work more closely with our Canadian partners and I've certainly advocated on that very issue to the administration."
Like other governors, including Republicans, Polis said reading Trump's intentions is challenging.
"I don't think any of us really know what's in the president's mind here. If this results in a place with lower tariffs and lower trade barriers, I'll be the first to compliment that," he continued.
"If it leads to additional tariffs, I will be the first to criticize that, because that will hurt the American people. It'll hurt manufacturing, it'll hurt exports, and it'll hurt consumers."
Ford was making his second lobbying trip to Washington in two weeks after leading premiers from across Canada to the American capital earlier in the month in his role as head of the Council of the Federation.
They met with officials, business leaders and managed to get a meeting in the White House, but with less influential aides than Miller and not with Trump.
Other premiers chose not to join Ford and Houston at the governor's meeting, held a 10-minute walk away from the White House where two men from California protested Trump's tariffs and a host of other controversial changes.
"I'm sorry for Canada, because Canada has done nothing except be a loyal ally to the United States," said Greg Nachtwey, a litigation consultant from Berkeley.
"I don't know why we are attacking our allies and supporting our enemies," added Dave Fogarty, a retiree, referring to Trump blaming Ukraine for being invaded by Russia.
"It just makes no sense at all."
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Subject: Political campaigns; Manufacturing; Governors; Tariffs; International trade; Economic impact; Tariff agreements; Foreign policy

Document 71 of 500

Will Ford squander his chance at greatness?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/will-ford-squander-his-chance-at-greatness/docview/3169737963/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Will+Ford+squander+his+chance+at+greatness%3F&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Typically, calling an early election that appears to be both unnecessary and opportunist does not go well for an incumbent. Both David Peterson and Justin Trudeau were ahead in the polls when they voluntarily shortened their terms: Peterson went down to outright defeat in 1990 while Trudeau returned a status-quo Parliament that looked pretty much like it did before he called the election in 2021.
So far, it appears that Doug Ford and his Progressive Conservatives are about to avoid this fate.
Most polls continue to report a double-digit lead, although with some tightening in the race between the PCs and Liberals, paralleled by a modest deterioration in NDP support. But these changes all appear to be small, predictable and unrelated to events in the campaign itself.
To add to this inertia, between the snow and the lack of enthusiasm for any of the available options, the odds are that voter turnout could also reach an all-time low as well.
So, unless something earth-shattering happens over the next week, it looks like we are in the midst of an election about nothing, where nothing happened.
Ford should win his third - and perhaps largest - majority government. The NDP will lose their official Opposition status to the Liberals, who will probably pick up seats, predominately in Toronto. And if both of these patterns occur, Crombie will most likely win her riding in Mississauga and take her seat in the legislature, while Stiles will lose hers and exit Queen's Park.
But just because nothing much happened during the campaign itself does not mean this will hold true once it is over.
What about the "mandate" Ford sought to protect Ontario's interests against the threat of American tariffs by ensuring his term will extend over the full length of U.S. President Donald Trump's?
During the campaign, this focus was clearly opportunistic and clever, because it allowed him both to run against a more unpopular opponent, who wasn't even on the ballot (Trump), and at the same time, play into his brand strength as a "straight talking, no-nonsense tough guy."
It also was extremely cynical and performative.
You don't need to be a constitutional scholar to know that a provincial premier has as much authority and jurisdiction to make a meaningful difference in international affairs as the prime minister has to deliver health-care services in Keswick.
So, unless he enjoys barking at the moon, it's unlikely that Ford's next term will be consumed with dialogue and negotiations with Trump.
That said, there is little question that between his regular appearances on U.S. cable news, his electoral focus on a major threat to the nation and chairing the Council of the Federation, he has been able to elevate his stature as the senior-most premier in the country.
Might he use this new status to play a larger role in federal-provincial relations and assume a "unifier" role in Canadian federalism; one who brokers differences between the provinces and Ottawa and even the provinces themselves, in the same tradition as Leslie Frost and Bill Davis?
Its hard to tell. Ford is a very different kind of politician, and his behaviour is very difficult to predict. For all his elbows-up, partisan bluster, he put aside what should have been an antagonistic relationship and showed a surprising willingness to compromise and co-operate with Trudeau during the pandemic. In the same way, once she was elected, he laid down his combative gloves and was able to strike a fairly civil and constructive relationship with Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow.
So maybe.
But then again, he just might revert to the old bombastic ways of his first six-and-a-half years in office and advocate for idiosyncratic measures which signal to his broad coalition that only he understands and can protect their interests.
If this comes to pass, we can probably expect four more years of one-off initiatives: those that make it easier for commuters to drive from the suburbs to their desired destination or for city dwellers to avoid playing roller derby with bicyclists; developer-friendly incursions into green spaces to accommodate more urban sprawl; tax relief and the relaxation of regulatory measures that appeal to small business owners and proprietors; edicts to municipal governments that make it more difficult to increase urban density, to the benefit of the single-family homeowner; and grandiose, pipe-dream schemes (like building a 60-kilometre tunnel under the 401). So, initiatives that represent magical thinking more than true vision.
This Ford Redux might happen ... but I don't think so.
What is different now, compared to his first two terms, is the question of Ford's legacy.
No matter what you might think of him, if he forms a third majority government, Doug Ford will become the most successful provincial politician in Ontario history.
And for a guy from a family who has been sold short and made the brunt of Toronto elite jokes all his life, this just might be a big deal.
In his next term, there will be no real need to pander to his base (largely at the expense of his distractors), so maybe Ford will see the next four years as his opportunity not just to be Ontario' s most successful premier, but Ontario's greatest premier.
Maybe he will recruit and assemble a team of experts who will revamp professional credentialization and recruit the physicians, nurses and heath-care workers necessary to revitalize a system badly in need of repair. Once resourced, he might then turn his attention to real innovation in structural change and adopt a plan like Jane Philpott has proposed where all Ontarians will have automatic access to community-based facilities, staffed by multidisciplinary health-care professionals.
Next, he could task Ontario's 15 research universities to convene with the Ontario Board of Trade and Chamber of Commerce to craft a blueprint to prioritize the province's economic strengths and weaknesses in order to revitalize our industrial base so we could compete more effectively in the global marketplace.
He might then reach out to urban planners and Ontario's mayors and challenge them to design a world-class, mixed transportation system for our cities that is sustainable and practical and designed both to alleviate congestion and facilitate mobility.
While they were still in the same room, he could bring in the construction industry to devise a plan to reduce development charges, rejig density requirements and build more modular housing. With these in hand, he could then go to the federal government and challenge them to find ways to tap capital markets to fund those plans.
And finally, to give Ontario a central role in the world's new artificial intelligence economy and to make sure that Indigenous Peoples in the province receive their rightful share of the riches that would flow from that place, he could sit down with Ontario' s First Nations, mining and transportation leaders and announce his intention to become the world leader in critical minerals by providing the infrastructure and leadership to develop the Ring of Fire.
Now that would be some mandate.
So, what will we get - Doug Ford the unifying force in Canadian federalism; the idiosyncratic appeaser; or the legacy seeker?
It's hard to know.
But because this has been an election about nothing, where nothing happened, should he win a third majority, the one thing we do know is that there are no guardrails and Doug Ford's mandate for the next four years will be whatever he wants it be.
Whether a source of fear or optimism, this election will have left him unbridled and unleashed.
Allan Gregg is an opinion researcher, a principal at Earnscliffe Strategies and co-host of the "Red-Tory" podcast.
CREDIT: Allan Gregg Contributor Allan Gregg is an opinion researcher, a principal at Earnscliffe Strategies and co-host of the "Red-Tory" podcast.;

Subject: Federalism; Political campaigns; Federal provincial relations; Politics; Political leadership

Location: Ontario Canada; Canada

People: Ford, Doug

Title: Will Ford squander his chance at greatness?

Document 72 of 500

Stiles struggling to make her voice heard

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/stiles-struggling-make-her-voice-heard/docview/3169737960/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Stiles+struggling+to+make+her+voice+heard&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Meet Marit Stiles.
If you don't know much about her, then you know this much: Stiles remains a political unknown to many Ontarians.
Up close on the campaign trail, the NDP leader has a winning way - wooing voters and waxing eloquent with volunteers. But zoom out to the big picture, and her New Democratic Party is falling behind - at least according to the telltale public opinion polls.
Why the disconnect? Why can Stiles connect face-to-face on the ground, while her party is losing ground provincewide?
Welcome to the winter election of 2025, where the public is disengaged - or engaged only on issues that aren't vote-winners for the NDP: Donald Trump, for example, and the economy, where the Tories poll best.
Everyone is talking about Trump's musings, and voters are hearing a lot from Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford as he seeks re-election as premier. All of which makes it harder for Stiles to be seen and heard - and recognized.
Blame the provincial NDP for her predicament. Nobody could be found to run against her for the party leadership two years ago, which is why Stiles won by acclamation - but without the critical acclaim that raises a politician's profile.
Blame the federal party. Nationally, the NDP is falling fast in public opinion polls, draining crossover support from Ontario voters and volunteers in the home-stretch of the province's Feb. 27 election.
All that said - and unseen - you wouldn't know it from watching Stiles on the stump. She is indefatigable and unflappable despite the bad tidings in the polling.
Hopping aboard her campaign bus Thursday, I watch her reaching out to party loyalists who are fanning out to "get out the vote" - GOTV in the GTA. Stiles begins her day by casting her own ballot in an advance poll, then heads out to record a podcast - as politicians do in 2025.
Painted in the party's distinctive orange livery, the bus motors through Toronto traffic and delivers us to the Danforth. This is where the NDP is hoping to reclaim the Beaches-East York riding it lost to the rival Liberals in 2022.
Behind the wheel on this whistle-stop tour is Ewen Steele, the trusty driver for hire whom the NDP always turns to for election charters. Over the years, he's kept the campaign on an even keel while ferrying a busload of seemingly unbalanced journalists.
In the election of 2025, however, there are fewer media - and so no media buses laid on by any of the campaigns. Today I am the sole political journalist invited on board, tagging along for a ride on the inside.
Steele steers the bus past fresh snowbanks dotting the Danforth and I slip inside to watch local candidate Kate Dupuis warming up the troops in the winter chill. They are primed for the leader's grand entrance:
"When they come in, we just let out a big cheer for 'The next premier of Ontario,' " Dupuis instructs them.
"Oh! Here she comes!"
On cue, the crowd lets out a lusty cheer and chants, "Marit! Marit! Marit!"
Impressively, if implausibly, Stiles keeps a straight face as she is introduced to supporters yet again as "the next premier of Ontario." After a decade working for ACTRA, the Canadian actors' union, and many more years in politics, she understands performance art.
"Wow! This is awesome!" the leader gushes to the gaggle of supporters, beaming widely. "That momentum is what I'm feeling all across the province."
Rallying the troops, Stiles raises the volume. When she's on a roll, this happy warrior lapses into traces of the Newfoundland accent she left behind when moving to Ontario for university.
Turning more serious, she bemoans the betrayals and bungling of Ford's Tories over their past two terms in power - on the Greenbelt boondoggle, housing shortages and health-care underfunding. But it is all about affordability.
Not unlike Ford and Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, Stiles never stops talking about pocketbook politics. She rails against grocery price "gouging," prompting party workers to hoist their placards proclaiming, "Fight Rising Costs" (with "Marit Stiles for Premier" printed on the obverse).
The NDP's answer? A monthly grocery rebate for eligible Ontarians depending on income, estimated to cost more than $4 billion a year.
Stiles also vows to remove road tolls for anyone and everyone travelling on Highway 407, whether truckers or commuters - at a multibillion-dollar cost she won't disclose (the party opposes price signals for vehicles, no matter the cost to the environment or the treasury).
Another pocketbook pitch, price to be determined.
And then her grand finale: "I will be the premier that's on the side of every single person in this province."
But she can only be premier if the small crowd of party loyalists in this local campaign office can execute on her exhortation to "flip this seat to (NDP) orange ... here in Beaches-East York." Put another way, the NDP is coveting a seat now held by Liberal MPP Mary-Margaret McMahon, a high-profile former city councillor who would be hard to knock off.
Which means that Stiles is once again pitting New Democrats against Liberals in this riding, as in others where Ford's Progressive Conservatives hope to profit from vote-splitting among progressive voters.
Targeting Liberals is a recurring theme for the NDP leader. I watched her make a direct appeal for Liberal votes at her campaign launch three weeks ago, when she caricatured Crombie as a closet Conservative who would fit comfortably in Ford's cabinet.
Back then, she asked traditional Liberal supporters to choose New Democrats as the true progressives who could beat Ford's Tories this time. The problem with that pitch is that the record shows New Democrats couldn't make it happen in the past two elections when the stars were truly in alignment - voters had confined a tired Liberal party to the penalty box after 15 years in power.
Now, the Liberal brand is making a slow comeback in Ontario - notably after Justin Trudeau announced his intention to resign as prime minster - and the NDP is falling further behind. Even from the cocoon of a campaign bus, it's impossible to avoid the data points, because pollsters are connecting the dots.
Most surveys show Ford's Tories holding more than 40 per cent support, Crombie's Liberals closing in on 30 per cent, and Stiles's NDP struggling to stay above 20 per cent. Beyond party preference, however, Stiles is lagging in leader preference.
Awkwardly, the latest Abacus Data poll shows only 16 per cent of voters think she'd make the best premier, well behind Crombie at 23 per cent and Ford at 35 per cent.
In the latest Leger poll, Stiles is strangely an also-ran: fully 31 per cent of Ontarians said they "don't know/do not recognize the name" of the NDP leader (compared to just 13 per cent who had no recognition or opinion of Crombie, and four per cent for Ford).
Which means that after two years on the job - where Job One is making a name for yourself and your party as the legislature's official opposition leader - one in three voters don't know or don't recognize her work in the campaign home-stretch.
Possibly that's because the NDP caucus was distracted by its own internal battles over the Israel-Gaza conflict a year ago, with Stiles expelling pro-Palestinian MPP Sarah Jama for defying her (and then facing pushback from Toronto-St. Paul's MPP Jill Andrew, who publicly challenged the leader's decision on Jama).
Now, the Liberals have turned the NDP's traditional vote-borrowing strategy against them: Crombie is asking New Democrats to help her close the gap with Ford's Tories and to stop fighting each other.
Sitting in the back of her campaign bus after the rally, Stiles is all smiles until I ask about the Liberal strategy. No matter that it is an echo of the old NDP playbook, she is scathing.
"It sounds desperate and it sounds to me like they've given up," she says in our interview, quickly contrasting the Liberal strategy with her own seemingly clear-eyed objectives:
"Bonnie Crombie has quit the fight. I will not quit," Stiles tells me. "I'm not running to be the opposition leader. I'm running to be the premier and to form a government."
She ridicules the Liberal appeal as risible, arguing that only New Democrats are true to themselves - notwithstanding the internal contradictions that riddle all party platforms, including hers.
"The difference is this: I am speaking to people and their values," she muses. "I'm not just saying, 'Put aside your values, put aside what you believe in and just vote for me.' "
The problem with the pitch is that it's not catching, not yet. Voters can't align with your values if they can't connect with your campaign.
As I leave her whistle-stop tour - next stop Brampton - Stiles insists she's unstoppable: "This last week is absolutely critical - elections matter."
Elections do matter. But only if your message matters to voters before election day, now just days away.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles
Age: 55
Birthplace: St. John's,
Newfoundland and Labrador
Education: BA Carleton
University
Family: Married with two daughters
Occupation before politics: National director at ACTRA, the performers' union
Political career: A one-time NDP staffer at Queen's Park under former premier Bob Rae, she was a Toronto District School Board trustee and president of the federal New Democrats before becoming the MPP for Davenport in 2018. After long-time leader Andrea Horwath retired in 2022, Stiles was acclaimed as party chief in 2023.
Fun fact: Her parents had a small organic farm in rural Newfoundland where it was sometimes too cold in the barn for the goats so they would sleep in the house to keep warm.
CREDIT: Martin Regg Cohn OPINION

Document 73 of 500

No one cares about the Ontario election. Was that Ford's plan?: In a recurring feature, Susan Delacourt, a small-l liberal, and Matt Gurney, a small-c conservative, bring their different perspectives - and shared commitment to civil disagreement - to the political debates of the moment.

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/no-one-cares-about-ontario-election-was-that/docview/3169737926/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=No+one+cares+about+the+Ontario+election.+Was+that+Ford%27s+plan%3F%3A+In+a+recurring+feature%2C+Susan+Delacourt%2C+a+small-l+liberal%2C+and+Matt+Gurney%2C+a+small-c+conservative%2C+bring+their+different+perspectives+-+and+shared+commitment+to+civil+disagreement+-+to+the+political+debates+of+the+moment.&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: SD I don't know if you've noticed, Matt, but we are days away from an Ontario election. While it's dangerous to make predictions in politics these days, you want to take a guess at what we might see when all the votes are tallied Thursday night?
MG You know, I'll get to your question in a moment. But I have to share something first. Readers will have guessed how Susan and I do these. We message back and forth, clean up the text and then send it to the editors. Do you know what happened, Susan, between you sending the first message and me replying with this one? It's very funny.
SD Given how many things you do in a day, I'm not sure I'd even hazard a guess.
MG Ha. Well, I'll just tell you. I saw your message and decided I'd rather type on my computer than my phone. So I went to my office. As I was heading there, I checked the mail slot at the side of my house. And therein I found ... a $200 cheque from the government of Ontario! I'd been wondering where my pre-election loot was.
SD So does that have you feeling all warm about Doug Ford's chances in this election?
MG It has me slightly closer to a new PS5. My feelings about Ford's chances haven't changed much in a long time. He's cruising to a majority. Perhaps an even larger one than he has. I don't think any poll has suggested anything else in a long time, and the recent announcement by the Liberals that they're pivoting to focus on the NDP is, despite Bonnie's Crombie's valiant effort to deny the blindingly obvious, a sign that the opposition parties know it's a fight for second. What can I say? If I reincarnate, in my next life, I want opponents like the ones Doug has faced over the years.
SD Forgive me, but what's a PS5?
MG A PlayStation 5. I find the bribe cheques so odious and craven that I'm insisting on spending the money on something ridiculous and unnecessary. I'll make a charity donation of my own money, but I'm taking what Ford is sending me and wasting it on a frill.
SD It wasn't so long ago that Chrystia Freeland made a fiery exit from Justin Trudeau's cabinet decrying "costly political gimmicks," and I don't get the sense she's been rewarded for that statement of principle. But that's a story for another day - another contest, also creeping up quickly on us.
Based on what is mainly anecdotal evidence, I do think that at least some Ontario voters are not keen to reward Ford for calling this election. Donald Trump also took some steam out of Ford's Captain Canada campaign when he granted the 30-day reprieve on some tariffs. So I do think Ford has probably had to work harder than he expected in this election to justify it happening.
MG Honestly, I don't see any sign that Ford is working particularly hard. He's not hesitating to take long breaks from campaigning to jet down to Washington and accomplish absolutely squat while he's there. This has been one of the strangest elections I've ever observed or covered, to the extent that I'm covering it. I don't think I have heard anyone discussing it out there in the real world even once. I know it's a busy news cycle on a lot of fronts, but it seems to me like the opposition parties are trying and failing to get anyone to notice them, Ford is trying to play act as Captain Canada, and another PC majority looms.
SD I'll tell you why I don't like the trail this has blazed for the federal election. You're right that Ford has mailed a bit of it in, especially when it comes to giving detailed, properly costed platform proposals. He and his candidates haven't been all that great at participating in debates. I can see how all parties, but especially federal Conservatives, would see this as a new standard in what one can get away with when it comes to campaigning.
MG The last few Ontario elections have been like this, though. I'm not saying there haven't been important issues. The last election was in the immediate aftermath of the pandemic and the provincial government's total abandonment of Ottawa during the convoy. There were lots of important issues. But the electorate seems massively checked out. That's probably something we can't really explore the depths of in this format. But I think it's something we should be aware of and probably be concerned about. What happens if you throw an election and no one shows up?
SD It's a very good question. I'll be watching those turnout numbers this week. It was less than 44 per cent in the 2022 election - an all-time low. I would not be surprised if this one sets a new apathy record. I do think it's interesting how we're being told to expect a change election federally, while Ontario voters seem pretty content with more of the same. Perhaps all the chaos we're seeing south of the Canada-U.S. border is making Canadians a little more nervous about rampant change. But that's just a theory.
MG It could be. My own theory is that it's closer to "I don't have the spare energy for Ontario politics. Doug is fine." Whatever it is, seems to be working out as the PC leader intended. Thanks for the PS5.
SD If the race is for second place, as you suggested, I will also be watching for clues about how Ontarians are feeling about the Liberals and New Democrats, federally. Some of the latest polls suggest that the Liberals, especially under prospective leader Mark Carney, are benefiting from a collapse of the NDP's support. Should the Ontario NDP place third on Thursday, there will be speculation about whether the federal NDP will be in any hurry to have a snap election anytime soon.
MG Yeah, there'll obviously be some lessons to glean from the provincial result that'll bear on the federal race. Frankly, if the provincial NDP totally tanks and federal polls keep showing Jagmeet Singh heading in the same direction, whether or not we have an election is probably going to stop being anything the federal NDP have a say in. If you're Carney, why wouldn't you pull the trigger?
SD A spring filled with snap elections - just the thing for a nervous nation. It gives us lots to write about, though.
Susan Delacourt is an Ottawa-based columnist for the Star, where she has been covering politics on Parliament Hill since the late 1980s. A political science graduate of Western University, she has written four political books; her latest, "Shopping for Votes," was a finalist for the 2014 Hilary Weston Writers' Trust prize in non-fiction. Susan is also a regular commentator on CBC and CTV, and has taught journalism and political communication at Carleton University. Matt Gurney is a Toronto-based journalist, broadcaster and freelance contributing columnist for the Star. He is co-editor of "The Line," an online magazine, which publishes at readtheLine.ca. He can be reached at matt@readtheline.ca
CAPTION: Ben Curtis The Associated Press

Document 74 of 500

On the campaign trail with Marit Stiles, enthusiasm is high - but recognition is low. Here's why the NDP leader is struggling to be heard

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/on-campaign-trail-with-marit-stiles-enthusiasm-is/docview/3169866593/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=On+the+campaign+trail+with+Marit+Stiles%2C+enthusiasm+is+high+-+but+recognition+is+low.+Here%27s+why+the+NDP+leader+is+struggling+to+be+heard&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Meet Marit Stiles.
If you don't know much about her, then you know this much: Stiles remains a political unknown to many Ontarians.
Up close on the campaign trail, the NDP leader has a winning way - wooing voters and waxing eloquent with volunteers.
But zoom out to the big picture, and her New Democratic Party is falling behind - at least according to the telltale public opinion polls.
Why the disconnect? Why can Stiles connect face-to-face on the ground, while her party is losing ground province-wide?
Welcome to the winter election of 2025, where the public is disengaged - or engaged only on issues that aren't vote-winners for the NDP: Donald Trump, for example, and the economy, where the Tories poll best.
Everyone is talking about Trump's presidential musings, and voters are hearing a lot from Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford as he seeks re-election as premier. All of which makes it harder for Stiles to be seen and heard - and recognized.
Blame the provincial New Democratic Party for her predicament. Nobody could be found to run against her for the party leadership two years ago, which is why Stiles won by acclamation - but without the critical acclaim that raises a politician's profile.
Blame the federal party. Nationally, the NDP is falling fast in public opinion polls, draining crossover support from Ontario voters and volunteers in the home-stretch of the province's Feb. 27 election.
All that said - and unseen - you wouldn't know it from watching Stiles on the stump. She is indefatigable and unflappable despite the bad tidings in the polling.
Hopping aboard her campaign bus Friday, I watch her reaching out to party loyalists who are fanning out to "get out the vote" - GOTV in the GTA. Stiles begins her day by casting her own ballot in an advance poll, then heads out to record a podcast - as politicians do in 2025.
Painted in the party's distinctive orange livery, the bus motors through Toronto traffic and delivers us to the Danforth. This is where the NDP is hoping to reclaim the Beaches-East York riding it lost to the rival Liberals in 2022.
Behind the wheel on this whistle-stop tour is Ewen Steele, the trusty driver for hire whom the NDP always turns to for election charters. Over the years, he's kept the campaign on an even keel while ferrying a busload of seemingly unbalanced journalists.
In the election of 2025, however, there are fewer media - and so no media buses laid on by any of the campaigns. Today I am the sole political journalist invited on board, tagging along for a ride on the inside.
Steele steers the bus past fresh snowbanks dotting the Danforth and I slip inside to watch local candidate Kate Dupuis warming up the troops in the winter chill. They are primed for the leader's grand entrance:
"When they come in, we just let out a big cheer for 'The next premier of Ontario,' " Dupuis instructs them.
"Oh! Here she comes!"
On cue, the crowd lets out a lusty cheer and chants, "Marit! Marit! Marit!"
Impressively, if implausibly, Stiles keeps a straight face as she is introduced to supporters yet again as "the next premier of Ontario." After a decade working for ACTRA, the Canadian actors' union, and many more years in politics, she understands performance art.
"Wow! This is awesome!" the leader gushes to the gaggle of supporters, beaming widely. "That momentum is what I'm feeling all across the province."
Rallying the troops, Stiles raises the volume. When she's on a roll, this happy warrior lapses into traces of the Newfoundland accent she left behind when moving to Ontario for university.
Turning more serious, she bemoans the betrayals and bungling of Ford's Tories over their past two terms in power - on the Greenbelt boondoggle, housing shortages and health care underfunding. But it is all about affordability.
Not unlike Ford and Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, Stiles never stops talking about pocketbook politics. She rails against grocery price "gouging," prompting party workers to hoist their placards proclaiming, "Fight Rising Costs" (with "Marit Stiles for Premier" printed on the obverse).
The NDP's answer? A monthly grocery rebate for eligible Ontarians depending on income, estimated to cost more than $4 billion a year.
Stiles also vows to remove road tolls for anyone and everyone travelling on Highway 407, whether truckers or commuters - at a multibillion-dollar cost she won't disclose (the party opposes price signals for vehicles, no matter the cost to the environment or the treasury).
Another pocketbook pitch, price to be determined.
And then her grand finale: "I will be the premier that's on the side of every single person in this province."
But she can only be premier if the small crowd of party loyalists in this local campaign office can execute on her exhortation to "flip this seat to (NDP) orange ... here in Beaches-East York." Put another way, the NDP is coveting a seat now held by Liberal MPP Mary-Margaret McMahon, a high profile former city councillor who would be hard to knock off.
Which means that Stiles is once again pitting New Democrats against Liberals in this riding, as in others where Ford's Progressive Conservatives hope to profit from vote-splitting among progressive voters.
Targeting Liberals is a recurring theme for the NDP leader. I watched her make a direct appeal for Liberal votes at her campaign launch three weeks ago, when she caricatured Crombie as a closet Conservative who would fit comfortably in Ford's cabinet.
Back then, she asked traditional Liberal supporters to choose New Democrats as the true progressives who could beat Ford's Tories this time. The problem with that pitch is that the record shows New Democrats couldn't make it happen in the last two elections when the stars were truly in alignment - voters had confined a tired Liberal party to the penalty box after 15 years in power.
Now, the Liberal brand is making a slow comeback in Ontario - notably after Justin Trudeau announced his intention to resign as prime minster - and the NDP is falling further behind. Even from the cocoon of a campaign bus, it's impossible to avoid the data points, because pollsters are connecting the dots.
Most surveys show Ford's Tories holding more than 40 per cent support, Crombie's Liberals closing in on 30 per cent, and Stiles's NDP struggling to stay above 20 per cent. Beyond party preference, however, Stiles is lagging in leader preference.
Awkwardly, the latest Abacus Data poll shows only 16 per cent of voters think she'd make the best premier, well behind Crombie at 23 per cent and Ford at 35 per cent.
In the latest Leger poll, Stiles is strangely an also-ran: fully 31 per cent of Ontarians said they "don't know/do not recognize the name" of the NDP leader (compared to just 13 per cent who had no recognition or opinion of Crombie, and 4 per cent for Ford).
Which means that after two years on the job - where Job One is making a name for yourself and your party as the legislature's official opposition leader - one in three voters don't know or don't recognize her work in the campaign home-stretch. Possibly that's because the NDP caucus was distracted by its own internal battles over the Israel-Gaza conflict a year ago, with Stiles expelling pro-Palestinian MPP Sarah Jama for defying her (and then facing pushback from Toronto-St. Paul's MPP Jill Andrew, who publicly challenged the leader's decision on Jama).
Now, the Liberals have turned the NDP's traditional vote-borrowing strategy against them: Crombie is asking New Democrats to help her close the gap with Ford's Tories and to stop fighting each other.
Sitting in the back of her campaign bus after the rally, Stiles is all smiles until I ask about the Liberal strategy. No matter that it is an echo of the old NDP playbook, she is scathing.
"It sounds desperate and it sounds to me like they've given up," she says in our interview, quickly contrasting the Liberal strategy with her own seemingly clear-eyed objectives:
"Bonnie Crombie has quit the fight. I will not quit," Stiles tells me. "I'm not running to be the opposition leader. I'm running to be the premier and to form a government."
She ridicules the Liberal appeal as risible, arguing that only New Democrats are true to themselves - notwithstanding the internal contradictions that riddle all party platforms, including hers.
"The difference is this: I am speaking to people and their values," she muses. "I'm not just saying, 'Put aside your values, put aside what you believe in and just vote for me.' "
The problem with the pitch is that it's not catching, not yet. Voters can't align with your values if they can't connect with your campaign.
As I leave her whistle-stop tour - next stop Brampton - Stiles insists she's unstoppable: "This last week is absolutely critical - elections matter."
Elections do matter. But only if your message matters to voters before election day, now just days away.

Document 75 of 500

A drop in public opinion polls has spooked Pierre Poilievre. Is his best strategy still Justin Trudeau?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/drop-public-opinion-polls-has-spooked-pierre/docview/3169866311/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+drop+in+public+opinion+polls+has+spooked+Pierre+Poilievre.+Is+his+best+strategy+still+Justin+Trudeau%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Are the federal Conservatives flailing?In the span of a week, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre has gone from his previous description of Canada as a country where "everything is broken" to basking in a sea of red and white, and an election-style speech asking supporters, staff and MPs: "Who's ready to put Canada First"? Days later, however, Poilievre was tacking further to the right. He held a press conference in Toronto Thursday in which he offered no comment about U.S. President Donald Trump's sellout of Ukraine (and he wasn't asked). He doubled down on the "broken" and the "radical woke" culture-war narrative that poisoned politics in the United States; peppered legitimate criticisms about his potential Liberal challenger with gross exaggerations, and took questions from only pre-approved media outlets, such as Rebel News and True North, and none from the mainstream English press.The narrowing of public opinion polls - an enormous reversal of an 18-month trend that had Conservatives leading by more than 20 per cent to now single digits - have Conservatives spooked. Several polls suggest that former Bank of Canada governor and current Liberal leadership front-runner Mark Carney would be a threat to Poilievre's efforts to become prime minister. A Carney-led Liberal party, according to the latest Leger poll, would be statistically tied in public opinion, and lead in Ontario, Atlantic Canada and Quebec which would, hypothetically, translate into a Liberal minority government.It's no wonder Poilievre is so focused on Carney. On Thursday alone, he mentioned Carney's name 48 times during his 27-minute press conference. But what's happened over the past few days is the public expression of the pulling and pushing of differing poles within the Conservative tent. On the one hand, there is the desire to respond to the threat of the moment: to Trump, his 51st-state musings, and his menacing tariffs. That was the Poilievre on display on Saturday, Feb. 15. Showing off his pride, Poilievre quoted from Conservative prime minister John A. Macdonald's famous speech in the Commons where he lobbied for more funding for the Pacific railway, a nation-building project that would tie Halifax to the Pacific Ocean. "Conservatives will fight ... to give us a great, a united, a rich, an improving, a developing Canada instead of making us a tributary to American laws, American railways, American bondage, and American tolls," Poilievre said, to the cheering crowd. (Nevermind that the Conservatives currently don't seem fond of investing billions of dollars into a newly-announced high speed rail project that would tie Quebec City to Toronto.) Poilievre noted last Saturday that "sometimes it does take a threat to remind us what we have, what we could lose." He spoke of building the country up, and noted policies he has long championed - some of which recall Trump's own promises - more oil and gas development, lower taxes, scrapping environmental assessment rules and slashing spending on bureaucrats, consultants and foreign aid.But on Trump, Poilievre was specific. He called on Canada to respond to the threat with "strength.""If they put tariffs on our steel and aluminum, I will put tariffs on their steel and aluminum. If they hit us with generalized tariffs, we will respond dollar for dollar," he said, echoing the position of other Liberal leadership candidates.A trade war, Poilievre predicted, would weaken both the U.S. and Canada. But then, he offered another way out of the (Trump-made) crisis: closer integration with Canada's southern neighbour. "Option two," Poilievre said, "we trade even more. And both our workers make more. Our consumers pay less. The resulting GDP boost allows Canada to further strengthen its border. And up our military spending to two per cent of GDP faster," he said, to large cheers from the crowd.That option did not find a fan in former Conservative prime minister Stephen Harper. Writing in the Globe and Mail this week, Harper said, "our focus now should not be on pursuing a deeper economic and security partnership. At this stage, whatever comes next from the President, Canada must avoid further dependency on the United States."But while Harper was telling Poilievre that he needed to adopt a different tact to respond to the American economic threat, the Conservative leader appears to have concluded he cannot win on a ballot question that involves negotiating with Trump.Instead, Poilievre pivoted back to his old strategy, ensuring the next election is fought on Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's record. His message was retooled for the moment. "Imagine if they (the Liberals) did this much damage before the tariffs. How dangerous they would be after tariffs?"That strategy has already been so successful it led to Trudeau's resignation. If he needs to pretend that Carney was Trudeau's puppet master over the past five years, he will.Tying NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh to the unpopular Trudeau government sunk that party's poll numbers. Now, the Conservatives hope it will demolish Carney, too.
CAPTION: Conservative Party Leader Pierre Poilievre speaks in Ottawa at the Canada First rally on Feb. 15.
CREDIT: Althia Raj
