Document 85 of 500

In a province as wealthy as Ontario, why are we sending our kids to schools that are falling apart?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/province-as-wealthy-ontario-why-are-we-sending/docview/3169366270/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=In+a+province+as+wealthy+as+Ontario%2C+why+are+we+sending+our+kids+to+schools+that+are+falling+apart%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With tariffs and the economy taking centre stage, education issues have seemingly taken a backseat in the current election campaign despite being one of the provincial government's most important responsibilities. Including pension costs, Ontario is projected to spend just under $40 billion on K-12 education in the current fiscal year. And yet there are several challenges that continue to plague our schools.
One of the big problems is the backlog of school repairs and renewals. A recent report from the Financial Accountability Office states that 37 per cent of schools are below a state of good repair. In the TDSB, nearly all schools (84 per cent) are in substandard condition. The FAO projects it will cost over $31 billion over 10 years to ensure that we have enough classrooms in growing parts of the province and that all schools in Ontario are in a state of good repair. In a province as wealthy as Ontario, we shouldn't have to send our kids to schools that are falling apart.
Another big challenge is the ongoing teacher shortage, which the Ontario Ministry of Education projects will get worse through 2027. According to the Ontario College of Teachers, whereas about 25 per cent of early-career teachers were unemployed in 2014, the amount of unemployment is now at "statistically negligible levels." This has led to lowering the standards of who we let into the classroom. You can only have standards to the degree that you have choices. And right now, schools don't have many choices. In some cases, school boards are employing emergency replacement teachers who have no training whatsoever. Ensuring that all classrooms have a competent, well-trained teacher at the helm should be a baseline expectation for any provincial government.
Progressive ConservativesWhile not making any significant new education promises, the PCs are running on their record since coming to power in 2018. Despite fears to the contrary, they have continued to invest record sums in Ontario's public schools. They have also made several significant reforms that continue to shape the system.
Perhaps the most consequential of these reforms was the elimination of academic streaming in Grade 9. Streaming is the practice of dividing students into a de facto hierarchy of classes based on perceived ability or interest. Announced in 2020, former education minister Stephen Lecce called streaming a "systemic, racist, discriminatory" practice. Research has long demonstrated that this practice leads to social marginalization, lower academic achievement and reduced educational opportunities for students placed in lower levels, who are disproportionately Black. While streaming in Grade 9 may have officially ended, many schools have struggled to implement the change effectively. A 2023 survey from People for Education found that only 20 per cent of Ontario high school principals reported having enough resources to support de-streaming for Grade 9 students. Principals reported needing additional educational assistants, increased teacher training, and lower class sizes in order for it to be implemented effectively. Without these additional supports, we run the risk of repeating what happened in the early 1990s when Ontario previously de-streamed Grade 9, only to reintroduce it five years later after ineffective implementation.
The PCs also introduced a "back to basics" agenda, which has directed school boards to focus on reading, writing and STEM education. This approach has been paired with a scaling back of the equity and social justice work that the PCs purported to care about just a few years ago. Premier Doug Ford has repeatedly accused schools of "indoctrinating" kids, and research by myself and colleagues has found that his government is now undermining the equity work taking place in school districts across the province.
A PC campaign spokesperson notes that they have doubled the amount of funding to build schools since they took office. As to why school facilities continue to be in such bad shape, both former education minister Stephen Lecce and current minister Jill Dunlop have repeatedly accused school boards of failing to effectively use the maintenance and capital funds they have already been allocated.
A re-elected PC government will likely continue down the current path of continued investment in public education, a "back to basics" instructional focus, and a scaling back of social justice work in schools.
NDP "Mold in schools, crumbling ceilings, freezing classrooms ... our kids shouldn't be in schools with those issues in Ontario in 2025," Chandra Pasma, the NDP's education critic, told me. To address these issues, the NDP has pledged to spend an extra $830 million per year, which it says will eliminate the repair backlog within 10 years.
To address the teacher shortage, the NDP has a multifaceted plan. The first is to incentivize the tens of thousands of people who are currently certified by the Ontario College of Teachers but are not actively teaching. A big part of the reason for this is the increased behavioural challenges faced by teachers in the classroom, particularly after the pandemic. Working with Ontario's education unions, the NDP put together a multifaceted Proposal for an Emergency Safe Schools Plan that includes increased training, consistent and uniform reporting of school violence, as well as the hiring of additional mental health professionals, educational assistants and other education workers.
The most bold and effective strategy put forth by Pasma to address the teacher shortage is to bring the length of Ontario's teacher education programs back down to one year instead of the current two years. As Pasma notes, "It was bumped up to two years when we had a surplus of teachers. It was never a pedagogical reason. So now, when we have a shortage. Why are we maintaining it at two years?"
The NDP has also promised to end the practice of streaming entirely. However, it is unclear how this would be achieved as we have not yet effectively implemented the de-streaming of Grade 9. As Pasma notes, "we didn't put the resources behind it for it to succeed ... de-streaming without support is still exclusion."
An NDP government would have the most comprehensive education plan to address Ontario's pressing education issues. However, the party has yet to detail how it will pay for these promises and its plan to reduce teacher education programs to one year would further strain the precarious finances of Ontario's universities.
Green PartyThe Green Party has direct experience informing its education plan. Matt Richter, the party's education critic, has been a teacher with the Trillium Lakelands District School Board for over two decades. Richter told me he has seen first-hand how teaching and learning conditions have deteriorated under both Liberal and PC governments. To remedy this, the Green Party has committed to capping class sizes at 24 students from Grades four to eight, and at 26 students in kindergarten. To meet the staffing requirements for this pledge and to address the teacher shortage, Richter says we need to increase enrollments in Ontario's teacher education programs and give school boards more money to hire full-time teachers. The Green Party also pledges to increase education funding by $1,500 per student, devote more money to clearing the backlog in school repairs, and provide more funding for school boards to hire additional support staff to deal with the growing issue of school violence. The party estimates that these reforms would cost an additional $2 to $3 billion per year. To pay for these reforms, Richter points to the $3 billion the current government spent on "bribe checks" as an example of money that could be reallocated. Another element of the Green platform is to eliminate EQAO testing, something that was being explored in the latter stages of the previous Wynne government. While this would not result in significant cost savings, it would have a dramatic impact on the pedagogy in our classrooms, particularly at the elementary level where a lot of time is spent on test preparation. Given that EQAO is supposed to assess the performance of the entire system, a better alternative than testing every single student each year would be to test a randomly sampled subset of Ontario students once every three years.
The education agenda of a Green Party government is the most transformative and the costliest. Reducing elementary class sizes and eliminating EQAO testing would be cheered by most teachers but might have unintended consequences in terms of reducing hiring standards even further and removing one of the few pressures in our school system to maintain academic rigour.
Liberal PartyThe Liberal Party has yet to release an education platform, although press secretary Bahoz Dara Aziz assured me that one will be unveiled soon. According to Dara Aziz, Liberal leader Bonnie Crombie promises to "fix the schools, hire more teachers and get teachers the tools they need." Although in the absence of a platform it is difficult to know what this would mean, we can look at what individual candidates have pledged. On their campaign websites, Scarborough-Guildwood candidate Andrew Hazell, Oxford candidate Bernia Martin, and Mississauga-Streetsville candidate Jill Promoli have all promised to reduce class sizes, although in which grades and by how much is unstated. Also unclear is where the additional teachers required to reduce class sizes would come from. It is worth noting that the slate of Liberal candidates contains several current and former school board trustees, including former chairs of the Hamilton-Wentworth District School Board and the Halton District School Board. Thus, if elected to office, the Liberal Party would have an experienced caucus from which to put together a comprehensive education plan.
CAPTION: Education is one of the pillars Ontario voters will be looking at when it comes to casting their ballots.
CREDIT: Sachin Maharaj Contributor

Subject: Teaching; Social justice; Students; Classrooms; Funding; Elementary schools; School principals; Teacher education; Teacher shortages; Class size; School boards; School violence; Cost control; Elections; School districts

Quiz answers

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/quiz-answers/docview/3169364551/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Quiz+answers&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=OP.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: 1. c) Did you know that in Canada, foreign policy is the responsibility of the federal government? And that in Toronto, roads and traffic are the responsibility of the municipal government? So who's to blame for our overcrowded classrooms and hallway health care? Look for the gentleman raging against bike lanes in the "Canada Is Not For Sale" toque.
2. d) What better time to sneak in a bit of democracy than in the midst of a tariff war, winter weather advisory, federal Liberal leadership race and dismantling of the U.S. government? "I think that was what Doug Ford was gambling on when he called an early vote," Prof. Lydia Miljan, head of political science at the University of Windsor, told the Star.
3. d) Every other party has signalled their unflinching support to spend as much borrowed money as needed to support industries impacted by U.S. tariffs. Still, the Progressive Conservative Leader claims he needs an even larger majority to deal with the economic impacts of a trade war.
4. c) In what a psychoanalyst might read as a deep-seated need for attention, Ford riffed on how much he'd love to send criminals to the electric chair. He blamed the federal Criminal Code for blunting his folksy thirst for vengeance, once again shifting the blame to another level of government.
5. a) It's almost as if Ford didn't connect Trump's oft-stated love of tariffs with the actual tariffs Trump is now threatening against Canada. Thankfully, and despite Ford's claims, there is no chance of the U.S. president sitting down with a sub-national leader from another country.
6. b) The Liberals have stayed on message with "Real Leaders Fix Healthcare," sometimes shockingly so: Their campaign bus features the grim message "11,000 people died waiting for surgery last year." They missed the opportunity to nickname the vehicle Ride Or Die.
7. d) As James Blair, Trump's deputy chief of staff, tweeted after meeting the provincial premiers, "we never agreed that Canada would not be the 51st state." Still, in a huge win for Ontario diplomacy, he characterized the meeting as "pleasant."
8. b) "I'm happy to challenge him any time on his turf or on mine," Crombie said. "My turf's the gym, so if he wants to come at me and we'll have a competition in the gym and maybe we'll do pushups. What do you think?" To date, Ford has not entertained the offer.
If your quiz performance were recognized by Elections Ontario, where would you be on Feb. 27?
0-3: Backbencher
You're not paying too much attention, but when push comes to shove, you'd rather be pushed by Doug Ford than shoved by Donald Trump.
4-6: Cabinet minister
Not bad, but the real question is this: Will we ever address the long-term structural problems in this province, or do we just lurch from one hot-button issue to another?
7-8: Premier
Look at you! Smart enough to run Canada's most populous province, a prominent position that, if adroitly positioned on a slow news day, might just get you a spot to make your case on Fox News.
CREDIT: ;

Macedonian community gala raises $825,000 for East York seniors home

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/macedonian-community-gala-raises-825-000-east/docview/3169364511/se-2?accountid=15090

Document 90 of 500

Millions of Ontarians don't have doctors. If our next Premier doesn't fix this problem it will get much worse

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/millions-ontarians-dont-have-doctors-if-our-next/docview/3169364228/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Millions+of+Ontarians+don%27t+have+doctors.+If+our+next+Premier+doesn%27t+fix+this+problem+it+will+get+much+worse&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Retired family physician Marlene Spruyt, the Green Party candidate for Lanark-Frontenac-Kingston, remembers a time when she called up a home care nurse for a dying patient. "We delivered palliative care to a woman who was blind, and she got to stay at home and die," she says. "Now, you can't deliver that quality of care."
For the 2.5 million Ontarians without a family doctor (up from 1.8 million in 2020), Spruyt's memory sounds like myth. But that level of care could return, if only leaders would summon up the political will.
Ontarians are now being asked to choose a new leader. Progressive Conservative leader Doug Ford called a snap election for February 27 in response to the threat of U.S. tariffs 16 months before an election was due. But according to a recent Nanos survey, nearly 30 per cent of voters say healthcare concerns will dictate how they cast their ballots.
The Nanos poll shows 28 per cent of voters favour the Liberals on health care while 26 per cent support Ford. The NDP trails at 21 per cent.
Perhaps most damning of all is the Nanos finding that one in five Ontarians don't trust any party on health care.
Ontarians know just how precarious the health system is. For two decades, governments of all stripes have failed to address the demands of an exploding, aging population.
The Ontario Medical Association predicts that by 2026 - that's next year - 4.4 million people won't have a family doctor. The OMA says Ontario needs 3,500 new family doctors now.
It gets worse. Close to 40 per cent of family doctors are nearing retirement. Poor pay combined with runaway inflation make running the business of family practice financially unsustainable. The falling number of medical school seats per capita means a shortage of doctors coming down the pipeline. And that's just the family doctor crisis.
The province also needs to fund new hospitals, maintain existing ones, and pay for seniors' long-term care.
Addressing health care is a gargantuan task, but Ford's majority government has had seven years to fix the mess. Last year, the Ministry of Health infuriated doctors by stating that the recruitment and retention of Ontario physicians was "not a major concern."
In 2024, the federal government transferred $3.1 billion to Ontario for health care. Last fall, Ford recruited former federal Liberal Health Minister Jane Philpott to implement her Primary Care Action Plan.
But this anemic, late-in-the-day proposal calls for just $1.8 billion to connect two million Ontarians to 305 vaguely defined primary care teams in a school-board type model based on residential postal codes.
Her plan emphasizes workforce retention and easing administrative burden but is sketchy on financial details. It also adds 500 new medical school seats by 2028.
The Greens, who boast only two MPPs, present a bolder platform. They say they'll recruit 3,500 family doctors. According to Marlene Spruyt, this plan represents a stark contrast from the PCs.
Spruyt cites the various ways Ford has failed to shore up health care, including lost savings from breaking a contract with The Beer Store. For a guy who says he doesn't drink, she calls his beer fixation "bizarre."
"What has Doug Ford done right?" she asks.
Spruyt says municipalities shouldn't have to fund health care. She wants the government to pay half the cost to build hospitals in towns like Bracebridge, where rich donors are scarce. Nobody asks citizens to chip in for a new police car, she adds.
For her part, NDP Leader Marit Stiles has pledged to spend $4 billion over four years to bring in 3,500 family doctors and 15,000 nurses to work in teams.
The party's health critic France Gelinas represents the northern riding of Nickel Belt, where 30,000 residents lack a family doctor.
She says the Ford government has knowingly allowed conditions to deteriorate, and adds that Ford's government listens to investors, while the NDP listens to front-line staff.
Gelinas says the NDP respects family physicians.
"They've been providing quality primary care for a very long time. They need to be paid fairly."
She's also frustrated that although Sudbury's Northern Ontario School of Medicine can increase its capacity from 56 to 100 undergraduate seats "right now," the government won't release the funding yet.
As for Bonnie Crombie's Liberals, they have also vowed to increase the number of physicians, promising to pair every Ontarian with a family doctor-led team of allied health professionals by spending $3.1 billion to "attract and retain 3,100 family doctors" by 2029. They will double medical school spots, entice 2,400 aging doctors to delay retirement and invest $250 million to recruit 650 doctors who've decamped elsewhere for better pay and less paperwork back to family practice.
Liberal Adil Shamji, the MPP for Don Valley East who, he says, is "the only elected physician" at Queen's Park says the government's track record speaks for itself, with lukewarm promises coming years too late. He says Ford found money for everything but health care, including $3.2 billion - which could pay for the Liberal health platform and then some - on $200 taxpayer rebates.
Shamji says Ontarians pay taxes for family doctors and worries that to cut costs the current government is diluting the definition of primary care by trying to "convince Ontarians that if they have a pharmacist who can prescribe for minor ailments, that counts as ... primary care, and it doesn't."
These juicy promises from political parties still trailing the incumbent in the polls sound like a Christmas wish list for patients and doctors alike.
Adam Stewart, who practices comprehensive family medicine in Madoc, a rural town halfway between Toronto and Ottawa, believes that money is the priority.
He sits on the board of the OMA's Section on General and Family Practice. Only 65 per cent of family doctors are left in comprehensive, longitudinal practices offering cradle to grave care. They're incurring "20, maybe 30 per cent increases" in overhead from post-pandemic inflation balanced against one per cent annual fee increases. Stewart says government needs to bump up family doctor compensation to be in line with BC and other provinces.
He also wants Ontario to recognize family doctors as specialists in looking after patients from birth to death. They're ready to assume higher patient loads by leading multi-professional teams.
Stewart also has some more unconventional recommendations. Put the joy back into medicine, he says, by providing doctors with AI technology for scribes. Implement a centralized referral system for rapid access to specialists and MRIs.
These reforms are crucial, says Stewart. "If things don't get fixed," he says, if they continue down the same sad trajectory, he might have to leave comprehensive practice, too.
Merrilee Fullerton, a retired family doctor and two-time Progressive Conservative MPP from Kanata-Carleton calls the Ford government's $1.8 billion offer "a hail Mary pass."
Fullerton says too often, governments laud shiny baubles, like AI, that are distractions from the fact that family doctors are the glue that holds the system together. She resigned her seat in 2023 because of "tremendous frustration" with the government's lackadaisical approach to healthcare. Fullerton wants policy makers, bureaucrats, and politicians to think ahead. She calls making hasty health care election promises, then forgetting them till the next election cycle "irresponsible".
In April 2005, faced with family doctor shortages, Liberal premier Dalton McGuinty announced new Family Health Teams to serve 2.5 million Ontarians.
But when family doctors tried to join those teams, McGuinty and his successor Kathleen Wynne froze the funding. It was too expensive. Some lucky Ontarians got team-based care, while millions more didn't and even fewer do now. McGuinty also imposed the Health Premium tax in 2004, a levy of up to $900 that Ontarians still pay annually.
There's nothing new here. Then, as now, Ottawa injected cash into Ontario's coffers. Then, as now, governments promised primary care teams to solve provincial health care woes. Will much-touted team funding be frozen again?
Not one Ontario political party in the past 20 years has demonstrated the grit needed to fix health care. The Progressive Conservatives still lead the Liberals by 15 percentage points. Barring an electoral miracle, Ford seems destined to be a three-time premier.
Patients are tired of not having family doctors to give their newborns their childhood shots, and are tired of waiting months to see the orthopedic surgeon while their knees crumble. Doctors are tired of working longer, harder, faster to keep their patients healthy with shrinking resources.
Adam Stewart thinks the solution is giving doctors more autonomy, and more responsibility. What he wants is for governments to free family doctors to do what they do best: looking after patients by leading their own teams. His solution might be the cure for our collective healthcare malaise.
CAPTION: Healthcare is top of mind for Ontarians this election. But one in five potential voters don't trust any party to improve the system.
CREDIT: Ferrukh Faruqui Contributor

Subject: Patients; Primary care; Polls & surveys; Retirement; Teams; Voters; Health care; Funding; Politics; Government; Physicians

Document 91 of 500

The choice on housing: Ontario is in the midst of a housing crisis. Which party has the best plan to fix it?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/choice-on-housing/docview/3169359866/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+choice+on+housing%3A+Ontario+is+in+the+midst+of+a+housing+crisis.+Which+party+has+the+best+plan+to+fix+it%3F&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=OP.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: By any reasonable metric, the Ford government deserves a failing grade for its handling of Ontario's housing crisis. This track record, however, does not appear to be harming the Progressive Conservatives in the polls, partly because other issues are more salient to voters, and partly because our crisis has gotten so large that young voters have come to believe they will never be able to afford a home, regardless of who is in power.
Rather than a single crisis, Ontario's housing system faces a set of interlocking crises that share some common causes. First, there is a lack of deeply affordable housing for lower-income Ontarians. Four decades of underinvestment in non-profit and co-op housing, coupled with stagnant wages and Ontario Works and ODSP shelter allowances that have not kept up with rent inflation, have priced too many people out of the market. Homelessness has grown by 25 per cent in Ontario since 2022, and food bank use has hit record levels.
Second is the rental crisis, though we have seen some relief in recent months, with rents on new leases falling 5 per cent in the last year. They remain, however, highly unaffordable to low- and middle-income workers.
Finally, there's the home ownership crisis. When Doug Ford first took office in June 2018, it took 282 weeks of median wages to finance a 20 per cent down payment and the first five years of mortgage payments on a single-family home in Ontario. Today, it takes 327 weeks. The comparable figures for Alberta and Nova Scotia are 203 and 156 weeks, respectively. When Ford was first elected, more families moved to Ontario from other provinces than the other way around. Last year, however, 25,000 more people moved out of Ontario to other provinces than moved in. Young Ontarians are increasingly seeking opportunities elsewhere.
The common cause of all three crises is population growth outpacing housing supply. Despite out-migration to other provinces, Ontario's population grew by 1.8 million over the past six years due to increased immigration targets and an international student boom, the latter resulting from colleges and universities massively increasing their international enrolments in response to the Ford government's domestic tuition freeze. In the previous six years, the province's population grew by less than 1 million. Ontario now has an estimated shortfall of 740,000 units. This figure understates the shortage, as the homes that have been built in Ontario in recent years have largely been one-bedroom condos and rental apartments, which naturally house fewer people than other forms of housing.
Ramping up homebuilding to keep up with such rapid levels of population growth was a near-impossible task, one made more difficult through a combination of municipal delays, continued underinvestment in social housing, skyrocketing development charges and other fees on new housing, zoning rules preventing the building of family-friendly gentle density and a lack of competition in the residential development land market, which left land prices prohibitively expensive.
The Ford government recognized many of these as problems and assembled a team of experts to draft the comprehensive Housing Affordability Task Force Report. The government also recognized that a growing population needs more housing, so it created a target to build 1.5 million homes and individual targets for 50 municipalities. These targets are proportional to the need created by accelerated population growth. However, the majority of the task force's recommendations have gone unheeded, and the lack of any requirement to adopt provincial targets in municipal planning has robbed those targets of their effectiveness.
Even when the Ford government has recognized a problem, its solutions have largely been ineffective. Ford has been right to point out that municipal red tape is an issue, but beyond the incentives that the government's Building Faster Fund created, little progress has been made. Ford did make large reforms to development charges - only to reverse those changes in response to opposition from municipalities. The government's decision to open up more land for development, including by taking land out of the Greenbelt, not only led to substantial backlash from Ontarians but also triggered an RCMP investigation.
In other housing policy areas, such as social housing, the Ford government has chosen not to act. In particular, it has fought against legalizing family-friendly density, calling attempts to allow for fourplexes as-of-right a "massive mistake." Earlier in this election campaign, Ford was endorsed onstage by Oakville Mayor Rob Burton. Burton praised Ford for not legalizing fourplexes and for not increasing height limits near transit stations. Ford has been consistent in allowing municipalities to set these rules for themselves, even though such rules amount to the kind of red tape Ford rails against.
The opposition parties are advocating for a different approach. The Liberals, New Democrats and Greens are all calling for increased legalization of family-friendly density. The NDP is also advocating for a return of the provincial government to the non-profit and co-op housing space, setting a target of 300,000 new permanently affordable rental homes. It's an ambitious target, though it's unclear how any government could achieve it, and the NDP has not provided an estimate as to what such a program might cost. Ontario has never built 30,000 such homes in any single year, let alone for 10 consecutive years. Even if the province could keep the cost of each home under $350,000, that would still entail more than $100 billion in spending over a decade. Given the increasing cost pressures our health-care system is under, such high levels of spending seem unattainable.
Both the Liberals and the Greens are advocating for eliminating development charges on most forms of housing, which would substantially help to bring down purchase prices. However, neither party has indicated how it would ensure that municipalities would be "made whole" and still have the funds needed to pay for infrastructure. Development charge reform is badly needed, but any replacement model must be fiscally sustainable.
Unfortunately, no party is offering substantial reforms to the availability of residential development land, which is unsurprising, given how contentious an issue it is with the public. However, given that land is often the single-largest expense in building a home, any housing affordability agenda is incomplete without a plan to lower the cost of development land. There is a belief that making available just enough land for development is sufficient; however, this ensures a lack of competition in the land market and creates the conditions for monopolization. Instead, we need to ensure that enough development land is available to create real competition in the land market. Adding residential development land does not require opening up the Greenbelt, nor should we open up land in areas prone to flooding or fires, as the most expensive home is the one that you need to build twice. But it does mean having some difficult conversations about how population growth creates a need to expand urban growth boundaries, as well as allowing for more flexibility to switch land use from industrial and commercial to residential.
We need a proper debate on how to fix Ontario's overlapping housing crises. It's unfortunate that this election is failing to provide one.
Mike Moffatt is the Founding Director of the Missing Middle Initiative and co-host of the podcast "The Missing Middle."

Number of weeks it takes a median wage earner to save a down payment and first five years of mortgage payments in Ontario

Full text: Canada flag insert in newspaper was welcome surprise
I would like to thank the Star for including a full-page Canadian flag in last Saturday's issue. I don't consider myself a flag-waver and generally display my patriotism verbally. I do, however, own an enormous Canadian flag that once flew over Centre Block on Parliament Hill and that was presented to me after 40 years of military service. Although I am extremely proud to own it, it is too large for me to display. Hence my delight upon opening the Star and discovering the full-colour flag. I felt a surge of national pride and immediately taped the paper version to my front door. Thank you, Canada.
Maurice Sacco, Toronto
Thank you for the Canadian flag insert in the Saturday Star. The closest thing we had previously was our Air Canada luggage tags. We tried to buy a flag when everyone was encouraged to show solidarity, but no surprise, everything was sold out. So instead, we taped the Star insert to one of our windows in honour of Flag Day, and we've decided to leave it there indefinitely. We're not the types who would ever even display a lawn sign during an election, but this moment feels different.
Nancy Miller, Toronto
NDP candidate shows leadership
Ontario NDP candidate withdraws in Eglinton-Lawrence, citing close PC-Liberal race, Feb. 14
NDP hopeful Natasha Doyle-Merrick provided some badly needed leadership by withdrawing her candidacy in Eglinton-Lawrence to give the Liberal candidate a better chance of defeating their Progressive Conservative rival in the upcoming Ontario election. We need this kind of leadership from progressive candidates now more than ever. It's high time the provincial and federal progressive parties united and formed a common front against the conservative parties, which are moving more to the right. As author Susan Weiman writes in her recent book "The Left Is Not Woke," progressive movements need to stop fighting among themselves and unite. We need to do this in Ontario and Canada before it's too late.
Joanne Clarke, Toronto
Better ways to protest than booing the U.S. anthem
Fans at 4 Nations boo U.S. anthem before three fights open spirited U.S.-Canada game, Feb. 15
I vehemently disagree with the idea of booing the U.S. national anthem at sporting events. However, I do think Canadian fans are right to show some displeasure. I've seen some situations in which people who want to protest something simply turn their backs on it. I think if Canadian fans were to turn their backs on the United States flag and remain absolutely silent while the anthem played, that would be a highly effective form of protest.
Michael Neill, Thornhill
What's up with Canadians booing the U.S. national anthem? If you have a beef with President Donald Trump, then boo him - don't boo the American people, who, for the most part, love our Canadian neighbours. Like you, most of us would prefer that Canada remain Canada and not become the 51st state. Americans and Canadians have always gotten along like siblings, so let's try to keep it that way and keep politics out of this.
John Lemandri, Williamsburg, Va.
The past few weeks have been very troubling for Canadians, given what Trump is imposing on our country and our people. It's not only the tariffs; it's also the insulting things he says about Canada. The result: Trump has unwittingly united us. However, booing the U.S. national anthem is wrong. The American people are not at fault. It's Trump who is taking these actions and making outrageous statements. The NHL and NBA players are not the cause of your anger: they're simply doing their job. If you choose to watch the game at home or in person, please show respect for the anthems and show your displeasure in other ways. Let's show the world that Canadians are better this. Please stand, remove your hat and either sing or stay quiet while the anthems play.
Susan Beals, Richmond Hill
I completely understand the sentiment of Canadian fans booing American teams at sporting events. It's a way to let Trump and his ilk know how we feel and, really, how often do we really get the chance to express ourselves in a way that reaches so many people?
I don't like to hear it, though, as it seems very rude and lowers us to a level that we Canadians don't generally stoop to. I wonder whether there might be a more gentle (dare I say polite?) or less mean way to convey our ire. How about we decline to sing their anthem, and instead stay silent and turn our backs to the whole event?
Annie Barrett, Alliston, Ont.
Kudos to those dedicated paper delivery people
I would like to shout out my thanks to the delivery people who are doing such a great job despite these snowy conditions. In particular, I want to single out our Star delivery person. Here in Collingwood, we always get more snow than folk down south, but this year it's been far worse than usual. This morning, my driveway was literally impassible, and I feared the worst for our copy of the newspaper. I was amazed to see what happened next: it was apparent that our delivery person had stopped on the road and walked the paper to our very front door. Much appreciated, and beyond the call of duty!
Al Truscott, Collingwood, Ont.

Document 93 of 500

Debating 'a fantasy,' finding MDs for all and jet-set lobbying: Five top takeaways from the campaign trail this week

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/debating-fantasy-finding-mds-all-jet-set-lobbying/docview/3169359716/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Debating+%27a+fantasy%2C%27+finding+MDs+for+all+and+jet-set+lobbying%3A+Five+top+takeaways+from+the+campaign+trail+this+week&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The leadership candidates began the week with their second - and final - debate and finished it with Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford flying to Washington to meet with U.S. governors, and the NDP and Liberals releasing costed platforms. The Greens were the first to release theirs, last Friday, and the PCs are expected to on Monday.
Here are the top takeaways from the campaign trail as the countdown to Thursday's vote nears.
End Highway 407 tolls
The NDP outlined $70 billion in new spending over the next three years, and $37 billion in total savings and new revenue to partly offset that. It also pledged an additional $830 million per year over 10 years for school capital improvements. The party wants to provide families with a grocery rebate, create a school nutrition program, double the Ontario Disability Support Payments, and ensure access to primary care teams - either a family doctor or nurse practitioner, or both - for all Ontarians, as well as more affordable housing. It would also boost capital gains taxes and put a tax on high-end homes. Its plan to remove tolls on Highway 407 is not costed, as the amount would have to be negotiated.
A doctor for every Ontarian
The Liberal platform includes $65 billion in new spending over four years, offset by about $28 billion in savings or repurposing existing funding. It is about "getting the basics right for Ontario" with "no new taxes, no tax increases but there are no fantasy tunnels, either," Crombie said Friday, referring to Ford's plan to tunnel under Highway 401 from Mississauga to Scarborough. The plan promises a family doctor for every Ontarian in four years, mental-health coverage, no interest on student loans, eliminating the HST on home heating, more teachers and education workers in schools, doubling disability payments, and cutting the teacher education program to one year.
'Say it to my face'
The New Democrats lost one candidate to controversy, and Crombie defended three candidates who have come under fire for questionable social media postings, saying they had all apologized and two of the trio made the comments at least a decade ago. Ford also defended an incumbent who has been accused of workplace misconduct toward employees. The NDP candidate in Elgin-Middlesex-London, Amanda Zavitz, apologized and later stepped down after comments she made last year surfaced in which she said she secretly wants "to be a Black woman."
With regards to the Liberal candidate remarks - which appeared to make light of intimate partner violence - Crombie had said she had been focused on preparing for Monday's debate, and not comments from the past. On Friday, the Liberals and the candidate parted ways. On Wednesday, she also accused the PCs of "wanting to sling mud," adding that "if Doug Ford has something to say to me, he should have the balls to say it to my face."
Highway 401 tunnel 'a fantasy'
The 90-minute all-candidates' debate on Family Day Monday, at CBC headquarters in downtown Toronto, touched on everything from health care to housing. NDP Leader Marit Stiles went after Ford for not addressing the housing shortage or health care, saying "the hallway health-care crisis was invented by the previous Liberal government, but Doug Ford and his government have poured gasoline all over it."
Crombie, meanwhile, again made light of Ford's plan to dig under Highway 401, saying "a fantasy tunnel, how dumb is that? After seven years of lies, why should anyone believe you?" Crombie continued her attacks on Ford, on Thursday telling the Canadian Club that since the PCs came to power, "every single metric of economic success has gotten worse in Ontario."
Trump's tariffs
Ford made an announcement with Nova Scotia Premier Tim Houston on Thursday - the day before the duo headed to Washington - both committing to eliminating interprovincial trade barriers to help improve the economy and ease the blow of tariffs threatened by U.S. President Donald Trump. "We're facing the biggest threat to our economic security and independence as a country, we need to rise to the challenge," Ford said in Milton. "We need to match these words with bold actions."
CAPTION: From left, PC Leader Doug Ford, Green Leader Mike Schreiner, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and NDP Leader Marit Stiles pose before the leaders' debate on Family Day.Chris Young The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Document 94 of 500

Gretzky no traitor but silence speaks loudly: After decades in U.S., it's unsurprising he has different views than many Canadians

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/gretzky-no-traitor-silence-speaks-loudly/docview/3169359683/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Gretzky+no+traitor+but+silence+speaks+loudly%3A+After+decades+in+U.S.%2C+it%27s+unsurprising+he+has+different+views+than+many+Canadians&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=S.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: These are curious times indeed at the intersection of sports and politics.
On one hand you have the Premier of Ontario, a "100 per cent" supporter of the convicted felon in residence at the White House, poised to win another majority in Canada's largest province. Doug Ford is a populist, and apparently his views make him popular.
Canadian hockey icon Wayne Gretzky, on the other hand, is under social media siege because he dares to support Donald Trump, and was Team Canada's honorary captain in a five-minute ceremony before the 4 Nations Face-Off final in Boston on Thursday night. The evidence suggests No. 99 and his family are strong MAGA supporters, and Gretzky certainly hasn't come out to protest the U.S. president's suggestion that Canada should become the "51st state" with the pride of Brantford as its "governor."
So, it's OK for Ford to embrace Trump because he has mastered the significant contortions required to convince enough people that he is also strongly pro-Canada. Gretzky, however, despite stepping up to represent his country on countless occasions as a player and executive, is being characterized as a traitor in some quarters.
Well, if you're looking for hockey people to be progressive or loudly anti-Trump when it comes to their views on Canada as a sovereign nation, you might be looking in the wrong place. Gretzky's apparent right-wing leanings are more consistent with mainstream thinking in the bro culture that dominates the male hockey world than you might want to acknowledge.
Most NHL players are white, aren't university educated and are more likely to follow social media than read a book. Their business prizes group think rather than individuality. These days, most are also affluent. All of that makes them easy pickings for the tribal MAGA world.
Should Gretzky have been Canada's honorary captain? He sure didn't come out waving the flag, but this wasn't the Olympics. It was a money-making event developed by the NHL and the players association to replace the all-star game. It was a business venture designed to generate ratings and profits, and Gretzky has always been an NHL loyalist. He has often said he owes everything to the sport and the league, including his significant wealth.
He has also lived more than half his life in California, since being sold off like livestock by his Canadian team to an American club in 1988. So, you shouldn't be surprised he's pro-Trump and has a different world view than many Canadians. Saddened and disappointed if you like, but not surprised.
Should Gretzky be held to a different standard? Well, Mario Lemieux didn't criticize Trump's anti-Canada statements before dropping the puck at the 4 Nations opener. Sidney Crosby hasn't said much publicly on the matter. But suddenly it's only Gretzky who's not Canadian enough?
Sure, his silence on Trump's anti-Canadian comments is disappointing. Just as disappointing as it was when Bobby Orr and Jack Nicklaus came out as pro-Trump. The fact that these revered sportsmen can simply look the other way as forces of hate spread stolen election lies and blame Ukraine for getting invaded is upsetting. But frankly, we should be more concerned that Ford and the premier of Alberta admire Trump than about what Gretzky thinks on political matters he likely hasn't given a great deal of thought.
Most professional hockey players confine their political convictions to tax rates, and present themselves as socially conservative. When the NHL tried to ban Pride tape, the vast majority of players said nothing. Only Travis Dermott had the stones to tell the Bettman administration to take a hike. Again, hockey players fit the profile of the MAGA crowd.
That said, having known Gretzky for more than 30 years on a professional basis, it's extremely unlikely he would endorse Trump's view of Canada's future. It's possible to visit Mar-a-Lago and reject the U.S. president's ideas on the matter.
The shame of Gretzky's pro-MAGA leanings is that he is apparently unaware of the fear and unease Trump is generating in Canada. He would probably be at a loss to explain why Canadian fans booed "The Star-Spangled Banner" at the 4 Nations tournament.
But to question his Canadian patriotism without a lot more evidence is unfair given all he has done to represent, promote and defend the Great White North. At the 2002 Salt Lake Olympics, it was Gretzky who spoke out angrily on the biggest of international stages because he was "tired of people taking shots at Canadian hockey."
But that was then. These days, he's more about promoting sports betting than hockey or his country of birth. He's in the business of being Wayne Gretzky.
So sure, we can hope he will spend some time investigating Trump's insults and try to understand why so many are upset that Gretzky hasn't come out to defend Canada now.
But don't hold your breath.
CAPTION: Wayne Gretzky's apparent right-wing leanings are more consistent with mainstream thinking in the bro culture that dominates the male hockey world, Damien Cox writes.4NFO via Getty Images

News Quiz by Benjamin Errett

d) Novelty hats and photo ops

Document 96 of 500

'Deceived': Anger mounts in Toronto's LGBTQ community after developer swaps midrise plans for a 48-storey highrise in the Village

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/deceived/docview/3169359635/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Deceived%27%3A+Anger+mounts+in+Toronto%27s+LGBTQ+community+after+developer+swaps+midrise+plans+for+a+48-storey+highrise+in+the+Village&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It seemed like a win-win. A developer's bid to transform a historic landmark in the Village, though fiercely opposed at first, had come to gain widespread support from the Toronto LGBTQ neighbourhood.
After months of consultations - with community members, local organizations and city staff - the builder delivered a proposal to convert popular, long-standing drag bar Crews & Tangos into a 14-storey tower with 165 residential units. The development would be in the heart of the Village, on Church Street, just south of Maitland Street. City council approvedthe plans in 2022.
Then something changed. In 2024, the developer, Graywood Developments, submitted a new application for a tower with 48 storeys and more than 570 units.
Crews & Tangos would still have the right to return, the developer said, but community members say they felt betrayed.
And while the new proposal was rejected by the city earlier this month - council said it was not in keeping with the Church Street Village Character Area policiesthat call for "limited growth" and a "low to midrise pedestrian oriented main street character" - the community isn't celebrating.
That's because Graywood Developments has appealed the decision to the Ontario Land Tribunal (OLT), which neighbourhood advocates fear is part of a trend of developers and real estate investors turning to the provincial tribunalto override the city's rules and create larger, higher-profit projects that don't take community interests into account.
"It'll be an Armageddon of the Village as we know it," said Connie Langille, president of the Church Wellesley Neighbourhood Association, noting the Village has long been known for its LGBTQ history, entertainment and community spaces.
"This is where people feel safe. It's a beacon, it's a sanctuary, it's a refuge," Langille said.
Another real estate giant wanting to build higher in the area is set to have its case heard at the OLTthis summer. Investment firm KingSett Capital filed an appeal after the city rejected its proposal for a 28-storey mixed-use tower on the northeast corner of Church and Wellesley in 2024. City staff had said the plan did not follow Church Street Character Area policies. Its OLT hearing is set for June.
Graywood Developments did not respond to the Star's request for comment. KingSett Capital declined to comment.
Advocates believe developers in the neighbourhood are watching how these cases unfold to see if they, too, can pursue large projects that city council would likely reject. The appeals come after changes by the Doug Ford government that advocates say have given developers more confidence to win at the OLT.
In 2021, the province merged the existing Local Planning Appeal Tribunal, Environmental Review Tribunal, Board of Negotiation, Conservation Review Board and Mining and Lands Tribunal into one body: the OLT. The Ford government created it in an effort to resolve land planning disputes faster and increase housing supply.
According to a 2022 analysis by the Hamilton Spectator, OLT decisions from January to August of that year favoured developers 97 per cent of the time.
Michael Cook, a land use planning and development lawyer at Davies Howe LLP, said the province has taken several steps over the last few years to get more housing built in the midst of a housing crisis.
While he doesn't believe there's one particular piece of legislation that would motivate developers to appeal now, he said, "I think there's been a turn in the developers' favour in terms of the policy regime; in other words, what's permitted by (provincial) policy."
The OLT, for its part, said, "each appeal is heard and determined on its individual merits."
"The Ontario Land Tribunal (OLT) is committed to high quality, independent, fair and principled resolutions of the matters brought before it," a spokesperson said in a statement to the Star. "Tribunal decisions are based on the facts, the applicable law and policy, the merits of the case, and the evidence presented."
'Everyone feels like
it's a golden ticket'
The Village has experienced a growing wave of development over the last 15 years, and with it a permanent loss of LGBTQ venues and heritage features, Langille said.
"Everyone feels like it's a golden ticket, and they want to cash in," Langille said, adding that developers don't have connections to the community and KingSett, in particular, has bought and sold several properties in the area.
The city in 2013 identified the Village as a Yonge Street "character area" to protect its heritage properties, pedestrian features, retail streetscape and more, and to ensure growth in the area respected the integrity of the neighbourhood.
In 2022, it seemed Graywood was respecting that preservation. That's whythe most recent proposal came as a surprise to those who had provided input and support for the developer's initial application, and who say they were not consulted about the more recent changes.
The 2022 plan was for a 14-storey mixed-use building with 165 units that included non-residential space at the ground level with room to accommodate entertainment uses. In its 2024 application for a 48-storey mixed-use building, Graywood proposed 576 residential units, retail space and "queer supportive programming" on the lot. Graywood noted in the application that, as requested through the previous process, Crews & Tangos and Boutique Bar both received offers to return.
The new application also said Graywood appreciated the efforts and collaboration of the community and key stakeholders throughout the process to permit a 14-storey building, and added that it was "committed to building on that established goodwill to advance the project and deliver new housing, commercial and community spaces that positively contribute to the future vitality of the Village."
Langille said she had been excited to work with Graywood on its original application, but the highrise later proposed by the developer would "dominate the entire landscape of the Village" and contribute to the loss of its unique character.
The latest design "rises straight off the street" without adequate setbacks, towering over the nearby buildings with materials that don't match the neighbourhood, Langille said.
"Folks need to realize that the iconic areas in Toronto aren't going to remain unless all of Toronto more or less bands together and says, 'These areas are important. They bring worth and value,' " she said. Coun. Chris Moise (Ward 13, Toronto Centre) said the community feels "deceived."
He emphasized that Canadians are not immune to the impact of another Donald Trump presidency, noting the U.S. government recently issued an executive order that refuses to acknowledge the existence of trans people.
"We're trying to protect the little that we have," he said.
What changed?
Kristyn Wong-Tam, NDP MPP for Toronto Centre and the former city councillor for the ward, said they worked alongside city planners, urban designers, preservation staff and even the economic development office to engage in months-long conversations with Graywood Developments and contribute to the proposal that was approved in 2022.
They believeBill 23, the More Homes Built Faster Act, motivated Graywood to change its plans because it puts "more power into the hands of developers."
"The (original) application was approved in June 2022, Bill 23 came into effect in November 2022, and now we see this new application which seems to throw everything we agreed upon, all the negotiated revisions, just out the door," they said.
The OLT also experienced a significant change in 2021. That year, provincial policy limited the rights of "participants" to making only written submissions. Previously, they could give presentations at the hearings.
Cook, the lawyer, explained that third-parties can present at OLT hearings by getting "party" status, rather than "participant" status, but doing so requires much more time and resources.
Paul Farrelly, the chair of the neighbourhood association's heritage committee, said that although developers on Yonge Street can build 70-storey buildings, the city's North Downtown Yonge Site and Area Specific Policy protects Church Street from this type of development.
The city passed the bylaw in 2013 (with several consultations, including with the neighbourhood association). Seventeen developers appealed it, Farrelly said, but the neighbourhood association raised $50,000 to hire an expert witness, and the case was settled so that the Village would maintain its lowrise character.
Now, KingSett's appeal threatens the policy again.
"(Developers) keep coming back, because there's no limit; they have all the money in the world to be able to come back and try again," Farrelly said.
Langille noted the tribunal's members are appointed. They may not be urban planners and they may not be from Toronto, but they've overridden many decisions the city has made.
Coun. Gord Perks, chair of Toronto's housing and planning committee, said generally most developers would prefer the certainty of getting approval from the city, rather than going to the OLT and involving a third party. But there's a "particular struggle" on Church Street, he said.
He added the city manages growth in a way that improves the livability of Toronto, designating certain areas as suitable for highrises and others for midrise development. It also makes considerations for areas, like the Village, that have social and cultural importance to the city.
"So our whole official plan is being called into question by developers saying, 'No, I just feel like building a tall building anywhere,' " Perks said.
The neighbourhood association, which has retained party status for KingSett Capital's OLT hearing, is now scrambling to raise $50,000 and hire an expert witness. They started a GoFundMe.
They believe the result of the KingSett case will set the stage for Graywood's case, and could eventually decide the fate of the Village.
CREDIT: Manuela Vega Housing Reporter

Document 97 of 500

No Bond villain is scarier than Bezos

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/no-bond-villain-is-scarier-than-bezos/docview/3169359621/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=No+Bond+villain+is+scarier+than+Bezos&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=C.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: James Bond now gets free shipping on spyware and martini shakers.
There are so many things happening that were once unthinkable. America betrays an ally and sides with a murderous dictator. The City of Toronto is bamboozled by snow. Humans are marrying robots. Vladimir Guerrero Jr. is now counting down the days until he is an ex-Jay.
And now 007 is an Amazon agent.
That news arrived Thursday. Amazon MGM Studios, which owns the rights to 4,000 movies and 17,000 TV shows, has gained "creative control" of James Bond. Independent producers Michael G. Wilson and Barbara Broccoli, who guided and guarded the franchise like junkyard dogs for decades, agreed to a new "joint venture." They will keep an ownership stake and lounge in the fog machine of semi-retirement when it comes to future casting and scripts.
Mike Hopkins, head honcho at Amazon MGM Studios, said in a statement: "Since his theatrical introduction over 60 years ago, James Bond has been one of the most iconic characters in filmed entertainment ... We are honoured to continue this treasured heritage, and look forward to ushering in the next phase of the legendary 007 ... "
Future titles: "From Fulfilment Centre With Love." "License to Shop." "Dr. No Bezos." "No Time to Refund." "Prime Days Never Die." "For Lauren's Eyes Only." "Alexapussy."
The fate of James Bond is now in the hands of Amazon.
The company already held distribution rights. But now it can shape the MI6 agent to better fit with current mores and corporate interests. Say, why is this brooding protagonist, first imagined by author Ian Fleming, slowly poisoning himself with vodka when we can promote a fantastic Whole Foods protein shake?
What's with all the womanizing? I know, in the third act of "The Prime Member Who Loved Me," James starts listening to relationship books on Audible and delivers a teary soliloquy on an Italian veranda as the bad guys close in with directed-energy bazookas.
"I wronged you, Bond Girls. I am sorry, Honey Ryder. Forgive me, Miss Taro. Where have you gone, Pussy Galore? For all the heartache I inflicted, I shall pick up all overnight shipping costs on thousands of wonderful products that will make you fondly remember I am but a dashing and highly disturbed lover of gadgets who does not play well with others."
I don't trust Jeff Bezos with James Bond any more than I'd trust Pornhub with Winnie the Pooh. Oh my God, what is going on? Why is Pooh going to an orgy? Tigger, put down that bottle of baby oil! You're not Diddy. No, Eeyore, that's not thistle - it's a sex toy!
James Bond is a lone wolf. He is not a corporate shill. Yeah, yeah, I know. Amazon MGM Studios has a C-suite full of Hollywood execs who will faithfully nurture the Bond universe for a new generation.
But what happens when Bezos wants a say? The broligarchs are now jockeying for cultural supremacy. They yearn to leave their DNA on everything. You don't think Elon Musk wants to gain control of the X-Men franchise just for the cross-branding opportunities? Mark Zuckerberg fantasizes about owning DC Comics just so he can create a new superhero that has no moral compass and turtles in political headwinds: It's a bird, it's a plane, it's Zuckupman!
Why would anyone trust these craven broligarchs? Bezos looks like a Bond villain. Does anyone at the Washington Post still believe it's fantastic to be owned by one of the world's richest men who promised to stay out of editorial but is now more hands on than a cephalopod?
That masthead slogan - "Democracy Dies in Darkness" - needs a rewrite.
"Journalism Dies in Meddling."
The Wall Street Journal recently reported that soon after Bezos dined with Donald and Melania Trump at Mar-a-Lago in December, Amazon offered $40 million to license the first lady's upcoming documentary about her transition back to the White House. That's like giving $40 million to Doug Ford to write a book about origami. After 27 folds, is it a swan ... or a Greenbelt?
Most other studios, including Netflix and Apple, took a hard pass on Melania's vanity project. But Jeff Bozo was eager to ingratiate himself to his new overlords. He knows this doc won't get more viewers than "The Chevy Chase Show." No matter.
If Barron pitched a new reality show - "Barron Wants a Hot Chick" - Bezos would have opened his chequebook as Melania regaled him with horror stories about how buying off the rack is a crime against humanity. If the president ever asks, James Bond will be wearing a MAGA cap in his next caper: "DEI is DOA."
Agent 007 better shake himself a strong martini or three this evening.
He now takes orders from Amazon. No villain could be more terrifying.
CAPTION: Daniel Craig as James Bond in "No Time to Die." With news that Amazon is taking over the franchise, Agent 007 better shake himself a strong martini or three, writes Vinay Menon.Nicola Dove MGM/tribune news service
CREDIT: Vinay Menon Toronto Star OPINION

Document 98 of 500

The choice on schools: Our kids need more teachers. Whether they get them depends on how we vote

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/choice-on-schools/docview/3169359559/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+choice+on+schools%3A+Our+kids+need+more+teachers.+Whether+they+get+them+depends+on+how+we+vote&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=OP.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With tariffs and the economy taking centre stage, education issues have seemingly taken a back seat in the current election campaign, despite being one of the provincial government's most important responsibilities. Including pension costs, Ontario is projected to spend just under $40 billion on K-12 education in the current fiscal year. And yet there are several challenges that continue to plague our schools.
One of the big problems is the backlog of school repairs and renewals. A recent report from the Financial Accountability Office states that 37 per cent of schools are below a state of good repair. In the Toronto District School Board, nearly all schools (84 per cent) are in substandard condition. The FAO projects it will cost more than $31 billion over 10 years to ensure that we have enough classrooms in growing parts of the province and that all schools in Ontario are in a state of good repair. In a province as wealthy as Ontario, we shouldn't have to send our kids to schools that are falling apart.
Another big challenge is the ongoing teacher shortage, which the Ontario Ministry of Education projects will get worse through 2027. According to the Ontario College of Teachers, whereas about 25 per cent of early-career teachers were unemployed in 2014, the amount of unemployment is now at "statistically negligible levels." This has led to lowering the standards of who we let into the classroom. You can only have standards to the degree that you have choices. And right now, schools don't have many choices. In some cases, school boards are employing emergency replacement teachers who have no training whatsoever. Ensuring that all classrooms have a competent, well-trained teacher at the helm should be a baseline expectation for any provincial government.
Progressive Conservatives
While not making any significant new education promises, the PCs are running on their record since coming to power in 2018. Despite fears to the contrary, they have continued to invest record sums in Ontario's public schools. They have also made several significant reforms that continue to shape the system.
Perhaps the most consequential of these reforms was the elimination of academic streaming in Grade 9. Streaming is the practice of dividing students into a de facto hierarchy of classes based on perceived ability or interest. Announced in 2020, former education minister Stephen Lecce called streaming a "systemic, racist, discriminatory" practice. Research has long demonstrated that this practice leads to social marginalization, lower academic achievement and reduced educational opportunities for students placed in lower levels, who are disproportionately Black. While streaming in Grade 9 may have officially ended, many schools have struggled to implement the change effectively. A 2023 survey from People for Education found that only 20 per cent of Ontario high school principals reported having enough resources to support destreaming for Grade 9 students. Principals reported needing additional educational assistants, increased teacher training, and lower class sizes in order for it to be implemented effectively. Without these additional supports, we run the risk of repeating what happened in the early 1990s when Ontario previously destreamed Grade 9, only to reintroduce it five years later after ineffective implementation.
The PCs also introduced a "back to basics" agenda, which has directed school boards to focus on reading, writing and STEM education. This approach has been paired with a scaling back of the equity and social justice work that the PCs purported to care about just a few years ago. PC Leader Doug Ford has repeatedly accused schools of "indoctrinating" kids, and research by myself and colleagues has found that his government is now undermining the equity work taking place in school districts across the province.
A PC campaign spokesperson notes that they have doubled the amount of funding to build schools since they took office. As to why school facilities continue to be in such bad shape, both Lecce and current Education Minister Jill Dunlop have repeatedly accused school boards of failing to effectively use the maintenance and capital funds they have already been allocated.
A re-elected PC government will likely continue down the current path of continued investment in public education, a "back to basics" instructional focus, and a scaling back of social justice work in schools.
NDP
"Mould in schools, crumbling ceilings, freezing classrooms ... our kids shouldn't be in schools with those issues in Ontario in 2025," Chandra Pasma, the NDP's education critic, told me. To address these issues, the NDP has pledged to spend an extra $830 million per year, which it says will eliminate the repair backlog within 10 years.
To address the teacher shortage, the NDP has a multi-faceted plan. The first is to incentivize the tens of thousands of people who are currently certified by the Ontario College of Teachers but are not actively teaching. A big part of the reason for this is the increased behavioural challenges faced by teachers in the classroom, particularly after the pandemic. Working with Ontario's education unions, the NDP put together a multi-faceted Proposal for an Emergency Safe Schools Plan that includes increased training, consistent and uniform reporting of school violence, as well as the hiring of additional mental health professionals, educational assistants and other education workers.
The most bold and effective strategy put forth by Pasma to address the teacher shortage is to bring the length of Ontario's teacher education programs back down to one year instead of the current two years. As Pasma notes, "It was bumped up to two years when we had a surplus of teachers. It was never a pedagogical reason. So now, when we have a shortage. Why are we maintaining it at two years?"
The NDP has also promised to end the practice of streaming entirely. However, it is unclear how this would be achieved as we have not yet effectively implemented the destreaming of Grade 9. As Pasma notes, "we didn't put the resources behind it for it to succeed ... destreaming without support is still exclusion."
An NDP government would have the most comprehensive education plan to address Ontario's pressing education issues. However, the party has yet to detail how it will pay for these promises and its plan to reduce teacher education programs to one year would further strain the precarious finances of Ontario's universities.
Green Party
The Green Party has direct experience informing its education plan. Matt Richter, the party's education critic, has been a teacher with the Trillium Lakelands District School Board for over two decades. Richter told me he has seen first-hand how teaching and learning conditions have deteriorated under both Liberal and PC governments. To remedy this, the Green Party has committed to capping class sizes at 24 students from Grades four to eight, and at 26 students in kindergarten. To meet the staffing requirements for this pledge and to address the teacher shortage, Richter says we need to increase enrolments in Ontario's teacher education programs and give school boards more money to hire full-time teachers. The Green Party also pledges to increase education funding by $1,500 per student, devote more money to clearing the backlog in school repairs, and provide more funding for school boards to hire additional support staff to deal with the growing issue of school violence. The party estimates that these reforms would cost an additional $2 billion to $3 billion per year. To pay for these reforms, Richter points to the $3 billion the current government spent on "bribe checks" as an example of money that could be reallocated. Another element of the Green platform is to eliminate EQAO testing, something that was being explored in the latter stages of the previous Wynne government. While this would not result in significant cost savings, it would have a dramatic impact on the pedagogy in our classrooms, particularly at the elementary level where a lot of time is spent on test preparation. Given that EQAO is supposed to assess the performance of the entire system, a better alternative than testing every single student each year would be to test a randomly sampled subset of Ontario students once every three years.
The education agenda of a Green Party government is the most transformative and the costliest. Reducing elementary class sizes and eliminating EQAO testing would be cheered by most teachers but might have unintended consequences in terms of reducing hiring standards even further and removing one of the few pressures in our school system to maintain academic rigour.
Liberal Party
The Liberal party has yet to release an education platform, although press secretary Bahoz Dara Aziz assured me that one will be unveiled soon. According to Dara Aziz, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie promises to "fix the schools, hire more teachers and get teachers the tools they need." Although in the absence of a platform it is difficult to know what this would mean, we can look at what individual candidates have pledged. On their campaign websites, Scarborough-Guildwood candidate Andrew Hazell, Oxford candidate Bernia Martin, and Mississauga-Streetsville candidate Jill Promoli have all promised to reduce class sizes, although in which grades and by how much is unstated. Also unclear is where the additional teachers required to reduce class sizes would come from. It is worth noting that the slate of Liberal candidates contains several current and former school board trustees, including former chairs of the Hamilton-Wentworth District School Board and the Halton District School Board. Thus, if elected to office, the Liberal Party would have an experienced caucus from which to put together a comprehensive education plan.
Sachin Maharaj is an assistant professor of educational leadership, policy and program evaluation at the University of Ottawa.
37
Percentage
of Ontario schools below state of good repair
CREDIT: Sachin Maharaj Contributor;

ERIC Subject: Teacher Employment; Personnel Selection; Class Size; State Government; Social Justice; Boards of Education; Test Preparation; School Violence; Teacher Education Programs; Teacher Shortage; Classrooms; Elementary Secondary Education; Grade 9; School Safety
