Document 99 of 500

Ontario's economy is facing a Trump-induced crisis. This is how your next premier intends to fix it

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontarios-economy-is-facing-trump-induced-crisis/docview/3169359557/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario%27s+economy+is+facing+a+Trump-induced+crisis.+This+is+how+your+next+premier+intends+to+fix+it&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's economy is in a sink-or-swim moment.
As the industrial heartland that has powered Canada - the world's 10th largest nation, measured by GDP - the province is confronting the harsh reality of an abruptly transformed economic relationship with the U.S., one on which we've coasted since the wake of the Second World War.
That relationship is now over. President Trump views Canada and Ontario as a potential asset to take over, not as a production and trading partner.
Whether or when tariffs materialize, uncertainty has shattered investment confidence. The immediate focus is on job losses in export sectors like auto manufacturing and steel. The threat extends to energy, ores and uranium, yet-to-be-mined critical minerals, and three of Canada's most crucial nodes of technology in Kitchener-Waterloo, Ottawa-Kanata, and Toronto.
Everything is contested, including our sovereignty. Progressive Conservative leader Doug Ford added an election to the chaotic mix.
Here's the economic reality almost certainly awaiting whichever party wins the beauty contest: Inflation will rise. More people will lose jobs, many of whom won't be eligible for EI. More people will go hungry. More people will lose their homes. That's on top of the existing housing crisis, which crowds out other spending, and the crisis in care, also a huge economic drag when it reduces people's ability to work.
Every party's plan will impact your economic future differently. I requested an interview with each to understand how they'd tackle the arenas of greatest economic consequence in this moment: minimizing economic decline; the healthcare crisis; and the housing crisis.
Their answers are summarized and ordered from first to last to respond. Ford's Conservatives never responded, so that section summarizes news reports. Cost estimates are only included for the Conservatives' announcements because the other parties have already published full platforms with costing.
Liberals: Focus on attracting/retaining capital and the best people
With healthcare a total shambles, Crombie has led with the promise to add 3,100 doctors to the system over the next four years, and improve pay for nurses and PSWs across the system to reduce churn.
A more costly and controversial gamble aimed at improving purchasing power is the pledge to cut taxes permanently by reducing the middle-income bracket tax rate from 9.15 to 7.15 per cent for everyone with taxable incomes between $51,446 - $75,000. This excludes the 63 per cent of Ontarians whose total taxable incomes fall below this threshold, but not those with incomes surpassing $75,000. The Liberals would also eliminate the HST on heating and hydro and cut small business taxes by 50 per cent.
The Liberals see the housing crisis as throttling the economy. They'd waive the Land Transfer Tax for first-time buyers, remove development charges on housing construction, and phase-in rent control.
The Liberals have also promised to double ODSP (provincial income support for the roughly 500,00 chronically disabled working age residents), which is currently at a maximum of $1,368 a month. There would be no increase for the 477,000 beneficiaries of the Ontario Works (OW) program, the other aspect of provincial welfare, whose benefits are still at 2018 levels.
Greens: Focus on the economic crises people are facing
The Greens say Trump's threats amp up pre-existing pressures, some created by the current government, like the crisis in housing and collapse of the care economy (not just healthcare). The Green platform is sweeping and fully costed. Housing is their top priority. They'd upload community housing, shelter and transit program costs back to the province; introduce retrofit and heat-pump programs for households under $100,000. The Greens would also waive Land Transfer Taxes for first-time buyers, and the HST on building non-profit housing. They also promise to eliminate zoning rules that restrict medium-density housing, while also vowing to build 310,000 more affordable rental units including 60,000 supportive housing units.
Also in the mix is a plan to tackle poverty by doubling social assistance (both ODSP and OW), as well as increasing the minimum wage to $20, then indexing it to inflation.
Another key part of the Green platform is to cut taxes, but only for low- to mid-incomes (individuals earning under $65,000, and households earning under $100,000). Conversely, they would raise taxes on the top tax bracket by 3 percentage points and introduce a wealth tax. They'd also divert rebates for electricity from high-income households.
They'd prevent further development on agricultural lands to ensure food security.
NDP - Focus on affordability
The NDP say the economy wasn't working for most people before Trump came along, as skyrocketing housing costs outstripped wage gains. They say the moment demands action on two main goals: fortifying social infrastructure (care, housing, education), which can offset higher costs and build resilience for whatever comes next; developing a procurement program for public services that builds domestic supply chains, reduces supply disruptions.
Affordability has two sides: poverty and price increases. The NDP would offer a monthly grocery rebate for low-to-mid income families ($122 for family of four) for "as long as needed" and would double ODSP and OW rates. They'd also introduce a consumer watchdog office and pass legislation that requires large retailers to advertise price increases of more than 2 per cent in a week (as they currently advertise price cuts). They'd remove tolls on publicly managed Highway 407 and privately owned 407 ETR.
To address the housing crisis, the NDP say they would decrease renovictions; close loopholes in rent control that have arisen since 2018; and build 60,000 units of supportive housing to reduce encampments, now in communities across Ontario. They also promise to create a new public agency to provide low-cost financing for "deeply affordable" housing.
The NDP has also made a range of promises relating to health care jobs. They've promised to recruit 3,500 new doctors and 15,000 more nurses. They'd end the use of private sector nursing staff agencies and ensure wage parity across healthcare settings to reduce outflow of staff from lower- to higher-paid settings for the same qualifications. They also say they'd expedite existing applications to create family health teams (at least 100 have been waiting for approval for months). They'd freeze taxes on the 98.2 percent of Ontarians making less than $220,000, and raise taxes on luxury homes (over $3 million).
Progressive Conservatives - Focus on Ford as the best person to stand up to Trump.
Dubbed "Captain Canada" after answering Trump's tariff threats with full-throated responses, he threatened to cut off energy exports to the U.S. (and backed off) in December; proclaimed he would "hit back twice as hard" (with no clear actions) in January; flip-flopped on tearing up a Starlink deal with Elon Musk within hours in February; and wore a "Canada is not for sale" cap after Trump suggested Canada become the 51st state.
The only new economic measure Ford appears to have promised during the election campaign in response to Trump is banning Chinese equity from Ontario government-funded energy, critical mineral and infrastructure assets. Plan A appears now to be to appease, not fight, Trump.
At the leader's debate on February 17, Ford repeatedly stated governments can't fix anything - health, housing, education - without a strong economy. The economy's strength is sapped by constant uncertainty. Ford's solution is more is more tax cuts, more capital spending, mostly on transportation and policing.
New tax cuts lean heavily towards business: $14 billion this year ($10 billion for six-month deferral of provincially administered taxes on Ontario businesses; $3 billion to reduce payroll taxes, $600 million to incentivize investments, $300 million for a manufacturing tax credit). The PCs would also extend the 5.7 cents-a-litre reduction in gas taxes, in place since July 2022 (roughly $1 billion) and cut tolls on the provincially operated part of Highway 407 (around $72 million).
You should have your tax rebate (a $200 cheque for 15 million Ontarians, costing $3 billion), announced before Trump's election, by now.
New spends announced during this campaign include a 55 km traffic expressway tunnel under Highway 401 from Mississauga to Markham area (no price estimate, no ETA); a police academy ($1 billion - the same cost as renovating Queen's Park, which was delayed); police helicopters and water bombers ($580 million); $22 billion more over the next three years for the 10-year Building Ontario Fund ($15 billion is for roads, $5 billion for infrastructure, and $2 billion for municipal supports to housing). Finally, they added $1.4 billion to the existing $400 million to create 305 new primary care teams (unspecified number of doctors) in four years, after the other parties' proposals were public. It is unclear how these measures relate to or offset the effects of Trump's threats and the economic challenges that lie ahead.
The PCs haven't offered a platform, costing, or budget for the post-Trump moment. There's only Ford's word.
*
Each opposition party offers a plan for the economic emergency we are in, from modest (the Liberals' four priorities) to transformative (the NDP and the Greens).
Personal tax cuts appeal to the Liberals and Greens, but the Greens target their cuts on the poor while raising taxes from the rich. The Greens and NDP favour delimited tax breaks for investments, while the Liberals favour halving taxes for small business.
All opposition parties say improving economic resilience is critical for weathering the coming storm. But that demands improving social infrastructure - the care economy and housing - not physical infrastructure like highways.
The Progressive Conservative Party is, in every sense, the outlier.
More than your vote, they want you to write a blank cheque and develop amnesia about their performance over the last seven years.
Whether kabuki theatre with Trump is enough for most Ontario voters remains to be seen.
Like the license plate says: Ontario - Yours to Discover.
CAPTION: The leaders of Ontario's political parties will have to tackle housing, healthcare and more if they are to adequately deal with the overall economic picture, writes economist Armine Yalnizyan.
CREDIT: Armine Yalnizyan

Business indexing term: Subject: Taxes Capital expenditures Tax rates Infrastructure Tax cuts Cost estimates Price cuts Rent control

Title: Ontario's economy is facing a Trump-induced crisis. This is how your next premier intends to fix it

The choice on the economy: The leaders have very different visions for how to help Ontarians avert disaster and get ahead

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/choice-on-economy/docview/3169359553/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+choice+on+the+economy%3A+The+leaders+have+very+different+visions+for+how+to+help+Ontarians+avert+disaster+and+get+ahead&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=OP.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's economy is in a sink-or-swim moment.
As the industrial heartland that has powered Canada - the world's 10th largest nation, measured by GDP - the province is confronting the harsh reality of an abruptly transformed economic relationship with the U.S., one on which we've coasted since the wake of the Second World War.
That relationship is now over. President Trump views Canada and Ontario as a potential asset to take over, not as a production and trading partner.
Whether or when tariffs materialize, uncertainty has shattered investment confidence. The immediate focus is on job losses in export sectors like auto manufacturing and steel. The threat extends to energy, ores and uranium, yet-to-be-mined critical minerals, and three of Canada's most crucial nodes of technology in Kitchener-Waterloo, Ottawa-Kanata, and Toronto.
Everything is contested, including our sovereignty. Progressive Conservative leader Doug Ford added an election to the chaotic mix.
Here's the economic reality almost certainly awaiting whichever party wins the beauty contest: Inflation will rise. More people will lose jobs, many of whom won't be eligible for EI. More people will go hungry. More people will lose their homes. That's on top of the existing housing crisis, which crowds out other spending, and the crisis in care, also a huge economic drag when it reduces people's ability to work.
Every party's plan will impact your economic future differently. I requested an interview with each to understand how they'd tackle the arenas of greatest economic consequence in this moment: minimizing economic decline; the health-care crisis; and the housing crisis.
Their answers are summarized and ordered from first to last to respond. Ford's Conservatives never responded, so that section summarizes news reports. Cost estimates are only included for the Conservatives' announcements because the other parties have already published full platforms with costing.
Liberals
Focus on attracting/retaining capital and the best people
With health care a total shambles, Crombie has led with the promise to add 3,100 doctors to the system over the next four years, and improve pay for nurses and PSWs across the system to reduce churn.
A more costly and controversial gamble aimed at improving purchasing power is the pledge to cut taxes permanently by reducing the middle-income bracket tax rate from 9.15 to 7.15 per cent for everyone with taxable incomes between $51,446 and $75,000. This excludes the 63 per cent of Ontarians whose total taxable incomes fall below this threshold, but not those with incomes surpassing $75,000. The Liberals would also eliminate the HST on heating and hydro and cut small business taxes by 50 per cent.
The Liberals see the housing crisis as throttling the economy. They'd waive the Land Transfer Tax for first-time buyers, remove development charges on housing construction, and phase-in rent control.
The Liberals have also promised to double ODSP (provincial income support for the roughly 500,00 chronically disabled working age residents), which is currently at a maximum of $1,368 a month. There would be no increase for the 477,000 beneficiaries of the Ontario Works (OW) program, the other aspect of provincial welfare, whose benefits are still at 2018 levels.
Greens
Focus on the economic crises people are facing
The Greens say Trump's threats amp up pre-existing pressures, some created by the current government, like the crisis in housing and collapse of the care economy (not just health care). The Green platform is sweeping and fully costed. Housing is their top priority. They'd upload community housing, shelter and transit program costs back to the province; introduce retrofit and heat-pump programs for households under $100,000. The Greens would also waive Land Transfer Taxes for first-time buyers, and the HST on building non-profit housing. They also promise to eliminate zoning rules that restrict medium-density housing, while also vowing to build 310,000 more affordable rental units including 60,000 supportive housing units.
Also in the mix is a plan to tackle poverty by doubling social assistance (both ODSP and OW), as well as increasing the minimum wage to $20, then indexing it to inflation.
Another key part of the Green platform is to cut taxes, but only for low- to mid-incomes (individuals earning under $65,000, and households earning under $100,000). Conversely, they would raise taxes on the top tax bracket by 3 percentage points and introduce a wealth tax. They'd also divert rebates for electricity from high-income households.
They'd prevent further development on agricultural lands to ensure food security.

Focus on affordability
The NDP say the economy wasn't working for most people before Trump came along, as skyrocketing housing costs outstripped wage gains. They say the moment demands action on two main goals: fortifying social infrastructure (care, housing, education), which can offset higher costs and build resilience for whatever comes next; developing a procurement program for public services that builds domestic supply chains, reduces supply disruptions.
Affordability has two sides: poverty and price increases. The NDP would offer a monthly grocery rebate for low-to-mid income families ($122 for family of four) for "as long as needed" and would double ODSP and OW rates. They'd also introduce a consumer watchdog office and pass legislation that requires large retailers to advertise price increases of more than 2 per cent in a week (as they currently advertise price cuts). They'd remove tolls on publicly managed Highway 407 and privately owned 407 ETR.
To address the housing crisis, the NDP say they would decrease renovictions; close loopholes in rent control that have arisen since 2018; and build 60,000 units of supportive housing to reduce encampments, now in communities across Ontario. They also promise to create a new public agency to provide low-cost financing for "deeply affordable" housing.
The NDP has also made a range of promises relating to health care jobs. They've promised to recruit 3,500 new doctors and 15,000 more nurses. They'd end the use of private sector nursing staff agencies and ensure wage parity across health-care settings to reduce outflow of staff from lower- to higher-paid settings for the same qualifications. They also say they'd expedite existing applications to create family health teams (at least 100 have been waiting for approval for months). They'd freeze taxes on the 98.2 per cent of Ontarians making less than $220,000, and raise taxes on luxury homes (over $3 million).
Progressive Conservatives
Focus on Ford as the best person to stand up to Trump.
Dubbed "Captain Canada" after answering Trump's tariff threats with full-throated responses, he threatened to cut off energy exports to the U.S. (and backed off) in December; proclaimed he would "hit back twice as hard" (with no clear actions) in January; flip-flopped on tearing up a Starlink deal with Elon Musk within hours in February; and wore a "Canada is not for sale" cap after Trump suggested Canada become the 51st state.

At the leader's debate on Feb. 17, Ford repeatedly stated governments can't fix anything - health, housing, education - without a strong economy. The economy's strength is sapped by constant uncertainty. Ford's solution is more is more tax cuts, more capital spending, mostly on transportation and policing.
New tax cuts lean heavily toward business: $14 billion this year ($10 billion for six-month deferral of provincially administered taxes on Ontario businesses; $3 billion to reduce payroll taxes, $600 million to incentivize investments, $300 million for a manufacturing tax credit). The PCs would also extend the 5.7 cents-a-litre reduction in gas taxes, in place since July 2022 (roughly $1 billion) and cut tolls on the provincially operated part of Highway 407 (around $72 million).
You should have your tax rebate (a $200 cheque for 15 million Ontarians, costing $3 billion), announced before Trump's election, by now.
New spends announced during this campaign include a 55 km traffic expressway tunnel under Highway 401 from Mississauga to Markham area (no price estimate, no ETA); a police academy ($1 billion - the same cost as renovating Queen's Park, which was delayed); police helicopters and water bombers ($580 million); $22 billion more over the next three years for the 10-year Building Ontario Fund ($15 billion is for roads, $5 billion for infrastructure, and $2 billion for municipal supports to housing). Finally, they added $1.4 billion to the existing $400 million to create 305 new primary care teams (unspecified number of doctors) in four years, after the other parties' proposals were public. It is unclear how these measures relate to or offset the effects of Trump's threats and the economic challenges that lie ahead.
The PCs haven't offered a platform, costing, or budget for the post-Trump moment. There's only Ford's word.
Each opposition party offers a plan for the economic emergency we are in, from modest (the Liberals' four priorities) to transformative (the NDP and the Greens).
Personal tax cuts appeal to the Liberals and Greens, but the Greens target their cuts on the poor while raising taxes from the rich. The Greens and NDP favour delimited tax breaks for investments, while the Liberals favour halving taxes for small business.
All opposition parties say improving economic resilience is critical for weathering the coming storm. But that demands improving social infrastructure - the care economy and housing - not physical infrastructure like highways.
The Progressive Conservative party is, in every sense, the outlier.
More than your vote, they want you to write a blank cheque and develop amnesia about their performance over the last seven years.
Whether kabuki theatre with Trump is enough for most Ontario voters remains to be seen.
Like the license plate says: Ontario - Yours to Discover.
Armine Yalnizyan is an
economist and the Atkinson Fellow on the Future of Workers.

Document 103 of 500

ER wait times worsened under Ford's leadership: Internal provincial report shows 'hallway medicine' has increased despite promises

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/er-wait-times-worsened-under-fords-leadership/docview/3169359518/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=ER+wait+times+worsened+under+Ford%27s+leadership%3A+Internal+provincial+report+shows+%27hallway+medicine%27+has+increased+despite+promises&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: We've all heard the stories: Your neighbour who waited almost 12 hours in agony to see if her appendix needed to be removed. An elderly woman with a suspected brain bleed lying on a stretcher for eight hours. A 99-year-old who waited four hours for an ambulance after collapsing on the floor of her retirement home.
They all got the care they needed, eventually. But the long waits they had to endure are illustrative of a disturbing trend happening in Ontario emergency departments, borne out by data that has never been made public.
According to an internal provincial report obtained by the Star, Ontario patients visiting EDs in the past three years waited longer than they did in the previous 13 years.
The report, prepared by Ontario Health but not made public, provides sweeping, multi-year data for key performance indicators going back to 2008, offering a long-term overview of the health of Ontario's EDs under successive provincial governments.
Health care in Ontario has become a big provincial election issue. Despite Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford's 2018 election promise to end hallway medicine, the data shows that wait times for ambulance off-loads, initial physician assessments and admissions to in-patient beds became notably worse in the last three years, while patients spent more time overall in EDs.
In a response to the Star, the Ministry of Health said the province's ED landscape, along with patient demographics, have evolved "in particular from the pandemic experience."
The ministry said it invested $134 million in 2023-24 to improve ED services in 165 hospitals, provided $14.7 million for a strategy to train and upskill emergency nurses, and launched a program to support physicians in small and rural hospitals. It added that it is also working to connect all residents with a family doctor through its "Primary Care Action Team," led by former federal health minister Dr. Jane Philpott.
"Wait times are really much more of a symptom than the disease itself, and more reflective of the stresses on our hospital system and our health-care system writ large," said Dr. Michael Herman, an Ottawa-area emergency physician. "We're trying to do the best with what we can, but it's an increasing struggle to try and meet the demands on our system."
The new data paints a picture of EDs across the province still struggling to recover from system-wide cracks exposed by the COVID-19 pandemic, from professional burnout, nurse shortages and increasingly complex patients, to insufficient numbers of in-patient beds, primary care options and long-term-care spaces.
Here we break down some of the most striking data from the confidential report.
ED length of stay
One of the clearest trends in the data, presented as measurements of the 90th percentile, is that between 2021-22 and October 2024 (the most recent month captured by the report), patients across Ontario spent more time in EDs compared to the previous 13 years.
The data show that patients at the 90th percentile were spending anywhere from about seven-and-a-half hours to just over 10 hours in EDs between 2008-09 and 2020-21. This means that 90 per cent of patients stayed in the ED for these lengths of time or less. But that also means 10 per cent of patients stayed longer.
During the last three years, the 90th percentile length of stay jumped to a range of about nine-and-a-half hours to a high of nearly 13 hours (January 2024). As of October 2024, length of stay at the 90th percentile was 11.7 hours.
Ottawa-based emergency physician Dr. James Worrall said most long wait times are related to what he calls the "back door" - getting patients out of the ED - rather than the number of people showing up at the front door. The problem, he said, is that when beds are full elsewhere in the hospital, admitted patients have to stay in the ED until space is available. The result? Stretchers in hallways.
"So the knock-on effect is crippling the efficiency of the emergency department, which drives up the wait times for everybody," Worrall said.
Worrall uses the analogy of someone washing the dishes at their kitchen sink who can't put the clean dishes in the drying rack.
"Very quickly your sink fills up with dishes that have already been washed and it's really challenging to wash any new dishes," he said. "That's basically what we're dealing with."
Time to initial assessment
by a doctor
The data shows that in the 13 years prior to 2021-22, most ED patients at the 90th percentile waited between about three and four hours for an initial assessment by a physician. That range rose to between four and just over four-and-a-half hours between 2021-22 and October 2024. Dr. Raghu Venugopal, an emergency physician who works the overnight shift at three busy Toronto EDs, said longer waits for an initial assessment are attributable to several causes: patients who are more elderly and medically complex; patients with lower-acuity problems but with no other option but to visit the ED; and fewer places to see patients due to overcrowding.
"An elderly patient with cognitive impairment who cannot speak for themselves and is found on the ground needs a trauma, cardiac, neurological, metabolic and infectious work-up," Venugopal said. "The share of these patients is increasing and that takes physician time. An ER visit for such a patient is pretty much a compressed admission to hospital. This makes others wait."
He added that he is seeing an increasing number of patients come to the ED with less urgent needs because they have nowhere else to go. This could include patients seeking ODSP food voucher signatures, insulin and hypertension medication and prescription refills.
"Because they have no physical complaint, they will wait."
Time to in-patient bed
In 2021-22, the 90th percentile for time to an in-patient bed began around 20 hours. This means that 90 per cent of patients spent 20 hours or less in the ED waiting for an in-patient bed once a decision was made to admit them.
While 20 hours is no doubt a long time to wait for a bed, this time was more or less in line with the trend of the previous 13 years.
But by January 2022, that time had reached 35 hours. For the next two-and-a-half years, wait times for an in-patient bed at the 90th percentile were between about 27 hours and just over 40 hours.
Herman, a member of the Canadian Association of Emergency Physicians' public affairs committee, explains that a certain percentage of beds in hospitals will be occupied by alternate-level-of-care (ALC) patients who are waiting to be discharged to long-term care or home care.
But when there aren't enough spaces in the community, these patients remain in hospital, creating backups that trickle down to the ED, which, Herman said, "is the one place in the health-care system where we can't say 'we're full.' "
This has the unfortunate result of patients waiting in ED hallways, alcoves and even in front of nursing stations bustling with activity, said Erin Ariss, a registered nurse and president of the Ontario Nurses' Association.
"You can imagine being a patient in front of a desk where it is chaos. There's phone calls, consultations between nurses and physicians," she said. "There's an inability to have any dignity or privacy in those spaces."
The Health Ministry said it has made "permanent investments" to support the creation of additional beds in regions struggling with this issue, increasing funding to the sector by four per cent in the last two fiscal years. It also says it has introduced "patient-flow initiatives" across the province.
Ambulance off-load times
The data shows that ambulance off-load times - the amount of time it takes for a patient to be transferred from paramedics to the care of a hospital emergency department - at the 90th percentile began to rise noticeably in 2021-22 from a low of about 40 minutes to a high of nearly 70 minutes.
The following year, off-load times rose even higher, reaching almost 110 minutes in October 2022, a far cry from what the province says should take 30 minutes at the 90th percentile. In 2023-24, times dropped slightly but in some months off-load times reached higher than 80 minutes.
For the first 10 months of 2024, concerted efforts by paramedic services and hospitals to target delays began to pay off, with off-load times falling back to levels seen before the pandemic.
Why is this indicator important? Ambulances waiting to off-load patients at hospitals are not able to respond to 911 calls, which can result in what some paramedic services call "Level Zero," when no ambulances are available to respond to calls.
In 2023, the Ottawa Paramedic Service, for example, was at Level Zero 1,672 times. That same year, Ottawa paramedics spent 102,105 hours in off-load delay, an increase of 8.6 per cent from 2022.
Ottawa paramedic chief Pierre Poirier notes that off-load times in Ottawa have improved significantly since October 2024 as a result of mitigation efforts, such as reducing patient transports and diverting patients away from EDs.
Off-load delays remain stubbornly high in some areas. In Toronto, for example, in-hospital wait times for paramedics at the 90th percentile from Jan. 1 to Feb. 12 this year were 111 minutes. During that time, Toronto paramedics spent more than 800 hours in hospital each day on average, up from an average of 750 hours a day during the same time frame in 2024, according to Toronto paramedic chief Bikram Chawla.
"As with other paramedic services across Ontario, in-hospital wait times for paramedics remains a significant challenge negatively impacting ambulance availability," Chawla said.
Worrall said that because EDs are often full with admitted patients, there aren't any resources available for patients who arrive in ambulances, meaning the patients must remain with the paramedics.
"I can go and talk to patients who are still with the paramedics, but I can't do a proper examination and I can't get any tests or treatments started until there is a bed available for the patient to be off-loaded," he said. "So that's obviously a real problem."
For its part, the ministry says it has increased funding for a dedicated off-load nurse program to allow hospitals to hire nurses and other health professionals whose focus is on transferring patients. This program, along with partnerships between paramedic services and hospitals, has helped contribute to a reduction in off-load times by more than 50 per cent since October 2022, the ministry said, with some regions seeing off-load times drop by close to 90 per cent.
ED patient volumes
The number of patients coming to Ontario EDs saw modest increases in the past 17 years, with fluctuations in volume ranging generally between about 400,000 and 500,000 annually.
In October 2024, ED volumes reached 516,295, about six per cent higher than October 2023.
The report notes that this increase can be attributed primarily to seriously ill patients who came to hospital but didn't need to be admitted.
While there has been a slight increase in the number of patients visiting EDs in recent years, volumes, on the whole, have not seen massive deviations for over a decade. So why are wait times getting longer?
In addition to an increase in the number of complex elderly patients presenting to the ED, as well as an insufficient number of in-patient beds, many patients arrive with multiple complaints stemming from a lack of access to family doctors, specialists or diagnostic imaging, said Venugopal.
As a result, getting in front of an ED doctor is an opportunity for a patient to have multiple problems addressed.
"People have legitimate problems piled up," Venugopal said. "You can see a patient with three to seven medical problems, easily."
CREDIT: Kenyon Wallace Toronto Star Megan Ogilvie Toronto Star

Can Ontario afford more Ford?: Please: Anyone but Doug Ford, Feb. 14 With the provincial election just days away, readers sound off on PC leader's record

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/can-ontario-afford-more-ford/docview/3169359515/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Can+Ontario+afford+more+Ford%3F%3A+Please%3A+Anyone+but+Doug+Ford%2C+Feb.+14+With+the+provincial+election+just+days+away%2C+readers+sound+off+on+PC+leader%27s+record&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=OP.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Please: Anyone but Doug Ford, Feb. 14
I am urging everyone to get out and vote on Feb. 27 - and to take a hard look at Doug Ford's failed leadership before they do so. Sure, Ford can be charming with his folksiness. But does anyone recall his cancellation of the very efficient cap-and-trade system? His plan to build a needless Highway 413? The Greenbelt fiasco he was forced to roll back? The list goes on and on.
Dave Brown, Waterloo
It is imperative that before going to the polls, Ontario voters take an unbiased look at Ford's record to date. Don't be fooled by his "one of the little guys" persona. He isn't one of us, and his record seems to indicate that he prefers the rich guys to the little guys. Why does Ford want a new mandate? I suspect the answer is simple: he doesn't want to risk having his support vanish when the RCMP investigation into the Greenbelt scandal goes public. He doesn't want his supporters to see him for what he is - a political opportunist, first and foremost.
Voters should take a critical look at the implications of giving Ford what he wants. Canada's trade war with the United States probably won't last four years, meaning Ford would have plenty of time to carry out his costly pie-in-the-sky ideas with a new mandate.
How many Americans wish they had taken a critical second look at the implications of putting their "X" beside Trump's name and are now living with the consequences? Let's not make the same mistake.
Patricia Steward, Toronto
As a Torontonian of 45 years, I am amazed and shocked to see that there are Ontarians who believe Doug Ford will have any influence on Canada-U.S. trade negotiations or anything else of importance to our way of life or our future. He's no more than a minor player. His trip to Washington was a waste of time and money, not to mention an insult to every Ontarian. Former premier David Peterson tried the early election ruse in 1990, but voters saw through it. Hopefully, Trump's bombast will not blind Ontarians to Ford's brand of opportunism 35 years later.
Ken Luckhardt, Toronto
Justin Ling has voiced the sentiments I've held since the 2022 election. If Ford is the best we've got to lead the province through difficult times, then we are in serious trouble. Everything he touches seems to be fraught with issues and costly to taxpayers. Ask yourself whether you're any better off now than you were seven years ago, when Ford was first elected. So many things in Ontario seem to have gotten worse since then, such as the cost of living, our health-care system (including a lack of family doctors and insufficient hospital funding), our education system, public transit and housing.
Brian Drake, Toronto
Ontario residents have been receiving their $200 "vote for me" cheques in the mail. In the televised leaders' debate this week, Ford again called it a rebate of taxpayers' own money, though the government was already running a projected $3-billion deficit. This so-called rebate is more like an extra $3.1-billion loan to be repaid with interest by Ontario taxpayers and their children over decades. Respect for taxpayers' money is nothing but an empty Ford slogan that voters should reject.
Mark Goldstein, Mississauga

Subject: Voters

Location: United States--US; Canada

Document 105 of 500

The choice on health: Every party has a plan to address Ontario's dire doctor shortage. Here's how they stack up

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/choice-on-health/docview/3169359500/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+choice+on+health%3A+Every+party+has+a+plan+to+address+Ontario%27s+dire+doctor+shortage.+Here%27s+how+they+stack+up&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=OP.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Retired family physician Marlene Spruyt, the Green Party candidate for Lanark-Frontenac-Kingston, remembers a time when she called up a home care nurse for a dying patient. "We delivered palliative care to a woman who was blind, and she got to stay at home and die," she says. "Now, you can't deliver that quality of care."
For the 2.5 million Ontarians without a family doctor (up from 1.8 million in 2020), Spruyt's memory sounds like myth. But that level of care could return, if only leaders would summon up the political will.
Ontarians are now being asked to choose a new leader. Progressive Conservative leader Doug Ford called a snap election for Feb. 27 in response to the threat of U.S. tariffs 16 months before an election was due. But according to a recent Nanos survey, nearly 30 per cent of voters say health care concerns will dictate how they cast their ballots.
The Nanos poll shows 28 per cent of voters favour the Liberals on health care while 26 per cent support Ford. The NDP trails at 21 per cent.
Perhaps most damning of all is the Nanos finding that one in five Ontarians don't trust any party on health care.
Ontarians know just how precarious the health system is. For two decades, governments of all stripes have failed to address the demands of an exploding, aging population.
The Ontario Medical Association predicts that by 2026 - that's next year - 4.4 million people won't have a family doctor. The OMA says Ontario needs 3,500 new family doctors now.
It gets worse. Close to 40 per cent of family doctors are nearing retirement. Poor pay combined with runaway inflation make running the business of family practice financially unsustainable. The falling number of medical school seats per capita means a shortage of doctors coming down the pipeline. And that's just the family doctor crisis.
The province also needs to fund new hospitals, maintain existing ones, and pay for seniors' long-term care.
Addressing health care is a gargantuan task, but Ford's majority government has had seven years to fix the mess. Last year, the Ministry of Health infuriated doctors by stating that the recruitment and retention of Ontario physicians was "not a major concern."
In 2024, the federal government transferred $3.1 billion to Ontario for health care. Last fall, Ford recruited former federal Liberal health minister Jane Philpott to implement her Primary Care Action Plan.
But this anemic, late-in-the-day proposal calls for just $1.8 billion to connect two million Ontarians to 305 vaguely defined primary care teams in a school-board type model based on residential postal codes.
Her plan emphasizes workforce retention and easing administrative burden but is sketchy on financial details. It also adds 500 new medical school seats by 2028.
The Greens, who boast only two MPPs, present a bolder platform. They say they'll recruit 3,500 family doctors. According to Marlene Spruyt, this plan represents a stark contrast from the PCs.
Spruyt cites the various ways Ford has failed to shore up health care, including lost savings from breaking a contract with The Beer Store. For a guy who says he doesn't drink, she calls his beer fixation "bizarre."
"What has Doug Ford done right?" she asks.
Spruyt says municipalities shouldn't have to fund health care. She wants the government to pay half the cost to build hospitals in towns like Bracebridge, where rich donors are scarce. Nobody asks citizens to chip in for a new police car, she adds.
For her part, NDP Leader Marit Stiles has pledged to spend $4 billion over four years to bring in 3,500 family doctors and 15,000 nurses to work in teams.
The party's health critic France Gelinas represents the northern riding of Nickel Belt, where 30,000 residents lack a family doctor.
She says the Ford government has knowingly allowed conditions to deteriorate, and adds that Ford's government listens to investors, while the NDP listens to front-line staff.
Gelinas says the NDP respects family physicians.
"They've been providing quality primary care for a very long time. They need to be paid fairly."
She's also frustrated that although Sudbury's Northern Ontario School of Medicine can increase its capacity from 56 to 100 undergraduate seats "right now," the government won't release the funding yet.
As for Bonnie Crombie's Liberals, they have also vowed to increase the number of physicians, promising to pair every Ontarian with a family doctor-led team of allied health professionals by spending $3.1 billion to "attract and retain 3,100 family doctors" by 2029. They will double medical school spots, entice 2,400 aging doctors to delay retirement and invest $250 million to recruit 650 doctors who've decamped elsewhere for better pay and less paperwork back to family practice.
Liberal Adil Shamji, the MPP for Don Valley East who, he says, is "the only elected physician" at Queen's Park says the government's track record speaks for itself, with lukewarm promises coming years too late. He says Ford found money for everything but health care, including $3.2 billion - which could pay for the Liberal health platform and then some - on $200 taxpayer rebates.
Shamji says Ontarians pay taxes for family doctors and worries that to cut costs the current government is diluting the definition of primary care by trying to "convince Ontarians that if they have a pharmacist who can prescribe for minor ailments, that counts as ... primary care, and it doesn't."
These juicy promises from political parties still trailing the incumbent in the polls sound like a Christmas wish list for patients and doctors alike.
Adam Stewart, who practices comprehensive family medicine in Madoc, a rural town halfway between Toronto and Ottawa, believes that money is the priority.
He sits on the board of the OMA's Section on General and Family Practice. Only 65 per cent of family doctors are left in comprehensive, longitudinal practices offering cradle to grave care. They're incurring "20, maybe 30 per cent increases" in overhead from post-pandemic inflation balanced against one per cent annual fee increases. Stewart says government needs to bump up family doctor compensation to be in line with BC and other provinces.
He also wants Ontario to recognize family doctors as specialists in looking after patients from birth to death. They're ready to assume higher patient loads by leading multi-professional teams.
Stewart also has some more unconventional recommendations. Put the joy back into medicine, he says, by providing doctors with AI technology for scribes. Implement a centralized referral system for rapid access to specialists and MRIs.
These reforms are crucial, says Stewart. "If things don't get fixed," he says, if they continue down the same sad trajectory, he might have to leave comprehensive practice, too.
Merrilee Fullerton, a retired family doctor and two-time Progressive Conservative MPP from Kanata-Carleton calls the Ford government's $1.8-billion offer "a hail Mary pass."
Fullerton says too often, governments laud shiny baubles, like AI, that are distractions from the fact that family doctors are the glue that holds the system together. She resigned her seat in 2023 because of "tremendous frustration" with the government's lackadaisical approach to health care. Fullerton wants policymakers, bureaucrats, and politicians to think ahead. She calls making hasty health care election promises, then forgetting them till the next election cycle "irresponsible."
In April 2005, faced with family doctor shortages, Liberal premier Dalton McGuinty announced new Family Health Teams to serve 2.5 million Ontarians.
But when family doctors tried to join those teams, McGuinty and his successor Kathleen Wynne froze the funding. It was too expensive. Some lucky Ontarians got team-based care, while millions more didn't and even fewer do now. McGuinty also imposed the Health Premium tax in 2004, a levy of up to $900 that Ontarians still pay annually.
There's nothing new here. Then, as now, Ottawa injected cash into Ontario's coffers. Then, as now, governments promised primary care teams to solve provincial health care woes. Will much-touted team funding be frozen again?
Not one Ontario political party in the past 20 years has demonstrated the grit needed to fix health care. The Progressive Conservatives still lead the Liberals by 15 percentage points. Barring an electoral miracle, Ford seems destined to be a three-time premier.
Patients are tired of not having family doctors to give their newborns their childhood shots, and are tired of waiting months to see the orthopedic surgeon while their knees crumble. Doctors are tired of working longer, harder, faster to keep their patients healthy with shrinking resources.
Adam Stewart thinks the solution is giving doctors more autonomy, and more responsibility. What he wants is for governments to free family doctors to do what they do best: looking after patients by leading their own teams. His solution might be the cure for our collective health care malaise.
Ferrukh Faruqui is an
Ottawa physician and
freelance journalist.
4.4M
How many people the Ontario Medical Association predicts won't have a family doctor by
2026
CREDIT: Ferrukh Faruqui Contributor;

Document 107 of 500

Ford back in Washington to win allies: PC leader renews appeal to U.S. lawmakers about Trump's tariffs, but admits it's a daunting task

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-back-washington-win-allies/docview/3169359482/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+back+in+Washington+to+win+allies%3A+PC+leader+renews+appeal+to+U.S.+lawmakers+about+Trump%27s+tariffs%2C+but+admits+it%27s+a+daunting+task&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A frustrated Doug Ford admitted he's fighting an uphill battle to change the hearts and minds of American lawmakers in his clash against U.S. President Donald Trump's threatened tariffs.
Buoyed by Canada's nail-biting overtime victory over Team U.S.A. in the 4 Nations hockey tournament, the Progressive Conservative leader and Nova Scotia Premier Tim Houston attended the winter meeting of the National Governors Association with bragging rights but a daunting task.
"No one down here knows what he wants, and we don't know what he wants," Ford said of Trump.
"What's shocking to me is how misinformed a lot of elected officials down here (are), how critical a role Canada plays in their economy," added Ford, speaking of electricity exports, critical minerals, steel and aluminum.
Ford's meetings with several governors were delayed after their meeting with Trump at the White House ran long.
Gov. Andy Beshear of Kentucky suggested state leaders from one party tend to be more sympathetic to Canadian concerns about tariffs and trade.
"Our Democratic governors certainly believe that we shouldn't be looking at a general tariff against Canada," added the second-term Democrat who said tariffs would push prices up for consumers in a "Trump tax."
North Dakota Gov. Kelly Armstrong, a former Republican congressman, said he believes Trump is using tariffs as a "negotiating tool" toward a renewed Canada-U.S.-Mexico free trade agreement.
"The White House wants to reset the deck and wants to bring manufacturing back home. And I think that's a great idea."
Neither governor thought much of Trump's repeated taunts about Canada becoming the 51st state.
"I love hunting in Canada, and I love my neighbours to the north, but I'm not particularly sure that's the world's greatest idea," said Armstrong, whose state borders Manitoba and Saskatchewan.
"Canada is a proud country. We ought to respect that relationship. I hope the people of Canada know that is not how most Americans feel," said Beshear.
He is concerned retaliatory tariffs could damage his state's bourbon industry, but noted its biggest exports to the north are aerospace and automotive products.
Trump stood firm on his plan for tariffs and making Canada the 51st state in a dinner speech to Republican governors Thursday night. Houston, a Progressive Conservative, was there in his red team Canada jersey.
There were few 51st state jokes, he said at a downtown hotel where the governors are meeting to discuss bipartisan national issues.
With Ford's lead in public opinion polls apparently narrowing, he is eager to back up his campaign premise that he would be better than Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and NDP Leader Marit Stiles to handle the tariff threat that could devastate Ontario's and Canada's economies while putting millions of American jobs at risk.
He met with the governors of New York, New Jersey, Vermont, Georgia, West Virginia and Nebraska as well as Canadian Ambassador Kirsten Hillman. Ford told the Star New York Gov. Kathy Hochul is interested in Ontario's development of small modular nuclear reactors to meet the state's carbon-free electricity needs.
It was his second Washington lobbying trip in just over a week.
Opposition leaders have mocked Ford's claims he is best to fight Trump tariffs after a hot-mic gaffe early in the campaign where he told people he "100 per cent" supported Trump to win a second term as president despite his campaign promises of tariffs.
In Etobicoke, where she was touting a platform that adds $9 billion in new spending to build schools and improve access to family doctors, Crombie mocked Ford's latest D.C. trip.
"Another photo opportunity (with) low-level advisers?" said Crombie, maintaining the Tory leader should be home fighting the $189-million snap election he triggered instead of waiting until the next scheduled vote in June 2026.
Later in a meeting with the Toronto Star editorial board, Crombie said Ford should be more worried about the 2.5 million Ontarians that don't have a family doctor than glad-handing in Washington.
Meanwhile, the Liberals parted ways with candidate Viresh Bansal in Oshawa over concerns about a homophobic comment and hateful remarks about Sikhs.
The riding has been held by New Democrat Jennifer French since 2014.
The New Democrats released the party's full platform that contained about $70 billion in new spending over the next three years. The spending would go toward a grocery rebate, boosting the number of doctors, and building affordable housing with an additional $830 million over 10 years for school capital improvements.
To help offset those costs, an NDP government would raise income taxes on people earning more than $300,000 a year, impose a "luxury tax" on residences costing $3 million or more and treat capital gains as income.
CAPTION: Doug Ford steps off his campaign bus at a manufacturing facility in Milton on Thursday. In Washington on Friday, Ford met with the governors of New York, New Jersey, Vermont, Georgia, West Virginia and Nebraska,as well as Canadian Ambassador Kirsten Hillman.Chris Young The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson, Robert Benzie and Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Document 108 of 500

Experts weigh in on Ford's 401 tunnel: The Star asked transit professionals about PC leader's idea to improve GTA traffic

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/experts-weigh-on-fords-401-tunnel/docview/3169359477/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Experts+weigh+in+on+Ford%27s+401+tunnel%3A+The+Star+asked+transit+professionals+about+PC+leader%27s+idea+to+improve+GTA+traffic&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford's tunnel vision has no off-ramp.
Amid scorn from political rivals, and warnings from transportation experts, the leader of Ontario's Progressive Conservatives is adamant that, if re-elected premier Feb. 27, his government will tunnel road lanes, and possibly a transit line, under at least 50 kilometres of Highway 401.
Ford says the tunnel would span Toronto and extend west to Brampton, but has offered few other details, saying his party is awaiting a feasibility study to know the potential cost and construction timeline. What Ford says he does know is that drivers like the idea.
"The easiest way to know if someone commutes on the 401, ask them what they think about building a tunnel," Ford said recently.
"These hard-working families, they just want to get to work, get to soccer practice after work, get home, without the headache of bumper-to-bumper traffic from one end of the 401 to the other."
At Monday's leader debate, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie launched a blistering attack on what she called "a fantasy project" that will take 40 years to build, do nothing to alleviate congestion and "bankrupt the province."
Some observers think the estimated tunnel cost could be as much $120 billion, which would gobble so much of the province's capital budget that it would be a non-starter.
Sometimes, politically, it doesn't matter. John Tory campaigned for mayor of Toronto in 2014 promising SmartTrack, a "surface subway" using existing rail lines, that engaged voters and helped him get elected - but now barely exists.
The Star asked three transit experts - Shoshanna Saxe, a civil engineer and infrastructure expert at U of T; Murtaza Haider, an expert in the economics and cities and regions who is research director of the Urban Analytics Institute; and Matti Siemiatycki, director of the U of T's Infrastructure Institute - key questions about Ford's tunnel vision. (Some answers have been edited for brevity and clarity.)
1. Based on what we know, how long do you think such a tunnel would take and how much could it cost?
Saxe It would take a decade or longer to do ground investigation, design the tunnel and then build it. It's possible that even if we were really gung-ho about it, it would open in the 2040s. At the moment, it's a fictional project so there's a lot of uncertainty. It would cost tens of billions of dollars but you need to plan it out and, at this point, it's just a thing Doug Ford has talked about.
Haider The construction of a tunnel spanning 60 kilometres or more - connecting Mississauga and Brampton in the west to Scarborough or beyond in the east - would likely face prohibitive financial and temporal challenges. Preliminary cost estimates for tunnelling in urban environments range from $250 million to over $1 billion per kilometre, though precise figures remain speculative without geotechnical surveys, environmental assessments and feasibility studies. Timelines for such megaprojects are inherently uncertain ... A fully underground tunnel accommodating both automotive and transit traffic could span decades to complete.
Siemiatycki I wouldn't even try to estimate this because whatever I say, the tunnel is going to take longer and cost more. You could do an internal cost estimate, where you take all the details of the project and build it bottom up, or you could benchmark it relative to the cost-per-kilometre to build other similar projects around the world. Either way, we're into the tens of billions of dollars and above. Toronto is a high-cost jurisdiction - our transit projects are costing $1.5 billion per kilometre.
2. If a tunnel is built under Highway 401 spanning the city and beyond, would it eventually make car commuting faster and if so by how much?
Saxe No, it won't make car commuting faster. For nearly 100 years, we've checked if adding road miles speeds up how fast people get to drive. Sometimes it does for a couple of years, but within five years everyone's back to driving the same speed as before - there's just more people stuck in traffic. It's also unclear if Ford wants to build a bypass, where you have to go one end to the other, or with entrances and exits that add congestion because it slows people down. Most people driving that stretch of Highway 401 are stopping somewhere between those 50 kilometres.
Haider The proposed tunnel aims to address unmet travel demand generated by decades of population growth and socioeconomic activity that existing road and transit networks cannot fully accommodate. While the tunnel would enhance throughput capacity - moving more people and vehicles during peak periods - its impact on reducing travel times across congested corridors like Highway 401 is expected to be marginal. Capacity expansion should be framed as a mechanism to absorb latent demand (travel not currently realized due to constraints) rather than a solution to congestion. Failure to address latent demand risks exacerbating gridlock and stifling economic productivity.
Siemiatycki Anyone commuting now - this tunnel project is not going to solve your commute, this is a decades-long project. You will very likely be retired by the time this is completed. That aside, what we've found with these big highway projects is that they solve the project in the short term, because they do add capacity, but they shift the bottlenecks to elsewhere in the network.
3. How much disruption would tunnel digging create? Is it possible to dig underneath without impacting traffic on the busy highway above?
Saxe Technically it is possible to dig with very little interaction at the surface. However, do we want any entrances or exits or air valves? Construction of them would cause disruption and traffic during tunnel construction. Given Ford is talking about building right under Highway 401, I'm guessing there would be a lot of traffic impacts above for a long time.
Haider A project of this scale would necessitate extensive engineering interventions, including large-scale earth removal, soil disposal, and logistical co-ordination of heavy machinery and labour. Numerous access and egress points would compound disruptions to surface-level traffic and adjacent land uses, potentially persisting throughout the multidecade construction period. Historical precedents, such as Boston's "Big Dig" - which saw costs escalate from $2 billion to $20 billion - highlight risks of budgetary overruns and scope creep, underscoring the need for cautious fiscal and operational planning.
Siemiatycki They can tunnel underneath it but, just like with the ongoing subway digging projects, the dirt has to come out somewhere. With a project of this scale there is going to be a huge amount of earth that will need to be trucked away somewhere.
All of those trucks create their own traffic and congestion, as well as safety concerns as we've seen with Ontario Line trucks. Even if you're digging underground, it's never impact-free above.
4. If Toronto has multi-billions of dollars to spend on reducing gridlock and commute times, what is the most effective use of those dollars?
Saxe The absolute most effective thing would be to invest in much better public transit. In the short term, more bus rapid transit (BRT, in dedicated lanes) and many more bike lanes can help reduce gridlock. In the long term, you can invest in more commuter rail transit. Something that wouldn't cost money, but would reduce gridlock, is better zoning and land-use planning to ensure growing communities are oriented to transit so those residents don't have to drive everywhere.
Haider A more pragmatic approach to enhancing regional mobility lies in prioritizing high-capacity bus rapid transit systems. Elevated BRT corridors, operating along Highway 401's median with articulated buses in platooned formations, could provide dedicated right-of-way at a fraction of tunnelling costs.
Such systems could integrate with existing transit networks via underground interchanges or elevated pedestrian bridges, as exemplified by elevated rail-pedestrian linkages in New York City.
Siemiatycki We are tunnelling now for public transit. Part of the reason projects are expensive here is we try to put many of them underground, in some cases where it's not the right tool. If I had tens of billions of dollars I would continue to invest in public transit and, as that transit comes online, add road tolls as a way to manage construction. I would also immediately add a bus lane to make commuting that way much quicker. In the leaders' debate, Doug Ford acknowledged tolls actually work because he talked about the potential impact of taking tolls off Highway 407, that it would get busier.
CAPTION: Construction vehicles dig in Boston in 2000 as part of the Central Artery project. Originally expected to cost $2 billion, the underground highway's pricetag ballooned to $20 billion.MICHAEL DWYER the Associated Press file photo
CREDIT: David Rider Senior Politics Reporter

Subject: Automobiles; Construction; Commuting; Public transportation; Tolls; Traffic; Roads & highways; Cost estimates

Business indexing term: Subject: Cost estimates

Location: Toronto Ontario Canada; Canada

People: Ford, Doug

All four provincial party leaders have now put forth plans to support Ontario through U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff plans along with pledges to improve affordability, build schools and more.

Document 110 of 500

Donald Trump reinforces threats of tariffs, Canada as 51st state on eve of Doug Ford's latest trip to Washington

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/donald-trump-reinforces-threats-tariffs-canada-as/docview/3169244318/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Donald+Trump+reinforces+threats+of+tariffs%2C+Canada+as+51st+state+on+eve+of+Doug+Ford%27s+latest+trip+to+Washington&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: WASHINGTON-President Donald Trump reinforced his tariff threat on the eve of Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford's second visit in two weeks to fight punishing levies that would devastate Canada's economy.
Speaking to Republican governors who Ford - entering this last week of Ontario's Feb. 27 election campaign - hopes to influence Friday at the winter meeting of the National Governors Association, Trump maintained there's only one way out for Canada.
"They have to become the 51st state," he said in an increasingly familiar refrain at the National Building Museum, a short motorcade drive east of the White House.
Ford, who was caught in a hot mic gaffe earlier this month saying he cheered for Trump to win despite tariff threats, has based his campaign for a third term as premier on being the best leader to take on Trump and is asking voters for a "stronger mandate."
But he could face an uphill battle with some of the 27 red state governors in the Republican association, out of 50 states, pledging to back Trump's agenda.
"Now, our states can support - and work hand-in-hand to implement - the Trump agenda that the American people voted overwhelmingly to support," association leader Gov. Brian Kemp of Georgia, who introduced Trump to the governors' dinner, wrote on FoxNews.com this week.
Ford said before leaving for Washington "we have to be eyes wide open" in dealing with Trump's threatened "economic chaos."
Along with Nova Scotia Premier Tim Houston, Ford will mix and mingle with state governors, Republican and Democrat, pressing their case against tariffs that would hurt workers on both sides of the border.
The National Governors Association meets to discuss bilateral issues of national importance, and it is not unusual for premiers to attend.
Trump claimed the U.S. will soon see "hundreds of billions of dollars in investments" from companies hoping to avoid tariffs of up to 25 per cent or more on products they import from Canada and other countries.
"I've been called by many big companies ... they want to beat the tariffs. And there's only one way to beat the tariffs," he said.
"All you have to do is build your plants in the United States," Trump added, noting American autoworkers would be "one of the biggest beneficiaries" if automakers move factories to the U.S.
In an alarming new development, the future of the Stellantis assembly plant in Brampton is on tenterhooks after the parent company of Jeep and Chrysler said Thursday it is "pausing" work on its next generation of Jeep Compass models. Production was to begin later this year with 2,200 employees who have been on the sidelines while the plant, which used to make muscle cars, is retooled.
Turning to sports, Trump referred to several NHL games where Canadian fans let their displeasure be known with U.S. teams, and the 4 Nations hockey tournament that saw Canada and the U.S. in a tense faceoff Thursday night with political overtones.
"You heard the people booing the national anthem, but I think ultimately they'll be praising the national anthem," said Trump, again referring to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau as "Governor Trudeau."
Houston was pictured on social media sporting a Team Canada jersey for his trip.
The Canadian government added seven transnational criminal groups to its terror list Thursday in hopes Trump would back off his tariff plan, in addition to other measures strengthening the border and appointing a "fentanyl czar" to stem to flow of drugs.
CAPTION: U.S. President Donald Trump delivers remarks at the Republican Governors Association meeting in Washington on Thursday.
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson

Ontario election polls show Doug Ford's PCs holding steady

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-election-polls-show-doug-fords-pcs/docview/3169163414/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+election+polls+show+Doug+Ford%27s+PCs+holding+steady&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With the Ontario election campaign underway, the Star has a poll tracker so you can follow all the twists and turns ahead of election day on Feb. 27.
We've got the numbers from the top pollsters in one place, with handy charts, and a rolling, last-four-poll average of all the results.
Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives are still in first place and holding steady. The Liberals remain in second place.

In Ontario's last election, in 2022, Ford's PCs took 40.8 per cent of the popular vote across the province, with the Liberals earning 23.9 per cent and the NDP taking 23.7 per cent. That led Ford to his second straight majority government, with 83 seats. Despite finishing narrowly behind the Liberals in the popular vote, the NDP took 31 seats and the Liberals just eight.
The Star is compiling polling for the 2025 Ontario election from polling firms that received a grade of A or A+ from 338Canada during the 2022 Ontario election. 338Canada is an independent media organization that created a statistical model of electoral projections based on opinion polls, electoral history, and demographic data.
These polling firms use a variety of polling methods, including online surveys and Smart Interactive Voice Response (IVR) systems. As online polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, margins listed here are for a comparable random sample of this size.
Percentage breakdowns are shares of committed or leaning voters, in line with industry conventions.
CAPTION: Clockwise from top: Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford, Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner, NDP Leader Marit Stiles.

Document 112 of 500

Frustrated Doug Ford admits it's tough convincing U.S. lawmakers to fight Donald Trump's tariffs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/frustrated-doug-ford-admits-tough-convincing-u-s/docview/3169163151/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Frustrated+Doug+Ford+admits+it%27s+tough+convincing+U.S.+lawmakers+to+fight+Donald+Trump%27s+tariffs&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: WASHINGTON-A frustrated Doug Ford admitted he's fighting an uphill battle to change the hearts and minds of American lawmakers in his clash against President Donald Trump's threatened tariffs.
Buoyed by Canada's nail-biting overtime victory over Team U.S.A. in the 4 Nations hockey tournament, the Progressive Conservative leader and Nova Scotia Premier Tim Houston attended the winter meeting of the National Governors Association with bragging rights but a daunting task.
"No one down here knows what he wants, and we don't know what he wants," Ford said of Trump.
"What's shocking to me is how misinformed a lot of elected officials down here (are), how critical a role Canada plays in their economy," added Ford, speaking of electricity exports, critical minerals, steel and aluminum.
"When we tell them all this, they're like, 'Wow, we didn't realize this.'"
Houston added: "We're doing what we can under the circumstances."
Ford's meetings with several governors were delayed after their meeting with Trump at the White House ran long.
Gov. Andy Beshear of Kentucky suggested state leaders from one party tend to be more sympathetic to Canadian concerns about tariffs and trade.
"Our Democratic governors certainly believe that we shouldn't be looking at a general tariff against Canada," added the second-term Democrat who said tariffs would push prices up for consumers in a "Trump tax."
North Dakota Gov. Kelly Armstrong, a former Republican congressman, said he believes Trump is using tariffs as a "negotiating tool" toward a renewed Canada-U.S.-Mexico free trade agreement.
"The White House wants to reset the deck and wants to bring manufacturing back home. And I think that's a great idea."
Neither governor thought much of Trump's repeated taunts about Canada becoming the 51st state.
"I love hunting in Canada, and I love my neighbours to the north but I'm not particularly sure that's the world's greatest idea," said Armstrong, whose state borders Manitoba and Saskatchewan.
"Canada is a proud country. We ought to respect that relationship. I hope the people of Canada know that is not how most Americans feel," said Beshear.
He is concerned retaliatory tariffs could damage his state's bourbon industry, but noted its biggest exports to the north are aerospace and automotive products.
"In Kentucky, you have two Republican senators saying we shouldn't be doing this, as well as one Democratic governor," he added, speaking of tariffs.
Trump stood firm on his plan for tariffs and making Canada the 51st state in a dinner speech to Republican governors Thursday night. Houston, a Progressive Conservative, was there in his red team Canada jersey.
There were few 51st state jokes, he said at a downtown hotel where the governors are meeting to discuss bipartisan national issues.
"I got a lot more fist bumps and 'good luck tonight.'"
With Ford's lead in public opinion polls apparently narrowing, he is eager to back up his campaign premise that he would be better than Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and NDP Leader Marit Stiles to handle the tariff threat that could devastate Ontario's and Canada's economies while putting millions of American jobs at risk.
He met with the governors of New York, New Jersey, Vermont, Georgia, West Virginia and Nebraska as well as Canadian Ambassador Kirsten Hillman. Ford told the Star New York Gov. Kathy Hochul is interested in Ontario's development of small modular nuclear reactors to meet the state's carbon-free electricity needs.
It was his second Washington lobbying trip in just over a week.
Opposition leaders have mocked Ford's claims he is best to fight Trump tariffs after a hot mic gaffe early in the campaign where he told people he "100 per cent" supported Trump to win a second term as president despite his campaign promises of tariffs.
In Etobicoke, where she was touting a platform that adds $9 billion in new spending to build schools and improve access to family doctors, Crombie mocked Ford's latest D.C. trip.
"Another photo opportunity (with) low-level advisers?" said Crombie, maintaining the Tory leader should be home fighting the $189-million snap election he triggered instead of waiting until the next scheduled vote in June 2026.
Later in a meeting with the Toronto Star editorial board, Crombie said Ford should be more worried about the 2.5 million Ontarians that don't have a family doctor than glad-handing in Washington.
Meanwhile, the Liberals parted ways with candidate Viresh Bansal in Oshawa over concerns about a homophobic comment and hateful remarks about Sikhs. The riding has been held by New Democrat Jennifer French since 2014.
The New Democrats released the party's full platform that contained about $70 billion in new spending over the next three years. The spending would go toward a grocery rebate, boosting the number of doctors, and building affordable housing with an additional $830 million over 10 years for school capital improvements.
To help offset those costs, an NDP government would raise income taxes on people earning more than $300,000 a year, impose a "luxury tax" on residences costing $3 million or more and treat capital gains as income.
CAPTION: Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie released her party's full platform with less than a week to go until election day. It promises $65 billion in new spending over four years, along with finding more than $28 billion in efficiencies, and the Liberals say the plan contains no new taxes or tax increases. (Feb. 21, 2025 / The Canadian Press)Doug Ford steps off his campaign bus as he makes a campaign stop at HPG, a manufacturing facility in Milton on Thursday.
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson, Robert Benzie, Kristin Rushowy
