Document 114 of 500

Trump reinforces his threats of tariffs, 51st state: Spoke to governors on eve of Ford's meeting with them

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trump-reinforces-his-threats-tariffs-51st-state/docview/3169001131/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trump+reinforces+his+threats+of+tariffs%2C+51st+state%3A+Spoke+to+governors+on+eve+of+Ford%27s+meeting+with+them&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: U.S. President Donald Trump reinforced his tariff threat on the eve of Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford's second visit in two weeks to fight punishing levies that would devastate Canada's economy.
Speaking to Republican governors who Ford - entering this last week of Ontario's Feb. 27 election campaign - hopes to influence Friday at the winter meeting of the National Governors Association, Trump maintained there's only one way out for Canada.
"They have to become the 51st state," he said in an increasingly familiar refrain at the National Building Museum.
Ford, who was caught in a hot mic gaffe this month saying he cheered for Trump to win despite tariff threats, has based his campaign for a third term as premier on being the best leader to take on Trump and is asking voters for a "stronger mandate."
But he could face an uphill battle with some of the 27 red state governors in the Republican association, out of 50 states, pledging to back Trump's agenda.
"Now, our states can support - and work hand-in-hand to implement - the Trump agenda that the American people voted overwhelmingly to support," association leader Gov. Brian Kemp of Georgia, who introduced Trump to the governors' dinner, wrote on FoxNews.com this week.
Along with Nova Scotia Premier Tim Houston, Ford will mix and mingle with state governors, Republican and Democrat, pressing their case against tariffs that would hurt workers on both sides of the border.
Trump claimed the U.S. will soon see "hundreds of billions of dollars in investments" from companies hoping to avoid tariffs of up to 25 per cent or more on products they import from Canada and other countries.
"I've been called by many big companies ... they want to beat the tariffs. And there's only one way to beat the tariffs," he said.
"All you have to do is build your plants in the United States," Trump added, noting American autoworkers would be "one of the biggest beneficiaries" if automakers move factories to the U.S.
In an alarming new development, the future of the Stellantis assembly plant in Brampton is on tenterhooks after the parent company of Jeep and Chrysler said Thursday it is "pausing" work on its next generation of Jeep Compass models.
Turning to sports, Trump referred to several NHL games where Canadian fans let their displeasure be known with U.S. teams, and the 4 Nations hockey tournament that saw Canada and the U.S. in a tense faceoff Thursday night with political overtones.
"You heard the people booing the national anthem, but I think ultimately they'll be praising the national anthem," said Trump, again referring to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau as "Governor Trudeau."
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Document 115 of 500

Plenty of hypocrisy around free speech

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/plenty-hypocrisy-around-free-speech/docview/3169000463/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Plenty+of+hypocrisy+around+free+speech&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.15&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: On Day One of the second term of his presidency, Donald Trump signed an executive order titled "Restoring Freedom of Speech and Ending Federal Censorship." He then doubled down on efforts to do exactly the opposite.
In the four-and-a-half weeks since that order, Trump and his allies have waged a campaign of what can only be described as censorship - muzzling government scientists, public health officials and diplomats; scrubbing words like "diversity," "gender" and "pronouns" from government websites in the name of fighting "wokeness"; and punishing news organizations that don't play along with the president's latest delusion.
That last move stands out for its sheer bloody-mindedness. The White House banned reporters from the Associated Press, the news service that feeds thousands of news organizations around the world, from the Oval Office and Air Force One. All because the AP won't follow Trump's dictat to rename the Gulf of Mexico as the "Gulf of America."
Penalizing a news organization for speech that Trump doesn't like is, as the AP's top editor said, "a plain violation of the First Amendment to the U.S. Constitution." You know, the one that says government should not do anything "abridging the freedom of speech, or of the press."
There's more. On top of all this, Trump and his Oval Office sidekick Elon Musk are using the courts and economic muscle to intimidate much of the U.S. media. Trump is suing several news organizations for coverage he doesn't like; Musk is doing the same. As Andy Craig noted on MSNBC, "no matter how baseless, a lawsuit from Musk can cost millions to defend."
Musk has also had journalists whose coverage he doesn't like thrown off his social media site, X. The result is a media prone to pull its punches, fearful of retribution from the most powerful and the wealthiest men in America.
The first and most obvious thing to note here is the blatant hypocrisy of it all. Trump boasts that "we have saved free speech in America" even as his administration silences critics, bans language itself, and cows the news media. His vice-president, JD Vance, goes to a security summit in Munich to tell European leaders that the biggest threat they face is their own failure to defend free speech, or at least his concept of it. Even as he consorts with the leader of Germany's far-right Alternative für Deutschland party and conspicuously fails to criticize the likes of Vladimir Putin.
The second notable thing is the silence of American conservatives who spent the past several years loudly complaining about censorship and "cancel culture" and accusing the left of trampling the very concept of free speech. As Rex Huppke writes in USA Today, "those folks are all pretty quiet right now." It turns out they were angry when the other side was riding high and policing their speech; now that their guy is in power and sticking it to their enemies, they don't have much to say.
There's plenty of hypocrisy to go around. Many on the left were indeed happy to dismiss free speech as at best outmoded and at worst racist. They did "cancel" people whose views they didn't like or stood by while the more extreme in their ranks drove supposed enemies off social media or had them fired for transgressing the progressive orthodoxies of two or three years ago.
They ignored warnings from some of us that defending the speech of those whose views you find objectionable (within common sense legal limits) is not just right in principle, but in your own best interest as well. It may not seem that way when you have the power to silence your critics. But when the tables are turned - as they inevitably will be - you'll realize the importance of upholding free expression for everyone.
In the United States, those tables have indeed turned with the rise of Trump. It's the progressive left that's now on the defensive and it's learning the hard way that the right to speak freely cannot be taken for granted. It can be easily eroded, even in the land of the First Amendment, by power and money.
All the more reason to defend it, especially for those you disagree with.
Andrew Phillips is a Toronto-based staff columnist for the Star's Opinion page. Reach him via email: aphillips@thestar.ca
CREDIT: Andrew Phillips OPINION;

Subject: Political campaigns; First Amendment-US; Litigation; Censorship; Social networks; Freedom of speech

Business indexing term: Subject: Social networks

Location: United States--US

Title: Plenty of hypocrisy around free speech

Publication title: Toronto Star; Toronto, Ont.

First page: A.15

Publication year: 2025

Publication date: Feb 21, 2025

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Document 116 of 500

Report blames city for congestion: Board of trade points to lack of planning around construction closures as biggest culprit

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/report-blames-city-congestion/docview/3168997180/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Report+blames+city+for+congestion%3A+Board+of+trade+points+to+lack+of+planning+around+construction+closures+as+biggest+culprit&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If Toronto wants to ease the traffic choking its streets, it should charge construction companies more for closing lanes - and consider removing bike lanes, on-street parking and curbside patios from its major thoroughfares, according to a new report from the Toronto Region Board of Trade.
The board's task force on congestion released a report Thursday in which it recommends a wide range of policies to tackle the city's clogged roads, including reducing the number of lane closures, enhancing traffic enforcement, unclogging arterial roads, clearing traffic on the Gardiner and implementing political "accountability mechanisms" at city hall.
"What the data makes clear is that Toronto's congestion crisis is a crisis of management and inadequate planning," the report says. The task force commissioned engineering firm Parsons and infrastructure consultancy firm Steer to do the analysis for its report.
The report points mainly at construction - a finding supported by city officials - as the reason for the city's congestion woes. "When a lane of traffic is closed for construction, its impacts on traffic flow can extend for blocks," says the report. "Given Toronto's volume of building activity, multiple lane closures reverberate citywide."
The report recommends limiting the number and duration of lane closures to help ease gridlock by increasing the price the city charges for closures equivalent to other major cities.
Toronto currently charges companies $37,000 per month, compared to London, where the fee to close a 40-metre lane for 30 days is $139,000 or Singapore and Sydney, where the charges are more than $50,000.
The city has proposed higher fees as part of its congestion management plan released in September, meant to incentivize developers to work faster, as the report suggest. Those charges have been approved by city council, but the new rates have yet to be announced.
The board's report also suggests allowing more off-peak and overnight construction on major arterials, pointing to Singapore, New York and London, where permit pricing and restrictions allow crews to work faster during times that are less busy. "In all these cities, the management principle at work is simple: every effort must be made to keep lanes open and streets clear for vehicles at peak travel times," says the report.
The board's report gives a dollar figure for how much closing road lanes for construction is costing the city. Closing one lane can cost $1.7 million a month or $20 million a year. Closing two lanes of the Gardiner, as the city has planned for the expressway's rehabilitation program until 2027, has socioeconomic costs of $200 million.
The report also says that congestion from lane closures due to construction has been "compounded by competing street uses such as bike lanes, on-street parking and curb-lane cafes." The report recommends removing those three elements from some of the city's most heavily used roads.
"Toronto's streets are trying to do everything for everyone, and in the process, they're failing to meet their fundamental purpose," said the board's CEO Giles Gherson in a press release.
Connector roads aren't just "community assets," Gherson added at Thursday's news conference. "They're also major roads that allow people to come in and out of the city."
In contrast, the city has touted guidelines for "complete streets" - roadways that are safe for all users and that can accommodate various modes of transportation and types of businesses.
According to the report, bike lanes should be on "secondary roads," echoing statements from Doug Ford, whose provincial government vowed to remove bike lanes on Bloor, University and Yonge Streets, as well as put restrictions on building new bike lanes.
The rationale, the report says, is to get "connector roads" such as Bloor Street flowing more efficiently with more road space. That's also the reason it recommends reviewing the city's program allowing curb lane cafés to take into account "the impact they have on our circulating traffic."
In 2024, more than 1,400 businesses participated in CaféTO, including 304 curb lane cafés, generating $130 million in economic benefits for the city, according to the Toronto Association of Business Improvement Areas.
The report also recommended moving on-street parking from major arterials into private parking lots and store-owned spaces.
Coun. Ausma Malik, who was at Thursday's press conference, said the city and the board of trade are aligned on some, but not all, of its recommendations.
The city, Malik said, is focused on "one of the biggest causes of congestion and gridlock, which is construction-related closures."
Raktim Mitra, a TMU professor and co-director of the school's TransFrom Research Laboratory, said that some of the proposed solutions in the task force's report don't "recognize the complexity of current urban systems and local economies."
"There's a danger in converting lively urban streets into road spaces that are primarily moving vehicles from one place to another," he said.
Mitra added that Toronto only has so much road, and pulling away from other road users isn't a solution to congestion.
"We don't want to build roads just for moving the vehicles, (but) streets that are also destinations where there are shops and local businesses."
The report also calls for stricter enforcement of the rules of the road, including using red light cameras for blocking the box and more enforcement on stopping and standings violations. Blocking the box has been a hot-button issue at city hall, with council raising fines.
Malik said she was glad to see the board call on the province "to provide Toronto with the tools that it so critically needs."
CAPTION: A new report from the Toronto Region Board of Trade recommends the city increase the price it charges for construction closures as well as consider the removal of bike lanes, on-street parking and curbside patios from its major thoroughfares.Steve Russell Toronto Star file photo
CREDIT: Andy Takagi Toronto Star

Subject: Construction; Task forces; Engineering firms; Enforcement; Roads & highways

Business indexing term: Subject: Engineering firms

Location: Singapore

Publication title: Toronto Star; Toronto, Ont.

Publication year: 2025

Publication date: Feb 21, 2025

Section: News

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Document 118 of 500

Buying Canadian could be good for Loblaw, CEO says: U.S. imports make up less than 10% of company's overall cost of goods

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/buying-canadian-could-be-good-loblaw-ceo-says/docview/3168997036/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Buying+Canadian+could+be+good+for+Loblaw%2C+CEO+says%3A+U.S.+imports+make+up+less+than+10%25+of+company%27s+overall+cost+of+goods&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Loblaw, Canada's largest food and drug chain, only buys a small fraction of its goods from the U.S., CEO Per Bank said on Thursday, tamping down fears that the looming Trump tariff war could cause a spike in grocery bills.
In a quarterly update, Bank said the company has been lining up alternatives to American suppliers in case U.S. President Donald Trump follows through on his 25 per cent tariff threat that would likely prompt retaliatory tariffs from Ottawa on U.S. goods entering Canada.
According to Bank, U.S. imports make up less than 10 per cent of Loblaw's overall cost of goods.
"And it's mostly in produce," he said during a conference call with financial analysts Thursday morning.
After years of high inflation, Bank said he isn't expecting consumers will fork over an extra 25 per cent for U.S. imports, prompting a major shift to Canadian-made products or imports from other countries.
He said Loblaw is already tracking a significant impact from the "Buy Canadian" movement among shoppers, with a roughly 10 per cent sales lift in Canadian-produced goods during one week earlier this month.
In the household goods and cleaning aisle, for example, Loblaw sources products from about 30 vendors in the U.S., but also sells Canadian-made versions under its private label "control brands" - President's Choice and No Name.
If Ottawa decides to slap tariffs on U.S. cleaning products, those big brands "will not be competitive anymore," Bank said, "and all the sales will go to our control brands."
"That's good for Canada, it's good for customers and it's good for us," he added. "If you have a 25 per cent tariff on Kellogg's, if that should come so far, then of course we'll probably promote our control brands instead of Kellogg's because nobody will buy it if it's 25 per cent more expensive."
Though it's not fully clear which American goods would be subject to tariffs from Canada, Ottawa has already telegraphed what it would target in the first round of a trade war with the U.S.
Before Trump agreed earlier this month to delay tariffs for 30 days to allow for negotiations, the Trudeau government announced a list of 25 per cent counter tariffs on $30-billion worth of American goods - the first phase of a broader, $155-billion tariff plan.
At the time, academics and food executives said the initial list appeared to have been designed to blunt the impact at grocery costs for Canadian shoppers.
Much of the U.S. food on the list, including poultry, eggs and dairy, have strong Canadian-made alternatives already on offer, making it easy to avoid more expensive U.S. goods - if they exist in stores at all. But those same executives warned that if the list gets longer, and starts to include harder-to-replace goods, there could be problems.
On Thursday, Bank said replacing produce from U.S. growing regions like California would be the main sore spot.
"If tariffs are applied on produce, that's where we will be mostly impacted," he said. "On produce, it's probably the most difficult place. I think we can probably mitigate half of our suppliers."
On top of the tariff threat, Loblaw also warned that global food companies have been pushing for higher-than-normal price increases.
"Many are requesting double digit price increases," chief financial officer Richard Dufresne told analysts. "This is compounded by the fact that we are working with a Canadian dollar that trades at its lowest level in over 20 years."
During the financial update on Thursday, Loblaw, a network of more than 2,800 stores that includes Shoppers Drug Mart, No Frills, Fortinos and Real Canadian Superstore, reported $14.6 billion in fourth-quarter retail sales, up three per cent compared to the previous year.
But profits at the chain dipped by 14.6 per cent to $462 million, which the company said was due to a re-evaluation of its liability for outstanding loyalty points, since more customers are participating in its PC Optimum program.
CREDIT: Jake Edmiston Toronto Star

Document 119 of 500

Crombie blames Ford for economic woes: PC leader says he'll drop trade barriers between provinces

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/crombie-blames-ford-economic-woes/docview/3168997007/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Crombie+blames+Ford+for+economic+woes%3A+PC+leader+says+he%27ll+drop+trade+barriers+between+provinces&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's been seven years of bad luck in Ontario since Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives won power, charges Bonnie Crombie.
In a speech Thursday to the Canadian Club at the Fairmont Royal York Hotel, the Liberal leader urged Ontarians to choose change in the Feb. 27 election.
"Since Doug Ford took office seven years ago, every single metric of economic success has gotten worse in Ontario. Business confidence is at the lowest it's been in a generation," Crombie told 250 attendees.
Her comments came as Ford emerged from several days out of the public eye to campaign with Nova Scotia's Tory premier, Tim Houston, who also recently flouted his province's fixed-election date law to hold a snap vote.
The Ontario PC leader said he and Houston agree it is time to finally eliminate interprovincial barriers as part of their fight against U.S. President Donald Trump's planned tariffs.
"We're facing the biggest threat to our economic security and independence as a country, we need to rise to the challenge. We need to match these words with bold actions. We need to strengthen the economic ties that bind our country together," Ford said in Milton.
"We can't afford decades of delays. We need to build, and we need to build now as we build up Canada's economy, we need to tear down internal trade barriers," he said, adding such bureaucracy and red tape costs the economy $200 billion annually.
But Crombie, noting Ford has done little to reduce interprovincial trade barriers since forming government in 2018, argued the Tories' economic plan is failing.
"It's hard to attract investment to a province where workers are worried about gridlock, can't afford to buy a house and can't find a family doctor," she said, taking aim at Trump's musings about making Canada the 51st state.
"Ontario would be the fifth-poorest U.S. state if we were actually forced to join Donald Trump's U.S.A. We'd be down there with Alabama or Arkansas. Think about that. We've lost hundreds of thousands of jobs in manufacturing and construction especially," said Crombie.
"We have the second-highest unemployment rate in the country. Youth unemployment is the worst in all of Canada."
Crombie, whose campaign is focused on the fact that 2.5 million Ontarians don't have a family doctor, pledged to spend an additional $1 billion to ensure more mental health services are covered by OHIP.
Tearfully recounting her own late father's struggles with alcohol addiction, she emphasized that mental health challenges also take a toll on the economy.
"We cannot sit back and hope things get better. As business leaders, you see the effects first hand. Productivity is directly related to mental health in your workforce," she told the Canadian Club.
Meanwhile, Ford, in his first day of campaign events open to the media since Monday's televised debate, was asked about a controversial comment he made about capital punishment for violent criminals, as first reported by the Star last week.
"It was a joke and the 1,200 people in the room knew it was a joke," he said defensively about his reference to "send 'em right to sparky," slang for the electric chair in a Jan. 29 speech at a London Police Service gala dinner.
On Friday, Ford will make his second campaign trip to Washington to lobby state leaders at the National Governors Association winter conference about the harm that would come from Trump's tariffs on both sides of the border.
"The worst thing we could do is sit back and say, 'Let's not bother going down there, let's not communicate with our largest trading partner.' It is critical," the PC leader said.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles cast her ballot at the Mary McCormick Recreation Centre on Thursday in her riding of Davenport. She urged people to take advantage of the advance polls that opened Thursday and will continue through Saturday.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie, Kristin Rushowy and Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Document 120 of 500

Stellantis jobs at risk after new plant paused: Brampton facility's future unclear as EV subsidies wane

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/stellantis-jobs-at-risk-after-new-plant-paused/docview/3168996989/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Stellantis+jobs+at+risk+after+new+plant+paused%3A+Brampton+facility%27s+future+unclear+as+EV+subsidies+wane&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The future's still on hold for 2,200 autoworkers at a Stellantis plant in Brampton - and industry sources say dwindling electric-vehicle subsidies and uncertainty caused by Donald Trump's tariff threats are to blame.
The automaker announced Thursday it's "pausing" work on the next generation of its Jeep Compass, which had been scheduled to go into production at its Brampton assembly plant in the fourth quarter this year. The news sent shock waves through the Ontario election campaign, just days before voters go to the polls Feb. 27.
"Stellantis continues to reassess its product strategy in North America to ensure it is offering customers a range of vehicles with flexible powertrain options to best meet their needs," said Stellantis spokesperson Lou Ann Gosselin in a written statement. "As a result, the Company is temporarily pausing work on the next generation Jeep Compass, including activities at the Brampton Assembly Plant."
The company insisted it's still committed to its retooling of the plant, underway since January 2024.
The federal and Ontario governments have committed hundreds of millions of dollars to the plant retooling, and to retooling an assembly plant in Windsor. The latter plant is already up and running again, and the company was expected to add a third production shift later this year.
Retooling of the two plants was expected to cost up to $3.6 billion, with industry sources estimating that government money accounted for up to 20 per cent of that. (Typically, the sources said, auto manufacturers incur the costs up front, and then get reimbursed by governments once work is actually completed.)
Most of the 2,200 workers at the Brampton plant have been off the job since retooling started. It had previously produced muscle cars, including the Dodge Charger, but was expected to produce electric and non-electric versions of the Compass in the future for the North American market. (The new models will debut in Europe later this year, with production taking place in Italy.)
In an interview, Unifor union national president Lana Payne said the announcement is a predictable result of Trump's tariff threats, as well as his threats to dismantle electric-vehicle subsidies initiated under his predecessor, Joe Biden.
"This is one of what I expect is going to be many examples, until we get into a place where we're fighting back hard against this," said Payne. "The crisis is now. The economic warfare is occurring now."
Earlier this week, Trump said he'd be imposing a 25 per cent tariff on all automotive imports.
Previously, he said he'll impose 25 per cent tariffs on steel and aluminum imports, and an across-the-board 25 per cent tariff on all imports from Canada and Mexico. He has also said the steel and aluminum tariffs would be on top of the across-the-board ones.
The Stellantis news brings more uncertainty to an industry already reeling from Trump's tariffs threats, said Flavio Volpe, president of the Automotive Parts Manufacturers' Association.
"If you're a supplier to that plant or a worker at the plant or one of the supplier plants, a pause doesn't ease your anxiety," said Volpe, who expressed cautious optimism at the company's public commitment to maintain its investment in the Brampton plant.
"I think it was important for all of us to hear the company say this doesn't change their investment commitment," said Volpe. "But until you know what product is in there, you're not going to relax."
Volpe added that shrinking EV subsidies in Canada, and the potential end of the U.S. Inflation Reduction Act - which includes tax credits for EV purchases - has hammered demand for EVs.
"Part of the business case for that Compass (was) the incentives and tax credits," Volpe said. "If their internal calculations said an electric Jeep Compass wouldn't sell in volume, given all the pressures that are on us right now, I don't mind them going for a reset."
The news sent shock waves through the Ontario election campaign, with voters set to go to the polls Feb. 27.
In a statement, Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford, campaigning for re-election, said the Stellantis move proves "the threat of President Trump's tariffs and the economic uncertainty it's creating is already having an impact on Ontario workers.
"And it's only the beginning. Now, more than ever, we need to invest in our front-line workers to protect good-paying jobs in Ontario," Ford said Thursday. "We will make sure that Stellantis upholds every aspect of our agreement and will do whatever it takes to protect workers and jobs in Brampton and across Ontario."
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said the Stellantis decision is an indictment of Ford's Tories, who have been in power since 2018. "This news will hit hard for the 2,200 autoworkers in Brampton who are waiting to return to work. Doug Ford's approach is failing workers," said Stiles. "As premier, I will fight for our auto sector, defend every job, and work to secure new markets."
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said "this news is devastating for Stellantis workers and their families."
"We need to urgently diversify our economy to protect against shocks like this," said Crombie. "The sad reality is that our economy is ill-prepared and vulnerable because Doug Ford has failed over the last seven years to attract investment, diversify our economy, and prepare our workforce for the serious challenges on the horizon."
In a post on X, federal Industry Minister François-Philippe Champagne said he's been in touch with both the company and union and. "We stand with those impacted and are in contact with Stellantis and Unifor. We will make sure that Stellantis honours its commitments and follows through on its plans for Brampton." Champagne said.
Brampton Mayor Patrick Brown said he's "grateful" for the company's commitment to investing in the assembly plant, but admitted it's a nerve-wracking time for the industry.
"I'm concerned for Canada's entire automotive sector right now," said Brown, who stressed that he's reaching out to other levels of government. "We are committed to working with our federal and provincial partners in supporting the ongoing success of Brampton's automotive sector," Brown added.
With files from Robert Benzie
CREDIT: Josh Rubin Toronto Star With files from Robert Benzie

Subject: Political campaigns; Workers; Automobile industry; Tax credits; Subsidies; Tariffs; Factories; Workforce planning; Layoffs

Full text: Indigenous issues MIA in Ontario election
In the same way that the climate crisis has been completely ignored in this election, as candidates address people's wallets and lack of services, discussion of Indigenous issues is entirely nonexistent. It's not as if the mercury poisoning in Grassy Narrows has been resolved, or decades-long boil water advisories, or the toxic pollution affecting the Aamjiwnaang nation near Sarnia. However, mining in the Ring of Fire is part of the energy agenda, despite the lack of free, prior and informed consent of all the Indigenous nations stewarding this land and its waters. All of these issues are linked and need to be debated openly. Sadly, this is not the case.
Lyba Spring, Toronto
U.S.-Russian alliance to cost Canada dearly
A new world order is in progress. Russia and the United States, or more exactly Vladimir Putin and Donald Trump, began forming their alliance almost immediately after the accession of Trump to the American throne. They are both closely tied to their respective oligarchs and political collaborators. In the U.S., Elon Musk is spearheading the dismantling and subjugation of the federal government. In Russia, that objective was achieved years ago. Canada will be among the nations that will pay a heavy price once this alliance is completed.
Raphael Vigod, Toronto
No guarantee high-speed rail will happen
Justin Trudeau says Toronto to Quebec high-speed rail project is 'real now.' Where do his successors stand on it? Feb. 19
Europe's first high-speed line from Paris to Lyon was approved in 1971 by the French government just four years after first being touted by the national railway, SNCF.
Via Rail Canada first proposed high-speed rail in 1984. Forty-one years and more than a dozen studies later, it's been approved by the Trudeau Liberals in the dying days of their government with the full knowledge that it will almost certainly be kiboshed by the Conservatives when they are next elected.
Trainloads of taxpayer dollars spent on planning a new high-speed line is no guarantee that it will actually get built. Back in July 1995, Mike Harris's Progressive Conservative government cancelled the Eglinton subway that was already under construction. Almost 30 years later, we still aren't riding the rails under Eglinton.
The Ligne à Grande Vitesse to Lyon opened in 1981, a mere 14 years after it was first proposed. After 41 years of waffling by successive governments over Canadian high-speed rail, I'll wager dollars to doughnuts that it will be at least 30 more years before we see this new line up and running.
Jason Shron, president of
Rapido Trains Inc., Markham

I'm not voting for someone who admires Trump
If Premier Doug Ford really called this early election because he is confident that he is the best person to prevail against U.S. President Donald Trump, it would have made more sense for him to wait until the fall of 2026 so that the people of Ontario could judge him on his actual success. Instead, he is asking us to judge him on his promises, which may be just as useless as his promise to end hallway medicine.
This is just an excuse for an unnecessary election. He was planning this election even before Trump won, as evidenced by his bribes to the electorate, such as getting beer into convenience stores one year early, despite the millions of dollars it cost to break the contract with the Beer Store.
Let's not forget that Ford said he was happy Trump won the U.S. election, despite Trump's tariffs against Canadian aluminum and steel in his first term and his talk about tariffs throughout his campaign this time. He likes Trump. That is enough of a reason for me not to vote for him (although I can think of many more reasons).
Susan Trankovits, North York
Michigan voted for Trump, so fix this disaster there
I'm a Republican from Michigan. Trump's auto tariffs are a terrible, destructive idea for both our countries, Feb. 20
Andrea Bitely paints an accurate picture of the damage that U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs will do to the auto sector on both sides of the border but remarkably tries to avoid any responsibility for putting us all in this situation.
Michigan voted for Trump in the last election and was, as usual, one of the key swing states that gave him the win. Yet, she put the onus on Canada to solve the disaster. According to Bitely, it's up to "how you're going to respond" to Trump's ravings that will make or break the auto industry and indeed the economy in general.
Here's a better idea - fix the problem at the source. You elected the guy. You should be pleading with your congressional representatives to solve this, not Canadians.
John Longo, Ajax

What replaces development charges?
Bonnie Crombie's housing plan would axe land-transfer tax for first-time home buyers, Dec. 14
Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie plans to axe development charges for new housing should she be elected. These fees currently pay for the infrastructure needed to support new housing, roads, sewers, water, police stations, libraries and recreation centres, etc. These charges can be more than $100,000 and are paid for by the developer and included with the price of the home. I have yet to hear how these services will be paid for and how she can guarantee that the savings will be passed onto the homeowner and not just increase the profit for developers.
Marion Thomas, Pickering

Boardwalk REIT founder was no saint to tenants
The business leaders who brought God to work - and weren't afraid to say so, Feb. 15
Here's an idea about why the founder of Boardwalk REIT felt he was a sinner and needed to volunteer for the Jesus Loves You Society. Reddit lists Boardwalk apartments' bedbugs, smells, mould, a laundry machine catching fire, broken agreements and gaslighting. Saddest tenant comment was, "There are certainly better buildings and better managers but at this price point I think they are decent." No one on a tight budget should have to settle for substandard housing. Until the government starts building affordable places again, it's that or joining 10,000 unhoused.
Elizabeth Nyburg, Toronto

Document 122 of 500

Crombie playing catch up to Ford

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/crombie-playing-catch-up-ford/docview/3168996790/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Crombie+playing+catch+up+to+Ford&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Liberal red campaign bus is on a roll, making tracks - slowly - in a snowy mid-winter campaign.
Sitting in the back is Bonnie Crombie, hoping to convert that traction into momentum on the campaign trail.
Ontario's Liberals were stuck in third place when she took over as leader barely 15 months ago. Now, coming off a strong performance in Monday's televised debate, every poll suggests she has moved her party into second place, leapfrogging ahead of the seemingly stalled NDP.
The good news for Crombie is that she's no longer in the back of the pack. The bad news is that the distance to catch the leader of the pack, Doug Ford, still seems insurmountable in those same public opinion surveys ahead of the Feb. 27 election.
Which is why she is trying to make news on the day I've chosen to tag along on the first of three campaign swings with the major party leaders.
Crombie has a two-pronged pitch - a Liberal confession in search of an NDP concession.
Liberal voters alone can't topple Ford's Tories, she concedes. Nor can New Democrats, she argues.
Unless they combine forces.
Hence her unabashed appeal to traditional NDP supporters: Lend me your votes.
New Democrats can "see that the wind is in our sails," Crombie says in an interview from her campaign bus as it lumbers down the Queen Elizabeth Way.
Emblazoned with her party's health-care appeal in bright red, the bus is blazing a trail that's not quite a path to Liberal victory - more like a plea for solidarity to prevent a third consecutive victory for Ford's Progressive Conservatives. With the Liberals rising steadily and the NDP stalling or declining, she repeats the pitch at every stop.
"There is a bandwagon effect," Crombie tells me, warming to her subject. If New Democrats "see our numbers are increasing and we look like we have our momentum - and our agenda speaks to their needs - absolutely they will join us."
It's an old political play being redeployed by the newest leader to arrive at Queen's Park after nearly a decade as Mississauga's high-profile mayor: If Liberals and New Democrats keep fighting each other - and splitting the anti-Ford vote - Tories will always win the day.
Crombie drives home the message both on the bus and off. Her first campaign stop is the Clifford Brewing Co. in Hamilton, where a gaggle of supporters and media are waiting to hear her speak.
Inside the gleaming craft brewery, where pallets of East Hamilton Lager and Devil's Punchbowl beer are stacked to the high ceilings, she huddles offstage with area candidates to make sure she has everyone's name and riding straight. On cue, onstage, the candidates form a human backdrop and the leader launches into her campaign stump speech.
There is no talk of buck a beer in this brewery, no promises of beer in corner stores - the empty slogans in Ford's previous campaigns that never resonated with the craft brewers like this one who sell premium products. Instead, Crombie is all business - and pocketbook politics - promising so many small business tax cuts, income tax cuts and reduced housing fees that it's hard to keep track of how they stack up against Ford's giveaways and the NDP's promised handouts.
Crombie talks passionately about the doctor shortage and hallway health care - critiques that are cornerstones of her campaign. Yet she says little about how she'd pay to fix everything, beyond cutting government waste and eliminating Ford's wasteful tax cuts - only to replace them with her own tax breaks.
Ceiling fans whir overhead as the Liberal leader puts her own spin on the polls - and the political calculation facing voters if they don't want to see Ford re-elected as premier yet again:
"If we all came together and ... voted with one voice, we can change the government," she tells the crowd. "We see the race narrowing between the premier and myself, and that's why I'm reaching out today to NDP voters, and I'm asking them, 'If you want to change our health-care system, please vote for Ontario's Liberals, and together we can change the government.' "
All that said - and speech delivered on script - Crombie's pitch may be a tough sell. Even if she can persuade skeptical NDP supporters that the Liberals have displaced their party in second place, she must also overcome the hostility of diehard New Democrats who might see her as a bad fit, and the party's weakness in rural and southwestern Ontario.
Crombie initially branded herself as a right-of-centre Liberal - a counterpoint to the last premier from her party, Kathleen Wynne, who leaned left. Crombie later rebranded herself as a fiscal conservative and a social progressive - a classic Ontario formulation.
Either way, Crombie insists, the battle to topple Ford isn't about ideology but winnability. It's about viability and survivability.
After all the scandals and setbacks besetting the Tories, "There comes a tipping point where people say, 'That's the line in the sand for me,' " Crombie tells me later. The challenge for the opposition parties is that voters "need a viable alternative" - and that's not the NDP in tough economic times, she insists.
Crombie faces an uphill battle to win, but she has come a long way since winning the provincial Liberal leadership in late 2023 to take over a losing party. A longtime mayor of Mississauga with a high media profile, she made the most of her strong name recognition in a hard-fought leadership campaign where she was consistently underestimated by her Liberal rivals in 2023 - much as she is by her NDP and PC rivals in 2025.
Before helming Mississauga, Ontario's sixth-biggest city, Crombie won election as a Liberal MP. She also had a life before politics, working as a corporate executive and management consultant (while managing a family).
"I've run companies, I've run a government" - business and mayoral experience that she says her NDP rival, Marit Stiles, lacks.
Crombie's star power helped her get noticed at the outset, but quickly dimmed in the aftermath of her leadership victory as she struggled to find her feet - and a seat in the legislature. Far from the media spotlight, the political veteran appeared rusty and uneven despite her years of experience.
But in the months leading up to the election campaign, Crombie has raised her game, becoming more nimble in campaign settings. In the televised debate, she stood out by standing up to Ford, delivering attack lines that scratched his Teflon coating slightly, even if they didn't quite singe him.
Yet Crombie consistently gets under his skin and has a knack for eliciting long tirades and highly personal put-downs from Ford, joking that she "lives rent-free in his head." While that may not be part of the job description for a future premier, it is a useful skill for a future opposition leader if Crombie should fall short on election night and is tasked with keeping Ford accountable for another four years.
Crombie also seems to exasperate Stiles, who kicked off the NDP-Liberal jousting on the campaign's first day by suggesting the Liberal leader would be a good fit in Ford's cabinet. In politics, criticism can be the sincerest form of flattery.
"Look, I understand that I'm a threat to her, and to Doug Ford, and that's why they're throwing snowballs at me," she muses. Crombie points to a procession of PC attack ads targeting her as posh and pampered - not to mention "queen of the carbon tax."
"They wouldn't do that if they didn't feel threatened by my presence in the race," she says.
As for Ford's carbon tax attack, it never seemed to stick - perhaps because she was the first prominent Liberal in the country to distance herself from the federal carbon levy. Whatever that says about her environmental credentials - probably not much, since every federal Liberal has since followed suit - it suggests a certain political agility to avoid the trap set by Ford, and the carbon fatigue felt by most voters.
Fatigue, however, is not a Crombie trait. On the morning after the evening debate, the sleep-starved leader who calls herself "the Energizer Bonnie" dives into a photo-op at the Clifford brewery, pouring samples for supporters and posing for photographers from behind the tasting bar.
And then, from Crombie, comes a spontaneous toast for the team:
"To victory!"
Or if not quite victory, to borrowed votes.
On the evidence, the Liberal leader is a work in progress and far from perfect. But she pops, and she persists, and she knows how to play the game of politics against a tough opponent like Ford - who is also, the record suggests, not all that perfect either.
The better question is whether Crombie is good enough. Good enough for the job at hand - and better than the alternative.
But an even bigger question - possibly the ballot question for some voters on Feb. 27 - is whether the Liberal leader is gaining traction. Not to mention momentum.
CREDIT: Martin Regg Cohn OPINION

41 years after high-speed rail was first proposed, there is still no guarantee it will happen

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/41-years-after-high-speed-rail-was-first-proposed/docview/3168996721/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=41+years+after+high-speed+rail+was+first+proposed%2C+there+is+still+no+guarantee+it+will+happen&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Justin Trudeau says Toronto to Quebec high-speed rail project is 'real now.' Where do his successors stand on it? Feb. 19
Europe's first high-speed line from Paris to Lyon was approved in 1971 by the French government just four years after first being touted by the national railway, SNCF.
Via Rail Canada first proposed high-speed rail in 1984. Forty-one years and over a dozen studies later, it has been approved by the Trudeau Liberals in the dying days of their government with the full knowledge that it will almost certainly be kiboshed by the Conservatives when they are next elected.
Trainloads of taxpayer dollars spent on planning a new high-speed line is no guarantee that it will actually get built. Back in July 1995, Mike Harris's Progressive Conservative government cancelled the Eglinton subway that was already under construction. Almost 30 years later, we still aren't riding the rails under Eglinton.
The Ligne à Grande Vitesse to Lyon opened in 1981, a mere 14 years after it was first proposed. After 41 years of waffling by successive governments over Canadian high-speed rail, I'll wager dollars to doughnuts that it will be at least 30 more years before we see this new line up and running.
Jason Shron, President Rapido Trains Inc., Markham
Who will pay axed development charges?
Bonnie Crombie's housing plan would axe land-transfer tax for first-time home buyers, Dec. 14
Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie plans to axe development charges for new housing should she be elected. These fees currently pay for the infrastructure needed to support new housing, roads, sewers, water, police stations, libraries, and recreation centres etc. These charges can be over $100,000 and are paid for by the developer and included with the price of the home. I have yet to hear how these services will be paid for and how she can guarantee that the savings will be passed onto the homeowner and not just increase the profit for developers.
Marion Thomas, Pickering
Indigenous issues MIA in Ontario election
In the same way that the climate crisis has been completely ignored in this election, as candidates address people's wallets and lack of services, discussion of Indigenous issues is entirely nonexistent. It's not as if the mercury poisoning in Grassy Narrows has been resolved, or decades-long boil water advisories, or the toxic pollution affecting the Aamjiwnaang nation near Sarnia. However, mining in the Ring of Fire is part of the energy agenda, despite the lack of free, prior and informed consent of all the Indigenous nations stewarding this land and its waters. All of these issues are linked and need to be debated openly. Sadly, this is not the case.
Lyba Spring, Toronto
Ford likes Trump so he won't get my vote
If Premier Doug Ford really called this early election because he is confident that he is the best person to prevail against U.S. President Donald Trump, it would have made more sense for him to wait until the fall of 2026 so that the people of Ontario could judge him on his actual success. Instead, he is asking us to judge him on his promises, which may be just as useless as his promise to end hallway medicine.
This is just an excuse for an unnecessary election. He was planning this election even before Trump won, as evidenced by his bribes to the electorate, such as getting beer into convenience stores one year early, despite the millions of dollars it cost to break the contract with the Beer Store.
Let's not forget that Ford said he was happy Trump won the U.S. election, despite Trump's tariffs against Canadian aluminum and steel in his first term and his talk about tariffs throughout his campaign this time. He likes Trump. That is enough of a reason for me not to vote for him (although I can think of many more reasons).
Susan Trankovits, North York
Michigan voted for Trump - fix this disaster at the sourceI'm a Republican from Michigan. Trump's auto tariffs are a terrible, destructive idea for both our countries, Feb. 20
Andrea Bitely paints an accurate picture of the damage that U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs will do to the auto sector on both sides of the border but remarkably tries to avoid any responsibility for putting us all in this situation. Michigan voted for Trump in the last election and was, as usual, one of the key swing states that gave him the win. Yet, sheput the onus on Canada to solve the disaster. According to Bitely, it's up to "how you're going to respond" to Trump's ravings that will make or break the auto industry and indeed the economy in general. Here's a better idea - fix the problem at the source. You elected the guy. You should be pleading with your congressional representatives to solve this, not Canadians.
John Longo, Ajax
Canada will pay a heavy price in the U.S.-Russian alliance
A new world order is in progress. Russia and the United States, or more exactly Vladimir Putin and Donald Trump, began forming their alliance almost immediately after the accession of Trump to the American throne. They are both closely tied to their respective oligarchs and political collaborators. In the United States, Elon Musk is spearheading the dismantling and subjugation of the federal government. In Russia, that objective was achieved years ago. Canada will be among the nations that will pay a heavy price once this alliance is completed.
Raphael Vigod, Toronto
Mark Carney is Canada's Winston Churchill
In 1940 Britain was facing invasion from Herr Hitler and his Nazis. Winston Churchill became the Prime Minister who inspired the English people to "never surrender" and led them to victory. Churchill had unknowingly been preparing for his date with destiny for his entire life. His skill set was perfect for that moment.
Mark Carney is our Winston Churchill. Like Churchill, Carney's whole life has prepared him for this very moment. No other individual has the exceptional skill set demanded to save us from the real and present danger that is confronting our beloved country today. This is Mark Carney's date with destiny. Canada is so fortunate to have him step forward in our hour of need just as Churchill did back then. From this moment on, he should be branded as "Winston Carney." We will never surrender.
Roger Hilderley, Stratford, ON

Perhaps it was the bedbugs and mould that made REIT founder feel like a sinner
The business leaders who brought God to work - and weren't afraid to say so, Feb. 15
Here's an idea about why the founder of Boardwalk REIT felt he was a sinner and needed to volunteer for the Jesus Loves You Society. Reddit lists Boardwalk apartments' bedbugs, smells, mould, a laundry machine catching fire, broken agreements and gaslighting. Saddest tenant comment was, "There are certainly better buildings and better managers but at this price point I think they are decent." No one on a tight budget should have to settle for substandard housing. Until the government starts building affordable places again, it's that or joining 10,000 unhoused.
Elizabeth Nyburg, Toronto
CAPTION: This photograph shows a Great Speed Train (Train a Grande Vitesse - TGV) of the French railway company SNCF on the way on the LGV Paris-Bordeaux-Nantes-Rennes, in Courtalain, north of France, on September 13, 2024.

Document 124 of 500

Bonnie Crombie blames Doug Ford for economic and health-care woes as he defends quip about 'sparky'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/bonnie-crombie-blames-doug-ford-economic-health/docview/3169244920/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Bonnie+Crombie+blames+Doug+Ford+for+economic+and+health-care+woes+as+he+defends+quip+about+%27sparky%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's been seven years of bad luck in Ontario since Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives won power, charges Bonnie Crombie.
In a speech Thursday to the Canadian Club at the Fairmont Royal York Hotel, the Liberal leader urged Ontarians to choose change in the Feb. 27 election.
"Since Doug Ford took office seven years ago, every single metric of economic success has gotten worse in Ontario. Business confidence is at the lowest it's been in a generation," Crombie told 250 attendees.
Her comments came as Ford emerged from several days out of the public eye to campaign with Nova Scotia's Tory Premier Tim Houston, who also recently flouted his province's fixed-election date law to hold a snap vote.
The Ontario PC leader said he and Houston agree it is time to finally eliminate interprovincial barriers as part of their fight against U.S. President Donald Trump's planned tariffs.
"We're facing the biggest threat to our economic security and independence as a country, we need to rise to the challenge. We need to match these words with bold actions. We need to strengthen the economic ties that bind our country together," Ford said in Milton.
"We can't afford decades of delays. We need to build, and we need to build now as we build up Canada's economy, we need to tear down internal trade barriers," he said, adding such bureaucracy and red tape costs the economy $200 billion annually.
But Crombie, noting Ford has done little to reduce interprovincial trade barriers since forming government in 2018, argued the Tories' economic plan is failing.
"It's hard to attract investment to a province where workers are worried about gridlock, can't afford to buy a house and can't find a family doctor," she said, taking aim at Trump's musings about making Canada the 51st state.
"Ontario would be the fifth-poorest U.S. state if we were actually forced to join Donald Trump's U.S.A. We'd be down there with Alabama or Arkansas. Think about that. We've lost hundreds of thousands of jobs in manufacturing and construction especially," said Crombie.
"We have the second-highest unemployment rate in the country. Youth unemployment is the worst in all of Canada."
Crombie, whose campaign is focused on the fact that 2.5 million Ontarians don't have a family doctor, pledged to spend an additional $1 billion to ensure more mental health services are covered by OHIP.
Tearfully recounting her own late father's struggles with alcohol addiction, she emphasized that mental health challenges also take a toll on the economy.
"We cannot sit back and hope things get better. As business leaders, you see the effects first hand. Productivity is directly related to mental health in your workforce," she told the Canadian Club.
Meanwhile, Ford, in his first day of campaign events open to the media since Monday's televised debate, was asked about a controversial comment he made about capital punishment for violent criminals, as first reported by the Star last week.
"It was a joke and the 1,200 people in the room knew it was a joke," he said defensively about his reference to "send 'em right to sparky," slang for the electric chair in a Jan. 29 speech at a London Police Service gala dinner.
On Friday, Ford will make his second campaign trip to Washington to lobby state leaders at the National Governors Association winter conference about the harm that would come from Trump's tariffs on both sides of the border.
"The worst thing we could do is sit back and say, 'Let's not bother going down there, let's not communicate with our largest trading partner.' It is critical," the PC leader said.
He was also in the American capital last week as part of a Council of the Federation delegation alarmed by Trump's threatened 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian goods that could be imposed within two weeks.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles, for her part, was urging people to take advantage of the advance polls that opened Thursday and will continue through Saturday.
"Voting is easy, fast, and a vital part of how we build a better future. My family and I voted this morning and had a great time!" said Stiles as she cast her ballot at the Mary McCormick Recreation Centre in her riding of Davenport.
CAPTION: Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie with Chef Massimo Capra during a campaign stop at Capra's Kitchen restaurant in Mississauga on Feb. 19, 2025.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie, Kristin Rushowy, Rob Ferguson

Subject: Political campaigns; Mental health; Unemployment; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Unemployment Tariffs

People: Trump, Donald J

Title: Bonnie Crombie blames Doug Ford for economic and health-care woes as he defends quip about 'sparky'

Document 125 of 500

Bonnie Crombie has a long way to go to catch Doug Ford. She has a plan to change that

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/bonnie-crombie-has-long-way-go-catch-doug-ford/docview/3169244420/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Bonnie+Crombie+has+a+long+way+to+go+to+catch+Doug+Ford.+She+has+a+plan+to+change+that&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Liberal red campaign bus is on a roll, making tracks - slowly - in a snowy mid-winter campaign.
Sitting in the back is Bonnie Crombie, hoping to convert that traction into momentum on the campaign trail.
Ontario's Liberals were stuck in third place when she took over as leader barely 15 months ago. Now, coming off a strong performance in Monday's televised debate, every poll suggests she has moved her party into second place, leapfrogging ahead of the seemingly stalled NDP.
The good news for Crombie is that she's no longer in the back of the pack. The bad news is that the distance to catch the leader of the pack, Doug Ford, still seems insurmountable in those same public opinion surveys ahead of the Feb. 27 election.
Which is why she is trying to make news on the day I've chosen to tag along on the first of three campaign swings with the major party leaders.
Crombie has a two-pronged pitch - a Liberal confession in search of an NDP concession.
Liberal voters alone can't topple Ford's Tories, she concedes. Nor can New Democrats, she argues.
Unless they combine forces.
Hence her unabashed appeal to traditional NDP supporters: Lend me your votes.
New Democrats can "see that the wind is in our sails," Crombie says in an interview from her campaign bus as it lumbers down the Queen Elizabeth Way.
Emblazoned with her party's health-care appeal in bright red, the bus is blazing a trail that's not quite a path to Liberal victory - more like a plea for solidarity to prevent a third consecutive victory for Ford's Progressive Conservatives. With the Liberals rising steadily and the NDP stalling or declining, she repeats the pitch at every stop.
"There is a bandwagon effect," Crombie tells me, warming to her subject. If New Democrats "see our numbers are increasing and we look like we have our momentum - and our agenda speaks to their needs - absolutely they will join us."
It's an old political play being redeployed by the newest leader to arrive at Queen's Park after nearly a decade as Mississauga's high-profile mayor: If Liberals and New Democrats keep fighting each other - and splitting the anti-Ford vote - Tories will always win the day.
Crombie drives home the message both on the bus and off. Her first campaign stop is the Clifford Brewing Co. in Hamilton, where a gaggle of supporters and media are waiting to hear her speak.
Inside the gleaming craft brewery, where palettes of East Hamilton Lager and Devil's Punchbowl beer are stacked to the high ceilings, she huddles offstage with area candidates to make sure she has everyone's name and riding straight. On cue, onstage, the candidates form a human backdrop and the leader launches into her campaign stump speech.
There is no talk of buck a beer in this brewery, no promises of beer in corner stores - the empty slogans in Ford's previous campaigns that never resonated with the craft brewers like this one who sell premium products. Instead, Crombie is all business - and pocketbook politics - promising so many small business tax cuts, income tax cuts and reduced housing fees that it's hard to keep track of how they stack up against Ford's giveaways and the NDP's promised handouts.
Crombie talks passionately about the doctor shortage and hallway health care - critiques that are cornerstones of her campaign. Yet she says little about how she'd pay to fix everything, beyond cutting government waste and eliminating Ford's wasteful tax cuts - only to replace them with her own tax breaks.
Ceiling fans whir overhead as the Liberal leader puts her own spin on the polls - and the political calculation facing voters if they don't want to see Ford re-elected as premier yet again:
"If we all came together and ... voted with one voice, we can change the government," she tells the crowd. "We see the race narrowing between the premier and myself, and that's why I'm reaching out today to NDP voters, and I'm asking them, 'If you want to change our health-care system, please vote for Ontario's Liberals, and together we can change the government.'"
All that said - and speech delivered on script - Crombie's pitch may be a tough sell. Even if she can persuade skeptical NDP supporters that the Liberals have displaced their party in second place, she must also overcome the hostility of diehard New Democrats who might see her as a bad fit, and the party's weakness in rural and Southwestern Ontario.
Crombie initially branded herself as a right-of-centre Liberal - a counterpoint to the last premier from her party, Kathleen Wynne, who leaned left. Crombie later rebranded herself as a fiscal conservative and a social progressive - a classic Ontario formulation.
Either way, Crombie insists, the battle to topple Ford isn't about ideology but winnability. It's about viability and survivability.
After all the scandals and setbacks besetting the Tories, "There comes a tipping point where people say, 'That's the line in the sand for me,'" Crombie tells me later. The challenge for the opposition parties is that voters "need a viable alternative" - and that's not the NDP in tough economic times, she insists.
Crombie faces an uphill battle to win, but she has come a long way since winning the provincial Liberal leadership in late 2023 to take over a losing party. A longtime mayor of Mississauga with a high media profile, she made the most of her strong name recognition in a hard-fought leadership campaign where she was consistently underestimated by her Liberal rivals in 2023 - much as she is by her NDP and PC rivals in 2025.
Before helming Mississauga, Ontario's sixth-biggest city, Crombie won election as a Liberal MP. She also had a life before politics, working as a corporate executive and management consultant (while managing a family).
"I've run companies, I've run a government" - business and mayoral experience that she says her NDP rival, Marit Stiles, lacks.
Crombie's star power helped her get noticed at the outset, but quickly dimmed in the aftermath of her leadership victory as she struggled to find her feet - and a seat in the legislature. Far from the media spotlight, the political veteran appeared rusty and uneven despite her years of experience.
But in the months leading up to the election campaign, Crombie has raised her game, becoming more nimble in campaign settings. In the televised debate, she stood out by standing up to Ford, delivering attack lines that scratched his Teflon coating slightly, even if they didn't quite singe him.
Yet Crombie consistently gets under his skin and has a knack for eliciting long tirades and highly personal put-downs from Ford, joking that she "lives rent-free in his head." While that may not be part of the job description for a future premier, it is a useful skill for a future opposition leader if Crombie should fall short on election night and is tasked with keeping Ford accountable for another four years.
Crombie also seems to exasperate Stiles, who kicked off the NDP-Liberal jousting on the campaign's first day by suggesting the Liberal leader would be a good fit in Ford's cabinet. In politics, criticism can be the sincerest form of flattery.
"Look, I understand that I'm a threat to her, and to Doug Ford, and that's why they're throwing snowballs at me," she muses. Crombie points to a procession of PC attack ads targeting her as posh and pampered - not to mention "Queen of the carbon tax."
"They wouldn't do that if they didn't feel threatened by my presence in the race," she says.
As for Ford's carbon tax attack, it never seemed to stick - perhaps because she was the first prominent Liberal in the country to distance herself from the federal carbon levy. Whatever that says about her environmental credentials - probably not much, since every federal Liberal has since followed suit - it suggests a certain political agility to avoid the trap set by Ford, and the carbon fatigue felt by most voters.
Fatigue, however, is not a Crombie trait. On the morning after the evening debate, the sleep-starved leader who calls herself "the Energizer Bonnie" dives into a photo-op at the Clifford brewery, pouring samples for supporters and posing for photographers from behind the tasting bar.
And then, from Crombie, comes a spontaneous toast for the team:
"To victory!"
Or if not quite victory, to borrowed votes.
On the evidence, the Liberal leader is a work in progress and far from perfect. But she pops, and she persists, and she knows how to play the game of politics against a tough opponent like Ford - who is also, the record suggests, not all that perfect either.
The better question is whether Crombie is good enough. Good enough for the job at hand - and better than the alternative.
But an even bigger question - possibly the ballot question for some voters on Feb. 27 - is whether the Liberal leader is gaining traction. Not to mention momentum.
Bonnie Crombie at a glance
Age: 65
Birthplace: Toronto
Education: MBA from Schulich School of Business, York University
Family: Divorced with two sons and a daughter
Occupation before politics: Manager of government relations for the Insurance Bureau of Canada after working in sales and communication roles at McDonald's in Boston and Disney in Los Angeles
Political career: Elected Liberal leader in December 2023, Crombie was a three-term mayor of Mississauga and before that served on city council and was a one-term Liberal MP.
Fun fact: She took up rock-climbing in her mid-50s and has scaled the Totem Pole, a spire of rock rising 46 metres in Arizona's Lower Devil's canyon.
CAPTION: Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie on her campaign bus doing tour stops at Capra's Kitchen restaurant in Mississauga on Feb. 19, 2025.
CREDIT: Martin Regg Cohn

As Trump trade war looms, Loblaw CEO says U.S. only accounts for a tiny fraction of products on shelves

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/as-trump-trade-war-looms-loblaw-ceo-says-u-s-only/docview/3169244363/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=As+Trump+trade+war+looms%2C+Loblaw+CEO+says+U.S.+only+accounts+for+a+tiny+fraction+of+products+on+shelves&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Loblaw, Canada's largest food and drug chain, only buys a small fraction of its goods from the U.S., CEO Per Bank said on Thursday, tamping down fears that the looming Trump tariff war could cause a spike in grocery bills.
In a quarterly update, Bank said the company has been lining up alternatives to American suppliers in case U.S. President Donald Trump follows through on his 25-per-cent tariff threat that would likely prompt retaliatory tariffs from Ottawa on U.S. goods entering Canada.
According to Bank, U.S. imports make up less than 10 per cent of Loblaw's overall cost of goods.
"And it's mostly in produce," he said during a conference call with financial analysts Thursday morning.
After years of high inflation, Bank said he isn't expecting consumers will fork over an extra 25 per cent for U.S. imports, prompting a major shift to Canadian-made products or imports from other countries.
He said Loblaw is already tracking a significant impact from the 'Buy-Canadian' movement among shoppers, with a roughly 10-per-cent sales lift in Canadian-produced goods during one week earlier this month.
In the household goods and cleaning aisle, for example, Loblaw sources products from about 30 vendors in the U.S., but also sells Canadian-made versions under its private label 'control brands' - President's Choice and No Name.
If Ottawa decides to slap tariffs on U.S. cleaning products, those big brands "will not be competitive anymore," Bank said, "and all the sales will go to our control brands."
"That's good for Canada, it's good for customers and it's good for us," he added. "If you have a 25 per cent tariff on Kellogg's, if that should come so far, then of course we'll probably promote our control brands instead of Kellogg's because nobody will buy it if it's 25 per cent more expensive."
Though it's not fully clear which American goods would be subject to tariffs from Canada, Ottawa has already telegraphed what it would target in the first round of a trade war with the U.S.
Before Trump agreed earlier this month to delay tariffs for 30 days to allow for negotiations, the Trudeau government announced a list of 25-per-cent counter tariffs on $30 billion worth of American goods - the first phase of a broader, $155-billion tariff plan.
At the time, academics and food executives said the initial list appeared to have been designed to blunt the impact at grocery costs for Canadian shoppers.
Much of the U.S. food on the list, including poultry, eggs and dairy, have strong Canadian-made alternatives already on offer, making it easy to avoid more expensive U.S. goods - if they exist in stores at all. But those same executives warned that if the list gets longer, and starts to include harder-to-replace goods, there could be problems.
On Thursday, Bank said replacing produce from U.S. growing regions like California would be the main sore spot.
"If tariffs are applied on produce, that's where we will be mostly impacted," he said. "On produce, it's probably the most difficult place. I think we can probably mitigate half of our suppliers."
On top of the tariff threat, Loblaw also warned that global food companies have been pushing for higher-than-normal price increases.
"Many are requesting double digit price increases," chief financial officer Richard Dufresne told analysts. "This is compounded by the fact that we are working with a Canadian dollar that trades at its lowest level in over 20 years."
During the financial update on Thursday, Loblaw, a network of more than 2,800 stores that includes Shoppers Drug Mart, No Frills, Fortinos and Real Canadian Superstore, reported $14.6 billion in fourth-quarter retail sales, up three per cent compared to the previous year.
But profits at the chain dipped by 14.6 per cent to $462 million, which the company said was due to a re-evaluation of its liability for outstanding loyalty points, since more customers are participating in its PC Optimum program.
CAPTION: Loblaw CEO Per Bank says that Ottawa slaps tariffs on certain U.S. products, then those big brands "will not be competitive anymore ... and all the sales will go to our control brands. That's good for Canada, it's good for customers and it's good for us."
CREDIT: Jake Edmiston

Ontario election promise tracker: From Trump tariffs to plans for the Science Centre, here's what every provincial party leader is pledging
