Document 128 of 500

'Am I going to live to see it?' Eglinton Crosstown LRT delays, funding of city transit spark protest

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/am-i-going-live-see-eglinton-crosstown-lrt-delays/docview/3168830258/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Am+I+going+to+live+to+see+it%3F%27+Eglinton+Crosstown+LRT+delays%2C+funding+of+city+transit+spark+protest&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With Ontario voters headed to the polls next week, keeping transit a priority in the election campaign was the focus of rally held Wednesday night.
"I love this city, I really do," said 17-year-old Jason Michael-Clarke, adding: "I can't tell you how many times I've been late to school, late to tests before because the subway."
Michael-Clark was among about 30 protesters at the rally, organized by transit advocacy group TTCriders, at the corner of Yonge and Eglinton - outside of the unopened Eglinton station on the Eglinton Crosstown LRT. Since he was four, the Eglinton Crosstown has been under construction. Michael-Clark has grown up watching his parents, whose business sits at the end of the planned line at Mount Dennis station, anxiously await the long-anticipated LRT line.
The protesters demanded more transparency from the province about when the beleaguered Eglinton Crosstown and its sister line, the Finch West LRT, will finally open - pulling out a mock cheque, red ribbon and oversized scissors to demand the opening of the Eglinton line. The group also called for more consistent funding of the TTC, their demand emphasized when speakers at the rally were late due to Line 1 subway delays.
Jason McDonald said he came to speak at the rally for his daughter.
"This LRT infrastructure, it might be for her children," he said, bracing himself in the bitter cold. "I mean, because at the pace that it's going ... am I going to live to see it?"
McDonald, the chair of the York-Eglinton BIA, says he and other small businesses have been made "desolate" because of years of Eglinton Crosstown LRT construction.
"We feel like we're hostages in our community," he said.
The $12.6-billion Eglinton Crosstown has been overseen by both Liberal and Conservative provincial governments since 2011, when construction started. Since that time, it has missed multiple deadlines for opening. With 25 stops stretching from Mount Dennis in the west to Kennedy in the east, the 19-kilometre, light-rail system was initially meant to be ready by 2020.
The line remains unopened even after Crosslinx - the consortium building the transit project - gave the Star an exclusive tour of the LRT in May 2023, unveiling slick, modern trains and tunnels on a stretch of the line.
No other details have been provided on why the line has been delayed, besides an update last November on a software design flaw that impacted the line's signalling system.
Documents obtained by a freedom of information request by the Star in April 2023 showed Metrolinx has at times wanted to give the public more information about the troubled transit line, but the provincial government directed the transit agency not to. In August 2024, then-premier Doug Ford's office directed Metrolinx to keep the opening date of the LRT a secret because it had doubts the date would be met.
When the Star asked to speak with Metrolinx experts who could shed light on the delays, spokesperson Andrea Ernesaks said the provincial transit agency - created in 2006 as an arm's length agency - wouldn't be able to provide an interview during the provincial election.
"We continue to make progress on the Eglinton Crosstown LRT, with testing and commissioning still underway," said Ernesaks on Friday. "As noted previously, we need to have confidence that key milestones are being met."
"Testing and commissioning" - the process of quality checking everything from the trains themselves to stations announcements - has been ongoing on the Eglinton LRT as far back as April 2023.
"Once we have a credible schedule, we will share that with the public," added Ernesaks.
The TTC, meanwhile, is gearing up to take over the LRT.
Nearly two-thirds of the LRT's operators have completed a 30-day training program, TTC chair Jamaal Myers said.
"This is a new system for the TTC, and while the LRT vehicles are similar to our new streetcars, there are operational differences, including infrastructure and signalling," Myers explained in a statement on Wednesday. The TTC will also handle a "revenue service demonstration," which will see trains run along the full track of the LRT.
On the campaign trail, party leaders are pointing fingers about the LRT's delay - and promising, if elected, to be the government that finally sees it up and running.
At an election campaign stop in Scarborough on Feb. 7, Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford made it sound like it would be operating sometime in 2025.
"What I'm hearing from Metrolinx (is) everything's up and going. I guess they are doing training right now on it," said Ford. "And as soon as everyone is trained, I'm really, really confident that it will be opening this year."
Shortly after his comments, NDP Leader Marit Stiles called out Ford for his government's lack of transparency on the project.
Stiles promised "on Day One," if her party is elected government, "I am going to tell people when that LRT is going to open."
A spokesperson for Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said this week that the Eglinton LRT should already be open, wouldn't say when exactly it would be if she formed the next government.
"Doug Ford needs to explain why he couldn't get this done, even though it was well underway when he took office," said press secretary Bahoz Dara Aziz. "Bonnie will commit to working with the city and transit partners to get this up and running as soon as possible."
A spokesperson for Mike Schreiner, Green Party leader, said that if elected, they would open the LRT this year.
"Businesses and communities have faced years of disruptions due to the delays under Liberal and Conservative governments, and Ontarians deserve a leader that's going to put an end to the chaos," said Cecilia Stuart.
But it's not just about the Eglinton Crosstown LRT, said Michael-Clarke at Wednesday night's rally.
"There are a lot of routes in this city that could move a lot more people a lot more efficiently with very, very cheap improvements," he said. "Just painted lanes, changes to traffic signals. These are things that I see in cities around the world. They get this ... but our city does not."
CAPTION: About 30 protesters attended the rally, organized by transit advocacy group TTCriders, intended to keep transit on Ontaro voters' minds as they head to the polls next week.A protester hold a sign at Wednesday night's rally outside the unopened Eglinton station on the Eglinton Crosstown LRT.
CREDIT: Andy Takagi

Ontario election polls show slight dip in support for Doug Ford's PCs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-election-polls-show-slight-dip-support/docview/3168743359/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+election+polls+show+slight+dip+in+support+for+Doug+Ford%27s+PCs&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With the Ontario election campaign underway, the Star has a poll tracker so you can follow all the twists and turns ahead of election day on Feb. 27.
We've got the numbers from the top pollsters in one place, with handy charts, and a rolling, last-four-poll average of all the results.
Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives are still in first place, but have seen a slight decline in support over the last several days.

In Ontario's last election, in 2022, Ford's PCs took 40.8 per cent of the popular vote across the province, with the Liberals earning 23.9 per cent and the NDP taking 23.7 per cent. That led Ford to his second straight majority government, with 83 seats. Despite finishing narrowly behind the Liberals in the popular vote, the NDP took 31 seats and the Liberals just eight.
The Star is compiling polling for the 2025 Ontario election from polling firms that received a grade of A or A+ from 338Canada during the 2022 Ontario election. 338Canada is an independent media organization that created a statistical model of electoral projections based on opinion polls, electoral history, and demographic data.
These polling firms use a variety of polling methods, including online surveys and Smart Interactive Voice Response (IVR) systems. As online polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, margins listed here are for a comparable random sample of this size.
Percentage breakdowns are shares of committed or leaning voters, in line with industry conventions.

Document 130 of 500

Remove bike lanes, CaféTO patios and on-street parking, says report on Toronto congestion

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/remove-bike-lanes-caféto-patios-on-street-parking/docview/3168743347/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Remove+bike+lanes%2C+Caf%26eacute%3BTO+patios+and+on-street+parking%2C+says+report+on+Toronto+congestion&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If Toronto wants to ease the traffic choking its streets, it should consider removing bike lanes, on-street parking and curbside patios from its major thoroughfares, according to a new report from the Toronto Region Board of Trade.
The board's task force on congestion released a report on Thursday in which it recommends a wide range of policies to tackle the city's clogged roads, including reducing lane closures, enhancing traffic enforcement, unclogging arterial roads, clearing traffic on the Gardiner and implementing political "accountability mechanisms."
"What the data makes clear is that Toronto's congestion crisis is a crisis of management and inadequate planning," according to the report. The task force commissioned engineering firm Parsons and infrastructure consultancy firm Steer to do the analysis for its report.
The report points mainly at construction - a finding supported by city officials - as the reason for the city's congestion woes, but adds that it has been "compounded by competing street uses such as bike lanes, on-street parking and curb-lane cafes." The report recommends removing those three elements from some of the city's most heavily used roads.
Bike lanes, according to the report, should be on "secondary roads," echoing statements from Doug Ford whose government has vowed to remove bike lanes on Bloor, University and Yonge Streets, as well as put restrictions on building new bike lanes.
The rationale, the report says, is to get "connector roads" like Bloor Street flowing more efficiently with more road space. That's also the reason it recommends reviewing the city's curb lane cafe program to take into account "the impact they have on our circulating traffic."
In 2024, more than 1,400 businesses participated in CaféTO, including 304 curb lane cafés, generating $130 million in economic benefits for the city, according to the Toronto Association of Business Improvement Areas.
The report also recommended moving on-street parking from major arterials into private parking lots and store-owned spaces.
The report also gives a dollar figure for how much closing road lanes for construction is costing the city. Closing one lane can cost $1.7 million a month or $20 million a year. Closing two lanes of the Gardiner, as the city has planned for the expressway's rehabilitation program until 2027, has socioeconomic costs of $200 million.
One way to help reduce offset the costs and speed the construction, the task force recommends, is to increase the price the city charges for lane closures equivalent to other major cities. Toronto currently charges companies $37,000 per month, compared with London, where the fee to close a 40-metre lane for 30 days is $139,000 or Singapore and Sydney, where the charges are more than $50,000.
The city proposed higher fees as part of a congestion management plan released in September, meant to incentivize developers to work faster. Those charges have been approved by city council, but the new rates have yet to be announced.
Raktim Mitra, a TMU professor and co-director of the school's TransFrom Research Laboratory, said that some of the proposed solutions in the task force's report don't "recognize the complexity of current urban systems and local economies."
"There's a danger in converting lively urban streets into road spaces that are primarily moving vehicles from one place to another," he said.
Mitra added that Toronto only has so much road, and pulling away from other road users isn't a solution to congestion.
"We don't want to build roads just for moving the vehicles, (but) streets that are also destinations where there are shops and local businesses."
The report also calls for stricter enforcement of the rules of the road, including using red light cameras for blocking the box and more enforcement on stopping and standings violations. Blocking the box has been a hot-button issue at city hall, with council raising fines.
"Despite years of conversation and well-intended short-term fixes, we remain gridlocked," said Giles Gherson, president of the board of trade. "Toronto's streets are trying to do everything for everyone, and in the process, they're failing to meet their fundamental purpose."
CAPTION: A new report from the board of trade on Toronto's congestion points to construction as being the main culprit behind the city's clogged streets, but with other elements also slowing the flow.
CREDIT: Andy Takagi

Subject: Task forces; Engineering firms; Enforcement; Roads & highways

Business indexing term: Subject: Engineering firms

Title: Remove bike lanes, CaféTO patios and on-street parking, says report on Toronto congestion

Document 131 of 500

I fact checked everything NDP Leader Marit Stiles said for a week during the Ontario election. Here's how many false claims she made

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/i-fact-checked-everything-ndp-leader-marit-stiles/docview/3168743337/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=I+fact+checked+everything+NDP+Leader+Marit+Stiles+said+for+a+week+during+the+Ontario+election.+Here%27s+how+many+false+claims+she+made&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's snap winter election - replete with heated political debate and frigid snow storms - continues on and so does the Star's fact-check project.
The three leading provincial party leaders are each being monitored for a seven-day stretch on the campaign trail, with their statements tracked, recorded and corrected (where needed) by Star reporters.
The first up was Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie. This time, it's NDP Leader Marit Stiles' turn and the final week will focus on the Progressive Conservatives' Doug Ford.
Most of what Stiles said from Feb. 9 to Feb. 15 - the seven days that her announcements, speeches and press events were being monitored - were sweeping statements about how Ford's Progressive Conservative government has failed Ontarians and how the NDP would do better. That general theme was repeated in everything from the perennial provincial election issues of health care and education to the subjects that are particularly prominent in this campaign: affordability and how to respond to U.S. President Donald Trump and his tariff threats. (Stiles also routinely extended her salvos to include what she sees as faults of the previous Liberal government.)
Much of that, along with promises of what a future NDP government might do, is not fact-checkable. But when it comes to statements that included numbers and specific details that could be checked, Stiles was usually accurate.
Out of the 22 claims the Star tracked, Stiles stretched the truth to the point of painting an incomplete picture twice and made two false statements.
Those related to overstating what can be factually proven when speaking about what she sees as failings of Ford and his government and previous Liberal governments.
When it comes to details though, Stiles was almost always accurate on both the big picture, such as the province-wide shortage of doctors and affordable housing, and how those numbers break down in the particular community she was campaigning in.
CAPTION: Stiles fact check poster
CREDIT: Kerry Gillespie

Subject: Provincial elections

Document 132 of 500

It's the dead of winter. You deserve a treat. Cosy up with one these special bottles of wine

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/dead-winter-you-deserve-treat-cosy-up-with-one/docview/3168743116/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=It%27s+the+dead+of+winter.+You+deserve+a+treat.+Cosy+up+with+one+these+special+bottles+of+wine&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's late February. It's freezing out. The news has been a downer. And many of us are staying put instead of jetting off for a February or March vacation. Time to hunker down, invite friends around and enjoy a good bottle of wine. Here are six ways to do so, starting with three bottles from Ontario.
2021 Rosewood Looking Glass Red, VQA Niagara Peninsula (rosewoodwine.com $30.20, free shipping over $200 purchase, 15 per cent discount code:15offLookingGlass)
Score 94
While this gorgeous red is not available at the LCBO, it's worth ordering directly from the winery. It's a brilliant, bone-dry blend of Cabernet Franc and Cabernet Sauvignon seasoned with Petit Verdot that swirls with intoxicating raspberry pie aromas. Then, it unfolds like rich silk on the palate, cascading with muddled black and red berries laced with charcoal, licorice root, cassis and cherry jam. A toasty, biscuity finish completes each sip. This unfiltered red is gratifyingly detailed and full of finesse. And it clocks just 11.8 per cent alcohol.
NV Malivoire Bisous Brut, VQA Beamsville Bench (Vintages 41977, $34.95; Malivoire.com $36.35, free shipping with min. $150 order)
Score 92
If you like fine Champagne, you'll appreciate this bottle - a blend of hand-harvested Chardonnay and Pinot Noir made bubbly via second fermentation in bottle. It's aged on the spent yeast for 36 months to build layers of flavour. And the result is an elegant, complex wine comparable to fine French fizz. The fragrance sings. The bubbles are pinprick tiny. And the taste is a vigorous symphony of lemon butter, croissant, marzipan and apple tart that tapers to a lengthy salt and stone finish. Sophisticated wine for celebrating.
2022 Tawse Quarry Road Organic Riesling VQA Vinemount Ridge (Vintages Essential 198853, $24.95)
Score 91
Always available as a Vintages Essential and quite consistent year-to-year, this bottle is a beautiful, bold expression of Ontario Riesling. The nose is deeply saline and plungingly lime-scented - like slicing a strip of lime peel and holding it to your nose. Then, an eruption of steely-bright flavour floods in and fans out with crushed stone and green mango before returning to the salted-lime character found on the nose. The kiss of sweetness balances the incising acidity of this aromatic charmer. One taste reveals what makes this sweet-tart wine so popular. Pour it as an aperitif or with spicy fare.
NV Nicolas Feuillatte Champagne Réserve Exclusive Rosé, France (Vintages 267039, $64.95)
Score 92
For an exciting splurge, consider this new release. It's a wild-salmon-hued glory of a wine that starts with scents of strawberry jam on toasted brioche. Then, it slips in svelte-tasting and poised with ripe, youthful red fruit, lively bubbles and a creamy-crisp mouth feel along with subtle allusions of blood orange, lemon curd and nutty nougat - the latter emerging on the finish. Serve this Champagne with a mushroom omelette for brunch, on its own as the sun sets or with roasted Atlantic salmon for a fabulous night in.
2020 Maison Sichel Château Argadens Bordeaux Supérieur, France (Vintages Essential 681643, $19.95)
Score 93
Bring France home with a glass of this undervalued Bordeaux. It's a classic blend of Merlot and Cabernet Sauvignon with a splash of Petit Verdot. In the glass, find the stately structure for which Bordeaux is known coupled with a lush fruit centre for a truly hedonistic experience that's terrifically fun to drink. Well-articulated notes of black forest fruit, graphite, tobacco and cedar can be found the nose and palate, along with a light sifting of cinnamon. This bottle is a long, leisurely dinner party waiting to happen.
2021 Villa Annaberta Amarone della Valpolicella DOCG, Veneto, Italy (LCBO 433961, $39.95)
Score 94
Amarone is a special wine. It's made from dried fruit, so it's pricey to make and to buy. But this bottle is a bargain and drinking very well right now. The perfume is a quiet smile. An invitation. A seduction, maybe. The fragrance opens with just the right level of intensity, nodding toward poached cherries with musky-floral undertones and a touch of espresso. Then, it slips in with understated power. The earthy brawniness calls to mind cassis, chocolate, cigar box and coffee crema, while juicy dark fruit lends a certain levity. Just yes.
CAPTION: 2021 Rosewood Looking Glass RedNV Malivoire Bisous Brut, VQA Beamsville Bench2022 Tawse Quarry Road Organic Riesling VQA Vinemount RidgeNV Nicolas Feuillatte Champagne Réserve Exclusive Rosé2020 Maison Sichel Château Argadens Bordeaux Supérieur2021 Villa Annaberta Amarone della Valpolicella DOCGOur wine critic picks six excellent bottles that deserve your attention.
CREDIT: Carolyn Evans Hammond

Document 133 of 500

High-speed rail project is 'real now' says Trudeau: 1,000-km line will run from Toronto to Quebec City

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/high-speed-rail-project-is-real-now-says-trudeau/docview/3168624305/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=High-speed+rail+project+is+%27real+now%27+says+Trudeau%3A+1%2C000-km+line+will+run+from+Toronto+to+Quebec+City&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With a price tag that can only climb into the billions, and an unknown timeline for construction, Justin Trudeau is now leaving a new transportation super-project to his successor as prime minister.
On Wednesday, Liberal leadership contenders vying for his job signalled they support the new plan to build Canada's first high-speed rail line - a 1,000-km rail system that Trudeau hailed as the "largest infrastructure project in Canadian history."
But others, including the federal Conservatives, criticized the announcement from a "lame duck government" with a prime minister who will leave office when the Liberals choose a new leader in less than three weeks.
Speaking Wednesday morning in Montreal, Trudeau unveiled a plan to halve train travel times through Canada's most populous region with a new rail line that will run completely on electricity and travel at speeds of up to 300 km/h between Toronto and Quebec City - with stops in Peterborough, Ottawa, Montreal, Laval and Trois-Rivières.
The government now plans to put up $3.9 billion over the next six years to fund the beginnings of a joint venture between a new Crown corporation and a group of companies that includes Air Canada and the firm formerly known as SNC-Lavalin. This money is the public contribution for the "development phase" of the project, when the Crown agency and private companies will design the project, choose the exact route, decide where to place new train stations, consult with affected Indigenous communities, conduct studies and meet regulatory requirements, Transport Minister Anita Anand said Wednesday.
This process could take up to five years, during which time Anand said the total cost of construction will be determined.
Anand later added that negotiations will now begin between the private companies and the Crown corporation, which was renamed Alto on Wednesday. The result of the talks will be a "complex contract" that is expected to be inked in the coming weeks, which Anand said would ensure the best value for taxpayer dollars.
The private group includes Air Canada, AtkinsRéalis (formerly called SNC-Lavalin), CDPQ INfra, Keolis, SYSTRA and SNCF Voyageurs.
In Montreal, Trudeau predicted the rail system would slash greenhouse gas emissions that cause climate change, add billions to Canada's economy, create thousands of jobs, and bring passenger rail traffic that can get people from Toronto to Montreal in three hours.
"Just think about the 4,000 kilometres of steel rail that we're going to need to lay. That's steel, aluminum, copper - resources we have, that we can use here in Canada," Trudeau said.
"It's a project fit for Canada: big and bold."
Anand, meanwhile, suggested the project is a no-brainer for whomever replaces the Trudeau government.
"It's pretty clear that this is a policy option that should be universally accepted, regardless of the government in place," Anand said.
With less than three weeks before the Liberal party elects a leader to replace Trudeau as prime minister, Liberal leadership contenders expressed support for the rail plan. Mark Carney, the former central banker, said in Scarborough Wednesday that it "has all the potential" to be the type of project the country needs for economic growth.
Former finance minister Chrystia Freeland and former Liberal House leader Karina Gould also expressed support for the project on Wednesday.
At the same time, opposition parties took aim at the rail plan, for different reasons.
The Conservatives alleged the high-speed rail plan is nothing more than "a lame duck statement from a lame duck government," with just weeks before Trudeau leaves as prime minister.
"The Liberals had nine years to make progress on high-speed rail, and all they can point to is money spent on high-priced consultants," said Conservative transport critic Philip Lawrence in an emailed statement.
Citing how the Canadian Pacific Railway took four years to build in the 19th century - an era in which Chinese workers were paid less than half what white workers made, and hundreds of them died, according to the B.C. government - Lawrence alleged the government is now merely announcing a plan to spend $3.9 billion "on planning and bureaucracy" before construction even begins.
"Common sense Conservatives are committed to making Canada's rail network more reliable, efficient and cost effective for Canadians and will take real action to improve our transportation system," Lawrence said. "We won't waste time and money."
The NDP, meanwhile, criticized the Liberals' plan on Wednesday since it includes a partnership with the private sector. The party argues that will drive up costs and delay construction, and also called for the project to be build with Canadian steel and aluminum.
"We want to see high-speed rail built publicly, for the public good," said NDP transport critic and B.C. MP Taylor Bachrach, in a statement Wednesday.
The Liberal government first proposed construction of a new "high frequency" rail line from Toronto to Quebec City in 2019. But, as Trudeau acknowledged Wednesday, the vision expanded since then towards a high-speed line that would more drastically slash train travel times.
He said this will ensure "high-speed land transportation" is available between major cities for the next 100 years.
In 2021, then-transport minister Omar Alghabra floated a rough cost estimate for the new rail project at between $6 billion and $12 billion. But the real cost might actually be more than double that, a government source told the Star last fall, depending on how much of the project is built for high speed, where the tracks end up getting built, and because of factors like inflation that have driven up construction costs in recent years.
A 2017 assessment of a possible "ultra-high speed" rail project from Portland, Oregon to Vancouver, B.C. estimated such a project - with up to 800 km of track - could cost between $32 billion and $57 billion.
For Paul Langan, a longtime high-speed rail advocate based in southwestern Ontario, such a project is long overdue. Japan, for instance, has had high-speed rail for decades. Other countries like France and China and other members of the G7 have had it for years, too.
The idea has also been studied repeatedly in Canada, but no project has ever proceeded. When Jean Chrétien was prime minister in the early 2000s, for example, the government studied a high-speed rail line in the Windsor-Québec City corridor. But the idea was soon shelved.
Asked about that history on Wednesday, Trudeau told reporters that the current project is "real now," with "serious" bids to help build it from the private sector, with the funding to get it started.
Langan expressed hope that Trudeau was right.
"It's as far as we've got in the past, so let's get to the next stage," he said.
"It's time."
CREDIT: Alex Ballingall Deputy Toronto Star

Subject: Construction costs; Railroads; High speed rail; Prime ministers; Cost estimates; Political leadership; Economic growth; Urban development

Document 134 of 500

I'm a Republican from Michigan. Trump's auto tariffs are a terrible, destructive idea for both our countries

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/im-republican-michigan-trumps-auto-tariffs-are/docview/3168623374/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=I%27m+a+Republican+from+Michigan.+Trump%27s+auto+tariffs+are+a+terrible%2C+destructive+idea+for+both+our+countries&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: For over 200 years, the United States and Canada have been more than just allies - we've been family. And like siblings, we may have our petty rivalries in hockey, baseball and basketball. But when it comes to national security and economic prosperity, we've stood together since the War of 1812 ended.
Our bond is so close that missing a highway exit in Detroit can land you in Windsor - and our economies are just as intertwined. Until recently, trade flowed freely, and border crossings were smooth. As a Michigander, I see the connection between us all the time: my neighbour drives a Canadian-built Toyota RAV4, while Michigan factories rely on Canadian parts. Every day, thousands of workers cross the Detroit-Windsor bridge or tunnel, keeping both economies strong.
But that's changing. President Donald Trump's tariff war threatens this partnership, and I worry about the potential long-term damage - not just to our national economies, but also to hardworking people in Michigan and Ontario. As an old-fashioned Republican, I believe in free markets and free trade. Tariffs interfere with business, drive up costs and weaken our economies.
Trump's plan to impose auto tariffs on Canada "in the neighbourhood of 25 per cent," as he put it on Tuesday, has broken the trust that once existed between our two countries. And given Trump's ever-changing trade policies, it's no wonder Canadians are nervous about what's next. His proposed steel and aluminum tariffs are already rattling the auto industry, putting every job in the sector - on both sides of the border - at risk. Ford CEO Jim Farley warned that such tariffs could devastate our joint auto industry like never before.
And when the auto industry suffers, so do Michigan and Ontario. Both economies depend on tax dollars from these companies and the workers who power them. Trade isn't just about money, after all - it's about people, families and communities, too. Tariffs mean job losses, higher prices and struggling small businesses.
In 2024, more than $700 billion in goods moved between the U.S. and Canada, and 25 per cent of that went through Detroit-Windsor. In December 2024 alone, trade between our countries hit $62 billion. Making trade more difficult threatens our shared success. It reminds me of the Prohibition era, when Michigan and Canadian smugglers carried liquor across the frozen Detroit River. Will this generation's outlaws be smuggling car parts rather than liquor? If Canada can't count on the U.S. to honour trade agreements, it'll look elsewhere.
Auto tariffs will disrupt trade and damage the foundations of our U.S.-Canada partnership. Michigan and Ontario would bear the brunt of the fallout, with job losses and business closures, but it wouldn't be just us for long. Michigan has historically led the U.S. by a few years when it comes to recessions. The Great Recession hit Michigan in 2001, for example, while the rest of the country didn't feel it until 2008. If the automotive industry goes dark in Michigan and Ontario, it won't take years for the rest of the North American economy to slow down. It will take months.
The best way forward is clear: Strengthen our partnership. Don't weaken it.
So, Justin Trudeau and Doug Ford, it's time to determine how you're going to respond to what Trump does next. The economies of Michigan and Ontario - not to mention the U.S. and Canada - are depending on you. Let's keep our economic bridge (and tunnel) open.
CAPTION: The automotive industry will likely be among the first to feel the effects of a Canada-U.S. trade war because of how highly integrated it is on both sides of the border, said Pedro Antunes, chief economist at the Conference Board of Canada.
CREDIT: Andrea Bitely, Contributor

Subject: Automobile industry; Prohibition era; Tariffs

Document 135 of 500

Poilievre and Ford both tried to ride the Trump wave. Now one of them risks crashing down

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/poilievre-ford-both-tried-ride-trump-wave-now-one/docview/3168623269/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Poilievre+and+Ford+both+tried+to+ride+the+Trump+wave.+Now+one+of+them+risks+crashing+down&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With anti-Trump feeling sweeping the nation, every Canadian politician opposes the U.S. President's menacing plan to take over Canada.
But Donald Trump's brazen aggression has created a bedevilling problem for federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre and Ontario PC Leader Doug Ford.
Both Poilievre and Ford - who have enjoyed popularity beyond their own hardcore conservative bases, racking up huge support in vote-rich Ontario - now have a serious Trump problem.
Politically, both men are very in tune with Trump, and have said nice things about his policies over the years - things that appealed to their conservative supporters and didn't attract much broader attention.
But, starting early last December, Trump's threats to make Canada the 51st state have overturned that applecart, leaving the two conservative leaders suddenly navigating very slippery political tightropes.
Poilievre, in particular, has been slow to grasp and adjust to the new Trump-created dynamic. Reluctant to abandon his successful strategy focused on attacking the carbon tax, the Conservative leader was restrained in his early reaction to the Trump threats, mostly stating without much emotion or fanfare that Canada would not become the 51st state.
By late December, Poilievre was still comfortable associating himself with Trump, telling the Times of London that he could rebuild Canada's relationship with Trump through a shared "warrior culture, not a woke culture."
Poilievre's subdued response - along with questions about how effective his sullen, smart-alecky jabs would be against a nuclear-armed, would-be dictator - have turned his rock-solid lead in the polls to jelly. A number of polls now have Poilievre in a close race against the unelected Mark Carney, who hasn't even secured the Liberal leadership yet, but who has been clear and unequivocal in his opposition to the U.S. "bully."
So, while Carney denigrates Trump as a bully, Poilievre has elevated Trump to "warrior" status.
Finally, at an Ottawa rally last weekend, Poilievre tried to distance himself from Trump, vowing to "pay any price" to protect Canada's sovereignty. But even this reset has some clear Trumpian vibes to it.
Realizing his old slogan "Canada is broken" isn't quite right now that Canadians have become fiercely patriotic, Poilievre has adopted the new slogan "Canada First" - which bears an uncanny resemblance to Trump's long-time slogan "America First."
The Conservative leader is surely aware of that resemblance, as well as the vile connections of the "America First" slogan, which became widely associated with the Ku Klux Klan and antisemitism before World War II.
Poilievre also sounded very Trumpian earlier this month when he stressed that he planned to cover the costs of a new military base he wants to build in Canada's north by using a payment method recently showcased by Trump - cutting foreign aid.
Presumably, these Trump-style flourishes are meant to reassure Poilievre's pro-Trump followers that nothing has really changed, even as he ramps up his "pay any price" rhetoric to satisfy ordinary Canadians.
Doug Ford has a similar Trump problem, but he got out in front of it. He quickly donned his "Canada is Not for Sale" cap, talked tough about blocking U.S. liquor sales in Ontario and briefly threatened to shred Ontario's $100 million contract with Elon Musk, the mega-billionaire who (along with his four-year-old son) appears to be running the Oval Office.
Ford's fondness for Trump was exposed briefly earlier this month when a video surfaced in which the premier privately describes himself as "one hundred per cent" happy about Trump's re-election, until the president "pulled out the knife."
Ford's premature election call may save him. Although completely unnecessary and hugely costly, it looks like it will enable him to lock in a new mandate next week before Ontarians fully grasp his long-time enthusiasm for the American megalomaniac who now looms ominously at our southern border.
Poilievre has proved less agile. And with a federal election still a couple of months away, his Trump problem will have more time to metastasize.
CAPTION: Local youth skate with a large Canadian flag on the Rideau Canal to launch celebrations for the 60th Anniversary of the National Flag of Canada Day, Friday, Feb. 14, 2025 in Ottawa. THE CANADIAN PRESS/Adrian Wyld
CREDIT: Linda McQuaig

Subject: Military bases; Leadership; Flags

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

Location: United States--US; Canada

Document 136 of 500

Advertisements to Americans should help Ontario, not Ford

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/advertisements-americans-should-help-ontario-not/docview/3168617271/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Advertisements+to+Americans+should+help+Ontario%2C+not+Ford&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: For months, the Ontario government has been promoting the narrative that "it's happening here" through a barrage of ads published in newspapers across the province and online. The ads state that "more people are working than ever before," with "over 700,000 more people working today than in 2018." That's the year Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives came to power.
Technically, these were non-partisan ads. They complied with the Government Advertising Act of 2004, which allows for the promotion of Ontario or any part of it as a desirable location for living, working, investing, studying or visiting. Yet the PCs previously took issue with this very approach, when they opposed a decision by Kathleen Wynne's Liberals to relax rules around partisan government advertising.
Once in power, the PCs apparently discovered that government advertising can be an effective tool for fostering positive impressions of the governing party.
When such advertising precedes an election, it supplements a party's paid election advertising. There would likely be an uproar if the "it's happening here" campaign ran during this snap election, as additional scrutiny is usually applied during an election period to ensure that advertisements funded by the government do not appear to support the governing party. But placing ads in the Wall Street Journal that portray Ontario as America's "secure economic partner in an insecure world" falls under advocacy advertising.
Ontario and Canada should both undertake media campaigns in the U.S. to convey strong messages to American audiences.
The Wall Street Journal ad, for example, advocates effectively for Canada's critical minerals sector: "We have an abundance of the critical minerals needed for both economic growth and the new technologies that keep our countries safe." It further states, "In this time of global instability, let's strengthen the economic bonds we've always shared."
This seems reasonable - until you consider that supporters of U.S. President Donald Trump's "America first" agenda would likely endorse both statements, with the rationale that "strengthening the economic bonds we've always shared" implies erasing the border.
Although the province's ads were no doubt intended to alter American decision-makers' and influencers' perceptions of Ontario and Canada, this is a fluid situation, to say the least. The Ford government is walking a fine line as it attempts to fight back against Trump's threats without antagonizing Trump's team.
One presumes the Ontario government tested these ads via focus group before spending massive amounts of money booking slots in U.S. media - but what about now? Do they still resonate after a month of Trump's executive orders? Do they firmly communicate our position?
Canada is a polite neighbour. We initially laughed off Trump's threats to annex the country and make it the 51st state. But it's time for a different narrative, one that's far less deferential.
Ford, who has styled himself as Ontario's protector and claims to need a stronger mandate for the fight ahead, might want his U.S. ad campaign to be more explicit.
The message should be: Ontario is open for business, but it's not for sale.
For now, these commercials and video clips of Ford's visits to Washington remain a win-win for the PCs, but not for Ontario taxpayers.
Éric Blais is president of Headspace Marketing in Toronto, a marketing communications firm helping clients build their brands in Quebec.
CREDIT: Éric Blais Contributor Éric Blais is president of Headspace Marketing in Toronto, a marketing communications firm helping clients build their brands in Quebec.;

Subject: Political campaigns; Marketing; Advertising; Critical minerals

Document 137 of 500

I was a longtime Liberal who swore off Trudeau. This is what changed for voters like me

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/i-was-longtime-liberal-who-swore-off-trudeau-this/docview/3168616170/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=I+was+a+longtime+Liberal+who+swore+off+Trudeau.+This+is+what+changed+for+voters+like+me&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It was a more innocent time, kinder and gentler. Life in Canada last September was simple and the politics predictable.
Jagmeet Singh was ripping up his deal with the Liberals, Pierre Poilievre was still axing the tax, and Justin Trudeau was pondering which pair of socks to wear. Just another day in Ottawa.
At the time, I vowed that I would never again vote for the Liberal party as long as Trudeau was leader.
The world has since been upended. Overnight, our nearest and dearest neighbour turned into a seething psychopath. Trudeau finally took his long-overdue walk in the snow. And I've found myself back inside the big red tent.
I was invited to write this column, to describe my turnaround, but I must admit it's awkward. I sound like a Liberal shill and I hate it, especially after a life in journalism carefully avoiding public partisanship. But I'm retired now, which changes things. And my reverse journey, I suspect, is probably one to which many Canadians across the country can relate.
It's a sentiment reflected in the national pulse. While only recently the Trudeau Liberals seemed doomed and the Conservatives certain winners, recent polls suggest that, with Mark Carney as leader, a federal election would be competitive again.
I likely would have returned to the Liberal tent even if the United States had not opted for the buffoonish malignancy now festering in its own body, and spreading to the rest of the world.
My bar was low. As long as the Liberals were no longer led by a man with disproportionate fondness for personal affectation and a penchant for optics I was ready to return.
The party, after all, has always occupied a centre-left spectrum that reflects my values. At their best, Liberal governments have been both progressive and fiscally responsible. But the unkept promises, virtue-signalled priorities, poor fiscal management and empty sloganeering of the last decade have not been their finest hour.
No wonder people like me abandoned the Liberal ship. And yet, to our dismay, there was really nowhere to jump.
Under Singh, the NDP has continued to be ... the NDP, earnest and ineffectual. And under Poilievre, the Conservatives simply mimic their snarky leader and his campaign posters. Obedient caucus members emerge from behind closed doors and spout talking points consisting of schoolyard labels and the alliteration and rhyme favoured by poetic adolescents.
They stand behind microphones and actually use "axe the tax" in a sentence, while referring with a straight face to "Carbon Tax Carney."
Which must be distressing for them since Mark Carney, like the other Liberal hopefuls, has already promised to axe that particular tax. Too bad the line has been the centrepiece of the Conservatives' relentless and costly ad campaign for the past year.
As for Carney, I'm delighted he's in the mix and, if polling holds, soon to be leader. More than merely an anti-Poilievre or anti-Trudeau, the guy is legitimately impressive.
Insecure people will dismiss his Harvard and Oxford past as elitist, but not me. I appreciate top-notch education and intellectual heft in my leaders. I also appreciate Carney's inside knowledge of how government, finance and trade operate here and abroad.
Steering Canada through the 2008 financial crisis as Bank of Canada governor; guiding the U.K. through Brexit as Bank of England governor; dealing at that time with the whims and disorder of the first Trumpian attempt at imperial rule - such battle-hardened experiences bode well for Canada.
Reacting to Trump 2.0 and the recent stab in Canada's back, Carney has made it clear he's committed to forging new trade alliances and realistically exploiting Canada for Canadians. Unlike Poilievre, who blathers on about the same goals he had when he was 20, Carney represents matured leadership.
He offers this country an opportunity to rejig its economy from a more practical and informed point of view. He offers us the chance to re-evaluate what we have, the vast resources that, to our shame, we have underutilized or sold. He offers the promise of new independence.
He embodies hope.
For me, at this darkly complicated time in the enduring magnificence that is Canada, that is enough.
CAPTION: Mark Carney, candidate for the leadership of the Liberal Party of Canada, speaks during a news conference in Vancouver, B.C., Thursday, Feb. 13, 2025.
CREDIT: Janice Kennedy Contributor

Subject: Leadership

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

Location: Canada

People: Carney, Mark

He embodies hope. For me, at this darkly complicated time in the enduring magnificence that is Canada, that is enough.
Janice Kennedy is a retired journalist. She lives in Ottawa.

Vladimir Guerrero can now seek a winning club

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/vladimir-guerrero-can-now-seek-winning-club/docview/3168616090/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Vladimir+Guerrero+can+now+seek+a+winning+club&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Guerrero plans to become free agent after season, fails to reach long-term deal with Blue Jays, Feb. 18
Blue Jays baseball's management (Mark Shapiro and Ross Atkins) is said to have offered Juan Soto more than $600 million and came up short again. Vladimir Guerero's superstar potential is off the charts but he was apparently not worth a $450 million long-term contract. I have always maintained that Guerrero grew tired of Jays' management incompetence, and he will now seek to sign with a winning club. Look to Bo Bichette to follow his dear friend out the Jay door. Personally, I have had enough and until such times as Jays find solid baseball executives to replace the dynamic duo, I am done attending games.
David Maki, Toronto
Player's salaries are ridiculousI have to admit that I am disappointed that we couldn't come to an agreement to keep the popular Vladimir Guerero. However, it never ceases to amaze me the ridiculous amount of money that we pay our professional sports figures. In real life you get paid for what you do on a day-to-day basis. Bonuses come at the end of the year after your performance has been measured. Not so in professional sports. They get paid for what we hope they will do in the future. So, these players make ridiculous amounts of money that is guaranteed for the duration of the contract regardless of how they perform during that contract. If a player has several poor years, like George Springer has demonstrated, do we get back some of the money that was given to him? I wish Vladdy well and I'm sure that some rich organization will give him what he thinks he's worth. I think that we have to give the Jay organization some credit here. There has to be some form of sanity regarding salary negotiations.
Steve Glover, Aurora
Outstanding reporting captures life-threatening ordeal
'Everyone's Upside Down,' Feb. 18
I would like to congratulate Mark Colley for his outstanding report on the crash of the Delta flight. Like many other Torontonians, I was glued to the TV for updates on the incident. None of the reports I saw captured the feelings of someone who got caught in a life-threatening situation as vividly, e.g. noting the interviewee said he didn't sit down because other people who sat down started crying, or that he said he was bouncing on his toes. How does one convey the stress and adrenaline rush unless one includes what others may dismiss as minor details? It's an empathetic piece and it made me feel I was going through the ordeal together with Nick Cottigan. Well done!
Gloria Fung, Unionville, ON
Trump's approach to Canada is craftier than Putin's in Ukraine
A Trump peace deal on Ukraine would signal his plans for the rest of us, Feb. 16
Andrew Phillips observes that Trump's aspirations for Canada parallel Russian President Vladimir Putin's intentions for Ukraine. Both depend on the claim that great powers are entitled to exercise dominion within their traditional spheres of influence.
I think the meaning of Phillips' important point merits enlargement. Putin has been using armed force to try to seize Ukraine. His overall goal, thus far unsuccessful, has been to recreate Greater Russia by swallowing up Russia's westerly neighbour, whose sovereignty he repudiates. Trump has proclaimed an interest in using economic force to compel Canada to become the 51st state. His overall goal, which parallels Putin's, has been to remake American greatness. Apparently, this now includes swallowing up America's northern neighbour.
Both cases speak not so much to spheres of influence as to spheres of imperial expansion. The difference between Putin's approach and Trump's approach is that economic force doesn't require bloodshed. That aside, Trump's approach is simply the craftier way for a bully to try - against historical precedent - to absorb a neighbour's land and people with what he hopes will be a minimum of effort.
We must stand in his way.
Jeff Rose, Toronto
Time to ask UK for help
Where's our King amid Trump's '51st state' threats? Charles stays frustratingly silent as Canada gets bullied, Feb. 16
Rosie DiManno is spot on in writing that it's time for King Charles to let U.S. President Donald Trump know that he is "not amused" by Trump's constant threats and insults to Canada. Of course, Charles cannot speak publicly about this but as DiManno says, British Royals have always found ways to opine. However, a more effective and direct way to message Trump would be for Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to ask British Prime Minister Keir Starmer to have a word with Trump. It's reported that Starmer is one of the few people who Trump respects. It's rare that one country asks another for diplomatic help when dealing with a third one, but it's warranted in this case. Keir Starmer, as UK Prime Minister, is skilled diplomatically and is the appropriate head of state to remind Trump that Canada is "spoken for' and that his constant denigration of Canada is not appreciated in the UK /Commonwealth.
Bruce Robb, Toronto
Freeland can lead the wayChrystia Freeland anticipated the troubled times we're living in years ago, Feb. 19
David Olive brought out Chrystia Freeland's brilliant anticipation of the world's advanced economics. His article showing her awareness over the years of the direction the world's economics were heading really points out her full understanding of where it was heading. Too bad she was ignored by all. We need her as Prime Minister to lead us back to being a positive country .
Chris Andrews, Vaughan
We must change the way we governIt's time for the Ontario Liberals and NDP to get over themselves and stop fighting for second place, Feb. 17
As a lifelong NDPer, I totally agree with the NDP and Liberals joining of forces. In Niagara Centre, where I am located, the Liberal candidate is a former Brock student now living in Hamilton - a vote drainer and splitter. I liked policies of both the NDP and the Liberals in the debate. The truth is that it is not popularity that counts but final seat count. Ontario must change direction, change the way we govern. A co-premier arrangement and agreement would allow two strong women to take on all of the problems that we face. Two strong equal forces would have Donald Trump shaking.
Larry Rosnuk, Port Colborne, On
May the best hockey team win using skill and civility
'It's our Game 7': Canada confident as 4 Nations Face-Off shifts to Boston, Feb. 16
Hockey is our national pride in sports. The referees need to warn the coaches for game 7 in the 4 Nations final that fighting will not be tolerated. There will be match misconduct penalties. May the best hockey team win using pride in demonstrating skill and civility and not by exercising goon hockey. Lifetime hockey fan on the line.
Morris Saldov, Toronto

Document 140 of 500

Ford got in front of Trump problem early, unlike Poilievre

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-got-front-trump-problem-early-unlike/docview/3168616059/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+got+in+front+of+Trump+problem+early%2C+unlike+Poilievre&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With anti-Trump feeling sweeping the nation, every Canadian politician opposes the U.S. president's menacing plan to take over Canada.
But Donald Trump's brazen aggression has created a bedevilling problem for federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre and Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford.
Both Poilievre and Ford - who have enjoyed popularity beyond their own hardcore conservative bases, racking up huge support in vote-rich Ontario - now have a serious Trump problem.
Politically, both men are very in tune with Trump, and have said nice things about his policies over the years - things that appealed to their conservative supporters and didn't attract much broader attention.
But, starting early last December, Trump's threats to make Canada the 51st state have overturned that applecart, leaving the two conservative leaders suddenly navigating very slippery political tightropes.
Poilievre, in particular, has been slow to grasp and adjust to the new Trump-created dynamic. Reluctant to abandon his successful strategy focused on attacking the carbon tax, the Conservative leader was restrained in his early reaction to the Trump threats, mostly stating without much emotion or fanfare that Canada would not become the 51st state.
By late December, Poilievre was still comfortable associating himself with Trump, telling the Times of London that he could rebuild Canada's relationship with Trump through a shared "warrior culture, not a woke culture."
Poilievre's subdued response - along with questions about how effective his sullen, smart-alecky jabs would be against a nuclear-armed, would-be dictator - have turned his rock-solid lead in the polls to jelly. A number of polls now have Poilievre in a close race against the unelected Mark Carney, who hasn't even secured the Liberal leadership yet, but who has been clear and unequivocal in his opposition to the U.S. "bully."
So, while Carney denigrates Trump as a bully, Poilievre has elevated Trump to "warrior" status.
Finally, at an Ottawa rally last weekend, Poilievre tried to distance himself from Trump, vowing to "pay any price" to protect Canada's sovereignty. But even this reset has some clear Trumpian vibes to it.
Realizing his old slogan "Canada is broken" isn't quite right now that Canadians have become fiercely patriotic, Poilievre has adopted the new slogan "Canada First" - which bears an uncanny resemblance to Trump's long-time slogan "America First."
The Conservative leader is surely aware of that resemblance, as well as the vile connections of the "America First" slogan, which became widely associated with the Ku Klux Klan and antisemitism before the Second World War.
Poilievre also sounded very Trumpian this month when he stressed that he planned to cover the costs of a new military base he wants to build in Canada's north by using a payment method recently showcased by Trump - cutting foreign aid.
Presumably, these Trump-style flourishes are meant to reassure Poilievre's pro-Trump followers that nothing has really changed, even as he ramps up his "pay any price" rhetoric to satisfy ordinary Canadians.
Ford has a similar Trump problem, but he got out in front of it. He quickly donned his "Canada is Not for Sale" cap, talked tough about blocking U.S. liquor sales in Ontario and briefly threatened to shred Ontario's $100-million contract with Elon Musk, the mega-billionaire who (along with his four-year-old son) appears to be running the Oval Office.
Ford's fondness for Trump was exposed briefly this month when a video surfaced in which the PC leader privately describes himself as "one hundred per cent" happy about Trump's re-election, until the president "pulled out the knife."
Ford's premature election call may save him. Although completely unnecessary and hugely costly, it looks like it will enable him to lock in a new mandate next week before Ontarians fully grasp his long-time enthusiasm for the American megalomaniac who now looms ominously at our southern border.
Poilievre has proved less agile. And with a federal election still a couple of months away, his Trump problem will have more time to metastasize.
Linda McQuaig is an author,
journalist and a freelance
contributing columnist for The Star.
CREDIT: Linda McQuaig OPINION Linda McQuaig is an author, journalist and a freelance contributing columnist for The Star.;

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Guerrero+plans+to+become+free+agent+after+season%2C+fails+to+reach+long-term+deal+with+Blue+Jays%2C+Feb.+18+%27Everyone%27s+Upside+Down%2C%27+Feb.+18+A+Trump+peace+deal+on+Ukraine+would+signal+his+plans+for+the+rest+of+us%2C+Feb.+16+Where%27s+our+King+amid+Trump%27s+%2751st+state%27+threats%3F+Charles+stays+frustratingly+silent+as+Canada+gets+bullied%2C+Feb.+16+Chrystia+Freeland+anticipated+the+troubled+times+we%27re+living+in+years+ago%2C+Feb.+19+It%27s+time+for+the+Ontario+Liberals+and+NDP+to+get+over+themselves+and+stop+fighting+for+second+place%2C+Feb.+17&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Vladdy can seek a winning club
Guerrero plans to become free agent after season, fails to reach long-term deal with Blue Jays, Feb. 18
Blue Jays baseball's management (Mark Shapiro and Ross Atkins) is said to have offered Juan Soto more than $600 million and came up short again. Vladimir Guerrero Jr.'s superstar potential is off the charts but he was apparently not worth a $450-million long-term contract. I have always maintained that Guerrero grew tired of the Jays' management incompetence and he will now seek to sign with a winning club. Look to Bo Bichette to follow his dear friend out the door. Personally, I have had enough and until such time as the Jays find solid baseball executives to replace the dynamic duo, I am done attending games.
David Maki, Toronto
Players' salaries are ridiculous
I have to admit that I am disappointed that we couldn't come to an agreement to keep the popular Vladimir Guerrero Jr. However, it never ceases to amaze me the ridiculous amount of money that we pay our professional sports figures. In real life you get paid for what you do on a day-to-day basis. Bonuses come at the end of the year after your performance has been measured. Not so in professional sports. They get paid for what we hope they will do in the future. So, these players make ridiculous amounts of money that is guaranteed for the duration of the contract regardless of how they perform during that contract. If a player has several poor years, like George Springer has demonstrated, do we get back some of the money that was given to him? I wish Vladdy well. I think that we have to give the Jay organization some credit here. There has to be some form of sanity regarding salary negotiations.
Steve Glover, Aurora
Great reporting on plane crash
'Everyone's Upside Down,' Feb. 18
I would like to congratulate Mark Colley for his outstanding report on the crash of the Delta Air Lines flight. Like many other Torontonians, I was glued to the TV for updates on the incident. None of the reports I saw captured the feelings of someone who got caught in a life-threatening situation as vividly, e.g. noting the interviewee said he didn't sit down because other people who sat down started crying, or that he said he was bouncing on his toes. How does one convey the stress and adrenalin rush unless one includes what others may dismiss as minor details? It made me feel I was going through the ordeal together with Nick Cottigan. Well done!
Gloria Fung, Unionville
Two different approaches but the same goal
A Trump peace deal on Ukraine would signal his plans for the rest of us, Feb. 16
Andrew Phillips observes that Donald Trump's aspirations for Canada parallel Russian President Vladimir Putin's intentions for Ukraine. Both depend on the claim that great powers are entitled to exercise dominion within their traditional spheres of influence.
Putin has been using armed force to try to seize Ukraine. His overall goal, thus far unsuccessful, has been to recreate Greater Russia by swallowing up Russia's westerly neighbour, whose sovereignty he repudiates. Trump has proclaimed an interest in using economic force to compel Canada to become the 51st state. His overall goal, which parallels Putin's, has been to remake American greatness. Apparently, this now includes swallowing up America's northern neighbour.
Both cases speak not so much to spheres of influence as to spheres of imperial expansion. The difference between Putin's approach and Trump's approach is that economic force doesn't require bloodshed. That aside, Trump's approach is simply the craftier way for a bully to try - against historical precedent - to absorb a neighbour's land and people with what he hopes will be a minimum of effort.
We must stand in his way.
Jeff Rose, Toronto
Time to ask the U.K. for help
Where's our King amid Trump's '51st state' threats? Charles stays frustratingly silent as Canada gets bullied, Feb. 16
Rosie DiManno is spot on in writing that it's time for King Charles to let U.S. President Donald Trump know that he is "not amused" by Trump's constant threats and insults to Canada. Of course, Charles cannot speak publicly about this but as DiManno says, but the Royals have always found ways to opine. However, a more effective and direct way to message Trump would be for Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to ask U.K. Prime Minister Keir Starmer to have a word with Trump. It's reported that Starmer is one of the few people who Trump respects. It's rare that one country asks another for diplomatic help when dealing with a third one, but it's warranted in this case. Starmer is skilled diplomatically and is the appropriate head of state to remind Trump that Canada is "spoken for" and that his constant denigration of Canada is not appreciated in the Commonwealth.
Bruce Robb, Toronto
Freeland can
lead the way
Chrystia Freeland anticipated the troubled times we're living in years ago, Feb. 19
David Olive brought out Chrystia Freeland's brilliant anticipation of the world's advanced economics. His article showing her awareness over the years of the direction the world's economics were heading really points out her full understanding of the issue. Too bad she was ignored. We need her as prime minister to lead us back to being a positive country.
Chris Andrews, Vaughan
We must change how we govern
It's time for the Ontario Liberals and NDP to get over themselves and stop fighting for second place, Feb. 17
As a lifelong NDPer, I totally agree with the NDP and Liberals joining of forces. In Niagara Centre, the Liberal candidate is a former Brock student now living in Hamilton - a vote drainer and splitter. I liked policies of both the NDP and the Liberals in the debate. The truth is that it is not popularity that counts, but final seat count. Ontario must change direction, change the way we govern. A co-premier arrangement and agreement would allow two strong women to take on all of the problems that we face. Two strong equal forces would have Donald Trump shaking.
Larry Rosnuk, Port Colborne, Ont.

Document 142 of 500

Crombie slams Ford over attacks: Liberal leader accuses PC leader of slinging 'mud' and ducking public events as election nears

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/crombie-slams-ford-over-attacks/docview/3168615971/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Crombie+slams+Ford+over+attacks%3A+Liberal+leader+accuses+PC+leader+of+slinging+%27mud%27+and+ducking+public+events+as+election+nears&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford is riding high and laying low.
With the Progressive Conservative leader - who polls suggest is the front-runner in the Feb. 27 election - ducking public events for days, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie came out swinging Wednesday.
"They want to sling mud," Crombie told reporters in Rockland, near Ottawa, referring to PC attacks against her.
"If Doug Ford has something to say to me, he should have the balls to say it to my face," she said.
"That's what I'm saying to him right now. You got something to complain about? Come to me. Show your face. Where's he been? He called an early unnecessary election at the cost of $189 million, he's been absent for 50 per cent of it," said the Liberal chief.
"Where has he been? Where is he today? He's been absent and that's taking voters for granted. That is disrespectful. Show your face and be accountable for your record."
Ford has had no public events since Monday's televised leaders' debate at the CBC Broadcast Centre.
The Tory was the only leader to skip the post-debate press conferences with reporters. He also dodged journalists following the northern issues debate in North Bay last Friday.
After Monday's plane crash at Pearson airport caused flight delays, his campaign abandoned plans for a two-day swing through Sault Ste. Marie, Sudbury and Thunder Bay.
While he was in Niagara on Wednesday, his campaign itinerary was kept secret and no events were open to the media.
Later in the day, Ford posted on social media about his private tours of two businesses there, where he talked "about how a re-elected PC government will protect Ontario workers and stand up for our province."
He is expected to meet with reporters on Thursday before flying to Washington the next day.
It will be his second campaign swing to the U.S. capital to lobby against President Donald Trump's threatened 25 per cent tariffs that would devastate Ontario's trade-dependent economy.
While the PC chief was staying out of public view, his campaign was on the attack, taking aim at Liberal and New Democratic candidates for past transgressions in a bid to keep his rivals on their toes.
Andrea Lawlor, a political science professor at McMaster University, said with his lead in the polls, "no news is good news" for Ford in the final week before election day.
"He does fairly well in campaigns, but he is also prone to making slip-ups and he's made a few in this campaign," said Lawlor, referring to the Tory's high-profile gaffes about being "100 per cent" happy Donald Trump won the U.S. election and musing about the death penalty for criminals.
"His team will be interested in, as much as possible, to locking him down and prevent the opportunity for those types of slip-ups to occur and ride the continued public opinion wave all the way to an electoral victory," she said.
Indeed, the most recent Abacus Data survey for the Star, which was conducted before the debate, found Ford's Tories with 41 per cent support compared to Crombie's Liberals at 28 per cent, Stiles's New Democrats at 21 per cent and Mike Schreiner's Greens at six per cent.
That was a five percentage point drop for the Tories and a four percentage point increase for the Liberals since the previous Abacus survey released Feb. 7.
Abacus surveyed 1,500 Ontarians from last Thursday through Saturday using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 2.7 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
Ford was not the only leader with no public campaign events on Wednesday.
Stiles was doing a series of broadcast interviews across the province as her New Democrats aim to shore up support.
"Doug Ford is in hiding," the NDP leader told Richard Southern of 680 News.
"He's unwilling to defend his record. He's hoping he can get away with that. He's hoping people just won't go out and vote," she said.
The Green leader, meanwhile, was in Georgetown, promising a break in employer health taxes to help small businesses cope with challenges like inflation and high rent increases that have forced some to move away from long-standing locations for cheaper premises.
"Small businesses struggle," Schreiner told supporters.
CAPTION: Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie made it a point Wednesday to call out PC Leader Doug Ford's lack of public appearances on the campaign trail. "He called an early unnecessary election at the cost of $189 million, he's been absentfor 50 per cent of it ... be accountable for your record," she said.R.J. Johnston Toronto Star
CREDIT: Robert Benzie, Rob Ferguson and Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau
