Document 156 of 500

Struggling to follow the Ontario election? Thank Elon Musk and Mark Zuckerberg

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/struggling-follow-ontario-election-thank-elon/docview/3168222576/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Struggling+to+follow+the+Ontario+election%3F+Thank+Elon+Musk+and+Mark+Zuckerberg&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario is in the middle of an election. But amidst a trade war and chaotic news environment, it would be no surprise if voters are having trouble paying attention.
After voter turnout reached historic lows in 2022, we may again be sleepwalking towards a similar outcome. Part of this is by design. After all, Doug Ford did call a snap election in the dead of winter while everyone was dumbstruck by Donald Trump threatening Canada with trade wars and annexation.
But it feels like there's more at play here than mere distraction. Ford has already made a handful of gaffes on the campaign trail that would derail or harm most campaigns in normal times. He has described welfare recipients as lazy youths who watch "The Flintstones." He's admitted he was originally "happy" Trump won the election. He also flippantly mused about the death penalty at a police charity gala. It's all had almost no effect.
It's unclear what, if anything, Ontario voters are hearing about any of these news stories. The truth is all of our elections now take place in the context of a profoundly broken news and information environment. Not only has it become harder to find reliable information, it's also harder to know what's true or what to care about.
Firstly, Ontario's election is happening at a moment when newspapers and broadcasters are laying off journalists and shutting down newsrooms.
Meanwhile social media platforms have become far worse for distributing election news.
Meta, the American social media giant run by Mark Zuckerberg, announced in 2023 that it would block all Canadian news content on Facebook and Instagram in response to the Liberal government's Online News Act; one report estimates the change meant Canadian news organizations lost a staggering 11 million views per day.
Two years earlier, the company announced it was tweaking its algorithm to reduce political content. Facebook's own data shows that following this change, engagement on top election news stories dropped around 90% between Ontario's 2018 and 2022 elections - the same year voter turnout hit rock bottom.
Meanwhile on "X," users keep fleeing the platform formerly known as Twitter as it degenerates into a fever swamp of far-right propaganda, hateful trolls and junk information under the ownership of billionaire tech oligarch and key Trump ally, Elon Musk.
Musk, who is the subject of multiple investigations by European regulators and prosecutors looking into allegations he is manipulating X's algorithm to boost far-right political messages, does admit making some algorithm tweaks to arbitrarily control what shows up on users' screens. One of those tweaks made it harder to see news content.
Whatever influence these platforms and their mysterious, unregulated algorithms have on voter behaviour, the disappearance or drowning out of legitimate news from social media feeds means voters are very likely less aware of what's happening. If it feels that many in Ontario are disengaged, that may well be why.
No democracy can function without informed citizens empowered to make their own choices about their own communities and their own country. But with both professional journalism and social media now significantly degraded, this feels harder than ever.
Worse, the connection between access to journalism and democratic participation has been documented by numerous studies from around the world. Studies in the United States show communities that lose local newspapers see corresponding declines in voter turnout, while one British study estimates a 1 per cent increase in daily news readership corresponds with a 0.3 per cent increase in voter turnout.
We may also be heading towards a real national crisis. With federal Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre vowing to defund CBC News and cut subsidies to Canadian news organizations, Poilievre will only pour gas on the fire and leave Canadians without our own platforms to share our stories.
News and social media platforms are the informational infrastructure of our public discourse. They create the conditions that allow us to develop coherent narratives about our world, form opinions about issues and make informed decisions that can have life or death implications.
When our information landscape becomes so degraded that we struggle to share news, debate ideas or even talk about our elections, can you blame anyone if they say they feel disconnected?
CAPTION: Tech billionaire Elon Musk's revamping of X, formerly known as Twitter, which now has false information, is part of why the contemporary information landscape has changed.
CREDIT: Luke LeBrun Contributor

Subject: Trade disputes; Political campaigns; Algorithms; Voters; Social networks; Elections; Voter behavior

Business indexing term: Subject: Trade disputes Social networks

People: Musk, Elon; Zuckerberg, Mark

Title: Struggling to follow the Ontario election? Thank Elon Musk and Mark Zuckerberg

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2025

Publication date: Feb 19, 2025

Section: opinion/contributors

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto

Country of publication: Canada

Source type: Blog, Podcast, or Website

Language of publication: English

Feel disconnected? Thank tech giants

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/feel-disconnected-thank-tech-giants/docview/3168222543/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Feel+disconnected%3F+Thank+tech+giants&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

When our information landscape becomes so degraded that we struggle to share news, debate ideas or even talk about our elections, can you blame anyone if they say they feel disconnected?
Luke LeBrun is the editor of PressProgress.
CAPTION: Tech billionaire Elon Musk's revamping of X, formerly known as Twitter and now rife with junk information, is part of why the contemporary information landscape has changed, writes Luke Lebrun.JIM WATSON AFP via Getty Images
CREDIT: Luke LeBrun Contributor Luke LeBrun is the editor of PressProgress.;

Document 158 of 500

Has Poilievre changed his message to supporters?: Some say context has changed, but Tory leader has been consistent

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/has-poilievre-changed-his-message-supporters/docview/3168222359/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Has+Poilievre+changed+his+message+to+supporters%3F%3A+Some+say+context+has+changed%2C+but+Tory+leader+has+been+consistent&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Pivot? What pivot?
That was the message Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre projected Saturday at a patriotic, campaign-style rally that framed the Conservatives as the party of "Canada First."
In doing so, Poilievre rejected the notion that his party was officially abandoning its relentless crusade against the "carbon tax" to take on the more immediate threat posed by U.S. President Donald Trump. Instead, he suggested protecting Canada's interests has been his core message all along.
"You know, before the tariff threats, we were talking about a carbon tax election. But now with these threats, I have to concede, I've had to reflect on whether it is a top of mind issue. And after much reflection, I have concluded that the carbon tax will be an even bigger issue in the coming election than we thought," Poilievre said in a rousing, hour-long speech that also served as his clearest condemnation of Trump's expansionist and protectionist agenda.
He took particular aim at Liberal leadership front-runner Mark Carney, who has pledged to scrap Ottawa's consumer carbon price-and-rebate-scheme and replace it with green incentives, maintain the carbon pricing system for heavy emitters, and put in place a "carbon border adjustment," or a tariff on imports from countries that have weaker climate change policies.
"If he wins, Canada loses. But Canadians will win. And we will do it with a new 'bring it home' economic plan," Poilievre said.
"We will bring home our jobs, investment and companies and break our dependence on the United States. We will unleash a fierce, free enterprise economy to bring home control of our economic future from outside forces. We will stand on our own two feet, be masters of our own destiny, and be in charge of our own future."
Whether you believe that Poilievre's call to "bring it home" - a slogan he favoured long before Trump's return to the White House - is proof that the Conservative leader has always been prepared for this political moment seems to be a matter of opinion.
"I do not observe it to be a pivot at all. I think the context has changed, but the content has not," said Kate Harrison, vice-chair of Summa Strategies, of the changes Trump has injected into Canada's political discourse. "One of the criticisms that (Poilievre) has faced is that he's maybe dancing around the issue of Trump or not willing to take him on. I think this weekend should really put that criticism to rest."
Harrison said the root of Poilievre's message has always been about "economic sovereignty," and that his recent messaging is simply a "continuation and amplification of the same content."
Mitch Heimpel, director of policy at Enterprise Canada and a former Conservative staffer, said that in that vein, "the fundamental core of the message for the last three years has been that the country needs to be built up."
One area in which Poilievre most strongly reflected that belief on Saturday was his focus on energy independence, Heimpel said.
In his speech, Poilievre said he would "incentivize Indigenous leaders" to build projects "by letting companies pay a share of their federal corporate taxes to local First Nations." He vowed, within 60 days of forming government, to repeal the Liberals' controversial Impact Assessment Act, which assesses resource and infrastructure projects based on environmental, social or economic impacts. He promised to replace the law with one that would see projects more quickly approved and, among other commitments, pledged to green light an East-West pipeline and federal permits for northern Ontario's Ring of Fire.
"This tariff crisis has proven Conservatives were right also about natural resources. And that's why we need a plan to bring home production to this country," Poilievre told hundreds of his red-and-white-clad supporters.
The framing that Poilievre has been right all along doesn't ring true for everyone.
"There's clearly been a pivot," said Scott Reid, a political analyst and communications director to former prime minister Paul Martin.
"No longer can they be content that it is enough to be opposed to Trudeau and be the beneficiaries of the time for a change. Trump and Carney have changed everything."
(While there are five candidates vying to replace Justin Trudeau, some polls have suggested a Carney win would make a decisive Conservative victory less certain.)
"They can deny it's a pivot, but they may as well deny the ground they're walking on or the sky above," said Reid, who noted that explicit references to a broken Canada were absent from Poilievre's remarks.
"It's plainly obvious that they're making some changes, and, by the way, that's probably very sensible. It's probably research-rooted," Reid said.
Harrison said that nevertheless, it makes "little sense" for the Conservatives to retreat from their carbon tax messaging, given that it is a "foundational piece of our economic and investment environment."
But Reid said that an emerging consensus from political leaders of all stripes on issues like Trump's threats, energy infrastructure, the consumer carbon price and the capital gains tax hike, could change the ballot box question for the Conservatives - and everyone else.
"I think one of the really interesting questions about where we are headed at the federal political level and this next stretch of time is if there is this sudden and stunning consensus on all the big issues - if everyone agrees more or less about everything, then all that's left for voters to decide is who do they trust most to implement that agenda?" Reid said.
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre's patriotic, campaign-style rally last Saturday in Ottawa framed the Tories as the party of "Canada First," but avoided references to a broken Canada, said one political analyst.
CREDIT: Raisa Patel Toronto Star

Full text: The Ontario Leaders' Debate has wrapped up. Ahead of the provincial election on Feb. 27, read our election promises tracker,see our election poll tracker. Follow our live updates Monday evening with the Star's Queen's Park bureau.
8:49 p.m. In her post-debate scrum Crombie says she mentioned Ford's privilege growing up. "Like the rest of Ontarians, I had to work hard for everything I have earned."
She was also grilled about controversial comments by a few candidates, but said she has been focusing "entirely on the debate, and not paying attention to social media posts from a decade ago and comments that candidates have apologized for. But she said she would look into one more recent online post from a year ago calling Prime Minister Justin Trudeau a "gay friend."
- Kristin Rushowy
8:28 p.m. In a post-debate scrum, Mike Schreiner tells reporters that Ford misrepresented a number of Green positions on issues during the debate.
He also urges the other parties to put out fully costed platforms, as his party has done.
"We need to protect Ontario from Doug Ford, and Donald Trump, especially when it comes to protecting our farmland," he says.
- Kristin Rushowy
8:17 p.m. Marit Stiles responds toFord's decision toskip post-debate scrums, saying "he doesn't want to be accountable to the media or anyone else."
Stiles also says one of the NDP proposals she didn't get to speak to during debate is universal meal program for kids. (Research has tied this to kids ability to learn.)
Stiles says "these next few days and weeks" are critical. "I'm fighting every minute of every day."
- Kristin Rushowy
8 p.m. The leaders' debate has wrapped up.
Ford is skipping the post-debate scrums with reporters, as he did in the northern debate last Friday in North Bay.
- Kristin Rushowy
7:55 p.m. In his closing statement, Doug Ford circles back to his opener: he needs a "strong mandate" to fight Donald Trump. (He doesn't mention he wanted Trump to win the U.S. election.)
Ford says "I will fight against Donald Trump like you've never seen before."
- Robert Benzie and Rob Ferguson
7:53 p.m. Crombie takes it to Ford again: "Doug, you don't get the plight of real people ... you had a company handed to you ... you're just like your friend Donald Trump, you make things up."
- Rob Ferguson
7:46 p.m. In a misplaced attempt to reduce debating distortions, the organizers have introduced debating contortions. Giving Doug Ford extra time to rebut every criticism of his last 7 years in power is adding an extra 7 minutes of television time to the incumbent. Bad call.
Every time Doug Ford's name is mentioned, he gets an extra 30 seconds. This is like a penalty shot for every imagined infraction.
- Martin Regg Cohn
7:39 p.m. "The three of you aren't very good with numbers," Ford says when asked by Stiles about a $1,500 cut in per student funding (which Ford said was not accurate).
Mike Schreiner notes his Green Party is the only one to have released a fully costed platform.
- Kristin Rushowy
7:30 p.m. How do leaders spend weekends off?
Doug Ford claims he has only been to his Muskoka cottage twice in the past year for a total of five hours to cut the grass because he is so busy working and returning calls.
Schreiner says he spends time with family in nature.
Crombie says she plans to go rock climbing in Thunder Bay, play pickleball with family.
Stiles says she loves swimming in Ontario lakes.
- Kristin Rushowy and Robert Benzie
7:29 p.m. "This is a tense room right now," says moderator David Common in starting segment with more personal questions.
- Kristin Rushowy
7:28 p.m. Crombie slips up when asked what she would do to fight gridlock, mentioning London didn't get a promised GO Train service. But it did. However, it was a four-hour trip to Toronto and ridership was low, so it was cancelled.
- Rob Ferguson
7:16 p.m. NDP Leader Marit Stiles says Ontario must get back in the business of building affordable homes to ease homelessness.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner questions NDP plan to create a government department to build homes. Says there are better ways to do it, including through non-profit organizations and co-ops.
- Rob Ferguson
7:16 p.m. "The economy will go down the tubes with all three of you," Ford says to rivals.
- Rob Ferguson

- Rob Ferguson

Crombie to Ford: "Doug, a fantasy tunnel. How dumb is that?"
"We are going to continue building transit, building infrastructure" and keep the economy strong, says Ford when challenged on the 401 tunnel proposal.
- Kristin Rushowy and Rob Ferguson

None of the leaders. ... Stiles says "our farmland is not up for grabs."
- Kristin Rushowy
Doug Ford concedes he flip-flopped on building on the Greenbelt. None of his three opponents note the RCMP is currently investigating the $8.28B land swap scandal.
"I said I was then I wasn't." - Doug Ford on his cancelled plan to build homes on the Greenbelt.
- Robert Benzie and Rob Ferguson
7:05 p.m. Stiles says Ford wants to put people in jail, she wants "to talk about how to prevent that" and "ensure people get their day in court."
- Kristin Rushowy
7:04 p.m. Opposition leaders all taking aim at Ford on court backlogs, which are leading to accused persons having their charges thrown out.
- Rob Ferguson
7:02 p.m. Interesting that the debate organizers keep giving extra time to Doug Ford to respond to attacks. It's normal for the incumbent to attract the most criticism; it's unusual for the incumbent to win extra time as a bonus.
- Martin Regg Cohn
7 p.m. Doug Ford blaming the federal Liberals for crime in Ontario while Bonnie Crombie reminds him he has been leading the province for seven years and things are getting worse.
- Robert Benzie
6:56 p.m. Just a matter of time until PC Leader Doug Ford went on the attack against Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie. We like to say that she lives rent-free in his head.
Interesting how the other party leaders are all posing questions to Doug Ford - giving the moderator a run for his money.
- Martin Regg Cohn
(Updated) 6:52 p.m. Another shot at Ford from Crombie: "You were promised a tax cut but never got it." A reference to Ford's unfulfilled 2018 promise for a middle-class income tax cut. Crombie has promised one.
"You didn't get it done, Doug, you didn't cut our taxes," Crombie says.
"I can't get a word in edgewise here," Ford protests in exchange with Crombie.
- Rob Ferguson
6:49 p.m. Mike Schreiner says "the rich insiders are getting richer" in Doug Ford's Ontario.
Marit Stiles promises rent controls and grocery rebates. Doug Ford touts the $200 cheques the Tories sent out last month just before he called the early election. - Robert Benzie
6:47 p.m. First zinger of the night... Ford touts hiring former federal health minister Dr. Jane Philpott to reform the system. Crombie shoots back "you had to hire a Liberal" to fix it. - Kristin Rushowy
6:46 p.m. Inevitable and predictable that NDP Leader Marit Stiles would pin hallway health care on a previous Liberal government, from 7 years ago. - Martin Regg Cohn
6:42 p.m. Ford now mentioning his primary care plan, which is actually $1.8 billion over four years. And for 2 million people, which is different from the OMA number that 2.5 million don't have a family doctor. - Rob Ferguson
6:41 p.m. Tough early round for Doug Ford, asking about health care. Progressive Conservative strategists will tell you privately, when we're talking about health care, we're losing.
Not that they're losing the election, but they'd rather talk about something else - like Donald Trump and the economy. Hence Ford's quick pivot to a healthy economy underpinning health care. - Martin Regg Cohn
(Updated) 6:39 p.m. Family doctor promises
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie promises to get everyone in Ontario a family doctor within four years. Right now, about 2.5 million people don't have one, according to the Ontario Medical Association.
Mike Schreiner, the Green Leader, also promises doctors for all within four years.
PC Leader Doug Ford says his government has attracted thousands of doctors and nurses already, but does not mention $2 billion plan to connect all those who do not have one to a doctor within four years.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles cites stats like long waits in ERs and thousands of patients turned loose from a Sault Ste. Marie health clinic last year, saying Ontario cannot afford to go on like this. - Robert Benzie and Rob Ferguson
6:37 p.m.More time to respond to name checks
CBC host David Common tells viewers that if any of the candidates name checks another, that person gets 30 seconds to respond. Idea is to keep candidates speaking about their own priorities and platforms. - Kristin Rushowy
6:37 p.m. The leaders are asked about their first jobs.
Crombie: cashier at Woolco
Stiles: babysitting, swimming instructor
Ford:delivering Globe & Mail as a kid, Canada Packers
Schreiner: feeding cattle/driving combine on family farm, mowing lawns
- Kristin Rushowy
6:34 p.m. Bonnie Crombie says "after seven years 2.5 million people don't have a family doctor" and 1 million are using food banks. She says Ontario isn't recognizable.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner says Ford has "abandoned his job." Schreiner blasts Ford for the Ontario Place spa, the 401 tunnel scheme and ripping up bike lanes. - Robert Benzie
6:32 p.m. The debate is underway
Doug Ford says the election he triggered early is all about who can best "protect Ontario," which is the Progressive Conservatives' campaign slogan.
Marit Stiles says she will be "on your side" fighting rising costs. "On Your Side" is the New Democrats' slogan. And the Liberal slogan ... "More for you."
- Robert Benzie and Kristin Rushowy
6:31 p.m. I love a debate, it's core to a democratic election, but I always remind my media friends: It's not a boxing match - it's a sales pitch, where politicians need to connect with voters. Stay tuned!
- Martin Regg Cohn
6:29 p.m. Hunkered down at home in front of the television, putting myself in the shoes of ordinary voters like me who are watching the debate from their couch - instead of the spin room or rarefied newsroom where there's too much electricity in the air! - Political Columnist Martin Regg Cohn
6:26 p.m. Kristin Rushowy from the Star's Queen's Park Bureau is at CBC headquarters, Queen's Park Bureau Chief Robert Benzie is covering from Queen's Park.
6:23 p.m. Ontario party leaders have been given the terms of the debate and are getting ready to begin, posing for photos at CBC headquarters' Front St. studio. All four leaders are expected to scrum when the debate concludes at 8 p.m. - Kris Rushowy
What to expect at the leaders' debate tonight
It is the main event of the Feb. 27 Ontario election campaign.
The four major party leaders square off on Monday night at CBC's Toronto Broadcast Centre for a 90-minute live televised debate.
It's the second and final time Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford, NDP Leader Marit Stiles, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and Green Leader Mike Schreiner will gather on the same stage during this campaign.
The four met Friday in North Bay for a midday debate on northern issues.
Read the full story from the Star's Queen's Park bureau
What time is the debate happening and who will participate?
The live debate will be held from 6:30 p.m. to 8 p.m.
The debate will feature Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, PC Leader Doug Ford, Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner and NDP Leader Marit Stiles.
Who will host the debate?
The debate will be moderated by CBC host David Common, CTV News Queen's Park Bureau Chief Siobhan Morris and Global Bureau Chief and President of the Queen's Park Press Gallery Colin D'Mello.
The Ontario Leaders' Debate is open to leaders of all parties that currently have an elected MPP sitting in the legislature.
Where can I watch the debate?
It will air on all partner television platforms. It will also be simulcast on various radio stations across the province and streamed online.
Poll suggests race is tightening, but Doug Ford still well ahead
Doug Ford remains well ahead, but the Feb. 27 Ontario election race is tightening somewhat as Bonnie Crombie's Liberals gain ground, a new poll suggests.
The Abacus Data survey for the Star found Ford's Progressive Conservatives at 41 per cent support, ahead of Crombie's Liberals at 28 per cent, Marit Stiles's New Democrats at 21 per cent and Mike Schreiner's Greens at six per cent.
That's a five percentage point drop for the Tories and a four percentage point increase for the Liberals since the last Abacus survey released Feb. 7. The NDP remained the same and the Greens went up one percentage point.
Read the full story from the Star's Robert Benzie
Inside Doug Ford's trip to Washington - and how he was received by Americans
In politics, as in war, symbolism matters.
So with Canada on the brink of a trade war with the United States, it was symbolic that the nation's provincial and territorial leaders secured a meeting in the White House.
Yes, it was at the last minute - and scheduled thanks to an $85,000-a-month lobbying firm retained by the Council of the Federation - and it was only with Jim Blair, one of President Donald Trump's deputy chiefs of staff, and White House personnel director Sergio Gor.
Read the full story from the Star's Robert Benzie
Rivals slam Doug Ford on health care, death penalty as he insists he's the best choice to take on Donald Trump
Bonnie Crombie tried to distance herself from previous Liberal governments in the first leaders' debate of the Feb. 27 election campaign, prompting Doug Ford to growl "what a joke."
In a testy exchange, the Liberal leader said she disagrees with some moves by Liberal premiers Dalton McGuinty and Kathleen Wynne during the 15 years they were in power - a span that ended with a 2018 win by Ford's Progressive Conservatives.
"As you know, I'm a new Liberal leader and I'm not taking responsibility for decisions made previously," Crombie said during a 90-minute debate on northern issues Friday at Canadore College in North Bay.
Read the full story from the Star's Queen's Park bureau
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie stands by candidate who made light of intimate partner violence
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie is standing by a candidate despite his social media posts making light of intimate partner violence in the wake for former CBC host Jian Ghomeshi's legal troubles.
"These are tweets from over a decade ago, and the candidate has apologized," Liberal campaign spokeswoman Bahoz Dara Aziz said Friday in regard to Brian Hamilton, running in Thunder Bay-Superior North.
Hamilton, a Thunder Bay councillor and coffee shop owner hoping to wrest the riding from the NDP's Lise Vaugeois in the Feb. 27 election, said he is a different person now.
Read the full story from the Star's Rob Ferguson
Ontario NDP candidate in Eglinton-Lawrence folds campaign to help Liberal opponent win
The NDP hopeful in Eglinton-Lawrence says she withdrew her candidacy to avoid a vote-split that could help Ontario's Progressive Conservatives keep the seat.
In a post on X Thursday evening, Natasha Doyle-Merrick said: "I recognize the race in Eglinton-Lawrence is a clear two-party contest between Liberals and Conservatives. To prevent a Conservative win and more years of neglect, I'm stepping aside to avoid a vote split," on the political left.
In an attached statement, the mental health worker said she made the difficult decision after much reflection, adding that she first sought the NDP nomination because she felt Robin Martin, the riding's PC MPP since 2018, had ignored some voices in the community.
Read the full story from the Star's David Rider
Ontario election promise tracker
Ontario is heading to the polls Feb. 27 and we're tracking what each party is promising ahead of the Ontario election.
All four provincial party leaders have now put forth plans to support Ontario through U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff plans along with pledges to improve affordability and more.
Here's what else the Progressive Conservatives, NDP, Liberals and Greens have pledged since the start of the campaign.

I fact checked everything Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said for a week in the Ontario election. Here's how many false claims she made
Hammering lawn signs into frozen soil. Touring the province in giant buses. Hot mic moments. Attack ads.
The 2025 Ontario cold snap election is well underway and that means speeches and promises - but also embellishments, stretches and at times, falsehoods.
The Star's fact-check project is back and every week until election day on Feb. 27, Star reporters will monitor, track, record and correct (where needed) the three leading provincial party leaders on the campaign trail.
Read the full story from the Star's Mahdis Habibinia
Doug Ford broke ethics rules with campaign ad, opponents charge
Doug Ford violated ethics rules against using government activities for partisan purposes in an online ad about his trip to Washington to lobby against Trump tariffs, opposition parties charge.
The ad on the Progressive Conservative leader's X feed Thursday pictured him walking past the U.S. Capitol. It used the party's "Protect Ontario" logo and slogan from his bid for a third term as premier in a snap election he called for Feb. 27.
The ad was quickly taken off social media and reposted a few hours later with no Conservative logo or slogans.
Read the full story from the Star's Rob Ferguson and Kris Rushowy
Recording reveals Doug Ford calling for return of death penalty in campaign speech: 'Send 'em right to sparky'
Doug Ford took his tough-on-crime talk to new heights in a campaign speech where he pushed for a return to capital punishment, the Star has learned.
Musing about stiffer penalties for home invaders who kill victims, the Progressive Conservative leader called for changes in the federal Criminal Code that would empower judges to "send 'em right to sparky."
The colloquial reference to the electric chair came two weeks ago on the first day of Ford's snap Feb. 27 Ontario election campaign, now at its midway point.
Read the full story from the Star's Robert Benzie and Rob Ferguson
Doug Ford denies his Washington trip is a taxpayer-funded campaign stop
As political rivals accuse him of electioneering on the taxpayer's dime, Doug Ford insists he can juggle governing and campaigning at the same time.
The Progressive Conservative leader, here to lobby American lawmakers and officials against U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs on Canada, rejected Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie's contention he is breaching tradition by going on a foreign mission during a campaign.
"I got cleared by the integrity commissioner, but that's neither here nor there," Ford said Tuesday, referring to the legislature's ethics watchdog.
Read the full story from the Star's Queen's Park Bureau
Doug Ford under attack in Super Bowl ads
Doug Ford's campaign fumble is in the Super Bowl spotlight.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie will be airing two different 30-second commercials during the NFL championship game Sunday night between the Kansas City Chiefs and the Philadelphia Eagles.
With Ontario voters headed to the polls on Feb. 27, Crombie's team wants viewers of what is expected to be the year's most-watched TV broadcast to see for themselves what Ford said last Monday.
Read the full story from the Star's Robert Benzie

Ontario's election day is Feb. 27. You're eligible to vote if you're a Canadian citizen aged 18 and over.

Document 160 of 500

Plane crash in Toronto: 18 passengers sent to hospital after Delta flight with 80 on board crashes at Pearson; Cause still under investigation; Delays at airport expected for days

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/plane-crash-toronto-18-passengers-sent-hospital/docview/3168093236/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Plane+crash+in+Toronto%3A+18+passengers+sent+to+hospital+after+Delta+flight+with+80+on+board+crashes+at+Pearson%3B+Cause+still+under+investigation%3B+Delays+at+airport+expected+for+days&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A Delta plane has crashed at Toronto Pearson International Airport with around 80 people on board the flight. Multiple people have been injured and police and paramedics are on the scene. Follow the Star's live updates on Monday.
8:20 p.m. (updated) The cause of the crash is still under investigation and being led by the Transportation Safety Board, Toronto Pearson fire chief Todd Aitken said Monday night.
He would not speculate on the cause.
Eighteen injured passengers have been sent to local hospitals, he said.
"The runway was dry and there was no crosswind conditions," Aitken said.
Pearson and Mississauga Fire sent 18 vehicles combined, said Aitken, who added that Peel paramedics also responded with 18 paramedic units.
7:13 p.m. Pearson airport update
Seventeen passengers were injured as a result of the crash, according to Pearson officials. Three people were taken to hospital. Twenty-two Canadians were on board, the rest were internationals.
Two runways at Pearson are closed and delays are expected for days to come, Pearson officials said in an update.
"We are very grateful that there is no loss of life and relatively minor injuries," Deborah Flint, CEO of the Greater Toronto Airports Authority, said.
"No airport CEO wants to have these types of press conferences, but this is exactly what our emergency, our operations and our first responder partners are all practiced and trained for."
7:11 p.m. After a few hours of people waiting idly for their luggage from their cancelled flights, most bags are now arriving.
Several baggage carousels are now operating through Pearson Airport's earlier-bustling Canada arrivals, gradually clearing out the backlog.
Most travellers are now heading out, with their belongings in tow, others are voicing their frustrations attempting to rebook their vacation plans while some are sitting by, unsure what to do.
7:03 p.m. The media update has started.
6:30 p.m. Uxbridge, Ont. resident Ryan Blanchet, 34, and group of Canadians were gathered at a bar at Pearson watching the Canada-Finland 4 Nations Face-Off hockey game while emergency crews responded to the crash to their left.
As he and others looked out the windows, they could see the flashing lights of emergency crews off in the distance.
"We were watching the game, hoping everyone was OK and then Canada scored, and cheers broke out," he said. "It was really mixed emotions but we really didn't know what was going on."
He says the people scheduled to board his Porter flight to Puerto Vallarta, Mexico, were the closest to the crash site.
"I saw the ORNGE chopper and I was like: Oh that's not good," he said.
6:17 p.m. One Pearson-bound flight from Lisbon, Portugal made it partway across the Atlantic before turning around. Another flight - this one a British Airways plane from London - is doing the same.
Put together, 46 flights were forced to divert while Pearson was closed, according to Flightradar24, an online air traffic tracker. Montreal's Pierre Elliott Trudeau International received the most diversions, with 11. Ottawa received another nine.
6:13 p.m. How are travellers coping?
Heidi Matthews had been trying to fly from Toronto to St. John's since Thursday when she learned her travel plans were once again delayed, this time by the collision.
She said the airport never updated travellers on what had happened but she could see a motorcade of ambulances and buses travel across the tarmac from her seat at the gate.
"I think everyone's disturbed," she said. "Once, we even got a message saying that the airport was closed and that we should leave."
Matthews added that the airport was already a mess and travellers were frustrated before the announcements were made.
6:12 p.m. As Guelph resident Brett Aoyadjian, 50, was heading out onto his Porter flight to Mexico, scheduled to depart Monday, he could see the damage left behind from the Delta plane crash.
"Terrified for the people," he said. "I didn't even realize what I was looking at when I first saw it. And then I realized that's the engine, wheels and then a wing over here and another wing there. I was like: What do you mean?"
His flight is now delayed to Tuesday.
6:11 p.m. "The hearts of the entire global Delta family are with those affected by today's incident at Toronto-Pearson International Airport," said Delta CEO Ed Bastian. "I want to express my thanks to the many Delta and Endeavor team members and the first responders on site. We are working to confirm the details and will share the most current information on http://news.delta.com as soon as it becomes available. In the meantime, please take care and stay safe."
Anyone with loved ones on the flight can contact Delta's passenger inquiry center at 1-866-629-4775 in Canada and 1-800-997-5454 in the United States.
The airline cancelled all its flights to and from Pearson for the rest of the day and has issued a travel waiver.
5:55 p.m. There will be a media briefing on the plane crash at 6:30 p.m., Pearson airport says. About a dozen members of media waiting for the news conference at the Greater Toronto Airports Authority (GTAA) head office near Pearson.
5:45 p.m. Scott Hamilton, an aviation consultant with over 40 years of experience, told the Star it's "extremely rare" for a plane to flip during a landing.
"Over the decades, there have been a number of incidents," Hamilton said. "Not many though, and some have been survivable while some have not."
In videos circulating online, Hamilton said it's clear that at least one of the plane's wings was separated in the crash.
"The fact that the wing separated and the fuel spill did not create a conflagration is, in my opinion, a miracle because that's normally how big fire-driven explosions happen," he said.
5:37 p.m. Toronto weather forecast
A winter weather travel advisory is in effect for Toronto beginning overnight. Local snow amounts of 4 cm to 8 cm are expected, with reduced visibility at times.
5:36 p.m. Mitsubishi CRJ900
Mitsubishi Heavy Industries, who owns the CRJ series aircraft that crashed, told the Star they are "aware that a Delta Air Lines flight CRJ900LR was involved in an incident at Toronto Pearson Airport" and they will "fully cooperate with the ongoing investigation."
5:30 p.m. GTAA arrivals
Nearly three hours after the collision, scheduled departing and arriving flights resumed at Pearson at 5 p.m. Airport authorities were stationed at the arrivals gate to provide support to the families of passengers who were on the flight.
5:29 p.m. Flights resume at Pearson airport
Departures and arrivals have resumed at Toronto Pearson as of 5 p.m. All 76 passengers and four crew from Delta flight 4819 were accounted for. A number of passengers were taken to local hospitals. GTAA staff are supporting families of passengers at arrivals, Pearson said on X.
5:17 p.m. John C. Munro Hamilton International Airport is also accepting diverted aircrafts according to Colleen Ryan, associate director of communications at the airport.
"The airport has robust protocols in place to manage the flow of aircraft while maintaining the safety of incoming and departing flights," she told the Star.
5:15 p.m. Around the time of the collision, Environment Canada recorded west-blowing winds of up to 65 km/h, speeds high enough to "break twigs off trees" and make walking into the wind "almost impossible" according to the weather agency. The most common runway configuration at Pearson directs arriving flights to come in from the east.
5:10 p.m. The last crash at Pearson International happened in May 2019, when an Air Canada Express flight from Pearson bound for Sudbury was turned back due to inclement weather, and struck a fuel car while taxiing for a gate. Five people were injured, according to records from the Aviation Safety Network.
4:58 p.m. Delta says there were 76 passengers and four crew members on board the flight.
4:55 p.m. PC Leader Doug Ford says he's "relieved there are no casualties" after the crash at Pearson airport.
In a post on X, he says provincial officials are in contact with the airport and local authorities and will provide help as needed. - Canadian Press
4:47 p.m. 12 people injured, paramedics say
Lawrence Saindon, a supervisor with Peel Regional Paramedic Services, said there were 15 patients, 12 of whom have injuries ranging from mild to moderate.
He said two were taken by air ambulance to St. Michael's Hospital and another who was transported by ground ambulance to SickKids.
"We have no life threatening injuries to anybody at this time," he said, stressing that there are no fatalities.
"All crew and passengers have been accounted for."
4:45 p.m. "I'm relieved to learn that all passengers and crew are accounted for after today's plane crash at Toronto Pearson," Toronto mayor Olivia Chow posted on X.
"Thank you to the first responders, crew and airport staff for their quick actions and commitment to keeping everyone safe," Chow said.
4:39 p.m. Ann Nguyen, 19, was at Pearson with her mother and sister. The group had planned to fly to Cancun, Mexico, during her reading week but said the flight has been delayed six times since Sunday, due to the weather, and then cancelled on Monday.
"We got here, and then as we were coming in we found out that the plane crashed," said Nguyen, expressing relief that there are no fatalities.
Frustrated over her travel situation, she said she rather receive a refund than have the flight rescheduled again.
4:37 p.m. Kevin Feng, a Toronto resident who was heading on a Delta flight to New York Monday afternoon, was among the passengers whose flights were cancelled. He said the notice he received made no mention of the crash but he learned what happened from a staff member in the airport.
"It's scary," he said, especially knowing it's the same airline he was set to fly with.
4:36 p.m. Delta says it is aware of the reports of an "incident" involving flight 4819 from Minneapolis to Toronto.
"We are working to confirm any details and will share the most current information on news.delta.com as soon as it becomes available," the airline said in a statement.
4:33 p.m. The Transportation Safety Board of Canada is deploying a team of investigators following the aircraft collision to gather information and assess the occurrence, a statement read.
4:31 p.m. Montreal airport taking diversion flights
The airport in Montreal posted to X on Monday afternoon, saying they are taking some diversion flights.
"Due to an accident involving a @Delta Airlines aircraft at @TorontoPearson's airport, #YUL is preparing to receive several diversion flights. Our teams are already in action to welcome them. However, given the large number of passengers at YUL, please note that the situation may cause delays at various points along the passenger process. Thank you for your patience and understanding."
4:30 p.m. Three people with critical injuries
The ambulance service Ornge says it has transported three people with critical injuries to hospital, including a young person.
In an email, spokesperson Joshua NcNamara says one "pediatric patient" is being sent to SickKids hospital with critical injuries.
McNamara says one man in his 60s with critical injuries was sent to St. Michael's Hospital and a woman in her 40s, also with critical injuries, was sent to Sunnybrook Health Sciences Centre.
The number of critically injured people reported by Ornge is more than those previously reported by a spokesperson for the local paramedic service, who had said one person had critical but non-life-threatening injuries, while seven others were said to have mild to moderate injuries. - Canadian Press
4:25 p.m. I'm closely following the serious incident at the Pearson Airport involving Delta Airlines flight 4819 from Minneapolis. All 80 passengers onboard are accounted for. Updates will follow," Anita Anand said on X.
4:23 p.m. "I am relieved to hear there are no casualties following the plane crash at Pearson airport today. Grateful to first responders and airport authorities for their work today, and I'm wishing those injured a swift and full recovery," Bonnie Crombie wrote on X.
4:17 p.m. On air traffic control, a man is heard describing the scene at Pearson.
"Just so you're aware, there are people outside walking around the aircraft there," he says.
"Yeah, we've got it," another man responds. "The aircraft is upside down and burning."
Video of the Delta plane crash
4:15 p.m. Just after 4 p.m., the U.S. Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) confirmed 80 people were onboard the flight at the time of the crash. All passengers were evacuated. Canada's Transportation Safety Board will be in charge of the investigation, the FAA said.
4:10 p.m. According to the U.S. Department of Transportation, there were 80 people onboard at the time of the crash.
4:05 p.m. "This airplane just crashed on 23," an air traffic controller is heard saying around 2:13 p.m., according to archives accessed by the Star. A controller then directs another flight to pull up and go around.
4 p.m. Minutes before the crash, around 2:08 p.m., air traffic control at Pearson directs the Delta flight to contact the air traffic control tower.
Then, about five minutes later, air traffic control tells another flight that its approach clearance has been cancelled.
"Runway 23 is currently closed," a controller says to another plane. "It's gonna be a little while here."
3:57 p.m. 80 people were reportedly on the plane.
3:52 p.m. Two passengers with serious injuries were transported to nearby hospitals, including one taken to SickKids, a Peel police spokesperson said. No fatalities have been reported, they said.
3:51 p.m. Over the intercom in Terminal 3 departures at Pearson airport just now: "All flights have been suspended. More information will be shared as soon as it becomes available. Sorry for the inconvenience and thanks for your patience."
3:47 p.m. The airport has confirmed the incident involves a Delta Air Lines plane arriving from Minneapolis, and images posted on social media show the plane flipped over on the tarmac.
3:43 p.m. All flights are suspended at Pearson airport.
3:42 p.m. According to Flightradar24, the plane - a Delta Airlines flight from Minneapolis - arrived at Pearson at 2:13 p.m. The plane, a Mitsubishi CRJ-900LR, has room for about 75 passengers.
3:31 p.m. One pediatric patient was transported to hospital with critical injuries according to ORNGE. The emergency response service said they do not have information on any other transported patients yet.
ORNGE said they sent three helicopters and two critical care ambulances.
3:20 p.m. Peel police responding to the crash
Police could not comment on any injuries but said that "most of the passengers are out."
A post on X from Toronto Pearson confirmed that "All passengers and crew are accounted for."
The crash happened "upon landing," according to a post from Toronto Pearson, "involving a Delta Airlines plane arriving from Minneapolis."
CAPTION: The cause of the crash is still being investiged by the Transportation Safety Board. Document 161 of 500

Bonnie Crombie and Marit Stiles scrap over who can defeat Doug Ford in Ontario election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/bonnie-crombie-marit-stiles-scrap-over-who-can/docview/3168006446/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Bonnie+Crombie+and+Marit+Stiles+scrap+over+who+can+defeat+Doug+Ford+in+Ontario+election&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: WTF - who terminates Ford?
That was the question posed by Doug Ford's main rivals as NDP Leader Marit Stiles and Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie appealed to each other's supporters to rally to defeat the Progressive Conservatives on Feb. 27.
The morning after the province-wide televised debate, Stiles and Crombie urged voters to choose them as the progressive alternative to Ford with advance polls opening Thursday and continuing to Saturday.
"There are people out there who have previously voted Liberal who I think will not see their values reflected in the current Liberal party and leadership," the New Democrat said Tuesday in the Spadina-Fort York campaign headquarters of incumbent Chris Glover in Toronto.
Stiles derided the Liberals, who only held nine of 124 seats in the legislature at dissolution last month compared to the 28 of the official opposition NDP.
"They're just trying to make party status," she said mockingly, referring to the threshold of 12 seats for official standing in the house.
Crombie, who was campaigning in the traditional NDP stronghold of Hamilton, said public-opinion polls suggest the Liberals have the best shot at defeating the Tories, who have been in office since 2018.
"As we see the race narrowing between the premier and myself that's why I'm reaching out today to NDP voters and I'm asking them if you want to change our health-care system, please vote for Ontario's Liberals," said the Grit Leader, who has pledged to cure the chronic shortage of family doctors in the province.
"So yes, it is a very strong message that will appeal to progressive voters, that we can come together, and when we come together, we can defeat the government, and we can ensure that everyone will have a family doctor," she said at Clifford Brewing Co.
According to the latest Abacus Data survey for the Star, Ford's PCs are at 41 per cent support with Crombie's Liberals at 28 per cent, Stiles's New Democrats at 21 per cent and Mike Schreiner's Greens at six per cent.
That's a five percentage point drop for the Tories and a four percentage point increase for the Grits since the previous Abacus survey released Feb. 7.
Abacus surveyed 1,500 Ontarians from last Thursday through Saturday using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 2.7 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
Ford, who had been scheduled to fly to Sault Ste. Marie, was forced to cancel all campaign events due to Monday's plane crash at Toronto Pearson International Airport.
With the PC campaign grounded, his rivals dominate the news the day after the 90-minute debate moderated by CBC Radio Metro Morning host David Common, which was broadcast on most major Ontario TV channels.
In that debate, Ford warned "the economy will go down the tubes" unless the Tories are re-elected next week.
"I know the three of you aren't very good with numbers. All you three believe in taxing people to death," the Tory leader chided Stiles, Crombie and Schreiner.
The PC chief was less sure-footed when the debate turned to the controversial $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal and his U-turn over allowing and then banning development on 7,400 acres of protected land.
"I think I was pretty clear. I said I was. Then I wasn't, and I went ahead and did it. I apologized to the people," he said.
Although the RCMP's ongoing criminal investigation into the Greenbelt debacle went unmentioned at the debate, Stiles noted Ford "says one thing in front of the camera and another thing behind closed doors."
"You can't trust him on this," she said.
Crombie, for her part, told the provincewide television audience that it's been "seven years of lies" since Ford was elected.
"You're just like your friend Donald Trump, you make things up. You are saying lies," she said.
That was a shot at Ford's rationale for calling the $189 million snap election 15 months ahead of a vote that had been scheduled for June 2026.
The PC chief has said he needs a four-year mandate to "outlive and outlast" Trump, because the U.S. president's threatened 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian goods would devastate Ontario's economy.
CAPTION: Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner, left to right, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, PC Leader Doug Ford, and NDP Leader Marit Stiles pose for a photo before the Ontario Leaders' debate at CBC's Broadcast Centre, in Toronto on Feb. 17, 2025.
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson, Robert Benzie, Kristin Rushowy

Document 162 of 500

Ontario election polls see Doug Ford's PC maintaining lead over Liberals

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-election-polls-see-doug-fords-pc/docview/3168006433/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+election+polls+see+Doug+Ford%27s+PC+maintaining+lead+over+Liberals&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With the Ontario election campaign underway, the Star has a poll tracker so you can follow all the twists and turns ahead of election day on Feb. 27.
We've got the numbers from the top pollsters in one place, with handy charts, and a rolling, last-four-poll average of all the results.
Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives are still in first place, well ahead ahead of the second-place Liberals in the latest poll.

In Ontario's last election, in 2022, Ford's PCs took 40.8 per cent of the popular vote across the province, with the Liberals earning 23.9 per cent and the NDP taking 23.7 per cent. That led Ford to his second straight majority government, with 83 seats. Despite finishing narrowly behind the Liberals in the popular vote, the NDP took 31 seats and the Liberals just eight.
The Star is compiling polling for the 2025 Ontario election from polling firms that received a grade of A or A+ from 338Canada during the 2022 Ontario election. 338Canada is an independent media organization that created a statistical model of electoral projections based on opinion polls, electoral history, and demographic data.
These polling firms use a variety of polling methods, including online surveys and Smart Interactive Voice Response (IVR) systems. As online polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, margins listed here are for a comparable random sample of this size.
Percentage breakdowns are shares of committed or leaning voters, in line with industry conventions.
CAPTION: Clockwise from top: Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford, Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner, NDP Leader Marit Stiles.
CREDIT: Star Staff

Document 163 of 500

Ford appears ready to spend, but not on fixing homelessness

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-appears-ready-spend-not-on-fixing/docview/3167861958/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+appears+ready+to+spend%2C+but+not+on+fixing+homelessness&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In a more sensible world, the sheer magnitude of the homelessness crisis in Ontario would make it a top issue during this provincial election, with the incumbent party called out and held to account for the sorry situation.
But as we reach the home stretch of this strange sideshow election in Ontario - even as a deluge of snow and cold weather has further highlighted the need for access to safe shelters and housing - Premier Doug Ford appears to be skating through without facing much blame.
Please don't kid yourself: homelessness is not just a municipal issue. Nor is it just about pointing fingers at federal immigration and housing policy. The provincial government has a major responsibility - definitely more responsibility than they have for international trade.
And the numbers suggest Ford's government deserves a lot of the blame. The situation has gotten much, much worse since 2018, when he took office.
In January, the Association of Municipalities of Ontario (AMO) published a report pegging the number of people who experienced homelessness in 2024 at 81,515. To put that into perspective, if you collected all those people in one place they'd represent the 33rd largest of Ontario's 444 municipalities; about the size of Peterborough.
It has not always been this bad.
In 2017, the last year of government led by former premier Kathleen Wynne, 54,664 people experienced homelessness, according to AMO. That's still too many, of course, but Ford's government has never posted a mark as low.
The recent trends are especially alarming, with the number increasing an average of 12 per cent annually over the past three years, coinciding with the end of most pandemic-era social support programs.
And we ain't seen nothing yet. According to AMO, if nothing changes, the population of "known homeless" in Ontario could grow to 294,266 by 2035.
This trend, it turns out, correlates with a lack of provincial spending on programs designed to reduce homelessness.
A report from the Maytree Foundation, released last week, found that inflation-adjusted provincial spending on homelessness and housing programs is stagnant and remains well below the level of spending in the final years of the Wynne government.
Compounding the problem, a greater share of the funding is going toward short-term shelter programs instead of longer-term solutions like low-income housing. When the Ford government cancelled the province's cap and trade carbon pricing system in 2018, the report says, the low-income housing projects that were to be funded with it got cancelled, too.
Even with rapidly rising homelessness numbers, there's been a real reluctance from Queen's Park to get back into funding affordable housing - or to do anything to meaningfully address the scale of the problem.
In December, the Ford government announced a new initiative to, in its words, "end encampments." But Maytree calculates the new funding for that initiative amounts to only about a 2.8 per cent increase in the amount the government will spend on addressing homelessness. That's a small change in a province that is seeing much higher year-over-year increases in the number of unhoused people.
In Toronto, the province's fiscal foot-dragging has translated into a frustrating waiting game, in which Queen's Park has been slow to match the funding commitments made by city hall and Ottawa for the city's 10-year housing plan.
As of last September, according to a report to Toronto city council, the city had committed to $7.5 billion, the federal government had committed $2.9 billion, while the province took a distant third place in a race with three participants, with $1.4 billion offered up to date.
It would be one thing if these tight purse strings for programs addressing homelessness could be chalked up to an effort to constrain costs across the board, but Ford has been a shameless big spender in other areas.
He's funded gimmicks like the removal of vehicle registration fees, forgoing more than $1 billion in annual revenue, and issuing $200 cheques for every taxpayer in the province, a $3-billion one-time expense.
He's made bizarre deals like his $612-million plan to get booze in corner stores immediately, instead of just waiting until contracts expired, or forgiving contractual penalties owed by the corporation that owns Highway 407, potentially worth $1 billion.
The big spending shows no sign of stopping. On the campaign trail, Ford has continued to tout his plans to build a tunnel underneath Highway 401, a project that could cost an unfathomable sum of $100 billion - or more.
According to AMO, the province could make a deal with municipalities to eliminate most chronic homelessness with an investment of $11 billion over 10 years; an additional $2 billion over eight years would greatly reduce the number of encampments.
Stacked up against all the money Ford has and is planning to shovel out the door, this sounds like one hell of a bargain.
Between now and election day, it's worth asking why a premier who has made so many bad deals won't make this good one.
CAPTION: It would be one thing if the tight purse strings for programs addressing homelessness could be chalked up to an effort to constrain costs across the board, Matt Elliott writes, but Premier Doug Ford has been a shameless big spender in other areas.Steve Russell Toronto Star file photo
CREDIT: Matt Elliott OPINION

Document 164 of 500

A 'miracle' no deaths in Pearson crash

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/miracle-no-deaths-pearson-crash/docview/3167861874/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+%27miracle%27+no+deaths+in+Pearson+crash&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: At least 18 people were injured Monday afternoon, including three critically, when a Delta Air Lines flight carrying 80 people from Minneapolis crashed at Toronto Pearson International Airport, leaving passengers dangling upside down from their seats and a mangled, charred fuselage on the runway of Canada's busiest airport.
Passengers fled the plane, missing at least one wing, as it laid upside down and was being hosed down by fire trucks.
The crash forced Pearson to close for more than two hours, delaying flights and diverting at least 46 planes to airports in Ottawa, Montreal and elsewhere across the country.
Delays will continue for days as two of Pearson's five runways remain closed, according to Deborah Flint, CEO of the Greater Toronto Airports Authority.
The cause of the crash is not yet known. Todd Aitken, fire chief at Pearson, said the runway was dry and there were no crosswinds.
Canada's Transportation Safety Board is investigating and the U.S. Federal Aviation Administration is sending investigators to Toronto.
Parminder Singh Chohan, a Toronto plane-spotting enthusiast, watched the crash from his post about 200 metres from runway 23.
"As soon as it hit the ground I could see that it went into flames and dragged along the ground," said Chohan. "It was a nightmare to see in front of your eyes. You can't imagine this happening."
Passenger Peter Carlson, seated by the window near the wing, described the impact as being "smacked into a wall" and thrown sideways. He said the plane "slid and slid and slid" before it came to a halt and he was upside down, suspended by a seatbelt.
Carlson, a paramedic from Northfield, Minn., checked to ensure he was mobile, unfastened his seatbelt, and then assessed the people around him.
Moving back and forth, he tended to a little boy who was hurt and a middle-aged woman with serious injuries.
Between caring for other passengers, Carlson said he managed to send a quick text to his wife telling her, "I love you, and I love our kids."
Minutes later, he and the other injured passengers escaped. He gave the bloodied, middle-aged woman his coat, and the passengers retreated from the site.
Carlson said they heard an "explosion" shortly after, and the fire trucks arrived. For a brief time, they were standing in the open, with heavy winds and blowing snow.
"It was like a strange dream," he recalled.
Passenger John Nelson told CNN it was a typical flight - until the plane hit the runway "super hard," went sideways and a "big fireball" was visible out the left side. Another passenger, Pete Koukov, said "we were sideways, and then we were upside down, hanging like bats."
Nelson said he unbuckled himself as the plane smelled of jet fuel. "We tried to get out of there as quickly as possible," he told CNN. "Even now, I smell like jet fuel."
"I was just in a plane crash," another passenger said in a video posted to social media. "Oh my God."
As officials on the ground clambered to respond, air traffic controllers directed planes to pull up and go around.
"Just so you're aware, there are people outside walking around the aircraft," one controller said, according to archives reviewed by the Star.
"Yeah, we've got it," another man responds. "The aircraft is upside down and burning."
Two people were taken by air ambulance to St. Michael's Hospital and one was taken by ground ambulance to SickKids, according to Peel Regional Paramedic Services supervisor Lawrence Saindon.
The plane, a Bombardier CRJ900 jet, was carrying 76 passengers and four crew. It had 22 Canadians on board, according to Flint.
Hubert Su's flight landed at Pearson Airport Terminal 3 at 2:05 p.m., arriving from Vancouver. As the plane was taxiing down the runway, he saw another aircraft in the distance speeding like a fireball, with thick black smoke rising high behind it.
"My mind went blank at first, and I felt deeply unsettled, not knowing what was happening," he said.
He added that many people on his flight saw the scene, and there were gasps of shock. A woman sitting next to him began to sob quietly.
A video he filmed through the cabin window shows, across a stretch of snow, thick black smoke rising like a mushroom cloud, stretching for hundreds of meters.
Guelph resident Brett Aoyadjian, 50, was waiting to board his Porter flight to Mexico when he saw the damage left behind by the crash.
"I didn't even realize what I was looking at when I first saw it," Aoyadjian said. "Then I realized: That's the engine, wheels and then a wing over here and another wing there."
The crash is the latest in a string of aviation incidents across North America. In late January, an American Airlines jet collided in mid-air with a U.S. army helicopter in Washington, killing all 67 people on board. This month, seven people died and 24 were injured when an air ambulance crashed in Philadelphia. Another 10 people on a small commercial aircraft died when it went down off the coast of Alaska nearly two weeks ago.
It is "extremely rare" for a plane to flip while landing as the Delta flight did, aviation consultant Scott Hamilton said. "The fact that the wing separated and the fuel spill did not create a conflagration is, in my opinion, a miracle," he said. "That's normally how big fire-driven explosions happen."
Investigators will be evaluating weather and runway conditions, winds, speed, mechanical conditions and operation of the plane, Hamilton said. They will also look into how the plane held up during landing.
Inside Pearson's terminals, the delays and cancellations - including at least two planes from Europe that turned around partway across the Atlantic - added to an already-frustrating long weekend that saw Toronto dumped with 26 centimetres of snow across Saturday and Sunday. The storm cancelled 359 flights out of Pearson and delayed another 171.
Ryan Blanchet, of Uxbridge was watching the Canada-Finland 4 Nations Face-Off game with a group of Canadians at an airport bar when emergency crews responded to the crash.
"We were watching the game, hoping everyone was OK, and then Canada scored and cheers broke out," said Blanchet, 34. "It was really mixed emotions but we really didn't know what was going on."
In a social media post, Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford said he was "relieved there are no casualties." Federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre said he was "extremely concerned" by the crash and his thoughts were with the passengers and crew.
Mayor Olivia Chow thanked first responders, crew and airport staff for their "commitment to keep everyone safe."
This was the first crash at Pearson since May 2019, when an Air Canada Express plane collided with a fuel truck on the tarmac while taxiing to its gate. Five people were injured, according to records from the Aviation Safety Network.
In 2005, during a thunderstorm, an Air France plane carrying 309 people skidded off the Pearson runway and into a ravine before catching fire. Twelve people were seriously injured in what then-deputy mayor Joe Pantalone said "felt like a miracle."
With files from Andy Takagi, Anastasia Blosser, Abby O'Brien, Estella Ren, Kristjan Lautens and The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Mark Colley, Calvi Leon and Ilyas Hussein Toronto Star With files from Andy Takagi, Anastasia Blosser, AbO'Brien, Estella Ren, Kristjan Lautens and The Canadian Press

Full text: How Trump treats Canada will hurt us all
One of my fondest childhood memories is of crossing the Peace Bridge travelling from Buffalo to Hamilton with my parents to visit our Canadian relatives. You see, early in the 20th century, my grandparents and their siblings emigrated from Italy. My grandparents settled in Buffalo; their siblings settled in Hamilton. Over the years there were frequent visits back and forth.
I remember my Canadian family with deep affection, especially my second cousin (since passed away) who was like a brother to me. These days I still get that same special feeling when I cross the bridge.
The reason I share all this is to tell my Canadian friends that there are millions of Americans like me who do not support our current government's disgraceful actions and are heartbroken to see our Canadian ally subjected to such shameful treatment. These actions hurt us all. It is my hope that our friendship remains strong and that you all continue to stand up to America's bully president.
At the age of 81, in light of current events, my only regret is that grandpa didn't settle in Canada with his brother.
Elizabeth Accordino,
Perrysburg, N.Y.
Ford blames families, not his bike lane policies
Dropping off your kids at school on an e-bike? Here's why you might not want to in Ontario, Feb. 10
Thanks to the Star for pointing out Doug Ford's cynical law that fines young families for putting their children on their e-bikes. With the cost of housing in Toronto, many young families (including our own) have found using e-bicycles makes living here more affordable; owning a car is often an unaffordable luxury. Ford's law puts children whose families rely on bikes (and the bike lanes!) at risk of accidents, so he is trying blame families for putting their children at risk rather than accepting liability for eliminating the bike lanes. Ford's actions can only be described as uncaring of Toronto families who need to use bicycles for their children's transportation.
Karin Brothers, Toronto
Ford must stop distracting and deal with reality
Doug Ford thinks he is the mayor of Toronto (get rid of bike lanes on main streets). Now he thinks he is the prime minister of Canada (bring back capital punishment). He is doing everything in his power to distract the voters from the real issues. Rarely does he talk about health care, education or housing - HIS REAL issues as premier.
Ken Perkin, Scarborough
Canada needs
to build more
modular housing
Leadership candidates pitch plans for homebuilding, doctors, Feb. 13
I am glad to see Mark Carney advocating for modular housing in the Liberal leadership race. With housing starts in Ontario dropping 22 per cent last year we need to use the most effective tools in our tool box to get affordable housing built fast. Modular housing factories produce housing in a fraction of the time and at a fraction of the cost in climate controlled and quality-controlled environments. New construction workers don't need to spend years fulfilling apprenticeships but can be under the watchful eyes of a journeyman foreman. Older construction workers would be retained in a safe and healthy work environment. New immigrants may also be trained. If you want to build a custom-built house go ahead, if you can afford it, but don't leave the rest of us unhoused because we can't afford "bespoke" construction. Bring housing construction into the 21st century.
Judy Smith, Cobourg
Support for North is our
best defence
It's striking to me that CBC News often addresses two ostensibly unrelated themes: the lack of Canadian military spending, and shortcomings in support for remote Indigenous and northern communities. It strikes me that these issues are very much related. Possibly a response to U.S. President Donald Trump would point out that the best defence of the North would be to support these vast communities. Community support should be considered to be part of our national defence and functioning communities would be more effective than trying to police the region with a few more helicopters.
Stephen Tulk, Toronto
The U.S. wants
our fresh water
For Canadians in favour of becoming the 51st state, be very careful what you wish for! Trump wants only one thing from us and that's our fresh water. If you choose to join the U.S., sometime in the future our rivers will suffer the same fate as the Colorado River. The St. Lawrence River will no longer reach the ocean; the water level in our lakes (including the Great Lakes) will drop to a similar level that Lake Mead and Lake Powell are currently at, or worse; our water table will drop drastically as it has in California. Canada will experience drought conditions and be unable to grow the essential produce that Canadians require. This is his hidden agenda.
William Michajluk, Scarborough
Responsible media is needed for democracy
Donald Trump is waging war on the media - here's why the media can't bow down, Feb. 13
Vinay Menon's article sounding the alarm on the threat of the demise of responsible media is right on the money and perhaps one of the most serious of all the threats bashing against the pillars of democracy. It is a necessity in this age of untruth and misinformation and we must all continue to bang the drum to keep it alive and relevant. Thank you to the Toronto Star for allowing their talented staff to do their jobs speaking the truth.
Virginia Moeser,
Kawartha Lakes, Ont.
Suffering from Trump overload
We are inundated hourly with media coverage of U.S. President Donald Trump and his escapades. All you get from the news these days is Trump, Trump, Trump. I for one am suffering from Trump Overload. He loves to be front and centre on the news stage several times a day. The best thing we all can do is to ignore him. He would hate that.
Brian Finbow, Port Hope, Ont.
