Document 166 of 500

Tesla sales are down, but not because of Musk: Experts say drop due to rising EV prices, end of government subsidy programs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tesla-sales-are-down-not-because-musk/docview/3167861471/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tesla+sales+are+down%2C+but+not+because+of+Musk%3A+Experts+say+drop+due+to+rising+EV+prices%2C+end+of+government+subsidy+programs&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Tesla sales in Canada plunged over the past month, but it wasn't because electric vehicle buyers are recoiling from the brand founded by Elon Musk.
While loud voices on social media seem to indicate that consumers are turning away from Tesla in protest after Musk took up a prominent role in U.S. President Donald Trump's new administration, which has aggressively targeted both climate action and Canada, the numbers do not reflect this.
Nationwide sales of Teslas were down 70 per cent between December 2024 and January 2025. Meanwhile, sales of all zero-emission vehicles (ZEVs), which include both battery-powered and plug-in hybrids, were down even more - 79 per cent, according to S&P Global, which provided the Star with a preview of ZEV sales numbers that have not yet been made public.
Experts say the dramatic drop is due to the end of government subsidy programs and a hike in EV prices, which has made EVs as much as $21,000 more expensive than they were only seven weeks ago.
"Consumers in Canada are price sensitive and with these changes to the federal and provincial rebates, it's negatively impacting what vehicles customers are looking to buy," said James Hearn, S&P Global Mobility's associate director of market reporting.
Unlike politics, the rise in upfront cost has hit all makes of ZEVs. Sales of Chevrolet ZEVs were down 65 per cent and Hyundai ZEVs were down 30 per cent month-over-month, the data show.
The federal $5,000 EV rebate ran out of money in January and Quebec, where nearly half of all ZEVs are sold, ended its $7,000 rebate at the end of last year.
"We're seeing extreme market disruption right now," said Brian Kingston, president of the Canadian Vehicle Manufacturers' Association. "The incentives have a huge influence on the market and their abrupt stoppage means that sales of EVs have effectively stalled."
Daniel Breton, president of Electric Mobility Canada, a group that advocates for EVs, said the Quebec market dominates in Canada and what happens there has ripple effects across the country.
The future of government EV rebates is in doubt, he said, with some parties pledging to renew the federal program and Quebec promising a less generous $4,000 rebate that will become available April 1.
"A lot of people were pushing to get their vehicles before the end of the year," Breton said. "Now they're postponing their purchases till April."
The drop in sales between December and January may have been big, but it wasn't enough to buck the larger trend of burgeoning growth in the EV sector. Sales grew so much in 2024 that January 2025 ZEV sales were still 27 per cent higher than they were a year prior.
Tesla sales, for example, rose more than 300 per cent in 2024, and remained about 30 per cent higher after the drop in January than they were the year before.
Globally, ZEV sales are growing rapidly, and while government incentives can create short-term swings in the local market, the overall upward trend is clear, Hearn said.
In 2018, when Doug Ford's government cancelled the EV subsidy in Ontario, sales dropped immediately and did not recover for three years. At the same time, however, sales grew so quickly in British Columbia and Quebec that overall sales of ZEVs in Canada continued to grow. (Discounting the pandemic lockdown year of 2020, when sales of all cars and trucks fell.)
Similarly, when the federal EV rebate was announced in 2019, sales tanked for several months until the subsidy became available, Breton said.
Tesla's sales numbers are still growing, but its control of the ZEV market has been in decline for some time - a trend that predates Musk's association with Trump.
Thanks largely to its early entrance into the EV market, Tesla has long held a dominant position, selling more battery electric vehicles than all other brands combined. But as new models entered the market, Tesla's market share has eroded.
According to S&P Global's numbers, as recently as the end of 2022, Tesla controlled more than 40 per cent of the ZEV market. But in the last two years, its share has eroded by more than half, sitting at only 17.5 per cent in the last quarter of 2024. (These numbers include sales of plug-in hybrids; Tesla commands a larger portion of the fully electric vehicle market.)
Tesla sales in Europe have not held up as well, and 2024 was the first year that the company reported a global annual decline in deliveries. Tesla sales in France were 63 per cent lower in January 2025 than they were the previous year. They dropped 59.5 per cent in Germany.
The company, which only produces EVs, has faced stiffer competition in Europe, where Chinese EVs face lower tariffs than they do in North America.
Meanwhile, other car manufacturers have announced dozens of new ZEV models, with many cheaper vehicles set to become available in the coming months.
This, more than politics or rebates, bodes well for the long-term future of EV sales, said Breton.
"The trend is still solid," he said. "It's going to be much easier for everyday Canadians to buy an electric car."
CAPTION: Tesla CEO Elon Musk has taken up a prominent role in U.S. President Donald Trump's administration, but despite online protests, the EV maker's 70 per cent drop in sales in Canada between December 2024 and January 2025 has no political drive.AndrewHarniktribune news service

CREDIT: Marco Chown Oved Toronto Star Climate Change Reporter

Subject: Automobiles; Rebates; Presidents; Politics; Subsidies; Electric vehicles

Business indexing term: Subject: Rebates Subsidies

Location: United States--US; Canada; Quebec Canada; Europe

People: Trump, Donald J; Musk, Elon

Company / organization: Name: Tesla Inc; NAICS: 336110, 336999

Document 167 of 500

Ontario Liberals, NDP best to unite

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-liberals-ndp-best-unite/docview/3167861467/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+Liberals%2C+NDP+best+to+unite&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's the 2022 election all over again, except everything is a little bit worse. It's February, not June, and the United States is led by Donald Trump, not Joe Biden. Doug Ford has amassed more scandals but seems poised to win an even bigger majority simply by saying nothing (hot-mic gaffes notwithstanding). Meanwhile, the Liberals and NDP continue to split the vote, with little that's obvious to distinguish the two parties.
Once again this election, I'm volunteering with NotOneSeat.ca in an effort to defeat Ford through strategic voting and a temporarily united opposition. This time, however, our tone is less about sunshine and unity, and more about asking, "Why is the opposition being wilfully ignorant?"
Everyone is talking about vote-splitting, again. Neither the Liberals nor the NDP has chosen a feasible plan to deal with this perennial problem by swallowing their partisanship pride and fielding candidates strategically. Instead, both parties have opted to fight it out for second place. We've all seen this movie before, and the ending stinks.
Yet if the opposition parties were to announce that they intended to work as a united front against Ford, they would likely receive a massive boost in both positive PR and the polls.
Such was the case for Natasha Doyle-Merrick, the NDP candidate for Eglinton-Lawrence, who withdrew shortly before the deadline to avoid splitting the vote and delivering the riding to Ford's party. The Liberals lost in Eglinton-Lawrence by 524 votes in 2022, with the NDP finishing more than 12,000 votes behind them, having received fewer than 4,000 votes.
Doyle-Merrick's decision has been widely praised on social media, but instead of the NDP turning it into a positive story about putting people before politics, they have so far said nothing. Vince Gasparro, the Liberal candidate for Eglinton-Lawrence, praised the decision in a statement as "a testament to who she is." His response was much classier than the Liberal campaign's text that read, "even the other party recognizes this is a Bonnie versus Doug race." Not according to the polls, it isn't.
Everyone is for co-operation if it means their opponent will drop out, and certainly the Liberals threw the New Democrats under the strategic bus when they collapsed in 2018. The NDP may point to their constitution, which requires them to run a candidate in every riding, as if they could not collectively agree to do things differently this one time. The Liberals have no such excuse; instead, they'll likely fall back on assuming that disillusioned Progressive Conservative and jaded NDP supporters will eventually vote for them - even though the polls haven't changed very much since 2022.
Even a no-cost joint announcement from the opposition parties that they intend to work together rather than prop up a Ford minority government, with the details to be negotiated later, would be positive PR.
Still, not all candidates in the running would rather eat glass than work the other parties.
York-South Weston's NDP candidate, Faisal Hassan, has been refreshingly pragmatic about taking a co-operative approach. "I want results," he told me. "I want to work for the community and get results for them, and that means we can work with (the Liberals) to make things better for the people."
Similarly, the Liberal candidate for Beaches-East York, Mary-Margaret McMahon, sees a lot of value in co-operation. "Ontarians want to see us working together for the greater good," she says. "They want to see collaboration."
If only such voices were louder.
The Liberals and NDP need to stop kidding themselves that the average voter sees a meaningful difference between them. The stark contrast between Ford and everyone else mirrors what's going on in the U.S., where anyone seems a better option than Trump. Nobody wants a two-party system in Ontario, mind you, but anyone would be better than Ford.
When my sink leaks, what matters most is preventing further water damage, not working out the minor differences between the plumbers who can fix it. In Ontario, the house is flooded, and Ford keeps ripping out pipes to sell for scrap. After years under his leadership, what Ontario needs now is to fix health care, the housing crisis, our broken schools, child care and unemployment - and to cancel the many deals Ford has made that appear to enrich billionaires to nobody else's benefit.
The Tories hold a commanding lead in the polls, but after seven years of a Ford government, many of the problems we face have become much worse. The Liberals and NDP need to work together and figure out the details among themselves - and perhaps, while they're at it, figure out a better electoral system so that we never have to do this again.
Sonal Champsee is a
Toronto writer who also runs NotOneSeat.ca, a grassroots, cross-partisan initiative
to defeat Doug Ford.
CREDIT: Sonal Champsee Contributor;

Ford, Crombie spar on taxes, tunnel: PC leader often on defensive during debate as rivals point to housing, affordability, Ontario Place

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-crombie-spar-on-taxes-tunnel/docview/3167861455/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%2C+Crombie+spar+on+taxes%2C+tunnel%3A+PC+leader+often+on+defensive+during+debate+as+rivals+point+to+housing%2C+affordability%2C+Ontario+Place&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Sparks flew between Doug Ford and Bonnie Crombie as the four party leaders clashed in the live provincewide televised debate ahead of the Feb. 27 election.
The Progressive Conservative leader repeatedly targeted the Liberal leader for her record as three-term Mississauga mayor.
"I almost fell off the stage, David, when Bonnie Crombie said she'll cut taxes," Ford told moderator David Common, the host of CBC Radio's "Metro Morning."
"My goodness, all she did was increase taxes in Mississauga," he said Monday night.
But Crombie reminded Ford that he "stood in the legislature and heaped praise on me and my record, said what a fantastic job creator I was and how the economy was booming in Mississauga.
"He may think differently today, but back when I was mayor he thought I was doing a pretty incredible job," said the Liberal leader, who mocked the Tory leader's scheme to build a tunnel underneath Highway 401 from Mississauga to Scarborough.
"Doug, a fantasy tunnel, how dumb is that? After seven years of lies why should anyone believe you. You make other promises, but you don't get it done," she said, circling back to pose a question to viewers watching from home.
"Is your life more affordable than it was seven years ago?" Crombie asked.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said "we cannot afford to continue on the road that we are on," pointing out that the cost of housing and groceries have all risen on Ford's watch.
"Rent in Ontario is up 38 per cent, right? Let that sink in: 38 per cent across the province on average since 2018 ... and it's not just in cities like Toronto, it's in every community across this province," said Stiles, noting homelessness has skyrocketed under the Tories and park encampments have mushroomed.
"Where is the housing, Doug? It's not being built," she said.
"Rising costs are really hurting a lot of people in this province and they are weakening us. He has left us weaker than ever before."
Stiles also pointed out "the hallway health care crisis was invented by the previous Liberal government, but Doug Ford and his government have poured gasoline all over it."
Crombie, who has made the fact 2.5 million Ontarians lack a family doctor the cornerstone of her campaign, blamed Ford for failing on health care.
"We're in a crisis and you're bragging about what you've done. It's been abysmal," she said, pointing out that the Tories waited until just before the election "to turn to a Liberal," Dr. Jane Philpott, a former federal health minister, to help fix the problem.
Taking aim at Crombie, Stiles and Green Leader Mike Schreiner, Ford said "each of them fail on the economy."
"I know the three of you aren't very good with numbers," the Tory chief said dismissively.
"All you three believe in taxing people to death," he said, insisting "the economy will go down the tubes" unless the PCs win the Feb. 27 election.
Schreiner, for his part, said Ford is only in it to help the wealthy.
"Unfortunately in Ontario right now, the rich insiders are getting richer and the rest of us are just struggling to get by," said the Green leader.
Ford was frequently on the defensive with all three of his rivals attacking him for the controversial redevelopment of Ontario Place.
While he remained calm for most of the debate, he struggled when the discussion turned to the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal, and his flip-flopping over allowing and then prohibiting development on 7,400 acres of protected land.
"I think I was pretty clear. I said I was. Then I wasn't, and I went ahead and did it. I apologized to the people," stammered Ford.
While the RCMP's ongoing criminal investigation into the Greenbelt fiasco wasn't mentioned once in the 90-minute debate, Stiles was withering toward the PC leader.
"He says one thing in front of the camera and another thing behind closed doors. You can't trust him on this," said the New Democrat.
Ford was also taken to task for being the multimillionaire scion of a rich family, though he maintained his "very first job was when I was in Grade 2, delivering the Globe and Mail newspaper in the freezing cold weather at 5 a.m."
Crombie reminded viewers her early years were spent growing up in a rooming house with a single mother after her alcoholic father left the family.
"Unfortunately, Doug, you don't get the plight of real people because you were raised privileged, with a silver spoon in your mouth. You didn't have to work for anything. You had a company handed to you, Doug. So you don't understand how expensive things are today," she said.
Bristling at his charge she favours the federal carbon tax when she rejected it as "wrong" in early December, Crombie said: "I have said that repeatedly. But you're just like your friend Donald Trump, you make things up. You are saying lies."
Mindful that Ford has enjoyed a double-digit lead in public-opinion polls since he triggered the snap $189-million election three weeks ago - 15 months before a vote that had been scheduled for June 2026 - Stiles and Crombie at times appeared to be jockeying for second place.
"I was really shocked to see that the Liberal leader, her party, has received $25,000 in donations from private health-care insiders ... I'd like to know how she can stand here and say she's going to save health care ... when she's receiving these kind of donations," the New Democrat said.
"I think the NDP leader is envious of my fundraising effort to date," Crombie retorted, suggesting the New Democrat platform is not realistic.
"I will say that the NDP thinks that money grows on trees. You just can't promise everything to everyone all the time," she said before pleading with NDP voters to come to the Grits to stop the Tories.
According to the latest Abacus Data survey for the Star, his Tories are at 41 per cent support, ahead of Crombie's Liberals at 28 per cent, Stiles's New Democrats at 21 per cent and Schreiner's Greens at six per cent.
Ford, whose Tories have been in office since 2018, said he called the election early because he needs a four-year mandate to "outlive and outlast" Trump, whose threatened 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian goods would devastate Ontario's economy.
CAPTION: From left, PC Leader Doug Ford, Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and NDP Leader Marit Stiles before the start of the Ontario leaders' debate at CBC's Broadcast Centre Monday evening.Chris Young The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy, Rob Ferguson and Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau

Document 169 of 500

Ford survives to battle another day

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-survives-battle-another-day/docview/3167861397/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+survives+to+battle+another+day&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Finally, just in time, an election on the issues that matter to voters.
Until today, Ontarians were snowed under by an absurdly early mid-winter campaign focused on the phoney issue of Donald Trump's tariffs - and Doug Ford's fatuous claim that only he, as Ontario's Progressive Conservative leader, could force the U.S. president to back down.
Now, thanks to Monday night's widely televised debate among the major party leaders, real issues were talked about - and are worth thinking about. They are also worth voting on.
Ever since he triggered the Feb. 27 election on the pretext of Trump's tariffs, Ford has claimed America was the only provincial issue that mattered - and that he was the only real leader with the means to address it. But with barely 10 days until voting day, Ford isn't setting the agenda anymore.
Facing his NDP, Liberal and Green opponents for the first time on a level playing field - alongside each other in a television studio - Ford had to answer pointed questions for 90 minutes. Not on foreign tariffs that fall under federal jurisdiction, but on critical provincial issues:
Housing shortages and doctor shortages. Hallway health care and education shortfalls. Traffic congestion and tax cuts. Affordability and credibility.
The questions - from the debate moderator but also hurled at Ford by NDP Leader Marit Stiles, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and the Green Party's Mike Schreiner - put the PC leader on the defensive for the first time in the campaign. Try as Ford did to frame this as a battle of wills between himself and Trump, the debate served as a reminder that this election is a four-way fight pitting Ford against his provincial opponents, not a foreign president.
The problem with Ford's pitch is that it rings hollow in a debate where all the other leaders are in complete agreement. They all oppose - equally passionately - the sale of Canada and the imposition of tariffs, and will spend whatever it takes to protect the Ontario economy.
No debate there. No disagreement anywhere.
Like Canadian politicians of all political stripes, Ontario politicians from rival parties are fully agreed and aligned on how to respond to the stars and stripes. And so, unable to hide behind his opposition to Trump in the White House, Ford had to face his opponents on their - our - home turf, on issues closer to home.
Ford flailed on his Greenbelt boondoggle and broken promises to preserve farmland, repeating his apology for going back on his word so that he could make a virtue of his vice. Ford fessed up that he'd had to hire a longtime Liberal (Dr. Jane Philpott) on the eve of the election to help him solve the doctor shortage, making a virtue of necessity.
All that said, voters aren't looking for perfection from Ford. They're just looking for an alternative to him and in the last two elections they haven't found one.
In Monday's debate, voters had a chance to look again. They got to see two party leaders appearing in a televised debate for the first time: the NDP's Stiles and the Liberals' Crombie. (Schreiner of the Greens, a veteran of election debates, got in his usual good lines, but his party remains on the sidelines of this election with two seats and roughly five per cent voter support.)
Stiles went on the attack against both Ford's Tories and Crombie's Liberals, blaming them both for hallway health care as she usually does. Whenever Ford is talking health care, he's losing votes, according to PC strategists, which is why he kept pivoting to remind viewers that a healthy economy underpins health care.
Crombie took a different tack, going on the attack against Ford but offering an olive branch to New Democrats by seeking their support instead of splitting the opposition vote: "I'm asking those of you who voted NDP in the last election to vote Liberal," Crombie said to the camera.
But it was the jousting between Crombie and Ford that elicited by far the greatest rancour on both sides. Crombie took the attack to Ford on both policy and personality, but also on credibility and class.
Calling him a son of "privilege" who had a "job handed to you" at his father's printing firm, she called him out of touch as premier, challenging him to say if he even knew the price of a dozen eggs.
"You're just like your friend Donald Trump, you make things up," she charged.
Yet, for all their disagreement on provincial issues, the four party leaders spent much of the night fighting over who was Ontario's foremost tax fighter. Each of them promised more giveaways to leave more money in people's pockets - by cutting tolls or taxes; and by cutting rebate cheques (Ford) or rebating grocery bills (a Stiles idea, now paused by B.C.'s NDP).
It was as if the ghost of Donald Trump was wafting across the debate stage - not by raising tariffs but cutting taxes and imprinting a new form of pocketbook populism on the province.
Will Monday night reset the election? The problem with provincial campaign debates - especially one put on by the big broadcasters over the supper hour on a holiday Monday - is that only the most politically engaged people tune in and only the most patient people watch the clips the next day.
While Crombie and Stiles both made their debate debut and benefited from the exposure, it's fair to say that Ford easily survived the encounter to fight another day (and spend another two days in Washington again this week).
The Tories have enjoyed a massive lead from the first, which is why they called an early election to lock it in. Now that they're in the home stretch, the PCs are counting on voters sleepwalking to the polls while Ford slow walks his victory lap.
CAPTION: Facing his opponents for the first time on a level playing field at Monday's leaders' debate, Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford had to answer pointed questions for 90 minutes, writes Martin Regg Cohn, but none about foreign tariffs, which fall under federal jurisdiction.Chris YoungThe Canadian Press
CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION

Document 170 of 500

Poilievre excelled at taking people down. Can he lift people up?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/poilievre-excelled-at-taking-people-down-can-he/docview/3167861381/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Poilievre+excelled+at+taking+people+down.+Can+he+lift+people+up%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: About 20 years ago, I worked with a team of speech writers in the Ontario Cabinet Office that wrote for whoever was sitting premier, as well as various senior public servants. We sent up multiple speeches per day for different personalities, sometimes writing for two of them on the same day.
In speech writing, matching tone to the speaker is everything. If the speaker reads a draft that they feel doesn't sound like them, it's sent back for a rewrite.
So, we came up with personas characterizing the different personalities to remind us of their speaking style. There was a John Wayne (giddy up, stoic certainty). We had a Jimmy Stewart (empathetic, belief in community), a Hillary Clinton (calm, rational explainer).
A couple of those references are a bit dated. Like I said, it was a while ago. And these weren't taken literally. People are unique and complicated, and every audience has different needs. But the comparison to these figures helped when editing a speech before sending it up the line. Did "John Wayne" have any $10 words? Would "Hillary Clinton" come off as too glib?
Those personas weren't adopted quickly out of political convenience. They were a guide to the leader's core beliefs. They spoke to deeply held values and world view. A "John Wayne" voice, for example, speaks to a belief in individual self-reliance. A "Jimmy Stewart" believes in the inherent goodness of people, even those who may hold differing views.
I've been thinking about these personas in watching federal Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre adjust to the rapidly shifting mood among Canadians in response to the tariff threat. Until recently, to my ear, Poilievre, brought to mind a Trump-lite tone, one that speaks more to self-interest and superiority.
Poilievre has described Canada as a place where "everything is broken." When mayors in Quebec and Montreal disagreed with his housing plan, they were called "incompetent." There was the speech he gave about one year ago to the Vancouver Board of Trade where he told business leaders that their lobbyists in Ottawa were "utterly useless" and that they should "stop sucking up to the people who are doing the damage to our country."
Brutish language. Simple absolutes. Us and them. Poilievre's diction has been peppered with "dismantle," "defund," "axe."
To be clear, Poilievre is not Trump. But his political identity was shaped in a global conservative ecosystem that rewards excessive rhetoric and mocks thoughtful discussion. The Trump-lite tone is cheered by his conservative base. His lead in the polls suggests that Poilievre's sense of grievance had been touching a nerve with moderate voters fatigued with a rising cost of living, housing affordability and homelessness, all while being led by a Prime Minister past his due date. Even many Liberal strategists would admit that Poilievre had a gift for communicating this brand, especially when speaking directly to Canadians via social media.
But now that Canada is staring at the pointy end of conservative populism, Poilievre's Trump-lite tone will hit the ears of Canadians much differently, reminding them of threats to their finances and the very future of their nation. The angry mood Poilievre once tapped into is shifting to anxiety. Anger is an indulgence. Anxiety we feel in our gut.
Poilievre and his advisers, of course, know this. And a change in his tone might have happened to some degree anyway, even without the tariff threat. Opposition leaders often adopt a more Prime Minister in waiting tone to close out a campaign.
But for Poilievre, the shift is happening overnight. There's an obvious, and hurried, attempt to appear calmer, more strategic and thoughtful. From the few podium appearances I've seen on news networks, he comes across as a bit wooden in the new persona - more reliance on notes, fewer changes in voice intonation, less engagement with the audience.
So, for Poilievre, this is a deeper question that goes beyond speech writing. Who is the human being behind the public voice? What does he believe in? All we've seen him do well is to take people down. What can he say now to lift people up?
Good speakers - and good leaders - get people believing in a future that does not yet exist, but which they can bring into existence, together. It's going to be a real challenge for Pierre Poilievre to shift his public speaking persona from "everything is broken" to "we can do it." Getting people angry is easy. Hope is hard work.
CAPTION: Leader of the Conservative Party Pierre Poilievre speaks with reporters in the Foyer of the House of Commons, in Ottawa, Monday, Dec. 16, 2024.
CREDIT: David McLaughlin, Contributor

Subject: Political campaigns; Speaking; Prime ministers; Tariffs

Title: Poilievre excelled at taking people down. Can he lift people up?

Document 171 of 500

Trump's shameful treatment of our Canadian ally leaves me heartbroken

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/trumps-shameful-treatment-our-canadian-ally/docview/3167861072/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trump%27s+shameful+treatment+of+our+Canadian+ally+leaves+me+heartbroken&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: One of my fondest childhood memories is of crossing the Peace Bridge travelling from Buffalo to Hamilton with my parents to visit our Canadian relatives. You see, early in the 20th century my grandparents and their siblings emigrated from Italy. My grandparents settled in Buffalo; their siblings settled in Hamilton. Over the years there were frequent visits back and forth. Food and wine and laughter flowed freely.
As years passed the visits continued along with weddings and funerals. Crossing the bridge to visit always felt like I was going to my second home. Now grown and married with children of my own I would mention familiar landmarks as we drove along the QEW, pointing out Hamilton's "Mountain" when it came into view to let the kids know we were almost there.
I remember my Canadian family with deep affection, especially my second cousin (since passed away) who was like a brother to me. These days I still get that same special feeling when I cross the bridge to attend the Shaw Festival at Niagara on the Lake, visit the Butterfly Museum or see a play in Toronto.
The reason I share all this is to tell my Canadian friends that there are millions of Americans like me who do not support our current government's disgraceful actions and are heartbroken to see our Canadian ally subjected to such shameful treatment. These actions hurt us all. It is my hope that our friendship remains strong and that you all continue to stand up to America's bully president .
At the age of 81, in light of current events, my only regret is that grandpa didn't settle in Canada with his brother.
Elizabeth Accordino, Perrysburg, New York
Ford is blaming families rather than accepting liability for eliminating the bike lanes
Dropping off your kids at school on an e-bike? Here's why you might not want to in Ontario, Feb. 10
Thanks to the Star for pointing out Doug Ford's cynical law that fines young families for putting their children on their e-bikes. With the cost of housing in Toronto, many young families (including our own) have found using e-bicycles makes living here more affordable; owning a car is often an unaffordable luxury. Ford's law puts children whose families rely on bikes (and the bike lanes!) at risk of accidents, so he is trying blame families for putting their children at risk rather than accepting liability for eliminating the bike lanes. Ford's actions can only be described as uncaring of Toronto families who need to use bicycles for their children's transportation.
Karin Brothers, Toronto
Stop distracting voters, Ford, and deal with health care, housing and education
Doug Ford thinks he is the mayor of Toronto (get rid of bike lanes on main streets). Now he thinks he is the Prime Minister of Canada (bring back capital punishment). He is doing everything in his power to distract the voters from the real issues. Rarely does he talk about health care, education or housing - HIS REAL issues as premier.
Ken Perkin, Scarborough
We need modular housingLeadership candidates pitch plans for homebuilding, doctors, Feb. 13
I am glad to see Mark Carney advocating for modular housing in the Liberal leadership race. With housing starts in Ontario dropping 22 per cent last year we need to use the most effective tools in our tool box to get affordable housing built fast. Modular housing factories produce housing in a fraction of the time and at a fraction of the cost in climate controlled and quality-controlled environments. New construction workers don't need to spend years fulfilling apprenticeships but can be under the watchful eyes of a journeyman foreman. Older construction workers would be retained in a safe and healthy work environment. New immigrants may also be trained. If you want to build a custom-built house go ahead, if you can afford it, but don't leave the rest of us unhoused because we can't afford "bespoke" construction. Bring housing construction into the 21st century.
Judy Smith, Cobourg

Support for northern communities is our best defence
It's striking to me that CBC news often addresses two ostensibly unrelated themes: the lack of Canadian military spending, and shortcomings in support for remote Indigenous and Northern communities. It strikes me that these issues are very much related. Possibly a response to U.S. President Donald Trump would point out that the best defence of the North would be to support these vast communities . Community support should be considered to be part of our national defence and functioning communities would be more effective than trying to police the region with a few more helicopters.
Stephen Tulk, Toronto
Trump wants our fresh water
For Canadians in favour of becoming the 51 state, be very careful what you wish for! Trump wants only one thing from us which he very carefully avoids mentioning, and that's our freshwater. If you choose to join the U.S., sometime in the future our rivers will suffer the same fate as the Colorado River. The St. Lawrence River will no longer reach the ocean; the water level in our lakes (including the Great Lakes) will drop to a similar level that Lake Mead and Lake Powell are currently at, or worse; our water table will drop drastically as it has in California. Canada will experience drought conditions and be unable to grow the essential produce that Canadians require just to mention a few results. This is his hidden agenda. Everything else that Trump mentions can be imported from the rest of the world. Not freshwater. Once our water is gone, it's gone forever. What will future generations of Canadian then drink to survive?
William Michajluk, Scarborough
Demise of responsible media is a serious threat to democracy
Donald Trump is waging war on the media - here's why the media can't bow down, Feb. 13
Vinay Menon's article sounding the alarm on the threat of the demise of responsible media is right on the money and perhaps one of the most serious of all the threats bashing against the pillars of democracy. It is a necessity in this age of untruth and misinformation and we must all continue to bang the drum to keep it alive and relevant. Thank you to the Toronto Star for allowing their talented staff to do their jobs speaking the truth.
Virginia Moeser, Kawartha Lakes
Suffering from Trump overload
We are inundated hourly with media coverage of US. President Donald Trump and his escapades. All you get from the news these days is Trump, Trump, Trump. I for one am suffering from Trump Overload. He loves to be front and centre on the news stage several times a day. The best thing we all can do is to ignore him. He would hate that.
Brian Finbow, Port Hope
Cover the news but please no more photos of Ford
Do you not realize that every time you put a picture of Premier Doug Ford in the paper, you are helping him? Yes, you have to report the news, but you do not have to continually print his image. Many people just look at pictures and captions, and even those who actually read the reports are affected by the visual bombardment.
Margaret Gillespie, Scarborough

Poll suggests tighter race, but Ford still well ahead: Liberals jump four points as Feb. 27 election nears

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/poll-suggests-tighter-race-ford-still-well-ahead/docview/3167861053/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Poll+suggests+tighter+race%2C+but+Ford+still+well+ahead%3A+Liberals+jump+four+points+as+Feb.+27+election+nears&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford remains well ahead, but the Feb. 27 Ontario election race is tightening somewhat as Bonnie Crombie's Liberals gain ground, a new poll suggests.
The Abacus Data survey for the Star found Ford's Progressive Conservatives at 41 per cent support, ahead of Crombie's Liberals at 28 per cent, Marit Stiles's New Democrats at 21 per cent and Mike Schreiner's Greens at six per cent.
That's a five percentage point drop for the Tories and a four percentage point increase for the Liberals since the last Abacus survey released Feb. 7. The NDP remained the same and the Greens went up one percentage point.
"It does show that Ford is not invincible in terms of his personal reputation," Abacus president David Coletto said Monday. "Ford was at an artificially high place when this campaign started," he noted, because of the attention surrounding U.S. President Donald Trump's threatened 25 per cent tariffs against Canadian goods.
"He's still the favourite. But an election campaign is bound to bring down the incumbent numbers - we've seen it in almost every election, the desire for change goes up," Coletto said.
Abacus surveyed 1,500 Ontarians from last Thursday through Saturday using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 2.7 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
The poll was conducted while Ford dominated the provincial campaign news cycle with a high-profile mission to Washington to lobby American lawmakers and business leaders against Trump's tariffs.
The poll was released before the four party leaders' live televised debate Monday night at the CBC Broadcast Centre in Toronto.
"For those who say this election is about Trump or 'standing up to the bully,' Ford still has a big advantage," Coletto said.
Indeed, 64 per cent of respondents who had an opinion on the matter believed the Tories were the best party "to deal with the impact of Donald Trump" compared to 16 per cent for the Liberals, 15 per cent for the NDP and five per cent for the Greens.
Ford called the snap provincial election 15 months early, framing it as a referendum on who could best deal with tariffs that would damage Ontario's trade-dependent economy.
On which party "will keep the cost of living as low as possible," the Tories lead with 37 per cent to 29 per cent for the Liberals, 21 per cent for the NDP and seven per cent for the Greens.
"They still have the advantage so they'll feel comfortable with an eight-point lead, but it's no longer that 15-point lead it was even a few weeks ago," Coletto noted.
On which party "will best manage the provincial economy," the Tories were at 41 per cent, the Liberals at 38 per cent, the NDP at 17 per cent and the Greens at three per cent. That's a significant change from the Feb. 7 poll that found the Tories at 47 per cent, the NDP at 15 per cent, the Liberals at 13 per cent and the Greens at three per cent.
"I think what's happened is some former PC supporters have moved to the Liberals and Bonnie Crombie is also benefiting from the fact that the Liberal brand is no longer as toxic as it was even a few weeks ago because of the prime minister stepping away," Coletto said, referring to Justin Trudeau's looming departure.
"The leadership race (to succeed Trudeau) and the dynamics that are happening at the federal level are probably helping her at the provincial level as well. I don't think being a Liberal today is as challenging a prospect as it was a month ago," he said.
On which party "will improve the health-care system," the Liberals were at 39 per cent, the NDP at 29 per cent, the Tories at 26 per cent and the Greens at four per cent.
On which party "will do the most to make housing more affordable," the NDP was at 41 per cent, the Liberals at 25 per cent, the Tories at 15 per cent and the Greens at 15 per cent. And on which "will improve the education system," 42 per cent said the Liberals, 27 per cent the Tories, 12 per cent the Greens and eight per cent the NDP.
Abacus found the approval rating of Ford's two-term PC government has continued to slide since the election started with 34 per cent approving, 43 per cent disapproving and three per cent neutral.
That's an overall minus nine per cent approval, whereas the Tories had a plus eight per cent approval in the first election poll released last month.
In terms of whether it is "time for a change in government," 55 per cent felt it was - that's up from 52 per cent earlier this month and 48 per cent in January's poll.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Document 173 of 500

Key takeaways from the leaders' debate

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/key-takeaways-leaders-debate/docview/3167860957/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Key+takeaways+from+the+leaders%27+debate&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The first question?
Tell us about your first job ... Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie worked as a cashier at Woolco at age 14 (and said she lied about her age to get the job), followed by other retail jobs at the mall ... NDP Leader Marit Stiles babysat and then became a swimming instructor (she was also a competitive swimmer) ... PC Leader Doug Ford delivered the Globe and Mail newspaper as a kid, then worked at Canada Packers ... and Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner, who grew up on a family farm, fed cattle and drove the combine before his dad gave him a loan to buy a lawn mower so he could started his own business.
The first zinger of the night ...
... went to Crombie, during a debate on health care. Ford spoke about hiring Dr. Jane Philpott, a former federal health minister in the Trudeau government, to help reform the system and connect all Ontarians with a family doctor (about 2.5 million are without one). Crombie shot back "You had to hire a Liberal" to fix the system. Ford said the reason why Philpott was hired is "because she sees the vision."
Who supports building housing on the Greenbelt?
Schreiner gave an emphatic "no," Crombie said "absolutely no" and Stiles added "our farmland is not up for grabs." Ford said he doesn't now, but "I think I was pretty clear. I said I was, then I wasn't, then I went ahead" and cancelled plans to build homes on the protected lands.
'This is a tense room right now'
So said moderator David Common, host of CBC Radio's "Metro Morning," pivoting to a personal question, asking the candidates what they'd do with a weekend off. Ford said he works and likes to return calls (so much so that he's only been to his cottage twice in the past year, for a total of five hours, to cut the grass); Schreiner likes spending time with his family in nature; Crombie likes to rock climb and would like to try it in Thunder Bay, and enjoys playing pickleball with her family; Stiles loves swimming in Ontario lakes and has taken a dip in all but Lake Superior.
Post-debate
Ford skipped taking questions from reporters, as he did on Friday in North Bay after the first leaders' debate. Stiles said she didn't get a chance to talk about the RCMP investigation into the Ford government's Greenbelt dealings and that he broke promises to Ontarians to fix health care.
'A little bit feisty'
Crombie, speaking to reporters afterwards, called herself "a little bit feisty" during the debate and made another appeal to NDP voters to switch to the Liberals as she did in her closing remarks. Asked about controversial online comments made by a few Liberal candidates, she said she has been focused on the debate and "not thinking about what candidates said 10 or 20 years ago."
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy, Rob Ferguson and Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau

Document 174 of 500

Doug Ford has money for $200 cheques and a 401 tunnel - where are the funds to address homelessness?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-has-money-200-cheques-401-tunnel-where/docview/3167751769/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+has+money+for+%24200+cheques+and+a+401+tunnel+-+where+are+the+funds+to+address+homelessness%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Big number: 81,515, the number of people who experienced homelessness in Ontario in 2024, according to a report by the Association of Municipalities of Ontario
In a more sensible world, the sheer magnitude of the homelessness crisis in Ontario would make it a top issue during this provincial election, with the incumbent party called out and held to account for the sorry situation.
But as we reach the home stretch of this strange sideshow election in Ontario - even as a deluge of snow and cold weather has further highlighted the need for access to safe shelters and housing - Premier Doug Ford appears to be skating through without facing much blame.
Please don't kid yourself: homelessness is not just a municipal issue. Nor is it just about pointing fingers at federal immigration and housing policy. The provincial government has a major responsibility - definitely more responsibility than they have for international trade.
And the numbers suggest Ford's government deserves a lot of the blame. The situation has gotten much, much worse since 2018, when he took office.
In January, the Association of Municipalities of Ontario (AMO) published a report pegging the number of people who experienced homelessness in 2024 at 81,515. To put that into perspective, if you collected all those people in one place they'd represent the 33rd largest of Ontario's 444 municipalities; about the size of Peterborough.
It has not always been this bad.
In 2017, the last year of government led by former premier Kathleen Wynne, 54,664 people experienced homelessness, according to AMO. That's still too many, of course, but Ford's government has never posted a mark as low.
The recent trends are especially alarming, with the number increasing an average of 12 per cent annually over the past three years, coinciding with the end of most pandemic-era social support programs.
And we ain't seen nothing yet. According to AMO, if nothing changes, the population of "known homeless" in Ontario could grow to 294,266 by 2035.
This trend, it turns out, correlates with a lack of provincial spending on programs designed to reduce homelessness. A report from the Maytree Foundation, released last week, found that inflation-adjusted provincial spending on homelessness and housing programs is stagnant and remains well below the level of spending in the final years of the Wynne government.
Compounding the problem, a greater share of the funding is going toward short-term shelter programs instead of longer-term solutions like low-income housing. When the Ford government cancelled the province's cap and trade carbon pricing system in 2018, the report says, the low-income housing projects that were to be funded with it got cancelled too.
Even with rapidly rising homelessness numbers, there's been a real reluctance from Queen's Park to get back into funding affordable housing - or to do anything to meaningfully address the scale of the problem.
In December, the Ford government announced a new initiative to, in its words, "end encampments." But Maytree calculates the new funding for that initiative amounts to only about a 2.8 per cent increase in the amount the government will spend on addressing homelessness. That's small change in a province that is seeing much higher year-over-year increases in the number of unhoused people.
In Toronto, the province's fiscal foot-dragging has translated into a frustrating waiting game, in which Queen's Park has been slow to match the funding commitments made by City Hall and Ottawa for the city's 10-year housing plan. As of last September, according to a report to Toronto city council, the city had committed to $7.5 billion, the federal government had committed $2.9 billion, while the province took a distant third place in a race with three participants, with $1.4 billion offered up to date.
It would be one thing if these tight purse strings for programs addressing homelessness could be chalked up to an effort to constrain costs across the board, but Ford has been a shameless big spender in other areas.
He's funded gimmicks like the removal of vehicle registration fees, forgoing more than $1 billion in annual revenue, and issuing $200 cheques for every taxpayer in the province, a $3 billion one-time expense. He's made bizarre deals like his $612-million plan to get booze in corner stores immediately, instead of just waiting until contracts expired, or forgiving contractual penalties owed by the corporation that owns Highway 407, potentially worth $1 billion.
The big spending shows no sign of stopping. On the campaign trail, Ford has continued to tout his plans to build a tunnel underneath Highway 401, a project that could cost an unfathomable sum of $100 billion - or more.
According to AMO, the province could make a deal with municipalities to eliminate most chronic homelessness with an investment of $11 billion over 10 years; an additional $2 billion over eight years would greatly reduce the number of encampments.
Stacked up against all the money Ford has and is planning to shovel out the door, this sounds like one hell of a bargain. Between now and election day, it's worth asking why a premier who has made so many bad deals won't make this good one.
CAPTION: A homeless encampment in Toronto's Alexandria Park in January, wherevolunteers with the Ve'ahavta humanitarian organization were handing out hot meals and supplies.
CREDIT: Matt Elliott

Document 175 of 500

Plane crash in Toronto: At least 12 people injured after Delta plane crashes at Pearson; All flights currently suspended

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/plane-crash-toronto-at-least-12-people-injured/docview/3168093209/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Plane+crash+in+Toronto%3A+At+least+12+people+injured+after+Delta+plane+crashes+at+Pearson%3B+All+flights+currently+suspended&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A Delta plane has crashed at Toronto Pearson International Airport with around 80 people on board the flight. Multiple people have been injured and police and paramedics are on the scene. Follow the Star's live updates on Monday.
4:58 p.m. Delta says there were 76 passengers and four crew members on board the flight.
4:55 p.m. PC Leader Doug Ford says he's "relieved there are no casualties" after the crash at Pearson airport.
In a post on X, he says provincial officials are in contact with the airport and local authorities and will provide help as needed. - Canadian Press
4:47 p.m. 12 people injured, paramedics say
Lawrence Saindon, a supervisor with Peel Regional Paramedic Services, said there were 15 patients, 12 of whom have injuries ranging from mild to moderate.
He said two were taken by air ambulance to St. Michael's Hospital and another who was transported by ground ambulance to SickKids.
"We have no life threatening injuries to anybody at this time," he said, stressing that there are no fatalities.
"All crew and passengers have been accounted for."
4:45 p.m. "I'm relieved to learn that all passengers and crew are accounted for after today's plane crash at Toronto Pearson," Toronto mayor Olivia Chow posted on X.
"Thank you to the first responders, crew and airport staff for their quick actions and commitment to keeping everyone safe," Chow said.
4:39 p.m. Ann Nguyen, 19, was at Pearson with her mother and sister. The group had planned to fly to Cancun, Mexico, during her reading week but said the flight has been delayed six times since Sunday, due to the weather, and then cancelled on Monday.
"We got here, and then as we were coming in we found out that the plane crashed," said Nguyen, expressing relief that there are no fatalities.
Frustrated over her travel situation, she said she rather receive a refund than have the flight rescheduled again.
4:37 p.m. Kevin Feng, a Toronto resident who was heading on a Delta flight to New York Monday afternoon, was among the passengers whose flights were cancelled. He said the notice he received made no mention of the crash but he learned what happened from a staff member in the airport.
"It's scary," he said, especially knowing it's the same airline he was set to fly with.
4:36 p.m. Delta says it is aware of the reports of an "incident" involving flight 4819 from Minneapolis to Toronto.
"We are working to confirm any details and will share the most current information on news.delta.com as soon as it becomes available," the airline said in a statement.
4:33 p.m. The Transportation Safety Board of Canada is deploying a team of investigators following the aircraft collision to gather information and assess the occurrence, a statement read.
4:31 p.m. Montreal airport taking diversion flights
The airport in Montreal posted to X on Monday afternoon, saying they are taking some diversion flights.
"Due to an accident involving a @Delta Airlines aircraft at @TorontoPearson's airport, #YUL is preparing to receive several diversion flights. Our teams are already in action to welcome them. However, given the large number of passengers at YUL, please note that the situation may cause delays at various points along the passenger process. Thank you for your patience and understanding."
4:30 p.m. Three people with critical injuries
The ambulance service Ornge says it has transported three people with critical injuries to hospital, including a young person.
In an email, spokesperson Joshua NcNamara says one "pediatric patient" is being sent to SickKids hospital with critical injuries.
McNamara says one man in his 60s with critical injuries was sent to St. Michael's Hospital and a woman in her 40s, also with critical injuries, was sent to Sunnybrook Health Sciences Centre.
The number of critically injured people reported by Ornge is more than those previously reported by a spokesperson for the local paramedic service, who had said one person had critical but non-life-threatening injuries, while seven others were said to have mild to moderate injuries. - Canadian Press
4:25 p.m. I'm closely following the serious incident at the Pearson Airport involving Delta Airlines flight 4819 from Minneapolis. All 80 passengers onboard are accounted for. Updates will follow," Anita Anand said on X.
4:23 p.m. "I am relieved to hear there are no casualties following the plane crash at Pearson airport today. Grateful to first responders and airport authorities for their work today, and I'm wishing those injured a swift and full recovery," Bonnie Crombie wrote on X.
4:17 p.m. On air traffic control, a man is heard describing the scene at Pearson.
"Just so you're aware, there are people outside walking around the aircraft there," he says.
"Yeah, we've got it," another man responds. "The aircraft is upside down and burning."
4:15 p.m. Just after 4 p.m., the U.S. Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) confirmed 80 people were onboard the flight at the time of the crash. All passengers were evacuated. Canada's Transportation Safety Board will be in charge of the investigation, the FAA said.
4:10 p.m. According to the U.S. Department of Transportation, there were 80 people onboard at the time of the crash.
4:05 p.m. "This airplane just crashed on 23," an air traffic controller is heard saying around 2:13 p.m., according to archives accessed by the Star. A controller then directs another flight to pull up and go around.
4 p.m. Minutes before the crash, around 2:08 p.m., air traffic control at Pearson directs the Delta flight to contact the air traffic control tower.
Then, about five minutes later, air traffic control tells another flight that its approach clearance has been cancelled.
"Runway 23 is currently closed," a controller says to another plane. "It's gonna be a little while here."
3:57 p.m. 80 people were reportedly on the plane.
3:52 p.m. Two passengers with serious injuries were transported to nearby hospitals, including one taken to SickKids, a Peel police spokesperson said. No fatalities have been reported, they said.
3:51 p.m. Over the intercom in Terminal 3 departures at Pearson airport just now: "All flights have been suspended. More information will be shared as soon as it becomes available. Sorry for the inconvenience and thanks for your patience."
3:47 p.m. The airport has confirmed the incident involves a Delta Air Lines plane arriving from Minneapolis, and images posted on social media show the plane flipped over on the tarmac.
3:43 p.m. All flights are suspended at Pearson airport.
3:42 p.m. According to Flightradar24, the plane - a Delta Airlines flight from Minneapolis - arrived at Pearson at 2:13 p.m. The plane, a Mitsubishi CRJ-900LR, has room for about 75 passengers.
3:31 p.m. One pediatric patient was transported to hospital with critical injuries according to ORNGE. The emergency response service said they do not have information on any other transported patients yet.
ORNGE said they sent three helicopters and two critical care ambulances.
3:20 p.m. Most of the passengers are out
Police could not comment on any injuries but said that "most of the passengers are out."
A post on X from Toronto Pearson confirmed that "All passengers and crew are accounted for."
The crash happened "upon landing," according to a post from Toronto Pearson, "involving a Delta Airlines plane arriving from Minneapolis."

Document 176 of 500

Ontario election: What to expect at the leaders' debate tonight

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-election-what-expect-at-leaders-debate/docview/3167716140/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+election%3A+What+to+expect+at+the+leaders%27+debate+tonight&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It is the main event of the Feb. 27 Ontario election campaign.
The four major party leaders square off on Monday night at CBC's Toronto Broadcast Centre for a 90-minute live televised debate.
It's the second and final time Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford, NDP Leader Marit Stiles, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and Green Leader Mike Schreiner will gather on the same stage during this campaign.
The four met Friday in North Bay for a midday debate on northern issues.
Sparks flew on the shores of Lake Nipissing with Crombie accusing Ford of telling "lies" about her record and him saying it was "a joke" when she tried to distance herself from some decisions made by Liberal premiers Dalton McGuinty and Kathleen Wynne between 2003 and 2018.
But that was just a warm-up for Monday's more closely watched debate.
With Ford, whose Tories have been in office since 2018, enjoying a big lead in public opinion polls, Stiles, Crombie and Schreiner will have their sights set on him.
Each leader has spent hours preparing with simulated debates using advisers as role-playing stand-ins in an effort to gird for expected attacks.
Mindful of the target on his back, Ford's team has implored him to "take the high road" and remain calm in the face of opposition barbs.
He wants to keep the conversation focused on his chosen ballot question: which leader is best equipped to "protect Ontario" from the harmful effects of U.S. President Donald Trump's threatened tariffs on Canadian goods.
"Over the last five weeks, we've seen what the next four years will look like under President Trump's White House. He'll shift the goalpost every single day on a whim, creating economic chaos every single time he does it," said Ford, who was caught on a "hot mic" early in the campaign saying he "100 per cent" wanted Trump to win the U.S. election last November and has felt betrayed by him.
As she did in the North Bay debate, Stiles will be asking Ontarians if they feel better off now than they were in 2018.
"After seven years of Doug Ford, we've had a lot of talk but it's never been worse," she said Friday, pointing to the high cost of housing and the homelessness crisis that has seen tent encampments mushroom across Ontario.
Crombie, for her part, will be reminding viewers that 2.5 million Ontarians can't access a family doctor and touting the Liberal plan to cure that problem.
"You can't trust him because he doesn't deliver. Back in 2018, he said he'd end hallway medicine, the wait times have doubled. Back in 2018, he said he'd cut your taxes. He hasn't cut your taxes. He didn't get it done," she said of Ford.
Schreiner, meanwhile, will be underscoring the Tories' record on building housing, and raising the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal that's now being investigating by the RCMP.
"Let's look at the record over the last seven years. Housing starts are at an all-time low in Ontario. Why is that most of the government's housing plans have been, 'How do we unlock profits for wealthy land speculators?' instead of how do we unlock affordable homes that people can afford?" he said.
Metro Morning radio host David Common will moderate the 6:30 p.m. to 8 p.m. debate that will be broadcast on CBC, CTV, CP24, CityNews, Global, TVO, CHCH, and CPAC as well radio stations across the province.
Colin D'Mello, Global News Queen's Park bureau chief, and CTV News Queen's Park bureau chief Siobhan Morris will join Common in asking questions of the candidates.
Coincidentally, the debate is being held on Family Day, a statutory holiday that was created in Ontario after being a cornerstone re-election promise of Liberal premier Dalton McGuinty in the 2007 campaign.
CAPTION: Clockwise from top left: Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford, NDP Leader Marit Stiles and Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie, Rob Ferguson, Kristin Rushowy

Subject: Political campaigns; Scandals; Housing starts; Elections

Document 177 of 500

How Donald Trump, tariffs and the Liberal leadership are erasing Ontario's election - and playing right into Doug Ford's hands

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/how-donald-trump-tariffs-liberal-leadership-are/docview/3167716139/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+Donald+Trump%2C+tariffs+and+the+Liberal+leadership+are+erasing+Ontario%27s+election+-+and+playing+right+into+Doug+Ford%27s+hands&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario voters clueing in to the fact they are in the middle of a quick, unexpected election could be forgiven for thinking their choices on the Feb. 27 provincial ballot will be Donald Trump, tariffs or Mark Carney.
Political observers and some door-knocking candidates say that international and national headlines blasting Ontarians like a fire hose, stoking dire financial fears and debate over who best to lead Canada, are almost erasing the provincial election - likely to the benefit of the Progressive Conservatives.
"I think that was what (Conservative Leader) Doug Ford was gambling on when he called an early vote," said Prof. Lydia Miljan, head of political science at the University of Windsor, in an interview. "He's seizing the uncertainty of the moment while (U.S. President) Donald Trump sucks up all the political oxygen with a continual lurching of, 'Here's a tariff, delay the tariff, maybe double the tariff' - it's exhausting.
"If it wasn't for the few Progressive Conservative signs I am seeing around, I would have forgotten that there is a provincial race going on at all because we are talking so much about the federal Liberal leadership race and Trump and all this tariff noise."
John Campbell, a former city councillor running for the Liberals against PC incumbent Kinga Surma in Etobicoke Centre, said voters answering the door during this rare mid-winter campaign seem unhappy about a lot of things, including Ford calling the election 15 months ahead of schedule and his leadership in general. But many also seem apathetic about forcing change.
"I think, by and large, people are just really disappointed with the whole news cycle," said Campbell, who is also a former Toronto District School Board chair.
"You've got Gaza, you've got Trump, you've got Ukraine, you got a mishmash in Ottawa - all of the leadership stuff there. You've got people in power here who nobody really seems to like that much but there's not a whole lot of hope in the air, and people are just kind of throwing their hands up and saying, 'What a mess it is.'"
Campbell said his pitch to shake off voter apathy is that Ford is only pretending to be a fiscal conservative, spending $612 million to get alcohol into corner stores before the election, and $3 billion to send every Ontarian a $200 cheque during the campaign, as the underfunding of health care, colleges and universities, and public schools are degrading Ontarians' quality of life.
"Most people are mixed on Doug Ford or not very happy," Campbell said. "I just hope this election gets some momentum soon."
Ford launched the campaign Jan. 29 saying he needs a "clear mandate" from Ontarians to fight Trump and his threatened 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian-made goods being sold in the U.S. that would severely damage the province's economy. "He wants to undermine us in any way he can," Ford said, days before being caught saying he cheered Trump's win after a campaign in which the Republican leader promised tariffs.
While Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and NDP Leader Marit Stiles campaigned in the cold and snow, Ford was in the international spotlight, making headlines along with other premiers and territorial leaders during a mission to Washington to fight tariffs.
They warned U.S. lawmakers that Trump's levies would ravage the economies of both nations in meetings that included a stop at the White House to chat with two Trump officials - but not the president.
Ontario's integrity commissioner approved Ford's midelection trip provided his activities weren't for partisan purposes. Ford's social media accounts posted a highlight reel of the trip that includes his party's logo and campaign slogan of "Protect Ontario," prompting Stiles to say she will ask for an integrity investigation of the trip. If that happens, any finding will come well after the Feb. 27 vote.
Miljan said provincial leaders' debates should give Crombie and Stiles the chance to remind voters who they are and what they stand for, and to put Ford on the hot seat. The debates, however, are unlikely to galvanize voters or drown out competing news events.
"Debates don't really matter that much - they're great for pundits and media to have something to focus on, and will be helpful to introduce Crombie and Stiles to the electorate, but Doug Ford's a known quantity and he's playing the 'Captain Canada' card," she said.
"You've also got federal Liberal leadership debates coming up," she noted, between five candidates including Carney, former governor of the Bank of Canada and then Bank of England, and Chrystia Freeland, MP and former deputy prime minister. Those debates could potentially cause further voter confusion.
In Oshawa, New Democrat Jennifer French takes a break from door-knocking in her bid for a third term in office, knowing she'll soon be heading back out into an evening snowstorm.
"I thought initially it would be too cold for conversations at the door because people are surrounded by political stories at all levels, but they've actually been really engaging at the door, they know there is a provincial election on," French said.
Existential fears over Trump's tariffs is a bit of déjà vu for Oshawa, she said, since the community in 2018 faced news of the coming closure of the local General Motors plant, and the loss of almost 3,000 jobs. A campaign against that closure ended with the production line being reopened and people rehired to make pickup trucks.
"Everyone is looking for strong, capable leadership," French said. "While Canada might not be for sale, this has been a unifying threat from Trump. I don't have to lead people to think about the ways that the Ford government has pushed privatization," she said.
"There are so many layers of politics happening all at once. When I was door-knocking in a building filled with seniors, who are at home and watching the news, there is a lot of anxiety and uncertainty with the volatility in the States, there is anticipation of a possible federal election," French said.
"The seniors are worried about costs going up, about their loved ones, about themselves," she said. "The conversation about provincial health care might not be what they lead with but it's the second point they raise: 'Have you've seen Trump? OK. Well, what are you going to do to help seniors and make sure they stay a priority? It becomes very community-based very quickly."
The Progressive Conservative campaign did not respond to the Star's requests for comment.
CAPTION: Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie talks to students at a campaign event at George Brown College in Toronto, on Feb. 11, 2025.
CREDIT: David Rider

Subject: Provincial elections; Political campaigns; Voters; Political leadership; Elections; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Tariffs

People: Trump, Donald J

Title: How Donald Trump, tariffs and the Liberal leadership are erasing Ontario's election - and playing right into Doug Ford's hands

Stage set for the main debate: Four party leaders ready for 90 minutes of televised verbal jousting

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/stage-set-main-debate/docview/3167578275/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Stage+set+for+the+main+debate%3A+Four+party+leaders+ready+for+90+minutes+of+televised+verbal+jousting&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/
