Document 179 of 500

Poilievre caught off-guard: Conservative leader wasn't prepared for rise of Carney and Trump upending race

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/poilievre-caught-off-guard/docview/3167578089/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Poilievre+caught+off-guard%3A+Conservative+leader+wasn%27t+prepared+for+rise+of+Carney+and+Trump+upending+race&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: "There are unknown unknowns." That's how former U.S. Secretary of Defence Donald Rumsfeld referred to the risks that come from situations so unexpected they would not normally be considered. Unknown unknowns are far more difficult to anticipate than known unknowns.
This typology of knowns forms the "Rumsfeld Matrix," which is often used in strategic planning in business and, presumably, by political strategists.
What were the "known unknowns" that federal Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre and his advisers could have anticipated during his meteoric rise in the polls?
This rise began in the spring of 2022, when the Conservatives and Liberals were closely matched and continued until early this year, when they had established a 24-point lead prior to Trudeau's resignation and a subsequent surge in support for the Liberals.
They must have known that, at some point, the relentless attacks against a "wacko" prime minister who "broke Canada" would succeed. That Canadians would grow weary of him. And he'd lose his caucus' support.
They must have known that the carbon tax election they demanded would become irrelevant if the Liberals changed their stance.
They must have known that a potential Donald Trump return to the White House could threaten Canada's economy with a stronger mandate to put America first.
And they must have known that Mark Carney was organizing to replace Prime Minister Justin Trudeau at a moment's notice.
The resignation of finance minister and deputy prime minister Chrystia Freeland, followed by the prime minister's, and the Liberals' pivot on carbon pricing were perhaps unknown unknowns. But the potential for a perfect storm triggered by Trump 2.0 must have been foreseen.
In business, these scenarios are usually gamed out with plans to handle various outcomes.
However, planning for a new narrative for the Conservative party seems to be occurring rather late. According to CBC reporting, the party has commissioned surveys to help adapt Poilievre's message to the new reality. This includes testing new lines of attack against the Liberals. Meanwhile, several Conservatives believe much money has been invested in Axe the Tax, with little time left to change course.
In my line of work, this means we have been hit by external factors we don't control and did not plan for. We're conducting research to figure out a new strategy. And there are strong differences of opinion among the C-suite on the go-forward strategy. That is usually quite destabilizing and puts you in a competitively weak position.
Meanwhile, Poilievre has a fresh sign on the lectern about "Canada First." And Carney - not a career politician - would apparently lift the Liberals in the polls to a tie with the Conservatives, thereby erasing the Conservatives' massive lead - if you believe recent polls.
Back in 2022, Maclean's appropriately asked on its cover "Why Is Pierre Poilievre So Angry?" We've learned why over the past three years. And now, as he faces a quickly changing situation, he no doubt isn't any happier than he was when he started. That's a challenge for a man who's been on the attack his entire political life.
Canadians don't want an attack dog. They now want a guard dog.
And that's a space another conservative, Doug Ford, Ontario's self-proclaimed protector, is more than happy to occupy during a snap election.
And, while it's too soon to know for sure, Carney is unlikely to be anyone's lap dog.
This asks the question: Can the Conservatives teach their dog new tricks?
Perhaps. But there's little time. Furthermore, one has to wonder if Poilievre would want to, or be able to, walk away from a political style that has served him so well thus far - a political persona the party tried to soften with "meet Pierre the husband and father" ads coinciding with a new look.
He still attacks and opposes much more naturally than he charms. He now needs to prove he can inspire trust and confidence as Canadians face the consequences of a known known: Donald Trump is going to affect our lives for at least four more years.
Éric Blais is president of
Headspace Marketing Inc.
CREDIT: Éric Blais Contributor Éric Blais is president of Headspace Marketing Inc.;

Document 180 of 500

It's time for the Ontario Liberals and NDP to get over themselves and stop fighting for second place

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/time-ontario-liberals-ndp-get-over-themselves/docview/3167578075/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=It%27s+time+for+the+Ontario+Liberals+and+NDP+to+get+over+themselves+and+stop+fighting+for+second+place&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's the 2022 election all over again, except everything is a little bit worse. It's February, not June, and the United States is led by Donald Trump, not Joe Biden. Doug Ford has amassed more scandals but seems poised to win an even bigger majority simply by saying nothing (hot-mic gaffes notwithstanding). Meanwhile, the Liberals and NDP continue to split the vote, with little that's obvious to distinguish the two parties.
Once again this election, I'm volunteering with NotOneSeat.ca in an effort to defeat Ford through strategic voting and a temporarily united opposition. This time, however, our tone is less about sunshine and unity, and more about asking, "Why is the opposition being wilfully ignorant?"
Everyone is talking about vote-splitting, again. Neither the Liberals nor the NDP has chosen a feasible plan to deal with this perennial problem by swallowing their partisanship pride and fielding candidates strategically. Instead, both parties have opted to fight it out for second place. We've all seen this movie before, and the ending stinks.
Yet if the opposition parties were to announce that they intended to work as a united front against Ford, they would likely receive a massive boost in both positive PR and the polls.
Such was the case for Natasha Doyle-Merrick, the NDP candidate for Eglinton-Lawrence, who withdrew shortly before the deadline to avoid splitting the vote and delivering the riding to Ford's party. The Liberals lost in Eglinton-Lawrence by 524 votes in 2022, with the NDP finishing more than 12,000 votes behind them, having received fewer than 4,000 votes.
Doyle-Merrick's decision has been widely praised on social media, but instead of the NDP turning it into a positive story about putting people before politics, they have so far said nothing. Vince Gasparro, the Liberal candidate for Eglinton-Lawrence, praised the decision in a statement as "a testament to who she is." His response was much classier than the Liberal campaign's text that read, "even the other party recognizes this is a Bonnie versus Doug race." Not according to the polls, it isn't.
Everyone is for co-operation if it means their opponent will drop out, and certainly the Liberals threw the New Democrats under the strategic bus when they collapsed in 2018. The NDP may point to their constitution, which requires them to run a candidate in every riding, as if they could not collectively agree to do things differently this one time. The Liberals have no such excuse; instead, they'll likely fall back on assuming that disillusioned Progressive Conservative and jaded NDP supporters will eventually vote for them - even though the polls haven't changed very much since 2022.
Even a no-cost joint announcement from the opposition parties that they intend to work together rather than prop up a Ford minority government, with the details to be negotiated later, would be positive PR.
Still, not all candidates in the running would rather eat glass than work the other parties.
York-South Weston's NDP candidate, Faisal Hassan, has been refreshingly pragmatic about taking a co-operative approach. "I want results," he told me. "I want to work for the community and get results for them, and that means we can work with [the Liberals] to make things better for the people."
Similarly, the Liberal candidate for Beaches-East York, Mary-Margaret McMahon, sees a lot of value in co-operation. "Ontarians want to see us working together for the greater good," she says. "They want to see collaboration."
If only such voices were louder.
The Liberals and NDP need to stop kidding themselves that the average voter sees a meaningful difference between them. The stark contrast between Ford and everyone else mirrors what's going on in the U.S., where anyone seems a better option than Trump. Nobody wants a two-party system in Ontario, mind you, but anyone would be better than Ford.
When my sink leaks, what matters most is preventing further water damage, not working out the minor differences between the plumbers who can fix it. In Ontario, the house is flooded, and Ford keeps ripping out pipes to sell for scrap. After years under his leadership, what Ontario needs now is to fix health care, the housing crisis, our broken schools, child care and unemployment - and to cancel the many deals Ford has made that appear to enrich billionaires to nobody else's benefit.
The Tories hold a commanding lead in the polls, but after seven years of a Ford government, many of the problems we face have become much worse. The Liberals and NDP need to work together and figure out the details among themselves - and perhaps, while they're at it, figure out a better electoral system so that we never have to do this again.
CAPTION: ONTARIO LEADERS ELECTION COMPOSITE Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles. Uploaded by: jordan tamblyn
CREDIT: Sonal Champsee, Contributor

Full text: The Ontario Leaders' Debate will be held Monday night at 6:30 p.m. ahead of the provincial election on Feb. 27. Read our election promises tracker. See our election poll tracker. Follow our live updates Monday evening with the Star's Queen's Park bureau.
7:05 p.m Who would build housing on the Greenbelt?
None of the leaders. ... Stiles says "our farmland is not up for grabs."
- Kristin Rushowy
7:05 p.m. Stiles says Ford wants to put people in jail, she wants "to talk about how to prevent that" and "ensure people get their day in court."
- Kristin Rushowy
7:04 p.m. Opposition leaders all taking aim at Ford on court backlogs, which are leading to accused persons having their charges thrown out.
- Rob Ferguson
7:02 p.m. Interesting that the debate organizers keep giving extra time to Doug Ford to respond to attacks. It's normal for the incumbent to attract the most criticism; it's unusual for the incumbent to win extra time as a bonus.
- Martin Regg Cohn
7 p.m. Doug Ford blaming the federal Liberals for crime in Ontario while Bonnie Crombie reminds him he has been leading the province for seven years and things are getting worse.
- Robert Benzie
6:56 p.m. Just a matter of time until PC Leader Doug Ford went on the attack against Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie. We like to say that she lives rent-free in his head.
Interesting how the other party leaders are all posing questions to Doug Ford - giving the moderator a run for his money.
- Martin Regg Cohn
(Updated) 6:52 p.m. Another shot at Ford from Crombie: "You were promised a tax cut but never got it." A reference to Ford's unfulfilled 2018 promise for a middle-class income tax cut. Crombie has promised one.
"You didn't get it done, Doug, you didn't cut our taxes," Crombie says.
"I can't get a word in edgewise here," Ford protests in exchange with Crombie.
- Rob Ferguson
6:49 p.m. Mike Schreiner says "the rich insiders are getting richer" in Doug Ford's Ontario.
Marit Stiles promises rent controls and grocery rebates. Doug Ford touts the $200 cheques the Tories sent out last month just before he called the early election. - Robert Benzie
6:47 p.m. First zinger of the night... Ford touts hiring former federal health minister Dr. Jane Philpott to reform the system. Crombie shoots back "you had to hire a Liberal" to fix it. - Kristin Rushowy
6:46 p.m. Inevitable and predictable that NDP Leader Marit Stiles would pin hallway health care on a previous Liberal government, from 7 years ago. - Martin Regg Cohn
6:42 p.m. Ford now mentioning his primary care plan, which is actually $1.8 billion over four years. And for 2 million people, which is different from the OMA number that 2.5 million don't have a family doctor. - Rob Ferguson
6:41 p.m. Tough early round for Doug Ford, asking about health care. Progressive Conservative strategists will tell you privately, when we're talking about health care, we're losing.
Not that they're losing the election, but they'd rather talk about something else - like Donald Trump and the economy. Hence Ford's quick pivot to a healthy economy underpinning health care. - Martin Regg Cohn
(Updated) 6:39 p.m. Family doctor promises
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie promises to get everyone in Ontario a family doctor within four years. Right now, about 2.5 million people don't have one, according to the Ontario Medical Association.
Mike Schreiner, the Green Leader, also promises doctors for all within four years.
PC Leader Doug Ford says his government has attracted thousands of doctors and nurses already, but does not mention $2 billion plan to connect all those who do not have one to a doctor within four years.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles cites stats like long waits in ERs and thousands of patients turned loose from a Sault Ste. Marie health clinic last year, saying Ontario cannot afford to go on like this. - Robert Benzie and Rob Ferguson
6:37 p.m.More time to respond to name checks
CBC host David Common tells viewers that if any of the candidates name checks another, that person gets 30 seconds to respond. Idea is to keep candidates speaking about their own priorities and platforms. - Kristin Rushowy

Crombie: cashier at Woolco
Stiles: babysitting, swimming instructor
Ford:delivering Globe & Mail as a kid, Canada Packers
Schreiner: feeding cattle/driving combine on family farm, mowing lawns

6:34 p.m. Bonnie Crombie says "after seven years 2.5 million people don't have a family doctor" and 1 million are using food banks. She says Ontario isn't recognizable.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner says Ford has "abandoned his job." Schreiner blasts Ford for the Ontario Place spa, the 401 tunnel scheme and ripping up bike lanes. - Robert Benzie
6:32 p.m. The debate is underway
Doug Ford says the election he triggered early is all about who can best "protect Ontario," which is the Progressive Conservatives' campaign slogan.
Marit Stiles says she will be "on your side" fighting rising costs. "On Your Side" is the New Democrats' slogan. And the Liberal slogan ... "More for you." - Robert Benzie and Kristin Rushowy
6:31 p.m. I love a debate, it's core to a democratic election, but I always remind my media friends: It's not a boxing match - it's a sales pitch, where politicians need to connect with voters. Stay tuned!
- Martin Regg Cohn
6:29 p.m. Hunkered down at home in front of the television, putting myself in the shoes of ordinary voters like me who are watching the debate from their couch - instead of the spin room or rarefied newsroom where there's too much electricity in the air! - Political Columnist Martin Regg Cohn
6:26 p.m. Kristin Rushowy from the Star's Queen's Park Bureau is at CBC headquarters, Queen's Park Bureau Chief Robert Benzie is covering from Queen's Park.
6:23 p.m. Ontario party leaders have been given the terms of the debate and are getting ready to begin, posing for photos at CBC headquarters' Front St. studio. All four leaders are expected to scrum when the debate concludes at 8 p.m. - Kris Rushowy
What to expect at the leaders' debate tonight
It is the main event of the Feb. 27 Ontario election campaign.
The four major party leaders square off on Monday night at CBC's Toronto Broadcast Centre for a 90-minute live televised debate.
It's the second and final time Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford, NDP Leader Marit Stiles, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and Green Leader Mike Schreiner will gather on the same stage during this campaign.
The four met Friday in North Bay for a midday debate on northern issues.
Read the full story from the Star's Queen's Park bureau
What time is the debate happening and who will participate?
The live debate will be held from 6:30 p.m. to 8 p.m.
The debate will feature Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, PC Leader Doug Ford, Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner and NDP Leader Marit Stiles.
Who will host the debate?
The debate will be moderated by CBC host David Common, CTV News Queen's Park Bureau Chief Siobhan Morris and Global Bureau Chief and President of the Queen's Park Press Gallery Colin D'Mello.
The Ontario Leaders' Debate is open to leaders of all parties that currently have an elected MPP sitting in the legislature.
Where can I watch the debate?
It will air on all partner television platforms. It will also be simulcast on various radio stations across the province and streamed online.
Poll suggests race is tightening, but Doug Ford still well ahead
Doug Ford remains well ahead, but the Feb. 27 Ontario election race is tightening somewhat as Bonnie Crombie's Liberals gain ground, a new poll suggests.
The Abacus Data survey for the Star found Ford's Progressive Conservatives at 41 per cent support, ahead of Crombie's Liberals at 28 per cent, Marit Stiles's New Democrats at 21 per cent and Mike Schreiner's Greens at six per cent.
That's a five percentage point drop for the Tories and a four percentage point increase for the Liberals since the last Abacus survey released Feb. 7. The NDP remained the same and the Greens went up one percentage point.
Read the full story from the Star's Robert Benzie
Inside Doug Ford's trip to Washington - and how he was received by Americans
In politics, as in war, symbolism matters.
So with Canada on the brink of a trade war with the United States, it was symbolic that the nation's provincial and territorial leaders secured a meeting in the White House.
Yes, it was at the last minute - and scheduled thanks to an $85,000-a-month lobbying firm retained by the Council of the Federation - and it was only with Jim Blair, one of President Donald Trump's deputy chiefs of staff, and White House personnel director Sergio Gor.
Read the full story from the Star's Robert Benzie
Rivals slam Doug Ford on health care, death penalty as he insists he's the best choice to take on Donald Trump
Bonnie Crombie tried to distance herself from previous Liberal governments in the first leaders' debate of the Feb. 27 election campaign, prompting Doug Ford to growl "what a joke."
In a testy exchange, the Liberal leader said she disagrees with some moves by Liberal premiers Dalton McGuinty and Kathleen Wynne during the 15 years they were in power - a span that ended with a 2018 win by Ford's Progressive Conservatives.
"As you know, I'm a new Liberal leader and I'm not taking responsibility for decisions made previously," Crombie said during a 90-minute debate on northern issues Friday at Canadore College in North Bay.
Read the full story from the Star's Queen's Park bureau
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie stands by candidate who made light of intimate partner violence
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie is standing by a candidate despite his social media posts making light of intimate partner violence in the wake for former CBC host Jian Ghomeshi's legal troubles.
"These are tweets from over a decade ago, and the candidate has apologized," Liberal campaign spokeswoman Bahoz Dara Aziz said Friday in regard to Brian Hamilton, running in Thunder Bay-Superior North.
Hamilton, a Thunder Bay councillor and coffee shop owner hoping to wrest the riding from the NDP's Lise Vaugeois in the Feb. 27 election, said he is a different person now.
Read the full story from the Star's Rob Ferguson
Ontario NDP candidate in Eglinton-Lawrence folds campaign to help Liberal opponent win
The NDP hopeful in Eglinton-Lawrence says she withdrew her candidacy to avoid a vote-split that could help Ontario's Progressive Conservatives keep the seat.
In a post on X Thursday evening, Natasha Doyle-Merrick said: "I recognize the race in Eglinton-Lawrence is a clear two-party contest between Liberals and Conservatives. To prevent a Conservative win and more years of neglect, I'm stepping aside to avoid a vote split," on the political left.
In an attached statement, the mental health worker said she made the difficult decision after much reflection, adding that she first sought the NDP nomination because she felt Robin Martin, the riding's PC MPP since 2018, had ignored some voices in the community.
Read the full story from the Star's David Rider

Ontario is heading to the polls Feb. 27 and we're tracking what each party is promising ahead of the Ontario election.
All four provincial party leaders have now put forth plans to support Ontario through U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff plans along with pledges to improve affordability and more.
Here's what else the Progressive Conservatives, NDP, Liberals and Greens have pledged since the start of the campaign.

I fact checked everything Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said for a week in the Ontario election. Here's how many false claims she made
Hammering lawn signs into frozen soil. Touring the province in giant buses. Hot mic moments. Attack ads.
The 2025 Ontario cold snap election is well underway and that means speeches and promises - but also embellishments, stretches and at times, falsehoods.
The Star's fact-check project is back and every week until election day on Feb. 27, Star reporters will monitor, track, record and correct (where needed) the three leading provincial party leaders on the campaign trail.
Read the full story from the Star's Mahdis Habibinia
Doug Ford broke ethics rules with campaign ad, opponents charge
Doug Ford violated ethics rules against using government activities for partisan purposes in an online ad about his trip to Washington to lobby against Trump tariffs, opposition parties charge.
The ad on the Progressive Conservative leader's X feed Thursday pictured him walking past the U.S. Capitol. It used the party's "Protect Ontario" logo and slogan from his bid for a third term as premier in a snap election he called for Feb. 27.
The ad was quickly taken off social media and reposted a few hours later with no Conservative logo or slogans.
Read the full story from the Star's Rob Ferguson and Kris Rushowy
Recording reveals Doug Ford calling for return of death penalty in campaign speech: 'Send 'em right to sparky'
Doug Ford took his tough-on-crime talk to new heights in a campaign speech where he pushed for a return to capital punishment, the Star has learned.
Musing about stiffer penalties for home invaders who kill victims, the Progressive Conservative leader called for changes in the federal Criminal Code that would empower judges to "send 'em right to sparky."
The colloquial reference to the electric chair came two weeks ago on the first day of Ford's snap Feb. 27 Ontario election campaign, now at its midway point.
Read the full story from the Star's Robert Benzie and Rob Ferguson
Doug Ford denies his Washington trip is a taxpayer-funded campaign stop
As political rivals accuse him of electioneering on the taxpayer's dime, Doug Ford insists he can juggle governing and campaigning at the same time.
The Progressive Conservative leader, here to lobby American lawmakers and officials against U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs on Canada, rejected Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie's contention he is breaching tradition by going on a foreign mission during a campaign.
"I got cleared by the integrity commissioner, but that's neither here nor there," Ford said Tuesday, referring to the legislature's ethics watchdog.
Read the full story from the Star's Queen's Park Bureau
Doug Ford under attack in Super Bowl ads
Doug Ford's campaign fumble is in the Super Bowl spotlight.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie will be airing two different 30-second commercials during the NFL championship game Sunday night between the Kansas City Chiefs and the Philadelphia Eagles.
With Ontario voters headed to the polls on Feb. 27, Crombie's team wants viewers of what is expected to be the year's most-watched TV broadcast to see for themselves what Ford said last Monday.
Read the full story from the Star's Robert Benzie

Want to get a head start? Here's how.

Document 182 of 500

Letters: Doug Ford is still Ontario's best option, Feb. 14 Here's the latest as Canada faces tariffs from United States President Donald Trump, Feb. 3 Carney as leader would have Liberals tied with Conservatives, Feb. 11

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3167578059/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Doug+Ford+is+still+Ontario%27s+best+option%2C+Feb.+14+Here%27s+the+latest+as+Canada+faces+tariffs+from+United+States+President+Donald+Trump%2C+Feb.+3+Carney+as+leader+would+have+Liberals+tied+with+Conservatives%2C+Feb.+11&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Voters forgetting
Ford's failures
Doug Ford is still Ontario's best option, Feb. 14
Well, it seems clear from election polls Premier Doug Ford will be re-elected in a landslide, even though most Ontarians were not happy with the early election.
He's done a masterful job. The hat slogan "Canada is not for sale," $200 cheques before an election and mentioning possibly 500,000 lost jobs likely all contribute to his strong poll numbers. He has rushed to Washington to plead Ontario's case regarding tariffs, with seemingly little success as the U.S. has just announced a 25 per cent tariff on our steel and aluminum. He's promised billions for infrastructure and a tunnel under Hwy. 401, but has completely forgotten to support our health-care system or end the long wait times in ERs.
A four-year mandate to fight tariffs seems to be in the cards. Voters are forgetting his health-care and housing failures, feeling he'll fight for them even though it's the federal government that has the power to counter the U.S.'s tariff attack.
Peter J. Middlemore Sr., Windsor
Better election coverage needed
The other night, I braved the weather and walked 20 minutes to an all-candidates debate for our upcoming provincial election, only to learn that my Conservative candidate could not be bothered to show up. Is the Ford government so confident that they don't feel they have to listen to concerns or garner any further support?
Of course, when I read the Star these days it is hard to even find mention of other parties running. If they are mentioned it is only in response to something Ford has said or done. I expect better coverage, as I expect better representation from my member of the legislature.
Kathy Garrison, Toronto
Ford is putting America first
I have just seen Premier Doug Ford's latest campaign ad, and I am livid. The premier's Washington visit was supposed to be about defending Canada's interests, but instead it appears Ford's main focus was creating sparkling footage for a campaign ad.
It's bad enough that the premiers emerged empty-handed from their meeting with mid-level White House staffers, but Ford's new campaign ad rubs salt in our collective wounds. In the voice-over for the ad, Ford repeatedly puts America first - "made in the USA" before "made in Canada," pride in the work of American workers before the work of Canadians, and the push for his Fortress Am-Can.
That's right, even the name of Ford's economic plan puts America first. Where's your hat, Doug? Did it blow off while you were busy bending the knee to Trump?
Carol Kroeger, Ottawa
Our leaders must play hardball with this bully
Here's the latest as Canada faces tariffs from United States President Donald Trump, Feb. 3
U.S. President Donald Trump is a hard-nosed egotistical businessman who loves to negotiate.
His implementation of tariffs and statement that Canada should become the 51st state are his opening bids; not where he plans to end up.
Trump knows that he could never invade Canada and absorb it into the U.S., but he could weaken our economy to the point where Canada is reduced to a vassal state. And how do our politicians respond to all this? They run down to Florida or Washington and, on bended knee, beg for mercy. This tactic is playing right into Trump's hands.
Our leaders must start playing hardball with this bully. They should tell Trump that if he continues to destroy a very successful and decades-old trading relationship, Canadians will be forced to develop new partnerships. There is a large country on the other side of the Pacific that not only has four times the population of the U.S; but also a growing economy almost as large. It is populated with intelligent, hard-working people who value education. Canada already has a well-established economic relationship with China and will work to expand it.
Bob Bryant, Fenelon Falls, Ont.
Trade alliance exclude the U.S.
If U.S. President Donald Trump and his inner circle of billionaires and sycophants insist on moving forward with their isolationist global agenda, perhaps we should help them. If an alliance of nations, including Canada, the U.K., the EU, Mexico (the list goes on) create a trading bloc excluding the U.S., we'll soon see how self-sufficient the U.S. really is.
I don't wish hardship on the millions of Americans who reject President Donald Trump's agenda, but unfortunately, they may have to become short-term collateral damage until the MAGA crowd backs down or is out of office.
Michael Bines, Toronto
Who exactly is Mark Carney?
Carney as leader would have Liberals tied with Conservatives, Feb. 11
The Canadian established media are painting Mark Carney as the "saviour of Canada" and heir apparent to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau. They say Carney can stand up to the "American bully," Trump. They say Carney will reverse Trudeau's pernicious policies and rebuild our country. But who, exactly, is Mark Carney?
For decades, Carney has been an agent of globalism, the movement that promotes transnational bureaucracy and undermines national independence. Is this the man who will keep Canada free? He is a founder and co-chair of the Glasgow Financial Alliance for Net Zero, an organization committed to decarbonizing the economy. Is this the man who will build the Canadian economy?
Since 2020, he has served as a senior adviser to Trudeau. Carney supported and praised Trudeau's Carbon Tax (until he didn't).
He applauded the Liberal government blocking $157 billion in energy investment (until he didn't).
He has shaped and supported the Liberal agenda (until he didn't). This is not the resumé of the man who will build a strong Canada and defeat the American bully.
Dave Gosse, Camrose, Alta.
Thank you all for navigating snow
A big shout out and thank you to our Toronto Star home delivery driver who delivered the newspaper and the garbage collection people who despite the difficult snow conditions collected our waste. To our neighbours who shovelled the sidewalks in front of their homes, thank you!
Kathryn Boire, Toronto

Document 183 of 500

Voters are forgetting Ford's health care and housing failures

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/voters-are-forgetting-fords-health-care-housing/docview/3167577948/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Voters+are+forgetting+Ford%27s+health+care+and+housing+failures&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford is still Ontario's best option, Feb. 14
Well, it seems clear from election polls Premier Doug Ford will be re-elected in a landslide even though most Ontarians were not happy with the early election. He's done a masterful job. The hat slogan "Canada is not for sale," $200 cheques before an election and mentioning possibly 500,000 lost jobs likely all contribute to his strong poll numbers. He has rushed to Washington to plead Ontario's case regarding tariffs with seemingly little success as the U.S. has just announced a 25 per cent tariff on our steel and aluminum. He's promised billions for infrastructures and a tunnel under Hwy. 401 but has completely forgotten to support our health-care system, end the long wait times in ERs. A four-year mandate to fight tariffs seems to be in the cards. Voters are forgetting his health care and housing failures, feeling he'll fight for them even though it's the federal government that has the power to counter the U.S.A.'s tariff attack.
Peter J. Middlemore Sr., Windsor
Better election coverage is needed
The other night, I braved the weather and walked 20 minutes to an all candidates debate for our upcoming provincial election only to learn that my Conservative candidate could not be bothered to show up. Is the Ford government so confident that they don't feel they have to listen to concerns or garner any further support? Of course, when I read the Star these days it is hard to even find mention of other parties running. If they are mentioned it is only in response to something Ford has said or done. I expect better coverage as I expect better representation from my member of the legislature.
Kathy Garrison, Toronto
Why is Ford putting America before Canada?
I have just seen Premier Doug Ford's latest campaign ad, and I am livid. The Premier's Washington, D.C. visit was supposed to be about defending Canada's interests, but instead it appears Ford's main focus was creating sparkling footage for a campaign ad. It's bad enough that the Premiers emerged empty-handed from their meeting with mid-level White House staffers, but Ford's new campaign ad rubs salt in our collective wounds. In the voice-over for the ad, Ford repeatedly puts America first - "made in the USA" before "made in Canada," pride in the work of American workers before the work of Canadians, and the push for his Fortress Am-Can. Kathryn Boire, Toronto
Thankful to have witnessed Paul Morris in actionMy memories of Paul Morris, the iconic voice of Maple Leaf Gardens, Feb. 11
I was sad to hear of the passing of former Leaf P.A. announcer Paul Morris. I would go to Leaf games as a kid with my father every Saturday night in the '60s and his voice that would resonate throughout Maple Leaf Gardens was a major part of that experience. It was a special time. A time when the fans went to actually watch the game and not to constantly be on their phones or talk amongst themselves rather than pay attention to the play. Paul had a delivery that will never be equalled. He just did his job and didn't take the cheesy, corny approach that most of the other P.A. announcers do in the game today. I am thankful I was a witness to Paul's great work from the '60s, right up to the late '90s. There will never be another one like him. Rest in peace buddy.
Dave Watson, Pickering
CAPTION: Ontario Progressive Conservative leader Doug Ford speaks to reporters, accompanied by other Council of the Federation members, at the Mayflower Hotel in Washington, D.C.

Document 184 of 500

What do experts, data say about Ford's economy?: Not everyone agrees premier has made all the right moves

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/what-do-experts-data-say-about-fords-economy/docview/3167577940/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=What+do+experts%2C+data+say+about+Ford%27s+economy%3F%3A+Not+everyone+agrees+premier+has+made+all+the+right+moves&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Today, the Star looks at how the economy has grown and how to create good jobs, an issue selected by 26 per cent of respondents.
Doug Ford often touts his track record of creating good jobs and powering the provincial economy. Ontarians struggling with high interest rates and unemployment might be skeptical.
Many economists say Canada is on the verge of recession with U.S. President Donald Trump's punitive tariffs threatening to hobble an already weak economy.
Yet, three years ago when the Ford government was re-elected, the economy was on fire.
National jobless levels in the summer of 2022 were at record lows while inflation ran rampant, peaking at eight per cent (it's now at 1.8 per cent as of December.)
Back then, Ford vowed to improve employment opportunities and affordability for Ontarians.
And while there's only so much provincial governments can do in response to national and global macroeconomic trends, the question of whether Ford helped the Ontario economy during a challenging post-pandemic recovery is still valid.
Some economists and public policy experts believe the province could have better spent its resources to support the prosperity of Ontarians.
Don Drummond, an economist and professor at Queen's University, warned against Ontario's high levels of debt relative to economic growth. "We desperately need to improve the competitive position of the Ontario and the Canadian economies," said Drummond, "all the more so if we're not going to have a special trade arrangement with the United States."
Growing the economy
The Star reached out to the Ministry of Economic Development, Job Creation and Trade for comment, but did not receive a response.
Like other provincial economies, Ontario's has been slowing since the Bank of Canada began raising interest rates in March 2022 in a bid to cool inflation.
Ontario's real gross domestic product (GDP) growth - the broadest metric of economic activity - came in slightly ahead national growth rates between 2018 and 2023, according to data from the Ontario Ministry of Finance.
That, however, doesn't mean Ontarians have been doing better than the rest of Canadians on an individual level.
That's because real GDP growth does not account for the population boom Ontario saw in recent years - a trend that has been largely attributed to federal policies around immigration.
Simply put, more people working in the economy means it naturally produces more as a whole.
In fact, provincial population growth has surpassed economic expansion, creating a feeling of "stagnation," according Brian Lewis, a fellow at the University of Toronto's Munk School of Global Affairs and Public Policy and former chief economist at the Ontario Ministry of Finance.
"The numbers look good," said Lewis, "but not when you take into account how many people we've added. The per capita numbers are all poor."
Real GDP per capita - the level of economic production per person - has continued to decline relative to pre-pandemic levels, according to the Ontario Chamber of Commerce (OCC).
"In other words, each Ontarian, on average, is producing and earning less after accounting for inflationary effects which contributes to lower living standards and overall economic well-being," the chamber wrote in its annual Ontario Economic Report, which described the overall state of the economy as fragile.
Going forward, the OCC believes the province should focus on improving business invest- ment in things like software and machinery, as well as reducing interprovincial trade barriers, both of which, in theory, could boost economic output per Ontarian and individual financial well-being.
Creating good jobs
Ontario saw a remarkable number of jobs - more than 400,000 positions - created since Ford's re-election.
And wages continued to grow as they catch up with post-pandemic inflation and job vacancies, according to the Financial Accountability Office of Ontario.
But the unemployment rate - the number of unemployed people expressed as a percentage of the labour force - has been creeping up, reaching 7.6 per cent in Ontario last January from 5.1 per cent in June 2022.
"The employment record is not bad," said Drummond. "Focusing on the unemployment rate is deceiving because it just reflects this extraordinary, unprecedented growth in the population," he added.
Meanwhile, as the federal government works to curb immigration, Ontario could see labour shortages worsen in the future, according to the OCC.
Ultimately, Drummond thinks policies such as alcohol liberalization or delivering $200 tax rebate cheques didn't help or hinder economic growth and job creation in the province.
"The great shame from my perspective is, they wasted valuable public resources that could have been used to help it."
CREDIT: Ana Pereira Toronto Star

Subject: Gross Domestic Product--GDP; Interest rates; Unemployment; Economic growth; Job creation; Economic conditions; Pandemics; Immigration; Public policy

Title: What do experts, data say about Ford's economy?:   Not everyone agrees premier has made all the right moves

Document 185 of 500

Crombie, Stiles hard-pressed to gain much ground on Ford

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/crombie-stiles-hard-pressed-gain-much-ground-on/docview/3167577820/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Crombie%2C+Stiles+hard-pressed+to+gain+much+ground+on+Ford&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In all the years I have been conducting public opinion research, I have never seen the mood of the Ontario electorate as bleak and negative as it is today.
Concerns about affordability that have dominated the public opinion agenda for two years persist, leaving vast swaths of the population feeling like they are treading water or falling behind. Concerns about a deteriorating health system have eroded what was once a source of pride and viewed as a hallmark differentiator between Canada and other nations. Added to these are anxieties about other serious issues, including global conflict and threats from the U.S.
Normally, this type of mood and sentiments are very bad news for a governing party, because it means they are running against the most formidable opponent an incumbent can face in electoral politics - "time for a change."
Virtually all polls published in the second week of the Ontario election campaign would indicate that Doug Ford has - at least for the time being - been able to avoid this confrontation. He and his team have been able to accomplish this by a combination of clever (some might say cynical and opportunist) strategy and luck.
By choosing to call an early election, before the federal parties go to the polls, he has been able to ride the tail winds on a very unpopular Liberal brand - the same one that also puts Pierre Poilievre 20 points ahead in some national polls.
Even more fortuitously, Donald Trump's chaotic behaviour and threats have left Ontarians feeling even more vulnerable to forces beyond their control.
The latter has not only allowed Ford to run against an opponent who is more unpopular and isn't in the race - Trump - it has also let him put masculinity on the ballot.
Indeed, as we have seen in the States and throughout the western world, and as pollsters are finding more and more, a sense of vulnerability and massive uncertainty has produced a voter demand for strong-man protection and authoritative solutions - without being too flip about what is clearly a serious and potentially dangerous scenario, voters seem to be looking for "Dad." Whether or not it is warranted, Ford - more than Stiles or Crombie - projects an image of strength to voters.
With a lead of more than 20 points, Ford and his PCs do not have to attract a single new vote to guarantee a massive electoral victory in two weeks - he just needs to make sure they don't lose a meaningful portion of their current base to the Liberals and NDP.
However, a big part of Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and NDP Leader Marit Stiles' dilemma is that, before they have any chance of attracting current PC supporters, they have to get Ontarians to know who they are.
With almost one half of the population not having any feelings, one way or another, about Stiles and over one third expressing the same ambivalence toward Crombie, why would Ford raise their profiles by even mentioning them, let alone engaging in any serious debates that also might cast a light on issues where he does not fare as well? Better to put on his "Dad" hat and fight Donald Trump by going to Washington or from his chair as head of the Council of the Federation - and ignore Crombie and Stiles.
And this is where the dilemma for the Liberals and NDP has the potential to enter the realm of moral hazard.
Even if Crombie and Stiles could raise their profiles and approval ratings before the Feb. 27 election day, and even if they could find a way to exploit Ford's vulnerabilities in this short period of time, the fact is, current PC voters aren't particularly inclined to switch their vote to either the Liberals or the NDP.
But Liberal voters are very likely to consider the NDP as their second choice and NDP voters are equally likely to choose the Liberals as their second choice. In other words, it is much easier - and more likely - for both opposition parties to make gains at the other's expense than it is for them to erode the PC base in a way that could change the outcome of the election.
And both opposition camps are acutely aware of this. If there is no movement in the polls in the next week, their focus could very well turn to one another in an attempt to win the second prize of Official Opposition.
Should Ford win this election ... and should Pierre Poilievre repeat in spring or early summer, it would not surprise me at all to see a serious conversation take place between the Liberals and NDP about confronting this reality by merging.
Allan Gregg is an opinion researcher, a principal at Earnscliffe Strategies and the co-host of the Red-Tory podcast.
CAPTION: With almost half the population not having any feelings about NDP Leader Marit Stiles, right, and over one third expressing the same ambivalence toward Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, left, why would PC Leader Doug Ford raise their profiles by even mentioning them, writes Allan Gregg.THE CANADIAN PRESS photos
CREDIT: Allan Gregg Contributor Allan Gregg is an opinion researcher, a principal at Earnscliffe Strategies and the co-host of the Red-Tory podcast.;

Subject: Public opinion; Political campaigns; Voters

People: Trump, Donald J; Poilievre, Pierre

Title: Crombie, Stiles hard-pressed to gain much ground on Ford

Document 186 of 500

Is this election being overshadowed?: Voters likely paying attention to Trump, Gaza or other news

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/is-this-election-being-overshadowed/docview/3167577770/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+this+election+being+overshadowed%3F%3A+Voters+likely+paying+attention+to+Trump%2C+Gaza+or+other+news&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario voters clueing in to the fact they are in the middle of a quick, unexpected election could be forgiven for thinking their choices on the Feb. 27 provincial ballot will be Donald Trump, tariffs or Mark Carney.
Political observers and some door-knocking candidates say that international and national headlines blasting Ontarians like a fire hose, stoking dire financial fears and debate over who best to lead Canada, are almost erasing the provincial election - likely to the benefit of the Progressive Conservatives.
"I think that was what (Conservative Leader) Doug Ford was gambling on when he called an early vote," said Prof. Lydia Miljan, head of political science at the University of Windsor, in an interview.
"He's seizing the uncertainty of the moment while (U.S. President) Donald Trump sucks up all the political oxygen with a continual lurching of, 'Here's a tariff, delay the tariff, maybe double the tariff' - it's exhausting," she added.
"If it wasn't for the few Progressive Conservative signs I am seeing around, I would have forgotten that there is a provincial race going on at all because we are talking so much about the federal Liberal leadership race and Trump and all this tariff noise."
John Campbell, a former city councillor running for the Liberals against PC incumbent Kinga Surma in Etobicoke Centre, said voters answering the door during this rare mid-winter campaign seem unhappy about a lot of things, including Ford calling the election 15 months ahead of schedule and his leadership in general. But many also seem apathetic about forcing change.
"I think, by and large, people are just really disappointed with the whole news cycle," said Campbell, who is also a former Toronto District School Board chair.
"You've got Gaza, you've got Trump, you've got Ukraine, you got a mishmash in Ottawa - all of the leadership stuff there. You've got people in power here who nobody really seems to like that much but there's not a whole lot of hope in the air, and people are just kind of throwing their hands up and saying, 'What a mess it is.' "
Campbell said his pitch to shake off voter apathy is that Ford is only pretending to be a fiscal conservative, spending $612 million to get alcohol into corner stores before the election, and $3 billion to send every Ontarian a $200 cheque during the campaign, as the underfunding of health care, colleges and universities and public schools are degrading Ontarians' quality of life.
"Most people are mixed on Doug Ford or not very happy," Campbell said.
"I just hope this election gets some momentum soon."
Ford launched the campaign on Jan. 29 saying he needs a "clear mandate" from Ontarians to fight Trump and his threatened 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian-made goods being sold in the U.S. that would severely damage the province's economy. "He wants to undermine us in any way he can," Ford said, days before being caught saying he cheered Trump's win after a campaign in which the Republican leader promised tariffs.
While Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and NDP Leader Marit Stiles campaigned in the cold and snow, Ford was in the international spotlight, making headlines along with other premiers and territorial leaders during a mission to Washington to fight tariffs.
They warned U.S. lawmakers that Trump's levies would ravage the economies of both nations in meetings that included a stop at the White House to chat with two Trump officials - but not the president.
Ontario's integrity commissioner approved Ford's midelection trip provided his activities weren't for partisan purposes. Ford's social media accounts posted a highlight reel of the trip that includes his party's logo and campaign slogan of "Protect Ontario," prompting Stiles to say she will ask for an integrity investigation of the trip. If that happens, any finding will come well after the Feb. 27 vote.
Miljan said provincial leaders' debates should give Crombie and Stiles the chance to remind voters who they are and what they stand for, and to put Ford on the hot seat. The debates, however, are unlikely to galvanize voters or drown out competing news events.
"Debates don't really matter that much - they're great for pundits and media to have something to focus on, and will be helpful to introduce Crombie and Stiles to the electorate, but Doug Ford's a known quantity and he's playing the 'Captain Canada' card," she said.
"You've also got federal Liberal leadership debates coming up," she noted, between five candidates including Carney, former governor of the Bank of Canada and then Bank of England, and Chrystia Freeland, MP and former deputy prime minister.
Those debates could potentially cause further voter confusion.
In Oshawa, New Democrat Jennifer French takes a break from door-knocking in her bid for a third term in office, knowing she'll soon be heading back out into an evening snowstorm.
"I thought initially it would be too cold for conversations at the door because people are surrounded by political stories at all levels, but they've actually been really engaging at the door, they know there is a provincial election on," French said.
Existential fears over Trump's tariffs is a bit of déjà vu for Oshawa, she said, since the community in 2018 faced news of the coming closure of the local General Motors plant, and the loss of almost 3,000 jobs. A campaign against that closure ended with the production line being reopened and people rehired to make pickup trucks.
"Everyone is looking for strong, capable leadership," French said.
"While Canada might not be for sale, this has been a unifying threat from Trump," she added.
"I don't have to lead people to think about the ways that the Ford government has pushed privatization," she said.
"There are so many layers of politics happening all at once. When I was door-knocking in a building filled with seniors, who are at home and watching the news, there is a lot of anxiety and uncertainty with the volatility in the States, there is anticipation of a possible federal election," French said.
"The seniors are worried about costs going up, about their loved ones, about themselves," she said.
"The conversation about provincial health care might not be what they lead with but it's the second point they raise: 'Have you seen Trump? OK. Well, what are you going to do to help seniors and make sure they stay a priority? It becomes very community-based very quickly."
The Progressive Conservative campaign did not respond to the Star's requests for comment.
CAPTION: Clockwise from top left Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford, NDP Leader Marit Stiles and Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner are the choices on the Feb. 27 provincial election ballot. Political observers and some candidates say international and national headlines are almost erasing the provincial election.The Canadian press photos

Document 187 of 500

Tesla sales are down - but it's not due to Elon Musk

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/tesla-sales-are-down-not-due-elon-musk/docview/3167352723/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tesla+sales+are+down+-+but+it%27s+not+due+to+Elon+Musk&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Tesla sales in Canada plunged over the last month, but it wasn't because electric vehicle buyers are recoiling from the brand founded by Elon Musk.
While loud voices on social media seem to indicate that consumers are turning away from Tesla in protest after Musk took up a prominent role in U.S. President Donald Trump's new administration, which has aggressively targeted both climate action and Canada, the numbers do not reflect this.
Nationwide sales of Teslas were down 70 per cent between December 2024 and January 2025. Meanwhile, sales of all zero-emission vehicles (ZEVs), which include both battery-powered and plug-in hybrids, were down even more - 79 per cent, according to S&P Global, which provided the Star with a preview of ZEV sales numbers that have not yet been made public.
Experts say the dramatic drop is due to the end of government subsidy programs and a hike in EV prices, which has made EVs as much as $21,000 more expensive than they were only seven weeks ago.
"Consumers in Canada are price sensitive and with these changes to the federal and provincial rebates, it's negatively impacting what vehicles customers are looking to buy," said James Hearn, S&P Global Mobility's associate director of market reporting.
Unlike politics, the rise in upfront cost has hit all makes of ZEVs. Sales of Chevrolet ZEVs were down 65 per cent and Hyundai ZEVs were down 30 per cent month-over-month, the data show.
The federal $5,000 EV rebate ran out of money in January and Quebec, where nearly half of all ZEVs are sold, ended its $7,000 rebate at the end of last year.
"We're seeing extreme market disruption right now," said Brian Kingston, president of the Canadian Vehicle Manufacturers' Association. "The incentives have a huge influence on the market and their abrupt stoppage means that sales of EVs have effectively stalled."
Daniel Breton, president of Electric Mobility Canada, a group that advocates for EVs, said the Quebec market dominates in Canada and what happens there has ripple effects across the country.
The future of government EV rebates is in doubt, he said, with some parties pledging to renew the federal program and Quebec promising a less generous $4,000 rebate that will become available April 1.
"A lot of people were pushing to get their vehicles before the end of the year," Breton said. "Now they're postponing their purchases till April."
The drop in sales between December and January may have been big, but it wasn't enough to buck the larger trend of burgeoning growth in the EV sector. Sales grew so much in 2024 that January 2025 ZEV sales were still 27 per cent higher than they were a year prior.
Tesla sales, for example, rose more than 300 per cent in 2024, and remained about 30 per cent higher after the drop in January than they were the year before.
Globally, ZEV sales are growing rapidly, and while government incentives can create short-term swings in the local market, the overall upward trend is clear, Hearn said.
In 2018, when Doug Ford's government cancelled the EV subsidy in Ontario, sales dropped immediately and did not recover for three years. At the same time, however, sales grew so quickly in British Columbia and Quebec that overall sales of ZEVs in Canada continued to grow. (Discounting the pandemic lockdown year of 2020, when sales of all cars and trucks fell.)
Similarly, when the federal EV rebate was announced in 2019, sales tanked for several months until the subsidy became available, Breton said.
Tesla's sales numbers are still growing, but its control of the ZEV market has been in decline for some time - a trend that predates Musk's association with Trump.
Thanks largely to its early entrance into the EV market, Tesla has long held a dominant position, selling more battery electric vehicles than all other brands combined. But as new models entered the market, Tesla's market share has eroded.
According to S&P Global's numbers, as recently as the end of 2022, Tesla controlled more than 40 per cent of the ZEV market. But in the last two years, its share has eroded by more than half, sitting at only 17.5 per cent in the last quarter of 2024. (These numbers include sales of plug-in hybrids; Tesla commands a larger portion of the fully electric vehicle market.)
Tesla sales in Europe have not held up as well, and 2024 was the first year that the company reported a global annual decline in deliveries. Tesla sales in France were 63 per cent lower in January 2025 than they were the previous year. They dropped 59.5 per cent in Germany.
The company, which only produces EVs, has faced stiffer competition in Europe, where Chinese EVs face lower tariffs than they do in North America.
Meanwhile, other car manufacturers have announced dozens of new ZEV models, with many cheaper vehicles set to become available in the coming months.
This, more than politics or rebates, bodes well for the long-term future of EV sales, said Breton.
"The trend is still solid," he said. "It's going to be much easier for everyday Canadians to buy an electric car."
CAPTION: Tesla and SpaceX CEO Elon Musk, left, and U.S. President Donald Trump are seen during an executive order signing in the Oval Office at the White House on Feb. 11, 2025, in Washington, D.C.
CREDIT: Marco Chown Oved

Document 188 of 500

What's disturbing about Ford's gaffes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/whats-disturbing-about-fords-gaffes/docview/3167313100/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=What%27s+disturbing+about+Ford%27s+gaffes&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: On Thursday, the Star broke the news that Ontario Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford was recorded during a closed-door campaign speech at a London Police Services gala where he said home invaders who kill their victims ought to be sent "right to sparky," the electric chair. In other words: bring back the death penalty. His campaign was quick to clarify, dismissing the remarks as a "poor taste joke."
Ford has a habit of saying things in poor taste, just as he has a history of bad judgment - like supporting Donald Trump. Not that it's going to cost him much. On the day the PC leader was riffing on putting people to death, Philippe Fournier's electoral model at 338 Canada had him cruising to another majority government, with 45 per cent of the popular vote and 98 seats out of the legislature's 124.
Those stratospheric numbers come not even two weeks after Ford was caught on video saying he'd been "100 per cent happy" that Donald Trump won the U.S. election - before the president won and promptly stabbed Canada in the chest. They also come after Ford called a cynical, unnecessary, $189-million election in February.
Ford doesn't simply skate by after making what, for other politicians, would be clear gaffes. It's almost as if he's rewarded for them.
During his speech to gathered police, lawyers and the sorts of people who have money to spend attending a gala, Ford said, "You know, I was warned by my campaign 'do not go off script ... do not say anything controversial. You're on a campaign.' Well, that's just not who I am." And then he proved it.
The Star reports the room was uncomfortable with the "sparky" remarks that soon followed. But will Ontarians be so squeamish? After all, Ford is the same guy who said of people on the province's disability support program, "You know what I don't like? ... Healthy young people that are sitting on the couch watching 'The Flintstones,' they should be out there, they should be working." Yes, he said this, of those who are disabled.
This is the same guy who said of immigrants that they are "bringing problems from everywhere else in the world" to Ontario. Yes his campaign has said he was just venting or joking, but it's not such a leap to imagine him being a proponent of the death penalty.
The thing is, he's not alone. Nearly 60 per cent of Ontarians support the death penalty. And I'm willing to bet nearly just as many like Ford's fun-but-not-PC-uncle demeanour. He says what he thinks! He's a regular guy, like me! People like that sort of thing because when it comes to casting a ballot, they ask themselves of politicians, as pollster Allan Gregg recently told me, "Are they for me?" and "Are they like me?"
While the laptop class and progressives might be appalled by Ford's comments, when you log off and go out into the world, you'll find that what many will see as Ford's "gaffes" are, for others, their beliefs. You might disagree with them (I do) just as you might find them worse than uncouth and cruel (I do), but there they are, out in the wild, held by real people living real lives.
As premier, as a politician, Ford must at least restrain himself and his impulses a bit. But he is more of himself in public than most politicians, a tendency that is at once endearing in style as it is off putting for those who find his impulse for revenge and his credulous individualism disqualifying. He benefits at once from a plain-spoken relatability that resonates with millions of voters, and from a simplistic, id-driven world view - force the bums back to work, fry the criminals with ol' sparky, etc. - that, apparently, many Ontarians share with him.
No one is going to beat Ford by attacking him as insufficiently polished, nor will anyone beat him by running against his casual reflections on the death penalty or misunderstanding of social assistance programs.
We ought to call him out, yes. But if there's a takeaway from Ford's gaffes for his opponents, it's that like it or not, Ford reflects so much of the province he leads. What does that say about us?
David Moscrop is an Ottawa-based politics writer and author of "Too Dumb For Democracy? Why We Make Bad Political Decisions and How We Can Make Better Ones."
CREDIT: David Moscrop Contributor David Moscrop is an Ottawa-based politics writer and author of "Too Dumb For Democracy? Why We Make Bad Political Decisions and How We Can Make Better Ones.";

Document 189 of 500

Symbolic gesture or a photo op?: How Ford's hastily arranged trip to Washington was received by Americans - and by election rivals

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/symbolic-gesture-photo-op/docview/3167311533/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Symbolic+gesture+or+a+photo+op%3F%3A+How+Ford%27s+hastily+arranged+trip+to+Washington+was+received+by+Americans+-+and+by+election+rivals&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In politics, as in war, symbolism matters.
So with Canada on the brink of a trade war with the United States, it was symbolic that the nation's provincial and territorial leaders secured a meeting in the White House.
Yes, it was at the last minute - and scheduled thanks to an $85,000-a-month lobbying firm retained by the Council of the Federation - and it was only with Jim Blair, one of President Donald Trump's deputy chiefs of staff, and White House personnel director Sergio Gor.
And, yes, Blair later trolled British Columbia Premier David Eby for daring to explain that Trump's dream of annexing Canada was a "non-starter."
"To be clear, we never agreed that Canada would not be the 51st state. We only agreed to share Premier Eby's comments," the deputy chief said on social media.
But to Doug Ford, the hastily arranged one-hour confab with a pair of youthful Trump aides was worth spending two precious days of the Feb. 27 Ontario election campaign in the American capital lobbying against the threatened 25 per cent tariffs.
"We appreciate the Trump administration facilitating this - literally in the last minute. We're grateful," enthused a visibly relieved Ford, standing on the chilly sidewalk outside the White House with Quebec's François Legault and Saskatchewan's Scott Moe at his side.
"It was a very positive conversation. It just shows the relationship that we have, and the respect they have ... regarding Canada," he said.
"They said it numerous times how they respect Canada and really appreciate the friendship over the years."
For Ford, the get-together helped justify his controversial decision to call a snap winter election 15 months before the scheduled June 2026 vote. Insisting he needs a four-year mandate to "outlive and outlast" the mercurial president's administration, the Progressive Conservative leader hopes that rationale trumps more prosaic matters like the doctors' shortage or housing affordability.
It's why last Tuesday he made the first of his two mid-campaign trips to Washington for a speech to the United States Chamber of Commerce and a lobbying effort including all 13 provincial and territorial premiers.
On Thursday, Ford will return to the U.S. capital for the National Governors' Association winter conference where he hopes to persuade fellow subnational leaders of the perils of a trade war.
So far, Ford's advisers believe his gambit is paying off because he is dominating the campaign, appearing like a statesman hovering above domestic Ontario politics.
"It could have gone a lot worse," conceded one PC strategist, who had expressed concern about a White House visit lest Ford be humiliated. "But he handled it well and the other meetings were worth the trip," said the insider, speaking confidentially in order to discuss internal deliberations.
That was a reference to the 13 premiers' discussions with Republican and Democratic lawmakers.
The source admitted there is a risk to such missions in the middle of an election campaign.
"We are ahead in the polls and really didn't need to take the chance so we're glad it worked out."
While Ford's U.S. Chamber of Commerce speech received little American media attention - and many of the 120 in attendance, including former PC leader Tim Hudak, Goldy Hyder, president and CEO of the Business Council of Canada, and Ontario Chamber of Commerce president Daniel Tisch, had flown in for the event - his anti-tariff message resonated with some.
Neil Herrington, the U.S. chamber's senior vice-president for the Americas, said it needs to be hammered home stateside that Trump's levies would send inflation skyrocketing.
"For Americans filling up their car in the Midwest, in Rocky Mountain states, tariffs on Canadian oil would drive up the price of gasoline by as much as 75 cents a gallon," said Herrington, who hosted Ford's speech.
"This in turn, raises costs for all kinds of retail goods transported around our country. Tariffs would drive up the price of a new car - even an inexpensive car - by as much as $3,000," he said. "In a country already facing housing affordability challenges, tariffs would add $40 billion to the $150 billion of cement, lumber, metals, minerals and other materials that we import from Canada alone in support of our construction industry."
While many in Washington were receptive to what Ford had to say, Eric Miller, president of Rideau Potomac Strategy Group, stressed he's "not sure much could change Donald Trump's mind at this point."
"This is a case where, unfortunately, President Trump has to touch the hot stove before he realizes how valuable and integrated this relationship is and begins to take a different course," said Miller, who was at Ford's speech.
"At this point ... you're on this inexorable march toward tariffs. But it doesn't mean that once tariffs are imposed and pain is felt, and stock markets go down and prices go up, that the tariffs remain," he said.
One Republican lawmaker, speaking privately to the Star, praised Ford for saying out loud what many elected officials in Trump's party say about tariffs behind closed doors.
"Sometimes the president says things to deflect attention from what he's actually trying to achieve. He's a brilliant negotiator and him saying this stuff about Canada being a 51st state is part of a larger negotiation," said the lawmaker, apologetically emphasizing that Americans don't really want to annex Canada.
"We'll both get through this," the Republican said, acknowledging there will be some difficult trade conversations between Washington and Ottawa in the coming weeks, months and years.
While Ford was glowing after his first foray to Washington, his main political opponents in the upcoming election were questioning both the point and the ethical appropriateness of the mission.
"I understand it wasn't very successful," Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said Thursday in Elmvale.
"They didn't meet with any high-ranking officials, so I don't think it was very effective. I don't think the advocacy has been very effective," said Crombie, panning the effort as "a photo opportunity."
NDP Leader Marit Stiles formally complained to integrity commissioner J. David Wake, the legislature's ethics watchdog, saying Ford "blurred the lines" between governing and campaigning on the trip.
While the PC campaign chartered a private jet for political staff to go to Washington, Ontario government bureaucrats helped organize his Chamber of Commerce event.
In an embarrassing gaffe Thursday, the Tories posted and then deleted a social-media ad of Ford walking past the U.S. Capitol, interspersed with the party's "Protect Ontario" slogan.
"If that isn't a sure sign of guilt, I don't know what is," said Stiles.
"I don't think anyone's fooled."
Snafu aside, an ebullient Ford insisted his Washington trips are not just symbolic gestures.
"We're down here to listen to our U.S. colleagues," he said Thursday, pointing out that both nations are "stronger together."
"So we have to make sure that gets communicated up to President Trump."
With files from Kristin Rushowy and Rob Ferguson
CAPTION: Last Tuesday, Premier Doug Ford made the first of his two mid-campaign trips to Washington for a speech to the United States Chamber of Commerce and a lobbying effort by all 13 provincial and territorial premiers.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief With files from Kristin Rushowy and Rob Ferguson
