Document 190 of 500

A 'stain' on our child welfare system: Star story brings new calls for inquiry in Neveah's death

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/stain-on-our-child-welfare-system/docview/3167311434/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+%27stain%27+on+our+child+welfare+system%3A+Star+story+brings+new+calls+for+inquiry+in+Neveah%27s+death&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Child welfare advocates are renewing their calls for answers and accountability in the death of a child whose remains were found in a Rosedale dumpster, a case one expert called a "stain" on Ontario's child protection system.
The outcry follows new revelations published in the Toronto Star about the life and death of Neveah - a four-year-old child who spent much of her life in Ontario's child protection system - after gaining rare access to nearly 1,500 pages of never-before-released court and police records from a recent child welfare case involving her family.
The documents raise pressing questions about why the girl, who had complex needs and Autism Spectrum Disorder, was returned to a mother with a lengthy history of neglect just months before she died, and expose how seemingly no one involved in her care noticed she was missing for months, let alone dead.
The revelations are further proof the case must be examined at a public inquest to learn what went wrong and prevent other children from falling through the cracks, said Irwin Elman, who served as the province's child advocate from 2008 to 2019.
"It's very difficult to understand why this happened," Elman said, "which is why an inquest needs to happen."
Neveah's decomposed body was found wrapped in blankets by contractors in May 2022, but for more than a year her name evaded investigators, who eventually resorted to complex DNA technology to identify her. Police say she died sometime in 2021 and had never been reported missing. (A publication ban prevents the Star from fully identifying Neveah or her relatives).
Her death, categorized as suspicious, remains under investigation and no arrests have been made.
After the Star revealed last year that Neveah died after the Children's Aid Society of Toronto (CAST) gave full custody to a parent with years of documented substance abuse and child neglect, the paper filed a court application for access to records in a recent case involving Neveah's siblings. The June 2024 family court hearing decided the custody of two younger siblings, but hinged on what happened to Neveah. It ended with a judge permanently removing Neveah's siblings from their mother's care.
The court recently granted the Star access to police reports, affidavits from CAST workers and transcripts from the trial.
"The public has an interest in understanding what happened in this case, the role that various public institutions and agencies played, and whether and how such a tragedy can be prevented in the future," Justice Manjusha Pawagi, who agreed to end all supervision of Neveah in March 2021, said in her decision to release the records.
The documents show that in the year police believe Neveah was already dead, CAST missed multiple opportunities to realize the child was nowhere to be found.
Reports of alleged child abuse or neglect throughout 2022 and 2023 summoned police officers to Neveah's mother's home to check on her three siblings, and each time Neveah was absent. Her mother variously claimed her daughter was in daycare or with her "godparents," but it's unclear what, if anything, CAST did to confirm those claims, according to the records.
Eventually, records show a CAST employee realized Neveah was unaccounted for just days before Toronto police independently clinched the case through DNA and a tip from the public - and two years after the last confirmed sighting of Neveah.
CAST has declined to comment on its handling of the case, citing confidentiality laws that prevent the society from confirming whether a child has been in their care. At the closed-door custody hearing last June, a CAST lawyer admitted returning Neveah to her mother was "clearly not a good decision" in hindsight.
In a recent statement to the Star, CAST said it takes seriously the responsibility "to ensure the safety and well-being of children, youth, their caregivers, and broader support networks," and is accountable to oversight bodies including the Ministry of Children, Community and Social Services (MCCSS) and Ontario's Ombudsman.
Elman said the repeat calls to police in the case, which prompted followups with CAS, should have triggered more rigorous attention from workers.
"We need to get to the bottom of that," he said, adding it's difficult to speculate what pressures workers were under at the time.
Last fall, the Star's revelations prompted Premier Doug Ford to admit Ontario's child protection system "failed" Neveah. His government also announced a "complete audit" of the system, although details are sparse and critics have called the move a "smokescreen" distracting from government underfunding.
Multiple child advocates have called for a coroner's inquest into Neveah's death, a hearing that doesn't assign blame but examines what went wrong and produces recommendations to prevent similar deaths.
Dr. Dirk Huyer, Ontario's chief coroner, has not called an inquest into the girl's death, saying it would be premature while the criminal investigation is ongoing. Although inquests can technically be held before a police probe is done, a spokesperson for the coroner's office said it would be "unlikely in this case given the potential impact" on the investigation.
Monique Taylor, the former provincial NDP critic for child welfare, called it "shameful" that an inquest has still not yet been called. It should proceed no matter the status of the police investigation, she said, noting the probe is now nearly three years on.
"I think it's incumbent on the coroner to do whatever he can to ensure that this does not happen to any other child, and that we have the proper safeguards in place."
Taylor added the provincial government must also work to break down silos within the child protection and public safety systems, "to ensure that when a child is missing, that there's no way that it can be years before anybody discovers that."
In a statement, a spokesperson for MCCSS, the Ontario ministry that oversees child protection, did not directly respond to questions about Neveah's case, but said there have been recent legislative changes to increase oversight of children in care "with more frequent visits mandated, and new penalties for non-compliance."
"While calling an inquest is an independent decision made by the Chief Coroner, we will fully support and co-operate with his office to help prevent future tragedies like this," the statement said.
The court documents also reveal what critics say is another systemic problem with the child welfare system: multiple generations of families needing protective custody. Neveah's mother was taken into CAS care at age 12, eventually becoming what was then known as a "Crown ward," meaning her care was the responsibility of the government.
Kiaras Gharabaghi, a professor with Toronto Metropolitan University's School of Child and Youth Care and dean of the Faculty of Community Services, said the multi-generational phenomenon is "sadly" common, and disproportionately affects racialized populations. It highlights the lack of support for youth once they age out of the system at 18, he said.
"We generally cease to support those young people, and then are surprised that they're ill-equipped to live life as adults and their kids show up in care," he said.
What's needed in Neveah's "brutal and dark" case, Gharabaghi said, is a fix far greater than what an inquest can provide. Simply put, Neveah's case "is a stain on an already pretty stained child welfare system in Ontario."
"It is time to ask much more fundamental questions, including this one: Are Children's Aid Societies the right vehicle to protect children like Neveah?" he asked. "How many more times do these institutions have to fail before we start contemplating building new ones?"
CREDIT: Jennifer Pagliaro and Wendy Gillis Toronto Star

The facts vs. Donald Trump: The U.S. president says a lot of things about Canada. Here's how they measure up to reality

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/facts-vs-donald-trump/docview/3167311366/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+facts+vs.+Donald+Trump%3A+The+U.S.+president+says+a+lot+of+things+about+Canada.+Here%27s+how+they+measure+up+to+reality&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Since his return to the White House, and even on the campaign trail, U.S. President Donald Trump has made a litany of comments, claims and complaints about his northern neighbours. A Star analysis dives into the truth, the fiction and everything in between as the Canada-U.S. relationship enters one of its most unpredictable chapters. As Trump's "America First" agenda grows, so will this list.
The claim"Illegal aliens" are flowing into the U.S. from Canada
Sources Truth Social, Nov. 25, 2024, White House Fact Sheet, Feb. 1, 2025
Trump says "more than 10 million illegal aliens" attempted to enter the U.S. during the Biden administration and that "thousands of people are pouring through Mexico and Canada."
The president has not provided a definition of "illegal" migrants, but the U.S. Department of Homeland Security considers "unauthorized immigrants" as those who entered the country without inspection, or temporary residents who remained in the U.S. after a visa expired or they were meant to leave.
U.S. data shows Trump's claim that irregular crossings across both borders are of equal scale is inaccurate.
The U.S. Customs and Border Protection agency (CBP) reports that in the 2022 fiscal year (which runs from October to September) there were 1,818 encounters, under the U.S. government's "Title 8" authority, at the Canadian border. That legal authority sets out how border agents process and remove anyone deemed inadmissible to enter the U.S. and anyone who enters the country illegally.
The following year, encounters climbed to 9,514, and more than doubled to 23,721 in 2024.
Trump is right that numbers are rising, but they are a drop in the bucket compared to the CBP's totals at the Mexican border.
Border agents logged 1,496,067 encounters in 2023 at the southwestern border, a number that grew to more than 1.5 million in 2024.
The claim Canada is a significant source of fentanyl entering the U.S.
Sources Truth Social, Nov. 25, 2024, White House Fact Sheet, Feb. 1, 2025
Trump claims illegal drugs are "pouring across" the borders with Canada and Mexico, causing a "national emergency" in the U.S., especially due to a flood of the deadly synthetic opioid fentanyl.
In reality, Canada is the country of origin for only a very small amount of the fentanyl smuggled into the U.S.
In the 2023 fiscal year, the U.S. Customs and Border Protection agency seized about 27,000 pounds of fentanyl. CBP's totals capture more than just the two land borders, but Of that, only two pounds were seized at the Canadian border, while 26,700 pounds were seized at the U.S.-Mexico border. In 2024, that number was about 21,900 pounds; seizures at the northern border climbed to 43 pounds - or 0.2 per cent of the total - and seizures at the southwestern border dropped to 21,100 pounds.
That 43-pound total from Canada is questionable. The Globe and Mail reported that about a third of the fentanyl intercepted by northern border agents last year was seized in Spokane, Wash. CBP told the Globe that there isn't yet any evidence connecting that seizure to Canada.
Nevertheless, a Feb. 1 executive order by Trump cites a January report from Canada's Financial Transactions and Reports Analysis Centre (Fintrac), the financial intelligence agency that tracks money laundering, titled "Laundering the Proceeds of Illicit Synthetic Opioids," and claims Fintrac acknowledged "Canada's heightened domestic production of fentanyl, largely from British Columbia, and its growing footprint within international narcotics distribution." That's not exactly correct.
The Fintrac report notes that several groups in Canada are suspected of driving the domestic fentanyl market, and that most of them are based in B.C. But it said those operations have led to B.C. possibly becoming a supplier for other provinces, and did not explicitly mention international markets.
Similarly, a 2024 report from Criminal Intelligence Service Canada noted several high-level criminal networks involved with fentanyl based in Western Canada "whose scope extends to various countries in North America" and among other regions. It acknowledged the presence of Latin-American cartel-linked crime organizations in Canada, something the U.S. executive order also noted.
However, there isn't clear consensus on either side of the border whether the fentanyl made in so-called "super labs" in Canada is primarily destined for the U.S.
The claim The U.S. is "subsidizing" Canada
to the tune of hundreds of billions of dollars
Source Truth Social, Feb. 2, 2025
Trump claims the U.S. "subsidizes" Canada and that "without this massive subsidy, Canada ceases to exist as a viable country."
He says that is because America runs a trade deficit - meaning it buys more from Canada than it sells - to the tune of anywhere from $100 billion and $250 billion (U.S.), and that "without this massive subsidy, Canada ceases to exist as a viable country."
There is little clarity on where Trump is getting those numbers.
In 2023, the U.S. trade deficit for goods with Canada was $64.3 billion, according to the U.S. Census Bureau. In 2024, it was $63.3 billion.
That's a lot less than "hundreds of billions of dollars."
Trump also doesn't mention that the majority of Canada's energy products, particularly crude oil, are the big reason for any trade imbalance with America. A TD Economics report says if energy is excluded from the 2024 calculations, the U.S. actually has a trade surplus with Canada.
"A trade deficit is not a subsidy," the report states.
"Americans are receiving value for the dollars spent in the form of goods and services. The trade deficit the U.S. runs with Canada reflects their economic outperformance and above-average spending of Americans that's driving a hunger for energy products."
The claim The tariffs are about winning a fairer trade deal with Canada
Sources Truth Social, Feb. 3, 2025
On the day Trump agreed to temporarily delay his threatened tariffs, he suddenly added a new condition, posting to Truth Social that the 30-day reprieve was "to see whether or not a final economic deal with Canada can be structured. FAIRNESS FOR ALL."
The U.S. already has an "economic deal" with Canada: the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement (USMCA, or CUSMA as it is legally called in Canada), which took effect in 2020.
It is scheduled for its first review in 2026, when all three nations could enact any proposed changes they agree on. Trump's claim that the U.S. is treated "unfairly" by Canada and Mexico has prompted both to undertake their studies much earlier.
While Trump could withdraw from the deal at any time, doing so would take six months to come into effect.
The president has tasked officials with scrutinizing existing U.S. trade relationships for "unfair" practices and delivering recommendations by April 1, along with instructing the U.S. trade representative to prepare now for the trade pact's review.
The claim American banks can't operate in Canada
Source Truth Social, Feb. 3, 2025
On Feb. 3 on his Truth Social account, Trump wrote, "Canada doesn't even allow U.S. banks to open or do business there. What's that all about?"
This claim is not accurate.
"Canada has a strong and competitive banking sector comprised of both domestic and foreign banks. There are 16 U.S.-based bank subsidiaries and branches with around $113 billion (Canadian) in assets currently operating in Canada," the Canadian Bankers Association wrote in a statement later that day.
"These banks specialize in a range of financial services, including corporate and commercial lending, treasury services, credit card products, investment banking and mortgage financing. They serve not only customers with cross-border business activities, but also Canada's domestic retail market. U.S. banks now make up approximately half of all foreign bank assets in Canada."
The list includes financial institutions like Citibank, Bank of America and JPMorgan Chase.
U.S. financial institutions operating in Canada fall into two categories: Schedule II and Schedule III. Schedule II applies to a subsidiary of a foreign bank that is permitted to accept deposits in Canada, and is subjected to regulations under Canada's Bank Act. Schedule III applies to Canadian branches of foreign banks, which face certain restrictions.
There are, however, downsides associated with both categories, making it more difficult for U.S. institutions to compete with Canadian banks.
The claim The U.S. can take Canadian water
to help with its shortages
Source News conference, Sept. 13, 2024
Last September, Trump, then the Republican presidential nominee, told a news conference that he had a plan to help Californians in their battle against natural disasters.
He said "millions of gallons of water" were "pouring down" from the "snow caps in Canada," and suggested that by turning on "a very large faucet" - something Trump said would take a full day to turn due it its "massive" size - all of the water would stop flowing into the Pacific Ocean and flow straight into Los Angeles.
Trump's claims that there is infrastructure that would allow meltwater from Canadian snowcaps to be diverted to solve California's water woes is false.
There is no so-called "faucet" in Canada, nor any means to transport massive volumes of water from the Canadian mountaintops to the U.S., said Tricia Stadnyk, a professor and Canada Research chair in hydrologic modelling at the University of Calgary.
What Stadnyk and other experts believe Trump may be talking about is the Columbia River, which runs through the Rocky Mountains in B.C. and eventually flows south into Washington, before turning west along the Washington-Oregon border and draining into the Pacific Ocean.
Stadnyk said Trump would need to build an open canal or a pipeline to divert the water to California. Geographically, that would mean moving water from a basin in the U.S., so building infrastructure in Canada would not be necessary.
But it's even more complicated, experts say.
Canada has a law that bans the bulk removal of water that crosses the U.S. border, and both countries have had a Boundary Waters Treaty since 1909 to manage disputes over shared waters. There are 13 river basins that straddle the border, and the Columbia River, which is one of them, is also subject to its own treaty.
That means there are limitations over what each country can do to the water in their own jurisdictions, Stadnyk said, particularly if the U.S. were to boost the outflow of water from its basin, which would pull more water from Canada.
What's more, Ralph Pentland, a former director of water planning and management for Environment Canada and the primary author of Canada's Federal Water Policy, said there's no economically feasible way to move large volumes of water across long distances.
Pentland said such projects have been contemplated before, like a "totally impractical" 1964 proposal from the North American Water and Power Alliance that would have seen water from the Yukon, Skeena, Fraser, Peace and Columbia rivers diverted as far as Mexico.
All that said, there are current problems: the Columbia River Treaty is in limbo after a historic and lengthy attempt to modernize it wasn't ratified before last year's presidential election.
That's an issue for a treaty that outlines power benefits for Canada, Stadnyk said, given that Trump wants "American sovereignty over energy production."
The claim The U.S. military protects
Canada, so Canada should become a U.S. state
Sources News conference, Jan. 7, 2025, Executive order signing, Feb. 9, 2025
Trump says Canada's military spending is low because "they assume that we're going to protect them," and that America's military is at Canada's "disposal."
Canada and the U.S. have a unique defence relationship that's meant to be mutually beneficial.
They are partners in continental air defence under the North American Aerospace Defense Command (Norad), and they are members of the North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO), along with 30 European countries.
The NATO treaty commits members to collective defence, meaning that an attack against one ally is an attack against them all. (The only time that principle, known as Article 5, was invoked was in support of the U.S. after the Sept. 11 attacks).
NATO countries commit to spending the equivalent of at least two per cent of economic output, measured by gross domestic product (GDP), on defence. Canada has long fallen short of this target, which it accepted in 2014, and has pledged to reach the two per cent benchmark by 2032.
Canada's defence spending ranked 27th of 32 member states last year, according to NATO's latest estimates. Poland was the top spender in terms of its ratio to GDP, but the U.S., which spent 3.4 per cent of its GDP, is by far the biggest spender in actual dollars.
"The premise for decades has been that the (country) that was going to come to everybody's help was going to be America," said retired vice-admiral Mark Norman, a fellow at the Canadian Global Affairs Institute.
When it comes to Norad, the world's only binational military command, Norman said it's also still not an entirely equal partnership.
"It was understood from the outset that it would never be equal because, although we're partners, the Americans are much bigger. They have more capability, but we would do everything we can," he said.
"That's why we had fighter bases, we had rockets, we had all these things back in the day, and we have slowly, over decades, kind of let that all slide."
The Liberals did pledge, in 2022, to spend $38.6 billion over 20 years to modernize Norad.
But Norman said most of Canada's promises have been "catch-up commitments," and that at the end of the day, Trump's comments about protecting Canada are really about defending the U.S.
"We just happen to be the territorial extension of their boundaries," Norman said.
David Perry, president of the Canadian Global Affairs Institute, said Canada does bring some benefits to the table: the monitoring of northern airspace via the North Warning System's radar network, and Canadian Forces Station Alert, located in Canada's northernmost island, which has a "pretty unique ability to collect signals intelligence out of Russia."
"I think we are more reliant on the United States than we should be," Perry said. However, he added, "We're already on a trajectory to lessen some of that reliance."
The claim Canada doesn't
accept U.S. agricultural products,
but the U.S. accepts Canada's
Source Executive order signing, Feb. 3, 2025
In early February, Trump said the U.S. doesn't need Canada "because we have all the agriculture we need."
"They don't take our agricultural product, for the most part, our milk and dairy. A little bit they do, but not much. We take theirs," the president said. He also calls the system "unfair" and demands greater access to the Canadian market.
In reality, Canada is one of the best customers for U.S. agricultural products, importing $28.38 billion (US) worth of products in 2024, including $1.14 billion in dairy products, second behind Mexico.
Most U.S. agricultural products have entered Canada duty-free since 1989 under NAFTA and CUSMA, but Canada's supply-managed system does restrict how certain American products - like milk, cheese, powder, yogurt and even ice cream - can enter Canada.
Disputes over supply management, which also sets production quotas for Canadian farmers and sets price minimums, has been a source of tension between the two countries for several years, even under the Biden administration.
During the CUSMA negotiations with the last Trump administration, Canada agreed to allow U.S. dairy farmers access to about 3.5 per cent of its market, but the U.S. has since accused Canada of violating its end of that deal.
The trade deal is up for negotiation in 2026.
The Trudeau government has pledged to protect the supply management system.
The claim The U.S. doesn't need
anything Canada has
Sources News conference, Jan.7, 2025, Truth Social, Feb. 2, 2025
Canada is a critical supplier of many things needed by the U.S., including lumber, auto parts, potash, steel, aluminum, uranium, critical minerals and, of course, energy. Let's take a look.

Lumber
American homebuilders and other industries rely on Canada for stable and reasonably priced lumber products because the U.S. does not produce enough to meet its needs, despite a long-running dispute over softwood lumber between the two countries.
Canada is the biggest source of imported forest products to the U.S. It imported $11 billion (US) worth of wood products from Canada in 2023, according to Canadian government data.
In August, the U.S. Department of Commerce raised tariffs on imports of Canadian softwood lumber after inflation eased, despite the objection of the National Association of Home Builders. Canadian companies already pay lumber duties ranging from 11 to 17 per cent.
Derek Nighbor, president of the Forest Products Association of Canada (FPAC), said Americans can't just "snap their fingers" and replace Canadian products, in part because of the deep integration between the two countries' supply chains.
According to analysis from FPAC, U.S. domestic production of softwood lumber has accounted for 69 per cent of American demand in the last decade, while Canadian imports have made up another 26 per cent.
There is one more caveat: Canadian wood is largely of higher quality than American wood because of the longer and colder growing seasons in Canada, Nighbor said.
Automotive parts
Canada is the second biggest source of auto parts imported by America in the last decade, following Mexico.
Trade between the two nations is nearly balanced when it comes to the automotive industry because the supply chains are highly integrated. Auto parts and components may cross the borders of Canada, the U.S. and Mexico as many as eight times before being installed in a final assembly.
Tariffs on auto parts would impact most vehicles produced in the U.S. as well because of the free flow of components across borders.
The Canadian government cites a Wells Fargo report that estimated a 25 per cent tariff on all automotive parts from Canada in 2025 will add $2,100 (US) to the cost of a vehicle assembled in the United States. For vehicles imported from Canada, the additional cost to consumers could be between $8,000 and $10,000 (US).
Trump claims the U.S could replace Canadian parts domestically - but that's easier said than done. According to a recent report by TD Economics, the U.S. would need to raise production by more than 10 per cent relative to current levels to replace imported Canadian auto parts.
Potash
The U.S. is highly dependent on Canada for potash, sourcing more than 80 per cent of its potash imports from its northern neighbours.
Without Canada, its next biggest sources of potash are Russia and Belarus, which account for roughly 15 per cent of its imports.
The Canadian government says a 25 per cent tariff on Canadian potash could cost $1.2 billion for U.S. farmers, since price increases would likely be passed along to users.
"This may result in reduced fertilizer use (lower crop yields) and higher food prices, contributing to inflationary pressures in the U.S.," said a fact sheet compiled by Ottawa.
The U.S.-based Fertilizer Institute warned that "any disruption to the intertwined and mutually beneficial cross-border fertilizer trade between the U.S. and Canada will have significant ripple effects, not just for farmers but for the broader food supply chain and ultimately the prices consumers pay at the grocery store."
Steel
The U.S. imports more steel from Canada than from anywhere else. In 2024, that was about $13 billion (US) worth of iron and steel shipped to the U.S., which accounts for about a quarter of all U.S. steel imports.
At the same time, 95 per cent of Canadian steel exports went to the U.S. in 2023.
The Canadian Steel Producers Association says that about $20 billion worth of steel products get shipped back and forth across the border each year, and are further processed in a market it says is deeply integrated.
The bigger threat to both industries is the glut of Chinese steel on global markets - rival products that Canada and the U.S. have slapped with big tariffs.
Aluminum
Canada produces 3.2 million tons of aluminum per year and exports 90 per cent of that, or $10.8 billion worth, to the U.S., according to the Aluminum Association of Canada, which represents Canada's three major aluminum producers: Alcor, Rio Tinto and Alouette.
Association head Jean Simard said U.S. statistics show the U.S. consumes five million tons of aluminum a year (primary product, not recycled) but produces only 670,000 tons domestically, so Canadian products are key to American industries that use aluminum.
About 75 per cent of their imported aluminum comes from Canada.
Canadian metal is certified for use in special alloys and forms, in the automotive and aerospace sectors, and supports 500,000 American jobs in manufacturing, processing and semi-fabrication, Simard said.
Critical minerals
The U.S. government relies on imports for 43 of the 50 minerals on its critical minerals list.
While Canada supplies 50 to 80 per cent of the U.S.'s needs for several critical minerals, such as zinc, tellurium, nickel and vanadium, the most dominant country in global production for the minerals America needs is China.
The Canadian government says a 25 per cent tariff on Canadian critical minerals would cause the U.S. to become increasingly reliant on China for products such as nickel, zinc and germanium.
The last Trump administration agreed to a strategy with the Trudeau government to increase collaboration on critical mineral supply chains in an effort to reduce reliance on China.
In a Jan. 20 executive order on energy, Trump proposed adding uranium to the American list of critical minerals.
Canada is the world's second largest producer and third largest low-cost source of uranium, an important fuel for nuclear energy, and according to Natural Resources Canada, supplies more than a quarter of the uranium used in the U.S.
The Canadian government says that in 2022, 27 per cent of uranium purchased by American nuclear reactors came from Canada, making Canada the largest foreign supplier of uranium to the United States.
Energy
Trump has said that the U.S. doesn't need Canada's oil and gas.
In 2023, Canadian exports of oil, natural gas and electricity to the U.S were valued at more than $120 billion (US), according to Canadian government figures.
This is more than four times the amount Canada imports from the U.S.
Ontario alone directly supplied electricity to 1.5 million U.S. homes in 2023, and is a major exporter of power to Michigan, Minnesota and New York.
The Canadian government says that in 2023, Canada supplied more than 99 per cent of U.S. natural gas imports, 60 per cent of U.S. crude oil imports and 21 per cent of refined petroleum product imports, as well as 85 per cent of U.S electricity imports.
It says Canadian electricity exports directly power nearly six million U.S. homes and Canadian uranium powers the equivalent of roughly 20 million U.S. homes.
The claim Taxes on goods and services like Canada's GST or its
new digital services tax are an unfair and discriminatory trade barrier to U.S. imports
On Feb. 13, Trump called Canada "very bad on trade," and in a presidential memo the same day said the U.S. will impose "reciprocal tariffs" to match other countries' non- tariff barriers or unfair trading practices.
The White House memo targets, among other things, "unfair, discriminatory, or extraterritorial taxes imposed by our trading partners on United States businesses, workers, and consumers, including a value-added tax."
Trump first flagged Canada's sales taxes, and the digital services tax in a call with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau on Feb. 3, saying the U.S. has no sales tax, according to a Canadian official. Trudeau pointed out that states like Florida have a sales tax.
Canada has a five per cent federal tax that applies to goods and services at point of sale. On top of that, the provinces - with the exception of Alberta - also impose their own sales taxes; Ontario's is eight per cent.
The United States does not have a federal sales tax.
But trade expert Lawrence Herman says international trade law allows countries to impose sales taxes on imports as long as the taxes are applied equally to domestic products, under "non-discriminatory" provisions enshrined by the World Trade Organization, which are meant to be binding on members like Canada and the U.S.
In other words, a value-added tax is not unfair or discriminatory.
Trump explicitly objects to a digital services tax as an unfair extraterritorial tax on U.S.-based tech giants like Apple, Google, Meta, Netflix and Amazon.
The White House fact sheet said, "Though America has no such thing, and only America should be allowed to tax American firms, trading partners hand American companies a bill for something called a digital service tax."
It claimed Canada and France "each use these taxes to each collect over $500 million per year from American companies."
The reality is, Canada enacted a three per cent digital services tax last June after multilateral negotiations to come up with a fair global system of taxing digital giants stalled. It is retroactive to 2022 and payments are due starting in June.
The fall economic statement projected revenues to be $2.3 billion in 2024-25 (reflecting revenues from the 2022, 2023 and 2024 taxation years) and $900 million in each of the following four years.
Under the Biden administration, the U.S. challenged this tax, using the dispute settlement process in the Canada-U.S.-Mexico Agreement on trade.
CREDIT: Raisa Patel Toronto Star Tonda MacCharles Toronto Star Mark Ramzy Toronto Star

People: Trump, Donald J

Title: The facts vs. Donald Trump:   The U.S. president says a lot of things about Canada. Here's how they measure up to reality

Document 192 of 500

Inside Doug Ford's trip to Washington - and how he was received by Americans

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/inside-doug-fords-trip-washington-how-he-was/docview/3167136192/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Inside+Doug+Ford%27s+trip+to+Washington+-+and+how+he+was+received+by+Americans&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: WASHINGTON-In politics, as in war, symbolism matters.
So with Canada on the brink of a trade war with the United States, it was symbolic that the nation's provincial and territorial leaders secured a meeting in the White House.
Yes, it was at the last minute - and scheduled thanks to an $85,000-a-month lobbying firm retained by the Council of the Federation - and it was only with Jim Blair, one of President Donald Trump's deputy chiefs of staff, and White House personnel director Sergio Gor.
And, yes, Blair later trolled British Columbia Premier David Eby for daring to explain that Trump's dream of annexing Canada was a "non-starter."
"To be clear, we never agreed that Canada would not be the 51st state. We only agreed to share Premier Eby's comments," the deputy chief said on social media.
But to Doug Ford, the hastily arranged one-hour confab with a pair of youthful Trump aides was worth spending two precious days of the Feb. 27 Ontario election campaign in the American capital lobbying against the threatened 25 per cent tariffs.
"We appreciate the Trump administration facilitating this - literally in the last minute. We're grateful," enthused a visibly relieved Ford, standing on the chilly sidewalk outside the White House with Quebec's François Legault and Saskatchewan's Scott Moe at his side.
"It was a very positive conversation. It just shows the relationship that we have, and the respect they have ... regarding Canada," he said.
"They said it numerous times how they respect Canada and really appreciate the friendship over the years."
For Ford, the get-together helped justify his controversial decision to call a snap winter election 15 months before the scheduled June 2026 vote.
Insisting he needs a four-year mandate to "outlive and outlast" the mercurial president's administration, the Progressive Conservative leader hopes that rationale trumps more prosaic matters like the doctors' shortage or housing affordability.
It's why last Tuesday he made the first of his two mid-campaign trips to Washington for a speech to the United States Chamber of Commerce and a lobbying effort including all 13 provincial and territorial premiers.
On Thursday, Ford will return to the U.S. capital for the National Governors' Association winter conference where he hopes to persuade fellow subnational leaders of the perils of a trade war.
So far, Ford's advisers believe his gambit is paying off because he is dominating the campaign, appearing like a statesman hovering above domestic Ontario politics.
"It could have gone a lot worse," conceded one PC strategist, who had expressed concern about a White House visit lest Ford be humiliated.
"But he handled it well and the other meetings were worth the trip," said the insider, speaking confidentially in order to discuss internal deliberations.
That was a reference to the 13 premiers' discussions with Republican and Democratic lawmakers.
The source admitted there is a risk to such missions in the middle of an election campaign.
"We are ahead in the polls and really didn't need to take the chance so we're glad it worked out."
While Ford's U.S. Chamber of Commerce speech received little American media attention - and many of the 120 in attendance, including former PC leader Tim Hudak, Goldy Hyder, president and CEO of the Business Council of Canada, and Ontario Chamber of Commerce president Daniel Tisch, had flown in for the event - his anti-tariff message resonated with some.
Neil Herrington, the U.S. chamber's senior vice-president for the Americas, said it needs to be hammered home stateside that Trump's levies would send inflation skyrocketing.
"For Americans filling up their car in the Midwest, in Rocky Mountain states, tariffs on Canadian oil would drive up the price of gasoline by as much as 75 cents a gallon," said Herrington, who hosted Ford's speech.
"This in turn, raises costs for all kinds of retail goods transported around our country. Tariffs would drive up the price of a new car - even an inexpensive car - by as much as $3,000," he said.
"In a country already facing housing affordability challenges, tariffs would add $40 billion to the $150 billion of cement, lumber, metals, minerals and other materials that we import from Canada alone in support of our construction industry."
While many in Washington were receptive to what Ford had to say, Eric Miller, president of Rideau Potomac Strategy Group, stressed he's "not sure much could change Donald Trump's mind at this point."
"This is a case where, unfortunately, President Trump has to touch the hot stove before he realizes how valuable and integrated this relationship is and begins to take a different course," said Miller, who was at Ford's speech.
"At this point ... you're on this inexorable march toward tariffs. But it doesn't mean that once tariffs are imposed and pain is felt, and stock markets go down and prices go up, that the tariffs remain," he said.
One Republican lawmaker, speaking privately to the Star, praised Ford for saying out loud what many elected officials in Trump's party say about tariffs behind closed doors.
"Sometimes the president says things to deflect attention from what he's actually trying to achieve. He's a brilliant negotiator and him saying this stuff about Canada being a 51st state is part of a larger negotiation," said the lawmaker, apologetically emphasizing that Americans don't really want to annex Canada.
"We'll both get through this," the Republican said, acknowledging there will be some difficult trade conversations between Washington and Ottawa in the coming weeks, months and years.
While Ford was glowing after his first foray to Washington, his main political opponents in the upcoming election were questioning both the point and the ethical appropriateness of the mission.
"I understand it wasn't very successful," Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said Thursday in Elmvale.
"They didn't meet with any high-ranking officials, so I don't think it was very effective. I don't think the advocacy has been very effective," said Crombie, panning the effort as "a photo opportunity."
NDP Leader Marit Stiles formally complained to integrity commissioner J. David Wake, the legislature's ethics watchdog, saying Ford "blurred the lines" between governing and campaigning on the trip.
While the PC campaign chartered a private jet for political staff to go to Washington, Ontario government bureaucrats helped organize his Chamber of Commerce event.
In an embarrassing gaffe Thursday, the Tories posted and then deleted a social-media ad of Ford walking past the U.S. Capitol, interspersed with the party's "Protect Ontario" slogan.
"If that isn't a sure sign of guilt, I don't know what is," said Stiles.
"I don't think anyone's fooled."
Snafu aside, an ebullient Ford insisted his Washington trips are not just symbolic gestures.
"We're down here to listen to our U.S. colleagues," he said Thursday, pointing out that both nations are "stronger together."
"So we have to make sure that gets communicated up to President Trump."
With files from Kristin Rushowy and Rob Ferguson
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks to reporters at the Mayflower Hotel in Washington on Feb. 12, 2025.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie

No more mandates for Ford

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/no-more-mandates-ford/docview/3167136165/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=No+more+mandates+for+Ford&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I refuse to be bribed by Premier Doug Ford's $200 gift from the public purse (a gift funded at least partially by my own tax money). It is an unnecessary and opportunistic attempt to extend his own job, despite his claims that he needs a new mandate to enable him to defend Ontario jobs from President Donald Trump's tariffs.
Surely it is part of his job description as Premier of Ontario to fight for Ontarians against internal and external threats or interference. Surely that does not require a new mandate. He has not previously found it necessary to acquire a new mandate for any of the other controversial and divisive decisions his government has made.
As I have received the $200 cheque, I have decided that the best use for it is to donate it to one of the opposition parties, and I encourage others to do the same.
Brian Mayhew, Puslinch
Trump's tariff proposals were a godsend to Ford. The premier is now seeking a stronger mandate ostensibly in order to convince Ontarians that only he is strong enough to combat the U.S. president.
In the meantime, the snap election slated for Feb. 27 appears to have been purposely planned to make sure the Ford government stays one step ahead of the RCMP investigation into the premier's infamous Greenbelt scandal.
Ford is one shrewd politician. It's shameful.
Jeffrey Manly, Toronto
In my 77 years on this planet, I have never been more disgusted with a premier. He seems able to get away with using the systems of government to his advantage and convincing voters that he is best person for the job. He is totally self-serving, and I believe that if he had any sense of decency, he would at least wait another year for an election. People need confidence and stability right now.
Richard Bino, Singhampton
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford walks towards the offices of Lt.-Gov. Edith Dumont before requesting to dissolve the provincial parliament and hold elections, at Queens Park, the provincial legislature, in Toronto on Jan. 28, 2025.

Ontario election: From electric chairs to push ups, here are the top moments from the campaign trail this week

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-election-electric-chairs-push-ups-here/docview/3167136149/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+election%3A+From+electric+chairs+to+push+ups%2C+here+are+the+top+moments+from+the+campaign+trail+this+week&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Health care was front and centre on the campaign trail this week, as Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford took a trip to Washington on a tariff-fighting mission - and, upon returning to Ontario, had to explain a "poor-taste joke" he made about capital punishment. On Friday, all four party leaders were in North Bay for their first debate. On Monday, they will be in Toronto for their second, and last, before the Feb. 27 vote.
Here are the top takeaways:
On the campaign trail
* The PCs and Greens are running candidates in all 124 provincial ridings, while the Liberals and NDP each unexpectedly ended up one short just before nominations closed. In Eglinton-Lawrence, the NDP candidate bowed out, saying she didn't want to split votes on the political left, and would instead be supporting Liberal Vince Gasparro. In Windsor West, the Liberals aren't running anyone against incumbent NDP candidate Lisa Gretzky, telling the Star it's "too bad Moe Chehab isn't running" - the candidate the party wanted to field - but the Liberals otherwise "have a strong slate of candidates across the province."
* The time wasn't right for the Liberals on Monday, when Leader Bonnie Crombie unexpectedly started her announcement half an hour early in Toronto, as reporters were still en route from an earlier news conference with the NDP. Crombie, whose team cited "an error in communication," ended up having to scramble and schedule a do-over - at the original time. "Who starts a press conference without the press?" quipped one frustrated supporter.
* Ford also had a tough time Monday, struggling to find the right words when asked about whale deaths at Marineland. "It's terrible of any animal dying in those, whales," he said. "You know, it'd be great just to let them free, but what I understand, if you let them free in the ocean, they're done. They're dead. So we're going to make sure that they still take care of these whales."
* The NDP is asking the province's integrity commissioner to investigate whether Ford broke ethics rules in a social media ad posted on his feed with him in Washington that also featured the "Protect Ontario" slogan. The ad was removed and reposted soon after with no PC logos.
Campaign promises
* The PCs pledged to ban Chinese components and equity in all government energy, critical mineral or infrastructure projects.
* The NDP wants to legislate nurse-patient ratios, and hire 15,000 nurses over three years. Its northern strategy, released Thursday, promised 911 in all communities, 350 new doctors and more spaces at the Northern Ontario School of Medicine, as well as home and long-term care for First Nations and Francophone patients in their language. An NDP government would also widen critical highways in the north.
* The Liberals pledged to create 40,000 paid internships for students, get rid of interest on Ontario Student Assistance Program loans and cap international student enrolment while boosting funding for colleges and universities. On Wednesday, they pledged to pay nurses and personal support workers a "living wage" whether they work in hospitals, home care or long-term care. They also highlighted the ongoing issue of hallway health care, which in some hospital emergency rooms is now chair care for patients who can't get beds.
* The Greens revealed their full platform on Wednesday, which included waiving the land transfer tax for first-time homebuyers and cutting taxes for those earning less than $65,000 or households earning less than $100,000. To help with home energy costs, the Greens would provide free heat pumps for households with income of less than $100,000 and interest-free loans for those above that. They would create an "Ontario Foodbelt" to preserve farmland, and also protect the Hudson Bay Lowlands. Echoing the other parties' promises, they also want to hire 3,500 new doctors in the next four years, including 350 for Northern Ontario.
In their own words
* Ford on U.S. tariffs: "If it comes our way ... (it's) a fight we never started, a fight that we don't want. But when someone you know gives you a pop in the nose, they're going to get that pop twice as hard back, I'll tell you that."
* NDP Leader Marit Stiles on tariffs and Ford calling the U.S. a "customer": "I think Ontarians are looking for a strong pushback and a fight for every single job and every single worker. In this case, I would say the customer is not always right, Doug. In fact, sometimes the customer needs to stand outside for a few minutes."
* Bonnie Crombie on the election: "It's Ford against Crombie in this race, and I'm happy to challenge him anytime on his turf - or on mine. And my turf is the gym, so if he wants to come at me and we'll have a competition in the gym, and maybe we'll do push ups. What do you think?"
* Crombie also said a Liberal government would help businesses with access to loans, creating "what we call the Fight Tariff Fund, the FTF - or F-Trump Fund too - however you want to take it."
* Ford was forced to walk back comments he made to a law enforcement crowd Jan. 29 about those who commit violent home invasions. "God forbid they kill an innocent person. I don't even go 25 years. I send 'em right to sparky and then we'll take everything from there," using slang for the electric chair. His team later said Ford made a "poor-taste joke out of frustration" and "does not support capital punishment but rather firmly believes more needs to be done to keep violent and repeat offenders behind bars where they belong."
CAPTION: Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles arrives to release her party's northern platform at Science North in Sudbury, Ont. on Feb. 13, 2025.
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy

Document 195 of 500

Just watch us

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/just-watch-us/docview/3167136132/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Just+watch+us&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Dear Canada, please accept our apologies, Feb. 4
Dear American friends, your letters of apology for President Donald Trump's trade war are touching, but they miss the mark. Forgive this deviation from standard Canadian politeness, but what we need isn't sympathy - it's solidarity . Where are the protest marches? Where's the public outcry against Trump's betrayal of an ally?
Right now, during this national crisis, Canadians should seek guidance from our creative thinkers. Let's talk about Margaret Atwood, for example, and her prescient 1972 book, Survival: A Thematic Guide to Canadian Literature. It reads like a playbook for our present predicament. Her trinity of wilderness, survival and cultural identity isn't just literary anymore - it's on our morning news. Trump's economic strong-arming is simply another existential wilderness to negotiate.
The evidence of Canada's national determination is written throughout our history: we survived the War of 1812, endured the Great Depression's harshest blows, patriated our Constitution in 1982, and emerged stronger from the 2008 financial crisis.
We thrive on precarity. So instead of collecting American apologies, let's embrace what George Grant called our "intimations of deprival." Knowing what we stand to lose should sharpen our resolve and fuel our will to remain Canadian. Moreover, we must engage with what Atwood dubbed our wilderness ethos. After all, Canadians aren't victims - we're survivors by design, perpetually reinventing ourselves in the shadow of a superpower.
Time to stop reading sympathy cards and start writing our own narrative. We will survive Trump and America's perverse moral decline. Just watch us.
Tony D'Andrea, Toronto
Dear friends in the United States, on behalf of myself and all rational Canadians, I gratefully accept your thoughtful and much-appreciated apologies. I read and re-read your words of encouragement and support. Indeed, the people of our two countries are at our best when we pull together in the face of adversity.
"The Star-Spangled Banner" does not belong to Trump, it belongs to you, the American people. I hope the Canadian fans at the NBA and NHL games who booed your national anthem will next time act instead upon the suggestion of a previous letter writer from Holly Hill, Florida, and shout, "Trump sucks!" I do believe that would be more appropriate.
Patricia Steward, Toronto
Over the past few days - and for the first time in quite a while - I have heard Canada's assorted party leaders actually trying to come up with good ideas instead of simply insisting that the current government only has bad ones.
I've heard NDP leader Jagmeet Singh propose strong tariffs on imported Tesla vehicles and Pierre Poilievre suggest Canada take a stronger role in protecting our Northern frontiers.
With our southern neighbour acting like a troll in spite of our long and mutually beneficial relationship, I think that it is time for all our leaders to unite for the benefit of Canada, just as many Canadians have already done, in boycotting U.S. goods and travel.
Let's show our American neighbours what jurisdictional co-operation looks like - that, while we respect them, we can happily solve our own problems, and that tariffs will simply compel us to look elsewhere for stable trading partners. We've weathered far worse storms than a power-hungry felon running a democracy into the ground.
Brigitte Nowak, Toronto
Ford's 401 folly
Doug Ford vows to forge ahead with tunnel under Hwy. 401, Feb. 8
Premier Doug Ford is old enough to know that building more roads with more lanes only encourages more cars to fill the space, creating more gridlock, more stress and more pollution that reduces the quality of life for all. Saddling the province with another highway - tunnelled under Highway 401, no less - would be ruinous for future generations, even if the idea delights Ford's backers.
Our provincial parties must embrace what has been proven over past decades. Rather than removing tolls from the 407, which would clog that highway in short order, toll the 401, with the market setting the price for peak times, shoulder times costing 50 per cent of peak, and off-peak being free. One has to cut peak volume down by only 5 to 10 per cent to maximize road efficiency. Tolls collected would be available for maintaining the road and for improving public transit.
The same logic applies to congestion in city cores. One needs time-of-day congestion charges to eliminate gridlock. Removing bike lanes will only make matters worse. Motorists are paying a heavy price for idling their vehicles in traffic. Their time is valuable, and they are placing their health at risk by breathing polluted air.
John Newell, Toronto
It seems to me that Ford should be going up, not down. Instead of blowing hundreds of billions of our dollars on a tunnel under Highway 401, it would make more sense to build up, above the road.
Another set of lanes could be built over the existing 401 for substantially less money than boring a tunnel underneath it would cost. The savings could be used to recruit more family doctors. Tens of thousands of Ford's constituents don't have a physician they can go to, and that should be more of a priority during his next expensive and unnecessary "mandate."
Don Williams, Thornhill
Breaking down barriers
U.S. turmoil has created an opportunity for Canada to lure top talent - if we act fast enough, Feb. 6
I agree with Alan Shepard that Canada needs to do more to attract American business and brains. However, that's easier said than done. As the article suggests, we need to remove interprovincial trade barriers. That would be a step in the right direction, and until a couple of weeks ago, only the Conservatives had even mentioned it.
As for attracting business, how do we plan to do that? Canada has an anti-business climate. "Profit" is almost a bad word. If American companies have to deal with too much red tape, they won't come. And they won't come if they have to deal with taxes that are too high either.
The same goes for brains and talent. Salaries for young professionals, tech workers, and university professors tend to be much lower in Canada, and their taxes are much higher. They might not like President Donald Trump, but moving to Canada is not going to happen in this climate. Politicians are trying to attract doctors to Canada, for example. But why would they come here for less pay, more taxes and more paperwork to satisfy government regulations?
What can be done now: Canada needs to operate as one country, not 13 different countries. The movement of goods is easier within European Union member states than it is between Canadian provinces and territories. If we're so proud to be Canadians, it's time to show some unity.
Claude Gannon, Markham
Words to the wise
Ford Bucks go a long way. I have found a good way to use the politically motivated $200 that the premier sent Ontarians. I will give $200 to Liberal leader Bonnie Crombie, and I get back $150 as a tax credit. I give this $150 to the federal party of my choice and get back $112 as a tax credit. Then I give the $112 to a local charity and get back $37 as a tax credit.
In total, I give away $462 and I still have $37 left for a free lunch on Ford's dime.
Glen Wedlock, Newmarket
CAPTION: The Ontario government says it has launched an enhanced security operation intended to beef up security along the border with the United States.

Document 196 of 500

A 'stain' on Ontario's child welfare system: Advocates renew calls for accountability in death of Neveah, girl found in Rosedale dumpster

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/stain-on-ontarios-child-welfare-system-advocates/docview/3167136117/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+%27stain%27+on+Ontario%27s+child+welfare+system%3A+Advocates+renew+calls+for+accountability+in+death+of+Neveah%2C+girl+found+in+Rosedale+dumpster&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Child welfare advocates are renewing their calls for answers and accountability in the death of a child whose remains were found in a Rosedale dumpster, a case one expert calls a "stain" on Ontario's child protection system.
The outcry follows new revelations published in the Toronto Star about the life and death of Neveah - a four-year-old child who spent much of her life in Ontario's child protection system - after gaining rare access to nearly 1,500 pages of never-before-released court and police records from a recent child welfare case involving her family.
The documents raise pressing questions about why the girl, who had complex needs and Autism Spectrum Disorder, was returned to a mother with a lengthy history of neglect just months before the child died, and expose how seemingly no one involved in her care noticed she was missing for months, let alone dead.
The revelations are further proof the case must be examined at a public inquest to learn what went wrong and prevent other children from falling through the cracks, said Irwin Elman, who served as the province's Child Advocate from 2008 to 2019.
"It's very difficult to understand why this happened," Elman said, "which is why an inquest needs to happen."
Neveah's decomposed body was found wrapped in blankets by contractors in May 2022, but for more than a year her name evaded investigators, who eventually resorted to complex DNA technology to identify her. Police say she died sometime in 2021 and had never been reported missing. (A publication ban prevents the Star from fully identifying Neveah or her relatives).
Her death, categorized as suspicious, remains under investigation and no arrests have been made.
After the Star revealed last year that Neveah died after the Children's Aid Society of Toronto (CAST) gave full custody to a parent with years of documented substance abuse and child neglect, the paper filed a court application for access to records in a recent case involving Neveah's siblings. The June 2024 family court hearing decided the custody of two younger siblings, but hinged on what happened to Neveah; it ended with a judge permanently removing Neveah's siblings from their mother's care.
The court recently granted the Star access to police reports, affidavits from CAST workers and transcripts from the trial.
"The public has an interest in understanding what happened in this case, the role that various public institutions and agencies played, and whether and how such a tragedy can be prevented in the future," Justice Manjusha Pawagi, who agreed to end all supervision of Neveah in March 2021, said in her decision to release the records.
The documents show that in the year police believe Neveah was already dead, CAST missed multiple opportunities to realize the child was nowhere to be found.
Reports of alleged child abuse or neglect throughout 2022 and 2023 summoned police officers to Neveah's mother's home to check on her three siblings, and each time Neveah was absent. Her mother variously claimed her daughter was in daycare or with her "godparents," but it's unclear what, if anything, CAST did to confirm those claims, according to the records.
Eventually, records show a CAST employee realized Neveah was unaccounted for just days before Toronto police independently clinched the case through DNA and a tip from the public - and two years after the last confirmed sighting of Neveah.
CAST has declined to comment on its handling of the case, citing confidentiality laws that prevent the society from confirming whether a child has been in their care. At the closed-door custody hearing last June, a CAST lawyer admitted returning Neveah to her mother was "clearly not a good decision" in hindsight.
In a recent statement to the Star, CAST said it takes seriously the responsibility "to ensure the safety and well-being of children, youth, their caregivers, and broader support networks," and is accountable to oversight bodies including the Ministry of Children, Community and Social Services (MCCSS) and Ontario's Ombudsman.
Elman said the repeat calls to police in the case, which prompted follow-ups with CAS, should have triggered more rigorous attention from workers.
"We need to get to the bottom of that," he said, adding it's difficult to speculate what pressures workers were under at the time.
Last fall, the Star's revelations prompted then-Ontario premier Doug Ford to admit Ontario's child protection system "failed"Neveah. His government also announced a "complete audit" of the system, though details are sparse and critics have called the move a "smokescreen" distracting from government underfunding.
Multiple child advocates have called for a coroner's inquest into Neveah's death, a hearing that doesn't assign blame but examines what went wrong and produces recommendations to prevent similar deaths.
Dr. Dirk Huyer, Ontario's chief coroner, has not called an inquest into the girl's death, saying it would be premature while the criminal investigation is ongoing. Though inquests can technically be held before a police probe is done, a spokesperson for the coroner's office said it would be "unlikely in this case given the potential impact" on the investigation.
Monique Taylor, the former provincial NDP critic for child welfare, called it "shameful" that an inquest has still not yet been called. It should proceed no matter the status of the police investigation, she said, noting the probe is now nearly three years on.
"I think it's incumbent on the coroner to do whatever he can to ensure that this does not happen to any other child, and that we have the proper safeguards in place," she said.
Taylor added the provincial government must also work to break down silos within the child protection and public safety systems, "to ensure that when a child is missing, that there's no way that it can be years before anybody discovers that."
In a statement, a spokesperson for MCCSS, the Ontario ministry that oversees child protection, did not directly respond to questions about Neveah's case, but said there have been recent legislative changes to increase oversight of children in care "with more frequent visits mandated, and new penalties for non-compliance."
"While calling an inquest is an independent decision made by the Chief Coroner, we will fully support and cooperate with his office to help prevent future tragedies like this ," the statement said.
The court documents also reveal what critics say is another systemic problem with the child welfare system: multiple generations of families needing protective custody. Neveah's mother was taken into CAS care at age 12, eventually becoming what was then known as a "Crown ward," meaning her care was the responsibility of the government.
Kiaras Gharabaghi, a professor with Toronto Metropolitan University's School of Child and Youth Care and Dean of the Faculty of Community Services, said the multi-generational phenomenon is "sadly" common, and disproportionately affects racialized populations. It highlights the lack of support for youth once they age out of the system at 18, he said.
"We generally cease to support those young people, and then are surprised that they're ill equipped to live life as adults and their kids show up in care," he said.
What's needed in Neveah's "brutal and dark" case, Gharabaghi said, is a fix far greater than what an inquest can provide. Simply put, Neveah's case "is a stain on an already pretty stained child welfare system in Ontario."
"It is time to ask much more fundamental questions, including this one: Are Children's Aid Societies the right vehicle to protect children like Neveah?" he asked. "How many more times do these institutions have to fail before we start contemplating building new ones?"
CAPTION: In November 2023,Neveah was given a municipal burial at Thornton Cemetery in Oshawa in a quiet plot courtesy of the cemetery.
CREDIT: Jennifer Pagliaro, Wendy Gillis

Subject: Child welfare; Child custody; Child abuse & neglect; Criminal investigations; Siblings; Inquests; Critics; Accountability

Crombie stands by northern candidate: Liberal joked about abusive behaviour in posts from 2014

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/crombie-stands-northern-candidate/docview/3166983218/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Crombie+stands+by+northern+candidate%3A+Liberal+joked+about+abusive+behaviour+in+posts+from+2014&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie is standing by a candidate despite his social media posts making light of intimate partner violence in the wake for former CBC host Jian Ghomeshi's legal troubles.
"These are tweets from over a decade ago, and the candidate has apologized," Liberal campaign spokesperson Bahoz Dara Aziz said Friday in regard to Brian Hamilton, running in Thunder Bay-Superior North.
Hamilton, a Thunder Bay councillor and coffee shop owner hoping to wrest the riding from the NDP's Lise Vaugeois in the Feb. 27 election, said he is a different person now.
"I made comments online that do not reflect my current views. That doesn't excuse them, but I have learned and grown over that time. I apologize and would not make those same comments today," Hamilton wrote in a statement.
The posts were flagged Friday morning in a news release from Doug Ford's Progressive Conservative campaign, saying they "not only glorified an individual associated with abusive behaviour against women but also trivialized the pain experienced by victims of intimate partner violence."
Hamilton's tweets reference Ghomeshi, who was fired by CBC over sex allegations in 2014 and acquitted two years later on charges of choking to overcome resistance and sexual assault related to three women.
Another sexual assault charge against Ghomeshi was withdrawn, but he did apologize in court for "sexually inappropriate behaviour" and admitted he "crossed boundaries" in the workplace.
"I miss Jian Ghomeshi, even if he is an ass slapping lady choker. His peculiarities don't neutralize his genius," Hamilton wrote on Twitter in December 2014.
In another post, Hamilton said Ghomeshi's departure from pop culture radio show "Q" made it "like a flower without the pedals (sic). A wind without the chill. A date without a kiss. Sad days @CBC."
He later added in a reply, "Or a date without the slap/choke. Whichever you prefer."
The tweets remained on his X account on Friday morning, along with others including "as a tribute to International Women's Day I'm going to be wrong all day" in 2015.
Another post that year read: "A new workout routine by Taylor Swift. It's where you cry yourself to sleep and wake up a soulless bitch. Swiftercise!"
The controversy renews concerns over the vetting and screening of candidates by the Liberals.
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Document 198 of 500

Rivals take aim at Tories in debate: Liberal leader splits with prior premiers as Green, NDP heads attack Ford in north-focused event

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/rivals-take-aim-at-tories-debate/docview/3166982749/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Rivals+take+aim+at+Tories+in+debate%3A+Liberal+leader+splits+with+prior+premiers+as+Green%2C+NDP+heads+attack+Ford+in+north-focused+event&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Bonnie Crombie tried to distance herself from previous Liberal governments in the first leaders' debate of the Feb. 27 election campaign, prompting Doug Ford to growl "what a joke."
In a testy exchange, the Liberal leader said she disagrees with some moves by Liberal premiers Dalton McGuinty and Kathleen Wynne during the 15 years they were in power - a span that ended with a 2018 win by Ford's Progressive Conservatives.
"As you know, I'm a new Liberal leader and I'm not taking responsibility for decisions made previously," Crombie said during a 90-minute debate on northern issues Friday at Canadore College in North Bay.
New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles and Green Leader Mike Schreiner also took shots at Ford in a bid to narrow his comfortable lead in public opinion polls with less than two weeks until voting day.
"After seven years of Doug Ford, we've had a lot of talk but it's never been worse," said Stiles, speaking about a housing shortage and high housing prices, a doctor shortage, worsening hallway health care in overcrowded hospitals and growing homelessness and encampments.
"Where are the homes, Doug?" she said. "There's a whole generation of young people who cannot even imagine buying a home and can't even afford an affordable rental home."
"He said he would end hallway health care," Schreiner added. "Seven years later, it's twice as worse."
Ford said voters should make their ballot decision on which leader is best positioned to stand up to U.S. President Donald Trump and his threatened tariffs that could ravage Ontario's trade-dependent economy.
"This election is about who's going to protect the people of Ontario," said Ford, who was in Washington this week to lobby U.S. lawmakers.
He stuck to his campaign script, urging voters to give him "a strong, stable four-year mandate" to outlast Trump.
On her differences with previous Liberal governments, Crombie cited the cancellation a decade ago of the Northlander train connecting northern residents from Cochrane and points south to Toronto.
Ford has promised to restore it, but service has not yet resumed despite a plan to purchase new trains and a pledge to have them running by mid-decade.
"I support the Northlander. It should never have been closed," Crombie said.
Ford warned opposition parties would "tax the people to death" and said Crombie raised taxes annually during the almost 10 years she was mayor of Mississauga, causing some residents to leave.
After the debate, opposition leaders took Ford to task over his off-the-cuff remarks at a police chief's gala in London two weeks ago that home-invasion killers should be sent "right to sparky" - a reference to the electric chair.
Ford's campaign scrambled to walk back the remarks after they were first reported in the Star, saying they were made in "poor taste" and insisted Ford does not support a return to capital punishment, abolished in 1976.
"We need a premier who's not going to make jokes about the death penalty," Schreiner said as reporters scrummed the leaders for their thoughts on the debate.
Ford left without taking questions.
The capital punishment quip was "disgusting and inappropriate," said Stiles.
Crombie said Ford has been unmasked again, as he was in hot mic comments last week about supporting Trump to win the U.S. election.
The leaders go head-to-head live on television on Monday at 6:30 p.m. in Toronto.
CAPTION: From left: Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, NDP Leader Marit Stiles, moderator and CBC Radio host Markus Schwabe, PC Party Leader Doug Ford and Green Leader Mike Schreiner take part in a debate in North Bay on Friday.Gino Donato The Canadian Press
