Document 199 of 500

Tories treating Carney as front-runner: Federal Conservatives release attack ad targeting candidate over carbon tax he pledges to repeal

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tories-treating-carney-as-front-runner/docview/3166982281/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tories+treating+Carney+as+front-runner%3A+Federal+Conservatives+release+attack+ad+targeting+candidate+over+carbon+tax+he+pledges+to+repeal&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.18&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The federal Conservatives are amping up criticism of Mark Carney, the Liberal leadership contender they appear to assume will replace Justin Trudeau as their chief opponent in the next federal election.
The criticism comes as the party stages a "Canada First" rally in Ottawa on Saturday, aimed at pitching Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre as the best answer to U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff threats.
While Conservative MPs huddled on Parliament Hill for a daylong caucus meeting Friday, the party released a new attack ad targeting Carney.
In it, the former central banker in Canada and the United Kingdom is portrayed as a supporter of the Trudeau government's "carbon tax" that has sent jobs to the United States.
Carney has pledged to repeal the consumer carbon price, which he deems as "divisive," and replace it with green incentives funded by industries with heavy emissions that cause climate change.
The new Conservative ad also suggests Carney is a hypocrite for previously opposing at least some Canadian fossil fuel development, even as an asset management company he worked for until running for Liberal leadership supported the industry in other countries.
The ad concludes with the line: "Carbon Tax Carney: if Carney wins, Canada loses."
In an emailed statement, Carney campaign spokesperson Emily Williams said Poilievre is pulling from "Donald Trump's playbook with empty and misleading slogans.
"While Pierre continues to think about Mark, Mark will continue to focus on his real plan to build up our economy and stand up for Canadian workers against President Trump's threats," Williams said.
Former finance minister Chrystia Freeland, former House leader Karina Gould and former MPs Frank Baylis and Ruby Dhalla are also running for the Liberal leadership.
But it is Carney on whom the Conservatives are directing their fire.
Michael Barrett, a Conservative MP from eastern Ontario, demanded Friday that Carney disclose information about his private investments after he served as an economic adviser to the Liberal party starting last fall.
Andrew Scheer, the Regina MP and former Conservative leader, took a run at the former central banker over comments he made during a leadership campaign speech in Kelowna, B.C.
Video from the event posted online by KelownaNow shows Carney - speaking about how Trump has demanded Canada crack down on the deadly opioid fentanyl - saying the opioid crisis is a "challenge" in Canada but a "crisis" in the U.S.
Scheer accused Carney of "downplaying the crisis that Canadians are suffering from," which he alleged was made worse by Liberal government drug policies that included allowing some jurisdictions to decriminalize drugs in an effort to provide safer supplies.
"To refuse to call it a crisis and just call it a challenge shows that he doesn't have to deal with these types of consequences of these policies. He lives in a completely different world," said Scheer, whose party is promising to bring in mandatory life sentences for people who traffic fentanyl.
According to Health Canada, 49,105 people were reported to have died from opioid toxicity from January 2016 to June 2024.
Deputy Conservative leader Melissa Lantsman also accused Carney of planning to bring in a "new and bigger carbon tax" that will hamper Canadian competitiveness at a time of insecurity.
Alongside preserving the industrial carbon pricing requirement introduced by the Liberals in 2019, Carney has said he would pursue a border tariff to protect Canadian businesses from losing out to products produced in countries with weaker environmental policies.
While Poilievre has been clear he would also scrap the consumer carbon price, he has not clarified what he would do with the national requirement for industrial carbon pricing.
Since Trump returned to the presidency in January, much of the Canadian political discussion has revolved around dealing with his threat of tariffs. The Conservatives, who until recently have hammered the government over cost-of-living concerns, are now routinely accusing the Liberals of leaving Canada vulnerable to Trump's threats.
In the Liberal leadership race, contenders have framed Trump's tariffs as the core issue. On Saturday night, Carney was scheduled to make a speech and host a viewing party for a hockey game between Canada and the U.S.
Earlier this week, Carney told supporters in Kelowna, B.C. that the U.S. wants Canada's resources, water and land, and that annexation would "destroy our way of life."
Carney also said he would cut taxes for the "middle class," and balance the government's "spending budget" within three years.
Williams said Friday that Carney was talking about Ottawa's operating budget - not the entire federal deficit, which grew to more than $61 billion in the most recent fiscal year.

Letters: Dear Canada, please accept our apologies, Feb. 4 U.S. turmoil has created an opportunity for Canada to lure top talent - if we act fast enough, Feb. 6 Doug Ford vows to forge ahead with tunnel under Hwy. 401, Feb. 8

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3166971326/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Dear+Canada%2C+please+accept+our+apologies%2C+Feb.+4+U.S.+turmoil+has+created+an+opportunity+for+Canada+to+lure+top+talent+-+if+we+act+fast+enough%2C+Feb.+6+Doug+Ford+vows+to+forge+ahead+with+tunnel+under+Hwy.+401%2C+Feb.+8&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=OP.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: This trade war will sharpen our resolve
Dear Canada, please accept our apologies, Feb. 4
Dear American friends, your letters of apology for President Donald Trump's trade war are touching, but they miss the mark. Forgive this deviation from standard Canadian politeness, but what we need isn't sympathy - it's solidarity . Where are the protest marches? Where's the public outcry against Trump's betrayal of an ally?
Right now, during this national crisis, Canadians should seek guidance from our creative thinkers. Let's talk about Margaret Atwood, for example, and her prescient 1972 book "Survival: A Thematic Guide to Canadian Literature." It reads like a playbook for our present predicament. Her trinity of wilderness, survival and cultural identity isn't just literary anymore - it's on our morning news. Trump's economic strong-arming is simply another existential wilderness to negotiate.
We thrive on precarity. So instead of collecting American apologies, let's embrace what George Grant called our "intimations of deprival." Knowing what we stand to lose should sharpen our resolve and fuel our will to remain Canadian. Moreover, we must engage with what Atwood dubbed our wilderness ethos. After all, Canadians aren't victims - we're survivors by design, perpetually reinventing ourselves in the shadow of a superpower.
Time to stop reading sympathy cards and start writing our own narrative. We will survive Trump and America's perverse moral decline. Just watch us.
Tony D'Andrea, Toronto
Dear friends in the United States, on behalf of myself and all rational Canadians, I gratefully accept your thoughtful and much-appreciated apologies. I read and re-read your words of encouragement and support. Indeed, the people of our two countries are at our best when we pull together in the face of adversity.
"The Star-Spangled Banner" does not belong to Trump, it belongs to you, the American people. I hope the Canadian fans at the NBA and NHL games who booed your national anthem will next time act instead upon the suggestion of a previous letter writer from Holly Hill, Fla., and shout, "Trump sucks!" I do believe that would be more appropriate.
Patricia Steward, Toronto
Over the past few days - and for the first time in quite a while - I have heard Canada's assorted party leaders actually trying to come up with good ideas instead of simply insisting that the current government only has bad ones.
I've heard NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh propose strong tariffs on imported Tesla vehicles and Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre suggest Canada take a stronger role in protecting our northern frontiers.
With our southern neighbour acting like a troll in spite of our long and mutually beneficial relationship, I think that it is time for all our leaders to unite for the benefit of Canada, just as many Canadians have already done, in boycotting U.S. goods and travel.
Let's show our American neighbours what jurisdictional co-operation looks like - that, while we respect them, we can happily solve our own problems, and that tariffs will simply compel us to look elsewhere for stable trading partners. We've weathered far worse storms than a power-hungry felon running a democracy into the ground.
Brigitte Nowak, Toronto
Let's break down trade barriers
U.S. turmoil has created an opportunity for Canada to lure top talent - if we act fast enough, Feb. 6
I agree with Alan Shepard that Canada needs to do more to attract American business and brains. However, that's easier said than done. As the article suggests, we need to remove interprovincial trade barriers. That would be a step in the right direction, and until a couple of weeks ago, only the Conservatives had even mentioned it.
As for attracting business, how do we plan to do that? Canada has an anti-business climate. "Profit" is almost a bad word. If American companies have to deal with too much red tape, they won't come. And they won't come if they have to deal with taxes that are too high either.
The same goes for brains and talent. Salaries for young professionals, tech workers and university professors tend to be much lower in Canada and their taxes are much higher. They might not like President Donald Trump, but moving to Canada is not going to happen in this climate. Politicians are trying to attract doctors to Canada, for example. But why would they come here for less pay, more taxes and more paperwork to satisfy government regulations?
What can be done now: Canada needs to operate as one country, not 13 different countries. The movement of goods is easier within European Union member states than it is between Canadian provinces and territories. If we're so proud to be Canadians, it's time to show some unity.
Claude Gannon, Markham
Ford's 401 folly
Doug Ford vows to forge ahead with tunnel under Hwy. 401, Feb. 8
Premier Doug Ford is old enough to know that building more roads with more lanes only encourages more cars to fill the space, creating more gridlock, more stress and more pollution that reduces the quality of life for all. Saddling the province with another highway - tunnelled under Highway 401, no less - would be ruinous for future generations, even if the idea delights Ford's backers.
Our provincial parties must embrace what has been proven over past decades. Rather than removing tolls from the 407, which would clog that highway in short order, toll the 401, with the market setting the price for peak times, shoulder times costing 50 per cent of peak and off-peak being free. One has to cut peak volume down by only five to 10 per cent to maximize road efficiency. Tolls collected would be available for maintaining the road and for improving public transit.
The same logic applies to congestion in city cores. One needs time-of-day congestion charges to eliminate gridlock. Removing bike lanes will only make matters worse. Motorists are paying a heavy price for idling their vehicles in traffic. Their time is valuable and they are placing their health at risk by breathing polluted air.
John Newell, Toronto
It seems to me that Ford should be going up, not down. Instead of blowing hundreds of billions of our dollars on a tunnel under Highway 401, it would make more sense to build up, above the road.
Another set of lanes could be built over the existing 401 for substantially less money than boring a tunnel underneath it would cost. The savings could be used to recruit more family doctors. Tens of thousands of Ford's constituents don't have a physician they can go to, and that should be more of a priority during his next expensive and unnecessary "mandate."
Don Williams, Thornhill
Words to the wise
Ford Bucks go a long way. I have found a good way to use the politically motivated $200 that the premier sent Ontarians. I will give $200 to Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, and I get back $150 as a tax credit. I give this $150 to the federal party of my choice and get back $112 as a tax credit. Then I give the $112 to a local charity and get back $37 as a tax credit.
In total, I give away $462 and I still have $37 left for a free lunch on Ford's dime.
Glen Wedlock, Newmarket

Title: Letters:   Dear Canada, please accept our apologies, Feb. 4 U.S. turmoil has created an opportunity for Canada to lure top talent - if we act fast enough, Feb. 6 Doug Ford vows to forge ahead with tunnel under Hwy. 401, Feb. 8

Document 201 of 500

Ford got knocked off his early election script

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-got-knocked-off-his-early-election-script/docview/3166971313/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+got+knocked+off+his+early+election+script&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Elections matter - no debate there.
Campaigns matter - no question there.
Do debates matter? Good question.
On Monday night, voters will be able to watch the one and only widely televised election debate of the campaign.
But on Friday afternoon, we got a sneak peek at the playbooks of the four rival leaders.
You may have missed it. But thanks to the little-noticed Northern Ontario debate - a traditional ritual of provincial election campaigns - we got to watch the leaders poking and prodding and interrupting each other in a dress rehearsal that proved revealing.
News flash: Doug Ford was thrown off his game.
Not because Ford flopped, but because he was forced off his canned campaign script. Friday's debate changed the channel by challenging the agenda Ford has been foisting on voters since he triggered this absurdly early election.
As the Progressive Conservative leader seeking re-election as premier - 15 months ahead of schedule - he has been hiding behind the pretext that he needed an urgent mandate to stand up to Donald Trump's threatened tariffs. That never made sense, because there are no discernible disagreements to debate in rebuffing the U.S. president.
How do you fight an election in this province when every politician is on the same side as the premiers from all the other provinces? On the campaign trail, it's hard for the other party leaders to stand out while Ford asserts he's standing up for Ontario.
On a debate stage, there's a level playing field. Without the megaphone of government, every leader gets a microphone of their own.
And so on Friday, for the first time, Ford was forced off his talking points by NDP Leader Marit Stiles, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and the Green's Mike Schreiner. Amid the bone chilling cold of this misplaced mid-winter election, the North Bay debate served as a warm-up act for Monday night.
Ford had to face all the other issues he has been trying to avoid - housing shortages, doctor shortages, transportation bottlenecks and the problems plaguing a province of 15 million people.
All that said, the competing politicians sounded eerily similar on one recurring talking point: Tax cuts.
Not just Ford but Crombie, Stiles and Schreiner outdid one another in boasting about their tax-fighter bona fides. For a moment, they might have been sharing a stage together in a Republican presidential primary.
And while the debate had its unscripted moments, the leaders seemed strangely dependent on their briefing books. With the exception of Schreiner, who spoke confidently and extemporaneously, the PC, NDP and Liberal leaders kept glancing down at their three-ring binders for reassurance.
Friday's debate served a larger purpose, just as Monday's will, by offering a platform to the lesser-known party leaders to introduce themselves to audiences.
Stiles relied on double-barrelled attacks against not only Ford's Tories but also Crombie, blaming her for the shortcomings of previous Liberal governments. Only the NDP - which hasn't held power for three decades - offered credibility on the economy, she argued.
"In tough times, who do you trust to be on your side?" was the closing pitch from Stiles, which seems a risky appeal from a party that tends to do poorly in bad economic times.
Crombie countered without missing a beat that she played no part in past Liberal governments (she was mayor of Mississauga): "I'm a new Liberal leader and I'm not taking responsibility for decisions made previously," she said bluntly.
She seemed to find her feet by repeatedly pointing an accusing finger at Ford. Crombie turned his 2022 campaign slogan ("Get it Done") against him by repeating over and over, "He didn't get it done."
To be sure, the perennial northern debate isn't a conventional election debate. It's held in front of a live audience of municipal politicians from the north, not a province-wide TV audience of voters.
But this one broke through because the truly big issues up north, nowadays, are also big issues all across the province (see above: housing shortages, doctor shortages, transportation).
That's the virtue of a debate - no one gets to define the ballot question or limit the topics. Which is why Ford won't agree to more televised debates beyond Monday - scheduled, unsurprisingly, at the dinner hour at the end of the Family Day weekend.
That's because Ford can be too unpredictable for his handlers, too mercurial for the media, too big a target for his opponents. Coasting on his handsome lead in the public opinion polls, the less said the better - he's already landed in trouble for unguarded comments on the campaign trail when he didn't think the public could hear his words.
The PC leader would rather talk about Trump's proclamations from day to day than his own performance from year to year: "This election is about who's going to protect the people of Ontario," Ford intoned gravely, just days after returning from his much-hyped Washington trip empty-handed.
An election campaign is supposed to define differences, so that voters can make an informed and educated democratic choice. Politicians are meant to debate what they disagree about, not restrict themselves to patriotic truisms that they all agree on.
Canada is not for sale. Can we now discuss what matters to Ontarians - before, during and after Trump's presidency?
Maybe on Monday night. Stay tuned.
CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION

Subject: Provincial elections; Political campaigns; Voters; Politicians; Debates; Elections

Document 202 of 500

Ford shows rivals how to meet the moment: Response to tariff threat is sloppy but reveals why Ontarians back him

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-shows-rivals-how-meet-moment/docview/3166970371/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+shows+rivals+how+to+meet+the+moment%3A+Response+to+tariff+threat+is+sloppy+but+reveals+why+Ontarians+back+him&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=OP.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Cometh the hour, cometh the man.
Whether or not the above was spoken by or about Winston Churchill is irrelevant. In a battle between truth and legend, print the legend. When Europe was facing its darkest hour as Nazi Germany's armies spread across the continent, it was Churchill who answered the call and kept the Allied powers in the game long enough to plot and then execute victory.
With the western world once again facing the prospect of blisteringly dark times, this time death by its own hand in the form of a U.S. administration busy knocking away the pillars of its own dominance, the time has come for serious leadership.
And so:
Enter Doug Ford. Yes, Doug Ford. Cometh the hour, cometh the man.
It's an enduring fiction that elections are decided by policies and a rational working through of outcomes. Most elections are vibe shifts. Tired of the (federal) Liberals after over a decade in power? Elect Stephen Harper (and then Justin Trudeau when Harper wears out his welcome). Tired of a decade-plus of Dalton McGuinty and Kathleen Wynne? Elect Doug Ford.
And so it is in the Year of our Lord 2025. Only this time the vibe shift has happened south of the border. The U.S. is now led by a petty, venal mercantilist who has tried to bully and dominate every room he's ever been in. The answer to the problem of Trump isn't so much hyper-literate policy countermeasures as it is street smarts. And Ford has always considered himself the man from Main Street.
No offence to Bonnie Crombie, Marit Stiles or Mike Schreiner, but this just isn't your fight. In this new world, you're a dial-up modem belching out squeals as you try to connect with your audience. Forget Trump. Ford will not be defeated by a forensic retelling of his failings, whether on the cost of freeing our alcohol or on Greenbelt development jobs for the boys. Or by better policy proposals, for that matter. And if you can't defeat Ford, you'll have no hope against Tariff Man.
Ford's response to Trump's tariff threat is exactly what Ontarians most like about him. It might be a bit all over the shop, but it's passionate and pro-The People he's paid to represent, whether in Confederation or around the world. His energy and attitude are what's required at this moment, not business as usual. To pull a Crombie, Stiles or Schreiner out of the drawer right now is to pull the butter knife out at the gun-and-bluster fight.
Take Crombie. A one-time (and wholly unremarkable) federal MP and the former mayor of Mississauga, Crombie says Ontario deserves a premier "who will take this pivotal moment seriously." And why isn't Doug Ford taking "this pivotal moment seriously?" Well, you see, he called an "early election" in the "midst of an economic crisis." Of course, Ford has already won an election in the midst of an economic crisis, in the form of his re-election in 2022 when we were in the depths of market turmoil post-COVID and post-Russia's invasion of Ukraine. It's hard to see why Ontarians would punish him for not waiting until 2026.
More to the point, does Crombie think it's better to tool up at the beginning of a fight or halfway through one? Ford's calculation was that, in a country dying for a change of direction at the top (although now apparently less so with Trudeau going), it was better to reconfirm his mandate before that change happened and before Trump could land more haymakers. This is no great betrayal of voters. Pace Crombie, Ford didn't "quit" on Ontarians, he's putting his faith in them. He's offering to "protect" them in the face of serious adversity.
As for Stiles, when your record is presiding over the selection of Jagmeet Singh as the president of the federal NDP and your acclamation as provincial leader of the Ontario NDP because nobody else could raise the $55,000 entry fee, convincing voters you're the best option to face a problem like Trump is to choose granola over experience. This is undoubtedly harsh on Stiles, but these are harsh times.
Another enduring fiction is that voters like to balance their provincial votes against their federal preferences. In this case, that would mean swerving left in Ontario with Canada set to swerve right when Parliament is reconvened only to be cast into the coming federal election. But the vibe shift in the U.S. means all of Canada will need to be yoked in the same direction - and quickly. It will not be time to break in new leadership or rookie politicians.
And while a federal government led by Pierre Poilievre would itself be reasonably amateur, it would benefit from some consistency and experience across its provincial counterparts. One of the artifacts of the post-Meech Lake period that needs to be reversed in the face of Trump is the decline of Canadian federalism. The country needs one song sheet and people who are ready to sing from it. And Doug Ford has always been strong of voice.
Would it be nice if the Ontario PC website had a section called "policy" on it? Yes. Would it be nice if Ford and his team hadn't bloated the provincial budget? Again, yes. Does any of this matter right now? No. Canadians' minds are concentrated at the prospect of a hanging. They want protection.
Which brings us to another reality often forgotten in politics. There are no options called "perfect." Elections are a choice between flawed options, and most voters go with their guts. What's more, they base their choice on the mood music that surrounds them. And right now, the current mood music is dire. The province needs a brawler with a bit of unpredictability. All of this points to a Ford win.
Cometh the hour, cometh the (flawed) man.
Andrew MacDougall is a
director at Trafalgar Strategy, a U.K.-based consultancy
and is a former director of
communications to former prime minister Stephen Harper.
CAPTION: The iconic portrait of former British prime minister Winston Churchill, taken by photographer Yousuf Karsh in 1941.Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford's response to U.S. tariffs is exactly what Ontarians most like about him: passionate and pro-The People he's paid to represent.National Portrait GalleryCole Burston THE CANADIAN PRESS file photo
CREDIT: Andrew MacDougall Contributor Andrew MacDougall is a director at Trafalgar Strategy, a U.K.-based consultancy and is a former director of communications to former prime minister Stephen Harper.;

Title: Ford shows rivals how to meet the moment:   Response to tariff threat is sloppy but reveals why Ontarians back him

Document 203 of 500

Please: Anyone but Doug Ford: His stunning failure to address the crises in housing, health care, education and more means he's unfit to lead, writes Justin Ling

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/please-anyone-doug-ford/docview/3166970297/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Please%3A+Anyone+but+Doug+Ford%3A+His+stunning+failure+to+address+the+crises+in+housing%2C+health+care%2C+education+and+more+means+he%27s+unfit+to+lead%2C+writes+Justin+Ling&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=OP.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: "Doug Ford here," the Progressive Conservative leader texted me recently. "I will stand up and fight US tariffs to save Ontario jobs." He informed me that I have two options: "REPLY > YES if you're with me and the PC team." Or, I could reply "stop to stop."
This column is my reply: stop.
Doug Ford is on track for a crushing third majority government. Voters - enthusiastic to endorse the incumbent in the face of a looming trade war, fond of Ford's moderately antagonist relationship with the federal government, charmed by his folksy aw-shucks sensibility - are replying a lower-case "yes." Nobody seems to think Doug Ford is good, but most seem to believe that he's good enough for this moment.
I may be a Toronto émigré, but please hear me out: Vote for anybody else.
Doug Ford is uniquely unqualified and unsuited to be a leader for this moment. His years in office have been defined by shortages - a lack of homes, doctors, teachers, jobs, skilled workers, subways, buses, and bike lanes. Worse yet, his plan for a third term is ambitious only in its plan to build a massive new white elephant.
Let's start with housing, inarguably the priority for any incoming government. But, somehow, Ford remains indifferent. Ontario is, in the full swing of a housing crisis, breaking ground on fewer units of new housing today than it was in 2012. It's a stunning failure.
The single most effective policy to reverse this anemic trend would be legalizing the construction of fourplexes - a move that would also unlock billions in federal housing money. Ford has refused. In 2024, Toronto saw its housing starts fall 20 per cent. In Montreal, where fourplexes are permitted, they rose 15 per cent.
There are few problems in Ontario which don't flow out of this acute housing shortage. Tent encampments, rising crime, the opioid crisis, sluggish growth, a stagnant culture, and declining productivity: All things that could be ameliorated by cheaper, abundant housing. Permitting and supercharging this "missing middle" would mean more artists in cheap lofts, more rooming houses for people who have fallen on hard times, more two-bedroom units for working class people.
His abdication of duty doesn't end there. Ontario needs more transit. Here, Ford deserves genuine credit for pushing ahead the Ontario Line and committing to a bold extension of the GO Train network - although these two projects are just barely dragging Toronto into a transit network for the new millennium. Ontario needs to be planning for 2050 and beyond. What's more, Ford has consistently micromanaged and meddled in various transit projects - only to see them marred by cost overruns, massive delays, and outright bungling. Other cities across the province desperately need to scale up transit options now, but the province hasn't been nearly as attentive to their needs.
The PC leader's nominal commitment to mass public transit is undercut by the fact that he is heaping subsidies onto motorists. He has cut the gas tax, promised to remove the tolls on Highway 407, and assumed the $2-billion cost of renovating the ailing Gardiner Expressway.
The costs of driving - high fuel prices, costly parking premiums, the useless toiling in gridlock - reflect that we have too many drivers on the road. Ford's euphemistic pledge to "make driving more affordable" is really a pledge to subsidize the cost of driving, which in turn will put more cars on the road.
If we actually want to reduce costs on those who need to drive, we need to take more vehicles off the road. Give people the freedom to take trains, streetcars, subways, and buses, and we can make our cities more efficient. Just look at New York City, where congestion pricing has reduced traffic and improved transit service.
His pathological NIMBYism aside, Ford isn't just bad for Ontario's urban centres. Across the province, the ratio of teachers-to-students is bad and getting worse. Its economic growth outlook is poor. And its health care is measurably worsening. These problems are all felt more acutely outside the big cities.
There was a time when a decline in the quality of Ontario's health-care system was grounds to oust a premier. Not anymore, apparently. Ontario's medicare system has slid into abject disrepair, and Ford's futzing around the edges seems to be making it worse. Hallway medicine is now the norm and Ford's solution has been to use public money to underwrite sparkling new knee replacement clinics for the wealthy.
Let's return, unfortunately, to the pandemic. Over that crisis, Ford banned children from playgrounds, which were perfectly safe, but refused to close down high-risk workplaces. Both Ontarians angry at the avoidable COVID-19 deaths and those furious at the curtailment of their civil liberties should unite in the feeling that Doug Ford does not deserve another term in office.
Even Ford's effort to (finally) liberalize Ontario's byzantine liquor laws has been a mess. He initially delayed the measure, then sped it up to coincide with his snap election. Now Ontario is stuck paying $500 million to private companies that run Ontario's inefficient and much-hated beer and wine monopolies. Why?
Ford is addicted to giving private companies public money, often for no benefit to the province. It's also no wonder the stench of corruption hangs over his government. The RCMP is currently pursuing a criminal investigation into how and why lands in the Greenbelt were opened up for development by the Ford government. What's more, the auditor general found that the PCs "favoured certain developers" during that process.
Ontarians are going to the polls right now because Ford believes his cartoonish pushback against Donald Trump will be popular. By donning a funny hat, Ford has become a mascot for the country when we need some hokey relief. But this admittedly comforting performance should be undercut by the fact that Ford cheered on Trump's victory, even though this trade war was a plainly evident outcome.
I don't envy the voters of Ontario. Picking between the Liberals and NDP is like being made to choose which scented bubbles you want for your tepid bath of managed decline. The Greens, long the most thoughtful party on the ballot, are well suited to be in opposition but are probably not ready for government. And while there are great individual progressive conservative candidates, they are beholden to a shameless and inept leader.
But while Ford may call himself a conservative, his two terms in office have been anything but. There is nothing conservative about his debt-ridden era of corporate welfare and mismanagement.
He has had nearly seven years to fix the province, and he hasn't. What is he proposing for the next four years?
Ford's health-care plan is absolutely anemic, little more than a vague hand-wave at the crisis. If he has actual new plans to boost housing construction, he's certainly been holding out on us. Does he have a real plan to improve education, reduce homelessness, hire more doctors, provide care for people struggling with addiction, or get real economic growth started again? No.
What Doug Ford has is a big dumb tunnel.
In his quixotic crusade to have the Ontario public subsidize his commute, Ford wants to bore underneath Highway 401, which cuts through the middle of Toronto - all for the marginal benefit of the 400,000 vehicles that drive over that section of the 401 each day. It is a plan that is impractical, unwise, wasteful, destructive, ugly, unnecessary, and certain to worsen traffic in the city for at least a decade. It is perhaps one of the single most offensively stupid policies pitched in this country in recent memory. (Right up there with a tunnel to Newfoundland proposed by the federal Liberals in 2021, and swiftly forgotten.)
Experts suggest that Ford's 50-kilometre tunnel would cost at least $60 billion, probably more. By comparison, Montreal's Réseau Express Métropolitain - its nearly-finished 67-kilometre light-rail system connecting the whole island, the airport and the suburbs - cost less than $8 billion. That means potholes in North Bay could remain unfilled because the leader of the Progressive Conservatives wants to plow tens of billions of dollars to add yet more lanes onto one of the world's widest highways.
It's a perfectly fitting metaphor for how Doug Ford does things. Ontario desperately needs a leader with genuine ambition on how to fix things. But Ford's plan for the province is only bold insofar as it is a daring attempt to waste an ungodly sum of money on something profoundly and genuinely wrong-headed.
So please, god, text Doug Ford right now and tell him: stop.
Justin Ling is an independent investigative journalist based in Montreal and a contributing columnist for the Star.
Reach him by email:
hello@justinling.ca
CAPTION: Ontarians are going to the polls because Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford believes his cartoonish pushback against Donald Trump will be popular, Justin Ling writes. But voters shouldn't forget that Ford cheered on Trump's victory, even though this trade war was a plainly evident outcome.Steve RussellToronto Star

Document 204 of 500

Liberals and NDP a candidate short: Tough talk on trade and health care on campaign trail

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/liberals-ndp-candidate-short/docview/3166970290/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberals+and+NDP+a+candidate+short%3A+Tough+talk+on+trade+and+health+care+on+campaign+trail&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Health care was front and centre on the campaign trail this week, as Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford took a trip to Washington on a tariff-fighting mission - and, upon returning to Ontario, had to explain a "poor-taste joke" he made about capital punishment. On Friday, all four party leaders were in North Bay for their first debate. On Monday, they will be in Toronto for their second, and last, before the Feb. 27 vote.
Here are the top takeaways:
On the campaign trail
The PCs and Greens are running candidates in all 124 provincial ridings, while the Liberals and NDP each unexpectedly ended up one short just before nominations closed. In Eglinton-Lawrence, the NDP candidate bowed out, saying she didn't want to split votes on the political left, and would instead be supporting Liberal Vince Gasparro. In Windsor West, the Liberals aren't running anyone against incumbent NDP candidate Lisa Gretzky, telling the Star it's "too bad Moe Chehab isn't running" - the candidate the party wanted to field - but the Liberals otherwise "have a strong slate of candidates across the province."
The time wasn't right for the Liberals on Monday, when Leader Bonnie Crombie unexpectedly started her announcement half an hour early in Toronto, as reporters were still en route from an earlier news conference with the NDP.
Ford also had a tough time Monday, struggling to find the right words when asked about whale deaths at Marineland. "It's terrible of any animal dying in those, whales," he said. "You know, it'd be great just to let them free, but what I understand, if you let them free in the ocean, they're done. They're dead. So we're going to make sure that they still take care of these whales."
The NDP is asking the province's integrity commissioner to investigate whether Ford broke ethics rules in a social media ad posted on his feed with him in Washington that also featured the "Protect Ontario" slogan. The ad was removed and reposted soon after with no PC logos.
Campaign promises
The PCs pledged to ban Chinese components and equity in all government energy, critical mineral or infrastructure projects.
The NDP wants to legislate nurse-patient ratios, and hire 15,000 nurses over three years. Its northern strategy promised 911 in all communities, 350 new doctors and more spaces at the Northern Ontario School of Medicine, as well as home and long-term care for First Nations and Francophone patients in their language.
The Liberals pledged to create 40,000 paid internships for students, get rid of interest on Ontario Student Assistance Program loans and cap international student enrolment while boosting funding for colleges and universities. On Wednesday, they pledged to pay nurses and personal support workers a "living wage" whether they work in hospitals, home care or long-term care.
The Greens revealed their full platform on Wednesday, which included waiving the land transfer tax for first-time homebuyers and cutting taxes for those earning less than $65,000 or households earning less than $100,000. To help with home energy costs, the Greens would provide free heat pumps for households with income of less than $100,000 and interest-free loans for those above that. They would create an "Ontario Foodbelt" to preserve farmland, and also protect the Hudson Bay Lowlands. Echoing the other parties' promises, they also want to hire 3,500 new doctors in the next four years, including 350 for northern Ontario.
In their own words
NDP Leader Marit Stiles on tariffs and Ford calling the U.S. a "customer": "I think Ontarians are looking for a strong pushback and a fight for every single job and every single worker. In this case, I would say the customer is not always right, Doug. In fact, sometimes the customer needs to stand outside for a few minutes."
Bonnie Crombie on the election: "It's Ford against Crombie in this race, and I'm happy to challenge him anytime on his turf - or on mine. And my turf is the gym, so if he wants to come at me and we'll have a competition in the gym, and maybe we'll do push ups. What do you think?"
Crombie also said a Liberal government would help businesses with access to loans, creating "what we call the Fight Tariff Fund, the FTF - or F-Trump Fund too - however you want to take it."
Ford was forced to walk back comments he made to a law enforcement crowd Jan. 29 about those who commit violent home invasions. "God forbid they kill an innocent person. I don't even go 25 years. I send 'em right to sparky and then we'll take everything from there," using slang for the electric chair. His team later said Ford made a "poor-taste joke out of frustration" and "does not support capital punishment but rather firmly believes more needs to be done to keep violent and repeat offenders behind bars where they belong."
CAPTION: Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles released her party's northern platform Thursday in Sudbury. The party promises more 911 services and 350 new doctors, as well as home and long-term care for First Nations and Francophone patients in their native languages.Gino Donato The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

No more mandates for Doug Ford: Ontarians need confidence and stability right now, not another election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/no-more-mandates-doug-ford/docview/3166970235/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=No+more+mandates+for+Doug+Ford%3A+Ontarians+need+confidence+and+stability+right+now%2C+not+another+election&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=OP.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I refuse to be bribed by Premier Doug Ford's $200 gift from the public purse (a gift funded at least partially by my own tax money). It is an unnecessary and opportunistic attempt to extend his own job, despite his claims that he needs a new mandate to enable him to defend Ontario jobs from U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs.
Surely it is part of his job description as premier of Ontario to fight for Ontarians against internal and external threats or interference. Surely that does not require a new mandate. He has not previously found it necessary to acquire a new mandate for any of the other controversial and divisive decisions his government has made.
As I have received the $200 cheque, I have decided that the best use for it is to donate it to one of the opposition parties, and I encourage others to do the same.
Brian Mayhew, Puslinch, Ont.
Trump's tariff proposals were a godsend to Ford. The premier is now seeking a stronger mandate ostensibly in order to convince Ontarians that only he is strong enough to combat the U.S. president.
In the meantime, the snap election slated for Feb. 27 appears to have been purposely planned to make sure the Ford government stays one step ahead of the RCMP investigation into the premier's infamous Greenbelt scandal.
Ford is one shrewd politician. It's shameful.
Jeffrey Manly, Toronto
In my 77 years on this planet, I have never been more disgusted with a premier. He seems able to get away with using the systems of government to his advantage and convincing voters that he is best person for the job. He is totally self-serving and I believe that if he had any sense of decency, he would at least wait another year for an election. People need confidence and stability right now.
Richard Bino, Singhampton, Ont.

Ontario election: Rivals slam Doug Ford on health care, death penalty as he insists he's the best choice to take on Donald Trump (edited)

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-election-rivals-slam-doug-ford-on-health/docview/3167206697/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+election%3A+Rivals+slam+Doug+Ford+on+health+care%2C+death+penalty+as+he+insists+he%27s+the+best+choice+to+take+on+Donald+Trump+%28edited%29&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Bonnie Crombie tried to distance herself from previous Liberal governments in the first leaders' debate of the Feb. 27 election campaign, prompting Doug Ford to growl "what a joke."
In a testy exchange, the Liberal leader said she disagrees with some moves by Liberal premiers Dalton McGuinty and Kathleen Wynne during the 15 years they were in power - a span that ended with a 2018 win by Ford's Progressive Conservatives.
"As you know, I'm a new Liberal leader and I'm not taking responsibility for decisions made previously," Crombie said during a 90-minute debate on northern issues Friday at Canadore College in North Bay.
New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles and Green Leader Mike Schreiner also took shots at Ford in a bid to narrow his comfortable lead in public opinion polls with less than two weeks until voting day.
"After seven years of Doug Ford, we've had a lot of talk but it's never been worse," said Stiles, speaking of a housing shortage and high housing prices, a doctor shortage, worsening hallway health care in overcrowded hospitals and growing homelessness and encampments.
"Where are the homes, Doug?" she said. "There's a whole generation of young people who cannot even imagine buying a home and can't even afford an affordable rental home."
"He said he would end hallway health care," Schreiner added. "Seven years later, it's twice as worse."
Ford said voters should make their ballot decision on which leader is best positioned to stand up to U.S. President Donald Trump and his threatened tariffs that could ravage Ontario's trade-dependent economy.
"This election is about who's going to protect the people of Ontario," said Ford, who was in Washington, D.C. this week to lobby U.S. lawmakers.
He stuck to his campaign script, urging voters to give him "a strong, stable four-year mandate" to outlast Trump.
"The stronger the mandate, the better."
On her differences with previous Liberal governments, Crombie cited the cancellation a decade ago of the Northlander train connecting northern residents from Cochrane and points south to Toronto.
Ford has promised to restore it, but service has not yet resumed despite a plan to purchase new trains and a pledge to have them running by mid-decade.
"I support the Northlander. It should never have been closed," Crombie said. "We'll be sure to open it because he hasn't gotten it done in the last seven years."
Ford warned opposition parties would "tax the people to death" and said Crombie raised taxes annually during the almost 10 years she was mayor of Mississauga, causing some residents to leave.
"Her city shrunk - the only city in Ontario that actually shrunk over a population boom. Unheard of," added Ford, who is seeking a third term as premier in a snap election he called at the end of January instead of waiting until the next scheduled vote in June 2026.
The election, which Ford's rivals call an unnecessary and expensive distraction from pressing issues on the home front, will cost $189 million.
After the debate, opposition leaders took Ford to task over his off-the-cuff remarks at a police chief's gala in London two weeks ago that home-invasion killers should be sent "right to sparky" - a reference to the electric chair.
Ford's campaign scrambled to walk back the remarks after they were first reported in the Star, saying they were made in "poor taste" and insisted Ford does not support a return to capital punishment, abolished in 1976.
"We need a premier who's not going to make jokes about the death penalty," Schreiner said as reporters scrummed the leaders for their thoughts on the debate.
Ford left without taking questions.
The capital punishment quip was "disgusting and inappropriate," said Stiles. "He says one thing in front of the public and another thing behind closed doors ... he has shown us who he is."
Crombie said Ford has been unmasked again, as he was in hot mic comments last week about supporting Trump to win the U.S. election.
"It speaks to character," she added. "He was in a room he thought was friendly, and he felt he could say what he wanted, much like that dinner he had with developers when he told them he would open up the Greenbelt."
Hosted by the Federation of Northern Ontario Municipalities, the North Bay debate was the first of two in the four-week campaign. The leaders go head-to-head again Monday at 6:30 p.m. in Toronto. The event will be broadcast live on television.
CAPTION: Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, left to right, NDP Leader Marit Stiles, moderator CBC Radio Morning North host Markus Schwabe, Progressive Conservative Party Leader Doug Ford and Green Leader Mike Schreiner take part in the Ontario election debate in North Bay on Feb. 14, 2025.
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy, Rob Ferguson, Robert Benzie

Document 207 of 500

Is Mark Carney the frontrunner for the Liberal leadership? Conservatives are acting like he is

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/is-mark-carney-frontrunner-liberal-leadership/docview/3167206625/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+Mark+Carney+the+frontrunner+for+the+Liberal+leadership%3F+Conservatives+are+acting+like+he+is&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - The federal Conservatives are amping up criticism of Mark Carney, the Liberal leadership contender they appear to assume will replace Justin Trudeau as their chief opponent in the next federal election.
The criticism comes as the party stages a "Canada First" rally in Ottawa on Saturday, aimed at pitching Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre as the best answer to U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff threats.
While Conservative MPs huddled on Parliament Hill for a daylong caucus meeting Friday, the party released a new attack ad targeting Carney.
In it, the former central banker in Canada and the United Kingdom is portrayed as a supporter of the Trudeau government's "carbon tax" that has sent jobs to the United States.
Carney has pledged to repeal the consumer carbon price, which he deems as "divisive," and replace it with green incentives funded by industries with heavy emissions that cause climate change.
The new Conservative ad also suggests Carney is a hypocrite for previously opposing at least some Canadian fossil fuel development, even as an asset management company he worked for until running for Liberal leadership supported the industry in other countries.
The ad concludes with the line: "Carbon Tax Carney: if Carney wins, Canada loses."
In an emailed statement, Carney campaign spokesperson Emily Williams said Poilievre is pulling from "Donald Trump's playbook with empty and misleading slogans.
"While Pierre continues to think about Mark, Mark will continue to focus on his real plan to build up our economy and stand up for Canadian workers against President Trump's threats," Williams said.
Former finance minister Chrystia Freeland, former House leader Karina Gould and former MPs Frank Baylis and Ruby Dhalla are also running for the Liberal leadership.
But it is Carney on whom the Conservatives are directing their fire.
Michael Barrett, a Conservative MP from eastern Ontario, demanded Friday that Carney disclose information about his private investments after he served as an economic adviser to the Liberal party starting last fall.
Andrew Scheer, the Regina MP and former Conservative leader, took a run at the former central banker over comments he made during a leadership campaign speech in Kelowna, B.C.
Video from the event posted online by KelownaNow shows Carney - speaking about how Trump has demanded Canada crack down on the deadly opioid fentanyl - saying the opioid crisis is a "challenge" in Canada but a "crisis" in the U.S.
Scheer accused Carney of "downplaying the crisis that Canadians are suffering from," which he alleged was made worse by Liberal government drug policies that included allowing some jurisdictions to decriminalize drugs in an effort to provide safer supplies.
"To refuse to call it a crisis and just call it a challenge shows that he doesn't have to deal with these types of consequences of these policies. He lives in a completely different world," said Scheer, whose party is promising to bring in mandatory life sentences for people who traffic fentanyl.
According to Health Canada, 49,105 people were reported to have died from opioid toxicity from January 2016 to June 2024.
Deputy Conservative leader Melissa Lantsman also accused Carney of planning to bring in a "new and bigger carbon tax" that will hamper Canadian competitiveness at a time of insecurity.
Alongside preserving the industrial carbon pricing requirement introduced by the Liberals in 2019, Carney has said he would pursue a border tariff to protect Canadian businesses from losing out to products produced in countries with weaker environmental policies.
While Poilievre has been clear he would also scrap the consumer carbon price, he has not clarified what he would do with the national requirement for industrial carbon pricing.
Since Trump returned to the presidency in January, much of the Canadian political discussion has revolved around dealing with his threat of tariffs. The Conservatives, who until recently have hammered the government over cost-of-living concerns, are now routinely accusing the Liberals of leaving Canada vulnerable to Trump's threats.
In the Liberal leadership race, contenders have framed Trump's tariffs as the core issue. On Saturday night, Carney was scheduled to make a speech and host a viewing party for a hockey game between Canada and the U.S.
Earlier this week, Carney told supporters in Kelowna, B.C. that the U.S. wants Canada's resources, water and land, and that annexation would "destroy our way of life."
Carney also said he cut taxes for the "middle class," and balance the government's "spending budget" within three years.
Williams said Friday that Carney was talking about Ottawa's operating budget - not the entire federal deficit, which grew to more than $61 billion in the most recent fiscal year.
CAPTION: Mark Carney speaks during a news conference in Vancouver on Feb. 13, 2025.
CREDIT: Alex Ballingall

Document 208 of 500

Doug Ford is far from a perfect leader. Here's why his opponents still aren't gaining ground

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-is-far-perfect-leader-heres-why-his/docview/3167206600/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+is+far+from+a+perfect+leader.+Here%27s+why+his+opponents+still+aren%27t+gaining+ground&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In all the years I have been conducting public opinion research, I have never seen the mood of the Ontario electorate as bleak and negative as it is today.
Concerns about affordability which have dominated the public opinion agenda for two years persist, leaving vast swaths of the population feeling they are treading water or falling behind. Concerns about a deteriorating health system have eroded what was once a source of pride and viewed as a hallmark differentiator between Canada and other nations. Added to these are anxieties about other serious issues including global conflict and threats from the U.S.
Normally, this type of mood and sentiments are very bad news for a governing party because it means they are running against the most formidable opponent an incumbent can face in electoral politics - "time for a change". Virtually all polls published in the second week of the Ontario election campaign would indicate that Doug Ford has - at least for the time being - been able to avoid this confrontation. He and his team have been able to accomplish this by a combination of clever (some might say cynical and opportunist) strategy and luck.
By choosing to call an early election, before the federal parties go to the polls, he has been able to ride the tail winds on a very unpopular Liberal brand - the same one that also puts Pierre Poilievre 20 points ahead in some national polls.
Even more fortuitously, Donald Trump's chaotic behaviour and threats have left Ontarians feeling even more vulnerable to forces beyond their control.
The latter has not only allowed Ford to run against an opponent who is more unpopular and isn't in the race - Trump - it has also let him put masculinity on the ballot.
Indeed, as we have seen in the States and throughout the western world, and as pollsters are finding more and more, a sense of vulnerability and massive uncertainty has produced a voter demand for strong man protection and authoritative solutions - without being too flip about what is clearly a serious and potentially dangerous scenario, voters seem to be looking for "Dad." Whether or not it is warranted, Ford - more than Stiles or Crombie - projects an image of strength to voters.
With a lead of more than 20 points, Doug Ford and his PCs do not have to attract a single new vote to guarantee a massive electoral victory in two weeks time - he just needs to make sure they don't lose a meaningful portion of their current base to the Liberals and NDP.
However, a big part of Liberal leader Bonnie Crombie and NDP leader Marit Stiles' dilemma is that, before they have any chance of attracting these current PC supporters, they have to get Ontarians to know who they are.
With almost one half of the population not having any feelings, one way or another, about Stiles and over one third expressing the same ambivalence towards Crombie, why would Ford raise their profiles by even mentioning them, let alone engaging in any serious debates which also might cast a light on issues where he does not fare as well? Better to put on his "Dad" hat and fight Donald Trump by going to Washington or from his chair as head of The Council of the Federation - and ignore Crombie and Stiles.
And this is where the dilemma for the Liberals and NDP has the potential to enter the realm of moral hazard.
Even if Crombie and Stiles could raise their profiles and approval ratings before the February 27 election day, and even if they could find a way to exploit Ford's vulnerabilities in this short period of time, the fact is current PC voters aren't particularly inclined to switch their vote to either the Liberals or the NDP ... but Liberal voters are very likely to consider the NDP as their second choice and NDP voters are equally likely to choose the Liberals as their second choice. In other words, it is much easier - and more likely - for both opposition parties to make gains at the other's expense than it is for them to erode the PC base in a way that could change the outcome of the election.
And both opposition camps are acutely aware of this. If there is no movement in the polls in the next week, their focus could very well turn to one another in an attempt to win the second prize of Official Opposition.
Should Ford win this election ... and should Pierre Poilievre repeat in spring or early summer, it would not surprise me at all to see a serious conversation take place between the Liberals and NDP about confronting this reality by merging.
CREDIT: Allan Gregg, Contributor

Subject: Public opinion; Political campaigns; Voters

People: Trump, Donald J; Poilievre, Pierre

Title: Doug Ford is far from a perfect leader. Here's why his opponents still aren't gaining ground

Document 209 of 500

Doug Ford got knocked off his phoney early election pitch during the leaders' debate. Will it happen again?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-got-knocked-off-his-phoney-early/docview/3167206518/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+got+knocked+off+his+phoney+early+election+pitch+during+the+leaders%27+debate.+Will+it+happen+again%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Elections matter - no debate there.
Campaigns matter - no question there.
Do debates matter? Good question.
On Monday night, voters will be able to watch the one and only widely-televised election debate of the campaign.
But on Friday afternoon, we got a sneak peek at the playbooks of the four rival leaders.
You may have missed it. But thanks to the little-noticed Northern Ontario debate - a traditional ritual of provincial election campaigns - we got to watch the leaders poking and prodding and interrupting each other in a dress rehearsal that proved revealing.
News flash: Doug Ford was thrown off his game.
Not because Ford flopped, but because he was forced off his canned campaign script. Friday's debate changed the channel by challenging the agenda Ford has been foisting on voters since he triggered this absurdly early election.
As the Progressive Conservative leader seeking re-election as premier - 15 months ahead of schedule - he has been hiding behind the pretext that he needed an urgent mandate to stand up to Donald Trump's threatened tariffs. That never made sense, because there are no discernible disagreements to debate in rebuffing the U.S. president.
How do you fight an election in this province when every politician is on the same side as the premiers from all the other provinces? On the campaign trail, it's hard for the other party leaders to stand out while Ford asserts he's standing up for Ontario.
On a debate stage, there's a level playing field. Without the megaphone of government, every leader gets a microphone of their own.
And so on Friday, for the first time, Ford was forced off his talking points by NDP Leader Marit Stiles, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and the Green's Mike Schreiner. Amid the bone chilling cold of this misplaced mid-winter election, the North Bay debate served as a warm-up act for Monday night.
Ford had to face all the other issues he has been trying to avoid - housing shortages, doctor shortages, transportation bottlenecks and the problems plaguing a province of 15 million people.
All that said, the competing politicians sounded eerily similar on one recurring talking point: Tax cuts.
Not just Ford but Crombie, Stiles and Schreiner outdid one another in boasting about their tax-fighter bona fides. For a moment, they might have been sharing a stage together in a Republican presidential primary.
And while the debate had its unscripted moments, the leaders seemed strangely dependent on their briefing books. With the exception of Schreiner, who spoke confidently and extemporaneously, the PC, NDP and Liberal leaders kept glancing down at their three-ring binders for reassurance.
Friday's debate served a larger purpose, just as Monday's will, by offering a platform to the lesser-known party leaders to introduce themselves to audiences.
Stiles relied on double-barrelled attacks against not only Ford's Tories but also Crombie, blaming her for the shortcomings of previous Liberal governments. Only the NDP - which hasn't held power for three decades - offered credibility on the economy, she argued.
"In tough times, who do you trust to be on your side?" was the closing pitch from Stiles, which seems a risky appeal from a party that tends to do poorly in bad economic times.
Crombie countered without missing a beat that she played no part in past Liberal governments (she was mayor of Mississauga): "I'm a new Liberal leader and I'm not taking responsibility for decisions made previously," she said bluntly.
She seemed to find her feet by repeatedly pointing an accusing finger at Ford. Crombie turned his 2022 campaign slogan ("Get it Done") against him by repeating over and over, "He didn't get it done."
To be sure, the perennial northern debate isn't a conventional election debate. It's held in front of a live audience of municipal politicians from the north, not a province-wide TV audience of voters.
But this one broke through because the truly big issues up north, nowadays, are also big issues all across the province (see above: housing shortages, doctor shortages, transportation).
That's the virtue of a debate - no one gets to define the ballot question or limit the topics. Which is why Ford won't agree to more televised debates beyond Monday - scheduled, unsurprisingly, at the dinner hour at the end of the Family Day weekend.
That's because Ford can be too unpredictable for his handlers, too mercurial for the media, too big a target for his opponents. Coasting on his handsome lead in the public opinion polls, the less said the better - he's already landed in trouble for unguarded comments on the campaign trail when he didn't think the public could hear his words.
The PC leader would rather talk about Trump's proclamations from day to day than his own performance from year to year: "This election is about who's going to protect the people of Ontario," Ford intoned gravely, just days after returning from his much-hyped Washington trip empty-handed.
An election campaign is supposed to define differences, so that voters can make an informed and educated democratic choice. Politicians are meant to debate what they disagree about, not restrict themselves to patriotic truisms that they all agree on.
Canada is not for sale. Can we now discuss what matters to Ontarians - before, during and after Trump's presidency?
Maybe on Monday night. Stay tuned.
CAPTION: Progressive Conservative Party Leader Doug Ford, left, speaks during the Ontario election debate as Ontario Green Leader Mike Schreiner looks on in North Bay on Feb. 14, 2025.
CREDIT: Martin Regg Cohn

Subject: Provincial elections; Political campaigns; Voters; Politicians; Debates; Elections

Ontario NDP candidate in Eglinton-Lawrence folds campaign to help Liberal opponent win

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-ndp-candidate-eglinton-lawrence-folds/docview/3166818985/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+NDP+candidate+in+Eglinton-Lawrence+folds+campaign+to+help+Liberal+opponent+win&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The NDP hopeful in Eglinton-Lawrence says she withdrew her candidacy to avoid a vote-split that could help Ontario's Progressive Conservatives keep the seat.
In a post on X Thursday evening, Natasha Doyle-Merrick said: "I recognize the race in Eglinton-Lawrence is a clear two-party contest between Liberals and Conservatives. To prevent a Conservative win and more years of neglect, I'm stepping aside to avoid a vote split," on the political left.
In an attached statement, the mental health worker said she made the difficult decision after much reflection, adding that she first sought the NDP nomination because she felt Robin Martin, the riding's PC MPP since 2018, had ignored some voices in the community.
Martin decided to not run again. Michelle Cooper, executive director of the Ontario Progressive Conservative fund, is the Eglinton-Lawrence PC candidate in what is expected to be a close race with Liberal candidate Vince Gasparro, former principal secretary to John Tory when he was Toronto mayor.
Doyle-Merrick wrote: "This election is crucial because the past seven years Doug Ford and the Ontario Conservatives have been absent and ineffective, showing zero interest in delivering the support ... I got into politics for the people and it would be a disservice for me to keep my name on the ballot knowing that the race in Eglinton-Lawrence is pivotal."
She withdrew before the 2 p.m. deadline so her name will not appear on the ballot for the provincial Feb. 27 election.
At publication time, the NDP campaign had not responded to a request for comment. Nor had Cooper or the Ontario PC campaign.
In a statement, Gasparro thanked Doyle-Merrick, saying her decision is "a testament to who she is." He vowed to "welcome everyone into our campaign with open arms."
In a text, the Liberal campaign referenced Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and PC Leader Doug Ford, saying: "Even the other party recognizes that this is a Bonnie versus Doug race."
The Liberals, however, lost a candidate of their own Thursday after the nominations closed in Windsor West without the expected hopeful. Six candidates did register, including one from the "None of the Above Direct Democracy Party," but no Liberal.
Lisa Gretzy, the riding's NDP MPP since 2014, tweeted: "The deadline to register as a candidate has closed and the Liberals decided that Windsor West is not worth fighting for and put (forward) no candidate."
"I'm the only candidate that continues to show up and fight like hell for Windsor West. And I'm the only choice to stop Doug Ford," Gretzy said.
The PCs are hoping their candidate, Tony Francis, can turn the riding blue like those surrounding it.
CAPTION: Natasha Doyle-Merrick has withdrawn her candidacy as the NDP hopeful for Eglinton-Lawrence, saying she doesn't want to split left-leaning votes and help the Progressive Conservative candidate get elected.
CREDIT: David Rider

Subject: Conservatism; Nominations; Elections

Document 211 of 500

That 'sparky' gaffe was Doug Ford being Doug Ford. Here's what's disturbing about that

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/that-sparky-gaffe-was-doug-ford-being-heres-whats/docview/3166747532/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=That+%27sparky%27+gaffe+was+Doug+Ford+being+Doug+Ford.+Here%27s+what%27s+disturbing+about+that&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: On Thursday, the Star broke the news that Ontario Progressive Conservative leader Doug Ford was recorded during a closed-door campaign speech at a London Police Services gala where he said home invaders who kill their victims ought to be sent "right to sparky," the electric chair. In other words: bring back the death penalty. His campaign was quick to clarify, dismissing the remarks as a "poor taste joke."
Ford has a habit of saying things poor taste, just as he has a history of bad judgment, - like supporting Donald Trump. Not that it's going to cost him much. On the day the PC leader was riffing on putting people to death, Philippe Fournier's electoral model at 338 Canada had him cruising to another majority government with 45% of the popular vote and 98 seats out of the legislature's 124. Those stratospheric numbers come not even two weeks after Ford was caught on video saying he'd been"100% happy" that Donald Trump won the US presidential election-- before the president won and promptly stabbed Canada in the chest. They also come after Ford called a cynical, unnecessary, $189 million election in February.
Ford doesn't simply skate by after making what, for other politicians, would be clear gaffes. It's almost as if he's rewarded for them.
During his speech to gathered police, lawyers, and the sorts of people who have money to spend attending a gala, Ford said "You know, I was warned by my campaign 'do not go off script ... do not say anything controversial. You're on a campaign.' Well, that's just not who I am." And then he proved it.
The Star reports the room was uncomfortable with the "sparky" remarks that soon followed. But will Ontarians be so squeamish? After all, Ford is the same guy who said of people on the province's disability support program, "You know what I don't like? . . . Healthy young people that are sitting on the couch watching the Flintstones, they should be out there, they should be working." Yes, he said this, of those who are disabled. This is the same guy who said of immigrants that they are "bringing problems from everywhere else in the world" to Ontario. Yes his campaign has said he was just venting or joking, but it's not such a leap to imagine him being a proponent of the death penalty.
The thing is, he's not alone. Nearly 60% of Ontarians support the death penalty. And I'm willing to bet nearly just as many like Ford's fun-but-not-PC-uncle demeanour. He says what he thinks! He's a regular guy, like me! People like that sort of thing because when it comes to casting a ballot, they ask themselves of politicians, as pollster Allan Gregg recently told me, "Are they for me?" and "Are they like me?"
While the laptop class and progressives might be appalled by Ford's comments, when you log-off and go out into the world, you'll find that what many will see as Ford's "gaffes" are, for others, their beliefs. You might disagree with them (I do) just as you might find them worse than uncouth, but cruel (I do) but there they are, out in the wild, held by real people living real lives.
As premier, as a politician, Ford must at least restrain himself and his impulses a bit. But he is more of himself in public than most politicians, a tendency that is at once endearing in style as it is off putting for those who find his impulse for revenge and his credulous individualism disqualifying. He benefits at once from a plain-spoken relatability that resonates with millions of voters, and from a simplistic, id-driven worldview - force the bums back to work, fry the criminals with ol' sparky, etc. - that, apparently, many Ontarians share with him.
No one is going to beat Ford by attacking him as insufficiently polished, nor will anyone beat him by running against his casual reflections on the death penalty or misunderstanding of social assistance programs. We ought to call him out, yes. But if there's a takeaway from Ford's gaffes for his opponents, it's that like it or not, Ford reflects so much of the province he leads. What does that say about us?
CAPTION: Ontario Progressive Conservative leader Doug Ford's comments about the death penalty were, according to his spokespeople, a joke. Trouble is, writes David Moscrop, many Ontarians agree with him.
CREDIT: David Moscrop Contributor

Subject: Political campaigns; Capital punishment; Politicians
