Ontario election polls see Doug Ford's PC party inching further ahead

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-election-polls-see-doug-fords-pc-party/docview/3166747127/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+election+polls+see+Doug+Ford%27s+PC+party+inching+further+ahead&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With the Ontario election campaign underway, the Star has a poll tracker so you can follow all the twists and turns ahead of election day on Feb. 27.
We've got the numbers from the top pollsters in one place, with handy charts, and a rolling, last-four-poll average of all the results.
Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives are still in first place, having inched a little further ahead of the second-place Liberals in the latest poll.

In Ontario's last election, in 2022, Ford's PCs took 40.8 per cent of the popular vote across the province, with the Liberals earning 23.9 per cent and the NDP taking 23.7 per cent. That led Ford to his second straight majority government, with 83 seats. Despite finishing narrowly behind the Liberals in the popular vote, the NDP took 31 seats and the Liberals just eight.
The Star is compiling polling for the 2025 Ontario election from polling firms that received a grade of A or A+ from 338Canada during the 2022 Ontario election. 338Canada is an independent media organization that created a statistical model of electoral projections based on opinion polls, electoral history, and demographic data.
These polling firms use a variety of polling methods, including online surveys and Smart Interactive Voice Response (IVR) systems. As online polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, margins listed here are for a comparable random sample of this size.
Percentage breakdowns are shares of committed or leaning voters, in line with industry conventions.

Document 214 of 500

Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie stands by candidate who made light of intimate partner violence

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/liberal-leader-bonnie-crombie-stands-candidate/docview/3166747014/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberal+Leader+Bonnie+Crombie+stands+by+candidate+who+made+light+of+intimate+partner+violence&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie is standing by a candidate despite his social media posts making light of intimate partner violence in the wake for former CBC host Jian Ghomeshi's legal troubles.
"These are tweets from over a decade ago, and the candidate has apologized," Liberal campaign spokeswoman Bahoz Dara Aziz said Friday in regard to Brian Hamilton, running in Thunder Bay-Superior North.
Hamilton, a Thunder Bay councillor and coffee shop owner hoping to wrest the riding from the NDP's Lise Vaugeois in the Feb. 27 election, said he is a different person now.
"I made comments online that do not reflect my current views. That doesn't excuse them, but I have learned and grown over that time. I apologize and would not make those same comments today," Hamilton wrote in a statement.
The posts were flagged Friday morning in a news release from Doug Ford's Progressive Conservative campaign, saying they "not only glorified an individual associated with abusive behaviour against women but also trivialized the pain experienced by victims of intimate partner violence."
Hamilton's tweets reference Ghomeshi, who was fired by CBC over sex allegations in 2014 and acquitted two years later on charges of choking to overcome resistance and sexual assault related to three women.
Another sexual assault charge against Ghomeshi was withdrawn but he did apologize in court for "sexually inappropriate behaviour" and admitted he "crossed boundaries" in the workplace.
"I miss Jian Ghomeshi, even if he is an ass slapping lady choker. His peculiarities don't neutralize his genius," Hamilton wrote on Twitter in December 2014.
In another post, Hamilton said Ghomeshi's departure from pop culture radio show Q made it "like a flower without the pedals (sic). A wind without the chill. A date without a kiss. Sad days @CBC."
He later added in a reply, "Or a date without the slap/choke. Whichever you prefer."
The tweets remained on his X account on Friday morning, along with others including "as a tribute to International Women's Day I'm going to be wrong all day" in 2015.
Another post that year read: "A new workout routine by Taylor Swift. It's where you cry yourself to sleep and wake up a soulless bitch. Swiftercise!"
Crombie's refusal to drop Hamilton as a candidate suggests the Liberals are struggling, Ford's campaign said in a statement a day after he came under fire for making a "poor taste" remark about capital punishment.
"After being unable to field a full slate of candidates for a second consecutive election, it is no surprise that Bonnie Crombie and the desperate Liberal Party of Ontario are willing to stand by candidates who have made disgusting misogynistic comments."
The controversy over Hamilton renews concerns over the vetting and screening of candidates by the Liberals.
The party fired its Parry Sound-Muskoka candidate in the 2022 election for a book in which he espoused his unproven a theory that homosexuality is caused by children "rebreathing" their own air.
CAPTION: Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie appears at an Ontario election event at George Brown College in Toronto, on Feb. 11, 2025.
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson

Subject: Domestic violence; Candidates; Sex crimes; Elections

Document 215 of 500

Carney won't rule out calling early election: NDP members told to expect snap vote if Liberal leadership candidate wins contest

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/carney-wont-rule-out-calling-early-election/docview/3166487513/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Carney+won%27t+rule+out+calling+early+election%3A+NDP+members+told+to+expect+snap+vote+if+Liberal+leadership+candidate+wins+contest&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Liberal leadership contender Mark Carneyis not ruling out calling an early election if he wins the party's leadership and replaces Justin Trudeau as prime minister on March 9.
At the same time, the Liberal party says it has told its organizers to set up campaign offices next month, when the Liberals choose their new leader and the minority government faces election-hungry opposition parties with the return of Parliament.
At a press conference in Vancouver on Thursday, Carney said that as Liberal leader he "will do" what is best for the country as it faces punishing trade tariffs from the United States. That could mean recalling Parliament or calling an early election, he said.
"If Parliament needs to be recalled for certain reasons, it will be," Carney said Thursday. "If it makes sense to get a strong mandate at that point, that is what will follow."
As it stands, Parliament is set to return on March 24, after Trudeau suspended the session with a prorogation when he announced his intention to resign as prime minister on Jan. 6.
All three main opposition parties - the Conservatives, Bloc Québécois and New Democrats - have said they want an election and would vote down the Liberal minority government as soon as possible.
NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh, however, has also said the government should speak to opposition parties about a possible relief package for Canadian workers who could face job losses in a trade war with the U.S.
But the NDP is also gearing up for a snap election that they expect Carney to call if he wins the Liberal leadership.
In an internal memo sent Wednesday to candidates and staff, the New Democrats' national campaign director urged party members to prepare for an election call as early as March 10, one day after Trudeau's successor is chosen.
"It is becoming more and more likely that Mark Carney will be the next Liberal leader on March 9," Jennifer Howard says in the memo obtained by the Star.
"If Mark Carney does win the leadership, we are hearing from many Ottawa sources that he intends on calling an election shortly after becoming Liberal leader."
Other leadership campaigns, with candidates who - unlike Carney - currently have seats in the House of Commons, were explicit about trying to work in the minority Parliament.
"As a sitting member of Parliament, Chrystia Freeland is ready to work with Parliament and all political parties to fight Donald Trump and to fight for Canada," said Freeland's spokesperson Katherine Cuplinskas.
Responding by email, a spokesperson for the Karina Gould campaign said they don't believe Canadians want an election right now.
"We're focused on fighting for Canadians interests and ensuring Parliament works to support Canadians and pass critical legislation," said spokesperson Emily Jackson. "We hope the other parties will be able to come together to keep Parliament functioning and not play politics."
Former MP Frank Baylis's campaign, meanwhile, has said he would not call an early election.
Two Liberal MPs confirmed to the Star on Thursday that the party has asked organizers to get ready for a coming general election campaign, though neither of them found the message unusual, given the overall political situation.
"The party is recommending that candidates start to look for campaign offices, get ready for an election," said one MP, who spoke about internal party matters on condition of anonymity.
In an emailed statement to the Star, Liberal spokesperson Parker Lund confirmed the party wants campaign teams to be ready with offices "in March," when the House is set to return.
Alleging Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre "can't be trusted to stand up for Canadians against Trump," Lund said "the Liberal Party of Canada is ready to fight and win the next election, whenever it may arise."
The Liberals currently have candidates nominated in 146 of the 343 ridings where Canadians will vote in the next election.
In the NDP memo, which was first reported by the Globe and Mail, Howard reiterates the NDP's position that Parliament be recalled to deal with U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff threats, but says no Liberal leadership contestant has reached out to opposition leaders about passing legislation in Parliament that deals with Trump.
"On that basis, we believe the rumours of Mr. Carney calling a snap election are credible. Taking a page from Doug Ford's playbook, the Liberals believe that they can turn this crisis into a political benefit for them," Howard said.
"For that reason, I am asking all campaigns to be prepared for an election call to come as early as March 10th."
The NDP says it has about 140 candidates nominated so far.
Vandana Kattar, a Liberal strategist who worked in Trudeau's office for more than five years, said she doesn't think Carney would call an immediate election if he is elected as Liberal leader.
She said she would expect the former central banker, who has no experience in electoral politics, to want to take some time to understand the Liberal party political apparatus, get briefed by government officials and deal with Trump's tariff threats.
She said it also makes sense the NDP is trying to prepare its staff in an effort to motivate them, since she sees Carney bringing momentum to the Liberals.
The Conservatives, meanwhile, have been targeting Carney for criticism, describing the current government as the "Carney-Trudeau" Liberals. They've alleged that, while in the private sector, Carney supported construction of fossil fuel pipelines in other countries, despite agreeing with the government's 2016 decision to reject the Northern Gateway pipeline through northern British Columbia.
And they contend the former central banker - who has previously espoused support for carbon pricing - can't be trusted when he says he will ditch the Trudeau government's consumer carbon price-and-rebate system and replace it with a scheme of industry-paid green incentives.
On Thursday, the Conservatives accused Carney of downplaying opioid overdoses in Canada after he referred to fentanyl as a "challenge" in Canada as opposed to a "crisis" in the U.S.
Trump has criticized Canada for allowing fentanyl to cross the border, and has used that to justify the threat of tariffs, even though less than one per cent of fentanyl intercepted by U.S. border agents comes through the Canadian border.
Sarah Fischer, a spokesperson for the Conservative party, said the Tories will be ready with "a full slate of candidates and a full bank account" whenever the next election happens.
With files from
Tonda MacCharles
CREDIT: Mark Ramzy and Alex Ballingall Toronto Star With files from Tonda MacCharles

Party leaders' transportation pledges miss the mark

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/party-leaders-transportation-pledges-miss-mark/docview/3166487319/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Party+leaders%27+transportation+pledges+miss+the+mark&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario election 2025: Doug Ford vows to forge ahead with tunnel under Hwy. 401, Feb. 8
The NDP's first election plank? Abolish road tolls. Is the war on the car over? Feb. 3
Recent pledges by some of the party leaders around transportation and relieving gridlock range from the absurd to the myopic. Premier Doug Ford is promoting his pet GTA tunnel - price be damned -and talking about removing tolls from the publicly owned portion of Hwy 407. Yet, he still won't tell us what the destructive Hwy. 413 will cost. NDP leader Marit Stiles' pledge to remove all tolls on the 407ETR is also ill-conceived. Those plans will cost a lot of money for benefits that will be short-lived when induced demand inevitably leads to more congestion within a few years. There are many better ways to reduce gridlock - including truck accommodation on the 407ETR, targeted existing highway expansion and, of course, major transit expansion (much of which has been proposed in recent years). The $100 billion and 20 years that building a vanity tunnel will likely need could be used to create a world-class mass transit system. That's a far more attractive image to envision.
Richard Schertzer, Milton
Premier Ford has been a disaster for OntarioDoug Ford wants to protect us from Donald Trump. But who will protect us from Doug Ford? Feb. 5
Are Ontarians better off than we were seven years ago? The answer is no. Just follow the money and the deals and policies to get a sense how badly Ontario has fared under the Ford Government. Ford spent big dollars to buy out Brewers Retail early, sent $200 dollar cheques to everyone, entered bad deals with a spa company for Ontario Place, spent millions on self-promotional advertising, plans a tunnel under Hwy. 401, cancelled the cap and trade which triggered the carbon tax, and of course created the Greenbelt/developer friends fiasco, all the while neglecting Ontario's infrastructure, our health-care system, and our educational system.
Gary Johnson, Stouffville, ON
Trump's MAGA supporters will lose big with tariffs
Donald Trump hits Canadian aluminum and steel with 25 per cent tariff, Feb. 10
Once again the president of the U.S. has shown a lack of understanding of the impacts of his impulsive actions. The most obvious is that he is still trying to gaslight his followers into thinking tariffs will be paid by the supplying country. Wrong. The U.S. companies that import the goods from Canada pay the tariffs. They are collected by the U.S. government and, as Donald Trump has said, will be used to bring down the tax rate on his billionaire bros. This means that the cost of everything imported under a tariff will get more expensive and eventually be passed on to the American consumer. It wasn't the Chinese that exported jobs to China and poverty to the U.S., it was major corporations that used the cheap offshore labour to make huge profits in the U.S. (Another Trump "misdirection.")
If you look at the goods being exchanged across the border, it gets more interesting. The ones coming from the U.S. to Canada are generally completed products. In contrast most of the exports to the U.S. are raw materials - oil, wood, aluminum, steel and energy - that supply the creation of their exports. So "tit-for-tat" tariff wars are a loser for the U.S., and a bigger loss for Trump's supporters.
A more thoughtful and nuanced, diplomatic approach would produce better outcomes for both countries.
Tom McElroy, Toronto
Let's build 100 per cent made in Canada electric vehicles
A few years ago the Automotive Parts Manufacturing Association built Project Arrow, an electric vehicle with 100 per cent Canadian parts. It bothered me then and bothers me more now that it was just for demonstration purposes rather than setting up an assembly line to build them. This was when the Ford government and the federal government were giving billions of dollars to automakers for EV manufacture. Canada needs a 100 per cent Canadian built electric car. We need it to get out from under the Trump tariffs and to offer an affordable alternative to the overpriced vehicles now on the market. Canadians are holding onto their vehicles on average for 10 years now. Why? Well who can afford new vehicles which average more than $60,000? New and used cars have increased in price by approximately 40 per cent since pre-pandemic days. Build a mid-sized Canadian EV for under $40,000 or a small EV less than $30,000. Bring back the EV incentive to the customer .
Judy Smith, Cobourg
Bring beer can manufacturing home
According to a CBC report (featuring one of our best microbreweries Great Lakes Brewery) Canada sends aluminum to the U.S. to be turned into beer cans, and Canadian breweries buy them back. I cannot believe we don't make our own beer cans. Microbreweries are greatly concerned about tariffs affecting both ends of beer can production. I'm from Guelph, home to a major brewery and four excellent microbreweries. Guelph is also home to Linamar, a maker of auto parts that is greatly concerned about potential tariffs. Perhaps Linamar should retool a plant and make some beer cans. This would keep some manufacturing jobs, keep production costs for Canadian brewers down, and keep prices for beer drinkers down too.
Aaron Duncan, Guelph
Garbage trucks need bigger windows
The windows in garbage trucks are so high and so tiny the drivers cannot see the people beneath them. I was side-swiped by a garbage truck a few years back and the driver didn't even see my car. When I spoke to the driver (fortunately I was not hurt), the window was so far above my head it was difficult to communicate.
I am not alone. Similar incidents - some fatal - occur in Toronto frequently. I cannot think of any good reason why the people inside the truck need such high tiny windows. It is most probably a cost-cutting measure, but is costing lives. The design definitely needs a rethink or a reworking.
Karen Quinton, Etobicoke
Newfoundland and Labrador tourism commercial is mesmerizing
Newfoundland and Labrador takes the cake for early tourism promotion. I have been watching for several years; they are the first province to promote in January of each year and it is always mesmerizing. I have been there twice and looking forward to going again. No other province or territory comes close to them. I have seen the whole country from coast to coast and Mont-Tremblant is the most romantic town in the country and Quebec City is every bit of Europe. But Newfoundland and Labrador earn the top spot for your tourism commercial.
Anant Nagpur, Ottawa
CAPTION: PC leader Doug Ford heads to his campaign bus. Doug Ford pledged to eliminate tolls, which can be up to $20 a ride, on the provincially run eastern section of Highway 407 if his Progressive Conservatives are re-elected Feb. 27.

Document 217 of 500

Ford is no Churchill, but he's more Churchillian than the alternatives

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-is-no-churchill-hes-more-churchillian-than/docview/3166486553/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+is+no+Churchill%2C+but+he%27s+more+Churchillian+than+the+alternatives&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Cometh the hour, cometh the man.
Whether or not the above was spoken by or about Winston Churchill is irrelevant. In a battle between truth and legend, print the legend. When Europe was facing its darkest hour as Nazi Germany's armies spread across the continent, it was Churchill who answered the call and kept the Allied powers in the game long enough to plot and then execute victory.
With the Western world once again facing the prospect of blisteringly dark times, this time death by its own hand in the form of a U.S. administration busy knocking away the pillars of its own dominance, the time has come for serious leadership.
And so:
enter Doug Ford. Yes, Doug Ford. Cometh the hour, cometh the man.
It's an enduring fiction that elections are decided by policies and a rational working through of outcomes. Most elections are vibe shifts. Tired of the (federal) Liberals after over a decade in power? Elect Stephen Harper (and then Justin Trudeau when Harper wears out his welcome). Tired of a decade-plus of Dalton McGuinty and Kathleen Wynne? Elect Doug Ford.
And so it is in the Year of our Lord 2025. Only this time the vibe shift has happened south of the border. The U.S. is now led by a petty, venal mercantilist who has tried to bully and dominate every room he's ever been in. The answer to the problem of Trump isn't so much hyper-literate policy countermeasures as it is street smarts. And Premier Ford has always considered himself the man from Main Street.
No offence to Bonnie Crombie, Marit Stiles or Mike Schreiner, but this just isn't your fight. In this new world, you're a dial-up modem belching out squeals as you try to connect with your audience. Forget Trump. Ford will not be defeated by a forensic retelling of his failings, whether on the cost of freeing our alcohol or on Greenbelt development jobs for the boys. Or by better policy proposals, for that matter. And if you can't defeat Ford, you'll have no hope against Tariff Man.
Ford's response to Trump's tariff threat is exactly what Ontarians most like about him. It might be a bit all over the shop, but it's passionate and pro-The People he's paid to represent, whether in Confederation or around the world. His energy and attitude are what's required at this moment, not business as usual. To pull a Crombie, Stiles or Schreiner out of the drawer right now is to pull the butter knife out at the gun-and-bluster fight.
Take Crombie. A one-time (and wholly unremarkable) federal MP and the former mayor of Mississauga, Crombie says Ontario deserves a premier "who will take this pivotal moment seriously." And why isn't Doug Ford taking "this pivotal moment seriously?" Well, you see, he called an "early election" in the "midst of an economic crisis." Of course, Ford has already won an election in the midst of an economic crisis, in the form of his re-election in 2022 when we were in the depths of market turmoil post-COVID and post-Russia's invasion of Ukraine. It's hard to see why Ontarians would punish him for not waiting until 2026.
More to the point, does Crombie think it's better to tool up at the beginning of a fight or halfway through one? Ford's calculation was that, in a country dying for a change of direction at the top (although now apparently less so with Trudeau going), it was better to reconfirm his mandate before that change happened and before Trump could land more haymakers. This is no great betrayal of voters. Pace Crombie, Ford didn't "quit" on Ontarians, he's putting his faith in them. He's offering to "protect" them in the face of serious adversity.
As for Stiles, when your record is presiding over the selection of Jagmeet Singh as the president of the federal NDP and your acclamation as provincial leader of the Ontario NDP because nobody else could raise the $55,000 entry fee, convincing voters you're the best option to face a problem like Trump is to choose granola over experience. This is undoubtedly harsh on Stiles, but these are harsh times.
Another enduring fiction is that voters like to balance their provincial votes against their federal preferences. In this case, that would mean swerving left in Ontario with Canada set to swerve right when Parliament is reconvened only to be cast into the coming federal election. But the vibe shift in the U.S. means all of Canada will need to be yoked in the same direction - and quickly. It will not be time to break in new leadership or rookie politicians.
And while a federal government led by Pierre Poilievre would itself be reasonably amateur, it would benefit from some consistency and experience across its provincial counterparts. One of the artifacts of the post-Meech Lake period that needs to be reversed in the face of Trump is the decline of Canadian federalism. The country needs one song sheet and people who are ready to sing from it. And Doug Ford has always been strong of voice.
Would it be nice if the Ontario PC website had a section called "policy" on it? Yes. Would it be nice if Ford and his team hadn't bloated the provincial budget? Again, yes. Does any of this matter right now? No. Canadians' minds are concentrated at the prospect of a hanging. They want protection.
Which brings us to another reality often forgotten in politics. There are no options called "perfect." Elections are a choice between flawed options, and most voters go with their guts. What's more, they base their choice on the mood music that surrounds them. And right now, the current mood music is dire. The province needs a brawler with a bit of unpredictability. All of this points to a Ford win.
Cometh the hour, cometh the (flawed) man.
CAPTION: Ontario Progressive Conservative leader Doug Ford speaks to reporters, accompanied by other Council of the Federation members, at the Mayflower Hotel in Washington, D.C.The iconic portrait of former British prime minister Winston Churchill, taken by photographer Yousuf Karsh in 1941.
CREDIT: Andrew MacDougall, Contributor

Subject: Leadership; Economic crisis; Voters; Elections; Tariffs

Document 218 of 500

Here is what the Canadian 'elite' consistently get wrong about this country

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/here-is-what-canadian-elite-consistently-get/docview/3166472194/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Here+is+what+the+Canadian+%27elite%27+consistently+get+wrong+about+this+country&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Why do the designated explainers in our country - journalists, leaders, the very rich - always seem puzzled when Canadian nationalism raises its head again? It never went away. It's always lurking. Where? Out there, where people are.
It's taken many forms, some perplexing. An early, post-Confederation version was called Canadian Imperialism. It wanted Canada to move beyond its colonial status while remaining part of the British Empire. Come again?
This anticipated Quebec nationalists a century later, who supported, said Yvon Deschamps, an independent Quebec in a strong and united Canada. Thank God for these contradictions, otherwise our politics would be tediously consistent and terminally boring. Trump and tariffs begat the current model.
One of the substrata where average Canadians live throws up models like "Trailer Park Boys," "Shoresy" and the McKenzie brothers - sophisticated self-parodies with hidden depths. The designated explainers show far less insight along with no humour.
The Globe and Mail ran a recent feature on "The week Canada remembered what it stands for." But "Shoresy" and Bob and Doug McKenzie never forgot, it's the Globe that needs periodic reminding.
This is symptomatic of what Bunuel called The Discreet Charm of the Bourgeoisie. Among their charmant delusions is that they can become someone else from somewhere else if they simply choose to, like the Russian upper classes in "War and Peace," who gave their kids names like Pierre and spoke French among themselves. I imagine they'd have been titillated to learn that Russian nationalism had broken out one day and would rush to explain it to the very people who'd kept it going.
Michael McCain, of McCain frozen fries, wrote this week, "I can't remember a time in my life when I've reflected more on what it means to be Canadian." I find that touching, but it also has the now-you-see-it-now-you-don't quality.
I think ordinary people are more constant, less extreme; they never ceased feeling Canadian. They tend to assume being Canadian, not reflect on it. Not because they're less reflective but because this thing is very strong, however we understand it, and it's part of us, no matter how we think of it.
Canadian nationalism never comes as a surprise to them because it's who one is, among other things. You love your country in the complicated way you love your family, i.e., sometimes you hate them. Why? Because there's no choice, they're the only country/family you're going to have.
Nor are Canadians prone to delirious nationalism, à la the U.S., since we look on from our box seats at how ridiculous, deluded and dangerous they are. Our pride tends to be not about our mythic greatness but about our health care or other institutions, which Brian Mulroney and his claque, who dragged us into free trade with the U.S. in 1988, liked to scoff at as a pathetic nationalism, but only because they envied the mythic B.S. of U.S. hypernationalism. It's a pride based on real stuff that we've built for each other.
A virtue of people's nationalism is that it doesn't go away between flowerings so it doesn't have to suddenly gush about everything from tariffs to hockey, like Columbus thinking he'd discovered America, or wherever. It's a sober - though only in the metaphorical sense - version of nationalism.
A final thought. There was life before free trade with the U.S., in fact a lot of it, including many nation building achievements, because Canadian life was lived without free trade till the 1990s, though many of today's chief mourners weren't around to see it and perhaps have trouble imaging a Canada without it.
We were a great trading nation even then, though without as many eggs in the U.S. basket. We tend to forget that we got into it because the U.S. pushed us there, using our most U.S.-leaning PM, Mulroney. (Picture a smooth, bilingual Doug Ford). Long before that, people in places like Poland used "Canada" as a term for a reward you'd reap if you had great success: "I heard you got Canada. Congratulations."
CAPTION: The creators of Canadian parodies, such as "Bob & Doug McKenzie" have never forgotten what it means to be Canadian, writes Rick Salutin.
CREDIT: Rick Salutin

Subject: Free trade; Nationalism; Tariffs

Document 219 of 500

Freeland eyes doctors, nurses: Candidate would offer cash to attract Canadians abroad

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/freeland-eyes-doctors-nurses/docview/3166471441/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Freeland+eyes+doctors%2C+nurses%3A+Candidate+would+offer+cash+to+attract+Canadians+abroad&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Liberal leadership candidate Chrystia Freeland said she would try to bring Canadian doctors and nurses home, offering them cash incentives to leave jobs in the U.S. and work here.
Freeland unveiled the policy Thursday and said as the U.S. administration attempts economic warfare, Canada should respond forcefully with tariffs, but also work to bring Canadian doctors back home.
"We should also pursue policies that capitalize on this moment to strengthen our wonderful country, including our universal public health-care system," she said in a statement.
Freeland said she would offer doctors a $200,000 signing bonus to work in Canada and pay off up to $300,000 in student loans. Nurses would be offered a $100,000 incentive to move back to Canada and up to $50,000 to help with student loans, she said. She is also pledging to work with provinces and territories to shorten foreign credential approvals for doctors and nurses down to one month.
The incentives would target Canadian citizens who are doctors or nurses working in the U.S.
As of 2022, more than 6.5 million Canadians didn't have a family doctor, according to a research report from the MAP centre for urban health solutions, a Toronto-based research institute.
Freeland said her proposals would help turn that tide.
"My three proposals to bring Canadian doctors and nurses back home and recognize foreign credentials would dramatically boost Canada's health-care workforce and improve Canadians' access to the care they deserve," she said.
With less than a month to go in the Liberal leadership race three of the other contenders in the race also had policy announcements.
Former Bank of Canada governor Mark Carney announced a housing initiative, pledging to reduce barriers to construction and incentivize modular construction to speed home construction while reducing costs.
In a statement, he said his government would do everything it could to confront the housing crisis.
"Strong communities aren't built on long commutes and financial worries. They're made possible when all Canadians can afford to live with dignity," he said. "We need strong leadership and a clear vision to confront the housing crisis head-on, and build the strongest economy in the G7."
Candidate Karina Gould made a commitment to modernize the Employment Insurance system. She said the entire system needs an overhaul to better reflect modern jobs that are often contract or gig positions.
"I would bring forward a plan to update our social safety net to really help Canadians and not just catch them when they fall, but also to enable them to bounce back even stronger than before," she said.
Gould said that would mean improving EI, but also ensuring adequate benefits for seniors and the disabled. Without offering specifics, she said a modernized EI system could put Canada on the path to a basic income.
A basic income program was piloted in Ontario but later cancelled by the Ford government. A basic income program usually means providing payments to individuals as a way to help them turn the corner on poverty, get retrained, or find employment.
Former MP Ruby Dhalla also pledged to provide mental-health care to all Canadians and appoint a health czar to find ways to improve the system.
Liberals will select a new leader on March 9.
CAPTION: Liberal leadership candidate Chrystia Freeland said she would offer a signing bonus to doctors and nurses and help pay off their student loans to get them to work in Canada.Richard Lautens Toronto Star
CREDIT: Ryan Tumilty Toronto Star

Document 220 of 500

What Canada's elites don't understand

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/what-canadas-elites-dont-understand/docview/3166471207/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=What+Canada%27s+elites+don%27t+understand&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.15&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Why do the designated explainers in our country - journalists, leaders, the very rich - always seem puzzled when Canadian nationalism raises its head again? It never went away. It's always lurking. Where? Out there, where people are.
It's taken many forms, some perplexing. An early, post-Confederation version was called Canadian Imperialism. It wanted Canada to move beyond its colonial status while remaining part of the British Empire. Come again?
This anticipated Quebec nationalists a century later, who supported, said Yvon Deschamps, an independent Quebec in a strong and united Canada. Thank God for these contradictions, otherwise our politics would be tediously consistent and terminally boring. Trump and tariffs begat the current model.
One of the substrata where average Canadians live throws up models like "Trailer Park Boys," "Shoresy" and the McKenzie brothers - sophisticated self-parodies with hidden depths. The designated explainers show far less insight along with no humour.
The Globe and Mail ran a recent feature on "The week Canada remembered what it stands for." But "Shoresy" and Bob and Doug McKenzie never forgot, it's the Globe that needs periodic reminding.
This is symptomatic of what Bunuel called The Discreet Charm of the Bourgeoisie. Among their charmant delusions is that they can become someone else from somewhere else if they simply choose to, like the Russian upper classes in "War and Peace," who gave their kids names like Pierre and spoke French among themselves. I imagine they'd have been titillated to learn that Russian nationalism had broken out one day and would rush to explain it to the very people who'd kept it going.
Michael McCain, of McCain frozen fries, wrote this week, "I can't remember a time in my life when I've reflected more on what it means to be Canadian." I find that touching, but it also has the now-you-see-it-now-you-don't quality.
I think ordinary people are more constant, less extreme; they never ceased feeling Canadian. They tend to assume being Canadian, not reflect on it. Not because they're less reflective but because this thing is very strong, however we understand it, and it's part of us, no matter how we think of it.
Canadian nationalism never comes as a surprise to them because it's who one is, among other things. You love your country in the complicated way you love your family, i.e., sometimes you hate them. Why? Because there's no choice, they're the only country/family you're going to have.
Nor are Canadians prone to delirious nationalism, à la the U.S., since we look on from our box seats at how ridiculous, deluded and dangerous they are. Our pride tends to be not about our mythic greatness but about our health care or other institutions, which Brian Mulroney and his claque, who dragged us into free trade with the U.S. in 1988, liked to scoff at as a pathetic nationalism, but only because they envied the mythic B.S. of U.S. hypernationalism. It's a pride based on real stuff that we've built for each other.
A virtue of people's nationalism is that it doesn't go away between flowerings so it doesn't have to suddenly gush about everything from tariffs to hockey, like Columbus thinking he'd discovered America, or wherever. It's a sober - though only in the metaphorical sense - version of nationalism.
A final thought. There was life before free trade with the U.S., in fact a lot of it, including many nation building achievements, because Canadian life was lived without free trade till the 1990s, though many of today's chief mourners weren't around to see it and perhaps have trouble imaging a Canada without it.
We were a great trading nation even then, though without as many eggs in the U.S. basket. We tend to forget that we got into it because the U.S. pushed us there, using our most U.S.-leaning PM, Mulroney. (Picture a smooth, bilingual Doug Ford.) Long before that, people in places like Poland used "Canada" as a term for a reward you'd reap if you had great success: "I heard you got Canada. Congratulations."
Rick Salutin is a freelance
contributing columnist. He is based in Toronto. Reach him on email: salutinrick@gmail.com
CREDIT: Rick Salutin OPINION;

Subject: Free trade; Nationalism; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Free trade Tariffs

Location: United States--US; Canada; Quebec Canada

Document 221 of 500

Ford called for return to capital punishment: 'Send 'em right to sparky,' PC leader said in campaign speech at London police gala

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-called-return-capital-punishment/docview/3166471151/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+called+for+return+to+capital+punishment%3A+%27Send+%27em+right+to+sparky%2C%27+PC+leader+said+in+campaign+speech+at+London+police+gala&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford took his tough-on-crime talk to new heights in a campaign speech where he pushed for a return to capital punishment, the Star has learned.
Musing about stiffer penalties for home invaders who kill victims, the Ontario Progressive Conservative leader called for changes in the federal Criminal Code that would empower judges to "send 'em right to sparky."
The colloquial reference to the electric chair came two weeks ago, on the first day of Ford's snap Feb. 27 Ontario election campaign, now at its midway point.
A recording of the speech to the London Police Service's sold-out inaugural "chief's gala appreciation dinner and awards night" - which was not listed on Ford's daily campaign itinerary provided to reporters at Queen's Park - was obtained by the Star.
Canada abolished capital punishment in 1976, replacing it with mandatory life sentences with no parole for at least 25 years in cases of first-degree murder.
"God forbid they kill an innocent person," Ford said on Jan. 29 to an audience of about 1,000 law enforcement personnel, legal types, politicians and business people at the downtown RBC Place London.
He was referring to his frustration with the alarming trend of violent home invasion robberies.
"I don't even go 25 years. I send 'em right to sparky and then we'll take everything from there," he added to sparse clapping and audible gasps in an audience that had moments earlier given him hearty applause for promising a $1-billion rebuild of the aging Ontario Police College in nearby Aylmer.
"We need to straighten out all these criminals ... Sorry for sidetracking. I just had to vent a little bit," Ford continued.
A lawyer who attended the event said the crowd was taken aback by the "sparky" line.
"The whole room seemed uncomfortable," added the lawyer, who is known to the Star but requested anonymity to speak freely. "I felt like I was in a bit of an alternate reality. I felt like I was in the States. It felt very Trumpian."
Earlier in the speech, Ford acknowledged his penchant for off-the-cuff remarks could get him in trouble.
"You know, I was warned by my campaign, 'Do not go off script ... do not say anything controversial. You're on a campaign.' Well, that's just not who I am," he said before advocating for stricter sentences and bail conditions.
Ford's campaign did not initially address the "sparky" capital punishment remark directly when asked by the Star if a return to the death penalty is official Progressive Conservative policy, saying only that his "No. 1 concern" is keeping communities and police safe.
But campaign officials put out a fresh statement six hours later in an attempt to control the damage, after the revelations first appeared on thestar.com.
"The premier made a poor-taste joke out of frustration for the many people affected by violent criminals on our streets because of the federal government's broken bail system," his campaign said in an email.
"The premier does not support capital punishment but rather firmly believes more needs to be done to keep violent and repeat offenders behind bars where they belong."
In his speech, Ford also expressed a desire to wade into national issues with a nod to a possible spring federal election.
"Hopefully, when a new government comes in, I think the provinces should take over some of the Criminal Code," he told the London crowd, taking shots at judges and justices of the peace who grant bail to repeat offenders in a call for tougher bail reform.
"I want pro-police, hard-on-crime JPs," Ford said. "We do have some good JPs. We have some really good judges, too. But from previous administrations and governments, we have some terrible JPs, terrible judges that actually let these criminals back out."
Ford did not explain in the speech how provinces could gain any control over the Criminal Code.
In their initial response to the Star, campaign officials said, "We will continue to push for reforms such as restoring mandatory minimum sentencing for serious crimes, removing bail availability for serious offences, and implementing a three-strikes rule that mandates pretrial detention for repeat offenders.
"Every day, we see convicted criminals out on bail who turn around and commit horrific crimes."
In Washington, D.C., earlier this week, Ford said in a speech to the United States Chamber of Commerce that the Canadian government should pass "strict" mandatory minimum sentences for those caught with fentanyl, a powerful and potentially deadly drug that is under the Trump administration's microscope.
"Any thug convicted of trafficking fentanyl or other illegal drugs gets thrown in jail for good. That's it. They're done. They're gone."
The "sparky" comment came five days before a hot mic gaffe at Ford's Etobicoke North campaign office, where he told a handful of supporters that he wanted Donald Trump to win the U.S. presidential election in November.
"On election day, was I happy the guy won? One hundred per cent I was. Then the guy pulled out the knife and f--ing yanked it in me," he said, making a stabbing motion with his hand.
That remark was widely ridiculed by rival party leaders who said that support for Trump shows Ford's hypocrisy.
Ford has staked his campaign for a third term as premier on fighting Trump's levies, and spent Tuesday and Wednesday in Washington lobbying U.S. politicians. He will return next week.
It's expected Ford's controversial comments will be fodder for opposition party leaders at Friday's northern Ontario debate in North Bay and the main leaders debate Monday night in Toronto.
An Abacus Data poll for the Star last week found that the hot mic gaffe about supporting Trump revealed risks to the Progressive Conservative campaign.
While 43 per cent in the online survey of 2,000 voters found Ford's remark had "no impact" on their voting intentions, 35 per cent said it would make them "less likely" to cast a ballot for the Progressive Conservative candidate in their riding and 22 per cent indicated they would be "more likely" to vote PC.
"It definitely reminded people who aren't Ford fans today why they probably aren't Ford fans," Abacus president David Coletto told the Star.
Nevertheless, Ford maintains a comfortable lead over Bonnie Crombie's Liberals and the New Democrats under Marit Stiles in public opinion polls.
Ford called the election 15 months early, portraying himself as the best leader to stand up to Trump's threat of 25 per cent tariffs on most imports from Canada, a move that would devastate a provincial economy that is heavily reliant on trade.
Opposition parties have accused Ford of calling an early and expensive election - at a cost of $189 million - to get ahead of any possible charges in the RCMP criminal investigation of the government's $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal.
They say his focus on Trump and tariffs is an attempt to distract voters from problems on the home front, such as a physician shortage that has left 2.5 million Ontarians without a family doctor, worsening "hallway health care" in overcrowded hospitals, a housing shortage that has sent prices skyrocketing, growing homeless encampments and affordability challenges following a period of high inflation in the wake of the COVID-19 pandemic.
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson and Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau

Document 222 of 500

Please: Anyone but Doug Ford

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/please-anyone-doug-ford/docview/3166471120/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Please%3A+Anyone+but+Doug+Ford&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: "Doug Ford here," the Progressive Conservative leader texted me recently. "I will stand up and fight US tariffs to save Ontario jobs." He informed me that I have two options: "REPLY > YES if you're with me and the PC team." Or, I could reply "stop to stop."This column is my reply: stop.Doug Ford is on track for a crushing third majority government. Voters - enthusiastic to endorse the incumbent in the face of a looming trade war, fond of Ford's moderately antagonist relationship with the federal government, charmed by his folksy awh-shucks sensibility - are replying a lower-case "yes." Nobody seems to think Doug Ford is good, but most seem to believe that he's good enough for this moment.I may be a Toronto émigré, but please hear me out: Vote for anybody else. Doug Ford is uniquely unqualified and unsuited to be a leader for this moment. His years in office have been defined by shortages - a lack of homes, doctors, teachers, jobs, skilled workers, subways, buses, and bike lanes. Worse yet, his plan for a third term is ambitious only in its plan to build a massive new white elephant.Let's start with housing, inarguably the priority for any incoming government. But, somehow, Ford remains indifferent. Ontario is, in the full swing of a housing crisis, breaking ground on fewer units of new housing today than it was in 2012. It's a stunning failure.The single most effective policy to reverse this anemic trend would be legalizing the construction of fourplexes - a move that would also unlock billions in federal housing money. Ford has refused. In 2024, Toronto saw its housing starts fall 20 per cent. In Montreal, where fourplexes are permitted, they rose 15 per cent.There are few problems in Ontario which don't flow out of this acute housing shortage. Tent encampments, rising crime, the opioid crisis, sluggish growth, a stagnant culture, and declining productivity: All things that could be ameliorated by cheaper, abundant housing. Permitting and supercharging this "missing middle" would mean more artists in cheap lofts, more rooming houses for people who have fallen on hard times, more two-bedroom units for working class people.His abdication of duty doesn't end there. Ontario needs more transit. Here, Ford deserves genuine credit for pushing ahead the Ontario Line and committing to a bold extension of the GO Train network - although these two projects are just barely dragging Toronto into a transit network for the new millennium. Ontario needs to be planning for 2050 and beyond. What's more, Ford has consistently micromanaged and meddled in various transit projects - only to see them marred by cost overruns, massive delays, and outright bungling. Other cities across the province desperately need to scale up transit options now, but the province hasn't been nearly as attentive to their needs.The PC leader's nominal commitment to mass public transit is undercut by the fact that he is heaping subsidies onto motorists. He has cut the gas tax, promised to remove the tolls on the 407, and assumed the $2 billion cost of renovating the ailing Gardiner Expressway. The costs of driving - high fuel prices, costly parking premiums, the useless toiling in gridlock - reflect that we have too many drivers on the road. Ford's euphemistic pledge to "make driving more affordable" is really a pledge to subsidize the cost of driving, which in turn will put more cars on the road.If we actually want to reduce costs on those who need to drive, we need to take more vehicles off the road. Give people the freedom to take trains, streetcars, subways, and buses, and we can make our cities more efficient. Just look at New York City, where congestion pricing has reduced traffic and improved transit service. His pathological NIBMYism aside, Ford isn't just bad for Ontario's urban centers. Across the province, the ratio of teachers-to-students is bad and getting worse. Its economic growth outlook is poor. And its healthcare is measurably worsening. These problems are all felt more acutely outside the big cities.There was a time when a decline in the quality of Ontario's healthcare system was grounds to oust a premier. Not anymore, apparently. Ontario's medicare system has slid into abject disrepair, and Ford's futzing around the edges seems to be making it worse. Hallway medicine is now the norm and Ford's solution has been to use public money to underwrite sparkling new knee replacement clinics for the wealthy.Let's return, unfortunately, to the pandemic. Over that crisis, Ford banned children from playgrounds, which were perfectly safe, but refused to close down high-risk workplaces. Both Ontarians angry at the avoidable COVID-19 deaths and those furious at the curtailment of their civil liberties should unite in the feeling that Doug Ford does not deserve another term in office.Even Ford's effort to (finally) liberalize Ontario's byzantine liquor laws has been a mess. He initially delayed the measure, then sped it up to coincide with his snap election. Now Ontario is stuck paying $500 million to private companies which run Ontario's inefficient and much-hated beer and wine monopolies. Why?Ford is addicted to giving private companies public money, often for no benefit to the province. It'salso no wonder the stench of corruption hangs over his government. The RCMP is currently pursuing a criminal investigation into how and why lands in the Greenbelt were opened up for development by the Ford government. What's more, the Auditor General found that the PCs "favoured certain developers" during that process.Ontarians are going to the polls right now because Ford believes his cartoonish pushback against Donald Trump will be popular. By donning a funny hat, Ford has become a mascot for the country when we need some hokey relief. But this admittedly comforting performance should be undercut by the fact that Ford cheered on Trump's victory, even though this trade war was a plainly evident outcome.I don't envy the voters of Ontario. Picking between the Liberals and NDP is like being made to choose which scented bubbles you want for your tepid bath of managed decline. The Greens, long the most thoughtful party on the ballot, are well suited to be in opposition but are probably not ready for government. And while there are great individual progressive conservative candidates, they are beholden to a shameless and inept leader. But while Ford may call himself a conservative, his two terms in office have been anything but. There is nothing conservative about his debt-ridden era of corporate welfare and mismanagement.He has had nearly seven years to fix the province, and he hasn't. What is he proposing for the next four years?Ford's healthcare plan is absolutely anemic, little more than a vague hand-wave at the crisis. If he has actual new plans to boost housing construction, he's certainly been holding out on us. Does he have a real plan to improve education, reduce homelessness, hire more doctors, provide care for people struggling with addiction, or get real economic growth started again? No.What Doug Ford has is a big dumb tunnel.In his quixotic crusade to have the Ontario public subsidize his commute, Ford wants to bore underneath the 401 highway which cuts through the middle of Toronto - all for the marginal benefit of the 400,000 vehicles that drive over that section of the 401 each day. It is a plan that is impractical, unwise, wasteful, destructive, ugly, unnecessary, and certain to worsen traffic in the city for at least a decade. It is perhaps one of the single most offensively stupid policies pitched in this country in recent memory. (Right up there with a tunnel to Newfoundland proposed by the federal Liberals in 2021, and swiftly forgotten.) Experts suggest that Ford's 50 kilometer tunnel would cost at least $60 billion, probably more. By comparison, Montreal's Réseau Express Métropolitain - its nearly-finished 67-kilometer light-rail system connecting the whole island, the airport, and the suburbs - cost less than $8 billion. That means potholes in North Bay could remain unfilled because the leader of the Progressive Conservatives wants to plow tens of billions of dollars to add yet more lanes onto one of the world's widest highways.It's a perfectly fitting metaphor for how Doug Ford does things. Ontario desperately needs a leader with genuine ambition on how to fix things. But Ford's plan for the province is only bold insofar as it is a daring attempt to waste an ungodly sum of money on something profoundly and genuinely wrong-headed.So please, god, text Doug Ford right now and tell him: stop.
CAPTION: Progressive Conservative leader Doug Ford dons a "Canada: Not For Sale" toque at Queens Park in Toronto on January 28, 2025.
CREDIT: Justin Ling

Subject: Trade disputes; Liquor laws & regulations; Light rail transportation; Health care policy; Economic growth; Voters; Housing starts; Elections; Roads & highways

Document 223 of 500

Letters: Ford vows to forge ahead with tunnel under Hwy. 401, Feb. 8 The NDP's first election plank? Abolish road tolls. Is the war on the car over? Opinion, Feb. 3 Doug Ford wants to protect us from Donald Trump. But who will protect us from Doug Ford? Opinion, Feb. 5 Donald Trump hits Canadian aluminum and steel with 25 per cent tariff, Feb. 10

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3166471110/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Ford+vows+to+forge+ahead+with+tunnel+under+Hwy.+401%2C+Feb.+8+The+NDP%27s+first+election+plank%3F+Abolish+road+tolls.+Is+the+war+on+the+car+over%3F+Opinion%2C+Feb.+3+Doug+Ford+wants+to+protect+us+from+Donald+Trump.+But+who+will+protect+us+from+Doug+Ford%3F+Opinion%2C+Feb.+5+Donald+Trump+hits+Canadian+aluminum+and+steel+with+25+per+cent+tariff%2C+Feb.+10&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Leaders' plans to tackle gridlock miss the mark
Ford vows to forge ahead with tunnel under Hwy. 401, Feb. 8
The NDP's first election plank? Abolish road tolls. Is the war on the car over?
Opinion, Feb. 3
Recent pledges by party leaders around transportation and relieving gridlock range from the absurd to the myopic.
PC Leader Doug Ford is promoting his pet GTA tunnel - price be damned - and talking about removing tolls from the publicly owned portion of Highway 407. Yet, he still won't tell us what the destructive Highway 413 will cost.
NDP leader Marit Stiles' pledge to remove all tolls on the 407 is also ill-conceived. Those plans will cost a lot of money for benefits that will be short-lived when induced demand inevitably leads to more congestion within a few years.
There are many better ways to reduce gridlock - including truck accommodation on the 407, targeted existing highway expansion and, of course, major transit expansion (much of which has been proposed in recent years).
The $100 billion and 20 years that building a vanity tunnel will likely need could be used to create a world-class mass transit system. That's a far more attractive image to envision.
Richard Schertzer, Milton
Doug Ford has been a disaster for Ontario
Doug Ford wants to protect us from Donald Trump. But who will protect us from Doug Ford? Opinion, Feb. 5
Are Ontarians better off than we were seven years ago? The answer is no.
Just follow the money and the deals and policies to get a sense how badly Ontario has fared under the Ford government.
Ford spent big dollars to buy out Brewers Retail early, sent $200 cheques to everyone, entered bad deals with a spa company for Ontario Place, spent millions on self-promotional advertising, planned to build a tunnel under Highway 401, cancelled the cap and trade, which triggered the federal carbon tax, and created the Greenbelt/developer friends fiasco, all while neglecting Ontario's infrastructure, health-care system and education system.
Gary Johnson, Stouffville
Trump backers will pay the price for his tariffs
Donald Trump hits Canadian aluminum and steel with 25 per cent tariff, Feb. 10
Once again the president of the U.S. has shown a lack of understanding of the impacts of his impulsive actions. He is still trying to gaslight his followers into thinking tariffs will be paid by the supplying country. Wrong. U.S. companies that import goods from Canada pay the tariffs. They are collected by the U.S. government and, as Donald Trump has said, will be used to bring down the tax rate on his billionaire bros. The cost of everything imported under a tariff will get more expensive and be passed on to the American consumer.
If you look at the goods being exchanged across the border, it gets more interesting. The ones coming from the U.S. to Canada are generally completed products. In contrast, most of the exports to the U.S. are raw materials - oil, wood, aluminum, steel and energy - that supply the creation of their exports. So "tit-for-tat" tariff wars are a loser for the U.S., and a bigger loss for Trump's supporters.
Tom McElroy, Toronto
Let's build a made-in-Canada electric vehicle
A few years ago, the Automotive Parts Manufacturing Association built Project Arrow, an electric vehicle with 100 per cent Canadian parts. It bothered me then and bothers me more now that it was just for demonstration purposes, rather than setting up an assembly line to build them. This was when the provincial and federal governments were giving billions of dollars to automakers for EV manufacturing.
Canada needs a 100 per cent Canadian-built electric car. We need it to get out from under the Trump tariffs and to offer an affordable alternative to the overpriced vehicles now on the market. Canadians are now holding onto their vehicles an average of 10 years. Why? Who can afford new vehicles that average more than $60,000? New and used cars have increased in price by about 40 per cent since pre-pandemic days.
Build a mid-sized Canadian EV for less than $40,000 or a small EV for less than $30,000. And bring back the EV incentives.
Judy Smith, Cobourg
Make beer cans in Canada
According to a CBC report (featuring one of our best microbreweries Great Lakes Brewery) Canada sends aluminum to the U.S. to be turned into beer cans, and Canadian breweries buy them back. I cannot believe we don't make our own beer cans. Microbreweries are greatly concerned about tariffs affecting both ends of beer can production. I'm from Guelph, home to a major brewery and four excellent microbreweries. Guelph is also home to Linamar, a maker of auto parts that is greatly concerned about potential tariffs. Perhaps Linamar should retool a plant and make some beer cans. This would keep some manufacturing jobs, keep production costs for Canadian brewers down, and keep prices for beer drinkers down too.
Aaron Duncan, Guelph
Garbage trucks need big windows
The windows in garbage trucks are so high and so tiny the drivers cannot see people beneath them. I was side-swiped by a garbage truck a few years back and the driver didn't even see my car. When I spoke to the driver (fortunately I was not hurt), the window was so far above my head it was difficult to communicate.
I am not alone. Similar incidents, some fatal, occur in Toronto frequently. I cannot think of any good reason why the people inside the truck need such high tiny windows. It is most probably a cost-cutting measure, but is costing lives. The design definitely needs a rethink or a reworking.
Karen Quinton, Etobicoke
Newfoundland tourism ads are mesmerizing
Newfoundland and Labrador takes the cake for early tourism promotion. They are the first province to promote in January of each year and it is always mesmerizing. I have been there twice and look forward to going again. No other province or territory comes close. I have seen the whole country. Mont-Tremblant is the most romantic town in the country and Quebec City is every bit of Europe. But Newfoundland and Labrador earn the top spot for their tourism commercials.
Anant Nagpur, Ottawa

NDP candidate withdraws to help Liberal

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ndp-candidate-withdraws-help-liberal/docview/3166471082/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=NDP+candidate+withdraws+to+help+Liberal&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The NDP hopeful in Eglinton-Lawrence says she withdrew her candidacy to avoid a vote-split that could help Ontario's Progressive Conservatives keep the seat.
In a post on X Thursday evening, Natasha Doyle-Merrick said: "I recognize the race in Eglinton-Lawrence is a clear two-party contest between Liberals and Conservatives. To prevent a Conservative win and more years of neglect, I'm stepping aside to avoid a vote split," on the political left.
In an attached statement, the mental health worker said she made the difficult decision after much reflection, adding that she first sought the NDP nomination because she felt Robin Martin, the riding's PC MPP since 2018, had ignored some voices in the community. Martin didn't run again.
Michelle Cooper, executive director of the Ontario Progressive Conservative fund, is the Eglinton-Lawrence PC candidate in what is expected to be a close race with Liberal candidate Vince Gasparro, former principal secretary to John Tory when he was Toronto mayor.
Doyle-Merrick withdrew before the 2 p.m. deadline so her name will not appear on the ballot for the provincial Feb. 27 election.
In a statement, Gasparro thanked Doyle-Merrick, saying her decision is "a testament to who she is." He vowed to "welcome everyone into our campaign with open arms."
CREDIT: David Rider Senior Politics Reporter

Document 225 of 500

Ford accused of breaking ethics rule: New Democrats claim PC leader 'blurred lines' between campaigning, governing with online ad

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-accused-breaking-ethics-rule/docview/3166468464/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+accused+of+breaking+ethics+rule%3A+New+Democrats+claim+PC+leader+%27blurred+lines%27+between+campaigning%2C+governing+with+online+ad&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford violated ethics rules against using government activities for partisan purposes in an online ad about his trip to Washington to lobby against Trump tariffs, opposition parties charge.
The ad on the Progressive Conservative leader's X feed Thursday pictured him walking past the U.S. Capitol. It used the party's "Protect Ontario" logo and slogan from his bid for a third term as premier in a snap election he called for Feb. 27.
The ad was quickly taken off social media and reposted a few hours later with no Conservative logo or slogans.
"If that isn't a sure sign of guilt, I don't know what is," NDP Leader Marit Stiles said in Sudbury, insisting the ad retains partisan overtones because it was posted in the context of the campaign.
"I don't think anyone's fooled," Stiles added, noting her party filed a complaint with Ontario's integrity commissioner J. David Wake that Ford has "blurred the lines" between campaigning and governing.
In a letter to Wake, Stiles requested an investigation into whether "taxpayer dollars were put to use in service of personal or partisan gain of Doug Ford."
Ford's campaign defended the ad.
"Footage of the premier being the premier is routinely used in political social content," spokesperson Ivana Yelich said in a statement to the Star.
"However, out of an abundance of caution, the video was reposted without campaign branding."
Wake ruled before the Washington trip that fighting tariffs threatened by U.S. President Donald Trump is "an urgent manner in the public interest" but cautioned that Ford and his cabinet members can take part in related events "provided that the activities are not then used for partisan purposes."
His office declined further comment Thursday.
The Conservative party slogan in the ad, in which Ford intones, "Let's focus on restoring the pride of made in the U.S.A and made in Canada," quickly raised concerns once it hit social media.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said the Conservative campaign knew of the caution issued by the integrity commissioner and chose to ignore it.
"Doug Ford plays fast and loose with the rules," she told reporters in Elmvale, near Barrie.
Ford appeared in the ad with PC candidates Vic Fedeli and Stephen Lecce, walking with the U.S. Capitol towering above them. Ford is pictured speaking to the U.S. Chamber of Commerce and meeting with U.S. officials and in news conferences and scrums with reporters.
As head of the Council of the Federation, he led a delegation of premiers to Washington to argue against tariffs on Canada. The Progressive Conservatives said they paid the travel expenses of Ford and his campaign staff.
Crombie had previously raised concerns the trip violated the so-called "caretaker convention," which holds that the only government business that can be carried out during an election campaign is routine or urgent matters in the public interest.
CAPTION: PC Leader Doug Ford is shown this week in Washington. An ad on his X feed Thursday showed him walking past the U.S. Capitol and used the party's "Protect Ontario" logo and slogan from his campaign bid. The ad was quickly removed and later reposted without the logo and slogan.Ben Curtis The Associated Press
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson and Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Doug Ford broke ethics rules with campaign ad, opponents charge

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-broke-ethics-rules-with-campaign-ad/docview/3166819265/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+broke+ethics+rules+with+campaign+ad%2C+opponents+charge&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford violated ethics rules against using government activities for partisan purposes in an online ad about his trip to Washington to lobby against Trump tariffs, opposition parties charge.
The ad on the Progressive Conservative leader's X feed Thursday pictured him walking past the U.S. Capitol. It used the party's "Protect Ontario" logo and slogan from his bid for a third term as premier in a snap election he called for Feb. 27.
The ad was quickly taken off social media and reposted a few hours later with no Conservative logo or slogans.
"If that isn't a sure sign of guilt, I don't know what is," NDP Leader Marit Stiles said in Sudbury, insisting the ad retains partisan overtones because it was posted in the context of the campaign.
"I don't think anyone's fooled," Stiles added, noting her party intends to file a complaint with Ontario's integrity commissioner J. David Wake, an independent officer of the legislature.
Ford's campaign defended the ad.
"Footage of the premier being the premier is routinely used in political social content," spokesperson Ivana Yelich said in a statement to the Star.
"However, out of an abundance of caution, the video was reposted without campaign branding."
Wake's office has ruled that fighting tariffs threatened by U.S. President Donald Trump is "an urgent manner in the public interest" but cautioned that Ford and his cabinet members can take part in related events "provided that the activities are not then used for partisan purposes."
The Conservative party slogan in the ad, in which Ford intones, "Let's focus on restoring the pride of made in the U.S.A and made in Canada," quickly raised concerns once it hit social media.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said the Conservative campaign knew of the caution issued by the integrity commissioner and chose to ignore it.
"Doug Ford plays fast and loose with the rules," she told reporters in Elmvale, near Barrie.
Ford appeared in the ad with PC candidates Vic Fedeli and Stephen Lecce, walking with the U.S. Capitol towering above them. Ford is pictured speaking to the U.S. Chamber of Commerce and meeting with U.S. officials and in news conferences and scrums with reporters.
As head of the Council of the Federation, he led a delegation of premiers to Washington to argue against tariffs on Canada. The Progressive Conservatives said they paid the travel expenses of Ford and his campaign staff.
The ad is a clear violation of ethics rules, Stiles said.
"They know they did something wrong, and yet that never seems to stop them."
Crombie had previously raised concerns the trip violated the so-called "caretaker convention," which holds that the only government business that can be carried out during an election campaign is routine or urgent matters in the public interest.
