Document 227 of 500

Chrystia Freeland proposes cash incentives to bring Canadian doctors home

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/chrystia-freeland-proposes-cash-incentives-bring/docview/3166818614/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Chrystia+Freeland+proposes+cash+incentives+to+bring+Canadian+doctors+home&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Liberal leadership candidate Chrystia Freeland said she would try to bring Canadian doctors and nurses home, offering them cash incentives to leave jobs in the U.S. and work here.
Freeland unveiled the policy Thursday and said as the U.S. administration attempts economic warfare, Canada should respond forcefully with tariffs, but also work to bring Canadian doctors back home.
"We should also pursue policies that capitalize on this moment to strengthen our wonderful country, including our universal public health-care system," she said in a statement.
Freeland said she would offer doctors a $200,000 signing bonus to work in Canada and pay off up to $300,000 in student loans. Nurses would be offered a $100,000 incentive to move back to Canada and up to $50,000 to help with student loans, she said. She is also pledging to work with provinces and territories to shorten foreign credential approvals for doctors and nurses down to one month.
As of 2022, more than 6.5 million Canadians didn't have a family doctor, according to a research report from the MAP centre for urban health solutions, a Toronto-based research institute.
Freeland said her proposals would help turn that tide.
"My three proposals to bring Canadian doctors and nurses back home and recognize foreign credentials would dramatically boost Canada's health-care workforce and improve Canadians' access to the care they deserve," she said.
With less than a month to go in the Liberal leadership race three of the other contenders in the race also had policy announcements.
Former Bank of Canada governor Mark Carney announced a housing initiative, pledging to reduce barriers to construction and incentivize modular construction to speed home construction while reducing costs.
In a statement, he said his government would do everything it could to confront the housing crisis.
"Strong communities aren't built on long commutes and financial worries. They're made possible when all Canadians can afford to live with dignity," he said. "We need strong leadership and a clear vision to confront the housing crisis head-on, and build the strongest economy in the G7."
Candidate Karina Gould made a commitment to modernize the Employment Insurance system. She said the entire system needs an overhaul to better reflect modern jobs that are often contract or gig positions.
"I would bring forward a plan to update our social safety net to really help Canadians and not just catch them when they fall, but also to enable them to bounce back even stronger than before," she said.
Gould said that would mean improving EI, but also ensuring adequate benefits for seniors and the disabled. Without offering specifics, she said a modernized EI system could put Canada on the path to a basic income.
A basic income program was piloted in Ontario but later cancelled by the Ford government. A basic income program usually means providing payments to individuals as a way to help them turn the corner on poverty, get retrained, or find employment.
Former MP Ruby Dhalla also pledged to provide mental health care to all Canadians and appoint a health czar to find ways to improve the system.
Liberals will select a new leader on March 9.
CAPTION: Liberal leadership candidate Chrystia Freeland speaks to the Toronto Star's editorial board on Feb. 10, 2025.

Subject: Leadership; Canadians; Student loans; Nurses; Employment; Basic income

Document 228 of 500

Mark Carney won't rule out calling early election if he wins leadership, as NDP memo warns candidates to prepare

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/mark-carney-wont-rule-out-calling-early-election/docview/3166217933/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Mark+Carney+won%27t+rule+out+calling+early+election+if+he+wins+leadership%2C+as+NDP+memo+warns+candidates+to+prepare&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Liberal leadership contender Mark Carney is not ruling out calling an early election if he wins the party's leadership and replaces Justin Trudeau as prime minister on March 9.
At the same time, the Liberal party says it has told its organizers to set up campaign offices next month, when the Liberals choose their new leader and the minority government faces election-hungry opposition parties with the return of Parliament.
At a press conference in Vancouver on Thursday, Carney said that as Liberal leader he "will do" what is best for the country as it faces punishing trade tariffs from the United States. That could mean recalling Parliament or calling an early election, he said.
"If Parliament needs to be recalled for certain reasons, it will be," Carney said Thursday. "If it makes sense to get a strong mandate at that point, that is what will follow."
As it stands, Parliament is set to return on March 24, after Trudeau suspended the session with a prorogation when he announced his intention to resign as prime minister on Jan. 6.
All three main opposition parties - the Conservatives, Bloc Québécois and New Democrats - have said they want an election and would vote down the Liberal minority government as soon as possible.
NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh, however, has also said the government should speak to opposition parties about a possible relief package for Canadian workers who could face job losses in a trade war with the U.S.
But the NDP is also gearing up for a snap election that they expect Carney to call if he wins the Liberal leadership.
In an internal memo sent Wednesday to candidates and staff, the New Democrats' national campaign director urged party members to prepare for an election call as early as March 10, one day after Trudeau's successor is chosen.
"It is becoming more and more likely that Mark Carney will be the next Liberal leader on March 9," Jennifer Howard says in the memo obtained by the Star.
"If Mark Carney does win the leadership, we are hearing from many Ottawa sources that he intends on calling an election shortly after becoming Liberal leader."
Other leadership campaigns, with candidates who - unlike Carney - currently have seats in the House of Commons, were explicit about trying to work in the minority Parliament.
"As a sitting member of Parliament, Chrystia Freeland is ready to work with Parliament and all political parties to fight Donald Trump and to fight for Canada," said Freeland's spokesperson Katherine Cuplinskas.
Responding by email, a spokesperson for the Karina Gould campaign said they don't believe Canadians want an election right now.
"We're focused on fighting for Canadians interests and ensuring Parliament works to support Canadians and pass critical legislation," said spokesperson Emily Jackson. "We hope the other parties will be able to come together to keep Parliament functioning and not play politics."
Former MP Frank Baylis's campaign, meanwhile, has said he would not call an early election.
Two Liberal MPs confirmed to the Star on Thursday that the party has asked organizers to get ready for a coming general election campaign, though neither of them found the message unusual, given the overall political situation.
"The party is recommending that candidates start to look for campaign offices, get ready for an election," said one MP, who spoke about internal party matters on condition of anonymity.
In an emailed statement to the Star, Liberal spokesperson Parker Lund confirmed the party wants campaign teams to be ready with offices "in March," when the House is set to return.
Alleging Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre "can't be trusted to stand up for Canadians against Trump," Lund said "the Liberal Party of Canada is ready to fight and win the next election, whenever it may arise."
The Liberals currently have candidates nominated in 146 of the 343 ridings where Canadians will vote in the next election.
In the NDP memo, which was first reported by the Globe and Mail, Howard reiterates the NDP's position that Parliament be recalled to deal with U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff threats, but says no Liberal leadership contestant has reached out to opposition leaders about passing legislation in Parliament that deals with Trump.
"On that basis, we believe the rumours of Mr. Carney calling a snap election are credible. Taking a page from Doug Ford's playbook, the Liberals believe that they can turn this crisis into a political benefit for them," Howard said.
"For that reason, I am asking all campaigns to be prepared for an election call to come as early as March 10th."
The NDP says it has about 140 candidates nominated so far.
Vandana Kattar, a Liberal strategist who worked in Trudeau's office for more than five years, said she doesn't think Carney would call an immediate election if he is elected as Liberal leader.
She said she would expect the former central banker, who has no experience in electoral politics, to want to take some time to understand the Liberal party political apparatus, get briefed by government officials and deal with Trump's tariff threats.
She said it also makes sense the NDP is trying to prepare its staff in an effort to motivate them, since she sees Carney bringing momentum to the Liberals.
The Conservatives, meanwhile, have been targeting Carney for criticism, describing the current government as the "Carney-Trudeau" Liberals. They've alleged that, while in the private sector, Carney supported construction of fossil fuel pipelines in other countries, despite agreeing with the government's 2016 decision to reject the Northern Gateway pipeline through northern British Columbia.
And they contend the former central banker - who has previously espoused support for carbon pricing - can't be trusted when he says he will ditch the Trudeau government's consumer carbon price-and-rebate system and replace it with a scheme of industry-paid green incentives.
On Thursday, the Conservatives accused Carney of downplaying opioid overdoses in Canada after he referred to fentanyl as a "challenge" in Canada as opposed to a "crisis" in the U.S.
Trump has criticized Canada for allowing fentanyl to cross the border, and has used that to justify the threat of tariffs, even though less than one per cent of fentanyl intercepted by U.S. border agents comes through the Canadian border.
Sarah Fischer, a spokesperson for the Conservative party, said the Tories will be ready with "a full slate of candidates and a full bank account" whenever the next election happens.
With files from Tonda MacCharles

The Leger survey suggests a Carney-led party would boost Liberal support by six points to 37 per cent, putting them in a dead heat with the Tories. Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives are still riding high in majority territory at 40 per cent.(Feb. 11, 2025 / The Canadian Press)Mark Carney, candidate for the leadership of the Liberal Party of Canada, makes an announcement at a campaign event in Halifax on Jan. 31.

Fly the flag Canada - show our unity

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/fly-flag-canada-show-our-unity/docview/3166092808/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Fly+the+flag+Canada+-+show+our+unity&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Former prime ministers call on Canadians to 'show the flag' as Trump pushes trade war, Feb. 11
Let's all fly the Canadian flag on Saturday Feb. 15 as a show of national solidarity and opposition to U.S. President Donald Trump's attacks on Canada. It's the 60th anniversary of its adoption. Fly it from your house, your car, or your hat to show our unity and resolve.
Steve Andrews, Toronto
Costly 401 tunnel would do nothing to alleviate congestion - cars have to surface somewhereDoug Ford's 401 tunnel completes the campaign pledge trifecta: costly, unworkable, ineffective, Feb. 11
As a transportation engineer with 40 years of experience, I am writing to add my voice to those opposing Premier Doug Ford's plan to tunnel under Highway 401. This incredibly costly project would do nothing to enhance Ontario's competitiveness or alleviate congestion. All the extra cars using this tunnel have to come to the surface somewhere, and they would simply clog the highway network even further. Construction would also disrupt communities all along Highway 401 for years. Everyone who cares about cost-effective infrastructure should ask their PC candidate whether they support this, and why.
Jim Gough, Toronto

A great role model and a beacon of positive thinking
Alzheimer's can make my dad hard to reach. Here's what I do to bring him back, Feb. 9
What a delightful, positive story by Joel Rubinoff, son of Al Rubinoff, who at 93 and with advanced Alzheimer's is still amazingly active. I remember "Alfie" Rubinoff as a member of Club Kenton, a group of guys who absolutely loved Stan Kenton, his music, and his lead vocalist June Christie. We all went to a wonderful Kenton concert at Palace Pier, back in the day when the property actually was a concert hall, long before any luxury condo towers were built. And of course, Alfie Rubinoff attended. Over subsequent years when Club Kenton met for lunch on many occasions, we kidded Alfie about probably coming to lunch on his bike, weather notwithstanding. As I approach my 92nd birthday, I am reminded of Alfie Rubinoff, a great role model, and a beacon of positive thinking.
Marty Meslin, Toronto
Buffy stripped of Order of Canada while geneticist who ordered the forced sterilizations of Indigenous kids keeps hersSinger-songwriter Buffy Sainte-Marie stripped of Order of Canada, Feb. 7
Singer Buffy Sainte-Marie's Order of Canada from 1997 has been "terminated" by the Governor General for no stated reason, but presumably because her Indigenous heritage was called into question.
Margaret Thompson, a renowned geneticist and genetic counsellor, was inducted into the Order in 1988, despite the fact that she served on the Alberta Eugenics Board from 1960 to 1963. Eugenics, a long-discredited pseudo-science that was notably championed in Nazi Germany, is based on ridding the gene pool of "mental defectives" through forced sterilizations.
In its run from 1928 to 1972, the Alberta Eugenics Board approved nearly 3,000 involuntary sterilizations of those under 18 with "arrested or incomplete development of mind, whether arising from inherent causes or induced by disease or injury."
According to one study, First Nations people were overrepresented as victims. They formed two to three per cent of Alberta's population but six per cent of all sterilizations. Of all cases involving Indigenous people, 74 per cent were approved for sterilization. The Alberta government has faced hundreds of lawsuits from victims.
Thompson, who died in 2014, never apologized for or walked back her role on the board. A request some years ago to revoke her Order of Canada was denied.
So Sainte-Marie is stripped of her Order of Canada because serious doubts were raised about her Indigenous identity, while Thomson, who served on the board that ordered the forced sterilizations of Indigenous kids, got to keep hers.
Ron Csillag, Toronto
Are we not judged on our character?
I question the decision to throw Buffy Sainte-Marie under the bus. Was she given the Order of Canada simply because she was a famous Indigenous person or because she did so much to promote Indigenous rights and human rights in general? Was she removed from the Order of Canada because she is not Indigenous and her lifetime of good work means nothing? Are we not judged on our character? Cancelling people is simple-minded nonsense and must stop now. I say reinstate her. Apologize and thank her for her good work.
Kurt Crist, Consecon, ON
Food insecurity has a lifelong impactToronto city council has a chance to do something about this shameful new statistic, Feb. 11
It is in Toronto's best interest to implement the Universal student food program not just to address hunger today but to prevent the future issues that children may have as adults. Children's hunger is different from adult hunger in that they are still growing and developing. A lack of proper nutrition can cause permanent damage to their cognitive development, physical growth and health. Their immune systems can be compromised and if they are malnourished they can suffer irreversible damage to their bodies and brains. This investment, if passed by council, will help ensure that the children of today will grow into healthy adults. A small investment in nutrition will also ameliorate pressure on our ravaged heath care system. To me it is just common sense.
Sandy Milakovic, Mississauga
Help our steel industry by promoting steel roofs
If the federal and provincial governments provided financial incentives for homeowners and builders to install steel roofing, we could help our steel industry and strengthen Team Canada in the tariff wars. I recently replaced my old tar shingles on my cottage in Port Carling with steel ones. Given the heavy snow that Muskoka receives, a steel roof is lighter and less prone to collapse. Also, steel roofs are more fire and animal resistant, prevent the growth of moss, and may last 40-50 years, adding value. Perhaps at a later date the steel materials used can be recycled. If we have incentives to buy EV's, why not for steel roofs? Hopefully homeowners and our governments will consider these options
Dave Rodman, Mississauga
The return of the 'Ugly American'Trump says he is serious about Canada becoming 51st state in Super Bowl interview, Feb. 11
Hell no! This is not happening! Donald Trump needs to fix his own homegrown deficit, health-care issues and border mayhem just to list a few before he dares to cast an eye on his neighbour's sovereignty. He is succeeding in bringing back the "Ugly American" to life.
Indera Narain, Scarborough

Document 230 of 500

Premiers take message to Washington: Provincial, territorial leaders press their case against tariffs with U.S. lawmakers, and hold 'frank' discussion with White House staff

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/premiers-take-message-washington/docview/3166092756/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Premiers+take+message+to+Washington%3A+Provincial%2C+territorial+leaders+press+their+case+against+tariffs+with+U.S.+lawmakers%2C+and+hold+%27frank%27+discussion+with+White+House+staff&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada has taken its concerns about U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs to the belly of the beast.
Federal, provincial and territorial officials were at the White House on Wednesday to urge the Trump administration not to impose the levies that would damage the Canadian and American economies.
The flurry of meetings came as Prime Minister Justin Trudeau warned Ottawa would respond "in an equally unequivocal way" if the president stacked tariffs upon tariffs, and that Canada would impose countermeasures against U.S. imports, despite Trump's warnings that he would further escalate U.S. levies.
In Washington, federal Finance Minister Dominic LeBlanc insisted diplomacy is working.
LeBlanc met with commerce secretary nominee Howard Lutnick and Kevin Hassett, Trump's top economic adviser, and updated them on the appointment of former RCMP deputy commissioner Kevin Brosseau as "fentanyl czar" to curb cross-border drug trafficking.
"So I'm optimistic that that conversation is very constructive. I'm also optimistic that we have a better understanding of the process that Mr. Lutnick is going through in terms of the April 1 ... global tariff decisions," LeBlanc said after his 90-minute meeting at the White House with Lutnick, Hassett and Kirsten Hillman, Canada's ambassador to the U.S.
"We also discussed, obviously, our concern around steel and aluminum tariffs," LeBlanc said, referring to Trump's planned 25 per cent tariffs that are supposed to take effect on March 12.
But LeBlanc said the president's repeated musings about making Canada "the 51st state" did not come up in the high-level discussion.
"I'm confident that we very much have their attention," he said. "I told them clearly that Canadians have become quite emotional in the last number of weeks and quite united in terms of doing what's necessary to avoid this punitive impact on our economy."
For the first time, all 13 provincial and territorial leaders were in the American capital at the same time, as they warned U.S. lawmakers that Trump's levies would cripple the economies of both nations.
The subnational leaders also secured an afternoon meeting at the White House with Trump's deputy chief of staff, Jim Blair, and Sergio Gor, director of the presidential personnel office.
Ontario's Doug Ford, here on the first of two diplomatic trips during the campaign for the Feb. 27 provincial election, said the provincial leaders were "very grateful" for the audience.
"People don't get last-minute meetings like this," Ford said outside the White House.
Prior to the one-hour sit-down, he reiterated the goal of all the Canadians lobbying Trump.
"What success looks like is zero tariffs. We're their largest trading partner and their No. 1 customer, and I'm not too sure that they fully understand the impact on both countries," said Ford, the Progressive Conservative leader whose trip has been criticized by Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and NDP Leader Marit Stiles.
"Interest rates will go up. Plants will be closed on both sides of the border. And the more we communicate, the better it is."
British Columbia Premier David Eby said "there were some frank moments across the table" with Blair and Gor.
"They urged us to take the president at his word, so we will certainly do that," said Eby, adding he told the staffers it was a "non-starter" that Canada would be annexed by the U.S.
Afterward, Blair tweeted: "To be clear, we never agreed that Canada would not be the 51st state. We only agreed to share Premier Eby's comments" with Trump.
While the premiers met the aides, Trump was nearby in the Oval Office again touting tariffs.
Mindful of that, Quebec Premier François Legault, also here as part of the Council of the Federation lobbying mission, said it is necessary to be ready to retaliate, given the planned tariffs on steel and aluminum.
Legault said "retaliation must be done if and only if there are tariffs that are really charged."
"So far, it won't be done before March 4, but we have to prepare ourselves. We already have an enhanced list of counter-tariffs. I think you should consider also putting exportation tariffs on products like aluminum, where they really need us," Legault said.
Alberta Premier Danielle Smith appealed for calm.
"I think diplomacy is working. The fact that we got a 30-day reprieve was because we agreed to work on the cross-border problem of fentanyl. I was pleased to see the federal government announce the fentanyl czar, and my administration has already been in touch with him," said Smith.
"What I've seen from this president is that he is prepared to make a deal," added the Alberta premier. "We've made a deal on that. I think we can make a deal on steel and aluminum by making very much the same argument that I'm making on energy and critical minerals."
"We've won the argument that those raw materials are essential to American success. It comes in, it makes value-added products, it creates good American jobs, it creates good American products that we buy back. Same thing for aluminum. They just do not make enough for their defence industry, their auto industry and their critical needs," she said.
"So I think aluminum can make the very same argument. And on steel, we buy as much steel product from them as they buy."
Hillman, for her part, said it was useful to have so many Canadian political leaders in Washington at once.
"Our job is to demonstrate in as much granularity as possible - across all regions of this country - what the (Canadian) relationship with the United States means for Americans," the ambassador said, adding that a heightened awareness of how the U.S. benefits from the relationship could "change the benefit calculation of the White House and the president with regards to the imposition of those tariffs."
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief Tonda MacCharles Toronto Star

Document 231 of 500

Despite the cold and the distractions we should all exert what influence we can on Feb. 27

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/despite-cold-distractions-we-should-all-exert/docview/3166092680/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Despite+the+cold+and+the+distractions+we+should+all+exert+what+influence+we+can+on+Feb.+27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The president of the U.S. is threatening to make Canada the 51st state and has already launched the first volleys in a trade war. An ascendant Liberal party, bolstered by Trump-induced anger and anxiety, has made the federal horse race more interesting than expected. The tensions - and drama - are high.
No wonder, then, that so few seem to be paying attention to an Ontario election that nobody much wanted, at a time of year not suited to elections, in which an incumbent leader (albeit with an approval rating of 38 per cent) is expected to romp to victory.
Turning out voters was already a struggle in this province. A historically low not-quite 44 per cent of 10.7 million eligible voters cast a ballot in the 2022 general election, and that one was in June, and everyone knew it was coming. The Tories formed a majority government with just 18 per cent of the total potential vote.
That's not good for democracy. And whether you love Doug Ford, tolerate him or detest his politics, it won't be good for democracy if he is allowed to waltz back into the premier's office without a real debate on the issues that matter.
Take family doctors. About two and a half million Ontarians were without one last year, and that figure is projected to increase. Every party is promising to invest in solving the problem, but they differ significantly on how, how urgently and at what cost. The NDP, for instance, has promised to spend $4 billion to recruit 3,500 doctors over the next four years, while the Progressive Conservatives propose to spend less than half as much on a more modest plan. The Liberals, as one might expect, fall somewhere in the middle.
We may not have wanted this election, but we now face an important choice: Are you willing to pay higher taxes or for the province to incur more debt for a more urgent response to the family-doctor shortage that torments so many families in this province? Or do you prefer a more affordable, but slower and partial solution?
Or take housing, an area of largely provincial jurisdiction in which all three parties are making expensive (yet uncosted) promises. So many of the challenges we face in this province stem from the lack of affordable housing. And while all parties are vowing to confront the crisis, they differ profoundly on rent controls, on nonmarket housing and on the controversial question of fourplexes. On the latter, both the Liberals and the NDP want developers to be able to build four-unit buildings without municipal approval, while the PCs oppose such a plan.
Again, Ontarians face a clear - and crucial - choice: Is it more important that fourplexes be constructed, allowing for what housing experts call gentle densification, or that municipalities have the power to reject such plans if they are worried about what it will mean for the infrastructure and character of their neighbourhoods?
And it goes way beyond the doctor shortage or the question of fourplexes. Education and municipalities, property rights and civil rights - these are at least partly provincial issues. In other words, much of the stuff that makes up the day-to-day life of your average Ontarian.
Of course, if you're reading this, you likely already know that provincial elections matter. But like many people in this province, you've got a lot going on right now: groceries are unaffordable, commutes are miserable, Trump is scary, the Raptors are terrible.
But this is why we must care, and why we should urge our friends to care, despite the cold and all the competing demands on our attention. On Feb. 27, we'll have a rare opportunity to exert a little influence, to try to preserve what's best in our province and city and fix what's broken. Well, maybe not the Raptors. But so much else.
CAPTION: A poll clerk puts up Elections Ontario voting signs outside of the Mississauga-Erin Mills polling station in this 2018 file photo.

Subject: Provincial elections; Voters; Elections

Title: Despite the cold and the distractions we should all exert what influence we can on Feb. 27

Document 232 of 500

I fact checked everything Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said for a week. Here's how many false claims she made

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/i-fact-checked-everything-liberal-leader-bonnie/docview/3166084042/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=I+fact+checked+everything+Liberal+Leader+Bonnie+Crombie+said+for+a+week.+Here%27s+how+many+false+claims+she+made&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hammering lawn signs into frozen soil. Touring the province in giant buses. Hot mic moments. Attack ads.
The 2025 Ontario cold snap election is well underway and that means speeches and promises - but also embellishments, stretches and at times, falsehoods.
The Star's fact-check project is back and every week until election day on Feb. 27, Star reporters will monitor, track, record and correct (where needed) the three leading provincial party leaders on the campaign trail.
However, tracking down hard facts to test the leaders' claims for a week straight isn't as simple as it may sound. Official and credible health organizations can offer competing data, and non-partisan experts might have different professional perspectives on sweeping statements simply because those claims lack nuance.
What's more, a fact-check needs a fact to check. Sentiments don't count because you can't tell someone how they feel is inaccurate. What leaders may or may not be hearing at every door knock is nearly impossible to verify unless you tail every waking moment on the campaign trail. And campaign promises aren't technically statements of fact - that would require a test of feasibility.
And because politicians, or anyone for that matter, rarely talk the way they would write, generalizations without context are extremely common.
That's where explanation and analysis comes in - starting with Liberal party Leader Bonnie Crombie, who was mostly truthful but got some statistics wrong in a few of her major campaign talking points.
Crombie is predominantly running a health-care platform - and anyone who's tried to fact check health-care data will quickly learn it is difficult to pin down with 100 per cent certainty. That's because provincial data on some issues is either not public or inaccessible, so data collection for public sharing is often left to health-care unions, non-profit organizations or other credible agencies that aren't affiliated with the government.
Out of 40 claims the Star tracked in Crombie's first week campaigning, she stretched the truth or lacked enough context that it painted an incomplete picture six times.
Crombie also cited a total of nine falsehoods - including one false statement that outright misrepresented a key health-care statistic that's plastered on the side of her big red campaign bus touring the province.
CAPTION: Crombie fact check poster

Subject: Negative campaigning

Title: I fact checked everything Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said for a week. Here's how many false claims she made

Document 233 of 500

Ford ducks accountability in D.C. sideshow

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-ducks-accountability-d-c-sideshow/docview/3166084028/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+ducks+accountability+in+D.C.+sideshow&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford auditioned for his big screen moment this week.
"Mr. Ford Goes to Washington" made the headlines in print and online, because headline writers can't resist a cinematic reference when a politician tries for the big time.
But life doesn't always imitate art. In real life, Doug Ford could never pass a screen test to win the role of Jefferson Smith, the truth-telling corruption fighter famously played by James Stewart in the 1939 Frank Capra classic, "Mr. Smith Goes to Washington."
That morality tale made movie history. Ford's phoney photo ops in Washington were made for the campaign trail, not a movie trailer.
Bad enough that this bad script doesn't ring true. What if Mr. Ford went to Washington without the audience that matters - neither an audience with Donald Trump (which he's been pining for publicly), nor an attentive audience among voters back home?
What if a provincial politician flew to the epicentre of a global power, as a subnational leader vowing to stare down the rogue president of a superpower, only to find that the truly important people wouldn't see him because everyone saw through him?
No matter. Everyone knows it's all make-believe.
Like the protagonist in a motion picture, Ford is a politician just going through the motions. We now know, from his unguarded comments on an open mic last week, that Ford was privately rooting "100 per cent" for Trump's return to the White House - notwithstanding his platform of reimposing tariffs all along.
Ford's embarrassing admission brings to mind those fake online ads claiming that a famous politician or newscaster has been caught on tape confessing to a bizarre secret. All too true in Ford's case.
In his quest to be re-elected as premier while riding high in the polls, all he needed was a pretext - any context at all - to justify the absurdly early election he triggered this month. Which is why Washington is nothing more than a backdrop for Ford's political play, just like a Hollywood sound stage in the original blockbuster movie.
Yes, Ontario's campaigning Progressive Conservative leader got into the White House on Wednesday - along with all the other premiers in the Canadian delegation - but not into the Oval Office. Despite talking up the idea of a personal talk with Trump, he never had the chance to cajole and confront the president personally.
Ford didn't gain entrée to the White House thanks to any imaginary "massive mandate" that he's seeking from voters on election day - more than two weeks from now. No, he was part of a posse of premiers fobbed off all day on mid-level officials with middling influence on Trump's tariff plans.
While federal Finance Minister Dominic LeBlanc was doing the heavy lifting in a meeting with U.S. Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick and the president's top economic adviser, Kevin Hassett, the premiers got in to see Trump's deputy chief of staff, Jim Blair. That's not nothing, and that's not to say it wasn't worth a try.
To be sure, Canada needs every bit of help. But not hype from someone on the hustings.
As rotating chair of the Council of the Federation that brings together all the premiers, Ford had a serious job to do in Washington this week alongside the Team Canada delegation. Instead of making that his priority, he contorted the trip into a campaign trail stop, complete with a chartered jet paid for by Ford's Tories in full election mode.
His cameo as Captain Canada is becoming repetitive. After winning some positive reviews early on, posing with baseball caps while purporting to defend Canada from a hostile takeover, Ford keeps trying to reprise his imagined role as saviour of the province, of the nation, of Ford Nation.
But what of the other premiers who were part of the goodwill mission to America in bad times? At least they were faithfully fulfilling their responsibilities to defend their provincial interests and the national interest, without prioritizing their self-interest.
Consider Quebec's François Legault, who is facing devastating tariffs on aluminum exports that emanate primarily from his province: Legault hasn't called an early election to exploit the political and economic uncertainty as Ford has next door.
One can only imagine the ambivalence of premiers like Legault - who are still on the job, not on the campaign trail - as they watch Ford repurpose his rotating chairmanship of their federation. Instead of politicizing the Team Canada trip for his own electoral gain, rather than dissolve the legislature and downgrade himself to the role of caretaker premier for the duration of the campaign, Ford should have been fully on the job like all the others, allowing Ontario's legally mandated fixed election date to take place as scheduled in June 2026 - nearly 16 months from now.
In truth, the real story of the campaign is that Ford's cynical attempts to profit from Trump's frontal attacks are now looking like a sideshow. On cue, Ford is trying to distract voters from local issues that matter - health care, homelessness and an RCMP probe into the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal that he opted to pre-empt with an early election call.
All of which would surely disqualify him from playing the role of the earnest rookie politician made famous by James Stewart in "Mr. Smith Goes to Washington." But Ford's love of performance knows no limits - he's already planning a return trip to Washington next week.
Stay tuned as he hobnobs and appears on U.S. television while keeping a safe distance from the campaign trail.
CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION

Subject: Political campaigns; Audiences; Scandals; Voters; Politicians; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Tariffs

Location: United States--US; Canada

Document 234 of 500

Greens to build two million homes: Liberals promise pay equity for health workers in hospitals, nursing homes and the community

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/greens-build-two-million-homes/docview/3166082073/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Greens+to+build+two+million+homes%3A+Liberals+promise+pay+equity+for+health+workers+in+hospitals%2C+nursing+homes+and+the+community&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Green Party of Ontario will build two million homes in a decade, cut taxes for low- and middle-income earners and create a "foodbelt" to protect farmland if it wins the election, leader Mike Schreiner pledged Wednesday as he released the party's platform.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, meanwhile, promised to end hospital hallway health care in part by paying nurses and personal support workers more and ensuring wage parity across the entire system.
Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford remains in Washington, in his capacity as Ontario premier as he attempts to head off looming U.S. tariffs, while NDP Leader Marit Stiles had no public events Wednesday.
The Greens are the first party to release a fully costed platform. The NDP and Liberals say they will release costed platforms soon, while the Conservatives committed to releasing their plan in the coming days, although it wasn't clear if it will be costed.
Speaking to the party faithful in Toronto, Schreiner cast this election as a self-interested political gambit by Ford that nonetheless presented an opportunity for the Greens to push for a fair and affordable province.
He mostly spared the Liberals and NDP from attacks, while accusing Ford of cosying up to billionaires at the expense of working Ontarians and rolling back environmental protections. He was flanked at the podium by the party's co-deputy leaders Aislinn Clancy, the only Green candidate other than Schreiner to win a seat, and Matt Richter, who's running again in Parry Sound-Muskoka after finishing just behind the winning PC candidate in the last election.
Schreiner said he would legalize fourplexes across the province and midrise residential buildings six to 11 storeys tall in larger cities. He would also end the land transfer tax for first-time homebuyers, which would cost about $1 billion over the next four years.
He would also cut taxes for those earning less than $65,000 or households earning less than $100,000, at a cost of $18.8 billion over four years, as well as double the rates of both the Ontario Disability Support Program and Ontario Works.
"Our plan addresses the fairness that we need to build affordable homes and protect renters, to bring fairness back to our health care and education systems, to protect the prime farmland that feeds us and lower your energy bills and reduce climate pollution," Schreiner said.
The Greens would also build 250,000 affordable rental and 60,000 supportive units over 10 years that would cost $2.7 billion over the next four years. They pledge to pay non-profits to operate the 60,000 supportive homes at a cost of nearly $2.5 billion over four years.
On the other side of the balance sheet, the Green plan includes a tax hike for the province's top earners. The platform estimates a three per cent increase to the top tax bracket would bring in $2.5 billion a year in new revenue.
The plan also comes up with just over $2 billion a year in new revenue through three new taxes targeting housing speculators: a vacant home tax, an "anti-flipping tax" on quick turnaround sales and a multiple property speculation tax. The speculation tax would start at 25 per cent on a third home and increase with each additional property, the platform says.
"We can't continue to live in a province without generational fairness, where a whole generation of young people, including my 25-year-old daughter, are wondering if they'll ever be able to own a home in this province. We have to fix this. Ontario Greens will fix it," Schreiner said.
The platform's largest single source of additional revenue - $4.7 billion a year starting in 2026 - comes from working with the federal government to implement a one-time wealth tax over three years, although the platform offers no details about how the Greens would make that proposal a reality.
Earlier Wednesday, Crombie said the Liberals would address a long-standing issue in health care: pay parity.
"It shouldn't matter where that nurse or where that PSW is employed," Crombie said. "Whether they are in the community in a long-term-care facility or in the hospital, there should be parity in their wages."
The COVID-19 pandemic laid bare the problem of unequal pay among nurses and personal support workers. Generally, hospitals paid nurses and PSWs more than long-term-care homes, which in turn paid more than home-care services.
The lack of pay parity played a major role in nursing home staffing shortages during the pandemic, Ontario's Long-Term Care COVID-19 Commission concluded.
Dr. Raghu Venugopal, an emergency department physician who spoke at Crombie's news conference as she announced her plan to end hallway medicine, said a "tragedy" is happening in emergency departments across Ontario.
He listed a number of stories he and his colleagues have collected about the situation he describes as "chair medicine."
"An unfortunate mother actively having a miscarriage, treated in a chair," Venugopal said.
"A palliative care patient with cancer riddling his bones and with pain, treated in a chair. A patient after surgery with complications with their abdomen open, treated in a chair."
He said many patients are waiting six to 12 hours to see an emergency department doctor and then waiting another 24 hours for a hospital bed.
"I believe Doug Ford has failed Ontario ERs," Venugopal said. "I believe he has failed our publicly funded health-care system, and I believe he has failed this great province."
Ford has defended his record on health care and blamed the previous Liberal government for cuts to medical school seats and nursing jobs.
CAPTION: Ontario Green Leader Mike Schreiner unveiled the party's election platform on Wednesday.It includes tax cuts for low- and middle-income earners and the creation of a "foodbelt" to protect farmland.Chris Young The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Liam Casey and Jordan Omstead

Document 235 of 500

Here's why your vote matters on Feb. 27

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/heres-why-your-vote-matters-on-feb-27/docview/3166080906/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Here%27s+why+your+vote+matters+on+Feb.+27&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The president of the U.S. is threatening to make Canada the 51st state and has already launched the first volleys in a trade war. An ascendant Liberal party, bolstered by Trump-induced anger and anxiety, has made the federal horse race more interesting than expected. The tensions - and drama - are high.
No wonder, then, that so few seem to be paying attention to an Ontario election that nobody much wanted, at a time of year not suited to elections, in which an incumbent leader (albeit with an approval rating of 38 per cent) is expected to romp to victory.
Turning out voters was already a struggle in this province. A historically low not-quite 44 per cent of 10.7 million eligible voters cast a ballot in the 2022 general election, and that one was in June, and everyone knew it was coming. The Tories formed a majority government with just 18 per cent of the total potential vote.
That's not good for democracy. And whether you love Doug Ford, tolerate him or detest his politics, it won't be good for democracy if he is allowed to waltz back into the premier's office without a real debate on the issues that matter.
Take family doctors. About two-and-a-half million Ontarians were without one last year, and that figure is projected to increase. Every party is promising to invest in solving the problem, but they differ significantly on how, how urgently and at what cost.
The NDP, for instance, has promised to spend $4 billion to recruit 3,500 doctors over the next four years, while the Progressive Conservatives propose to spend less than half as much on a more modest plan. The Liberals, as one might expect, fall somewhere in the middle.
We may not have wanted this election, but we now face an important choice: Are you willing to pay higher taxes or for the province to incur more debt for a more urgent response to the family-doctor shortage that torments so many families in this province? Or do you prefer a more affordable, but slower and partial solution?
Or take housing, an area of largely provincial jurisdiction in which all three parties are making expensive (yet uncosted) promises. So many of the challenges we face in this province stem from the lack of affordable housing.
And while all parties are vowing to confront the crisis, they differ profoundly on rent controls, on non-market housing and on the controversial question of fourplexes. On the latter, both the Liberals and the NDP want developers to be able to build four-unit buildings without municipal approval, while the PCs oppose such a plan.
Again, Ontarians face a clear - and crucial - choice: Is it more important that fourplexes be constructed, allowing for what housing experts call gentle densification, or that municipalities have the power to reject such plans if they are worried about what it will mean for the infrastructure and character of their neighbourhoods?
And it goes way beyond the doctor shortage or the question of fourplexes. Education and municipalities, property rights and civil rights - these are at least partly provincial issues. In other words, much of the stuff that makes up the day-to-day life of your average Ontarian.
Of course, if you're reading this, you likely already know that provincial elections matter. But like many people in this province, you've got a lot going on right now: groceries are unaffordable, commutes are miserable, Trump is scary, the Raptors are terrible.
But this is why we must care, and why we should urge our friends to care, despite the cold and all the competing demands on our attention. On Feb. 27, we'll have a rare opportunity to exert a little influence, to try to preserve what's best in our province and city and fix what's broken. Well, maybe not the Raptors. But so much else.
CAPTION: Just 44 per cent of 10.7 million eligible voters cast a ballot in the 2022 Ontario election. The Tories formed a majority government with just 18 per cent of the total potential vote.MARTA IWANEK THE CANADIAN PRESS FILE PHOTO

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Former+prime+ministers+call+on+Canadians+to+%27show+the+flag%27+as+Trump+pushes+trade+war%2C+Feb.+11+Doug+Ford%27s+401+tunnel+completes+the+campaign+pledge+trifecta%3A+costly%2C+unworkable%2C+ineffective%2C+Opinion%2C+Feb.+11+Alzheimer%27s+can+make+my+dad+hard+to+reach.+Here%27s+what+I+do+to+bring+him+back%2C+Feb.+9+Singer-songwriter+Buffy+Sainte-Marie+stripped+of+Order+of+Canada%2C+Feb.+7+Toronto+city+council+has+a+chance+to+do+something+about+this+shameful+new+statistic%2C+Opinion%2C+Feb.+11&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Let's fly the flag
this Saturday
to show our unity
Former prime ministers call on Canadians to 'show the flag' as Trump pushes trade war, Feb. 11
Let's all fly the Canadian flag on Saturday as a show of national solidarity and opposition to U.S. President Donald Trump's attacks on Canada. It's the 60th anniversary of its adoption. Fly it from your house, your car or your hat to show our unity and resolve.
Steve Andrews, Toronto
Costly 401 tunnel would do nothing to cut congestion
Doug Ford's 401 tunnel completes the campaign pledge trifecta: costly, unworkable, ineffective, Opinion, Feb. 11
As a transportation engineer with 40 years of experience, I am writing to add my voice to those opposing Premier Doug Ford's plan to tunnel under Highway 401. This incredibly costly project would do nothing to enhance Ontario's competitiveness or alleviate congestion. All the extra cars using this tunnel have to come to the surface somewhere, and they would simply clog the highway network even further. Construction would also disrupt communities along Highway 401 for years. Everyone who cares about cost-effective infrastructure should ask their PC candidate whether they support this, and why.
Jim Gough, Toronto
'Alfie' Rubinoff
is a beacon of positive thinking
Alzheimer's can make my dad hard to reach. Here's what I do to bring him back, Feb. 9
What a delightful, positive story by Joel Rubinoff, son of Al Rubinoff, who at 93 and with advanced Alzheimer's is still amazingly active. I remember "Alfie" Rubinoff as a member of Club Kenton, a group of guys who absolutely loved Stan Kenton, his music and his lead vocalist June Christie. We all went to a wonderful Kenton concert at Palace Pier, back in the day when the property actually was a concert hall, long before any luxury condo towers were built. And of course, Alfie attended. Over subsequent years when Club Kenton met for lunch on many occasions, we kidded Alfie about probably coming to lunch on his bike, weather notwithstanding. As I approach my 92nd birthday, I am reminded of Alfie, a great role model, and a beacon of positive thinking.
Marty Meslin, Toronto
Strip the Order
of Canada from eugenics scientist
Singer-songwriter Buffy Sainte-Marie stripped of Order of Canada, Feb. 7
Singer Buffy Sainte-Marie's Order of Canada from 1997 has been "terminated" by the Governor General for no stated reason, but presumably because her Indigenous heritage was called into question.
Margaret Thompson, a renowned geneticist and genetic counsellor, was inducted into the Order in 1988, despite the fact she served on the Alberta Eugenics Board from 1960 to 1963. Eugenics, a long-discredited pseudo-science that was notably championed in Nazi Germany, is based on ridding the gene pool of "mental defectives" through forced sterilizations.
In its run from 1928 to 1972, the Alberta Eugenics Board approved nearly 3,000 involuntary sterilizations of those under 18 with "arrested or incomplete development of mind, whether arising from inherent causes or induced by disease or injury."
According to one study, First Nations people were over- represented as victims. They formed two to three per cent of Alberta's population but six per cent of all sterilizations. Of all cases involving Indigenous people, 74 per cent were approved for sterilization. The Alberta government has faced hundreds of lawsuits from victims.
Thompson, who died in 2014, never apologized for, or walked back, her role on the board. A request years ago to revoke her Order of Canada was denied.
So Sainte-Marie is stripped of her Order of Canada because serious doubts were raised about her Indigenous identity, while Thomson, who served on the board that ordered the forced sterilizations of Indigenous kids, got to keep hers.
Ron Csillag, Toronto
Judge singer
on her character
I question the decision to throw Buffy Sainte-Marie under the bus. Was she given the Order of Canada simply because she was a famous Indigenous person or because she did so much to promote Indigenous rights and human rights in general? Was she removed from the Order of Canada because she is not Indigenous and her lifetime of good work means nothing? Are we not judged on our character? Cancelling people is simple-minded nonsense and must stop now. I say reinstate her. Apologize and thank her for her good work.
Kurt Crist, Consecon, Ont.
Food insecurity has a lifelong impact on kids
Toronto city council has a chance to do something about this shameful new statistic, Opinion, Feb. 11
It is in Toronto's best interest to implement the universal student food program, not just to address hunger today, but to prevent the future issues that children may have as adults. Children's hunger is different from adult hunger, in that they are still growing and developing. A lack of proper nutrition can cause permanent damage to their cognitive development, physical growth and health. Their immune systems can be compromised and, if they are malnourished, they can suffer irreversible damage to their bodies and brains. This investment, if passed by council, will help ensure the children of today will grow into healthy adults. A small investment in nutrition will also ameliorate pressure on our ravaged heath care system. To me it is just common sense.
Sandy Milakovic, Mississauga
Promote roofs
made of steel
to help industry
If the federal and provincial governments provided financial incentives for homeowners and builders to install steel roofing, we could help our steel industry and strengthen Team Canada in the tariff wars. I recently replaced my old tar shingles on my cottage in Port Carling with steel ones. Given the heavy snow Muskoka receives, a steel roof is lighter and less prone to collapse. Also, steel roofs are more fire and animal resistant, prevent the growth of moss, and may last 40 to 50 years, adding value. Perhaps at a later date the steel materials used can be recycled. If we have incentives to buy EV's, why not for steel roofs?
Dave Rodman, Mississauga

Document 237 of 500

Canada's premiers meet with Trump advisers at White House: 'I think diplomacy is working'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canadas-premiers-meet-with-trump-advisers-at/docview/3166313504/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada%27s+premiers+meet+with+Trump+advisers+at+White+House%3A+%27I+think+diplomacy+is+working%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: WASHINGTON - Canada has taken its concerns about U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs to the belly of the beast.
Federal, provincial and territorial officials were at the White House on Wednesday to urge the Trump administration not to impose the levies that would damage the Canadian and American economies.
The flurry of meetings came as Prime Minister Justin Trudeau warned Ottawa would respond "in an equally unequivocal way" if the president stacked tariffs upon tariffs in response to any countermeasures by Canada against U.S. imports.
Speaking in Brussels after meetings with European and NATO leaders, Trudeau said if Trump makes good on his threats, it could undermine Canada's relationship with the U.S.
"It's not our choice. We don't want to be doing that, but we will, regardless of the level of tariffs that eventually the administration might move forward on, we will be equally unequivocal in our response," he said.
In Washington, federal Finance Minister Dominic LeBlanc insisted diplomacy is working.
LeBlanc met here with Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick and Kevin Hassett, Trump's top economic adviser, and updated them on the appointment of former RCMP deputy commissioner Kevin Brosseau as "fentanyl czar" to curb cross-border drug trafficking.
"So I'm optimistic that that conversation is very constructive. I'm also optimistic that we have a better understanding of the process that Mr. Lutnick is going through in terms of the April 1 ... global tariff decisions," Leblanc said after his 90-minute meeting at the White House with Lutnick, Hassett and Kirsten Hillman, Canada's ambassador to the U.S.
"We also discussed, obviously, our concern around steel and aluminum tariffs," LeBlanc said, referring to Trump's planned 25 per cent tariffs that are supposed to take effect on March 12.
But LeBlanc said the president's repeated musings about making Canada "the 51st state" did not come up in the high-level discussion.
"I'm confident that we very much have their attention. I told them clearly that Canadians have become quite emotional in the last number of weeks and quite united in terms of doing what's necessary to avoid this punitive impact on our economy," he said.
For the first time, all 13 provincial and territorial leaders were in the American capital at the same time, where they warned U.S. lawmakers that Trump's levies would cripple the economies of both nations.
The subnational leaders also secured an afternoon meeting at the White House with Trump's deputy chief of staff, Jim Blair.
Some of the premiers, here as part of the Council of the Federation lobbying mission, say it is necessary to be ready to retaliate, given Trump's planned tariffs on steel and aluminum.
Quebec Premier François Legault said "retaliation must be done if and only if there are tariffs that are really charged."
"So far, it won't be done before March 4, but we have to prepare ourselves. We already have an enhanced list of counter-tariffs. I think you should consider also putting exportation tariffs on products like aluminum, where they really need us," said Legault.
Alberta Premier Danielle Smith appealed for calm.
"I think diplomacy is working. The fact that we got a 30-day reprieve was because we agreed to work on the cross-border problem of fentanyl," Smith said. "I was pleased to see the federal government announce the fentanyl czar, and my administration has already been in touch with him.
"What I've seen from this president is that he is prepared to make a deal. We've made a deal on that. I think we can make a deal on steel and aluminum by making very much the same argument that I'm making on energy and critical minerals," added the Albertan.
"We've won the argument that those raw materials are essential to American success. It comes in, it makes value-added products, it creates good American jobs, it creates good American products that we buy back. Same thing for aluminum. They just do not make enough for their defence industry, their auto industry and their critical needs," she said.
"So I think aluminum can make the very same argument. And on steel, we buy as much steel product from them as they buy."
Ontario's Doug Ford, here on the first of two diplomatic trips during the Feb. 27 provincial election campaign, said the premiers have to show the maple leaf flag.
"What success looks like is zero tariffs. We're their largest trading partner and their number-one customer and I'm not too sure that they fully understand the impact on both countries," said Ford, the Progressive Conservative leader whose trip has been criticized by Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and NDP Leader Marit Stiles.
"Interest rates will go up. Plants will be closed on both sides of the border. And the more we communicate, the better it is. We're down here to listen to our U.S. colleagues and we're down here to communicate with the U.S. colleagues and business people on both sides of the border, and we have to make sure that we communicate how important it is to be stronger together," he said before the White House meeting.
Hillman said it was useful to have so many Canadian political leaders in Washington at once.
"Our job is to demonstrate in as much granularity as possible - acros all regions of this country - what the (Canadian) relationship with the United States means for Americans," the ambassador said, adding that a heightened awareness of how the U.S. benefits from the relationship could "change the benefit calculation of the White House and the president with regards to the imposition of those tariffs."
CAPTION: Doug Ford speaks to reporters, accompanied by other Council of the Federation members, at the Mayflower Hotel in Washington on Feb. 12, 2025.

Ontario election polls show Bonnie Crombie's Liberals seeing an uptick in support

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-election-polls-show-bonnie-crombies/docview/3165834484/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+election+polls+show+Bonnie+Crombie%27s+Liberals+seeing+an+uptick+in+support&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With the Ontario election campaign underway, the Star is launching a poll tracker so you can follow all the twists and turns ahead of election day on Feb. 27.
We've got the numbers from the top pollsters in one place, with handy charts, and a rolling, last-four-poll average of all the results.
There hasn't been much change since the campaign began Jan. 29, with the Progressive Conservatives and Doug Ford far ahead of the second-place Liberals and Bonnie Crombie. The NDP and Marit Stiles are well back in third place.
In Ontario's last election, in 2022, Ford's PCs took 40.8 per cent of the popular vote across the province, with the Liberals earning 23.9 per cent and the NDP taking 23.7 per cent. That led Ford to his second straight majority government, with 83 seats. Despite finishing narrowly behind the Liberals in the popular vote, the NDP took 31 seats and the Liberals just eight.
The Star is compiling polling for the 2025 Ontario election from polling firms that received a grade of A or A+ from 338Canada during the 2022 Ontario election. 338Canada is an independent media organization that created a statistical model of electoral projections based on opinion polls, electoral history, and demographic data.
These polling firms use a variety of polling methods, including online surveys and Smart Interactive Voice Response (IVR) systems. As online polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, margins listed here are for a comparable random sample of this size.
Percentage breakdowns are shares of committed or leaning voters, in line with industry conventions.
CAPTION: Clockwise from top: Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford, Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner, NDP Leader Marit Stiles.

Document 239 of 500

Ford's plans won't end congestion, experts argue: Highway projects likely to result in minimal improvement - or possibly make it worse

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-plans-wont-end-congestion-experts-argue/docview/3165674350/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+plans+won%27t+end+congestion%2C+experts+argue%3A+Highway+projects+likely+to+result+in+minimal+improvement+-+or+possibly+make+it+worse&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford's drive to expand highway capacity will win votes for his Progressive Conservatives, but do little or nothing to reduce the congestion infuriating motorists in the GTA and beyond, according to experts.
Since first elected Ontario premier seven years ago, Ford has made drivers a priority with projects including widening parts of Highway 401, planning construction of Highway 413 through the Greenbelt in York, Peel and Halton regions, promising to eliminate tolls on the provincially run eastern section of Highway 407, and envisioning a new highway tunnelled under the 401 from Brampton to Scarborough.
The PC party says that, in this snap Feb. 27 election, it is voters' only option to "deliver on projects to get drivers out of gridlock across Ontario."
Marit Stiles' New Democrats say they would end tolls on all of Highway 407 and widen highways including 11/17, 40 and 69. The NDP accuses the Ford government of making highway planning "politicized and lobbyist driven."
Bonnie Crombie's Liberals are, so far, focusing on transit promises they say Ford has failed to deliver - including all-day, two-way GO service to Kitchener-Waterloo, Milton and Niagara - to help alleviate gridlock.
Zachary Taylor, a University of Western Ontario political scientist, says several factors combine to make it politically popular to champion vehicle use even as governments, including Ford's, say they are trying to reduce greenhouse gas emissions.
The Greater Toronto-Hamilton area's population boomed in recent decades but construction of new highways and transit systems largely stalled from the 1980s into the 2000s. That turned clear-sailing highway drives into bumper-to-bumper agony, especially on the maxed out 400-series highways.
When Ford came to power, Taylor said, he parked previous Liberal government policies aimed at protecting rural land, halting sprawl and intensifying existing development in a way that de-emphasized the car as a necessity.
"What we've seen with this Ford government is a renewed emphasis on car drivers as a political constituency. There's just way more car driving voters than there are non-car-driving voters," Taylor said, noting that after moving from Toronto to London, he quickly realized that public transit wasn't enough for him to efficiently get around.
"I think also part of what Ford has brought with him, coming out of the Toronto context, is a real frustration shared by many Torontonians - that you just can't get around the city in your car very efficiently."
But can Ford deliver us from gridlock? Two transportation experts canvassed by the Star said highway building and widening will, at great expense, yield minor or no reduction in traffic congestions, and some projects might even make it worse.
Eric Miller, director of the University of Toronto Transportation Research Institute, said research clearly shows that adding vehicle lanes will incentivized people to drive more and quickly fill up those lanes.
"Despite identifying roadway congestion in the GTA as a priority issue, the Ford government's policies to date will do nothing to offer long-term relief," Miller said. "If built, Highway 413 will make congestion worse, in that it will promote increased auto-dominated suburban sprawl, generating more traffic, not less, and do nothing to relieve congestion in the GTA core.
"We are never going to solve our congestion problem by just looking at road-based solutions. Public transit is the only long-term solution to get cars off the road and seriously reduce congestion on the roadways."
Miller says the province could reduce highway gridlock by reinstating funding to municipalities to operate and maintain transit systems cut by then-premier Mike Harris in the 1990s, and to "aggressively" use artificial intelligence to help control vehicle movements on highways and city streets.
Said Easa, a civil engineering professor at Toronto Metropolitan University, calls the Ford government's highway expansion push, coupled with transit expansion programs including Ontario Line subway construction and GO Transit improvements, "overall a well-balanced plan."
However, Easa added in an email, "the heavy emphasis on highway projects may provide only short-term relief from congestion and could harm the environment - particularly with projects like Highway 413. A stronger focus on public transit would yield long-term benefits by reducing reliance on cars and promoting sustainability."
Here are what the experts say about the Ford government's main highway projects:
Highway 413
The controversial 52-kilometre highway, fast-tracked late last year by legislation that includes exemption from environmental review, is set to stretch from Highway 400 between King Road and Kirby Road to the 401/407 ETR interchange near Mississauga, Milton and Halton Hills.
Easa said Highway 413, mostly designed but not yet under construction, offers the best hope at gridlock relief compared to Ford's other highway options. However, cutting through a swath of Greenbelt is the worst option in terms of minimizing environmental impacts, he said.
Miller said Highway 413 will only encourage sprawl and more driving, calling it "yet another play for developers and engineering companies to make a lot of money." It may be politically popular in some areas, he said, but it won't solve the GTA's transportation problems.
Highway 407
The toll road, built by the provincial government as a Highway 401 alternative but leased to a private operator for 99 years, is under scrutiny because it has excess capacity - sometimes within eyeshot of gridlocked 401 drivers.
Easa considers buying out the 407 lease - or just subsidizing truck use to free up space on the 401 - environmentally sound because the road is already built, although getting drivers onto transit would be better. For potential gridlock relief, he ranks it below Highway 413.
Miller believes talk of the 407's congestion-fighting potential is overblown, adding he's surprised the NDP is proposing making the road toll-free.
"I saw a report that said buying out the lease would cost more than $30 billion and there are much more effective ways to spend that money, if the treasury even has it," he said. "Think of the transit we could build."
Tunnelling under the 401
Ford's announcement last year that he wants to build 50 kilometres of roadway, and potentially transit, under existing highway was panned by opposition parties and some experts but hailed as bold and "visionary" by the Toronto Region Board of Trade. The Progressive Conservatives say planning continues for the project, which does not yet have a price tag but would cost tens of billions of dollars.
"While the tunnelling project could alleviate congestion in downtown Toronto," Easa said, "it may negatively impact the already visually unappealing infrastructure along the proposed segment, especially with the addition of the new exit and entrance ramps connecting the tunnel."
Miller said costs could reach $100 billion for gridlock benefits that make the idea "silly and ridiculous."
If provincial transportation agency Metrolinx is studying 401 tunnel feasibility, he said, "it's a complete and utter waste of money."
CAPTION: Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives say they are voters' only option to "deliver on projects to get drivers out of gridlock across Ontario." But transportation experts say research shows adding vehicle lanes incentivizes people to drive more.R.J. JohnstonToronto Star file photo
CREDIT: David Rider Senior Politics Reporter

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Corrections&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A Feb. 10 letter to the editor expressing a reader's view on Therme spa incorrectly stated that the Ontario government has a 99-year lease agreement with Therme Canada as part of its revitalization of Ontario Place. In fact, the lease is 95 years - 75 years with an option to renew for an additional 20 years.
A Feb. 11 article about Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford visiting U.S. lawmakers and business leaders, incorrectly stated Republican Sen. Kevin Cramer represents South Dakota. He represents North Dakota.
