Document 241 of 500

E-bike ride to school could cost you: Popular option for carrying kids, but it remains illegal in Ontario

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/e-bike-ride-school-could-cost-you/docview/3165674302/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=E-bike+ride+to+school+could+cost+you%3A+Popular+option+for+carrying+kids%2C+but+it+remains+illegal+in+Ontario&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Liam Roach was pedalling his one-year-old daughter home from daycare on his bright orange cargo e-bike when he heard the "whoop whoop" of a police car behind him.
He assumed the officer was trying to pull over a car and moved aside to let him pass. When the military police officer got out and walked up to his bike, he says, he was confused.
The cop informed him that Ontario law prohibits having a child on an e-bike - something that sounded unbelievable to Roach, considering he had just purchased the purpose-built child-carrying e-bike at a local shop and he was using a certified child seat.
"I was pretty upset about the whole thing," Roach said of the incident last September on the Canadian Forces Base in Kingston.
While he was let off with a warning, Roach had to pull his e-bike's battery out, rendering it a regular bike, in order to ride home legally.
"I don't disagree that there should be some regulations on e-bikes and children, especially maximum speeds and stuff like that.
"But to have a blanket ban where any bicycle with a motor on it isn't allowed to carry children seems a little ridiculous to me, especially when the rest of North America and most of the world is in line with this as a suitable method of transportation."
Last November, Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives overturned a law regulating electric bikes and replaced it with one that bans anyone under 16 from riding an e-bike - even as a passenger.
The ban has actually been in effect since 2006, according to the Ministry of Transportation, when e-bikes were regulated in the same way as scooters. In 2021, the Ford government brought in legislation that created a new category for e-bikes that would allow underage passengers. But the regulations were never proclaimed into law, and the Safer Roads and Communities Act, which received royal assent in November, overrules the previous law and maintains the ban.
And so while bike shops are selling them, parents are buying them, and a growing number of child-carrying e-bikes are rolling on the streets - doing so it illegal.
The province's webpage on e-bike regulations doesn't mention the ban on children.
While February flurries mean bike traffic is currently at a seasonal low, come spring when a growing number of parents pull out their e-bikes and start ferrying their kids between school, daycare and activities, they'll be risking a ticket and a fine of as much as $1,000.
Toronto police could not say if any tickets have been issued. Spokesperson Laura Brabant said she is not aware of any plans for an enforcement campaign.
While other cities and provinces have rebate programs to encourage families to buy e-bikes, Ontario stands out as the only jurisdiction in the world that bans children on e-bikes, says Jamie Stuckless, the former executive director of the cycling advocacy non-profit Share the Road Coalition, who has been following the regulation of e-bikes in Ontario for more than a decade.
"We've been trying to change the e-bike laws in Ontario forever," she said.
The way the law defines e-bikes is very broad and includes larger electric and gasoline-powered scooters that don't need to be pedalled, said Stuckless.
"A decade or so ago, when these rules were made, e-bikes were not as prominent or as well understood, so perhaps it was just an abundance of caution," she said. "But now we see there are a lot of purpose-built e-bikes for safely carrying families."
"It's time to update the law," she said, adding that in the time she's been advocating for e-bikes, she's become a mother and bought an e-bike for her young family. "I don't want people to be getting in trouble for this because tons of parents are riding and bike shops are selling these bikes with child seats and not letting you know that it's illegal (to ride with children)."
The offices of Ford and Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria did not respond to questions about the ban.
Large "cargo" e-bikes are widely available online and in bike shops across the province and can be outfitted with child seats, safety bars and even a canvas rain cover. While some e-bikes operate without pedalling, most have "pedal-assisted" propulsion that uses an electric motor to make it easier to pedal. Popular e-bike brands such as Rad Power Bikes prominently display photos of parents and kids in their promotional material.
Child-carrying e-bikes are the biggest growth segment in the business, says Kevin McLaughlin, who has been selling all kinds of e-bikes out of Zygg, his Roncesvalles-area shop, since 2020.
"This is what people are excited about. People want to be able to take their kids to school and then keep on going to work and pick up groceries on the way home," he said.
But McLaughlin said he had no idea a ban on child passengers was in effect.
"It's very concerning, because people had been told that it was OK and there's been no communication from the government."
Instead of banning kids, the province would do well to start enforcing rules that actually relate to safety, like battery size, maximum speed and certification standards, he said.
During the legislative committee debate on the law, Progressive Conservative member Ric Bresee gave a hint that the government would use the new legislation to properly regulate the chaotic and rapidly growing micromobility market.
"There are a huge number of different styles and structures of these bikes," he said, according to a transcript of the meeting. "We need to make sure that we get that right, as to which ones require licensing, which ones don't etc., the age restrictions - all of that. That move to get those regulations requires that we, I'll say, clear the path for those definitions, and that is exactly what this legislation is doing."
Michael Longfield, the executive director of the bicycle advocacy group CycleTO, flagged the child passenger ban in a letter to the government while the e-bike bill was winding its way through the legislative process.
"I think this perpetuates a myth that kids riding on e-bikes are in some way dangerous, which is quite misleading given that traffic collisions in cars tend to be a much higher percentage of young people's injuries and deaths," he said.
There are years of data from other countries where e-bikes are popular and none of it points to any safety concerns for children, as long as the bikes are properly designed and certified, Toronto emergency room doctor Edward Xie said.
"I see tons of children on e-bikes, but I have not seen any e-bike injuries," he added.
To the contrary, because of the health benefits associated with an active lifestyle, "we should encourage families to use these bikes," said Xie. "The evidence is very clear on this that the health benefits of riding a bike are immense, primarily due to prevention of chronic disease such as diabetes and heart disease."
Opposition Transportation critic Jennifer French said the ban tells you everything you need to know about the Ford government's attitude toward cyclists.
"This is not a government that is interested in hearing from cyclists. I think that they seem to consider them a fringe group," said French, who linked the e-bike rules to the province's plan to rip out bike lanes.
French said parents who came to committee to express their concerns were brushed aside by the Progressive Conservatives.
"It doesn't make any sense that they would go forward with something that creates such havoc when I think the spirit of any e-bike legislation is meant to make things easier," she said.
Kevin Shields and his wife, Ewa, sold their car when they moved to Toronto, and say biking, walking and transit are far better ways of getting around. They take their two kids, ages four and 10, on an e-bike to and from school everyday.
"It is our car. We use it 24/7/365 for everything we do," he said. "The kids love it. They play 'I spy.' They sing. It could be raining or snowing and they're under the cover, totally oblivious."
E-bikes are a great solution for families feeling the squeeze of inflation, says CycleTO's Longfield, with sales as strong as they are, going forward there are going to be more e-bikes with kids around - even if they're not legal for use.
"They're sold at Canadian Tire," he said. "I don't know if people know they are buying a vehicle they're not legally allowed to use.
"Could you imagine buying a car and later finding out you can't drive it?"
CREDIT: Marco Chown Oved Climate Change Reporter

Full text: Make the effort to meet your candidate
Doug Ford triggers snap Ontario election, Jan. 28
I had a wonderful learning experience your readers should consider.
I had initially rejected the Green Party candidate in my riding outright. Green was not "my party." But I went to meet him anyway and came back so very impressed that I will get involved in the election this time.
He was much more articulate, knowledgeable, charming and independent of party control than I ever dreamed. Our current MPP seems to be a puppet who can do nothing without leadership permission.
The point is I used to vote by party, but forgot that parties have changed over the years . Our health care is being destroyed because people did that.
Please tell your readers to get out and meet the candidates, ask questions and investigate facts and just maybe they too will find that it is time to change to get change. Vote on the basis of what has been done, not what has been promised. Question all promises and be sure it is not misinformation you are being fed.
It is our grandchildren that will pay the price if we accept lies and vote by habit, not thought.
Roberta Docherty, Meaford, Ont.
Ford clearly has tunnel vision
Ontario election 2025: Doug Ford vows to forge ahead with tunnel under Hwy. 401, Feb. 7
Before bringing up grandiose plans like tunnelling under Highway 401, PC Leader Doug Ford might want to check if the basics are in place.
Is there an agreed-upon plan to end the health-care crisis that he keeps us updated on? No.
Has adequate funding to the public school system and colleges and universities been provided? No.
Have lineups at food banks disappeared? No.
Are the people who have been sleeping in tents in public parks all winter now in housing? No.
I hope one of his advisers points these things out to him, because our premier seems oblivious to the larger issues at play in Ontario.
David Smith, Toronto
Hold PC candidates
to account
Everyone should ask their local Progressive Conservative candidate one question at every all-candidates meeting: Why is PC Leader Doug Ford, who has a majority government and 18 months left on his mandate, spending approximately $180 million of our taxpayer dollars to hold an election?
Their answer must not contain the words "Trump" or "tariffs."
Quite likely, not one of them will be able to do it.
Andy Blackwood, Simcoe, Ont.
Feature on Alzheimer's struck a chord
Alzheimer's can make my dad hard to reach. Here's what I do to bring him back, Feb. 9
Thank you to the Star for this beautiful story about inner strength. In this case, the power it gives one to accept the advent of Alzheimer's by both the family and the one who has it.
I found Joel Rubinoff's description of the process of Scouter Al's decline very special. The many disturbing and unfortunate occurrences like running away, failing of a good mind and the distress of his father in trying to understand it all was emotionally coupled with the very comforting realization that - guess what? The person who they are inside is still there.
My mother was a kind and loving person. Even as she mentally declined with dementia before passing, I saw her show this everyday. Whether a person has dementia or Alzheimer's specifically, they continue to connect in many ways that are deep and soulful.
Your father is an amazing man. The way you and your sons have supported him on this journey is tremendously life-affirming.
Barbara Chernin, Toronto
This Canadian has zero respect for Gretzky
Donald Trump says he urged Wayne Gretzky to run for prime minister in Christmas visit, Dec. 25
Canada is under siege. Trump has exposed our weaknesses, thanks to years under Liberal leadership (and NDP support). Both economically and militarily, we are sitting ducks.
One of Trump's top loyalists said the U.S. president is after more than just tariffs from Canada. If that isn't ominous, I don't know what is.
When I saw Canadian icon Wayne Gretzky go the White House to congratulate Trump on his victory I was stunned. Now that we see Wayne's buddy has set his sights on Canada, I have no respect for Gretzky anymore.
Lynda Anderson, Spruce Grove, Alta.
American friends, we appreciate you
I would like to give the American letter writers a great big thank you for their very kind and caring words.
It is so thoughtful of them to reach out to us and send their apologies on behalf of Trump's unbelievable attitude toward Canada. Many of us love and respect our American neighbours.
Many of us love to travel in the U.S. For many of us, the U.S. is like our second home.
It brings me comfort to know our neighbours support us and they empathize with the way we are feeling right now. We cannot let one person destroy this very harmonious relationship.
I will forever be very grateful to our American friends for reaching out.
Melanie Kohek, Whitby
Booing the U.S. anthem is not
the way to go
Toronto Raptors fans loudly boo American national anthem at Scotiabank Arena in response to Trump's tariffs, Feb. 2
Even before reading the many letters of support from Americans to Canada published in the Star, it occurred to me we should not diminish ourselves by booing the American national anthem. Our issue is not with most American citizens, but rather the current administration.
I also applaud every Canadian who is now or has always supported Canadian businesses and industries.
Also, I would also suggest if we are truly proud of our country that we show it by voting in all our elections.
Mike Dupuis, Belleville, Ont.

Document 243 of 500

Greens include affordability in campaign

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/greens-include-affordability-campaign/docview/3165674293/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Greens+include+affordability+in+campaign&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The political climate is changing dramatically in America and unpredictably in Canada.
So how does a politician talk about climate change in a provincial election when voters on the doorstep are distracted and disengaged?
Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner is painfully aware that when voters are hurting, environmentalism isn't always top of mind. Which means he's trying to connect the dots on the campaign trail so that even in the middle of winter, global warming matters to voters.
"The number-one issue is housing, affordability," Schreiner told the Democracy Forum I hosted Tuesday for Toronto Metropolitan University.
Where does that leave a Green Party whose raison d'être is protecting the environment?
"So how I've been talking about climate in this election is talking about it as an affordability issue," said Schreiner, who joined via Zoom from the campaign trail in Guelph.
"The housing crisis is intimately connected to the climate crisis. The number-one driver of climate pollution in Ontario is transportation emissions. The number-one driver of transportation emissions is low-density, sprawl development."
That means talking about the high price of housing in the same breath as he talks about the cost of urban sprawl and the burden of environmental degradation - which sparks flooding, fires and weather warnings.
Greens have always tried to avoid coming across as a single-issue party. Now more than ever, with environmental issues such a hard sell, Schreiner is quick to rattle off a long list of other concerns - not just housing but health care, congestion and corruption.
"That's why we're talking about housing affordability. That's why we're talking about protections for renters. That's why we're talking about having government get back into building non-profit and co-op housing ... it's directly connected to climate change."
All that said, politics is more transactional and less transformational than ever. As a Kansas-born immigrant to Canada, Schreiner is keenly aware of the differences in political cultures, but also the similarities.
Populism is rising in both countries. But the trajectory and history of populism in Canada is unique.
"I think populist politics is different in Canada than it is in the United States - populist politics in the U.S. is very individual oriented, whereas populist politics in Canada is much more community oriented," Schreiner told me. "The biggest difference is, 'Are you going to fight for the community, or are you going to fight for the individual?' And I like the fact that in Canada, we fight for our communities."
In the era of populism, Schreiner concedes that policy matters less than personality and popularity.
"I say this as somebody who got into politics as a policy wonk - I've had to learn that policy doesn't matter as much as somebody like me would like it to matter," he mused.
The fact that Ford was caught on an open mic last week saying that he'd been rooting "100 per cent" for Donald Trump to be re-elected - notwithstanding his campaign promises to reprise the tariffs he'd imposed in the past - exposes his anti-Trump campaign as a pretext to tighten his Progressive Conservative party's grip on power, Schreiner argued.
The Green Party leader doesn't buy Ford's claim that he's fighting for Ontario during his trip to Washington this week. His powers are circumscribed by the premier's caretaker status in mid-campaign, and he's not getting the audience he sought with Trump to talk about tariffs.
"It's so irresponsible for the premier to call an election when we're facing this tariff threat," Schreiner said. "We need to be working across party lines, jurisdictional and sectoral lines to show strength through unity, to push back against Trump. I think it's very selfish of the premier to play politics with tariffs."
Ford enjoys a massive lead in the public opinion polls, but Schreiner argued that voters have the power to change the equation and deprive the Tories of another majority government. While the other major party leaders who attended the Democracy Forum on campus last week - the NDP's Marit Stiles and Bonnie Crombie of the Liberals - said they could not conceive of propping up Ford's Tories in a minority government, Schreiner chose his words carefully:
"I think it would be very challenging for us to think of working in coalition with a Ford government, given the fact that he's dismantled all the climate programs in Ontario," he told me.
"I can see where we could find commonalities with the other two opposition parties."
One audience member asked Schreiner about his dalliance with the rival Liberals two years ago, when party elders courted him to jump ship in the middle of their leadership race. The Green Party leader allowed speculation to grow for weeks before finally deciding against running to lead the Liberals.
What took him so long? Why was he so conflicted?
"I wanted to take the time to talk to people and I travelled around the province, asked Green Party members ... and the answer was clear that I am most effective as the leader of the Ontario Green Party."
Sometimes, it's hard to choose. But democracy is about choosing - for both voters and leaders.

Disclosure I'm a senior fellow at The Dais at Toronto Metropolitan University, which supports the TMU Democracy Forum.
CAPTION: Ontario Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner, shown Tuesday with MPP Aislinn Clancy in Kitchener, is trying to connect the issues of affordability and climate change on the campaign trail, Martin Regg Cohn writes.Mathew McCarthyWaterloo Region Record
CREDIT: Martin Regg Cohn OPINION

Document 244 of 500

Ford denies U.S. trip a campaign stop: Crombie says Progressive Conservative leader's sojourn breaks legislature's 'caretaker convention'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-denies-u-s-trip-campaign-stop/docview/3165674188/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+denies+U.S.+trip+a+campaign+stop%3A+Crombie+says+Progressive+Conservative+leader%27s+sojourn+breaks+legislature%27s+%27caretaker+convention%27&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As political rivals accuse him of electioneering on the taxpayer's dime, Doug Ford insists he can juggle governing and campaigning at the same time.
The Progressive Conservative leader, here to lobby American lawmakers and officials against U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs on Canada, rejected Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie's contention he is breaching tradition by going on a foreign mission during a campaign.
"I got cleared by the integrity commissioner, but that's neither here nor there," Ford said Tuesday, referring to the legislature's ethics watchdog.
In Toronto, Crombie charged that he was "breaking the caretaker convention" of how governments should operate after the dissolution of the legislature and the start of the Feb. 27 election.
"He plays fast and loose with the rules," she said at George Brown College's waterfront campus.
"Now, he's making a campaign stop in the United States in a desperate attempt to be taken seriously by Republican politicians."
Pointing to his role as chair of the Council of the Federation, a defensive Ford countered that "the Canadian people want us here - not just Ontarians, Canadians."
"They want all the representatives, all their premiers, here and we're Team Canada right now with all 10 provinces and three territories and we're working very well together," the PC leader said.
"Make no mistake about it, the White House knows we're down here."
Ford's office confirmed he spoke to Howard Lutnick, Trump's commerce secretary, and Doug Burgum, the secretary of the interior, before flying here. He will also meet with North Dakota Sen. Kevin Cramer and representatives Lisa McClain of Michigan and Rob Wittman of Virginia.
On Wednesday, he and other provincial and territorial leaders will fan out over the U.S. capital to plead Canada's case against tariffs.
After a speech to the United States Chamber of Commerce, where many of the 120 attendees were Canadians in Washington to lobby against the Trump tariffs, Ford was asked if he was here as premier or as Tory leader.
"I am the premier of Ontario. Officially, I'm still the premier of Ontario until I'm no longer the premier of Ontario. But let's hope that doesn't happen," he said.
Still, Ford tacitly admitted he was on the hustings, confirming the PC campaign chartered a private jet for him and his political staff to come to Washington.
"I don't want to go and take money out of taxpayers' pockets. We're in the election mode right now ... also the opposition thought it'd be a good idea that we pay for it ourselves," he said, flanked by Tory candidates Vic Fedeli and Stephen Lecce, who sit in his cabinet.
However, taxpayers are footing the tab for Ontario bureaucrats to be on hand and for a 25-page colour booklet called Building Fortress Am-Can, which outlines the benefits of trade with Ontario and was distributed at the speech.
Neil Herrington, senior vice-president for the Americas, of the U.S. Chamber of Commerce, the world's largest business organization, praised Ford for delivering the anti-tariff message.
"Tariffs are a tax paid by Americans and their broad, indiscriminate use would stifle growth at the worst possible time," said Herrington.
Eric Miller, president of Rideau Potomac Strategy Group, said while he gives Ford "huge credit for coming to try to fight the good fight ... I'm not sure much could change Donald Trump's mind at this point."
"Unfortunately, I think you're going to have to see pain. It's when you start seeing stock markets go down, when you start seeing vehicle plants close, when you start seeing the price of beef go up, when you start seeing the price of energy go up, that is what will get through to President Trump - when his allies on Capitol Hill call and say, 'why are we doing this?' "
In Kitchener, NDP Leader Marit Stiles said she's "not a fan of Fortress Am-Can" and other messages Ford is pushing in Washington in an attempt to get Trump on side.
"We should be looking right now at how we diversify our trade partners and allies out there," Stiles told reporters, mocking the PC leader's penchant for referring to the U.S. as a "customer" of Ontario.
"I would say the customer is not always right, Doug."
Stiles said Ford's decision to call an election at a critical time is not doing the province any favours - given how vulnerable its massive steel, automotive, agricultural and other industries are to tariffs - and dismissed his claims he's making a difference.
"This is just a lot of campaign slogans and not an actual fight for our very future ... not actually action to deliver support for working people here," the New Democrat added.
"Nothing that Doug Ford has done so far has worked anyway," she said, noting steel and aluminum tariffs orders have been signed by Trump and questioning why Ford did not attend Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's tariff-related economic summit in Toronto last Friday with industry leaders and trade experts.
"I'd like him to come back and fix our health-care system."
CAPTION: PC Leader Doug Ford attends a U.S. Chamber of Commerce event Tuesday in Washington. Ford and other provincial and territorial officials are in the U.S. Capitol to plead Canada's case against tariffs.Mark Schiefelbein the associated press
CREDIT: Robert Benzie, Rob Ferguson and Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Document 245 of 500

Chow's 2025 budget gets the green light: TTC fare freeze, police funding boost and 6.9% property tax increase included in spending plan

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/chows-2025-budget-gets-green-light/docview/3165674142/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Chow%27s+2025+budget+gets+the+green+light%3A+TTC+fare+freeze%2C+police+funding+boost+and+6.9%25+property+tax+increase+included+in+spending+plan&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's official.
Mayor Olivia Chow's 2025 city budget - including a 6.9 per cent property tax increase, a TTC fare freeze, and a funding boost for the police - coasted through council on Tuesday without major amendments, after a daylong debate whose most dramatic moment came when protesters rushed the council floor.
In remarks to council on Tuesday, Chow called the $18.9-billion spending plan - her second since being elected in a special byelection in 2023 - "fiscally responsible," and defended it from colleagues who accused her of balancing growing city costs on the backs of already overburdened taxpayers.
"No one is ever happy when we're asking people to pay more. But at this point we have to fix the city," Chow said, arguing that transit infrastructure and other vital city assets had suffered from "many years of not investing" under previous administrations.
She vowed the additional revenue would help improve services that vulnerable Torontonians depend on, and help ensure residents will be able "to afford to stay in this city that we love," she said.
The residential property tax increase is made up of 5.4 per cent to fund the operating budget and 1.5 per cent to fund transit and housing infrastructure. City staff estimate it will cost the average homeowner about $268 more this year. It follows the record high 9.5 per cent increase in Chow's first budget in 2024.
Some councillors charged the successive increases were unsustainable.
"We're going too fast, we're going too far with these tax hikes, and we need to take our foot off the pedal," said Coun. Vincent Crisanti (Ward 1, Etobicoke North), who unsuccessfully moved a motion that would have shaved two percentage points off the increase.
"People are struggling with affordability, they're struggling to put food on the table, and they're struggling to pay their bills. This is what I hear all the time," he said.
Councillors approved the 2025 tax rates, including the residential increase, in a vote of 19 to 5.
Chow's budget chief, Coun. Shelley Carroll (Ward 17, Don Valley North), told reporters afterward that while she believed the budget delivered on residents' priorities, she hoped the property tax hike in the 2026 budget - the final one before the mayor and council face re-election - would be lower than this year's.
She said the 2025 raise was about 2.5 percentage points lower than in 2024, and "we want to do that again next year, maybe (decrease it) even more."
Chow's $18.9-billion operating budget includes about $94 million in spending for new initiatives such as expanding school nutrition programs to thousands more students, keeping libraries and outdoor swimming pools open longer, and adding 25 more traffic wardens.
Toronto Police Services - whose $1.2-billion budget is among the biggest expenditures in the spending plan each year - will receive an additional $46.2 million to support a multi-year hiring plan that will add 109 net new officers this year. There's also money for 95 new paramedics and 52 additional firefighters, which Chow said would improve emergency response times.
TTC fares have been flatlined at last year's levels, and funding has been set aside for a pilot project to tackle "bunching" on the agency's 10 worst bus routes.
Meanwhile, the 10-year, $59.6-billion capital plan commits $4.5 billion to housing programs, and $17 billion to transit, and includes funding for 55 new subway trains for Line 2, paid for in part through the "new deal" Chow struck with Premier Doug Ford.
City staff said they found $680 million in spending reductions and offsets this year, through line-by-line reviews and efficiencies. Coun. Jon Burnside (Ward 16, Don Valley East) was among those who argued the city could find further savings if it did more to combat fare evasion, which the TTC said costs $141 million per year.
One of the lingering questions headed into Tuesday's meeting was how members would decide to spend $3 million Chow had set aside for their priorities.
On Tuesday, they endorsed a motion from Carroll reflecting councillors' requests to allocate the spending to a slew of smaller programs related to street tree maintenance, ravine restoration, drop-in services, community development, food security, pickleball, a cultural plan for Little Jamaica, and an anti-hate summit.
With the threat of U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs against Canadian goods hanging over the meeting, council passed motions to develop an economic "resiliency strategy," boost local procurement efforts, and temporarily exclude Tesla vehicles from an electric car grant program for limousine and taxi drivers.
The mayor said the anti-Tesla measure, which she introduced, was intended to "send a message to the president's right-hand person, (Tesla owner) Elon Musk." She acknowledged the motion would have a "modest impact" on the electric car giant, and some of her colleagues questioned its legality, but the mayor asserted it was a meaningful gesture.
Coun. Brad Bradford (Ward 19, Beaches-East York) wanted the city do more to brace for the tariffs. He introduced motions to reduce taxes on industry and small business by 25 per cent, which he said would "give our business a fighting chance" in the event of a trade war.
Some of the mayor's allies countered that the measures would benefit major U.S.-based corporations and drain city reserves, however, and Bradford's motions werevoted down.
About an hour after the meeting resumed for its afternoon session, it was temporarily suspended when two protesters breached the secure area of the chamber. The pair were part of a group of advocates who came to the meeting to protest the city's response to homelessness, some of whom were forcibly removed by security. The room was cleared and the meeting resumed less than 40 minutes later.
CAPTION: Mayor Olivia Chow called the $18.9-billion spending plan "fiscally responsible," saying that transit infrastructure and other vital city assets had suffered from "many years of not investing."AndrewFrancisWallaceToronto Star
CREDIT: Ben Spurr Toronto Star Chief

It's election time. Make the effort to meet your candidate

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/election-time-make-effort-meet-your-candidate/docview/3165674134/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=It%27s+election+time.+Make+the+effort+to+meet+your+candidate&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Meeting the candidates can make all the differenceDoug Ford triggers snap Ontario election, Jan. 28
I had a wonderful learning experience your readers should consider.
I had initiallyrejected the Green Party candidate in my riding outright. Green was not "my party." But I went to meet him anyway and came back so very impressed that I will get involved in the election this time.
He was much more articulate, knowledgeable, charming and independent of party control than I ever dreamed. Our current MPP seems to be a puppet who can do nothing without leadership permission.
The point is I used to vote by party, but forgot that parties have changed over the years . Our health care is being destroyed because people did that.
Please tell your readers to get out and meet the candidates, ask questions and investigate facts and just maybe they too will find that it is time to change to get change. Vote on the basis of what has been done, not what has been promised. Question all promises and be sure it is not misinformation you are being fed.
It is our grandchildren that will pay the price if we accept lies and vote by habit, not thought.
Roberta Docherty, Meaford, Ont.
Doug Ford clearly has tunnel visionOntario election 2025: Doug Ford vows to forge ahead with tunnel under Hwy. 401, Feb. 7
Before bringing up grandiose plans like tunneling under Hwy. 401, Premier Doug Ford might want to check if the basics are in place.
Is there an agreed-upon plan to end the health care crisis that he keeps us updated on? No.
Has adequate funding to the public school system and colleges and universities been provided? No.
Have lineups at food banks disappeared? No.
Are the people who have been sleeping in tents in public parks all winter now in housing? No.
I hope one of his advisers points these things out to him, because our premier seems oblivious to the larger issues at play in Ontario.
David Smith, Toronto
Hold PC candidates to account
Everyone should ask their local Progressive Conservative candidate one question at every all-candidates meeting: Why is Premier Doug Ford, who has a majority government and 18 months left on his mandate, spending approximately $180 million of our taxpayer dollars to hold an election?
Their answer must not contain the words 'Trump' or 'tariffs.'
Quite likely, not one of them will be able to do it.
Andy Blackwood, Simcoe, Ont.
Feature on Alzheimer's struck a chordAlzheimer's can make my dad hard to reach. Here's what I do to bring him back, Feb. 9
Thank you to Toronto Star for this beautiful story about inner strength. In this case, the power it gives one to accept the advent of Alzheimer's by both the family and the one who has it.
I found Joel's description of the process of Scouter Al's decline very special. The many disturbing and unfortunate occurrences like running away, failing of a good mind and the distress of his father in trying to understand it all was emotionally coupled with the very comforting realization that - guess what? The person who they are inside is still there.
My mother was a kind and loving person. Even as she mentally declined with dementia before passing, I saw her show this everyday. Whether a person has dementia or Alzheimer's specifically, they continue to connect in many ways that are deep and soulful.
Your father is an amazing man. The way you and your sons have supported him on this journey is tremendously life-affirming.
Barbara Chernin, Toronto
This Canadian now has zero respect for Wayne GretzkyDonald Trump says he urged Wayne Gretzky to run for prime minister in Christmas visit, Dec. 25
Canada is under siege. Trump has exposed our weaknesses, thanks to years under Liberal leadership (and NDP support). Both economically and militarily, we are sitting ducks.
One of Trump's top loyalists said the U.S. president is after more than just tariffs from Canada. If that isn't ominous, I don't know what is.
When I saw Canadian icon Wayne Gretzky go the White House to congratulate Trump on his victory I was stunned. Now that we see Wayne's buddy has set his sights on Canada, I have no respect for Gretzky anymore.
Lynda Anderson, Spruce Grove, Alta.
American friends, we appreciate you!
I would like to give the American letter writers a great big thank you for their very kind and caring words.
It is so thoughtful of them to reach out to us and send their apologies on behalf of Trump's unbelievable attitude towards Canada. Many of us love and respect our American neighbours. Many of us love to travel in the U.S. For many of us, the U.S. is like our second home.
It brings me comfort to know our neighbours support us and they empathize with the way we are feeling right now. We cannot let one person destroy this very harmonious relationship.
I will forever be very grateful to our American friends for reaching out.
Melanie Kohek, Whitby
Booing the U.S. anthem is not the way to goToronto Raptors fans loudly boo American national anthem at Scotiabank Arena in response to Trump's tariffs, Feb. 2
Even before reading the many letters of support from Americans to Canada published in the Star, it occurred to me we should not diminish ourselves by booing the American national anthem. Our issue is not with most American citizens, but rather the current administration.
I also applaud every Canadian who is now or has always supported Canadian businesses and industries.
Also, I would also suggest if we are truly proud of our country that we show it by voting in all our elections.
Mike Dupuis, Belleville, Ont.
Canadians unifying as Trump sets his sights north of the borderTrump's national security adviser says he doesn't 'think there's any plans to invade Canada,' Feb. 9
It's truly comical that Mike Waltz, Trump's national security adviser, has come to the conclusion all Canadians prefer becoming American based solely on the views of his Canadian snowbird neighbours in Florida.
I'm sure the entire nation of Canada is just waiting for a thumbs-up from a few retirees by the pool. It's clear Trump and his team are governing by "American exceptionalism." This strategy will surely backfire while Canadians unite in a rare, determined effort to educate Americans about who we are, what we really think and why we won't be joining them at the pool.
Mary McGrath, Cambridge, Ont.

Document 247 of 500

Ford's 401 tunnel costly, unworkable

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-401-tunnel-costly-unworkable/docview/3165673937/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+401+tunnel+costly%2C+unworkable&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford continues to insist that part of his plan to combat gridlock in the GTA will include the construction of a tunnel under Highway 401, stretching all the way from Mississauga to Markham.
The Progressive Conservative leader says it would be the world's longest tunnel, but no dollar figure or timeline has been attached, because feasibility studies must be carried out first.
Rather than wasting millions of dollars on that, here is my feasibility study, offered for the very reasonable price of free: no.
If there is another example in recent political history of a leader making a promise this recklessly expensive and completely impracticable, it does not leap to mind. On top of that, a tunnel wouldn't even do much to address the problem it was intended to solve. It's the rare election-pledge trifecta: costly, unworkable, ineffective.
Start with the money. The Scarborough subway extension, which is less than eight kilometres long, has an estimated cost of around $6 billion.
Extrapolating from that, a tunnel under the 401 at something like 50 kilometres long would cost in the ballpark of $60 billion. That's probably a conservative estimate, too, given that Ford's (fever) dream involves a transit component as well as lanes for vehicular traffic. These would have to be extremely wide tunnels. Add in the usual cost escalations on an infrastructure project that could easily take decades, and the final price tag could easily approach $100 billion.
To put that in context, Ford's decision to send out $200 cheques to everyone in the province cost an estimated $3 billion.
Even if the 401 tunnel cost $60 billion, that would be the equivalent of having sent out $4,000 cheques instead. Not cheap.
And even if that kind of outlay could be justified, how such a project might actually work is hard to fathom. A tunnel of the length proposed would need multiple entry and exit points, but the stretch of highway that Ford is considering is already covered on the surface with existing roads, homes and businesses. Where would all the tunnels to get in and out of the big tunnel go?
The scale, too, seems wildly impractical. For the Beijing Olympics in 2022, China built a tunnel under the mountains of Zhangjiakou to complete the final stretch of the route from the city to the alpine venue. That tunnel felt like it went on forever, and after early testing, the organizers realized they had to install high-intensity lights through the length of it because drivers were becoming fatigued in the darkened space. It was only nine kilometres long. What kinds of hazards would be created by a 50-kilometre tunnel? This is the stuff of disaster movies.
But for the sake of argument, let's imagine the budgetary and logistical issues could be overcome, and Ford's tunnel plan could somehow become a reality. Would it significantly ease traffic congestion on Highway 401? Well, no. Ask any traffic engineer or planner if adding an additional lane to an existing road frees up traffic, and they will explain that, no, more space just means that more vehicles will cram in to fill the void. The Doug Ford Induced Demand Subterranean Expressway would serve only to encourage even more vehicle traffic along the route.
Congestion, on the other hand, forces drivers to weigh other alternatives, and the transit part of Ford's proposal might actually be worth considering. If the tunnel were connected to existing transit routes, it could encourage commuters to take a more efficient mode of transportation across that corridor. But Ford himself talks about the tunnel plan as a boon primarily to drivers, and the transit part is only mentioned as a tacked-on bonus - even as actual transit infrastructure projects in the province are many years and billions of dollars overbudget.
It's also worth noting here that the world's big tunnel projects, like the one in Zhangjiakou, are built specifically to go under mountains or bodies of water - where it's a practical necessity that justifies the expense of building underground.
Put another way, there's a simple explanation for why Ford's tunnel would be the world's longest: because no one else would even consider building such a thing.
Scott Stinson is a journalist based in suburban Toronto.
CREDIT: Scott Stinson Contributor Scott Stinson is a journalist based in suburban Toronto.;

Subject: Traffic; Roads & highways

Location: Toronto Ontario Canada; Canada

Title: Ford's 401 tunnel costly, unworkable

Publication title: Toronto Star; Toronto, Ont.

First page: A.11

Publication year: 2025

Publication date: Feb 12, 2025

Section: Opinion

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto, Ont.

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

ISSN: 03190781

Source type: Newspaper

Language of publication: English

Document type: Commentary, Opinions

Document 249 of 500

Doug Ford denies his Washington trip is a taxpayer-funded campaign stop

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-denies-his-washington-trip-is-taxpayer/docview/3165928323/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+denies+his+Washington+trip+is+a+taxpayer-funded+campaign+stop&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: WASHINGTON-As political rivals accuse him of electioneering on the taxpayer's dime, Doug Ford insists he can juggle governing and campaigning at the same time.
The Progressive Conservative leader, here to lobby American lawmakers and officials against U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs on Canada, rejected Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie's contention he is breaching tradition by going on a foreign mission during a campaign.
"I got cleared by the integrity commissioner, but that's neither here nor there," Ford said Tuesday, referring to the legislature's ethics watchdog.
In Toronto, Crombie charged that he was "breaking the caretaker convention" of how governments should operate after the dissolution of the legislature and the start of the Feb. 27 election.
"He plays fast and loose with the rules," she said at George Brown College's waterfront campus.
"Now, he's making a campaign stop in the United States in a desperate attempt to be taken seriously by Republican politicians."
Pointing to his role as chair of the Council of the Federation, a defensive Ford countered that "the Canadian people want us here - not just Ontarians, Canadians."
"They want all the representatives, all their premiers, here and we're Team Canada right now with all 10 provinces and three territories and we're working very well together," the PC leader said.
"Make no mistake about it, the White House knows we're down here."
Ford's office confirmed he spoke to Howard Lutnick, Trump's commerce secretary, and Doug Burgum the secretary of the interior, before flying here. He will also meet with North Dakota Sen. Kevin Cramer and representatives Lisa McClain of Michigan and Rob Wittman of Virginia.
On Wednesday, he and other provincial and territorial leaders will fan out over the U.S. capital to plead Canada's case against tariffs.
After a speech to the United States Chamber of Commerce, where many of the 120 attendees were Canadians in Washington to lobby against the Trump tariffs, Ford was asked if he was here as premier or as Tory leader.
"I am the premier of Ontario. Officially, I'm still the premier of Ontario until I'm no longer the premier of Ontario. But let's hope that doesn't happen," he said.
Still, Ford tacitly admitted he was on the hustings, confirming the PC campaign chartered a private jet for him and his political staff to come to Washington.
"I don't want to go and take money out of taxpayers' pockets. We're in the election mode right now ... also the opposition thought it'd be a good idea that we pay for it ourselves," he said, flanked by Tory candidates Vic Fedeli and Stephen Lecce, who sit in his cabinet.
However, taxpayers are footing the tab for Ontario bureaucrats to be on hand and for a 25-page colour booklet called Building Fortress Am-Can, which outlines the benefits of trade with Ontario and was distributed at the speech.
Neil Herrington, senior vice-president for the Americas, of the U.S. Chamber of Commerce, the world's largest business organization, praised Ford for delivering the anti-tariff message.
"Tariffs are a tax paid by Americans and their broad, indiscriminate use would stifle growth at the worst possible time," said Herrington.
Eric Miller, president of Rideau Potomac Strategy Group, said while he gives Ford "huge credit for coming to try to fight the good fight ... I'm not sure much could change Donald Trump's mind at this point."
"Unfortunately, I think you're going to have to see pain. It's when you start seeing stock markets go down, when you start seeing vehicle plants close, when you start seeing the price of beef go up, when you start seeing the price of energy go up, that is what will get through to President Trump - when his allies on Capitol Hill call and say, 'why are we doing this?'"
In Kitchener, NDP Leader Marit Stiles said she's "not a fan of Fortress Am-Can" and other messages Ford is pushing in Washington in an attempt to get Trump on side.
"We should be looking right now at how we diversify our trade partners and allies out there," Stiles told reporters, mocking the PC leader's penchant for referring to the U.S. as a "customer" of Ontario.
"I would say the customer is not always right, Doug."
Stiles said Ford's decision to call an election at a critical time is not doing the province any favours - given how vulnerable its massive steel, automotive, agricultural and other industries are to tariffs - and dismissed his claims he's making a difference.
"This is just a lot of campaign slogans and not an actual fight for our very future ... not actually action to deliver support for working people here," the New Democrat added.
"Nothing that Doug Ford has done so far has worked anyway," she said, noting steel and aluminum tariffs orders have been signed by Trump and questioning why Ford did not attend Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's tariff-related economic summit in Toronto last Friday with industry leaders and trade experts.
"I'd like him to come back and fix our health-care system."
CAPTION: Ontario Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford listens as Neil Herrington, senior vice president of the Americas Program for the U.S. Chamber of Commerce, speaks during an event with the chamber on Feb. 11, 2025, in Washington.

Document 250 of 500

Ontario election 2025: When and where you can vote in advanced polls

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-election-2025-when-where-you-can-vote/docview/3165928320/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+election+2025%3A+When+and+where+you+can+vote+in+advanced+polls&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's election day is Feb. 27. You're eligible to vote if you're a Canadian citizen over 18. Want to get a head start? Here's how. How do I register to vote? Before you vote by mail or in person, you'll need to register (if you haven't already). If you're a voter living in Ontario, you'll need to add your name and details to the Permanent Register of Electors for Ontario, more commonly known as "the Register."If you're temporarily living outside the province but plans to return to Ontario, you should instead add your name to the Register of Absentee voters. To register, you'll need your name, address, contact information and other personal details, along with a piece of ID, like a driver's licence or photo card. If you've already registered, but need to update your information, you can do so at the links above. How can I vote at advance polls?You can vote at advance polls in your electoral district from Feb. 20 to 22, from 10 a.m. to 8 p.m. You can find these locations on the Elections Ontario website. You can search by electoral district at this link, and scroll down to "Advance voting locations."(Note that many districts won't have advance voting locations listed until Feb. 14.)How do I vote by mail?Apply to vote by mail before the deadline on Feb. 21 at 6 p.m., eastern time and you'll receive a voting kit by mail. Fill it out and mail it in so that it's received by Elections Ontario before the deadline of Feb. 27 at 6 p.m. Voting on Election DayIf you want to vote in person on the day of the election, you can do so at your assigned voting location from 9 a.m. to 9 p.m. eastern time. Don't forget to bring your ID. A list of acceptable ID can be found at this link.The election falls on Thursday, a workday for many, but you can vote after 5 p.m. You can find out more on the Elections Ontario website.
CAPTION: The party leaders: PC Doug Ford; NDP Marit Stiles; Green Party Mike Schreiner; Liberal Bonnie Crombie.

Subject: Absentee voting; Election districts; Elections

Document 251 of 500

'We will not be bullied': Trump's new steel tariffs could affect 'thousands' of local jobs, says Hamilton mayor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/we-will-not-be-bullied-trumps-new-steel-tariffs/docview/3165492317/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27We+will+not+be+bullied%27%3A+Trump%27s+new+steel+tariffs+could+affect+%27thousands%27+of+local+jobs%2C+says+Hamilton+mayor&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hamilton Mayor Andrea Horwath is condemning newly imposed steel tariffs and warning they could affect "thousands and thousands" of local jobs.
She also called Monday for a continued "Team Canada" approach to mitigate tariff impacts she said would be "quite damaging" to the economy of the nation's most famous steel town.
Late Monday, U.S. President Donald Trump signed an executive order imposing 25 per cent tariffs on all imported steel and aluminum, including from Canada, with no exemptions.
The abrupt move came barely a week after the president offered an apparent month-long reprieve from his original vow to impose tariffs on virtually all products imported from Canada.
Such across-the-board tariffs might have taken a $1-billion bite out of Hamilton's economy, the city said - and created a "doomsday" scenario for Canadian steel producers, according to an industry association.
But even a more targeted tariff war would be "really quite damaging" to Hamilton's economy, Horwath said in an interview.
"At the end of the day, this is a town that still does a lot of steelmaking," she said. "We have thousands and thousands of people employed in primary steelmaking, but also in secondary processing as well."
Horwath said she has spoken to both provincial and federal leaders about the importance of "fighting for us and with us."
"I'm all in for this Team Canada approach," she said, noting the local chamber of commerce is organizing an advocacy mission to Washington and city council is expected to formally direct staff Wednesday to explore the potential for "Buy Canadian" procurement options.
Details of the federal government's response to the new tariffs were not immediately available Monday evening.
But Hamilton Liberal MP Chad Collins, who recently met with officials at ArcelorMittal Dofasco to talk about the threat, noted online that the tariffs violate existing trade agreements and that Canada will "take retaliatory action" to protect workers.
"We will not be bullied," he said.
That long-running threat of tariffs was already hurting local businesses long before Monday's announcement, according to city economic development and steel industry advocates.
Ron Wells, who heads the union representing Stelco workers, said Monday there has been a hiring freeze locally and "slow" sales since shortly after Trump was elected, threatening tariffs against Canada.
"They've told us sales are slow, the order books are low," the president of USW Local 1005 said in an interview Monday. "(A tariff war) is not going to help our business, obviously."
Keanin Loomis, CEO of the Canadian Institute of Steel Construction, previously told The Spectator he was aware of contract cancellations, job losses and project disruptions blamed on the threat of tariffs. City economic development staff have also cited knowledge of delayed business expansions and shrinking orders for some companies.
The city dealt with similar Trumpian tariffs imposed in 2018, which lasted about a year before trade negotiations ended the spat.
Steel exports to the U.S. from Canada fell 38 per cent during the 2018-19 trade war, said McMaster University economics professor Colin Mang. A similar, sustained drop in demand for Canadian steel this time "would be pretty significant" for the industry, he said Monday.
"That would definitely lead to job losses."
The 2018 tariff war hit some businesses harder than others.
In parliamentary hearings that year, family-owned Janco Steel of Stoney Creek reported its business in the U.S. dropped 60 per cent soon after tariffs were imposed, spurring cancelled orders and worries about potential layoffs.
"The success of Janco Steel's business model is predicated upon having an open trade border with the United States," senior manager Stephen Young told a parliamentary committee at that time.
The Spectator has reached out to Janco Steel about the implications of the latest tariffs, but has yet to hear back.
By comparison, Cleveland-Cliffs CEO Lourenco Goncalves - whose U.S.-based company recently took over Stelco - told reporters earlier this month he believes the Hamilton steelmaker will "benefit" from Trump's tariffs, which he supports. He pointed to Stelco's strong 2018 financial results to back up his argument.
Stelco was able to sell more steel domestically and at higher prices during a year-long steel tariff spat that started in 2018, enabling the company to make money and avoid layoffs.
Hamilton's biggest steelmaker, ArcelorMittal Dofasco, has so far declined to comment publicly about tariff impacts.
But the head of Dofasco's global parent company, ArcelorMittal CEO Aditya Mittal, noted in a recent earnings call with analysts that 25 per cent steel tariffs imposed in 2018 cost the company about $100 million per quarter. At that time, those costs were "more than offset by revenue," he added.
The only guarantee in a tariff war is the cost of steel will go up on both sides of the border, suggested David Ogilvie, owner of Waterdown's Service Steel and Manufacturing Inc., which supplies tool-and-die shops and special purpose machinery builders.
"We've been through this before ... And the prices went up - way up. Everyone cranked their prices," he said. "What that means is more of my operating line is going to raw materials."
Ogilvie said he expects "some impact" from a tariff war, if it lasts - but added his small business is flexible enough to survive.
"I wouldn't want to have 50 or 60 (employees). That'd be risky."
The renewed tariff threat blew up on the Ontario election campaign trail Monday.
"This is the next four years. Shifting goalposts and constant chaos, putting our economy at risk," wrote Progressive Conservative leader Doug Ford, who announced plans to go to Washington Tuesday to lobby U.S. politicians. Ford has cited the tariff threat as a reason he called an Ontario election more than a year early.
NDP leader Marit Stiles, meanwhile, made a campaign stop at a Tim Hortons on Ottawa Street Monday with labour leaders, including Wells from the United Steelworkers.
She criticized Ford for not doing more to "tariff-proof" the economy since 2018.
"I will fight like hell for every single job," she said. "I will make sure the jobs here in Hamilton are front and centre."
Liberal leader Bonnie Crombie slammed a "self-serving" Ford for calling an election in the middle of a trade war.
"You don't see another premier who would think of calling an election at a time of crisis," she said during a Toronto campaign stop.
Matthew Van Dongen is a reporter at The Spectator. mvandongen@thespec.com
CAPTION: Mayor Andrea Horwath is condemning the latest "irresponsible" threat of steel tariffs from U.S. President Donald Trump that she warned could affect "thousands and thousands" of local jobs.U.S. President Donald Trump's steel and aluminum tariffs will affect "thousands and thousands" of Hamilton jobs, Mayor Andrea Horwath says. Here, steel rolls sit in ArcelorMittal Dofasco yard.Galvanized steel sits on the mill floor at Stelco.

Ontario election 2025 promise tracker: From Trump tariffs to family doctors, here's what every provincial party leader is pledging

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-election-2025-promise-tracker-trump/docview/3165403833/se-2?accountid=15090

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+election+2025+promise+tracker%3A+From+Trump+tariffs+to+family+doctors%2C+here%27s+what+every+provincial+party+leader+is+pledging&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario is heading to the polls Feb. 27 and we're tracking what each party is promising ahead of the Ontario election.
All four provincial party leaders have now put forth plans to support Ontario through U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff threats, which have been postponed for 30 days.
Here's what else the Progressive Conservatives, NDP, Liberals and Greens have pledged since the start of the campaign.

Ford blasts China ahead of U.S. trip: PC leader would ban Chinese parts and firms from critical energy, mineral and infrastructure projects

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-blasts-china-ahead-u-s-trip/docview/3165307464/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+blasts+China+ahead+of+U.S.+trip%3A+PC+leader+would+ban+Chinese+parts+and+firms+from+critical+energy%2C+mineral+and+infrastructure+projects&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's election campaign is taking a detour to Washington, D.C. - thanks to U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff gambit.
Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford, who chairs the Council of the Federation, is leading a delegation there Tuesday to lobby American lawmakers and business leaders.
At midday, Ford will give a speech to the United States Chamber of Commerce, the world's largest business organization, before he and a dozen provincial and territorial leaders meet elected officials to share their concerns about Trump's moves.
The chamber has warned the president against imposing tariffs because the levies would hurt businesses and consumers on both sides of the border.
In the evening, Ford will co-host a reception at the Library of Congress for premiers and U.S. lawmakers with South Dakota Republican Sen. Kevin Cramer, a key Trump ally who co-chairs the bipartisan American Canadian economy and security caucus.
The Ontarian will make a second visit to the U.S. capital on Feb. 20 to discuss the trade crisis with delegates to the National Governors' Association winter conference.
His two trips are coming in the middle of a snap Feb. 27 election Ford triggered because he wanted a new four-year mandate to "outlive and outlast" the Trump administration.
Revving up for his unusual stateside push, Ford was taking aim at China on Monday.
The PC leader said he would ban Chinese parts from energy projects and prohibit participation by any Chinese state-owned enterprises in critical mineral or infrastructure projects if elected to a third term.
Trump's latest scheme to place 25 per cent tariffs on steel and aluminum - despite a pledge last week to hold off on any levies until March - shows why Ontario must be flexible, Ford told reporters in Oakville after a fundraising breakfast.
"We're going to face this for four years, shifting goalposts constantly and constant chaos," he said.
While he has focused his campaign on fighting tariffs, he suffered a setback a week ago when he was caught on a "hot mic" saying that he "100 per cent" supported Trump to win the November U.S. election over Kamala Harris.
"We're in a battle for the future of our economy," Ford said Monday, emphasizing it's crucial to convince Americans that U.S.-Canada economic co-operation is the best way to meet trade threats from China and put Trump off his talk of making Canada the 51st state.
"I'm going to tell them exactly what I'm telling you, Ontario is taking action to secure our border and delivering results."
Previously planned moves to take U.S. spirits, wines and beers off LCBO shelves and ban American companies from major contracts remain on hold for now, the PC leader said.
"Let's first see what he's going do to," he added, sending a signal south of the border that Canada as a whole has a plan for a "hard" response.
"We are not going to roll over. There's one thing that President Trump has underestimated - the will of the Canadian people," said Ford.
"We never started a fight we don't want. But when someone you know gives you a pop in the nose, they're going to get that pop twice as hard back."
In Toronto, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said the tough talk from Ford is ironic given the Tory's professed admiration for Trump in last week's headline-grabbing gaffe.
Crombie noted the president campaigned on placing tariffs on Canada and Mexico during the U.S. election.
Her Liberals are airing television ads they hope will draw widespread public attention to that and eat into Ford's current lead in public opinion polls.
"We're showing him for who he is, someone who wanted a self-serving election," said Crombie, whose campaign has been mostly about improving health care by guaranteeing every Ontarian access to a family doctor.
"You don't see another premier who would think of calling an election at a time of crisis, when we're responding to a tariff threat, and a president who wants to wage a trade war and our premier supports that president," she said.
New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles, who met with steelworkers in Hamilton on Monday morning after highlighting her push for a higher nurse-to-patient ratio in hospitals to improve care, said Ford is exploiting the trade crisis to divert attention from pressing issues on the home front.
"Doug Ford doesn't want to talk about the fact that people in Ontario are losing their family members in hallways in our hospitals because there aren't enough beds and there aren't enough nurses," Stiles told supporters in Port Colborne before taking a shot at Ford's support for Trump.
"Doug Ford has shown us what he's made of," she added. "I don't think we can trust Doug Ford to stand up for Ontarians and to protect all our jobs and defend our workers and our communities."
Along with Green Leader Mike Schreiner, the party chiefs are gearing up for a Friday debate on northern issues in North Bay and another next Monday night in Toronto at the CBC Broadcast Centre.
CAPTION: Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford, shown Monday at a manufacturing facility, is leading a delegation of elected officials to Washington Tuesday to lobby American lawmakers and business leaders about their concerns over Donald Trump's trade threats.Nick LachanceToronto Star

Document 255 of 500

A message to Justin Trudeau from an American: You can lead the world to stop our would-be dictator

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/message-justin-trudeau-american-you-can-lead/docview/3165307289/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+message+to+Justin+Trudeau+from+an+American%3A+You+can+lead+the+world+to+stop+our+would-be+dictator&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: America has always preached from the bully pulpitCanada won't back down, will scale up retaliation against Trump's tariffs as needed, government says, Feb. 2
It truly boggles my mind to now see politicians of all stripes, decision makers, businesses and many Canadians are belatedly stepping on the Canada First bandwagon, now that Donald Trump coughed, metaphorically.
Where were you when it was downright evident for decades past that America was and is a selfish and self-centred bully that does everything and anything to suit herself and her interests only, the world be damned?
You were all sleepwalking, singing the praises about our southern neighbour, visiting the country on a regular basis, patronizing their businesses, rather than shopping locally, to save a few pennies. A little jolt by a bully has now awakened you all.
All our interests, economically and otherwise, were put into the giant's basket for sheer laziness on the part of politicians and businesses instead of large-scale diversifying to other countries years ago. Not a single automobile is made of 100 per cent Canadian parts. Why? Relying too much on our "friends" to the south?
I told you so, I can now say. I am not being a hypocrite, as I have not visited the United States since 2000, do not own property there, do not buy a return trip by air to another country via America, do not cross the border to shop.
I have consciously been patronizing local businesses and Canadian goods exclusively, as best I can.
Aquil Ali, Toronto
Canada must stand its ground in the face of aggression
Canadian Prime Minister Justin Trudeau must choose whether he should follow the course of Neville Chamberlain or Winston Churchill.
For the survival of Canadians, and of people throughout the world, I hope he chooses the latter.
Canadians can lead the world to show the American would-be dictator that no matter the economic hardship or individual sacrifice, Canadians will stand together, stand strong and wield their sword to protect their homes and rights.
Millions of Americans are with you.
Jeff C. Tavares, Greenwood, Indiana
Reagan warned of demagogues like Trump
In his weekly radio address to the American people on Nov. 26, 1988, then-president Ronald Reagan offered his insights about U.S. politicians who peddled American protectionism against Canada, which now seems rather prescient under Donald Trump's presidency: "We too often talk about trade while using the vocabulary of war. In war, for one side to win, the other must lose. But commerce is not warfare. Our peaceful trading partners are not our enemies; they are our allies. We should beware of the demagogues who are ready to declare a trade war against our friends, weakening our economy, our national security and the entire free world all while cynically waving the American flag."
Obviously, Trump's Republicans are no longer the party of Reagan.
Luis Silva, Toronto
Hit Trump where it hurts: the wallet
Well, Ding Dong Don got his desired result and has everyone running around in a tizzy.
It's obvious diplomacy doesn't work with him but firmness, discomfort, fear of strong negotiators like Chrystia Freeland, pushback from his base, other oligarchs and organized, unrelenting vocal protest groups do.
Canadians haven't been this united in a long time and seem to be embracing the boycott principle.
This is an excellent strategy, as long as we don't weaken.
I am 85 and have travelled all 50 states, many times, since the age of nine and have always found the citizens to be kind and generous. I will not travel across the border until sanity is restored in Washington.
My plan is to contact newspaper editors, chambers of commerce, travel associations, politicians and any other organization with a vested interest in restoring sanity and let them know what non-American car, machine or product I purchased. I would also supply the cost of my latest travel to a non-U.S. country.
Sad to say, but the only thing worthy of respect in Trump's world is money. So if the bottom line deteriorates, he will respond.
He's a coward. Stand up to him.
John Russell, Fonthill, Ont.
Dear Canada: Many Americans stand united with you
This American would like to apologize to the citizens of the great nation of Canada for the insults and affronts being heaped upon you by Trump.
Canada has stood beside the United States during two world wars and the 9/11 attacks. I feel terrible that our solid friendship has been tarnished, hopefully not irreparably. Please remember that while Trump did win the popular vote here he did not win a majority, and his plurality was razor thin.
I don't know what it will take to get rid of Trump, but I look forward to that day and to repairing the United States' relationship with our neighbours to the north.
Bill Gottdenker, Mountainside, N.J.
My $200 is going to the Green PartyThe Ford government's $200 'tax rebate' cheques are coming soon, Jan. 29
I refuse to be bribed by Doug Ford's $200 gift from the public purse (at least partially funded by my own tax money). It is an unnecessary and opportunistic attempt to extend his own job (and indexed pension) which he claims is needed to enable him to fight to defend Ontario jobs from Trump's tariffs.
Surely it is part of his job description as premier of Ontario to fight for the rights of Ontario citizens against internal or external threats or interference and does not require a new mandate. He has not previously found it necessary to acquire a new mandate for any of the other controversial and divisive decisions his government has made.
As I have received the $200, I have decided the best use for it is to donate it to one of the opposition parties, and I encourage others to do the same. In my case it will be the Green Party of Ontario, who seem to me to have the most thoughtful and beneficial plans for the province.
Brian Mayhew, Puslinch, Ont.
Ford takes with one hand and gives with the other
My $200 rebate cheque arrived today. It feels like somebody borrowed my credit card, took out a $200 cash advance, gave me the credit card back and the $200 and expects my gratitude for what they did.
Michael Wolczyk, Mississsauga
A tunnel under the 401? Pure follyOntario election 2025: Doug Ford vows to forge ahead with tunnel under Hwy. 401, Feb. 7
Ontario Premier Doug Ford again is raising his idea of building a tunnel under Hwy. 401 to relieve congestion.
In that light, let's look at his idea. "Folly" is defined as lack of good judgement or a foolish idea or act. Folly lacks good sense, and the person who designs a folly lacks understanding of the costs of building the folly.
A folly is usually named after the individual who commissioned the project.
So here in Ontario we now have Ford's Folly.
Terry Middlemiss, Hamilton
CAPTION: A letter writer states Canadians should not be surprised at Trump's tactics, saying the U.S. has always been a bully.
