Document 256 of 500

First Nation says it will safeguard environment: Chief says band has 'stayed on top of' Therme's spa plans

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/first-nation-says-will-safeguard-environment/docview/3165305256/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=First+Nation+says+it+will+safeguard+environment%3A+Chief+says+band+has+%27stayed+on+top+of%27+Therme%27s+spa+plans&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Mississaugas of the Credit First Nation wants people to know that the band is helping safeguard Ontario Place's natural environment during a controversial redevelopment, even as critics cite the project's lack of transparency and call for a full environmental review.
"I know we've got a lot of public criticism and a lot of people that don't understand the broader process," said Chief Claire Sault in MCFN's first public comments about its partnership with Therme Canada, which is building a "urban well-being oasis" on the former amusement park's West Island. The spa is a key part of the Ontario Place revitalization launched by Doug Ford's Progressive Conservative government in 2019.
Construction preparation has included tree removals to make way for Therme's towering glass spa resort.
"For the environmental work, MCFN employed a biologist, we evaluated Therme's aquatic habitat work prior to the submission of permit applications," Sault said. "MCFN's knowledge and expertise, I believe, strengthened Therme's plans for the shoreline and creating expanded and improved areas for species of importance to the Nation."
As far as any environmental issues at the Ontario Place site, Sault is clear: "We have stayed on top of it."
The provincial revamp of the man-made islands has triggered criticism from wildlife biologists and other advocates for a more natural environment with no spa resort.
Last August, the Haudenosaunee Development Institute, created by the Haudenosaunee confederacy of First Nations, said the federal government had failed to meet Indigenous engagement obligations, adding that it was "concerned with the impact of the proposed Ontario Place development on Lake Ontario and on aquatic life."
Sault said removal of a thicket of 800 trees on the West Island to make way for the spa last summer was particularly sensitive with MCFN members, including elders and knowledge keepers. They reviewed Therme's plans to replant more trees than were removed, held tree ceremonies on the island and ultimately supported the plans, she added.
Sault said her band, based in Haldimand County with traditional territories spanning much of the Golden Horseshoe, must also derive long-term financial benefits from the consultations.
Those consultations started with an approach from Therme before the Vienna-based resort developer submitted its bid to the Ontario government that eventually yielded its 95-year lease as an anchor tenant.
"I'm very passionate about economic reconciliation for our nation," said Sault, elected chief to succeed Stacy Laforme in late 2023. "Those joint ventures - if we can, for example, create a company that provides landscaping" for the West Island, "I'm all in."
She predicted that Ontarians will accept and enjoy the West Island once construction and replanting is complete: "Especially when they see our presence, our footprint there, they'll recognize that we have a long treaty history here that is important to us.
"I get you have to consider all these other parties, but our community is, to some degree, living in poverty," Sault said. "We need to be reconciled too."
Asked to detail those financial benefits, Sault said her band has so far received "some consultation dollars here and there, but it's not a huge amount," adding that she expects greater benefits in future. MCFN currently, she said, has a "capacity agreement with Therme - they provide capacity dollars for us to do the work we need to do."
Ontario Place for All, a group opposed to any privatization of the site, met late last year with Sault and two band councillors after trying and failing to get a meeting with Laforme, who had also not responded to the Star's request to discuss MCFN's Ontario Place support.
Ann Elisabeth Samson, a member of the group, said she appreciates the MCFN's stewardship of the site but, given that details of the arrangement are confidential like many other parts of the redevelopment plan, it's no replacement for a full environmental review.
"They assured us they were the environmental expertise on the project," Samson said.
"But the province passed the Rebuilding Ontario Place Act in order to avoid an open, transparent, accountable environmental assessment.
"Sure, what the Mississaugas are doing is great, but it would also be great to let the rest of Ontarians be part of that process and to understand what's being done to protect the environment," she said, adding that Ontario Place for All's concerns are with the plans of Therme and the provincial government.
"I told them I'm sorry the whole site wasn't given to the Mississaugas."
Therme, in an email to the Star, said it approached MCFN in 2019. The band was already engaged with another proponent, preventing a formal partnership then, but agreed to work with Therme after it won the contract.
Band members, the firm said, have helped shape the project's environmental and landscape design and facilitated "multiple in-community consultations and knowledge-sharing sessions related to how the West Island of Ontario Place could, for the first time, reflect the history of MCFN."
A provincial government spokesperson said the Infrastructure Ministry is consulting on the redevelopment with Indigenous communities, including MCFN, that have treaty rights that cover the Ontario Place site, and that a goal of the revamp is "to create a place to celebrate Indigenous cultures and share Indigenous history and traditions."
CAPTION: Construction preparation at Ontario Place has included tree removals to make way for Therme's glass spa resort. The provincial revamp of the man-made islands has triggered criticism from wildlife biologists and other advocates for a more natural environment with no spa.Steve Russell Toronto Star file photo
CREDIT: David Rider Senior Politics Reporter

Document 259 of 500

Letters: Canada won't back down, will scale up retaliation against Trump's tariffs as needed, government says, Feb. 2 The Ford government's $200 'tax rebate' cheques are coming soon, Jan. 29

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3165304538/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Canada+won%27t+back+down%2C+will+scale+up+retaliation+against+Trump%27s+tariffs+as+needed%2C+government+says%2C+Feb.+2+The+Ford+government%27s+%24200+%27tax+rebate%27+cheques+are+coming+soon%2C+Jan.+29&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: U.S. has always preached from bully pulpit
Canada won't back down, will scale up retaliation against Trump's tariffs as needed, government says, Feb. 2
It boggles my mind to see politicians of all stripes, decision makers, businesses and many Canadians are belatedly stepping on the Canada First bandwagon, now that Donald Trump coughed, metaphorically.
Where were you when it was downright evident for decades past that America was and is a selfish and self-centred bully that does everything and anything to suit herself and her interests only, the world be damned? You were all sleepwalking, singing the praises about our southern neighbour, visiting the country on a regular basis, patronizing their businesses, rather than shopping locally, to save a few pennies. A little jolt by a bully has now awakened you all.
All our interests, economically and otherwise, were put into the giant's basket for sheer laziness on the part of politicians and businesses instead of large-scale diversifying to other countries years ago. Not a single automobile is made of 100 per cent Canadian parts. Why? Relying too much on our "friends" to the south?
I told you so, I can now say. I am not being a hypocrite, as I have not visited the United States since 2000, do not own property there, do not buy a return trip by air to another country via America, do not cross the border to shop. I have consciously been patronizing local businesses and Canadian goods exclusively, as best I can.
Aquil Ali, Toronto
Canada must stand its ground
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau must choose whether he should follow the course of Neville Chamberlain or Winston Churchill.
For the survival of Canadians, and of people throughout the world, I hope he chooses the latter. Canadians can lead the world to show the American would-be dictator that no matter the economic hardship or individual sacrifice, Canadians will stand together, stand strong and wield their sword to protect their homes and rights.
Millions of Americans are with you.
Jeff C. Tavares,
Greenwood, Ind.
Reagan warned of demagogues like Trump
In his weekly radio address to the American people on Nov. 26, 1988, then-president Ronald Reagan offered his insights about U.S. politicians who peddled American protectionism against Canada, which now seems rather prescient under Donald Trump's presidency.
"We too often talk about trade while using the vocabulary of war. In war, for one side to win, the other must lose. But commerce is not warfare. Our peaceful trading partners are not our enemies; they are our allies. We should beware of the demagogues who are ready to declare a trade war against our friends, weakening our economy, our national security and the entire free world all while cynically waving the American flag."
Obviously, Trump's Republicans are no longer the party of Reagan.
Luis Silva, Toronto
Hit Trump
where it hurts
Well, Ding Dong Don got his desired result and has everyone running around in a tizzy.
It's obvious diplomacy doesn't work with him but firmness, discomfort, fear of strong negotiators like Chrystia Freeland, pushback from his base, other oligarchs and organized, unrelenting vocal protest groups do.
Canadians haven't been this united in a long time and seem to be embracing the boycott principle.
This is an excellent strategy, as long as we don't weaken.
I am 85 and have travelled all 50 states, many times, since the age of nine and have always found the citizens to be kind and generous. I will not travel across the border until sanity is restored in Washington.
My plan is to contact newspaper editors, chambers of commerce, travel associations, politicians and any other organization with a vested interest in restoring sanity and let them know what non-American car, machine or product I purchased. I would also supply the cost of my latest travel to a non-U.S. country.
Sad to say, but the only thing worthy of respect in Trump's world is money. So if the bottom line deteriorates, he will respond.
He's a coward. Stand up to him.
John Russell, Fonthill, Ont.
Many Americans stand united
with Canada
This American would like to apologize to the citizens of the great nation of Canada for the insults and affronts being heaped upon you. Canada has stood beside the United States during two world wars and the 9/11 attacks. I feel terrible that our solid friendship has been tarnished, hopefully not irreparably. Please remember that while Trump did win the popular vote here, he did not win a majority, and his plurality was razor thin. I don't know what it will take to get rid of Trump, but I look forward to that day and to repairing the United States' relationship with our neighbours to the north.
Bill Gottdenker,
Mountainside, N.J.
My $200 is going to Green party
The Ford government's $200 'tax rebate' cheques are coming soon, Jan. 29
I refuse to be bribed by Doug Ford's $200 gift from the public purse. It is an unnecessary and opportunistic attempt to extend his own job (and indexed pension) which he claims is needed to enable him to fight to defend Ontario jobs from Trump's tariffs. Surely it is part of his job description as premier of Ontario to fight for the rights of Ontario citizens against internal or external threats or interference and does not require a new mandate. He has not previously found it necessary to acquire a new mandate for any of the other controversial and divisive decisions his government has made. As I have received the $200, I have decided the best use for it is to donate it to one of the opposition parties, and I encourage others to do the same. In my case it will be the Green Party of Ontario, who seem to me to have the most thoughtful and beneficial plans for the province.
Brian Mayhew, Puslinch, Ont.
Ford takes with one hand and gives with other
My $200 rebate cheque arrived today. It feels like somebody borrowed my credit card, took out a $200 cash advance, gave me the credit card back and the $200 and expects my gratitude for what they did.
Michael Wolczyk, Mississauga
CREDIT: ;

Document 260 of 500

Ontario election polls show Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives far ahead of Bonnie Crombie's Liberals

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-election-polls-show-doug-fords/docview/3165492312/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+election+polls+show+Doug+Ford%27s+Progressive+Conservatives+far+ahead+of+Bonnie+Crombie%27s+Liberals&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With the Ontario election campaign underway, the Star is launching a poll tracker so you can follow all the twists and turns ahead of election day on Feb. 27.
We've got the numbers from the top pollsters in one place, with handy charts, and a rolling, three-day average of all the results.
There hasn't been much change since the campaign began Jan. 29, with the Progressive Conservatives and Doug Ford far ahead of the second-place Liberals and Bonnie Crombie. The NDP and Marit Stiles are well back in third place.
As of Feb. 6, the tracker shows that Ford and co. are being backed by 43.3 per cent of decided or leaning voters, with Crombie's Liberals at 29.3 per cent. Stiles and the NDP have just 18 per cent, while Mike Schreiner's Greens have the backing of 5.7 per cent.
In Ontario's last election, in 2022, Ford's PCs took 40.8 per cent of the popular vote across the province, with the Liberals earning 23.9 per cent and the NDP taking 23.7 per cent. That led Ford to his second straight majority government, with 83 seats. Despite finishing narrowly behind the Liberals in the popular vote, the NDP took 31 seats and the Liberals just eight.
The Star is compiling polling for the 2025 Ontario election from polling firms that received a grade of A or A+ from 338Canada during the 2022 Ontario election. 338Canada is an independent media organization that created a statistical model of electoral projections based on opinion polls, electoral history, and demographic data.
These polling firms use a variety of polling methods, including online surveys and Smart Interactive Voice Response (IVR) systems. As online polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, margins listed here are for a comparable random sample of this size.
Percentage breakdowns are shares of committed or leaning voters, in line with industry conventions.
CAPTION: Clockwise from top: Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford, Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner, NDP Leader Marit Stiles.

Subject: Voters

People: Schreiner, Mike

Title: Ontario election polls show Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives far ahead of Bonnie Crombie's Liberals

Document 261 of 500

Two Trudeau cabinet ministers say they won't seek re-election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/two-trudeau-cabinet-ministers-say-they-wont-seek/docview/3165492303/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Two+Trudeau+cabinet+ministers+say+they+won%27t+seek+re-election&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Two members of Justin Trudeau's cabinet, both representing GTA ridings, announced Monday they will not seek re-election in 2025, a year in which a federal election is expected sooner rather than later.
International Trade Minister Mary Ng and Justice Minister Arif Virani each said they had informed Trudeau that they will not be running again.
Ng, who has represented Markham-Thornhill since longtime Liberal MP John McCallum stepped down in 2017, wrote in a statement that the political arena now faces a "critical challenge" that "transcends partisan lines": a deluge of disinformation and "attacks on the character of our leaders," she said, is deterring people from entering politics.
"My faith in Canada's future remains unshakable," wrote Ng. "Though I won't see re-election, I remain committed to building this future - because Canada is a place where anything is possible."
Earlier in the day, Virani wrote on social media that his "fight for Canada" will continue outside the House of Commons after the next election.
"I say this with a heavy heart, and after considerable soul-searching for the past several weeks," Virani wrote in a statement.
The ministers will keep their portfolios and will not leave Trudeau's front bench immediately, particularly as Ng shapes Canada's trade diversification strategy amid U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff threats.
"My first priority has been safeguarding our access to the world's largest economic partnership through CUSMA, our free trade agreement between Canada, the United States, and Mexico," Ng wrote, adding that Canada also has "everything it takes to succeed" as it faces off against its southern neighbour.
Ng became small business minister in 2018 before taking on the international trade file in 2019. She had previously worked in the Ontario government and served as director of public appointments in the Prime Minister's Office under Trudeau.
Virani practised law for 15 years before entering public life nearly a decade ago. He spent eight years as parliamentary secretary to numerous ministers covering a range of portfolios before being named justice minister and attorney general in 2023.
He wrested the Toronto riding of Parkdale-High Park away from the New Democrats in 2015 in a close race that saw the Liberals edge out two-term incumbent MP Peggy Nash by a margin of just over 1,000 votes.
In his statement, Virani said he plans to "continue, doggedly, to fight alongside the Liberal party" and its next leader. The justice minister is one of a handful of Liberal cabinet ministers to back former deputy prime minister Chrystia Freeland's bid to take on that mantle.
Virani's riding, which will go by the name Taiaiako'n - Parkdale-High Park in the next election, is already being challenged by Jagmeet Singh's NDP.
In September, NDP MPP Bhutila Karpoche announced her intention to seek the federal nomination in the riding, which she now represents provincially. Karpoche, who became the party's official candidate late last year, is widely seen as a key part of the federal NDP's plan to capitalize on discontent with the Liberals by winning back a number of long-lost Toronto seats.
Virani cited his family as his primary reason for leaving federal politics, saying his two children and his wife, Suchita, have "only known a life with their husband and dad in politics, frequently out of the city, sometimes out of the country.
"People say that is the price of public service," his statement read. "It is my turn now to give back to the people I love most."
Ng did not provide a reason for her departure, but thanked Trudeau for his leadership and his "commitment to creating seats at the table." She has not publicly endorsed any of his would-be successors.
Ng's riding has only been held by Liberals since its creation in 2015. In the 2021 election, she won 61.5 per cent of the vote.
CAPTION: Justice Minister Arif Virani rises during question period in the House of Commons on Dec. 9, 2024.

Wake up, Ontario. Citizens get the government they deserve

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/wake-up-ontario-citizens-get-government-they/docview/3165067849/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Wake+up%2C+Ontario.+Citizens+get+the+government+they+deserve&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford says a 'clear mandate' from Ontario voters is the best way to deal with Donald Trump, Jan. 22
If you are paying one bit of attention to the gutting of democracy in the United States, understand one thing: people get the government they deserve. Through a lack of attention to events, warnings, and simply not voting, the American people have that very government. We have no reason to be smug here in Ontario. As Premier Doug Ford's spin doctors turn incompetence - the blatant waste of our tax dollars and the constant lack of real attention to housing, education and health care - into high ratings it is clear that we now have the government we deserve. And, by not voting in this election, held in the coldest month of the year, we would submit to his plan of voter suppression. I fear that's what we're about to do as most people are not paying attention and simply will not vote. If you are reading this, speak to friends and family who are unaware by their own choice. The collective shame is on us. Wake up, Ontario.
David Ottenbrite, Cambridge, ON
Therme spa at Ontario Place another big mistake for conservatives
Doug Ford promises to axe tolls on eastern portion of Highway 407, Feb. 5
Sad and hilarious, Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford says "It was a big mistake" for former PC Premier Mike Harris to sign a 99-year lease with a private consortium. Then why did Ford sign a 99-year lease with a private foreign company, Therme, to build and operate something-or-other on our public land at Ontario Place? Just another big mistake, I guess.
Jay Butler, Etobicoke
Vote beyond those hats and slogansDoug Ford overheard saying he was happy Donald Trump became president - 'then the guy pulled out the knife, Feb. 4'
The people of Ontario deserve something better than hats, slogans and duplicity from their premier. Doug Ford has never been Captain Canada, has always been a Trump supporter and will be instrumental in electing Pierre Poilievre, a protege of Stephen Harper who is currently the chair of IDU, a global network of right and far-right political parties, with strong ties to the U.S. Republican Party. That's what this election is really about.
Kat Duffy, Mississauga
Once tariff crisis dies down, do you want four more years of Ford?It's wartime. Do without. Shop locally, Feb. 5
Heather Mallick got it right. We should not vote for Doug Ford. He can project an aura of strength, like Donald Trump, but he too is a man of moral vacuity. Once the tariff crisis dies down, as it will, we don't want to be stuck with four more years of sleazy deals and ill-conceived policies. Do we even think that Trump will grant Ford a face-to-face audience so that Ford can flash his bona fides as a scrapper? Mallick is also correct in her assessment of Pierre Poilievre as a narrow-minded, spiteful man. As we try to draw together as Team Canada to face the felon to the south, Poilievre snipes from the sidelines, trying to gain political advantage. Whose side is he on, anyway?
Isobel Raven, Toronto
Trump's economic warfare is a national emergency
Donald Trump postpones threatened tariffs for 30 days after Canada offers more border security measures, Feb. 4
The bullying behaviour of U.S. President Donald Trump and, indeed, the U.S. federal government, must be recognized for what it is: economic terrorism. When Trump enacts 25 per cent tariffs to coerce our annexation, he declares economic war on our country. Our federal government will have no choice but to invoke the Emergencies Act. Certainly, the tariffs and coercion fall within the act's definition of a public order emergency " ... that arises from threats to the security of Canada that are so serious as to be a national emergency; ... that seriously endangers the health and safety of Canadians; ... that seriously threatens the ability of the Government of Canada to preserve the sovereignty, security and territorial integrity of Canada." This will give our government the real tools it needs to effectively stand up to Trump, beyond futile appeasement and counter-tariffs. As a start, embargoing oil, electricity, water, aluminum, steel and rare earth minerals, and expropriating U.S. assets here should get his attention.
Peter Madott, Mississauga
Amazon leaving Quebec is all about corporate greed
Amazon Canada says it will close all seven of its warehouses in Quebec, Jan. 24
The constant attacks on collective bargaining need to stop. Canada was founded on the backs of workers and Sec. 2(d) of the Charter of Rights and Freedoms says we have the right to freedom of association. A right ... not a wish or a suggestion, a right workers have fought for long and hard. We see employers trampling over these rights all the time and it has to stop.
In Quebec, Amazon workers looked forward to unionizing but what did Amazon do - 1,950 employees will soon be out of a job. Amazon says it has nothing to do with unions. I call B.S. It has everything to do with unions and corporate greed. Amazon had a projected profit of more than $40,000,000,000 in 2024. That's $40,000,000,000 profit from just last year. It can afford to pay its workers fairly. The company doesn't seem to want to.
Amazon was at first excited come to Quebec, a move it said in 2021 "gives us the opportunity to better serve our customers across" the province. In April 2024, the first union was formed in Laval representing 200 employees. Amazon took them to court to contest the decision. Amazon was told they must stop interfering with union affairs by an Administrative Labour Tribunal judge in August and was given a $30,000 fine. Let's do the math: $30,000 fine compared to $40,000,000,000 equals an infinitesimal dent in the company's profit. Seriously? What deterrent is that? These fines are now a small cost of doing business instead of the economic penalty they were designed to be.
It happens time and time again. Massive multinational corporations come with big pocketbooks and trample over workers' rights with no consequences. Union busting must stop.
Tasha Salesse, Saint John, NB
Governments should harness ideas shared on letters pages
It always amazes me when I read the letters to the editor regarding issues of the day. The insight, practicality and intelligent solutions addressing the ills of the world can be found within these letters. Forget convening special assemblies of politicians. All levels of government should utilize the incredible ideas of its informed citizenry. Dub them the Citizens Forum.
Johana Kelly, Mississauga

Document 263 of 500

The Liberal Party is not the only party that needs a new leader

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/liberal-party-is-not-only-that-needs-new-leader/docview/3165067660/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+Liberal+Party+is+not+the+only+party+that+needs+a+new+leader&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: While on the topic of the Liberal leadership race, we should also talk about another party that's in dire straits and desperately needs a new leader: the NDP.
When Jagmeet Singh won the leadership in late 2017, he was a breath of fresh air for the frumpy NDP, which had long been associated with socks-with-sandals lefties and equally unfashionable table-pounding union leaders. He was young, diverse, social-media savvy and from outside the Ottawa bubble. As a fashion plate who enjoys the finer things in life, he cut a dash through Ontario provincial politics and the Toronto cultural scene.
But like many first-round draft picks, he flopped in the majors.
He should have done better. The rise in populism throughout the West has happened on both the right and left. Right-wing populism may get more media coverage and be more electorally successful, but left-wing populism, particularly that which appeals to students and activist youth, has nonetheless brought new energy to left-leaning parties.
Examples include the leftward shift among Democrats in the United States, kicked off by Senator Bernie Sanders; the British Labour Party's near-victory in 2017 under Jeremy Corbyn; and the French parliamentary elections held this past summer, in which an alliance of left-wing parties unexpectedly surged, shaking up President Macron's political order.
The NDP could have ridden the upward momentum of the populist left to a stronger position, but it didn't.
So what are the key performance indicators for Jagmeet Singh? The seat count, the popular vote, byelection results, and long-term polling trends for the party. How do the numbers look? Not good at all.
The 2019 federal election was Singh's first. Going in, the NDP had 39 seats. Coming out, only 24. A complete rout. Its popular vote also dropped to 16 per cent. This, despite campaigning against a Liberal government that Canadians had already turned against.
In the 2021 election, the NDP's results were flat, and the party gained only one seat.
In other words, across two election cycles during which the left was globally ascendant, facing an incumbent who dropped seats, the NDP lost support.
And it hasn't recovered. Byelections, generally considered risk-free protest votes against incumbents, present opportunities for the opposition. Did the NDP put points on the board?
Through 19 byelections that took place between 2018 and 2025, the NDP did not flip a single seat to its column. The party held two seats in loyal ridings but lost two others, for an overall net loss of two seats. What's worse, its popular vote dropped in 17 of the 19 contests as compared to previous results.
The opinion polls have also been bleak for a long time. According to the latest from the CBC's poll tracker, the NDP's numbers have been stuck in the high teens for the past three years, with no upward movement even during this lengthy period of plunging support for the Liberals.
As a Conservative, my views on what's best for Canada are obviously different from the NDP's - but dare I say that a healthy NDP is good for our democracy? (And it's not that a strong NDP bleeds votes from the left flank of the Liberals, allowing Tories to eke out victories where they otherwise could not. I don't subscribe to that theory.)
The left needs a large, brokerage-style coalition party that can responsibly absorb, mediate between and articulate on behalf of a wide array of like-minded supporters. That would be much better for Canadian politics than a collection of small, cause-driven, single-issue parties prone to disorder and extremism.
In short, Canada needs a vital NDP, and we're not getting it under Jagmeet Singh. Under his leadership, the NDP has been on a long losing streak with no sign of reversal. Sorry, but he's a no-hoper. It's time for the NDP to change the captain.
CAPTION: NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh talks to media in the foyer outside the House of Commons prior to Question Period in Ottawa on Wednesday, Dec.4, 2024. THE CANADIAN PRESS/Sean Kilpatrick
CREDIT: Mark Johnson, Contributor

Subject: Union leadership; Populism

Business indexing term: Subject: Union leadership

People: Singh, Jagmeet

'This is the next four years': Canadian officials react to Donald Trump's steel and aluminum tariff threats

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/this-is-next-four-years-canadian-officials-react/docview/3165067600/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27This+is+the+next+four+years%27%3A+Canadian+officials+react+to+Donald+Trump%27s+steel+and+aluminum+tariff+threats&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Just hours before Super Bowl LIX kicked off in New Orleans, U.S. President Donald Trump told reporters aboard Air Force One that he intended to announce 25 per cent tariffs on all steel and aluminum imports on Monday, including for Canada and Mexico.
Following dinner with French President Emmanuel Macron in Paris, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau did not answer questions from reporters about the tariffs and whether Canada would retaliate.
But by the time the Philadelphia Eagles were sealing their victory over the Kansas City Chiefs, several prominent Canadian public officials and organizations weighed in on the latest chapter in the ongoing saga between Canada and its largest trading partner.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford wrote on the social media platform X that "shifting goalposts and constant chaos" will be hallmarks of Trump's second administration.
"This is the next four years," Ford wrote.
François-Philippe Champagne, the federal Minister of Innovation, Science and Industry, wrote that "Canadian steel and aluminum support key industries in the U.S. from defence, shipbuilding and auto."
Ontario MP Karina Gould, who is running for leadership of the Liberal party of Canada, said that Canada "will respond in kind and let the president know just how much he depends on Canada."
In a post in French, Bloc Québécois leader Yves-François Blanchet wrote that Trump's tariffs would expose U.S. industries to serious inflation and encouraged him to negotiate, while Ontario NDP leader Marit Stiles wrote that "Ontario's workers are not going to take this lying down."
Catherine Cobden, the president and CEO of the Canadian Steel Producers Association, said in a statement that Canada must "retaliate immediately."
"We are urgently demanding that the Government of Canada act again with resolve and purpose to combat this threat and ensure any measure taken against our sector is met with retaliatory measures and action to offset the devastating impacts tariffs would have on our sector and our workers," Cobden said.
A representative for the Aluminum Association of Canada told the Star that they would not be making a comment as they wait for further clarity from the White House.
Lana Payne, the president of the trade union Unifor, proclaimed on X that this "is what economic warfare looks like."
Trump did not give any indication what Canada would need to do to avoid the tariffs he plans on officially announcing Monday.
Trump imposed steel and aluminum tariffs at 25 and 10 per cent respectively during his first term in March 2018, using national security as justification.
Canada was initially given an exemption to these duties, but was ultimately hit by the tariffs on May 31, 2018. Canada responded with a series of counter-tariffs on American products like Florida orange juice.
Nearly a year later, on May 17, 2019, the White House announced a deal had been reached to prevent "surges" in the steel and aluminum supplies from Canada and Mexico, ending the trade dispute.
In an interview with Fox News on Sunday, Trump reiterated his desire to make Canada part of the U.S.
"I think Canada would be much better off being the 51st state because we lose $200 billion a year with Canada," Trump told Fox's Bret Baier in a pretaped interview from Florida.
With files from The Associated Press and The Canadian Press.
CAPTION: President Donald Trump says he'll announce on Monday that the United States will impose 25% tariffs on all steel and aluminium imports, including from Canada and Mexico, as well as other import duties later in the week. Trump also commented on the wars in Gaza and Ukraine as he flew on Sunday from Florida to attend the Super Bowl in New Orleans. (AP Video / Feb. 9, 2025)Canadian officials react to Trump's announcement of steel and aluminum tariffs

Document 265 of 500

Doug Ford talks tough on China ahead of Washington trip to fight Donald Trump's tariffs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-talks-tough-on-china-ahead-washington/docview/3165067581/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+talks+tough+on+China+ahead+of+Washington+trip+to+fight+Donald+Trump%27s+tariffs&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's election campaign is taking a detour to Washington, D.C. - thanks to U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff gambit.
Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford, who chairs the Council of the Federation, is leading a delegation there Tuesday to lobby American lawmakers and business leaders.
At midday, Ford will give a speech to the United States Chamber of Commerce, the world's largest business organization, before he and a dozen provincial and territorial leaders meet elected officials to share their concerns about Trump's moves.
The chamber has warned the president against imposing tariffs because the levies would hurt businesses and consumers on both sides of the border.
In the evening, Ford will co-host a reception at the Library of Congress for premiers and U.S. lawmakers with South Dakota Republican Sen. Kevin Cramer, a key Trump ally who co-chairs the bipartisan American Canadian economy and security caucus.
The Ontarian will make a second visit to the U.S. capital on Feb. 20 to discuss the trade crisis with delegates to the National Governors' Association winter conference.
His two trips are coming in the middle of a snap Feb. 27 election Ford triggered because he wanted a new four-year mandate to "outlive and outlast" the Trump administration.
Revving up for his unusual stateside push, Ford was taking aim at China on Monday.
The PC leader said he would ban Chinese parts from energy projects and prohibit participation by any Chinese state-owned enterprises in critical mineral or infrastructure projects if elected to a third term.
Trump's latest scheme to place 25 per cent tariffs on steel and aluminum - despite a pledge last week to hold off on any levies until March - shows why Ontario must be flexible, Ford told reporters in Oakville after a fundraising breakfast.
"We're going to face this for four years, shifting goalposts constantly and constant chaos," he said.
While he has focused his campaign on fighting tariffs, he suffered a setback a week ago when he was caught on a "hot mic" saying that he "100 per cent" supported Trump to win the November U.S. election over Kamala Harris.
"We're in a battle for the future of our economy," Ford said Monday, emphasizing it's crucial to convince Americans that U.S.-Canada economic co-operation is the best way to meet trade threats from China and put Trump off his talk of making Canada the 51st state.
"I'm going to tell them exactly what I'm telling you, Ontario is taking action to secure our border and delivering results."
Previously planned moves to take U.S. spirits, wines and beers off LCBO shelves and ban American companies from major contracts remain on hold for now, the PC leader said.
"Let's first see what he's going do to," he added, sending a signal south of the border that Canada as a whole has a plan for a "hard" response.
"We are not going to roll over. There's one thing that President Trump has underestimated - the will of the Canadian people," said Ford.
"We never started a fight we don't want. But when someone you know gives you a pop in the nose, they're going to get that pop twice as hard back."
In Toronto, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said the tough talk from Ford is ironic given the Tory's professed admiration for Trump in last week's headline-grabbing gaffe.
Crombie noted it the president campaigned on placing tariffs on Canada and Mexico during in the U.S. election.
Her Liberals are airing television ads they hope will draw widespread public attention to that and eat into Ford's current lead in public opinion polls.
"We're showing him for who he is, someone who wanted a self-serving election," said Crombie, whose campaign has been mostly about improving health care by guaranteeing every Ontarian access to a family doctor.
"You don't see another premier who would think of calling an election at a time of crisis, when we're responding to a tariff threat, and a president who wants to wage a trade war and our premier supports that president," she said.
New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles, who met with steelworkers in Hamilton on Monday morning after highlighting her push for a higher nurse-to-patient ratio in hospitals to improve care, said Ford is exploiting the trade crisis to divert attention from pressing issues on the home front.
"Doug Ford doesn't want to talk about the fact that people in Ontario are losing their family members in hallways in our hospitals because there aren't enough beds and there aren't enough nurses," Stiles told supporters in Port Colborne before taking a shot at Ford's support for Trump.
"Doug Ford has shown us what he's made of," she added. "I don't think we can trust Doug Ford to stand up for Ontarians and to protect all our jobs and defend our workers and our communities."
Along with Green Leader Mike Schreiner, the party chiefs are gearing up for a Friday debate on northern issues in North Bay and another next Monday night in Toronto at the CBC Broadcast Centre.
CAPTION: Ontario Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford appears at a campaign even at BWXT Precision Manufacturing Inc., in Oakville on Feb. 10, 2025.

Document 266 of 500

Dropping off your kids at school on an e-bike? Here's why you might not want to in Ontario

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/dropping-off-your-kids-at-school-on-e-bike-heres/docview/3164933265/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Dropping+off+your+kids+at+school+on+an+e-bike%3F+Here%27s+why+you+might+not+want+to+in+Ontario&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Liam Roach was pedalling his one-year-old daughter home from daycare on his bright orange cargo e-bike when he heard the "whoop whoop" of a police car behind him.
He assumed the officer was trying to pull over a car and moved to the side of the road to let him pass. When the military police officer got out and walked up to his bike, he says, he was confused.
The cop informed him that Ontario law prohibits having a child on an e-bike - something that sounded unbelievable to Roach, considering he had just purchased the purpose-built child-carrying e-bike at a local shop and he was using a certified child seat.
"I was pretty upset about the whole thing," Roach said of the incident last September on the Canadian Forces Base in Kingston.
While he was let off with a warning, Roach had to pull his e-bike's battery out, rendering it a regular bike, in order to ride home legally.
"I don't disagree that there should be some regulations on e-bikes and children, especially maximum speeds and stuff like that.
"But to have a blanket ban where any bicycle with a motor on it isn't allowed to carry children seems a little ridiculous to me, especially when the rest of North America and most of the world is in line with this as a suitable method of transportation."
Last November, Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives overturned a law regulating electric bikes and replaced it with one that bans anyone under 16 from riding an e-bike - even as a passenger.
The ban has actually been in effect since 2006, according to the Ministry of Transportation, when e-bikes were regulated in the same way as scooters. In 2021, the Ford government brought in legislation that created a new category for e-bikes that would allow underage passengers. But the regulations were never proclaimed into law, and the Safer Roads and Communities Act, which received royal assent in November, overrules the previous law and maintains the ban.
And so while bike shops are selling them, parents are buying them, and a growing number of child-carrying e-bikes are rolling on the streets - doing so it illegal.
The province's webpage on e-bike regulations doesn't mention the ban on children.
While February flurries mean bike traffic is currently at a seasonal low, come spring when a growing number of parents pull out their e-bikes and start ferrying their kids between school, daycare and activities, they'll be risking a ticket and a fine of as much as $1,000.
Toronto police could not say if any tickets have been issued. Spokesperson Laura Brabant said she is not aware of any plans for an enforcement campaign.
While other cities and provinces have rebate programs to encourage families to buy e-bikes, Ontario stands out as the only jurisdiction in the world that bans children on e-bikes, says Jamie Stuckless, the former executive director of the cycling advocacy non-profit Share the Road Coalition, who has been following the regulation of e-bikes in Ontario for more than a decade.
"We've been trying to change the e-bike laws in Ontario forever," she said.
The way the law defines e-bikes is very broad and includes larger electric and gasoline-powered scooters that don't need to be pedalled, said Stuckless.
"A decade or so ago, when these rules were made, e-bikes were not as prominent or as well understood, so perhaps it was just an abundance of caution," she said. "But now we see there are a lot of purpose-built e-bikes for safely carrying families."
"It's time to update the law," she said, adding that in the time she's been advocating for e-bikes, she's become a mother and bought an e-bike for her young family. "I don't want people to be getting in trouble for this because tons of parents are riding and bike shops are selling these bikes with child seats and not letting you know that it's illegal (to ride with children)."
The offices of Ford and Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria did not respond to questions about the ban.
Large "cargo" e-bikes are widely available online and in bike shops across the province and can be outfitted with child seats, safety bars and even a canvas rain cover. While some e-bikes operate without pedalling, most have "pedal-assisted" propulsion that uses an electric motor to make it easier to pedal. Popular e-bike brands such as Rad Power Bikes prominently display photos of parents and kids in their promotional material.
Child-carrying e-bikes are the biggest growth segment in the business, says Kevin McLaughlin, who has been selling all kinds of e-bikes out of Zygg, his Roncesvalles-area shop, since 2020.
"This is what people are excited about. People want to be able to take their kids to school and then keep on going to work and pick up groceries on the way home," he said.
But McLaughlin said he had no idea a ban on child passengers was in effect.
"It's very concerning, because people had been told that it was OK and there's been no communication from the government."
Instead of banning kids, the province would do well to start enforcing rules that actually relate to safety, like battery size, maximum speed and certification standards, he said.
During the legislative committee debate on the law, Progressive Conservative member Ric Bresee gave a hint that the government would use the new legislation to properly regulate the chaotic and rapidly growing micromobility market.
"There are a huge number of different styles and structures of these bikes," he said, according to a transcript of the meeting. "We need to make sure that we get that right, as to which ones require licensing, which ones don't etc., the age restrictions - all of that. That move to get those regulations requires that we, I'll say, clear the path for those definitions, and that is exactly what this legislation is doing."
Michael Longfield, the executive director of the bicycle advocacy group CycleTO, flagged the child passenger ban in a letter to the government while the e-bike bill was winding its way through the legislative process.
"I think this perpetuates a myth that kids riding on e-bikes are in some way dangerous, which is quite misleading given that traffic collisions in cars tend to be a much higher percentage of young people's injuries and deaths," he said.
There are years of data from other countries where e-bikes are popular and none of it points to any safety concerns for children, as long as the bikes are properly designed and certified, Toronto emergency room doctor Edward Xie said.
"I see tons of children on e-bikes, but I have not seen any e-bike injuries," he added.
To the contrary, because of the health benefits associated with an active lifestyle, "we should encourage families to use these bikes," said Xie. "The evidence is very clear on this that the health benefits of riding a bike are immense, primarily due to prevention of chronic disease such as diabetes and heart disease."
Opposition Transportation critic Jennifer French said the ban tells you everything you need to know about the Ford government's attitude toward cyclists.
"This is not a government that is interested in hearing from cyclists. I think that they seem to consider them a fringe group," said French, who linked the e-bike rules to the province's plan to rip out bike lanes.
French said parents who came to committee to express their concerns were brushed aside by the Progressive Conservatives.
"It doesn't make any sense that they would go forward with something that creates such havoc when I think the spirit of any e-bike legislation is meant to make things easier," she said.
Kevin Shields and his wife, Ewa, sold their car when they moved to Toronto, and say biking, walking and transit are far better ways of getting around. They take their two kids, ages four and 10, on an e-bike to and from school everyday.
"It is our car. We use it 24/7/365 for everything we do," he said. "The kids love it. They play 'I spy.' They sing. It could be raining or snowing and they're under the cover, totally oblivious."
E-bikes are a great solution for families feeling the squeeze of inflation, says CycleTO's Longfield, with sales as strong as they are, going forward there are going to be more e-bikes with kids around - even if they're not legal for use.
"They're sold at Canadian Tire," he said. "I don't know if people know they are buying a vehicle they're not legally allowed to use.
"Could you imagine buying a car and later finding out you can't drive it?"
CAPTION: Kevin Shields rides an e-bike through the snowy Toronto streets with his daughters.

Subject: Parents & parenting; Families & family life; Bans; Legislation; Children & youth; Electric bicycles; Passengers; Military police; Bicycling

Title: Dropping off your kids at school on an e-bike? Here's why you might not want to in Ontario

Canadian public officials react to Trump's tariffs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/canadian-public-officials-react-trumps-tariffs/docview/3164933199/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canadian+public+officials+react+to+Trump%27s+tariffs&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau did not answer questions from reporters following dinner with French President Emmanuel Macron in Paris about the tariffs and whether Canada would retaliate.
But several prominent Can-
adian public officials and organizations weighed in on the latest chapter in the ongoing saga between Canada and its largest trading partner.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford wrote on the social media platform X that "shifting goalposts and constant chaos" will be hallmarks of Trump's second administration. "This is the next four years," Ford wrote.
Ontario MP Karina Gould, who is running for leadership of the Liberal party of Canada, said that Canada "will respond in kind and let the president know just how much he depends on Canada."
In a post in French, Bloc Québécois leader Yves-François Blanchet wrote that Trump's tariffs would expose U.S. industries to serious inflation and encouraged him to negotiate. Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles wrote that "Ontario's workers are not going to take this lying down."
Catherine Cobden, the president and CEO of the Canadian Steel Producers Association, said in a statement that Canada must "retaliate immediately."
"We are urgently demanding that the Government of Canada act again with resolve and purpose to combat this threat and ensure any measure taken against our sector is met with retaliatory measures and action to offset the devastating impacts tariffs would have on our sector and our workers," Cobden said.
A representative for the Aluminum Association of Canada told the Star that they would not be making a comment as they wait for further clarity from the White House.
Lana Payne, the president of the trade union Unifor, proclaimed on X that this "is what economic warfare looks like."
Trump did not give any indication what Canada would need to do to avoid the tariffs he plans on officially announcing Monday.
Trump imposed steel and aluminum tariffs at 25 and 10 per cent respectively during his first term in March 2018, using national security as justification.
Canada was initially given an exemption, but was ultimately hit by the tariffs on May 31, 2018. Canada responded with a series of counter-tariffs on American products like Florida orange juice.
Nearly a year later, on May 17, 2019, the White House announced a deal had been reached to prevent "surges" in the steel and aluminum supplies from Canada and Mexico, ending the trade dispute.
With files from
The Associated Press and
The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Hayden Godfrey Toronto Star With files from The Associated Press and The Canadian Press

Document 268 of 500

Battle heats up, but campaign trail runs cold: Canvassing in winter brings new challenges for candidates

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/battle-heats-up-campaign-trail-runs-cold/docview/3164932967/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Battle+heats+up%2C+but+campaign+trail+runs+cold%3A+Canvassing+in+winter+brings+new+challenges+for+candidates&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Up in Sudbury, it's -16 C and incumbent MPP France Gélinas layers up with three sets of long johns and handmade wool socks before she even gets to putting on her outfit for the day - one that will be hidden beneath her parka and thermal boots as she trudges through the snow campaigning.
While it's not that cold in Toronto - around -5 C some days, with bitter winds - there, too, candidates are bundled up in big winter coats, tuques and mitts with hand-warmers tucked inside, working in two-hour shifts, sometimes ducking in to canvass highrise buildings to get a break.
"It's cold - it's really cold, to be honest," says incumbent Progressive Conservative MPP and cabinet minister Stan Cho, who is running for re-election in Willowdale, where the tall buildings create a wicked wind tunnel that "slaps you in the face pretty hard."
Cho's mother cooks daily for all his volunteers so when they return to his Yonge Street campaign office for a respite, there are fresh sandwiches, snacks and hot coffee and tea.
"We're saying, if you're tired, come back," said Cho. "Warm up, have a break ... know your limits and wear your layers, and bring those hand-warmers."
Ontarians, who head to the polls Feb. 27, haven't experienced a winter provincial election since 1981. While opposition parties have called the snap election unnecessary and opportunistic, Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford said he needs a strong mandate to counter U.S. President Donald Trump and the threat of tariffs.
But campaigning in the cold is a first for candidates, and because teams are used to much milder weather, they're facing some new challenges. Among the biggest is how to get signs in the frozen ground.
"Bane of my existence," joked one campaign manager.
Sign crews are using drills to break ground, with rebar instead of the usual wooden stakes, but the process takes much longer than usual.
Local businesses Lakefront Graphix Technology and Metal Supermarkets (Etobicoke) have been inundated with orders from hundreds of campaigns.
"We are working 16 hours a day at the moment," said Lisa Herbst of Graphix, who recalls a winter 2006 federal election when they sent out drill bits with signs.
Up in the Sudbury riding of Nickel Belt, Gélinas - a veteran New Democrat first elected in 2007 - says that's not an option, so they instead prop up signs in the snow.
"You can put a sign in a snow bank," said Gélinas, who got her first taste of winter campaigning as a volunteer during the 2006 federal election.
"We can't all carry shovels, so we pack the snow to put the sign in," she added. But if it snows again, she has teams who fan out over the sprawling riding.
"You have to redo all your signs because the snowplow goes by, and either takes them away or buries them."
Cho's volunteers "go out with a drill, but they also go with a pretty big hammer," he said. "So it's kind of like a MacGyver solution. Most of the team doesn't know who MacGyver is, but they're doing their best and calling audibles whenever necessary."
Sean Conway was a Liberal MPP and cabinet minister who ran in the 1981 provincial election, and doesn't recall it being as cold as this winter.
"The campaign was launched about the 2nd or 3rd of February, and I remember thinking, 'Oh God, this is going to be a winter campaign and the weather will be ...' well, guess what the weather was for candidates? The only big problem we had with that campaign was fog" in his Pembroke-area riding.
"It was an unusually mild February and March, and I just remember waiting for the really cold weather to come, and it never did," said Conway, who lives in Barry's Bay. "I was able to do a lot more canvassing than I thought I was going to be able to do with my various teams."
This election, going door to door in frigid weather is teaching valuable campaign lessons, Gélinas said. "The first thing? If you want people to vote for you, don't bring a whole bunch of dirt and snow with your boots" into their front foyer.
"People don't like to clean up after a candidate who makes a big mess," she said. "And thing No. 2 is that talking to them at the door has to be extremely brief, because keeping your door open when it's -16 C with the wind is not a good way to make a friend either."
For Cho, the Korean saying gosaeng is fitting in this election, and some voters have uttered the term to him at the door. It means suffering, he explained, "but in Korean, it's not an insult, it's actually a compliment - you're suffering for something you believe in, a cause. That's my mentality."
Vince Gasparro, the Liberal candidate in Eglinton-Lawrence, said voters seem to appreciate candidates hustling.
He's been out with his team since being nominated last October, noting "this weather now, it's a little more intense."
But, he added, "it's been interesting - the reception at the door, people are impressed that you have a political candidate who's out there trying to earn their support when it's freezing cold and snowing."
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters-editor/docview/3164932439/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters+to+the+editor%3A+Doug+Ford+says+a+%27clear+mandate%27+from+Ontario+voters+is+the+best+way+to+deal+with+Donald+Trump%2C+Jan.+22+Doug+Ford+promises+to+axe+tolls+on+eastern+portion+of+Highway+407%2C+Feb.+5+Doug+Ford+overheard+saying+he+was+happy+Donald+Trump+became+president+-+%27then+the+guy+pulled+out+the+knife%2C+Feb.+4%27+It%27s+wartime.+Do+without.+Shop+locally%2C+Feb.+5+Donald+Trump+postpones+threatened+tariffs+for+30+days+after+Canada+offers+more+border+security+measures%2C+Feb.+4+Amazon+Canada+says+it+will+close+all+seven+of+its+warehouses+in+Quebec%2C+Jan.+24&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Citizens get
the government they deserve
Doug Ford says a 'clear mandate' from Ontario voters is the best way to deal with Donald Trump, Jan. 22
If you are paying one bit of attention to the gutting of democracy in the United States, understand one thing: people get the government they deserve. Through a lack of attention to events, warnings, and simply not voting, the American people have that very government. We have no reason to be smug here in Ontario. As Premier Doug Ford's spin doctors turn incompetence - the blatant waste of our tax dollars and the constant lack of real attention to housing, education and health care - into high ratings it is clear that we now have the government we deserve. And, by not voting in this election, held in the coldest month of the year, we would submit to his plan of voter suppression. I fear that's what we're about to do as most people are not paying attention and simply will not vote. If you are reading this, speak to friends and family who are unaware by their own choice. The collective shame is on us. Wake up, Ontario.
David Ottenbrite,
Cambridge, Ont.
Therme spa
a similar mistake to Hwy. 407 sale
Doug Ford promises to axe tolls on eastern portion of Highway 407, Feb. 5
Sad and hilarious, Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford says "It was a big mistake" for former PC premier Mike Harris to sign a 99-year lease with a private consortium. Then why did Ford sign a 99-year lease with a private foreign company, Therme, to build and operate something-or-other on our public land at Ontario Place? Just another big mistake, I guess.
Jay Butler, Etobicoke
Vote beyond
hats and slogans
Doug Ford overheard saying he was happy Donald Trump became president - 'then the guy pulled out the knife, Feb. 4'
The people of Ontario deserve something better than hats, slogans and duplicity from their premier. Doug Ford has never been Captain Canada, has always been a Trump supporter and will be instrumental in electing Pierre Poilievre, a protege of Stephen Harper who is currently the chair of IDU, a global network of right and far-right political parties, with strong ties to the U.S. Republican Party. That's what this election is really about.
Kat Duffy, Mississauga
Do you want
four more years of Doug Ford?
It's wartime. Do without.
Shop locally, Feb. 5
Heather Mallick got it right. We should not vote for Doug Ford. He can project an aura of strength, like Donald Trump, but he too is a man of moral vacuity. Once the tariff crisis dies down, as it will, we don't want to be stuck with four more years of sleazy deals and ill-conceived policies. Do we even think that Trump will grant Ford a face-to-face audience so that Ford can flash his bona fides as a scrapper? Mallick is also correct in her assessment of Pierre Poilievre as a narrow-minded, spiteful man. As we try to draw together as Team Canada to face the felon to the south, Poilievre snipes from the sidelines, trying to gain political advantage. Whose side is he on, anyway?
Isobel Raven, Toronto
A national emergency
Donald Trump postpones threatened tariffs for 30 days after Canada offers more border security measures, Feb. 4
The bullying behaviour of U.S. President Donald Trump and, indeed, the U.S. federal government, must be recognized for what it is: economic terrorism. When Trump enacts 25 per cent tariffs to coerce our annexation, he declares economic war on our country. Our federal government will have no choice but to invoke the Emergencies Act. Certainly, the tariffs and coercion fall within the act's definition of a public order emergency " ... that arises from threats to the security of Canada that are so serious as to be a national emergency; ... that seriously endangers the health and safety of Canadians; ... that seriously threatens the ability of the Government of Canada to preserve the sovereignty, security and territorial integrity of Canada." This will give our government the real tools it needs to effectively stand up to Trump, beyond futile appeasement and counter-tariffs. Embargoing oil, electricity, water, aluminum, steel and rare earth minerals, and expropriating U.S. assets here, should get his attention.
Peter Madott, Mississauga
Amazon leaving Quebec is all about greed
Amazon Canada says it will close all seven of its warehouses in Quebec, Jan. 24
The constant attacks on collective bargaining need to stop. Canada was founded on the backs of workers and Sec. 2(d) of the Charter of Rights and Freedoms says we have the right to freedom of association. A right ... not a wish or a suggestion, a right workers have fought for long and hard. We see employers trampling over these rights all the time and it has to stop.
In Quebec, Amazon workers looked forward to unionizing but what did Amazon do - 1,950 employees will soon be out of a job. Amazon says it has nothing to do with unions. I call B.S. It has everything to do with unions and corporate greed. Amazon had a projected profit of more than $40 billion in 2024. It can afford to pay its workers fairly; it doesn't seem to want to.
In April 2024, the first union was formed in Laval and represented 200 employees. Amazon took them to court to contest the decision. Amazon was told they must stop interfering with union affairs by an Administrative Labour Tribunal judge in August and was given a $30,000 fine. Seriously? What deterrent is that? These fines are now a small cost of doing business instead of the economic penalty they were designed to be. It happens time and time again. Massive multinational corporations come with big pocketbooks and trample over workers' rights with no consequences. Union busting must stop.
Tasha Salesse, Saint John, N.B.
Governments should harness ideas in letters
It always amazes me when I read the letters to the editor. The insight, practicality and intelligent solutions addressing the ills of the world can be found within these letters. Forget convening special assemblies of politicians. All levels of government should use the incredible ideas of its informed citizenry. Dub them the Citizens Forum.
Johana Kelly, Mississauga
CAPTION:

'This is the next four years': Canadian officials react to Donald Trump's steel and aluminum tariff threats

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/this-is-next-four-years-canadian-officials-react/docview/3165178411/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27This+is+the+next+four+years%27%3A+Canadian+officials+react+to+Donald+Trump%27s+steel+and+aluminum+tariff+threats&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Just hours before the Super Bowl kicked off in New Orleans, U.S. President Donald Trump told reporters aboard Air Force One that he intended to announce 25 per cent tariffs on all steel and aluminum imports on Monday, including for Canada and Mexico.
Following dinner with French President Emmanuel Macron in Paris, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau did not answer questions from reporters about the tariffs and whether Canada would retaliate.
Ontario Progressive Conservative leader Doug Ford wrote on the social media platform X that "shifting goalposts and constant chaos" will be hallmarks of Trump's second administration.
"This is the next four years," Ford wrote.
In a post in French, Bloc Québécois leader Yves-François Blanchet wrote that Trump's tariffs would expose U.S. industries to serious inflation and encouraged him to negotiate.
Trump imposed steel and aluminum tariffs at 25 and 10 per cent respectively during his first term in March 2018, using national security as justification.
Canada was initially given an exemption to these duties, but was ultimately hit by the tariffs on May 31, 2018. Canada responded with a series of counter-tariffs on American products like Florida orange juice.
Nearly a year later, on May 17, 2019, the White House announced a deal had been reached to prevent "surges" in the steel and aluminum supplies from Canada and Mexico, ending the trade dispute.
In an interview with Fox News on Sunday, Trump reiterated his desire to make Canada part of the U.S.
"I think Canada would be much better off being the 51st state because we lose $200 billion a year with Canada," Trump told Fox's Bret Baier in a pretaped interview from Florida.
With files from The Associated Press and The Canadian Press.
More to come.
CAPTION: Canadian officials react to Trump's announcement of steel and aluminum tariffs
