Document 271 of 500

Pierre Poilievre was riding a Trump-like wave. Is it about to drown him?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/pierre-poilievre-was-riding-trump-like-wave-is/docview/3164813689/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pierre+Poilievre+was+riding+a+Trump-like+wave.+Is+it+about+to+drown+him%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In a recurring feature, Susan Delacourt, a small-l liberal, and Matt Gurney, a small-c conservative, bring their different perspectives - and shared commitment to civil disagreement - to the political debates of the moment.
Susan Delacourt: Donald Trump is no friend to progressives in Canada, to state the obvious. But I'm sure I'm not the only person wondering these days how much of a pal he is to Canadian conservatives, both provincially and federally.
Trump's 30-day reprieve of tariffs on Canadian imports kind of pulled the rug out from Doug Ford's whole provincial election pitch to Ontarians, who will now vote on Feb. 27 without any clue on what Trump will do next. Meanwhile, polls are showing that Pierre Poilievre and the federal Conservatives are taking a hit in popularity as Canadians shift their attention to who's best to deal with the havoc Trump is wreaking. So my question to you, Matt: is it time for Poilievre's team to get nervous?
Matt Gurney: No, except to the background extent of how nervous the rest of us are and should be about the total crap show that is the world today.
It's clear that Ford's plan was upended a bit by the tariff delay and polling also shows that Ontarians are convinced that Ford called the election for selfish reasons - but the polls also show Ford is cruising to a big majority anyway. Likewise, polls have suggested (admittedly, with some pretty spectacular divergence) that the federal race has tightened, but most still show a commanding Conservative lead. The race has tightened from a historic Conservative blowout to merely an unusually large Conservative win.
The Conservatives themselves are adjusting their strategy - belatedly, I think, but all the same - and they haven't really begun to campaign against Mark Carney yet, who's mostly keeping a low profile. I definitely think the time may come for them to get nervous, but I don't think that time is now, and I don't think they are yet. After all, dropping Justin Trudeau for Carney isn't exactly something they haven't had some time to muse about and prepare for.
SD: I disagree (which is good, because that's what we're supposed to be doing here). To me, the Conservatives look entirely unprepared for Carney. Changing their slogans to the "Carney-Trudeau Liberals" looks like a communications strategy built from Popsicle sticks and Elmer's glue.
MG: You're not the target audience.
SD: I'll grant you that. But Poilievre needs Liberals who have drifted. I'm wary, as you know, of casting this Liberal leadership race as a coronation for Carney; I think all of the candidates deserve a fair hearing. But one of the things that Liberals have lacked over the past few years is what the pros like to call a "narrative" - a story to tell about why Canada needs a middle-ground party. Trump's bullying is giving them a story again.
MG: There's always been a story. What the Liberals lacked was the means to tell it, since their ability to communicate had collapsed into a void of incompetence, as had their ability to execute on their policy agenda - and, weirdly, their ability to perceive electoral facts and react accordingly, especially as pertains to an outgoing prime minister's job performance. I don't see any evidence here of anything beyond the Liberals hitting rock bottom and bouncing up a bit now that they're (almost) free of the prime minister, who should have either jumped or been shoved into retirement 18 months ago. There's been no campaign yet, no defining issues (although we can clearly take a good guess at what one will likely be). Conservatives should be wary and alert, and I think they are. But nervous? No. Maybe later. But not yet.
SD: So we'll put you down as a "maybe" on the Liberals' return from the dead?
MG: Ha. Well, that would be accurate. There's definitely a scenario in which the Conservative party snatches defeat from the jaws of victory, and every single prediction I'd make these days comes with a Donald Trump-sized asterisk. So yes, put me down as a maybe on that. But more relevant to the point, put me down as skeptical that the Conservatives are nervous, because something you said here is worth zooming in on. You said Poilievre needs Liberals who've drifted. He might. He'd certainly need them to win some seats. But I think the lesson for us all in recent years is that elections can be won by motivating new voters. Poilievre has been doing that. Some Toronto Liberals getting Carney fever may not do much to offset that.
SD: I will say this: this upsurge in Canadian patriotism is a wild card. Conservatives have been very good in the past about wrapping themselves in the flag - and I'm not talking about the F--- Trudeau one. I can see ways in which Poilievre can capitalize on that, although he'll need to be a bit more careful about his "Canada is broken" line if he doesn't want to be seen as agreeing with Trump. I already see signs that he's toning that down a bit.
MG: I do, too. The smart play for him will be to keep hammering on that, but with a more optimistic and urgent message: "It's time to fix what's broken and make Canada stronger." Something like that. I think there is an appetite out there for some real change, and that's why Carney is trying to present himself as an outsider. I have doubts it'll work.
SD: The fact that Trump and Carney are forcing the Conservatives to pivot reinforces to me that it is time for them to be nervous - or at least go back to the drawing board on all the slogans that worked so well over the last couple of years.
MG: I hope we get a real campaign, with real debates over real issues. I think we'll get different slogans and Carney ducking the media as much as possible while the Conservatives keep the Twitter war machine running all the time. In other words, we'll get the same strategy with slightly new tactics. It's not what the country needs, but I think it's what's coming.
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre speaks during a news conference in Vancouver on Feb. 5, 2025.

Doctor orders: All provincial parties promise access to care for all Ontarians in coming years

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doctor-orders-all-provincial-parties-promise/docview/3164813686/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doctor+orders%3A+All+provincial+parties+promise+access+to+care+for+all+Ontarians+in+coming+years&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The doctor will soon be in.
The three main political parties have released their plans to secure a family doctor for all Ontarians by 2029, spending up to $4 billion to do so.
The province has estimated some two million people in the province don't have a doctor - though other estimates peg that much higher, at 2.5 million and rising to 4.4 million by 2026.
Here's what the parties are proposing:
 PCs: Before the election, the PC government had promised $1.8 billion over four years to get two million Ontarians rostered with a family doctor or nurse practitioner by 2029. The Tories say about 90 per cent of Ontarians already have access to primary health care. They had also previously named Dr. Jane Philpott, who authored a book on primary care homes - with integrated teams of doctors, nurse practitioners and other specialized services working in one location, serving patients based on their postal code - to help reform the health care system.
 NDP: The New Democrats are promising to connect every Ontarian to a family doctor or nurse practitioner with a total price tag of $4.05 billion over four years, based on 2.5 million needing a doctor. They say they'd bring in 3,500 new doctors and reduce paperwork loads to give physicians more time to spend with patients, and would focus on integrated teams. The NDP is also planning to "clear the path" for 13,000 internationally trained doctors who are now unable to practise in the province, and boost the number of residency spots in existing medical schools.
 Liberals: Would spend $3 billion over four years to provide a family doctor to everyone who needs one - hiring about 3,100 physicians - while creating two new medical schools and increasing spots in existing med schools as well as residency spots, doubling capacity. A $250-million fund would help to retain doctors who have left their practice or are close to retirement. Team-based care would lead to evening and weekend availability. Liberals would also allow 1,200 foreign trained doctors to train and practice in the province.
CAPTION: Tories say about 90 per cent of Ontarians already have access to primary health care, but all three parties say they'll improve it.

Document 273 of 500

Donald Trump is proving that the power of politics is a 'knife fight,' says Bob Rae. Here's why

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/donald-trump-is-proving-that-power-politics-knife/docview/3164789293/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Donald+Trump+is+proving+that+the+power+of+politics+is+a+%27knife+fight%2C%27+says+Bob+Rae.+Here%27s+why&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's not just about Donald Trump.
It's not just about America.
Bob Rae sees the political tensions up close living in New York. And he deals with the diplomatic tensions working on the global stage as Canada's ambassador to the United Nations.
But he also worries about the political fallout when he's back home in Canada - not least the anti-American reaction taking root here.
"I think we make a mistake when we say it's just one person," Rae told Friday's Democracy Forum that I hosted at Toronto Metropolitan University.
The problem isn't just the American presidency but U.S. democracy - and democracy at large.
When democracies are in trouble, the whole world is in trouble. If neighbouring voters can't get along in their own countries, it's hardly surprising that neighbouring countries aren't getting along on this continent and around the globe.
Trump personifies the problem. But he also embodies the people who voted for him in America, just as people keep voting for chauvinists and populists in democracies around the world.
"We need to understand the kind of tensions ... what's happening to public opinion in the United States ... that this is reflected in our own country, too."
Democracy is not a panacea for the problems that preoccupy voters.
"This is a forum on democracy," he reminded our audience. "One of the key tensions on democracy is, don't assume that the majority of people are always open-minded, generous and internationally sensitive in their responses to what's going on in the world. It's not the case."
Self-interest and the national interest, nationalism and internationalism are often at cross-purposes. Democracies have always struggled with these contradictions, but the miscalculations are more costly today.
"That's why I think we have to make a much stronger argument with everyone - not speaking down to people and not wagging our fingers at them but just saying: 'It's actually in our interest to express greater solidarity with the rest of the world. It's not a win-lose proposition, it actually ... makes us more secure, actually helps us deal with our problems.'"
The most powerful countries have always alternated between expansionism and isolationism, between chauvinism and co-operation. Without guardrails and ground rules to keep everyone on track, at home and abroad, countries will keep going off course - domestically and internationally.
"It's the same challenge that we've always had, and it's just ... we have not had an American president who had such a nationalist and expansionist view of American power in office in my lifetime," Rae argued.
"It's a challenge between those people who say, 'Politics is about power - it's not about the rule of law, it's about the rule of men and it's a knife fight ... or the Canadian perspective, which is, 'We value laws, we do value rules, we do value the relationships that we establish.'
"Not everything is a transaction. Some things are relationships."
Rae knows whereof he speaks. His role as UN ambassador is the culmination of a career in politics and public service - he was Ontario's 21st premier and later served as a roving troubleshooter and Canadian envoy on humanitarian and refugee issues.
He knows more than most that public opinion can be fickle. He catapulted to power in 1990 after then-premier David Peterson called an early election to lock in his lead in public opinion polls - not unlike the snap election Ontario is now immersed in; Rae's NDP government went down to defeat in 1995 amid high deficit spending (not unlike today).
While his political views have changed (Rae later joined the federal Liberals and served as interim leader from 2011-13) his views on defence spending haven't.
Long before the Americans ramped up the pressure on defence, "I have been a consistent advocate of understanding that defence, development and diplomacy go together."
Yet in a volatile world of wars - Russia versus Ukraine, Sudanese against Sudanese, Hamas against Israel - politics remains the only possible solution. Rae was "profoundly saddened by and outraged" by the bloodshed and bloody-mindedness in the Middle East - the Hamas massacre of Oct. 7 and the Israeli overkill in Gaza that followed.
"We have to begin to turn towards the process of creating political agency for the Palestinians" - which means self-determination - while "the Israelis have to be assured that they're going to be living in security."
That means understanding the fears and hopes of public opinion in both polities in the Middle East. In the same way, politicians must manage public opinion in Canada and the U.S. amid the latest tensions over Trump's tariff talk.
"Politics is not about counting heads, politics is about turning heads," Rae told the Democracy Forum. "It's about convincing people."
It's also about keeping our heads when our blood is boiling.
"I don't have an anti-American bone in my body," he says diplomatically but also truthfully, having lived as a boy in Washington (where his father was a Canadian diplomat) and now for the past five years in New York.
"I really don't."
(Disclosure: I'm a Senior Fellow at The Dais at Toronto Metropolitan University, which supports the TMU Democracy Forum.)
CAPTION: In a time of rising conflict and democratic retreat, the United Nations is under renewed stress. Ambassador Bob Rae returns to the TMU Democracy Forum to talk about Canada's place in the world of diplomacy amid the new global disorder. (Feb. 7, 2025)Star columnist Martin Regg Cohn talks with Bob Rae, Canada's ambassador to the UN, on Friday at the Toronto Metropolitan University Democracy Forum.

Document 274 of 500

Don't let Ford off the hook

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/dont-let-ford-off-hook/docview/3164789265/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Don%27t+let+Ford+off+the+hook&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If you want to know if Doug Ford's decision to issue writs for an early provincial election was unnecessary, opportunistic and possibly even dangerous, you are probably also wondering why he would make such a move. The "why" may be easily explained away as a nakedly ambitious move to remain in power, but there is much more at stake than Ford winning another term as Ontario's premier.
A year ago, Ford hedged his bets. His preferred opponent was going to be Prime Minister Justin Trudeau. By planning an election before the feds went to the polls, Ford was not only going to ride the tailwinds of a wildly unpopular federal Liberal party, but he would also avoid having to run against federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre who, should he form the next federal government, would undoubtedly be less popular the day after the win, potentially casting a shadow on PC fortunes across the board.
With Trudeau's surprise withdrawal, not only did Ford lose his preferred opponent but he faced the prospect of a reinvigorated Liberal party - especially if they choose a new leader with stronger centrist and management credentials.
But luck was on Ford's side when Trudeau's exit created a federal leadership vacuum - with no prospect of resolution before the Liberal leadership vote on March 9.
Amid these shifting sands, the astonishing triumph of U.S. President Donald Trump and his threat of imposing economically crippling tariffs on Canada handed Ford yet another exploitable opportunity.
The turmoil has served up a cynical, but likely effective, campaign strategy that lets Ford wear a defiant "Canada Is Not For Sale" baseball cap and assume the role of Captain Canada against another villain who is not on the Ontario ballot.
There is no doubt that Ford's early election call serves little purpose other than his own quest to retain power - all at a massive financial cost to taxpayers. Just the price tag of the election itself is staggering, at between $150 million and $175 million, but when you add in the cost of the expedited beer and wine distribution program ($612 million), the so-called affordability and car licence plate rebates ($3 billion and $1 billion a year, respectively), to name a few items on the roster, this "opportunity" accrues benefit to no one other than Ford himself.
Perhaps in 2025 we've become numb to the practice of politicians putting their thumb on the scale of election fortune to their own advantage. But we should not view Ford's machinations as just another day in the life of our politicians. This move has potentially damaging consequences for Canada as a whole.
By choosing to run against Trump in this election, the central thrust of Ford's campaign will be to demonstrate that he is best able to defend Ontario's interests against a foreign adversary. Given the cynical opportunism that is driving this strategy, it is not much of a leap to foresee that what helps him win re-election will continue to be used, even at the expense of other parts of the country.
For example, if he unilaterally threatens to ban or tax Ontario's electricity exports to the U.S., how will that impact Quebec and Alberta whose economies rely on energy exports? More broadly speaking, Canada's strength as a country is rooted in her provinces and their respective resources working together and acting as one voice to advance both our individual and collective interests.
Ontario has a proud and historic role as a unifier in Confederation. One lone premier taking advantage of a confluence of events to retain power presents an imminent threat to federalism, potentially dividing the country when it has never been more important to strengthen the ties that bind and form a co-ordinated bulwark against an unprecedented threat to the well-being of our citizens.
Allan Gregg is an opinion researcher, a principal at Earnscliffe Strategies and the co-host of the Red-Tory podcast.
CREDIT: Allan R. Gregg Special to the Star;

Subject: Provincial elections; Political campaigns; Leadership

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

Location: United States--US; Canada

Title: Don't let Ford off the hook

Document 275 of 500

A new prescription for primary care: Kingston's 'health home' has attracted physicians and taken thousands off the doctor wait-list

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/new-prescription-primary-care/docview/3164789232/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+new+prescription+for+primary+care%3A+Kingston%27s+%27health+home%27+has+attracted+physicians+and+taken+thousands+off+the+doctor+wait-list&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The sprawling second-floor office, located above a pharmacy on the city's main street, at one time was home to eight doctors serving the busy neighbourhood. But the physicians, with large patient rosters, were burning out and six were ready to retire, but couldn't find anyone willing to take over the practice.
Two years ago, they all left, the clinic shuttered and 8,000 patients suddenly found themselves adrift - joining about two million others across the province with no access to a family doctor.
It's not a problem unique to Kingston - many communities across the province are grappling with a lack of primary care - and it has emerged as a key issue in Ontario's Feb. 27 election. The opposition parties have hammered the PCs for being in power for seven years as the situation they inherited worsened, which even the government acknowledged has taken too long to fix and left too many without a family doctor.
But what has happened here since that clinic closed is a new model that has been able to attract physicians and taken thousands off the wait-list, a model that appears to have the support of the PCs, Liberals and NDP.
That same Princess Street office reopened last summer as a "primary care home," as proposed by Dr. Jane Philpott, a former federal Liberal health minister and dean of the medical school at Queen's recently hired by the PC government to help fix the province's health problems.
Her vision, outlined in her 2024 book "Health for All" - which she calls the periwinkle model - guarantees primary care for everyone based on their postal code, much like families being assigned a local school based on their address.
Two days before the provincial election campaign kicked off last week, the government and Philpott announced funding with a promise of primary care for all Ontarians by 2029, a total of $1.8 billion for every Ontarian to have a family doctor or primary care team within four years.
The government, however, says two million Ontarians need a physician, which is based on work by organizations like the Canadian Institute for Health Information, but the Ontario Medical Association says the number is 2.5 million and could rise to 4.4 million by 2026.
Last fall, Philpott spoke at the Liberal policy convention, and Leader Bonnie Crombie announced Thursday that if elected, she would spend $3 billion over four years to connect everyone with a family doctor. On Friday, NDP Leader Marit Stiles unveiled a $4.05-billion, four-year plan to get all Ontarians access to a doctor or nurse practitioner, pledging more family health teams.
Philpott's model, based on similar ones in the Netherlands and the United Kingdom, ideally provide patients access to care seven days a week, taking pressure of emergency rooms and steering them away from walk-in clinics. They are treated by practitioners they know, and who know their histories; medical records are in one place, and once a full network of sites are in place, no one would worry about finding a family doctor if they move.
The Midtown Kingston Health Home opened last July, two years after a proposal by various health-care partners in the city obtained funding from the provincial government.
At the health home, each family doctor works in a team with a nurse practitioner, sharing "provider pod" offices, with a host of in-demand services on site - diabetes care, COPD care, social worker, family health educator - mostly for those within the K7M postal code. Community clinics for well baby care, sexual health and quick STI testing are open to all. Practical assistance workers help patients who might need help co-ordinating insulin injections for their child while at school, or finding housing, or applying for a birth certificate.
It's hoping to secure funding to add occupational therapists, physiotherapists, mental health case management and footcare, to make it more of a one-stop community hub for patients.
"The doctors, they were asking me all kinds of questions about my history and this and that, and I was just blown away, because my (previous) doctor would not engage with anything you would have asked him, and I always felt rushed," said lifelong Kingston resident Mario Borsato, 56, now a patient of the health home.
He and several of his family members had sought care at the original clinic for 20 years, and found themselves without a doctor for two years, so he knows the struggles of those who are still looking.
"If you don't have your health, you don't have nothing," he added. "And our family care (in the province) has gone down the tubes."
About 4,400 residents in the K7M postal code were on the wait-list through Health Care Connect, but it is estimated the actual need is double that. So far, the health home has rostered 1,418 patients and - and treated another 1,535 through its various clinics - and expects to have 4,000 patients signed up by the end of March and 8,000 patients by March 2026.
Winn Studsrud, a patient of the original clinic, was directed to the health home after seeking help with a prescription, but it wasn't until later that she realized she'd been taken on by a family doctor there, even though she doesn't live in the postal code.
"I freaking started crying," she said. "I couldn't stop smiling, actually. And I just kept saying 'thank you Lord, thank you Lord.' So honestly, it's like a miracle."
Dr. Mary Rowland, clinical lead, calls the health home "a really nice blend" and likens it to a secondary school, where "this school has the IB program, this school has French Immersion. We have a catchment, and we have these specialized programs for more supports for particular populations."
Dr. Eileen Nicolle, who moved with her family from Toronto to Kingston to be a part of the health home, said "you can provide the most holistic care and also take the time you need to see people ... I think if you take a more long-term approach, there's very much a cost saving and also a time saving because those problems that you don't deal with right up front, they come back."
Currently, the clinic is open five days a week with evening clinic hours, and has managed to attract five physicians so far, with one to come, plus locums, as well as three nurse practitioners.
Staff are on salary, have flexible hours and a team-centred approach. Doctors like it because they don't have to run a business as they would setting up their own practice. They work more reasonable hours, and can leave when they want, "but they don't" because it's such an attractive set up, said Meghan O'Leary, director of clinical services for Kingston Community Health Centres.
Nurse practitioner Edward Cho was the first hire, and saw the patient roster grow - though initially there was some upset as those from the old clinic weren't necessarily able to re-register.
Cho values being paired with a doctor, because "there are more opportunities for patients to get followups" in a timely manner.
"I'm not worried about my patients" when away, he added. "I always know they're covered. If there are urgent issues, I know they're well taken care of ... having two eyes, rather than one, is always a good thing."
Personally, he said the way the interdisciplinary model works gives him the "perfect work-life balance. I'm not stressed. I go home knowing that my inbox is taken care of, and I won't come back to 10 million messages. I finish all my charting on time."
For Nicolle, working with a male nurse practitioner gives patients choice. "Maybe they want to see a female for their Pap smear. Or maybe the men would rather see Ed for certain things. So it gives you a bit of diversity within the practice."
Research on team-based care has been shown to benefit patients' health and means a drop in hospitalizations and visits to the ER. Patients report higher levels of satisfaction and care, and health-care providers report higher job satisfaction and are less likely to burn out, says Jennifer Rayner, director of policy and research at the Alliance for Healthier Communities.
Raisa Deber, professor emeritus at the Institute of Health Policy, Management and Evaluation at the University of Toronto, said there are a number of team-based models in the province, and there must be flexibility in the system because what works in a large, urban centre may not in a rural or remote area where it might be difficult to get additional services.
Dr. Tara Kiran, family physician and researcher at St. Michael's Hospital and the University of Toronto, works in a family health team that includes nurses, nurse practitioners, social workers, pharmacists and others, focusing on a vulnerable population.
When more than one in five people in Canada don't have access to any primary care, with services varying by region, "we need to do things differently to try and even out the way services are provided ... so that we don't have the have and have-not, but rather, everybody has something."
The periwinkle model does address that and needs to be evaluated to see if it does attract doctors and is able to better clear wait-lists.
"There is a bit of an experiment here, and we do need to learn from it," Kiran added.
The Kingston model is part of a wider network that has a community board of governors, which is key, said Kate Mulligan, a professor at the Dalla Lana School of Public Health at the University of Toronto.
"If we're going to go to all the trouble of doing team-based primary care, which is the right next step, we shouldn't miss the opportunity to leverage the huge capacity that's there, to really connect that into our health care system," Mulligan said.
CAPTION: The Midtown Kingston Health Home opened last July, two years after a proposal by various health-care partners in the city obtained funding from the provincial government. The care model appears to have the support of the PCs, Liberals and NDP. Nurse practitioner Edward Cho and clinical lead Dr. Mary Rowland. Cho was the first hire for the health home and values being paired with a doctor, because "there are more opportunities for patients to get followups" in a timely manner. Former health minister Dr. Jane Philpott's primary care model, based on similar ones in the Netherlands and the U.K., ideally provide patients access to care seven days a week, taking pressure of emergency rooms and steering them away from walk-in clinics.Jilly MacIver photos for the Toronto StarChris YoungTHE CANADIAN PRESS file photo
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Document 276 of 500

the family doctor prescription: Orillia has a chronic shortage of family physicians. The city thinks it's found a cure

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/family-doctor-prescription/docview/3164789186/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=the+family+doctor+prescription%3A+Orillia+has+a+chronic+shortage+of+family+physicians.+The+city+thinks+it%27s+found+a+cure&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Standing on doorsteps or chatting outside local shops, Don McIsaac put the same question to hundreds of people during his 2022 campaign to be mayor of Orillia.
After introducing himself with a handshake, he'd ask: "What sort of problems do you have?"
Some people complained about rising taxes. Others worried about soaring rents. Many people - more than McIsaac expected - said they did not have a family doctor.
"That was the No. 1 thing they said they needed help with," McIsaac said. "Just finding a physician. That's what our residents want."
About 12,000 people in Orillia - one-third of the population of the city two hours north of Toronto - don't have a family doctor. It wants to change that by becoming the first municipality in Ontario to hire family doctors and pay them as municipal employees.
Though the plan is in its early stages, McIsaac said the city is prepared to take on the startup costs and administrative burden of running a family practice, which physician advocacy groups say are among the barriers preventing more doctors from entering family medicine.
If the proposal is greenlighted in the spring, the goal is for the city to hire eight to 12 new family doctors. McIsaac said that will connect everyone in Orillia to a physician within a year.
"I can't start a medical school," he said. "I can't treat patients. But we can put skin in the game and hire doctors."
More than 2.5 million Ontarians do not have a family physician. That number is expected to rise to 4.4 million in the next few years as the population grows, more physicians leave the profession and fewer doctors choose to pursue the specialty.
Last month, the crisis made headlines after hundreds of people in Walkerton lined up for hours in the cold and snow for a chance to sign up with a physician. Health-care leaders said the striking scene was proof that people understand how critical it is to have a family doctor and should signal to governments that they must ensure access to primary care.
Days before Premier Doug Ford triggered the snap Feb. 27 election, the Progressive Conservative government announced $1.4 billion in new funding to connect every Ontarian with a family doctor by 2029. Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie has also pledged to fix the doctor shortage with $3.1 billion in spending. The New Democratic Party on Friday released its Family Health Guarantee that includes recruiting and supporting 3,500 new doctors and will cost $4.05 billion over four years.
McIsaac said Orillia's residents don't want to wait years for a family physician.
"Twelve thousand people in our city don't have primary care. That's not acceptable."
Melanie Delion, who works as a community physicianrecruiter with Orillia & Lake Country Physician Recruitment, said the area's doctor shortage extends beyond city limits. The Couchiching region, which includes Orillia and surrounding townships, has a population of about 100,000, and Delion said roughly one-quarter of those people - 25,000 to 28,000 residents - are not attached to primary care.
Compounding the crisis, one-fifth of the region's 43 doctors who practise comprehensive family medicine are nearing retirement age, she said. So the area needs to recruit physicians both for the unattached patient population and for the roughly 8,500 people whose doctors are over age 60.
"Right now, we're estimating a need for about 15 to 17 family doctors for our communities," Delion said.
Longtime Orillia Coun. Ralph Cipolla said McIsaac approached him last year with the idea to recruit family doctors and hire them as municipal employees. A working group is investigating the feasibility of a city-operated medical clinic, with Orillia's council last month approving $500,000 in initial funding. The group has been directed to consult health-care organizations and report to council in late March.
Cipolla said in addition to paying the physicians' salaries and providing health benefits, the city would set up their medical offices, likely on the second floor of city hall, currently vacant. The city would hire and pay for support staff and an office manager to oversee operations.
As in typical family practices, doctors would bill OHIP directly for providing medical services to patients. Cipolla said the city would recoup the costs from the physicians, using those funds to pay the doctors' salaries and fund the operation of the clinic.
"We don't think it will be a burden on our taxpayers," he said, adding that Orillia council "owes it to our residents and to our families" to investigate the possibility of a city-operated clinic.
McIsaac said he's been told no other Ontario municipality has hired family doctors to work in a city-run clinic. Instead, he's looking to Colwood.
Colwood (pop. 19,000), a city on Vancouver Island west of Victoria, announced in December that it had hired the first family physician for its new municipal clinic. The city, which will manage the clinic, plans to recruit and employ more family doctors, who will receive a full municipal benefit package, including medical coverage, a defined pension, paid vacation and maternity leave. The Colwood Clinic, which the city calls a "first-in-Canada municipal health-care model," will open this year.
Before pushing for a similar city-run clinic, McIsaac said he confirmed with Health Minister Sylvia Jones that the province would be on board.
"She's indicated that this can be done with doctors billing directly to OHIP," he said, adding that Jones said she'd connect ministry officials with city staff.
In response to questions, a Health Ministry spokesperson sent a brief statement but did not address whether Jones had spoken with McIsaac or had an opinion on the proposed city-run clinic.
"Ontario will continue to work with municipalities, through the Primary Care Action Team, to help meet local needs to achieve the goal of connecting everyone in the province to a family doctor or primary care team," Bill Campbell said. The team, announced in October, is part of the government's recent investment in primary care and is led by Queen's University medical school director Dr. Jane Philpott.
Dr. Jonathan Fitzsimon, an assistant professor in the University of Ottawa's department of family medicine, said Orillia's proposed clinic would likely appeal to some physicians looking for a different way to be compensated for practising family medicine.
Most family doctors are small-business owners and ineligible for employer benefits, paid vacation or sick leave. They use revenue generated from OHIP billings to pay overhead costs, including support staff and rent.
Fitzsimon said an increasing number of new physicians are looking for an employment model that offers the stability of fixed hours, paid vacation, sick leave and other benefits, as well as an employer who runs the actual business, leaving them to dedicate their time to patient care.
"For too long, we've been afraid to think outside the box," said Fitzsimon, a family doctor who leads innovative programs in rural Ontario that use both virtual and in-person care to help more people get attached to and access a family physician. "The same employment model (of running your own business) that worked 40 to 50 years ago doesn't work for many, if not most, family physicians now."
In a statement, Ontario Medical Association president Dr. Dominik Nowak noted physician recruitment and retention is a challenge across Ontario, but particularly in family medicine. He said the province needs to "step up with immediate and sustainable funding" and that "municipalities should not be forced to pay for doctors."
"This sets up a system where we have communities competing with each other," Nowak said. "In a competition, there are winners and losers, and we don't want anyone to lose out on health care."
Delion said the doctor shortage is already pitting communities against each other, with some using cash incentives and other bonuses to lure doctors to their cities.
The Orillia & Lake Country Physician Recruitment Committee, run in large part by volunteers and funded by the municipality and community donations, has decided not to use such incentives. Rather, Delion said, the committee highlights the collegiality of its current physician workforce, the city's lower cost of living and its proximity to the GTA and to the region's lakes, trails and ski hills.
"We've had medical students here on rotation who say, 'How wonderful was it that I could go 15 minutes from the clinic and be on my paddleboard,' " she said.
Alhough Orillia's proposal for a city-run medical clinic is in its early stages, Delion said the idea will intrigue new medical school graduates, some of whom are not interested in managing their own business.
McIsaac, who returned to his hometown in 2022 after close to 30 years as a chartered professional accountant and business executive in the U.S., said he's committed to fixing the doctor shortage in his community. He said both of his parents were mayors of Orillia - his father in 1969, his mother in the early 1980s - and that watching them in their roles, along with his decades of professional experience, taught him not to back away from tough problems.
"The province wants to connect everyone to a doctor in five years," McIsaac said. "We think we can do it in a year. There's a lot of work ahead, but we think it can be done."
CREDIT: Megan Ogilvie Toronto Star

Document 277 of 500

Rae talks Trump, politics as 'knife fight'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/rae-talks-trump-politics-as-knife-fight/docview/3164789048/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Rae+talks+Trump%2C+politics+as+%27knife+fight%27&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's not just about Donald Trump.
It's not just about America.
Bob Rae sees the political tensions up close living in New York. And he deals with the diplomatic tensions working on the global stage as Canada's ambassador to the United Nations.
But he also worries about the political fallout when he's back home in Canada - not least the anti-American reaction taking root here.
"I think we make a mistake when we say it's just one person," Rae told Friday's Democracy Forum that I hosted at Toronto Metropolitan University.
The problem isn't just the American presidency, but U.S. democracy - and democracy at large.
When democracies are in trouble, the whole world is in trouble. If neighbouring voters can't get along in their own countries, it's hardly surprising that neighbouring countries aren't getting along, on this continent and around the globe.
Trump personifies the problem. But he also embodies the people who voted for him in America, just as people keep voting for chauvinists and populists in democracies around the world.
"We need to understand the kind of tensions ... what's happening to public opinion in the United States ... that this is reflected in our own country, too," Rae said.
Democracy is not a panacea for the problems that preoccupy voters.
"This is a forum on democracy," he reminded our audience. "One of the key tensions on democracy is, don't assume that the majority of people are always open-minded, generous and internationally sensitive in their responses to what's going on in the world. It's not the case."
Self-interest and the national interest, nationalism and internationalism are often at cross-purposes.
Democracies have always struggled with these contradictions, but the miscalculations are more costly today.
"That's why I think we have to make a much stronger argument with everyone - not speaking down to people and not wagging our fingers at them, but just saying: 'It's actually in our interest to express greater solidarity with the rest of the world. It's not a win-lose proposition, it actually ... makes us more secure, actually helps us deal with our problems.' "
The most powerful countries have always alternated between expansionism and isolationism, between chauvinism and co-operation. Without guardrails and ground rules to keep everyone on track, at home and abroad, countries will keep going off course - domestically and internationally.
"It's the same challenge that we've always had, and it's just ... we have not had an American president who had such a nationalist and expansionist view of American power in office in my lifetime," Rae argued.
"It's a challenge between those people who say, 'Politics is about power - it's not about the rule of law, it's about the rule of men and it's a knife fight ... or the Canadian perspective, which is, 'We value laws, we do value rules, we do value the relationships that we establish.'
"Not everything is a transaction. Some things are relationships."
Rae knows whereof he speaks. His role as UN ambassador is the culmination of a career in politics and public service - he was Ontario's 21st premier and later served as a roving troubleshooter and Canadian envoy on humanitarian and refugee issues.
He knows more than most that public opinion can be fickle. He catapulted to power in 1990 after then-premier David Peterson called an early election to lock in his lead in public opinion polls - not unlike the snap election Ontario is now immersed in. Rae's NDP government went down to defeat in 1995 amid high deficit spending (not unlike today).
While his political views have changed (Rae later joined the federal Liberals and served as interim leader from 2011-13) his views on defence spending haven't.
Long before the Americans ramped up the pressure on defence, "I have been a consistent advocate of understanding that defence, development and diplomacy go together."
Yet in a volatile world of wars - Russia versus Ukraine, Sudanese against Sudanese, Hamas against Israel - politics remains the only possible solution.
Rae was "profoundly saddened by and outraged" by the bloodshed and bloody-mindedness in the Middle East - the Hamas massacre of Oct. 7 and the Israeli overkill in Gaza that followed.
"We have to begin to turn towards the process of creating political agency for the Palestinians" - which means self-determination - while "the Israelis have to be assured that they're going to be living in security."
That means understanding the fears and hopes of public opinion in both polities in the Middle East. In the same way, politicians must manage public opinion in Canada and the U.S. amid the latest tensions over Trump's tariff talk.
"Politics is not about counting heads, politics is about turning heads," Rae told the Democracy Forum. "It's about convincing people."
It's also about keeping our heads when our blood is boiling.
"I don't have an anti-American bone in my body," he says diplomatically, but also truthfully, having lived as a boy in Washington (where his father was a Canadian diplomat) and now for the past five years in New York.
"I really don't."
Disclosure: I'm a Senior Fellow at The Dais at Toronto
Metropolitan University,
which supports the TMU
Democracy Forum.
CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION Disclosure: I'm a Senior Fellow at The Dais at Toronto Metropolitan University, which supports the TMU Democracy Forum.

Document 278 of 500

Grits target Ford in Super Bowl ads

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/grits-target-ford-super-bowl-ads/docview/3164789040/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Grits+target+Ford+in+Super+Bowl+ads&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford's campaign fumble is getting the Super Bowl spotlight.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie will be airing two different 30-second commercials during the NFL championship game Sunday night between the Kansas City Chiefs and the Philadelphia Eagles.
With Ontario voters headed to the polls on Feb. 27, Crombie's team wants viewers of what is expected to be the year's most-watched TV broadcast to see for themselves what Ford said last Monday.
In a "hot mic" gaffe at his Etobicoke campaign office, the Progressive Conservative leader said he wanted Donald Trump to win last November's U.S. presidential election.
"On election day, was I happy this guy won? One hundred per cent I was. Then the guy pulled out the knife and f---ing yanked it in me," said Ford, not realizing a media pool camera was still rolling.
The Liberals have cut a new version of a 60-second ad they released Tuesday on the misstep.
"Doug Ford just said he was happy Donald Trump won the election. Happy? Was he happy Canadians might lose their jobs?" intones a female narrator with footage of Trump boasting "the tariff will be substantial" and that he'd like to see "Canada become our 51st state."
"Or does he just care so little about the people of this province that he saw an attack on Ontario as an opportunity for himself," she continues before a clip of Ford telling reporters in January that he "needs a clear mandate" to deal with Trump, which is why he called a $189-million election 15 months early.
The second Liberal spot airing on Super Bowl Sunday features Crombie talking about Ontario's health-care challenges.
"Leadership is about priorities and for seven years, Doug Ford has had the wrong ones," she says against the backdrop of video of her touring a hospital and chatting with doctors and nurses.
"The result? A health-care system in crisis. People are afraid to get sick. As premier, I'll have one priority: fixing our health care," the Liberal leader continues.
"We'll take the money that Ford wastes on bad deals and invest it where it matters, starting with getting you a family doctor," she continues.
"I'm Bonnie Crombie, the new leader of Ontario's Liberals and I'll get more for you."
A senior Liberal official, speaking confidentially in order to discuss strategy, said the Super Bowl play is a big one for a party that was in office from 2003 until 2018, but finished third in seats in the past two elections behind Ford's Tories and the New Democrats now led by Marit Stiles.
"It's been a long time since we have had the kind of fundraising machine and clear, focused message that makes this kind of advertising possible," said the Grit insider.
"When you talk about what actually matters to people, you'll quickly find that people want to get behind you and support that message."
The Trump blunder is potentially harmful to Ford's re-election bid because he has framed the campaign as a referendum on who could best deal with the U.S. president's threatened tariffs.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Poilievre was riding a Trump-like wave. Is it about to drown him?: In a recurring feature, Susan Delacourt, a small-l liberal, and Matt Gurney, a small-c conservative, bring their different perspectives - and shared commitment to civil disagreement - to the political debates of the moment

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/poilievre-was-riding-trump-like-wave-is-about/docview/3164789038/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Poilievre+was+riding+a+Trump-like+wave.+Is+it+about+to+drown+him%3F%3A+In+a+recurring+feature%2C+Susan+Delacourt%2C+a+small-l+liberal%2C+and+Matt+Gurney%2C+a+small-c+conservative%2C+bring+their+different+perspectives+-+and+shared+commitment+to+civil+disagreement+-+to+the+political+debates+of+the+moment&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: SD Donald Trump is no friend to progressives in Canada, to state the obvious. But I'm sure I'm not the only person wondering these days how much of a pal he is to Canadian conservatives, both provincially and federally.
Trump's 30-day reprieve of tariffs on Canadian imports kind of pulled the rug out from Doug Ford's whole provincial election pitch to Ontarians, who will now vote on Feb. 27 without any clue on what Trump will do next. Meanwhile, polls are showing that Pierre Poilievre and the federal Conservatives are taking a hit in popularity as Canadians shift their attention to who's best to deal with the havoc Trump is wreaking. So my question to you, Matt: is it time for Poilievre's team to get nervous?
MG No, except to the background extent of how nervous the rest of us are and should be about the total crap show that is the world today.
It's clear that Ford's plan was upended a bit by the tariff delay and polling also shows that Ontarians are convinced that Ford called the election for selfish reasons - but the polls also show Ford is cruising to a big majority anyway. Likewise, polls have suggested (admittedly, with some pretty spectacular divergence) that the federal race has tightened, but most still show a commanding Conservative lead. The race has tightened from a historic Conservative blowout to merely an unusually large Conservative win.
The Conservatives themselves are adjusting their strategy - belatedly, I think, but all the same - and they haven't really begun to campaign against Mark Carney yet, who's mostly keeping a low profile. I definitely think the time may come for them to get nervous, but I don't think that time is now, and I don't think they are yet. After all, dropping Justin Trudeau for Carney isn't exactly something they haven't had some time to muse about and prepare for.
SD I disagree (which is good, because that's what we're supposed to be doing here). To me, the Conservatives look entirely unprepared for Carney. Changing their slogans to the "Carney-Trudeau Liberals" looks like a communications strategy built from Popsicle sticks and Elmer's glue.
MG You're not the target audience.
SD I'll grant you that. But Poilievre needs Liberals who have drifted. I'm wary, as you know, of casting this Liberal leadership race as a coronation for Carney; I think all of the candidates deserve a fair hearing. But one of the things that Liberals have lacked over the past few years is what the pros like to call a "narrative" - a story to tell about why Canada needs a middle-ground party. Trump's bullying is giving them a story again.
MG There's always been a story. What the Liberals lacked was the means to tell it, since their ability to communicate had collapsed into a void of incompetence, as had their ability to execute on their policy agenda - and, weirdly, their ability to perceive electoral facts and react accordingly, especially as pertains to an outgoing prime minister's job performance. I don't see any evidence here of anything beyond the Liberals hitting rock bottom and bouncing up a bit now that they're (almost) free of the prime minister, who should have either jumped or been shoved into retirement 18 months ago. There's been no campaign yet, no defining issues (although we can clearly take a good guess at what one will likely be). Conservatives should be wary and alert, and I think they are. But nervous? No. Maybe later. But not yet.
SD So we'll put you down as a "maybe" on the Liberals' return from the dead?
MG Ha. Well, that would be accurate. There's definitely a scenario in which the Conservative party snatches defeat from the jaws of victory, and every single prediction I'd make these days comes with a Donald Trump-sized asterisk. So yes, put me down as a maybe on that. But more relevant to the point, put me down as skeptical that the Conservatives are nervous, because something you said here is worth zooming in on. You said Poilievre needs Liberals who've drifted. He might. He'd certainly need them to win some seats. But I think the lesson for us all in recent years is that elections can be won by motivating new voters. Poilievre has been doing that. Some Toronto Liberals getting Carney fever may not do much to offset that.
SD I will say this: this upsurge in Canadian patriotism is a wild card. Conservatives have been very good in the past about wrapping themselves in the flag - and I'm not talking about the F--k Trudeau one. I can see ways in which Poilievre can capitalize on that, although he'll need to be a bit more careful about his "Canada is broken" line if he doesn't want to be seen as agreeing with Trump. I already see signs that he's toning that down a bit.
MG I do, too. The smart play for him will be to keep hammering on that, but with a more optimistic and urgent message: "It's time to fix what's broken and make Canada stronger." Something like that. I think there is an appetite out there for some real change, and that's why Carney is trying to present himself as an outsider. I have doubts it'll work.
SD The fact that Trump and Carney are forcing the Conservatives to pivot reinforces to me that it is time for them to be nervous - or at least go back to the drawing board on all the slogans that worked so well over the last couple of years.
MG I hope we get a real campaign, with real debates over real issues. I think we'll get different slogans and Carney ducking the media as much as possible while the Conservatives keep the Twitter war machine running all the time. In other words, we'll get the same strategy with slightly new tactics. It's not what the country needs, but I think it's what's coming.
Susan Delacourt is an Ottawa-based columnist for the Star, where she has been covering politics on Parliament Hill since the late 1980s. A political science graduate of Western University, she has written four political books; her latest, "Shopping for Votes," was a finalist for the 2014 Hilary Weston Writers' Trust prize in non-fiction. Susan is also a regular commentator on CBC and CTV,and has taught journalism and political communication at Carleton University. Matt Gurney is a Toronto-based journalist, broadcaster and freelance contributing columnist for the Star. He is co-editor of "The Line," an online magazine, which publishes at readtheLine.ca. He can be reached at matt@readtheline.ca
CAPTION: ETHAN CAIRNS THE CANADIAN PRESS
CREDIT: matt gurney OPINION susan delacourt OPINION

Document 280 of 500

Parties promise to secure family doctors for all by 2029

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/parties-promise-secure-family-doctors-all-2029/docview/3164788986/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Parties+promise+to+secure+family+doctors+for+all+by+2029&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The doctor will soon be in.
The three main political parties have released their plans to secure a family doctor for all Ontarians by 2029, spending up to $4 billion to do so. The province has estimated some two million people in the province don't have a doctor - though other estimates peg that much higher, at 2.5 million and rising to 4.4 million by 2026.
What the parties are proposing:
PCs Before the election, the PC government had promised $1.8 billion over four years to get two million Ontarians rostered with a family doctor or nurse practitioner by 2029. The Tories say about 90 per cent of Ontarians already have access to primary health care. They had also previously named Dr. Jane Philpott, who authored a book on primary care homes - with integrated teams of doctors, nurse practitioners and other specialized services working in one location, serving patients based on their postal code - to help reform the health-care system.
NDP are promising to connect every Ontarian to a family doctor or nurse practitioner with a total price tag of $4.05 billion over four years, based on 2.5 million needing a doctor. They say they'd bring in 3,500 new doctors and reduce paperwork loads to give physicians more time to spend with patients, and would focus on integrated teams. The NDP is also planning to "clear the path" for 13,000 internationally trained doctors who are now unable to practise in the province, and boost the number of residency spots in existing medical schools.
Liberals Would spend $3 billion over four years to provide a family doctor to everyone who needs one - hiring about 3,100 physicians - while creating two new medical schools and increasing spots in existing med schools as well as residency spots, doubling capacity. A $250-million fund would help to retain doctors who have left their practice or are close to retirement. Team-based care would lead to evening and weekend availability. Liberals would also allow 1,200 foreign trained doctors to train and practice in the province.
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Subject: Nurse practitioners

Document 281 of 500

Don't have a family doctor? What if Ontario could give you all the help you need at a 'health care home'?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/dont-have-family-doctor-what-if-ontario-could/docview/3164788959/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Don%27t+have+a+family+doctor%3F+What+if+Ontario+could+give+you+all+the+help+you+need+at+a+%27health+care+home%27%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: KINGSTON-The sprawling second-floor office, located above a pharmacy on the city's main street, at one time was home to eight doctors serving the busy neighbourhood. But the physicians, with large patient rosters, were burning out and six were ready to retire, but couldn't find anyone willing to take over the practice.
Two years ago, they all left, the clinic shuttered and 8,000 patients suddenly found themselves adrift - joining about two million others across the province with no access to a family doctor.
It's not a problem unique to Kingston - many communities across the province are grappling with a lack of primary care - and it has emerged as a key issue in Ontario's Feb. 27 election. The opposition parties have hammered the PCs for being in power for seven years as the situation they inherited worsened, which even the government acknowledged has taken too long to fix and left too many without a family doctor.
But what has happened here since that clinic closed is a new model that has been able to attract physicians and taken thousands off the wait-list, a model that appears to have the support of the PCs, Liberals and NDP.
That same Princess Street office reopened last summer as a "primary care home," as proposed by Dr. Jane Philpott, a former federal Liberal health minister and dean of the medical school at Queen's recently hired by the PC government to help fix the province's health problems.
Her vision, outlined in her 2024 book Health for All - which she calls the periwinkle model - guarantees primary care for everyone based on their postal code, much like families being assigned a local school based on their address.
Two days before the provincial election campaign kicked off last week, the government and Philpott announced funding with a promise of primary care for all Ontarians by 2029, a total of $1.8 billion for every Ontarian to have a family doctor or primary care team within four years.
The government, however, says two million Ontarians need a physician, which is based on work by organizations like the Canadian Institute for Health Information, but the Ontario Medical Association says the number is 2.5 million and could rise to 4.4 million by 2026.
Last fall, Philpott spoke at the Liberal policy convention, and Leader Bonnie Crombie announced Thursday that if elected, she would spend $3 billion over four years to connect everyone with a family doctor. On Friday, NDP Leader Marit Stiles unveiled a $4.05 billion four-year plan to get all Ontarians access to a doctor or nurse practitioner, pledging more family health teams.
Philpott's model, based on similar ones in the Netherlands and the United Kingdom, ideally provide patients access to care seven days a week, taking pressure of emergency rooms and steering them away from walk-in clinics. They are treated by practitioners they know, and who know their histories; medical records are in one place, and once a full network of sites are in place, no one would worry about finding a family doctor if they move.
The Midtown Kingston Health Home opened last July, two years after a proposal by various health care partners in the city obtained funding from the provincial government.
At the health home, each family doctor works in a team with a nurse practitioner, sharing "provider pod" offices, with a host of in-demand services on site - diabetes care, COPD care, social worker, family health educator - mostly for those within the K7M postal code. Community clinics for well baby care, sexual health and quick STI testing are open to all. Practical assistance workers help patients who might need help co-ordinating insulin injections for their child while at school, or finding housing, or applying for a birth certificate.
It's hoping to secure funding to add occupational therapists, physiotherapists, mental health case management and footcare, to make it more of a one-stop community hub for patients.
"The doctors, they were asking me all kinds of questions about my history and this and that, and I was just blown away, because my (previous) doctor would not engage with anything you would have asked him, and I always felt rushed," said lifelong Kingston resident Mario Borsato, 56, now a patient of the health home.
He and several of his family members had sought care at the original clinic for 20 years, and found themselves without a doctor for two years, so he knows the struggles of those who are still looking.
"If you don't have your health, you don't have nothing," he added. "And our family care (in the province) has gone down the tubes."
About 4,400 residents in the K7M postal code were on the wait-list through Health Care Connect, but it is estimated the actual need is double that. So far, the health home has rostered 1,418 patients and - and treated another 1,535 through its various clinics - and expects to have 4,000 patients signed up by the end of March and 8,000 patients by March 2026.
Winn Studsrud, a patient of the original clinic, was directed to the health home after seeking help with a prescription, but it wasn't until later that she realized she'd been taken on by a family doctor there, even though she doesn't live in the postal code.
"I freaking started crying," she said. "I couldn't stop smiling, actually. And I just kept saying 'thank you Lord, thank you Lord.' So honestly, it's like a miracle."
Dr. Mary Rowland, clinical lead, calls the health home "a really nice blend" and likens it to a secondary school, where "this school has the IB program, this school has French Immersion. We have a catchment, and we have these specialized programs for more supports for particular populations."
Dr. Eileen Nicolle, who moved with her family from Toronto to Kingston to be a part of the health home, said "you can provide the most holistic care and also take the time you need to see people ... I think if you take a more long-term approach, there's very much a cost saving and also a time saving because those problems that you don't deal with right up front, they come back."
Currently, the clinic is open five days a week with evening clinic hours, and has managed to attract five physicians so far, with one to come, plus locums, as well as three nurse practitioners.
Staff are on salary, have flexible hours and a team-centred approach. Doctors like it because they don't have to run a business as they would setting up their own practice. They work more reasonable hours, and can leave when they want, "but they don't" because it's such an attractive set up, said Meghan O'Leary, director of clinical services for Kingston Community Health Centres.
Nurse Practitioner Edward Cho was the first hire, and saw the patient roster grow - though initially there was some upset as those from the old clinic weren't necessarily able to re-register.
Cho values being paired with a doctor, because "there are more opportunities for patients to get followups" in a timely manner.
"I'm not worried about my patients" when away, he added. "I always know they're covered. If there are urgent issues, I know they're well taken care of ... having two eyes, rather than one, is always a good thing."
Personally, he said the way the interdisciplinary model works gives him the "perfect work-life balance. I'm not stressed. I go home knowing that my inbox is taken care of, and I won't come back to 10 million messages. I finish all my charting on time."
For Nicolle, working with a male nurse practitioner gives patients choice. "Maybe they want to see a female for their Pap smear. Or maybe the men would rather see Ed for certain things. So it gives you a bit of diversity within the practice."
Research on team-based care has been shown to benefit patients' health and means a drop in hospitalizations and visits to the ER. Patients report higher levels of satisfaction and care, and health-care providers report higher job satisfaction and are less likely to burn out, says Jennifer Rayner, director of policy and research at the Alliance for Healthier Communities.
Raisa Deber, professor emeritus at the Institute of Health Policy, Management and Evaluation at the University of Toronto, said there are a number of team-based models in the province, and there must be flexibility in the system because what works in a large, urban centre may not in a rural or remote area where it might be difficult to get additional services.
Dr. Tara Kiran, family physician and researcher at St. Michael's Hospital and the University of Toronto, works in a family health team that includes nurses, nurse practitioners, social workers, pharmacists and others, focusing on a vulnerable population.
When more than one in five people in Canada don't have access to any primary care, with services varying by region, "we need to do things differently to try and even out the way services are provided ... so that we don't have the have and have-not, but rather, everybody has something."
The periwinkle model does address that and needs to be evaluated to see if it does attract doctors and is able to better clear wait-lists.
"There is a bit of an experiment here, and we do need to learn from it," Kiran added.
The Kingston model is part of a wider network that has a community board of governors, which is key, said Kate Mulligan, a professor at the Dalla Lana School of Public Health at the University of Toronto.
"If we're going to go to all the trouble of doing team based primary care, which is the right next step, we shouldn't miss the opportunity to leverage the huge capacity that's there, to really connect that into our health care system," Mulligan said.
CAPTION: NursepractitionerEdward Cho and Dr. Mary Rowland at theMidtown Kingston Health Home in Kingston, Ontario.

Subject: Health care access; Nurse practitioners; Patients; Primary care; Drug stores; Flexible hours; Clinics; Funding; Physicians

Title: Don't have a family doctor? What if Ontario could give you all the help you need at a 'health care home'?
