Document 282 of 500

Donald Trump has thrown a massive obstacle onto Pierre Poilievre's path to power

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/donald-trump-has-thrown-massive-obstacle-onto/docview/3164648518/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Donald+Trump+has+thrown+a+massive+obstacle+onto+Pierre+Poilievre%27s+path+to+power&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: For Conservative strategists, this wasn't supposed to happen.
Just a few weeks ago, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre appeared to be on his way to winning a crushing majority government in the next election, and the conversation in Ottawa was about whether the Liberals would end up in third or fourth place.
Last weekend, however, U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff threat and musing about making Canada a 51st state changed the game. A Conservative victory is now less certain - and Poilievre, who spent the past two years telling Canadians the country was "broken," is struggling to rebrand.
On Friday, a Pallas poll suggested the Conservative lead in public support heading into the next election campaign is now just six percentage points - a stunning plunge from the 20-point lead pollsters registered a month ago, and the apparent confirmation of movement in Ontario and Quebec reported by competing pollsters.
More worrisome perhaps for them is that Pallas has the Conservatives and Liberals neck and neck if Mark Carney becomes the Grits's next leader.
The Pallas poll comes on the heels of a Leger survey that had support for the Liberals surging in Quebec, tied with the Bloc Québécois and ahead if Carney was their leader.
Ironically, if Poilievre had not been so successful in his prosecution of Trudeau, blaming him for everything from crime in Toronto's subways to the rising cost of groceries, the prime minister's unpopularity might not have prompted Liberal MPs to push him towards the exit.
If Poilievre had not been so successful in prosecuting the case against the consumer price on carbon - the "carbon tax" - the Liberal leadership contestants would not have pledged to scrap it immediately. Ditto for Poilievre's prosecution of the capital gains tax increase.
Poilievre was left with neither the opponent he wanted nor the issue he hoped to run on.
But those were January problems. With tweaks to Poilievre's talking points, the Tories would frame the next Liberal leader as a carbon copy of Trudeau and spend enough money to make sure the message got across.
Then came Trump.
The U.S. president's threat to slap a 25 per cent tariff on all Canadian imports was met with a response from Trudeau that even Conservatives admitted had hit the mark. Trudeau urged his fellow Canadians "to remain united" and "to support one another." He complemented the provinces - "This is team Canada at its best" - and pledged that the federal government would "be there for Canadians."
That hope and optimism in the face of despair not only clashed with Poilievre's anger narrative but also risked undermining the Conservatives' campaign promises and the party's internal coalition. After all, Poilievre had spent years pledging to cut government spending, balancing each dollar's worth of new spending with a dollar's worth of cuts. Was that now what Canadians would want?
On Sunday, Poilievre attempted a pivot. When the tariff threat was still on the table, he appeared more statesmanlike, even agreeing with Trudeau on the need for retaliatory tariffs.
But when the tariff threat was pushed back by 30 days, Poilievre pivoted again. "Whether or not we agree with (Trump) should not matter," he told Canadians. The Liberals had caused the drug crisis, he said, and he would crack down on it - not to please Trump, but because it was the right thing to do.
Part of Poilievre's challenge is that his coalition includes Ontario Conservatives, who want someone like Premier Doug Ford who'll fight Trump with everything the country can throw at him, and Alberta Conservatives. who support Premier Danielle Smith and her approach of validating Trump's concerns. Recall that back in October, another Leger poll found more Conservative voters (45 per cent) wanted Trump to win the U.S. presidential election than wanted Kamala Harris (42 per cent) to win.
Pallas CEO Joseph Angolano is not surprised by the uptick in Liberal support. He sees it as an emotional reaction to Trump's tariff threat. "Public opinion tends to favour incumbents in times of crisis," he told the Star.
Canadians certainly did respond emotionally, wrapping themselves with national pride and checking labels to ensure they were not buying American products. Even support for sovereignty in Quebec dropped to its lowest level in more than five years.
"Overnight, sovereignty dropped like eight points," noted polling aggregator Philippe J. Fournier of Canada338.com. "That's a rally-around-the-flag effect, at least in Quebec - and if it happened in Quebec, it may happen elsewhere."
The Conservatives are hoping that any rallying happens around them. They've moved on to a new tag line, "Canada First," and next week, as first reported in the Hill Times, they plan a big rally on National Flag of Canada Day to cement that message.
But after trying to brand Canada Day with red, white and blue during prime minister Stephen Harper's time in office, you might say it's a sign of desperate times that the Tories are encouraging everyone to ditch the blue in favour of just red and white.
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre speaks at a news conference in Vancouver on Feb. 5, 2025.

Document 283 of 500

Ontario election 2025: Doug Ford's plan to deliver us from gridlock won't end congestion, experts say

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-election-2025-doug-fords-plan-deliver-us/docview/3164648517/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+election+2025%3A+Doug+Ford%27s+plan+to+deliver+us+from+gridlock+won%27t+end+congestion%2C+experts+say&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford's drive to expand highway capacity will win votes for his Progressive Conservatives, but do little or nothing to reduce the congestion infuriating motorists in the GTA and beyond, according to experts.
Since first elected Ontario premier seven years ago, Ford has made drivers a priority with projects including widening parts of Highway 401, planning construction of Highway 413 through the Greenbelt in York, Peel and Halton regions, promising to eliminate tolls on the provincially run eastern section of Highway 407, and envisioning a new highway tunnelled under the 401 from Brampton to Scarborough.
The PC party says that, in this snap Feb. 27 election, it is voters' only option to "deliver on projects to get drivers out of gridlock across Ontario."
Marit Stiles' New Democrats say they would end tolls on all of Highway 407 and widen highways including 11/17, 40, and 69. The NDP accuses the Ford government of making highway planning "politicized and lobbyist driven."
Bonnie Crombie's Liberals are, so far, focusing on transit promises they say Ford has failed to deliver - including all-day, two-way GO service to Kitchener-Waterloo, Milton and Niagara - to help alleviate gridlock.
Zachary Taylor, a University of Western Ontario political scientist, says several factors combine to make it politically popular to champion vehicle use even as governments, including Ford's, say they are trying to reduce greenhouse gas emissions.
The Greater Toronto-Hamilton area's population boomed in recent decades but construction of new highways and transit systems largely stalled from the 1980s into the 2000s. That turned clear-sailing highway drives into bumper-to-bumper agony, especially on the maxed out 400-series highways.
When Ford came to power, Taylor said, he parked previous Liberal government policies aimed at protecting rural land, halting sprawl and intensifying existing development in a way that de-emphasized the car as a necessity.
"What we've seen with this Ford government is a renewed emphasis on car drivers as a political constituency. There's just way more car driving voters than there are non-car-driving voters," Taylor said, noting that after moving from Toronto to London, he quickly realized that public transit wasn't enough for him to efficiently get around.
"I think also part of what Ford has brought with him, coming out of the Toronto context, is a real frustration shared by many Torontonians - that you just can't get around the city in your car very efficiently."
But can Ford deliver us from gridlock? Two transportation experts canvassed by the Star said highway building and widening will, at great expense, yield minor or no reduction in traffic congestions, and some projects might even make it worse.
Eric Miller, director of the University of Toronto Transportation Research Institute, said research clearly shows that adding vehicle lanes will incentive people to drive more and quickly fill up those lanes.
"Despite identifying roadway congestion in the GTA as a priority issue, the Ford government's policies to date will do nothing to offer long-term relief," Miller said. "If built, Highway 413 will make congestion worse, in that it will promote increased auto-dominated suburban sprawl, generating more traffic, not less, and do nothing to relieve congestion in the GTA core.
"We are never going to solve our congestion problem by just looking at road-based solutions. Public transit is the only long-term solution to get cars off the road and seriously reduce congestion on the roadways."
Miller says the province could reduce highway gridlock by reinstating funding to municipalities to operate and maintain transit systems cut by then-premier Mike Harris in the 1990s, and to "aggressively" use artificial intelligence to help control vehicle movements on highways and city streets.
Said Easa, a civil engineering professor at Toronto Metropolitan University, calls the Ford government's highway expansion push, coupled with transit expansion programs including Ontario Line subway construction and GO Transit improvements, "overall a well-balanced plan."
However, Easa added in an email, "the heavy emphasis on highway projects may provide only short-term relief from congestion and could harm the environment - particularly with projects like Highway 413. A stronger focus on public transit would yield long-term benefits by reducing reliance on cars and promoting sustainability."
Here are what the experts say about the Ford government's main highway projects:
Highway 413: The controversial 52-kilometre highway, fast-tracked late last year by legislation that includes exemption from environmental review, is set to stretch from Highway 400 between King Road and Kirby Road to the 401/407 ETR interchange near Mississauga, Milton and Halton Hills.
Easa said Highway 413, mostly designed but not yet under construction, offers the best hope at gridlock relief compared to Ford's other highway options. However, cutting through a swath of Greenbelt is the worst option in terms of minimizing environmental impacts, he said.
Miller said Highway 413 will only encourage sprawl and more driving, calling it "yet another play for developers and engineering companies to make a lot of money." It may be politically popular in some areas, he said, but it won't solve the GTA's transportation problems.
Highway 407: The toll road, built by the provincial government as a Highway 401 alternative but leased to a private operator for 99 years, is under scrutiny because it has excess capacity - sometimes within eyeshot of gridlocked 401 drivers.
Easa considers buying out the 407 lease - or just subsidizing truck use to free up space on the 401 - environmentally sound because the road is already built, although getting drivers onto transit would be better. For potential gridlock relief, he ranks it below Highway 413.
Miller believes talk of the 407's congestion-fighting potential is overblown, adding he's surprised the NDP is proposing making the road toll-free. "I saw a report that said buying out the lease would cost more than $30 billion and there are much more effective ways to spend that money, if the treasury even has it," he said. "Think of the transit we could build."
Tunnelling under the 401: Ford's announcement last year that he wants to build 50 kilometres of roadway, and potentially transit, under existing highway was panned by opposition parties and some experts but hailed as bold and "visionary" by the Toronto Region Board of Trade. The Progressive Conservatives say planning continues for the project, which does not yet have a price tag but would cost tens of billions of dollars.
"While the tunnelling project could alleviate congestion in downtown Toronto," Easa said, "it may negatively impact the already visually unappealing infrastructure along the proposed segment, especially with the addition of the new exit and entrance ramps connecting the tunnel."
Miller said costs could reach $100 billion for gridlock benefits that make the idea "silly and ridiculous, almost not worth talking about."
If provincial transportation agency Metrolinx is studying 401 tunnel feasibility, he said, "it's a complete and utter waste of money."
CAPTION: Ontario Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford speaks during an event on Feb.4, 2025 in Ottawa.

Subject: Transportation planning; Automobiles; Public transportation; Environmental impact; Tolls; Voters; Roads & highways

Document 284 of 500

For Marit Styles to win, she needs to tell a great story

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/marit-styles-win-she-needs-tell-great-story/docview/3164584959/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=For+Marit+Styles+to+win%2C+she+needs+to+tell+a+great+story&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario is going to the polls in late February to decide which party, and which party leader, will helm the province for the next four years. As we are still early in the campaign, the Star asked four political commentators to weigh in on what each leader needs to do over the coming weeks to give their party a chance at winning.
There is a bizarre phenomenon today in which right-wing populists win the support of lower income voters, claiming to fight for them. Winning power, they revert to rewarding their real base: privileged, affluent, older, white voters. There is no better master of this political card trick than Doug Ford.
That situation means that Canada's Liberals are increasingly squeezed between fiscal conservative messaging on the one hand, and social justice on the other. Bonnie Crombie's flip flops illustrate the cross pressures. Her backers initially tried pushing her to the centre, as a contrast with Kathleen Wynne's stance as a progressive politician. It did not sell.
As the leader of Ontario's NDP, Marit Stiles' challenge is to see off these pretenders, fighting off those looking to poach the New Democrats' traditional voter base and policy turf.
But this particular electoral competition is not about policy, despite the media's fascination with hairsplitting differences over issues. It is instead about focus and authenticity. Many party activists, heavily Toronto-centric, want Stiles to take loud and risky stands on Gaza, on Islamophobia, on a tough green agenda and more. Many older, more traditional party veterans want her to use assaults on the character of the other two leaders as wedges.
But angry rhetoric and tired wedge politics will deliver defeat. Voters will be asking for something a great deal more difficult to deliver: authenticity. They will ask: "Can I trust you? Are you really looking out for me? Why should I believe you ?" Despite the NDP being the Official Opposition, the media is seemingly addicted to framing the election as a two horse race. Though that means Stiles risks being pushed to the side, she must resist the temptations of nastier, divisive attack politics.
I have known Ms. Stiles for more than a decade and watched with fascination her ability to climb the greasy political pole without being seen as just another partisan flame-thrower. She has been tough on many issues but as a fighter for what's important to working people rather than a doctrinaire politician.
Contrast that with her competition. Bonnie Crombie's political experience has been solely municipal, with the exception of three unremarkable years as a backbencher in Ottawa. She will also have a hard time explaining how she is different than Ford on developer-driven issues like paving the greenbelt, especially as a large chunk of her biggest financial backing is from the same developers.
Stiles is nearly a decade younger that Bonnie Crombie, but she seems much younger still. Her support among young women may be a result of her speaking in their voice, about their issues. The toughest challenge in running against two very seasoned war horses, is not to let yourself be dragged into their name-calling games.
If she can win the authenticity stakes, if she is seen as a more credible champion of working families, and if she demonstrates iron discipline about her message, she can frame the ballot question. If, with laser like focus, she sticks to a message that is about affordability, she will do well. If she folds to internal pressure to highlight issues focused on identity, or Trump bashing, she will not.
Stiles' most valuable skill is a gift for story-telling - not the fairy tales at which Doug Ford excels, but rather, narratives that ring true. This is a skill that former NDP leader Stephen Lewis had and used to powerful effect. Stiles should cite real stories of a family in Sudbury whose grandfather died on a gurney in a hospital corridor, or the single mom in Scarborough who struggles to fight eviction.
It's that focus on the things most Ontarians care about that will allow to carve out a space in voters' minds. And if she can force her opponents to accept her framing of the ballot question as "Who can I really trust to fight for my family every day?" she could well become Premier.
CAPTION: NDP Leader Marit Stiles launches her campaign for the provincial election in Toronto, January 29, 2025.

Subject: Authenticity; Political activism; Voters

PC leader promises to tunnel under 401: His opponents prefer to focus on adding doctors

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pc-leader-promises-tunnel-under-401/docview/3164584906/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=PC+leader+promises+to+tunnel+under+401%3A+His+opponents+prefer+to+focus+on+adding+doctors&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Some have warned it's a money pit, but Doug Ford is plowing ahead with a tunnel under Highway 401 from Mississauga to Scarborough despite not knowing the cost.
The Progressive Conservative leader seeking a third term as premier made the promise Friday as the NDP's Marit Stiles and Liberal chief Bonnie Crombie touted their plans to get everyone in Ontario a family doctor.
"It will be the biggest tunnel in the entire world," Ford boasted of his plan unveiled last fall to skepticism from opposition parties over concerns it would cost tens of billions of dollars and take many years.
Ford acknowledged at a Scarborough auto parts plant that a feasibility study is not yet complete, nor is a cost estimate, for what he conceded would be an "ambitious" effort to dig an "express" tunnel with traffic and transit lanes. "It won't happen overnight," he cautioned.
"That Doug Ford would throw out this idea with no cost estimate and no timeline is just insulting to the people of Ontario," said Green Leader Mike Schreiner.
Stiles, who was in Sault Ste. Marie detailing a $4-billion plan to lure 3,500 more doctors to Ontario, said the faster way to ease gridlock on the 401 is to negotiate with the private owners of the less busy Highway 407 to scrap charges for motorists.
"Ford would rather spend $100 billion on a Fantasy Tunnel than just remove the damn tolls on the 407. I'll get them removed, permanently," she wrote on social media.
Crombie also took a shot from Thunder Bay.
"While Doug Ford wastes your money on a tunnel that will never actually get built, Ontario's Liberals will get you a family doctor," she wrote on X.
Ford said forecasts show the 400-series highways in the Toronto area will be at capacity in the next 10 years, so "we owe it to the drivers" to proceed with a tunnel.
He pledged this week to remove tolls from the provincially owned portion of the 407, from Brock Road in Pickering east to Highway 115/35. Tolls would remain on the rest of the highway.
With the Ontario Medical Association saying 2.5 million Ontarians don't have a family doctor, opposition parties are trying to make health care an issue in the Feb. 27 campaign.
The Conservatives have pledged to get two million Ontarians a family doctor within four years.
CAPTION: Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford conceded it would be an "ambitious" effort to dig an express tunnel under Hwy. 401 in Toronto.Toronto Star
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Document 286 of 500

Doug Ford has to convince Ontarians he is their protector

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-has-convince-ontarians-he-is-their/docview/3164584841/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+has+to+convince+Ontarians+he+is+their+protector&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario is going to the polls in late February to decide which party, and which party leader, will helm the province for the next four years. As we are still early in the campaign, the Star asked four political commentators to weigh in on what each leader needs to do over the coming weeks to give their party a chance at winning.
My editor has asked me to answer a simple question: How can Doug Ford's PCs win-and win bigger?
I'm not sure I'm best to answer this question. When Mr. Ford and I faced off as leaders of dueling campaigns during the 2010 mayoral election-he not only beat me, he crushed me.
What's more, the Progressive Conservative leader enjoys a substantial, some would say unsurmountable, lead in the polls.
But here goes.
The temptation will be to frame the ballot question as "Which of the party leaders do you trust to go head-to-head with Donald Trump and win?" That would be a mistake. It would be akin to looking for someone to tame a tornado.
The president is not a rational actor and it is a fool's errand to think that anyone can effectively go toe-to-toe with him. It is a battle you won't win because it is a battle that can't be won.
Rather, if Ford wants to win and win big, then the ballot question needs to be: "Which of the leaders do you trust to protect you, your family and Ontario from the economic shocks of the Trump administration?"
It is framing the question this way that will see the PCs returned to the legislature with a strengthened majority.
And it is a question which is, simply put, a gift to Ford and his campaign.
The chaos that has emerged from south of the border has provoked both fury and fear in Canadians - including Ontarians - unlike anything in recent memory.
Ontarians are looking for their leaders to stand up for them, to be sure. Matching tariffs, border-state diplomacy, ad campaigns aimed at American voters, and business-to-business pressure are all necessary tactics. Those deal with the fury.
But the fear must also be addressed. And that's where Ontarians are looking to their own province's leaders to protect them. Because it's not just theoretical macroeconomic shocks that has them worried; rather, it is the price shock of suddenly more expensive gas, groceries and other imported products that has them up at night.
No question, calling an early election is always a risk. But the timing of the political contest comes with a calculated set of strategic advantages for the PCs:
With Justin Trudeau and the Liberals still in power federally, the PCs can rely on oppositional sentiment toward Trudeau.Doug Ford, personally, has never been so popular. Just look at how comfortably he has assumed the face of the "Team Canada" response.The PCs have a major cash advantage that will allow them to dominate the airwaves.Liberal operatives are distracted by the federal leadership race. Case in point: they've been slow to nominate candidates in key ridings.The government's fiscal situation is not going to improve anytime soon-and economic tensions with the United States won't help. Things might not be great now, but they won't be any better in fifteen months, when Ford would have had to call this election anyway.
Taken together, these factors will undoubtedly be major contributors should the PCs win re-election.
But none will matter as much as Doug Ford's ability to convince Ontarians he is the best candidate not to negotiate with a madman but to protect their pocketbooks from a madman.
Elections aren't won on hypotheticals - they're won on trust. And right now, Ontarians don't need a leader who promises to "figure it out" when the storm hits. The storm has hit. They want someone who has been through the storm before and knows how to steer the ship.
Doug Ford's path to victory isn't about making Ontarians believe he can tame Donald Trump - it's about proving he can shield them from the chaos that Trump is likely to spread for four years.
If the Progressive Conservative leader can make that case, this election won't just be a win for the PCs - it will be a landslide.
CAPTION: Ontario Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford announced speaks to media on Friday, January 31 2025.

Subject: Political campaigns; Leadership; Local elections

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

People: Trump, Donald J

Title: Doug Ford has to convince Ontarians he is their protector

Document 287 of 500

Tories maintain healthy lead, poll finds: Ford's gaffe about celebrating Trump's victory has so far had little effect with voting intentions

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tories-maintain-healthy-lead-poll-finds/docview/3164584819/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tories+maintain+healthy+lead%2C+poll+finds%3A+Ford%27s+gaffe+about+celebrating+Trump%27s+victory+has+so+far+had+little+effect+with+voting+intentions&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford's "hot mic" gaffe about wanting Donald Trump to win the U.S. presidency may not have damaged the Progressive Conservatives, but it has exposed potential risks for their campaign, a new poll suggests.
The Abacus Data poll for the Star found Ford's Tories with 46 per cent support, well ahead of Bonnie Crombie's Liberals at 24 per cent, Marit Stiles's New Democrats at 21 and Mike Schreiner's Greens at five.
While 43 per cent of those surveyed said the PC leader's misstep had "no impact" on their voting intentions in the Feb. 27 election, 35 per cent said it would make them "less likely" to cast a ballot for him and 22 per cent said they would be "more likely" to vote Tory.
"It definitely reminded people who aren't Ford fans today why they probably aren't Ford fans," Abacus president David Coletto said Friday.
In an embarrassing snafu caught on a hot mic at his Etobicoke campaign office Monday, Ford said of Trump, "On election day, was I happy this guy won? One hundred per cent I was. Then the guy pulled out the knife and f---ing yanked it in me."
The Liberals have launched an attack ad blitz using the clip.
"He's overwhelmingly the favourite on who is best to stand up to Trump the bully," said Coletto, noting 57 per cent of those polled said the Tories would "best deal with the impact of Donald Trump" compared to 11 per cent for the Liberals, 16 per cent for the NDP and three per cent for the Greens.
"So I think the risk for them in this is that he's the guy who said, 'I liked the bully, I supported the bully.' If this becomes a narrative, it becomes damaging," the pollster said, adding that explains why the Liberals are airing ads with Ford's outburst.
"In this survey, we did not show them the clip. (The question) simply said he was caught on camera saying this - and that is not going to be as impactful as if millions of Ontarians actually see him say it," he said.
"So our next survey could be really interesting as the (Liberal) ads go out this weekend."
Ford called the snap provincial election 15 months early, framing it as a referendum on who could best deal with Trump's threatened 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian imports, which would devastate Ontario's trade-reliant economy.
Abacus surveyed 2,000 Ontarians from Tuesday through Thursday using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform. Although opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 2.3 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
Coletto found a slight slippage in Ford's popularity from the previous Abacus poll released last week.
About 52 per cent of those surveyed were "definitely wanting (a) change of government," up from 48 per cent in January.
Similarly, the government's approval rating has dropped to 38 per cent from 42 per cent, while its disapproval rating has jumped to 40 per cent from 34 per cent.
"Our survey last time was probably as good as it kind of gets for Ford in Ontario right now," said Coletto.
"In terms of the voter intent, it's holding up, and there's been no shift there. But there are some indicators that there are some other things shifting," he said.
"That desire-for-change number's rising, but not so rapidly that they need to worry about it. With just under three weeks left now, all signs point to a highly likely PC re-election, but with the (Trump) tariffs on the back burner ... it creates more space for the opposition party leaders to try to make this about something else."
So far, the poll suggests the opposition parties have not yet found traction on other issues they would like to talk about.
Asked which party "will keep the cost of living as low as possible," the Tories were at 33 per cent, the Liberals 18 per cent, the NDP 16 per cent and the Greens at four per cent, with 22 per cent undecided.
On which party "will best manage the provincial economy," 47 per cent said the Tories, 15 per cent the NDP, 13 per cent the Liberals and three per cent the Greens, with 19 per cent undecided.
On which "will best improve the health-care system," 36 per cent said the NDP, 22 per cent the Liberals, 21 per cent the Tories and seven per cent the Greens, with 12 per cent undecided.
On which "will do the most to make housing more affordable," 32 per cent said the Liberals, 23 per cent the Tories, 15 per cent the NDP and zero per cent the Greens, with 30 per cent undecided.
And on which party "will improve the education system," 28 per cent said the Tories, 17 per cent the Liberals, 14 per cent the NDP and three per cent the Greens, with 36 per cent undecided.
CAPTION: A new Abacus poll found voters see Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford as the best party leader to stand up to U.S. President Donald Trump. He's also their choice to control the cost of living, manage the economy and improve education.R.J. JohnstonToronto Star
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Document 288 of 500

Bonnie Crombie must take a big swing on a health care election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/bonnie-crombie-must-take-big-swing-on-health-care/docview/3164584742/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Bonnie+Crombie+must+take+a+big+swing+on+a+health+care+election&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario is going to the polls in late February to decide which party, and which party leader, will helm the province for the next four years. As we are still early in the campaign, the Star asked four political commentators to weigh in on what each leader needs to do over the coming weeks to give their party a chance at winning.
If the world was fair and Ontario was operating according to typical rules of political gravity, Bonnie Crombie would be winning. Or, at the very least, Doug Ford would be faltering.
Why? Because that's what the numbers say.
Ontario voters say it's time for a change - fully 70 per cent of them.
Ontario voters say the province is headed in the wrong direction - at least 68 per cent of them.
And Ontario voters - about 60 per cent of them - say that Doug Ford is more interested in rewarding his friends than addressing key priorities like affordability and health care.
These are the sort of numbers, coming from pollster Relay Strategies, that chew up and spit out incumbents. The kind of conditions that sweep opposition leaders into office and Premiers into history.
Unfortunately for Crombie, the world isn't always fair. And so far, Ontario is defying political gravity, holding Ford high above his rivals. High as in half of all intended voters in the province. High as in historically high.
All of this leaves Crombie and her Liberals with an excruciating choice.
Doug Ford is succeeding because all that anyone in Ontario can talk about or think about is the orange menace of Donald Trump and his would-be devastating tariffs. As if on script with the PC war room, the first ten days of this election have been dominated by ransom notes from the oval office. And on that issue the voters are decided: Doug Ford is their preferred leader to manage the Trump tariff crisis.
This is true even as health care remains a top-of-mind concern in every part of the province, with 2.5 million-plus people, parents and patients living without a family doctor. Even as the desire for change remains steady and stubborn, Trump is setting the terms of our provincial election campaign. And that is rewarding Ford.
To get in the game, to crack the conversation and to find some currency, Crombie and her team must feel a sickening lure toward the topic of tariffs. If protecting Ontario jobs from MAGA madness is the issue of the day, don't the Liberals risk looking irrelevant and being looked over by talking about anything else?
Except voters don't want change when it comes to fighting Trump. And they don't think we're headed in the wrong direction when it comes to Ford's response to White House hostility. A focus on tariffs might push Crombie higher in the headlines, but it won't pry voters away from Ford.
Worse than that, a move toward tariffs might surrender key territory to the NDP, leaving the Liberals stranded between a rock and a hopeless place.
The only choice is the hard choice: to find a way, any way, to make this campaign about health care and affordability and Doug Ford's indifference toward these core responsibilities of office. The case for change must be made forcefully with a combination of pointed advertising, powerful campaign appearances and resonating stories from families with no family doctor. It will require specificity, intensity and probably some luck. It also has to happen immediately while tariffs are temporarily paused and voters are perhaps again willing to be reminded about what else they're looking for from a premier.
Crombie needs to take a big swing. She needs to take a big risk. Not by shifting strategy but by sticking with it. Not by taking the fight to where Ford wants it to be. But by forcing the fight to where she needs it to be.
Better to fight with hope on health care than fail for certain on tariffs.
CAPTION: Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie arrives to speak to the media during a campaign stop outside the Barrie Primary Care Campus in Barrie, Ont., on Wednesday, January 29, 2025.
CREDIT: Scott Reid Contributor

Subject: Political campaigns; Provincial elections; Voters; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Tariffs

Title: Bonnie Crombie must take a big swing on a health care election

Document 289 of 500

The Greens need to make this the Greenbelt election: While election victory may be a long shot, the Greens often stand alone in highlighting important issues, writes Jo-Ann Roberts

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/greens-need-make-this-greenbelt-election/docview/3164584719/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+Greens+need+to+make+this+the+Greenbelt+election%3A+While+election+victory+may+be+a+long+shot%2C+the+Greens+often+stand+alone+in+highlighting+important+issues%2C+writes+Jo-Ann+Roberts&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=OP.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Success looks different for the Green Party of Ontario compared to the PCs, Liberals or NDP in a first-past-the-post, winner-take-all election. They must run a campaign as if they will form government, with a complete platform and a full slate of candidates. However, they must also manage expectations and be realistic, as they only have a fraction of the money and staff of the other parties.
Given those factors, what does success look like for the Greens? The first sign of success would be for the party to hold their two current seats: Mike Schreiner's in Guelph and Aislinn Clancy's in Kitchener Centre. Each was hard won, and the party won't take them for granted, especially Clancy's, which she won in a byelection three years ago. Volunteers will work hard to make sure both MPPs are re-elected.
The next goal must be to add a seat. The party will aim to run a full slate with strong candidates. Still, I'd say the best chance for a win is in a rural riding, where the party is focusing on candidates who are local champions willing to fight for the community. The Greens will target ridings where the incumbent MPP is seen as supporting the PC Party agenda, rather than what's good for the local area.
I'm watching Parry-Sound-Muskoka, where Matt Richter is running. He's a very popular teacher, rooted in the community, and a tireless campaigner, who is due for a win. Richter has run five times in this Conservative stronghold. He finished fourth in 2007 with four thousand five hundred votes. In 2022, he finished second with 18-thousand votes. He's determined and is definitely a local champion.
There are other ridings where the Green candidates have municipal experience and are seen as champions defending rural values. It would be a massive success if the Greens were to win or come second in Bruce-Grey-Owen Sound, Dufferin-Carleton, and Wellington-Halton Hills. In these ridings, Green candidates are making the point that under the Ford government, Ontario is losing farmland at a rate of 300 acres per day. Mike Schreiner and Aislin Clancy have been fighting the government on issues like the loss of the Greenbelt, protecting watersheds from big corporations, and support for farmers. While Doug Ford is running this election on "Protecting Ontario," many rural voters don't think he is actually protecting what they value. It just might be enough to swing PC votes to the Green candidate if that's the case.
Another sign of success for Ontario Greens would be to hold and increase their popular vote. That won't be easy, with voter turnout dropping dramatically during every election in the province. Also, snap elections traditionally have very low turnouts, especially if you hold them in February. However a growing popular vote is a sign of success because it shows that the Green platform has more support than is reflected in the seat count.
Another reason every vote counts is because Ontario has a per-vote subsidy. The GPO gets 63 cents for every vote cast for a Green candidate. The funding allows the party to work between elections, diversify its membership, and reach out to youth and disengaged voters, strengthening democracy. It also means there is still a party talking about climate change and challenging the status quo. That's vital, because the Green platform influences the other parties in every province with elected Greens.
Mike Schreiner and Aislinn Clancy have demonstrated that green candidates can win and become MPPs who make a difference. The challenge for the Greens will be to remind voters of that winning formula. If they can, they just might make the kind of gains they need to become an increasingly important presence in Ontario politics.
Jo-Ann Roberts is the author of "Storm The Ballot Box: An Insider's Guide to a Voting Revolution."
CAPTION: Arlyn McAdorey THE CANADIAN PRESS

CREDIT: Jo-Ann Roberts;

Subject: Success; Candidates; Voters; Elections; Voter behavior

People: Schreiner, Mike

Title: The Greens need to make this the Greenbelt election:   While election victory may be a long shot, the Greens often stand alone in highlighting important issues, writes Jo-Ann Roberts

Document 290 of 500

$200 'tax rebate' cheques are being sent to dead Ontarians. Here's what to do if one lands in your mailbox

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/200-tax-rebate-cheques-are-being-sent-dead/docview/3164584612/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%24200+%27tax+rebate%27+cheques+are+being+sent+to+dead+Ontarians.+Here%27s+what+to+do+if+one+lands+in+your+mailbox&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Last week, 90-year-old Constance Williams was surprised to receive a "taxpayer rebate" cheque addressed to her deceased sister, Hazel.
"I figured it was a mistake," said Williams. "I did not open (the envelope)."
Instead, she mailed it back to the Ministry of Finance with a note explaining that her sister had died last year on Jan. 1.
Fifteen million Ontarians are slated to receive a $200 cheque from the provincial government, a controversial move expected to cost the treasury $3 billion.
The rollout of the program, which began on Jan. 17, has experienced some challenges, including cheques addressed to names that individuals haven't used in years.
And it appears Williams is not the only person to get a cheque in the mail addressed to the dead.
Commenters on the online forum Reddit have talked about receiving cheques meant for recently deceased relatives.
"My mother got Ford's check today for the $200 thing for my decreased father. We're wondering what we should do about it," wrote one Redditor, who did not respond to requests for comment.
Here's what to do if you get such a cheque in the mail.
What to do about the cheques
Williams filed her sister's taxes on her behalf last April, making her sister eligible for the rebate posthumously. Williams assumed she wouldn't be able to deposit the cheque, given that it was addressed to someone who didn't have a bank account.
When issuing the cheques, the Ministry of Finance used "the most recent tax data available, as is standard practice for provincial and federal tax payments," a spokesperson said in an email.
Cheques can be cashed by the executor of the estate by bringing proof of death to a bank, the email said, noting that different financial institutions may have different processes.
Anyone having issues depositing their cheques should contact their financial institution, the statement added. Otherwise, people can call ServiceOntario at 1-833-351-0409, the ministry said.
A challenging rollout
A scam alert recently appeared on the province's "taxpayer rebate" webpage warning residents receiving the cheques to be on the lookout for scammers.
Some people, meanwhile, have struggled to deposit their cheques because they've been printed with the wrong names, such as maiden names they haven't used in years.
Williams thinks the cheques were a political move meant to buy votes. There are better uses for the money, she said. For one, it could have been used to support the hospital system, added Williams, a retired pediatric nurse and researcher, or toward the education system or food banks.
What to do if you haven't received your cheque yet
Waiting for your cheque can be pretty anxiety-provoking, especially if you're still recovering from holiday spending. The government is working "as quickly as possible" to distribute the cheques, said a government spokesperson in an email.
Your cheque may still be on its way.
You can check your address and rebate status on the ServiceOntario website. You'll just need an account login, the last four digits or your Social Insurance Number, your date of birth, and the postal code of the mailing address you used in your tax return.
You can't get your cheque by direct deposit; the "rebates" are being paid by cheque only. So check your mailbox if you haven't in a while.
For more information visit Ontario.ca/TaxpayerRebate.
CAPTION: Doug Ford's government began sending out taxpayer rebate cheques on Jan. 17, but some residents raised questions after receiving some addressed to deceased loved ones.

Subject: Social insurance numbers; Financial institutions

Business indexing term: Subject: Financial institutions

Company / organization: Name: ServiceOntario; NAICS: 926120

Title: $200 'tax rebate' cheques are being sent to dead Ontarians. Here's what to do if one lands in your mailbox

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2025

Publication date: Feb 8, 2025

Section: news/ontario

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto

Country of publication: Canada

The Greens need to make this the Greenbelt election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/greens-need-make-this-greenbelt-election/docview/3164584609/se-2?accountid=15090

If they can, they just might make the kind of gains they need to become an increasingly important presence in Ontario politics.
CAPTION: Mike Schreiner, leader of the Green Party of Ontario, speaks during a press conference at Queen's Park in Toronto, on Monday, Feb 26, 2024.

Document 292 of 500

Trudeau says 51st state not a joke: Trump annexation talk driven by desire for Canada's critical minerals, PM tells economic summit

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeau-says-51st-state-not-joke/docview/3164584588/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+says+51st+state+not+a+joke%3A+Trump+annexation+talk+driven+by+desire+for+Canada%27s+critical+minerals%2C+PM+tells+economic+summit&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: American President Donald Trump raised a long list of complaints against Canada and said the Canada-U.S. boundary could disappear when he spoke with the prime minister Monday, according to details Justin Trudeau revealed behind closed doors on Friday, the Star has learned.
The Star first broke news that Trudeau told a group of nearly 200 business, industry and union leaders that Trump's statements about annexing Canada are not a joke and are driven in part by his desire to benefit from Canada's critical minerals.
But there was much more to Trudeau's revelations about his high-stakes call with the U.S. president hours before punishing tariffs were set to take effect - including that Trump suggested breaking a 1908 boundary treaty, was dismissive of Canada's contributions to Norad and rhymed off a litany of irritants that the White House has with Canada, according to five sources who were in the room.
All had been invited to Toronto to participate in the economic summit billed as a way to share information and plot tactics and strategy for the weeks ahead, in the hopes Canada can ultimately stave off U.S. tariffs permanently.
In a series of candid comments that were caught on a hot mic and heard by the Star, Trudeau first inquired if the media were out of earshot, saying, "Are we safe? Excellent." He then said, "I suggest that not only does the Trump administration know how many critical minerals we have but that may be even why they keep talking about absorbing us and making us the 51st state."
"They're very aware of our resources, of what we have, and they very much want to be able to benefit from those," Trudeau said in answer to a question from the floor, after media had been ushered out.
"But Mr. Trump has it in mind that one of the easiest ways of doing that is absorbing our country, and it is a real thing," Trudeau added.
A portion of Trudeau's closed-door comments was inadvertently broadcast. A worker was ordered to pull the plug on the speaker after staff realized the session was still audible.
"When the PM made that comment, you could have heard a pin drop," said Gary Sands of the Canadian Federation of Independent Grocers.
Trudeau went on to say Trump came armed with a list of trade and other irritants, and pointed to a 1908 treaty between the United States and Great Britain that established the 49th parallel as the boundary between the U.S. and the then-Dominion of Canada, suggesting it could be erased.
Trump's 1908 treaty reference sent Trudeau and his aides scrambling during the call to "Google it" in order to understand what the president was talking about, according to the sources who spoke on condition they not be named.
According to sources, Trudeau said he replied to Trump that the treaty was replaced by the Canadian Constitution.
He also reminded the president that Trudeau's father, former prime minister Pierre Elliott Trudeau, had patriated the constitution from Great Britain to make it clear that Canada has sovereignty over its own territory.
Trudeau said Trump appeared to have at hand a memo that the federal government believes was drafted not by Trump's cabinet secretaries and nominees - whom Canadian ministers have been dealing with and who have not flagged all those concerns - but by the White House, and that there were some errors in it.
One of the complaints Trump raised on the call, according to a source, was an erroneous claim that U.S. banks can't operate in Canada, an assertion Trump first made while speaking to reporters on Monday. The Canadian Bankers Association refuted that, and says 16 U.S.-based bank subsidiaries and branches with around $113 billion in assets are currently operating in Canada.
Trudeau told the group that Trump raised with him Canada's level of defence spending, and questioned whether Canada even does "anything" on defence and the North American Aerospace Defense Command (Norad), which provides joint command and air defence systems on the northern half of the continent, according to sources.
Trump did not appear to have been briefed in detail on Canada's $1.3-billion border plan, Trudeau said to his audience, although Canadian ministers and diplomats who have presented it to Trump's cabinet secretaries and nominees say they've been assured that the president had been told of Canada's plan.
Three sources said Trudeau relayed to them some of Trump's comments in the aggressive tone the president had used on the call, with one saying it was a "pretty good" Trump impression, and another characterizing it not as an imitation but as Trudeau recalling what the president said to him.
Trudeau said Trump's cabinet secretaries all support the president, but added that they are also thoughtful in listening to Canada's arguments, and that lines of communication into the White House are still chaotic as the new administration settles in.
In December, the Trudeau government pledged to tighten border security to address Trump's purported concerns about illegal fentanyl smuggling and undocumented migrants that he claims are "pouring" across the Canada-U.S. border - even though Ottawa says Canada is not a significant source of either.
Trudeau told Trump on Monday that his Liberal government would throw in another $200 million to fight organized crime and fentanyl smuggling, and would appoint a "fentanyl czar," add Mexican cartels to the list of terrorist entities,and assured the president that 10,000 front-line personnel "are and will be" working on border security in this country.
After that, Trump agreed to delay his decision to implement 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian products with a 10 per cent levy on Canadian oil until March 4. Trump posted on social media that he was "very pleased with this initial outcome" and the tariffs would be paused "to see whether or not a final economic deal with Canada can be structured."
That's raised concerns that he may wield the tariff threat in order to force changes to the Canada-U.S.-Mexico free trade agreement, which Trump has ordered his officials to review and report by April 1 on how to correct "unfair" trade practices.
In the portion of the prime minister's remarks to the summit that were open to media, Trudeau won applause when he emphasized the need to drop interprovincial trade barriers.
"It's about time we had genuine free trade within Canada," he said.
The prime minister said all premiers support the idea, but there are "challenges within bureaucracies" which insist, " 'It'll be hard to recognize teaching certificates from one part of the country versus another,' 'It'll be hard to get proper labour mobility,' or 'There's a reason we have a specific tire requirement on our trucks for our province, or a specific need for a first aid kit in those trucks.' "
Speaking to reporters, Labour Minister Steve MacKinnon said he was inspired to see that everyone at the summit was committed to working to improve Canada's strategic advantages, adding, "The message I want to send to Canadians is this country is strong. Don't screw with us."
Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford, running for a third term as Ontario premier in a snap election he called for Feb. 27, scoffed at the summit as he campaigned Friday at an auto parts plant in Scarborough.
"I don't need to be in a room looking at the people I've talked to 1,000 times," Ford told reporters.
"You know what the Trump administration wants? They've been very, very clear," Ford said. "They need to tighten up the border."
"Let's stop the flow of drugs going back and forth across the border," he added, recommending retired general Rick Hillier as the fentanyl czar.
"I'm sick and tired of politicians talking. Let's get the helicopters in the air, the planes and the boots and the drones and the ships going up and down the water. I'm done with the talking. Let's show some action."
Industry Minister François-Philippe Champagne said the government isn't rethinking its critical minerals strategy, which he has pitched as a way to deepen co-operation with the U.S. He said it gives Canada "leverage" in its talks with Trump.
"They need us more than ever," he said "They understand, particularly the military guys ... that they cannot rebuild their industrial base, their defence, military industrial base, without Canada."
In an interview, Beth Burke, head of the Canadian American Business Council, said most Americans don't believe Trump is serious about his annexation threats, but see them as a negotiating strategy.
"While I know it is wildly offensive to Canadians, I think a lot of Americans think he's joking and see it as a joke and don't fully understand and appreciate the back story of how offensive it is," Burke said.
With files from Rob Ferguson
CAPTION: Audience members say Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, pictured at Evergreen Brick Works on Friday, told an economic summit that lines of communication into the White House are still chaotic as the new administration settles in.Katherine KY Cheng GETTY IMAGES
CREDIT: Tonda MacCharles Toronto Star Josh Rubin Toronto Star With files from Rob Ferguson

Document 293 of 500

Doug Ford under attack in Super Bowl ads

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-under-attack-super-bowl-ads/docview/3164584559/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+under+attack+in+Super+Bowl+ads&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford's campaign fumble is getting the Super Bowl spotlight.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie will be airing two different 30-second commercials during the NFL championship game Sunday night between the Kansas City Chiefs and the Philadelphia Eagles.
With Ontario voters headed to the polls on Feb. 27, Crombie's team wants viewers of what is expected to be the year's most-watched TV broadcast to see for themselves what Ford said last Monday.
In a "hot mic" gaffe at his Etobicoke campaign office, the Progressive Conservative leader said he wanted Donald Trump to win last November's U.S. presidential election.
"On election day, was I happy this guy won? One hundred per cent I was. Then the guy pulled out the knife and f---ing yanked it in me," said Ford, not realizing a media pool camera was still rolling.
The Liberals have cut a new version of a 60-second ad they released Tuesday on the misstep.
"Doug Ford just said he was happy Donald Trump won the election. Happy? Was he happy Canadians might lose their jobs?" intones a female narrator with footage of Trump boasting "the tariff will be substantial" and that he'd like to see "Canada become our 51st state."
"Or does he just care so little about the people of this province that he saw an attack on Ontario as an opportunity for himself," she continues before a clip of Ford telling reporters in January that he "needs a clear mandate" to deal with Trump, which is why he called a $189-million election 15 months early.
The second Liberal spot airing on Super Bowl Sunday features Crombie talking about Ontario's health-care challenges.
"Leadership is about priorities and for seven years, Doug Ford has had the wrong ones," she says against the backdrop of video of her touring a hospital and chatting with doctors and nurses.
"The result? A health-care system in crisis. People are afraid to get sick. As premier, I'll have one priority: fixing our health care," the Liberal leader continues.
"We'll take the money that Ford wastes on bad deals and invest it where it matters, starting with getting you a family doctor," she continues.
"I'm Bonnie Crombie, the new leader of Ontario's Liberals and I'll get more for you."
A senior Liberal official, speaking confidentially in order to discuss strategy, said the Super Bowl play is a big one for a party that was in office from 2003 until 2018 but finished third in seats in the past two elections behind Ford's Tories and the New Democrats now led by Marit Stiles.
"It's been a long time since we have had the kind of fundraising machine and clear, focused message that makes this kind of advertising possible," said the Grit insider.
"When you talk about what actually matters to people, you'll quickly find that people want to get behind you and support that message."
The Trump blunder is potentially harmful to Ford's re-election bid because he has framed the campaign as a referendum on who could best deal with the U.S. president's threatened 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian goods.
CAPTION: This Ontario Liberal ad will be aired during the Super Bowl and targets recent comments made by Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford.This Ontario Liberal ad outlines their plans for health care.Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford during a press conference in Etobicoke on Feb. 3, 2025.

Document 294 of 500

Bonnie Crombie must take a big swing on a health care election: Getting caught up in tariff debates will only hurt the Liberals, writes Scott Reid. Better to focus on the things Ontarians care about: health care and affordability

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/bonnie-crombie-must-take-big-swing-on-health-care/docview/3164584534/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Bonnie+Crombie+must+take+a+big+swing+on+a+health+care+election%3A+Getting+caught+up+in+tariff+debates+will+only+hurt+the+Liberals%2C+writes+Scott+Reid.+Better+to+focus+on+the+things+Ontarians+care+about%3A+health+care+and+affordability&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=OP.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If the world was fair and Ontario was operating according to typical rules of political gravity, Bonnie Crombie would be winning. Or, at the very least, Doug Ford would be faltering.
Why? Because that's what the numbers say.
Ontario voters say it's time for a change - fully 70 per cent of them.
Ontario voters say the province is headed in the wrong direction - at least 68 per cent of them.
And Ontario voters - about 60 per cent of them - say that Doug Ford is more interested in rewarding his friends than addressing key priorities like affordability and health care.
These are the sort of numbers, coming from pollster Relay Strategies, that chew up and spit out incumbents. The kind of conditions that sweep opposition leaders into office and Premiers into history.
Unfortunately for Crombie, the world isn't always fair. And so far, Ontario is defying political gravity, holding Ford high above his rivals. High as in half of all intended voters in the province. High as in historically high.
All of this leaves Crombie and her Liberals with an excruciating choice.
Doug Ford is succeeding because all that anyone in Ontario can talk about or think about is the orange menace of Donald Trump and his would-be devastating tariffs. As if on script with the PC war room, the first ten days of this election have been dominated by ransom notes from the oval office. And on that issue the voters are decided: Doug Ford is their preferred leader to manage the Trump tariff crisis.
This is true even as health care remains a top-of-mind concern in every part of the province, with 2.5 million-plus people, parents and patients living without a family doctor. Even as the desire for change remains steady and stubborn, Trump is setting the terms of our provincial election campaign. And that is rewarding Ford.
To get in the game, to crack the conversation and to find some currency, Crombie and her team must feel a sickening lure toward the topic of tariffs. If protecting Ontario jobs from MAGA madness is the issue of the day, don't the Liberals risk looking irrelevant and being looked over by talking about anything else?
Except voters don't want change when it comes to fighting Trump. And they don't think we're headed in the wrong direction when it comes to Ford's response to White House hostility. A focus on tariffs might push Crombie higher in the headlines, but it won't pry voters away from Ford.
Worse than that, a move toward tariffs might surrender key territory to the NDP, leaving the Liberals stranded between a rock and a hopeless place.
The only choice is the hard choice: to find a way, any way, to make this campaign about health care and affordability and Doug Ford's indifference toward these core responsibilities of office. The case for change must be made forcefully with a combination of pointed advertising, powerful campaign appearances and resonating stories from families with no family doctor. It will require specificity, intensity and probably some luck. It also has to happen immediately while tariffs are temporarily paused and voters are perhaps again willing to be reminded about what else they're looking for from a premier.
Crombie needs to take a big swing. She needs to take a big risk. Not by shifting strategy but by sticking with it. Not by taking the fight to where Ford wants it to be. But by forcing the fight to where she needs it to be.
Better to fight with hope on health care than fail for certain on tariffs.
Scott Reid is a principal at Feschuk.Reid, a CTV political analyst and was a Senior Adviser in the office of Prime Minister Paul Martin.
CAPTION: Nathan Denette THE CANADIAN PRESS
CREDIT: Scott Reid;

Subject: Provincial elections; Political campaigns; Voters; Tariffs

Document 295 of 500

What the leaders of Ontario's political parties must to do with the election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/what-leaders-ontarios-political-parties-must-do/docview/3164584448/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=What+the+leaders+of+Ontario%27s+political+parties+must+to+do+with+the+election&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario is going to the polls in late February to decide which party - and which party leader - will helm the province for the next four years. As we are still early in the campaign, the Star asked four political commentators to weigh in on what each leader needs to do over the coming weeks to give their party a chance at winning. Here are their responses.
Jaime Watt - Doug Ford shouldn't try to make sense of a madman. He should protect Ontarians
Robin V. Sears - Marit Stiles needs to focus on storytelling and authenticity.
Jo-Ann Roberts - The Greens have their strengths and they should play to them
Scott Reid - Bonnie Crombie should forget tariffs and focus on what Ontarians care about: health care and affordability

Document 296 of 500

Doug Ford has to convince Ontarians he is their protector: Ford has to persuade voters that he is the best one to deal with the orange menace to our south, writes Jaime Watt

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/doug-ford-has-convince-ontarians-he-is-their/docview/3164584300/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+has+to+convince+Ontarians+he+is+their+protector%3A+Ford+has+to+persuade+voters+that+he+is+the+best+one+to+deal+with+the+orange+menace+to+our+south%2C+writes+Jaime+Watt&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=OP.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: My editor has asked me to answer a simple question: How can Doug Ford's PCs win - and win bigger?
I'm not sure I'm best to answer this question. When Mr. Ford and I faced off as leaders of dueling campaigns during the 2010 mayoral election - he not only beat me, he crushed me.
What's more, the Progressive Conservative leader enjoys a substantial, some would say unsurmountable, lead in the polls.
But here goes.
The temptation will be to frame the ballot question as "Which of the party leaders do you trust to go head-to-head with Donald Trump and win?" That would be a mistake. It would be akin to looking for someone to tame a tornado.
The president is not a rational actor and it is a fool's errand to think that anyone can effectively go toe-to-toe with him. It is a battle you won't win because it is a battle that can't be won.
Rather, if Ford wants to win and win big, then the ballot question needs to be: "Which of the leaders do you trust to protect you, your family and Ontario from the economic shocks of the Trump administration?"
It is framing the question this way that will see the PCs returned to the legislature with a strengthened majority.
And it is a question which is, simply put, a gift to Ford and his campaign.
The chaos that has emerged from south of the border has provoked both fury and fear in Canadians - including Ontarians - unlike anything in recent memory.
Ontarians are looking for their leaders to stand up for them, to be sure. Matching tariffs, border-state diplomacy, ad campaigns aimed at American voters, and business-to-business pressure are all necessary tactics. Those deal with the fury.
But the fear must also be addressed. And that's where Ontarians are looking to their own province's leaders to protect them. Because it's not just theoretical macroeconomic shocks that has them worried; rather, it is the price shock of suddenly more expensive gas, groceries and other imported products that has them up at night.
No question, calling an early election is always a risk. But the timing of the political contest comes with a calculated set of strategic advantages for the PCs:
1. With Justin Trudeau and the Liberals still in power federally, the PCs can rely on oppositional sentiment toward Trudeau.
2. Doug Ford, personally, has never been so popular. Just look at how comfortably he has assumed the face of the "Team Canada" response.
3. The PCs have a major cash advantage that will allow them to dominate the airwaves.
4. Liberal operatives are distracted by the federal leadership race. Case in point: they've been slow to nominate candidates in key ridings.
5. The government's fiscal situation is not going to improve anytime soon - and economic tensions with the United States won't help. Things might not be great now, but they won't be any better in fifteen months, when Ford would have had to call this election anyway.
Taken together, these factors will undoubtedly be major contributors should the PCs win re-election.
But none will matter as much as Doug Ford's ability to convince Ontarians he is the best candidate not to negotiate with a madman but to protect their pocketbooks from a madman.
Elections aren't won on hypotheticals - they're won on trust. And right now, Ontarians don't need a leader who promises to "figure it out" when the storm hits. The storm has hit. They want someone who has been through the storm before and knows how to steer the ship.
Doug Ford's path to victory isn't about making Ontarians believe he can tame Donald Trump - it's about proving he can shield them from the chaos that Trump is likely to spread for four years.
If the Progressive Conservative leader can make that case, this election won't just be a win for the PCs - it will be a landslide.
Jaime Watt is the executive chairman of Navigator Ltd.
and a Conservative strategist. He is a freelance columnist for the Star.
CAPTION: Bob Tymczyszyn the st. catharines Standard
CREDIT: Jaime Watt;
