Document 310 of 500

Canadians, show your patriotism

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/canadians-show-your-patriotism/docview/3164230981/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canadians%2C+show+your+patriotism&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.15&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canadians try to do the right thing, while recognizing that the right thing is often fuzzy and doing it might have unexpected consequences. Let's think this through, we say.
But these days, we need moral clarity, spoken right out loud.
MAGA Republicans try to do the right thing, while claiming that the right thing is always crystal clear, but doing it might have unexpected consequences. Firing those Jan. 6 FBI agents might bite them in the years to come. But who cares, this is now.
Doing the right thing matters. I have been so heartened by Canadians coming together after U.S. President Donald Trump's dark convulsive tariff attack on Canada. It wasn't right. It wouldn't be right to back down. The quest to find and buy Canadian-made goods felt good, like backing a great hockey team called Canada.
But that's obviously splendid. Let's move further into the murk. The U.S. under Presidents Elon Musk and Trump is a brutal place right now for almost everyone except the ultra-rich. But that's their gilded problem, not ours.
What concerns us is what the U.S. will do to the rest of the planet. Like a bug, Trump has landed on Canada, Mexico, Greenland, China, Europe, and any nation receiving help from USAID. The damage will be generational.
As in Gaza. President Joe Biden believed he was doing the right thing, mired as he was in misunderstanding the lessons of the Second World War. So, it was easy for Canadians to avoid mentioning the Israeli slaughter of civilians. Americans are violent. This is what they do.
I admired Canadians of many identities and beliefs who marched in the streets against the catastrophe in Gaza. Good for them. Those Canadians who agreed with them but did not speak up for fear of being targeted will suffer "moral injury."
It means the damage done to their conscience and values - the soul, I think - from deciding to go along to get along. They stayed silent.
But what are we to do with Trump, who envisions owning Gaza and clearing and renovating the place?
Right now, Gaza is a "demolition site" but "this could be the Riviera of the Middle East," Trump said, with Palestinians taken from their land to somewhere else.
They would "be thrilled," he said later in the Oval Office with Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, who kept smiling - while Chief of Staff Susie Wiles blanched - but might have been taken aback. Netanyahu knows crazy when he sees it.
As Naomi Klein wrote about Gaza on Bluesky to which I urge TwiX people to move with haste, "It's not a demolition site it's a mass grave. It's not a demolition site it's a crime scene. It's not a demolition site it's a homeland."
Trump's shocks will continue. In the years ahead, we might see a global trade war, ethnic cleansing, mass global death from a virus without vaccines, tens of millions killed by climate change, etc.
Canadians will have to shed their habitual timidity and speak up. What does Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre think of emptying Gaza? Why does he back American-style life sentences for drug crimes? Is Poilievre just a weak-tea version of Trump?
Why did Ontario Premier Doug Ford rejoice at Trump's re-election? Is Alberta Premier Danielle Smith a mere extension of Trump, a Wyoming to his Florida?
Why did Mark Carney refuse Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's prolonged invitation to join the Liberal government? Maybe he has no hunger to lead.
What if American troops cross the border? What if they claim our north?
Canadians, you need to speak up now, fight back, hold onto all the patriotism you showed since Trump first threatened to gun us down.
It's the best kind of patriotism.
Heather Mallick is a Toronto-based columnist covering
current affairs for the Star. Follow her on Bluesky:
@HMallick.bsky.social
CAPTION: The shocks will continue from U.S. President Donald Trump, who envisions owning Gaza and clearing and renovating the place into the "Riviera of the Middle East," writes Heather Mallick.Abdel Kareem Hana The Associated Press

Consider moving the new science centre to Old City Hall

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/consider-moving-new-science-centre-old-city-hall/docview/3164230714/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Consider+moving+the+new+science+centre+to+Old+City+Hall&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Old City Hall is a monument to our big-time ambitions. Letting it rot is a reminder how we've let Toronto decay, Feb. 6
For the city of Toronto, the vacant Old City Hall will become a money pit. There is no viable commercialization path for it. It's too big for another museum and unsuited for transformation into a mall. So, let's transfer it to the province on condition it becomes the home for the new science centre. The province picks up the cost to renovate, saving the cost of building a smaller, less than useful new science centre. It is far more accessible than Ontario Place, and will be a prestigious building. Any unneeded space can be either commercialized or converted to residential. And it will be ready far sooner than any new build.
Joel Berson, Etobicoke
Hands off Old City Hall
Talk about nostalgia. Edward Keenan's article about the Old City Hall was an eye opener to perhaps the most iconic building in Toronto. Please find a way to preserve and protect this important part of Toronto's history. For me, Keenan's tribute brought back memories. It was about 1955 when my motorcycle club the Humber Valley Riders was invited to parade down Bay Street. We arrived at the entrance to the front doors and were greeted by a man with the whitest hair I had ever seen. Sure enough it was Mayor Nathan Phillips for which the square is now named. The mayor thanked us for our charitable contributions to the city and shortly thereafter we roared off to Sunnyside which was another landmark until it was torn down. Keep your treasures. They are the mark of a great city.
Al Truscott, Collingwood
Don't split the vote. Choose the best progressive candidate in each riding
Doug Ford's hot mic blunder is a weapon his rivals are turning against him, Feb. 5
Despite Marit Stiles asserting that NDP members want candidates to vote for even in ridings where they have almost no chance to win doesn't apply to me and several of the other NDPers and Greens in my riding. With our first past the post system vote splitting allows the Conservatives to win without majorities. Some of us are working for and voting for Liberals who, in our opinions, are much better than the Conservatives. It's time the Liberals, the NDPers and the Greens worked together. Choose the best progressive candidate in each riding.
Peggy Stevens, Newmarket
Ford too busy creating his own emergencies to read TrumpDoug Ford's hot mic blunder is a weapon his rivals are turning against him, Feb. 5
Too bad Premier Doug Ford did not have the foresight to read the writing on the wall as he rooted for U.S. President Donald Trump. Oh, wait I forgot Ford's mind was filled with his own emergencies. No, these weren't health care, homelessness, housing, drugs or crime. They were booze in corner stores, bike lanes, underground highways, the science centre, Ontario Place and his own nonsense election.
Brian Mellor, Midland
Can Canada come up with 10,000 border guards?
Trump agrees to 30-day pause on tariffs to Canada and Mexico, Feb. 3
Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum promising U.S. President Donald Trump to station 10,000 troops along the Mexican-U.S.A. border placed Canada in a difficult tariff bargaining situation. Therefore, the Trudeau government also promised Trump 10,000 border guards, plus other measures to stave off tariffs. This has gained Canada a 30-day delay and some breathing room.
This promise was made even though an estimated 12 million asylum seekers entered the U.S.A. from Mexico. Actual numbers of asylum seekers who entered the U.S.A. from Canada is unknown but certainly is minuscule in comparison. American authorities have reported seizing 21,000 pounds of fentanyl coming from Mexico and 46 pounds from Canada in 2024.
There is no doubt the RCMP should have been doing much more to trace precursor drugs needed to make illicit fentanyl as well as locating rogue drug laboratories. Let's not forget proper border security is essential to protect Canada's national security and Canadian lives. But with the Canadian military and the RCMP both struggling to recruit, coming up with 10,000 border guards seems a high hill to climb with a month to do so.
Larry Comeau, retired RCMP Superintendent, Ottawa
Canada should move closer to the small countries of the Arctic Council Donald Trump's rage toward Canada hides a deeper agenda, Feb. 6
U.S. President Donald Trump threatened to annex Canada by economic force and Greenland (a territory belonging to Denmark) by military force. It therefore seems desirable that Canada move closer to Denmark and form an alliance with it in order to better monitor their northern territories, which, with global warming, will be increasingly coveted. In fact, a rapprochement should take place between all the small countries of the Arctic Council: Canada, Denmark, Finland, Iceland, Norway and Sweden. This is to make it clear to the two other countries on the council, the powerful United States (which is no longer trustworthy) and Russia, that they do not intend to cede their territories. Recently, Denmark announced that it wanted to devote some $3 billion to security in the Arctic and the North Atlantic. At the very least, Canada should follow suit and show its teeth too.
Sylvio Le Blanc, Montreal
The reasons for the decline in early childhood educatorsPercentage of early childhood educators in Ontario child care declining, despite goal, Dec. 23
Government officials estimate Ontario could be short 8,500 early childhood educators, Jan. 14
I have been a Registered Early Childhood Educator (RECE) for the past 20 years, and never before has it been harder to keep qualified staff in the field. Low wages, lack of benefits, the aftereffects of COVID and the province's push to deliver $10-a-day childcare are all contributing. With the cost of living rising, educators are choosing to leave or not enter the field at all.
While there has been some increase in salary in 2025, it isn't enough to keep qualified RECE in the sector. Some programs are hiring non-ECE personnel to fill positions and spaces, but that is a short-term solution to a larger problem and a slippery slope for the field. It's time the province did more than just talk about solutions and finally took meaningful action to attract and retain qualified RECE with better working conditions, higher wages, benefits, and pensions. Only then can we truly create a child-care system that works for both families and educators.
Zaneta Pereira, Toronto
CAPTION: Consider Old City Hall for the new science centre, a reader writes.

Health care, homes in focus: Crombie, Ford spar over physician shortage, Stiles vows 'real rent control'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/health-care-homes-focus/docview/3164229508/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Health+care%2C+homes+in+focus%3A+Crombie%2C+Ford+spar+over+physician+shortage%2C+Stiles+vows+%27real+rent+control%27&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie is rubbing salt on Doug Ford's wounds over the chronic health-care challenges that ail Ontario.
Blasting the Progressive Conservative leader as the Feb. 27 election campaign began its second week, Crombie noted treatment of patients in hospital corridors has doubled and the doctor shortage has worsened since he took office in 2018. "Doug Ford said he'd end hallway medicine. He didn't get it done. He said he'd cut your taxes. He didn't get it done. He said he'd build 1.5 million homes. He didn't get it done," she said Thursday in Scarborough.
Crombie - whose bright red campaign bus is emblazoned with the slogan "2.5 Million People Have No Family Doctor: This is Doug Ford's Ontario" - has promised to cure the physician shortage through a slew of measures that could cost $3.1 billion.
They include spending $250 million over four years in targeted retention aimed at 2,400 doctors who have left family medicine or are nearing retirement, offering bonuses of $150,000 to repatriate Canadian doctors from abroad to Ontario, and recruiting 650 of the 6,000 family doctors currently working in other disciplines.
She also wants to help doctors by slashing "the administrative burden taking up 50 per cent of their time."
"Let's get rid of fax machines at long last and implement a centralized referral system and ensure that they have the right staff (and) offer more evening and weekend care," she said.
In Waterloo, Ford, who is seeking his third term in office, blamed previous Liberal governments for the shortage of physicians.
"If the Liberals didn't cut nurses and they didn't cut medical (school) seats, we wouldn't be in this spot," he told reporters after receiving the endorsement of the 25,000-member Ontario Pipe Trades Council.
"We've hired Dr. Jane Philpott to connect every single Ontarian with a family doctor," Ford said of the former federal Liberal health minister who is now director of the medical school at Queen's University in Kingston.
On the eve of Ford's snap election call, the PCs announced $1.4 billion in new funding to help Philpott expand the province's network of primary health-care teams with a goal of getting every Ontarian a family doctor by 2029.
"There's no doubt that people have been waiting a long time, too long, frankly, to get connected to a family care practitioner in their community," MPP Sylvia Jones, serving as Ford's health minister, acknowledged on Jan. 27.
But that announcement raised questions as to whether the $1.4 billion is enough because there's a huge discrepancy between numbers provided by Jones (Dufferin-Caledon) and the Ontario Medical Association.
The OMA says there are currently 2.5 million Ontarians without a family doctor, and predicts that figure will rise to 4.4 million next year as the population grows and doctors retire or leave the province.
But Jones maintained just two million Ontarians will need to be connected to a family doctor in the next four years.
Ford boasted his government approved two new medical schools in Ontario. However, students will not graduate from them for several more years.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles, meanwhile, was in Hamilton to promise "real rent control" that would further protect tenants from higher costs and renovictions.
"After seven years of Doug Ford, rent is higher than ever. It's the biggest expense in your family's budget and it's only getting worse," said Stiles.
Later, she headed to Sault Ste. Marie, where her party hopes to pick up a key northern riding vacated by the retirement of veteran Tory Ross Romano.
Also Thursday, Green Leader Mike Schreiner was in Guelph to tout his party's plan to increase Ontario Disability Support Program and Ontario Works payments and build 310,000 affordable homes.
"Poverty in Ontario is escalating at an alarming rate," said Schreiner, noting the Association of Municipalities of Ontario found 81,000 people were homeless in the province last year.
"Over the past two years, food bank usage in Ontario has increased by 78 per cent," he said.
"Doug Ford isn't just failing to solve the problem - he's making it worse by keeping Ontarians with disabilities locked in legislated poverty."
The Tory campaign is confident it has weathered the worst of his Monday gaffe when he was caught on a hot mic saying that he "100 per cent" wanted Donald Trump to win the American presidency last November. "On election day, was I happy this guy won? One hundred per cent I was. Then the guy pulled out the knife and f--king yanked it in me," Ford said at his campaign office in Etobicoke.
Since the misstep, the PC leader, who has framed the snap Ontario election as a referendum on who could best deal with Trump's threatened 25 per cent tariffs against Canada, has faced questions about how he could have supported someone who had cut off personal protective equipment imports to Canada during the COVID-19 pandemic. "There's Trump 1.0 and then there was Trump 2.0," he said Wednesday.
CAPTION: Bonnie Crombie, centre, appears at a campaign event Thursday in Scarborough. The Ontario Liberal leader said if she's elected, she'll spend $250 million over four years to retain doctors who have left family medicine or are working abroad or in other disciplines.Chris Young The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief Rob Ferguson and Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Document 313 of 500

We need to hold onto our rage

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/we-need-hold-onto-our-rage/docview/3164229196/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=We+need+to+hold+onto+our+rage&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.15&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canadians are mad. Oh boy, are we mad! I feel it; I bet you feel it too. Almost every commentator I read this week is trying to outdo all the others in channelling our collective rage at Donald Trump.
Anger can be good. It can propel you into doing things you might not dare to do otherwise. Especially when it's combined with a dose of humiliation - i.e. how could we have allowed ourselves to get into a position where we can so easily be shoved around?
We should find a way to bottle the feeling we have now. If we're really going to do what needs to be done, we'll need a way to remind ourselves of the sour taste of this week. Because it will fade, inevitably, and as it fades our resolve to escape the trap we're in will weaken. We'll need a sip of rage to keep going when the going gets tough.
And it will get tough, very quickly. Right now there's an across-the-board consensus. When it comes to what Canada must do in the face of the Trump threat, you can hardly tell who's talking. Pierre Poilievre, Mark Carney, Chrystia Freeland, the premiers, every business leader and analyst who pops up on the news shows - they're all saying essentially the same thing:
We've got to tear down those notorious internal barriers to trade. We've got to diversify our trade. Explore new markets for our energy and critical minerals. Be more competitive. Get serious about our national security. And we've got to do it now!
The truth is that apparent consensus is a mile wide and an inch deep (OK, maybe six inches by now). We're at the beginning of a once-in-several-generations national debate on what kind of Canada we want, a debate that should be the focus of the fast-approaching federal election campaign. And the deeper we get into that debate, the more this week's consensus will crumble.
There will no doubt be some easy wins in the next few months. Some of those internal trade barriers will be torn down, finally. Ontario Premier Doug Ford promises "full steam ahead" on that front; British Columbia wines will soon be freely available in the LCBO, he says. Hooray!
Other barriers will fall simply because they're silly. Did you know you have to be an Ontario resident to buy and sell bullfrogs in this province? I didn't till I checked. Hopefully we'll soon have free trade in bullfrogs.
But other barriers will be much more contentious. Most fish caught in Newfoundland must be processed there; ending that restriction could cost hundreds of jobs where they're badly needed. Some provinces will pay lip service to national co-operation while blocking it when it matters.
Quebec Premier François Legault was out this week simultaneously pledging solidarity against Trump while hemming and hawing on whether his province is prepared to do the biggest thing it could do on that front - drop its opposition to a cross-Canada energy pipeline. There's still "no social acceptability" for that in Quebec, he said (i.e. it's not popular and he won't take a political risk).
More broadly, the vision for a tougher, Trump-proof Canada sketched out this week by Poilievre and the leading Liberal leadership candidates skews to the right. They disagree on details, but generally the direction is toward cutting taxes, speeding up big projects like pipelines, boosting energy exports and making the country more attractive to investors, even if access to the U.S. market isn't guaranteed.
All of that is highly contentious. The left in all its manifestations - the NDP, Greens, some provinces, labour unions, environmentalists, many Indigenous organizations - will soon mobilize against it on the grounds that opposing Trump should not involve Trumpifying Canada.
One person's pointless red tape or inefficient internal trade barrier is another person's vital health and safety regulation or guarantee of a local job.
That's when this week's consensus will fray and may finally come apart. Governments, especially the new federal government to be chosen this year, will have to bull through some formidable obstacles. And to do that they will need the support of a public fed up with dithering and delay.
That's when we'll need to reach for that bottle of rage and take a big gulp.
Andrew Phillips is a Toronto-based staff columnist for the Star's Opinion page. Reach him via email: aphillips@thestar.ca

Full text: Move the new science centre to Old City Hall
Old City Hall is a monument to our big-time ambitions. Letting it rot is a reminder how we've let Toronto decay,
Keenan, Feb. 6
For the city of Toronto, the vacant Old City Hall will become a money pit. There is no viable commercialization path for it. It is too big for another museum and unsuited for transformation into a mall. So, let's transfer it to the province on condition it becomes the home for the new science centre. The province picks up the cost to renovate, saving the cost of building a smaller, less than useful new science centre. It is far more accessible than Ontario Place, and will be a prestigious building. Any unneeded space can be either commercialized or converted to residential. And it will be ready far sooner than any new build.
Joel Berson, Etobicoke
We must treasure city's landmarks
Talk about nostalgia. Edward Keenan's column about the Old City Hall was an eye opener to perhaps the most iconic building in Toronto. Please find a way to preserve and protect that which is essence of Toronto's history. For me, Keenan's tribute brought back memories that are forever etched in my memory. It was about 1955 when my motorcycle club, the Humber Valley Riders, was invited to parade down Bay Street. We arrived at the entrance to the front doors and were greeted by a man with the whitest hair I had ever seen. Sure enough, it was Mayor Nathan Phillips, for which the square is now named. The mayor thanked us for our charitable contributions to the city and, shortly thereafter, we roared off to Sunnyside, which was another landmark until it was torn down. Keep your treasures. They are the mark of a great city.
Al Truscott, Collingwood, Ont.
Splitting the vote helps Tories win
Doug Ford's hot mic blunder is a weapon his rivals are turning against him, Feb. 5
Despite NDP Leader Marit Stiles asserting that her party members want candidates to vote for, even in ridings where they have almost no chance to win, doesn't apply to me and several of the other NDPers and Greens. With our first-past-the-post system, vote-splitting allows the Conservatives to win without majorities. Some of us are working for and voting for Liberals who, in our opinions, are much better than the Conservatives. It's time the Liberals, the NDPers and the Greens worked together. Choose the best progressive candidate in each riding.
Peggy Stevens, Newmarket
Ford created his own emergencies
Doug Ford's hot mic blunder is a weapon his rivals are turning against him, Feb. 5
Too bad Premier Doug Ford did not have the foresight to read the writing on the wall as he rooted for U.S. President Donald Trump. Oh wait, I forgot Ford's mind was filled with his own emergencies. No it wasn't health care, homelessness, housing, drugs or crime. It was booze in corner stores, bike lanes, underground highways, the Science Centre, Ontario Place and his own nonsense election.
Brian Mellor, Midland, Ont.s
Can Canada even recruit 10,000 border guards?
Trump agrees to 30-day pause on tariffs to Canada and Mexico, Feb. 3
Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum promising U.S. President Donald Trump to station 10,000 troops along the border placed Canada in a difficult bargaining position. Therefore, the Trudeau government also promised 10,000 border guards, plus other measures, to delay Trump's tariffs.
This promise was made even though an estimated 12 million asylum seekers entered the U.S. from Mexico, their entry aided by Mexican drug cartels. Actual numbers of asylum seekers who entered the U.S. from Canada is unknown, but certainly is minuscule in comparison. American authorities seized 21,000 pounds of fentanyl coming from Mexico and 46 pounds from Canada in 2024.
With the Canadian military 16,500 recruits short and the RCMP struggling to recruit to even meet attribution, coming up with 10,000 border guards seems a high hill to climb with a month to do so. There is no doubt the RCMP should have been doing much more to trace precursor drugs needed to make fentanyl, as well as locating rogue drug labs. Proper border security is essential to protect Canada's national security and Canadian lives.
Larry Comeau, retired RCMP superintendent, Ottawa
Canada should align with Arctic Council partners
Donald Trump's rage toward Canada hides a deeper agenda, McQuaig, Feb. 6
U.S. President Donald Trump threatened to annex Canada by economic force and Greenland (a territory belonging to Denmark) by military force. It therefore seems desirable that Canada move closer to Denmark and form an alliance with it in order to better monitor their northern territories, which, with global warming, will be increasingly coveted. In fact, a rapprochement should take place between all the small countries of the Arctic Council: Canada, Denmark, Finland, Iceland, Norway and Sweden. This would make it clear to the two other countries on the council, the powerful United States (which is no longer trustworthy) and the great Russia, that they do not intend to cede their territories. Recently, Denmark announced it will devote $3 billion to security in the Arctic and North Atlantic. At the very least, Canada should follow suit and show its teeth, too.
Sylvio Le Blanc, Montreal
Early childhood educators need good pay, benefits
Officials estimate Ontario could be short 8,500 early childhood educators, Jan. 14
As a Registered Early Childhood Educator (RECE) for the past 20 years, I continue to see how retention of staff has been difficult, especially in the past five years after COVID and the expansion of $10-a-day child care. It has become harder to keep qualified RECEs, due to low wages and a lack of benefits at most non-profit and for-profit centres. With the cost of living rising, educators are choosing to leave or not enter the field at all. It's time for the province to take meaningful action to attract and retain qualified RECE with better working conditions, higher wages, benefits and pensions. Only then can we truly create a child-care system that works for both families and educators.
Zaneta Pereira, Toronto

Document 315 of 500

Canada has truly international thinkers, yet our largest province is poised to re-elect Doug Ford

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canada-has-truly-international-thinkers-yet-our/docview/3163835131/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada+has+truly+international+thinkers%2C+yet+our+largest+province+is+poised+to+re-elect+Doug+Ford&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: This is what makes Canadians different from Americans, Feb. 5
To this letter writer's splendid list of why Canadians should take pride in our country, let's add the fact that we don't idolize outlaws, and would never elect one as Prime Minister. We have truly international thinkers, like Margaret Atwood, author of "The Handmaid's Tale," that alerted the world to the dangerous misogyny of the religious far-right; Naomi Klein, whose books "No Logo" and "The Shock Doctrine" helped explain the greed of unfettered corporations, and how superpower leaders do outrageous things in order to deflect public awareness of their ultra-capitalist goals. Other international thinkers include Marshall McLuhan, John Ralston Saul, Malcolm Gladwell, David Frum and public intellectual Jordan Peterson, love him or hate him. Most Canadians understand that Donald Trump is primarily a real-estate developer who has zero empathy, who is beholden to oligarchs like himself, and who is playing a kind of primitive chess game with the world. And yet, despite our valuing Canada's natural splendour, our most populated province is poised to re-elect Doug Ford, a man who is primarily a shill for his developer friends, and who is the very opposite of an environmentalist.
Ron Charach, Toronto
Ford and Trump are of the same ilk
Why would anyone be surprised that Premier Doug Ford wanted U.S. President Donald Trump to win? They are of the same ilk. Both were born with silver spoons in their mouths and amazingly both elitists have convinced regular folk that they are one of them. They both support the wealthy. They say they will do things and then backtrack. Their politics are identical. When all is said and done it says more about the voters.
Roy Wilkie, Richmond Hill
Trump attracts a certain type
Premier Doug Ford said, "Election Day, was I happy this guy won? One hundred per cent I was. But then the guy pulled out the knife and f - king yanked it into us."
And that is why U.S. President Donald Trump loves the ignorant.
Ron Barrett, Hamilton
Why we all need to vote
In a democracy it is a responsibility of all citizens to vote. Yet in our last election, as I recall, 41 per cent of eligible voters failed to fulfil this task. We need major public announcements (i.e. commercials) stressing the necessity of voting. Even if one thinks no one is qualified or acceptable, someone will end up winning. We all need to assess the candidates and parties, and make a decision. Otherwise, we risk losing our democracy.
Elizabeth Mintoft, Toronto

While tightening our border, please stop the gunsDonald Trump postpones threatened tariffs for 30 days after Canada offers more border security measures, Feb. 4
I fully realize that tightening the border is in response to U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs. We are supposed to be targeting fentanyl and illegal border crossings on behalf of the United States. Do you think we could put in just a little extra effort on behalf of Canadians? Could we at least try and stop the guns?
Clyde McDonald, Bracebridge
Why is Canada not demanding the U.S. stop the flow of guns north?
These border issues between the U.S. and Canada appears to be very one-sided. U.S. President Donald Trump has demanded that Canada be responsible for stopping the flow of fentanyl and illegal migrants from flowing into the U.S. We are doing our level best to comply with this to ward off the threat of tariffs. My question is why haven't we in turn demanded the U.S. be responsible for stopping the flow of guns into Canada?
Gary Johnson, Stouffville ON
Where are Canada's border demands?
So, let me get this right. Canada is going to spend $1.3 billion to stop the flow of illegal immigrants and fentanyl into the U.S. while U.S. President Donald Trump isn't going to spend an additional red cent to stop the flow of illegal guns into Canada. What's wrong with this picture?
William Michajluk, Scarborough
Trump's plan for Gaza is insensitive and ignorantTrump says he wants US to take charge of Gaza Strip at press conference with Netanyahu, Feb. 4
U.S. President Donald Trump's comments on Gaza have sparked intense controversy. His suggestions of resettling Palestinians and redeveloping Gaza are not only insensitive but also ignorant. Although Islam promotes peace and justice,defending one's homeland is also a fundamental aspect of faith. As an Ahmadi Muslim, I feel Trump's remarks areoutrageous and I dread the potential effects they will have on ongoing cease fire talks and the risk of reigniting conflict in the region.
Nadia Ahmad, Vaughan
Where's the outrage? Where's the respect?
Where's the outrage, Canada, after U.S. President Donald Trump's press secretary told reporters that "Canada is bending the knee ..."? Trump is always complaining that Canada does not treat America with respect . But is this statement from Trump's press secretary respectful? Am I the only Canadian that is outraged by that statement?
Duncan Craig, Gravenhurst
Many Americans are horrified by Trump's actions
Please know that 48.3 per cent of United States voters did NOT vote for Donald Trump and are horrified by what he is doing to our relationships and people around the world. What is happening here is embarrassing and catastrophic on so many levels. All I can hope is that leaders and sane, compassionate people around the world will do what they can to thwart this egomaniac.
Melissa Wolff-Burke, Winchester, VA
Bar convicted felon Donald Trump from G-7 nation gathering in Alberta
Leaders of the U.S., France, Germany, Japan, the United Kingdom, Italy, and Canada, as well as the European Union, are expected to attend the June 15-17 gathering of G-7 nations in Kananaskis, Alberta, just west of Calgary. I sincerely hope we will not allow the convicted felon Donald Trump to set foot in our country - especially after his statements about forcing economic hardship on Canada to make it "his" 51st state.
Mike Priaro, Calgary AB
Canada should join BRICS alliance
If the U.S. President follows through on his threat to tear up NAFTA, then Canada has a few options. We can live without being a member of any trading alliance, as we did in pre-NAFTA days, or we can join some other alliance. The stagnant EU is one possibility, but it represents just five per cent of world population, 15 per cent of global GDP, and the war in Ukraine has left many European nations on the verge of a recession.
By contrast, the rapidly expanding BRICS alliance already represents 55 per cent of the world's population, 42 per cent of global GDP and its members include some of the most robust, fastest growing economies on earth. True, Canada has foolishly placed itself at odds with BRICS largest members - Russia, China and India - but history has shown that such relationships can be repaired. Who knows? It might even give Trump reason to reconsider his ultimatums.
Mike Ward, Duncan, BC
CAPTION: Author Margaret Atwood, one of Canada's great thinkers, gives a speech after receiving the H.C. Andersen Literature Prize 2024 at the concert hall Odeon in Odense, Denmark on Oct. 27, 2024. (Thomas Sjoerup/Ritzau Scanpix via AP)

Subject: Canadians; Presidents; Voters; Fentanyl; Tariffs

Document 316 of 500

Thieving turkeys, giant earmuffs and the Guess Who: The new Canadian comedy 'Universal Language' is an absurdist delight

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/thieving-turkeys-giant-earmuffs-guess-who-new/docview/3163834917/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Thieving+turkeys%2C+giant+earmuffs+and+the+Guess+Who%3A+The+new+Canadian+comedy+%27Universal+Language%27+is+an+absurdist+delight&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In the cockeyed Canada of Matthew Rankin's nutty comedy "Universal Language," Winnipeg is colourful and bustling while Montreal is dreary and almost comatose.
This depiction may run contrary to your imaginings, but free your mind and your compass will follow.
The film's whimsical Winnipeg is populated mainly by Iranian immigrants who must contend with such daily distractions as eyewear-stealing turkeys, walking Christmas trees and bank notes embedded in ice.
Montreal, meanwhile, looks like a grey urban hellhole from an old Soviet newsreel. No wonder the story's sad-faced protagonist, Matthew, played by filmmaker Rankin, is eager to quit his soul-destroying government job - where a man in a nearby cubicle sobs inconsolably - and take a bus home to see his elderly mom back in the Peg.
It's a city caught between countries and cultures - call it "Tehrannipeg" - in a droll entertainment Rankin describes as "an absurdist city symphony." It arrives with many laurels, among them the inaugural audience award at the Directors' Fortnight at the 2024 Cannes Film Festival. "Universal Language" was Canada's Oscar submission for the best international feature (it made the shortlist, but not the final five contenders). It's also likely the only movie with a soundtrack featuring both traditional Persian music and "These Eyes" by Winnipeg's own the Guess Who.
Everyone in Tehrannipeg speaks Farsi, Tim Hortons (where we're told there's "a relaxing view of the highway") serves Persian tea, and the currency is the Iranian rial, which happily rhymes with Louis Riel, the iconic Manitoba founder, Métis leader and rebel.
Yet the climate is distinctly Canadian. Snow and ice abound and the locals toil at places like the Winnipeg Earmuff Authority (where they make absurdly large and colourful earmuffs), the Kleenex Repository and a turkey store run by some kind of cowboy.
There's also an elementary school where the French teacher, Mr. Bilodeau (Mani Soleymanlou) furiously tells his rambunctious students, "All of you will fail!" and cancels classes because a nearsighted kid named Omid (Sobhan Javadi) had his glasses stolen by a rampaging gobbler.
Help might be on the way for Omid: two of his classmates, Negin (Rojina Esmaeili) and her sister Nazgol (Saba Vahedyousefi), find a 500-rial bank note embedded in ice they can use to buy him new glasses, if only they can get it out.
Matthew has his own quest: to find his missing mom, who used to live in a bungalow next to a chain saw shop.
It's disorienting, but don't worry; there's a tour guide on hand named Massoud (Pirouz Nemati), who resembles Groucho Marx. He proudly shows off local hot spots: a transit bench with a briefcase that's been abandoned since 1978 (it's been declared a UNESCO world heritage site); the scene of the Great Parallel Parking Incident of 1958; and a clock inside the Portage Place shopping mall, which we're told has no hands because it's "timeless."
The screenplay by Rankin, Pirouz Nemati and Ila Firouzabadi makes the setting seem at once natural and completely insane. It's like a child-centred adventure by the late Iranian master Abbas Kiarostami that somehow got mixed with the formalist whimsy of Wes Anderson (and Roy Andersson) and the surreal charms of Guy Maddin, a fellow Winnipegger.
Cinematographer Isabelle Stachtchenko frequently uses wide shots to capture antlike human activities against imposing structures. When her camera moves in close, it often seems distracted by other people and creatures, especially the town's beloved but pesky turkeys.
"That gobbler breathed life into my soul," a cowboy says, lamenting the passing of one of the birds.
This is Rankin's second feature. It follows "The Twentieth Century," a delightfully unhinged romp through the weird schemes of William Lyon Mackenzie King, played by Daniel Beirne, as he labours to become Canada's most eccentric prime minister.
The Winnipeg-born and Montreal-schooled Rankin is clearly enamoured with alternate Canadian history, leading us to wonder what's next? I'd love to see a similar take on Doug Ford's Ontario or Justin Trudeau's Ottawa.
As the credits roll and the last strains of "These Eyes" enliven a parade of strutting turkeys, one can't help but feel a peculiar sense of displacement, as if we've just stumbled out of a fever dream induced by a potent combination of maple syrup and saffron.
"Universal Language" leaves us in a state of delightful bewilderment, much like finding oneself suddenly transported to a Tim Hortons where the Timbits come with a steaming mug of existential crisis.
CAPTION: Universal Language Trailer #1 (2024)A walking Christmas tree is just one of many absurd sights in Matthew Rankin's "Universal Language."

The Trump effect on Canadian politics + Metrolinx forcing people out of their homes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/trump-effect-on-canadian-politics-metrolinx/docview/3163834651/se-2?accountid=15090

Ontario election: Bonnie Crombie slams Doug Ford for not curing the province's health-care woes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-election-bonnie-crombie-slams-doug-ford/docview/3163834649/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+election%3A+Bonnie+Crombie+slams+Doug+Ford+for+not+curing+the+province%27s+health-care+woes&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie is rubbing salt on Doug Ford's wounds over the chronic health-care challenges that ail Ontario.
Blasting the Progressive Conservative leader as the Feb. 27 election campaign began its second week, Crombie noted treatment of patients in hospital corridors has doubled and the doctor shortage has worsened since he took office in 2018.
"Doug Ford said he'd end hallway medicine. He didn't get it done. He said he'd cut your taxes. He didn't get it done. He said he'd build 1.5 million homes. He didn't get it done," she said Thursday in Scarborough.
Crombie - whose bright red campaign bus is emblazoned with the slogan "2.5 Million People Have No Family Doctor: This is Doug Ford's Ontario" - has promised to cure the physician shortage through a slew of measures that could cost $3.1 billion.
They include spending $250 million over four years in targeted retention aimed at 2,400 doctors who have left family medicine or are nearing retirement, offering bonuses of $150,000 to repatriate Canadian doctors from abroad to Ontario, and recruiting 650 of the 6,000 family doctors currently working in other disciplines.
As well, she wants to help doctors by slashing "the administrative burden taking up 50 per cent of their time."
"Let's get rid of fax machines at long last and implement a centralized referral system and ensure that they have the right staff (and) offer more evening and weekend care," she said.
In Waterloo, Ford, who is seeking his third term in office, blamed previous Liberal governments for the shortage of physicians.
"If the Liberals didn't cut nurses and they didn't cut medical (school) seats, we wouldn't be in this spot," he told reporters after receiving the endorsement of the 25,000-member Ontario Pipe Trades Council.
"We've hired Dr. Jane Philpott to connect every single Ontarian with a family doctor," Ford said of the former federal Liberal health minister who is now director of the medical school at Queen's University in Kingston.
On the eve of Ford's snap election call, the PCs announced $1.4 billion in new funding to help Philpott expand the province's network of primary health care teams with a goal of getting every Ontarian a family doctor by 2029.
"There's no doubt that people have been waiting a long time, too long, frankly, to get connected to a family care practitioner in their community," MPP Sylvia Jones, serving as Ford's health minister, acknowledged on Jan. 27.
But that announcement raised questions as to whether the $1.4 billion is enough because there's a huge discrepancy between numbers provided by Jones (Dufferin-Caledon) and the Ontario Medical Association.
The OMA says there are currently 2.5 million Ontarians without a family doctor, and predicts that figure will rise to 4.4 million next year as the population grows and doctors retire or leave the province.
But Jones maintained just two million Ontarians will need to be connected to a family doctor in the next four years.
Ford boasted his government approved two new medical schools in Ontario. However, students will not graduate from them for several more years.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles, meanwhile, was in Hamilton to promise "real rent control" that would further protect tenants from higher costs and renovictions.
"After seven years of Doug Ford, rent is higher than ever. It's the biggest expense in your family's budget and it's only getting worse," said Stiles.
Later, she headed to Sault Ste. Marie, where her party hopes to pick up a key northern riding vacated by the retirement of veteran Tory Ross Romano.
Also Thursday, Green Leader Mike Schreiner was in Guelph to tout his party's plan to increase Ontario Disability Support Program and Ontario Works payments and build 310,000 affordable homes.
"Poverty in Ontario is escalating at an alarming rate," said Schreiner, noting the Association of Municipalities of Ontario found 81,000 people were homeless in the province last year.
"Over the past two years, food bank usage in Ontario has increased by 78 per cent," he said.
"Doug Ford isn't just failing to solve the problem - he's making it worse by keeping Ontarians with disabilities locked in legislated poverty."
The Tory campaign is confident it has weathered the worst of his Monday gaffe when he was caught on a hot mic saying that he "100 per cent" wanted Donald Trump to win the American presidency last November.
"On election day, was I happy this guy won? One hundred per cent I was. Then the guy pulled out the knife and f---ing yanked it in me," Ford said at his campaign office in Etobicoke.
Since the misstep, the PC leader, who has framed the snap Ontario election as a referendum on who could best deal with Trump's threatened 25 per cent tariffs against Canada, has faced questions about how he could have supported someone who had cut off personal protective equipment imports to Canada during the COVID-19 pandemic.
"There's Trump 1.0 and then there was Trump 2.0," he said Wednesday.
CAPTION: Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie appears at a campaign event in Scarborough on Feb. 6, 2025.

Document 319 of 500

Don't cast your vote for Ontario's next premier unless they make this promise

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/dont-cast-your-vote-ontarios-next-premier-unless/docview/3163710161/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Don%27t+cast+your+vote+for+Ontario%27s+next+premier+unless+they+make+this+promise&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: All of the Ontario party leaders and candidates in the Feb. 27 election need to understand that the Greenbelt is not just something nice to have - it's absolutely essential to Ontario's future success and security.
It's not just about green space - it's about our economy, our food security, and our water resources. The Greenbelt is already one of the best tools we have for taking care of what matters most. We must be looking at ways to protect and expand it, not chip away at it.
Ontarians understand the importance of the Greenbelt. Polls show consistently that more than eight out of 10 residents want it protected. They understand that the Greenbelt is land that puts food on our tables, water in our glasses, money in people's pockets and protects us from the worst excesses caused by climate change.
The Greenbelt is also an asset that contributes $9.6 billion to Ontario's GDP and supports 177,000 jobs. A protected Greenbelt ensures that our economy remains secure and resilient, regardless of whatever tariffs and trade barriers other countries may impose.
Here's the thing: Ontario relies heavily on food imports. A study in 2014 found that our province imports roughly $10 billion more food than we export. We get a lot of what we eat from other parts of the world, which means when there's a disruption in global trade - like tariff wars or supply chain issues - we feel it.
The Greenbelt can help offset that reliance by providing local, homegrown food, especially when global supply chains start to buckle. Keeping this land protected helps keep food affordable for Ontario families.
The Greenbelt has demonstrated already how crucial it is for securing our food and water. Right now, there are more than 750,000 acres of farmland protected within it; had this not been protected, it surely would have been gobbled up by urban sprawl.
Outside the Greenbelt, nearly half a million acres of farmland have been lost in Ontario since 1991 and we're still losing an average of 320 acres per day. This massive decline puts us at risk of having to rely even more on food imports; the Greenbelt protects against this.
While U.S. President Donald Trump muses about breaking century-old agreements and grabbing Canadian water, the Greenbelt provides clean, fresh water to more than seven million people in Ontario. This is free to us - all it requires is protecting the Greenbelt so it can do its job.
The Greenbelt also helps prevent $224 million in flood damage every year. With climate change throwing more extreme weather our way, it's clear that the Greenbelt's role in securing clean water and flood prevention for future generations is more important than ever.
The pressure is mounting. Global agricultural conglomerates and developers are pushing harder than ever to erase this treasure for their own benefit.
After public pressure, the premier and his government said it would protect the Greenbelt, but they still vow to ram two needless superhighways right through it, which would cause permanent damage to its farmland, water resources and forests. The public needs to ask all party leaders who seek to form the next government: Will you protect, strengthen and expand the Greenbelt?
Ontario can't afford to treat its Greenbelt like an afterthought and certainly not as a place that can be easily chopped up and paved over. We need to protect it permanently to ensure that our province remains resilient in the face of economic shocks, climate change, and all the trade and economic challenges the future might throw at us.
CAPTION: Corn fields are seen in Vaughan. "The public needs to ask all party leaders who seek to form the next government: Will you protect, strengthen and expand the Greenbelt?" writeDavid Crombie and Margaret Prophet.
CREDIT: David Crombie and Margaret Prophet Contributors

Subject: Agricultural land; Food; Climate change; Water resources

Title: Don't cast your vote for Ontario's next premier unless they make this promise

Document 320 of 500

Allan Gregg: Doug Ford is running against Donald Trump. Here's why that's a detriment to the whole country

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/allan-gregg-doug-ford-is-running-against-donald/docview/3163709972/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Allan+Gregg%3A+Doug+Ford+is+running+against+Donald+Trump.+Here%27s+why+that%27s+a+detriment+to+the+whole+country&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If you want to know if Ontario Premier Doug Ford's decision to issue writs for an early provincial election was unnecessary, opportunistic and possibly even dangerous, you are probably also wondering why he would make such a move. The 'why' may be easily explained away as a nakedly ambitious move to remain in power, but there is much more at stake than Ford winning another term as Ontario's Premier.
A year ago, Doug Ford hedged his bets. His preferred opponent was going to be Prime Minister Justin Trudeau. By planning an election before the feds went to the polls, Ford was not only going to ride the tailwinds of a wildly unpopular federal Liberal Party, but he would also avoid having to run against the Progressive Conservative leader, Pierre Poilievre who, should he form the next federal government, would undoubtedly be less popular the day after the win, potentially casting a shadow on PC fortunes across the board.
With Trudeau's surprise withdrawal, not only did Ford lose his preferred opponent but he faced the prospect of a reinvigorated Liberal party - especially if they chose a new leader with stronger centrist and management credentials.
But luck was on Ford's side when, rather than breathing new life into the Liberal Party, Trudeau's exit created a federal leadership vacuum - with no prospect of resolution before the Liberal leadership vote on March 9.
Amid these shifting sands, the astonishing triumph of US President Donald Trump and his threat of imposing economically crippling tariffs on Canada handed Ford yet another exploitable opportunity.
The turmoil has served up a cynical but likely effective campaign strategy that lets Ford wear a defiant "Canada Is Not For Sale" baseball cap and assume the role of Captain Canada against another villain who is not on the Ontario ballot.
There is no doubt that Ford's early election call serves little purpose other than his own quest to retain power - all at a massive financial cost to taxpayers. Just the price tag of the election itself is staggering at between $150 and $175 million, but when you add in the cost of the expedited beer and wine distribution program ($612 million), the so-called affordability and car license plate rebates ($3 billion and $1 billion a year, respectively), to name a few items on the roster, this 'opportunity' accrues benefit to no one other than Ford himself.
Perhaps in 2025 we've become numb to the practice of politicians putting their thumb on the scale of election fortune to their own advantage. But we should not view Ford's machinations as just another day in the life of our politicians. This move has potentially damaging consequences for Canada as a whole.
By choosing to run against Donald Trump in this election, the central thrust of Doug Ford's campaign will be to demonstrate that he is best able to defend Ontario's interests against a foreign adversary. Given the cynical opportunism that is driving this strategy, it is not much of a leap to foresee that what helps him win re-election will continue to be used, even at the expense of other parts of the country.
For example, if he unilaterally threatens to ban or tax Ontario's electricity exports to the U.S., how will that impact Quebec and Alberta whose economies rely on energy exports? More broadly speaking, Canada's strength as a country is rooted in her provinces and their respective resources working together and acting as one voice to advance both our individual and collective interests.
Ontario has a proud and historic role as a unifier in Confederation. One lone Premier taking advantage of a confluence of events to retain power presents an imminent threat to federalism, potentially dividing the country when it has never been more important to strengthen the ties that bind and form a coordinated bulwark against an unprecedented threat to the wellbeing of our citizens.
CREDIT: Allan R. Gregg, Special to the Star

Subject: Provincial elections; Political campaigns; Leadership

Document 321 of 500

Rivals seize on Ford's hot mic blunder

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/rivals-seize-on-fords-hot-mic-blunder/docview/3163709897/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Rivals+seize+on+Ford%27s+hot+mic+blunder&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford's stunning admission that he kept faith with Donald Trump - right up to voting day in America - breaks faith with voters here in Ontario as the Feb. 27 election looms, says NDP Leader Marit Stiles.
She would say that.
After all, her Ontario New Democrats need a catastrophic slip-up by Ford to stop his Progressive Conservatives from coasting to the big win projected by opinion polls. So is Ford's big blunder big enough to block his majority trajectory?
Stiles still believes that campaigns matter, that politicians are unpredictable, that voters are not automatons. The rookie leader says Ontarians are just now getting to know her - and don't know Ford well enough.
"Donald Trump had made it very clear - even before he was elected president for the second time - that he was coming after Canada," Stiles told the Democracy Forum I hosted at Toronto Metropolitan University on Wednesday.
"The idea that he would still, in light of that, support somebody like Donald Trump says a lot about Doug Ford," given that the U.S. president was an election denier and tariff promoter. "It speaks again to Doug Ford's character - it speaks to what he is."
How much will it hurt him? A better question is how much it will help Stiles.
After all, she's not the only one trying to topple him as premier. The story of Ontario politics, the history of its elections, is a perennial battle between New Democrats and Liberals fighting it out over the so-called progressive, anti-PC vote - while the PCs come out on top.
So who can defeat Doug Ford?
Predictably, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie told the Democracy Forum the day before that she could finish off Ford.
Unsurprisingly, Stiles said in her appearance the next day that only the NDP could do the job.
It's a critical question, because the competing policies of the two rival parties have far more in common with each other than with Ford's Tories. It's also the question that came up most often from audience members at the Democracy Forum wondering why Liberals and New Democrats don't make common cause against Ford.
The NDP has an answer. It's about positioning - and choosing.
"We are best positioned, without question, to defeat Ford," Stiles counters. "We have the second-most seats compared to Mr. Ford, we have seats in every region."
Yet the NDP keeps losing byelections and it's running third in the polls, behind the Liberals and far behind Ford's Tories.
Stiles insists her supporters "want that choice, they're not happy with Liberals ... and I like that we have choices in our political system."
Which is why Stiles is fighting campaigns on two fronts - attacking Ford in one breath while criticizing Crombie in the next. She told our audience that Crombie would fit comfortably in a cabinet of Ford's Tories.
And yet both leaders sound remarkably similar in their critiques of Ford's failures on health care, doctor shortages, housing shortfalls and funding for colleges and universities. Both are focused, almost as much as Ford, on affordability.
The biggest point of commonality, despite their rivalry? Echoing Crombie the day before, Stiles went on record as saying she would never prop up Ford's Tories should they fall short of a majority - and that she'd work with the Liberals.
"I will absolutely not prop Doug Ford and the Conservatives up, you can bank on that - I'm not supporting him, no way, no how, in no scenario," the NDP leader told me. "I will work with anyone on issues that we share, where we share common values."
One of Stiles's signature promises is to remove costly tolls on Highway 407 - not just for trucks, as the NDP pledged in the last election campaign, but for all drivers within 100 days of taking power. Facilitating an urban sprawl free-for-all might seem like a U-turn for a party that fights for more bike lanes and fewer cars on the roads, but Stiles sees a vote-winner.
Whatever the environmental toll of more cars on highways, Stiles won't talk about the financial cost either. She argues that it would undermine her negotiations with the private company that leases the highway and sets the tolls.
"We're going to negotiate - I am not going to tell that company what I'm willing to pay right now," she insisted.
The only certainty is that it will be costly. And the dollar amount likely won't be included in the supposedly fully costed campaign platform that Stiles is promising (she wouldn't say when).
"We're staging it out," Stiles teased. "I wish I could tell you the actual, the whole package, but again - this is a campaign."
That's democracy.

The appearances this week by both the NDP's Stiles and the Liberals' Crombie at the Democracy Forum can be viewed online. I'll be hosting Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner at the Democracy Forum next Tuesday, while PC Leader Doug Ford has declined an invitation to appear during the election. Disclosure: I'm a senior fellow at the Dais at TMU, which sponsors the events.
CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION

Letters: This is what makes Canadians different from Americans, Letters, Feb. 5 Trump postpones threatened tariffs for 30 days after Canada offers more border security measures, Feb. 4 Trump says he wants US to take charge of Gaza Strip, Feb. 4

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3163706634/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+This+is+what+makes+Canadians+different+from+Americans%2C+Letters%2C+Feb.+5+Trump+postpones+threatened+tariffs+for+30+days+after+Canada+offers+more+border+security+measures%2C+Feb.+4+Trump+says+he+wants+US+to+take+charge+of+Gaza+Strip%2C+Feb.+4&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In Canada,
we don't idolize or elect outlaws
This is what makes Canadians different from Americans, Letters, Feb. 5
To this letter writer's splendid list of why Canadians should take pride in our country, let's add the fact that we don't idolize outlaws, and would never elect one as prime minister.
We have truly international thinkers, like Margaret Atwood, author of "The Handmaid's Tale," which alerted the world to the dangerous misogyny of the religious far right; Naomi Klein, whose books "No Logo" and "The Shock Doctrine" helped explain the greed of unfettered corporations, and how superpower leaders do outrageous things in order to deflect public awareness of their ultra-capitalist goals.
Other international thinkers include Marshall McLuhan, John Ralston Saul, Malcolm Gladwell, David Frum and public intellectual Jordan Peterson, love him or hate him.
Most Canadians understand that Donald Trump is primarily a real estate developer who has zero empathy, who is beholden to oligarchs like himself, and who is playing a kind of primitive chess game with the world.
And yet, despite our valuing Canada's natural splendour, our most populated province is poised to re-elect Doug Ford, a man who is primarily a shill for his developer friends, and who is the very opposite of an environmentalist.
Ron Charach, Toronto
Ford and Trump are of same ilk
Why would anyone be surprised that Premier Doug Ford wanted U.S. President Donald Trump to win? They are of the same ilk. Both were born with silver spoons in their mouths and amazingly both elitists have convinced regular folk that they are one of them. They both support the wealthy. They say they will do things and then backtrack. Their politics are identical. When all is said and done it says more about the voters.
Roy Wilkie, Richmond Hill
Premier Doug Ford said, "Election Day, was I happy this guy won? One hundred per cent I was. But then the guy pulled out the knife and f--king yanked it into us." And that is why U.S. President Donald Trump loves the ignorant.
Ron Barrett, Hamilton
Failing to vote risks democracy
In a democracy it is a responsibility of all citizens to vote. Yet in our last provincial election, 56 per cent of eligible voters failed to fulfil this task. We need public announcements stressing the necessity of voting. Even if you think no one is qualified or acceptable, someone will win. We all need to assess the candidates and parties, and make a decision. Otherwise, we risk losing our democracy.
Elizabeth Mintoft, Toronto
While tightening our border, let's stop the guns
Trump postpones threatened tariffs for 30 days after Canada offers more border security measures, Feb. 4
I fully realize that tightening the border is in response to U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs. We are supposed to be targeting fentanyl and illegal border crossings on behalf of the U.S. Do you think we could put in just a little extra effort on behalf of Canadians? Could we at least try and stop the guns?
Clyde McDonald, Bracebridge
The border issues between the U.S. and Canada appear to be very one-sided. U.S. President Donald Trump has demanded that Canada be responsible for stopping the flow of fentanyl and illegal migrants into the U.S. My question is, why haven't we in turn demanded the U.S. be responsible for stopping the flow of guns into Canada?
Gary Johnson, Stouffville
So, let me get this right. Canada is going to spend $1.3 billion to stop the flow of illegal immigrants and fentanyl into the U.S. while U.S. President Donald Trump isn't going to spend a red cent to stop the flow of illegal guns into Canada. What's wrong with this picture?
William Michajluk, Scarborough
Canada should align with BRICS
If the U.S. President follows through on his threat to tear up NAFTA, then Canada has a few options. We can live without being a member of any trading alliance, as we did in pre-NAFTA days, or we can join some other alliance. The stagnant EU is one possibility, but it represents just five per cent of world population, 15 per cent of global GDP, and the war in Ukraine has left many European nations on the verge of a recession.
By contrast, the rapidly expanding BRICS alliance already represents 55 per cent of the world's population, 42 per cent of global GDP and its members include some of the most robust, fastest-growing economies on Earth. True, Canada has foolishly placed itself at odds with BRICS largest members - Russia, China and India - but history has shown that such relationships can be repaired. Who knows? It might even give Trump reason to reconsider his ultimatums.
Mike Ward, Duncan, B.C.
Trump's plan for Gaza is ignorant
Trump says he wants US to take charge of Gaza Strip,
Feb. 4
U.S. President Donald Trump's comments on Gaza have sparked intense controversy. His suggestions of resettling Palestinians and redeveloping Gaza are not only insensitive, but also ignorant. Although Islam promotes peace and justice, defending one's homeland is also a fundamental aspect of faith. As an Ahmadi Muslim, I feel Trump's remarks are outrageous and I dread the effects they will have on ongoing ceasefire talks and the risk of reigniting conflict in the region.
Nadia Ahmad, Vaughan
Now who's being disrespectful?
Where's the outrage, Canada, after U.S. President Donald Trump's press secretary told reporters that "Canada is bending the knee"? Trump is always complaining that Canada does not treat America with respect. But is this statement from Trump's press secretary respectful? Am I the only Canadian who is outraged?
Duncan Craig, Gravenhurst, Ont.
Trump horrifies many Americans
Please know that 48.3 per cent of United States voters did not vote for Donald Trump and are horrified by what he is doing to our relationships and people around the world. What is happening here is embarrassing and catastrophic on so many levels. All I can hope is that leaders and sane, compassionate people around the world will do what they can to thwart this egomaniac.
Melissa Wolff-Burke,
Winchester, Va.

Document 323 of 500

Leaders roll out pricey promises: Ford vows to trim 407 tolls, Stiles offers supportive housing, Crombie eyes disability payments

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/leaders-roll-out-pricey-promises/docview/3163706543/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Leaders+roll+out+pricey+promises%3A+Ford+vows+to+trim+407+tolls%2C+Stiles+offers+supportive+housing%2C+Crombie+eyes+disability+payments&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hang onto your wallets - it was a day of politicians making expensive spending promises on the Ontario campaign trail.
Doug Ford pledged to eliminate tolls, which can be up to $20 a ride, on the provincially run eastern section of Highway 407 if his Progressive Conservatives are re-elected Feb. 27.
New Democratic Leader Marit Stiles promised to build 60,000 supportive housing units to deal with homeless encampments, which would mean untold billions in new construction expenditures.
Bonnie Crombie vowed to double Ontario Disability Support Program (ODSP) payments, which could cost an additional $6 billion within two years.
"With President Trump continuing to threaten tariffs that would skyrocket costs for hard-working families, it's never been more important to protect people's wallets by putting more money back into their pockets," Ford said Wednesday at a Highway 407 maintenance yard.
Only tolls on the 43-kilometre provincially operated portion of Highway 407 - from Brock Road in Pickering east to Highway 35/115 north of Clarington - will be scrapped. It's a move that will cost the treasury $72 million annually.
The PC leader admitted tolls will remain on the privately operated 108-kilometre section of Highway 407 from the Queen Elizabeth Way in Burlington to Brock Road.
Depending on the time of day, it can cost $86 to drive that stretch of Highway 407.
Ford said it was "a big mistake" for former Tory premier Mike Harris to sign a 99-year lease with a private consortium for $3.1 billion in 1999. It would cost an estimated $35 billion to buy out the lease.
His big-ticket pledge comes as he scrambled to change the channel after he was caught Monday on a hot mic saying that he "100 per cent" wanted Donald Trump to win the American presidency last November.
"There's Trump 1.0 and then there was Trump 2.0," a defensive Ford said Wednesday when pressed on how he could have wanted Trump to win the election after the president had cut off personal protective equipment imports to Canada during the COVID-19 pandemic, among other transgressions.
His Monday misstep has rocked the snap provincial election that the PC leader has insisted is a referendum on who could best deal with Trump's threatened 25 per cent tariffs against Canada.
Rattled by the controversy, Ford's campaign is now limiting media questions and more tightly controlling access to the leader.
He's complained journalists "are obsessed with" his surprise admission to Tory candidates Christine Hogarth and David Piccini and several firefighters that he had hoped the Republican Trump would beat Democrat Kamala Harris last November.
In an embarrassing moment of candour at his Etobicoke campaign office Monday, he said: "On election day, was I happy this guy won? One hundred per cent I was. Then the guy pulled out the knife and f--king yanked it in me."
The Liberals immediately launched a new attack ad using the clip and the New Democrats mocked him for the blunder.
Ford's promise to remove the tolls follows months of pressure from opposition parties who complained the Tories ignored obvious and immediate solutions to ease gridlock in the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area - particularly on the heavily congested Highway 401.
Then came his contentious proposal last September to build a tunnel under Highway 401, possibly from the Pearson International Airport area east to Scarborough.
The scheme, which would pose major engineering and cost challenges and take years to build, was derided by rival parties as a distraction from quicker fixes for gridlock, such as removing tolls for trucks on Highway 407 to lure commercial traffic away from Highway 401.
Without providing a price tag, the NDP has promised to eventually make all of Highway 407 toll-free for motorists.
In Toronto, where about 1,000 homeless people camp in tents in city parks, Stiles blamed the Tories for failing on housing.
The NDP leader told the Star's Martin Regg Cohn at Toronto Metropolitan University's Democracy Forum that encampments are Ford's legacy after seven years in power.
"It is shocking. I live in the city of Toronto, but I get to travel everywhere - every town, every city, every community, has an encampment," said Stiles, adding the NDP would build 60,000 new supportive housing units for people in encampments or staying in homeless shelters and upload such services from cash-strapped municipalities.
"Homelessness is a pandemic in the province of Ontario," she said.
In Hamilton, Crombie pledged to double ODSP payments to the most vulnerable in Ontario.
"People with disabilities are dying in this province because Doug Ford has neglected to provide the basics. Keeping people healthy starts with getting them the basics," she said, noting nearly 500,000 people rely on the payments that are currently a maximum of $1,368 a month.
The Liberal leader said the fact poverty is so visible and widespread in an indictment of the Tories' time in power.
"Every community ... is seeing encampments. I don't remember seeing them eight years ago or nine years ago or 10 years ago. This is a failure of Doug Ford's Ontario," she said.
CAPTION: PC Leader Doug Ford pledged Wednesday to eliminate tolls on the provincially run eastern section of Highway 407, which would cost $72 million per year. Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie promised to double Ontario Disability Support Program payments, which could cost an extra $6 billion within two years.R.J. Johnston Toronto StarChris Young The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson, Robert Benzie and Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Full text: According to data obtained through Freedom of Information requests from Ontario Health by the think tank SecondStreet.org, 11,500 patients in Ontario died waiting for surgeries and diagnostic scans between April 2022 and March 2023, while 8,814 died between April 2021 and March 2022. A Feb. 1 opinion article about Ontario Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford mistakenly cited 11,581 patient deaths while on wait-lists in 2022 alone.
