Document 325 of 500

Next premier must protect Greenbelt

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/next-premier-must-protect-greenbelt/docview/3163706516/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Next+premier+must+protect+Greenbelt&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: All of the Ontario party leaders and candidates in the Feb. 27 election need to understand that the Greenbelt is not just something nice to have - it's absolutely essential to Ontario's future success and security.
It's not just about green space - it's about our economy, our food security and our water resources. The Greenbelt is already one of the best tools we have for taking care of what matters most. We must be looking at ways to protect and expand it, not chip away at it.
Ontarians understand the importance of the Greenbelt. Polls show consistently that more than eight out of 10 residents want it protected. They understand that the Greenbelt is land that puts food on our tables, water in our glasses, money in people's pockets and protects us from the worst excesses caused by climate change.
The Greenbelt is also an asset that contributes $9.6 billion to Ontario's GDP and supports 177,000 jobs. A protected Greenbelt ensures that our economy remains secure and resilient, regardless of whatever tariffs and trade barriers other countries may impose.
Here's the thing: Ontario relies heavily on food imports. A study in 2014 found that our province imports roughly $10 billion more food than we export. We get a lot of what we eat from other parts of the world, which means when there's a disruption in global trade - like tariff wars or supply chain issues - we feel it.
The Greenbelt can help offset that reliance by providing local, homegrown food, especially when global supply chains start to buckle. Keeping this land protected helps keep food affordable for Ontario families.
The Greenbelt has demonstrated already how crucial it is for securing our food and water. Right now, there are more than 750,000 acres of farmland protected within it; had this not been protected, it surely would have been gobbled up by urban sprawl.
Outside the Greenbelt, nearly half a million acres of farmland have been lost in Ontario since 1991 and were still losing an average of 320 acres per day. This massive decline puts us at risk of having to rely even more on food imports; the Greenbelt protects against this.
While U.S. President Donald Trump muses about breaking century-old agreements and grabbing Canadian water, the Greenbelt provides clean, fresh water to more than seven million people in Ontario. This is free to us - all it requires is protecting the Greenbelt so it can do its job.
The Greenbelt also helps prevent $224 million in flood damage every year.
With climate change throwing more extreme weather our way, it's clear that the Greenbelt's role in securing clean water and flood prevention for future generations is more important than ever.
The pressure is mounting. Global agricultural conglomerates and developers are pushing harder than ever to erase this treasure for their own benefit.
After public pressure, the premier and his government said it would protect the Greenbelt, but they still vow to ram two needless superhighways right through it, which would cause permanent damage to its farmland, water resources and forests.
The public needs to ask all party leaders who seek to form the next government: Will you protect, strengthen and expand the Greenbelt?
Ontario can't afford to treat its Greenbelt like an afterthought and certainly not as a place that can be easily chopped up and paved over. We need to protect it permanently to ensure that our province remains resilient in the face of economic shocks, climate change, and all the trade and economic challenges the future might throw at us.
David Crombie is former chair of the Greenbelt Council. Margaret Prophet is co-ordinator of the Ontario Greenbelt Alliance.
CREDIT: David Crombie and Margaret Prophet Contributors David Crombie is former chair of the Greenbelt Council. Margaret Prophet is co-ordinator of the Ontario Greenbelt Alliance.;

Document 326 of 500

Thieving turkeys, giant earmuffs and Guess Who: New Canadian comedy is an absurdist delight

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/thieving-turkeys-giant-earmuffs-guess-who/docview/3163706348/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Thieving+turkeys%2C+giant+earmuffs+and+Guess+Who%3A+New+Canadian+comedy+is+an+absurdist+delight&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=C.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In the cockeyed Canada of Matthew Rankin's nutty comedy "Universal Language," Winnipeg is colourful and bustling while Montreal is dreary and almost comatose.
This depiction may run contrary to your imaginings, but free your mind and your compass will follow.
The film's whimsical Winnipeg is populated mainly by Iranian immigrants who must contend with such daily distractions as eyewear-stealing turkeys, walking Christmas trees and bank notes embedded in ice.
Montreal, meanwhile, looks like a grey urban hellhole from an old Soviet newsreel. No wonder the story's sad-faced protagonist, Matthew, played by filmmaker Rankin, is eager to quit his soul-destroying government job - where a man in a nearby cubicle sobs inconsolably - and take a bus home to see his elderly mom back in the Peg.
It's a city caught between countries and cultures - call it "Tehrannipeg" - in a droll entertainment Rankin describes as "an absurdist city symphony." It arrives with many laurels, among them the inaugural audience award at the Directors' Fortnight at the 2024 Cannes Film Festival. "Universal Language" was Canada's Oscar submission for the best international feature (it made the shortlist, but not the final five contenders). It's also likely the only movie with a soundtrack featuring both traditional Persian music and "These Eyes" by Winnipeg's own the Guess Who.
Everyone in Tehrannipeg speaks Farsi; Tim Hortons (where we're told there's "a relaxing view of the highway") serves Persian tea and the currency is the Iranian rial, which happily rhymes with Louis Riel, the iconic Manitoba founder, Métis leader and rebel.
Yet the climate is distinctly Canadian. Snow and ice abound and the locals toil at places like the Winnipeg Earmuff Authority (where they make absurdly large and colourful earmuffs), the Kleenex Repository and a turkey store run by some kind of cowboy.
There's also an elementary school where the French teacher, Mr. Bilodeau (Mani Soleymanlou) furiously tells his rambunctious students, "All of you will fail!" and cancels classes because a nearsighted kid named Omid (Sobhan Javadi) had his glasses stolen by a rampaging gobbler.
Help might be on the way for Omid: two of his classmates, Negin (Rojina Esmaeili) and her sister Nazgol (Saba Vahedyousefi), find a 500-rial bank note embedded in ice they can use to buy him new glasses, if only they can get it out.
Matthew has his own quest: to find his missing mom, who used to live in a bungalow next to a chainsaw shop.
It's disorienting, but don't worry; there's a tour guide on hand named Massoud (Pirouz Nemati), who resembles Groucho Marx. He proudly shows off local hot spots: a transit bench with a briefcase that's been abandoned since 1978 (it's been declared a UNESCO world heritage site); the scene of the Great Parallel Parking Incident of 1958; and a clock inside the Portage Place shopping mall, which we're told has no hands because it's "timeless."
The screenplay by Rankin, Nemati and Ila Firouzabadi makes the setting seem at once natural and completely insane. It's like a child-centred adventure by the late Iranian master Abbas Kiarostami that somehow got mixed with the formalist whimsy of Wes Anderson (and Roy Andersson) and the surreal charms of Guy Maddin, a fellow Winnipegger.
Cinematographer Isabelle Stachtchenko frequently uses wide shots to capture antlike human activities against imposing structures. When her camera moves in close, it often seems distracted by other people and creatures, especially the town's beloved but pesky turkeys.
"That gobbler breathed life into my soul," a cowboy says, lamenting the passing of one of the birds.
This is Rankin's second feature. It follows "The Twentieth Century," a delightfully unhinged romp through the weird schemes of William Lyon Mackenzie King, played by Daniel Beirne, as he labours to become Canada's most eccentric prime minister.
The Winnipeg-born and Montreal-schooled Rankin is clearly enamoured with alternate Canadian history, leading us to wonder what's next? I'd love to see a similar take on Doug Ford's Ontario or Justin Trudeau's Ottawa.
As the credits roll and the last strains of "These Eyes" enliven a parade of strutting turkeys, one can't help but feel a peculiar sense of displacement, as if we've just stumbled out of a fever dream induced by a potent combination of maple syrup and saffron.
"Universal Language" leaves us in a state of delightful bewilderment, much like finding oneself suddenly transported to a Tim Hortons where the Timbits come with a steaming mug of existential crisis.
Universal Language
(out of 4)
Starring Rojina Esmaeili, Saba Vahedyousefi, Matthew Rankin, Sobhan Javadi and Pirouz Nemati. Written by Matthew Rankin,
Pirouz Nemati and Ila Firouzabadi. Directed by Matthew Rankin.
89 minutes. Opens Friday at TIFF Lightbox. STC
CAPTION: A walking Christmas tree is just one of many absurd sights in Matthew Rankin's "Universal Language."In the quirky comedy, Winnipeg is populated mainly by Iranian immigrants and everyone speaks Farsi.Maison 4:3Telefilm CanadaTHE CANADIAN PRESS

Document 327 of 500

Doug Ford's hot mic blunder is a weapon his rivals are turning against him

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-hot-mic-blunder-is-weapon-his-rivals/docview/3163933396/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+hot+mic+blunder+is+a+weapon+his+rivals+are+turning+against+him&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford's stunning admission that he kept faith with Donald Trump - right up to voting day in America - breaks faith with voters here in Ontario as the Feb. 27 election looms, says NDP Leader Marit Stiles.
She would say that.
After all, her Ontario New Democrats need a catastrophic slip-up by Ford to stop his Progressive Conservatives from coasting to the big win projected by opinion polls. So is Ford's big blunder big enough to block his majority trajectory?
Stiles still believes that campaigns matter, that politicians are unpredictable, that voters are not automatons. The rookie leader says Ontarians are just now getting to know her - and don't know Ford well enough.
"Donald Trump had made it very clear - even before he was elected president for the second time - that he was coming after Canada," Stiles told the Democracy Forum I hosted at Toronto Metropolitan University on Wednesday.
"The idea that he would still, in light of that, support somebody like Donald Trump says a lot about Doug Ford," given that the U.S. president was an election denier and tariff promoter. "It speaks again to Doug Ford's character - it speaks to what he is."
How much will it hurt him? A better question is how much it will help Stiles.
After all, she's not the only one trying to topple him as premier. The story of Ontario politics, the history of its elections, is a perennial battle between New Democrats and Liberals fighting it out over the so-called progressive, anti-PC vote - while the PCs come out on top.
So who can defeat Doug Ford?
Predictably, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie told the Democracy Forum the day before that she could finish off Ford.
Unsurprisingly, Stiles said in her appearance the next day that only the NDP could do the job.
It's a critical question, because the competing policies of the two rival parties have far more in common with each other than with Ford's Tories. It's also the question that came up most often from audience members at the Democracy Forum wondering why Liberals and New Democrats don't make common cause against Ford.
The NDP has an answer. It's about positioning - and choosing.
"We are best positioned, without question, to defeat Ford," Stiles counters. "We have the second-most seats compared to Mr. Ford, we have seats in every region."
Yet the NDP keeps losing byelections and it's running third in in the polls, behind the Liberals and far behind Ford's Tories.
Stiles insists her supporters "want that choice, they're not happy with Liberals ... and I like that we have choices in our political system."
Which is why Stiles is fighting campaigns on two fronts - attacking Ford in one breath while criticizing Crombie in the next. She told our audience that Crombie would fit comfortably in a cabinet of Ford's Tories.
And yet both leaders sound remarkably similar in their critiques of Ford's failures on health care, doctor shortages, housing shortfalls and funding for colleges and universities. Both are focused, almost as much as Ford, on affordability.
The biggest point of commonality, despite their rivalry? Echoing Crombie the day before, Stiles went on record as saying she would never prop up Ford's Tories should they fall short of a majority - and that she'd work with the Liberals.
"I will absolutely not prop Doug Ford and the Conservatives up, you can bank on that - I'm not supporting him, no way, no how, in no scenario," the NDP leader told me. "I will work with anyone on issues that we share, where we share common values."
One of Stiles's signature promises is to remove costly tolls on Highway 407 - not just for trucks, as the NDP pledged in the last election campaign, but for all drivers within 100 days of taking power. Facilitating an urban sprawl free-for-all might seem like a U-turn for a party that fights for more bike lanes and fewer cars on the roads, but Stiles sees a vote-winner.
Whatever the environmental toll of more cars on highways, Stiles won't talk about the financial cost either. She argues that it would undermine her negotiations with the private company that leases the highway and sets the tolls.
"We're going to negotiate - I am not going to tell that company what I'm willing to pay right now," she insisted.
The only certainty is that it will be costly. And the dollar amount likely won't be included in the supposedly fully costed campaign platform that Stiles is promising (she wouldn't say when).
"We're staging it out," Stiles teased. "I wish I could tell you the actual, the whole package, but again - this is a campaign."
That's democracy.
* * *
The appearances this week by both the NDP's Stiles and the Liberals' Crombie at the Democracy Forum can be viewed online. I'll be hosting Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner at the Democracy Forum next Tuesday, while PC Leader Doug Ford has declined an invitation to appear during the election. Disclosure: I'm a senior fellow at the Dais at TMU, which sponsors the events.
CAPTION: Marit StilesStar columnist Martin Regg Cohn speaks with Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles atToronto Metropolitan University's Democracy Forum on Feb. 5, 2025.

Document 328 of 500

Doug Ford promises to axe tolls on eastern portion of Highway 407

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-promises-axe-tolls-on-eastern-portion/docview/3163933379/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+promises+to+axe+tolls+on+eastern+portion+of+Highway+407&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hang onto your wallets - it was a day of politicians making expensive spending promises on the Ontario campaign trail.
Doug Ford pledged to eliminate tolls, which can be up to $20 a ride, on the provincially run eastern section of Highway 407 if his Progressive Conservatives are re-elected Feb. 27.
New Democratic Leader Marit Stiles promised to build 60,000 supportive housing units to deal with homeless encampments which would mean untold billions in new construction expenditures.
Bonnie Crombie vowed to double Ontario Disability Support Program (ODSP) payments, which could cost an additional $6 billion within two years.
"With President Trump continuing to threaten tariffs that would skyrocket costs for hard-working families, it's never been more important to protect people's wallets by putting more money back into their pockets," Ford said Wednesday at a Highway 407 maintenance yard.
Only tolls on the 43-kilometre provincially operated portion of Highway 407 - from Brock Road in Pickering east to Highway 35/115 north of Clarington - will be scrapped. It's a move that will cost the treasury $72 million annually.
The PC leader admitted tolls will remain on the privately operated 108-kilometre section of Highway 407 from the Queen Elizabeth Way in Burlington to Brock Road.
Depending on the time of day, it can cost $86 to drive that stretch of Highway 407.
Ford said it was "a big mistake" for former Tory premier Mike Harris to sign a 99-year lease with a private consortium for $3.1 billion in 1999. It would be an estimated $35 billion to buy out the lease.
His big-ticket pledge comes as he scrambled to change the channel after he was caught Monday on a hot mic saying that he "100 per cent" wanted Donald Trump to win the American presidency last November.
"There's Trump 1.0 and then there was Trump 2.0," a defensive said Ford Wednesday when pressed on how he could have wanted Trump to win the election after the president had cut off personal protective equipment imports to Canada during the COVID-19 pandemic, among other transgressions.
His Monday misstep has rocked the snap provincial election that the PC leader has insisted is a referendum on who could best deal with Trump's threatened 25 per cent tariffs against Canada.
Rattled by the controversy, Ford's campaign is now limiting media questions and more tightly controlling access to the leader.
He's complained journalists "are obsessed with" his surprise admission to Tory candidates Christine Hogarth and David Piccini and several firefighters that he had hoped the Republican Trump would beat Democrat Kamala Harris last November.
In an embarrassing moment of candour at his Etobicoke campaign office Monday, he said: "On election day, was I happy this guy won? One hundred per cent I was. Then the guy pulled out the knife and f---ing yanked it in me."
The Liberals immediately launched a new attack ad using the clip and the New Democrats mocked him for the blunder.
Ford's promise to remove the tolls follows months of pressure from opposition parties who complained the Tories ignored obvious and immediate solutions to ease gridlock in the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area - particularly on the heavily congested Highway 401.
Then came his contentious proposal last September to build a tunnel under Highway 401, possibly from the Pearson International Airport area east to Scarborough.
The scheme, which would pose major engineering and cost challenges and take years to build, was derided by rival parties as a distraction from quicker fixes for gridlock, such as removing tolls for trucks on Highway 407 to lure commercial traffic away from Highway 401.
Without providing a price tag, the NDP has promised to eventually make all of Highway 407 toll-free for motorists.
In Toronto, where about 1,000 homeless people camp in tents in city parks, Stiles blamed the Tories for failing on housing.
The NDP leader told the Star's Martin Regg Cohn at Toronto Metropolitan University's Democracy Forum that encampments are Ford's legacy after seven years in power.
"It is shocking. I live in the city of Toronto, but I get to travel everywhere - every town, every city, every community, has an encampment," said Stiles, adding the NDP would build 60,000 new supportive housing units for people in encampments or staying in homeless shelters and upload such services from cash-strapped municipalities.
"Homelessness is a pandemic in the province of Ontario," she said.
In Hamilton, Crombie pledged to double ODSP payments to the most vulnerable in Ontario.
"People with disabilities are dying in this province because Doug Ford has neglected to provide the basics. Keeping people healthy starts with getting them the basics," she said, noting nearly 500,000 people rely on the payments that are currently a maximum of $1,368 a month.
The Liberal leader said the fact poverty is so visible and widespread in an indictment of the Tories' time in power.
"Every community ... is seeing encampments. I don't remember seeing them eight years ago or nine years ago or 10 years ago. This is a failure of Doug Ford's Ontario," she said.
CAPTION: Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie steps onto her campaign bus after visiting Golden Grain Bakery during a campaign stop in Brampton, Ont. on Feb. 4, 2025.

Corrections

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/corrections/docview/3163570667/se-2?accountid=15090

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Corrections&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A Feb. 1 article about Doug Ford incorrectly stated that the number of new housing starts last year in Ontario was lower than in any other province. In fact, the number of year-over-year housing starts in 2024 was lower than any other province. A Feb. 1 article about Art Metropole incorrectly stated that it started in a small office in Greektown and has since hopscotched across the city in various locations, including Queen Street West. In fact, the art bookstore's first location was in the studio of General Idea, in a building called Art Metropole, at Yonge and Dundas. There was never a Queen St. W. location, but rather King St. W. and Richmond St. W.

Trump's tariff turmoil leads to staycations + Ottawa lays out border plans

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/trumps-tariff-turmoil-leads-staycations-ottawa/docview/3163484418/se-2?accountid=15090

Document 331 of 500

This is what makes Canadians different from Americans

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/this-is-what-makes-canadians-different-americans/docview/3163369763/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=This+is+what+makes+Canadians+different+from+Americans&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I may have figured out Trump's sudden obsession with Canada, Feb. 4
Andrew Phillips says Canadians struggle to articulate what makes us different from Americans. Well, Canada takes pride in accepting and tolerating many cultures, and refugees from war: Vietnamese, Syrians, Ukrainians, Afghanis and more. We have countless festivals celebrating different cultures. Chinese New Year festivities and the summer Caribbean parade. We support a French speaking province. Canadians help other nations, the U.S.A. in particular, with the California fires, the hurricanes and disaster support. Our schools teach evolution and respect science. Our universities and colleges celebrate learning and are innovators in many fields, such as the discovery of insulin. We have the Noble prize winner, Geoffery Hinton. We invented the Canadarm used in space missions. Though we have problems with gangs and guns, our nation is not obsessed with gun ownership. Often the guns that cause crime come from the U.S. We can go to the doctor and the emergency department and not be bankrupted with payments afterwards. Though unsupported by our Ontario Premier, our health-care system is world class, even though we may be forced to wait longer. I am happy and proud to be Canadian.
Diane Sullivan, Toronto
Promote interprovincial trade
Trudeau says U.S. tariffs will be paused for 30 days; Ontario halts Starlink cancellation, liquor removal, Feb. 3
U.S. President Donald Trump is like a warning shot across our bow. Let's not invest all our eggs in one basket. Diversify. We need a government that promotes Canadian interprovincial trade and the development of new trade partners. We now know the States are not a trustworthy trade partner anymore.
David Miller, Keswick, ON
Canada should close our border for 'maintenance'
Both our Ontario and Quebec electricity suppliers to the American states need to shut off the supply for maintenance immediately. The oil pipelines from Alberta to Republican States need to be shut down for environmental inspections immediately. We will notify the White House when they'll be operational again. Our end of the International Bridges may be unsafe. Our engineers have to conduct thorough inspections to determine if remediation is required. Therefore, America cargo destined for Canada needs to remain on the American side until the status of the structure is determined to be safe. We wouldn't want to risk American lives.
Mark Buczek, Milton
Extra security on the Canadian side could stop guns and drugs coming inPierre Poilievre calls on Ottawa to send troops to the Canada-U.S. border, Feb. 3
Sending troops to patrol the Canada/U.S. border sounds like a great idea but not for the reasons Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre and U.S. President Donald Trump are giving. If the U.S. Border Patrol can't stop illegal drugs and firearms from crossing over into Canada, extra security on the Canadian side should be able to catch them coming in. Pretty sure that there will be far more guns and drugs caught going north than drugs going south.
Michael Bines, Toronto
Two good news stories
Lost in all the awful noise created by U.S. President Donald Trump are two "good news" stories. First, America's national bird, the bald eagle, has been taken off the endangered species list as its population has rebounded. Second, Canada's new national bird, a special one-finger salute directed toward Trump , is thriving. Go Canada.
Patrick Stewart, Toronto
Make defunding Catholic schools an election issue
This GTA board just voted to restrict the Pride flag inside, Jan. 30
The party leader who promises to defund the Catholic school system will have my vote.
Anne Morris-Dadson, Richmond Hill
Ford cut university tuition, and no one seems to care
Doug Ford hasn't needed a mandate to make major changes in Toronto. So why does he need one for Donald Trump? Feb. 2
Shawn Micallef is bang on when he says Premier Doug Ford did a lot without a mandate. One thing he missed was Ford's decision to cut university tuition by 10 per cent across the board and freeze any tuition increases. He did not campaign on this. This action, combined with no increase in funds to universities, has resulted in a situation where universities are operating with 2014 budgets but paying 2025 prices. Even after his own hand selected "blue ribbon" task force said this was unsustainable, he did not change course.
What is more disappointing is that the Ontarians, who repeatedly indicate strong interest in university education for their children, do not seem to care that the universities are being choked of funds, and that the inevitable outcome is fewer seats for their kids. In a world full of misinformation, universities are central to knowledge creation, preservation, and critical thinking.
Dan Malleck, St. Catharine's
City should use empty TTC ad spaces to publicize its services
If you ride the subway these days, you'll notice there are quite a lot of empty advertising spaces. If those advertising spaces are going unused - if there aren't enough paying customers for those spaces - couldn't the city of Toronto to use those spaces to advertise city facilities, services and programs?
Could it be possible to have city of Toronto ads (or provincial ads or federal ads) available on "stand by" to be put into advertising spaces that would otherwise go empty? If ad spaces on the TTC are not generating revenue, then they might as well be used to inform riders of publicly-funded things that they could use.
Paul Kern, Toronto
We cannot wait 30 days to removed inter provincial trade barriersWe're on the verge of a trade war with an agent of chaos. Here's what Canadians need to know, Feb. 4
Kevin Lynch and Paul Deegan are absolutely right: we need to fix our economy. Most importantly they point out that we urgently need to remove interprovincial trade barriers: provinces need to stop treating other provinces as if they were foreign countries. We cry foul when other countries want to impose trade barriers on us, and we can't even remove our own on each other? We cannot wait 30 days for this: I hope that provincial leaders are working on this as you're reading this.
Claude Gannon, Markham
Trump's that annoying kid
Remember that annoying little kid who used to loudly announce changes to the rules in mid-game ... in his favour? It didn't take long before the other kids refused to play with him anymore.
Can we vote for both Carney and Freeland?Chrystia Freeland, Mark Carney emerge as top contenders in Liberal leadership race, Jan. 17
At this critical juncture in our history, we are blessed with two strong, educated, experienced and now very determined wonderful Liberal leadership candidates to be our next Prime Minister. If you don't think that to be true, just look south. I hope many Canadians note that they are both being so careful not to undermine our critical unity especially at this time of the hostile attack on Canada from the deranged U.S. President Donald Trump. Both Mark Carney and Chrystia Freeland are conducting their campaigns not with themselves in mind, but with substantive sensitivity, admirable tact and constraint that is quickly lost in the discourse of many past leadership contests. From this Canadian, thank you. However, this creates a problem come March 9: can we vote for both of you?
Patrick J. Mullin, Owen Sound
CAPTION: The Mas Band the Toronto Revellers at the Toronto Caribbean Carnival on the Canadian National Exhibition Grounds in Toronto on Aug. 5, 2023 -- one e of countless festivals celebrating different cultures in Canada.

Subject: Educational leadership; Drugs; Political campaigns; Presidents; Canadians; Colleges & universities; Firearms; Border patrol; Political advertising; Interprovincial trade; Festivals

Business indexing term: Subject: Colleges & universities Interprovincial trade

Location: United States--US; Canada

People: Carney, Mark; Trump, Donald J; Freeland, Chrystia

Document 332 of 500

Canada needs to wean itself off its relationship with the United States. Here's how

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canada-needs-wean-itself-off-relationship-with/docview/3163369744/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada+needs+to+wean+itself+off+its+relationship+with+the+United+States.+Here%27s+how&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canadians may be shocked by the dramatic change in our relationship with the U.S., but the new White House administration is right to complain that Ottawa wasn't remotely interested in border security prior to President Donald Trump's return to power.
According to a new report by Criminal Intelligence Service Canada, the number of Canadian organized crime groups manufacturing drugs such as fentanyl nearly doubled in one year, from 51 in 2023 to 99 in 2024.
We can't fault Trump for the fact that it's been almost five years since the auditor general criticized the Liberals for having lost track of 34,700 foreign nationals facing deportation, including convicted criminals. The number of outstanding immigration arrest warrants hit 37,326 in 2023, as I railed about here last summer.
The expectation that Trump would be satiated by the two new RCMP helicopters Ottawa just leased is, given the border's 8,891-kilometre length, laughable.
One bright spot is our 19-year contribution to America's drug interdiction strategy. Hopefully someone has told Trump that the Royal Canadian Navy just sent another new offshore patrol ship to the Caribbean in search of drug smugglers. Also on board is a U.S. Coast Guard Law Enforcement Detachment, making this one of several joint anti-narcotic missions planned for 2025.
Last Fall, similar teams nabbed C$60 million of cocaine off the shores of Acapulco, Mexico. It's not fentanyl, but the Canadian military has helped prevent 123,000 kilograms of cocaine - with a street value of $12.2 billion, at today's prices - from reaching the shores of our two nations over this collaboration's lifetime.
Trump may have nothing but disdain for Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, but this unnecessary economic bun fight must spur Canada to wean itself off its unreliable relationship with America, especially in the following sectors.
Autos: whether U.S. automakers move their remaining Ontario production stateside, Canada must capitalize on the dozens of free trade agreements signed by Stephen Harper's government. Take Korea: Hyundai's Canadian sales just hit a record high, and we could induce them to join Honda and Toyota and finally set up a Canadian assembly plant. Canada is in the market for 12 new patrol submarines, and Korean shipyards are a viable option.
The added bonus is that we'd immediately hit NATO's 2 per cent target.
Energy: Shale gas helped make the U.S. self-sufficient, but the Liberals refused to pivot to new export markets. That cost us more than $150 billion in energy infrastructure projects, according to the Financial Post. Trump gives the Liberals cover for a deathbed conversion on new east-west pipelines and China-focused LNG plants.
Health care: each day, almost 2,000 Windsor-area nurses cross the border to work in Michigan. Ontario is short more than 24,000 nurses, and Queen's Park can now justify financial incentives to bring back our talent. With a key U.S. Senate seat in play next year, Republicans won't be able to explain to voters why Trump cost them these essential workers.
Infrastructure: American politicians already forced Canada to pay the entire $6 billion cost of the new Gordie Howe International Bridge, which will soon connect Detroit and Windsor. If Trump can unilaterally reopen USMCA, perhaps Canada should terminate the bridge's revenue-sharing agreement?
Terrorism: in the wake of the 9/11 terrorist attacks, Canada sent thousands of troops to Afghanistan to root out Osama bin Laden and his al-Qaeda disciples. 158 Canadian personnel lost their lives in that theatre, and countless more brought home scars that will never heal. The mission made sense at the time, even if our diplomatic relationship with the U.S. has temporarily been relegated to "all business."
It's been a while since General Sir Issac Brock and Chief Tecumseh captured Detroit, but American hockey fans certainly won't have forgotten the pluck that Saskatchewan's Gordie Howe showed when he won four Stanley Cups with the Red Wings.
In the wake of Trump's decision to brawl with a loyal ally, Canada must channel Mr. Hockey's legendary grit and show America that we can fight with the best - and still put the puck in the net.
CAPTION: The Canadian Chamber of Commerce says a decision by U.S. President Donald Trump to delay the implementation of tariffs on Canadian goods headed to the U.S. leaves businesses, workers, and families in the lurch. President Trump listens after signing executive orders in the Oval Office of the White House, Monday, Feb. 3, 2025, in Washington. THE CANADIAN PRESS/AP/Evan Vucci
CREDIT: Mark McQueen

Subject: Noncitizens; Cocaine; Arrest warrants; Fentanyl

Location: United States--US; Canada; Detroit Michigan

People: Howe, Gordie; Trump, Donald J

Title: Canada needs to wean itself off its relationship with the United States. Here's how

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Document 333 of 500

It's wartime. Do without. Shop locally

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/wartime-do-without-shop-locally/docview/3163369192/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=It%27s+wartime.+Do+without.+Shop+locally&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The trouble with writing about the American catastrophe is that it's tough to zero in on what Canadians should do right now. Each hour brings worse news to claim our fragmented attention.
It's like getting ready to line up at the long Trump dispensary counter of cruelty, ignorance, corruption and violence, as comedian Stephen Colbert suggested, and every ticket from the machine says #1. So, we all rush in. We fill our bags which are too small to contain the latest wretched overflow of abominations.
Let's focus. What should we do as U.S. tariffs are on pause? We should begin to boycott American goods for however long U.S. President Donald Trump stays in power.
Many males find boycotting difficult to conceptualize - clearly their mothers and wives shop for them - but as I tell small children on monkey bars, have an exit strategy.
Make lists. Check every label. Buy South American citrus. Online, most shopping sites will let you block American sellers. Purists will avoid U.S.-owned sites like Etsy and Wayfair but you can limit-search for Canadian or other foreign sellers only. Oh, and millennials, those U.S.-owned decor hives like the endless Pottery Barn/West Elm/ Crate and Barrel/Crate Kids/CB2 centipede are closed for the duration.
Shop Canadian. Smythe, Gildan, Biblioasis. Independent bookstores except Indigo. Shop French-owned Vestiaire Collective for non-U. S. vintage because most clothing sold in Canada is freshly American. Shop Swedish-owned COS for clothing. Shop EQ3 for Canadian furniture, often made of solid wood instead of dreaded MDF and veneer.
It's wartime. Do without. Shop locally.
Don't vote for Ontario Premier Doug Ford, who was filmed recently telling supporters, "On Election Day was I happy this guy won? 100 per cent I was. Then the guy pulled out the knife and f - ing yanked it in me."
Much as I admired Ford's fast action on tariffs, dumping American booze and killing a $100-million contract with Elon Musk's Starlink, why did he sign with Musk in the first place? And what did Ford love about the sour orange man?
Ford is the reason many Ontarians don't have a doctor, and universities are starved of funds. Sounds MAGA to me. The hapless NDP so dislikes ex-MPP Sarah Jama's kaffiyeh that I don't recognize them. I'll vote Liberal, thanks.
Federally, don't vote Conservative. Pierre Poilievre is a strange, narrow-minded, spiteful man without experience in management, human relations, and basic courtesy (people, people and people). He's a shouter. He makes Don Jr. look socially adept. He seems to want vengeance.
Don't be distracted by the appalling things Presidents Musk and Trump are doing to Americans. I know it feels so right to be horrified by the purge of the FBI officials who pursued the Jan. 6 insurrectionists. But it's standard. As Margaret Atwood says, as with Hitler and Stalin, first they kill the true believers.
But Americans have to walk through Trump's House of Horror before they understand pain. They're a very literal people. In order to ensure that a takeover by the ultra-rich never happens again, they will have to suffer themselves rather than watch the suffering of others.
Remember, Trump's presidency is not our battle. What he does to his own people is of interest but not our concern. We have to bond more closely with our allies, do better by NATO, and concentrate on our own survival.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's speech after Trump's tariff threat was brilliant. It struck the right emotional tone, measured and explanatory. It told the story of the American attack without a blizzard of hot adjectives. We love Canada, he said. The rush of love for country that Canadians displayed in the days that followed has powered our hearts.
Interestingly, wasn't Chrystia Freeland prescient in opposing handouts in order to "keep our fiscal powder dry" for emergencies? Good for her. She's my candidate for the Liberal leadership but if she doesn't win, Mark Carney seems reliable, knowledgeable, and well-versed in talking to allies. He's calm.
I'll close with a shoutout to Canadian comic Rick Mercer's 2001 docu-comedy, "Talking to Americans." It's on YouTube. The Americans he met back then were credulous, dim-witted, and loved invading places and shooting people. They haven't changed. They were Trumpers even then.
CAPTION: President Donald Trump blames both Canada and Mexico for the flow in fentanyl into the U.S.

Subject: Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Tariffs

Location: United States--US; Canada

People: Trump, Donald J

Document 334 of 500

It's wartime, so let's shop locally

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/wartime-so-lets-shop-locally/docview/3163368282/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=It%27s+wartime%2C+so+let%27s+shop+locally&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 335 of 500

Has this election hit a turning point?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/has-this-election-hit-turning-point/docview/3163368205/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Has+this+election+hit+a+turning+point%3F&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Bonnie Crombie wants to make the Ontario election about Ontario again.
The rookie Liberal leader may now get her way, thanks to Doug Ford himself.
Hard as Ford has tried to make the campaign about just Donald Trump alone, Crombie is now confident that his re-election play has been exposed as a cynical ploy to retain power as premier. Right out of the gate this week, Ford dropped the ball when he was caught on an open mic telling his Progressive Conservative supporters the precise opposite, in private, of what he tells Ontarians in public.
Ford confessed he "100 per cent" rooted for Trump to win last Nov. 5 - the very politician who promised to impose ruinous tariffs on Ontario. In one blunder, Ford exposed his secret love-hate relationship with the American president.
That accidental moment of truth reveals the depths of his duplicity, Crombie told the Democracy Forum that I hosted Tuesday at Toronto Metropolitan University. And she believes it's a game-changer in an election she now wants to turn into a referendum on Ford - not Trump.
"I think the referendum will be on Doug Ford and his performance as premier, but he wants to make it about tariffs - even though the election is not about tariffs," she told me. "We know he used that as an excuse to call an election - he said so basically himself."
Crombie is convinced Ford's unintentional confessional will dog him until voting day on Feb. 27.
"I don't think it's a one-day wonder - there are always these moments in a campaign that are kind of pivot points, turning points, that are revealing about a person's character," she told me.
"So what does it say about a man who is a leader in this province, who would stand with the president, knowing that he was going to be hurtful to our economy?"
Stunning as Ford's mistake might seem, the bigger question is whether Crombie can truly capitalize on it. He has blundered before - on the Greenbelt scandal and the beer retailing boondoggle, with billions of dollars at stake - yet voters have forgiven and forgotten, leaving him far ahead in first place according to opinion polls.
Who can defeat Ford, as he eyes a third consecutive majority government? Can Crombie, who is also under attack from NDP Leader Marit Stiles?
The Liberal leader insists she can do it by shifting the "ballot question" from Trump to Ford, from America to Ontario, and from tariff tactics to the problems facing Ontarians at home. That means sidestepping Ford's traps as he tries to drive political wedges on divisive issues.
"I will not, sorry to say, be talking about booze, bike lanes, big spas or my rich buddies. I will be speaking about health care, affordability, education to some degree, and building homes and tax cuts."
The Liberal leader has made health care the centrepiece of her campaign, focusing on a $3-billion plan to fix the shortage of family doctors that affects more than two million Ontarians. But she also spoke passionately - and personally - about the housing crisis that affects middle-class people and afflicts homeless people.
Crombie has promised major investment to increase security on public transit, where many of the unhoused can be found riding the subway or streetcars - but she condemned Ford's plan to remove encampments in public parks if people have nowhere else to go.
Her opposition to Ford's tactics would compel Crombie to make common cause with other opposition parties - notably the New Democrats and Greens - if the Tories were to fall short of a governing majority in this election. Should the Liberals hold the balance of power, there is no circumstance under which she would prop up a Ford government.
"Would not, no. No, I would not keep him in power. I think he's doing ... immense damage to this province and the people of this province, and I think he has to go. He has to be sent packing," Crombie told the Democracy Forum.
She criticized the Tories for leaving colleges and universities underfunded after they cut tuition by 10 per cent in 2019, leaving it frozen ever since. Crombie pledged to make the post-secondary sector whole again with fresh infusions of cash flow, but stressed that she still wouldn't increase tuition.
She also cast herself as a tax cutter who'd still find more money for health and housing. How to reconcile all these seemingly opposed promises? Crombie claims she'd do it by eliminating the giveaways favoured by Ford - like the $3-billion cost of mailing out $200 rebate cheques to every Ontarian in the middle of an election campaign, billions more wasted on the beer boondoggle and overspending on Ontario Place.
That's money out the door, of course. But Crombie promised more precision in a fully costed campaign platform that's coming soon.

Crombie's appearance at the Democracy Forum can be viewed online. I'll be hosting NDP Leader Marit Stiles at Toronto Metropolitan University on Wednesday at a noon event livestreamed on thestar.com. Disclosure: I'm a senior fellow at the Dais at TMU, which sponsors the events.
CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION

Document 336 of 500

Tariff plans say more about Ford than his opponents

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tariff-plans-say-more-about-ford-than-his/docview/3163368176/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tariff+plans+say+more+about+Ford+than+his+opponents&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Spare a thought for the LCBO workers who spent days removing California wine, Kentucky bourbon and all the other American tipples from their shelves only to have to put them all back on Monday evening.
And pour one out for them, as it were, should they end up having to go through the whole exercise again. Which certainly seems possible. Donald Trump's 30-day pause on tariffs on Canadian imports, which was supposed to go into effect Tuesday, could turn back into tariffs a month from now. Or in three months, or indeed three days, given the U.S, president's wild unpredictability on this file (or any file, really).
Should the tariffs go ahead, it's a given that liquor-store workers will be drafted into Ontario's official response, because NDP Leader Marit Stiles and Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie have echoed Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford's plan to avoid purchasing U.S. goods wherever possible.
The two opposition leaders are, broadly speaking, not in opposition to anything Ford proposed as part of his Trump response plan. For that brief moment on Monday, when the three major parties were releasing their tariff-countering proposals, what was striking was how little they differed. Immediate financial support for impacted businesses. Tax relief. A "Buy Ontario" mandate. An increased emphasis on interprovincial trade and a determination to find new trading partners beyond the United States.
Some of the proposals had different numbers attached, depending on the party - the NDP didn't burden itself with such specifics at all - but they all pointed to one thing: a provincial government that is prepared to direct a firehose of money at the sectors and workers targeted by Trump's tariffs.
As Crombie put it, while speaking to reporters on the campaign trail on Monday, "there is no daylight right now between my approach and (that of) Doug Ford." She also said she agreed with him "on most of the measures."
This is a fairly remarkable statement in the early days of an election campaign. There is a lot of theatre to these things, yes, but party leaders trying to replace a majority government will usually treat every proposal from their rivals as the worst possible idea at the worst possible time. "Actually, those are some pretty good ideas" is not usually heard at the daily scrum.
But that the parties are in lockstep on the tariff response says more about Ford than it does about his opponents. It wasn't so long ago that the Progressive Conservative leader was publicly supportive of Trump, a fellow conservative (in theory) who is also not a career politician. Even as he was announcing retaliatory plans on Monday, Ford was caught on a hot mic saying he "100 per cent" wanted Trump to win the presidency in November, a clip that will follow him for the rest of the campaign. Before he declared "Canada Is Not for Sale" on a baseball cap, it was easy to imagine Ford trying to thread the needle of defending Ontario while downplaying Trump's threats. He might even have tried the Kevin O'Leary doctrine of near-total appeasement.
Instead, he's gone the other way, emphasizing what he sees as Trump's betrayal and all but vowing to smash those bourbon bottles himself if it comes to it. His willingness to spend tens of billions of dollars to backstop business and workers at the outset of a trade war is similarly aggressive, as well as mildly surprising given Ford is nominally a fiscal conservative. If Ford had ever hoped to present a balanced budget, that plan wouldn't survive his tariff-countering measures.
There's a whiff of Ford's COVID-19 response to all this. In the pandemic, there were people, especially on the right of the political spectrum, who downplayed the health concerns and wanted governments to do as little as possible to deal with the virus. Ford went completely the other way, and for a time, Ontario's response was as aggressive as almost anyone's.
That old line about there being no atheists in foxholes applies: in a time of crisis, Ford is less wedded to his conservatism.
But if Ford benefits from being a wartime general, it is also true that, at least for now, that war has been averted. A campaign that might have been about standing up to Trump will now need some kind of second act.
One imagines that Stiles and Crombie won't be quite so accommodating to Ford when he announces the rest of his plans.
Scott Stinson is a journalist based in suburban Toronto.
CREDIT: Scott Stinson contributor Scott Stinson is a journalist based in suburban Toronto.;

Document 337 of 500

Canada needs to wean itself off U.S.

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/canada-needs-wean-itself-off-u-s/docview/3163367939/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada+needs+to+wean+itself+off+U.S.&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canadians may be shocked by the dramatic change in our relationship with the U.S., but the new White House administration is right to complain that Ottawa wasn't remotely interested in border security prior to President Donald Trump's return to power.
According to a new report by Criminal Intelligence Service Canada, the number of Canadian organized crime groups manufacturing drugs such as fentanyl nearly doubled in one year, from 51 in 2023 to 99 in 2024.
We can't fault Trump for the fact that it's been almost five years since the auditor general criticized the Liberals for having lost track of 34,700 foreign nationals facing deportation, including convicted criminals. The number of outstanding immigration arrest warrants hit 37,326 in 2023, as I railed about here last summer.
The expectation that Trump would be satiated by the two new RCMP helicopters Ottawa just leased is, given the border's 8,891-kilometre length, laughable.
One bright spot is our 19-year contribution to America's drug interdiction strategy. Hopefully someone has told Trump that the Royal Canadian Navy just sent another new offshore patrol ship to the Caribbean in search of drug smugglers. Also on board is a U.S. Coast Guard Law Enforcement Detachment, making this one of several joint anti-narcotic missions planned for 2025.
Last fall, similar teams nabbed $60 million of cocaine off the shores of Acapulco, Mexico. It's not fentanyl, but the Canadian military has helped prevent 123,000 kilograms of cocaine - with a street value of $12.2 billion, at today's prices - from reaching the shores of our two nations over this collaboration's lifetime.
Trump may have nothing but disdain for Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, but this unnecessary economic bun fight must spur Canada to wean itself off its unreliable relationship with America, especially in the following sectors.
Autos Whether U.S. automakers move their remaining Ontario production stateside, Canada must capitalize on the dozens of free trade agreements signed by Stephen Harper's government. Take Korea: Hyundai's Canadian sales just hit a record high, and we could induce them to join Honda and Toyota and finally set up a Canadian assembly plant. Canada is in the market for 12 new patrol submarines, and Korean shipyards are a viable option. The added bonus is that we'd immediately hit NATO's two per cent target.
Energy Shale gas helped make the U.S. self-sufficient, but the Liberals refused to pivot to new export markets. That cost us more than $150 billion in energy infrastructure projects, according to the Financial Post. Trump gives the Liberals cover for a deathbed conversion on new east-west pipelines and Chinafocused LNG plants.
Health careEach day, almost 2,000 Windsor-area nurses cross the border to work in Michigan. Ontario is short more than 24,000 nurses, and Queen's Park can now justify financial incentives to bring back our talent. With a key U.S. Senate seat in play next year, Republicans won't be able to explain to voters why Trump cost them these essential workers.
Infrastructure American politicians already forced Canada to pay the entire $6-billion cost of the new Gordie Howe International Bridge, which will soon connect Detroit and Windsor. If Trump can unilaterally reopen USMCA, perhaps Canada should terminate the bridge's revenue-sharing agreement?
Terrorism In the wake of the 9/11 terrorist attacks, Canada sent thousands of troops to Afghanistan to root out Osama bin Laden and his al-Qaida disciples; 158 Canadian personnel lost their lives in that theatre and countless more brought home scars that will never heal. The mission made sense at the time, even if our diplomatic relationship with the U.S. has temporarily been relegated to "all business."
It's been a while since General Sir Issac Brock and Chief Tecumseh captured Detroit, but American hockey fans certainly won't have forgotten the pluck that Saskatchewan's Gordie Howe showed when he won four Stanley Cups with the Red Wings.
In the wake of Trump's decision to brawl with a loyal ally, Canada must channel Mr. Hockey's legendary grit and show America that we can fight with the best - and still put the puck in the net.
MARK MCQUEEN IS A TORONTOBASED ENTREPRENEUR, A FORMER BOARD CHAIR OF TWO FEDERAL AGENCIES AND AN ADVISER IN BRIAN MULRONEY'S PMO AND A COLUMNIST FOR THE STAR. FOLLOW HIM ON X: @MARKRMCQUEEN

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+I+may+have+figured+out+Trump%27s+sudden+obsession+with+Canada%2C+Feb.+4+Trudeau+says+U.S.+tariffs+will+be+paused+for+30+days%3B+Ontario+halts+Starlink+cancellation%2C+liquor+removal%2C+Feb.+3+Pierre+Poilievre+calls+on+Ottawa+to+send+troops+to+the+Canada-U.S.+border%2C+Feb.+3+Doug+Ford+hasn%27t+needed+a+mandate+to+make+major+changes+in+Toronto.+So+why+does+he+need+one+for+Donald+Trump%3F+Feb.+2+This+GTA+board+just+voted+to+restrict+the+Pride+flag+inside%2C+Jan.+30&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canadians are different from Americans
I may have figured out Trump's sudden obsession with Canada, Feb. 4
Andrew Phillips says Canadians struggle to articulate what makes us different from Americans. Well, Canada takes pride in accepting and tolerating many cultures, and refugees from war: Vietnamese, Syrians, Ukrainians, Afghans and more. We have countless festivals celebrating different cultures. Chinese New Year festivities and the summer Caribbean parade. We support a French-speaking province. Canadians help other nations, the U.S. in particular, with wildfires, the hurricanes and disaster support. Our schools teach evolution and respect science. Our universities and colleges celebrate learning and are innovators in many fields, such as the discovery of insulin. We have Noble prize winner Geoffery Hinton. We invented the Canadarm used in space missions. Though we have problems with gangs and guns, our nation is not obsessed with gun ownership. We can go to the doctor and the emergency department and not be bankrupted with payments afterward. Though unsupported by our Ontario premier, our health-care system is world class, even though we may be forced to wait longer. I am happy and proud to be Canadian.
Diane Sullivan, Toronto
Promote trade with provinces, new partners
Trudeau says U.S. tariffs will be paused for 30 days; Ontario halts Starlink cancellation, liquor removal, Feb. 3
U.S. President Donald Trump is like a warning shot across our bow. Let's not invest all our eggs in one basket. Diversify. We need a government that promotes Canadian interprovincial trade and the development of new trade partners. We now know the U.S. is not a trustworthy trade partner anymore.
David Miller, Keswick, Ont.
Canada should close our border for 'maintenance'
Electricity suppliers in both Ontario and Quebec need to shut off the supply to the U.S. for maintenance immediately. The oil pipelines from Alberta to Republican states need to be shut down for environmental inspections immediately. We will notify the White House when they'll be operational again.
Our end of the international bridges may be unsafe. Our engineers have to conduct thorough inspections to determine if remediation is required. Therefore, American cargo destined for Canada needs to remain on the American side until the status of the structure is determined to be safe. We wouldn't want to risk American lives.
Mark Buczek, Milton
Extra security could stop guns, drugs at border
Pierre Poilievre calls on Ottawa to send troops to the Canada-U.S. border, Feb. 3
Sending troops to patrol the Canada-U.S. border sounds like a great idea, but not for the reasons Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre and U.S. President Donald Trump are giving.
If the U.S. Border Patrol can't stop illegal drugs and firearms from crossing over into Canada, extra security on the Canadian side should be able to catch them coming in.
Pretty sure that there will be far more guns and drugs caught going north than drugs going south.
Michael Bines, Toronto
Two good news stories lost amid the U.S. chaos
Lost in all the awful noise created by U.S. President Donald Trump are two "good news" stories. First, America's national bird, the bald eagle, has been taken off the endangered species list as its population has rebounded. Second, Canada's new national bird, a special one-finger salute directed toward Trump, is thriving. Go Canada.
Patrick Stewart, Toronto
City should use empty ad spaces on TTC vehicles
If you ride the subway these days, you'll notice there are quite a lot of empty advertising spaces. If those advertising spaces are going unused - if there aren't enough paying customers for those spaces - couldn't the city of Toronto to use those spaces to advertise city facilities, services and programs?
Could it be possible to have city of Toronto ads (or provincial ads or federal ads) available on "stand by" to be put into advertising spaces that would otherwise go empty? If ad spaces on the TTC are not generating revenue, then they might as well be used to inform riders of publicly funded things that they could use.
Paul Kern, Toronto
Ford squeezed our universities without mandate
Doug Ford hasn't needed a mandate to make major changes in Toronto. So why does he need one for Donald Trump? Feb. 2
Shawn Micallef is bang on when he says Premier Doug Ford did a lot without a mandate. One thing he missed was Ford's decision to cut university tuition by 10 per cent across the board and freeze any tuition increases. He did not campaign on this.
This action, combined with no increase in funds to universities, has resulted in a situation where universities are operating with 2014 budgets but paying 2025 prices. Even after his own hand-selected "blue ribbon" task force said this was unsustainable, he did not change course.
What is more disappointing is that the Ontarians, who repeatedly indicate strong interest in university education for their children, do not seem to care that the universities are being choked of funds, and that the inevitable outcome is fewer seats for their kids. In a world full of misinformation, universities are central to knowledge creation, preservation and critical thinking.
Dan Malleck,
St. Catharines, Ont.
Make defunding Catholic schools an election issue
This GTA board just voted to restrict the Pride flag inside, Jan. 30
The party leader who promises to defund the Catholic school system will have my vote.
Anne Morris-Dadson,
Richmond Hill

Document 339 of 500

Both Ford and Trump reveal their true colours

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/both-ford-trump-reveal-their-true-colours/docview/3163367792/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Both+Ford+and+Trump+reveal+their+true+colours&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Picture this: Inmates in the prison yard getting some fresh air. It's all fun and games until a fight breaks out. There is tussling and shoving. The guards come running and separate the crowd.
As everyone retreats, we see one prisoner on the ground, bleeding on the concrete. Clutching his abdomen, he gasps and chokes out: "On election day, was I happy this guy won? One hundred per cent I was. Then the guy pulled out the knife and f--king yanked it in me."
This is the story of Premier Doug Ford and U.S. President Donald Trump. Now, guess who pulled out the knife and who got yanked?
A hot mic caught Ford saying he was happy about a Trump election victory.
Let me stop chuckling because this is a ridiculous situation. Not "ha-ha" ridiculous, but "are-you-for-real" ridiculous. As someone who keeps up with politics at home and abroad, I cannot comprehend why Ford would be surprised that Trump, the man who has absolutely no allegiance to anyone, would not have any loyalty to Ontarians or Canadians? I mean, we saw him waving the knife around like a lunatic for the past eight years, threatening anyone and everyone.
And yet, Ford thought a Trump victory was something to celebrate.
Like many Canadians, I wasn't celebrating Trump's win. I still don't understand why anyone - American citizens, undocumented workers, marginalized peoples, anyone who isn't a tech billionaire - would trust that Trump wouldn't shove a shiv in their stomach and happily watch them bleed out. Trump promised to rip up the U.S. Constitution, why would he hesitate to shank us, their friendly neighbour to the north?
It was never a question of if Trump would betray Canada. It was when and how he would do it. Truthfully, the entire tariff crisis feels like manufactured pandemonium. I'm not saying we won't wake up 30 days from now to 25 per cent tariffs slapped on us, but Trump's ultimate goal is to create enough confusion to distract from some more nefarious plans.
Now, with three weeks until Ontario's upcoming provincial election, Ford is out here playing Captain Canada - appearing on CNN talking about how much these tariffs would harm both Canadian and American workers. He urged Ontarians to buy Canadian products, threatened tear up a $100-million contract from Elon Musk's Starlink and pull American liquor out of the LCBO. Strong countermeasures, no doubt.
It's also great stagecraft during an election campaign, keeping Ford on local, national and international news every night, looking like he's standing up for his constituents.
Ford is learning from one of the best game show hosts ever, The Donald.
Why would Ford, who blamed immigrants for antisemitic violence, nearly triggered a general strike in protest Bill 28, has a weird fixation with Ontarians accessing alcohol outside the LCBO or Beer Stores, and refused to act during convoy blockade in Ottawa, have wanted Trump to win the 2024 U.S. election?
Birds of a feather and all that.
If you're surprised that Ford would prefer a Trump presidency over a Kamala Harris presidency, you haven't been paying attention.
Truthfully, I don't think that Ford is looking for world domination the way Trump is. Ford is trying to hold onto power and scrape together an election victory now he's seemingly in our good graces. But, aside from $200 cheques that bounce and boondoggles that keep us all scratching our heads, I am befuddled as to what this premier has done that would make anyone give him another mandate.
The great, wise African American poet Maya Angelou said, "When someone shows you who they are, believe them the first time. People know themselves much better than you do. That's why it's important to stop expecting them to be something other than who they are."
Ford has shown us, over and over again, who he is. Why do we ever expect anything different?
Shellene Drakes-Tull is a Toronto-based storyteller, educator and the founder of Sweet Lime Communications. She is a freelance contributing columnist for the Star.
CAPTION: If you're surprised that Progressive Conservative Doug Ford welcomed Donald Trump's win over Kamala Harris last fall, you haven't been paying attention, writes Shellene Drakes-Tull.Adrian Wyld THE CANADIAN PRESS
CREDIT: Shellene Drakes-Tull OPINION Shellene Drakes-Tull is a Toronto-based storyteller, educator and the founder of Sweet Lime Communications. She is a freelance contributing columnist for the Star.;

Subject: Provincial elections; Political campaigns; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Tariffs

Location: United States--US; Canada

People: Harris, Kamala; Trump, Donald J

Title: Both Ford and Trump reveal their true colours

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Corrections&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A Feb. 1 article about Doug Ford incorrectly stated that the number of new housing starts last year in Ontario was lower than in any other province. In fact, the number of year-over-year housing starts in 2024 was lower than any other province.
A Feb. 1 article about Art Metropole incorrectly stated that it started in a small office in Greektown and has since hopscotched across the city in various locations, including Queen Street West. In fact, the art bookstore's first location was in the studio of General Idea, in a building called Art Metropole, at Yonge and Dundas. There was never a Queen Street West location, but rather King Street West and Richmond Street West.

Document 341 of 500

What's more shocking: That Trump shivved Ford or that Ford was surprised?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/whats-more-shocking-that-trump-shivved-ford-was/docview/3163367716/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=What%27s+more+shocking%3A+That+Trump+shivved+Ford+or+that+Ford+was+surprised%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Picture this: Inmates in the prison yard getting some fresh air. It's all fun and games until a fight breaks out. There is tussling and shoving. The guards come running and separate the crowd.
As everyone retreats, we see one prisoner on the ground, bleeding on the concrete. Clutching his abdomen, he gasps and chokes out: "On election day, was I happy this guy won? One hundred per cent I was. Then the guy pulled out the knife and f - ing yanked it in me."
This is the story of Premier Doug Ford and President Donald Trump. Now, guess who pulled out the knife and who got yanked?
A hot mic caught Ford saying he was happy about a Trump election victory.
Let me stop chuckling because this is a ridiculous situation. Not "ha-ha" ridiculous, but "are-you-for-real" ridiculous. As someone who keeps up with politics at home and abroad, I cannot comprehend why Ford would be surprised that Trump, the man who has absolutely no allegiance to anyone, would not have any loyalty to Ontarians or Canadians? I mean, we saw him waving the knife around like a lunatic for the last eight years, threatening any and everyone.
And yet, Ford thought a Trump victory was something to celebrate.
Like many Canadians, I wasn't celebrating Trump's win. I still don't understand why anyone - American citizens, undocumented workers, marginalized peoples, anyone who isn't a tech billionaire - would trust that Trump wouldn't shove a shiv in their stomach and happily watch them bleed out. Trump promised to rip up the U.S. Constitution, why would he hesitate to shank us, their friendly neighbour to the north?
It was never a question of if Trump would betray Canada. It was when and how he would do it. Truthfully, the entire tariff crisis feels like manufactured pandemonium. I'm not saying we won't wake up 30 days from now to 25 per cent tariffs slapped on us, but Trump's ultimate goal is to create enough confusion to distract from some more nefarious plans.
Now, with three weeks until Ontario's upcoming provincial election, Ford is out here playing Captain Canada - appearing on CNN talking about how much these tariffs would harm both Canadian and American workers. He urged Ontarians to buy Canadian products, threatened tear up a $100-million contract from Elon Musk's Starlink and pull American liquor out of the LCBO. Strong countermeasures, no doubt.
It's also great stagecraft during an election campaign, keeping Ford on local, national and international news every night, looking like he's standing up for his constituents.
Ford is learning from one of the best game show hosts ever, The Donald.
Why would Ford, who blamed immigrants for antisemitic violence, nearly triggered a general strike in protest Bill 28, has a weird fixation with Ontarians accessing alcohol outside the LCBO or Beer Stores, and refused to act during convoy blockade in Ottawa, have wanted Trump to win the 2024 U.S. election?
Birds of a feather and all that.
If you're surprised that Ford would prefer a Trump presidency over a Kamala Harris presidency, you haven't been paying attention.
Truthfully, I don't think that Ford is looking for world domination the way Trump is. Ford is trying to hold onto power and scrape together an election victory now he's seemingly in our good graces. But, aside from $200 cheques that bounce and boondoggles that keep us all scratching our heads, I am befuddled as to what this premier has done that would make anyone give him another mandate.
The great, wise African American poet Maya Angelou said, "When someone shows you who they are, believe them the first time. People know themselves much better than you do. That's why it's important to stop expecting them to be something other than who they are."
Ford has shown us, over and over again, who he is. Why do we ever expect anything different?
CAPTION: Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford arrives for a press conference in Etobicoke on Monday. "If you're surprised that Ford would prefer a Trump presidency over a Kamala Harris presidency, you haven't been paying attention," writes Shellene Drakes-Tull.

Business indexing term: Subject: Tariffs

Location: United States--US; Canada

People: Harris, Kamala
