Document 342 of 500

Ford defends comment about Trump: PC leader says he'll work 'with anyone' after he was caught on mic praising U.S. president's win

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-defends-comment-about-trump/docview/3163367644/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+defends+comment+about+Trump%3A+PC+leader+says+he%27ll+work+%27with+anyone%27+after+he+was+caught+on+mic+praising+U.S.+president%27s+win&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: So much for Doug Ford's Trump card.
Ford struggled Tuesday to explain why he wanted Donald Trump to win the U.S. presidential election after being caught on a hot mic in a gaffe that has jolted the Ontario campaign.
It was an embarrassing setback for the Progressive Conservative leader, who has framed the snap Feb. 27 vote as a referendum on who could best deal with Trump's threatened 25 per cent tariffs against Canada.
Complaining to reporters in Ottawa that "you guys are obsessed with that," Ford was repeatedly pressed about the hot mic admission that he had hoped the Republican Trump would beat Democrat Kamala Harris last November.
"On election day, was I happy this guy won? One hundred per cent I was. Then the guy pulled out the knife and f--king yanked it in me," the Tory leader said Monday in Etobicoke, making a stabbing motion with his hand.
The following day, Ford was forced to do damage control for his self-inflicted wound.
"Well, you know something, I always believe in working with anyone," he said in Ottawa, where he promised Queen's Park would upload OC Transpo's troubled LRT line.
"On the hot mic, yeah, he makes me swear. I think he makes a lot of people swear in this country," Ford acknowledged, insisting he believed the president would somehow be less provocative in his second term.
Trump touted tariffs during the recent American campaign in which he also called the Jan. 6, 2021 attack on the U.S. Capitol "a day of love," even though the FBI dubbed it domestic terrorism that threatened the peaceful transfer of power.
"I thought it would be a little different, a little change, but obviously he decided to pull the knife out and stab us," Ford said on the shop floor of a door company.
His main rivals pounced on his Monday comments made to Tory candidates Christine Hogarth and David Piccini and several members of the Ontario Professional Fire Fighters Association.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie released a 60-second ad to highlight the PC leader's apparent hypocrisy by repeatedly playing the clip: "Was I happy this guy won? One hundred per cent I was."
"Doug Ford just said he was happy Donald Trump won the election. Happy the guy who campaigned on a trade war with Canada became president?" intones a female narrator against footage of Trump boasting "the tariff will be substantial in some cases" and that he is "a tariff man standing on a tariff platform."
"He was happy? Was he happy Canadians might lose their jobs? Was he happy about the threat of Canada becoming a 51st state?" continues the announcer.
"Or does he just care so little about the people of this province ... that he saw an attack on Ontario as an opportunity for himself. What else does he say when he thinks you're not listening?"
Speaking to the Star's Martin Regg Cohn at Toronto Metropolitan University's Democracy Forum, Crombie said Ford's support for Trump was revealing, given that Trump was his rationale for an "unnecessary" $189-million election 15 months before Ontario's next scheduled general election in June 2026.
"He used that as an excuse to call an election. He said basically said so himself ... in his hot mic moment," the Liberal leader told Cohn, predicting it could be a "turning point."
"I don't think it's a one-day wonder. There are always these moments in a campaign that are kind of pivot points ... that are revealing about a person's character," she said, noting Trump "threatened our economy."
New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles said "it doesn't surprise me at all" that Ford would say he preferred Trump.
"It should make Ontarians question his judgment and his character," she told reporters at a campaign stop in Kingston.
"This is who Doug Ford is. He says one thing when he's out in front of the microphones ... and he says another one behind closed doors," Stiles said earlier Tuesday in Ottawa, where she highlighted her education plan that includes a universal school food program.
"It's a little bit sad, you know, that Doug Ford clearly is a big fan of Donald Trump, but Donald Trump doesn't seem to be aware of who Doug Ford is."
Public opinion research has suggested Canadians overwhelmingly preferred Harris over Trump last fall.
Pollara Strategic Insights found 62 per cent of respondents had positive feelings about Harris with only 18 per cent having negative feelings toward the then-vice-president.
The firm found 66 per cent had negative feelings about Trump with 20 per cent having positive feelings toward him.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie, Rob Ferguson and Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Document 343 of 500

Is Doug Ford's hot mic mistake the turning point in the Ontario election campaign?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/is-doug-fords-hot-mic-mistake-turning-point/docview/3163570727/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+Doug+Ford%27s+hot+mic+mistake+the+turning+point+in+the+Ontario+election+campaign%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Bonnie Crombie wants to make the Ontario election about Ontario again.
The rookie Liberal leader may now get her way, thanks to Doug Ford himself.
Hard as Ford has tried to make the campaign about just Donald Trump alone, Crombie is now confident that his re-election play has been exposed as a cynical ploy to retain power as premier. Right out of the gate this week, Ford dropped the ball when he was caught on an open mic telling his Progressive Conservative supporters the precise opposite, in private, of what he tells Ontarians in public.
Ford confessed he "100 per cent" rooted for Trump to win last Nov. 5 - the very politician who promised to impose ruinous tariffs on Ontario. In one blunder, Ford exposed his secret love-hate relationship with the American president.
That accidental moment of truth reveals the depths of his duplicity, Crombie told the Democracy Forum that I hosted Tuesday at Toronto Metropolitan University. And she believes it's a game-changer in an election she now wants to turn into a referendum on Ford - not Trump.
"I think the referendum will be on Doug Ford and his performance as premier, but he wants to make it about tariffs - even though the election is not about tariffs," she told me. "We know he used that as an excuse to call an election - he said so basically himself."
Crombie is convinced Ford's unintentional confessional will dog him until voting day on Feb. 27.
"I don't think it's a one-day wonder - there are always these moments in a campaign that are kind of pivot points, turning points, that are revealing about a person's character," she told me.
"So what does it say about a man who is a leader in this province, who would stand with the president, knowing that he was going to be hurtful to our economy?"
Stunning as Ford's mistake might seem, the bigger question is whether Crombie can truly capitalize on it. He has blundered before - on the Greenbelt scandal and the beer retailing boondoggle, with billions of dollars at stake - yet voters have forgiven and forgotten, leaving him far ahead in first place according to opinion polls.
Who can defeat Ford, as he eyes a third consecutive majority government? Can Crombie, who is also under attack from NDP Leader Marit Stiles?
The Liberal leader insists she can do it by shifting the "ballot question" from Trump to Ford, from America to Ontario, and from tariff tactics to the problems facing Ontarians at home. That means sidestepping Ford's traps as he tries to drive political wedges on divisive issues.
"I will, not sorry to say, be talking about booze, bike lanes, big spas, or my rich buddies. I will be speaking about health care, affordability, education to some degree, and building homes and tax cuts."
The Liberal leader has made health care the centrepiece of her campaign, focusing on a $3-billion plan to fix the shortage of family doctors that affects more than two million Ontarians. But she also spoke passionately - and personally - about the housing crisis that affects middle class people and afflicts homeless people.
Crombie has promised major investment to increase security on public transit, where many of the unhoused can be found riding the subway or streetcars - but she condemned Ford's plan to remove encampments in public parks if people have nowhere else to go.
Her opposition to Ford's tactics would compel Crombie to make common cause with other opposition parties - notably the New Democrats and Greens - if the Tories were to fall short of a governing majority in this election. Should the Liberals hold the balance of power, there is no circumstance under which she would prop up a Ford government.
"Would not, no. No, I would not keep him in power. I think he's doing ... immense damage to this province and the people of this province, and I think he has to go. He has to be sent packing," Crombie told the Democracy Forum.
She criticized the Tories for leaving colleges and universities underfunded after they cut tuition by 10 per cent in 2019, leaving it frozen ever since. Crombie pledged to make the post-secondary sector whole again with fresh infusions of cash flow, but stressed that she still wouldn't increase tuition.
She also cast herself as a tax cutter who'd still find more money for health and housing. How to reconcile all these seemingly opposed promises? Crombie claims she'd do it by eliminating the giveaways favoured by Ford - like the $3 billion cost of mailing out $200 rebate cheques to every Ontarian in the middle of an election campaign, billions more wasted on the beer boondoggle and overspending on Ontario Place.
That's money out the door, of course. But Crombie promised more precision in a fully-costed campaign platform that's coming soon.
* * *
Crombie's appearance at the Democracy Forum can be viewed online. I'll be hosting NDP Leader Marit Stiles at Toronto Metropolitan University on Wednesday at a noon event livestreamed here on thestar.com. Disclosure: I'm a senior fellow at the Dais at TMU, which sponsors the events.
CAPTION: Star columnist Martin Regg Cohn speaks with Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie at Toronto Metropolitan University'sDemocracy Forum on Feb. 4, 2025.

Document 344 of 500

Why Canada's handling of Trump's trade war is a test case for the rest of the world

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/why-canadas-handling-trumps-trade-war-is-test/docview/3163212715/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+Canada%27s+handling+of+Trump%27s+trade+war+is+a+test+case+for+the+rest+of+the+world&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: For a few hours on Monday, Ontario Premier Doug Ford led the news in France.
Compatriots from Paris to Perpignan probably couldn't locate Canada's largest province on a map.
But they were sufficiently shocked that the country's economic capital was set to clear American liquor from LCBO shelves, rip up contracts with Elon Musk's Starlink and ban U.S. firms from bidding on government contracts in retaliation for President Donald Trump's 25 per cent tariff threat.
And it is no exaggeration to say that the world's capitals were furiously refreshing their social media feeds, waiting for word that Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's talks with Trump had resulted in a 30-day reprieve.
That's because Canada is a test case for how Trump's altered America is willing to treat its allies.
The Brits will factor the North American trade-and-border clash into their recalibrated approach to the so-called "special relationship" between London and Washington.
Paris and Berlin must decide whether to roll out a red carpet along the transatlantic bridge linking Europe to Washington - or to guard against its sabotage and demolition.
Members of the NATO military alliance are weighing the potential costs of not radically increasing defence spending to five per cent of GDP, as Trump has repeatedly mused.
And Ukraine faces the prospect of paying a steep price for the defence of its besieged democracy.
What lessons can the world draw from Canada's temporary stay of execution at the 11th hour?
First, that Trump aims to remake a name for himself as a leader for whom nothing is sacred and everything is negotiable, even more than during his first term.
Second, that he is a dog who - for the moment, at least - prefers barking to biting.
Colombian President Gustavo Petro, a former left-wing guerrilla, was among the first to cross swords with Trump when he, on principle, turned back two American military planes carrying Colombian deportees. The passengers had been bound with handcuffs, like the criminals Trump makes migrants out to be. An outraged Trump threatened retaliatory tariffs - and to double them if there was no capitulation within a week.
Petro was defiant in his reply: "I resisted torture, and I resist you."
And a deal was reached, though the exact terms are unclear.
The White House declared victory, saying that Petro had "agreed to all of President Trump's terms" including deportation flights aboard U.S. military aircraft. Colombia, too, could claim a win, in that it sent its own military aircraft to repatriate its citizens and proposed formalizing the procedure to ensure future deportees are treated in a "dignified" manner.
For Panama, Trump's bark was enough.
The country fell into America's crosshairs, in part, over a perceived rise in Chinese influence at the Panama Canal, a key crossing for ships travelling to and from the Pacific Ocean.
Trump threatened to seize control of the canal, deeming it a threat to U.S. national security.
That, and a visit by U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio, was all it apparently took for Panamanian President José Raúl Mulino to agree to abandon an infrastructure deal with China.
The morning after Trudeau and Trump hashed out an agreement to avert an all-out trade war, it's not clear whether the concessions that the U.S. President has extracted are all that major or new.
Certainly, the concessions he's gained from Canada and Mexico are nowhere near commensurate with the harm he was apparently prepared to inflict on the economies of all three countries.
The headline from Trump's last-minute Mexico agreement was the deployment of 10,000 soldiers to the U.S. border to stop the flow of fentanyl and migrants into the U.S.
But Mexico did more than that in 2019 - and without the tariff threat - in response to an uptick in migrants crossing the border. At that time, the country sent 15,000 troops to its border with the U.S. and an additional 10,000 to the southern border with Guatemala.
It also struck a deal with former president Joe Biden in 2021 to maintain 10,000 troops on its southern border to prevent migrants from entering the country.
Canada, for its part, appears to have escaped the Trump tariff threat - at least for the next 30 days - on the strength of its $1.3-billion border security plan. Drafted in anticipation of Trump, it was announced last Dec. 17, more than a month before he swore the oath of office.
The only new elements that emerged from Monday's talks were the position of a Canadian "Fentanyl Czar" - presumably with the mandate to co-ordinate federal efforts - and a promise to list drug cartels as terrorist organizations.
Those were enough to appease Trump's tenuous allegation that the Canadian border is a major source of the fentanyl fuelling the U.S. toxic drug crisis. (In fact, less than 0.1 per cent of U.S. fentanyl seizures are intercepted at the Canadian border.)
Regardless, in just his third week in office, Trump watchers can spot a pattern: shrill proclamations and threats followed by more reasonable deals.
It's a pattern that might allow Greenland and the Kingdom of Denmark to catch their breath.
After weeks of hyperventilating over Trump's wish to buy the Arctic island - or seize it using military force - his track record so far would suggest that a middle-ground solution is not so far out of reach.
European allies are divided, but many will also be tempted to view Trump's other demands through the same prism.
Take his desire for NATO allies to commit five per cent of their GDP to defence spending, which would take a gargantuan budgetary effort by most member states.
Or Monday's out-of-the-blue revelation that he wants Ukraine to repay the U.S. military support they've been receiving by granting access to its rare-earth metals, which happen to sit mostly beneath the ground occupied by Russian forces.
"There's nobody who doesn't want to make deals," Trump said Monday.
But there is a risk that grows with each over-the-top, Trumpian threat that is bargained down to a more modest tradeoff.
And that risk is heightened by the presence of America's adversaries, particularly Russia and China, who are paying close attention to the president's modus operandi, and the art of his deal-making.
Trump knows that they are watching. And he also knows that in order for his bulldog bark to be heard and to be feared, pretty soon he will have to bite.
CAPTION: U.S. President Donald Trump, who threatened a trade war only to accept concessions nowhere near commensurate to the harm hewas apparently prepared to inflict.

Subject: Military aircraft; Defense spending; Tariffs; Trade disputes; Presidents; Fentanyl; Armed forces

Business indexing term: Subject: Tariffs Trade disputes

Location: Mexico; United States--US; Canada; China; Ukraine

People: Trump, Donald J

Company / organization: Name: North Atlantic Treaty Organization--NATO; NAICS: 928120

Title: Why Canada's handling of Trump's trade war is a test case for the rest of the world

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2025

Publication date: Feb 4, 2025

Section: news/world

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

Dear Canada, please accept our apologies

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/dear-canada-please-accept-our-apologies/docview/3163212540/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Dear+Canada%2C+please+accept+our+apologies&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Letters from America: Dear Canada, we're sorryHere are some ways Trump's tariffs might hurt Canadians, from food to housing to small businesses, Feb. 2
To our dear Canadian friends: I write to you today with a deep sense of regret. Our two nations share far more than just a border. We share history, values and a friendship that has endured through times of war and peace, hardship and prosperity.
It is with this in mind that I offer our sincerest apologies for the unjust and unwarranted tariffs imposed upon Canadian products by our leadership.
These tariffs do not reflect the goodwill American people hold toward Canada. They do not honour the spirit of co-operation that has long defined our relationship. Instead, these tariffs represent a moment of hostility that runs counter to the respect and trust that should guide our shared economic and diplomatic endeavours.
I recognize these actions will place undue strain on Canadian businesses, workers and families, on people who have stood with us as allies and friends. From the beaches of Normandy to peacekeeping missions around the world, from trade agreements that have strengthened both our economies to the everyday bonds of culture and kinship, Canada has been a steadfast partner. The imposition of these tariffs is not only an insult to that partnership, but a betrayal of the mutual prosperity we have built together.
Please know that many Americans feel as I do and do not support these policies, nor do we condone the aggressive and dismissive rhetoric that has accompanied them. You deserve better from us, and I pray in the future we will once again work to ensure that the true nature of our friendship is one based on fairness, co-operation and respect.
I look forward to the day when this rift is healed, and we can once again move forward as the close allies we were always meant to be.
With respect and regret,
Andrew Lewis, Decatur, Ga.
Canada has always welcomed us
Dear friends of Canada. I live in Minnesota where we share a border. I have travelled to most provinces in wonderful Canada and have always felt welcome and loved every visit I have made. I am writing to express my dismay that the current occupant of our White House has decided to create such a divide between our two countries.
Stay strong Canadians, and increase the price you charge for your oil.
Karen Hovland, St. Paul, Minn.
Hit the U.S. where it hurts most: the walletCanada won't back down, will scale up retaliation against Trump's tariffs as needed, government says, Feb. 2
Tariffs will not solve the fentanyl crisis or illegal immigrants going to the United States. I call upon all Canadians to buy Canadian goods and services, to vacation in Canada. It's a lose-lose scenario with these punitive tariffs brought on by the U.S.
We didn't ask for tariffs. I thought the free trade deal was a reasonable compromise and a measure of fair diplomacy between nations.
President Trump, you must stop this war mongering; it will make your election promises seem like empty rhetoric in the next year or two.
Canada, provinces: hit the States where it hurts, in the pocketbook of Americans.
Provincial leaders, I ask you to open up the trade barriers between provinces.
Encourage all of us to trade west-east, instead of north-south.
Richard MacFarlane, Toronto
Don't spend a dime in the U.S. until tariffs are rescinded
As a dual U.S./Canadian citizen permanently residing in the U.S., I am disgusted that President Donald Trump would impose these vindictive tariffs on an ally and neighbouring country. As such, I encourage all Canadians to avoid visiting the U.S. in response to the Trump tariffs until they are rescinded.
A significant portion of state tax revenue, especially for southern states such as Florida, Texas and Arizona, comes from Canadian visitors and vacationers and the loss of or reduction of this revenue will be painful and likely have a significant impact on these states. This would result in pressure on the American federal government to end the tariffs.
For those Canadians seeking sunshine, there are many alternatives in Mexico, Cuba, the Caribbean and around the world to explore. So please pass on the U.S. this year and send a message to the Trump administration that the U.S. tariff policy is unacceptable to Canadians.
David Sells, Sarasota, Fla.
Trudeau does Canadians proudJustin Trudeau strikes back with retaliatory levies after Trump imposes tariffs on Canadian goods, Feb. 1
In his press conference announcing Canada's response to Trump's tariffs, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau showed us again that he is articulate, rational, and classy: everything that Trump is not. He conducted himself as a true leader should and made me proud to be Canadian.
Thomas Mulligan, Grimsby
Doug Ford has once again failed OntarioDoug Ford has stripped himself of credibility with a Trumped-up snap election, Jan. 29
Martin Regg Cohn's article about Premier Doug Ford not having any remaining credibility hit the nail on the head.
Premier Ford has a mandate for another 16 months and has failed to deliver on health care, housing and numerous other important issues. Now he has called an election in hopes of another four-year mandate, and he has given working Ontarians a $200 bribe. Either us, our children or grandchildren will at some point have to pay this loan back. It would have been appropriate for the Ontario government to put those monies towards health care and/or work towards other promises which have not been fulfilled.
Now, instead of working in conjunction with the other provincial leaders and the federal government to plan a reasonable strategy to deal with U.S. President Donald Trump and his tariffs, Ford will be out campaigning and making more promises he has no intention of keeping.
In my humble opinion, the premier has failed us more than not.
Henry Goverde, Oshawa, Ont.
Doug Ford must strive to unite, not divide
Kudos to Martin Regg Cohn for his insightful and informative column.
My first take away is Doug Ford needs to understand he is not the prime minister of our country, he is the premier of one of 10 provinces and three territories. As such, it is his job to unite with the other premiers and throw their collective support completely and unequivocally behind our federal officials, creating a united front that will deal more effectively with Donald Trump.
Also, Doug Ford's speeches definitely have a self-serving, self-aggrandizement ring to them. Ford is an opportunist and sees this country-wide trade war as a golden opportunity to increase his mandate here in Ontario. He must be thanking his lucky stars for Trump's hubris.
I realize Ford's primary concern is Ontario, but it is time for him to broaden his thinking so he focuses on what we in Ontario can do for the benefit of the entire country, not just his personal political position.
Patricia Steward, East York

Ontario party leaders are in lockstep on Trump's tariffs. That says more about Ford than anyone else

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-party-leaders-are-lockstep-on-trumps/docview/3163212527/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+party+leaders+are+in+lockstep+on+Trump%27s+tariffs.+That+says+more+about+Ford+than+anyone+else&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Spare a thought for the LCBO workers who spent days removing California wine, Kentucky bourbon, and all the other American tipples from their shelves only to have to put them all back on Monday evening.And pour one out for them, as it were, should they end up having to go through the whole exercise again. Which certainly seems possible. Donald Trump's 30-day pause on tariffs on Canadian imports, which was supposed to go into effect Tuesday, could turn back into tariffs a month from now. Or in three months, or indeed three days, given the U.S, president's wild unpredictability on this file (or any file, really).Should the tariffs go ahead, it's a given that liquor-store workers will be drafted into Ontario's official response, because NDP leader Marit Stiles and Liberal leader Bonnie Crombie have echoed Conservative leader Doug Ford's plan to avoid purchasing American goods wherever possible.The two opposition leaders are, broadly speaking, not in opposition to anything Ford proposed as part of his Trump response plan. For that brief moment on Monday, when the three major parties were releasing their tariff-countering proposals, what was striking was how little they differed. Immediate financial support for impacted businesses. Tax relief. A "Buy Ontario" mandate. An increased emphasis on interprovincial trade and a determination to find new trading partners beyond the United States.Some of the proposals had different numbers attached, depending on the party - the NDP didn't burden itself with such specifics at all - but they all pointed to one thing: a provincial government that is prepared to direct a firehose of money at the sectors and workers targeted by Trump's tariffs.As Crombie put it, while speaking to reporters on the campaign trail on Monday, "there is no daylight right now between my approach and (that of) Doug Ford." She also said she agreed with him "on most of the measures."This is a fairly remarkable statement in the early days of an election campaign. There is a lot of theatre to these things, yes, but party leaders trying to replace a majority government will usually treat every proposal from their rivals as the worst possible idea at the worst possible time. "Actually, those are some pretty good ideas" is not usually heard at the daily scrum.But that the parties are in lockstep on the tariff response says more about Ford than it does about his opponents. It wasn't so long ago that the Progressive Conservative leader was publicly supportive of Trump, a fellow conservative (in theory) who is also not a career politician. Even as he was announcing retaliatory plans on Monday, Ford was caught on a hot mic saying he "100 per cent" wanted Trump to win the presidency in November, a clip that will follow him for the rest of the campaign. Before he declared "Canada Is Not for Sale" on a baseball cap, it was easy to imagine Ford trying to thread the needle of defending Ontario while downplaying Trump's threats. He might even have tried the Kevin O'Leary doctrine of near-total appeasement.Instead, he's gone the other way, emphasizing what he sees as Trump's betrayal and all but vowing to smash those bourbon bottles himself if it comes to it. His willingness to spend tens of billions of dollars to backstop business and workers at the outset of a trade war is similarly aggressive, as well as mildly surprising given Ford is nominally a fiscal conservative. If Ford had ever hoped to present a balanced budget, that plan wouldn't survive his tariff-countering measures.There's a whiff of Ford's COVID-19 response to all this. In the pandemic, there were people, especially on the right of the political spectrum, who downplayed the health concerns and wanted governments to do as little as possible to deal with the virus. Ford went completely the other way, and for a time, Ontario's response was as aggressive as almost anyone's.That old line about there being no atheists in foxholes applies: in a time of crisis, Ford is less wedded to his conservatism.But if Ford benefits from being a wartime general, it is also true that, at least for now, that war has been averted. A campaign that might have been about standing up to Trump will now need some kind of second act.One imagines that Stiles and Crombie won't be quite so accommodating to Ford when he announces the rest of his plans.
CAPTION: PC Leader Doug Ford (left), Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie (centre) and NDP Leader Marit Stiles are out campaigning today.
CREDIT: Scott Stinson

Why Donald Trump paused his tariffs on Canada + Doug Ford defends his choice to hold an election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/why-donald-trump-paused-his-tariffs-on-canada/docview/3163212506/se-2?accountid=15090

Document 348 of 500

Doug Ford tries to explain his hot mic comment about Donald Trump: 'I always believe in working with anyone'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-tries-explain-his-hot-mic-comment-about/docview/3163211966/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+tries+to+explain+his+hot+mic+comment+about+Donald+Trump%3A+%27I+always+believe+in+working+with+anyone%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: So much for Doug Ford's Trump card.
Ford struggled Tuesday to explain why he wanted Donald Trump to win the U.S. presidential election after being caught on a hot mic in a gaffe that has jolted the Ontario campaign.
It was an embarrassing setback for the Progressive Conservative leader, who has framed the snap Feb. 27 vote as a referendum on who could best deal with Trump's threatened 25 per cent tariffs against Canada.
Complaining to reporters in Ottawa that "you guys are obsessed with that," Ford was repeatedly pressed about the hot mic admission that he had hoped the Republican Trump would beat Democrat Kamala Harris last November.
"On election day, was I happy this guy won? One hundred per cent I was. Then the guy pulled out the knife and f---ing yanked it in me," the Tory leader said Monday in Etobicoke, making a stabbing motion with his hand.
The following day, Ford was forced to do damage control for his self-inflicted wound.
"Well, you know something, I always believe in working with anyone," he said in Ottawa, where he promised Queen's Park would upload OC Transpo's troubled LRT line.
"On the hot mic, yeah, he makes me swear. I think he makes a lot of people swear in this country," Ford acknowledged, insisting he believed the president would somehow be less provocative in his second term.
Trump touted tariffs during the recent American campaign in which he also called the Jan. 6, 2021 attack on the U.S. Capitol "a day of love," even though the FBI dubbed it domestic terrorism that threatened the peaceful transfer of power.
"I thought it would be a little different, a little change, but obviously he decided to pull the knife out and stab us," Ford said on the shop floor of a door company.
His main rivals pounced on his Monday comments made to Tory candidates Christine Hogarth and David Piccini and several members of the Ontario Professional Fire Fighters Association.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie released a 60-second ad to highlight the PC leader's apparent hypocrisy by repeatedly playing the clip: "Was I happy this guy won? One hundred per cent I was."
"Doug Ford just said he was happy Donald Trump won the election. Happy the guy who campaigned on a trade war with Canada became president?" intones a female narrator against footage of Trump boasting "the tariff will be substantial in some cases" and that he is "a tariff man standing on a tariff platform."
"He was happy? Was he happy Canadians might lose their jobs? Was he happy about the threat of Canada becoming a 51st state?" continues the announcer.
"Or does he just care so little about the people of this province ... that he saw an attack on Ontario as an opportunity for himself. What else does he say when he thinks you're not listening?"
Speaking to the Star's Martin Regg Cohn at Toronto Metropolitan University's Democracy Forum, Crombie said Ford's support for Trump was revealing, given that Trump was his rationale for an "unnecessary" $189-million election 15 months before Ontario's next scheduled general election in June 2026.
"He used that as an excuse to call an election. He said basically said so himself ... in his hot mic moment," the Liberal leader told Cohn, predicting it could be a "turning point."
"I don't think it's a one-day wonder. There are always these moments in a campaign that are kind of pivot points ... that are revealing about a person's character," she said, noting Trump "threatened our economy."
In Ottawa, New Democratic Leader Marit Stiles said "it doesn't surprise me at all" that Ford would say he preferred Trump.
"This is who Doug Ford is. He says one thing when he's out in front of the microphones ... and he says another one behind closed doors," said Stiles, as she highlighted her education plan that includes a universal school food program.
"It's a little bit sad, you know, that Doug Ford clearly is a big fan of Donald Trump, but Donald Trump doesn't seem to be aware of who Doug Ford is," she said.
Public-opinion research has suggested Canadians overwhelmingly preferred Harris over Trump last fall.
Pollara Strategic Insights found 62 per cent of respondents had positive feelings about Harris with only 18 per cent having negative feelings toward the then-vice-president.
The firm found 66 per cent had negative feelings about Trump with 20 per cent having positive feelings toward him.
Ford, whose campaign slogan is "Protect Ontario," has styled himself as the anti-Trump, sporting a blue hat that reads "Canada Is Not For Sale" to counter the president's trademark red cap emblazoned with "Make America Great Again."
On Monday, Trump paused his threatened 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian goods until at least next month, meaning the levies will not be in place before Ontarians vote.
CAPTION: YoutubePC Leader Doug Ford (left), Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie (centre) and NDP Leader Marit Stiles are out campaigning today.

Document 349 of 500

Our patriotism is missing something

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/our-patriotism-is-missing-something/docview/3163150221/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Our+patriotism+is+missing+something&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If sports fandom is an entry-level study in loyalty and belonging, the loud boos during the "Star Spangled Banner" at the Raptors' game on Sunday seem worth considering. Just ask my feelings, which rattled around like a pinball.
As a longtime critic of American imperialism, I felt smug: I was repelled by the stars and stripes hanging off of Canadian rafters years ago. Welcome! As a mom, I felt badly for the young singer, her big moment disrupted by men comparing tariff sizes. And as the spouse of one particularly loud booer - a lifetime non-joiner who is now, apparently, here for the maple leaf - I wondered: are we patriots now?
And if yes, can we define that first?
Nothing unites a fan base, or a population, like a common enemy. Judging by the cheers that went up for "the true north strong and free" at various games this past weekend, Canadians are primed to hate America's tariffs together (though now, after calls between Prime Minister Trudeau and President Donald Trump, those tariffs will be paused for at least a month).
But it's going to take more than replacing some F Trudeau flags with F Trump ones to make this team spirit last. And while politicians are lining up to convince us to follow them through this confusing, possibly soon-to-be-expensive time, none are really presenting a vision of a Canada worth cheering for.
Defining ourselves against the flaws of the U.S. has long been core to Canada's identity. Lessons on how much worse all the bad stuff is there, whether higher medical bills or racism, was my entire junior high curriculum, I think. As the 21st century arrived, though, the holes in our social fabric grew bigger, and more of us fell through.
On the other side, things looked very different. I admit, it took me until Idle No More to really see how this country's prosperity was never shared fairly. It's probably not a coincidence that happened as the number of us who do get to count on it really began shrinking.
I genuinely remember public health care and education systems that were imperfect, but reliable. Both are buckling due to decades of neglect. You know the deal: Ontario has no family doctors, no teachers and no plans to fix that. Our emergency rooms and classrooms are clogged, and despite the best efforts of those on the front lines, people are suffering.
In my own life, one retired family member is waiting three months for a medical procedure despite a lifetime of paying taxes. At the same time, the Toronto District School Board's $12.7-billion state of repair budget hole left the washroom closest to my child's classroom closed for months - a classroom that also gets dangerously hot far too often.
Premier Doug Ford hasn't addressed these problems in nearly seven years in office, and he isn't going to take care of Ontarians now. Instead, he's betting on himself, calling a snap election and using $3 billion not to hire doctors, but to refund us each $200 of our own money. His government doesn't protect us from day-to-day stress, yet he wants us to believe he'd protect us from the bully down south.
While Ford's "me and my hat are here for you" routine is particularly annoying, it's not like another politician or a party is currently putting country before self. Spin the wheel, and I'll tell you how each one has let us down. Whatever is happening in the U.S. is absolutely an undemocratic, scary mess. Canadians still deserve a better offer than simply not being ... that.
Back to the Raptors, whose rivals on Sunday were the L.A. Clippers. Their forward, Kawhi Leonard, was of course key to our sunshiny championship year, the only one he played here. The crowd gave him a rousing cheer before tipoff then immediately turned on him, jeering every free throw and foul.
Fandom is fickle. Ask the Raptors themselves, who are on their second, if not third, if not fourth, rough post-championship season. The once-packed arena now has plenty of empty seats. It's easy to heckle a rival and cheer for a winner. It's much harder to put in the time and effort to build a team - or a country - that really works.
Denise Balkissoon is executive editor at The Narwhal, an online magazine about the natural world in Canada. She and her team worked with the Star to investigate the Doug Ford government's handling of the Greenbelt, reporting that won the 2024 Michener Award.

Document 350 of 500

Canada wins reprieve: Trump pauses tariffs for 30 days as Trudeau promises new border measures

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/canada-wins-reprieve/docview/3163149598/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada+wins+reprieve%3A+Trump+pauses+tariffs+for+30+days+as+Trudeau+promises+new+border+measures&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada won an 11th-hour reprieve from Donald Trump's threatened U.S. tariffs on Monday, just as Mexico received, after pledging to inject millions more into border security and committing to actions that may not stave off American duties experts say could be devastating to the Canadian economy.
Hours before a midnight deadline, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau said threatened U.S. tariffs against Canada will be paused for 30 days after his second call with the U.S. president Monday, a conversation that lasted 45 minutes and set in motion four more weeks of talks with the U.S. president's team.
Trump posted on Truth Social that he was "very pleased with this initial outcome" and the tariffs would be paused "to see whether or not a final economic deal with Canada can be structured."
The prime minister pledged another $200 million to fight organized crime and fentanyl smuggling, saying Canada would appoint a "fentanyl czar," add Mexican cartels to the list of terrorist entities, and take other moves to show it was serious about addressing Trump's concerns.
Trudeau posted on X that he laid out Canada's previous $1.3-billion plan promised in November and said, "Nearly 10,000 front-line personnel are and will be working on protecting the border."
That's the same number of additional soldiers Mexico earlier pledged to send to its border with the U.S., and Trump echoed that language later.
The prime minister said Ottawa will ensure "24-7 eyes on the border (and) launch a Canada-U.S. Joint Strike Force to combat organized crime, fentanyl and money laundering."
"I have also signed a new intelligence directive on organized crime and fentanyl and we will be backing it with $200 million. Proposed tariffs will be paused for at least 30 days while we work together," the prime minister posted.
The second high-stakes call between Trudeau and Trump ended with Canada achieving the same break Mexico scored earlier in the day when Trump spoke to Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum.
As Canadians awaited the results of the Trump-Trudeau call, tensions were running high.
On the Ontario election campaign trail Monday, Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford was caught on a hot mic expressing frustration with the president.
"On election day, was I happy this guy won? One hundred per cent I was. Then the guy pulled out the knife and f--king yanked it in me," said Ford.
Stock markets had slumped, reflecting concern about the midnight deadline for Trump's 25 per cent tariffs on most Canadian exports, with a slightly lower 10 per cent levy on oil shipments. Canada had pledged matching counter-tariffs on $30 billion worth of American imports.
After word spread that Canada had avoided the midnight onset of a trade war, Ford called it "good news," reversing the province's ban on U.S. booze and his earlier decision to cancel Ontario's $100-million contract with Elon Musk's satellite internet provider Starlink.
"We have temporarily averted tariffs that would have severely damaged our economy, giving time for more negotiation and time for cooler heads to prevail. Thank you to the countless workers, union leaders, businesses and everyday proud Canadians who rallied together to make this happen," Ford said.
Ontario will pause its retaliatory measures but if Trump goes ahead, Ford said, "we won't hesitate to remove American products off LCBO shelves or ban American companies from provincial procurement."
"No one should let their guard down or breathe a sigh of relief at this 30-day pause on the tariffs," said Lana Payne, head of Unifor, Canada's largest private-sector union. "As a country, we must use the days ahead to continue to bring Canadians together, to continue to plan for a potential trade war, and to use every single lever we have to build a strong, resilient and diverse economy."
Trump told American reporters Monday before his second call with Trudeau that Canada should become his country's 51st state and then there would be "no tariffs."
"I'd love to see that, but some people say that would be a long shot. If people wanted to play the game right, it would be 100 per cent certain that they'd become a state," he said. "But a lot of people don't like to play the game because they don't have a threshold of pain. And there would be some pain, but not a lot. The pain would be really theirs."
A Canadian federal official, speaking on condition they not be identified, said Trump did not raise his desire to make Canada a 51st state in the call with Trudeau. And while Trump raised banking as an irritant in the morning call, it did not come up in their afternoon conversation. Nor did Trump raise water as a concern, as he has publicly in the past. The Trudeau team says it is clear that the president has a long list of irritants with Canada on the trade side, but the two leaders focused on the border in the afternoon call.
Public Safety Minister David McGuinty said Canada needs to take Trump's comments about becoming a 51st state "seriously," but he said in a CBC interview that "I think that comment is just not serious."
While McGuinty said some of the latest changes were in the works already, it's not yet clear what a "fentanyl czar" will do.
Mexican and Canadian officials have had several exchanges over the past weeks, but the official said Trudeau did not consult or co-ordinate with Sheinbaum on Monday.
Trump had earlier held out little hope for a deal to avoid tariffs on Canada.
Before he spoke Trudeau on Monday afternoon, Trump welcomed Mexico's pledge to deploy an additional 10,000 soldiers at the border but said, "Canada is very tough. They're very, very tough to do business with, and we can't let them take advantage of the U.S."
Trump raised a number of complaints, including his latest, that American banks "cannot" do business in Canada - a declaration that is not accurate.
The president reiterated a statement that the U.S. does not need anything Canada makes, from cars to lumber, as he underlined that he had a "very good relationship" with Sheinbaum.
Trump said his final decision on Mexico would be made following a 30-day period of negotiations led by Secretary of State Marco Rubio.
The Canadian Bankers Association rebuffed Trump's claim about banking in a statement and said that "Canada has a strong and competitive banking sector comprised of both domestic and foreign banks."
It said there are 16 U.S.-based bank subsidiaries and branches with around $113 billion in assets currently operating in Canada.
"These banks specialize in a range of financial services, including corporate and commercial lending, treasury services, credit card products, investment banking and mortgage financing. They serve not only customers with cross-border business activities, but also Canada's domestic retail market. U.S. banks now make up half of all foreign bank assets in Canada."
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre said Monday that Canada should deploy soldiers to the border too, posting on X what he called a plan to "take back control of the border and save" Canada-U.S. trade. "Send Canadian Forces troops, helicopters & surveillance to the border now."
Ontario's threat to cancel its Starlink contract had directly targeted Starlink owner Musk, a key Trump adviser.
Musk briefly responded on X, the social media platform he owns, with a shrug: "Oh well."
The Ontario government signed a $100-million deal in November for Starlink to deliver high-speed internet to remote residents in rural and northern Ontario.
Trudeau had responded to Trump's tariff threat with a plan to impose 25 per cent tariffs on a range of American products worth up to $30 billion, with another layer of counter-duties on $125 billion worth of American imports to come in three weeks. All that is now on hold.
Ontario, Manitoba, Nova Scotia and British Columbia said they would ban American liquor from store shelves as political leaders pushed a "Buy Canadian" response to Trump's weekend announcement he would impose tariffs, even as two western premiers urged Ottawa to "de-escalate" the rhetoric around tariffs.
CREDIT: Tonda MacCharles Toronto Star Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Document 351 of 500

Our newfound, America-booing patriotism is missing something crucial

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/our-newfound-america-booing-patriotism-is-missing/docview/3163149523/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Our+newfound%2C+America-booing+patriotism+is+missing+something+crucial&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If sports fandom is an entry-level study in loyalty and belonging, the loud boos during the "Star Spangled Banner" at the Toronto Raptors' game on Sunday seem worth considering. Just ask my feelings, which rattled around like a pinball. As a longtime critic of American imperialism, I felt smug: I was repelled by the stars and stripes hanging off of Canadian rafters years ago. Welcome! As a mom, I felt badly for the young singer, her big moment disrupted by men comparing tariff sizes. And as the spouse of one particularly loud booer - a lifetime non-joiner who is now, apparently, here for the maple leaf - I wondered: are we patriots now? And if yes, can we define that first?Nothing unites a fanbase, or a population, like a common enemy. Judging by the cheers that went up for "the true north strong and free" at various games this past weekend, Canadians are primed to hate America's tariffs together (though now, after calls between Prime Minister Trudeau and President Trump, those tariffs will be paused for at least a month).But it's going to take more than replacing some F Trudeau flags with F Trump ones to make this team spirit last. And while politicians are lining up to convince us to follow them through this confusing, possibly soon-to-be-expensive time, none are really presenting a vision of a Canada worth cheering for. Defining ourselves against the flaws of the U.S. has long been core to Canada's identity. Lessons on how much worse all the bad stuff is there, whether higher medical bills or racism, was my entire junior high curriculum, I think. As the 21st century arrived, though, the holes in our social fabric grew bigger, and more of us fell through.On the other side, things looked very different. I admit, it took me until Idle No More to really see how this country's prosperity was never shared fairly. It's probably not a coincidence that happened as the number of us who do get to count on it really began shrinking.I genuinely remember public healthcare and education systems that were imperfect, but reliable. Both are buckling due to decades of neglect. You know the deal: Ontario has no family doctors, no teachers and no plans to fix that. Our emergency rooms and classrooms are clogged, and despite the best efforts of those on the front lines, people are suffering.In my own life, one retired family member is waiting three months for a medical procedure despite a lifetime of paying taxes. At the same time, the Toronto District School Board's $12.7-billion state of repair budget hole left the washroom closest to my child's classroom closed for months - a classroom that also gets dangerously hot far too often. Premier Doug Ford hasn't addressed these problems in nearly seven years in office, and he isn't going to take care of Ontarians now. Instead, he's betting on himself, calling a snap election and using $3 billion not to hire doctors, but to refund us each $200 of our own money. His government doesn't protect us from day-to-day stress, yet he wants us to believe he'd protect us from the bully down south. While Ford's "me and my hat are here for you" routine is particularly annoying, it's not like another politician or a party is currently putting country before self. Spin the wheel, and I'll tell you how each one has let us down. Whatever is happening in the U.S. is absolutely an undemocratic, scary mess. Canadians still deserve a better offer than simply not being ... that. Back to the Raptors, whose rivals on Sunday were the L.A. Clippers. Their forward, Kawhi Leonard, was of course key to our sunshiney championship year, the only one he played here. The crowd gave him a rousing cheer before tipoff then immediately turned on him, jeering every free throw and foul. Fandom is fickle. Ask the Raptors themselves, who are on their second, if not third, if not fourth, rough post-championship season. The once-packed arena now has plenty of empty seats. It's easy to heckle a rival and cheer for a winner. It's much harder to put in the time and effort to build a team - or a country - that really works.
CAPTION: Toronto Raptors forward Bruce Brown, left to right, forward Scottie Barnes and forward Chris Boucher react as fans boo the American national anthem before first half NBA action against the LA Clippers in Toronto on Sunday, Feb. 2, 2025. THE CANADIAN PRESS/Frank Gunn

Document 352 of 500

A caretaker premier taking care of himself

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/caretaker-premier-taking-care-himself/docview/3163148983/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+caretaker+premier+taking+care+of+himself&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It is the worst of times for Ontario. It is the best of times for Doug Ford.
With Donald Trump pointing a gun at our head, Ford pulled the trigger on an early and unnecessary $189-million provincial election for Feb. 27. And so while Ontarians are reeling from the worst bilateral crisis in memory, Ford is focused on reeling in more votes.
What was the point? Politics.
Trump isn't the only politician who wants to win the dominance game.
Ford, too, is counting on coming out ahead in this conflict - not by winning the economic war with America, just by defeating his political opponents at home.
He's in no position to stand up to Trump, because Ford opted to stand down when he thrust us into an election campaign - he's off the job while running to be re-elected to a third consecutive term as premier. By his own choice, until election day, he's a man without a mandate in our moment of maximum peril, seeking maximum power for the future.
Elections aside, it was never in the job description of a premier to stare down a president - that's what the position of prime minister is all about. Justin Trudeau may be a lame duck, but for the moment this duck is acting like a straight shooter who's not quacking to get more votes.
Trudeau is on the job and on the phone with Trump, fending off his on-again, off-again threat of 25 per cent tariffs. Meanwhile, Ford is hustling and bluffing on the campaign trail - a caretaker premier taking care of himself.
An unpopular prime minister who is not seeking re-election is able to act in the national interest. A long-popular premier who is campaigning hard for a bigger mandate is acting primarily in his own political interest.
Ford is posing as a mythical Captain Canada with a baseball cap in endless photo ops, rather than focusing on the job of premier to fix problems of his own making - from doctor shortages to housing shortages, and from overspending on beer in corner stores to overreaching on the Greenbelt boondoggle.
America is a diversion, a ploy to distract people from his provincial problems while attracting more votes to his governing Progressive Conservatives. Four more years of Trump's presidency is precisely what he needs to ensure four more years as premier.
And so Ford has tied his political fortunes to Trump. How is that going?
So far, not so good - for Ford or for us.
Handcuffed by his caretaker status, relegated to his electoral role as leader of Ontario's governing Progressive Conservatives, Ford unabashedly boasts that he's on American TV daily. Watch him interviewed by Fox News or any other cable news channel that will have him on, as if he's moving the dial.
The theory was that Trump would tune into Fox and come away charmed, or else terrified by our campaigning premier talking tough. Hasn't happened.
Trump is bullying and bargaining with the national leaders of Mexico and Canada, not a candidate for the subnational jurisdiction of Ontario who is obsessed with what alcohol is or isn't on sale, from beer to bourbon.
We're now watching the spectacle of a politician who grants exclusive interviews to American networks but won't sit down with major Ontario media. Warming up to his heroic role, Ford has scheduled two trips to Washington this month, taking the campaign trail to America when he should be showing up for voters at home every day in a 29-day campaign.
As for his public musings about meeting the president personally to talk turkey? Those idle boasts are now forgotten - no face time in the White House - although that hasn't stopped Ford from mocking the mere idea of his major political opponents dealing with Trump.
All we know for sure is his private musings - caught on an open mic Monday - that Ford "100 per cent" wanted Trump re-elected.
Let's look more closely at how his positions compare to those of NDP Leader Marit Stiles and Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie:
All three major parties are aligned in resisting Trump's bullying. All three leaders understand that Ottawa will take the lead in retaliatory tariffs. All three politicians have committed to billions of dollars in government supports to prop up the economy in the event of a downturn.
At first, Ford dismissed demands from Crombie's Liberals to cancel a sole-sourced contract with Elon Musk's Starlink, insisting the company was best-suited for the job. Belatedly, Ford's Tories have declared they're prepared to rip it up as retaliation if Trump's tariffs go ahead.
Apart from Ford's Starlink climbdown, it's hard to find any substantive differences in their positions. Which means it's hard to find any significant reason for this election.
Ford knew what he was doing when he plunged us into a partisan contest while calling for national unity and provincial solidarity to protect the livelihoods of Ontarians. It never made sense - unless his underlying and undying goal wasn't to strengthen his hand in a mythical showdown with Trump over the next few days but to bolster his grip on power over the next four years.
It was never about protecting Ontario from America. Ford's plan was always about protecting the Progressive Conservatives and crushing the opposition.
So far, all is going according to plan - Ford is riding high in the polls at our lowest point. Unless, at some point, voters decide that Ford has been playing them all along - and overplayed his hand.
CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION

Subject: Political campaigns; Provincial elections; Voters; Politics; Prime ministers; Elections; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Tariffs

Location: Canada

Title: A caretaker premier taking care of himself

Document 353 of 500

Ford defends early election call: Premier says he can manage Trump and campaign, will drop Starlink deal if tariffs imposed

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-defends-early-election-call/docview/3163148978/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+defends+early+election+call%3A+Premier+says+he+can+manage+Trump+and+campaign%2C+will+drop+Starlink+deal+if+tariffs+imposed&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A defensive Doug Ford insists it was not reckless to call a provincial election against the spectre of a trade war that could devastate Ontario's economy.
That came as Ford was caught on a hot mic Monday saying that he "100 per cent" wanted Donald Trump to win the American presidency last November.
The Progressive Conservative leader, who last week triggered the Feb. 27 vote 15 months early, maintained he is not constrained from governing while electioneering.
"I totally disagree," Ford said as he accepted the endorsement of the Ontario Professional Fire Fighters Association in Etobicoke.
He noted he would be prepared to scrap Ontario's $100-million contract with Starlink, which is owned by key Trump ally Elon Musk, if tariffs end up being imposed.
That flip-flop came after Ford had initially defended the deal with the satellite internet provider on Jan. 24.
"We went out for a transparent bid, they came out top," he said that day after journalists asked him if he would follow Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie's call to cancel the Starlink agreement.
In a moment of candour caught on a hot mic at his Etobicoke campaign office, Ford admitted to Tory candidates Christine Hogarth and David Piccini and several firefighters that he wanted the Republican Trump to beat Democrat Kamala Harris last November.
"On election day, was I happy this guy won? One hundred per cent I was. Then the guy pulled out the knife and f--king yanked it in me," he said, motioning his arm as if stabbing someone.
Ford told reporters he at first thought Trump's election "might be a good change down in the U.S." but "it's been a disaster."
On Monday, he claimed he can do his day job and campaign at the same time.
"I just pulled off a billion dollars from LCBO shelves of U.S. products. This is 25 days left (in the campaign). This gives us a strong four-year mandate to outlive and outlast the Trump administration. People want certainty," said the Tory leader, referring to another retaliatory measure now on hold with the one-month pause in Trump tariffs.
"I'm out in the international media every single day. I was on Fox and going on CNN, going back on Fox. What I'm doing, I'm getting the message out to the American people," he said.
Ford added that "if any emergencies come, I'll reconvene cabinet."
To ease the strain from potential tariffs, his Progressive Conservatives promised $3 billion in breaks on small-business payroll taxes, a six-month deferral of business taxes, a hike in the LCBO's wholesale discount for bars and restaurants to 15 per cent from 10 per cent, a $300-million expansion of tax credits for manufacturers investing in buildings, machinery and equipment, and $40 million for municipalities facing "severe economic disruption."
But PC MPP Peter Bethlenfalvy, who serves as Ford's finance minister, acknowledged none of that aid can be provided until after election day, presuming his party wins a third term.
"We need a mandate from the people of Ontario to spend their money," Bethlenfalvy told reporters in Woodbridge.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said the Tories not done enough to "tariff-proof Ontario."
"With Doug Ford, all you have to do is look at his record. His record is side deals, bad deals, deals that sell off and sell out Ontario companies every time - and we've seen it again and again (with) Staples, Walmart, Starlink," Stiles said at Oshawa's Canadian Automotive Museum, standing in front of an NDP orange Bricklin SV1 sports car, which was made in New Brunswick.
"Doug Ford didn't create this crisis, Donald Trump did. But Ford's failures and bad deals have left Ontario's economy weaker, and the families who live here vulnerable," she said.
"We need to make Ontario stronger and more resilient so we can withstand this crisis and move beyond it."
In Kitchener-Waterloo, Crombie conceded that on tariff response "there is no daylight between my approach right now and Doug Ford," but said it was irresponsible for him to call an "unnecessary" and "opportunistic" $189-million snap election during a trade crisis.
"I agree with him on most of the measures, obviously the procurement measures and buying local and buying Ontario and buying Canadian but there are lots of other things that we can be doing," she said after a speech to the Greater Kitchener Waterloo Chamber of Commerce.
Crombie pledged to fund $150,000 bonuses to "patriotic nurses and doctors who want to come back home to Canada from the U.S. and help solve Doug Ford's health-care crisis."
Green Leader Mike Schreiner expressed concern that the Tories are focused on the hustings instead of housing.
Schreiner promised a "first-time homebuyers plan that would remove fees and taxes on the purchase of new starter homes built within urban boundaries."
"Young people are leaving the province because they've given up hope of ever being able to afford a home in Ontario. Meanwhile, the only kind of housing Doug Ford wants to build is mansions in the Greenbelt that no one can afford," he said.
That's a reference to $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal that is the subject of an ongoing RCMP criminal investigation.
Ford, who has denied any wrongdoing in the cancelled land rezoning debacle, called the snap election in part to get in front of any potential charges by the Mounties who have been on the case since October 2023.
The Tory chief also wanted to take advantage of the fact he leads in public-opinion polls. As well, he wanted to face voters before a federal election expected in the spring.
CAPTION: Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford steps out of his campaign bus Monday in Etobicoke. He was caught on a hot mic Monday saying he was happy Donald Trump won the U.S. presidential election last fall, but later told reporters that "it's been a disaster."Chris Young THE CANADIAN PRESS
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Correction

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/correction/docview/3163148832/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Correction&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A Feb. 1 opinion column about an early Ontario election mistakenly said former Ontario premier David Peterson lost after an early election call in the 1980s. In fact, it was in 1990. As well, the recent Abacus Data poll done for the Star found that Doug Ford's Tories are 23 percentage points - with 47 per cent support - ahead of the Ontario Liberals at 24 per cent. The article mistakenly said the Tories were ahead in the polls by 20 per cent.

Document 355 of 500

How to fight 'the dumbest trade war in history'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/how-fight-dumbest-trade-war-history/docview/3163148775/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+to+fight+%27the+dumbest+trade+war+in+history%27&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's true that U.S. President Donald Trump would like the U.S. to annex Canada.
Trump is a career dealmaker. An American acquisition of Canada would be a deal-making triumph eclipsing Thomas Jefferson's Louisiana Purchase.
"Canada and the United States, that would really be something," Trump said at a press conference last month.
"You get rid of that artificially drawn line, and you take a look at what that looks like ... it would also be much better for national security."
But Trump won't annex Canada. Given the chance, he will instead wreck it.
The tariffs Trump threatens to impose on Canada and Mexico in about a month's time could weaken them as U.S. competitors, raise a hoped-for $1.5 trillion for the U.S. treasury, and bolster U.S. manufacturing prowess at the expense of America's neighbours.
Trump launched his tariff action, or what the Wall Street Journal has called "the dumbest trade war in history," on Feb. 1 when he imposed a 25 per cent tariff on most of Canada's $592 billion in annual exports to the U.S., and a 10 per cent surtax on energy.
He imposed a 25 per cent tariff on Mexican imports the same day.
The tariffs were to take effect Feb. 3 but were postponed that day for about a month after Canada and Mexico each committed to enhanced protection of their borders with the U.S.
Canada said a new Canada-U.S. "joint strike force" would combat organized crime, fentanyl trafficking, and money laundering.
But the tariffs are coming. And Trump is just getting started.
Trump said on Jan. 31 that his tariffs "could rise with time." He wants to use what he calls "economic force" to move Ontario's auto sector to the U.S.
Trump would like to see the output of Canadian aluminum smelters, steel mills and nickel refineries also move stateside.
To get that done, Trump could raise tariffs to 100 per cent, the level of surtaxes that Canada and the U.S. have imposed on imported Chinese electric vehicles.
Trump's tariffs have devalued the loonie, raising the cost of all imported goods priced in U.S. dollars.
There's reason to doubt sanguine economists who expect the tariffs to last no more than a year before Canada and the U.S. resolve their issues and the tariffs are lifted.
Once in place, tariffs are notoriously difficult to revoke.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford's estimate of 500,000 job losses in Ontario alone from Trump's tariffs might not come to pass depending on the duration and scope of the U.S. surtaxes.
But without question Trump's tariffs have put tens of thousands of Canadian jobs at risk.
Trump can cook up as many rationales as required to justify America's attacks on Canada. And he already has. As the Washington Post reported last week, "Trump's myriad justifications for the tariffs have left the Canadian government puzzled over the best response."
Trump justified his initial 25 per cent tariffs on Canada with a wildly exaggerated claim of illicit fentanyl and undocumented migrants pouring into the Lower 48 from Canada.
Later, in inflicting even greater economic force on Canada, Trump will cite a U.S. trade deficit in goods with Canada that according to his own government is less than that of China, Mexico, the European Union (EU) and Japan.
Later still, Trump will point to Canadian underspending on defence as a national security threat to the U.S.
In fact, Trump's tariff war is a threat to America's national security. "Any tariffs with Canada on minerals would have heavy consequences on defence, nuclear energy and heavy manufacturing," notes a January report by the Washington, D.C.-based Center for Strategic & International Studies (CSIS).
"Canada is a critical ally in the United States' efforts to reduce reliance on China for critical minerals."
Canada's efforts to appease Trump to avoid tariffs have failed.
There are options in this fight besides appeasement.
"We need to learn from Churchill and Zelenskyy," said Michel Leblanc, CEO of the Chamber of Commerce of Metropolitan Montreal, on Feb. 1.
"We do not back down to states that behave like bullying thugs."
Leblanc was referring to wartime leaders Winston Churchill of Britain and Ukraine's Volodymyr Zelenskyy.
We can "Buy Canadian," as Ottawa and several premiers have urged. We can buy any non-American goods, though maybe not at the U.S.-owned Walmart, Costco, Home Depot and Winners.
Canada can accept China's recent invitation to improve our trade relations.
We can suspend our membership in NATO, which is effectively an extension of the Pentagon.
Canadian businesses can hasten to make better use of our free-trade access to the EU and our Asia-Pacific free-trade partners.
And we can call Trump's bluff by imposing export taxes on Canadian commodities like electricity, oil, potash, and uranium that Trump claims America doesn't need.
But Trump won't be appeased. Certainly not at a price that leaves Canada recognizable.
CREDIT: David Olive Toronto Star OPINION

Subject: International trade; Trade disputes; Surtax; National security; Fentanyl; Tariffs
