https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3163148773/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Canadians+brace+for+far-reaching+effects+of+tariffs%2C+Feb.+3+Canada+not+backing+down%2C+Feb.+3+Canada+hits+back+on+Trump+tariffs%2C+Feb.+2+Ford+has+stripped+himself+of+credibility%2C+Cohn%2C+Jan.+30&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Dear Canada, we're sorry
Canadians brace for far-reaching effects of tariffs, Feb. 3
To our dear Canadian friends: I write to you today with a deep sense of regret. Our two nations share far more than just a border. We share history, values and a friendship that has endured through times of war and peace, hardship and prosperity.
It is with this in mind that I offer our sincerest apologies for the unjust and unwarranted tariffs imposed upon Canadian products by our leadership.
These tariffs do not reflect the goodwill American people hold toward Canada. They do not honour the spirit of co-operation that has long defined our relationship. Instead, these tariffs represent a moment of hostility that runs counter to the respect and trust that should guide our shared economic and diplomatic endeavours.
I recognize these actions will place undue strain on Canadian businesses, workers and families, on people who have stood with us as allies and friends. From the beaches of Normandy to peacekeeping missions around the world, from trade agreements that have strengthened both our economies to the everyday bonds of culture and kinship, Canada has been a steadfast partner.
Please know that many Americans feel as I do and do not support these policies, nor do we condone the aggressive and dismissive rhetoric that has accompanied them. You deserve better from us.
I look forward to the day when this rift is healed and we can once again move forward as the close allies we were always meant to be.
With respect and regret,
Andrew Lewis, Decatur, Ga.

Dear friends of Canada, I live in Minnesota where we share a border. I have travelled to most provinces in wonderful Canada and have always felt welcome and loved. I am writing to express my dismay that the current occupant of our White House has decided to create such a divide between our two countries.
Stay strong Canadians, and increase the price you charge for your oil.
Karen Hovland, St. Paul, Minn.
It's time to
put Canada first
Canada not backing down,
Feb. 3
Tariffs will not solve the fentanyl crisis or illegal immigrants going to the United States. I call upon all Canadians to buy Canadian goods and services, to vacation in Canada. It's a lose-lose scenario with these punitive tariffs brought on by the U.S.
We didn't ask for tariffs. I thought the free trade deal was a reasonable compromise and a measure of fair diplomacy between nations.
President Trump, you must stop this war mongering; it will make your election promises seem like empty rhetoric in the next year or two.
Canada, provinces: hit the U.S. where it hurts, in the pocketbook of Americans.
Provincial leaders, I ask you to open up the trade barriers between provinces.
Encourage all of us to trade west-east, instead of north-south.
Richard MacFarlane, Toronto
Don't spend a dime in the U.S.
As a dual U.S.-Canadian citizen permanently residing in the U.S., I am disgusted that President Donald Trump would impose these vindictive tariffs on an ally and neighbouring country. As such, I encourage all Canadians to avoid visiting the U.S. in response to the Trump tariffs until they are rescinded.
A significant portion of state tax revenue, especially for southern states such as Florida, Texas and Arizona, comes from Canadian visitors and vacationers and the loss of or reduction of this revenue will be painful and likely have a significant impact on these states. This would result in pressure on the U.S. federal government to end the tariffs.
For those Canadians seeking sunshine, there are many alternatives in Mexico, Cuba, the Caribbean and around the world to explore.
So please pass on the U.S. this year and send a message to the Trump administration that the U.S. tariff policy is unacceptable to Canadians.
David Sells, Sarasota, Fla.
Trudeau does Canadians proud
Canada hits back on Trump tariffs, Feb. 2
In his press conference announcing Canada's response to Trump's tariffs, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau showed us again that he is articulate, rational and classy: everything that Trump is not. He conducted himself as a true leader should and made me proud to be Canadian.
Thomas Mulligan, Grimsby, Ont.
Doug Ford
has once again failed Ontario
Ford has stripped himself of credibility, Cohn, Jan. 30
Martin Regg Cohn's article about Premier Doug Ford not having any remaining credibility hit the nail on the head.
Premier Ford has a mandate for another 16 months and has failed to deliver on health care, housing and numerous other important issues.
Now he has called an election in hopes of another four-year mandate, and he has given working Ontarians a $200 bribe. Either us, our children or grandchildren will at some point have to pay this loan back.
It would have been appropriate for the Ontario government to put those monies toward health care and/or work toward other promises that have not been fulfilled.
Now, instead of working in conjunction with the other provincial leaders and the federal government to plan a reasonable strategy to deal with U.S. President Donald Trump and his tariffs, Ford will be out campaigning and making more promises he has no intention of keeping.
In my humble opinion, the premier has failed us more than not.
Henry Goverde, Oshawa
Kudos to Martin Regg Cohn for his insightful and informative column.
My first take away is Doug Ford needs to understand he is not the prime minister of our country, he is the premier of one of 10 provinces and three territories.
As such, it is his job to unite with the other premiers and throw their collective support completely and unequivocally behind our federal officials, creating a united front that will deal more effectively with Donald Trump.
Also, Ford's speeches definitely have a self-serving, self- aggrandizement ring to them. Ford is an opportunist and sees this countrywide trade war as a golden opportunity to increase his mandate here in Ontario. He must be thanking his lucky stars for Trump's hubris.
I realize Ford's primary concern is Ontario, but it is time for him to broaden his think- ing so he focuses on what we in Ontario can do for the bene- fit of the entire country, not just his personal political position.
Patricia Steward, East York

Document 357 of 500

Be alert for $200 'tax rebate' scams, Ontario warns

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/be-alert-200-tax-rebate-scams-ontario-warns/docview/3163272514/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Be+alert+for+%24200+%27tax+rebate%27+scams%2C+Ontario+warns&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario residents receiving $200 cheques from Doug Ford's government are being warned to be on their guard for scammers trying to take advantage of the "taxpayer rebates," per a "scam alert" on the province's website.
An alert appeared on the province's "taxpayer rebate" webpage in the last few days cautioning residents that Ontario would not contact them proactively about the cheques.
The provincial government is sending out cheques to 15 million Ontarians, to the tune of roughly $3 billion. With so much money changing hands, scammers might be looking to cash in.
But scams aren't the only obstacle coming between Ontarians and their cheques. Some have struggled to deposit their cheques because they've been printed with the wrong names, such as maiden names they haven't used in years.
Here's how to avoid scams and other issues so you can cash in on your cheque.
How do I avoid getting scammed?
If someone from the government reaches out to you directly, unprompted, it might be a scammer.
The Ontario government will only contact you directly if you've asked a question about your rebate, notes Ontario's website.
The government will not contact you on its own - including by text, email or phone - to offer you money or a reward, sign you up for a rebate program, or ask for personal information like your social insurance number, the site continues.
Do I qualify?
You'll qualify for the $200 rebate if you meet a few criteria: you were 18 years or older at the end of 2023, you were a resident in Ontario on Dec. 31, 2023, you filed your income tax returns by Dec. 31, 2024, and you were not bankrupt or incarcerated in 2024.
If you receive the Canada Child Benefit (CCB), you'll get $200 for each of your children under 18, too. This means that a family of four would receive $800.
If you have kids and you haven't applied to the CCB yet, you have until March 31 to apply on the Government of Canada website.
What do I do if there's a problem with my cheque?
Some Ontarians have had issues depositing their cheques. For example, the Star previously reported about one Ontario resident, Lynda Chapple, who received a cheque with her maiden name instead of her married name, which meant she wasn't able to deposit her cheque.
She filed her taxes and has most of her government IDs under her married name, she said.
The Ontario Ministry of Finance is aware of an issue with a "small number" of cheques, caused by a "technical error," wrote a spokesperson in an email.
If you receive a cheque with a previous or different name, you should contact Service Ontario at 1-833-351-0409 or 1-888-821-9056, the spokesperson said. Your cheque will be cancelled and a new one will be sent out within three to four weeks.
What if I haven't received my cheque?
Waiting for your cheque can be pretty anxiety-provoking, especially if you're still recovering from holiday spending. The government is working "as quickly as possible" to distribute the cheques, said a government spokesperson in an email.
As of Feb. 2, approximately 10.25 million cheques had been mailed out. Your cheque may still be on its way.
You can check your address and rebate status on the ServiceOntario website. You'll just need an account login, the last four digits or your Social Insurance Number, your date of birth, and the postal code of the mailing address you used in your tax return.
You can't get your cheque by direct deposit; the "rebates" are being paid by cheque only. So check your mailbox if you haven't in a while.
For more information visit Ontario.ca/TaxpayerRebate.
CAPTION: As of Feb. 2, approximately 10.25 million cheques had been mailed out, the province said.

Subject: Rebates; Social insurance numbers; Fraud

Business indexing term: Subject: Rebates

Location: Canada

Title: Be alert for $200 'tax rebate' scams, Ontario warns

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2025

Section: news/ontario

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Document 358 of 500

As a caretaker premier, Doug Ford is only taking care of himself

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/as-caretaker-premier-doug-ford-is-only-taking/docview/3163272464/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=As+a+caretaker+premier%2C+Doug+Ford+is+only+taking+care+of+himself&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It is the worst of times for Ontario. It is the best of times for Doug Ford.
With Donald Trump pointing a gun at our head, Ford pulled the trigger on an early and unnecessary $189-million provincial election for Feb. 27. And so while Ontarians are reeling from the worst bilateral crisis in memory, Ford is focused on reeling in more votes.
What was the point? Politics.
Trump isn't the only politician who wants to win the dominance game.
Ford, too, is counting on coming out ahead in this conflict - not by winning the economic war with America, just by defeating his political opponents at home.
He's in no position to stand up to Trump, because Ford opted to stand down when he thrust us into an election campaign - he's off the job while running to be re-elected to a third consecutive term as premier. By his own choice, until election day, he's a man without a mandate in our moment of maximum peril, seeking maximum power for the future.
Elections aside, it was never in the job description of a premier to stare down a president - that's what the position of prime minister is all about. Justin Trudeau may be a lame duck, but for the moment this duck is acting like a straight shooter who's not quacking to get more votes.
Trudeau is on the job and on the phone with Trump, fending off his on-again, off-again threat of 25 per cent tariffs. Meanwhile, Ford is hustling and bluffing on the campaign trail - a caretaker premier taking care of himself.
An unpopular prime minister who is not seeking re-election is able to act in the national interest. A long-popular premier who is campaigning hard for a bigger mandate is acting primarily in his own political interest.
Ford is posing as a mythical Captain Canada with a baseball cap in endless photo ops, rather than focusing on the job of premier to fix problems of his own making - from doctor shortages to housing shortages, and from overspending on beer in corner stores to overreaching on the Greenbelt boondoggle.
America is a diversion, a ploy to distract people from his provincial problems while attracting more votes to his governing Progressive Conservatives. Four more years of Trump's presidency is precisely what he needs to ensure four more years as premier.
And so Ford has tied his political fortunes to Trump. How is that going?
So far, not so good - for Ford or for us.
Handcuffed by his caretaker status, relegated to his electoral role as leader of Ontario's governing Progressive Conservatives, Ford unabashedly boasts that he's on American TV daily. Watch him interviewed by Fox News or any other cable news channel that will have him on, as if he's moving the dial.
The theory was that Trump would tune into Fox and come away charmed, or else terrified by our campaigning premier talking tough. Hasn't happened.
Trump is bullying and bargaining with the national leaders of Mexico and Canada, not a candidate for the subnational jurisdiction of Ontario who is obsessed with what alcohol is or isn't on sale, from beer to bourbon.
We're now watching the spectacle of a politician who grants exclusive interviews to American networks but won't sit down with major Ontario media. Warming up to his heroic role, Ford has scheduled two trips to Washington this month, taking the campaign trail to America when he should be showing up for voters at home every day in a 29-day campaign.
As for his public musings about meeting the president personally to talk turkey? Those idle boasts are now forgotten - no face time in the White House - although that hasn't stopped Ford from mocking the mere idea of his major political opponents dealing with Trump.
All we know for sure is his private musings - caught on an open mic Monday - that Ford "100 per cent" wanted Trump re-elected.
Let's look more closely at how his positions compare to those of NDP Leader Marit Stiles and Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie:
All three major parties are aligned in resisting Trump's bullying. All three leaders understand that Ottawa will take the lead in retaliatory tariffs. All three politicians have committed to billions of dollars in government supports to prop up the economy in the event of a downturn.
At first, Ford dismissed demands from Crombie's Liberals to cancel a sole-sourced contract with Elon Musk's Starlink, insisting the company was best-suited for the job. Belatedly, Ford's Tories have declared they're prepared to rip it up as retaliation if Trump's tariffs go ahead.
Apart from Ford's Starlink climbdown, it's hard to find any substantive differences in their positions. Which means it's hard to find any significant reason for this election.
Ford knew what he was doing when he plunged us into a partisan contest while calling for national unity and provincial solidarity to protect the livelihoods of Ontarians. It never made sense - unless his underlying and undying goal wasn't to strengthen his hand in a mythical showdown with Trump over the next few days but to bolster his grip on power over the next four years.
It was never about protecting Ontario from America. Ford's plan was always about protecting the Progressive Conservatives and crushing the opposition.
So far, all is going according to plan - Ford is riding high in the polls at our lowest point. Unless, at some point, voters decide that Ford has been playing them all along - and overplayed his hand.
CAPTION: Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford holds a news conference in Etobicoke on Feb. 3, 2025.

Subject: Provincial elections; Political campaigns; Voters; Politics; Prime ministers; Elections; Tariffs

Document 359 of 500

We didn't want this trade war. But now we must fight

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/we-didnt-want-this-trade-war-now-must-fight/docview/3162976750/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=We+didn%27t+want+this+trade+war.+But+now+we+must+fight&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The trade war we are now fighting with the United States - a war we never wanted - is a momentous test for our country.
The cost of living, already at a crisis level for many, will rise. Canadian businesses, already struggling amid a weak economy, will be hurt. The dollar will fall. Jobs will be lost.
As we start to feel this pain, the temptation will be to point fingers at one another. The political reflex of the moment is to divide at every opportunity, to use any crisis as a wedge to separate not only one party from its rivals, but also its supporters from everyone else.
This is what Donald Trump wants. He wants us to buy that his tariffs are the inevitable consequence of our border policies, never mind that the flow of fentanyl from our country is a trickle while the flow of guns from his is a flood. He wants us to be distracted by our anger and distrust, to believe that our standing in the world has been so weakened that we have no choice but to accept his terms. He wants us to blame ourselves and each other and acquiesce.
It's true that we have failed to adjust our posture relative to our most important trading partner, even as it has become capricious and belligerent. We have failed to diversify our trading relationships despite the evident trouble on the horizon. We have failed to ease arbitrary interprovincial trade barriers, update our antiquated industrial strategy or take other measures to make ourselves more self-sufficient. But he is dead wrong if he thinks we have been weakened to the point where we no longer believe that our sovereignty is real or worth protecting.
There's been much speculation about Trump's motives. Since the border excuse is obviously bogus, what's he actually after? If this is, as he claims, also about saving American jobs, it's a funny way of going about it. Surely part of the actual reason is simply revenue, particularly to offset the costs of the tax cuts he has promised to impose. But key, too, is what he might call the art of the deal. He appears to be after leverage, not just for renegotiating CUSMA, but also to pursue whatever his regime determines is in his country's interests, whatever they might be. In other words, he's trying to bully us - and how we and other like-minded, similarly threatened countries respond will shape the direction of the world in the years to come.
Power has always mattered. But Trump represents power unconstrained by legal or moral guardrails. He believes there is no place for a country such as ours, a middle power that has often been a leader and beneficiary of the rules-based liberal international order, wielding our soft power to change the world, or at least nudge it in a better direction. He sees no value in the peace and order our constitution guarantees. Now the question is whether our commitment to these values and to our shared citizenship eclipses the differences we have become so focused on.
In the days since Trump confirmed his tariffs, we have seen glimmers of hope, an outpouring of something like patriotism, a decidedly un-Canadian sentiment. We have seen leaders from across the political spectrum - Jean Chrétien and Stephen Harper alike - call for unity. We have seen businesses support retaliatory measures and call on governments to support furloughed workers, knowing these will come at a cost. Many citizens have been quick to take matters into their own hands, signing petitions, vowing to boycott American goods and to change travel plans. Justin Trudeau and Doug Ford have both projected resolve and strength, taking strategic and targeted countermeasures, as they should.
These Canadians understand what all of us must now grasp: No one has ever won by appeasing a bully. No one has ever won by negotiating with a knife to their throat. But again and again, battles have been won by those who were counted out, who had no right to survive, never mind thrive, but did because they found strength in each other and a shared commitment to ideals and together did the hard work necessary to overcome. It has never been harder to band together despite our differences, and never more important.
CAPTION: President Donald Trump speaks to reporters as he signs executive orders in the Oval Office of the White House, Friday, Jan. 31.

Subject: Trade disputes; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Trade disputes Tariffs

Document 360 of 500

Donald Trump delays threatened tariffs on Canada for 30 days

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/donald-trump-delays-threatened-tariffs-on-canada/docview/3162834106/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Donald+Trump+delays+threatened+tariffs+on+Canada+for+30+days&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Canada won an 11th-hour reprieve from Donald Trump's threatened U.S. tariffs on Monday, just as Mexico received, after pledging to inject millions more into border security and committing to actions that may not stave off American duties experts say could be devastating to the Canadian economy.
Hours before a midnight deadline, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau said threatened U.S. tariffs against Canada will be paused for 30 days after his second call with the U.S. president Monday, a conversation that lasted 45 minutes and set in motion four more weeks of talks with the U.S. president's team.
Trump posted on Truth Social that he was "very pleased with this initial outcome" and the tariffs would be paused "to see whether or not a final economic deal with Canada can be structured."
The prime minister pledged another $200 million to fight organized crime and fentanyl smuggling, saying Canada would appoint a "fentanyl czar," add Mexican cartels to the list of terrorist entities, and take other moves to show it was serious about addressing Trump's concerns.
Trudeau posted on X that he laid out Canada's previous $1.3-billion plan promised in November and said, "Nearly 10,000 front-line personnel are and will be working on protecting the border."
That's the same number of additional soldiers Mexico earlier pledged to send to its border with the U.S., and Trump echoed that language later.
The prime minister said Ottawa will ensure "24/7 eyes on the border, launch a Canada-U.S. Joint Strike Force to combat organized crime, fentanyl and money laundering."
"I have also signed a new intelligence directive on organized crime and fentanyl and we will be backing it with $200 million. Proposed tariffs will be paused for at least 30 days while we work together," the prime minister posted.
The second high-stakes call between Trudeau and Trump ended with Canada achieving the same break Mexico scored earlier in the day when Trump spoke to Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum.
As Canadians awaited the results of the Trump-Trudeau call, tensions were running high.
On the Ontario election campaign trail Monday, Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford was caught on a hot mic expressing frustration with the president.
"On election day, was I happy this guy won? One hundred per cent I was. Then the guy pulled out the knife and f---ing yanked it in me," said Ford.
Stock markets had slumped, reflecting concern about the midnight deadline for Trump's 25 per cent tariffs on most Canadian exports, with a slightly lower 10 per cent levy on oil shipments. Canada had pledged matching counter-tariffs on $30-billion worth of American imports.
After word spread Canada had avoided the midnight onset of a trade war, Ford called it "good news," reversing the province's ban on U.S. booze and his earlier decision to cancel Ontario's $100-million contract with Elon Musk's satellite internet provider Starlink.
"We have temporarily averted tariffs that would have severely damaged our economy, giving time for more negotiation and time for cooler heads to prevail. Thank you to the countless workers, union leaders, businesses and everyday proud Canadians who rallied together to make this happen," Ford said.
Ontario will pause its retaliatory measures but if Trump goes ahead, Ford said, "we won't hesitate to remove American products off LCBO shelves or ban American companies from provincial procurement."
"No one should let their guard down or breath a sign of relief at this 30-day pause on the tariffs," said Lana Payne, head of Unifor, Canada's largest private sector union. "As a country, we must use the days ahead to continue to bring Canadians together, to continue to plan for a potential trade war, and to use every single lever we have to build a strong, resilient and diverse economy."
Trump told American reporters Monday before his second call with Trudeau that Canada should become his country's 51st state and then there would be "no tariffs."
"I'd love to see that, but some people say that would be a long shot. If people wanted to play the game right, it would be 100 per cent certain that they'd become a state," he said.
"But a lot of people don't like to play the game because they don't have a threshold of pain. And there would be some pain, but not a lot. The pain would be really theirs."
A Canadian federal official, speaking on condition they not be identified, said Trump did not raise his desire to make Canada a 51st state in the call. And while Trump raised banking as an irritant in the morning call, it did not come up in their afternoon conversation. Nor did Trump raise water as a concern, as he has publicly in the past. The Trudeau team says it is clear that the president has a long list of irritants with Canada on the trade side, but the two leaders focused on the border in the afternoon call.
Public Safety Minister David McGuinty said Canada needs to take Trump's comments about becoming a 51st state "seriously," but he said in a CBC interview that "I think that comment is just not serious."
While McGuinty said some of the latest changes were in the works already, it's not yet clear what a "fentanyl czar" will do.
Mexican and Canadian officials have had several exchanges over the past weeks, but the official said Trudeau did not consult or co-ordinate with Sheinbaum on Monday.
Trump had earlier held out little hope for a deal to avoid tariffs on Canada.
Before he spoke Trudeau on Monday afternoon, Trump welcomed Mexico's pledge to deploy an additional 10,000 soldiers at the border but said, "Canada is very tough. They're very, very tough to do business with, and we can't let them take advantage of the U.S."
Trump raised a number of complaints including his latest - that American banks "cannot" do business in Canada - a declaration which is not accurate.
The president reiterated a statement that the U.S. does not need anything Canada makes, from cars to lumber, as he underlined that he had a "very good relationship" with Sheinbaum.
Trump said his final decision on Mexico would be made following a 30-day period of negotiations led by Secretary of State Marco Rubio.
I
The Canadian Bankers Association rebuffed Trump's claim about banking in a statement and said that "Canada has a strong and competitive banking sector comprised of both domestic and foreign banks."
It said there are 16 U.S.-based bank subsidiaries and branches with around $113 billion (CDN) in assets currently operating in Canada.
"These banks specialize in a range of financial services, including corporate and commercial lending, treasury services, credit card products, investment banking and mortgage financing. They serve not only customers with cross-border business activities, but also Canada's domestic retail market. U.S. banks now make up half of all foreign bank assets in Canada."
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre said Monday that Canada should deploy soldiers to the border too, posting on X what he called a plan to "take back control of the border and save" Canada-U.S. trade. "Send Canadian Forces troops, helicopters & surveillance to the border now."
Ontario's threat to cancel its Starlink contract had directly targeted Starlink owner Musk, a key Trump adviser.
Musk briefly responded on X, the social media platform he owns, with a shrug: "Oh well."
The Ontario government signed a $100-million deal in November for Starlink to deliver high-speed internet to remote residents in rural and northern Ontario.
Trudeau had responded to Trump's tariff threat with a plan to impose 25 per cent tariffs on a range of American products worth up to $30 billion, with another layer of counter-duties on $125 billion worth of American imports to come in three weeks. All that is now on hold.
Ontario, Manitoba, Nova Scotia and British Columbia said they would ban American liquor from store shelves as political leaders pushed a "Buy Canadian" response to Trump's weekend announcement he would impose tariffs, even as two western premiers urged Ottawa to "de-escalate" the rhetoric around tariffs.
CAPTION: PC Leader Doug Ford issued a release on tariffs on Monday, saying Ontario is banning American companies from provincial contracts.Prime Minister Justin Trudeau addresses media following the imposition of a raft of tariffs by U.S. President Donald Trump against Canada, Mexico and China on Saturday.Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford speaks from the podium during a visit to Walker Construction in St. Catharines, Ont. on Friday.

Document 361 of 500

Doug Ford defends snap election call despite Trump trade war

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-defends-snap-election-call-despite/docview/3162833962/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+defends+snap+election+call+despite+Trump+trade+war&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A defensive Doug Ford insists it was not reckless to call a provincial election against the backdrop of a trade war that could devastate Ontario's economy.
The Progressive Conservative leader, who last week triggered the Feb. 27 vote 15 months early, maintained his hands are not tied during the campaign and he can still govern while electioneering.
"I totally disagree," Ford said Monday as he accepted the endorsement of the Ontario Professional Fire Fighters Association in Etobicoke.
He noted he unilaterally scrapped a $100 million contract with Starlink, which is owned by Elon Musk, a key ally of U.S. President Donald Trump.
That change of heart came after Ford had initially defended the deal with the satellite internet provider on Jan. 24 in Brampton.
"We went out for a transparent bid, they came out top," he said that day after journalists asked him if he would follow Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie's call to scrap the Starlink agreement.
On Monday, Ford noted he can do his day job and campaign at the same time.
"I just pulled off a billion dollars from LCBO shelves of U.S. products. This is 25 days left (in the campaign). This gives us a strong four year mandate to outlive and outlast the Trump administration. People want certainty," said the Tory leader.
"I'm out in the international media every single day. I was on Fox and going on CNN, going back on Fox. What I'm doing, I'm getting the message out to the American people," he said.
Ford added that "if any emergencies come, I'll reconvene cabinet."
To ease the strain from tariffs, his Conservatives promised $3 billion in breaks on small-business payroll taxes, a six-month deferral of business taxes, a hike in the LCBO's wholesale discount for bars and restaurants to 15 per cent from 10 per cent, a $300 million expansion of tax credits for manufacturers investing in buildings, machinery and equipment, and $40 million for municipalities facing "severe economic disruption."
But Progressive Conservative MPP Peter Bethlenfalvy, who served as Ford's finance minister, acknowledged none of that aid can be provided until after Election Day, presuming his party wins a third term.
"We need a mandate from the people of Ontario to spend their money," Bethlenfalvy told reporters in Woodbridge.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said the Tories not done enough to "tariff-proof Ontario."
"With Doug Ford, all you have to do is look at his record. His record is side deals, bad deals, deals that sell off and sell out Ontario companies every time - and we've seen it again and again (with) Staples, Walmart, Starlink," Stiles said at Oshawa's Canadian Automotive Museum, standing in front of an NDP orange Bricklin SV1 sports car, which was made in New Brunswick.
"Doug Ford didn't create this crisis, Donald Trump did. But Ford's failures and bad deals have left Ontario's economy weaker, and the families who live here vulnerable," she said.
"We need to make Ontario stronger and more resilient so we can withstand this crisis and move beyond it."
In Kitchener-Waterloo, Crombie conceded that on tariff response "there is no daylight between my approach right now and Doug Ford," but said it was irresponsible for him to call an "unnecessary" and "opportunistic" $189 million snap election during a trade crisis.
"I agree with him on most of the measures, obviously the procurement measures and buying local and buying Ontario and buying Canadian but there are lots of other things that we can be doing," she said after a speech to the Greater Kitchener Waterloo Chamber of Commerce.
Crombie pledged to fund $150,000 bonuses to "patriotic nurses and doctors who want to come back home to Canada from the U.S. and help solve Doug Ford's health-care crisis."
Green Leader Mike Schreiner expressed concern that the Tories are focused on the hustings instead of housing.
Schreiner promised a "first-time homebuyers plan that would remove fees and taxes on the purchase of new starter homes built within urban boundaries."
"Young people are leaving the province because they've given up hope of ever being able to afford a home in Ontario. Meanwhile, the only kind of housing Doug Ford wants to build is mansions in the Greenbelt that no one can afford," he said.
That's a reference to $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal that is the subject of an ongoing RCMP criminal investigation.
Ford, who has denied any wrongdoing in the cancelled land rezoning debacle, called the snap election in part to get in front of any potential charges by the Mounties who have been on the case since October 2023.
The Tory chief also wanted to take advantage of the fact he leads in public-opinion polls. As well, he wanted to face voters before a federal election expected in the spring.
CAPTION: Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford speaks from the podium during a visit to Walker Construction in St. Catharines, Ont. on Jan. 31, 2025.

Trump tariffs:Ontario banning American companies from provincial contracts, ripping up deal with Starlink; Loonie falls to lowest level since 2003

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/trump-tariffs-ontario-banning-american-companies/docview/3162833943/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trump+tariffs%3AOntario+banning+American+companies+from+provincial+contracts%2C+ripping+up+deal+with+Starlink%3B+Loonie+falls+to+lowest+level+since+2003&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The latest news as Donald Trump's administration imposes tariffs on Canada. Web links to longer stories if available. Follow the Star's live coverage on Monday.
8:15 a.m. PC Leader Doug Ford issued a release on tariffs on Monday, saying Ontario is banning American companies from provincial contracts.
"Starting today and until U.S. tariffs are removed, Ontario is banning American companies from provincial contracts.
Every year, the Ontario government and its agencies spend $30 billion on procurement, alongside our $200 billion plan to build Ontario. U.S.-based businesses will now lose out on tens of billions of dollars in new revenues. They only have President Trump to blame.
We're going one step further. We'll be ripping up the province's contract with Starlink. Ontario won't do business with people hellbent on destroying our economy.
8 a.m. The Canadian dollar fell to its lowest level in more than two decades after U.S. President Donald Trump announced sweeping tariffs to be enacted Tuesday.
The loonie was trading for 68.13 cents US, its lowest level against the U.S. dollar since 2003, compared with 69.04 cents US on Friday.
The futures market also pointed to large drops for U.S. stock markets when they begin trading this morning.
CAPTION: U.S. President Donald Trump sits in the Oval Office of the White House after signing executive orders on Jan. 23.

Document 363 of 500

Experts predicting low election turnout

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/experts-predicting-low-election-turnout/docview/3162729337/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Experts+predicting+low+election+turnout&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The last time Ontarians went to the polls, the province recorded the lowest voter turnout in its election history. Observers say they don't expect a significant change in this month's snap election.
In June 2022, just 44 per cent of 10.7 million eligible voters cast a ballot in the election that yielded a sweeping victory for Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives. As Ford seeks an even bigger electoral mandate, some expect that trend to continue in the Feb. 27 vote.
University of Toronto political science Prof. Randy Besco said voter frustration, anger and a competitive race drive higher turnouts, but those elements don't seem to be at play in this election.
"If they're angry about things and they want change, then they get out and vote," he said. "And the other thing is that turnout tends to be high when ... the results are close and it's unclear who is going to win."
Observers point out that Ford already had a large majority government, his party is polling well, and his "Team Canada" approach U.S. tariff threats has only boosted his profile.
Lydia Miljan, a political science professor at the University of Windsor, said in addition to a perceived lack of competition and needle-moving voter anger, other factors may also lead to lacklustre turnout.
She said Ford's decision to call an election nearly 11/2 years before the fixed date took Ontarians "off guard," and the short campaign leading up to a very rare February vote - when some people may be travelling for winter vacations - could lower voter participation.
Chaos in federal politics, with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau set to step down as soon as the Liberal party chooses its new leader in early March, may turn out to be another distraction for Ontario voters, Miljan said.
"Simultaneously, people are gearing up for a federal election campaign," she said.
Getting out the votes in northern parts of the province, where winter weather is harsher, could prove especially hard, Miljan suggested.
"I think that actually we might even have a lower turnout than last time's historic low."
But Besco said weather is unlikely to affect voter turnout, unless blizzards or extreme cold hit parts of the province on election day.
Elections Ontario has said there are "contingency plans" in case of severe weather.
"We are working closely with local and provincial authorities to monitor the situation and provide frequent updates for those of you who are unable to make it to a polling station," the province's chief electoral officer, Greg Essensa, said.
Essensa said efforts are underway to make voting easy for the 10.8 million Ontarians who can cast a ballot in this election. That includes options to vote by mail, at an advanced polling station or on election day. Under certain circumstances, some voters may also be able to vote from home or a hospital.
Both Besco and Miljan said low voter turnout is not necessarily an issue.
It means people are "fine with the status quo and there's no big problems ... you know, sometimes politics is boring," Besco said.
Miljan said she doesn't equate low voter turnout with low public engagement, or see it as "somehow a problem for democracy."
Myer Siemiatycki, professor emeritus of politics at Toronto Metropolitan University, agrees that fewer people are likely to vote in this election, but said he could be proven wrong.
If Ontarians deem U.S. President Donald Trump's cross-border policies an "existential moment," they will be motivated to elect a premier who can fight back, he said.
The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Sharif Hassan The Canadian Press

Document 364 of 500

Canada not backing down: Ottawa prepared to scale up response to Trump tariffs as needed: minister

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/canada-not-backing-down/docview/3162729281/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada+not+backing+down%3A+Ottawa+prepared+to+scale+up+response+to+Trump+tariffs+as+needed%3A+minister&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Everything from American meat, dairy, chocolate, wine, booze, cosmetics, clothing, wood and tobacco products to guns and ammunition is about to get more expensive in Canada.
But not Tesla, the electric car produced by U.S. President Donald Trump's close adviser Elon Musk. Not yet.
Canada's initial counterpunch aimed at Trump's whopping 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian products amounts to a sharp jab at $30 billion worth of American imports, and a hook to come three weeks from now that will aim at another $125 billion worth of American products.
The Liberal government is slapping 25 per cent counter-tariffs on things like Florida juice, fruit and vegetables; South Carolina- and Ohio-made appliances; motorcycles from Pennsylvania - where Harley-Davidson's main production plant is located - and all U.S. coffee exports, officials said during a background technical briefing.
With its first move, the Trudeau government has not aimed a knockout punch: it doesn't apply to the full range of about $600 billion in American imports to this country - unlike what Trump has done. And Ottawa is not applying higher prices or export tariffs to Canadian energy shipments to the U.S.
But a tariff war that economists and business leaders warn will have a devastating impact on Canadian businesses and workers kicks off Tuesday as Canada published a detailed list showing how it will penalize 1,256 American products.
Mexico warned it too will hit America with unspecified retaliatory counter-tariffs and nontariff countermeasures, and U.S. Democrats appealed to Trump to halt the trade war.
Restaurants Canada is already begging for an exemption for food products, saying the $120-billion food service industry "is the fourth largest employer in the country, employing 1.2 million people across small and medium-sized businesses" and cannot absorb more cost increases.
For now, the Canadian government is trying to use a scalpel, not a sledgehammer to apply pain to politically sensitive products coming from states where Republican lawmakers or influencers might be mobilized to persuade Trump to reverse course.
If that isn't possible, Finance Minister Dominic LeBlanc told the Star, Canada will scale up its response to Trump's tariffs as needed and is not backing down despite the U.S. president's threat to escalate penalties against Canada if Ottawa retaliates.
LeBlanc's immediate plan is to target consumer and household goods that represent chunks of the American import market in Canada, as Ottawa tries to spread the pain without unduly burdening any one region, sector or province, federal officials said.
It will allow Canadian importers to apply to have the duties temporarily waived "in certain exceptional circumstances," say, if the listed goods are key inputs to Canadian manufacturers that can't be found domestically or from non-U.S. suppliers.
The senior Canadian officials, who spoke on condition they not be identified, would not say how much revenue the Canadian counter-tariffs would raise, noting that an emerging push to "Buy Canadian" driven by premiers and business leaders could cause consumer behavioural shifts that change what might be expected, which theoretically is about $38 billion in counter-tariff revenue collected on $155 billion worth of American imports.
Meanwhile, Ottawa will pursue any legal recourse it has under the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement (USMCA) which entrenched tariff-free North American free trade, and will try to challenge Trump's duties that the federal government says are illegal and unjustified under that deal and World Trade Organization rules.
But nobody's kidding themselves that the revised NAFTA deal that Trump signed in 2018 offers any immediate remedy for the economic pain to come in Canada.
Canadian officials declined to say just how much of an economic hit Ottawa anticipates, saying with all his different declarations over the past two months, they had not modelled the exact scenario Trump laid out - 25 per cent surcharges on all Canadian goods with a lower 10 per cent levy on Canadian oil and gas.
They pointed only to estimates south of the border that Trump's tariffs could lead to a 1.7 per cent reduction in America's own economic activity. Some Canadian economists have projected a hit of up to three per cent on Canada's gross domestic product.
In an interview Sunday, LeBlanc said, "If these (U.S.) tariffs are still around weeks and months from now, and if it's an ongoing economic turbulence, then we'll look at other additional measures, including nontariff measures, but it's a proportionate response that will be scaled up, as we see where the Canadian economy is over the next number of weeks."
The Trudeau government has not ruled out additional measures such as higher costs for energy permits or fees that would make Canadian energy exports more costly for American businesses. But LeBlanc said Canada is not looking at turning off any taps or cutting off the flow of any energy shipments.
LeBlanc declined to comment on Trump's social media taunts Sunday, saying it is not "constructive for us to respond to every social media post that the president makes."
"Our job is to, in this particular moment, figure out what's the appropriate response, to convince the Americans that it's not in their economic interest to continue this."
Trump wrote Sunday on his platform Truth Social that without being able to sell energy, cars and lumber into the U.S., "Canada ceases to exist as a viable Country. Harsh but true! Therefore, Canada should become our Cherished 51st State. Much lower taxes, and far better military protection for the people of Canada - AND NO TARIFFS!"
The president rejected critics, including in mainstream American media, that warned his moves will hurt U.S. businesses and consumers who will have to pay the price of his surcharges on Canadian products. In capital letters, Trump wrote, "Will there be some pain? Yes, maybe. (And maybe not!) But we will make America great again, and it will all be worth the price that must be paid."
LeBlanc said the federal government will support the companies and workers Trump is targeting, saying a "number of measures" are already in place, including the employment insurance program, that can be adjusted very quickly to respond to the impact of a trade war. Critics say the EI program is ill-suited for this kind of crisis.
"If the issue is liquidity for businesses (or) access cash flow for businesses, we can consider things like deferring tax payments," said LeBlanc. "There are a number of authorities we have with Crown corporations, Business Development Bank, Export Development Canada, to put additional liquidity or supports into the economy."
LeBlanc called them "built-in shock absorbers" that follow economic adjustments on an ongoing basis.
LeBlanc said that Ottawa is not instructing or co-ordinating moves by provinces like Ontario, Nova Scotia, B.C. and Manitoba - which all announced Sunday that they are yanking U.S. alcohol products off their provincial liquor store shelves.
Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford said, "Every year, LCBO sells nearly $1 billion worth of American wine, beer, spirits and seltzers. Not anymore."
"Starting Tuesday, we're removing American products from LCBO shelves. As the only wholesaler of alcohol in the province, LCBO will also remove American products from its catalogue so other Ontario-based restaurants and retailers can't order or restock U.S. products."
Ford urged customers to buy Ontario-made products instead and to "drink responsibly."
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre at a news conference Sunday shifted to take a harder line on the U.S., and condemned Trump's "massive, unjust and unjustified tariffs," challenging the Trump administration to explain the uneven treatment of China which faces "only a 10 per cent tariff."
"Even if you believe in tariffs," Poilievre said, "in what strategic mindset does that make sense if it's to do with fentanyl? Fentanyl is coming from China. It's killing our people too."
Poilievre supports dollar-for-dollar retaliatory counter-tariffs on products where Canadians can find replacements, and identified steel and aluminum for counter-tariffs, saying those products are made in Canada. He demanded all counter-tariff revenue be poured into aiding businesses and workers, an immediate tax cut to help businesses through the crisis, and the elimination of interprovincial trade barriers, to boost Canada's economy.
The Conservatives and the NDP have called on Trudeau to immediately recall Parliament to deal with the crisis.
NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh again called on the federal government to "cut off the supply of critical minerals to the United States."
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau said Saturday he is weighing "several nontariff measures, including some relating to critical minerals, energy, procurement and other partnerships."
Months ago, Trudeau said his job was to work in Canada's interests even if they diverged from Mexico's. Now he is looking to enlist allies. Trudeau spoke Saturday to Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum.
A readout from the Prime Minister's Office that summarized their conversation said they spoke about "their respective efforts to enhance border security and combat the production and distribution of illegal drugs. The leaders agreed to continue working together in areas of common interest and to enhance the strong bilateral relations between Canada and Mexico."
CREDIT: Tonda MacCharles Toronto Star

Document 365 of 500

As NDP targets tolls, is war on cars over?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/as-ndp-targets-tolls-is-war-on-cars-over/docview/3162729261/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=As+NDP+targets+tolls%2C+is+war+on+cars+over%3F&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Before the Ontario election campaign had officially kicked off, NDP Leader Marit Stiles announced a big policy plank.
This is usually when parties set out a defining message, the kind of thing they want voters to associate with them for the rest of the campaign. What would the NDP present as a signature issue? Health care? Housing? Education?
Road tolls. Specifically, the removal of them.
"It's time we put some money back in the pockets of Ontarians, and got them home to their families faster," Stiles said in declaring that she would remove the tolls on Highway 407 as premier.
Leaving aside the questionable feasibility and extraordinary cost of such a plan - the privately owned highway north of Toronto is by some estimates worth more than $30 billion - it is notable that the most progressive of the main parties at Queen's Park wants, as one its first acts, to eliminate road pricing.
In the early days of Doug Ford's political rise, back when he was mostly a sidekick to his late brother, the pair used to make great hay out of the "war on cars." They opposed surface-level transit, bike lanes, road tolls, anything that would be perceived as an inconvenience to drivers. Non-drivers be damned.
The NDP's anti-toll position is the end of that fight. The war on cars is over. The cars won.
To be fair to Stiles, she's not the first NDP leader to recoil at tolls, even though the idea behind them, to encourage the use of public transit and other more environmentally friendly modes of transportation, are the kinds of things that are normally championed by progressives. Andrea Horwath, her predecessor at Queen's Park, complained about even the smallest of toll projects, such as the brief stretches of highway in Toronto's west end that are free to high-occupancy vehicles but tolled for single drivers. Horwath decried them as "Lexus lanes," never seeming to quite get the point that allowing willing drivers to pay to use those lanes benefited the drivers on the rest of the road.
Horwath's rival at the time, then-premier Kathleen Wynne, was all over the public record as supporting road pricing as a way to ease gridlock and fund transit. But she repeatedly bailed on the idea, first by rejecting the advice of expert panels on traffic and transit that she convened, and later by blocking the request of Toronto city council to put tolls on the Don Valley Parkway and Gardiner Expressway. (Her first slip into Fordian car-first politics came years earlier, when she backed the wildly expensive Scarborough subway extension to save a byelection campaign.)
Over more than a decade when the Ontario Liberals were in power, the same Liberals that got rid of coal-fired power plants and protected the Greenbelt for environmental reasons, only former mayor John Tory, a conservative, truly supported road tolls.
Which is the funny thing about tolls. They can be supported by parties of the right, because they are market-based and impose costs only on users, or by parties of the left, because they discourage pollution. The Liberals, as business-friendly environmentalists, ought to love them.
But, no. Given many chances, the Liberals have veered away from tolls. And when the NDP begins its campaign by essentially waving a More Free Roads banner, that battle is lost.
Premier Ford would sooner wear a "Canada Is For Sale, Actually" hat than put a toll on a highway, so committed is he to the pro-car bit. Even in his fever dream of a 50-kilometre tunnel under Highway 401, he's firmly opposed to the idea that the road's users pay a fee for the privilege. Tolls are an impediment to drivers, like bikes and pedestrians, and Ford won't have that.
He is, at least, consistent. It's just a surprise that the party on the opposite side of the political spectrum has so fully embraced his way of thinking.
Scott Stinson is a journalist based in suburban Toronto.
CREDIT: Scott Stinson Contributor Scott Stinson is a journalist based in suburban Toronto.;

Document 366 of 500

LCBO to remove U.S. booze from shelves

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/lcbo-remove-u-s-booze-shelves/docview/3162729245/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LCBO+to+remove+U.S.+booze+from+shelves&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The provincial government is ordering the Liquor Control Board of Ontario to remove thousands of American products from its shelves and catalogue in response to U.S. President Donald Trump's order to impose tariffs on Canadian goods Tuesday.
"Every year, LCBO sells nearly $1 billion worth of American wine, beer, spirits and seltzers," Progressive Conservative party Leader Doug Ford wrote in an online statement. "Not anymore"
The removal will roll out Tuesday and will also prohibit Ontario-based restaurants and businesses from restocking and ordering American beer, wine and spirits since the LCBO is the only alcohol wholesaler in the province.
"The LCBO has been instructed to suspend the purchase of all U.S. products indefinitely," Ivana Yelich, a spokesperson for the provincial government told the Star.
She said the order extends to sales of U.S. products to LCBO wholesale customers, and no new wholesale orders or deliveries to restaurants, bars, grocery stores, and other retail channels will be processed.
"LCBO is the importer of record for all U.S. alcohol products into Ontario, with annual sales of up to $965 million," they said in a statement. "We currently list more than 3,600 products from 35 U.S. states."
Jim Lisser, the executive director of Drinks Ontario, an organization representing alcohol importers in the province, told the Star his organization understands the "importance" of Ontario's decision, noting that a "consistent national approach is best" in face of the tariffs.
The tariffs on U.S. imports will also include American beer, wine and bourbon, fruits and vegetables, perfume, clothing, furniture, sports equipment, materials like lumber and plastics, and more.
The move follows British Columbia Premier David Eby's directive Saturday to the BC Liquor Distribution Branch to immediately stop buying American liquor from "red states" and pull existing stock from store shelves.
In a statement on Saturday, Ford wrote that Canada has "no choice but to hit back and hit back hard," adding that he is in full support of a "strong and forceful" response that matches U.S. tariffs dollar for dollar.
"The coming days and weeks will be incredibly difficult. Trump's tariffs will devastate our economy," he said.
"They'll put 450,000 jobs at risk across the province. Every sector and region will feel the impact."
Provincial opposition leaders echoed the need for Ontario to fight back against the tariffs.
Bonnie Crombie, leader of the Ontario Liberal party, said she supports Ford's decision to remove American products from LCBO shelves but thinks he hasn't done enough to diversify Ontario's economy. She further criticized Ford, accusing him of abandoning the province during a major crisis by focusing on his re-election campaign.
"Donald Trump has declared war on us. On our workers, our jobs, our factories and our people. In the face of such a devastating threat - with hundreds of thousands of jobs hanging by a thread - we need a premier who puts our people first. And his own politics last," Crombie said in a post on X Saturday evening.
Ontario NDP leader Marit Stilescalled Ford's decision to remove American products from the LCBO a "step in the right direction" and said every province needs to stand united with the federal government.
With files from
Abby O'Brien, Kristjan Lautens and Robert Benzie
CREDIT: Anastasia Blosser Toronto Star With files from AbO'Brien, Kristjan Lautens and Robert Benzie

Document 367 of 500

Protecting what and from whom?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/protecting-what-whom/docview/3162729244/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Protecting+what+and+from+whom%3F&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford, who was already premier and whose Progressive Conservatives occupy 79 of the 124 seats at Queen's Park, has repeatedly stated he needs a "strong mandate" to deal with U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs. Last Tuesday, having triggered a multimillion-dollar election one-and-a-half years early and in the dead of winter ostensibly to secure such a mandate, Ford revealed his party's new campaign slogan: "Protect Ontario."
Protect Ontario what? Hospitals? Schools? Place? Can't be. Ontario hospitals are overcrowded, Ontario schools are underfunded and Ontario Place has been leased to foreign interests. No, "from America" is what's implied. And Ford has done everything in his power to make you infer it: If you can't give the voters something to rally around, give them something to rally against.
This is nothing we haven't seen before: Right-wing populism, which these days is just mainstream conservatism, is remarkably consistent across space and time, and after almost seven years of Ford rule in Ontario, things have gone more or less as you'd expect.
You've got your program cuts (e.g., safe-consumption sites), your creeping privatization (e.g., the health-care system) and your vibes-based policy-making (e.g., removing bike lanes to ease traffic congestion). You've got your insistent claims of fiscal prudence and your biggest, most expensive cabinet in history. And you've got your millionaire man of the people professing to shield you from threats foreign (Trump, explicitly) and domestic (cyclists, presumably).
It's all very predictable stuff, and if, as predicted, Ford wins on Feb. 27, there's a good chance you'll get more of the same. But after the bike lanes are removed and the safe-consumption sites are eradicated, after the Ontario Science Centre meets whatever grim fate awaits it and Ontario Place becomes a parking garage, after Ford realizes his vision for this province, what will be left of it to protect? The Bradford Bypass?
See, this is one of the many reasons cultural and educational institutions matter: they give us a sense of our shared heritage, of what Ontario has been and could be and of why that's worth protecting. I thought conservatives loved that sort of thing, but politicians like Ford keep proving me wrong. No one's patriotic feelings are stirred by a privately operated luxury spa. No one's sense of self is located in corner-store beer.
This is not a liberal-versus-conservative thing. It was a conservative who established Ontario Place. It was a conservative who established the Ontario Science Centre. It was a conservative who established our system of community colleges and our public broadcaster. In fact, it was all the same guy: the late Bill Davis, premier from 1971 to 1985 and, one can only assume, sworn enemy of Doug Ford, who sold off the first thing, let rot the second thing, hobbled the third thing, and mostly ignored the fourth thing.
Meanwhile, what has Ford contributed to the cultural life of this province? What has he contributed to our sense of pride in Ontario? If he wants to protect us from America, he could start by showing us a future that's worth protecting. His sloganeering might briefly rouse the jingoist inside us, but it's leaders like Davis who manage to instil things that are more enduring, things that can't be expressed via baseball cap.
Alas, also things that don't send voters to the polls. And that's the point, really. Ontarians may be united against Trump (although even that's questionable), but that doesn't mean they're united. A substantial number of Ontarians - a substantial number of whom won't vote - don't want to give Ford a stronger mandate, because they saw what he did with the first two. Saving the economy from Trump's tariffs is obviously important, but so is being able to afford a house, see a family doctor, and bicycle to work without getting killed.
"Protect Ontario," sure. Only give us something to protect.
Graeme Bayliss is an opinion editor at the Star.
CAPTION: Having triggered a multimillion-dollar election one-and-a-half years early and in the dead of winter, Doug Ford revealed his party's new campaign slogan: "Protect Ontario."Steve Russell Toronto Star
CREDIT: Graeme Bayliss Contributor Graeme Bayliss is an opinion editor at the Star.;

Document 368 of 500

Brimming with a political message

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/brimming-with-political-message/docview/3162729216/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Brimming+with+a+political+message&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In politics, effective messaging is everything. We have limited attention spans, and even more so than ever, a picture is worth a thousand words. Enter the political slogan hat.
In mid-January, Ontario Premier Doug Ford showed up in Ottawa for a meeting between the premiers and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau wearing a hat reading "Canada Is Not For Sale." The hat was both a refute to U.S. President Donald Trump's threats to make Canada the 51st state, and a nod to Trump's own embrace of the red "Make America Great Again" hat that has become a potent symbol of the new American right.
Then last week, Ontario Liberal leader Bonnie Crombie wore her own trucker hat on the election campaign trail in Barrie, printed with the slightly less catchy phrase: "Real Leaders Fix Healthcare."
"The hat was a nod to the current political moment, where slogans and gimmicks are too common in our politics," Ashley Csanady, Liberal strategist for Crombie's campaign, told the Star. "That we can subvert the statements of Doug Ford and Donald Trump into something that actually means something to people: health care that works for you. There is no bigger issue than health care in Ontario - and fixing health care is real and concrete action."
The MAGA hat, of course, is the blueprint. Now ubiquitous, it was actually a fluke. Corey Lewandowski, who was campaign manager for Trump on his 2016 run, has said there were no marketing professionals involved in its conception.
Just a standard red hat with "default" Times New Roman text, to quote CNN's Cassie Spodak, it was introduced by the merch-happy Trump campaign. Trump, himself a marketer at heart, first wore the hat at a rally about a month after his initial campaign launch, and it caught on fast with his supporters. The original "made-in-America" version was soon subsumed by cheaper dupes manufactured offshore.
It was an efficient visual tool. Like the "Sneetches on Beaches" - those Dr. Seussian critters divided by those with "stars on thars" and those without - the sea of red hats in crowds served to instantly demarcate Trump's supporters.
The hats' slogan became so recognizable that it became an acronym and the name of a movement. A proud badge of belonging and an object of scorn for detractors, the hats were the definition of polarizing, even though President Joe Biden wore one once, at a 9/11 ceremony, in an attempt to create solidarity. Actress Alyssa Milano, an outspoken Trump opponent, tweeted in 2019 that "The red MAGA hat is the new white hood."
The day after Trump was elected in 2016, former Ontario Court Justice Bernd Zabel wore a MAGA hat to court in Hamilton. He was later suspended after 81 complaints were filed with the Ontario Judicial Council about displaying political affiliations in what should be a neutral courtroom.
So why are trucker hats such a potent symbol? First, they are basically walking billboards, advertising a short and sharp message that has to fit across a forehead. But this style of hat also symbolizes the everyman; they are working-class coded. That's a powerful tool for politicians looking to connect with the masses, something Trump, the "anti-elite" billionaire, harnessed to help his followers connect to him.
Originally, ball caps were worn by baseball players circa 1860 in Brooklyn. The New York City borough was known for its style even then, and the "Brooklyn-style" hat soon graduated the baseball diamonds to the streets.
Trucker hats, which are worn higher on the forehead, have their origins in agriculture. In the 1970s, farm and feed supply companies handed out branded hats to farmers and truck drivers to market their products. These workers needed sun protection, hence the broad brim, ventilating mesh back and sweat-absorbing foam front, which sat tall on the head to better display the brand name.
Then came the early 2000s, when celebrities such as Ashton Kutcher and Pharrell Williams co-opted the rural-coded look for parties and premieres. Nicole Richie and Paris Hilton wore trucker hats throughout their reality show "The Simple Life," starting in 2003, in which the Beverly Hills socialites moonlit as menial labourers in the Midwest. Young Hollywood wore truckloads of caps by Von Dutch, an American brand then headed by French designer Christian Audigier, which took the basic trucker hat and charged hundreds for it just by adding a logo.
You would think that cultural plunge would make it go away, but no. Justin Bieber brought the style back circa 2010, wearing trucker caps backwards. And it resurfaced again in Y2K-obsessed 2024, with Kendall Jenner's tequila brand 818 launching branded trucker hats and singer Charlie XCX paying homage to the original trend with a song called "Von Dutch" on her smash album, "Brat."
For good, for evil, or for fashion, putting a message on a hat is a brilliant way to communicate - our eyes go to people's faces first. The trick is finding a slogan that will cut through the noise.
CAPTION: Popularized by Donald Trump and worn by Premier Doug Ford and Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, the slogan hat is more effective than lawn signs to broadcast a message.The Canadian Press file photos
CREDIT: Leanne Delap Special to the Star

Subject: Political campaigns; Marketing; Politics; Headgear

Business indexing term: Subject: Marketing

Location: Canada

People: Trump, Donald J
