Document 369 of 500

We didn't want this, but now we must fight

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/we-didnt-want-this-now-must-fight/docview/3162728953/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=We+didn%27t+want+this%2C+but+now+we+must+fight&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The trade war we are now fighting with the United States - a war we never wanted - is a momentous test for our country.
The cost of living, already at a crisis level for many, will rise. Canadian businesses, already struggling amid a weak economy, will be hurt. The dollar will fall. Jobs will be lost.
As we start to feel this pain, the temptation will be to point fingers at one another. The political reflex of the moment is to divide at every opportunity, to use any crisis as a wedge to separate not only one party from its rivals, but also its supporters from everyone else.
This is what Donald Trump wants. He wants us to buy that his tariffs are the inevitable consequence of our border policies, never mind that the flow of fentanyl from our country is a trickle while the flow of guns from his is a flood. He wants us to be distracted by our anger and distrust, to believe that our standing in the world has been so weakened that we have no choice but to accept his terms. He wants us to blame ourselves and each other and acquiesce.
It's true that we have failed to adjust our posture relative to our most important trading partner, even as it has become capricious and belligerent. We have failed to diversify our trading relationships despite the evident trouble on the horizon. We have failed to ease arbitrary interprovincial trade barriers, update our antiquated industrial strategy or take other measures to make ourselves more self-sufficient. But he is dead wrong if he thinks we have been weakened to the point where we no longer believe that our sovereignty is real or worth protecting.
There's been much speculation about Trump's motives. Since the border excuse is obviously bogus, what's he actually after? If this is, as he claims, also about saving American jobs, it's a funny way of going about it. Surely part of the actual reason is simply revenue, particularly to offset the costs of the tax cuts he has promised to impose. But key, too, is what he might call the art of the deal. He appears to be after leverage, not just for renegotiating the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement, but also to pursue whatever his regime determines is in his country's interests, whatever they might be. In other words, he's trying to bully us - and how we and other like-minded, similarly threatened countries respond will shape the direction of the world in the years to come.
Power has always mattered. But Trump represents power unconstrained by legal or moral guardrails. He believes there is no place for a country such as ours, a middle power that has often been a leader and beneficiary of the rules-based liberal international order, wielding our soft power to change the world, or at least nudge it in a better direction. He sees no value in the peace and order our constitution guarantees. Now the question is whether our commitment to these values and to our shared citizenship eclipses the differences we have become so focused on.
In the days since Trump confirmed his tariffs, we have seen glimmers of hope, an outpouring of something like patriotism, a decidedly un-Canadian sentiment. We have seen leaders from across the political spectrum - Jean Chrétien and Stephen Harper alike - call for unity. We have seen businesses support retaliatory measures and call on governments to support furloughed workers, knowing these will come at a cost. Many citizens have been quick to take matters into their own hands, signing petitions, vowing to boycott American goods and to change travel plans. Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Premier Doug Ford have both projected resolve and strength, taking strategic and targeted countermeasures, as they should.
These Canadians understand what all of us must now grasp: No one has ever won by appeasing a bully. No one has ever won by negotiating with a knife to their throat. But again and again, battles have been won by those who were counted out, who had no right to survive, never mind thrive, but did because they found strength in each other and a shared commitment to ideals and together did the hard work necessary to overcome. It has never been harder to band together despite our differences, and never more important.
CREDIT: ;

Subject: Tariffs; International trade; Economic impact; Political leadership; Sovereignty

Document 370 of 500

Canada won't back down, will scale up retaliation against Trump's tariffs as needed, government says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canada-wont-back-down-will-scale-up-retaliation/docview/3162976726/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada+won%27t+back+down%2C+will+scale+up+retaliation+against+Trump%27s+tariffs+as+needed%2C+government+says&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Canada will scale up its retaliation to Donald Trump's tariffs as needed and is not backing down in spite of the U.S. president's threat to escalate his own penalties against Canada, Finance Minister Dominic LeBlanc said.
In an interview with the Star Sunday, LeBlanc said what was announced is only "the initial response of the Government of Canada."
"But if these (U.S.) tariffs are still around weeks and months from now, and if it's an ongoing economic turbulence, then we'll look at other additional measures, including non-tariff measures, but it's a proportionate response that will be scaled up, as we see where the Canadian economy is over the next number of weeks."
The Trudeau government has not ruled out additional measures such as higher fees on energy permits or other fees that would make Canadian energy exports more costly for American businesses, even though Trump has so far said Canadian oil and gas would face a lower tariff of 10 per cent, starting Feb. 4. But LeBlanc said Canada is not looking at cutting off the flow of any energy.
LeBlanc said Canadian officials continue to talk to counterparts in the Trump administration, and he declined to comment on Trump's social media taunts Sunday.
"I don't think it's constructive for us to respond to every social media post that the president makes, or our job is not to react hour by hour to what people are saying, either on social media or in American media, our job is to, in this particular moment, figure out what's the appropriate response, to convince the Americans that it's not in their economic interest to continue this."
Trump wrote on his social media platform Truth Social that without being able to sell its energy, cars and lumber into the U.S., "Canada ceases to exist as a viable Country. Harsh but true! Therefore, Canada should become our Cherished 51st State. Much lower taxes, and far better military protection for the people of Canada - AND NO TARIFFS!"
The U.S. president rejected critics, including in mainstream American media outlets, that warned his moves will hurt American businesses and consumers who will have to pay the price of his 25-per-cent surcharges on Canadian products. In capital letters, he wrote, "will there be some pain? Yes, maybe. (And maybe not!) But we will make America great again, and it will all be worth the price that must be paid."
In Canada, the finance minister said the government will support the sectors, companies and workers Trump is targeting.
LeBlanc said there are a "number of measures" already in place in the employment insurance program that can be adjusted very quickly to respond to the impact of a trade war, brought on by Trump's tariffs that will raise the cost of Canadian goods in America, potentially hurting Canadian exporters and workers.
Counter-tariffs levied by the federal government can be expected likewise to raise the cost of American goods bought in Canada.
"If the issue is liquidity for businesses (or) access cash flow for businesses, we can consider things like deferring tax payments," said LeBlanc. "There are a number of authorities we have with Crown corporations, Business Development Bank, Export Development Canada, to put additional liquidity or supports into the economy."
LeBlanc called them "built in shock absorbers" that follow economic adjustments on an ongoing basis. "But if we have to consider other measures or look at other ways, the good news is the provinces are entirely positive about partnering with us."
LeBlanc said that Ottawa is not instructing or co-ordinating moves by provinces like Ontario to restrict U.S. alcohol sales in Canada, but said those provinces did advise the government Saturday of steps they would take.
But he said he was "encouraged" overall that provinces are all on board with the Trudeau government's initial counter-tariff plan, which could see Canada's retaliatory 25-per-cent surcharges ramp up over three weeks to cover $155-billion worth of American imports.
Progressive Conservative leader Doug Ford said Sunday that Ontario liquor stores will yank all U.S. products off their shelves starting Tuesday.
"Every year, LCBO sells nearly $1 billion worth of American wine, beer, spirits and seltzers. Not anymore.
"Starting Tuesday, we're removing American products from LCBO shelves. As the only wholesaler of alcohol in the province, LCBO will also remove American products from its catalogue so other Ontario-based restaurants and retailers can't order or restock U.S. products."
He urged customers to buy Ontario-made products instead and to "drink responsibly."
Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre at a news conference Sunday condemned Trump's "massive, unjust and unjustified tariffs," and challenged the Trump administration to explain the uneven treatment of Canada and China which faces "only a 10 per cent tariff."
"Even if you believe in tariffs," Poilievre said, "in what strategic mindset does that make sense if it's to do with fentanyl. Fentanyl is coming from China. It's killing our people too."
Poilievre supports dollar-for-dollar retaliatory counter-tariffs on products where Canadians can find replacements, and identified steel and aluminum for counter-tariffs, saying those products are made in Canada. I believe we're already in a trade war, and I believe no one will win. I think both countries will lose, and that's my message to our American friends. Why?
All tariff revenue should be poured into aiding businesses and workers, he said. Poilievre is also demanding an immediate tax cut to help businesses through the crisis and elimination of interprovincial trade barriers.
The Conservatives and the NDP have called on Trudeau to immediately recall Parliament to deal with the crisis.
Poilievre said it's "insane" Parliament is not sitting while the Liberal party deals with an internal "power struggle" over replacing the prime minister.
NDP leader Jagmeet Singh again called on the federal government to "cut off the supply of critical minerals to the United States."
Trudeau in his news conference Saturday night said he was considering with the provinces and territories "several non-tariff measures, including some relating to critical minerals, energy, procurement and other partnerships."
In the past few weeks, the Canadian government has made efforts to speak to other allies in Mexico and Europe about how to respond to Trump. He said that he intends to impose tariffs on the European Union as well.
Months ago, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau had mused aloud about working solely in Canada's interests even if it diverged from Mexico. But now he is looking to enlist allies.
Trudeau spoke to Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum spoke Saturday after Trump signed the executive order directing the imposition of the tariffs against Canada and Mexico.
A readout released by the Prime Minister's Office Sunday that summarized their conversation said they discussed "the importance of preserving North American competitiveness and prosperity."
Regarding U.S. tariffs on both countries, it said they spoke about "their respective efforts to enhance border security and combat the production and distribution of illegal drugs. The leaders agreed to continue working together in areas of common interest and to enhance the strong bilateral relations between Canada and Mexico."
CAPTION: Canada's Prime Minister Justin Trudeau speaks, flanked by Minister of Foreign Affairs Melanie Joly and Minister of Finance Dominic LeBlanc, during a news conference Saturday on Parliament Hill in Ottawa.

Document 371 of 500

Canadian leaders call for retaliation, unity in response to Donald Trump's tariffs on Canadian imports

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canadian-leaders-call-retaliation-unity-response/docview/3162633122/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canadian+leaders+call+for+retaliation%2C+unity+in+response+to+Donald+Trump%27s+tariffs+on+Canadian+imports&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Leaders across Canada are calling for retaliation and unity in response to U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs on Canadian imports.
Early Saturday evening, Trump signed an executive order declaring 25 per cent tariffs on almost all Canadian goods, and a 10 per cent tariff on Canadian energy products, will begin on Tuesday.
Federal leaders react
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau was expected to hold a press conference on Saturday evening and reacted to the threat in a post on X Friday.
"No one - on either side of the border - wants to see American tariffs on Canadian goods. I met with our Canada-U.S. Council today. We're working hard to prevent these tariffs, but if the United States moves ahead, Canada's ready with a forceful and immediate response," Trudeau said.
Conservative party leader Pierre Poilievre on Saturday condemned the "massive, unjust and unjustified tariffs" before calling for the Liberal party to recall Parliament and pass a new bill to combat tariffs.
Poilievre's plan, that was detailed in a post on X, includes dollar-for-dollar tariffs that are "carefully aimed at maximizing impact on American companies while minimizing impact on Canadian consumers," and specifically retaliates against American steel and aluminum.
Poilievre also said the plan would involve giving all the tariff revenues to affected workers and businesses, passing a massive emergency tax cut, knocking down interprovincial trade barriers, rebuilding the military and green-lighting liquified natural gas plants, pipelines, mines, factories and port expansions to overseas markets.
In a statement released Saturday morning, NDP leader Jagmeet Singh said "Trump's tariffs have Canadian workers worried about their jobs and even higher prices."
"We can't let them pay the price. Our plan puts workers first - by fighting back with retaliatory tariffs and increasing supports to create good union jobs in Canada," added Singh.
Former Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland suggested Canada should target Elon Musk and alcohol in retaliation.
"If Donald Trump slaps 25% tariffs on Canadian goods, we must hit back - dollar for dollar - starting with 100% tariffs on all Tesla vehicles and U.S. wine, beer, and spirits. We must protect Canadian workers and businesses," Freeland said in a post on X Friday.
Marc Garneau, the former Minister of Foreign Affairs, also responded to the proposed tariffs in a statement on X early Saturday morning.
"Sometimes threats can force positive long-term change. This is such a moment. In addition to counter-tariffs, Canada must also focus, more than ever before, on improving its labour productivity, lowering interprovincial trade barriers, and increasing its defence spending," said Garneau.
Unifor National president Lana Payne said the implementation of the tariffs is a declaration of a trade war with Canada.
"Trump's decision to go to battle with America's largest trading partner will hurt working people on both sides of the border and inflict real economic damage to both countries. The U.S. tariffs are reportedly scheduled to come into effect on Tuesday. I believe Trump has underestimated Canadians," said Payne who added "we will never forget this act of hostility against our workers, and we must take every measure possible - utilize every ounce of creativity we have - to build a strong, resilient, and diverse economy to never be held hostage by America again."
Ontario leaders react
Days after calling a snap election, at a campaign event in Brampton on Saturday, PC Leader Doug Ford called the tariffs "reckless" and accused Trump of trying to "break" Canada.
"He thinks he can break Canada," Ford told reporters. "He thinks he can buy Canada. He doesn't know what we know, the Canadian spirit can never be broken and, friends, Canada is not for sale."
Closing off his comments, Ford spoke directly to the U.S. president.
"To President Trump, I can only say this: this is not a smart move. It's selfish. It not only hurts Canadians, it hurts your own people. It hurts you, and your administration. It makes America poor. It makes Americans poor."
In a brief statement later released on X, Ford said "hundreds of thousands" of Ontario jobs would be risked by the tariffs.
"The coming days and weeks will be hard. Our economy will feel the impact of President Trump's tariffs almost immediately, with hundreds of thousands of jobs at risk," Ford said adding that "united, we'll get through this"
Leader of the Ontario NDP party Marit Stiles spoke about the threat in a video posted to X Saturday afternoon.
"Trump will try to pick us off one by one. But make no mistake: I'm ready to lead the fight or every single worker and every single job in Ontario. Tough tariff responses. Income protection. A guarantee that you can pay the bills, and keep people working. Strong. United - from the shop floor to Queen's Park and Parliament Hill," Stiles said.
Premiers across Canada
Quebec Premier François Legault addressed the proposed tariffs Saturday, calling them "unjust."
"Mr. Trump spoke specifically about aluminum and steel, key sectors that sustain our regions with good-paying jobs. We will protect our economy and defend our workers. We will respond quickly and without hesitation. But the goal is to end these tariffs as quickly as possible. As soon as we know the exact content of the presidential decrees concerning these unjustified tariffs, I will react to inform Quebecers of what happens next," Legault said in a post on X.
B.C. Premier David Eby said he spent Saturday morning with workers, business and Indigenous leaders to plan a response to America's tariff threat.
"America's plans to impose tariffs on Canada will hurt families on both sides of the border. It will make costs go up at a time when people are already struggling. It doesn't make sense, but if the Americans go down this road we will respond," Eby said in a post on X.
Saskatchewan Premier Scott Moe reacted by saying Canada's priority should be border security.
"Canada's largest trading partner has been clear in their priority. Saskatchewan has been equally clear about stopping the flow of fentanyl and illegal migrants," Moe said in a post on X, adding that "we have asked the federal government to commit to accelerating funding to meet our 2% NATO commitment. We further support Alberta's position in calling on the federal government to immediately appoint a Border Czar to ensure our borders are secure for Canadians and Americans. To protect the Canadian economy and avoid potential tariffs tomorrow, today is the day for the Government of Canada to act swiftly and appropriately."
Josh Rubin, Tonda MacCharles, Raisa Patel, and Victoria Gibson
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and U.S. President Donald Trump at a NATO summit in 2019.Ontario Premier Doug Ford, wearing a "Canada Is Not For Sale" hat, speaks as he arrives for a first ministers' meeting in Ottawa earlier this month.

People: Trump, Donald J; Trudeau, Justin

Company / organization: Name: North Atlantic Treaty Organization--NATO; NAICS: 928120

Document 372 of 500

Doug Ford hasn't needed a mandate to make major changes in Toronto. So why does he need one for Donald Trump?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-hasnt-needed-mandate-make-major-changes/docview/3162563204/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+hasn%27t+needed+a+mandate+to+make+major+changes+in+Toronto.+So+why+does+he+need+one+for+Donald+Trump%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The White House Situation Room must be dutifully preparing for the possibility of Premier Doug Ford receiving a strong mandate from Ontario voters to negotiate with U.S. President Donald Trump.
After all, Ford says he needs a mandate to negotiate with Trump, so it must be important. But as those of us in Toronto know, lacking one has never stopped him before.
As over the top and dramatic as Ford is being by framing the need for an election so bizarrely, the premier is offering a valuable way to look back on his nearly six years as premier and remind ourselves of all he's done in and around the GTA without a strong and specific "mandate."
Go right back to the first full month of his government in July 2018 when - out of the blue and with nary a mention of it on the recent campaign trail - Ford cut Toronto city council in half during a democratic municipal election. Unprecedented and shocking, the move upended Ontario's norms and conventions, not to mention the lives of hundreds of people involved in the election, many who put their jobs and family on hold in order to run for local government.
That's just the kind of big move a politician like Ford might want to go to the polls over, yet he didn't.
A more recent Toronto surprise was legislation to remove bike lanes, another major incursion into a traditional municipal jurisdiction. If removing civic infrastructure created through a long and sometimes excruciating process of local democracy - and paid for by local taxpayers - didn't need a specific mandate, why should negotiating with Trump?
Then there is the Greenbelt scandal, the subject of an ongoing RCMP criminal investigation. Ford even said he wouldn't touch the Greenbelt, but then he did. No special mandate needed there, it seems, despite possible illegalities and objections of many thousands of GTA residents.
Related to this was the move to gut the Conservation Authorities Act that gives organizations like the Toronto and Region Conservation Authority (TRCA) their power - done under the guise of removing barriers to development, particularly housing. The TRCA has been critical to biodiversity, flood mitigation and water safety in the GTA for decades, preventing costly environmental problems down the road. Yet, those drastic changes were proposed without an election.
MZOs, controversial "Ministerial Zoning Orders," have been used to override local zoning in order to build in specific places. In one instance, an MZO was mysteriously used in an attempt to demolish the historic Dominion Foundry on the downtown eastside, resulting in neighbours and other Torontonians launching a grassroots opposition movement. Another time an MZO was used to develop a Pickering wetland.
In December, Ontario's auditor general said the Ford government used MZOs 114 times between 2019 and 2023, a 17-fold increase in usage from the previous 20 years. So different from the norm that the auditor called for the process to be overhauled. These are all fairly dramatic moves by a government who says it needs a specific mandate just to talk about tariffs.
The Ford government also made major changes to Ontario's planning rules, like removing a municipality's ability to levy development charges to pay for public amenities. Like MZOs, they were presented as ways to build more housing, but Ontario still lags behind the rest of the country in homebuilding.
The closure and dismantling of Ontario Place and the Science Centre, privatizing what were treasured public buildings and space while costing taxpayers billions of dollars in the process, also happened without an explicit election mandate.
Ford also spent hundreds of millions of dollars to speed up alcohol expansion by breaking the contract with The Beer Store early. Such massive amounts of public funds should, based on Ford mandate logic, also require their own mandates to spend.
There's more, like the government's restrictions on overdose prevention sites, something advocates say will cost human lives. Again, an important thing that Ford had strong opinions on, but not strong enough to go to the polls over.
Ford's election reasoning is confusing, especially considering that in the face of an external threat like the one Trump currently presents, calling an unnecessary election actually creates internal instability. Even the notion of a government "mandate" is vague in Canada: a government is elected to lead or not based on a platform presented to voters.
A premier's basic job is to represent and negotiate on behalf of Ontario as issues come up. Ford has a strong majority so his power as leader is already unequivocal. Ford got the job, so just do it.
A recent poll suggests most Ontarians see this election call as self-serving for Ford, but it does provide a window into how he and his advisers think of the Ontario electorate: they assume voters will buy such thin, translucent reasoning.
If Doug Ford says he needs a strong mandate for Trump, we should reflect over the next few weeks on what he's done without one with the help of this timely election reminder.
CAPTION: Doug Ford didn't seem to think he needed a mandate to continue with the controversial Ontario Place redevelopment scheme. So why ask for one to take on Trump tariffs?

Title: Doug Ford hasn't needed a mandate to make major changes in Toronto. So why does he need one for Donald Trump?

Trump tariffs: Trudeau urges people to buy made in Canada; Loblaws CEO says chain is taking steps to minimize impact; Trump says 'pain' will be 'worth the price'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/trump-tariffs-trudeau-urges-people-buy-made/docview/3162563176/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trump+tariffs%3A+Trudeau+urges+people+to+buy+made+in+Canada%3B+Loblaws+CEO+says+chain+is+taking+steps+to+minimize+impact%3B+Trump+says+%27pain%27+will+be+%27worth+the+price%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The latest news as Donald Trump's administration imposes tariffs on Canada. Web links to longer stories if available. More from the Canadian Press. Follow the Star for live updates on Sunday.
2:45 p.m. The president of the United Steelworkers union is asking Trump to reverse the decision to impose tariffs on Canada.
David McCall says about US$1.3 trillion worth of goods cross the Canada-U.S. border annually, and the tariffs will threaten the stability of industries on both sides of the border.
He says the U.S. should work to reform the trade system through targeted tariffs on countries that violate U.S. trade laws and by working closely with allies, and not what he calls "sweeping actions that undermine crucial relationships."
2:23 p.m. Loblaws CEO says chain is taking steps to minimize impact of tariffs
Loblaws CEO Per Bank says the country's largest grocery chain is taking some steps to minimize the impact of U.S. tariffs on its customers. That includes make an extra effort to buy food grown and made in Canada, and looking for alternatives to U.S. imports from other countries, including Mexico "which has also been subjected to these unnecessary tariffs."
2 p.m. Ottawa Senators fans boo American national anthem following Trump's tariff announcement
Crowds at an Ottawa Senators game booed during the American national anthem hours after U.S. President Donald Trump announced 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian imports and 10 per cent on Canadian energy on Saturday.
Videos posted to X and elsewhere on social media showed fans jeering once Ottawa-born singer Mandia Nantsios began singing the opening lines of "The Star-Spangled Banner" before being greeted by enthusiastic cheering when she performed "O Canada."
Read the full story from the Star's Anastasia Blosser
1:30 p.m. From groceries to clothing, here are the U.S. items subject to Canada's counter-tariffs
Ready or not, Canada - these American-made items are about to get more expensive thanks to a trade war that was started by U.S. President Donald Trump.
Effective Tuesday, the Canadian government is imposing 25 per cent counter-tariffs on $30 billion in goods imported from the United States in response to Trump's executive order imposing a 25 per cent surcharge on Canadian goods, as well as a 10 per cent one on energy imports.
Some of the American products subject to the Canadian tariffs this week include grocery items such as meat and milk, as well as carpets and curtains.
Read the full story from Star staff
Trudeau urging people to buy made in Canada
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau is urging people to buy products made in Canada.
"Check the labels. Let's do our part. Wherever we can, choose Canada," he wrote on social media.
12:10 p.m. Donald Trump drives a wedge between Canada and the U.S. with a trade war. Could we join the EU?
Canada is on the other end of the Atlantic - but that still might not stop it from becoming the 28th member state of the European Union, some experts believe.
As U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs and talk of annexation continue to sour U.S.-Canada relations, some - including, reportedly, Germany's former foreign minister Sigmar Gabriel - have floated the idea of Canada joining the EU.
Supporters of the idea have cited the EU's need for minerals and other natural resources, of which Canada has plenty. The two also share similar views on governance, social issues and the climate crisis. "A rich new joiner would help the EU's finances," argued a well-circulated editorial from the Economist earlier this month.
Read the explainer from the Star's Kevin Jiang
11:30 a.m. Read Trudeau's full speech to Canadians - and Americans from Saturday
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau has announced 25 per cent retaliatory tariffs beginning as early as Tuesday in response to U.S. President Donald Trump's surcharges on Canadian goods.
Trudeau addressed Canadians - and Americans - in a press conference Saturday evening. Here are his full remarks in English (French remarks not included).
Read the full speech
11:11 a.m. (updated) Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre is urging the Liberal government to recall Parliament to put in place a "Canada first" plan to handle the economic repercussions from the tariffs.
The plan he's proposing includes retaliating "dollar for dollar" by targeting industries that will have a maximum impact on the United States while harming Canadian consumers as little as possible.
He's also proposing an emergency tax cut for Canadians, greenlighting job-creating projects including pipelines and mines, knocking down interprovincial trade barriers, rebuilding Canada's military and securing borders.
11:05 a.m. Here are some goods in the crosshairs of Trump's tariffs on Mexico, Canada and China
Canada is by far America's biggest foreign supplier of crude oil. From January through November last year, Canada shipped $90 billion (U.S.) worth of crude, well ahead of No. 2 Mexico at $11 billion (U.S.).
For many U.S. refineries, there's not much choice.
Canada produces the "type of crude oil that American refineries are geared to process,'' said Scott Lincicome, a trade analyst at the libertarian Cato Institute.
"It's a heavier crude. All the fracking and all the oil and gas we make here in the United States - or most of it - is a lighter crude that a lot of American refineries don't process, particularly in the Midwest.''
Read the full story from the Associated Press
10:22 a.m. (updated) Canada will not back down, finance minister says
Canada will scale up its retaliation to Donald Trump's tariffs as needed and is not backing down in spite of the U.S. president's threat to escalate his own penalties against Canada, Finance Minister Dominic LeBlanc said.
In an interview with the Star Sunday, LeBlanc said what was announced is only "the initial response of the Government of Canada."
"But if these (U.S.) tariffs are still around weeks and months from now, and if it's an ongoing economic turbulence, then we'll look at other additional measures, including non-tariff measures, but it's a proportionate response that will be scaled up, as we see where the Canadian economy is over the next number of weeks."
Read the full story from the Star's Tonda MacCharles
9:50 a.m. U.S. President Donald Trump posted on his Truth Social account Sunday morning, leaning harder into his suggestion that Canada become the 51st state.
"We pay hundreds of Billions of Dollars to SUBSIDIZE Canada. Why? There is no reason. We don't need anything they have. We have unlimited Energy, should make our own Cars, and have more Lumber than we can ever use," Trump said.
"Without this massive subsidy, Canada ceases to exist as a viable Country. Harsh but true! Therefore, Canada should become our Cherished 51st State. Much lower taxes, and far better military protection for the people of Canada - AND NO TARIFFS!."
In an earlier post on Sunday, Trump suggested that Canada and other countries have been "ripping off" the U.S. for years and that the pain of tariffs would be worth it.
"THIS WILL BE THE GOLDEN AGE OF AMERICA! WILL THERE BE SOME PAIN? YES, MAYBE (AND MAYBE NOT!). BUT WE WILL MAKE AMERICA GREAT AGAIN, AND IT WILL ALL BE WORTH THE PRICE THAT MUST BE PAID," Trump said.
9:35 a.m. Here's a look at key imports from the three counties that could become more costly
Trump's upcoming 25 per cent tariffs on goods from Canada and Mexico and 10 per cent duties on products from China are set to ricochet through households, raising prices for a wide range of items including vegetables, fruits and cars.
Read the full story from Bloomberg
9:25 a.m. U.S. businesses brace for Trump's tariffs on Canada, Mexico and China to drive up costs
From an ice cream parlor in California to a medical supply business in North Carolina to a T-shirt vendor outside Detroit, U.S. businesses are bracing to take a hit from the taxes President Donald Trump imposed Saturday on imports from Canada, Mexico and China - America's three biggest trading partners.
The levies of 25 per cent on Canadian and Mexican and 10 per cent on Chinese goods will take effect Tuesday. Canadian energy, including oil, natural gas and electricity, will be taxed at a lower 10 per cent rate.
Mexico's president immediately ordered retaliatory tariffs and Canada's prime minister said the country would put matching 25 per cent tariffs on up to $155 billion in U.S. imports.
Read the full story from the Associated Press
9:05 a.m. Will the housing crisis worsen in Ontario because of the trade war?
Ontario's residential construction council fears the fledgling trade war between Canada and the U.S. will only worsen the province's housing progress, at a time when builders have already been challenged by higher interest rates and increasing materials and labour costs.
"The move is reckless," president Richard Lyall wrote in a press release Sunday, suggesting the decision out of the White House "will cause economic hardship in both the U.S. and Canada, affecting tens of billions of dollars of trade in construction materials alone."
Lyall noted the homebuilding industry in Canada relies "heavily" on imported material from the U.S., as the U.S. relies on Canada for most of its imported lumber as well as other supplies like cement and gypsum used for drywall.
"Builders in Canada will likely look to alternative sources, such as domestic Canadian producers or suppliers from other countries, but the disruption - along with increased costs - could lead to delayed or canceled projects or slowdowns in new home construction as supply chains adjust," Lyall warned.
"Such a scenario could exacerbate existing housing shortages and drive-up prices." - Victoria Gibson
9 a.m. (update) Ontario removing U.S. products from shelves
The LCBO will be removing American products from its shelves and catalogue in response to U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs being imposed on Canadian goods Tuesday.
"Every year, LCBO sells nearly $1 billion worth of American wine, beer, spirits and seltzers," Progressive Party Leader Doug Ford wrote in an online statement. "Not anymore"
The removal will roll out Tuesday and will also prohibit Ontario-based restaurants and businesses from restocking and ordering American beer, wine and spirits since the LCBO is the only alcohol wholesaler in the province.
Read the full story from the Star's Anastasia Blosser
7:55 a.m. Minnesota Governor Tim Walz was vocal on X Saturday night, saying, "Trump's tariffs are a tax increase for Minnesota families, plain and simple."
"While Trump raises prices, I'm proposing the first sales tax rate cut in our state's history to help Minnesotans weather the storm," he said in post.
7:45 a.m. U.S. Senate minority leader Chuck Schumer has been vocal on X, posting about how the tariffs will raise the prices of goods in the country.
"You're watching the Super Bowl next week. Wait till Trump's tariffs raise your pizza prices," Schumer said in a post Saturday evening on X.
How will this affect Canada?
Sunday 7:30 a.m. Business associations on both sides of the border are sounding the alarm over the impact of U.S. President Donald Trump's incoming tariffs on the North American economy.
Trump is putting a 25-per-cent tariff on Canadian goods and a 10-per-cent tariff on Canadian energy starting Tuesday.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau says Canada will hit back with counter-tariffs on $30-billion worth of goods starting the same day.
Read the full story from the Canadian Press
11 p.m. If the only way to stop a bully is to punch them in the nose, who is balling up their fists and putting Donald Trump in their sights?
In the less than two weeks since he returned to the White House, Trump has fired federal prosecutors who targeted him in criminal cases, stripped critical former top officials with threats on their lives of security details, and ordered that the Guantanamo Bay military base-home since 2002 to detained enemy combatants-be turned into a migrant detention camp.
That's on top of his threats to take Greenland and the Panama Canal by force, make Canada the 51st American state, and slap tariffs on any country that doesn't submit to his will.
Read the full story from the Star's Allan Woods
What did Trudeau say?
11 p.m. Hours after Donald Trump slapped strict tariffs on Canadian imports, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau warned the U.S. that the better path is to partner with Canada, "not punish it."
"Canada has the ingredients necessary to build a booming and secure partnership for the North American economy, and we stand at-the-ready to work together," Trudeau said, speaking at a Saturday evening press conference in Ottawa. "Unfortunately, the actions taken today by the White House split us apart."
The comments came hours after Trump signed an executive order imposing a 25 per cent tariff on almost all Canadian goods and a 10 per cent tariff on energy, marking an era of economic uncertainty on both sides of the border. Both China and Mexico face steep tariffs as well.
Read the full story from the Star's Abby O'Brien
Sunday 11 p.m. Despite weeks of cross-border meetings, the creation of expert councils, and the swift implementation of a $1.3-billion Canadian border security plan, U.S. President Donald Trump made good on his punishing tariff threats on Saturday, with Canada following suit just hours later.
"We don't want to be here. We didn't ask for this. But we will not back down," Prime Minister Justin Trudeau told reporters on Parliament Hill late in the evening.
And with that, Canada's relationship with its largest trading partner entered a new - and decidedly uncertain - chapter.
Read the full story from the Star's Ottawa Bureau
Sunday 10:00 p.m.: Catherine Cobden, President and CEO of the Canadian Steel Producers Association (CSPA), called Trump's announcement about tariffs on Canadian products "deeply disappointing."
"The North American steel industry is highly integrated across the Canadian and U.S. border. With $20B in annual steel trade between our nations, these tariffs will cause significant disruption and economic hardship in both Canada and the United States. Disruptions that will have negative effects on businesses, workers and their families," Cobden said in a statement.

Lacking a mandate has never stopped Ford from meddling

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/lacking-mandate-has-never-stopped-ford-meddling/docview/3162497240/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Lacking+a+mandate+has+never+stopped+Ford+from+meddling&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The White House Situation Room must be dutifully preparing for the possibility of Premier Doug Ford receiving a strong mandate from Ontario voters to negotiate with U.S. President Donald Trump.
After all, Ford says he needs a mandate to negotiate with Trump, so it must be important. But as those of us in Toronto know, lacking one has never stopped him before.
As over the top and dramatic as Ford is being by framing the need for an election so bizarrely, the premier is offering a valuable way to look back on his nearly six years as premier and remind ourselves of all he's done in and around the GTA without a strong and specific "mandate."
Go right back to the first full month of his government in July 2018 when - out of the blue and with nary a mention of it on the recent campaign trail - Ford cut Toronto city council in half during a democratic municipal election. Unprecedented and shocking, the move upended Ontario's norms and conventions, not to mention the lives of hundreds of people involved in the election, many who put their jobs and family on hold in order to run for local government.
That's just the kind of big move a politician like Ford might want to go to the polls over, yet he didn't.
A more recent Toronto surprise was legislation to remove bike lanes, another major incursion into a traditional municipal jurisdiction. If removing civic infrastructure created through a long and sometimes excruciating process of local democracy - and paid for by local taxpayers - didn't need a specific mandate, why should negotiating with Trump?
Then there is the Greenbelt scandal, the subject of an ongoing RCMP criminal investigation. Ford even said he wouldn't touch the Greenbelt, but then he did. No special mandate needed there, it seems, despite possible illegalities and objections of many thousands of GTA residents.
Related to this was the move to gut the Conservation Authorities Act that gives organizations like the Toronto and Region Conservation Authority (TRCA) their power - done under the guise of removing barriers to development, particularly housing.
The TRCA has been critical to biodiversity, flood mitigation and water safety in the GTA for decades, preventing costly environmental problems down the road. Yet, those drastic changes were proposed without an election.
MZOs, controversial "Ministerial Zoning Orders," have been used to override local zoning in order to build in specific places. In one instance, an MZO was mysteriously used in an attempt to demolish the historic Dominion Foundry on the downtown eastside, resulting in neighbours and other Torontonians launching a grassroots opposition movement. Another time an MZO was used to develop a Pickering wetland.
In December, Ontario's auditor general said the Ford government used MZOs 114 times between 2019 and 2023, a 17-fold increase in usage from the previous 20 years. So different from the norm that the auditor called for the process to be overhauled. These are all fairly dramatic moves by a government that says it needs a specific mandate just to talk about tariffs.
The Ford government also made major changes to Ontario's planning rules, like removing a municipality's ability to levy development charges to pay for public amenities. Like MZOs, they were presented as ways to build more housing, but Ontario still lags behind the rest of the country in homebuilding.
The closure and dismantling of Ontario Place and the Science Centre, privatizing what were treasured public buildings and space while costing taxpayers billions of dollars in the process, also happened without an explicit election mandate.
Ford also spent hundreds of millions of dollars to speed up alcohol expansion by breaking the contract with The Beer Store early. Such massive amounts of public funds should, based on Ford mandate logic, also require their own mandates to spend.
There's more, like the government's restrictions on overdose prevention sites, something advocates say will cost human lives. Again, an important thing that Ford had strong opinions on, but not strong enough to go to the polls over.
Ford's election reasoning is confusing, especially considering that in the face of an external threat like the one Trump currently presents, calling an unnecessary election actually creates internal instability. Even the notion of a government "mandate" is vague in Canada: a government is elected to lead or not based on a platform presented to voters.
A premier's basic job is to represent and negotiate on behalf of Ontario as issues come up. Ford has a strong majority so his power as leader is already unequivocal. Ford got the job, so just do it.
A recent poll suggests most Ontarians see this election call as self-serving for Ford, but it does provide a window into how he and his advisers think of the Ontario electorate: they assume voters will buy such thin, translucent reasoning.
If Doug Ford says he needs a strong mandate for Trump, we should reflect over the next few weeks on what he's done without one with the help of this timely election reminder.

Document 375 of 500

No one is listening to the left anymore. This is why

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/no-one-is-listening-left-anymore-this-why/docview/3162497195/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=No+one+is+listening+to+the+left+anymore.+This+is+why&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Last November, about a week after I returned from covering the second election of Donald Trump, I went to a performance of the National Ballet of Canada, at the Four Seasons Centre in Toronto. I was having a difficult time that week. Like the rest of the world, I had spent much of the past nine years thinking about Trump - and in my case following him, watching him and writing about him, too. I had almost convinced myself, despite all the evidence, that I wouldn't have to do that, any of it, anymore.
There were three separate performances that night. For reasons that are more complicated than complimentary, I still can't get the first one - a solo by Guillaume Côté, a retiring principal dancer in the company - out of my head. It was such a strange, brief thing. Somewhere between a warm-up and a swan song, it lasted less than six minutes end to end. The audience had barely settled in before the lights were up again and everyone was being prodded to applaud.
It's not that "body of work" - which Côté first performed in 2014 - is a bad piece of dancing. It's fine for what it is. It just didn't seem like it was being put on that night for the benefit of anyone in the crowd. The dance was billed in the program less as a performance than a kickoff to Côté's retirement tour, and that's what it felt like: A valedictory, or an instruction. It was more an opportunity for the audience to applaud Côté than it was a chance for him to awe.
I've been thinking a lot about the idea of performance since the election. Trump has cemented himself now as one of the most consequential, and perhaps the single greatest, political performer in American history. The parts of the Kamala Harris campaign I saw in person, on the other hand, featured some of the strangest political performances I have ever seen. They seemed, again and again, like Côté's solo, to have been planned without an audience in mind. You already like us, they screamed, all we have to do is arrive.
In one particularly striking example, the night before the election, Harris put on a baffling and endless rally in Philadelphia. Long before she finally made it on stage, near midnight, thousands of her most ardent supporters were openly booing her warm-up acts.
I wrote at the time that one self-defeating rally probably didn't mean that much in the grander scheme of a presidential election. Two months on, I'm less and less sure that's the case. I tend to see that night now as a sign of a much larger malaise, not just in the Harris campaign, but in progressivism more broadly. Put simply, progressives don't seem to have any idea how to perform politics in our current, socially mangled age.
On the Saturday before Trump's inauguration, I spent several hours following a protest march through the streets of Washington, D.C. The event was supposed to be a repeat of the Women's March that took over the U.S. capital the day after Trump was first inaugurated. It is largely lost to time now, but that demonstration was one of the largest acts of protest of its kind in American history. It was a fierce statement of dissent and a genuine mass movement.
The event last Saturday, rebranded as the People's March, was none of that. Instead, it was small and impossibly splintered. As I walked along one branch, shuffling past the Washington Monument, it seemed like a different faction had control every 10 metres. There were different bullhorns amplifying different messages, all melting into a monotonous buzz.
The day after the march, I met the artist Isabelle Brourman at the Phillips Collection, a gallery northwest of the U.S. Capitol. Brourman spent much of 2024 drawing and painting Trump, first in court, in New York, then later on the campaign. She was in Washington to paint Trump's first dance at one of his presidential balls.
Brourman's work perfectly conveys the non-stop sensation dump of watching Trump live. It's never flattering. Her final pieces often look chaotic and menacing (much like Trump himself). But Trump's people had nonetheless given her extraordinary access to his rallies, and even to his home. When I asked her why she thought that was the case, Brourman told me about a meeting she had with Susie Wiles, who is now Trump's chief of staff, at Trump Tower during the campaign. Brourman was trying to negotiate access to Trump's events around the country, so she showed Wiles some of her work.
"I said, obviously this isn't traditional sketch art," Brourman told me. "And she said, 'We like different at Trump.' "
That same sense of difference, Brourman believes, was totally lacking from the Harris campaign. Brourman had tried throughout the summer and fall to paint Harris, but the vice-president's camp never let her get close. To Brourman, that was one indication of a more significant failing.
"Trump understands his crowd and engages with them and is fuelled by them," she said. "I think that her team was not engaged on the ground in the same way at all. And there's a lot of frustration about that from people that are surprised by the results, that she wasn't planning things, that she seemed disconnected. She seemed almost untouchable."
To walk around Washington the week of the inauguration was to be surrounded by the ecstatic followers of a coherent movement. You can oppose that movement, and I do. I think Trump's second coming will mean almost incalculable misery for migrants, Trans Americans and many others. But progressives are never going to beat that movement unless they get over the idea that all they need to do is show up and be themselves. "We are here and we're not him" is not enough anymore. They need to speak to people in a new way.
The second act of the ballet that night in Toronto was a solid but unremarkable performance of a late work by the English choreographer Frederick Ashton. It was the third and final piece that shook me. "Silent Screen," by Sol Leon and Paul Lightfoot undid my expectations of dance. The movement, the sets, the sounds all combined to create something visceral and explicitly new. I wasn't watching the performance so much as feeling it. That's why it broke through.
Trumpism won't die in four years just because Donald Trump can't run again. If progressives actually want to beat the movement Trump has built, they need to forge something new and visceral for themselves, something coherent and different that engages people where they are in 2025.
As I walked around the People's March that Saturday, chatting to participants and listening to chants, I was struck by two contradictory thoughts. On the one hand, I believe with deep certainty that the people who came out that miserable day are on the right side of history. They will be proud in 20 years to be able to say that they were there, that they stood up.
At the same time, as I looked down at the marchers from the steps of the Lincoln Memorial, I knew their form of protest - splintered, hectoring and uninterested in anything broad - would never be enough. Not against Trump.
(This is not just an American phenomenon. Canadian progressives seem, if anything, even more lost than their U.S. counterparts. Few acts have ever worn more thin than Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's increasingly glassy-eyed cheer. NDP leader Jagmeet Singh is still pumping out his delusionally bad TikTok's to no return. Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie showed up to her campaign launch last week wearing a hat that ripped off Premier Doug Ford ripping off Donald Trump. It made me wonder if she had advisers who are being paid to make her lose.)
Politics have changed. The old performances don't work anymore. Trump knows that, perhaps more than any politician alive. I wonder if there's a single progressive leader, in Canada or the U.S., who knows it too.
CAPTION: Donald Trump is pictured at an election night watch party on Nov. 6, 2024, in West Palm Beach, Fla.Supporters watch returns at a campaign election night watch party for Republican presidential nominee former President Donald Trump at the Palm Beach Convention Center on Nov. 6, 2024, in West Palm Beach, Fla.

Premiers call for retaliation, unity: Trump's tariffs are a 'declaration of economic war against a trusted ally,' B.C. leader Eby says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/premiers-call-retaliation-unity/docview/3162497055/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Premiers+call+for+retaliation%2C+unity%3A+Trump%27s+tariffs+are+a+%27declaration+of+economic+war+against+a+trusted+ally%2C%27+B.C.+leader+Eby+says&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Leaders across Canada are calling for retaliation and unity in response to U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs on Canadian imports.
In a statement issued Saturday evening, Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford said he was "extremely disappointed" by Trump's decision to "walk away from a trading relationship that, for decades, has made life better for millions."
"I wish we weren't here. I wish Ontario and Canada were working together with our American friends and allies to make our two countries the richest, most successful, safest, most secure on the planet," said Ford's statement, sent by a spokesperson to the Star. "Instead, President Trump has chosen to move forward with tariffs that will only hurt America and make Americans poorer."
Ford said the coming weeks could be "incredibly difficult" for Ontario, estimating the tariffs could put nearly 500,000 jobs across the province at risk and urging the federal government to come back with a "forceful" response that matches the U.S. tariffs "dollar for dollar."
Speaking on CNN not long after, Ford stressed that impacts would be felt on both sides of the border and maintained imposing reciprocating tariffs on Ontario imports, including electricity, oil and automotive products, was not off the table. Currently, Canada provides more than 60 per cent of America's total oil imports.
"This is going to hurt Americans, it's going to hurt Canada, we're going to see inflation happen down in the U.S. and Canada, and it's unjustified," Ford said.
Quebec Premier François Legault addressed the proposed tariffs Saturday, calling them "unjust."
"Mr. Trump spoke specifically about aluminum and steel, key sectors that sustain our regions with good-paying jobs. We will protect our economy and defend our workers. We will respond quickly and without hesitation. But the goal is to end these tariffs as quickly as possible. As soon as we know the exact content of the presidential decrees concerning these unjustified tariffs, I will react to inform Quebecers of what happens next," Legault said in a post on X.
B.C. Premier David Eby announced in a news release on Saturday his province's initial response to the American tariffs, calling the move an "unprecedented attack."
Eby said his first step is "directing the BC Liquor Distribution Branch to immediately stop buying American liquor from 'red states'," and having the top-selling "red-state" brands removed from shelves, while directing his government and Crown corporations "to buy Canadian goods and services first."
"President Trump's 25% tariffs are a complete betrayal of the historic bond between our countries and a declaration of economic war against a trusted ally," Eby said.
In January, B.C. released a preliminary assessment of the tariffs that showed the province could see a cumulative loss of $69 billion in economic activity between 2025-28, along with the loss of more than 120,000 jobs, according to the release.
The report also estimates a 25 per cent tariff on Canadian mineral exports alone would cost American companies more than $11 billion (U.S.).
Alberta Premier Danielle Smith said in a post to social media that she is disappointed by the looming tariffs on Canadian goods and claimed the reduced tariff for energy was in part a recognition of her government's advocacy.
Smith has pursued diplomacy in the face of tariff threats, including visits to Trump's Florida home and to Washington to defend cross-border energy trade.
Manitoba Premier Wab Kinew says in a social media post that the tariff "is an attack on Canada and who we are," before he referenced Trump's promise in his last presidency to build a wall between the U.S. and Mexico.
"Trump built a wall, but it's a tariff wall targeting us," said Kinew.
Saskatchewan Premier Scott Moe reacted by saying Canada's priority should be border security.
"Canada's largest trading partner has been clear in their priority. Saskatchewan has been equally clear about stopping the flow of fentanyl and illegal migrants," Moe said in a post on X, adding that "we have asked the federal government to commit to accelerating funding to meet our 2% NATO commitment. We further support Alberta's position in calling on the federal government to immediately appoint a Border Czar to ensure our borders are secure for Canadians and Americans. To protect the Canadian economy and avoid potential tariffs tomorrow, today is the day for the Government of Canada to act swiftly and appropriately."
New Brunswick Premier Susan Holt says Canada must stand strong and united against the threat of tariffs, and that her team will use every tool to put an end to them.
Conservative party Leader Pierre Poilievre on Saturday condemned the "massive, unjust and unjustified tariffs," before calling for the Liberal party to recall Parliament and pass a new bill to combat tariffs.
Poilievre's plan, that was detailed in a post on X, includes dollar-for-dollar tariffs that are "carefully aimed at maximizing impact on American companies while minimizing impact on Canadian consumers," and specifically retaliates against American steel and aluminum.
Poilievre also said the plan would involve giving all the tariff revenues to affected workers and businesses, passing a massive emergency tax cut, knocking down interprovincial trade barriers, rebuilding the military and green-lighting liquified natural gas plants, pipelines, mines, factories and port expansions to overseas markets.
With files from Josh Rubin,
Tonda MacCharles, Raisa Patel, Victoria Gibson
and The Canadian Press.

"I wish Ontario and Canada were working together with our American friends and allies to make our two countries the richest, most successful, safest, most secure on the planet."David Ryder GETTY IMAGES

Document 377 of 500

Gaming Nation's No. 1 fan talks Trump, tariffs and why Canada is 'amazing at making games'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/gaming-nations-no-1-fan-talks-trump-tariffs-why/docview/3162207383/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Gaming+Nation%27s+No.+1+fan+talks+Trump%2C+tariffs+and+why+Canada+is+%27amazing+at+making+games%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Paul Fogolin wants Canadians to take more pride in the country's outsized contributions to the global videogame industry.
According to a study commissioned by the Entertainment Software Association of Canada (ESAC) the industry employs more than 34,000 Canadians across 821 studios, 86 per cent of who are full time, and earn an average of six-figures. Together, they contribute $5.1 billion to the country's GDP.
"As Canadians, we tend to be pretty humble people, but there are some industries that we're proud of, like we're great at making cars, or on the resources side - even our entertainment talent, with Canadian actors and the musicians," says Fogolin, who took over as CEO of the ESAC in November. "This industry is just like that; we are amazing at making games, so let's be proud of it."
With a global market size of more than $221 billion (U.S.) in 2024, the gaming industry is bigger than the global film industry ($105 billion U.S.) and music industries ($48.6 billion U.S.) combined, with Canada one of its top contributors.
A lifelong gamer himself, Fogolin began his career at Queen's Park, followed by a series of roles with industry associations, before joining the gaming association in 2018.
"I stumbled on a posting for a job at ESAC and I thought it was too good to be true," he says from his home office in Oakville. "I said, 'wait a minute, I can apply my knowledge of policy and government relations to the video game industry?'"
After landing a job as the industry association's vice president of policy and government affairs Fogolin left last year to lead the Canadian Association of Recycling Industries before returning as CEO of ESAC months later.
After a pandemic boom, the digital gaming sector was forced to consolidate in the wake of recent global economic challenges. With that recovery in the rear-view mirror, the threat of tariffs on the horizon could have significant effects on a domestic industry that is 88 per cent reliant on global export revenue, with the U.S. as its biggest customer.
At the same time, 2025 promises to be an exciting year for gamers, with the release of new consoles like the Switch 2, flagship games like Grand Theft Auto (GTA) 6, the growth of esports and emerging gaming platforms featuring augmented and virtual reality.
The Star recently caught up with Fogolin to discuss the growth of gaming, the many misconceptions about its makers and players, and how Canada continues to be a global leader in what has become a highly competitive industry.
Have you always been a gamer?
From my earliest days. I vividly remember Christmas when I was five, and I got the original Nintendo Entertainment System, with Duck Hunt and Mario Brothers, and I fell in love with games right away.
Why study politics?
We had newspapers on the breakfast table every morning, so current events, news, politics were always being discussed in the house from a young age, and I just became fascinated with it.
Did you plan to spend your life in politics?
After I graduated, I volunteered for a local MP in Vaughan, and I think I made a good impression on some of the campaign folks, because they passed my resume to some friends at Queen's Park, and that's how I ended up working there. I thought it would just be short-term - I ended up there for eight years, working for premier Dalton McGuinty and a few ministers. It was an incredible experience, but to be honest, I didn't see myself as a civil servant.
After McGuinty was voted out of office I left Queen's Park and worked for a few trade associations, but I remained loving games. It's kind of funny that I didn't even know there was a trade association for the video game industry at the time.
What does the ESAC do?
We protect and promote the industry. The protect side is all about advocacy, ensuring a strong regulatory environment. The promote side is all about telling the story of the industry, putting out reports like the one we just commissioned about the industry's role in the economy.
What are Canada's contributions to this sector?
This industry remains a cornerstone of our digital economy. Canada is a powerhouse in making games.
The sector is maturing, because it's been in Canada for decades, so we have more senior talent, more stability, more leadership, more unique experience and it's coast-to-coast. Quebec is the heart of the industry, with over 15,000 jobs, but we've got around 11,000 in B.C., 6,000-plus in Ontario, and with advances in technology you can work for a game studio from just about anywhere.
Canada is home to both the largest game studio in the world by square footage - EA based in Burnaby, B.C. - and the largest studio by head count, Ubisoft in Montreal, Quebec. Over 80 per cent of the employment comes from the major companies, but there's also a thriving small business community; over 80 per cent of our studios have less than 25 employees.
How did Canada become such a gaming powerhouse?
The province of Quebec was first to introduce a tax credit for digital media in the '90s mimicking the film and television credit, which was instrumental in creating Hollywood North. They wanted to do the same for videogames. Now, pretty much every province has some form of the digital media tax credit that supports the industry.
We've developed a very skilled workforce, combined with lots of great academic institutions. We have great immigration programs for highly skilled talent, which is critical, and unlike the film industry - where you come for a few months to shoot and then go home - games take years to make, so many put down roots.
Beyond that the proximity to the U.S. makes a difference, and frankly our dollar makes it an attractive place to build a studio. And despite the weather, Canada is a great place to live, and all of that comes together to create an amazing ecosystem.
Has global competition gotten fiercer?
Canada used to be unique in offering tax incentives but now we have competition all over the world. Australia, Italy, Ireland, Singapore all have programs, because everybody wants what we have.
At the same time, we're seeing the market explode around the world, because games are no longer physical or just on consoles; you can play on your phone, your iPad, your PC, and most of the distribution is now digital, so you don't have to ship boxes across the planet. According to our study, 88 per cent of games are exported.
Does that mean there could be challenges if President Trump imposes Tariffs on Canadian goods?
Article 13 of the USMCA says you cannot place tariffs on digital goods, and that includes games, so that offers protection for our industry. That said, the USMCA is up for renegotiation in 2026, and the President has mused about doing it sooner, so we must stay vigilant.
We have great government relationships across all parties, we have a very strong partnership with the ESA in the United States and should anything happen we'll be at the table doing our part.
What are some of the biggest misconceptions about gamers?
One of the stats I love to share is that there's a 50/50 male to female split in gaming. Another one is the largest demographic of players is women over 50; there are more women over 50 playing games than boys under 18.
When they think about gaming a lot of people picture the teenager with the hoodie and the Mountain Dew and Doritos playing Halo in the basement, and we love that player, but gaming is also puzzles on your phone on the subway. Its families playing together, and we're starting to see a lot more of that. As the first generations of players are aging up, they're starting to play with their families.
Why do Canadians love gaming?
I think it's the best form of entertainment, but our research has shown that people are playing games to make new friends and build on existing friendships, as stress relief - they're reporting it helps with anxiety - and to build new skills, like problem solving. There was a recent study by neuroscientists at Western University that found people who play games perform as well on cognitive tests as those 13.7 years younger.
What can gamers expect from the industry in the future?
With games like GTA 6 on the way, such a monolith has a spillover effect because it brings a bigger audience into gaming, so I'm super excited about that. The Switch 2 was just announced, and with the last generation of Xbox and PlayStation consoles, when they came out at the start of the pandemic, a lot of games were already in development. We're only starting to see the results of a development cycle that utilizes all the technology that's available, and I think you'll see a lot of exciting things coming out this year.
In the future I think virtual reality will expand, but I don't think it will become a dominant mode; it's just not for everybody. What I'm more excited about is the potential for augmented reality. The biggest hit there was Pokémon Go, and I think there's a lot of potential in that space.
What I'm most excited about are the surprises. When the original Wii came out, suddenly you had these controllers you could wave around, and now your aunt was bowling at Thanksgiving, and it changed things. Who knows what the next iteration of that is?
CAPTION: "I thought it was too good to be true," says Paul Fogolin when he came across a job posting at the Entertainment Software Association of Canada. He's now the gaming advocacy group's CEO.

Subject: Canadians; Computer & video games; Associations; Tax credits; Politics; Play; Pandemics; Motion picture industry; Tariffs
