Document 378 of 500

Why Doug Ford's early election call could give voters in Toronto something to cheer about

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/why-doug-fords-early-election-call-could-give/docview/3162207381/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+Doug+Ford%27s+early+election+call+could+give+voters+in+Toronto+something+to+cheer+about&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When Premier Doug Ford pulled the trigger on a Feb. 27 election, hauling Ontarians out in the cold to vote more than a year earlier than necessary, it was possible (if you squinted hard) to see it as providing his opponents an opportunity.
From the point of view of many Torontonians - the ones who elected Mayor Olivia Chow when Ford campaigned against her - this is a chance to change a government they see as damaging, and even to prevent or reverse some of the damage. Safe consumption drug sites in Toronto ordered closed by Ford's government are still operating until after election day; bike lanes the province plans to rip out are still there on the street today; the controversial (and potentially Charter of Rights defying) encampment-clearing law has not yet been passed. Could all of that be reversed by a new government? Maybe.
And while it may be too late to save the trees on the West Island of Ontario Place, or prevent the closure of the Ontario Science Centre, certainly a new government could take a fresh look at what's going on there. Perhaps new oversight could get Metrolinx construction projects in gear. Perhaps Toronto city hall could be spared more of the endless, impulsive meddling that has seen the government and its finances reshaped on the premier's whims. Perhaps we could avoid any further ridiculous talk of tunnelling under the 401.
None of that was possible for opponents of Ford to hope for a week ago, because Ford did not have to face an election until well into 2026. Today, it is possible.
Which doesn't make it likely.
On Wednesday when the election call formally came, there was a conspicuous absence of audible cheering from Toronto city hall and from the opposition benches at Queen's Park. There's a reason, after all, that Ford called the election now - ahead of a federal election that could shift voter alignments, ahead of any Greenbelt investigation findings, with Ford's party ahead in polls by 20 per cent or more just as $200 tax rebate cheques are hitting people's mailboxes and Ford is putting on a widely applauded performance as Captain Canada in this sequel edition of the Trump Follies. A lot of the things I cited above as reasons some Torontonians want to get rid of him (bike lanes, encampment clearing) are reasons that people in other parts of Toronto and across Ontario like him.
Partly because of that, it remains to be seen, even from Toronto city hall's vantage point, how the parties vying to replace Ford position themselves on these issues, and on how they'll approach the beneficial things Ford is delivering to the city, from transit funding to the "new deal for Toronto" talks still underway.
If there's a chance here of changing the government and reversing course, there still also seems to be a likelihood of locking both in for another four years.
Still, that doesn't mean it's all without risk for Ford. The cautionary tale of Premier David Peterson's early and ultimately unsuccessful early election call in the 1980s is already being widely cited. There's a chance voters who generally approve of Ford's performance ask why they're being called out into the cold so early - especially when the party of "gettin' it done" has clearly not finished the job. Building transit, building housing and fixing health care may be the three highest priorities Ford set for himself, and they are three areas where a lot of talk (and lots of dollars) have led to little in the way of measurable results.
To which Ford's response is that the threat of Donald Trump requires a new mandate. But that carries risks for Ford too. Ford's trips to Washington during the campaign could make him look diminished if he's ignored by American lawmakers and media. Trump could change his mind about tariffs in an instant, making the need for a new mandate redundant, or could make new and unexpected threats that make Ford's approach seem suddenly like a tactical error. Trump is an agent of chaos. Many American politicians have learned that when you hop a ride on the back of this wild tiger, it's not a safe bet you'll reach your desired destination.
Which is all to say, a lot can happen during a month-long campaign. Besides, Ford's opponents in the legislature and in the streets don't have a choice in this timing. The decision was Ford's, and he's made it.
And in doing so, he opens up a possibility for those who are unhappy with his government, including those in Toronto who think his obsession with this city has led us to a bad place. Ford has put the choice in the hands of voters; they could elect to change course.
It may seem unlikely. But the job now for those who want change is to argue, work and vote to make it more likely. It may not be the opportunity they were looking for. But it's the only one they're likely to get for a long time.
CAPTION: By calling an election, Doug Ford has put the choice in the hands of the voters, who could decide to change course.

Subject: Political campaigns; Voters

Document 379 of 500

Trump's trade war will be difficult, but Canadians have no choice but to stand firm

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/trumps-trade-war-will-be-difficult-canadians-have/docview/3162207317/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trump%27s+trade+war+will+be+difficult%2C+but+Canadians+have+no+choice+but+to+stand+firm&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With the election of Donald Trump, Canada finds itself in an unprovoked trade war with the United States, its erstwhile closest friend and ally.
Trump's 25 per cent tariffs on exports to the U.S. threatens significant economic damage, higher costs for consumers and producers, supply-chain disruption, and the loss of investment and jobs, especially in continentally integrated industries such as the auto sector. Canadian promises to retaliate with tariffs on US goods will escalate the war, causing further trade disruption and economic uncertainty.
So, what now? What should we do, and what can we expect?
Canadians need to keep calm, stick together, respond in kind, and remind Americans that hitting Canada also hurts American consumers and producers, and threatens the whole North American economy.
This is not Canada's first trade war with the U.S. In fact, Canada-U.S. relations have been dominated by the question of our economic relationship with and access to the American market. Tariff and trade battles, from softwood lumber to oil to automobiles, have been more common than not.
In the 1960s, a trade war over cars resulted in President Lyndon Johnson's willingness to sign the 1965 Canada-US auto pact, which led to the mutually beneficial integration of the Canada-U.S. auto industry. In the 1970s, Richard Nixon imposed a 10 per cent tariff on all goods, but Canadians managed to gain an exemption to this tax, given how damaging it would be to the Canadian (and American) economies.
In the 1980s, continuing trade wars and threats of U.S. protectionism led Prime Minister Brian Mulroney to negotiate the Canada-U. S. Free Trade Agreement with Ronald Reagan. In the 1990s, fear of a U.S.-Mexico agreement prompted Canada to push its way into NAFTA.
When it comes to Trump, Canadians have also been here before. In 2017, Trump made similar threats, eventually imposing targeted tariffs on steel and aluminum. Canada responded by targeting Kentucky bourbon. Ultimately, this skirmish led to NAFTA's renegotiation, creating the 2020 USMCA, which updated the continental trade agreement and made it more U.S.-friendly. Despite his statements otherwise, making USMCA tilt further towards the U.S. might be Trump's objective again.
Trump's tariff threat is particularly ominous for the Canadian auto sector. Integrated into the U.S, industry since the 1960s, Canada's auto sector and the entire industry is undergoing a costly, necessary, and difficult transition to electrification. Because cars and parts travel across the border multiple times, 25 per cent tariffs would not only make cars far more expensive, but could also jeopardize billions in Canadian new plant investments as manufacturers contemplate shifting production southward to avoid these penalties.
For manufacturers such as GM, Ford, Toyota, Honda, and VW, producing cars and parts in Canada has its benefits and because of the integrated nature of the sector, more than 80 per cent of Canadian production is shipped southward. While a sustained trade war could prove fatal to the Canadian sector, tariff disruption and investment uncertainty would also be tremendously costly for U.S. firms and consumers, and for states like Michigan with close trade ties to Canada.
It would rekindle inflation and tip the North American economy into recession. During the last Trump administration, this risk helped to convince Trump to stand down on tariffs once he'd "ended" NAFTA, one of his long-standing policy goals.
This time, Trump is using trade to demand Canadians take action on illegal migration and the drug trade, matters unrelated to trade considerations and far more salient on the U.S.-Mexico border. Unquestionably, Trump's disruptive approach will likely cause economic pain for Canada. Nonetheless, Canadians have no choice but to stand firm in the face of these bullying tactics.
If the past provides any lessons, Canadians should remember that they have a deep experience in dealing with our complicated and often difficult trade relationship with the U.S. While Trump represents the most volatile and challenging situation Canadians have faced in some time, Americans need to be reminded that trade wars with their closest economic and political ally will certainly hurt us, but will hurt them, too.
CAPTION: U.S. President Donald Trump hold up an executive order, "Unleashing prosperity through deregulation," that he signed in the Oval Office on Friday in Washington, D.C. Trump spoke to reporters about tariffs against China, Canada and Mexico and how the newly confirmed Interior Secretary Doug Burghum will coordinate with the Energy Department and the Environmental Protection Agency.
CREDIT: Dimitry Anastakis Contributor

Subject: Trade disputes; Canadians; North American Free Trade Agreement; Trade relations; Tariffs

Document 380 of 500

Snow boots, drill bits and rebar: How candidates are tackling Ontario's first winter election in decades

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/snow-boots-drill-bits-rebar-how-candidates-are/docview/3162112222/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Snow+boots%2C+drill+bits+and+rebar%3A+How+candidates+are+tackling+Ontario%27s+first+winter+election+in+decades&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Up in Sudbury, it's -16 C and incumbent MPP France Gélinas layers up with three sets of long johns and handmade wool socks before she even gets to putting on her outfit for the day - one that will be hidden beneath her parka and thermal boots as she trudges through the snow campaigning.
While further south, in Toronto, the temperature hasn't gone that low - but still, around -5 C some days with bitter winds making it feel much colder - candidates, too, are bundled up in big winter coats, toques and mitts with hot hand warmers tucked inside, working in two-hour shifts, sometimes ducking in to canvass high-rise buildings to get a break.
"It's cold - it's really cold, to be honest," says incumbent Progressive Conservative MPP and cabinet minister Stan Cho, who is running for re-election in Willowdale, where the tall buildings create a wicked wind tunnel that "slaps you in the face pretty hard."
Cho's mom cooks daily for all his volunteers so when they return to his Yonge Street campaign office for a respite, there are fresh sandwiches, snacks, and hot coffee and tea.
"We're saying, if you're tired, come back," said Cho. "Warm up, have a break ... know your limits and wear your layers, and bring those hand warmers."
Ontarians, who head to the polls Feb. 27, haven't experienced a winter provincial election since 1981. While opposition parties have called the snap call unnecessary and opportunistic, Ontario PC leader Doug Ford said he needs a strong mandate to counter U.S. President Donald Trump and the threat of tariffs.
But campaigning in the cold is a first for candidates, and because teams are used to much milder weather, they're facing some new challenges - and among the biggest is how to get signs in the frozen ground.
"Bane of my existence," joked one campaign manager.
Sign crews are using drills to break ground, using rebar instead of the usual wooden stakes - but the process takes much longer than they are used to.
Local businesses Lakefront Graphix Technology and Metal Supermarkets (Etobicoke) have been inundated with orders from hundreds of campaigns.
"We are working 16 hours a day at the moment," said Lisa Herbst of Graphix, who recalls a winter 2006 federal election where they sent out drill bits with signs.
Up in the Sudbury riding of Nickel Belt, Gélinas - a veteran New Democrat first elected in 2007 - says that's not an option, so they instead prop up signs in the snow.
"You can put a sign in a snow bank," said Gélinas, who got her first taste of winter campaigning as a volunteer during the 2006 federal election.
"We can't all carry shovels, so we pack the snow to put the sign in," she added. But if it snows again, she has teams who fan out over the sprawling riding because "you have to redo all your signs because the snow plow goes by, and either takes them away or buries them."
Cho's volunteers "go out with a drill, but they also go with a pretty big hammer," he said. "So it's kind of like a MacGyver solution. Most of the team doesn't know who MacGyver is, but they're doing their best and calling audibles whenever necessary."
Sean Conway was a Liberal MPP and cabinet minister who ran in the 1981 provincial election, and doesn't recall it being as cold as this winter.
"The campaign was launched about the second or third of February, and I remember thinking, 'oh God, this is going to be a winter campaign and the weather will be ... well, guess what the weather was for candidates? The only big problem we had with that campaign was fog" in his Pembroke-area riding.
"It was an unusually mild February and March, and I just remember waiting for the really cold weather to come, and it never did," said Conway, who lives in Barry's Bay. "I was able to do a lot more canvassing than I thought I was going to be able to do with my various teams."
This election, going door-to-door in frigid weather is teaching valuable campaign lessons, Gélinas said. "The first thing? If you want people to vote for you, don't bring a whole bunch of dirt and snow with your boots" into their front foyer.
"People don't like to clean up after a candidate who makes a big mess," she said. "And thing number two is that talking to them at the door has to be extremely brief, because keeping your door open when it's -16 C with the wind is not a good way to make a friend either."
For Cho, the Korean saying "gosaeng" is fitting this election, and some voters have uttered the term to him at the door. It means suffering, he explained, "but in Korean, it's not an insult, it's actually a compliment - you're suffering for something you believe in, a cause. That's my mentality."
Vince Gasparro, the Liberal candidate in Eglinton-Lawrence, said voters seem to appreciate candidates hustling.
He's been out with his team since being nominated last October, noting "this weather now, it's a little more intense."
But, he added, "it's been interesting - the reception at the door, people are impressed that you have a political candidate who's out there trying to earn their support when it's freezing cold and snowing."
CAPTION: Progressive Conservative MPPStan Cho uses a hammer and drill to get his signs into the ground with volunteer Tyler Zheng in the riding of Willowdale.

Subject: Cold; Provincial elections; Signs; Teams; Candidates; Voters; Volunteers; Elections; Snow

Quiz answers

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/quiz-answers/docview/3162112116/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Quiz+answers&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=OP.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: 1. d) A, B and C were variations on a theme from the NDP's Marit Stiles, the Greens' Mike Schreiner, and the Liberals' Bonnie Crombie. The final statement was Ford's response when asked if remembered what happened to David Peterson's Liberals when they called a snap election in 1990. (Peterson lost.)
2. c) In the 1955 Ontario general election, Leslie Frost's Conservatives won 84 of the legislature's 98 seats, or more than 85 per cent of the chamber. To beat that today, Ford will need 106 of the chamber's 124 seats, or 23 more than the Ontario Progressive Conservatives won in 2022. So if he falls short of that, we can officially say this election was a flop.
3. b) Political scientist Frank Underhill noted this quirk of Canadian politics in 1946, and since then it's held up 88 per cent of the time. This pales in comparison to Cole's Law, which states that shredded cabbage and mayonnaise can be combined into a side dish and is true 100 per cent of the time.
4. a) The Liberals aimed to adopt a more transactional tone to rival Ford's focus on pocketbook issues. As TVO's Steve Paikin wrote disapprovingly when the new tag line was unveiled, "the new slogan doesn't make an appeal to what we can achieve together or what we owe one another. It's about the individual." And since said individuals will be getting $200 cheques from the government next month, exactly how much more will the Liberals be sending us?
5. a) Crombie told the Star she was surprised at how difficult it was to lead the Liberal Party of Ontario, given that she garnered 78 per cent of the vote as mayor of Mississauga - though to be fair, she also said, "There's a lot of work to do, and I'm up for it."
6. a) This particular investigation is sensitive if not international. The critical question being asked of all parties, according to Star reporting from last November, is "Where did the direction come from to remove those lands from the Greenbelt?" The RCMP is looking directly at the premier's office, though Ford will likely be starting his third term by the time the investigation concludes.
7. d) Stiles ran unopposed for the NDP leadership, which instantly made her the Leader of His Majesty's Official Opposition in the legislature. The Davenport MPP put a positive spin on the acclamation, saying that it "shows that we are a united party; we're not interested in being divided."
8. c) Schreiner has represented the riding of Guelph since 2018, and this time the Greens are focusing their efforts on some of the province's most hyphenated electoral districts: Parry Sound-Muskoka, Bruce-Grey-Owen Sound and Wellington-Halton Hills.
How plausible is your knowledge of Ontario politics?
0-3: As plausible as a 401 tunnel
The current government has used every tool at their disposal to get your attention, and it hasn't worked. So congratulations! There's a certain quiet dignity to staying above the fray.
4-6: As plausible as the Eglinton LRT opening this year
You're unsure how ripping out bike lanes will fight Trump's tariffs, but you're sadly aware of both issues. Buckle in for a February of being continually addressed as "folks."
7-8: As plausible as a barren Ontario Place
As our unofficial provincial anthem says, our hopes are as high as the tallest tree. It's not referring to the trees at Ontario Place, as Ford's government has chopped them all down.

Document 382 of 500

Where does Donald Trump's assault on the climate leave Canada?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/where-does-donald-trumps-assault-on-climate-leave/docview/3162112093/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Where+does+Donald+Trump%27s+assault+on+the+climate+leave+Canada%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: For Canadian clean-energy advocates, the sight of U.S. President Donald Trump rescinding many of his predecessor's clean-energy initiatives on his first day in office on Jan. 20 was discouraging, but not alarming.
The global clean-energy transition has gained too much momentum for anyone to stop it. And its progress is especially evident in Canada.
The past 12 months have seen new clean-energy projects announced or going onstream in almost every province.
The projects include the first electricity generated by the $16 billion Site C hydro-electric dam on B.C.'s Peace River and completion in November of the refurbishment of a reactor at Ontario's Darlington nuclear station, extending its life by 30 years.
In a global survey of corporate CEOs released in January, consulting firm PricewaterhouseCoopers (PwC) found that 72 per cent of Canadian CEOs said they had made climate-related investments in their businesses in the past 12 months.
And in the U.S., about 90 per cent of increased capacity at American power utilities in the first half of 2024 came from renewable sources, including solar (59 per cent of the total) and wind (12 per cent).
Solar and wind installations are cost competitive with fossil fuels and often cheaper as the technology improves. And so, global investment in clean energy amounted to about $2.9 trillion last year, almost twice the investment in fossil fuels.
"This limits the degree to which the (Trump) administration can mount a more significant role for fossil fuels in the domestic energy system," says economist Francis Fong of TD Economics.
Severe weather events caused or made worse by climate change have become commonplace.
Those events have "permanently moved climate change out of an 'environmental policy' pigeonhole into a far more potent public policy space occupied by such issues as health care and crime," says Rick Smith, president of the Canadian Climate Institute.
Global sales of electric vehicles (EVs) jumped by 24 per cent last year, despite slowing EV sales growth in North America. And global sales of electric heat pumps are forecast to increase by more than 60 per cent from the current level to $205 billion by 2028.
Canada's clean-tech sector, which makes everything from EV charging equipment to LED lighting for greenhouses, exported $7.6 billion worth of goods to the U.S. in 2022.
The sector is bracing for the tariffs on Canadian and Mexican goods that Trump has said he will impose on Feb. 1.
Economists expect tariffs to be lifted once Canada and the U.S. negotiate an end to their differences, though that can take a year judging from the tariffs Trump imposed in his first presidency.
Another sustaining factor for clean energy is that governments have such a large influence in the transition, though private-sector utilities and tech firms also play major roles.
Somewhat insulated from the vagaries of the private sector, governments can stay the course on big-ticket energy projects that can take decades to plan and build.
And decisions on projects are shifting from national capitals to subgovernments, which increases the number of project sponsors and creates diversity in decision-making.
Provincial and state governments in North America are closer to the impact of severe climate events from which they are expected to protect their citizens.
Jurisdictions like Ontario, Quebec and B.C. are bigger players in planning future energy supplies than Ottawa.
And in the U.S., the more than 20 member states of the U.S. Climate Alliance are determined to continue promoting clean energy and have the Constitutional authority to do so.
Ontario is forecast to spend about $400 billion upgrading its power capacity by 2050 to reinforce its status as one of the world's biggest clean-power producers.
Expansions and overhauls are underway at the province's Bruce Power, Darlington and Pickering nuclear plants, and this month Ontario Premier Doug Ford announced plans for a new nuclear plant near Darlington.
Ontario is developing small modular reactor (SMR) technology that it hopes to export. And it seeks to double its energy production from wind, solar and other clean-energy sources.
Ford's response to the Trump tariff threat includes a proposal to more thoroughly integrate the energy systems of Canada and the U.S.
Ford says the project, which also calls for integrated critical mineral production, would help the U.S. "decouple" from China.
The idea might seem fanciful. It would eclipse the size of a St. Lawrence Seaway jointly built by Canada and the U.S.
Then again, "The U.S. president has declared an 'energy emergency' to build new critical infrastructure," wrote Yudullah Hussain of RBC Economics in a client note last week.
"Could some of it be built in Canada given the integrated nature of the two country's energy sectors?"
Ford's binational strategy might be worth pursuing just for its potential tech advances and cost efficiencies. And it would be ready for a Trump successor to embrace if Trump himself doesn't see its merit.
CAPTION: An aerial view of the Bruce Power nuclear generating station. Expansions and overhauls are underway at the province's Bruce Power, Darlington and Pickering nuclear plants, and this month Ontario Premier Doug Ford announced plans for a new nuclear plant near Darlington.

Document 383 of 500

Tariffs will be applied in stages, Trump suggests: No concessions will be made, president insists, as confusion surrounds rollout

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tariffs-will-be-applied-stages-trump-suggests/docview/3162112084/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tariffs+will+be+applied+in+stages%2C+Trump+suggests%3A+No+concessions+will+be+made%2C+president+insists%2C+as+confusion+surrounds+rollout&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's a brief reprieve. But for Donald Trump's doubters, or Canadians living in hope who thought he wouldn't act on his tariff threat, the White House says forget that.
The U.S. president said he will fulfil his threat to impose 25 per cent surcharges on Canadian and Mexican imports starting Saturday, in order to halt fentanyl and illegal immigrants at America's northern and southern borders, and to correct what he calls unfair trade deficits.
However, Trump suggested there would be a staggered rollout, telling reporters he will target Canadian oil and gas shipments with a lower 10 per cent tariff rate, setting a later date of Feb. 18 for implementation.
Trump said he will "eventually" aim sectoral tariffs at imports of semiconductor chips, "next month" steel and aluminum products, "ultimately copper," and pharmaceuticals at some unspecified date.
And he said there is nothing Canada or Mexico could do to avoid tariffs. "We're not looking for a concession," Trump said, insisting that tariffs are a way for him to raise "big money" in revenue.
The president warned he will "absolutely" impose tariffs on the European Union soon as well.
On Friday evening, three Trudeau cabinet ministers at a news conference in Washington said the federal government remains in the dark about exactly what tariffs Trump will levy first. They declined to interpret his remarks, saying the Canadian government would respond only to formal written decisions issued by Trump, not to the rhetoric.
"I'm not in the head space of President Trump," said Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly.
She said efforts to persuade two key Trump officials - border czar Tom Homan and Secretary of State Marco Rubio - that Canada has a strong $1.3-billion border security plan, and that Canada is a source of less than one per cent of illegal immigration and fentanyl into the U.S., were met with a "positive response."
But Immigration Minister Marc Miller conceded that there are "no guarantees" Trump would change his mind as of Friday night.
Earlier on Friday, Trump rejected one of Canada's biggest arguments: that tariffs will trigger everyday prices to rise.
"Tariffs don't cause inflation. They cause success. They cause big success. So we're gonna have great success. There could be some temporary, short-term disruption," he acknowledged, "and people will understand that." But he said in the long run, it would force other countries, such as those in Europe, to drop barriers to American imports like farm products.
Earlier Friday, White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt had thrown cold water on a Reuters report that suggested Trump would delay the application of tariffs against Canada and Mexico until March 1, calling the story "false."
"These are promises made and promises kept by the president," said Leavitt, repeating that Trump's objective is to halt "the illegal fentanyl that they have sourced and allowed to distribute into our country."
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, in remarks Friday morning before meeting with an advisory council on Canada-U.S. relations, said he would not "sugar-coat" the impact that tariffs could be expected to have on Canadian exports and the overall economy.
Some economists say Trump's threatened 25 per cent "across-the -board" tariffs would put Canada into a recession, with estimates that U.S. tariffs could cause up to a three per cent drop in economic activity here.
"Our nation could be facing difficult times in the coming weeks," said Trudeau.
The Canadian government will retaliate with countermeasures against any U.S. tariffs imposed by Trump, Trudeau said, and would support Canadian workers and industries who may be hard hit, especially if tariffs are sustained.
"If the president does choose to implement any tariffs against Canada, we're ready with a response. A purposeful, forceful but reasonable, immediate response," he said. "It's not what we want, but if he moves forward, we will also act."
Canada has planned an escalating series of measures that could potentially ramp up to impose counter-tariffs on $150 billion worth of American imports, and could include limits or export tariffs on Canadian energy shipments to the U.S.
The federal government says Canada provides more of the crude oil the U.S. imports than the rest of the world combined, a trading relationship that it says allows for "reliable supply and lower pump prices for Americans."
Trump and his threats have shaped a lot of the political rhetoric in a Liberal leadership race to replace Trudeau on March 9.
Earlier Friday, the two front-runners Mark Carney and Chrystia Freeland repeated their arguments, with Carney saying he stands "four-square" behind efforts by Trudeau's cabinet team in Washington trying prevent the tariffs.
"President Trump thinks Canada will cave in. We will never, ever bow down to a bully," said Carney, who has backed dollar-for-dollar countermeasures.
Freeland, on the other hand, took a tougher line. She, too, backs a dollar-for-dollar response, but one that is more forceful and immediate. On Friday, she said Trudeau should target up to $200 billion in American imports and take direct aim at Trump's adviser Elon Musk, owner of Tesla.
"Canada must threaten to impose a 100 per cent tariff on all Tesla vehicles and a 100 per cent tariff on U.S. wine, beer and spirits if unfair tariffs are imposed on Canadians tomorrow," Freeland said in a release.
Trudeau has not tipped his government's hand on what American products would be hit with countermeasures, but federal officials have confirmed the first tranche would be targeted at politically sensitive American products, and actions would ramp up over time.
Trudeau said an advisory group of former premiers, business and union leaders are helping to make Ottawa's argument in the U.S. that Trump's tariffs against Canada "would have disastrous consequences for the United States, putting American jobs at risk, raising prices on Americans and undermining our collective security."
Trudeau's remarks prompted questions during a White House briefing, with reporters asking Leavitt if the president could guarantee that tariffs won't stoke inflation, or increase prices at gas pumps and in grocery stores for Americans. One journalist asked if Canada's vow to retaliate showed the U.S. is on the brink of a trade war.
Leavitt bristled and said, "I don't think so."
Trump's purpose, she said, is to halt fentanyl and migrants from "pouring into" America, adding "the president is intent on doing this, and I think Justin Trudeau would be wise to talk to President Trump directly before pushing outlandish comments like that to the media."
Reuters had also reported that the Trump administration would provide a process for the countries to seek exemptions for certain imports. Leavitt did not directly answer when asked if the tariffs would be in place for the long term or whether Trump would remove them if Canada, Mexico or China offered concessions.
"If the president at any time decides to roll back those tariffs, I'll leave it to him to make that decision, but starting tomorrow, those tariffs will be in place," she said.
In Niagara Falls, Ontario Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford fully backed Trudeau's retaliation plan.
"Dollar for dollar, tariff for tariff, the federal government has my full support to hit back and hit back hard," Ford said as he campaigned for a third term as premier in a Feb. 27 vote.
"If President Trump proceeds with these tariffs, Canada can and should make him regret them," Ford told reporters, adding he is prepared to take "enormous action" to protect Ontario workers, businesses and families.
With files from Rob Ferguson
CREDIT: Tonda MacCharles Toronto Star With files from Rob Ferguson

Document 384 of 500

Clean-energy industry won't be stopped

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/clean-energy-industry-wont-be-stopped/docview/3162111899/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Clean-energy+industry+won%27t+be+stopped&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: For Canadian clean-energy advocates, the sight of U.S. President Donald Trump rescinding many of his predecessor's clean-energy initiatives on his first day in office on Jan. 20 was discouraging, but not alarming.
The global clean-energy transition has gained too much momentum for anyone to stop it. And its progress is especially evident in Canada.
The past 12 months have seen new clean-energy projects announced or going on-stream in almost every province.
The projects include the first electricity generated by the $16-billion Site C hydroelectric dam on B.C.'s Peace River and completion in November of the refurbishment of a reactor at Ontario's Darlington nuclear station, extending its life by 30 years.
In a global survey of corporate CEOs released in January, consulting firm PricewaterhouseCoopers (PwC) found that 72 per cent of Canadian CEOs said they had made climate-related investments in their businesses in the past 12 months.
And in the U.S., about 90 per cent of increased capacity at American power utilities in the first half of 2024 came from renewable sources, including solar (59 per cent of the total) and wind (12 per cent).
Solar and wind installations are cost competitive with fossil fuels and often cheaper as the technology improves. And so, global investment in clean energy amounted to about $2.9 trillion last year, almost twice the investment in fossil fuels.
"This limits the degree to which the (Trump) administration can mount a more significant role for fossil fuels in the domestic energy system," says economist Francis Fong of TD Economics.
Severe weather events caused or made worse by climate change have become commonplace.
Those events have "permanently moved climate change out of an 'environmental policy' pigeonhole into a far more potent public policy space occupied by such issues as health care and crime," says Rick Smith, president of the Canadian Climate Institute.
Global sales of electric vehicles (EVs) jumped by 24 per cent last year, despite slowing EV sales growth in North America. And global sales of electric heat pumps are forecast to increase by more than 60 per cent from the current level to $205 billion by 2028.
Canada's clean-tech sector, which makes everything from EV charging equipment to LED lighting for greenhouses, exported $7.6 billion worth of goods to the U.S. in 2022.
The sector is bracing for the tariffs on Canadian and Mexican goods that Trump has said he will impose.
Economists expect tariffs to be lifted once Canada and the U.S. negotiate an end to their differences, though that can take a year judging from the tariffs Trump imposed in his first presidency.
Another sustaining factor for clean energy is that governments have such a large influence in the transition, though private-sector utilities and tech firms also play major roles.
Somewhat insulated from the vagaries of the private sector, governments can stay the course on big-ticket energy projects that can take decades to plan and build.
And decisions on projects are shifting from national capitals to subgovernments, which increases the number of project sponsors and creates diversity in decision-making.
Provincial and state governments in North America are closer to the impact of severe climate events from which they are expected to protect their citizens.
Jurisdictions like Ontario, Quebec and British Columbia are bigger players in planning future energy supplies than Ottawa. And in the U.S., the more than 20 member states of the U.S. Climate Alliance are determined to continue promoting clean energy and have the constitutional authority to do so.
Ontario is forecast to spend about $400 billion upgrading its power capacity by 2050 to reinforce its status as one of the world's biggest clean-power producers.
Expansions and overhauls are underway at the province's Bruce Power, Darlington and Pickering nuclear plants, and this month Ontario Premier Doug Ford announced plans for a new nuclear plant near Darlington.
Ontario is developing small modular reactor (SMR) technology that it hopes to export. And it seeks to double its energy production from wind, solar and other clean-energy sources.
Ford's response to the Trump tariff threat includes a proposal to more thoroughly integrate the energy systems of Canada and the U.S.
Ford says the project, which also calls for integrated critical mineral production, would help the U.S. "decouple" from China. The idea might seem fanciful. It would eclipse the size of a St. Lawrence Seaway jointly built by Canada and the U.S.
Then again, "the U.S. president has declared an 'energy emergency' to build new critical infrastructure," Yudullah Hussain of RBC Economics wrote in a client note last week. "Could some of it be built in Canada given the integrated nature of the two country's energy sectors?"
Ford's binational strategy might be worth pursuing just for its potential tech advances and cost efficiencies. And it would be ready for a Trump successor to embrace if Trump himself doesn't see its merit.
CREDIT: David Olive Toronto Star OPINION

Subject: Energy industry; Fossil fuels; Clean technology; Climate change; Tariffs; Canada-US relations; International trade

Pickering airport land expropriation made me an activist 53 years ago

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/pickering-airport-land-expropriation-made-me/docview/3162111691/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pickering+airport+land+expropriation+made+me+an+activist+53+years+ago&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Pickering Lands to be used to expand national park space as federal government grounds plans for future airport, Jan. 27
I read with joy this morning that federal government is about to end the Pickering airport fiasco. On March 2, 1972, the Toronto morning newspaper announced the federal expropriation of thousands of acres to build an airport east of Toronto and the province announced the expropriation of thousands of additional acres to build Cedarwood City. These actions changed my life . Political decisions were ruining my community, and I evolved from a "hippie" mom in a small hamlet to a political activist and eventually a politician. Hundreds of families lost their homes and land. Lives and livelihoods were destroyed. Millions of taxpayer dollars have been wasted. The airport disaster process is documented in the book "The Paper Juggernaut" by Walter Stewart, which recounts how this all started with a seven-page memo written by one bureaucrat over a weekend. I wonder if that man has any idea what chaos, angst, anger and activism he created? I would like to thank all the members of People Or Planes (POP) and Land Over Landings who stood up against this misuse of class 1 agricultural land and the destruction of rural communities. Against all odds this small band of warriors lead by Dr. Charles Godfrey proved that you can "fight City Hall " and win - even if it does take 53 years.
Norah Stoner, Tiny, ON
When it comes to Musk, judge him by his actions
Elon Musk is a challenge for journalists to cover, Jan. 24
Elon Musk should not be a challenge for journalists. In George Orwell's "Animal Farm," Clover the mare at one point didn't remember that the Fourth Commandment, written on a wall, forbid animals sleeping "in a bed with sheets." She thought it just forbid animals sleeping in a bed - which it, in fact, used to. But she thought her memory was mistaken. Well, I remember.
I remember what I learned in Hebrew school. I remember that the favourite defence of antisemites is "But some of my best friends are Jews."
Elon Musk twice appeared to make a Hitler salute in front of the Presidential seal - emboldening white supremacists. He supports the German far-right party. He constantly amplifies antisemitic postings on X. Judge him by his actions.
Louis Kalikow, Toronto
Ford should focus on fulfilling the promises he made to the people of Ontario
Doug Ford insists he's not rushing with snap Feb. 27 Ontario election, Jan. 24
I am beyond angry with Premier Doug Ford right now. He is misleading the people of Ontario by saying he needs to call an election so he has a clear mandate to fight Donald Trump. He has a majority government. To claim he needs another mandate is not true.
We have the Prime Minister and cabinet ministers to lead the fight against U.S. President Donald Trump. Ford should be focusing on the people of Ontario and fulfilling the promises that he made when he came to power in 2018. His promise to build housing has fallen far short of the target of 150,000 homes per year. When it comes to hallway medicine, things have gotten much worse under Ford's watch and people are now lining up in the cold for a chance at getting a primary care provider. Schools are falling apart, and teachers are burning out, but Ford has decided Trump is where he will put his attention.
This early election call is an attempt to suppress voter turnout and undermine our democracy. There are snowbirds who will be away and unable to vote. Seniors who will find it difficult to get to the ballot box because of the winter weather, and candidates who are now forced to go door knocking in the snow and ice. Voters of Ontario do not give this man what he wants. Deny him another four years as our premier.
Kathryn Murrell, New Hamburg, ON
Liberals and NDP parties need new strategies to defeat Ford
In France, rival parties worked together as they determined a united approach was necessary to avoid Conservative leadership. The Ontario Liberals and NDP should pursue the same strategy. With ranked voting not a possibility, Liberals should remove their candidate from 50 per cent of the ridings where the likelihood of winning is low and the NDP should remove candidates for the other 50 per cent. This would mean in these ridings the Conservatives cannot count on winning the riding by having the electorate splitting their vote across Liberal and NDP.
If the Liberals and NDP fail to do this, you can chalk it up to ego and lack of courage. If Conservatives win another majority (despite getting the minority of the vote) I do not want to hear complaints when options are available to avoid this outcome. Ford is depending on NDP and Liberals to enter this election the same old way. New thinking is needed immediately.
Cameron McCuaig, Mississauga
Get out and vote!
Here is our opportunity, fellow Ontarians! Unlike the past two Ontario elections, which saw record-low turnouts (apathy), let us not miss this great chance Premier Doug Ford is giving us. Even if he cannot be dislodged, his "mandate" may be severely weakened. This is one election in which we all must participate. "Respect For Taxpayers?" Once again, Ford has proven that he does not walk the talk.
Roger Williams, Toronto
Look at what Ford has done to Ontario health care
Premier Doug Ford must think we're so easily distracted by the (very real) external threat, that we will give him a pass on his destruction of public health care. Well, I won't. Although Ford insists he's spending more than ever on health care, the spending is going to private nursing agencies who pay nurses more than he will allow hospitals to pay; it's going to private surgical clinics who he pays more than the OHIP rate to do surgeries that could be done in OR's, and Ontarians are increasingly turning to subscriber-based private virtual care in desperation. Hospital budgets are being slashed: Ontario's bed capacity is the lowest in Canada, our per capita spending on public health care is the lowest in Canada. Health is a cornerstone of life, Premier. I will not be distracted.
Ann Peel, Toronto
CAPTION: The future of the Pickering Lands has been uncertain for half a century and has often been a source of conflict between environmental advocates and many who felt the airport was needed for a rapidly growing region.

Document 387 of 500

Who can defeat Doug Ford? Here's who the Tories are scared of this Ontario election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/who-can-defeat-doug-ford-heres-tories-are-scared/docview/3162111684/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Who+can+defeat+Doug+Ford%3F+Here%27s+who+the+Tories+are+scared+of+this+Ontario+election&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As Progressive Conservatives assess the first days of their snap election call, they are firmly convinced only one candidate can defeat Doug Ford.
With Ford's Tories at 47 per cent support in the latest Abacus Data poll for the Star - well ahead of Bonnie Crombie's Liberals at 24 per cent, Marit Stiles's New Democrats at 19 per cent and Mike Schreiner's Greens at seven per cent - the governing party's strategists feel confident.
They believe they have more money, more staff, more volunteers and more momentum heading into the Feb. 27 vote being held 15 months ahead of an election previously scheduled for June 2026.
They are convinced their rationale for a snap $189-million election - a renewed mandate to combat U.S. President Donald Trump's threatened 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian goods slated to begin Saturday - is the right frame for their campaign.
They just fret about one wild card, a candidate who could scuttle their hopes and dreams for a four-year term.
And the name of that candidate is Doug Ford.
"Only Doug Ford can beat Doug Ford," said a Tory insider, speaking confidentially in order to discuss internal deliberations.
"When he's on his game, he's terrific," agreed another party operative, "When he's not, well, he's not."
Case in point - on the eve of the start of the official campaign, Ford was in Brampton to tout that the next phase of the still-incomplete Hazel McCallion Line LRT on Hurontario Street would be tunnelled.
To the distress of aides in attendance at the cacophonous Brampton City Hall atrium on Jan. 24, the Tory leader was chippy and bellicose, and almost messianic when a reporter pressed him on why he needed an election 15 months ahead of schedule.
"Maybe you're one of the jobs that I'm going to have to protect," he thundered to the broadcast journalist.
"Because when the tariffs hit, it affects the media, it affects manufacturing. It affects every single sector in this province. So you better pray that we get elected, because we're going to protect everyone's job, including the media's job."
That cringeworthy encounter dismayed some senior Tories on site as well as Ford's campaign advisers monitoring the live broadcast.
Some were unhappy that he also confirmed the date of the election five days before the formal issuing of the writs.
"So we gave the other parties (almost) a week more to get ready," said one Tory with a sigh, adding Ford was clearly just fed up with journalists peppering him about election timing.
The following morning, at an all-day "super caucus" meeting in a hotel ballroom at a Pearson International Airport hotel, PC MPPs and candidates were repeatedly told by top strategists that humility and seriousness must be the hallmarks of the re-election bid.
Trump's tariffs pose an existential threat to trade-dependent Ontario's economy because they undermine potential investment in the province, the Tory candidates heard Jan. 25.
Therefore, the PC campaign must be focused on the 500,000 jobs imperiled by American levies and the need to re-elect a stable majority government that can "outlast" Trump's four-year term that ends in 2029, the strategists emphasized to attendees.
With Ford basking in his role as Council of the Federation chair of premiers with his blue anti-Trump "Canada Is Not For Sale" cap, his campaign gurus assured candidates that Ontarians believe Stiles and Crombie are untested and little-known rookie leaders.
Mindful that voters find hubris and arrogance off-putting, the Tory MPPs and hopefuls were instructed to be humble on the hustings no matter how well public-opinion polls suggest things are going.
One candidate who apparently absorbed that message was Doug Ford.
Listening raptly to his staff like a quarterback whose coach is urging him to stick to the playbook instead of calling audibles at the line of scrimmage, the Tory leader was attentive and patient, according to witnesses.
In contrast to the braggadocio in Brampton, Ford was measured when he gathered for press cameras on Tuesday with Aroland First Nation Chief Sonny Gagnon to sign an accord to build some Northern Ontario infrastructure.
It was one of his last official acts before visiting Lt.-Gov. Edith Dumont in her second floor suite at Queen's Park two hours later to request the dissolution of the legislature so the election could begin Wednesday.
Ford parried with reporters as senior aides watched approvingly in his government's new $310,000 subterranean communications centre - derided as "the bunker" for its poor acoustics and lousy cellular reception.
Pointed media questions did not get under his skin and he resisted the temptation to boast when asked about the Abacus poll indicating he enjoys a massive lead over Crombie, Stiles and Schreiner.
"I didn't see that poll. What are the numbers in that poll?" he said impishly, aware that the assembled journalists know his obsession with the latest public-opinion research surveys.
Nor did he take the bait when it was pointed out the poll found 48 per cent of respondents were "unhappy" with such an expensive election.
"My favourite saying is - no matter if they're good or if they're bad - polls are for dogs. The only poll that counts is on election day," he said.
On the trail in Windsor the following day, aboard the custom-wrapped Ford RV that serves as his campaign "bus" - press gallery wags immediately dubbed it "the Winter-bago" - the PC leader was again on his best behaviour.
"You may not always agree with me, and that's OK, but my record is clear," he said on Wednesday.
"When times are tough, I will do whatever it takes to keep you safe. I will work with anyone, and I will stand up to anyone, in order to protect our province and our people," he said.
Ford was exuding reasonableness, sticking to his central campaign theme, just as he did Thursday in London and again Friday in Niagara Falls.
For now, at least, the Tories are not worried about the one candidate they fear.
But if a week is a long time in politics, the nearly four weeks before election day could be a lifetime.
With files from Rob Ferguson
CAPTION: Ontario Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford arrives for a visit to Heddle Shipyard in St. Catharines, Ont. on Jan. 31, 2025.

Some Ontarians report issues cashing cheques

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/some-ontarians-report-issues-cashing-cheques/docview/3162111665/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Some+Ontarians+report+issues+cashing+cheques&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Lynda Chapple hasn't used her maiden name in 43 years. Save for her birth certificate and social insurance card, all her government IDs are in her married name.
She has filed taxes countless times, and the government even has her new address in Selkirk, Ont., where she and her husband Randy moved to a little over a year ago.
So when her $200 "tax rebate" cheque from the province arrived in the mail earlier this week with a name she hasn't used since the 1980s, she was a bit surprised. Randy called their bank to see if the cheque could still be deposited, but was told they could not.
"I'm extremely frustrated," Lynda said.
"If they have my all my information in my married name and have had for so many years, how can they do something so crazy?"
The bank also told Randy when he called that she wasn't alone in her situation, Lynda recounted to the Star over the phone Friday morning.
"He was told I was in good company."
Others online have complained that they have been unable to deposit their payouts from the province due to misprints as the rebate cheques announced by the Conservative government last fall arrive in mailboxes.
A spokesperson for the Ontario Ministry of Finance wrote in an emailed statement to the Star that the department was aware of the issue and cited a "technical error in the data used to administer the payments." The data, the spokesperson said, came from the Canada Revenue Agency.
Anyone who received a cheque with a previous or different name should contact Service Ontario at 1-833-351-0409 or 1-888-821-9056, according to the spokesperson, who added that the cheque will then be cancelled and replaced within 3-4 weeks.
When Lynda called about her issue, she was told it would take closer to 4-6 weeks.
In total, approximately 15 million Ontarians who filed their taxes in 2023 and their children should expect to get a cheque. The province estimates the payouts will cost the treasury $3 billion, despite a $6.6-billion budget deficit.
The misprinted names on some of the 7.5 million cheques sent out so far by the province is not the first issue to happen during the rollout of a payouts.
Earlier this week, Progressive Conservative party Leader Doug Ford blamed RBC following reports of people being unable to deposit their cheques. RBC later apologized and said the problem had been resolved.
The timing of the cheque rollout - which began on Jan. 17 - overlaps with Ford's call for a snap election earlier this week. Ontarians head to the polls on Feb. 27.
Opposition parties have accused the government of using taxpayer money to buy votes, with NDP Leader Marit Stiles previously calling it a "cynical" ploy when the plan was announced in the fall.
What should I do if I haven't
received my cheque?
The cheque may still be on its way.
Ontarians can verify the cheque amounts for adults and children, the mailing address of the cheque and whether or not it has been mailed out on the Service Ontario website.
The system will require an account login, the last four digits of an individual's Social Insurance Number, their date of birth and the postal code of the mailing address used in the Canada Revenue Agency tax return.
A person can only check their status a maximum of three times a day, the website reads.
CREDIT: Nathan Bawaan Toronto Star

Document 389 of 500

Ford's main opponents are successful and savvy in ways he can't imagine

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-main-opponents-are-successful-savvy-ways-he/docview/3162110848/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+main+opponents+are+successful+and+savvy+in+ways+he+can%27t+imagine&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: To protect us from Donald Trump, Doug Ford needs the mother of all mandates.
Right, but what about the two women who also want to be premier?
Not up for the job. So said Ford in disqualifying Ontario's two major opposition leaders, while justifying the snap election he triggered this week.
He didn't quite say it's a man's job. But listen to his oration - and use your imagination:
"If you could just imagine - just imagine this, folks - imagine (Liberal) Bonnie Crombie or (New Democrat) Marit Stiles sitting across from President Trump, negotiating a deal, it would be an absolute disaster," he told reporters.
What, pray tell, makes that image so unimaginable? Lord knows.
"God help us if that ever happens," he intoned.
To be fair, he didn't explicitly complain about two female politicians aspiring to be premier in a man's world. It's the difference between sexist and subtext - subtle.
Now Ford has plunged us into a mid-winter election in which everything is up for grabs. For we face a grave threat from an American president who is waging economic and psychological warfare against Ontario.
They say that every crisis offers an opportunity. And Ford never misses an opportunity to mess with the opposition. For all his faux gravitas, and his phoney calls for national unity, Ford is leading a provincial battle against his opposition rivals that is all about partisan disunity at home.
To be sure, that's democracy. But it's not unity, it's adversity.
It matters not to Ford that the NDP's Stiles and the Liberals' Crombie have proferred their support for whatever it takes to resist Trump and reassure Ontarians. He insisted on a democratic election to crush any opposition, while suppressing voter participation with a mid-winter campaign 15 months ahead of the legally - democratically - mandated election date in June 2026.
Just as Trump dreams of dominating the continent without any dissent, so too Ford fantasizes about ruling over Ontario without argument. The populist politician who has been premier for the past seven years now wants unchallenged power for another four years.
According to the public opinion polls, nothing will stop Ford from achieving his ambition of an unrivalled dynasty that endures for more than a decade. Nothing, that is, except those two women whom he can't imagine doing his job.
Let's revisit the quotation about his imagination for a moment:
"Imagine Bonnie Crombie or Marit Stiles sitting across from President Trump, negotiating a deal."
In fact, provincial premiers don't negotiate with superpower presidents.
It's not in Ford's job description, nor is a White House meeting on his itinerary when he visits Washington as a candidate for two planned campaign stops in February.
In truth, Ford has been trash-talking Trump alongside Crombie and Stiles in the same breath - while talking up his own bona fides for weeks. So how exactly does he measure up - how do his qualifications for premier or negotiator compare to those of his opposition rivals?
To be clear, he didn't cite their lack of educational or professional qualifications: Stiles and Crombie are both university graduates (Crombie also has an MBA), whereas Ford has no diploma to his name after dropping out of college within months.
To be candid, Ford didn't talk about their lack of work experience, since he's never had to look for work: Stiles worked for a labour union, while Crombie joined major multinational firms and served three terms as mayor of Mississauga; Ford was hired by his father to work at the family's label printing firm.
To be precise, he didn't draw any contrasts between his negotiating savvy and theirs: Ford was bamboozled by developers on the Greenbelt scandal, snookered by big breweries on a billion-dollar beer boondoggle, and suckered by a foreign multinational on the Ontario Place lease; Stiles boasts that she bargained with major American firms on behalf of a national union, while Crombie has negotiated her share of deals as a big-city mayor, free from Ford-like scandals.
No one should underestimate Ford. Equally, it would be a mistake for Ford or anyone to underestimate the opposition leaders. They are rookie leaders, untested and unknown, yet successful and savvy, each in their own way, in ways that he apparently cannot imagine.
A campaign can be static or dynamic. Earthquakes do happen - imperceptible tremors and fault lines that rearrange the landscape in unexpected ways, as then-premier David Peterson learned in 1990 when his governing Liberals lost a huge lead after calling an early election.
What would it take to shake things up again? An opposition that finds its footing and throws the Tories off balance.
As Ford covets the mother of all mandates, he might be mindful of the two women who want his job. If one of them breaks through to connect with voters, Ford's fears of "an absolute disaster" could yet come true - for him.
Speaking of opposition leaders, I'll be hosting them at the Democracy Forum at Toronto Metropolitan University: Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie on Tuesday at 1:30 p.m., and NDP Leader Marit Stiles on Wednesday at noon. Both events will be livestreamed on thestar.com.
CAPTION: Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford speaks during a campaign stop at Walker Construction in St. Catharines on Friday. As Ford covets the mother of all mandates, he might be mindful of the two women who want his job, Martin Regg Cohn writes.Peter Power THE CANADIAN PRESS
CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION

Full text: Pickering airport fiasco made me an activist decades ago
Pickering Lands to be used to expand national park space as federal government grounds plans for future airport, Jan. 27
I read with joy this week that federal government is about to end the Pickering airport fiasco. On March 2, 1972, it was reported that the federal government would expropriate thousands of acres to build an airport east of Toronto and the province announced the expropriation of thousands of additional acres to build Cedarwood City.
These actions changed my life. Political decisions were ruining my community and I evolved from a "hippie" mom in a small hamlet to a political activist, and eventually a politician. Hundreds of families lost their homes and land. Lives and livelihoods were destroyed. Millions of taxpayer dollars have been wasted.
The airport disaster process is documented in the book "The Paper Juggernaut" by Walter Stewart, which recounts how this all started with a seven-page memo written by one bureaucrat over a weekend. I wonder if that man has any idea what chaos, angst, anger and activism he created?
I would like to thank all the members of People Or Planes and Land Over Landings who stood up against this misuse of Class 1 agricultural land and the destruction of rural communities. Against all odds, this small band of warriors lead by Dr. Charles Godfrey proved that you can "fight city hall" and win - even if it does take 53 years.
Norah Stoner, Tiny, Ont.
When it comes
to Musk, let's
call it what it is
Elon Musk is a challenge
for journalists to cover, Jan. 24
Elon Musk should not be a challenge for journalists. In George Orwell's "Animal Farm," Clover the mare at one point didn't remember that the Fourth Commandment, written on a wall, forbid animals sleeping "in a bed with sheets." She thought it just forbid animals sleeping in a bed - which it, in fact, used to. But she thought her memory was mistaken. Well, I remember.
I remember what I learned in Hebrew school. I remember that the favourite defence of antisemites is "But some of my best friends are Jews."
Elon Musk twice appeared to make a Hitler salute in front of the presidential seal - emboldening white supremacists. He supports the German far-right party. He constantly amplifies antisemitic postings on X. Judge him by his actions.
Louis Kalikow, Toronto
Get out and vote!
Here is our opportunity, fellow Ontarians. Unlike the past two Ontario elections, which saw record-low turnouts (apathy), let us not miss this great chance Premier Doug Ford is giving us. Even if he cannot be dislodged, his "mandate" may be severely weakened. This is one election in which we all must participate. "Respect For Taxpayers?" Once again, Ford has proven that he does not walk the talk.
Roger Williams, Toronto
Ford should focus on fulfilling the promises he made
Doug Ford insists he's not rushing with snap Feb. 27 Ontario election, Jan. 24
I am beyond angry with Premier Doug Ford. He is misleading the people of Ontario by saying he needs to call an election so he has a clear mandate to fight U.S. President Donald Trump. He has a majority government. To claim he needs another mandate is not true.
We have the prime minister and cabinet ministers to lead the fight against Trump. Ford should be focusing on the people of Ontario and fulfilling the promises that he made when he came to power in 2018. His promise to build housing has fallen far short of the target of 150,000 homes per year. When it comes to hallway medicine, things have gotten much worse under Ford's watch and people are now lining up in the cold for a chance at getting a primary care provider. Schools are falling apart and teachers are burning out, but Ford has decided Trump is where he will put his attention.
This early election call is an attempt to suppress voter turnout and undermine our democracy. There are snowbirds who will be away and unable to vote. Seniors who will find it difficult to get to the ballot box because of the winter weather and candidates who are now forced to go door-knocking in the snow and ice. Voters of Ontario, do not give this man what he wants. Deny him another four years as our premier.
Kathryn Murrell, New Hamburg, Ont.
Liberals, NDP need new strategy to defeat Ford
In France, rival parties worked together as they determined a united approach was necessary to avoid Conservative leadership. The Ontario Liberals and NDP should pursue the same strategy. With ranked voting not a possibility, Liberals should remove their candidate from 50 per cent of the ridings where the likelihood of winning is low and the NDP should remove candidates for the other 50 per cent. This would mean in these ridings the Conservatives cannot count on winning the riding by having the electorate splitting their vote across Liberal and NDP.
If the Liberals and NDP fail to do this, you can chalk it up to ego and lack of courage. If Conservatives win another majority (despite getting the minority of the vote) I do not want to hear complaints when options are available to avoid this outcome. Ford is depending on the NDP and Liberals to enter this election the same old way. New thinking is needed immediately.
Cameron McCuaig, Mississauga
