Document 392 of 500

Ford doesn't want us to look at his record

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-doesnt-want-us-look-at-his-record/docview/3162110763/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+doesn%27t+want+us+to+look+at+his+record&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=OP.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When U.S. President Donald Trump threatened to impose massive tariffs on the Canadian economy, he inadvertently did the premier of Ontario a huge political favour.
Doug Ford had been laying the groundwork for a self-serving snap election since at least last fall, but it was Trump who gave him the perfect pretext to call one. In order to protect "the livelihoods of workers on factory floors, construction sites and in local stores," Ford wrote in op-ed for the National Post ahead of Tuesday's election call, "I'm asking the people for a strong mandate - a strong, stable, four-year mandate that will outlive and outlast the Trump administration."
Ontarians, like the vast majority of Canadians, rightly and overwhelmingly dislike Trump and favour a tough response from their governments. But despite Ford's pretence, the actual reasons for his election call are somewhat less patriotic and high-minded. Going to the polls early has enabled the Progressive Conservatives to skirt the regulations that usually limit government-sponsored advertising in the lead up to a campaign, allowing them to make liberal use of public ad dollars before the dropping of the writ. It also likely pre-empts any criminal charges related to the Greenbelt scandal, which saw Ford's government effectively conspire with a tiny group of hand-picked developers to inflate the value of protected public land it planned to sell off (before a public backlash compelled it to abandon the scheme). By some happy accident, every Ontarian of voting age is also getting a cheque for $200 just as the election gets underway.
More than anything else, however, Trump's still-looming tariff threat gives Ford a ready-made narrative that conveniently elides the kinds of questions voters typically ask about incumbent governments seeking re-election: Has life gotten easier or harder? Do things generally seem to be working? Are we better off now than we were before? (And if it also helps people forget about the "unwavering support" he's expressed for Trump in the past, so much the better.) Ford's desire to sidestep these questions makes sense, because the answers paint a decidedly unflattering portrait of his government and its record.
For all his folksy bluster and supposed populist billing, Ontario's premier hails from a long line of politicians who have mistakenly viewed corporate patronage as synonymous with the wider public good. Accordingly, the Ford government's approach in major areas, from housing and health care to wages and work, has typically been to give as much latitude as possible to profiteers and private industry, shrug its shoulders and hope for the best. After six-plus years, the upshot is a province where vital services are crumbling, economic insecurity is rampant, and the necessities of life are more expensive than ever.
Consider housing. Following his election victory in 2018, Ford moved to eliminate rent control on newly constructed units, the purported justification being that it would help address shortages by creating "market-based incentives for supply growth." In a similar vein, the government has steadily eliminated regulations related to health, safety, and environmental protection with a view to lowering costs for developers. The result? Even Ford's own finance minister, Peter Bethlenfalvy, concedes it will fall well short of its own targets, with the number of new housing starts last year being lower than in any other province. Between January 2021 and July 2024, meanwhile, the cost of housing rose an astonishing 24 per cent, with rents also continuing to soar.
According to one estimate cited by Toronto Metropolitan University's Bryan Evans and York University's Carlo Fanelli in a new study of the Ford government, nearly half of all Ontario households now spend at least 30 per cent of their total income on shelter. For countless others, the situation is even worse. Last year, per a recent report from the Association of Municipalities of Ontario, some 81,000 people were homeless - an increase of tens of thousands since 2022. Between April 2023 and March 2024, a record 1,001,150 Ontarians (or roughly 1 in 16) were forced to visit a food bank.
There are, of course, various factors at play not directly or wholly related to provincial policy, from high interest rates and federal inaction to the spiralling cost of groceries. But the Ford government's dogmatic preference for cuts, deregulation, and market-based approaches to every problem bears a great deal of responsibility for Ontario's growing climate of economic insecurity and social pain.
Social assistance rates have been slashed to bare-bones levels. Chronic underinvestment in vital programs, meanwhile, has steadily weakened public services: the province's Financial Accountability Office revealed last June that the government's spending plans are nearly $4 billion short of what would be needed even to maintain them at current levels. Here, the effects of underfunding have been exacerbated by other policies, notably Bill 124, which capped annual wage increases for public sector workers at 1 per cent and directly impacted the incomes of roughly one in three households.
Though ultimately ruled unconstitutional by the courts, the legislation has nonetheless helped drive massive staffing shortages, especially in the health-care sector. According to figures obtained by the Canadian Press - figures that the Ford government tried to keep secret - the province is now short some 33,000 nurses and 50,000 personal support workers. Meanwhile, the Ontario Medical Association has reported a further shortage of 2,000 doctors. (The consequences of such shortages are anything but abstract: according to one estimate, some 11,581 patients died awaiting surgeries and other procedures in 2022 alone.) Instead of simply funding health care at adequate levels, Ford's government has increasingly sought to make it a vehicle for private profit - opening the door to a U.S.-style system that is costlier, less efficient and geared more toward the interests of investors than the needs of patients.
While the PCs are going into the current election campaign with a lead in the polls, plenty of voters have clearly grown more pessimistic about their government and its direction. Ford himself has the lowest approval ratings of any premier in Canada. Last March, an Angus Reid survey asking Ontarians to rate the government's performance in key issue areas, including health care, inflation, housing affordability, and education, found that only 23 per cent believe it's doing a "good job" - down from 42 per cent in 2019.
Given all this, it's little wonder that Doug Ford wants to spend the next month shadowboxing with Donald Trump. He should be made to answer for his government's disastrous record instead.
Luke Savage is a writer based in Toronto whose work has appeared in the Washington Post, The Atlantic and the Guardian.
CAPTION: The dogmatic approach of Doug Ford government's deserves much blame for Ontario's economic and social problems, argues Luke Savage.Justin Tang The Canadian Press

Document 393 of 500

Ford hits the trail with a goal: stay on message: Tory insiders point to key risk for leader

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-hits-trail-with-goal-stay-on-message/docview/3162110708/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+hits+the+trail+with+a+goal%3A+stay+on+message%3A+Tory+insiders+point+to+key+risk+for+leader&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As Progressive Conservatives assess the first days of their snap election call, they are firmly convinced only one candidate can defeat Doug Ford.
With Ford's Tories at 47 per cent support in the latest Abacus Data poll for the Star - well ahead of Bonnie Crombie's Liberals at 24 per cent, Marit Stiles's New Democrats at 19 per cent and Mike Schreiner's Greens at seven per cent - the governing party's strategists feel confident.
They believe they have more money, more staff, more volunteers and more momentum heading into the Feb. 27 vote, being held 15 months ahead of an election previously scheduled for June 2026.
They are convinced their rationale for a snap $189-million election - a renewed mandate to combat U.S. President Donald Trump's threatened 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian goods, slated to begin Saturday - is the right frame for their campaign.
They just fret about one wild card, a candidate who could scuttle their hopes and dreams for a four-year term.
And the name of that candidate is Doug Ford.
"Only Doug Ford can beat Doug Ford," said a Tory insider, speaking confidentially in order to discuss internal deliberations.
"When he's on his game, he's terrific," agreed another party operative, "When he's not, well, he's not."
Case in point - days before the start of the official campaign, Ford was in Brampton to tout that the next phase of the still-incomplete Hazel McCallion Line LRT on Hurontario Street would be tunnelled.
To the distress of aides in attendance at the cacophonous Brampton City Hall atrium on Jan. 24, the Tory leader was chippy and bellicose, and almost messianic when a reporter pressed him on why he needed an election 15 months ahead of schedule.
"Maybe you're one of the jobs that I'm going to have to protect," he thundered to the broadcast journalist.
"Because when the tariffs hit, it affects the media, it affects manufacturing. It affects every single sector in this province. So you better pray that we get elected, because we're going to protect everyone's job, including the media's job."
That cringeworthy encounter dismayed some senior Tories on site as well as Ford's campaign advisers monitoring the live broadcast.
Some were unhappy that he also confirmed the date of the election five days before the formal issuing of the writs.
"So we gave the other parties (almost) a week more to get ready," said one Tory with a sigh, adding Ford was clearly just fed up with journalists peppering him about election timing.
The following morning, at an all-day "super caucus" meeting in a hotel ballroom near Pearson airport, Tory MPPs and candidates were repeatedly told by top strategists that humility and seriousness must be the hallmarks of the re-election bid.
Trump's tariffs pose an existential threat to trade-dependent Ontario's economy because they undermine potential investment in the province, the Tory candidates heard on Jan. 25.
Therefore, the PC campaign must be focused on the 500,000 jobs imperilled by American levies and the need to re-elect a stable majority government that can "outlast" Trump's four-year term that ends in 2029, the strategists emphasized to attendees.
With Ford basking in his role as Council of the Federation chair of premiers with his blue anti-Trump "Canada Is Not for Sale" cap, his campaign gurus assured candidates that Ontarians believe Stiles and Crombie are untested and little-known rookie leaders.
Mindful that voters find hubris and arrogance off-putting, the Tory MPPs and hopefuls were instructed to be humble on the hustings no matter how well public opinion polls suggest things are going.
One candidate who apparently absorbed that message was Doug Ford.
Listening raptly to his staff like a quarterback whose coach is urging him to stick to the playbook instead of calling audibles at the line of scrimmage, the Tory leader was attentive and patient, according to witnesses.
In contrast to the braggadocio in Brampton, Ford was measured when he gathered for press cameras on Tuesday with Aroland First Nation Chief Sonny Gagnon to sign an accord to build some northern Ontario infrastructure.
It was one of his last official acts before visiting Lt.-Gov. Edith Dumont in her second-floor suite at Queen's Park two hours later to request the dissolution of the legislature so the election could begin Wednesday.
Ford parried with reporters as senior aides watched approvingly in his government's new $310,000 subterranean communications centre - derided as "the bunker" for its poor acoustics and lousy cellular reception.
Pointed media questions did not get under his skin and he resisted the temptation to boast when asked about the Abacus poll indicating he enjoys a massive lead over Crombie, Stiles and Schreiner.
"I didn't see that poll. What are the numbers in that poll?" he said impishly, aware that the assembled journalists know his obsession with the latest public opinion research surveys.
Nor did he take the bait when it was pointed out the poll found 48 per cent of respondents were "unhappy" with such an expensive election.
"My favourite saying is - no matter if they're good or if they're bad - polls are for dogs. The only poll that counts is on election day," he said.
On the trail in Windsor the following day, aboard the custom-wrapped Ford RV that serves as his campaign "bus" - press gallery wags immediately dubbed it "the Winter-bago" - the PC leader was again on his best behaviour.
"You may not always agree with me, and that's OK, but my record is clear," he said on Wednesday.
"When times are tough, I will do whatever it takes to keep you safe. I will work with anyone, and I will stand up to anyone, in order to protect our province and our people," he said.
Ford was exuding reasonableness, sticking to his central campaign theme, just as he did Thursday in London and again Friday in Niagara Falls.
For now, at least, the Tories are not worried about the one candidate they fear.
But if a week is a long time in politics, the nearly four weeks before election day could be a lifetime.
With files from Rob Ferguson
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief With files from Rob Ferguson

Document 394 of 500

Carney aims to end politically 'divisive' carbon levy policy: Ex-central banker backs green incentives, would also implement tariff on imports from nations with weaker climate policies

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/carney-aims-end-politically-divisive-carbon-levy/docview/3162110702/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Carney+aims+to+end+politically+%27divisive%27+carbon+levy+policy%3A+Ex-central+banker+backs+green+incentives%2C+would+also+implement+tariff+on+imports+from+nations+with+weaker+climate+policies&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Former Bank of Canada governor Mark Carney says he will replace Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's consumer carbon levy-and-rebate scheme with an array of green incentives if he wins the Liberal leadership race.
Carney unveiled his climate policy vision on Friday in Nova Scotia, declaring that the Liberal government's fuel levy is too politically "divisive" and tainted by "lies" peddled by Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre.
Carney said he would immediately lift the federal fuel levy from consumers and small businesses. In its place, he would create a system of incentives that would see the government sell emissions credits to heavy industries, and use the money to pay people unspecified amounts when they make green purchases like electric cars, energy-efficient appliances and improved home insulation.
He would also maintain the national requirement for provinces to have carbon pricing systems for heavy industries. And he would keep the Liberal government's incoming emissions cap for the oil and gas sector, a policy that Alberta strongly opposes and the Conservatives have promised to scrap if they take power.
While Carney did not specify how much his policies would reduce Canada's greenhouse gas emissions, he said his plan would ensure they keep going down. A spokesperson for his campaign said they haven't yet calculated how much Carney's plan would reduce emissions, but that he would keep Canada's current emissions targets and "at least match" the reductions that are expected from existing policies.
"It's time for a new, more effective climate plan that everyone can get behind," Carney said Friday.
"You'll no longer have to pay more to fuel your car or heat your home."
On Friday, Poilievre's office continued trying to link Carney with Trudeau's carbon levy, calling him "Carbon Tax Carney" and dismissing his plan as nothing more than a "rebrand" of the government's current policy. "Canadians will continue to suffer under a brand new name!" Poilievre's spokesperson Sebastian Skamski said in a written statement Friday morning.
Poilievre later posted a video claiming Carney would only "pause" the consumer carbon price until after the next election.
The NDP criticized Carney for overseeing an asset management firm that invested in fossil fuels. "How can Canadians trust him with a climate plan now?" the party asked in a statement Friday.
Now that Trudeau is on his way out, Carney and other contenders to replace him as prime minister have distanced themselves from the policy. Chrystia Freeland, the former finance minister whose resignation in December helped push Trudeau towards the door, has promised to scrap the consumer carbon price.
Leadership candidate Karina Gould, the former government House leader, says she would freeze it at its current level. Gould has also promised to roll out a series of green incentives for consumers.
The federal levy is in place in several provinces, including Ontario, and comes with rebates to all households four times a year. A household of four in Ontario was slated to get $1,120 in rebates in the current fiscal year.
The minimum carbon price is also scheduled to increase annually, along with the size of the rebates. This year it is set at $80 per tonne of emissions, which the federal government says adds 17.6 cents to the cost of a litre of gasoline. It is supposed to increase until it hits $170 per tonne in 2030, adding 37.4 cents per litre at the pump.
While Carney would ditch the consumer levy, he said he would keep the scheduled carbon price increases for the industrial systems.
Carney also said he would implement a "carbon border adjustment," which is a tariff on imports from other countries with weaker climate policies that is meant to ensure Canadian businesses aren't disadvantaged by domestic green initiatives.
"What that does is promote jobs here at home" and prevents emitting industries from moving to other jurisdictions, he said.
Skamski, Poilievre's spokesperson, said this amounts to a "new carbon tax at the border" and predicted it would increase the cost of gas, home heating and food.
Under the Trudeau government, Canada has pledged to slash emissions to at least 40 per cent below 2005 levels by 2030, and at least 45 per cent below 2005 levels by 2035.
According to a preliminary assessment the government published in December, Canada's emissions were 8.5 per cent below 2005 levels in 2023.
CAPTION: Liberal leadership candidate Mark Carney signs a copy of his book at a campaign event in Halifax on Friday. While Carney would ditch the consumer levy, he said he would keep the scheduled carbon price increases for the industrial systems.Darren CalabreseTHE CANADIAN PRESS
CREDIT: Alex Ballingall and Ryan Tumilty Toronto Star

Document 395 of 500

Canada is a 'powerhouse' at making video games: Industry employs more than 34,000 across 821 studios

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/canada-is-powerhouse-at-making-video-games/docview/3162110687/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada+is+a+%27powerhouse%27+at+making+video+games%3A+Industry+employs+more+than+34%2C000+across+821+studios&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Paul Fogolin wants Canadians to take more pride in the country's outsized contributions to the global video game industry.
According to a study commissioned by the Entertainment Software Association of Canada (ESAC), the industry employs more than 34,000 Canadians across 821 studios, 86 per cent of who are full time and earn an average of six figures. Together, they contribute $5.1 billion to the country's GDP.
"As Canadians, we tend to be pretty humble people, but there are some industries that we're proud of, like we're great at making cars, or on the resources side - even our entertainment talent, with Canadian actors and the musicians," says Fogolin, who took over as CEO of the ESAC in November. "This industry is just like that; we are amazing at making games, so let's be proud of it."
With a global market size of more than $221 billion (U.S.) in 2024, the gaming industry is bigger than the global film industry ($105 billion) and music industries ($48.6 billion) combined, with Canada one of its top contributors.
A lifelong gamer himself, Fogolin began his career at Queen's Park, followed by a series of roles with industry associations, before joining the gaming association in 2018.
"I stumbled on a posting for a job at ESAC and I thought it was too good to be true," he says from his home office in Oakville. "I said, 'wait a minute, I can apply my knowledge of policy and government relations to the video game industry?' "
After landing a job as the industry association's vice-president of policy and government affairs, Fogolin left last year to lead the Canadian Association of Recycling Industries before returning as CEO of ESAC months later.
After a pandemic boom, the digital gaming sector was forced to consolidate in the wake of recent global economic challenges.
With that recovery in the rear-view mirror, the threat of tariffs on the horizon could have significant effects on a domestic industry that is 88 per cent reliant on global export revenue, with the U.S. as its biggest customer.
At the same time, 2025 promises to be an exciting year for gamers, with the release of new consoles like the Switch 2, flagship games like "Grand Theft Auto 6", the growth of esports and emerging gaming platforms featuring augmented and virtual reality.
The Star recently caught up with Fogolin to discuss the growth of gaming, the many misconceptions about its makers and players, and how Canada continues to be a global leader in what has become a highly competitive industry.
Have you always been a gamer?
From my earliest days. I vividly remember Christmas when I was five, and I got the original Nintendo Entertainment System, with "Duck Hunt" and "Mario Brothers," and I fell in love with games right away.
Why study politics?
We had newspapers on the breakfast table every morning, so current events, news, politics were always being discussed in the house from a young age, and I just became fascinated with it.
Did you plan to spend your life
in politics?
After I graduated, I volunteered for a local MP in Vaughan, and I think I made a good impression on some of the campaign folks, because they passed my resume to some friends at Queen's Park, and that's how I ended up working there. I thought it would just be short-term - I ended up there for eight years, working for premier Dalton McGuinty and a few ministers.
It was an incredible experience, but to be honest, I didn't see myself as a civil servant.
After McGuinty was voted out of office I left Queen's Park and worked for a few trade associations, but I remained loving games. It's kind of funny that I didn't even know there was a trade association for the video game industry at the time.
What does the ESAC do?
We protect and promote the industry. The protect side is all about advocacy, ensuring a strong regulatory environment. The promote side is all about telling the story of the industry, putting out reports like the one we just commissioned about the industry's role in the economy.
What are Canada's contributions
to this sector?
This industry remains a cornerstone of our digital economy. Canada is a powerhouse in making games.
The sector is maturing, because it's been in Canada for decades, so we have more senior talent, more stability, more leadership, more unique experience and it's coast-to-coast. Quebec is the heart of the industry, with over 15,000 jobs, but we've got around 11,000 in B.C., 6,000-plus in Ontario, and with advances in technology you can work for a game studio from just about anywhere.
Canada is home to both the largest game studio in the world by square footage - EA based in Burnaby, B.C. - and the largest studio by head count, Ubisoft in Montreal, Quebec. Over 80 per cent of the employment comes from the major companies, but there's also a thriving small business community; over 80 per cent of our studios have less than 25 employees.
How did Canada become
such a gaming powerhouse?
The province of Quebec was first to introduce a tax credit for digital media in the '90s mimicking the film and television credit, which was instrumental in creating Hollywood North. They wanted to do the same for video games. Now, pretty much every province has some form of the digital media tax credit that supports the industry.
We've developed a very skilled workforce, combined with lots of great academic institutions. We have great immigration programs for highly skilled talent, which is critical, and unlike the film industry - where you come for a few months to shoot and then go home - games take years to make, so many put down roots.
Beyond that, the proximity to the U.S. makes a difference, and frankly our dollar makes it an attractive place to build a studio.
And despite the weather, Canada is a great place to live, and all of that comes together to create an amazing ecosystem.
Has global competition
gotten fiercer?
Canada used to be unique in offering tax incentives but now we have competition all over the world. Australia, Italy, Ireland, Singapore all have programs, because everybody wants what we have.
At the same time, we're seeing the market explode around the world, because games are no longer physical or just on consoles; you can play on your phone, your iPad, your PC, and most of the distribution is now digital, so you don't have to ship boxes across the planet. According to our study, 88 per cent of games are exported.
Does that mean there could be challenges if U.S. President
Donald Trump imposes tariffs
on Canadian goods?
Article 13 of the USMCA says you cannot place tariffs on digital goods, and that includes games, so that offers protection for our industry. That said, the USMCA is up for renegotiation in 2026, and the president has mused about doing it sooner, so we must stay vigilant.
We have great government relationships across all parties, we have a very strong partnership with the ESA in the United States and should anything happen we'll be at the table doing our part.
What are some of the biggest misconceptions about gamers?
One of the stats I love to share is that there's a 50/50 male to female split in gaming. Another one is the largest demographic of players is women over 50; there are more women over 50 playing games than boys under 18.
When they think about gaming a lot of people picture the teenager with the hoodie and the Mountain Dew and Doritos playing "Halo" in the basement, and we love that player, but gaming is also puzzles on your phone on the subway. Its families playing together, and we're starting to see a lot more of that. As the first generations of players are aging up, they're starting to play with their families.
Why do Canadians love gaming?
I think it's the best form of entertainment, but our research has shown that people are playing games to make new friends and build on existing friendships, as stress relief - they're reporting it helps with anxiety - and to build new skills, like problem solving. There was a recent study by neuroscientists at Western University that found people who play games perform as well on cognitive tests as those 13.7 years younger.
What can gamers expect
from the industry in the future?
With games like ("Grand Theft Auto 6") on the way, such a monolith has a spillover effect because it brings a bigger audience into gaming, so I'm super excited about that. The Switch 2 was just announced, and with the last generation of Xbox and PlayStation consoles, when they came out at the start of the pandemic, a lot of games were already in development.
We're only starting to see the results of a development cycle that utilizes all the technology that's available, and I think you'll see a lot of exciting things coming out this year.
In the future I think virtual reality will expand, but I don't think it will become a dominant mode; it's just not for everybody. What I'm more excited about is the potential for augmented reality ...
What I'm most excited about are the surprises. When the original Wii came out, suddenly you had these controllers you could wave around, and now your aunt was bowling at Thanksgiving, and it changed things. Who knows what the next iteration of that is?
This interview has been
edited and condensed.
CAPTION: Canada has "developed a very skilled workforce, combined with lots of great academic institutions," says Paul Fogolin, CEOof the Entertainment Software Association of Canada. "We have great immigration programs for highly skilled talent, which is critical, and unlike the film industry - where you come for a few months to shoot and then go home - games take years to make, so many put down roots."Michelle Mengsu ChangToronto Star

CREDIT: Jared Lindzon Special to the Star This interview has been edited and condensed.

Document 396 of 500

Early election call may be worth cheering

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/early-election-call-may-be-worth-cheering/docview/3162110656/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Early+election+call+may+be+worth+cheering&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When Premier Doug Ford pulled the trigger on a Feb. 27 election, hauling Ontarians out in the cold to vote more than a year earlier than necessary, it was possible (if you squinted hard) to see it as providing his opponents an opportunity.
From the point of view of many Torontonians - the ones who elected Mayor Olivia Chow when Ford campaigned against her - this is a chance to change a government they see as damaging, and even to prevent or reverse some of the damage. Safe consumption drug sites in Toronto ordered closed by Ford's government are still operating until after election day; bike lanes the province plans to rip out are still there on the street today; the controversial (and potentially Charter of Rights defying) encampment-clearing law has not yet been passed. Could all of that be reversed by a new government? Maybe.
And while it may be too late to save the trees on the West Island of Ontario Place, or prevent the closure of the Ontario Science Centre, certainly a new government could take a fresh look at what's going on there. Perhaps new oversight could get Metrolinx construction projects in gear. Perhaps Toronto city hall could be spared more of the endless, impulsive meddling that has seen the government and its finances reshaped on the premier's whims. Perhaps we could avoid any further ridiculous talk of tunnelling under the 401.
None of that was possible for opponents of Ford to hope for a week ago, because Ford did not have to face an election until well into 2026. Today, it is possible.
Which doesn't make it likely.
On Wednesday when the election call formally came, there was a conspicuous absence of audible cheering from Toronto city hall and from the opposition benches at Queen's Park. There's a reason, after all, that Ford called the election now - ahead of a federal election that could shift voter alignments, ahead of any Greenbelt investigation findings, with Ford's party ahead in polls by 20 per cent or more just as $200 tax rebate cheques are hitting people's mailboxes and Ford is putting on a widely applauded performance as Captain Canada in this sequel edition of the Trump Follies. A lot of the things I cited above as reasons some Torontonians want to get rid of him (bike lanes, encampment clearing) are reasons that people in other parts of Toronto and across Ontario like him.
Partly because of that, it remains to be seen, even from Toronto city hall's vantage point, how the parties vying to replace Ford position themselves on these issues, and on how they'll approach the beneficial things Ford is delivering to the city, from transit funding to the "new deal for Toronto" talks still underway.
If there's a chance here of changing the government and reversing course, there still also seems to be a likelihood of locking both in for another four years.
Still, that doesn't mean it's all without risk for Ford. The cautionary tale of premier David Peterson's early and ultimately unsuccessful early election call in the 1980s is already being widely cited. There's a chance voters who generally approve of Ford's performance ask why they're being called out into the cold so early - especially when the party of "gettin' it done" has clearly not finished the job. Building transit, building housing and fixing health care may be the three highest priorities Ford set for himself, and they are three areas where a lot of talk (and lots of dollars) have led to little in the way of measurable results.
To which Ford's response is that the threat of Donald Trump requires a new mandate. But that carries risks for Ford too. Ford's trips to Washington during the campaign could make him look diminished if he's ignored by American lawmakers and media. Trump could change his mind about tariffs in an instant, making the need for a new mandate redundant, or could make new and unexpected threats that make Ford's approach seem suddenly like a tactical error. Trump is an agent of chaos. Many American politicians have learned that when you hop a ride on the back of this wild tiger, it's not a safe bet you'll reach your desired destination.
Which is all to say, a lot can happen during a month-long campaign. Besides, Ford's opponents in the legislature and in the streets don't have a choice in this timing. The decision was Ford's, and he's made it.
And in doing so, he opens up a possibility for those who are unhappy with his government, including those in Toronto who think his obsession with this city has led us to a bad place. Ford has put the choice in the hands of voters; they could elect to change course.
It may seem unlikely. But the job now for those who want change is to argue, work and vote to make it more likely. It may not be the opportunity they were looking for. But it's the only one they're likely to get for a long time.
CAPTION: From the viewpoint of many Torontonians, the electionis a chance to change a government they see as damaging, Edward Keenan writes.Steve Russell Toronto Star file photo

Crombie slams Ford over transit: Liberal leader says some TTC users 'just don't feel safe', calls for more investment in security

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/crombie-slams-ford-over-transit/docview/3162110636/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Crombie+slams+Ford+over+transit%3A+Liberal+leader+says+some+TTC+users+%27just+don%27t+feel+safe%27%2C+calls+for+more+investment+in+security&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It is more dangerous to ride the TTC after seven years of Doug Ford's Progressive Conservative government, charges Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie.
"He'd rather spend $189 million on an unnecessary and expensive early election - when he has a majority government - rather than investing in things that matter to people on our public transit system," Crombie said Friday outside the Kennedy subway station in Scarborough.
"We know that over a million people ride the TTC each and every day, and for myself and for others, there are situations where you just don't feel safe," Crombie said, flanked by Scarborough Liberal candidates.
There are people who need help with mental health crises on transit as well as growing numbers of homeless people under Ford's watch, she added at the east-end transit hub as morning commuters streamed past.
"You have people using the TTC as a shelter from somewhere to come in off the cold and there are times on our subway cars where, quite frankly, you just feel unsafe."
There are more than 81,000 homeless people in the province, according to the Association of Municipalities of Ontario, and as many as 1,000 stay in tent encampments in Toronto parks and ride transit to keep warm.
Some can be seen sleeping on long benches on subways and at the back of streetcars.
Crombie said if the Liberals are elected Feb. 27, they would give the TTC and other municipal transit services like OC Transpo in Ottawa funds to hire an additional 300 transit constables, double funding for mobile crisis intervention teams, and add new cameras and other security measures, including the installation of costly "protective barriers" on subway station platforms.
Queen's Park would foot the tab, which she said she would disclose when the Liberals unveil their "fully costed" election platform in the days ahead.
Ford defended his record on transit Friday during a campaign event at a Niagara Falls construction company, but would not commit to building barriers in subway stations - a $4-billion idea that has been considered by the TTC and never implemented.
"We're building the largest transit system in North America," he said, referring the new Ontario Line subway, a Scarborough subway line, light rail transit lines in Hamilton and Brampton, along with a new "one fare" system saving money for riders on different GTA transit systems.
"We're going to continue expanding GO."
But the Tory leader ducked a question on the much-delayed Eglinton Crosstown light rail transit line, a project launched more than a decade ago by a previous Liberal government that is years overdue and remains unopened.
In December, Ford promised to earmark an additional $75.5 million for shelters and affordable housing and toughen trespassing laws to quell the explosion in tent encampments.
"We want to help people, we don't want to punish people," he said at the time.
"These encampments are taking over public spaces with illegal drug use happening out in the open, creating huge safety risks."
Critics have said the province needs to do far more to tackle the problems and that the extra funding is not enough to make a difference. Of the $75.5 million, $20 million is to expand shelters with most of the rest for "ready-to-build" affordable housing projects.
At a campaign stop in Windsor, NDP Leader Marit Stiles said there is increasing violence on public transit and against transit workers.
"We want to make it safe for people," she said with area candidates at a union hall, including Lisa Gretzky (Windsor West), the only New Democrat incumbent west of London after the party lost two local ridings to Ford's PCs in the 2022 provincial election.
The problems in transit go beyond safety, Stiles told reporters before cancelling an afternoon appearance in Sarnia.
"A lot of people right now are struggling with the cost of using public transit, and we have a government under Doug Ford and the Conservatives that have promised transit projects, bungled them and never opened many of them."
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson, Robert Benzie and Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Document 399 of 500

Ontario snowbirds worried their ballots won't arrive in time

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-snowbirds-worried-their-ballots-wont/docview/3162110619/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+snowbirds+worried+their+ballots+won%27t+arrive+in+time&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Diane Mandeville has long taken pride in being able to vote.
When Ontario's snap election was called she began looking in to applying to vote by mail and learned voting kits would be shipped out by FedEx. The only problem is Mandeville and her husband are snowbirds, spending their winter in Belize, a country FedEx doesn't operate in.
The couple are just two of many snowbirds and seniors from Ontario left wondering if their votes will be counted in the province's election on Feb. 27.
Mandeville, who lives in Simcoe County in the summer, said she knows people from London, Ottawa, Owen Sound and Brantford who are also spending the winter in Belize and are concerned about being able to vote.
"It's just very frustrating with the short time frame," said Mandeville. "Even snail mail could get here and get back to Ontario within two months, but a one month election call is crazy."
Ontario residents who will be outside of the province or unable to vote in person can do so by mail, but need to apply with Elections Ontario by Feb. 21 to get their voter kits in the mail, and must return them by 6 p.m. on Feb. 27 for their votes to be counted.
The voting kits will go out with a prepaid envelope for return, but people outside of Canada will have to pay for return postage themselves.
Mandeville said she and her husband don't get mail at all while in Belize, but that letters and packages are usually kept in a warehouse in Florida then shipped out to the Central American country all at once on a monthly basis.
"It takes a good month, six weeks, sometimes eight, to get anything," she said.
Mandeville told the Star she emailed Progressive Conservative leader Doug Ford's office with her concerns, but has yet to hear back. The couples' frustration is compounded by the fact that they're set to return to Ontario just a few days after the election on March 1.
Snowbirds' concerns about voting in this election have caught the attention of candidates running for premier.
"I feel for the seniors that aren't here that are worrying about whether or not they're going to get to vote," Ontario NDP leader Marit Stiles said while speaking in Windsor on Friday.
"But this is the cost of Doug Ford, right?" she quipped. "He calls a snap election - not to protect other peoples' jobs - because he's only looking out for his own job."
Ontario Liberal leader Bonnie Crombie also weighed in on the matter while speaking at Kennedy subway station.
Crombie described being contacted by someone already out of the country who was trying to apply to vote by mail but had a hard time uploading their drivers' license online.
"It was blurry once they uploaded it and they weren't accepting it," she said, blaming Ford's Progressive Conservatives for triggering a $189-million winter campaign before Elections Ontario could prepare.
"So there are a lot of challenges - of course, this is a snap election, an unnecessary, very expensive election and ... they haven't thought through all the pitfalls," said Crombie.
Ford countered by saying snowbirds aren't stranded when it comes to voting.
"You call Elections Ontario, there is a program that you can get a ballot down there and they will be able to vote," he told reporters in Niagara Falls Friday.
With files from Robert Benzie
CREDIT: Serena Austin Toronto Star With files from Robert Benzie

Document 401 of 500

An Ontario woman's $200 'tax rebate' cheque had an odd error and couldn't be deposited. What to do if yours does, too

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-womans-200-tax-rebate-cheque-had-odd/docview/3162383473/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=An+Ontario+woman%27s+%24200+%27tax+rebate%27+cheque+had+an+odd+error+and+couldn%27t+be+deposited.+What+to+do+if+yours+does%2C+too&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Lynda Chapple hasn't used her maiden name in 43 years. Save for her birth certificate and social insurance card, all her government IDs are in her married name. She has filed taxes countless times, and the government even has her new address in Selkirk, Ont., where she and her husband Randy moved to a little over a year ago.
So when her $200 "tax rebate" cheque from the province arrived in the mail earlier this week with a name she hasn't used since the 1980s, she was a bit surprised. Randy called their bank to see if the cheque could still be deposited, but was told they could not.
"I'm extremely frustrated," Lynda said. "If they have my all my information in my married name and have had for so many years, how can they do something so crazy?"
The bank also told Randy when he called that she wasn't alone in her situation, Lynda recounted to the Star over the phone Friday morning.
"He was told I was in good company."
Others online have complained that they have been unable to deposit their payouts from the province due to misprints as the rebate cheques announced by the Conservative government last fall arrive in mailboxes.
A spokesperson for the Ontario Ministry of Finance wrote in an emailed statement to the Star that the department was aware of the issue and cited a "technical error in the data used to administer the payments." The data, the spokesperson said, came from the Canada Revenue Agency.
Anyone who received a cheque with a previous or different name should contact Service Ontario at 1-833-351-0409 or 1-888-821-9056, according to the spokesperson, who added that the cheque will then be cancelled and replaced within 3-4 weeks.
When Lynda called about her issue, she was told it would take closer to 4-6 weeks.
In total, approximately 15 million Ontarians who filed their taxes in 2023 and their children should expect to get a cheque. The province estimates the payouts will cost the treasury $3 billion, despite a $6.6-billion budget deficit.
The misprinted names on some of the 7.5 million cheques sent out so far by the province is not the first issue to happen during the rollout of a payouts.
Earlier this week, Progressive Conservative party Leader Doug Ford blamed RBC following reports of people being unable to deposit their cheques. RBC later apologized and said the problem had been resolved.
The timing of the cheque rollout - which began on Jan. 17 - overlaps with Ford's call for a snap election earlier this week. Ontarians head to the polls on Feb. 27.
Opposition parties have accused the government of using taxpayer money to buy votes, with NDP Leader Marit Stiles previously calling it a "cynical" ploy when the plan was announced in the fall.
What should I do if I haven't received my cheque?
The cheque may still be on its way.
Ontarians can verify the cheque amounts for adults and children, the mailing address of the cheque and whether or not it has been mailed out on the Service Ontario website.
The system will require an account login, the last four digits of an individual's Social Insurance Number, their date of birth and the postal code of the mailing address used in the Canada Revenue Agency tax return.
A person can only check their status a maximum of three times a day, the website reads.
CAPTION: Progressive Conservative Party Leader Doug Ford announced the $200 cheque payouts in October.

Document 402 of 500

Doug Ford's opponents offer more than he'll admit as he counts on voters to sleepwalk to the polls

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-opponents-offer-more-than-hell-admit/docview/3162383462/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+opponents+offer+more+than+he%27ll+admit+as+he+counts+on+voters+to+sleepwalk+to+the+polls&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: To protect us from Donald Trump, Doug Ford needs the mother of all mandates.
Right, but what about the two women who also want to be premier?
Not up for the job. So said Ford in disqualifying Ontario's two major opposition leaders, while justifying the snap election he triggered this week.
He didn't quite say it's a man's job. But listen to his oration - and use your imagination:
"If you could just imagine - just imagine this, folks - imagine (Liberal) Bonnie Crombie or (New Democrat) Marit Stiles sitting across from President Trump, negotiating a deal, it would be an absolute disaster," he told reporters.
What, pray tell, makes that image so unimaginable? Lord knows.
"God help us if that ever happens," he intoned.
To be fair, he didn't explicitly complain about two female politicians aspiring to be premier in a man's world. It's the difference between sexist and subtext - subtle.
Now Ford has plunged us into a mid-winter election in which everything is up for grabs. For we face a grave threat from an American president who is waging economic and psychological warfare against Ontario.
They say that every crisis offers an opportunity. And Ford never misses an opportunity to mess with the opposition.
For all his faux gravitas, and his phoney calls for national unity, Ford is leading a provincial battle against his opposition rivals that is all about partisan disunity at home.
To be sure, that's democracy. But it's not unity, it's adversity.
It matters not to Ford that the NDP's Stiles and the Liberals' Crombie have proferred their support for whatever it takes to resist Trump and reassure Ontarians. He insisted on a democratic election to crush any opposition, while suppressing voter participation with a mid-winter campaign 15 months ahead of the legally - democratically - mandated election date in June 2026.
Just as Trump dreams of dominating the continent without any dissent, so too Ford fantasizes about ruling over Ontario without argument. The populist politician who has been premier for the past seven years now wants unchallenged power for another four years.
According to the public opinion polls, nothing will stop Ford from achieving his ambition of an unrivalled dynasty that endures for more than a decade. Nothing, that is, except those two women whom he can't imagine doing his job.
Let's revisit the quotation about his imagination for a moment:
"Imagine Bonnie Crombie or Marit Stiles sitting across from President Trump, negotiating a deal."
In fact, provincial premiers don't negotiate with superpower presidents. It's not in Ford's job description, nor is a White House meeting on his itinerary when he visits Washington as a candidate for two planned campaign stops in February.
In truth, Ford has been trash-talking Trump alongside Crombie and Stiles in the same breath - while talking up his own bona fides for weeks. So how exactly does he measure up - how do his qualifications for premier or negotiator compare to those of his opposition rivals?
To be clear, he didn't cite their lack of educational or professional qualifications: Stiles and Crombie are both university graduates (Crombie also has an MBA), whereas Ford has no diploma to his name after dropping out of college within months.
To be candid, Ford didn't talk about their lack of work experience, since he's never had to look for work: Stiles worked for a labour union, while Crombie joined major multinational firms and served three terms as mayor of Mississauga; Ford was hired by his father to work at the family's label printing firm.
To be precise, he didn't draw any contrasts between his negotiating savvy and theirs: Ford was bamboozled by developers on the Greenbelt scandal, snookered by big breweries on a billion-dollar beer boondoggle, and suckered by a foreign multinational on the Ontario Place lease; Stiles boasts that she bargained with major American firms on behalf of a national union, while Crombie has negotiated her share of deals as a big city mayor, free from Ford-like scandals.
No one should underestimate Ford. Equally, it would be a mistake for Ford or anyone to underestimate the opposition leaders. They are rookie leaders, untested and unknown yet successful and savvy, each in their own way, in ways that he apparently cannot imagine.
A campaign can be static or dynamic. Earthquakes do happen - imperceptible tremors and fault lines that rearrange the landscape in unexpected ways, as then-premier David Peterson learned in 1990 when his governing Liberals lost a huge lead after calling an early election.
What would it take to shake things up again? An opposition that finds its footing and throws the Tories off balance.
As Ford covets the mother of all mandates, he might be mindful of the two women who want his job. If one of them breaks through to connect with voters, Ford's fears of "an absolute disaster" could yet come true - for him.
* * * *
Speaking of opposition leaders, I'll be hosting them at the Democracy Forum at Toronto Metropolitan University: Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie on Feb. 4 at 1:30 p.m., and NDP Leader Marit Stiles on Feb. 5 at noon. Both events will be livestreamed on thestar.com.
CAPTION: Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford speaks during a visit to Walker Construction in St. Catharines on Jan. 31, 2025.

Document 403 of 500

Ontario snowbirds worried their mail in ballots won't arrive in time for the provincial election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-snowbirds-worried-their-mail-ballots-wont/docview/3162383426/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+snowbirds+worried+their+mail+in+ballots+won%27t+arrive+in+time+for+the+provincial+election&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Diane Mandeville has long taken pride in being able to vote.
When Ontario's snap election was called she began looking in to applying to vote by mail and learned voting kits would be shipped out by FedEx. The only problem is Mandeville and her husband are snowbirds, spending their winter in Belize, a country FedEx doesn't operate in.
The couple are just two of many snowbirds and seniors from Ontario left wondering if their votes will be counted in the province's election on Feb. 27.
Mandeville, who lives in Simcoe County in the summer, said she knows people from London, Ottawa, Owen Sound and Brantford who are also spending the winter in Belize and are concerned about being able to vote.
"It's just very frustrating with the short time frame," said Mandeville. "Even snail mail could get here and get back to Ontario within two months, but a one month election call is crazy."
Ontario residents who will be outside of the province or unable to vote in person can do so by mail, but need to apply with Elections Ontario by Feb. 21 to get their voter kits in the mail, and must return them by 6 p.m. on Feb. 27 for their votes to be counted.
The voting kits will go out with a prepaid envelope for return, but people outside of Canada will have to pay for return postage themselves.
Mandeville said she and her husband don't get mail at all while in Belize, but that letters and packages are usually kept in a warehouse in Florida then shipped out to the Central American country all at once on a monthly basis.
"It takes a good month, six weeks, sometimes eight, to get anything," she said.
Mandeville told the Star she emailed Progressive Conservative leader Doug Ford's office with her concerns, but has yet to hear back. The couples' frustration is compounded by the fact that they're set to return to Ontario just a few days after the election on March 1.
Snowbirds' concerns about voting in this election have caught the attention of candidates running for premier.
"I feel for the seniors that aren't here that are worrying about whether or not they're going to get to vote," Ontario NDP leader Marit Stiles said while speaking in Windsor on Friday.
"But this is the cost of Doug Ford, right?" she quipped. "He calls a snap election - not to protect other peoples' jobs - because he's only looking out for his own job."
Ontario Liberal leader Bonnie Crombie also weighed in on the matter while speaking at Kennedy subway station.
Crombie described being contacted by someone already out of the country who was trying to apply to vote by mail but had a hard time uploading their drivers' license online.
"It was blurry once they uploaded it and they weren't accepting it," she said, blaming Ford's Progressive Conservatives for triggering a $189-million winter campaign before Elections Ontario could prepare.
"So there are a lot of challenges - of course, this is a snap election, an unnecessary, very expensive election and ... they haven't thought through all the pitfalls," said Crombie.
Ford countered by saying snowbirds aren't stranded when it comes to voting.
"You call Elections Ontario, there is a program that you can get a ballot down there and they will be able to vote," he told reporters in Niagara Falls Friday.
With files from Robert Benzie
CAPTION: Diane Mandeville and her husband are spending their winter in Belize and are doubtful their voting kits will make it there and back in the mail in time for election day.

Document 404 of 500

Donald Trump says U.S. tariffs on imports from Canada will begin Saturday

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/donald-trump-says-u-s-tariffs-on-imports-canada/docview/3161901516/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Donald+Trump+says+U.S.+tariffs+on+imports+from+Canada+will+begin+Saturday&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-It's a brief reprieve. But for Donald Trump's doubters, or Canadians living in hope who thought he wouldn't act on his tariff threat, the White House says forget that.
The U.S. president said he will fulfil his threat to impose 25 per cent surcharges on Canadian and Mexican imports starting Feb. 1, in order to halt fentanyl and illegal immigrants at America's northern and southern borders, and to correct what he calls unfair trade deficits.
However, Trump suggested there would be a staggered rollout, telling reporters he will target Canadian oil and gas shipments with a lower 10 per cent tariff rate, setting a later date for implementation of Feb. 18.
Trump said he will "eventually" aim sectoral tariffs at imports of semiconductor chips, "next month" at steel and aluminum products, "ultimately copper," and pharmaceuticals at some unspecified date.
"We're not looking for a concession," Trump said, insisting that tariffs are a way for him to raise "big money" in revenue.
"You see the power of the tariff. I mean, the tariff is good, and nobody can compete with us, because we have by far the biggest piggy bank," he said.
The president warned he will "absolutely" soon impose tariffs on the European Union as well.
Trump rejected suggestions that tariffs would trigger everyday prices to rise.
"Tariffs don't cause inflation. They cause success. They cause big success. So we're gonna have great success. There could be some temporary, short-term disruption," he acknowledged, "and people will understand that." But he said in the long run, it would force other countries, such as those in Europe, to drop barriers to American imports like farm products.
Earlier Friday, White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt had thrown cold water on a Reuters report that suggested Trump would delay the application of tariffs against Canada and Mexico until March 1, calling the story "false."
"These are promises made and promises kept by the president," said Leavitt, repeating that Trump's objective is to halt "the illegal fentanyl that they have sourced and allowed to distribute into our country."
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, in remarks Friday morning before meeting with an advisory council on Canada-U.S. relations, said he would not "sugar-coat" the impact that tariffs could be expected to have on Canadian exports and the overall economy.
Some economists say Trump's threatened 25 per cent "across the board" tariffs would put Canada into a recession, with estimates that U.S. tariffs could cause up to a three per cent drop in economic activity here.
"Our nation could be facing difficult times in the coming weeks," said Trudeau.
The Canadian government will retaliate with countermeasures against any U.S. tariffs imposed by Trump, Trudeau said, and would support Canadian workers and industries who may be hard hit, especially if tariffs are sustained.
"If the president does choose to implement any tariffs against Canada, we're ready with a response, a purposeful, forceful but reasonable, immediate response," he said. "It's not what we want, but if he moves forward, we will also act."
Canada has planned an escalating series of measures that could potentially ramp up to impose counter-tariffs on $150 billion worth of American imports, and could include limits or export tariffs on Canadian energy shipments to the U.S.
The federal government says Canada provides more of the crude oil the U.S. imports than the rest of the world combined, a trading relationship that it says allows for "reliable supply and lower pump prices for Americans."
Trump and his threats have shaped a lot of the political rhetoric in a Liberal leadership race to replace Trudeau on March 9.
Earlier Friday, the two front-runners Mark Carney and Chrystia Freeland repeated their arguments, with Carney saying he stands "foursquare" behind efforts by Trudeau's cabinet team in Washington trying prevent the tariffs.
"President Trump thinks Canada will cave in. We will never, ever bow down to a bully," said Carney, who has backed dollar-for-dollar countermeasures.
Freeland, on the other hand, took a tougher line. She, too, backs a dollar-for-dollar response, but one that is more forceful and immediate. On Friday, she said Trudeau should target up to $200 billion in American imports and take direct aim at Trump's adviser Elon Musk, owner of Tesla.
"Canada must threaten to impose a 100 per cent tariff on all Tesla vehicles and a 100 per cent tariff on U.S. wine, beer, and spirits if unfair tariffs are imposed on Canadians tomorrow," Freeland said in a release.
Trudeau has not tipped his government's hand on what American products would be hit with countermeasures, but federal officials have confirmed the first tranche would be targeted at politically sensitive American products, and actions would ramp up over time.
Trudeau said an advisory group of former premiers, business and union leaders are helping to make Ottawa's argument in the U.S. that Trump's tariffs against Canada "would have disastrous consequences for the United States, putting American jobs at risk, raising prices on Americans and undermining our collective security."
Trudeau's remarks prompted questions during a White House briefing, with reporters asking Leavitt if the president could guarantee that tariffs won't stoke inflation, or increase prices at gas pumps and in grocery stores for Americans. One journalist asked if Canada's vow to retaliate showed the U.S. is on the brink of a trade war.
Leavitt bristled and said, "I don't think so."
Trump's purpose, she said, is to halt fentanyl and migrants from "pouring into" America, adding "the president is intent on doing this, and I think Justin Trudeau would be wise to talk to President Trump directly before pushing outlandish comments like that to the media."
Reuters had also reported that the Trump administration would provide a process for the countries to seek exemptions for certain imports. Leavitt did not directly answer when asked if the tariffs would be in place for the long term or whether Trump would remove them if Canada, Mexico or China offered concessions.
"If the president at any time decides to roll back those tariffs, I'll leave it to him to make that decision, but starting tomorrow, those tariffs will be in place," she said.
In Niagara Falls, Ontario Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford fully backed Trudeau's retaliation plan.
"Dollar for dollar, tariff for tariff, the federal government has my full support to hit back and hit back hard," Ford said as he campaigned for a third term as premier in a Feb. 27 vote.
"If President Trump proceeds with these tariffs, Canada can and should make him regret them," Ford told reporters, adding he is prepared to take "enormous action" to protect Ontario workers, businesses and families.
Ford called an early election this week seeking a mandate to spend "tens of billions" if necessary to shield Ontario's economy from tariff fallout with up to 500,000 jobs on the line.
But he has yet to lay out the full details of that economic support plan.
"We can't determine who needs the money until we see the tariffs," Ford said. "Then we'll allocate those funds to protect people, protect their businesses and the communities."
With files from Rob Ferguson
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau says Canada is ready to deliver a "purposeful, forceful but reasonable immediate" response if U.S. President Donald Trump imposes tariffs on Canadian imports. (Jan. 31, 2025 / The Canadian Press)President Donald Trump speaks about a collision involving an American Airlines passenger jet and a military helicopter during a press briefing at the White House in Washington on Jan. 30, 2025.

Document 405 of 500

Doug Ford wants to protect us from Donald Trump. But who will protect us from Doug Ford?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-wants-protect-us-donald-trump-who-will/docview/3161901413/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+wants+to+protect+us+from+Donald+Trump.+But+who+will+protect+us+from+Doug+Ford%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford, who is already premier and whose Progressive Conservatives occupy 79 of the 124 seats at Queen's Park, has repeatedly stated that he needs a "strong mandate" to deal with U.S. president Donald Trump's tariffs. On Tuesday, having triggered a multimillion-dollar election one and a half years early and in the dead of winter ostensibly to secure such a mandate, already-premier Ford revealed his party's new campaign slogan: "Protect Ontario."Protect Ontario what? Hospitals? Schools? Place? Can't be: Ontario hospitals are overcrowded, Ontario schools are underfunded and Ontario Place has been leased to foreign interests. No, "from America" is what's implied. And Ford has done everything in his power to make you infer it: if you can't give the voters something to rally around, give them something to rally against.This is nothing we haven't seen before: right-wing populism, which these days is just mainstream conservatism, is remarkably consistent across space and time, and after almost seven years of Ford rule in Ontario, things have gone more or less as you'd expect.You've got your program cuts (e.g., safe-consumption sites), your creeping privatization (e.g., the health-care system) and your vibes-based policymaking (e.g., removing bike lanes to ease traffic congestion). You've got your insistent claims of fiscal prudence and your biggest, most expensive cabinet in history. And you've got your millionaire man of the people professing to shield you from threats foreign (Trump, explicitly) and domestic (cyclists, presumably).It's all very predictable stuff, and if, as predicted, Ford wins on February 27, there's a good chance you'll get more of the same. But after the bike lanes are removed and the safe-consumption sites are eradicated, after the Ontario Science Centre meets whatever grim fate awaits it and Ontario Place becomes a parking garage, after Ford realizes his vision for this province, what will be left of it to protect? The Bradford Bypass?See, this is one of the many reasons cultural and educational institutions matter: they give us a sense of our shared heritage, of what Ontario has been and could be and of why that's worth protecting. I thought conservatives loved that sort of thing, but politicians like Ford keep proving me wrong. No one's patriotic feelings are stirred by a privately operated luxury spa. No one's sense of self is located in corner-store beer.This is not a liberal-versus-conservative thing. It was a conservative who established Ontario Place. It was a conservative who established the Ontario Science Centre. It was a conservative who established our system of community colleges and, honest to God, our public broadcaster. In fact, it was all the same guy: the late Bill Davis, premier from 1971 to 1985 and, one can only assume, sworn enemy of Doug Ford, who sold off the first thing, let rot the second thing, hobbled the third thing, and mostly ignored the fourth thing.Meanwhile, what has Ford contributed to the cultural life of this province? What has he contributed to our sense of pride in Ontario? If he wants to protect us from America, he could start by showing us a future that's worth protecting. His sloganeering might briefly rouse the jingoist inside us, but it's leaders like Davis who manage to instill things that are more enduring, things that can't be expressed via baseball cap.Alas, also things that don't send voters to the polls. And that's the point, really. Ontarians may be united against Trump (although even that's questionable), but that doesn't mean they're united. A substantial number of Ontarians - a substantial number of whom won't vote - don't want to give Ford a stronger mandate, because they saw what he did with the first two. Saving the economy from Trump's tariffs is obviously important, but so is being able to afford a house, see a family doctor, and bicycle to work without getting killed."Protect Ontario," sure. Only give us something to protect.
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford speaks as he arrives for a first ministers meeting in Ottawa on Jan. 15, 2025.
CREDIT: Graeme Bayliss
