Document 406 of 500

Doug Ford vs. Bonnie Crombie and the rise of the trucker hat as a powerful political tool

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-vs-bonnie-crombie-rise-trucker-hat-as/docview/3161901384/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+vs.+Bonnie+Crombie+and+the+rise+of+the+trucker+hat+as+a+powerful+political+tool&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In politics, effective messaging is everything. We have limited attention spans, and even more so than ever, a picture is worth a thousand words. Enter the political slogan hat.
In mid-January, Ontario Premier Doug Ford showed up in Ottawa for a meeting between the premiers and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau wearing a hat reading "Canada Is Not For Sale." The hat was both a refute to U.S. President Donald Trump's threats to make Canada the 51st state, and a nod to Trump's own embrace of the red "Make America Great Again" hat that has become a potent symbol of the new American right.
Then this week, Ontario Liberal leader Bonnie Crosbie wore her own trucker hat on the election campaign trail in Barrie, printed with the slightly less catchy phrase: "Real Leaders Fix Healthcare."
"The hat was a nod to the current political moment, where slogans and gimmicks are too common in our politics," Ashley Csanady, Liberal strategist for Crombie's campaign, told the Star. "That we can subvert the statements of Doug Ford and Donald Trump into something that actually means something to people: health care that works for you. There is no bigger issue than health care in Ontario - and fixing health care is real and concrete action."
The MAGA hat, of course, is the blueprint. Now ubiquitous, it was actually a fluke. Corey Lewandowski, who was campaign manager for Trump on his 2016 run, has said there were no marketing professionals involved in its conception.
Just a standard red hat with "default" Times New Roman text, to quote CNN's Cassie Spodak, it was introduced by the merch-happy Trump campaign. Trump, himself a marketer at heart, first wore the hat at a rally about a month after his initial campaign launch, and it caught on fast with his supporters. The original "made-in-America" version was soon subsumed by cheaper dupes manufactured offshore.
It was an efficient visual tool. Like the "Sneetches on Beaches" - those Dr. Seussian critters divided by those with "stars on thars" and those without - the sea of red hats in crowds served to instantly demarcate Trump's supporters.
The hats' slogan became so recognizable that it became an acronym and the name of a movement. A proud badge of belonging and an object of scorn for detractors, the hats were the definition of polarizing, even though President Joe Biden wore one once, at a 9/11 ceremony, in an attempt to create solidarity. Actress Alyssa Milano, an outspoken Trump opponent, tweeted in 2019 that "The red MAGA hat is the new white hood."
The day after Trump was elected in 2016, former Ontario Court Justice Bernd Zabel wore a MAGA hat to court in Hamilton. He was later suspended after 81 complaints were filed with the Ontario Judicial Council about displaying political affiliations in what should be a neutral courtroom.
So why are trucker hats such a potent symbol? First, they are basically walking billboards, advertising a short and sharp message that has to fit across a forehead. But this style of hat also symbolizes the everyman; they are working-class coded. That's a powerful tool for politicians looking to connect with the masses, something Trump, the "anti-elite" billionaire, harnessed to help his followers connect to him.
Originally, ball caps were worn by baseball players circa 1860 in Brooklyn. The New York City borough was known for its style even then, and the "Brooklyn-style" hat soon graduated the baseball diamonds to the streets.
Trucker hats, which are worn higher on the forehead, have their origins in agriculture. In the 1970s, farm and feed supply companies handed out branded hats to farmers and truck drivers to market their products. These workers needed sun protection, hence the broad brim, ventilating mesh back and sweat-absorbing foam front, which sat tall on the head to better display the brand name.
Then came the early 2000s, when celebrities such as Ashton Kutcher and Pharrell Williams co-opted the rural-coded look for parties and premieres. Nicole Richie and Paris Hilton wore trucker hats throughout their reality show "The Simple Life," starting in 2003, in which the Beverly Hills socialites moonlit as menial labourers in the Midwest. Young Hollywood wore truckloads of caps by Von Dutch, an American brand then headed by French designer Christian Audigier, which took the basic trucker hat and charged hundreds for it just by adding a logo.
You would think that cultural plunge would make it go away, but no. Justin Bieber brought the style back circa 2010, wearing trucker caps backwards. And it resurfaced again in Y2K-obsessed 2024, with Kendall Jenner's tequila brand 818 launching branded trucker hats and singer Charlie XCX paying homage to the original trend with a song called "Von Dutch" on her smash album, "Brat."
For good, for evil, or for fashion, putting a message on a hat is a brilliant way to communicate - our eyes go to people's faces first. The trick is finding a slogan that will cut through the noise.
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford at a first ministers meeting in Ottawa on Jan. 15, 2025.Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie with Barrie Liberal candidate Dr. Rose Zacharias during a campaign stop outside the Barrie Primary Care Campus on Jan. 29, 2025.Then Republican presidential candidate Donald Trump at a 2016 rally in Sacramento.Donald Trump throws autographed red hats to the crowd during the Bedminster Invitational LIV Golf tournament on Aug. 13, 2023.Ontario Premier Doug Ford and Liberal party leader Bonnie Crombie both wore slogan trucker hats in January. Photo:Hats were passed out before Donald Trump spoke to the City of New York Police Benevolent Association at an event on Aug. 14, 2020.

Document 407 of 500

Bonnie Crombie says Doug Ford has put transit users at risk: 'You just don't feel safe'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/bonnie-crombie-says-doug-ford-has-put-transit/docview/3161901343/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Bonnie+Crombie+says+Doug+Ford+has+put+transit+users+at+risk%3A+%27You+just+don%27t+feel+safe%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It is more dangerous to ride the TTC after seven years of Doug Ford's Progressive Conservative government, charges Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie.
"He'd rather spend $189 million on an unnecessary and expensive early election - when he has a majority government - rather than investing in things that matter to people on our public transit system," Crombie said Friday outside the Kennedy subway station in Scarborough.
"We know that over a million people ride the TTC each and every day, and for myself and for others, there are situations where you just don't feel safe," Crombie said, flanked by Scarborough Liberal candidates.
There are people who need help with mental health crises on transit as well as growing numbers of homeless people under Ford's watch, she added at the east-end transit hub as morning commuters streamed past.
"You have people using the TTC as a shelter from somewhere to come in off the cold and there are times on our subway cars where, quite frankly, you just feel unsafe."
There are more than 81,000 homeless people in the province, according to the Association of Municipalities of Ontario, and as many as 1,000 stay in tent encampments in Toronto parks and ride transit to keep warm.
Some can be seen sleeping on long benches on subways and at the back of streetcars.
Crombie said if the Liberals are elected Feb. 27, they would give the TTC and other municipal transit services like OC Transpo in Ottawa funds to hire an additional 300 transit constables, double funding for mobile crisis intervention teams, and add new cameras and other security measures, including the installation of costly "protective barriers" on subway station platforms.
Queen's Park would foot the tab, which she said she would disclose when the Liberals unveil their "fully costed" election platform in the days ahead.
Ford defended his record on transit Friday during a campaign event at a Niagara Falls construction company, but would not commit to building barriers in subway stations - a $4 billion idea that has been considered by the TTC and never implemented.
"We're building the largest transit system in North America," he said, referring the new Ontario Line subway, a Scarborough subway line, light rail transit lines in Hamilton and Brampton, along with a new "one fare" system saving money for riders on different GTA transit systems.
"We're going to continue expanding GO."
But the Tory leader ducked a question on the much-delayed Eglinton Crosstown light rail transit line, a project launched more than a decade ago by a previous Liberal government that is years overdue and remains unopened.
In December, Ford promised to earmark an additional $75.5 million for shelters and affordable housing and toughen trespassing laws to quell the explosion in tent encampments.
"We want to help people, we don't want to punish people," he said at the time.
"These encampments are taking over public spaces with illegal drug use happening out in the open, creating huge safety risks."
Critics have said the province needs to do far more to tackle the problems and that the extra funding is not enough to make a difference. Of the $75.5 million, $20 million is to expand shelters with most of the rest for "ready-to-build" affordable housing projects.
At a campaign stop in Windsor, NDP Leader Marit Stiles said there is increasing violence on public transit and against transit workers.
"We want to make it safe for people," she said with area candidates at a union hall, including Lisa Gretzky (Windsor West), the only New Democrat incumbent west of London after the party lost two local ridings to Ford's PCs in the 2022 provincial election.
The problems in transit go beyond safety, Stiles told reporters before cancelling an afternoon appearance in Sarnia.
"A lot of people right now are struggling with the cost of using public transit, and we have a government under Doug Ford and the Conservatives that have promised transit projects, bungled them and never opened many of them."
CAPTION: Ontario Liberal Party Leader Bonnie Crombie speaks to media at Kennedy subway station in Scarborough on Jan. 31, 2025.

Document 408 of 500

The military is back on the agenda. We may be forced to relive dark days of our history

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/military-is-back-on-agenda-we-may-be-forced/docview/3161732566/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+military+is+back+on+the+agenda.+We+may+be+forced+to+relive+dark+days+of+our+history&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: So, militarism is back. Defence - itself a euphemism, the term used to be War Department - is, hectored the Globe and Mail in an editorial, "one of the two areas of statehood that require a firm adult hand." Stop snickering, war has gravitas.
I can see why they want it to be prominent, from the right wing/Poilievre POV. Poilievre's Conservatives are finally getting their election, but they've prepped on only two issues: Justin gotta go, and Axe the Tax. Suddenly, their cupboard is bare and they've nothing to stock it with. They literally have no policies.
Cue the manly virtues of warmaking. And in fact it's widespread. Trump is demanding everyone in NATO spend not just two per cent of GDP on "defence," he's upped that to five and NATO's fallen in line. (Sound off!) Lithuania is going for six, which will surely make the Russians cancel their invasion plans. Sadly, there are a few stragglers - Spain, Italy, Belgium, us.
There's more to it than nostalgia for war movies and as usual the more is economic. We're in an economic crisis. The last one this grave was the Great Depression of the 1930s. It resulted from what economist Karl Polanyi called a utopian experiment in unfettered, "self-regulating" markets, which began in the 1800s and culminated in two world wars and the Depression.
This one is the result of the neo-liberal onslaught of the last 45 years begun under Reagan and Thatcher, featuring free trade, deregulation, financialization and letting capitalism do its thing. In other words, a utopian experiment in unfettered markets. It worked, for a handful of odious tech titans, who were ranged behind Trump at his inauguration. All others, like the young, might survive if they scuffle.
The 1930s Depression was mitigated by FDR's New Deal but was only ended by the Second World War, in which the government fettered the market and mobilized it for war, employing everyone it didn't put in uniform. The great fear at that war's end wasn't the Red Menace of Stalinist Russia but the return of the Depression. It never happened because the wartime economy was extended into peacetime through the Cold War and permanent militarization, mostly in the U.S., but extending to others via the defence "umbrella" it provided.
What is the solution to the current catastrophe proposed by Doug Ford, Pierre Poilievre and their legions? Unleash the free market and let it rip. This meets the classic definition of insanity. (I loved it when Vassy Kapelos asked Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre if he really thinks he can create millions of homes merely by not enforcing zoning laws.)
OK, time for the elephant in the room: Russian (or Chinese) aggression. If you're going to build support for a militarized economy, you need an enemy. I don't mean antagonisms don't exist, but if they didn't, they'd need to be invented. Failing that, the conflicts could often be resolved. Do you really think Putin wants the headache of ruling France or Germany? As for us, the only real threat of invasion we face now, comes, explicitly, from the U.S. Yet, the military we're told to embrace, is aimed at the Mideast or Europe.
It's easy to forget why the world managed to avoid major wars in the last half of the past century, except for what the big powers tended to view as sideshows like Vietnam - despite war's utility in nation-building, its economic benefits etc.
It was embodied in the UN charter of 1945: "We the peoples of the United Nations, determined to save succeeding generations from the scourge of war, which twice in our lifetime has brought untold sorrow ... ." Its authors weren't moral exemplars, they were the same affluent, powerful types who always run things. But they'd been through an awful grinder and it changed them.
Now militarism is back and those lessons have been forgotten, it's the nature of our species. If we lived longer - to 300 or 400 - we might remember not to forget what things like war and "defence" really consist of.
CAPTION: U.S. President Donald J Trump salutes as he participates in a Reviewing the Troops Ceremony as part of his Inaugural activities in Emancipation Hall in the U.S. Capitol in Washington, D.C., on Jan. 20.

Document 409 of 500

Doug Ford wants us to focus on Donald Trump. But what about his own disastrous record?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-wants-us-focus-on-donald-trump-what/docview/3161732240/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+wants+us+to+focus+on+Donald+Trump.+But+what+about+his+own+disastrous+record%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When U.S. President Donald Trump threatened to impose massive tariffs on the Canadian economy, he inadvertently did the premier of Ontario a huge political favour.
Doug Ford had been laying the groundwork for a self-serving snap election since at least last fall, but it was Trump who gave him the perfect pretext to call one. In order to protect "the livelihoods of workers on factory floors, construction sites and in local stores," Ford wrote in op-ed for the National Post ahead of Tuesday's election call, "I'm asking the people for a strong mandate - a strong, stable, four-year mandate that will outlive and outlast the Trump administration."
Going to the polls early has enabled the Progressive Conservatives to skirt the regulations that usually limit government-sponsored advertising in the lead up to a campaign, allowing them to make liberal use of public ad dollars before the dropping of the writ. It also likely pre-empts any criminal charges related to the Greenbelt scandal, which saw Ford's government effectively conspire with a tiny group of handpicked developers to inflate the value of protected public land it planned to sell off (before a public backlash compelled it to abandon the scheme). By some happy accident, every Ontarian of voting age is also getting a cheque for $200 just as the election kicks off.
More than anything else, however, Trump's still-looming tariff threat gives Ford a ready-made narrative that conveniently elides the kinds of questions voters typically ask about incumbent governments seeking re-election: Has life gotten easier or harder? Do things generally seem to be working? Are we better off now than we were before? (And if it also helps people forget about the "unwavering support" he's expressed for Trump in the past, so much the better.) Ford's desire to sidestep these questions makes sense, because the answers paint a decidedly unflattering portrait of his government and its record.
For all his folksy bluster and supposed populist billing, Ontario's premier hails from a long line of politicians who have mistakenly viewed corporate patronage as synonymous with the wider public good. Accordingly, the Ford government's approach in major areas, from housing and health care to wages and work, has typically been to give as much latitude as possible to profiteers and private industry, shrug its shoulders and hope for the best. After six-plus years, the upshot is a province where vital services are crumbling, economic insecurity is rampant, and the necessities of life are more expensive than ever.
Consider housing. Following his election victory in 2018, Ford moved to eliminate rent control on newly constructed units, the purported justification being that it would help address shortages by creating "market-based incentives for supply growth." In a similar vein, the government has steadily eliminated regulations related to health, safety, and environmental protection with a view to lowering costs for developers. The result? Even Ford's own finance minister, Peter Bethlenfalvy, concedes it will fall well short of its own targets, with the number of new housing starts last year being lower than in any other province. Between January 2021 and July 2024, meanwhile, the cost of housing rose an astonishing 24 percent, with rents also continuing to soar.
According to one estimate cited by Toronto Metropolitan University's Bryan Evans and York University's Carlo Fanelli in a new study of the Ford government, nearly half of all Ontario households now spend at least 30 per cent of their total income on shelter. For countless others, the situation is even worse. Last year, per a recent report from the Association of Municipalities of Ontario, some 81,000 people were homeless - an increase of tens of thousands since 2022. Between April 2023 and March 2024, a record 1,001,150 Ontarians (or roughly 1 in 16) were forced to visit a food bank.
There are, of course, various factors at play not directly or wholly related to provincial policy, from high interest rates and federal inaction to the spiralling cost of groceries. But the Ford government's dogmatic preference for cuts, deregulation, and market-based approaches to every problem bears a great deal of responsibility for Ontario's growing climate of economic insecurity and social pain.
Social assistance rates have been slashed to barebones levels. Chronic underinvestment in vital programs, meanwhile, has steadily weakened public services: the province's Financial Accountability Office revealed last June that the government's spending plans are nearly $4 billion short of what would be needed even to maintain them at current levels. Here, the effects of underfunding have been exacerbated by other policies, notably Bill 124, which capped annual wage increases for public sector workers at 1 per cent and directly impacted the incomes of roughly one in three households.
Though ultimately ruled unconstitutional by the courts, the legislation has nonetheless helped drive massive staffing shortages, especially in the health-care sector. According to figures obtained by the Canadian Press - figures that the Ford government tried to keep secret - the province is now short some 33,000 nurses and 50,000 personal support workers. Meanwhile, the Ontario Medical Association has reported a further shortage of 2,000 doctors. (The consequences of such shortages are anything but abstract: according to one estimate, some 11,581 patients died awaiting surgeries and other procedures in 2022 alone.) Instead of simply funding health care at adequate levels, Ford's government has increasingly sought to make it a vehicle for private profit - opening the door to a U.S.-style system that is costlier, less efficient and geared more towards the interests of investors than the needs of patients.
While the PCs are going into the current election campaign with a lead in the polls, plenty of voters have clearly grown more pessimistic about their government and its direction. Ford himself has the lowest approval ratings of any premier in Canada. Last March, an Angus Reid survey asking Ontarians to rate the government's performance in key issue areas, including health care, inflation, housing affordability, and education, found that only 23 per cent believe it's doing a "good job" - down from 42 per cent in 2019.
Given all this, it's little wonder that Doug Ford wants to spend the next month shadow-boxing with Donald Trump. He should be made to answer for his government's disastrous record instead.
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford speaks as he arrives for a first ministers meeting in Ottawa on Jan. 15, 2025.
CREDIT: Luke Savage

If city sells off its infrastructure, we will still pay for city services, just not through taxes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/if-city-sells-off-infrastructure-we-will-still/docview/3161731925/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=If+city+sells+off+its+infrastructure%2C+we+will+still+pay+for+city+services%2C+just+not+through+taxes&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There is a better way to pay for Toronto's future than through property tax hikes, Jan. 22
Offering his alternative to a property tax increase, Giles Gherson speaks of "leveraging" and "strategic opportunities" but leaves many of us unclear about what he is proposing. The public wants public infrastructure maintained and updated, he correctly says, but they don't want to pay taxes to do it. So, city: sell or lease the public's infrastructure to pension plans and private hedge funds, bank the proceeds, and don't raise taxes. The pension plan or hedge fund will then spend its money on maintenance and upgrading. They will ensure they get a solid eight to 10 per cent profit out of it, either by charging users (rents, water rates, hydro rates, road tolls) or simply charging costs back to the city . Result: we the people pay more for the same maintenance and upgrading, but we don't pay it in the form of taxes. It becomes invisible. Neat trick.
Bill Winegard, Etobicoke
Privatize management of Toronto's assets? Just look at the Eglinton Crosstown
So the Toronto Region Board of Trade would have us look to the west (Copenhagen) and the east (Hong Kong) for examples of how well private management of public assets can work to purportedly offset municipal funding gaps. Instead, they should look north, all the way to Eglinton Avenue to the public-private Crosstown LRT, years late and billions over budget. Or a little further north to the 407 highway. Built with public money and sold for a song by Conservative privatizer Mike Harris, the 407 remains underused while residents idle in gridlock. Instead of handing billions to the private sector whose profits come at great public expense, Ontario cities need new revenue powers such as a commercial parking levy.
Andria Babbington, Toronto
It's already easy to shop Canadian
Make it easy to buy Canadian, Jan. 27
It may be nice, but Canadians do not need a maple leaf sticker to buy Canadian products. I have been shopping Canadian as my first choice for years, followed by products from developing countries and other Western countries, besides the U.S. Fortunately, products available in Canada from perishables, to jams, oils, washer/dryers, furniture, etc., all have country of origin labels so making a choice is very easy. To further respond to Trump's tariffs, Canadians should stop crossing the border to buy groceries, gas, etc., and not patronize American companies, e.g. Walmart, Costco, Uber, etc.
Aquil Ali, Toronto
How everyday people can respond to Trump's tariffsDonald Trump signals 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian imports could be coming Feb. 1, Jan. 21
While others ponder an appropriate national response to Trump's tariffs, here is something we can all do now: Cancel plans to holiday in the U.S.A. That won't cost Canada a dime in lost trade.
Gregory Sorbara, Toronto
Don't buy American
In Canada we vote with our ballots. On the international stage we vote with our wallets. Why are we waiting for the threatened "tariff axe" to fall? We as consumers can take action now by boycotting U.S. products and buying Canadian, wherever possible. Win/win. We pump more money into the local economy, encourage Canadian entrepreneurship and innovation, and send a clear message that we will not be cowed by the threats of a misinformed despot. And then we can talk about retaliation.
Michael Bines, Toronto
The comparisons between Trump and Hitler are strikingDonald Trump's Jan. 6 pardons lay the foundation for a personal militia. But he can't erase their crimes, Jan. 23
Rosie DiManno has it right: the released Oath Keepers and Proud Boys who attacked the U.S. Capitol four years ago will form the basis for U.S. President Donald Trump's "private militia that will always do his bidding, constitution be damned." The comparisons between Trump and Adolf Hitler are striking: the Oath Keepers and Proud Boys are like Hitler's Brown Shirts. Trump's idea to take over Canada, Greenland, and the Panama Canal are similar to Hitler's wish to give Germany "Lebensraum," room in which to live.. "One of the endearing traits of our species is its capacity to learn from experience combined with the failure to do so." Rick Salutin wrote in 2012.
Douglas Buck, Toronto
Canada's carbon rebate addresses two problems
Many can't afford to eat let alone pay the carbon tax, Jan. 23
Jeremy Thorton's letter about the carbon tax fails to acknowledge one vital fact: The Canada Carbon Rebate more than covers carbon charges for over 80 per cent of Canadian households. Indeed. If Mr. Thorton checks his mail or bank account this week, he will "discover" a rebate of $210 for his household (assuming a two-person household). This is a quarterly payment so over a year he receives $840 at the current rate. This amount should cover whatever he paid in carbon tax over the year and in most cases leaves some extra for groceries. If he doesn't drive a gas car or heat his home with natural gas, he would be further rewarded by pocketing all of the rebate without paying any carbon taxes. That's $840 in additional income to buy groceries. The willful ignorance of this rebate by angry letter writers and Conservative politicians (Pierre Poilievre and Doug Ford, I'm looking at you!) is a major barrier to addressing the climate and affordability crisis that we all face.
Brian Kelly, Whitby
Preserving Kingsway culture
A proposed fourplex in Etobicoke was rejected after opposition from more than 40 neighbours, Jan. 28
A contributor fears that more houses "will turn the neighbourhood into a slum yard of undesirable inhabitants who will bring crime and filth and drug activity and illegal aliens into a long-established neighbourhood." By "filth" does he mean street musicians and mimes?
Tom Cmajdalka, Oakville
Pride flags bannedThis GTA board just voted to restrict the Pride flag inside schools. It's stoking intense division, Jan. 30
What a disgusting move by the Dufferin-Peel Catholic School Board. Where is the inclusion in this decision? This is out and out discrimination. What would Jesus say?
Bryan Bakker, Mississauga
Tax dollars should not pay for religious schools
Why, in this day and age, are our tax dollars still funding religious indoctrination and bigotry?
Andrew Elrick, Whitby
Add transit to your election platform Premier FordDoug Ford triggers snap Ontario election, Jan. 29
In all the excitement of the unnecessary provincial election call, have the uncompleted transit projects been forgotten? We've seen nothing! Or is that the Ford government's goal?
Sharon Kirkham, Toronto

Document 411 of 500

Mark Carney says he would immediately scrap Justin Trudeau's consumer carbon price. Here's what he'd replace it with

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/mark-carney-says-he-would-immediately-scrap/docview/3161729771/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Mark+Carney+says+he+would+immediately+scrap+Justin+Trudeau%27s+consumer+carbon+price.+Here%27s+what+he%27d+replace+it+with&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Former Bank of Canada governor Mark Carney says he will replace Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's consumer carbon levy-and-rebate scheme with an array of green incentives if he wins the Liberal leadership race.
Carney unveiled his climate policy vision on Friday in Nova Scotia, declaring that the Liberal government's fuel levy is too politically "divisive" and tainted by "lies" peddled by Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre.
Carney said he would immediately lift the federal fuel levy from consumers and small businesses. In its place, he would create a system of incentives that would see the government sell emissions credits to heavy industries, and use the money to pay people unspecified amounts when they make green purchases like electric cars, energy-efficient appliances and improved home insulation.
He would also maintain the national requirement for provinces to have carbon pricing systems for heavy industries. And he would keep the Liberal government's incoming emissions cap for the oil and gas sector, a policy that Alberta strongly opposes and the Conservatives have promised to scrap if they take power.
While Carney did not specify how much his policies would reduce Canada's greenhouse gas emissions, he said his plan would ensure they keep going down.
"It's time for a new, more effective climate plan that everyone can get behind," Carney said Friday.
"You'll no longer have to pay more to fuel your car or heat your home."
The consumer carbon price has long been a political lightning rod for the Liberal government. Poilievre has lampooned it as a driver of inflation that has failed to significantly reduce emissions, while provincial governments from Alberta to Ontario have challenged and criticized the policy for years.
A December study from University of Calgary economists concluded that carbon pricing has had a "minimal" impact on inflation.
On Friday, Poilievre's office continued trying to link Carney with Trudeau's carbon levy, calling him "Carbon Tax Carney" and dismissing his plan as nothing more than a "rebrand" of the government's current policy.
"Canadians will continue to suffer under a brand new name!" Poilievre's spokesperson Sebastian Skamski said in a written statement Friday morning.
The NDP criticized Carney for overseeing an asset management firm that invested in fossil fuels. "How can Canadians trust him with a climate plan now?" the party asked in a statement Friday.
Now that Trudeau is on his way out, Carney and other contenders to replace him as prime minister have distanced themselves from the policy. Chrystia Freeland, the former finance minister whose resignation in December helped push Trudeau towards the door, has promised to scrap the consumer carbon price. Leadership candidate Karina Gould, the former government House leader, says she would freeze it at its current level. Gould has also promised to roll out a series of green incentives for consumers.
But the consumer carbon price has also been a key part of the Trudeau government's plan to slash Canada's emissions that contribute to the global climate crisis. According to the Canadian Climate Institute, the consumer carbon levy is expected to cover eight to 14 per cent of the country's projected emissions reductions by 2030.
The institute also concluded that the bulk of emissions reductions are slated to come from industrial carbon pricing and the oil-and-gas cap - policies Carney confirmed Friday he intends to keep.
Many economists, along with Liberal environment ministers who have endorsed Carney for the Liberal leadership, have argued that carbon pricing is the least costly way to encourage businesses and consumers to reduce their emissions. And leading scientists have rung the alarm through the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change, publishing warnings that time is running out to avoid the damaging extremes of global warming.
In recent months, however, more politicians have turned against the policy. NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh suggested last year that his party would stress imposing costs on industrial emitters over consumer carbon pricing, while B.C.'s NDP premier said he would remove the province's long-standing carbon price if the national requirement to have one disappears.
That requirement was introduced in 2019, after the Liberal government passed a law that mandates all provinces and territories to have carbon pricing systems that meet a minimum level of stringency. If they don't, there is a federal system that has two prongs: an industrial pricing system for heavy emitters, and a consumer carbon levy.
The federal levy is in place in several provinces, including Ontario, and comes with rebates to all households four times a year. A household of four in Ontario was slated to get $1,120 in rebates in the current fiscal year.
The minimum carbon price is also scheduled to increase annually, along with the size of the rebates. This year it is set at $80 per tonne of emissions, which the federal government says adds 17.6 cents to the cost of a litre of gasoline. It is supposed to increase until it hits $170 per tonne in 2030, adding 37.4 cents per litre at the pump.
While Carney would ditch the consumer levy, he said he would keep the scheduled carbon price increases for the industrial systems.
Carney also said he would implement a "carbon border adjustment," which is a tariff on imports from other countries with weaker climate policies that is meant to ensure Canadian businesses aren't disadvantaged by domestic green initiatives.
"What that does is promote jobs here at home" and prevents emitting industries from moving to other jurisdictions, he said.
Skamski said this amounts to a "new carbon tax at the border" and predicted it would increase the cost of gas, home heating and food.
Under the Trudeau government, Canada has pledged to slash emissions to at least 40 per cent below 2005 levels by 2030, and at least 45 per cent below 2005 levels by 2035.
According to a preliminary assessment the government published in December, Canada's emissions were 8.5 per cent below 2005 levels in 2023.
CAPTION: Liberal leadership candidate Mark Carney, seen at his campaign launch earlier this month, says in a policy statement he'd create incentives to "reward" people for green initiative purchases such as electric cars and energy-efficient appliances.

Ford warns Poilievre on EV subsidies: PC leader says he expects Ottawa to keep supporting auto sector no matter who's elected PM

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-warns-poilievre-on-ev-subsidies/docview/3161713294/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+warns+Poilievre+on+EV+subsidies%3A+PC+leader+says+he+expects+Ottawa+to+keep+supporting+auto+sector+no+matter+who%27s+elected+PM&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford took a veiled shot at federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre Thursday, warning "whoever ends up being our next prime minister" must continue electric-vehicle manufacturing subsidies.
Aware that Poilievre, who polls suggest would win a national election expected as early as this spring, has questioned such corporate supports, Ford used his provincial campaign to highlight the need for government aid.
The Progressive Conservative leader said if he is re-elected on Feb. 27, Queen's Park would maintain its share of funding for EV battery production subsidies to automakers even if U.S. President Donald Trump tears up the Inflation Reduction Act.
Canada's subsidies, which could cost up to $13 billion for a new Volkswagen EV battery plant south of London alone, match former president Joe Biden's supports stateside and are designed to attract new investment and protect thousands of Ontario jobs.
"I expect whoever ends up being our next prime minister to do the exact same thing," Ford said here, a 30-minute drive north of the massive VW "gigafactory" under construction near St. Thomas.
While he pointedly did not mention Poilievre by name, tensions between the more centrist provincial Tories and the more right-leaning federal party have festered for many months.
"Anyone who's prime minister needs to step up. They need to support the workers here in Ontario," said Ford, noting Trump has repeatedly said U.S. automakers should abandon Canada to build vehicles in America as he threatens 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian products as soon as Saturday.
"There's a bullet aimed straight at the heart of our auto sector," he said.
Poilievre's office did not respond to Ford's barb.
When the VW production tax credits were announced two years ago, Poilievre questioned the billions in spending that Prime Minister Justin Trudeau - who worked closely with Ford on the aid - called a "generational investment."
"This money belongs to Canadians. Not to a foreign corporation. Not to Justin Trudeau," Poilievre said at the time, before asking the Parliamentary Budget Office to examine the deal.
In Mississauga at the opening of her campaign office, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said she was "concerned about the future of the (EV) sector."
That's in part because Ford "has not worked to insulate our economy by putting all eggs in one basket."
"It's not how I would operate. We needed to have diversified our economy," she said in a strip-plaza office in Mississauga East-Cooksville that previously housed a Jamaican grocery store.
But Crombie, who supports the existing EV investments, emphasized that she would work with a potential Poilievre Conservative government in Ottawa to protect the auto industry.
"He and I served together while I was in Ottawa for that one term (as a Liberal MP). We have a very cordial relationship and I'm sure the door is open to enhance that," she said, noting they spoke recently and he praised her housing plan.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said she supports EV subsidies, and while "there's been a lot of money invested at the federal and provincial government, I want to make sure that we protect all sectors, that we're standing up for all jobs."
Stiles spoke to reporters outside Queen's Park before boarding a campaign bus emblazoned with her photo and the party's slogan, "On Your Side."
"We have to see what the Trump government's really got planned for their country, and for ours," she said. "But I can tell you one thing, I believe in the EV sector. It is important for the future of our own economy."
Stiles - who questioned Ford's prowess at deal-making after revelations Monday he spent an additional $612 million to expedite the liberalization of beer, wine and pre-mixed cocktail sales - said the Tory leader needs to be more on the ball.
"We're going to have to figure out ... how we're going to build our industry here, market our products here, if (Trump) is going to cut us off, if he's going to ramp it up with tariffs," she added.
Ford is focusing his campaign on the fight against tariffs while opposition parties criticize Ontario's worsening doctor shortage, long emergency room waits, overcrowded schools, rising homelessness and an RCMP investigation into his government's $8.28-billion Greenbelt scandal.
PC strategists hope the early election, which is costing $189 million and coming 15 months before the previously scheduled vote, secures Ford a third term before Poilievre can win federally.
That's because Ontarians traditionally prefer different parties in power in Ottawa and Queen's Park at the same time.
As the campaign heats up, Crombie's team is taking aim at some key downtown Toronto seats that the New Democrats won from the Liberals in 2018 and retained in 2022.
In Toronto-St. Paul's, prominent broadcaster Stephanie Smyth is hoping to unseat two-term NDP MPP Jill Andrew, while in Spadina-Fort York, former local city council candidate April Engelberg is challenging MPP Chris Glover.
Across town in the traditional NDP stronghold of Toronto-Danforth, Pan American Games silver medallist kayaker Connor Taras will try to topple veteran New Democrat Peter Tabuns.
CAPTION: Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford arrives for a tour of Starlim North America Corp. on Thursday in London, Ont.Geoff Robins THE CANADIAN PRESS
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson, Robert Benzie and Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Document 413 of 500

Militarism returns. Is the misery next?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/militarism-returns-is-misery-next/docview/3161713084/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Militarism+returns.+Is+the+misery+next%3F&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.15&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: So, militarism is back. Defence - itself a euphemism, the term used to be War Department - is, hectored the Globe and Mail in an editorial, "one of the two areas of statehood that require a firm adult hand." Stop snickering, war has gravitas.
I can see why they want it to be prominent, from the right wing/Poilievre POV. Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives are finally getting their election, but they've prepped on only two issues: Justin gotta go and Axe the Tax. Suddenly, their cupboard is bare and they've nothing to stock it with. They literally have no policies.
Cue the manly virtues of warmaking. And in fact it's widespread. Trump is demanding everyone in NATO spend not just two per cent of GDP on "defence," he's upped that to five and NATO's fallen in line. (Sound off!) Lithuania is going for six, which will surely make the Russians cancel their invasion plans. Sadly, there are a few stragglers - Spain, Italy, Belgium, us.
There's more to it than nostalgia for war movies and as usual the more is economic. We're in an economic crisis. The last one this grave was the Great Depression of the 1930s. It resulted from what economist Karl Polanyi called a utopian experiment in unfettered, "self-regulating" markets, which began in the 1800s and culminated in two world wars and the Depression.
This one is the result of the neo-liberal onslaught of the past 45 years begun under Reagan and Thatcher, featuring free trade, deregulation, financialization and letting capitalism do its thing. In other words, a utopian experiment in unfettered markets. It worked, for a handful of odious tech titans, who were ranged behind Trump at his inauguration. All others, like the young, might survive if they scuffle.
The 1930s Depression was mitigated by FDR's New Deal, but was only ended by the Second World War, in which the government fettered the market and mobilized it for war, employing everyone it didn't put in uniform. The great fear at that war's end wasn't the Red Menace of Stalinist Russia but the return of the Depression. It never happened because the wartime economy was extended into peacetime through the Cold War and permanent militarization, mostly in the U.S., but extending to others via the defence "umbrella" it provided.
What is the solution to the current catastrophe proposed by Doug Ford, Poilievre and their legions? Unleash the free market and let it rip. This meets the classic definition of insanity. (I loved it when Vassy Kapelos asked Poilievre if he really thinks he can create millions of homes merely by not enforcing zoning laws.)
OK, time for the elephant in the room: Russian (or Chinese) aggression. If you're going to build support for a militarized economy, you need an enemy. I don't mean antagonisms don't exist, but if they didn't, they'd need to be invented. Failing that, the conflicts could often be resolved. Do you really think Putin wants the headache of ruling France or Germany? As for us, the only real threat of invasion we face now, comes, explicitly, from the U.S. Yet, the military we're told to embrace, is aimed at the Mideast or Europe.
It's easy to forget why the world managed to avoid major wars in the last half of the past century, except for what the big powers tended to view as sideshows like Vietnam - despite war's utility in nation-building, its economic benefits, etc.
It was embodied in the UN charter of 1945: "We the peoples of the United Nations, determined to save succeeding generations from the scourge of war, which twice in our lifetime has brought untold sorrow." Its authors weren't moral exemplars, they were the same affluent, powerful types who always run things. But they'd been through an awful grinder and it changed them.
Now militarism is back and those lessons have been forgotten, it's the nature of our species. If we lived longer - to 300 or 400 - we might remember not to forget what things like war and "defence" really consist of.
Rick Salutin is a freelance contributing columnist for the Star. He is based in Toronto. Reach him via email: salutinrick@gmail.com.
CREDIT: Rick Salutin OPINION;

Subject: War; Militarism

Location: United States--US

Document 414 of 500

Gould pledges to reduce GST, hit big-profit firms

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/gould-pledges-reduce-gst-hit-big-profit-firms/docview/3161712956/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Gould+pledges+to+reduce+GST%2C+hit+big-profit+firms&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Pitching herself as "laser-focused" on economic fairness and affordability, Liberal leadership candidate Karina Gould says she wants to hike taxes on big-profit corporations to help pay for a one-year decrease in the federal sales tax.
Gould, who resigned as Government House Leader to run in the race to replace Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, said Thursday that she would increase federal corporate taxes from 15 to 17 per cent for corporations that earn more than $500 million in profit per year. This increase, which she envisions as a permanent tax hike, would bring in roughly $6 billion per year, Gould said.
At the same time, Gould said she would decrease the federal Goods and Services Tax (GST) from the current five per cent to four per cent, a change that she said would cost about $11 billion.
Gould added that she would toughen federal regulations governing business competition to better protect consumers from unfair prices.
"I am laser-focused on our economy, but in a way that works for Canadians," Gould told reporters at an Ottawa bookstore.
"Because the economy isn't just about, you know, being the greatest economy in the G7. It's not about all of the macroeconomic indicators. What it's about is making sure that Canadians have the resources that they need to pay the bills that they have."
That was an apparent swipe at former Bank of Canada governor Mark Carney, who has leaned on his economic credentials as a central banker of two major Western democracies and wants to "build the fastest-growing economy in the G7."
Having received endorsements from at least a dozen cabinet ministers and a host of MPs, Carney has emerged as the preferred candidate for much of the Liberal party establishment. But Gould pushed back when asked whether she is worried Carney is on track for an easy victory.
"We don't need a coronation in the Liberal Party of Canada. What we need is a debate of ideas," she said. "We're talking about choosing the next prime minister, and that means that we need to be putting these ideas on the table. That's what (party) members and Canadians deserve."
The opposition Conservatives have taken to demanding policy clarity from Carney, accusing him of hiding from the media. On Friday, Carney is expected to unroll a policy promise in Nova Scotia.
Gould's policy pledges Thursday add to previous commitments that include freezing the federal government's carbon price at the current $80 per tonne of greenhouse gas emissions. Chrystia Freeland, the former finance minister, has said she would scrap the consumer carbon price altogether. Carney also plans to walk away from that policy, as the Star has reported.
Gould's campaign also told the Star this week that she supports the construction of high-speed rail lines from Toronto to Quebec City and between Calgary and Edmonton.
And, on Thursday, she agreed it will be important for the next prime minister to empower MPs and cabinet ministers, after Freeland vowed to halve the size of the Prime Minister's Office (PMO) and cabinet to decentralize authority inside the federal government.
Since Trudeau announced his intention to resign after the Liberals elect a new leader on March 9, the leadership contest has been carried on under the shadow of U.S. President Donald Trump's threat to impose devastating tariffs on all Canadian goods on Saturday. Each candidate has tried to position themself as the best one to face that challenge.
Freeland, who resigned as Trudeau's finance minister and deputy prime minister in December, penned a letter at the time suggesting the government was spending too much on political "gimmicks" like a two-month GST holiday for a host of consumer goods. Last week, she reversed course on the capital gains tax increase she put in the 2024 federal budget, telling reporters the about-face was warranted because of Trump's tariff threats.
Political leaders, business groups and unions have warned those tariffs could deliver a severe economic blow. The Canadian Chamber of Commerce has warned 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian exports to the U.S. could decrease national gross domestic product (GDP) by 2.6 per cent and cause a recession this year. The British Columbia government, meanwhile, projects the tariffs could mean 124,000 lost jobs by 2028.
For Gould, the next Liberal leader must weigh whether they should try to govern with the current minority Parliament after March 9, or to call an election before the opposition parties vote them down. She criticized Ontario Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford, who this week called a snap election even though he has a majority government that wasn't mandated to go the polls until June 2026.
Ford justified the move by pointing to the U.S. tariff threats, and said he wants a mandate to confront them.
"From my perspective, I think we need to see where we are in a couple of months' time and make the decision that is responsible and right for Canada," Gould said about the timing of a federal election.
CAPTION: Liberal leadership candidate Karina Gould told reporters at a campaign stop in Ottawa Thursday that she is "laser- focused on our economy, but in a way that works for Canadians."Spencer Colby The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Alex Ballingall Deputy Toronto Star

Document 415 of 500

What do Canadian Conservatives really stand for? They're showing us right now

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/what-do-canadian-conservatives-really-stand/docview/3161712917/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=What+do+Canadian+Conservatives+really+stand+for%3F+They%27re+showing+us+right+now&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With an election call in Ontario, and one looming federally, Conservatives are riding high in the polls.
But if you're casting a vote for one of those Conservative parties, do you really know what you're voting for and if they're on your side?
Looking at the federal Conservative party under Pierre Poilievre, one is hard pressed to find any remnant of the pragmatic centrism reflected in the party of John Diefenbaker, Robert Stanfield, Joe Clark and Brian Mulroney.
In a similar way, the case of Ontario's Progressive Conservatives provides real insights into this transformation of the Conservative movement.
Under Premier Doug Ford, the Conservatives departed from Ontario's long-held view as a place of propriety, moderation and tempered progressivism - where a public sphere and public interest were widely understood as necessary elements in a well-functioning economy and society. In the last two elections, the PC party's campaign slogans were "For the People" and "Get It Done." But just what was getting done and for which people?
At various points in Ontario's history, the policies of the Ontario government were directed toward building public education, health care, housing, cultural institutions and popular public assets. It was an Ontario where social and economic development policies were directed at modernizing an industrial economy. Both John Robarts' and Bill Davis's Conservative governments invested heavily in education, establishing new public universities and community colleges, rent controls, new hospitals, - even going as far as to purchase a 25 per cent stake in the energy company Suncor.
The elements for an unprecedented degree of social and economic inclusivity were put in place, even if they were unequally distributed. Not all social groups had a seat at the table, but the provincial government applied its legal and fiscal powers to reshape what had been a remarkably staid and unequal society.
By the mid-1990s, however, Conservatives of that earlier era were all but gone.
With Ford, like Mike Harris before him, facilitating business investment became the primary function of government. But instead of across-the-board cuts in public expenditures and taxes like Harris made, the Ford Conservatives have been much more selective, increasing public expenditures and subsidies for the corporate sector, especially for large infrastructure projects, while restraining public expenditures in health, education and other social programs.
The Ford government has also taken a more autocratic approach to governing by using restrictive procedural mechanisms to pass controversial legislation. It has overruled municipal decisions, contravened Charter-protected labour rights when it removed the rights of 55,000 education workers to collectively bargain, and has threatened to do it again to with homeless encampments.
Still, Ford is no Trump-style populist. While sharing certain similarities - generational wealth, press hostility and anti-establishment tirades - Ford does not share the racial intolerance, paranoia and anti-immigrant sentiment that predominate populist movements elsewhere.
Ford does, though, share a penchant for simple slogans, exaggeration, disregard for facts and limitations on executive power. Ford's brand of "retail politics" offered to make life easier for "the people" in small but measurable ways - think, buck a beer or $200 rebate cheques - that expelled the special interests of elites and cleaned-up government corruption. Whether he was successful is a different story.
The Ford Conservatives' success - which Poilievre is replicating nationally - lies in mobilizing people's individual fears into class politics. People are motivated by a variety of issues - faith and politics, age, gender, economic security and more. He doesn't deny climate change, for instance, but thinks that working people shouldn't bear the costs of carbon reduction plans.
In both 2018 and 2022, the PCs won not only in the predominantly white rural districts of southern Ontario, but in the multiracial suburbs of Toronto, including Scarborough and Mississauga, exurban and industrial towns. Ford's strongest support came from those who identified as East Asian, South Asian and Eastern European, especially those with strong religious and socially traditional views.
Conservatives also won the votes of workers in former NDP strongholds, including Windsor, Hamilton, Timmins, London and Oshawa, areas that experienced sharp manufacturing losses and declining incomes.
The Ford government's electoral success shows that working-class conservatism can thrive in places other than the white, Christian, rural heartlands most often imagined as the "natural" home of conservativism. Former federal Conservative party leader Erin O'Toole, was explicit about chasing votes from unionized workers - which Poilievre has continued to run with.
Since the Ford government has come to power, Ontario is more clearly divided between those who must work longer and harder, and those who live off the work of others. And class identity, historically weak and uneven in Canada, is confoundingly weaker than ever. Now more than ever we need to ask serious questions about what can really be done "for the people" - and who's best to do it.
CAPTION: NA-FORDPOILIEVRE28 Doug Ford versus Pierre Poilievre: Insiders explain what it is that divides the Conservative leaders. Uploaded by: Cohen, Ben

Title: What do Canadian Conservatives really stand for? They're showing us right now

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Document 416 of 500

Alt-comedy pioneer Tim Heidecker on navigating satire during the Trump era and his (stellar) new folk-rock LP

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/alt-comedy-pioneer-tim-heidecker-on-navigating/docview/3161712886/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Alt-comedy+pioneer+Tim+Heidecker+on+navigating+satire+during+the+Trump+era+and+his+%28stellar%29+new+folk-rock+LP&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As a comedian, Tim Heidecker has spent over two decades probing the murky depths of the male psyche.
From the dark surrealism of the sketch comedy duo Tim & Eric to the deadpan in-jokes of the "On Cinema" universe, Heidecker has crafted an arsenal of hilarious yet haunting caricatures of toxic masculinity: the lecherous boss, the hapless grifter, the "anti-woke" crusader, the puerile man-child.
For those of us who spent hours of our bleary-eyed youth watching Heidecker's absurd brand of anti-comedy, part of his magic stemmed from his staunch unwillingness to break character. On a 2017 appearance on the Stephen Colbert show, Tim and his comedy partner, Eric Wareheim, arrived dressed in full clown regalia. In a classic Heideckerian twist, Tim quickly derails the interview by recounting the grim details of his recent divorce. "I didn't want to get into this," he tells Colbert, choking up beneath a bright red wing and foam nose. "It's been a really hard couple of months."
I'll be the first to admit that it was difficult, at first, to fathom the idea of Tim Heidecker as a Serious Musician. Nearly a decade ago, when I hit play on his 2016 album "In Glendale" - a collection of brightly earnest indie rock songs - it took me a few minutes to realize that there was no gag coming. A few tracks later, a second revelation arrived: the music was not only serious, but pretty damn good.
Today, Heidecker - who will perform a mixture of music and comedy at the Danforth on Feb. 4 - has emerged as not only a prolific musical artist (he's released six studio albums since 2016), but a talented songwriter with a knack for capturing the existential contours of societal decay.
In October, he released "Slipping Away," an album that seeks to reconcile the cynicism and paranoia of our current era with the all-too-human need for hope and joy.
"I like to think of it as a record about a man in a mid-life crisis, who is also facing a world that's in a mid-life crisis," Heidecker told me. "A world where it feels like something terrible is about to happen."
"But it's a lot of fun," he assured me. "It's not a happy subject matter, but I try to approach it the way Warren Zevon or Randy Newman might approach it, with a little bit of dark humour."
"It's not an emo record or anything like that," he added, stretching the word as if to draw out its inherent irony.
-
I caught up with Heidecker via Zoom at the start of his current North American tour, which he describes as "a bit like a revue": fans can expect music, comedy and "all kinds of stuff." In Toronto, the show will open with a set from Neil Hamburger, a stand-up comedian and persona created by alt-comedy legend Gregg Turkington (a character Heidecker calls "the funniest f--king guy in the world.")
Heidecker, 48, lives in Glendale with his wife and two young kids. He told me he was looking forward to embracing the cold in Toronto next week. It's a city he's visited at least 10 times, including an extended stint last year to film the vampire mockumentary series "What We Do in the Shadows."
"That was a lovely experience," he recalled. "I stayed right downtown and played a ton of pickleball at an indoor racket club." The food was also a highlight - specifically the schnitzel at Allen's on the Danforth ("fantastic") and the seafood at Joso's in Yorkville ("so f--king good.")
Chatting from inside what looked like a tour bus, Heidecker came off as thoughtful and sincere - a far cry from the obnoxious maniacs he's spent much of his career portraying.
Born in Allentown, Pa., Heidecker met his comedy partner Eric Wareheim at Temple University; they hit it off immediately and began collaborating on what would evolve into the demented comedy world of Tim & Eric.
The duo started off creating short films and sketches in the early 2000s. With a bit of help from comedian Bob Odenkirk, they landed a sketch series on Cartoon Network called "Tim and Eric Awesome Show, Great Job!"
Running between 2007 and 2010, the series - which was loosely inspired by the type of bland public access television programs you might stumble upon late at night - consistently pushed the boundaries of alternative comedy, with garish colours, aggressively disjointed sound and film editing and actors often sourced from Craigslist. The result was a disorienting, abrasive experience, not unlike that of a nearly bad drug trip.
(Among Heidecker's most enduring characters from the Tim & Eric era are the flirty Channel 5 host Jan Skylar - a member of "the only married news team in the tri-county area" - and "Spagett," the ludicrously inept host of a hidden camera prank show. Another sketch with incredible staying power sees Tim and Eric portraying Doctors Jimes Tooper and H. Donna Gust, space "experts" who trade progressively ridiculous analogies to explain the nature of the Universe.)
I asked Heidecker about the influence of the late filmmaker David Lynch, a master of surrealism, on the tone and feel of those early sketches.
"He gave us permission to explore that combination of comedy and darkness," he said. "I was just watching (Lynch's 1977 body horror fantasy) 'Eraserhead,' and I forgot how funny and insane it is. I don't think we even considered it possible to do that before finding and digging into his work - that you could jump in and out of humour and darkness."
"Tim and Eric Awesome Show" baffled most critics, but attracted a cult following among weirdos, stoners and alt-comedy fans. It also became a place where high-profile comedians and actors might drop by to get a little freaky: over the years, the show featured recurring appearances from John C. Reilly, Will Ferrell, Zach Galifianakis, Ed Begley Jr. and Paul Rudd. It also laid the groundwork for a generation of comedy surrealists like Eric André, Nathan Fielder and Tim Robinson of "I Think You Should Leave."
"I don't know if our stuff will ever receive the appreciation that we all probably think it deserves," Heidecker reflected thoughtfully. "It gets talked about as very influential and groundbreaking and all that stuff, but I think it's underrated personally."
But if Tim & Eric represented the bleeding edge of alt-comedy, it was Heidecker's next project, "On Cinema at the Cinema," that shattered the limits of the genre's storytelling conventions.
Launched as a podcast with Turkington in 2011, "On Cinema" started out as a simple, deadpan spoof of Siskel and Ebert, starring Heidecker as the surly, easily distractible dilettante and Turkington as the mind-numbingly pedantic "movie expert." As the series moved online and found its footing, "On Cinema" evolved into a sprawling black comedy, centred on the toxic, codependent friendship between Tim and Gregg.
Over time, the show expanded into bizarre new directions, incorporating a host of new characters and increasingly insane subplots involving drug abuse, alternative medicine, divorce, alt-right politics, cryptocurrency and murder.
Today, "On Cinema" - currently in the midst of its 15th season - is best understood as a giant fictional universe, not unlike the MCU - a universe that has spawned an ever-growing list of spinoffs, including movies, multiple music projects and faux TV series.
"It's like we've been writing a novel for 20 years, an absurd 'Confederacy of Dunces' type of story that doesn't seem to ever want to end," Heidecker told me. "For years I've explored the idea of the male toxic persona and what it means to be an American man in the 21st century, and that character has been such a great deposit for my thoughts on that."
I asked Heidecker a question that is on the mind of every "Timhead" and "Gregghead": how long will the show keep going?
"It's inspired by the hell world that we live in, which gives us new ideas every day. And it doesn't seem to get old. When I got cuts from the latest season, I was still laughing like I did 10 years ago."
-
In recent years, Heidecker's satire has sharpened and shifted focus. Concurrent with the rise of Trumpism and the ongoing culture wars, Heidecker has carved out something of a niche skewering the bland mediocrity of mainstream comedy and the vapid, pseudo-intellectualism that currently dominates the manosphere.
In 2020, Heidecker released his first ever standup special, debuting a persona he'd been working on for years. Referred to among fans as the "No More Bulls--t" guy, Heidecker casts himself as a blustering, painfully unfunny, "anti-woke" comic, serving up blandly misogynistic one-liners and pining for a time before liberals made it impossible to get a real hamburger and a Coke. "If I wanted a Pepsi I would have asked for a Pepsi!" he screams at the audience, his eyes bulging with rage.
A year later, Heidecker's podcast, "Office Hours LIVE," went viral after sharing an incisive parody of "The Joe Rogan Experience."
Wearing a SpaceX baseball cap, Heidecker delivers a pitch-perfect Rogan impression, leading a meandering, hours-long discussion with comedians Jeremy Levick and Rajat Suresh, touching on a host of right-wing hot button topics like cancel culture, psychedelic drugs and nutrition.
"I think Dwayne 'The Rock' Johnson will run for president, and I think he will win" Heidecker-as-Rogan states confidently to his credulous guests, predicting the disintegration of the two-party system and the current structure of society, thus paving the way for "a benevolent monarchy, a magnanimous kingdom."
It's extremely funny, but there's also a thread of exasperation that drives this mode of Heidecker's satire.
"What I consider mainstream comedy now just seems to be made up of full-on bootlickers and servants to the state, you know? Rogan, Theo Von, that whole podcast world," he said. "I saw this clip of them and they were drooling over the idea of going to Mar-a-Lago. And then they're all at the inauguration ... there's just something really not cool about being such supplicants for people in power. I don't find anything interesting about that."
"Then I watch their stuff and it's this very boring s - t, you know? I watched this clip somebody sent to me (of Rogan and his guests) talking about watching "The Wizard of Oz" with "Dark Side of the Moon" as the backdrop. And I'm like, this is s--t that I would talk with my friends about in high school. This isn't what a billion people need to be listening to on their way to work."
With Trump back in the White House, Heidecker is adjusting his approach to comedy, at least in a live setting. On the first night of the current tour, the opening act fell through. He decided to revive the "No More Bulls--t" persona to fill the gap.
"It's a very fun world to live in for 20 or 30 minutes and it was easy to write those jokes," he said. "But at the same time, I felt like, what is the point? I can say horrible s--t, but everybody at my show is already on the same page. It's not really effective when it's just confirmation bias."
Heidecker confirmed that the comedy portion of this tour will be different than in previous years.
"I never want to be boxed into anything," he said. "I always want to try something else, and I want you to see something else from me."
-
Music has always played a role in Heidecker's craft - there's the Tim & Eric catalogue, which includes beloved tracks like "Petite Feet" and "Goatee," plus a long list of karaoke songs from hell. And of course there is Dekkar, a goofy post-grunge rock band that emerged from the "On Cinema" universe. Heidecker has also collaborated with an impressive list of indie rock stars, including Mac DeMarco, Father John Misty and Weyes Blood.
But on "Slipping Away," Heidecker and his "Very Good Band" - who cite Crazy Horse and the Band as their key influences - sound better than ever, from the lilting groove of "Well's Running Dry" to the expansive jam that concludes the album's finale, "Bells Are Ringing."
There's a quiet darkness that hovers over songs like "Bows and Arrows" and "Something Somewhere," but the album also contains moments of tenderness and hope. "And if it rains, well, that's OK / We can take shelter behind hom? plate / Either way / We'll h?ad out after the bottom of the eighth," Heidecker sings on "Bottom of the 8th," a charming homage to fatherhood (his daughter Amelia also appears on the album).
Written during the pandemic, the album's relatable mixture of worry and hope feels perfectly suited to the current cultural moment.
"It's really no different than 'On Cinema,'" Heidecker suggested. "We're all feeling a certain way, and we're all dealing with stuff in different ways, so let's sing about it, or let's make jokes about it. Let's bring it out into the open and see if it's helpful."
As our conversation wraps, I ask Heidecker how he manages to balance his career, which seems to swing wildly between these moments of exaggerated caricature and earnest songwriting.
"Everything I do always starts from the same place: is it fun to do? Is it enriching? I don't collect things, I don't really have any hobbies. I just like making music and I like making funny things with my friends," he said. "Sometimes you're in the mood to be silly, and sometimes you want to work from an emotional place. Sometimes you need to get some stuff out.
"I'm just so grateful that there are people out there that like my music," he added. "I do these shows and people sing along to my songs, and it's very moving. I'm sure Bruce Springsteen feels the exact same way."
