Document 417 of 500

What do our conservatives stand for?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/what-do-our-conservatives-stand/docview/3161712733/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=What+do+our+conservatives+stand+for%3F&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.15&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With an election call in Ontario, and one looming federally, conservatives are riding high in the polls.
But if you're casting a vote for one of those conservative parties, do you really know what you're voting for and if they're on your side?
Looking at the federal Conservative party under Pierre Poilievre, one is hard pressed to find any remnant of the pragmatic centrism reflected in the party of John Diefenbaker, Robert Stanfield, Joe Clark and Brian Mulroney.
In a similar way, the case of Ontario's Progressive Conservatives provides real insights into this transformation of the conservative movement.
Under Premier Doug Ford, the party has departed from Ontario's long-held view as a place of propriety, moderation and tempered progressivism - where a public sphere and public interest were widely understood as necessary elements in a well-functioning economy and society. In the past two elections, the PC party's campaign slogans were "For the People" and "Get It Done." But just what was getting done, and for which people?
At various points in Ontario's history, the policies of the Ontario government were directed toward building public education, health care, housing, cultural institutions and popular public assets. It was an Ontario where social and economic development policies were directed at modernizing an industrial economy. Both John Robarts' and Bill Davis's Tory governments invested heavily in education, establishing new public universities and community colleges, rent controls, new hospitals - even going as far as to purchase a 25 per cent stake in the energy company Suncor.
The elements for an unprecedented degree of social and economic inclusivity were put in place, even if they were unequally distributed. Not all social groups had a seat at the table, but the provincial government applied its legal and fiscal powers to reshape what had been a remarkably staid and unequal society.
By the mid-1990s, however, conservatives of that earlier era were all but gone.
With Ford, like Mike Harris before him, facilitating business investment became the primary function of government. But instead of across-the-board cuts in public expenditures and taxes like Harris made, the Ford PCs have been much more selective, increasing public expenditures and subsidies for the corporate sector, especially for large infrastructure projects, while restraining public expenditures in health, education and other social programs.
The Ford government has also taken a more autocratic approach to governing by using restrictive procedural mechanisms to pass controversial legislation. It has overruled municipal decisions, contravened Charter-protected labour rights when it removed the rights of 55,000 education workers to collectively bargain, and has threatened to do it again to with homeless encampments.
Still, Ford is no Trump-style populist. While sharing certain similarities - generational wealth, press hostility and anti-establishment tirades - Ford does not share the racial intolerance, paranoia and anti-immigrant sentiment that predominate populist movements elsewhere.
Ford does, though, share a penchant for simple slogans, exaggeration, disregard for facts and limitations on executive power. Ford's brand of "retail politics" offered to make life easier for "the people" in small but measurable ways - think buck a beer or $200 rebate cheques - that expelled the special interests of elites and cleaned-up government corruption. Whether he was successful is a different story.
The Ford Conservatives' success - which Poilievre is replicating nationally - lies in mobilizing people's individual fears into class politics. People are motivated by several issues, such as faith and politics, gender, age and economic security. He doesn't deny climate change, for instance, but thinks that working people shouldn't bear the costs of carbon reduction plans.
In both 2018 and 2022, the PCs won not only in the predominantly white rural districts of southern Ontario, but in the multiracial suburbs of Toronto, including Scarborough and Mississauga, exurban and industrial towns. Ford's strongest support came from those who identified as East Asian, South Asian and eastern European, especially those with strong religious and socially traditional views.
Conservatives also won the votes of workers in former NDP strongholds, including Windsor, Hamilton, Timmins, London and Oshawa, areas that experienced sharp manufacturing losses and declining incomes.
Ford's electoral success shows that working-class conservatism can thrive in places other than the white, Christian, rural heartlands most often imagined as the "natural" home of conservatism. Former federal Conservative leader Erin O'Toole, was explicit about chasing votes from unionized workers - which Poilievre has continued to run with.
Since the Ford government has come to power, Ontario is more clearly divided between those who must work longer and harder, and those who live off the work of others. And class identity, historically weak and uneven in Canada, is confoundingly weaker than ever. Now more than ever we need to ask serious questions about what can really be done "for the people" - and who's best to do it.
Bryan Evans is a Professor in the Department of Politics and Public Administration at Toronto Metropolitan University. Carlo Fanelli is Associate Professor of Work and Labour Studies at York University. Together, they are the editors of "Against the People: How Ford Nation is Dismantling Ontario" (Fernwood Publishing).

Document 418 of 500

Liberal leadership contender Karina Gould pledges to raise taxes on big-profit firms while cutting the sales tax

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/liberal-leadership-contender-karina-gould-pledges/docview/3161994663/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberal+leadership+contender+Karina+Gould+pledges+to+raise+taxes+on+big-profit+firms+while+cutting+the+sales+tax&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Pitching herself as "laser-focused" on economic fairness and affordability, Liberal leadership candidate Karina Gould says she wants to hike taxes on big-profit corporations to help pay for a one-year decrease in the federal sales tax.
Gould, who resigned as Government House Leader to run in the race to replace Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, said Thursday that she would increase federal corporate taxes from 15 to 17 per cent for corporations that earn more than $500 million in profit per year. This increase, which she envisions as a permanent tax hike, would bring in roughly $6 billion per year, Gould said.
At the same time, Gould said she would decrease the federal Goods and Services Tax (GST) from the current five per cent to four per cent, a change that she said would cost about $11 billion.
Gould added that she would toughen federal regulations governing business competition to better protect consumers from unfair prices.
"I am laser-focused on our economy, but in a way that works for Canadians," Gould told reporters at an Ottawa bookstore.
"Because the economy isn't just about, you know, being the greatest economy in the G7. It's not about all of the macroeconomic indicators. What it's about is making sure that Canadians have the resources that they need to pay the bills that they have."
That was an apparent swipe at former Bank of Canada governor Mark Carney, who has leaned on his economic credentials as a central banker of two major Western democracies and wants to "build the fastest growing economy in the G7."
Having received endorsements from at least a dozen cabinet ministers and a host of MPs, Carney has emerged as the preferred candidate for much of the Liberal party establishment. But Gould pushed back when asked whether she is worried Carney is on track for an easy victory.
"We don't need a coronation in the Liberal Party of Canada. What we need is a debate of ideas," she said. "We're talking about choosing the next prime minister, and that means that we need to be putting these ideas on the table. That's what (party) members and Canadians deserve."
The opposition Conservatives have taken to demanding policy clarity from Carney, accusing him of hiding from the media. On Friday, Carney is expected to unroll a policy promise in Nova Scotia.
Gould's policy pledges Thursday add to previous commitments that include freezing the federal government's carbon price at the current $80 per tonne of greenhouse gas emissions. Chrystia Freeland, the former finance minister, has said she would scrap the consumer carbon price altogether. Carney also plans to walk away from that policy, as the Star has reported.
Gould's campaign also told the Star this week that she supports the construction of high speed rail lines from Toronto to Quebec City and between Calgary and Edmonton.
And, on Thursday, she agreed it will be important for the next prime minister to empower MPs and cabinet ministers, after Freeland vowed to halve the size of the Prime Minister's Office (PMO) and cabinet to decentralize authority inside the federal government.
Since Trudeau announced his intention to resign after the Liberals elect a new leader on March 9, the leadership contest has been carried on under the shadow of U.S. President Donald Trump's threat to impose devastating tariffs on all Canadian goods on Saturday. Each candidate has tried to position themself as the best one to face that challenge.
Freeland, who resigned as Trudeau's finance minister and deputy prime minister in December, penned a letter at the time suggesting the government was spending too much on political "gimmicks" like a two-month GST holiday for a host of consumer goods. Last week, she reversed course on the capital gains tax increase she put in the 2024 federal budget, telling reporters the about-face was warranted because of Trump's tariff threats.
Political leaders, business groups and unions have warned those tariffs could deliver a severe economic blow. The Canadian Chamber of Commerce has warned 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian exports to the U.S. could decrease national gross domestic product (GDP) by 2.6 per cent and cause a recession this year. The British Columbia government, meanwhile, projects the tariffs could mean 124,000 lost jobs by 2028.
For Gould, the next Liberal leader must weigh whether they should try to govern with the current minority Parliament after March 9, or to call an election before the opposition parties vote them down. She criticized Ontario Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford, who this week called a snap election even though he has a majority government that wasn't mandated to go the polls until June 2026.
Ford justified the move by pointing to the U.S. tariff threats, and said he wants a mandate to confront them.
"From my perspective, I think we need to see where we are in a couple of months' time and make the decision that is responsible and right for Canada," Gould said about the timing of a federal election.
CAPTION: Liberal Party of Canada leadership candidate Karina Gould speaks during an announcement in Ottawa, on Jan. 30, 2025.

Document 419 of 500

Ontario says half of the $200 'tax rebate' cheques have been mailed out. What to do if you're still waiting

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-says-half-200-tax-rebate-cheques-have/docview/3161994660/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+says+half+of+the+%24200+%27tax+rebate%27+cheques+have+been+mailed+out.+What+to+do+if+you%27re+still+waiting&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If you're still waiting to get your $200 "rebate" cheque from the province, you're far from alone - only about half of them have been mailed out so far.
As of Jan. 28, the Ontario Ministry of Finance said 7.5 million of the approximately 15 million payments have been sent out. However, there have already been reports of issues with the rollout, along with confusion of when exactly some people can expect to receive their $200.
Here's everything you need to know about the rebate cheques and what to do if you're having any trouble getting or depositing them.
Who is eligible for a cheque?
All Ontario taxpayers over 18 years old who filed their 2023 income tax will receive a cheque by mail, the province has said. Families who qualify for the federal Canada Child Benefit payment could also receive an additional $200 for every child under 18 in their household.
Those who were bankrupt or incarcerated last year do not qualify for a payment, however.
The province estimates the payouts will cost the treasury $3 billion, despite a $6.6-billion budget deficit.
The timing of the cheque rollout - which began on Jan. 17 - overlaps with Ford's call for a snap election earlier this week.
Progressive Conservative Party Leader Doug Ford, whose Conservative government announced plans to send out cash to Ontarians back in the fall, said at his election campaign kickoff event in Windsor on Wednesday that the payouts are meant to give people back their money to reinvest in the economy.
"The worst place you can give your money is the government because they know how to waste it," he added.
What should I do if I can't deposit my cheque?
Earlier this week, some people on social media posted about having issues depositing their cheques with their bank.
Ford put the blame on RBC on Wednesday, and for its part, the bank apologized for the "inconvenience" in an emailed statement to the Star.
"We undertake several steps to authenticate cheques that are cashed for deposit, ensuring that they are legitimate and received by the intended recipient. On very rare occasions, our protocols may result in a cheque being returned when it should have been processed, which is what occurred in this instance with a handful of cheques," RBC continued.
Anyone who had issues with RBC does not need to redeposit their cheques, the bank wrote, noting that it resolved the problem on its end and credited peoples' accounts.
Ford also acknowledged that Ontario residents who have moved or not updated information with the provincial government could also experience issues cashing in their money.
"We do have a hotline set up for people," Ford said.
According to the provincial government's website, people should contact Service Ontario at 1 (833) 351-0409 or 1 (888) 821-9056 with any problems they face.
What should I do if I haven't received my cheque?
The cheque may still be on its way.
Ontarians can verify the cheque amounts for adults and children, the mailing address of the cheque and whether or not it has been mailed out on the Service Ontario website.
The system will require an account login, the last four digits of an individual's Social Insurance Number, their date of birth and the postal code of the mailing address used in the Canada Revenue Agency tax return.
A person can only check their status a maximum of three times a day, the website reads.
What if I want to donate my cheque?
Some people online have posted that they plan to donate their $200 to charity - or even the Ontario NDP or Liberals - due to what they see as a waste of taxpayer money.
"There are a lot of people who $200 is nothing. But together it can do a lot," Erin Brubacher, an organizer with 200dollars.ca, told the Star over the phone. The 200dollars.ca website was created soon after Ford announced his plan to send out the payments as a way to "redirect as much of the $3 billion to public good."
People don't give their donation to 200dollars.ca, but rather pledge to give it to another organization.
According to Brubacher, the website has seen 10,000 visits - which she noted doesn't equate to a donation pledge - and pledges from people across over 100 communities around Ontario. Most of these donations are to food banks, she said, but housing and health care are other popular areas.
Brubacher also stressed that not everyone should donate their cheque money.
"If $200 is making a difference for your family when you receive it, then we wouldn't want you to do anything but keep it."
Other organizations, like Daily Bread Food Bank and Parkdale Food Centre have also started social media campaigns to encourage people donate to their causes.
With files from Estella Ren
CAPTION: Doug Ford speaks during his campaign launch next to the Ambassador Bridge in Windsor on Wednesday.

Countries affected by Trump's tariffs should form a union

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/countries-affected-trumps-tariffs-should-form/docview/3161487733/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Countries+affected+by+Trump%27s+tariffs+should+form+a+union&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: White House says Colombia agrees to take deported migrants after Trump tariff showdown, Jan. 27
Unions were formed to give strength, in numbers, to those who might, otherwise, have been picked-off, one-by-one by powerful and ruthless industry barons . A union of like-minded workers levelled the playing-field. At least that was the intent. The interests of the investor class were often supported by like-minded "puppets" in politics. That relationship came under the rubric "you-scratch-my-back-I'll-scratch-yours."
Last weekend, Colombia initially refused to let aircraft (carrying illegal immigrants to the USA) to land in its country . U.S. President Donald Trump immediately reacted by imposing withering tariffs on Columbia, and the country immediately backed down.
Perhaps, more than anything, Trump seized the opportunity to demonstrate how tough the U.S. was going to be against any nation who dared to stand up against this Trump-style bullying. This was similar to tactics that have been used by captains of industry against workers, whether unionized or not.
Countries like Canada and Mexico and Colombia and European countries, and so-on, should form a union. The rallying cry would be, "An attack against one of us is an attack against all of us." That means any tariff unjustly levelled against a member of the union would precipitate immediate counteraction by all members of the union. Such action would cause equal or greater harm to the U.S. than any offending measures (planned by the U.S.) would cause the union-member(s).
Rather than restricting exports to the US, or imposing tariffs on imports from the U.S. - both of which would harm the union-member - export taxes would be applied to exports to the U.S. and would match - in percentage terms - any penalties imposed by the U.S. Matching export taxes with U.S.-imposed tariffs doubles the pain for American individuals and businesses. At the same time, and until both sides back down place. Revenues raised by export taxes could be used, in those counties, to help moderate the impact of such economic disruptions.
In Canada, export taxes would be applied to energy - hydrocarbons (in all forms), electricity, uranium (in all forms) - and to lumber (much needed for reconstruction following fires in California and floods in the southeast). If we all act in unison and with solidarity, the pain will be as great on America as their measures would have been on members of the proposed union. The plan is meant to discourage such antics and, hopefully, will never need to be executed.
David Puxley, Mahone Bay, NS
Justice David Rose's ruling is not justiceTwo girls who pleaded guilty in alleged fatal swarming sentenced to probation, Jan. 28
The manslaughter two which these two girls pleaded guilty is a serious crime and sentencing should reflect that. Alleged Charter Rights violations should be dealt with separately. Nothing will bring Mr. Lee back, while the girls who murdered him will continue with their lives as though nothing happened. It's embarrassing application of the law.
Lutfi Haj-Assaad, Mississauga
All NATO countries suffered for U.S military folliesDonald Trump calls on NATO members - including Canada - to massively increase defence spending, Jan. 23
Most of the destabilization in the world today can be traced directly back to the U.S in one way or another, Iraq (the big lie), Afghanistan, Libya, anything really in the Middle East, all were affected negatively by flawed U.S. foreign policy. All NATO countries in some way suffered for these bungled U.S military follies. The idea now that NATO should double up to float the U.S, while they constantly sell lethal military armaments to rogue nations is a joke. The U.S. should be paying double to NATO for all the chaos they have sown throughout the world as we know it.
Richard Kadziewicz, Scarborough
Cancel military contracts with U.S.Donald Trump's press secretary says Feb. 1 deadline for tariffs 'still on the books, Jan. 28'
Why are we buying 88 F35A fighters from Lockheed-Martin and 14 to 16 P-8 Poseidon maritime patrol aircraft from Boeing when U.S. President Donald Trump has started a trade war with Canada? Time to cancel these contracts due to "force majeure." I doubt Trump has included our military spending in his trade deficit "analysis," not to mention the lower CAD.
Craig Steels, Calgary, Alta.
Drugs and guns are crossing the border ... into CanadaTariff fight takes centre stage as Ontario election campaign kicks off, Jan. 29
To help Premier Doug Ford negotiate Trump's proposed tariffs, I suggest that Ford admit that drugs are crossing the border. However, in this case, 853 kg of cocaine crossed from Mexico to the U.S.A. then into Canada, according to Toronto Police, who made the recent and largest drug bust in its history. This has to stop. On top of that, hundreds of Canadians are being killed every year by guns, illegally smuggled into Canada from the U.S. Ford can tell U.S. President Donald Trump that he will forgo tariffs once the drug and gun smuggling stops.
Grant Ranalli, Hamilton
Let Canada post perform its mandate
'We are facing insolvency.' Canada Post CEO tells hearing financial situation is 'not sustainable', Jan. 27
Do we honestly expect Canada Post to turn a profit when its mandate requires it to service all of Canada? For years alternate delivery services have been able cherry pick those areas with the greatest revenues. Enough is enough. Let's get back to allowing Canada Post to perform its mandate for all Canadians.
Michael Mahon, Toronto

How are loans that cannot be repaid loans?Of a loan the government of Canada recently extended to Canada Post, the corporation's CEO has said, "It is a temporary loan to ensure we have the money on hand to keep the lights on, continue operating, and pay everyone's wages." Loans that cannot be repaid in the foreseeable future should probably be called permanent loans. Or, has the government already promised Canada Post that this loan of just over one billion dollars will be written off the moment Pierre Poilievre becomes prime minister?
Patrick Cowan, North York

Document 421 of 500

Ford has stripped himself of credibility

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-has-stripped-himself-credibility/docview/3161409375/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+has+stripped+himself+of+credibility&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: On a mid-winter Wednesday morning in Windsor, with a chill wind blowing across the Detroit River, Doug Ford posed for a phoney photo-op.
Against the backdrop of the Ambassador Bridge that ties us inextricably and unpredictably to Donald Trump's America, Ford cast himself as our reliable protector against a volatile U.S. president.
He did it - he called an early election no one wants, at a time no one imagined.
So much for "Get It Done" - his 2022 campaign slogan. Back then, the Progressive Conservative leader earnestly sought a full four-year mandate to get it done if re-elected as premier - on housing, on hallway health care, on the economy.
Ford solemnly sought a contract with voters, expiring only in June 2026, to do all those things. All undone this week, 15 months ahead of schedule.
Promises unkept? Never mind, for there are new promises to keep us safe.
The politician who first won power in 2018 - posing near the American border to proclaim earnestly that "Ontario is open for business" - now says business in Ontario is so vulnerable that he needs a new mandate. The mother of all mandates, crushing the opposition for four more years, all justified by his latest campaign slogan: "Protect Ontario."
Protect? Or a pretext to protect Ford himself - after seven years as premier - from a police probe, from a housing shortfall, from a doctor shortage?
For the next four weeks we'll be watching a scripted campaign without a point for a politician without a purpose - other than perpetuating his time in power. Riding high in the polls, with the province at its lowest point, Ford succumbed to the exhortations of the PC campaign machine.
He called a snap election in hopes of a no-surprises campaign. And yet, despite the urgent call to arms, the truth is that this early election was a long time coming.
Long before Trump regained power, well before anyone imagined a tariff war, Ford's Tories had been urgently planning an early election since early last year. Mindful of the fiscal, political and criminal risks that might get in the way of a third term as premier, they contrived to find a new pretext to pre-empt any bad news.
First they imagined that digging a tunnel under Highway 401 would clear the way to the campaign trail. Then they tried to drive a wedge by bulldozing bike lanes, but that too failed to gain traction on the trail. Belatedly they brought beer to corner stores, until it became a billion-dollar boondoggle.
And so the PC brain trust cooked up a fresh casus belli: Trump's threat to wage economic warfare with 25 per cent tariffs was just the livelihood-threatening crisis that Ford's spin doctors ordered.
Now the legislature has been dissolved so that the Tories can be deployed into battle with all the precision of a military campaign.
Stumbling through snow banks, trudging through slush and salt, bracing against the winter wind, New Democrats, Greens and Liberals will try to recruit supporters and rouse the anti-Ford vote on the doorstep. It will be an uphill climb, which is precisely why the governing PCs have chosen February for this exercise in democracy, hoping that winter will discourage even the most diehard opponents from rising up against them.
But in politics, the best-laid plans and the best-looking polls have a way of melting away when the winds change. His high standing in the polls belies the big risk he is taking.
Ford claims with a straight face that he needs a bigger mandate to spend "tens of billions" bailing out Ontario's economy and protecting its workforce, yet he conveniently ignores the public pledges from his opposition rivals to offer a united front in the legislature. That would be a true show of strength, but the Tories don't want unity, they crave adversity - notwithstanding Ford's eloquent pan-Canadian orations.
True, his big lead appears to be baked into the polls. But the foundational premises upon which this election is based are lodged in quicksand.
Ford faces three wild cards:
What if Trump doesn't impose 25 per cent tariffs this Saturday as threatened? His nominee for commerce secretary, Howard Lutnick, suggested on Wednesday in Washington that they weren't coming as long as Canada acted on its border, while Ford was cheerfully fear-mongering in Windsor.
We will see by late February just how successful Ford is in wooing Washington under the media spotlight after he makes two separate trips. The presidential audience he pined for a few weeks ago still isn't on the schedule; what if Mr. Ford goes to Washington and nobody notices - other than a few Ontario reporters tagging along to see what a campaigning politician sounds like on the stump, stateside?
We will know before voting day whether the president has absorbed Canada through military, economic or political coercion. Assuming we remain an independent country, Ford's favourite pose with a baseball cap - proclaiming "Canada is not for sale" - will wear thin on his head.
If all these political pillars sink, Ford will have a lot of explaining to do - not just about his early election call over a short-term emergency that wasn't so urgent, but about the long-neglected problems closer to home. Not just on hallway health care and housing shortages, but on spending boondoggles that have beset his government on the Greenbelt and beer in corner stores, adding up to billions of dollars.
The more Ford's credibility dissolves over a trumped-up election campaign against Trump, the more his believability will be doubted on election issues closer to home. And the more his major opponents - the NDP's Marit Stiles and Bonnie Crombie of the Liberals - will have the opportunity to call him out.
CAPTION: Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford meets with supporters during his campaign launch in Windsor on Wednesday.Dax Melmer THE CANADIAN PRESS
CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION

Ford's daughter raising funds for husband's police hearing: Officer faces misconduct charges for refusing vaccine

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-daughter-raising-funds-husbands-police/docview/3161408723/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+daughter+raising+funds+for+husband%27s+police+hearing%3A+Officer+faces+misconduct+charges+for+refusing+vaccine&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Krista Ford Haynes is appealing for public donations to help her husband, a staff sergeant with the Toronto Police, fight misconduct charges following his refusal to follow the service's vaccine mandate during the pandemic.
In a social media post, Premier Doug Ford's daughter says the couple wants to raise $100,000 to hire a lawyer independent of the police association, which typically represents officers facing internal charges. Staff Sgt. Ernest "Dave" Haynes faces misconduct allegations from Toronto police and is set to appear at the tribunal Feb. 25.
"I don't have confidence that he will be given a fair hearing or investigation based on our unique political affiliation among other bona fide reasons," said Ford Haynes in an Instagram post on Monday.
Though he declined to comment on this case, Toronto Police Association (TPA) president Clayton Campbell said members are free to seek their own lawyer. The police union typically provides legal support for members who face misconduct allegations.
"If a TPA member decides not to avail themselves of the legal support we provide, they can find, fund, and manage someone on their own," said Campbell.
Efforts to reach Dave and Krista Ford Haynes for comment were unsuccessful.
Haynes, a 22-year veteran of Toronto police, is facing allegations of discreditable conduct, insubordination and breach of confidence, according to a list of charges from a previously scheduled police misconduct hearing.
During the pandemic, TPS mandated that any officers who did not get vaccinated by Nov. 31, 2021, would be placed on indefinite unpaid absence.
Police confirm that Haynes is not currently suspended but has been assigned to the Toronto Police College.
CREDIT: Raju Mudhar Toronto Star

Document 423 of 500

Candidates spar over campaign issues: Leaders trade blows on health care, economy in first day on hustings

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/candidates-spar-over-campaign-issues/docview/3161408164/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Candidates+spar+over+campaign+issues%3A+Leaders+trade+blows+on+health+care%2C+economy+in+first+day+on+hustings&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Trump tariffs, a dearth of doctors, affordability anxieties.
Ontario's snap winter campaign officially kicked off Wednesday with the major party leaders pushing competing narratives to define the Feb. 27 vote.
Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford, trying to win his third majority government, was in Windsor against the backdrop of the Ambassador Bridge, railing against U.S. President Donald Trump's threatened 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian goods.
"I'm asking the people for a strong, stable four-year mandate to do whatever it takes to protect Ontario," said Ford, who, despite already having a legislative majority, triggered a vote 15 months early that Elections Ontario says will cost $189 million.
New Democratic Leader Marit Stiles was in Toronto's Regent Park to emphasize how "unaffordable" life has become since the Tories took office in 2018.
"People are leaving our province, forced out of communities they grew up in. Young people are losing hope. Seniors are ending up homeless. It's tough out there." said Stiles, noting unemployment has risen on Ford's watch.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie was in Barrie with local candidate Dr. Rose Zacharias, the former president of the Ontario Medical Association, to put a spotlight on the fact that 2.5 million people in the province don't have a doctor.
"We're launching our tour here in Barrie because 55,000 people residing in this area don't have access to a family doctor," said Crombie, sporting a white cap with the slogan "Real Leaders Fix Healthcare" that countered Ford's blue anti-Trump "Canada Is Not For Sale" hat.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner, who was in Guelph, said "this election is a referendum on Doug Ford's poor track-record of the past seven years."
In Windsor, where Ford unveiled his campaign vehicle - an RV emblazoned with his photo and the slogan "Protect Ontario" that press gallery wags immediately christened "the Winter-bago" - the Tory leader said the Trump threat will loom even if the president doesn't impose 25 per cent tariffs on Saturday.
"Ontario won't start a fight with the U.S., but you better believe we'll be ready to win one," he said as an icy wind blew over the Detroit River from across the border and a smattering of protesters from a teachers' union rallied nearby.
Ford pledged to spend "tens of billions" in economic supports to fight tariffs if necessary, similar to the fight against COVID-19.
"You may not always agree with me, and that's OK, but my record is clear," he said, appealing to voters leaning toward his rivals.
"There's experience on one side with the PC Party and zero experience with the other three parties."
Asked about his alcohol sales expansion costing taxpayers an additional $612 million, Ford said he had no regrets.
"I believe the people should have a choice and the convenience if they want to grab a beer," added the PC leader, who does not drink alcohol.
"What I'm happy about is convenience stores are making a little more money, they're hiring more people," he told reporters in the New Democrat-held riding of Windsor West.
Ford's opponents say the additional expense proves the booze deal was botched and that he's not up to the job of going head-to-head with Trump on trade issues.
Stiles, whose campaign slogan is "On Your Side," mocked the Tory leader's deal-making prowess.
"He wants us to hire him to be our negotiator with Trump. Doug Ford our negotiator? Is he kidding? Over and over, he makes bad deals for Ontarians," she said, pointing to the Ontario Place redevelopment when "Doug Ford got taken to the cleaners by a foreign corporation."
In a rally with NDP MPPs and candidates at the Daniels Spectrum community art centre in Regent Park, Stiles also attacked Crombie, accusing the Liberal leader of making a right turn away from predecessors like former premier Kathleen Wynne.
"Today's Liberal leader would be right at home as a cabinet minister in Doug Ford's government. Bonnie Crombie doesn't want to get rid of Doug Ford - she wants to be Doug Ford," she quipped in the NDP-held riding.
After governing for almost 15 years between 2003 and 2018, the Grits have been unable to win enough seats to obtain official party status in the past two elections.
"There will probably always be an Ontario Liberal party ... I think they're going to be in the repair shop for a few more years," said Stiles.
In Tory-held Barrie, Crombie countered that the New Democrats have failed to hold the Tories accountable as the Official Opposition.
"They've also had seven years to make a difference and make a change - they haven't gotten it done," she said, conceding she's "a different kind of Liberal" with a business background before she was a MP and the three-term mayor of Mississauga.
"I understand government and I know how to get things done."
But Crombie reiterated "this isn't a necessary election" and said Ford is scrambling to "outrun an RCMP investigation" in the Tories' $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal.
At Queen's Park, Ontario chief electoral officer Greg Essensa said it will cost Elections Ontario $189 million to conduct the first winter vote in the province since 1981.
Essensa, who needs to hire 55,000 workers to staff some 7,000 polling stations in all 124 provincial ridings, urged anyone looking for a temporary job during the writ period to apply at Elections Ontario's employment portal.
With weather a factor at this time of year, the chief electoral officer said "contingency" planning has begun to ensure Ontarians are able to exercise their franchise even if there are blizzards across the province.
Some 10.8 million people are eligible to vote, including the estimated 81,000 who are homeless. They can use the address of the shelter they stay at when they cast their ballots.
Parties are allowed to spend $10.8 million on their campaigns.
Schreiner, meanwhile, blasted "Ford's wasteful spending that only benefits wealthy land speculators and fossil fuel giants like Enbridge, all while ripping off everyday Ontarians who are struggling to make ends meet.
"People deserve better than Doug Ford and this is our chance to turn things around for the many communities that feel abandoned by this government," he said.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie, Kristin Rushowy and Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Tunnel plan is about to ruin a rare GTA transit success story

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tunnel-plan-is-about-ruin-rare-gta-transit/docview/3161406546/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tunnel+plan+is+about+to+ruin+a+rare+GTA+transit+success+story&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Last week, Premier Doug Ford announced an extension of the Hurontario LRT, a light-rail line running through Mississauga right into downtown Brampton. The premier, a well-known subway enthusiast, announced this extension would be built in a tunnel.
This is, broadly speaking, a bad idea, whether for citizens, for the efficient use of tax dollars - or indeed, for transit in the GTA.
True, modern streetcars in tunnels are nothing new; the Harbourfront streetcar runs in a tunnel to Union Station and the much-delayed Eglinton Crosstown is roughly half underground. Tunnels can be used to great effect: in hyper-dense areas, they can sometimes be the only solution. Since they allow transit to duck below streets and sidewalks they allow vehicles to run more often, faster and more reliably. These are all important things.
That doesn't make tunnels a panacea. In Brampton's case the tunnel has generally been framed as "for the preservation of Brampton's historic downtown." Nobody tell our politicians that the Coliseum in Rome has streetcars in front of it (or that it was completed in roughly half the time of the Crosstown)!
There is also the problem of a mixed route. Tunnels can handle trains running far more often than surface streets, but the street is still part of the route. By digging a tunnel, you spend a lot of money to run more trains, but without actually being able to do so.
If this tunnel is actually going to be built, it's critical that additional surface routes are planned in the future to converge in it, say, a route heading east of Shoppers World to Bramalea GO - where frequent GO trains in Brampton terminate, so that the expensive tunnel doesn't sit underused forever.
While the Crosstown probably should have been a subway, it's not clear that this line shouldn't just be a tram.
All the same, while the tunnel itself is likely excessive, Brampton might have earned it. The city is possibly Canada's biggest public transit success story of the past decade, and the new light rail has nothing to do with it.
Instead, it's all about high-quality suburban buses. While Toronto isn't winning any subway contests, urbanists laud the city for its frequent buses through the city's outer reaches - which, despite running along streets like Finch Avenue, attract similar ridership to bus corridors in dense cities like New York. It's no big surprise why these buses are popular: they are affordable and also incredibly frequent, making them a very convenient option.
Brampton has supercharged this model with its frequent Züm express buses, and as a result more people ride the bus every day in Brampton than ride streetcars in Toronto.
This proposed tunnel isn't the first expensive project this government has built (to their credit, they are building a lot). But since Ford also announced his government will be seeking a renewed mandate in a February election, it does make sense to question whether we're headed in the right direction.
Toronto is set to build what may be the most expensive single subway project in world history - the Ontario Line - despite not being as dense as Hong Kong, or as historic as Paris. And costs have only escalated under the new "fiscally conservative" government, even as researchers make it clear what the problems are. At the same time, Metrolinx, responsible for the Crosstown, still doesn't even have a page on their website saying "what went wrong" or "how we are avoiding these mistakes happening again." Now, we are rushing headlong into building even more tunnelled trams.
Not only, then, are these projects mismanaged and public funds misallocated; but the mistakes that led to those consequences are being made again. Another needless, overly expensive tunnel is being built without regard for ever escalating costs and delays.
The Toronto region needs every bit of funding to build transit to fight congestion. But as long as it costs so much to build here, gold plating is something we just can't afford.
Reece Martin is a Toronto based transit and infrastructure writer and technologist. Follow him on Bluesky: @rmtransit.BSKY.SOCIAL
CAPTION: A long-proposed LRT line in Brampton now appears to be set to be buried in a tunnel under the city's downtown, according to an announcement made by Premier Doug Ford.Andrew Francis Wallace Toronto Star
CREDIT: Reece Martin Contributor Reece Martin is a Toronto based transit and infrastructure writer and technologist. Follow him on Bluesky: @rmtransit.BSKY.SOCIAL;

Document 425 of 500

Trump nominee warns of further threats: Border changes just the first stage as Washington eyes Canadian markets

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trump-nominee-warns-further-threats/docview/3161406502/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trump+nominee+warns+of+further+threats%3A+Border+changes+just+the+first+stage+as+Washington+eyes+Canadian+markets&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: U.S. tariffs against Canada could be imposed in two steps, with the first blow coming as early as Saturday if Ottawa can't show it has "shut" its border to illegal immigration and fentanyl smuggling, says Donald Trump's nominee to oversee the contentious move.
The second blow would come later, after April 1, the deadline for U.S. trade officials to report back to Trump on what the U.S. president views as unfair trade practices by America's competitors and allies alike, said Howard Lutnick, Trump's nominee for commerce secretary.
Lutnick, who met with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau in Poland on Monday, suggested during his confirmation hearing in Washington on Wednesday there may an off-ramp in the short term if the president's border concerns are addressed.
"The (drug) labs in Canada are run by Mexican cartels so this (immediate) tariff model is simply to shut their borders," said Lutnick. "So it is not a tariff per se. It is an action of domestic policy. Shut your border and stop allowing fentanyl into our country, killing our people.
"As far as I know, they are acting swiftly, and if they execute it, there will be no tariff. And if they don't, then there will be," Lutnick said.
Trudeau briefed premiers during a virtual meeting Wednesday on his talks with Lutnick, which a federal official said came at Canada's request and were held before a ceremony marking the 80th anniversary of the liberation of the Auschwitz-Birkenau concentration camp.
Trudeau also briefed premiers on his decision to adopt a tougher initial response of retaliatory counter-tariffs on American products than had previously been planned, saying Canada would leave room to escalate its countermeasures later, according to provincial and federal officials, who the Star agreed not to identify in order to speak frankly about the private discussions.
A federal official said Trudeau continues to say everything must be on the table - including energy export controls opposed by Alberta and Saskatchewan - but that Canada's response depends on what Trump does.
With the immediate prospect of U.S. tariffs looming, and under pressure by provinces, Ottawa is prepared to impose countermeasures against a broader list of American products.
Countermeasures against more than $37 billion worth of imports would be the next step, which could ramp up to surcharges on $150 billion worth of American imports, according to officials.
Sources also said several premiers pressed Trudeau to accelerate defence spending to meet, if not exceed, NATO's target of two per cent of GDP, and bolster Arctic security.
A federal official said Alberta Premier Danielle Smith went so far as to suggest Canada should offer to allow the U.S. military to operate bases in Canada's north.
Ivana Yelich, a spokesperson for Premier Doug Ford, who is campaigning for a new electoral mandate to respond to Trump, said in a statement that, "with tariffs now potentially days away, the premier is glad to see that the federal government's initial retaliatory actions are more aligned with the proportional, dollar-for-dollar response he's been advocating for, concentrated in states we know will do the most damage.
"Canada won't start this fight, but we must be ready to respond with strength when President Trump imposes tariffs."
Ford, she said, stressed the "urgent need for the federal government to invest more in Canada's military and demonstrate how it is making Canada's border more secure.
"The federal government also needs to engage with the Trump administration and all U.S. lawmakers on a vision that achieves American objectives of lower costs, energy security and economic growth - the kind of vision the premier has been promoting as part of Fortress Am-Can. Had the federal government done all of this sooner, we might have been able to avoid tariffs."
Meanwhile, cabinet members like Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly and Public Safety Minister David McGuinty continue to scramble to do just that - avoid tariffs - by lining up meetings in Washington with counterparts.
Alberta's Smith said the federal commitment and action by provinces on their borders have been "noticed" in Washington.
"The question of whether it will be enough, I guess we'll find out on Feb. 1," she said.
Lutnick's testimony elaborated on his and Trump's staunch pro-tariff views.
In the long run, Lutnick said, Trump wants to increase America's penetration into markets around the world. He cited barriers to U.S. dairy products in Canada as a particular irritant, and said Trump aims to bring more investment and manufacturing to America, especially in sectors like semiconductors, auto and artificial intelligence.
Under questioning, Lutnick said his preference is not for "targeted tariffs" against specific sectors or countries, but for broad, across-the-board tariffs against America's biggest competitors, even if they are some of its closest allies.
"We are treated horribly by the global trading environment," he said. "They all have higher tariffs, non-tariff trade barriers and subsidies. They treat us poorly. We need to be treated better. We need to be treated with respect, and we can use tariffs to create reciprocity. So fairness and respect."
Lutnick said America's "great" allies in Europe, Japan and Korea "have taken advantage of our good nature," pointing to European barriers to American beef or fertilizer exports, and to "steel in Japan and appliances in Korea."
He said "the big tariff view is going to be studied," referring to Trump's executive order asking the Commerce Department and the United States Trade Representative's office to report back by April 1 on measures to correct what the president sees as unfair trade deficits with countries like China, Canada and Mexico.
But he did not specify what tariff rate Trump might impose on Canadian imports on the weekend.
Trump has not backed off his vow of 25 per cent tariffs in the past week.
CAPTION: U.S. Vice-President JD Vance shakes hands with Howard Lutnick, Donald Trump's nominee for commerce secretary, on Wednesday. Lutnick said Canada could avoid tariffs in the short term if the U.S. president's border concerns are addressed.Kayla Bartkowski GETTY IMAGES

CREDIT: Tonda MacCharles Toronto Star

Document 426 of 500

Towing firm shuts down amid violence: Urban Auto driver shot Tuesday latest in series of attacks

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/towing-firm-shuts-down-amid-violence/docview/3161406467/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Towing+firm+shuts+down+amid+violence%3A+Urban+Auto+driver+shot+Tuesday+latest+in+series+of+attacks&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Management for a Scarborough tow truck operation say they're being forced out of business because of continued industry violence.
In a letter Wednesday, Urban Auto Collision managers identified one of their drivers, Samuel Durani, as the tow truck driver seriously injured in a shooting near Kennedy Road and Progress Avenue on Tuesday night.
The letter's recipients included Durham, Toronto and Ontario Provincial police, along with Premier Doug Ford, a Toronto Star reporter and other media.
Urban operations manager Ranjit Kumar said in an interview Wednesday the letter follows many violent incidents against his workers and efforts to get police help.
"Nobody is taking any action," Kumar said.
"Over the past year, our shop ... has been shot at more than 15 times, and today's incident brings the total number of our drivers hospitalized due to gunshot wounds to four," the letter reads.
"This does not include the additional eight times our tow trucks have been shot."
The letter states the company has had to suspend its operations, leaving more than 20 drivers without jobs.
"We are unable to continue if the police and government cannot help us."
Urban Auto said is has "made repeated attempts to raise awareness within law enforcement about the dangers (of) the towing industry" but has seen a "lack of meaningful action" in return.
The letter accuses officials, legal firms, operators and other industry players of collusion, which the company says has "exacerbated" the situation.
When reached for comment, the Toronto Police Service said it could not comment on Urban's specific concerns, but highlighted recent efforts to curb violence and corruption in the industry, including its new towing task force, Project Dodger, launched this month.
At the time, police said at least 70 per cent of the shootings in the city so far this year had been linked to the industry.
Urban, however, expressed concerns about Project Dodger.
"History has a tendency to repeat itself, particularly within the tow truck industry, which has been grappling with significant issues for some time," the letter states. "These issues often culminate in operations or 'projects' that not only result in the arrest of tow truck owners but also highlight the involvement of corrupt police officers."
The letter also comes in the wake of a Toronto Star report that area tow drivers are being illegally "taxed" for the right to work on 400 series highways, and that drivers who don't, or can't, pay are often targets of violence.
Those illegal "taxes," also called "union dues," are suspected by police to be at the root of at least one murder and a rash of arsons in the GTA since midsummer.
Police announced Project Dodger on Jan. 13, amid what investigators described as a "troubling increase in gun violence" connected to the industry.
The team will investigate violent incidents, including firearm discharges, shootings and arsons, which have been tied to ongoing territorial disputes within the tow truck industry, the service said.
Project Dodger is the successor to "Project Beacon," a task force that ran from June to August 2024.
"Project Beacon resulted in the arrest of six individuals and 184 charges laid related to a homicide and 13 tow truck-related firearm discharge incidents," police said.
"We remain committed to addressing tow-truck-related crimes with a service-wide approach, acknowledging that this violence stems from a small segment of the industry," said Supt. Paul MacIntyre of Organized Crime Enforcement in a statement.
In 2024, there were 63 firearm discharge and shooting incidents linked to tow-truck-related disputes, accounting for almost 13 per cent of all shootings in Toronto that year.
CREDIT: Peter Edwards and AbO'Brien Toronto Star

Document 427 of 500

How can you vote in Ontario's election while out of the province or country?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/how-can-you-vote-ontarios-election-while-out/docview/3161406455/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+can+you+vote+in+Ontario%27s+election+while+out+of+the+province+or+country%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario Premier Doug Ford called a snap election this week, giving Ontarians little over a month to hit the polls.
Here's what you need to know about casting your vote if you'll be outside of the province or country leading up to the election.
When is the election?
Ford paid a visit to Lt.-Gov. Edith Dumont on Tuesday and asked to dissolve the legislature so an election could be called.
With less than 30 days notice, election day has been set for Feb. 27., the first provincial winter vote since 1981.
Who is eligible to vote?
To vote in the provincial election, you must be at least 18 years old, a Canadian citizen and a resident of Ontario, according to Elections Ontario's website.
You can confirm, update or add your information to the Elections Ontario registry by Feb. 17 to receive your voter card, with information on when and where to vote, in the mail.
How can I cast my vote from outside Ontario?
Ontario residents who will be outside of the province leading up to the election and unable to vote early in-person can cast their vote by mail instead.
Anyone planning to do so will need to fill out a vote by mail application and provide a copy of at least one piece of government-issued ID that includes their name and address.
Applications can be filled out online or manually by downloading the form the Elections Ontario website.
Applications must be submitted to Elections Ontario by 6 p.m. on Feb. 21 - six days out from election day - to be considered. Once the application has been reviewed and approved, a voting kit with a ballot will be mailed out to the address provided.
The kits will be mailed out with a pre-paid envelope for their return, but people outside of Canada will have to cover the cost of international postage to return their voting kits themselves.
You can track the status of your voting kit using a confirmation number provided upon submission.
Elections Ontario must receive your completed voting kit by 6 p.m. on election day for the vote to be counted.
Registering as an absentee voter
People who are temporarily living outside of the province can also put themselves on the absentee voter registry to ensure they automatically receive a voting kit in the mail when an election is called.
To be eligible you have to be a Canadian citizen 18 years-of-age or older who lived in Ontario for at least 12 consecutive months before leaving, with intentions to return.
Once registered, absentee voters remain eligible for up to two years from the date they left Ontario.
However, once registered, voters can't make changes to their absentee record starting five weeks out from election day, and will need to update their information once they've returned to Ontario or the two years have passed.
CAPTION: People who will be out of the province leading up to Ontario's provincial election on Feb. 27 can still cast their vote by mail.

Subject: Absentee voting; Provincial elections; Voters; Elections

Full text: Countries hit by tariffs should band together
White House says Colombia agrees to take deported
migrants after Trump tariff showdown, Jan. 27
Unions were formed to give strength, in numbers, to those who might, otherwise, have been picked-off, one-by-one by powerful and ruthless industry barons. A union of like-minded workers levelled the playing-field.
Last weekend, Colombia initially refused to let aircraft carrying illegal immigrants from the U.S. to land in its country. U.S. President Donald Trump reacted by imposing withering tariffs on Columbia, and the country immediately backed down.
Perhaps, more than anything, Trump seized the opportunity to demonstrate how tough the U.S. was going to be against any nation who dared to stand up against his bullying. This was similar to tactics that have been used by captains of industry against workers, whether unionized or not.
Canada, Mexico, Colombia and European countries should form a union. The rallying cry would be, "An attack against one of us is an attack against all of us." Any tariff unjustly levelled against a member of the union would precipitate immediate counteraction by all members of the union. Such action would cause equal or greater harm to the U.S. than any offending measures would cause the union members.
Rather than restricting exports to the U.S., or imposing tariffs on imports from the U.S. - both of which would harm the union members - export taxes would be applied to exports to the U.S. and would match any penalties imposed by the U.S. Matching export taxes with U.S.-imposed tariffs doubles the pain for American individuals and businesses. Revenues raised from export taxes could be used to help moderate the impact of such economic disruptions.
In Canada, export taxes would be applied to energy and to lumber.
If we all act in unison and with solidarity, the pain will be as great on America as their measures would have been on members of the proposed union. The plan is meant to discourage such antics and, hopefully, will never need to be executed.
David Puxley, Mahone Bay, N.S.
Teens' sentence for manslaughter is not justice
Two girls who pleaded guilty in alleged fatal swarming sentenced to probation, Jan. 28
The manslaughter to which these two girls pleaded guilty is a serious crime and sentencing should reflect that.
Alleged Charter violations should be dealt with separately.
Nothing will bring Kenneth Lee back, while the girls who murdered him will continue with their lives as though nothing happened. It's embarrassing application of the law.
Lutfi Haj-Assaad, Mississauga
NATO countries all suffered over
bad U.S. policies
Donald Trump calls on NATO members - including Canada - to massively increase defence spending, Jan. 23
Most of the destabilization in the world today can be traced directly back to the U.S in one way or another: Iraq (the big lie); Afghanistan; Libya; anything really in the Middle East - all were affected negatively by flawed U.S. foreign policy.
All NATO countries in some way suffered for these bungled U.S military follies. The idea now that NATO should double up to float the U.S., while they constantly sell lethal military armaments to rogue nations is a joke.
The U.S. should be paying double to NATO for all the chaos it has sown throughout the world as we know it.
Richard Kadziewicz,
Scarborough
Canada should cancel military contracts
Donald Trump's press
secretary says Feb. 1 deadline for tariffs 'still on the books,' Jan. 28
Why are we buying 88 F35A fighters from Lockheed-Martin and 14 to 16 P-8 Poseidon maritime patrol aircraft from Boeing when U.S. President Donald Trump has started a trade war with Canada?
Time to cancel these contracts due to "force majeure." I doubt Trump has included our military spending in his trade deficit "analysis," not to mention the lower Canadian dollar.
Craig Steels, Calgary
Drugs and guns cross the border ... into Canada
Tariff fight takes centre stage as Ontario election campaign kicks off, Jan. 29
To help Premier Doug Ford negotiate U.S. President Donald Trump's proposed tariffs, I suggest he admit that drugs are crossing the border.
However, in this case, 853 kilograms of cocaine crossed from Mexico to the U.S. then into Canada, according to Toronto police, who made the recent and largest drug bust in its history.
This has to stop.
On top of that, hundreds of Canadians are being killed every year by guns illegally smuggled into Canada from the U.S.
Ford can tell Trump that he will forgo tariffs once the drug and gun smuggling stops.
Grant Ranalli, Hamilton
Nationwide mandate hurts Canada Post
'We are facing insolvency.' Canada Post CEO tells hearing financial situation is
'not sustainable', Jan. 27
Do we honestly expect Canada Post to turn a profit when its mandate requires it to serve all of Canada?
For years, alternate delivery services have been able to cherry pick those areas with the greatest revenues.
Enough is enough. Let's get back to allowing Canada Post to perform its mandate for all Canadians.
Michael Mahon, Toronto
Loans that cannot be repaid are not loans
Of a loan the government of Canada recently extended to Canada Post, the corporation's CEO has said, "It is a temporary loan to ensure we have the money on hand to keep the lights on, continue operating, and pay everyone's wages."
Loans that cannot be repaid in the foreseeable future should probably be called permanent loans.
Or, has the government already promised Canada Post that this loan of just over $1 billion will be written off the moment Pierre Poilievre becomes prime minister?
Patrick Cowan, North York

Document 429 of 500

Opponents can make Ford's move backfire

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/opponents-can-make-fords-move-backfire/docview/3161406291/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Opponents+can+make+Ford%27s+move+backfire&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford's stated reason for calling an election more than a year early is plainly ridiculous.
The premier no more needs a new mandate to deal with the threat of U.S. tariffs than he has needed one to implement any number of policy positions in recent years, from transit to housing to alcohol sales to sending out $200 cheques to everyone and their dog. He didn't need the explicit approval of the electorate to do any of these things because he already had it. To paraphrase Don Draper from "Mad Men": that's what the majority government is for.
But his opponents shouldn't spend too much time in the coming campaign complaining about the fact that the campaign is happening: that horse has well and truly bolted. As much as Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and NDP Leader Marit Stiles might want to criticize Ford for an unnecessary election - and to paint the premier as motivated by self-interest - that argument can only carry them so far.
Election campaigns move on, and even if the public has no great desire for this one, eventually voters will accept that it's happening. No one is going to cast a ballot a month from now to say that they really are mad about casting that ballot.
Besides, opportunities abound. Though comparisons have been made to David Peterson's ill-fated decision to send Ontario to the polls in 1990, when his Liberal government was toppled by Bob Rae's upstart NDP, there's a more recent example of a leader who got greedy and paid for it.
Justin Trudeau called a snap election in 2021 in a situation that has some parallels to Ford's move. The prime minister had untested opponents (particularly then-Conservative leader Erin O'Toole), and he was trying to lock in a majority before the other parties had time to prepare themselves for a fight. It was deeply cynical, especially as it came at a point in the pandemic when some voters were at greater risk of serious illness, yet no one spent weeks grousing about the fact that the campaign was on. Trudeau was criticized for it at the start, then everyone moved along.
The PM's decision to seek a new mandate backfired. The results were almost identical to the federal vote that had taken place two years earlier, but Trudeau's position was significantly weakened. It has never recovered.
Ontario's opposition leaders have a chance to deliver a similar wound to Ford. The threat of tariffs should be a non-issue: everyone knows that, whatever Canada's response to whatever U.S. President Donald Trump eventually does is, it won't be dependent on how many MPPs Ontario's premier has at the legislature. And, having been granted an early chance to challenge Ford on his record, Crombie and Stiles should be eager to pounce. Crombie, in particular, has been the Liberal leader for more than a year now, but she still doesn't have a seat in the legislature and hasn't been able to introduce herself to Ontarians in any meaningful way. It's tough to set an agenda when you have a few scattered MPPs and little money. But Ford's early call has gifted her the platform from which to make her pitch. No longer reduced to speaking from the sidelines at Queen's Park, Crombie now has a starring role.
And while opposition leaders in a majority government are relegated to complaining about what the ruling party does without being able to do much of anything about it, Crombie and Stiles have been handed the chance to renew their influence. Now they can say what they would do as premier and, crucially, have the opportunity to do it.
Ford could have kept his rivals boxed in and used his healthy majority to govern as he saw fit well into 2026. Instead, he's opened himself up to judgment. His opponents should be embracing the good fortune he has provided them.
The polls suggest that the Progressive Conservatives enjoy a strong lead, which is why Ford has sought to press his advantage. But everyone who calls a snap vote assumes easy victory is a given.
It isn't, of course. That's what elections are for.
Scott Stinson is a journalist based in suburban Toronto.
CREDIT: Scott Stinson Contributor Scott Stinson is a journalist based in suburban Toronto.;

Subject: Voters; Elections; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Tariffs

Location: United States--US
