Document 430 of 500

Will the dream of high speed rail become a casualty of Canada's current political turmoil?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/will-dream-high-speed-rail-become-casualty/docview/3161406147/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Will+the+dream+of+high+speed+rail+become+a+casualty+of+Canada%27s+current+political+turmoil%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Can the elusive dream of high speed rail through Canada's most populous region survive the political shifts of the coming months?
At least one Liberal leadership contender - Karina Gould - is backing the idea, but questions remain around how much the project would cost, and whether a Conservative government under Pierre Poilievre would proceed with the idea.
Expectations of a decision on a proposed superproject to build faster - possibly much faster - train service from Toronto to Quebec City ramped up last fall, and although further details are still under wraps, Transportation Minister Anita Anand said this week that the government maintains plans to reduce rail travel times between Toronto and Montreal to under four hours.
But events have barrelled down the track in recent weeks. Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, pushed out by caucus unrest and the shock resignation of his finance minister and longtime political partner, shut down Parliament so his party could run a leadership race to replace him. All three main opposition parties now say they want an election, and intend to topple the minority Liberal administration after Parliament resumes March 24.
At the same time, the political and business classes are transfixed by U.S. President Donald Trump's threat to impose trade tariffs on all Canadian imports that groups like the Canadian Chamber of Commerce warn could trigger a recession and jeopardize hundreds of thousands of jobs.
So where does that leave the proposal for the possible high speed rail (HSR) line?
The Crown corporation charged with managing the project, VIA HFR, says it's ready to proceed. After accepting bids to develop a new dedicated rail line to beat the travel times currently available from Via Rail, the government was expected to choose a private consortium to develop it at some point last fall.
In October, Radio-Canada reported the Trudeau government would announce plans for a high speed rail line from Toronto to Quebec City in the coming weeks.
Three months later, the government still hasn't made an announcement, after it extended the time frame for the bids from the private consortia that were submitted in July 2024. Anand's office said the extension is standard but further details are secret because they're commercially sensitive.
"We're working to revolutionize passenger rail with a vision for faster, more reliable service that could connect Montreal and Toronto in under four hours," Anand said in a statement from her office that took aim at Poilievre.
She accused him of planning spending cuts while the Liberals are "focused on what we're building to transform Canada for future generations."
A key question is how fast trains on a proposed new line would travel. Martin Imbleau, the head of VIA HFR, has likened the vision to the high speed trains in France. Last year, he floated the prospect of travelling by rail from Toronto to Montreal in little more than three hours, down from the more-than five hours it takes now. The private groups that have made bids to build the new line have each submitted two proposals: one for trains that go slower than 200 km/h, and one for high speed rail that exceeds 200 km/h.
Some Liberals, including at least one cabinet minister, are keen to see high speed rail from Toronto to Quebec City. Procurement Minister Jean-Yves Duclos is calling on the party's leadership contenders to endorse the vision in their drives to replace Trudeau as prime minister.
"This project would be of great, great value for all sorts of obvious reasons," Duclos told the Star this week, arguing it would spur economic growth and help reduce greenhouse gas emissions that cause climate change.
"My personal point of view as a member of the Liberal party is that this is a very important project, and I would encourage leader the candidates in the leadership race to consider that project in their policy proposals," he said.
Gould, the Burlington MP running for the Liberal leadership, would "absolutely" strive to build high speed rail from Toronto to Quebec City if she becomes prime minister, her campaign told the Star.
"The work being done right now on the proposed corridor will be vital to grow the economy in Ontario and Quebec," it said in a statement.
"We need to support projects like this around the country, which is why she is also supportive of Edmonton-Calgary high speed rail."
Former Bank of Canada and Bank of England governor Mark Carney, who is also running for the Liberal leadership, stopped short in his own statement of endorsing construction of HSR from Toronto to Quebec City. But his campaign said Carney - who has spoken about the world being in an "investment era" for reducing greenhouse gas emissions and advancing technology - wants to "build the fastest growing economy in the G7, including with the right plan to strengthen our transportation infrastructure from coast to coast to coast."
His campaign promised more details in the coming weeks.
Chrystia Freeland, the former finance minister who resigned in December and is now running for leader, will have "more to say" very soon as she rolls out her policy vision, according to campaign spokesperson Katherine Cuplinskas.
Other leadership campaigns did not respond this week when asked about high speed rail.
The Conservatives also did not respond when asked if a Poilievre government would keep the Crown corporation and pursue a high speed rail line through that corridor. Previously, the party's transport critic has cast doubt on the project, comparing the proposal to a "photo op" and arguing the public can't trust the Liberal government to get it built.
Meanwhile, the Bloc Québécois has expressed support for the project. The New Democrats want to see high speed rail from Toronto to Quebec City, but want any project to be exclude private corporations that the party argue will make it more expensive to build, NDP spokesperson Erin Morrison said.
Neither the Crown corporation nor the government has said how much the project could cost, given how critical aspects - like the speed of the trains - remain undecided.
In 2021, then-transport minister Omar Alghabra suggested it might cost up to $12 billion to build. But a government source told the Star last fall the cost would likely be higher, depending on factors like inflation over the past four years, and how much of the new line would be built for high speeds.
Paul Langan, a long-time advocate for high speed rail in Canada, said Wednesday that several possible projects have been derailed by political changes in the past. That includes a proposed high speed train that was abandoned in the early 2000s when Paul Martin took over from Jean Chrétien as prime minister, and how Doug Ford's Progressive Conservative government shelved plans created under Liberal premier Kathleen Wynne for a high-speed train from London to Toronto, Langan said.
Langan fears the shape of government that emerges from the current political churn will be one that does not favour an expensive new - and, in his view, beneficial and long overdue - project like this. Until then, he looks with envy to countries in Europe and Asia which have had high speed rail for decades.
"Politics at any level has a direct effect of us joining the rest of the modern world," he said.
CAPTION: Karina Gould speaks to reporters after filing her paperwork to run for the Liberal leadership in Ottawa on Jan. 23, 2025.

Subject: Greenhouse gases; Emissions; Political leadership; Prime ministers; High speed

Document 431 of 500

Doug Ford has stripped himself of credibility with a Trumped-up snap election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-has-stripped-himself-credibility-with/docview/3161573878/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+has+stripped+himself+of+credibility+with+a+Trumped-up+snap+election&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: On a mid-winter Wednesday morning in Windsor, with a chill wind blowing across the Detroit River, Doug Ford posed for a phoney photo-op.
Against the backdrop of the Ambassador Bridge that ties us inextricably and unpredictably to Donald Trump's America, Ford cast himself as our reliable protector against a volatile U.S. president.
He did it - he called an early election no one wants, at a time no one imagined.
So much for "Get It Done" - his 2022 campaign slogan. Back then, the Progressive Conservative leader earnestly sought a full four-year mandate to get it done if re-elected as premier - on housing, on hallway health care, on the economy.
Ford solemnly sought a contract with voters, expiring only in June 2026, to do all those things. All undone this week, 15 months ahead of schedule.
Promises unkept? Never mind, for there are new promises to keep us safe.
The politician who first won power in 2018 - posing near the American border to proclaim earnestly that "Ontario is open for business" - now says business in Ontario is so vulnerable that he needs a new mandate. The mother of all mandates, crushing the opposition for four more years, all justified by his latest campaign slogan:
"Protect Ontario."
Protect? Or a pretext to protect Ford himself - after seven years as premier - from a police probe, from a housing shortfall, from a doctor shortage?
For the next four weeks we'll be watching a scripted campaign without a point for a politician without a purpose - other than perpetuating his time in power. Riding high in the polls, with the province at its lowest point, Ford succumbed to the exhortations of the PC campaign machine.
He called a snap election in hopes of a no-surprises campaign. And yet, despite the urgent call to arms, the truth is that this early election was a long time coming.
Long before Trump regained power, well before anyone imagined a tariff war, Ford's Tories had been urgently planning an early election since early last year. Mindful of the fiscal, political and criminal risks that might get in the way of a third term as premier, they contrived to find a new pretext to pre-empt any bad news.
First they imagined that digging a tunnel under Highway 401 would clear the way to the campaign trail. Then they tried to drive a wedge by bulldozing bike lanes, but that too failed to gain traction on the trail. Belatedly they brought beer to corner stores, until it became a billion-dollar boondoggle.
And so the PC brain trust cooked up a fresh casus belli: Trump's threat to wage economic warfare with 25 per cent tariffs was just the livelihood-threatening crisis that Ford's spin doctors ordered.
Now the legislature has been dissolved so that the Tories can be deployed into battle with all the precision of a military campaign.
Stumbling through snow banks, trudging through slush and salt, bracing against the winter wind, New Democrats, Greens and Liberals will try to recruit supporters and rouse the anti-Ford vote on the doorstep. It will be an uphill climb, which is precisely why the governing PCs have chosen February for this exercise in democracy, hoping that winter will discourage even the most diehard opponents from rising up against them.
But in politics, the best-laid plans and the best-looking polls have a way of melting away when the winds change. His high standing in the polls belies the big risk he is taking.
Ford claims with a straight face that he needs a bigger mandate to spend "tens of billions" bailing out Ontario's economy and protecting its workforce, yet he conveniently ignores the public pledges from his opposition rivals to offer a united front in the legislature. That would be a true show of strength, but the Tories don't want unity, they crave adversity - notwithstanding Ford's eloquent pan-Canadian orations.
True, his big lead appears to be baked into the polls. But the foundational premises upon which this election is based are lodged in quicksand.
Ford faces three wild cards:
What if Trump doesn't impose 25 per cent tariffs this Saturday as threatened? His nominee for commerce secretary, Howard Lutnick, suggested on Wednesday in Washington that they weren't coming as long as Canada acted on its border, while Ford was cheerfully fear-mongering in Windsor.
We will see by late February just how successful Ford is in wooing Washington under the media spotlight after he makes two separate trips. The presidential audience he pined for a few weeks ago still isn't on the schedule; what if Mr. Ford goes to Washington and nobody notices - other than a few Ontario reporters tagging along to see what a campaigning politician sounds like on the stump, stateside?
We will know before voting day whether the president has absorbed Canada through military, economic or political coercion. Assuming we remain an independent country, Ford's favourite pose with a baseball cap - proclaiming "Canada is not for sale" - will wear thin on his head.
If all these political pillars sink, Ford will have a lot of explaining to do - not just about his early election call over a short-term emergency that wasn't so urgent, but about the long-neglected problems closer to home. Not just on hallway health care and housing shortages, but on spending boondoggles that have beset his government on the Greenbelt and beer in corner stores, adding up to billions of dollars.
The more Ford's credibility dissolves over a trumped-up election campaign against Trump, the more his believability will be doubted on election issues closer to home. And the more his major opponents - the NDP's Marit Stiles and Bonnie Crombie of the Liberals - will have the opportunity to call him out.
CAPTION: Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford meets with supporters during his campaign launch in Windsor on Jan. 29, 2025.

Document 432 of 500

Scarborough towing company suspends operations citing ongoing violence, after drivers shot

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/scarborough-towing-company-suspends-operations/docview/3161573692/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Scarborough+towing+company+suspends+operations+citing+ongoing+violence%2C+after+drivers+shot&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Management for a Scarborough tow truck operation say they're being forced out of business because of continued industry violence.
In a letter, sent Wednesday, Urban Auto Collision management identified one of their drivers, Samuel Durani, as the tow truck driver seriously injured in a shooting near Kennedy Road and Progress Avenue on Tuesday night.
The letter's recipients included Durham, Toronto and Ontario Provincial police, along with the Office of Premier Doug Ford along with a Toronto Star reporter and other media.
Urban operations manager Ranjit Kumar said in an interview Wednesday that the letter follows numerous violent incidents against his workers and efforts to get police action.
"Nobody is taking any action," Kumar said.
"Over the past year, our shop (..) has been shot at more than 15 times, and today's incident brings the total number of our drivers hospitalized due to gunshot wounds to four," the letter reads.
"This does not include the additional eight times our tow trucks have been shot," it continues.
The letter claims the company has had to suspend its operations, leaving more than 20 drivers without jobs.
"We are unable to continue if the police and government cannot help us," the letter says.
Urban Auto said they've "made repeated attempts to raise awareness within law enforcement about the dangers (of) the towing industry" but have seen a "lack of meaningful action" in return.
The letter goes on to accuse officials, legal firms, operators, and other industry players of collusion that the company says has "exacerbated" the situation.
When reached for comment, the Toronto Police Service said it could not comment on Urban's specific concerns, but highlighted recent efforts to curb violence and corruption in the industry, including its new towing task force, Project Dodger, launched earlier this month.
At the time, police said at latest 70 per cent of the shootings in the city so far this year had been linked to the industry.
Urban, however, expressed concerns about Toronto's Project Dodger.
"History has a tendency to repeat itself, particularly within the tow truck industry, which has been grappling with significant issues for some time," the letter states. "These issues often culminate in operations or "projects" that not only result in the arrest of tow truck owners but also highlight the involvement of corrupt police officers."
The letter also comes in the wake of a Toronto Star report that area tow drivers are being illegally "taxed" for the right to work on 400 series highways, and that drivers who don't - or can't - pay up are often targets of violence.
Those illegal "taxes," also called "union dues," are suspected by police to be at the root of at least one murder and a rash of arsons in the GTA since midsummer.
TPS launches Project Dodger
Police announced Project Dodger on Jan. 13 amidst what investigators described as a "troubling increase in gun violence" connected to the industry.
At the time, police said more than 70 per cent of the shootings in the city so far this year had been linked to the industry.
The team will investigate violent incidents, including firearm discharges, shootings and arsons, which have been tied to ongoing territorial disputes within the tow truck industry, the service said.
Project Dodger is the successor to "Project Beacon," a task force that ran from June to August 2024.
"Project Beacon resulted in the arrest of six individuals and 184 charges laid related to a homicide and 13 tow truck-related firearm discharge incidents," police said.
"We remain committed to addressing tow truck-related crimes with a servicewide approach, acknowledging that this violence stems from a small segment of the industry," said Superintendent Paul MacIntyre of Organized Crime Enforcement in a prepared statement.
In 2024, there were 63 firearm discharge and shooting incidents linked to tow truck-related disputes, accounting for almost 13 per cent of all shootings in Toronto last year.
CAPTION: Toronto police launched an initiative to address a rise in criminal and violent incidents in the tow truck industry. A tow truck drives along the Don Valley Parkway in Toronto in a file photo.

Subject: Task forces; Shootings; Criminal investigations; Gun violence

Krista Ford Haynes launches Gofundme campaign to raise funds for husband's legal representation at Toronto police misconduct hearing

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/krista-ford-haynes-launches-gofundme-campaign/docview/3160911379/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Krista+Ford+Haynes+launches+Gofundme+campaign+to+raise+funds+for+husband%27s+legal+representation+at+Toronto+police+misconduct+hearing&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Krista Ford Haynes is appealing for public donations to help her husband, a staff sergeant with the Toronto Police, fight misconduct charges following his refusal to follow the service's vaccine mandate during the pandemic.
In a social media post, Premier Doug Ford's daughter says the couple wants to raise $100,000 to hire a lawyer independent of the police association, which typically represents officers facing internal charges.
Staff Sgt. Ernest "Dave" Haynes faces misconduct allegations from Toronto police and is set to appear at the tribunal Feb. 25.
"I don't have confidence that he will be given a fair hearing or investigation based on our unique political affiliation among other bona fide reasons," said Ford in an Instagram post on Monday evening.
Though he declined to comment on this case, Toronto Police Association (TPA) president Clayton Campbell said members are free to seek their own lawyer. The police union typically provides legal support for members who face misconduct allegations.
"If a TPA member decides not to avail themselves of the legal support we provide, they can find, fund, and manage someone on their own," said Campbell.
Efforts to reach Dave and Krista Ford Haynes for comment were unsuccessful.
Haynes, a 22-year veteran of Toronto police, is facing allegations of discreditable conduct, insubordination and breach of confidence, according to a list of charges from a previously scheduled police misconduct hearing.
During the pandemic, TPS mandated that any officers who did not get vaccinated by Nov. 31, 2021, would be placed on indefinite unpaid absence.
Nadine Ramadan, a senior communication adviser at Toronto police said the service will not comment on personnel matters until the officer appears before the disciplinary tribunal. Police confirm that Haynes is not currently suspended but has been assigned to the Toronto Police College.
Haynes Ford expressed anti-vaccination and anti-lockdown views during the pandemic, and posts from her account as well as language from the fundraising post point to those views playing a role.
"In the last 4 years, my husband has been sent home on unpaid leave for 7 months for asking for more information about the efficacy of a vaccine being forced upon its members," the Gofundme post states.
Haynes Ford's Instagram account - which shows that bodybuilding is a passion - has pinned posts about vaccine mandates, questioning their necessity. Those views on vaccinations are believed to have caused a rift in the Ford family, leading to her eventually being left off the Ford Family Christmas card in 2022.
At the time, she wrote that it was her choice not to be part of it, writing online "I love my family. We're very much in different positions."
CAPTION: Krista Ford Haynes launched a Gofundme with an Instagram post on Monday evening, seeking to raise $100,000 to hire legal representation for her husband who faces misconduct charges from the Toronto Police Services.

Subject: Official misconduct; Vaccines; Immunization; Pandemics

Solution to homelessness is homes, not handcuffs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/solution-homelessness-is-homes-not-handcuffs/docview/3160911326/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Solution+to+homelessness+is+homes%2C+not+handcuffs&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ford's approach to the homeless is cold and uncaringFord pledges tough new legislation to dismantle homeless encampments, Dec. 5
Just before the holidays, Premier Doug Ford proposed an inhumane new approach to homeless encampments that punches down on people already suffering. It will do nothing to help them while making the problem of encampments worse.
The so-called "Safer Municipalities Act" would give Ontario cities unnecessary new powers to clear homeless encampments with police force and arrest our neighbours experiencing homelessness.
No one believes a tent in a park is an appropriate place to live, but this is not the answer. It's ineffective, it's expensive and it's cruel. It's an unserious plan for a very serious and urgent problem our most vulnerable neighbours are facing.
I don't support Premier Ford trampling the basic human rights of our neighbours when there are effective evidence-based solutions we can use instead to house people quickly. And it's high time we got serious about addressing the root cause of this crisis, which is a lack of affordable housing, something the Ford government has done little to solve.
We need an urgent solution to encampments and that solution is homes, not handcuffs.
Lorraine Barnaby, Toronto
Ontario Place redevelopment must be stoppedAuditor general slams Ontario Place redevelopment process as unfair, subjective, Dec. 3
I am concerned about the Ontario government's plan to build a huge private spa at Ontario Place. It is estimated every taxpayer in the province will pay at least $65 so a foreign-owned private spa can have an underwater parking lot.
The deal with Doug Ford's government also includes a 99-year lease to Therme, an Austrian company, with all the details kept secret. The plan includes removing hundreds of trees, an environmental assessment being deemed unnecessary for this project.
I am very concerned the government is wasting our taxes on something that will only be enjoyed by very few. Public money would be better spent on health care, affordable housing, roads and infrastructure for the benefit of the entire province.
Please contact your MPP to oppose this wasteful plan.
Cathy F., Toronto
What's going on in youth hockey? It's sadHometown minor hockey is at risk in Ontario. Is freedom to choose where you play worth it? Jan. 27
There has been considerable commentary recently about the mentality held by junior hockey organizations. One can see where that begins; right at the lowest levels of organized hockey . What happened to kids enjoying themselves, playing the game at whatever skill level, for fun and friendship?
Now it seems some parents live in the hopes their child will be the next Connor McDavid if they could just play with the best teams. Living vicariously, it seems.
What parent in their right mind would actually move or pretend to move or get a divorce so that their wunderkind could play on a certain team? Sad.
Ann Sullivan, Peterborough, Ont.
Underfunding colleges is a betrayal to the studentsWhy doesn't Doug Ford care about funding colleges and universities? Because you don't care either, Jan. 24
Thanks to Martin Regg Cohn for his column on post-secondary education. There's more to say; namely, that underfunding colleges and expecting them to operate as market-driven entities betrays students and fails to serve the provincial economy.
Based on a 20-year experience as a college instructor, I saw large numbers of students pass through programs that are cheap to run and popular among students who are ill-informed about the job market. The most popular courses were flight attendant, fire protection and law enforcement. Graduates of these courses were often unable to find decent jobs.
Qualifications for the Toronto Police Service make no mention of a college diploma. Many law enforcement grads ended up as security guards. The flight attendant students had no idea about the working conditions and pay scale of their chosen career. Some of the coursework consisted of memorizing the codes of airports around the world and learning the chief tourist attractions of select destinations. Airlines run their own training so the college certificate is a ticket to nowhere.
The jobs for fire protection students were almost nil with the exception of the sprinkler system specialty. In contrast, the public dental hygiene program, which runs only at George Brown, fills up quickly and turns down students. This program leads to a career that is promising and well paid but, because the program is more expensive to run, needing special equipment and space, not all students who want to participate can. Underfunding triggers this strictly "bums in seats" approach.
Colleges are supposed to keep statistics on graduates, but the stats are based on grads finding jobs that are "related to" their course. Quite possibly, a security guard position would qualify as a success for a law enforcement grad. Mr. Cohn might do well to investigate college stats on grads of flight attendant and other courses.
Thanks again for your attention to college education.
Joyce Hall, Toronto
Is technology a barrier to living a happy, healthy life?So you want to live forever? Scientists reveal the secrets to a long, healthy life - it's simpler than you might expect, Jan. 24
In listing the factors that may contribute to a longer life, I was struck by the comparison of the two photos accompanying the article.
In the main photo you have a group of men sitting around a table, no phones, no electronic devices, just a bunch of elderly gentlemen socializing.
The other photo is of three children sitting on a couch, each oblivious of the other, focused on their individual electronic devices.
Different generations, different life styles.
But what will be the outcome for the children?
The new technologies are creating significant challenges for healthy social development that will affect the quality and perhaps the longevity of the children's lives. When the children are old, will they sit around and socialize or will they sit on a couch with their phones?
David Gladstone, Toronto
CAPTION: A homeless tent encampment is seen in Dufferin Grove Park.

Document 435 of 500

Ontario election kicks off with leaders highlighting competing visions

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-election-kicks-off-with-leaders/docview/3160911032/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+election+kicks+off+with+leaders+highlighting+competing+visions&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Trump tariffs, a dearth of doctors, affordability anxieties.
Ontario's snap winter campaign officially kicked off Wednesday with the major party leaders pushing competing narratives to define the Feb. 27 vote.
Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford, trying to win his third majority government, was in Windsor against the backdrop of the Ambassador Bridge, railing against U.S. President Donald Trump's threatened 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian goods.
"I'm asking the people for a strong, stable four-year mandate to do whatever it takes to protect Ontario," said Ford, who, despite already having a legislative majority, triggered a vote 15 months early that Elections Ontario says will cost $189 million.
New Democratic Leader Marit Stiles was in Toronto's Regent Park to emphasize how "unaffordable" life has become since the Tories took office in 2018.
"People are leaving our province, forced out of communities they grew up in. Young people are losing hope. Seniors are ending up homeless. It's tough out there." said Stiles, noting unemployment has risen on Ford's watch.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie was in Barrie with local candidate Dr. Rose Zacharias, the former president of the Ontario Medical Association, to put a spotlight on the fact that 2.5 million people in the province don't have a doctor.
"We're launching our tour here in Barrie because 55,000 people residing in this area don't have access to a family doctor," said Crombie, sporting a white cap with the slogan "Real Leaders Fix Healthcare" that countered Ford's blue anti-Trump "Canada Is Not For Sale" hat.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner, who was in Guelph, said "this election is a referendum on Doug Ford's poor track-record of the past seven years."
In Windsor, where Ford unveiled his campaign vehicle - an RV emblazoned with his photo and the slogan "Protect Ontario" that press gallery wags immediately christened "the Winter-bago" - the Tory leader said the Trump threat will loom even if the president doesn't impose 25 per cent tariffs on Saturday.
"Ontario won't start a fight with the U.S., but you better believe we'll be ready to win one," he said as an icy wind blew over the Detroit River from across the border and a smattering of protesters from a teachers' union rallied nearby.
Ford pledged to spend "tens of billions" in economic supports to fight tariffs if necessary, similar to the fight against COVID-19.
"You may not always agree with me, and that's OK, but my record is clear," he said, appealing to voters leaning toward his rivals.
"There's experience on one side with the PC Party and zero experience with the other three parties."
Asked about cost taxpayers an additional $612 million, Ford said he had no regrets.
"I believe the people should have a choice and the convenience if they want to grab a beer," added the PC leader, who does not drink alcohol.
"What I'm happy about is convenience stores are making a little more money, they're hiring more people," he told reporters in the New Democrat-held riding of Windsor West.
Ford's opponents say the additional expense proves the booze deal was botched so he's not up to the job of going head-to-head with Trump on trade issues.
Stiles, whose campaign slogan is "On Your Side," mocked the Tory leader's deal-making prowess.
"He wants us to hire him to be our negotiator with Trump. Doug Ford our negotiator? Is he kidding? Over and over, he makes bad deals for Ontarians," she said, pointing to the Ontario Place redevelopment when "Doug Ford got taken to the cleaners by a foreign corporation."
In a rally with NDP MPPs and candidates at the Daniels Spectrum community art centre in Regent Park, Stiles also attacked Crombie, accusing the Liberal leader of making a right turn away from predecessors like former premier Kathleen Wynne.
"Today's Liberal leader would be right at home as a cabinet minister in Doug Ford's government. Bonnie Crombie doesn't want to get rid of Doug Ford - she wants to be Doug Ford," she quipped in the NDP-held riding.
After governing for almost 15 years between 2003 and 2018, the Grits have been unable to win enough seats to obtain official party status in the past two elections.
"There will probably always be an Ontario Liberal party ... I think they're going to be in the repair shop for a few more years," said Stiles.
In Tory-held Barrie, Crombie countered that the New Democrats have failed to hold the Tories accountable as the Official Opposition.
"They've also had seven years to make a difference and make a change - they haven't gotten it done," she said, conceding she's "a different kind of Liberal" with a business background before she was a MP and the three-term mayor of Mississauga.
"I understand government and I know how to get things done."
But Crombie reiterated "this isn't a necessary election" and said Ford is scrambling to "outrun an RCMP investigation" in the Tories' $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal.
At Queen's Park, Ontario chief electoral officer Greg Essensa said it will cost Elections Ontario $189 million to conduct the first winter vote in the province since 1981.
Essensa, who needs to hire 55,000 workers to staff some 7,000 polling stations in all 124 provincial ridings, urged anyone looking for a temporary job during the writ period to apply at Elections Ontario's employment portal.
With weather a factor at this time of year, the chief electoral officer said "contingency" planning has begun to ensure Ontarians are able to exercise their franchise even if there are blizzards across the province.
Some 10.8 million people are eligible to vote, including the estimated 81,000 who are homeless. They can use the address of the shelter they stay at when they cast their ballots.
Parties are allowed to spend $10.8 million on their campaigns.
Schreiner, meanwhile, blasted "Ford's wasteful spending that only benefits wealthy land speculators and fossil fuel giants like Enbridge, all while ripping off everyday Ontarians who are struggling to make ends meet.
"People deserve better than Doug Ford and this is our chance to turn things around for the many communities that feel abandoned by this government," he said.
CAPTION: Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles, Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford, Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner and Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie.

Subject: Political campaigns; Elections; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Tariffs

Location: United States--US

People: Schreiner, Mike

Document 437 of 500

Nobody asked for provincial vote

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/nobody-asked-provincial-vote/docview/3160792824/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Nobody+asked+for+provincial+vote&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As much as it hurts me to say this, I appreciate the forcefulness of Premier Doug Ford attacking U.S. President Donald Trump about these tariffs. Sometimes a blustering bully requires someone who isn't scared to bully them back.
Ford is scrappy. He's not debonair and that's fine - that's good, because Trump is an agent of chaos who thrives on mudslinging.
In normal circumstances, I'd prefer negotiations versus threats of removing American liquor from the LCBO or flipping the power switch and plunging some states into darkness. But, in this particular situation, I support Ford and the Team Canada approach that "nothing is off the table."
I even found myself saying, "I'm down with Ford! He's strong in the face of this guttersnipe!" (Yes, I use words like guttersnipe in my daily life.)
That didn't last long. My goodwill evaporated when Ford announced a snap election for Feb. 27? With a year and a half left until the scheduled provincial election in June 2026, this is what we're doing?
Ontarians may support Ford's tough stance on Trump, but that doesn't mean we're ready to head to the polls. We're already gearing up for a federal election sometime this spring - why add provincial upheaval to the mix?
The reason is obvious: Ford is hoping that we'll forget the things he delivered that we never asked for - and the things he completely ignored that we really needed. The list is long, but here are some examples.
Take the Ontario Science Centre. Last June, the provincial government closed it abruptly due to structural concerns with the roof. While second engineering report said significant repairs were needed, they said the centre wasn't in such dire condition that it had to be closed permanently and without notice. What it really came down to was Ford's desire to move the science centre to Ontario Place. Save Ontario's Science Centre, a grassroots group protested but ultimately, a smaller science centre will be relocated to Ontario Place, with the opening date now pushed to 2029.
Or let's talk about the Greenbelt scandal. The Ford government opened more than 7,400 acres of protected land to housing development. Yes, there is a housing crisis, but building on environmentally sensitive land wasn't solving our housing problems. What was really happening is that developers, with ties to the government, bought parcels of land in the Greenbelt - they were ready for Ford to announce the land was open for development. That had me, and many Ontarians, side-eyeing Ford due to allegations of favouritism and corruption. Following public backlash, Ford apologized in September 2023 and restored the Greenbelt's protected status.
Let's not forget the move to a two-tier health care system. In 2023, our provincial government announced plans to expand private clinics to perform surgeries and diagnostic tests to reduce wait times. I won't lie, after being in a couple of emergency rooms last spring, I am not against shorter wait times. I don't believe that private clinics poaching the health care workers is going to make wait times for most people shorter.
For those in the province who can afford to skip the line and get taken care of at a private clinic, great. They will get the MRI or CT scan in minutes by paying top dollar. The rest of us? We will have to wait in ever longer lines.
But we all know, when money is the motivator, private, for-profit clinic will prioritize their profits over the well-being of patients. In no way is that a good thing - the tragedies at Ontario's for-profit long-term care homes during COVID taught us that.
Premier Ford, while we stand behind you against Trump and his threat of tariffs, we see this election call for what it is: you trying to ride this wave of support to another victory. It could happen - we are a forgetful bunch.
Maybe send us another $200 cheque to seal the deal.
Shellene Drakes-Tull is a Toronto-based storyteller, educator and the founder of Sweet Lime Communications. She is a freelance contributing columnist for the Star.
CAPTION: Ontarians may support Premier Doug Ford and his approach to Donald Trump's tariff threats, but that doesn't mean they're ready to go to the polls in less than a month, Shellene Drakes-Tull writes.Steve Russell Toronto Star
CREDIT: Shellene Drakes-Tull OPINION Shellene Drakes-Tull is a Toronto-based storyteller, educator and the founder of Sweet Lime Communications. She is a freelance contributing columnist for the Star.;

Document 438 of 500

Chow eyes 'constructive solution': Province hires engineering firm, while advocates call move 'a distraction'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/chow-eyes-constructive-solution/docview/3160792329/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Chow+eyes+%27constructive+solution%27%3A+Province+hires+engineering+firm%2C+while+advocates+call+move+%27a+distraction%27&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Just hours before he called the provincial election, Premier Doug Ford took another step toward removing the bike lanes from three Toronto streets - though the mayor's office said it still hoped for a "constructive solution."
On Tuesday morning, the Ford government announced it had "retained an engineering company" to begin design work to remove bike lanes on Bloor Street, Yonge Street and University Avenue. The provincial government has maintained that removing the bike lanes will help alleviate Toronto's gridlock.
In an emailed response to Tuesday's announcement, a spokesperson for Mayor Olivia Chow said the city "continues to engage with the provincial government to find a constructive solution."
"We do not anticipate any changes to infrastructure this winter," the statement added.
Michael Longfield - the executive director of Cycling Toronto - said the latest move by the Ford government was more "pre-election posturing," as the Ford government ignores "its own failures in delivering real solutions to keep people moving," referencing the still-incomplete Finch West and Eglinton Crosstown LRTs.
The province's first steps toward removing bike lanes shows just how "laborious and costly" the process will really be, Longfield said, despite the Ford government's arguments to the contrary.
There's still a pathway for the Ford government to reverse course and focus on more important issues, said Longfield.
"These bike lanes are taking up a lot of oxygen in the room," said Longfield, adding that Ford's fixation on bike lanes draws attention away from other, more pressing provincial issues. "This is a distraction."
Cycle Toronto filed a Charter challenge against the Ford government in December, arguing that the removal of bike lanes violates the Charter-protected rights to life and security of person. The hearing for the Charter challenge will take place in April, but Cycle Toronto has also requested an injunction to stop any work on removing the bike lanes. The hearing for the injunction is expected to take place in March.
The Ford government's Bill 212, the Reducing Gridlock, Saving You Time Act, received royal assent last month, granting it the power to remove existing bike lanes, as well as veto new ones.
While the province has agreed to foot the bill for the bike lane removals, Ford called the city's estimate that it would cost $48 million to remove them "hogwash" and claimed his government would "do it for a lot less and get traffic moving."
Ford and his transportation minister have yet to put forward a timeline or cost estimate on the bike lane removals.
Ford has repeatedly echoed a 2011 Statistics Canada figure to argue that "one per cent of population cannot take up 50 per cent of the roads." More recent statistics showed 3.1 per cent of Torontonians commuted to work by cycling.
CREDIT: Andy Takagi Toronto Star

Subject: Injunctions; Cost estimates

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters+to+the+editor%3A+Ford+pledges+tough+new+legislation+to+dismantle+homeless+encampments%2C+Dec.+5+Auditor+general+slams+Ontario+Place+redevelopment+process+as+unfair%2C+subjective%2C+Dec.+3+Hometown+minor+hockey+is+at+risk+in+Ontario.+Is+freedom+to+choose+where+you+play+worth+it%3F+Jan.+27+Why+doesn%27t+Doug+Ford+care+about+funding+colleges+and+universities%3F+Because+you+don%27t+care+either%2C+Jan.+24+So+you+want+to+live+forever%3F+Scientists+reveal+the+secrets+to+a+long%2C+healthy+life+-+it%27s+simpler+than+you+might+expect%2C+Jan.+24&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ford's approach to homelessness is cold, uncaring
Ford pledges tough new legislation to dismantle homeless encampments, Dec. 5
Just before the holidays, Premier Doug Ford proposed an inhumane new approach to homeless encampments that punches down on people already suffering. It will do nothing to help them while making the problem of encampments worse.
The so-called "Safer Municipalities Act" would give Ontario cities unnecessary new powers to clear homeless encampments with police force and arrest our neighbours experiencing homelessness.
No one believes a tent in a park is an appropriate place to live, but this is not the answer. It's ineffective, it's expensive and it's cruel. It's an unserious plan for a very serious and urgent problem our most vulnerable neighbours are facing.
I don't support Premier Ford trampling the basic human rights of our neighbours when there are effective evidence-based solutions we can use instead to house people quickly. And it's high time we got serious about addressing the root cause of this crisis, which is a lack of affordable housing, something the Ford government has done little to solve.
We need an urgent solution to encampments and that solution is homes, not handcuffs.
Lorraine Barnaby, Toronto
Ontario Place redevelopment must be stopped
Auditor general slams Ontario Place redevelopment process as unfair, subjective, Dec. 3
I am concerned about the Ontario government's plan to build a huge private spa at Ontario Place. It is estimated every taxpayer in the province will pay at least $65 so a foreign-owned private spa can have an underwater parking lot.
The deal with Doug Ford's government also includes a 99-year lease to Therme, an Austrian company, with all the details kept secret. The plan includes removing hundreds of trees, an environmental assessment being deemed unnecessary for this project.
I am very concerned the government is wasting our taxes on something that will only be enjoyed by very few. Public money would be better spent on health care, affordable housing, roads and infrastructure for the benefit of the entire province.
Please contact your MPP to oppose this wasteful plan.
Cathy F., Toronto
What's going
on in youth hockey is sad
Hometown minor hockey is at risk in Ontario. Is freedom to choose where you play worth it? Jan. 27
There has been considerable commentary recently about the mentality held by junior hockey organizations. One can see where that begins; right at the lowest levels of organized hockey. What happened to kids enjoying themselves, playing the game at whatever skill level, for fun and friendship?
Now it seems some parents live in the hopes their child will be the next Connor McDavid if they could just play with the best teams. Living vicariously, it seems.
What parent in their right mind would actually move or pretend to move or get a divorce so that their wunderkind could play on a certain team? Sad.
Ann Sullivan, Peterborough, Ont.
Underfunding
is a betrayal to college students
Why doesn't Doug Ford care about funding colleges and universities? Because you don't care either, Jan. 24
Thanks to Martin Regg Cohn for his column on post-secondary education. There's more to say; namely, that underfunding colleges and expecting them to operate as market-driven entities betrays students and fails to serve the provincial economy.
Based on a 20-year experience as a college instructor, I saw large numbers of students pass through programs that are cheap to run and popular among students who are ill-informed about the job market. The most popular courses were flight attendant, fire protection and law enforcement. Graduates of these courses were often unable to find decent jobs.
Qualifications for the Toronto Police Service make no mention of a college diploma. Many law enforcement grads ended up as security guards. The flight attendant students had no idea about the working conditions and pay scale of their chosen career. Some of the coursework consisted of memorizing the codes of airports around the world and learning the chief tourist attractions of select destinations. Airlines run their own training so the college certificate is a ticket to nowhere.
The jobs for fire protection students were almost nil with the exception of the sprinkler system specialty. In contrast, the public dental hygiene program, which runs only at George Brown, fills up quickly and turns down students. This program leads to a career that is promising and well paid but, because the program is more expensive to run, needing special equipment and space, not all students who want to participate can. Underfunding triggers this strictly "bums in seats" approach.
Colleges are supposed to keep statistics on graduates, but the stats are based on grads finding jobs that are "related to" their course. Quite possibly, a security guard position would qualify as a success for a law enforcement grad. Mr. Cohn might do well to investigate college stats on grads of flight attendant and other courses.
Thanks again for your attention to college education.
Joyce Hall, Toronto
Is tech a barrier to living a happy, healthy life?
So you want to live forever? Scientists reveal the secrets to a long, healthy life - it's simpler than you might expect, Jan. 24
In listing the factors that may contribute to a longer life, I was struck by the comparison of the two photos accompanying the article.
In the main photo you have a group of men sitting around a table, no phones, no electronic devices, just a bunch of elderly gentlemen socializing.
The other photo is of three children sitting on a couch, each oblivious of the other, focused on their individual electronic devices.
Different generations, different life styles.
But what will be the outcome for the children?
The new technologies are creating significant challenges for healthy social development that will affect the quality and perhaps the longevity of the children's lives. When the children are old, will they sit around and socialize or will they sit on a couch with their phones?
David Gladstone, Toronto

Document 440 of 500

Response to foreign meddling 'far from perfect': Inquiry finds no evidence of 'traitors' but final report flags risks to democracy

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/response-foreign-meddling-far-perfect/docview/3160792279/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Response+to+foreign+meddling+%27far+from+perfect%27%3A+Inquiry+finds+no+evidence+of+%27traitors%27+but+final+report+flags+risks+to+democracy&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There are no "traitors" in Parliament.
Ottawa's handling of interference threats has been "far from perfect."
And disinformation whipped up by foreign and domestic agents "poses the single biggest risk" to Canada's democracy.
Those were among the starkest conclusions drawn by Justice Marie-Josée Hogue in her final report, released Tuesday, as the Foreign Interference Commission capped off more than a year of work publicly probing foreign meddling in Canadian affairs.
"On the one hand, trust in Canada's democratic institutions has been shaken, and it is imperative to restore it," Hogue wrote in the introduction to her seven-part report.
"On the other hand, while the government has the primary responsibility for ensuring national security and protecting our democratic institutions, what I have read and heard convinces me that society as a whole must help defend these institutions."
As she concluded in her first report last spring, Hogue said the results of the 2019 and 2021 elections are not in doubt, though she reiterated that in some ridings, foreign interference may have influenced the outcome.
She also found major concerns with how intelligence is shared in the government and across security agencies.
In her report, Hogue specifically took aim at an explosive June report from the National Security and Intelligence Committee of Parliamentarians (NSICOP), a group of MPs and senators with top security clearance who are permanently bound to secrecy to receive highly classified information. The NSICOP report found that unnamed parliamentarians collaborated with foreign governments, and noted the committee had seen "troubling intelligence that some parliamentarians were, in the words of the intelligence services, 'semi-witting or witting' participants in the efforts of foreign states."
Hogue said she had not seen "any evidence of 'traitors' in Parliament plotting with foreign states to act against Canada" and said references to "witting" parliamentarians "were more definitive than the underlying intelligence could support." She reconfirmed no parliamentarians were named in the classified version of the report and said she had reviewed all of NSICOP's findings as well as the related intelligence.
The report cast ripples of suspicion across Parliament Hill, while NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh and Green Leader Elizabeth May, who both received security clearances to review the unredacted committee report, came away with vastly different conclusions. Singh said he believed some parliamentarians were "indeed traitors" to the country, something May strongly disputed. Nathalie Drouin, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's national security and intelligence adviser, testified before the commission that she was "very uncomfortable" with NSICOP's conclusions.
Singh said Tuesday that he was relying on the information he had at the time, which was less extensive than the records Hogue had access to, while May told reporters she was pleased Hogue arrived at the same conclusion she did.
Hogue also found significant errors in part of the NSICOP report that stated the Canadian Security Intelligence Service (CSIS) identified an MP as having been "compromised" by a foreign state. In fact, CSIS had not used that language but instead said it came from a source's description of the MP.
She found another reference where NSICOP said an MP had contact with an undeclared intelligence officer, or a spy, when in fact they were a legitimate foreign official. The NSICOP report identified a former MP as having attempted to arrange a meeting in a foreign country with intelligence officials, but Hogue said there was little backing that up.
"The NSICOP report's conclusion was likely based on the same limited information. This is an important consideration, given the seriousness of the allegation," Hogue wrote.
Hogue said while NSICOP had raised "unwarranted" alarm, there are nevertheless real concerns.
"To be clear, this does not mean that the conduct reported is not concerning," Hogue wrote. "There are legitimate concerns about parliamentarians potentially having problematic relationships with foreign officials, exercising poor judgment, behaving naively and perhaps displaying questionable ethics."
At the time, NSICOP was chaired by Liberal MP David McGuinty, who was appointed Canada's public safety minister in December.
In an interview with the Star, McGuinty said he sees many similarities regarding the larger problem of foreign interference between Hogue's report and the NSICOP findings.
He said he had yet to review the full report, but chalked up any differences in both reports to the additional resources Hogue had.
"This is a reflection of the thoroughness with which Madame Justice Hogue was able to pursue her work. She had over, I think, a dozen counsels. Her mandate was different. She had an inquiries act. She had deeper and longer resourcing," McGuinty said.
In total, Hogue made 51 recommendations to the government.
She called for CSIS to write clearer intelligence reports to ministers and to exercise caution about how often they send reports. She also recommended adding an external member to the government's elections task force who could communicate with the public about threats and misinformation. She further recommended that the government develop a "duty to warn" policy for when intelligence agencies believe they see a risk to a Canadian from a foreign entity. She said there should be a whole of government response to foreign interference and multiple changes to election laws.
During his final appearance before Hogue last fall, Trudeau said he had the "names of a number of parliamentarians, former parliamentarians and or candidates in the Conservative Party of Canada who are engaged, or at high risk of, or for whom there's clear intelligence, around foreign interference." He blasted Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre for his refusal to obtain the necessary security clearance that would allow that information to be shared.
Hogue recommended that leaders of all parties in the House of Commons receive a clearance, but Poilievre's spokesperson Sebastian Skamski said his position was unchanged.
"Our position on security briefings is long-standing and well known. Unlike others who are willing to limit their ability to hold the government accountable on important issues of national security, Mr. Poilievre will not be gagged and unable to speak or act on information he may receive," he said in an email.
On the topic of the government's response to the interference issue overall, Hogue concluded that Ottawa, at times, took "too long to act," that the flow of information was not always as "effective" as it should have been, and that the government has "proven to be a poor communicator and insufficiently transparent" about foreign meddling.
She noted that in some cases, information that should have reached the desks of certain cabinet ministers - and the prime minister - did not.
Hogue highlighted one case where CSIS applied for a warrant that seemed to languish in then public safety minister Bill Blair's office for weeks. Hogue does not directly identify who the warrant targeted, but Markham, Ont. deputy mayor and former Ontario Liberal cabinet minister Michael Chan has previously said he was the subject of a "wiretap" by CSIS that required a government-approved warrant.
Hogue found Blair approved the warrant on the day it was presented to him, but his chief of staff Zita Astravas had the warrant for weeks before that. Hogue said she cannot find why CSIS, public safety and Astravas allowed it to languish.
"It seems to me that everyone involved dropped the ball. When a minister of public safety does not know he has to review a warrant application he cannot exercise his statutory duty," Hogue said.
Hogue also concluded that in her view, "it is no exaggeration to say that at this juncture, information manipulation (whether foreign or not) poses the single biggest risk to our democracy," and that it presents an "existential threat."
She noted, in particular, the disinformation campaign that targeted the Conservative party in 2021, under then-leader Erin O'Toole, though she was unable to establish a "definitive link" to China in that case.
Over the course of the commission's 18 months of work, Hogue heard from more than 100 witnesses, 64 of whom appeared more than once.
Both stages of the exercise featured testimony from members of Canada's diaspora communities, federal party officials, MPs, top security and intelligence officials, cabinet ministers, members of the Prime Minister's Office and Privy Council Office and Trudeau.
The commission has tasked Ottawa with updating Parliament in one year about the progress it has made in implementing the recommendations, thought it is not known what a snap election call and a possible change in government would mean for that request.
CREDIT: Raisa Patel and Ryan Tumilty Toronto Star

Document 441 of 500

Are Ford's opponents prepared for a campaign?: With the legislature now dissolved, the PCs are the only party with a full slate of candidates

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/are-fords-opponents-prepared-campaign/docview/3160792264/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Are+Ford%27s+opponents+prepared+for+a+campaign%3F%3A+With+the+legislature+now+dissolved%2C+the+PCs+are+the+only+party+with+a+full+slate+of+candidates&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: They are gearing up to campaign - but how ready are the major political parties for a provincial election?
Leaders will launch their campaigns this week now that Doug Ford has dissolved the legislature, but only the Progressive Conservatives already have a full slate of 124 candidates for the Feb. 27 vote.
Ford will be in Windsor on Wednesday, NDP Leader Marit Stiles in Toronto, and Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie will be in Barrie but formally kick off her campaign on Thursday in Mississauga, where she served as mayor for almost a decade.
It's the first provincial campaign for both Stiles and Crombie as party leaders, and Ford's third.
The Liberals are close to a full slate of nominated candidates - at more than 100 either confirmed or about to be - and, after months of speculation, Crombie announced Tuesday she'll seek a seat in Mississauga East-Cooksville.
The Greens have 74 candidates and the Official Opposition New Democrats about 60, although the NDP predicts it will have 80 by the end of the week.
"One of the major factors influencing Ford's decision to call an early election is the relative unpreparedness and lack of resources of the other parties, among other things," said Dax D'Orazio, a political scientist at Queen's University.
He said the PCs have also "done an excellent job in fundraising over the past couple years, used government advertising to their benefit on several occasions, announced new measures and policies that might help connect with the base on the campaign trail - supervised consumption sites, alcohol distribution, and bike lanes - and showered the entire province with ($200) cheques" that have recently been mailed out.
"All of this will soften the ground when candidates are knocking on doors and mobilizing for the election," D'Orazio said.
For Western University political science professor Laura Stephenson, the fact that dozens of candidates have yet to be named isn't a huge concern as "things can come together quite quickly once there's an election call."
While Ford has been hinting at an election for months, "the actual timeline came about last week ... (now) you're going to see things moving pretty rapidly," and even last-minute candidates will have party supporters to help them, Stephenson said. The opposition parties have accused Ford of calling an unnecessary election, with Stiles saying Tuesday that "in the coming weeks, we have the power to choose a better future for our province. We have the power to fight back against Donald Trump and turn the page on Doug Ford. Together, we can win an NDP government that's on your side."
The Liberals already have a slogan - a "More For You" message unveiled last fall at their policy convention - and the PCs say they will "Protect Ontario," referring to the threat of tariffs from the U.S., which has been Ford's rationale for calling the election.
The NDP says it will soon unveil its campaign slogan.
The PCs, Liberals and NDP will have campaign vehicles touring the province for the next four weeks - with the NDP managing to snag the same well-liked and experienced bus driver they've had for the past two campaigns.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner, who will be travelling in a Chevy Equinox EV, was one of two of his party's MPPs in the legislature, along with Aislinn Clancy.
He'll launch his campaign in Clancy's Kitchener Centre riding on Wednesday night, following a rally earlier in the day in his riding of Guelph.
"To date we've had our best January ever for fundraising, on the heels of a record-breaking (fourth quarter) in 2024," the Greens said in a statement about the $2.2 million they've received from 8,500 donors.
The NDP raised $5.34 million last year. The Liberals ended 2024 with a fundraising high of more than $5.4 million from 11,227 donors.
Crombie is running in Mississauga East-Cooksville, which was held by Independent MPP Kaleed Rasheed, a former Tory who was forced from the PC caucus over a Greenbelt-related controversy. He is not seeking re-election.
The Liberal leader is running against PC candidate Silvia Gualtieri, who is the mother-in-law of Brampton Mayor Patrick Brown, a former PC leader. Brown and Crombie have clashed in the past over issues in Peel Region.
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Singh may back Liberal tariff package: But NDP leader planning to topple federal government as soon as possible

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/singh-may-back-liberal-tariff-package/docview/3160792107/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Singh+may+back+Liberal+tariff+package%3A+But+NDP+leader+planning+to+topple+federal+government+as+soon+as+possible&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh is not ruling out helping the Liberals pass a potential relief package for workers if Canada enters a trade war with the United States.
But the New Democrat leader stressed there have been no discussions about such a package, and that his party still intends to try and topple the Liberals' minority government and trigger an election as soon as possible.
"I've maintained my position that I'll be voting against the government at the earliest opportunity," Singh said Tuesday.
"If the Liberals are serious though, about something, a plan that should be put in place to support workers, call the opposition leaders together. If they're serious about it, discuss that plan with us the way we did during the (COVID-19) pandemic. I've not seen any of that initiative."
A spokesperson from the Prime Minister's Office did not respond Tuesday when asked if the government planned to discuss any relief proposals with opposition parties.
Having shut down Parliament when he announced his intention to resign once a new Liberal leader is chosen, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and his minority government are grappling with U.S. President Donald Trump's threat to impose tariffs on all Canadian goods. On Tuesday, a spokesperson from the White House reiterated that Trump intends to implement those 25 per cent tariffs on Saturday.
In the meantime, the federal government has been preparing a potential response - including a possible relief package for workers and businesses affected by a trade war with the country's vital trading partner.
Finance Minister Dominic LeBlanc said last week that it's best not to compare any possible relief package to the massive government aid that flowed to individuals and businesses during the pandemic. But he did say the government would support workers, businesses, economic sectors and specific regions of the country in the event of a "sustained" response to American tariffs.
LeBlanc added that he has had reassuring conversations about potential supports with provincial governments, including in Ontario, and that Ottawa is planning to use revenues collected from counter-tariffs on American imports to support sectors and workers impacted by a trade war.
"If we get into that scenario, people should be very reassured that in the case of Ontario, both (the federal and provincial) governments will very quickly work together to put in place the supports necessary," LeBlanc told the Star.
Parliament is suspended until March 27, with the governing Liberals set to elect Trudeau's replacement as prime minister on March 9. But a senior government official told the Star on Tuesday that some supports for trade war relief would not require legislation to move forward, such as changes to more quickly provide employment insurance to workers who might lose their jobs.
Speaking Tuesday from his home riding in Burnaby, B.C., Singh called on the Liberals to speak with opposition leaders about bringing forward a plan to support workers. He said there has been no outreach so far, and that any relief plan should target affected sectors and be tied to employment levels and keep wages and jobs in place.
"There needs to be strict rules in place that if any business is going to get support, that it has to be tied to workers," Singh said.
On Parliament Hill, Bloc Québécois Leader Yves-François Blanchet dismissed talk of a relief package as a gimmick to extend the life of the Liberal government. He said the Liberals should call an election sooner - perhaps after a new Liberal leader is chosen on March 9 - if they want to help people impacted by any tariff spat with the U.S.
"I believe that the intention would be to create a context in which the government would get one week - oh, two weeks - oh, three weeks - and then they remain there longer than supposed," Blanchet said. "I'm not getting into any tricks."
The Conservatives, under Pierre Poilievre, have been demanding an election for months, and have tabled several failed confidence motions to try and defeat the Liberal government. Poilievre has also called on the Liberal government to reopen Parliament so the legislature can pass new border measures, discuss tariff retaliation, and prepare a plan to boost the economy.
"Had the Liberals not put their party ahead of their country, we could be debating and considering options to deal with this critical issue right now. Instead, they suspended Parliament to save their political skins, putting the livelihoods of Canadians at risk in face of this unjustified threat," said Conservative Deputy Leader Melissa Lantsman, in an emailed statement to the Star.
With files from Tonda MacCharles
CREDIT: Mark Ramzy and Alex Ballingall Toronto Star With files from Tonda MacCharles
