Document 443 of 500

Ford gets parties started: Premier triggers Feb. 27 vote and Ontario's first winter campaign since 1981

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-gets-parties-started/docview/3160790607/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+gets+parties+started%3A+Premier+triggers+Feb.+27+vote+and+Ontario%27s+first+winter+campaign+since+1981&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: And they're off.
Doug Ford plunged Ontario into a Feb. 27 election on Tuesday, more than a year early in a bid for a third majority government.
Ford met with Lt.-Gov. Edith Dumont to request the dissolution of the legislature, setting the stage for a 29-day campaign officially beginning Wednesday. It will be the first winter provincial vote since 1981.
Ford is rushing Ontarians to the polls amid political instability in Ottawa after Prime Minister Justin Trudeau announced that he would resign and economic uncertainty over U.S. President Donald Trump's threat of 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian goods.
"This is going to be a battle for the next four years, and I want to make sure I have a strong mandate to outlast President Trump," Ford told reporters at Queen's Park before his ceremonial visit to Dumont.
The Progressive Conservative leader will formally launch his campaign in Windsor on Wednesday against the backdrop of the Detroit skyline.
That's to underscore that his rationale for the snap vote is to get a renewed mandate to deal with hefty levies on Canadian exports that Trump is threatening to begin this Saturday.
But Ford, who is well ahead of his rivals in public-opinion surveys, has been scrambling for a reason to go early since last May.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and Green Leader Mike Schreiner argue he's opportunistically trying to get in front of potential fallout from the RCMP's ongoing criminal investigation into the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal.
As first disclosed by the Star, the Mounties have interviewed past and current Ford staffers as part of their probe, and sources say the detectives' work is continuing during the writ period.
The opposition leaders would prefer Ford focus on the trade war with the U.S. and wait for the scheduled June 2026 vote instead of triggering an election that will cost between $150 million and $175 million.
"While the people of Ontario are anxious about the grave threat of tariffs, Doug Ford is pursuing his own political gain," said Stiles.
"People need a premier who will fight like hell for every single job that's at risk, not run to the polls over a year early. Doug Ford thinks that he can call an early election to escape his record of failure on housing, health care, education and affordability. We won't let him," she said.
Crombie accused Ford of "wasting $175 million on an early election instead of using it to fix our broken health-care system and make your life more affordable.
"Doug Ford's Ontario has failed you. I'll deliver more for you by guaranteeing you'll have a family doctor, cutting taxes on your paycheque and home heating and building homes you can afford," she said.
Schreiner pointed to Ford's failure to deliver on housing.
"He's had seven years with a majority government to do something about it and the problem has only gotten worse," said the Green leader.
"It's time to kick out this NIMBY government and elect a government that will urgently build the homes we need to address the housing crisis."
Ford's rivals also contend his efforts should also be spent tackling Ontario's doctor shortage instead of electioneering.
But the Tory leader insisted he needs a new mandate because any Trump tariffs could necessitate tens of billions of dollars in stimulus spending to keep the economy afloat and people employed. Stiles, Crombie and Schreiner say they would back any such emergency expenditures.
As chair of the Council of the Federation, Ford has aligned himself with Trudeau, forcefully making the case that Canada must retaliate with counter-tariffs against the U.S.
His campaign maintains the election timing has to do with that and not the Greenbelt scandal stemming from him granting permission for developers to build on 7,400 acres of environmentally sensitive land. He reversed that decision after damning reports in August 2023 from the auditor general and the integrity commissioner revealed wealthy landowners with ties to the Tories had been "favoured."
Despite his September 2023 U-turn to thwart the development plans, the Mounties swept in the following month and about a dozen of his current and former aides have been interviewed.
Ford, maintaining his government has "nothing to hide" on the Greenbelt, set the table for a snap campaign with voter-friendly $200 "rebate" cheques that are now being mailed out to 15 million Ontario adults and children.
That $3-billion payout is designed to offset Ontarians' affordability concerns at a time of rising prices and unemployment rates creeping up.
He also spent an additional $612 million to expedite the liberalization of booze sales so beer, wine and premixed cocktails could be sold in all supermarkets, corner stores and big box outlets in time for the snap election.
In keeping with his populist bent and a penchant for intervening on matters traditionally within the purview of municipalities, Ford announced portions of existing bike lanes on Bloor Street West in Etobicoke and University Avenue and Yonge Street in downtown Toronto would be removed to help alleviate the city's chronic gridlock.
Mindful that the $6.2-million Transportation Tomorrow Survey found 3.1 per cent of Toronto residents commute to work by bicycle each morning - and 3.6 per cent use their bikes "all day" in a city of three million people where 164,806 daily cycling trips are made - the Tories believe curbing some bike lanes could be popular with voters who drive.
Similarly, Ford is closing most supervised drug injection sites by forcing them to locate at least 200 metres away from schools and daycares while boosting provincial supports for addiction treatment as of March 31.
While that change was criticized by some health advocates, who predict more overdoses, it was hailed by some Toronto residents who are fed up with drug dealers.
Among the sites closing is one in Leslieville's South Riverdale Community Health Centre, near where mother-of-two Karolina Huebner-Makurat was killed by a stray bullet in July 2023, a brazen daylight crime that shocked the city.
As the legislature rose Dec. 12 for the winter break, Ford announced new measures to address the homeless tent encampments that have sprung up in parks across Toronto and throughout the province, promising an additional $75.5 million for shelters and affordable housing - and toughening up trespassing laws.
Since he took office almost seven years ago, the housing shortage has increased in fast-growing Ontario and there are now an estimated 81,000 homeless people in the province. After his Tories won power in 2018, ending an almost 15-year Liberal dynasty under premiers Kathleen Wynne and Dalton McGuinty, Ford was re-elected in 2022 following a global COVID-19 pandemic in which Ontario fared better than any neighbouring province or American state during the health emergency.
Despite his current standing in the polls, not all PC operatives are happy about a snap election.
They are concerned about history repeating itself, recalling that in 1990, popular Liberal premier David Peterson held an election a year early and was punished by voters, losing to Bob Rae's New Democrats in one of the largest upsets in Canadian political history. Peterson is now chair of Torstar, the Star's parent company.
Along with that precedent, some Ford loyalists worry about the "Underhill balance theory," which posits that when Ontarians elect Liberals federally, they tend to choose Tories provincially. Conversely, when they elect federal Tory governments, the Liberals - and, once, in Rae's case, the NDP - usually win provincially at Queen's Park.
With polls suggesting federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre will triumph in a national election expected later this year, the "balance" phenomenon was another consideration in going to provincial voters early.
At dissolution, there were 79 Tories, including Speaker Ted Arnott, who is not seeking re-election, 28 New Democrats, nine Liberals, six Independents and two Greens in the 124-member house.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Document 444 of 500

Waterfront plans get $975M boost

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/waterfront-plans-get-975m-boost/docview/3160790565/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Waterfront+plans+get+%24975M+boost&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The three levels of government will invest nearly a billion dollars in the transformation of Toronto's eastern waterfront, officials said Tuesday - funds that will be used to lay the groundwork needed to develop an estimated 14,000 new homes in the area.
The $975 million in new money - $325 million each from city hall, the province and the federal government, plus an extra $200 million from Ottawa to match previous city and provincial dollars - will go toward efforts like the Broadview flood protection project, as well as critical infrastructure for the new housing at Ookwemin Minising and Quayside, which is expected to break ground next year.
Ookwemin Minising, formerly referred to as Villiers Island, is the new 39.6-hectare neighbourhood carved out of former industrial lands southeast of downtown. Quayside was once the site of an ambitious proposal from Sidewalk Labs to create a new district with self-driving cars and data-gathering sensors, but that plan was abandoned in 2020, after which city hall handed the reins to a team of developers including Dream and Great Gulf.
"We're ready to deliver on that housing," said George Zegarac, CEO of Waterfront Toronto, the tripartite agency overseeing the waterfront's development. Zegarac added that building infrastructure like sewers is the next step in the redevelopment of Toronto's waterfront now that the rehabilitation of the mouth of the Don River is complete, flood protecting about 600 acres of land.
"Today is historic ... you can have all the land ready, you can have all the developers," said Mayor Olivia Chow at Tuesday's announcement, alongside Ontario Infrastructure Minister Kinga Surma and federal Housing and Infrastructure Minister Nate Erskine-Smith. "But if you don't have the infrastructure - the roads, the pipes and sewage - how are you going to build the homes?"
The announcement came the same day Ontario Premier Doug Ford requested the dissolution of the legislature, triggering a 29-day election. The federal government is also facing a potential election in the months ahead.
Kevin Greene, chief operating officer for Waterfront Toronto, said in an interview that once the needed infrastructure is built on the waterfront lands, the present development plan includes eight new buildings at Quayside, ranging from 12 to 14 storeys on Queens Quay and 55 to 70 storeys closer to Lake Shore Boulevard.
The nearby island of Ookwemin Minising, created during the rerouting of the Don River, is expected to be home to at least 15 towers and about 9,000 homes, which translates to about 15,000 residents.
In each location, some units are expected to be set aside at moderately affordable prices, based on the city's income-based definition.
Former city councillor turned Waterfront Toronto chief of staff Joe Cressy said they "fully anticipate" the density in Ookwemin Minising will increase. "This is the starting point, not the end point," he said. "Now that there's funding to enable the infrastructure ... we have the opportunity to build out with more density."
Council voted in December to look into increasing housing density on the island. This was previously a hot-button issue for advocates and planners outside city hall who wanted more ambitious housing targets. City staff's draft in 2017 for the island had planned about 4,000 units, but an updated plan last year more than doubled that number.
The new money announced Tuesday did not include funding for the Waterfront East Light Rail Transit line, which city hall has framed as fundamental to serve the thousands of new residents expected in the area and isn't finished being designed yet.
When asked about funding for that project, Erskine-Smith pointed to the Canada Public Transit Fund, which allocates $3 billion a year for transit investments across the country starting in 2026, and said there will be money set aside to fund the LRT.
"It's just a question of what that looks like, what the project costs are, and making sure we get into the details and get a deal done," he said.
At Chow's executive committee at city hall on Tuesday, councillors voted to advance design work for the Waterfront East LRT project, as well as directing city staff, the TTC and Waterfront Toronto to report back to council in the beginning of 2026 with updates on cost estimates and negotiations with higher levels of government.
"There's no time to waste," said Chow at the waterfront press conference. "If we're going to start the construction of all the infrastructure now, building the housing in 2026, people moving in (by) 2030, we need some way to have people getting in and out of the neighbourhood."
CAPTION: The announcement of $975 million in funding for Toronto's waterfront was made by all three levels of government on Tuesday morning.Andrew WallaceToronto Star
CREDIT: Mahdis Habibinia Toronto Star Victoria Gibson Toronto Star

Document 445 of 500

Ford's early election call nothing like Peterson's in 1990

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-early-election-call-nothing-like-petersons/docview/3160790464/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+early+election+call+nothing+like+Peterson%27s+in+1990&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There's a simple reason why, in the matter of early election calls in Ontario, 2025 is not like 1990. That would be the Meech Lake accord.
As will be recalled, then-premier David Peterson pulled the plug early 35 years ago, a strategy that resulted in the vaporization of his Liberal government and election of Bob Rae's New Democrats.
Some experienced voices have recently warned that current Premier Doug Ford faces the same peril with the Ontario election call Tuesday. But the circumstances are considerably different.
In his day, Peterson had immersed himself in a high-stakes game of constitutional dice-rolling arranged by then-prime minister Brian Mulroney. Peterson was aligned with the prime minister and then-Quebec premier Robert Bourassa as leaders of a bid to right historical wrongs and gather Quebec into the constitutional fold.
From its signing in 1987 to its failed ratification in 1990, Meech Lake had dominated the political classes and media.
Those involved obsessed about constitutional minutiae that was hardly the stuff of dinner-table conversation beyond that rarefied bubble. And the central players conveyed an overblown opinion of themselves as saviours of the nation.
The exercise reeked of arrogant, blue-suited elitism, a closed-door, top-down process waged for the greater glory of the white, male first-ministers involved. And the pushback, even within Ontario Liberal ranks, was considerable.
After Ontarians had punished Peterson at the polls in 1990 - as happened to most of the Meech Lake principals as soon as they faced their publics - the former premier ruefully acknowledged the comeuppance.
"Meech was terribly hard on our party," Peterson was quoted as saying in the book "Not Without Cause," by Dan Rath and Georgette Gagnon.
"There is no question: there is an issue that I, in a sense, imposed my will. The leader has the prerogatives, right or wrong, and this is an area where I exercised my prerogatives and fought like hell for it. But there's no question, not everybody felt as strongly as I did about this issue."
Not nearly. And Peterson's government was vigorously consigned to history's dustbin.
True, Doug Ford does allow himself the occasional flight of fancy into what seem unwise policy pursuits. But by and large he has kept his message fixed on what the general public cares about.
He has not created an emotional backlash bent on his ouster for forgetting his priorities. Instead, he has seized on an opportunity easy to understand - a battle of baseball caps.
If the guy south of the border has made a fetish out of red Make America Great Again caps, Ford recently entered the haberdashery dust-up with his own blue cap declaring that Canada Is Not for Sale.
It doesn't take a Ph.D in constitutional affairs to get that. It's easily understandable and inherently emotive. Just like the premier.
There's nothing intellectual about Doug Ford. There's nothing particularly ideological about him either. He's a populist. About the last place he would want to be is locked up for days on end parsing constitutional proposals. To him, and most who support him, that would be a new ring of hell.
Ford could hardly have scripted a better circumstance than casting himself as the hero in a fight against manifest destiny. So long as that emotionally galvanizing item is the ballot concern in this election, Ford can hardly lose.
Just as with Meech Lake as the mover of votes in 1990, David Peterson was destined to lose.
True, Ford has a track record full of targets. But, if he succeeds in casting himself as Captain Canada, they will hardly matter.
All who imagine Ford is vulnerable to an exhaustive itemization of his policy failings and reversals ignore what Drew Westen, a clinical psychologist and political adviser, made clear in his 2007 book "The Political Brain: The Role of Emotion in Deciding the Fate of the Nation."
"The political brain is an emotional brain. It is not a dispassionate calculating machine, objectively searching for the right facts, figures and policies to make a reasoned decision.
"In politics, when reason and emotion collide, emotion invariably wins."
Early election or not, Doug Ford gets that from the tips of his toes to the top of his nifty blue ball cap.
Jim Coyle is a former Toronto Star reporter and feature writer. In his 40-year career, he also wrote for The Canadian Press and the Ottawa Citizen.

Document 446 of 500

Doug Ford triggers snap Ontario election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-triggers-snap-ontario-election/docview/3161369701/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+triggers+snap+Ontario+election&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: And they're off.
Doug Ford plunged Ontario into a Feb. 27 election on Tuesday, more than a year early in a bid for a third majority government.
Ford met with Lt.-Gov. Edith Dumont to request the dissolution of the legislature, setting the stage for a 29-day campaign officially beginning Wednesday. It will be the first winter provincial vote since 1981.
Ford is rushing Ontarians to the polls amid political instability in Ottawa after Prime Minister Justin Trudeau announced that he would resign and economic uncertainty over U.S. President Donald Trump's threat of 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian goods.
"This is going to be a battle for the next four years, and I want to make sure that I have an make sure I have a strong mandate to outlast President Trump," Ford told reporters at Queen's Park before his ceremonial visit to Dumont.
The Progressive Conservative leader will formally launch his campaign in Windsor on Wednesday against the backdrop of the Detroit skyline.
That's to underscore that his rationale for the snap vote is to get a renewed mandate to deal with hefty levies on Canadian exports that Trump is threatening to begin this Saturday.
But Ford, who is well ahead of his rivals in public-opinion surveys, has been scrambling for a reason to go early since last May.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and Green Leader Mike Schreiner argue he's opportunistically trying to get in front of potential fallout from the RCMP's ongoing criminal investigation into the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal.
As first disclosed by the Star, the Mounties have interviewed past and current Ford staffers as part of their probe, and sources say the detectives' work is continuing during the writ period.
The opposition leaders would prefer Ford focus on the trade war with the U.S. and wait for the scheduled June 2026 vote instead of triggering an "unnecessary election" that will cost between $150 million and $175 million.
"While the people of Ontario are anxious about the grave threat of tariffs, Doug Ford is pursuing his own political gain," said Stiles.
"People need a premier who will fight like hell for every single job that's at risk, not run to the polls over a year early. Doug Ford thinks that he can call an early election to escape his record of failure on housing, health care, education and affordability. We won't let him," she said.
Crombie accused Ford of "wasting $175 million on an early election instead of using it to fix our broken health-care system and make your life more affordable.
"Doug Ford's Ontario has failed you. I'll deliver more for you by guaranteeing you'll have a family doctor, cutting taxes on your paycheque and home heating and building homes you can afford," she said.
Schreiner pointed to Ford's failure to deliver on housing.
"He's had seven years with a majority government to do something about it and the problem has only gotten worse," said the Green leader.
"It's time to kick out this NIMBY government and elect a government that will urgently build the homes we need to address the housing crisis."
Ford's rivals also contend his efforts should also be spent tackling Ontario's doctor shortage instead of electioneering.
But the Tory leader insisted he needs a new mandate because any Trump tariffs could necessitate tens of billions of dollars in stimulus spending to keep the economy afloat and people employed. Stiles, Crombie and Schreiner say they would back any such emergency expenditures.
As chair of the Council of the Federation, Ford has aligned himself with Trudeau, forcefully making the case that Canada must retaliate with counter-tariffs against the U.S.
His campaign maintains the election timing has to do with that and not the Greenbelt scandal stemming from him granting permission for developers to build on 7,400 acres of environmentally sensitive land. He reversed that decision after damning reports in August 2023 from the auditor general and the integrity commissioner revealed wealthy landowners with ties to the Tories had been "favoured."
Despite his September 2023 U-turn to thwart the development plans, the Mounties swept in the following month and about a dozen of his current and former aides have been interviewed.
Ford, maintaining his government has "nothing to hide" on the Greenbelt, set the table for a snap campaign with voter-friendly $200 "rebate" cheques that are now being mailed out to 15 million Ontario adults and children.
That $3-billion payout is designed to offset Ontarians' affordability concerns at a time of rising prices and unemployment rates creeping up.
He also spent an additional $612 million to expedite the liberalization of booze sales so beer, wine and premixed cocktails could be sold in all supermarkets, corner stores and big box outlets in time for the snap election.
In keeping with his populist bent and a penchant for intervening on matters traditionally the purview of municipalities, Ford announced portions of existing bike lanes on Bloor Street West in Etobicoke and University Avenue and Yonge Street in downtown Toronto would be removed to help alleviate the city's chronic gridlock.
Mindful that the $6.2-million Transportation Tomorrow Survey found 3.1 per cent of Toronto residents commute to work by bicycle each morning - and 3.6 per cent use their bikes "all day" in a city of three million people where 164,806 daily cycling trips are made - the Tories believe curbing some bike lanes could be popular with voters who drive.
Similarly, Ford is closing most supervised drug injection sites by forcing them to locate at least 200 metres away from schools and daycares while boosting provincial supports for addiction treatment as of March 31.
While that change was criticized by some health advocates, who predict more overdoses, it was hailed by some Toronto residents who are fed up with drug dealers.
Among the sites closing is one in Leslieville's South Riverdale Community Health Centre, near where mother-of-two Karolina Huebner-Makurat was killed by a stray bullet in July 2023, a brazen daylight crime that shocked the city.
As the legislature rose Dec. 12 for the winter break, Ford announced new measures to address the homeless tent encampments that have sprung up in parks across Toronto and throughout the province, promising an additional $75.5 million for shelters and affordable housing - and toughening up trespassing laws.
Since he took office almost seven years ago, the housing shortage has increased in fast-growing Ontario and there are now an estimated 81,000 homeless people in the province.
After his Tories won power in 2018, ending an almost 15-year Liberal dynasty under premiers Kathleen Wynne and Dalton McGuinty, Ford was re-elected in 2022 following a global COVID-19 pandemic in which Ontario fared better than any neighbouring province or American state during the health emergency.
Despite his current standing in the polls, not all PC operatives are happy about a snap election.
They are concerned about history repeating itself, recalling that in 1990, popular Liberal premier David Peterson held an election a year early and was punished by voters, losing to Bob Rae's New Democrats in one of the largest upsets in Canadian political history. Peterson is now chair of Torstar, the Star's parent company.
Along with that precedent, some Ford loyalists worry about the "Underhill balance theory," which posits that when Ontarians elect Liberals federally, they tend to choose Tories provincially. Conversely, when they elect federal Tory governments, the Liberals - and, once, in Rae's case, the NDP - usually win provincially at Queen's Park.
With polls suggesting federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre will triumph in a national election expected later this year, the "balance" phenomenon was another consideration in going to provincial voters early.
At dissolution, there were 79 Tories, including Speaker Ted Arnott, who is not seeking re-election, 28 New Democrats, nine Liberals, six Independents and two Greens in the 124-member house.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford is joined by Greg Rickford, Minister of Indigenous Affairs and First Nations Economic Reconciliation, and Sonny Gagnon, Chief of Aroland First Nation, to provide remarks and hold a media availability.https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCKvajOhOW6H8Okrs8wSb-bQPremier Doug Ford, wearing a "Canada Is Not For Sale" hat, speaks as he arrives for a first ministers' meeting in Ottawa on Jan. 15, 2025.

Document 447 of 500

Jagmeet Singh won't rule out backing a Liberal tariff package but still plans to topple the government as soon as possible

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/jagmeet-singh-wont-rule-out-backing-liberal/docview/3161368805/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Jagmeet+Singh+won%27t+rule+out+backing+a+Liberal+tariff+package+but+still+plans+to+topple+the+government+as+soon+as+possible&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh is not ruling out helping the Liberals pass a potential relief package for workers if Canada enters a trade war with the United States.
But the New Democrat leader stressed there have been no discussions about such a package, and that his party still intends to try and topple the Liberals' minority government and trigger an election as soon as possible.
"I've maintained my position that I'll be voting against the government at the earliest opportunity," Singh said Tuesday.
"If the Liberals are serious though, about something, a plan that should be put in place to support workers, call the opposition leaders together. If they're serious about it, discuss that plan with us the way we did during the (COVID-19) pandemic. I've not seen any of that initiative."
A spokesperson from the Prime Minister's Office did not respond Tuesday when asked if the government planned to discuss any relief proposals with opposition parties.
Having shut down Parliament when he announced his intention to resign once a new Liberal leader is chosen, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and his minority government are grappling with U.S. President Donald Trump's threat to impose tariffs on all Canadian goods. On Tuesday, a spokesperson from the White House reiterated that Trump intends to implement those 25 per cent tariffs on Feb. 1.
In the meantime, the federal government has been preparing a potential response - including a possible relief package for workers and businesses impacted in a trade war with the country's vital trading partner.
Finance Minister Dominic LeBlanc said last week that it's best not to compare any possible relief package to the massive government aid that flowed to individuals and businesses during the pandemic. But he did say the government would support workers, businesses, economic sectors and specific regions of the country in the event of a "sustained" response to American tariffs.
LeBlanc added that he has had reassuring conversations about potential supports with provincial governments, including in Ontario, and that Ottawa is planning to use revenues collected from counter-tariffs on American imports to support sectors and workers impacted by a trade war.
"If we get into that scenario, people should be very reassured that in the case of Ontario, both (the federal and provincial) governments will very quickly work together to put in place the supports necessary," LeBlanc told the Star.
Parliament is suspended until March 27, with the governing Liberals set to elect Trudeau's replacement as prime minister on March 9. But a senior government official told the Star on Tuesday that some supports for trade war relief would not require legislation to move forward, such as changes to more quickly provide employment insurance to workers who might lose their jobs.
Speaking Tuesday from his home riding in Burnaby, B.C., Singh called on the Liberals to speak with opposition leaders about bringing forward a plan to support workers. He said there has been no outreach so far, and that any relief plan should target affected sectors and be tied to employment levels and keep wages and jobs in place.
"There needs to be strict rules in place that if any business is going to get support, that it has to be tied to workers," Singh said.
On Parliament Hill, Bloc Québécois Leader Yves-François Blanchet dismissed talk of a relief package as a gimmick to extend the life of the Liberal government. He said the Liberals should call an election sooner - perhaps after a new Liberal leader is chosen on March 9 - if they want to help people impacted by any tariff spat with the U.S.
"I believe that the intention would be to create a context in which the government would get one week - oh, two weeks - oh, three weeks - and then they remain there longer than supposed," Blanchet said. "I'm not getting into any tricks."
The Conservatives, under Pierre Poilievre, have been demanding an election for months, and have tabled several failed confidence motions to try and defeat the Liberal government. Poilievre has also called on the Liberal government to reopen Parliament so the legislature can pass new border measures, discuss tariff retaliation, and prepare a plan to boost the economy.
"Had the Liberals not put their party ahead of their country, we could be debating and considering options to deal with this critical issue right now. Instead, they suspended Parliament to save their political skins, putting the livelihoods of Canadians at risk in face of this unjustified threat," said Conservative Deputy Leader Melissa Lantsman, in an emailed statement to the Star.
The Star has previously reported that the federal government is looking at a potential retaliation to Trump's tariffs that could see Canada impose counter-tariffs on $600 billion worth of American imports, which would raise roughly $150 billion in collected duties.
Trump - who has mused about using "economic force" to annex Canada and called it the "51st state" - has complained and exaggerated his country's trade deficit with Canada, and also pointed to illegal immigration and drugs entering the U.S. from the north.
In response, the Liberal government announced a $1.3-billion plan to beef up border security at the border, which includes more surveillance drones and two leased Blackhawk helicopters to patrol the frontier.
But that plan so far has not prompted Trump to back down, with his spokesperson Karoline Leavitt telling reporters on Tuesday that the U.S. president still intended to impose the tariffs on Feb. 1 when she last spoke to him about it Monday night.
The Canadian Chamber of Commerce has warned that Trump's 25 per cent tariffs could reduce Canada's gross domestic product (GDP) by 2.6 per cent and cause a recession by the middle of the year.
With files from Tonda MacCharles

Document 448 of 500

Doug Ford is trying to catch his opponents off guard with a snap election. Just how ready are they?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-is-trying-catch-his-opponents-off-guard/docview/3161368798/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+is+trying+to+catch+his+opponents+off+guard+with+a+snap+election.+Just+how+ready+are+they%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: They are gearing up to campaign - but how ready are the major political parties for a provincial election?
Leaders will launch their campaigns this week now that Doug Ford has dissolved the legislature, but only the Progressive Conservatives already have a full slate of 124 candidates for the Feb. 27 vote.
Ford will be in Windsor on Wednesday, NDP Leader Marit Stiles in Toronto, and Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie will be in Barrie but formally kick off her campaign on Thursday in Mississauga, where she served as mayor for almost a decade.
It's the first provincial campaign for both Stiles and Crombie as party leaders, and Ford's third.
The Liberals are close to a full slate of nominated candidates - at more than 100 either confirmed or about to be - and, after months of speculation, Crombie announced Tuesday she'll seek a seat in Mississauga East-Cooksville.
The Greens have 74 candidates and the Official Opposition New Democrats about 60, although the NDP predicts it will have 80 by the end of the week.
"One of the major factors influencing Ford's decision to call an early election is the relative unpreparedness and lack of resources of the other parties, among other things," said Dax D'Orazio, a political scientist at Queen's University.
He said the PCs have also "done an excellent job in fundraising over the past couple years, used government advertising to their benefit on several occasions, announced new measures and policies that might help connect with the base on the campaign trail - supervised consumption sites, alcohol distribution, and bike lanes - and showered the entire province with ($200) cheques" that have recently been mailed out.
"All of this will soften the ground when candidates are knocking on doors and mobilizing for the election," D'Orazio said.
For Western University political science professor Laura Stephenson, the fact that dozens of candidates have yet to be named isn't a huge concern as "things can come together quite quickly once there's an election call."
While Ford has been hinting at an election for months, "the actual timeline came about last week ... (now) you're going to see things moving pretty rapidly," and even last-minute candidates will have party supporters to help them, Stephenson said.
The opposition parties have accused Ford of calling an unnecessary election, with Stiles saying Tuesday that "in the coming weeks, we have the power to choose a better future for our province. We have the power to fight back against Donald Trump and turn the page on Doug Ford. Together, we can win an NDP government that's on your side."
The Liberals already have a slogan - a "More For You" message unveiled last fall at their policy convention - and the PCs say they will "Protect Ontario," referring to the threat of tariffs from the U.S., which has been Ford's rationale for calling the election.
The NDP says it will soon unveil its campaign slogan.
The PCs, Liberals and NDP will have campaign vehicles touring the province for the next four weeks - with the NDP managing to snag the same well-liked and experienced bus driver they've had for the past two campaigns.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner, who will be travelling in a Chevy Equinox EV, was one of two of his party's MPPs in the legislature, along with Aislinn Clancy.
He'll launch his campaign in Clancy's Kitchener Centre riding on Wednesday night, following a rally earlier in the day in his riding of Guelph.
"To date we've had our best January ever for fundraising, on the heels of a record-breaking (fourth quarter) in 2024," the Greens said in a statement about the $2.2 million they've received from 8,500 donors.
At the start of 2025, the NDP had $10.4 million from more than 220,000 contributions.
The Liberals ended 2024 with a fundraising high of more than $5.4 million from 11,227 donors.
Crombie is running in Mississauga East-Cooksville, which was held by Independent MPP Kaleed Rasheed, a former Tory who was forced from the PC caucus over a Greenbelt-related controversy. He is not seeking re-election.
The Liberal leader is running against PC candidate Silvia Gualtieri, who is the mother-in-law of Brampton Mayor Patrick Brown, a former PC leader. Brown and Crombie have clashed in the past over issues in Peel Region.
CAPTION: From left, Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles, Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford, Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner and Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie are shown in these recent file photos.

Document 449 of 500

Former Humber Polytechnic executives, major donor call on Ford government to expand probe of board dispute

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/former-humber-polytechnic-executives-major-donor/docview/3160636481/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Former+Humber+Polytechnic+executives%2C+major+donor+call+on+Ford+government+to+expand+probe+of+board+dispute&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Former Humber presidents, board chairs and a top donor, "alarmed about the risk of damage" to the Toronto polytechnic, are calling on Doug Ford's government to investigate whether the colleges ministry overstepped in a dispute that resulted in the mass resignation of board members.
Humber Polytechnic is currently facing a third-party review that was triggered, sources say, by tensions over governance matters between board governors and the president.
In the letter sent late Monday afternoon, and seen by the Star, ex-Humber executives urged that the review be thorough and fully independent, but also that its scope be broadened to look "at the ministry's role in affording due process to the college board in dealing with the third-party complaint that led to the governance review."
"Board resignations may have been avoided and a more positive resolution of issues achieved had consultation and discussion taken place prior to issuing directives on board conduct going into the review," the letter stated. "In the absence of transparency and explanation of the substance of complaints, board members were unable to execute their duties and responsibilities."
The letter was addressed to Nolan Quinn, the minister of colleges and universities, and cc'd Ford. Signatories included past Humber presidents Chris Whitaker and Robert Gordon, as well as former chairs Anthony Longo, Brien Gray, William Ardell, Robert Hull, Audrey McKinney and Ana Fernandes. It was also signed by Bob Barrett, whose family foundation donated $30 million to Humber in 2023 and whose name graces Humber's centre for technology innovation.
Nine board members tendered their resignations just over a week ago in the wake of a letter from the deputy minister of colleges and universities, who asked that the board refrain from proceeding with a performance review of Humber president and CEO Ann Marie Vaughan and to put any discussions of board recruitment or structure on hold while a "fact-finding exercise" into governance practices got underway.
David Wai's Jan. 14 letter, which the Star has seen, also directed members to refrain from in-camera meetings or from emailing intermediaries or staff, asking that all communication be exchanged directly between the board chair and CEO.
Sources told the Star that board members were left feeling they couldn't do their jobs. On Jan. 17, eight members including the chair and vice-chair stepped down, followed by the resignation of a Humber student representative on Jan. 21.
Seven members remain, including Vaughan, who was hired as president in 2022.
Wai first alerted the board in early December that concerns had been raised about governance practices at Humber and that the ministry was initiating a third-party review. The second letter in January provided more specifics, including the directive on how the board should conduct business while Linda Franklin, former president of Colleges Ontario, led a "fact-finding exercise to get an assessment of the current situation at Humber." A "governance review exercise" would follow, wrote Wai, with an expectation of it all to be wrapped up by March 15.
It is unclear who made the request for government intervention. A source said board members were surprised by the move. The ministry told the Star that the concerns were brought forward by "external parties."
College boards help set an institution's strategic goals and are responsible for the hiring, evaluating, and if necessary, dismissal of college presidents. However, the province has oversight authority of colleges.
"We expect that post-secondary leaders put the best interests of students at the forefront of their work," ministry spokesperson Dayna Smockum said last week. "As the review gets underway, the board will continue to carry out their functions in accordance with their duties and their fiduciary responsibilities."
Last week, Humber spokesperson Andrew Leopold directed Star questions about the review to the ministry but added that the polytechnic welcomed and supported the inquiry and that the school was in the midst of appointing new board members. "Humber's operations and campus life continue to flourish and remain unaffected, and our operations continue business as usual," he said.
Monday's letter demanded that the polytechnic postpone filling the board positions until the ministry's report is released.
"It is crucial that Humber College navigates this situation effectively to maintain its integrity and culture," the letter stated. "An institution with a reputation for excellence, best practice and model for innovation, we have benefited from stable, progressive governance and leadership for over 55 years. This is an institution that the province cannot afford to allow to degrade in any way."
More than 66,000 students attend Humber, one of the largest polytechnics in Canada.
CAPTION: Monday's letter was signed by past Humber presidents as well as former board chairs.

Document 450 of 500

Balancing the budget and lowering taxes is the best way for the next Ontario government to deal with Trump

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/balancing-budget-lowering-taxes-is-best-way-next/docview/3160453041/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Balancing+the+budget+and+lowering+taxes+is+the+best+way+for+the+next+Ontario+government+to+deal+with+Trump&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford, who will trigger an election onWednesday, recently said he plans to "spend billions of dollars" to stimulate Ontario's economy if President Donald Trump makes good on his threat to slap a 25 per cent tariff on Canadian exports into the United States.
But rather than piling on even more spending, the next Ontario government - whoever that may be - should enact policies that finally get provincial finances back in order and make Ontario an attractive place to work and invest.
Relief can't come soon enough. The Ford government has woefully mismanaged provincial finances. When first elected in 2018, Premier Ford promised to balance the budget and reduce government debt - something Ford's former finance minister Vic Fedeli described as a "moral" imperative. Yet since then, the government has run deficits in five of six years and the government's net debt burden has increased by an estimated $70.3 billion.
As a result, in 2023 Ontario had the second-highest debt burden of any province (only Newfoundland and Labrador had a larger burden) when measured on a per-person basis.
Based on the Ford government's latest fiscal update, the reckless mismanagement has continued into this fiscal year (2024/25). Despite enjoying lower-than-expected debt interest costs and higher-than-expected revenues - which combined could have nearly eliminated the budget deficit - the Ford government instead chose to again increase spending and keep running deficits.
Why should Ontarians care?
Because the Ford government's penchant for spending and borrowing is hurting Ontario's economy. When the government runs a deficit and accumulates more debt, it competes with individuals, households and businesses for borrowing. This drives up interest rates (i.e. the cost of borrowing) for everyone, which can reduce the level of investment in the economy. Moreover, because rising debt and higher interest rates equal higher interest payments, the government faces pressure to raise taxes. And the brunt of the new tax burden will fall on younger generations of Ontarians.
Also this week, Ford said Trump "wants to attract businesses from Ontario to come down to the United States," which will eliminate jobs in the province.
And Ford's right. When policymakers create the conditions to attract people and investment, their economies grow and people prosper.
If the Ontario government wants to beat Trump at his own game, it should lower personal income taxes and make the province a more attractive destination for high-skilled workers such as engineers and entrepreneurs who contribute greatly to the economy and create jobs. Lower taxes also improve the incentive for individuals to engage in productive activities such as working, saving and investing. In 2023, Ontario had the third-highest top combined (provincial and federal) personal income tax rate in Canada and the U.S.
The government should also lower business taxes to make Ontario more competitive with the U.S. in attracting businesses and investment - the pillars of job-creation and prosperity.
Regardless of who wins the election, the next Ontario government should finally restore some semblance of fiscal responsibility and balance the budget. And it should lower taxes for workers and businesses to help create prosperity across the province. That's a much more sensible and sustainable way to counter threats from Trump (or anyone else) than spending billions of dollars borrowed on the backs of Ontarians.
Jake Fuss and Grady Munro are fiscal policy analysts at the Fraser Institute.
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford will call an election on Wednesday. Ford speaks to the media during the fall meetings of Canada's premiers in Toronto on Dec. 16, 2024.
CREDIT: Jake Fuss and Grady Munro, Contributors

Subject: Taxes; Tax rates; Borrowing; Interest rates; Personal income; Debt restructuring; Budget deficits

Business indexing term: Subject: Taxes Tax rates Borrowing Interest rates Personal income Debt restructuring Budget deficits

Location: United States--US; Canada

Document 451 of 500

Doug Ford's election call is plainly ridiculous. And, if the opposition leaders get it together, it could backfire

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-election-call-is-plainly-ridiculous-if/docview/3160452719/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+election+call+is+plainly+ridiculous.+And%2C+if+the+opposition+leaders+get+it+together%2C+it+could+backfire&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford's stated reason for calling an election more than a year early is plainly ridiculous.
The premier no more needs a new mandate to deal with the threat of U.S. tariffs than he has needed one to implement any number of policy positions in recent years, from transit to housing to alcohol sales to sending out $200 cheques to everyone and their dog. He didn't need the explicit approval of the electorate to do any of these things because he already had it. To paraphrase Don Draper from Mad Men: that's what the majority government is for.
But his opponents shouldn't spend too much time in the coming campaign complaining about the fact that the campaign is happening: that horse has well and truly bolted. As much as Liberal leader Bonnie Crombie and NDP leader Marit Stiles might want to spend many days criticizing Ford for an unnecessary election - and to paint the premier as motivated by self-interest - that argument can only carry them so far.
Election campaigns move on, and even if the public has no great desire for this one, eventually voters will accept that it's happening. No one is going to cast a ballot a month from now to say that they really are mad about casting that ballot.
Besides, opportunities abound. Though comparisons have been made to David Peterson's ill-fated decision to send Ontario to the polls in 1990, when his Liberal government was toppled by Bob Rae's upstart NDP, there's a more recent example of a leader who got greedy and paid for it.
Justin Trudeau called a snap election in 2021 in a situation that has some parallels to Ford's move this week. The prime minister had untested opponents (particularly then-Conservative leader Erin O'Toole), and he was trying to lock in a majority before the other parties had time to prepare themselves for a fight. It was deeply cynical, especially as it came at a point in the pandemic when some voters were at greater risk of serious illness, yet no one spent weeks grousing about the fact that the campaign was on. Trudeau was criticized for it at the start, then everyone moved along.
The PM's decision to seek a new mandate backfired. The results were almost identical to the federal vote that had taken place two years earlier, but Trudeau's position was significantly weakened. It has never recovered.
Ontario's opposition leaders have a chance to deliver a similar wound to Ford. The threat of tariffs should be a non-issue: everyone knows that, whatever Canada's response to whatever President Donald Trump eventually does is, it won't be dependent on how many MPPs Ontario's premier has at the legislature. And, having been granted an early chance to challenge Ford on his record, Crombie and Stiles should be eager to pounce. Crombie, in particular, has been the Liberal leader for more than a year now, but she still doesn't have a seat in the legislature and hasn't been able to introduce herself to Ontarians in any meaningful way. It's tough to set an agenda when you have a few scattered MPPs and little money. But Ford's early call has gifted her the platform from which to make her pitch. No longer reduced to speaking from the sidelines at Queen's Park, Crombie will now have a starring role.
And while opposition leaders in a majority government are reduced to complaining about what the ruling party does without being able to do much of anything about it, Crombie and Stiles have been handed the chance to renew their influence. Now they can say what they would do as premier and, crucially, have the opportunity to do it.
Ford could have kept his rivals boxed in and used his healthy majority to govern as he saw fit well into 2026. Instead, he's opened himself up to judgment. His opponents should be embracing the good fortune he has provided them.
The polls suggest that the Progressive Conservatives enjoy a strong lead, which is why Ford has sought to press his advantage. But everyone who calls a snap vote assumes easy victory is a given.
It isn't, of course. That's what elections are for.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford reacts to a comment made by federal Finance and Intergovernmental Affairs Minister Dominic LeBlanc, not shown, as they speak to reporters at Queen's Park on Jan. 14.
CREDIT: Scott Stinson

Subject: Voters; Elections; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Tariffs

Document 452 of 500

Margaret Atwood backs council motion pushing for more powers for Toronto: 'The status quo ... must end'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/margaret-atwood-backs-council-motion-pushing-more/docview/3160304163/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Margaret+Atwood+backs+council+motion+pushing+for+more+powers+for+Toronto%3A+%27The+status+quo+...+must+end%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Margaret Atwood has added her name to the chorus of voices wanting to see Toronto gain more independence from the province, including council exploring becoming a so-called "charter city," which would give it more control over its own affairs, including the power to create additional taxes.
"The status quo - a complete lack of protected city authority; continual provincial interference in local affairs and our dependence on other levels of government to cover even our basic expenses - must end," said a letter form Atwood sent to city hall.
In a motion going to Mayor Olivia Chow's executive committee Tuesday, councillors Josh Matlow and Jamaal Myers want to establish a citizens' advisory board to look into how Toronto can gain municipal autonomy, if ever- a debate happening on the same day the Ford government started the process to hire a company to rip up Toronto bike lanes.
Atwood's email was among more than 150 others, including former Toronto mayor John Sewell, sent to the city backing the motion.
"I'm under no delusion about Doug Ford's support for our city ... He doesn't want to empower Toronto. He hurts Toronto," said Matlow on Monday at a TTC press conference, pointing to the controversial Bill 212 that limits new bike lanes and authorizes the province to remove the ones on Yonge, University and Bloor.
The motion notes that large cities like Toronto are responsible for delivering and funding a significant share of its transit, roads, social housing, recreation and other daily services. "Yet the province can overturn any of our considered and debated decisions on a whim," it said.
While the motion emphasizes merely creating a task force of local advocates and governance experts to see how Toronto can get more independence, it points to the benefits of a city charter.
Proponents of constitutionally protected charter cities argue the current power dynamic was established at a time when Canada was primarily a rural country and that province-wide policies are often unfit to meet the needs of major urban metropolises like Toronto today.
"It just doesn't make sense," said Doug Earl, a member of advocacy group Charter City Toronto which formed in 2018. "A city of three million people just has to have power over its own local affairs or else why have the city?"
Chow didn't immediately reject the idea of Toronto becoming a charter city. "People who live in cities deserve better. We deserve to have our democratic choices respected," the mayor said in an emailed statement Monday. "It's about time the law governing cities catches up with the modern-day reality that 80 per cent of Canadians live in cities."
A charter city is a centuries-old concept which gives a city its own legislation protected under the constitution allowing it to manage its own affairs, without interference from other governments. It gives municipalities such powers as the ability to raise more revenue by creating new taxes, borrowing funds and making financial arrangements with other governments.
Common in the U.S., the concept gained traction in Toronto in summer 2019, after the Ford government cut city council to 25 wards from a planned 47 during an election campaign and announced a new transit plan that ignored priorities already approved by council.
Once the pandemic hit, the idea of the charter city fell by the wayside.
"But with the bike lanes controversy, the momentum has been something unlike anything that we've seen," said Earl.
While Vancouver, Winnipeg, Montreal and Saint John are considered Canadian versions, Earl said a defining characteristic of "true charter cities in America" is that only local governments can amend their charters. In Canada, those cities' respective provincial governments can still change or even revoke the charter because it's not protected under the constitution.
To make Toronto a true charter city, Ottawa and Queen's Park need to agree on an Ontario-specific amendment to the constitution. Since that means Toronto needs approval from the very entity it's trying to separate from, it's a long shot, said Shauna Brail, associate professor at the University of Toronto's Institute for Management & Innovation.
When asked if the premier would consider the idea, Ford's spokesperson Grace Lee said in an email: "The response is no."
Matlow anticipated this reaction in his motion, but he said Monday "the work needs to begin now because Doug Ford will not be premier forever," he said. "Hopefully, he won't be premier in a month."
However, becoming a charter city could pose its own challenges such as with transportation planning that crosses political boundaries, costs and property taxes, Brail said.
"So if we're trying to move toward greater collaboration but on the other hand, we're trying to separate ourselves, this creates a disconnect," said Brail, adding that past attempts at gaining more power ended with allowances like the City of Toronto Act in 2007 and the "strong mayor" powers in 2022.
For both Earl and Brail, having a conversation about municipal autonomy, whether it ends with a city charter or not, is healthy for civic engagement . "It may provide the basis for which the city can lobby for other types of change," said Earl.
"Why would we not have this conversation?" Atwood's letter form said.
CAPTION: Margaret Atwood has sent an email in support of a motion at Tuesday's executive committee that would look at more independence for Toronto.

Canada Post facing insolvency, CEO says: Union accuses company of using money woes to make 'drastic' service cuts

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/canada-post-facing-insolvency-ceo-says/docview/3160304149/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada+Post+facing+insolvency%2C+CEO+says%3A+Union+accuses+company+of+using+money+woes+to+make+%27drastic%27+service+cuts&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Just days after Canada Post parted ways with their chief financial officer and two other senior executives, the company's leaders sounded the alarm on their financial situation in the first public hearing before the federal Industrial Inquiry Commission (IIC).
Doug Ettinger, the company's president and CEO, described the current situation as an "edge-of-the-cliff juncture." The Crown corporation is losing ground to its various competitors, he said, leaving it in financial dire straits in the aftermath of the five-week national postal strike.
"We are literally driving a 1967 Ford in a Formula One race in 2025," Ettinger said of the company's decline in letter and parcel volume. "This is simply - and I'll underline this - not sustainable and we are facing insolvency as a result."
The commission, appointed by Federal Labour Minister Steven MacKinnon, will have until May to probe potential ways to reach a new contract agreement between the country's national postal service and the 55,000-worker union.
Until then, the strike, which began on Nov. 15, will be on pause and workers and management will operate under the terms of the existing contract, which expired more than a year ago.
In remarks before the Ottawa-based commission, newly installed CFO Rindala El-Hage said that the company was projected to "run out of cash" by the end of July 2025 and that its losses are expected to increase, a projection that didn't include the strike. Last week, the federal government extended a $1.034 billion loan to Canada Post to "maintain its solvency and ensure it can continue its operations."
"The status quo is not an option," El-Hage said.
In its response, the Canadian Union of Postal Workers (CUPW) accused Canada Post of union-busting and said that the company is "trying to use its financial struggles as a pretext to implement drastic service cutbacks."
"In the past, the corporation has made inaccurate financial projections and misrepresented its financial condition to justify service cuts and demand concessions from the union in bargaining," CUPW president Jan Simpson said before the commission.
Ettinger said that the federal government's loan serves only as a stopgap.
"It is a temporary loan to ensure we have the money on hand to keep the lights on, continue operating, and pay everyone's wages," Ettinger said.
"This is clearly not a long-term fix. It is a liability we are expected to pay back. It just buys us time."
The commission, which is being led by mediator and arbitrator William Kaplan, will have subsequent hearings in February and March before presenting a report to the labour minister by May 15.
Throughout the five-hour hearing, Kaplan pressed both sides on their plans to resolve the labour dispute and secure longer term financial viability for the postal service.
Last week, the Star reported that Canada Post "completed a reorganization" of its senior leadership team that resulted in the elimination of five senior executive positions and the departure of their chief financial officer.
In a memo to employees obtained by the Star, Ettinger wrote that the changes would "enhance decision-making by reducing layers and bringing new, strategic perspectives to the table."
"These decisions were not easy, but they reflect the financial realities we face as a corporation and are effective immediately," Ettinger wrote.
By the weekend, Canada Post had removed the headshots and biographies of the executives they had let go from their website.
With files from Josh Rubin and The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Hayden Godfrey Toronto Star With files from Josh Rubin and The Canadian Press

Old City Hall a historic landmark worth preserving

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/old-city-hall-historic-landmark-worth-preserving/docview/3160304100/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Old+City+Hall+a+historic+landmark+worth+preserving&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Old City Hall a venerable landmark worth savingIt's been called 'precious' and a historic architectural gem. So why is this Toronto landmark about to be abandoned? Jan. 23
If walls could speak, Toronto's Old City Hall would have countless stories to tell, from legislation to incarceration and fascination.
No other building attracted as many visitors every year on the Doors Open Toronto weekend. Ten thousand people annually can't be wrong.
Let's not miss an opportunity to repurpose this landmark as a destination for us all - a public library, a youth-and-elder hostel, a place where tourists begin their visit to Toronto, a gallery/museum and shop to tell and sell Toronto's stories and tours.
Jane French, Toronto
It's time to show Doug Ford the doorDoug Ford insists he's not rushing with snap Feb. 27 Ontario election, Jan. 24
I'm absolutely thrilled our premier has decided to call an early election, even though it's entirely for his own political benefit.
Finally, we can elect someone who puts Ontario's true needs first instead of wasting billions of our dollars on unnecessary and unwanted projects like moving the Ontario Science Centre, building a spa at Ontario Place, tunnelling under Hwy. 401 and constructing unwanted highways.
I advise the new premier to listen to the people and spend his or her time and our money primarily on underfunded medical-related projects, like closed ERs, etc. Ford's best-before date came and went a long time ago when he and his brother were on Toronto city council. There, those two traditionally voted against the other ward councillors on almost every issue.
Let's finally get rid of Doug Ford and penalize him for calling an early election at our expense.
Seize the opportunity, Ontarians.
Norm Gibbons, Stouffville, Ont.
Donate your $200 rebate to the opposition
Rarely in my 77 years on this earth have I seen a more blatantly opportunistic political move such as this.
The current Ontario government has an overwhelming majority and has used that majority to enact legislation and policies with which many Ontarians disagree, some of which have been successfully challenged in the courts.
I do not support this government and will try to persuade my family and friends to vote against the incumbent provincial government.
I will also donate my $200 bribe to the opposition party of my choice, and I urge all Ontarians do the same.
Peter Bradley, Mississauga
Our money could be spent so much better
I just got my cheque today from the Ontario government for $200. Interestingly, the same government announced a snap election.
Now, I knew this cheque was coming, and I'd thought about how I might spend it: a shopping spree; a payment towards our heating bill; a splendid meal out. Ultimately, I can do none of the above. This is money you and I have given in good faith through our taxes to provide us with child care while we work, for healthcare when we are sick, for decent education for our kids, grandkids, even ourselves.
And it's been diverted to spend at our discretion courtesy of the government in power.
So how can I spend it, in good conscience?
James Wellwood, Williamsford, Ont.
Ontario can do much better in caring for seniorsStop 'warehousing' seniors and build small homes for long-term care: aging institute, Jan. 21
I read with interest Moira Welsh's article on small homes. Small non-profit homes of six people located in actual neighborhoods would work well for older adults. But 14 bed "units" located within a building? That hardly meets the criteria of maintaining frail elders in the community where they want to be.
Ontario has missed the mark by institutionalizing so many older people in dehumanizing facilities. Small homes in the community run by non-profit agencies are one solution, available to every other disability group.
Why not seniors?
Even better, put assisted living services into where older people needing care already live. PACE (Program of All Inclusive Care of the Elderly) already does that. Why is the government not funding that? Why is it not funding naturally occurring retirement communities with support services (NORC-SSPs)?
Why does the NIA report not address these concepts?
Ceciley White, Ajax
Seniors deserve more options in this province
As an older adult I have no interest in being placed in a 14-bed unit located within a long-term care institution or in a "neighborhood" of "small" homes. My preference, should I need care, is to remain in my own home and, if that is not possible, at least in my own neighbourhood.
In looking up green houses, many are operated for-profit and we saw what the result of that was in Ontario in long-term care facilities. No thank you.
Why won't the Ontario government fund non-profit, neighbourhood-based organizations to work with older adults in providing residential care?
Seniors are tired of being ignored when it comes to care options that affect them.
Melanie Kitchen, Oshawa
Trump making it all up as he goesDonald Trump pushes his tariff threats and '51st state' taunts against Canada on the world stage, Jan. 23
Since being sworn in, President Donald Trump has been dissing Canada and calling us to join America as the 51st state.
The impulsive megalomaniac is saying there are many Canadians who would gladly join the United States. Of course, he is making this up to suit his ridiculous agenda. I would invite him to listen to the lyrics of a song released by the Canadian band The Guess Who in 1970, American Woman.
Not much has changed in America over the last 54 years. I think I can safely say most Canadians agree with the lyrics of the song and feel good about being Canadian.
As the song says, "American woman stay away from me."
Trump? Stay away from Canada, we don't need you.
John Argiropoulos, Toronto
Canadians must unite in face of Trump's threats
We immigrated to Canada some 30 years ago and made a home here. We worked, kids went to school, then got jobs and life was good and it still is. Canada is a beautiful country with good, kind, capable and hardworking people.
When I heard Trump saying Canada should become a 51st state of America I was very upset and angry.
I can only imagine how otherCanadians must feel. We have a neighbour who is not a friend but a bully. When a child is bullied, we take action.
So why not now?
I also read there is a small percentage of people who wouldn't mind Canada being a part of America. Go live there, I tell them.
This beautiful, big country has gone through some hardships before so I know we'll survive this. We have friends all over the world, so let's ask for help if its needed.
We can do it ... let's go Canada!
Bozana Benic, Mississauga
CAPTION: A letter writer states Toronto's Old City Hall should be repurposed as a destination for tourists and locals alike.
