Document 455 of 500

City has opportunity to use electoral chaos to gain independence

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/city-has-opportunity-use-electoral-chaos-gain/docview/3160303201/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=City+has+opportunity+to+use+electoral+chaos+to+gain+independence&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With Premier Doug Ford - for some weird reason - set to call a provincial election tomorrow and a federal leadership race already underway to choose a successor to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, I've been thinking a lot lately about what promises Mayor Olivia Chow and Toronto city hall should try to get from all this campaign chaos.
I keep coming back to the same simple thing: an escape from the chaos. An ability to not have to ride this ride anymore. The ability to care just a little bit less.
For Chow and the municipal government, the ideal promise would be to free Toronto from this status quo, so the city's fortunes aren't so inextricably and consequentially tied to the whims and dramatics of these two other levels of government.
More autonomy and more power, in other words.
To that end, a motion from Coun. Josh Matlow set to be considered Tuesday by Chow's cabinet-like executive committee seems well-timed. Matlow wants the committee and council to endorse the creation of a "program advisory body" of local advocates and governance expert who will come up with a structure and a process for giving Toronto's local government more power.
If it wasn't clear already, the case for more local autonomy has been made repeatedly over the last couple of weeks, as city hall has been debating its proposed budget for 2025.
Consider, for example, that Toronto's proposed $18.8-billion operating budget includes about $4.8 billion in funds from the provincial and federal governments, but a significant percentage of that money is in service of the city delivering programs on behalf of those other governments. About $1.2 billion this year will go to delivery of child-care subsidies through the federal $10-a-day daycare program, for example, while $1.3 billion will go to social support payments city hall gives out on behalf of the provincial government for programs like Ontario Works.
Both are important programs, but city hall has little say over how they're managed, and has no real choice on whether to deliver them. And cash from Ottawa and Queen's Park to support programs like these has never been particularly dependable on a year-to-year basis - or enough to cover all the costs.
A briefing document released last week suggested that 25 per cent of all municipal property tax revenue this year will go toward things generally considered provincial or federal responsibilities. The $1.4 billion in spending will cover things like social housing, public health and increased paramedic costs driven in large part by crowded hospitals, making it challenging for paramedics to off-load patients from their ambulances. It will also cover shortfalls in those child care and social support programs.
The number keeps going up. The $1.4-billion figure for city-funded provincial and federal responsibilities is up from an estimated $1.1 billion in 2023 - a 25 per cent increase in just two years. Downloading costs and responsibilities onto cities didn't stop with former premier Mike Harris. It just got a bit more subtle.
Meanwhile, another report attached to this year's budget compared Toronto's governance structure to 16 other large cities across the world and determined that, well, Toronto is one hell of a unique snowflake.
And not in a good way.
No other city in the comparison is responsible for funding transit and the major emergency services - police, fire and paramedics - the report says. And while most of the other cities had some sort of formalized agreements on cost-sharing with their state and federal governments, for Toronto there are "no requirements for provincial or federal governments to consult on policies that may impact municipal finances." Heck, as we saw recently, there's not even any requirement that the provincial government consult with city hall before tearing out bike lanes.
The report also points out that, while most other cities at least have some kind of revenue-sharing agreement where local government gets a share of income or sales tax, Toronto is limited to a small menu of potential taxes and fees, and mostly reliant on property taxes.
It's telling that none of the other big cities in the study had a governance model quite like Toronto. It's a governance model that just doesn't work.
I get that there might be a lot of eye-rolling over Matlow's motion. Convening a "program advisory body" hardly sounds like meaningful progress. And the notion that Ford, especially, would listen to anything the advisory body advises seems unlikely.
But unlikely things have happened before. During the 2023 mayoral byelection, a lot of people - including, um, me - rolled their eyes when former councillor Ana Bailão pledged to get Ford to take on the costs of maintaining the Gardiner Expressway. But just months after the election, Chow managed to get Ford to agree to do it.
Giving Toronto official "charter city" status and real autonomy will likely require a single-province constitutional amendment supported by both the House of Commons and the Ontario legislature. It's not an impossible idea, especially if you think long-term and accept there's no sustainable path to a better Toronto without a new governance model. It's not a question of if. It's a question of when. An advisory body to get city hall ready for when opportunity strikes is a sensible move - any small step toward inevitability is a small step worth taking.
CAPTION: For Mayor Olivia Chow and city hall, the ideal campaign promise from Queen's Park and Ottawa would be to free the city from the status quo, Matt Elliott writes.Nick Lachance Toronto Star file photo
CREDIT: Matt Elliott OPINION

Subject: Paramedics; Mayors; Local government; Budgets; Costs; Cities; Revenue sharing

Business indexing term: Subject: Budgets Costs Revenue sharing

Title: City has opportunity to use electoral chaos to gain independence

Publication title: Toronto Star; Toronto, Ont.

Publication date: Jan 28, 2025

Document 456 of 500

I get why four in 10 young Canadians want to join the U.S.

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/i-get-why-four-10-young-canadians-want-join-u-s/docview/3160303193/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=I+get+why+four+in+10+young+Canadians+want+to+join+the+U.S.&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: No way, no how. That's the message from Canadians to Donald Trump's taunts about becoming the 51st state. At least that's what the opinion polls show, isn't it?
Yes, if you look at the overall findings. Support for joining the U.S. of A. is down around 13 per cent (Leger Marketing) or 10 per cent (Angus Reid Institute). Those are the kind of numbers that support Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's confident statement that "there's not a snowball's chance in hell" of Canada becoming an American state.
Trudeau is right about that, but the top lines in those polls don't show the whole picture. Inside a more recent survey is a story with more troubling implications for the future of this country.
Ipsos tested the waters just before Trump's inauguration and found a surprisingly large number of younger Canadians (those aged 18 to 34) are prepared to support the notion of becoming the 51st state if a couple of basic conditions are met.
More than four in 10 (43 per cent) said they'd vote to join the States if Canadians were offered full American citizenship and had their Canadian assets converted into U.S. dollars. (By comparison, only 17 per cent 55 and older would go along with the idea.) Almost a third of young Canadians think "it's only a matter of time" before Canada and the U.S. merge into one country.
Those are big numbers. I'm not suggesting they show a merger (or takeover) will happen. Even if we all liked the idea, the Republicans who control the U.S. Congress wouldn't welcome a huge new blue state, whatever Trump thinks.
But the Ipsos data does show a worrisome lack of faith among younger Canadians in the value of this country and its long-term future as an independent concern.
The pollsters didn't ask why they feel this way, but here are a couple of guesses. First, younger Canadians haven't been dealt a good hand, certainly not as good a hand as those older people, who are much more likely to tell Trump to get lost.
Last year I wrote about something called the World Happiness Report, which ranked Canada a respectable 15th out of 143 nations when it comes to overall happiness. But those results concealed an enormous generation gap: older Canadians ranked eighth, while those under 30 were way down at 58th in the world. A McGill University scholar commented at the time that "we have a very, very unhappy youth."
Young people trying to get established in this country are grappling with huge issues their parents didn't face - the cost of housing, most obviously, but also the collapse of trust in almost every institution, the toxic impact of social media and the aftershocks of the pandemic. No wonder their faith in the country has been shaken and they're more willing to contemplate a radical break with the status quo.
Last year this disaffection showed up in a big swing among younger Canadians toward supporting the Conservatives, a sharp break from their traditional pattern of favouring progressive parties. Now it seems to manifest as loss of belief in Canada itself.
The other factor at play is surely the devaluation of the country over the past decade or so by political elites themselves. Many have noted the string of apologies Trudeau offered over the years for historic sins ranging from residential schools to the century-old Komagata Maru incident, when a shipload of Punjabi migrants was turned away.
It's tough finding the balance between recognizing past wrongs and writing off our common history as a shameful litany of wrongdoing. But the academic and intellectual classes have tipped way over toward condemnation in recent years, just when those young Canadians were forming their image of the country.
It's easier for older people with longer memories to put all that in a larger context, where there's also space for pride in a country that overall is a very decent place to live. But for younger people, I expect it's harder to believe in something when it's been publicly rubbished throughout your adult life.
No wonder so many are prepared to give up on the country - if the price is right. That's a problem, even if Trump's threats never become reality.
CAPTION: More than four in 10 (43 per cent) Canadians between the ages of 18 and 34 said they'd vote to join the States if Canadians were offered full American citizenship and had their Canadian assets converted into U.S. dollars.

Full text: Old City Hall
is worth saving
It's been called 'precious' and a historic architectural gem. So why is this Toronto landmark about to be abandoned? Jan. 23
If walls could speak, Toronto's Old City Hall would have countless stories to tell, from legislation to incarceration and fascination. No other building attracted as many visitors every year on the Doors Open Toronto weekend.
Let's not miss an opportunity to repurpose this landmark as a destination for us all - a public library, a youth-and-elder hostel, a place where tourists begin their visit to Toronto, a gallery/museum and shop to tell and sell Toronto's stories and tours.
Jane French, Toronto
It's time to show Ford the door
Doug Ford insists he's not rushing with snap Feb. 27 Ontario election, Jan. 24
I'm absolutely thrilled our premier has decided to call an early election, even though it's entirely for his own political benefit.
Finally, we can elect someone who puts Ontario's true needs first instead of wasting billions of our dollars on unnecessary and unwanted projects like moving the Ontario Science Centre, building a spa at Ontario Place, tunnelling under Hwy. 401 and constructing unwanted highways.
I advise the new premier to listen to the people and spend his or her time and our money primarily on underfunded medical-related projects, like closed ERs, etc.
Let's finally get rid of Ford.
Norm Gibbons, Stouffville
Donate rebate
to opposition
Rarely in my 77 years on this earth have I seen a more blatantly opportunistic political move such as this. The current Ontario government has an overwhelming majority and has used that majority to enact legislation and policies with which many Ontarians disagree, some of which have been successfully challenged in the courts.
I do not support this government and will try to persuade my family and friends to vote against the incumbent provincial government. I will also donate my $200 bribe to the opposition party of my choice, and I urge all Ontarians do the same.
Peter Bradley, Mississauga
Our money could be spent better
I just got my cheque from the Ontario government for $200. Interestingly, the same government announced a snap election.
Now, I knew this cheque was coming, and I'd thought about how I might spend it: a shopping spree; a payment towards our heating bill; a splendid meal out. Ultimately, I can do none of the above. This is money you and I have given in good faith through our taxes to provide us with child care while we work, for health care when we are sick, for decent education for our kids, grandkids, even ourselves. And it's been diverted to spend at our discretion courtesy of the government in power.
So how can I spend it, in good conscience?
James Wellwood,
Williamsford, Ont.

Stop 'warehousing' seniors and build small homes for long-term care: aging institute, Jan. 21

Why is the government not funding that? Why is it not funding naturally occurring retirement communities with support services (NORC-SSPs)?
Ceciley White, Ajax
Seniors deserve more options
As an older adult I have no interest in being placed in a 14-bed unit in a long-term-care institution or in a "neighbourhood" of "small" homes. My preference, should I need care, is to stay in my own home and, if that is not possible, at least in my own neighbourhood.

Seniors are tired of being ignored.
Melanie Kitchen, Oshawa

Donald Trump pushes his tariff threats and '51st state' taunts against Canada on the world stage, Jan. 23
Since being sworn in, President Donald Trump has been dissing Canada and calling us to join America as the 51st state.

Document 458 of 500

Premier set to remove some city bike lanes: Province using its power to reinstate vehicle lanes on three main downtown arteries

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/premier-set-remove-some-city-bike-lanes/docview/3160302972/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Premier+set+to+remove+some+city+bike+lanes%3A+Province+using+its+power+to+reinstate+vehicle+lanes+on+three+main+downtown+arteries&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is pedalling furiously toward removing bike lanes from "three of Toronto's most congested streets."
With an election campaign set to officially kick off Wednesday, Ford's Progressive Conservative government "has retained an engineering company to complete design work, as it prepares to reinstate vehicle lanes on Bloor Street West, Yonge Street and University Avenue," the province said.
"Our government has heard loud and clear from residents and businesses about the problems with gridlock. The failed approach of installing bike lanes without a second thought for drivers or local businesses is not working," said Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria.
"By freeing up some of Toronto's most important roads, we're bringing common sense back to municipal planning,"
Sarkaria said in one of his final acts before the government enters "caretaker mode" through the Feb. 27 election.
It is unclear when the bike lanes would actually be taken out.
Under Sarkaria's Bill 212, the Reducing Gridlock, Saving You Time Act, which received royal assent last month, Queen's Park now has the power to remove existing bike lanes and veto new ones the province feels would contribute to traffic jams.
The City of Toronto, which opposed the law, has estimated it would cost $48 million to dismantle $27 million in bike lane infrastructure, a claim Ford has dismissed as "hogwash."
Provincial taxpayers will foot the tab for taking out the lanes.
"One per cent of the population cannot take up 50 per cent of the roads, "Ford said in December when the bill passed, understating the number of Torontonians who cycle and overstating the amount of space bike lanes take up in the city.
The $6.2-million Transportation Tomorrow Survey found 3.1 per cent of Toronto residents commute to work by bicycle each morning, triple the number the premier often cites.
That jumps to 3.6 per cent of those who reported using their bikes "all day" in a city of three million people where 164,806 daily cycling trips were made.
However, that's far lower than some cycling advocates have argued in opposing the removal of bike lanes.
In comparison, 46 per cent of Torontonians drove their own cars or trucks in the morning with another 11 per cent being passengers in private vehicles and 25 per cent took public transit while 13 per cent walked.
The numbers change slightly when people were surveyed about "all day" modes of transportation: 50 per cent drove, 12 per cent were passengers, 22 per cent took transit, and 10 per cent walked.
CAPTION: Provincial taxpayers would foot the bill for taking out the bike lanes on Bloor Street West, Yonge Street and University Avenue. The government has retained an engineering company to complete the design work.R.J. Johnston Toronto Star
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Title: Premier set to remove some city bike lanes:   Province using its power to reinstate vehicle lanes on three main downtown arteries

Source type: Newspaper

Language of publication: English

Document 459 of 500

Atwood backs motion for city autonomy: More than 150 people support citizens' advisory board to explore becoming 'charter city'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/atwood-backs-motion-city-autonomy/docview/3160302962/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Atwood+backs+motion+for+city+autonomy%3A+More+than+150+people+support+citizens%27+advisory+board+to+explore+becoming+%27charter+city%27&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Margaret Atwood has added her name to the chorus of voices wanting to see Toronto gain more independence from the province, including council exploring becoming a so-called "charter city," which would give it more control over its own affairs, including the power to create additional taxes.
"The status quo - a complete lack of protected city authority; continual provincial interference in local affairs and our dependence on other levels of government to cover even our basic expenses - must end," said an emailed letter Atwood sent to city hall.
In a motion going to Mayor Olivia Chow's executive committee Tuesday, councillors Josh Matlow and Jamaal Myers want to establish a citizens' advisory board to look into how Toronto can gain municipal autonomy - a debate happening on the same day the Ford government started the process to hire a company to rip up Toronto bike lanes.
Atwood's email was among more than 150 others, including former Toronto mayor John Sewell, sent to the city, backing the motion.
"I'm under no delusion about Doug Ford's support for our city ... He doesn't want to empower Toronto. He hurts Toronto," said Matlow on Monday at a TTC press conference, pointing to the controversial Bill 212 that limits new bike lanes and authorized the province to remove the ones on Yonge, University and Bloor.
The motion notes that large cities like Toronto are responsible for delivering and funding a significant share of its transit, roads, social housing, recreation and other daily services. "Yet the province can overturn any of our considered and debated decisions on a whim," it said.
While the motion emphasizes merely creating a task force of local advocates and governance experts to see how Toronto can get more independence, it points to the benefits of a city charter.
Proponents of constitutionally protected charter cities argue that the current power dynamic was established at a time when Canada was primarily a rural country and that provincewide policies are often unfit to meet the needs of major urban metropolises like Toronto today.
"It just doesn't make sense," said Doug Earl, a member of advocacy group Charter City Toronto which formed in 2018. "A city of three million people just has to have power over its own local affairs or else why have the city?"
Chow didn't immediately reject the idea of Toronto becoming a charter city. "People who live in cities deserve better. We deserve to have our democratic choices respected," the mayor said in a written statement Monday. "It's about time the law governing cities catches up with the modern-day reality that 80 per cent of Canadians live in cities."
A charter city is a centuries-old concept that gives a city its own legislation protected under the constitution allowing it to manage its own affairs, without interference from other governments. It gives municipalities such powers as the ability to raise more revenue by creating new taxes, borrowing funds and making financial arrangements with other governments.
Common in the U.S., the concept first gained traction in Toronto in summer 2019, after the Ford government cut city council to 25 wards from a planned 47 during an election campaign and announced a new transit plan that ignored priorities already approved by council.
While Vancouver, Winnipeg, Montreal and Saint John are considered Canadian versions, Earl said a defining characteristic of "true charter cities in America" is that only local governments can amend their charters. In Canada, their respective provincial governments can still change or even revoke the charter because it's not protected under the constitution.
To make Toronto a charter city, Ottawa and Queen's Park need to agree on an Ontario-specific amendment to the constitution. Since that means Toronto needs approval from the very entity it's trying to separate from, it's a long shot, said Shauna Brail, associate professor at the University of Toronto's Institute for Management & Innovation.
When asked if the premier would consider the idea, Ford's spokesperson Grace Lee said in an email: "The response is no."
CREDIT: Mahdis Habibinia Toronto Star

Document 460 of 500

No 'traitors' in Parliament, but Trudeau government's handling of foreign meddling 'far from perfect,' inquiry report finds

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/no-traitors-parliament-trudeau-governments/docview/3160302960/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=No+%27traitors%27+in+Parliament%2C+but+Trudeau+government%27s+handling+of+foreign+meddling+%27far+from+perfect%2C%27+inquiry+report+finds&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-There are no "traitors" in Parliament.
Ottawa's handling of interference threats has been "far from perfect."
And disinformation whipped up by foreign and domestic agents "poses the single biggest risk" to Canada's democracy.
Those were among the starkest conclusions drawn by Justice Marie-Josée Hogue in her final report, released Tuesday, as the Foreign Interference Commission capped off more than a year of work publicly probing foreign meddling in Canadian affairs.
"On the one hand, trust in Canada's democratic institutions has been shaken, and it is imperative to restore it," Hogue wrote in the introduction to her seven-part report.
"On the other hand, while the government has the primary responsibility for ensuring national security and protecting our democratic institutions, what I have read and heard convinces me that society as a whole must help defend these institutions."
As she concluded in her first report last spring, Hogue said the result of the 2019 and 2021 elections are not in doubt, though she reiterated that in some ridings, foreign interference may have influenced the outcome. She also found major concerns with how intelligence is shared in the government and across security agencies.
In her report, Hogue specifically took aim at an explosive June report from the National Security and Intelligence Committee of Parliamentarians (NSICOP), a group of MPs and senators with top security clearance who are permanently bound to secrecy to receive highly classified information. The NSICOP report found that unnamed parliamentarians collaborated with foreign governments, and noted the committee had seen "troubling intelligence that some parliamentarians were, in the words of the intelligence services, 'semi-witting or witting' participants in the efforts of foreign states."
Hogue said she had not seen "any evidence of 'traitors' in Parliament plotting with foreign states to act against Canada" and said references to "witting" parliamentarians "were more definitive than the underlying intelligence could support." She reconfirmed no parliamentarians were named in the classified version of the report and said she had reviewed all of NSICOP's findings as well as the related intelligence.
The report cast ripples of suspicion across Parliament Hill, while NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh and Green Leader Elizabeth May, who both received security clearances to review the unredacted committee report, came away with vastly different conclusions. Singh said he believed some parliamentarians were "indeed traitors" to the country, something May strongly disputed. Nathalie Drouin, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's national security and intelligence adviser, testified before the commission that she was "very uncomfortable" with NSICOP's conclusions.
Singh said Tuesday that he was relying on the information he had at the time, which was less extensive than the records Hogue had access to, while May told reporters she was pleased Hogue arrived at the same conclusion she did.
Hogue also found significant errors in part of the NSICOP report that stated the Canadian Security Intelligence Service (CSIS) identified an MP as having been "compromised" by a foreign state. In fact, CSIS had not used that language but instead said it came from a source's description of the MP.
She found another reference where NSICOP said an MP had contact with an undeclared intelligence officer, or a spy, when in fact they were a legitimate foreign official. The NSICOP report identified a former MP as having attempted to arrange a meeting in a foreign country with intelligence officials, but Hogue said there was little backing that up.
"The NSICOP report's conclusion was likely based on the same limited information. This is an important consideration, given the seriousness of the allegation," Hogue wrote.
Hogue said while NSICOP had raised "unwarranted" alarm, there are nevertheless real concerns.
"To be clear, this does not mean that the conduct reported is not concerning," Hogue wrote. "There are legitimate concerns about parliamentarians potentially having problematic relationships with foreign officials, exercising poor judgment, behaving naively and perhaps displaying questionable ethics."
At the time, NSICOP was chaired by Liberal MP David McGuinty, who was appointed Canada's public safety minister in December.
In an interview with the Star, McGuinty said he sees many similarities regarding the larger problem of foreign interference between Hogue's report and the NSICOP findings.
He said he had yet to review the full report, but chalked up any differences in both reports to the additional resources Hogue had.
"This is a reflection of the thoroughness with which Madame Justice Hogue was able to pursue her work. She had over, I think, a dozen counsels. Her mandate was different. She had an inquiries act. She had deeper and longer resourcing," McGuinty said.
In total, Hogue made 51 recommendations to the government.
She called for CSIS to write clearer intelligence reports to ministers and to exercise caution about how often they send reports. She also recommended adding an external member to the government's elections task force who could communicate with the public about threats and misinformation. She further recommended that the government develop a "duty to warn" policy for when intelligence agencies believe they see a risk to a Canadian from a foreign entity. She said there should be a whole of government response to foreign interference and multiple changes to election laws.
During his final appearance before Hogue last fall, Trudeau said he had the "names of a number of parliamentarians, former parliamentarians and or candidates in the Conservative Party of Canada who are engaged, or at high risk of, or for whom there's clear intelligence, around foreign interference." He blasted Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre for his refusal to obtain the necessary security clearance that would allow that information to be shared.
Hogue recommended that leaders of all parties in the House of Commons receive a clearance, but Poilievre's spokesperson Sebastian Skamski said his position was unchanged.
"Our position on security briefings is long-standing and well known. Unlike others who are willing to limit their ability to hold the government accountable on important issues of national security, Mr. Poilievre will not be gagged and unable to speak or act on information he may receive," he said in an email.
On the topic of the government's response to the interference issue overall, Hogue concluded that Ottawa, at times, took "too long to act," that the flow of information was not always as "effective" as it should have been, and that the government has "proven to be a poor communicator and insufficiently transparent" about foreign meddling.
She noted that in some cases, information that should have reached the desks of certain cabinet ministers - and the prime minister - did not.
Hogue highlighted one case where CSIS applied for a warrant that seemed to languish in then public safety minister Bill Blair's office for weeks. Hogue does not directly identify who the warrant targeted, but Markham Ont. deputy mayor and former Ontario Liberal cabinet minister Michael Chan has previously said he was the subject of a "wiretap" by CSIS that required a government-approved warrant.
Hogue found Blair approved the warrant on the day it was presented to him, but his chief of staff Zita Astravas had the warrant for weeks before that. Hogue said she cannot find why CSIS, public safety and Astravas allowed it to languish.
"It seems to me that everyone involved dropped the ball. When a minister of public safety does not know he has to review a warrant application he cannot exercise his statutory duty," Hogue said.
Hogue also concluded that in her view, "it is no exaggeration to say that at this juncture, information manipulation (whether foreign or not) poses the single biggest risk to our democracy," and that it presents an "existential threat."
She noted, in particular, the disinformation campaign that targeted the Conservative party in 2021, under then-leader Erin O'Toole, though she was unable to establish a "definitive link" to China in that case.
Over the course of the commission's 18 months of work, Hogue heard from more than 100 witnesses, 64 of whom appeared more than once. Both stages of the exercise featured testimony from members of Canada's diaspora communities, federal party officials, MPs, top security and intelligence officials, cabinet ministers, members of the Prime Minister's Office and Privy Council Office and Trudeau.
The commission has tasked Ottawa with updating Parliament in one year about the progress it has made in implementing the recommendations, thought it is not known what a snap election call and a possible change in government would mean for that request.
CAPTION: Marie-Josee Hogue, the commissioner of the public inquiry on foreign interference, released her final report which says while there is evidence of some concerning behaviour by MPs, there is no evidence any MPs plotted with foreign states against Canada's interests. Hogue's final report issued many recommendations to insulate Canada from clear attempts to influence its democratic institutions saying Canada needs a whole-of-society response to tackle the problem. (Jan. 28, 2025 / The Canadian Press)Commissioner Marie-Josee Hogue asks a question to David Vigneault, former director of the Canadian Security Intelligence Service, third from right, as CSIS representatives appear at the Foreign Interference Commission in Ottawa on Sept. 27, 2024.

Document 461 of 500

I get why young Canadians envy the U.S.

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/i-get-why-young-canadians-envy-u-s/docview/3160302948/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=I+get+why+young+Canadians+envy+the+U.S.&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: No way, no how. That's the message from Canadians to Donald Trump's taunts about becoming the 51st state. At least that's what the opinion polls show, isn't it?
Yes, if you look at the overall findings. Support for joining the U.S. of A. is down around 13 per cent (Leger Marketing) or 10 per cent (Angus Reid Institute). Those are the kind of numbers that support Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's confident statement that "there's not a snowball's chance in hell" of Canada becoming an American state.
Trudeau is right about that, but the top lines in those polls don't show the whole picture. Inside a more recent survey is a story with more troubling implications for the future of this country.
Ipsos tested the waters just before Trump's inauguration and found a surprisingly large number of younger Canadians (those aged 18 to 34) are prepared to support the notion of becoming the 51st state if a couple of basic conditions are met.
More than four in 10 (43 per cent) said they'd vote to join the States if Canadians were offered full American citizenship and had their Canadian assets converted into U.S. dollars. (By comparison, only 17 per cent 55 and older would go along with the idea.) Almost a third of young Canadians think "it's only a matter of time" before Canada and the U.S. merge into one country.
Those are big numbers. I'm not suggesting they show a merger (or takeover) will happen. Even if we all liked the idea, the Republicans who control the U.S. Congress wouldn't welcome a huge new blue state, whatever Trump thinks.
But the Ipsos data does show a worrisome lack of faith among younger Canadians in the value of this country and its long-term future as an independent concern.
The pollsters didn't ask why they feel this way, but here are a couple of guesses. First, younger Canadians haven't been dealt a good hand, certainly not as good a hand as those older people, who are much more likely to tell Trump to get lost.
Last year I wrote about something called the World Happiness Report, which ranked Canada a respectable 15th out of 143 nations when it comes to overall happiness. But those results concealed an enormous generation gap: older Canadians ranked eighth, while those under 30 were way down at 58th in the world. A McGill University scholar commented at the time that "we have a very, very unhappy youth."
Young people trying to get established in this country are grappling with huge issues their parents didn't face - the cost of housing, most obviously, but also the collapse of trust in almost every institution, the toxic impact of social media and the aftershocks of the pandemic. No wonder their faith in the country has been shaken and they're more willing to contemplate a radical break with the status quo.
Last year this disaffection showed up in a big swing among younger Canadians toward supporting the Conservatives, a sharp break from their traditional pattern of favouring progressive parties. Now it seems to manifest as loss of belief in Canada itself.
The other factor at play is surely the devaluation of the country over the past decade or so by political elites themselves. Many have noted the string of apologies Trudeau offered over the years for historic sins ranging from residential schools to the century-old Komagata Maru incident, when a shipload of Punjabi migrants was turned away.
It's tough finding the balance between recognizing past wrongs and writing off our common history as a shameful litany of wrongdoing. But the academic and intellectual classes have tipped way over toward condemnation in recent years, just when those young Canadians were forming their image of the country.
It's easier for older people with longer memories to put all that in a larger context, where there's also space for pride in a country that overall is a very decent place to live. But for younger people, I expect it's harder to believe in something when it's been publicly rubbished throughout your adult life.
No wonder so many are prepared to give up on the country - if the price is right. That's a problem, even if Trump's threats never become reality.
ANDREW PHILLIPS IS A TORONTO-BASED STAFF COLUMNIST FOR THE STAR'S OPINION PAGE. REACH HIM AT APHILLIPS@THESTAR.CA.
CAPTION: Flags fly at the Ambassador Bridge in Windsor. Younger Canadians haven't been dealt a good hand, certainly not as good a hand as those older people, who are much more likely to tell Donald Trump to get lost, Andrew Phillips writes.Rob Gurdebeke THE CANADIAN PRESS file photo

Document 462 of 500

Poll suggests strong support for Ford: Nearly 50% of Ontarians surveyed ahead of election call would back Progressive Conservatives

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/poll-suggests-strong-support-ford/docview/3160302935/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Poll+suggests+strong+support+for+Ford%3A+Nearly+50%25+of+Ontarians+surveyed+ahead+of+election+call+would+back+Progressive+Conservatives&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives are in the poll position as the governing party revs up for a snap election call Wednesday, a new public opinion survey suggests.
The Abacus Data poll for the Star found Ford's Tories at 47 per cent support, far ahead of Bonnie Crombie's Liberals at 24 per cent, Marit Stiles's New Democrats at 19 per cent and Mike Schreiner's Greens at seven per cent.
"You can't imagine a better time to be calling an election if this is the polling that they're seeing as well," said Abacus president David Coletto, referring to the PC's internal research.
"It lays out the case why the Tories feel like now's a good time for them to go."
The election campaign officially begins Wednesday, but Ford has been in election-readiness mode since last May, even though the next scheduled vote was slated for June 2026.
Abacus surveyed 1,021 Ontarians from last Wednesday through Sunday using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 3.1 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
The firm asked respondents for their feelings about an early election, which would cost at least $150 million.
Nearly half of all Ontarians - 48 per cent - were "unhappy" or "very unhappy" at the expenditure, with 20 per cent "happy" or "very happy" and 32 per cent neutral.
When the cost of the election is not factored in, only 24 per cent were "unhappy" or "very unhappy," while 34 per cent were "happy" or "very happy" with a snap election and 42 per cent neutral.
Coletto said that suggests many voters believe Ford is being opportunistic to seek another four-year term some 15 months early.
"They don't love it, but is that enough to not vote for him? That is going to be the question," he said.
When it comes to the issues that voters wanted debated during the campaign, 60 per cent said "reducing the cost of living" was top of mind.
Forty per cent said "improving the health care system overall," with 38 per cent saying "making housing more affordable," 32 per cent saying "dealing with the impact of Donald Trump's tariffs," 26 per cent saying "growing the economy and creating good jobs" and 23 per cent saying "reducing homelessness and poverty."
Coletto said that indicates "Ford is vulnerable on health care."
"If this becomes a health-care election, which I don't think it will be, but if it does, that could be the ticket to a better outcome" for Crombie and Stiles, he said. "The problem, again, remains they are splitting (the anti-Ford) vote."
In terms of opinions on the party leaders, 41 per cent had a positive impression of Ford while 37 per cent had a negative view, with 21 per cent neutral and two per cent didn't know enough to say. That's a net impression rating for him of plus four per cent.
Impressions of Stiles were 28 per cent positive, with 28 per cent negative, 25 per cent neutral and 19 per cent unsure, for an even net rating.
The impressions of Crombie, the subject of millions of dollars of Tory attack ads for more than a year, were 26 per cent positive, 34 per cent negative, 25 per cent neutral and 14 per cent unsure, for a minus eight per cent net rating.
Impressions of Schreiner were 23 per cent positive, 22 per cent negative, 31 per cent neutral and 24 per cent unsure, for a net rating of plus one per cent.
Some 42 per cent approve of Ford's two-term PC government, while 34 per cent disapproved and 19 per cent were neutral and four per cent didn't know. That's an overall plus eight per cent approval, the highest Abacus has recorded for the administration.
"It's the first time since we've been tracking it that he's gotten above water," Coletto said.

Document 463 of 500

With campaign chaos looming in Ottawa and Queen's Park, Toronto should finally get this one thing that other major cities already have

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/with-campaign-chaos-looming-ottawa-queens-park/docview/3160302926/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=With+campaign+chaos+looming+in+Ottawa+and+Queen%27s+Park%2C+Toronto+should+finally+get+this+one+thing+that+other+major+cities+already+have&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With Premier Doug Ford - for some weird reason - set to call a provincial election tomorrow and a federal leadership race already underway to choose a successor to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, I've been thinking a lot lately about what promises Mayor Olivia Chow and Toronto city hall should try to get from all this campaign chaos.
I keep coming back to the same simple thing: an escape from the chaos. An ability to not have to ride this ride anymore. The ability to care just a little bit less.
For Chow and the municipal government, the ideal promise would be to free Toronto from this status quo, so the city's fortunes aren't so inextricably and consequentially tied to the whims and dramatics of these two other levels of government.
More autonomy and more power, in other words.
To that end, a motion from Coun. Josh Matlow set to be considered Tuesday by Chow's cabinet-like executive committee seems well-timed. Matlow wants the committee and council to endorse the creation of a "program advisory body" of local advocates and governance expert who will come up with a structure and a process for giving Toronto's local government more power.
If it wasn't clear already, the case for more local autonomy has been made repeatedly over the last couple of weeks, as city hall has been debating its proposed budget for 2025.
Consider, for example, that Toronto's proposed $18.8 billion operating budget includes about $4.8 billion in funds from the provincial and federal governments, but a significant percentage of that money is in service of the city delivering programs on behalf of those other governments. About $1.2 billion this year will go to delivery of childcare subsidies through the federal $10-a-day daycare program, for example, while $1.3 billion will go to social support payments city hall gives out on behalf of the provincial government for programs like Ontario Works.
Both are important programs, but city hall has little say over how they're managed, and has no real choice on whether to deliver them. And cash from Ottawa and Queen's Park to support programs like these has never been particularly dependable on a year-to-year basis - or enough to cover all the costs.
A briefing document released last week suggested that 25 per cent of all municipal property tax revenue this year will go toward things generally considered provincial or federal responsibilities. The $1.4 billion in spending will cover things like social housing, public health and increased paramedic costs driven in large part by crowded hospitals, making it challenging for paramedics to offload patients from their ambulances. It will also cover shortfalls in those childcare and social support programs.
The number keeps going up. The $1.4-billion figure for city-funded provincial and federal responsibilities is up from an estimated $1.1 billion in 2023 - a 25 per cent increase in just two years. Downloading costs and responsibilities onto cities didn't stop with former premier Mike Harris. It just got a bit more subtle.
Meanwhile, another report attached to this year's budget compared Toronto's governance structure to 16 other large cities across the world and determined that, well, Toronto is one hell of a unique snowflake. And not in a good way.
No other city in the comparison is responsible for funding transit and the major emergency services - police, fire and paramedics - the report says. And while most of the other cities had some sort of formalized agreements on cost-sharing with their state and federal governments, for Toronto there are "no requirements for provincial or federal governments to consult on policies that may impact municipal finances." Heck, as we saw recently, there's not even any requirement that the provincial government consult with city hall before tearing out bike lanes.
The report also points out that, while most other cities at least have some kind of revenue-sharing agreement where local government gets a share of income or sales tax, Toronto is limited to a small menu of potential taxes and fees, and mostly reliant on property taxes.
It's telling that none of the other big cities in the study had a governance model quite like Toronto. It's a governance model that just doesn't work.
I get that there might be a lot of eye-rolling over Matlow's motion. Convening a "program advisory body" hardly sounds like meaningful progress. And the notion that Ford, especially, would listen to anything the advisory body advises seems unlikely.
But unlikely things have happened before. During the 2023 mayoral byelection, a lot of people - including, um, me - rolled their eyes when former councillor Ana Bailão pledged to get Ford to take on the costs of maintaining the Gardiner Expressway. But just months after the election, Chow managed to get Ford to agree to do it.
Giving Toronto official "charter city" status and real autonomy will likely require a single-province constitutional amendment supported by both the House of Commons and the Ontario legislature. It's not an impossible idea, especially if you think long-term and accept there's no sustainable path to a better Toronto without a new governance model. It's not a question of if. It's a question of when. An advisory body to get city hall ready for when opportunity strikes is a sensible move - any small step toward inevitability is a small step worth taking.
CAPTION: For Mayor Olivia Chow and city hall, the ideal campaign promise from Queen's Park and Ottawa would be to free the city from the status quo.

Subject: Paramedics; Mayors; Local government; Budgets; Costs; Cities; Revenue sharing

Ford urged to expand probe of Humber board dispute: Former executives want examination of ministry's response to complaint

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-urged-expand-probe-humber-board-dispute/docview/3160302914/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+urged+to+expand+probe+of+Humber+board+dispute%3A+Former+executives+want+examination+of+ministry%27s+response+to+complaint&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Former Humber presidents, board chairs and a top donor, "alarmed about the risk of damage" to the Toronto polytechnic, are calling on Doug Ford's government to investigate whether the colleges ministry overstepped in a dispute that resulted in the mass resignation of board members.
Humber Polytechnic is currently facing a third-party review that was triggered, sources say, by tensions over governance matters between board governors and the president.
In the letter sent late Monday afternoon, and seen by the Star, ex-Humber executives urged that the review be thorough and fully independent, but also that its scope be broadened to look "at the ministry's role in affording due process to the college board in dealing with the third-party complaint that led to the governance review."
"Board resignations may have been avoided and a more positive resolution of issues achieved had consultation and discussion taken place prior to issuing directives on board conduct going into the review," the letter stated. "In the absence of transparency and explanation of the substance of complaints, board members were unable to execute their duties and responsibilities."
The letter was addressed to Nolan Quinn, the minister of colleges and universities, and cc'd Ford. Signatories included past Humber presidents Chris Whitaker and Robert Gordon, as well as former chairs Anthony Longo, Brien Gray, William Ardell, Robert Hull, Audrey McKinney and Ana Fernandes. It was also signed by Bob Barrett, whose family foundation donated $30 million to Humber in 2023 and whose name graces Humber's centre for technology innovation.
Nine board members tendered their resignations just over a week ago in the wake of a letter from the deputy minister of colleges and universities, who asked that the board refrain from proceeding with a performance review of Humber president and CEO Ann Marie Vaughan and to put any discussions of board recruitment or structure on hold while a "fact-finding exercise" into governance practices got underway.
David Wai's Jan. 14 letter, which the Star has seen, also directed members to refrain from in-camera meetings or from emailing intermediaries or staff, asking that all communication be exchanged directly between the board chair and CEO.
Sources told the Star that board members were left feeling they couldn't do their jobs. On Jan. 17, eight members including the chair and vice-chair stepped down, followed by the resignation of a Humber student representative on Jan. 21.
Seven members remain, including Vaughan, who was hired as president in 2022.
Wai first alerted the board in early December that concerns had been raised about governance practices at Humber and that the ministry was initiating a third-party review. The second letter in January provided more specifics, including the directive on how the board should conduct business while Linda Franklin, former president of Colleges Ontario, led a "fact-finding exercise to get an assessment of the current situation at Humber."
A "governance review exercise" would follow, wrote Wai, with an expectation of it all to be wrapped up by March 15.
It is unclear who made the request for government intervention. A source said board members were surprised by the move. The ministry told the Star that the concerns were brought forward by "external parties."
College boards help set an institution's strategic goals and are responsible for the hiring, evaluating, and if necessary, dismissal of college presidents. However, the province has oversight authority of colleges.
"We expect that post-secondary leaders put the best interests of students at the forefront of their work," ministry spokesperson Dayna Smockum said last week. "As the review gets underway, the board will continue to carry out their functions in accordance with their duties and their fiduciary responsibilities."
Last week, Humber spokesperson Andrew Leopold directed Star questions about the review to the ministry but added that the polytechnic welcomed and supported the inquiry and that the school was in the midst of appointing new board members.
"Humber's operations and campus life continue to flourish and remain unaffected, and our operations continue business as usual," he said.
Monday's letter demanded that the polytechnic postpone filling the board positions until the ministry's report is released.
"It is crucial that Humber College navigates this situation effectively to maintain its integrity and culture," the letter stated. "An institution with a reputation for excellence, best practice and model for innovation, we have benefited from stable, progressive governance and leadership for over 55 years. This is an institution that the province cannot afford to allow to degrade in any way."
More than 66,000 students attend Humber, one of the largest polytechnics in Canada.
CAPTION: Humber Polytechnic is currently facing a third-party review that was triggered, sources say, by tensions over governance matters between board governors and the president.MichelleMengsuChang Toronto Star
CREDIT: Janet Hurley Senior Writer

PCs urged to focus on tariff threat, not election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pcs-urged-focus-on-tariff-threat-not-election/docview/3160302901/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=PCs+urged+to+focus+on+tariff+threat%2C+not+election&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Labour leaders across Ontario are urging the Ford government to trash its election plans and launch a task force to deal with the impact of the looming U.S. tariff threat on workers.
Defending jobs "must be the premier's sole focus," Laura Walton, president of the Ontario Federation of Labour, said at a news conference Monday morning in Toronto.
"Throwing Ontario into an unnecessary snap election 15 months early - being premier of Ontario is not a part-time job at this critical moment in our history," she said. "In this crisis, it is more than a full-time job."
Premier Doug Ford plans to call an election that officially begins Wednesday, while still taking a leading role in fighting off tariffs that U.S. President Donald Trump has threatened to impose on Canada.
Ford is head of the Council of the Federation, which represents the country's premiers.
He has said he's not rushing into an early vote, but that he needs a strong mandate "from the people to fight against Donald Trump's tariffs, the attack he's coming against our families, our businesses, our communities."
But Walton - who made the announcement alongside leaders from the United Steelworkers, Ontario Nurses' Association, Elementary Teachers' Federation of Ontario, Ontario English Catholic Teachers' Association and the Ontario Secondary School Teachers' Association - said "the people of Ontario expect Doug Ford to put in some overtime if Trump makes good his threats of tariffs on Feb. 1" which he can't do if he's also trying to win an election.
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Document 466 of 500

Province vows funding to ease doctor shortage: Health minister also pledges $1.4 billion for expanded primary care health teams

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/province-vows-funding-ease-doctor-shortage/docview/3160302863/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province+vows+funding+to+ease+doctor+shortage%3A+Health+minister+also+pledges+%241.4+billion+for+expanded+primary+care+health+teams&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: On the eve of an early election, Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives are looking to fix a political vulnerability with a plan to get every Ontarian a family doctor within four years.
The pledge Monday from Health Minister Sylvia Jones includes $1.4 billion in new funding for expanded primary care health teams at a time when the government is under fire over a worsening doctor shortage.
"There's no doubt that people have been waiting a long time, too long, frankly, to get connected to a family care practitioner in their community," Jones acknowledged, saying two million residents of the province don't have a doctor now.
But the announcement quickly raised questions as to whether the money is enough to connect every resident of the province to a family physician by 2029. That's because there is a huge discrepancy between numbers provided by the Ontario Medical Association (OMA) and by Jones.
And opposition parties scoffed that the $1.4 billion earmarked for doctors is similar to what Ontario's independent Financial Accountability Office said is the actual cost for Ford to put beer, wine, cider and premixed cocktails into convenience stores a year early.
The OMA says there are 2.5 million Ontarians without a family doctor, a number forecast to reach 4.4 million by this time next year as doctors retire or leave the province and the population grows.
That's more than double what Jones is anticipating.
"Since 2018, for example, about 8,600 physicians have either retired or left their practices," the group representing doctors said in a statement calling for stronger action from the province. "For every three physicians added to the system, two are lost."
Jones disputed the OMA figures on the impact of the doctor shortage, saying her numbers are based on studies from organizations including the Canadian Institute for Health Information.
"I trust those numbers," she told reporters.
"We have 10 per cent of Ontario residents who are still looking for a primary care provider."
New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles said the "last minute" timing of the announcement from Jones before the Feb. 27 campaign officially begins Wednesday is telling.
"Doug Ford and the Conservatives have had seven years to fix the health crisis," Stiles told reporters in Brampton.
The plan appears to be an attempt to match Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie's promise to connect every Ontarian with a family doctor in four years.
That at least two million people do not have a family doctor well into the government's second term in office is shameful, Crombie told reporters Monday.
"Doug Ford has failed to prioritize your health care," she said, noting hundreds of people lined up in bitter cold and snow for hours last week to sign up for a new family doctor in Walkerton - a sign of how desperate people are to get a family physician.
Dr. Jane Philpott, a former federal Liberal health minister now director of the medical school at Queen's University, has been contracted by the provincial government to design a new primary care system building on a model being used in Kingston that connects patients with family doctors based on their postal codes, in a similar way to how children are assigned schools.
She began work Dec. 1.
"This plan is ambitious, and the changes will not happen overnight," Philpott, a family physician, told a news conference where she appeared beside Jones.
"We know there is a lot of work to do to recruit and retain the workforce needed ... this is just the beginning," she added, batting aside questions that she was being used as a political prop with an election campaign about to begin.
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Document 467 of 500

Premier's beer boondoggle tab could hardly have arrived at a worse time

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/premiers-beer-boondoggle-tab-could-hardly-have/docview/3160302799/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Premier%27s+beer+boondoggle+tab+could+hardly+have+arrived+at+a+worse+time&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford had it all figured out - until it fizzled out Monday, just days out from his big campaign launch.
The premier's best laid plans were to trigger an early election this Wednesday, the better to beat the bad news. But the big news beat him to it.
Busted. Over beer - and a lot more besides.
Ontario's financial watchdog has just called out Ford's scandalous wastefulness: Beer in corner stores could cost taxpayers an additional $612 million to satisfy the demands of big American and international brewers after his government raised its hand - and put its hands in our pockets - to renegotiate a signed agreement without good reason.
It is a devastating critique from an independent overseer of Ontario's finances, coming at the worst possible time for Ford. In his zeal to bring beer to corner stores early - ahead of this week's planned early election - the premier gave away the store.
And yet this boondoggle is about much more than beer money. It is also about missed opportunities and misplaced priorities - billions for beer in corner stores, but not enough funding for health care nor his promise of 1.5 million homes.
Now, as the governing Progressive Conservatives plunge us into an unwanted election, let us connect the dots before anyone marks their ballots on Feb. 27.
The additional spending - which the Financial Accountability Office says could reach $1.9 billion by 2030 - comes on top of $7 billion in annual hydro subsidies perpetuated by Ford to keep rates artificially low, another $3 billion in vote-buying rebate cheques and reckless overspending on Ontario Place. Not to mention as much as $175 million for an unnecessary early election.
Why starve hospitals and deprive patients of family physicians while pouring money down the drain for beer and wine? He was determined to outdo the previous Liberal government - which did the heavy lifting of allowing beer and wine into supermarkets - so that he could run a victory lap before hitting the campaign trail.
Now, we are all paying the price. And the big winner?
Donald Trump's corporate America - including big foreign-owned brewers - is laughing all the way to the bank at the expense of us suckers who are stuck holding the bag. Or the six-pack of beer.
On the eve of a potential tariff war, the Americans have won a windfall thanks to Ford's inability to negotiate a decent deal. So much for the art of the deal.
Last week, Ford mocked his opposition rivals, saying he couldn't imagine them negotiating on behalf of Ontario against the American president. As if the premier will somehow save the day after losing his shirt.
It all started with Ford's populist impulse in 2018 to connect with ordinary Ontarians by making beer a cornerstone of his campaign. Remember "buck a beer?"
Unable to deliver on that promise - brewers couldn't meet that price - the teetotalling premier was determined to deliver on beer in convenience stores at any cost, in hopes of neutralizing the issue. What makes Ford's financial transgressions so exasperating is that they were so unnecessary, for beer in corner stores was never a priority or emergency like a pandemic or tariff war.
Now, Ford's message to voters this week is that he needs the mother of all mandates, a massive majority unlike any before, in order to show the Americans that he means business. Which means less opposition scrutiny of his upside down agenda.
Ford had the past seven years to make good on his promises - on health care and housing, if not booze. Yet only on Monday, on the eve of an election, did his government come forward with a last-minute plan to give two million more Ontarians access to a family doctor within four years (in time for another election).
The pledge includes $1.4 billion in new funding for expanded primary care health teams, as proposed by Dr. Jane Philpott, head of the Queen's University medical school. It's worth noting that Philpott is a former federal Liberal health minister who was in talks with the provincial Liberals about running as a candidate before the government recruited her to help it plot a path forward.
Why keep Ford's rudderless Tories in power if they need prominent Liberals to help them navigate government? It's a question that will dog the premier as he clings to the pretext that this bizarrely early election is about defending Ontarians against Trump's tariff war, instead of focusing on the real priorities of voters.
Perhaps the PC campaign machine's calculations are correct. Certainly the latest polling from Abacus Data for the Star shows Ford with a seemingly unassailable lead.
But what if he's busted by more unseemly revelations - not just on beer boondoggles but police probes into the Greenbelt?
Elections are about stories that stick - and the first issue in the first week of the campaign is about Ford's lack of situational awareness.
Who would have thought that beer - the issue that defined Ford's first election campaign seven years ago - would come back to bite him in the behind while he's so far out in front?
CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION

Document 468 of 500

Rush to sell booze in corner stores cost additional $612M: Watchdog tallies price of Ford's move to hasten expansion

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/rush-sell-booze-corner-stores-cost-additional/docview/3160302783/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Rush+to+sell+booze+in+corner+stores+cost+additional+%24612M%3A+Watchdog+tallies+price+of+Ford%27s+move+to+hasten+expansion&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It cost taxpayers an additional $612 million so Premier Doug Ford could expand booze sales in time for his early Feb. 27 election, a fiscal watchdog has found.
In a new report, the Financial Accountability Office of Ontario estimates the liberalization tab will jump to $1.4 billion by the end of 2030 due to lower retail tax revenue for the LCBO, industry subsidies and other market changes.
Of that, "$817 million relates to the planned expansion of the beverage alcohol marketplace starting on Jan. 1, 2026, and $612 million relates to the decision to accelerate that expansion to begin in 2024," the independent office said.
The report comes one day before Ford is to visit Lt.-Gov. Edith Dumont for the drawing up of the writs for a campaign that officially begins Wednesday.
A new Abacus Data poll for the Star, conducted between Wednesday and Sunday, found the governing Tories at 47 per cent, ahead of the Liberals at 24 per cent, the NDP at 19 per cent and the Greens at seven per cent.
The early election will cost between $150 million and $175 million and coincides with $200 "rebate" cheques being mailed to 15 million Ontarians. That's a $3-billion expense for the treasury despite Ontario's $6.6-billion budget deficit.
Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy's office said the watchdog's report "confirms what we've been saying from the start, LCBO revenues will increase as it plays an important role as both a retailer and the exclusive wholesaler for all other retailers."
"Any potential drop in tax revenue is due to our government's deliberate decisions to reduce markups and remove fees, keeping the cost of beer and wine down for consumers," said Bethlenfalvy's spokesperson Colin Blachar.
"We were elected twice on a promise to deliver people with more choice and convenience, and to end one of the worst deals in the province's history that shut out local businesses in favour of large foreign corporations," said Blachar, adding the opposition Liberals "would rather protect the status quo that favoured big businesses over local convenience stores, and raise taxes and fees on beer and wine."
While the Progressive Conservative leader first promised liberalization of alcohol sales during the 2018 election, he only managed to keep that pledge last September when beer, wine, cider and pre-mixed cocktails were allowed to be sold in licensed convenience stores.
Most supermarkets and big box outlets were permitted to sell booze as of Oct. 31, although the provincially owned LCBO retains the monopoly on the sale of spirits such as whisky, rum, vodka and gin.
Last May, when Ford first began musing about calling an election well before the scheduled June 2026 vote, the government paid the privately run Beer Store $225 million to break the 10-year "master framework agreement" former Liberal premier Kathleen Wynne signed in 2015, which allowed wine and six-packs of beer to be sold in 450 supermarkets.
Even though that deal would have ended on Dec. 31, 2025 with minimal cost to taxpayers, Ford scrambled to deliver the promise earlier to give himself the flexibility to call a snap election.
In 2019, he was forced to retreat from an initial bid to unilaterally scrap the Beer Store accord after the United States Chamber of Commerce, the world's largest business organization, accused his government of "sending a negative signal to U.S. and other international investors about the business and investment climate in Ontario."
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie dismissed Ford's pre-election move as a "$1-billion booze boondoggle," and has questioned the need for an early election with U.S. President Donald Trump threatening 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian goods as of this Saturday.
"Doug Ford has been caught red-handed," Crombie said at Queen's Park. "Doug Ford has been lying about the true costs of this ridiculous scheme."
She added that money would have been better used to ease the doctor shortage or improve schools, and she suggested Ford, who insists he's the best person to deal with Trump, is a poor dealmaker.
"Doug Ford has the wrong priorities. Doug Ford has been focused on booze, bike lanes, big spas and his rich buddies, all because he thinks he can distract you with beer and pre-election cheques," she said.
New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles said the extra cost to push booze into corner stores early for Ford's own political convenience was a "terrible deal."
"Doug Ford likes to pretend he's some great negotiator, and he obviously can't get a good deal done," added Stiles, who pledged to remove tolls on Highway 407 if she wins next month's election. "He rushed it though so that he could get it done before the election."
Ford has said Trump's levies are the main reason he is going to the polls early.
But Crombie, Stiles and Green Leader Mike Schreiner say the Tory leader is pulling the plug early to get in front of the RCMP criminal probe of the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal.
Ford also hopes to take advantage of the fact Liberal activists may be too busy on the March 9 federal leadership race to help Crombie's provincial campaign.
Also, he wanted to go before a federal election expected this spring that public opinion polls suggest Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre could win.
Because Ontario voters traditionally elect different parties at Queen's Park and in Ottawa at the same time, a Poilievre victory might be bad for Ford.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie and Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau
