Ford government takes next step to remove Toronto bike lanes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-takes-next-step-remove-toronto/docview/3160302723/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+takes+next+step+to+remove+Toronto+bike+lanes&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is pedalling furiously toward removing bike lanes from "three of Toronto's most congested streets."
With an election campaign set to officially kick off Wednesday, Ford's Progressive Conservative government "has retained an engineering company to complete design work, as it prepares to reinstate vehicle lanes on Bloor Street West, Yonge Street and University Avenue," the province said.
"Our government has heard loud and clear from residents and businesses about the problems with gridlock. The failed approach of installing bike lanes without a second thought for drivers or local businesses is not working," said Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria.
"By freeing up some of Toronto's most important roads, we're bringing common sense back to municipal planning," Sarkaria said in one of his final acts before the government enters "caretaker mode" through the Feb. 27 election.
It is unclear when the bike lanes would actually be taken out.
Under Sarkaria's Bill 212, the Reducing Gridlock, Saving You Time Act, which received royal assent last month, Queen's Park now has the power to remove existing bike lanes and veto new ones the province feels would contribute to traffic jams.
The City of Toronto, which opposed the law, has estimated it would cost $48 million to dismantle $27 million in bike lane infrastructure, a claim Ford has dismissed as "hogwash."
"We're going to show them how to do it for a lot less and get traffic moving. We're going to keep bike riders safe, simple as that," he said in November.
Provincial taxpayers will foot the tab for taking out the lanes.
"One per cent of the population cannot take up 50 per cent of the roads, " Ford said in December when the bill passed, understating the number of Torontonians who cycle and overstating the amount of space bike lanes take up in the city.
The $6.2-million Transportation Tomorrow Survey found 3.1 per cent of Toronto residents commute to work by bicycle each morning, triple the number the premier often cites.
That jumps to 3.6 per cent of those who reported using their bikes "all day" in a city of three million people where 164,806 daily cycling trips were made.
However, that's far lower than some cycling advocates have argued in opposing the removal of bike lanes.
In comparison, 46 per cent of Torontonians drove their own cars or trucks in the morning with another 11 per cent being passengers in private vehicles and 25 per cent took public transit while 13 per cent walked.
The numbers change slightly when people were surveyed about "all day" modes of transportation: 50 per cent drove, 12 per cent were passengers, 22 per cent took transit, and 10 per cent walked.
Co-ordinated by the Ministry of Transportation in partnership with 24 municipalities and transit agencies, including the City of Toronto, the TTC and Metrolinx, the exhaustive survey is normally conducted every five years and involves thousands of interviews.
Sarkaria's bill indemnifies the province against any lawsuits from people or their families from suing if someone is killed or injured on a road where a bike lane is taken out.
While the new law affects cities across Ontario, Ford has acknowledged the most vexing bike lane to him is on the western stretch of Bloor West near his Etobicoke home.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and Green Leader Mike Schreiner opposed his bill.
CAPTION: A bike lane on University Avenue in Toronto is shown on Nov. 8, 2024.

Document 470 of 500

Doug Ford's $612M beer boondoggle tab could hardly have arrived at a worse time for him

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-612m-beer-boondoggle-tab-could-hardly/docview/3160636617/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+%24612M+beer+boondoggle+tab+could+hardly+have+arrived+at+a+worse+time+for+him&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford had it all figured out - until it fizzled out Monday, just days out from his big campaign launch.
The premier's best laid plans were to trigger an early election this Wednesday, the better to beat the bad news. But the big news beat him to it.
Busted. Over beer - and a lot more besides.
Ontario's financial watchdog has just called out Ford's scandalous wastefulness: Beer in corner stores could cost taxpayers an additional $612 million to satisfy the demands of big American and international brewers after his government raised its hand - and put its hands in our pockets - to renegotiate a signed agreement without good reason.
It is a devastating critique from an independent overseer of Ontario's finances, coming at the worst possible time for Ford. In his zeal to bring beer to corner stores early - ahead of this week's planned early election - the premier gave away the store.
And yet this boondoggle is about much more than beer money. It is also about missed opportunities and misplaced priorities - billions for beer in corner stores, but not enough funding for health care nor his promise of 1.5 million homes.
Now, as the governing Progressive Conservatives plunge us into an unwanted election, let us connect the dots before anyone marks their ballots on Feb. 27.
The additional spending - which the Financial Accountability Office says could reach $1.9 billion by 2030 - comes on top of $7 billion in annual hydro subsidies perpetuated by Ford to keep rates artificially low, another $3 billion in vote-buying rebate cheques and reckless overspending on Ontario Place. Not to mention as much as $175 million for an unnecessary early election.
Why starve hospitals and deprive patients of family physicians while pouring money down the drain for beer and wine? He was determined to outdo the previous Liberal government - which did the heavy lifting of allowing beer and wine into supermarkets - so that he could run a victory lap before hitting the campaign trail.
Now, we are all paying the price. And the big winner?
Donald Trump's corporate America - including big foreign-owned brewers - is laughing all the way to the bank at the expense of us suckers who are stuck holding the bag. Or the six-pack of beer.
On the eve of a potential tariff war, the Americans have won a windfall thanks to Ford's inability to negotiate a decent deal. So much for the art of the deal.
Last week, Ford mocked his opposition rivals, saying he couldn't imagine them negotiating on behalf of Ontario against the American president. As if the premier will somehow save the day after losing his shirt.
It all started with Ford's populist impulse in 2018 to connect with ordinary Ontarians by making beer a cornerstone of his campaign. Remember "buck a beer?"
Unable to deliver on that promise - brewers couldn't meet that price - the teetotalling premier was determined to deliver on beer in convenience stores at any cost, in hopes of neutralizing the issue. What makes Ford's financial transgressions so exasperating is that they were so unnecessary, for beer in corner stores was never a priority or emergency like a pandemic or tariff war.
Now, Ford's message to voters this week is that he needs the mother of all mandates, a massive majority unlike any before, in order to show the Americans that he means business. Which means less opposition scrutiny of his upside down agenda.
Ford had the past seven years to make good on his promises - on health care and housing, if not booze. Yet only on Monday, on the eve of an election, did his government come forward with a last-minute plan to give two million more Ontarians access to a family doctor within four years (in time for another election).
The pledge includes $1.4 billion in new funding for expanded primary care health teams, as proposed by Dr. Jane Philpott, head of the Queen's University medical school. It's worth noting that Philpott is a former federal Liberal health minister who was in talks with the provincial Liberals about running as a candidate before the government recruited her to help it plot a path forward.
Why keep Ford's rudderless Tories in power if they need prominent Liberals to help them navigate government? It's a question that will dog the premier as he clings to the pretext that this bizarrely early election is about defending Ontarians against Trump's tariff war, instead of focusing on the real priorities of voters.
Perhaps the PC campaign machine's calculations are correct. Certainly the latest polling from Abacus Data for the Star shows Ford with a seemingly unassailable lead.
But what if he's busted by more unseemly revelations - not just on beer boondoggles but police probes into the Greenbelt?
Elections are about stories that stick - and the first issue in the first week of the campaign is about Ford's lack of situational awareness. Who would have thought that beer - the issue that defined Ford's first election campaign seven years ago - would come back to bite him in the behind while he's so far out in front?
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford lifts packs of beer onto a shelf at a Costco store on Islington Avenue in Toronto on Oct. 31, 2024.

Former Humber Polytechnic executives call on Doug Ford's government to expand probe of board dispute

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/former-humber-polytechnic-executives-call-on-doug/docview/3160636570/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Former+Humber+Polytechnic+executives+call+on+Doug+Ford%27s+government+to+expand+probe+of+board+dispute&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Former Humber presidents, board chairs and a top donor, "alarmed about the risk of damage" to the Toronto polytechnic, are calling on Doug Ford's government to investigate whether the colleges ministry overstepped in a dispute that resulted in the mass resignation of board members.
Humber Polytechnic is currently facing a third-party review that was triggered, sources say, by tensions over governance matters between board governors and the president.
In the letter sent late Monday afternoon, and seen by the Star, ex-Humber executives urged that the review be thorough and fully independent, but also that its scope be broadened to look "at the ministry's role in affording due process to the college board in dealing with the third-party complaint that led to the governance review."
"Board resignations may have been avoided and a more positive resolution of issues achieved had consultation and discussion taken place prior to issuing directives on board conduct going into the review," the letter stated. "In the absence of transparency and explanation of the substance of complaints, board members were unable to execute their duties and responsibilities."
The letter was addressed to Nolan Quinn, the minister of colleges and universities, and cc'd Ford. Signatories included past Humber presidents Chris Whitaker and Robert Gordon, as well as former chairs Anthony Longo, Brien Gray, William Ardell, Robert Hull, Audrey McKinney and Ana Fernandes. It was also signed by Bob Barrett, whose family foundation donated $30 million to Humber in 2023 and whose name graces Humber's centre for technology innovation.
Nine board members tendered their resignations just over a week ago in the wake of a letter from the deputy minister of colleges and universities, who asked that the board refrain from proceeding with a performance review of Humber president and CEO Ann Marie Vaughan and to put any discussions of board recruitment or structure on hold while a "fact-finding exercise" into governance practices got underway.
David Wai's Jan. 14 letter, which the Star has seen, also directed members to refrain from in-camera meetings or from emailing intermediaries or staff, asking that all communication be exchanged directly between the board chair and CEO.
Sources told the Star that board members were left feeling they couldn't do their jobs. On Jan. 17, eight members including the chair and vice-chair stepped down, followed by the resignation of a Humber student representative on Jan. 21.
Seven members remain, including Vaughan, who was hired as president in 2022.
Wai first alerted the board in early December that concerns had been raised about governance practices at Humber and that the ministry was initiating a third-party review. The second letter in January provided more specifics, including the directive on how the board should conduct business while Linda Franklin, former president of Colleges Ontario, led a "fact-finding exercise to get an assessment of the current situation at Humber." A "governance review exercise" would follow, wrote Wai, with an expectation of it all to be wrapped up by March 15.
It is unclear who made the request for government intervention. A source said board members were surprised by the move. The ministry told the Star that the concerns were brought forward by "external parties."
College boards help set an institution's strategic goals and are responsible for the hiring, evaluating, and if necessary, dismissal of college presidents. However, the province has oversight authority of colleges.
"We expect that post-secondary leaders put the best interests of students at the forefront of their work," ministry spokesperson Dayna Smockum said last week. "As the review gets underway, the board will continue to carry out their functions in accordance with their duties and their fiduciary responsibilities."
Last week, Humber spokesperson Andrew Leopold directed Star questions about the review to the ministry but added that the polytechnic welcomed and supported the inquiry and that the school was in the midst of appointing new board members. "Humber's operations and campus life continue to flourish and remain unaffected, and our operations continue business as usual," he said.
Monday's letter demanded that the polytechnic postpone filling the board positions until the ministry's report is released.
"It is crucial that Humber College navigates this situation effectively to maintain its integrity and culture," the letter stated. "An institution with a reputation for excellence, best practice and model for innovation, we have benefited from stable, progressive governance and leadership for over 55 years. This is an institution that the province cannot afford to allow to degrade in any way."
More than 66,000 students attend Humber, one of the largest polytechnics in Canada.
CAPTION: Monday's letter was signed by past Humber presidents as well as former board chairs.

Document 472 of 500

'We are facing insolvency.' Canada Post CEO tells first union contract hearing financial situation is 'not sustainable'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/we-are-facing-insolvency-canada-post-ceo-tells/docview/3160636482/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27We+are+facing+insolvency.%27+Canada+Post+CEO+tells+first+union+contract+hearing+financial+situation+is+%27not+sustainable%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Just days after Canada Post parted ways with their chief financial officer and two other senior executives, the company's leaders sounded the alarm on their financial situation in the first public hearing before the federal Industrial Inquiry Commission (IIC).
Doug Ettinger, the company's president and CEO, described the current situation as an "edge-of-the-cliff juncture." The Crown corporation is losing ground to its various competitors, he said, leaving them in financial dire straits in the aftermath of the five-week national postal strike.
"We are literally driving a 1967 Ford in a Formula One race in 2025," Ettinger said of the company's decline in letter and parcel volume. "This is simply - and I'll underline this - not sustainable and we are facing insolvency as a result."
The commission, appointed by Federal Labour Minister Steven MacKinnon, will have until May to probe potential ways to reach a new contract agreement between the country's national postal service and the 55,000-worker union.
Until then, the strike, which began on Nov. 15, will be on pause and workers and management will operate under the terms of the existing contract, which expired more than a year ago.
In remarks before the Ottawa-based commission, newly installed CFO Rindala El-Hage said that the company was projected to "run out of cash" by the end of July 2025 and that its losses are expected to increase, a projection that didn't include the strike. Last week, the federal government extended a $1.034 billion loan to Canada Post to "maintain its solvency and ensure it can continue its operations."
"The status quo is not an option," El-Hage said.
In its response, the Canadian Union of Postal Workers (CUPW) accused Canada Post of union-busting and said that the company is "trying to use its financial struggles as a pretext to implement drastic service cutbacks."
"In the past, the corporation has made inaccurate financial projections and misrepresented its financial condition to justify service cuts and demand concessions from the union in bargaining," CUPW president Jan Simpson said before the commission.
Ettinger said that the federal government's loan serves only as a stopgap.
"It is a temporary loan to ensure we have the money on hand to keep the lights on, continue operating, and pay everyone's wages," Ettinger said. "This is clearly not a long-term fix. It is a liability we are expected to pay back. It just buys us time."
The commission, which is being led by mediator and arbitrator William Kaplan, will have subsequent hearings in February and March before presenting a report to the labour minister by May 15.
Throughout the five-hour hearing, Kaplan pressed both sides on their plans to resolve the labour dispute and secure longer term financial viability for the postal service.
Last week, the Star reported that Canada Post "completed a reorganization" of its senior leadership team that resulted in the elimination of five senior executive positions and the departure of their chief financial officer.
In a memo to employees obtained by the Star, Ettinger wrote that the changes would "enhance decision-making by reducing layers and bringing new, strategic perspectives to the table."
"These decisions were not easy, but they reflect the financial realities we face as a corporation and are effective immediately," Ettinger wrote.
By the weekend, Canada Post had removed the headshots and biographies of the executives they had let go from their website.
With files from Josh Rubin and The Canadian Press
CAPTION: In remarks before an Ottawa-based commission, Canada Post's CEO said that their current trajectory is not sustainable and that the Crown corporation is facing insolvency.

Doug Ford has strong support as election call looms, survey suggests

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-has-strong-support-as-election-call/docview/3160636408/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+has+strong+support+as+election+call+looms%2C+survey+suggests&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives are in the poll position as the governing party revs up for a snap election call Wednesday, a new public opinion survey suggests.
The Abacus Data poll for the Star found Ford's Tories at 47 per cent support, far ahead of Bonnie Crombie's Liberals at 24 per cent, Marit Stiles's New Democrats at 19 per cent and Mike Schreiner's Greens at seven per cent.
"You can't imagine a better time to be calling an election if this is the polling that they're seeing as well," said Abacus president David Coletto, referring to the PC's internal research.
"It lays out the case why the Tories feel like now's a good time for them to go."
The election campaign officially begins Wednesday, but Ford has been in election-readiness mode since last May, even though the next scheduled vote was slated for June 2026.
Abacus surveyed 1,021 Ontarians from last Wednesday through Sunday using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 3.1 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
The firm asked respondents for their feelings about an early election, which would cost at least $150 million.
Nearly half of all Ontarians - 48 per cent - were "unhappy" or "very unhappy" at the expenditure, with 20 per cent "happy" or "very happy" and 32 per cent neutral.
When the cost of the election is not factored in, only 24 per cent were "unhappy" or "very unhappy," while 34 per cent were "happy" or "very happy" with a snap election and 42 per cent neutral.
Coletto said that suggests many voters believe Ford is being opportunistic to seek another four-year term some 15 months early.
"They don't love it, but is that enough to not vote for him? That is going to be the question," he said.
When it comes to the issues that voters wanted debated during the campaign, 60 per cent said "reducing the cost of living" was top of mind.
Forty per cent said "improving the health-care system overall," with 38 per cent saying "making housing more affordable," 32 per cent saying "dealing with the impact of Donald Trump's tariffs," 26 per cent saying "growing the economy and creating good jobs" and 23 per cent saying "reducing homelessness and poverty."
Coletto said that indicates "Ford is vulnerable on health care."
"If this becomes a health-care election, which I don't think it will be, but if it does, that could be the ticket to a better outcome" for Crombie and Stiles, he said.
"The problem, again, remains they are splitting (the anti-Ford) vote."
In terms of opinions on the party leaders, 41 per cent had a positive impression of Ford while 37 per cent had a negative view, with 21 per cent neutral and two per cent didn't know enough to say. That's a net impression rating for him of plus four per cent.
Impressions of Stiles were 28 per cent positive, with 28 per cent negative, 25 per cent neutral and 19 per cent unsure, for an even net rating.
The impressions of Crombie, the subject of millions of dollars of Tory attack ads for more than a year, were 26 per cent positive, 34 per cent negative, 25 per cent neutral and 14 per cent unsure, for a minus eight per cent net rating.
Impressions of Schreiner were 23 per cent positive, 22 per cent negative, 31 per cent neutral and 24 per cent unsure, for a net rating of plus one per cent.
Some 42 per cent approve of Ford's two-term PC government, while 34 per cent disapproved and 19 per cent were neutral and four per cent didn't know. That's an overall plus eight per cent approval, the highest Abacus has recorded for the administration.
"It's the first time since we've been tracking it that he's gotten above water," Coletto said.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford is shown in Toronto on Jan. 13, 2025.

Subject: Negative campaigning; Voters; Public opinion surveys

Business indexing term: Subject: Public opinion surveys

Document 474 of 500

It's been 8 years since the Quebec mosque shooting. Fear can't be the new normal

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/been-8-years-since-quebec-mosque-shooting-fear/docview/3160049536/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=It%27s+been+8+years+since+the+Quebec+mosque+shooting.+Fear+can%27t+be+the+new+normal&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Last month, I had a conversation with Mohamed Labidi, the president of Quebec City's Centre Culturel Islamique de Quebec, about how his community has dealt with the violent Islamophobia they've experienced over the years. A few minutes into the chat, we landed on the topic of mosque safety.
He stopped mid-sentence to show me a small piece of blue plastic.
"See this?" asked Labidi, "We have over 4,000 of these. These are actually keys."
The chips are key cards for congregants who want to enter the CCIQ. The mosque serves thousands of worshippers, hence thousands of chips. It's the only way to get in, no exceptions.
It wasn't always like this. Mosques generally remain open for worshippers, neighbours, and the curious. But on the night of Jan. 29, 2017, a 27-year-old man changed that for many Quebec Muslims. After consuming large amounts of Islamophobic and anti-refugee content online, the man stepped into the CCIQ with a 9-mm Glock and opened fire. Six people died, 19 were injured.
After that, the CCIQ chose to keep their doors locked except for those with a blue chip.
Years after that tragic night, an Islamophobic attack in London, Ont. killed four members of a Muslim family. These incidents shook the nation.
A memorial was erected outside the CCIQ entrance. The federal government made moves to support security for religious buildings. Prime Minister Justin Trudeau gave speeches in both Quebec City and London.
Canadians seemed awakened to the problem of violent Islamophobia. Yet today, the overall situation in both places looks worse than ever.
In 2018, following the mass shooting, Quebec elected Premier François Legault. The following year, his government passed Bill 21 which bars public employees from wearing religious symbols like hijabs and kippahs at work, a violation of Canadians' religious freedoms which disproportionately impacts Muslims. Last month, the premier threatened to ban prayers in public areas, like parks. Legault has even said that he'll work to strengthen this exclusionary brand of "secularism" in Quebec to send a "very clear message to Islamists," a direct smear against Quebec's Muslims. Labidi told me he knows at least 50 Muslim families that have recently left Quebec.
Unfortunately, London, Ont. has also remained a troubling centre point for Islamophobic incidents. I recently had a long conversation with a relative of the family that perished in the 2021 attack. A psychotherapist, the relative said she received an inquiry from a person who tried to be her client. They sent hateful messages, mocking her family and those who were killed.
A few weeks ago, a Muslim man was attacked in London's Ivey Park while with his family. The attacker spewed Islamophobic and racist comments at them. Two Muslim women were attacked in front of their children by a man with a knife who yelled hateful, anti-Palestinian remarks. And a home that erected lawn signs calling for peace in the Gaza Strip had the placards destroyed. The suspect later set part of their house on fire.
In March 2022, the Ontario NDP introduced the Our London Family Act. It is meant to address systemic Islamophobia through legislation for the first time in Ontario. It has still not been passed.
Meanwhile, those whose lives were touched by tragedy are tired of looking over their shoulders. They're tired of having more meetings about whether to redesign the mosque entrance just in case someone tries to ram a car into it. They are calling for structural changes that enshrine the protection of their communities into law.
Thousands of Muslims in Quebec must carry around a key to get into their mosque. They're too scared to keep the doors open. Thousands more Canadians are wearing a piece of green felt this month as part of the national Green Square Campaign to commemorate those we lost on Jan. 29, 2017.
In a country known for multicultural acceptance, people are walking around in fear.
They can't afford to move on. They need leaders to step up.
That means passing the Our London Family Act. It means repealing Bill 21.
Eight years after the worst anti-Muslim hate crime in North American history, we are running out of excuses.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, middle left to right, Quebec City mayor Regis Labeaume and Quebec Premier Philippe Couillard march with a banner during a vigil to commemorate the one-year anniversary of the Quebec City mosque shooting in Quebec City, Monday, Jan. 29, 2018.After the Quebec mosque shooting, Canadians seemed awakened to the problem of violent Islamophobia. Yet today, the overall situation in both places looks worse than ever.
CREDIT: Steven Zhou, Contributor

Subject: Canadians; Shootings; Mass murders; Islamophobia; Muslims; Xenophobia; Prime ministers; Mosques & temples

Doug Ford's alcohol sales expansion deal cost taxpayers an additional $612M, watchdog says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-alcohol-sales-expansion-deal-cost/docview/3160049418/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+alcohol+sales+expansion+deal+cost+taxpayers+an+additional+%24612M%2C+watchdog+says&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It cost taxpayers an additional $612 million so Premier Doug Ford could expand booze sales in time for his early Feb. 27 election, a fiscal watchdog has found.
In a new report, the Financial Accountability Office of Ontario estimates the liberalization tab will jump to $1.4 billion by the end of 2030 due to lower retail tax revenue for the LCBO, industry subsidies and other market changes.
Of that, "$817 million relates to the planned expansion of the beverage alcohol marketplace starting on Jan. 1, 2026, and $612 million relates to the decision to accelerate that expansion to begin in 2024," the independent office said.
The report comes one day before Ford is to visit Lt.-Gov. Edith Dumont for the drawing up of the writs for a campaign that officially begins Wednesday.
A new Abacus Data poll for the Star, conducted between Wednesday and Sunday, found the governing Tories at 47 per cent, ahead of the Liberals at 24 per cent, the NDP at 19 per cent and the Greens at seven per cent.
The early election will cost between $150 million and $175 million and coincides with $200 "rebate" cheques being mailed to 15 million Ontarians. That's a $3 billion expense for the treasury despite Ontario's $6.6-billion budget deficit.
Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy's office said the FAO "report confirms what we've been saying from the start, LCBO revenues will increase as it plays an important role as both a retailer and the exclusive wholesaler for all other retailers."
"Any potential drop in tax revenue is due to our government's deliberate decisions to reduce markups and remove fees, keeping the cost of beer and wine down for consumers," said Bethlenfalvy's spokesman Colin Blachar.
"We were elected twice on a promise to deliver people with more choice and convenience, and to end one of the worst deals in the province's history that shut out local businesses in favour of large foreign corporations," said Blachar, adding the opposition Liberals "would rather protect the status quo that favoured big businesses over local convenience stores, and raise taxes and fees on beer and wine."
While the Progressive Conservative leader first promised liberalization of alcohol sales during the 2018 election, he only managed to keep that pledge last September when beer, wine, cider and premixed cocktails were allowed to be sold in licensed convenience stores.
Most supermarkets and big box outlets were permitted to sell booze as of Oct. 31, although the provincially owned Liquor Control Board of Ontario retains the monopoly on the sale of spirits such as whisky, rum, vodka and gin.
Last May, when Ford first began musing about calling an election well before the scheduled June 2026 vote, the government paid the privately run Beer Store $225 million to break the 10-year "master framework agreement" former Liberal premier Kathleen Wynne signed in 2015, which allowed wine and six-packs of beer to be sold in 450 supermarkets.
Even though that deal would have ended on Dec. 31, 2025 with minimal cost to taxpayers, Ford scrambled to deliver the promise earlier to give himself the flexibility to call a snap election.
In 2019, he was forced to retreat from an initial bid to unilaterally scrap the Beer Store accord after the United States Chamber of Commerce, the world's largest business organization, accused his government of "sending a negative signal to U.S. and other international investors about the business and investment climate in Ontario."
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie dismissed Ford's pre-election move as a "$1-billion booze boondoggle," and has questioned the need for an early election with U.S. President Donald Trump threatening 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian goods as of this Saturday.
"Doug Ford has been caught red-handed," Crombie said at Queen's Park. "Doug Ford has been lying about the true costs of this ridiculous scheme."
She added that money would have been better used to ease the doctor shortage or improve schools and suggests Ford, who insists he's the best person to deal with Trump, is a poor dealmaker.
"Doug Ford has the wrong priorities. Doug Ford has been focused on booze, bike lanes, big spas and his rich buddies, all because he thinks he can distract you with beer and pre-election cheques," she said.
New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles said the extra cost to push booze into corner stores early for Ford's own political convenience was a "terrible deal."
"Doug Ford likes to pretend he's some great negotiator, and he obviously can't get a good deal done," added Stiles, who pledged to remove tolls on Highway 407 if she wins next month's election. "He rushed it though so that he could get it done before the election."
Ford has said Trump's levies are the main reason he is going to the polls early.
But Crombie, Stiles and Green Leader Mike Schreiner say the Tory leader is pulling the plug early to get in front of the RCMP criminal probe of the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal.
Ford also hopes to to take advantage of the fact Liberal activists may be too busy on the March 9 federal leadership race to help Crombie's provincial campaign.
Also, he wanted to go before a federal election expected this spring that public-opinion polls suggest Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre could win.
Because Ontario voters traditionally elect different parties at Queen's Park and in Ottawa at the same time, a Poilievre victory might be bad for Ford.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford arrives for a first ministers meeting in Ottawa on Jan. 15.

Subject: Political campaigns; Leadership; Alcohol; Sales; Beer; Liquor; Liberalization; Supermarkets

Document 476 of 500

Doug Ford government pledges more money to ease doctor shortage on eve of election call

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-government-pledges-more-money-ease/docview/3160049296/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+government+pledges+more+money+to+ease+doctor+shortage+on+eve+of+election+call&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: On the eve of an early election, Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives are looking to fix a political vulnerability with a plan to get every Ontarian a family doctor within four years.
The pledge Monday from Health Minister Sylvia Jones includes $1.4 billion in new funding for expanded primary care health teams at a time when the government is under fire over a worsening doctor shortage.
"There's no doubt that people have been waiting a long time, too long, frankly, to get connected to a family care practitioner in their community," Jones acknowledged, saying two million residents of the province don't have a doctor now.
But the announcement quickly raised questions as to whether the money is enough to connect every resident of the province to a family physician by 2029. That's because there is a huge discrepancy between numbers provided by the Ontario Medical Association and by Jones.
And opposition parties scoffed that the $1.4 billion earmarked for doctors is similar to what Ontario's independent Financial Accountability Office said is the actual cost for Ford to put beer, wine, cider and premixed cocktails into convenience stores a year early.
The Ontario Medical Association (OMA) says there are 2.5 million Ontarians without a family doctor, a number forecast to reach 4.4 million by this time next year as doctors retire or leave the province and the population grows.
That's more than double what Jones is anticipating.
"Since 2018, for example, about 8,600 physicians have either retired or left their practices," the group representing doctors said in a statement calling for stronger action from the province. "For every three physicians added to the system, two are lost."
Jones disputed the OMA figures on the impact of the doctor shortage, saying her numbers are based on studies from organizations including the Canadian Institute for Health Information.
"I trust those numbers," she told reporters. "We have 10 per cent of Ontario residents who are still looking for a primary care provider."
New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles said the "last minute" timing of the announcement from Jones before the Feb. 27 campaign officially begins Wednesday is telling.
"Doug Ford and the Conservatives have had seven years to fix the health crisis," Stiles told reporters in Brampton.
"The number of people without a family doctor has just gone through the roof," she added. "Doug Ford and the Conservatives have made the problem worse, not better."
The plan appears to be an attempt to match Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie's promise to connect every Ontarian with a family doctor in four years.
That at least two million people do not have a family doctor well into the government's second term in office is shameful, Crombie told reporters Monday.
"Doug Ford has failed to prioritize your health care," she said, noting hundreds of people lined up in bitter cold and snow for hours last week to sign up for a new family doctor in Walkerton - a sign of how desperate people are to get a family physician.
Dr. Jane Philpott, a former federal Liberal health minister now director of the medical school at Queen's University, has been contracted by the provincial government to design a new primary care system building on a model being used in Kingston that connects patients with family doctors based on their postal codes, in a similar way to how children are assigned schools.
She began work Dec. 1.
"This plan is ambitious, and the changes will not happen overnight," Philpott, a family physician, told a news conference where she appeared beside Jones.
"We know there is a lot of work to do to recruit and retain the workforce needed...this is just the beginning," she added, batting aside questions that she was being used as a political prop with an election campaign about to begin.
"There's nothing political about the work going on here," Philpott said. "I will continue to serve at the discretion of whatever government is in place."
CAPTION: Health Minister Sylvia Jones listens to questions from reporters following a press conference in Etobicoke on Jan. 11.

Subject: Primary care

Title: Doug Ford government pledges more money to ease doctor shortage on eve of election call

Ford more years?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-more-years/docview/3160049292/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+more+years%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford tells his ministers to fast-track any announcements with Feb. 27 election call just days away, Jan. 23
With an Ontario election looming, it's time to reflect on what has gone on under Doug Ford's premiership. Anybody been to the hospital recently? How were the wait times? I hear ORs at SickKids are not able to function properly due to a lack of nurses. Anybody have a kid in public school or at an Ontario college? Given the chronic lack of funding during Ford's tenure, how's that working out ? Have you been to the grocery store? We know consumers are being gouged, but has Ford stepped in to help us? Instead of listening to voters, Ford pushed through with the closure of the Ontario Science Centre, and he plans to add a foreign-owned luxury spa to Ontario Place. People need to recognize that other leaders are as well suited to talk business with Trump, or even better. Ford is not the people's premier. He's in it to make himself and his buddies rich at the expense of public education, health care, and affordability. He's not our guy.
Jennifer Chambers, Toronto
Dear Doug Ford: do not call an early election. Ontario is facing a threat of epic proportions in President Donald Trump. You already have a mandate to deal with him. Aware of the seriousness of the current situation, the opposition parties have pledged to work with you. Do not get distracted. This is not the time for crass, self-serving politicking. A real Captain Canada wouldn't abandon the fight just as it was about to begin.
Trish Plant, Toronto
Ford's plan to announce a provincial election on January 29 is a sham. He is again wasting taxpayers' money to keep himself in power, and he is clearly using Trump's tariff threats as a pretext for doing so. Ford already has a clear majority to do as he and his party see fit. Why does he need a "clear mandate" to address potential American tariffs? I feel that this just an excuse to deflect attention away from the issues Ford is facing. These include the RCMP investigation into the Greenbelt scandal; the underfunding of health care, education, and social programs; the closing of safe-injection sites; and delays to the Ontario Line and the Eglinton Crosstown LRT. Ford may get a clear mandate to fight the tariffs, but that will take his government away from dealing with issues that are more relevant to Ontarians.
Joe Bortolussi, Toronto
The election Ford craves will reportedly cost between $150 million and $175 million. If you include the total cost of the $200 buy-a-vote cheques that the government is sending out now - which will add about $3.2 billion to the deficit and thus the total debt - the election will really cost nearly $3.4 billion. Last election, about 4.7 million votes were cast. To allow for some population growth, let's estimate that maybe 5 million votes will be cast. That would make this the most expensive election ever, at about $700 per vote.
My cheque, labelled the "Ontario Taxpayer Rebate," arrived today. It will be pinned to the wall and never cashed so that I will not be responsible for having added to the debt that Ford is using his existing strong mandate to create. Goodness knows how much more debt he can create with a "very" strong mandate. Curiously, the blurb attached to the cheque says, "to help with the costs of the federal carbon tax and interest rates, the Ontario government is putting money back into peoples' pockets by providing a tax-free, $200 rebate...." I already get a much, much better rebate on the federal carbon price from the federal government.
Say, wasn't it Doug Ford who, during his first strong mandate, scrapped the cap-and-trade plan that existed when he took office and thus forced the federal carbon tax on the people of Ontario? Can we afford any more mandates for Doug Ford?
Graeme Elliott, Toronto
Pride flag not anti-Catholic
'This is a culture war': GTA school board upholds Pride flag policy amid fierce debate, Jan. 22of
School boards such as the publicly funded Dufferin-Peel Catholic District School Board should be more than ashamed to deny any their pupils not only their rights but also their dignity. Displaying a Pride flag does not mean endorsing conduct contrary to Catholic teachings. All schools must offer lessons of tolerance and acceptance. Stereotypes underlie hate. I have a right to be different and to be accepted as different. I am happy to be Roman Catholic - I just want to be myself. To quote Article 1 of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights, "all human beings are born free and equal in dignity and rights".
Marvin Zuker, Toronto
Giving seniors a choice
Smaller nursing homes are cropping up across Canada. While experts say they are better for residents and staff, why is Ontario going another route? Jan. 24
The National Institute on Aging report on small homes misses the mark in not going far enough to advocate for nonprofit, neighbourhood-based homes small enough (no more than six people) to safely deliver emotion-focused care. These kinds of residential options are available to every other disability group in the province - except seniors, the only group besides convicted felons that's still mass-institutionalized.
Older adults should be consulted on their residential care preferences, and these need to be reflected in research. Seniors are not interested in ending up in 14-bed "small homes" that are not small, are located on long-term-care grounds, or are in age-segregated "neighbourhoods." What we want is to not have to leave our own communities to receive care.
Patricia Spindel, Ajax
Make it easy to buy Canadian
More cracks showing in Canada's response to Donald Trump's tariff threat, Jan. 22
In the face of President Donald Trump's tariff threats, Canada is struggling to formulate a unified and compelling response. Yet there's been a recent upsurge in patriotism in this country, as well as an overwhelming rejection of Trump's idea that Canada become a U.S. state. How can individual Canadians make a difference? By buying Canadian products instead of American ones, wherever the choice to do so exists.
How can we make this easy on consumers? Why not stick a maple leaf on products that are made in Canada? We should ask or require Canadian producers and manufacturers to put such an icon on all packaging materials, just the way there used to be a Good Housekeeping Seal of Approval.
Jim Thomas, Toronto

Elephants are not people under the law, the Colorado Supreme Court says. But they still deserve rights, Jan. 22
My cat would like to express her appreciation for Vinay Menon's column advocating for the rights of animals. But she also says she is quite distressed that no politician ever seems to mention the global mass extinction currently underway at the hands of humans. What will become of us? she asks.
Raphael Vigod, Toronto

Document 478 of 500

The city says funding for new Line 2 subway cars is a done deal. But with a federal election looming, others aren't so sure

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/city-says-funding-new-line-2-subway-cars-is-done/docview/3159971294/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+city+says+funding+for+new+Line+2+subway+cars+is+a+done+deal.+But+with+a+federal+election+looming%2C+others+aren%27t+so+sure&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With a spring federal election looming, advocates and city politicians are voicing concern about what a change in government might mean for recently promised funding for Toronto's much-needed Line 2 subway cars.
Inquiries made to all levels of government by the Star suggest that the promised funding is not yet set in stone, raising the spectre of changing political fortunes somehow pulling the plug on the transit project - as history has shown can happen.
Despite the federal government's public pledge in November to help buy the cars, the dollars are not officially committed until the city's request is signed. While officials said they see no obstacle to that, with no dollars set to flow until 2026 they acknowledge there are no guarantees should there be a change in government, something current polls suggest is likely.
Mayor Olivia Chow has urged the Liberal government to get the money flowing as soon as possible. The government isn't budging on the timeline, however, saying Toronto and other cities promised funds have been told they won't "be made available for disbursement starting in fiscal year 2026-27. The Government of Canada has made this timeline clear to all of its partners throughout the application process."
A need for new cars
The 26-kilometre Line 2, which opened in 1966 and is today responsible for more than 400,000 daily boardings, has been in dire need of modernization, with the current cars reaching the end of their 30-year service life in 2026.
The TTC said it needed full funding for the new trains by March 2025, and expects the first of batch of cars to be delivered starting in 2030.
The $2.3-billion purchase of a new fleet of cars for Line 2 was seen as a collaborative effort, with funding from Ottawa and Queen's Park each covering a third of the cost and the city setting aside the remaining third.
As part of its "new deal" with Toronto announced in November 2023, Doug Ford's government promised more than $1 billion in funding for public transit in Toronto - including the $758 million that was its share of the new Line 2 cars. But that money was contingent on Justin Trudeau's government kicking in its share.
A funding announcement
In late November last year, then-finance minister Chrystia Freeland finally announced Ottawa's portion of the funding.
"We know that Toronto is growing and we need to have public transit that grows with the needs of our growing city," Freeland said at the press conference.
Freeland quit her post just weeks after the Line 2 announcement, which led to an upheaval within the federal government that ultimately ended with Trudeau's resignation.
Though the November press conference made it sound like the federal government had committed to the $758 million in Line 2 funding, the press secretary for Nate Erskine-Smith, the new federal infrastructure minister, now says the government is "considering" the TTC's funding application.
"Once approved and finalized by the minister and all partners," spokesperson Sofia Ouslis said last week, federal funding will begin to flow in the 2026 fiscal year.
In the meantime, the city has already started the process of choosing a supplier, the mayor's office said, even though federal funding remains under consideration. In early December, the TTC issued a request for proposal for 55 new subway cars, as well as a new automatic train control system.
"The funding is committed from both the federal and provincial governments," Zeus Eden, a spokesperson for Mayor Olivia Chow, said last week. "The (request for proposals) has been issued and we look forward to seeing riders on the new cars."
Transit questions unanswered
While the Trudeau government has more than doubled its transit funding for Toronto, Pierre Poilievre and his Conservatives, who are polling well ahead of any opposition, have been opaque on their approach to transit funding.
Poilievre, in a post in French on X in June 2023, took a stance against a proposed Quebec tramway project, accusing the Trudeau government of waging a "war on cars" and ignoring suburban commuters. He has also proposed tying funding for transit projects to high-density housing construction.
The Star repeatedly reached out to Poilievre for comment on whether he would alter the federal government support for the Line 2 subway cars should he be elected in the spring. He did not respond.
The risks of keeping trains running past the end of their service life was underscored when the Scarborough RT derailed in 2023. In June of last year, then-TTC CEO Rick Leary warned there could be similar problems on Line 2 if its trains weren't replaced.
Once the proposals to build the subway cars are in, it will take months before a vendor is selected, and then another several years before trains are produced and go onto the tracks, TTC staff explained.
"Given the political uncertainty in Ottawa, if the promised cheque isn't delivered now, not only could subway delays become more common, Line 2 could eventually shutdown completely," Coun, Josh Matlow, who sits on the TTC board, warned.
"This would disrupt the lives of millions, and the economy of Canada's largest city"
A lesson from the past
Though the Liberal government appears to be safe from opposition efforts to trigger a non-confidence vote and snap election until at least March 24, if it is defeated in a spring election, a new government could alter its commitment, or pull funding altogether.
That happened in 1995, when then-premier Mike Harris unilaterally pulled funding from the Eglinton West subway, filling in tunnels that had just been dug a year prior. And the same occurred in 2010, when several planned light-rail lines were cancelled by former mayor Rob Ford once he took office, with the approval of the McGuinty provincial government.
TTCriders, the transit advocacy group, had tried to get Toronto MPs to commit to supporting federal funding for the TTC. They received pledges from MPs of all stripes, including Erskine-Smith - but not from any Conservatives.
"Transit riders are urging Minister Erskine-Smith to find a way to accelerate the Canada Public Transit Fund, especially the urgently needed new subway trains," said Pizey-Allen, executive director of TTCriders, in an email.
"Until federal money flows and contracts are signed, it is hard to feel confident that the new subway trains are secure."
CAPTION: The subway cars on Line 2 will reach the end of their 30-year service life in 2026.

Full text: Ford more years?
Doug Ford tells his ministers to fast-track any announcements with Feb. 27 election call just days away, Jan. 23
With an Ontario election looming, it's time to reflect on what has gone on under Doug Ford's premiership. Anybody been to the hospital recently? How were the wait times? I hear ORs at SickKids are not able to function properly due to a lack of nurses. Anybody have a kid in public school or at an Ontario college? Given the chronic lack of funding during Ford's tenure, how's that working out? Have you been to the grocery store? We know consumers are being gouged, but has Ford stepped in to help us? Instead of listening to voters, Ford pushed through with the closure of the Ontario Science Centre, and he plans to add a foreign-owned luxury spa to Ontario Place. People need to recognize that other leaders are as well suited to talk business with Trump, or even better. Ford is not the people's premier. He's in it to make himself and his buddies rich at the expense of public education, health care and affordability.
Jennifer Chambers, Toronto
Dear Doug Ford: do not call an early election. Ontario is facing a threat of epic proportions in Donald Trump. You already have a mandate to deal with him. Aware of the seriousness of the current situation, the opposition parties have pledged to work with you. Do not get distracted. This is not the time for crass, self-serving politicking.
Trish Plant, Toronto
Ford's plan to announce a provincial election this week is a sham. He is again wasting taxpayers' money to keep himself in power, and he is clearly using Trump's tariff threats as a pretext for doing so. Ford already has a clear majority to do as he and his party see fit. Why does he need a "clear mandate" to address potential American tariffs? I feel that this just an excuse to deflect attention away from the issues Ford is facing. These include the RCMP investigation into the Greenbelt scandal; the underfunding of health care, education and social programs; the closing of supervised consumption sites; and delays to the Ontario Line and the Eglinton Crosstown LRT. Ford may get a clear mandate to fight the tariffs, but that will take his government away from dealing with issues that are more relevant to Ontarians.
Joe Bortolussi, Toronto
The election Doug Ford craves will reportedly cost between $150 million and $175 million. If you include the total cost of the $200 buy-a-vote cheques that the government is sending out now - which will add about $3.2 billion to the deficit and thus the total debt - the election will really cost nearly $3.4 billion. Last election, about 4.7 million votes were cast. To allow for some population growth, let's estimate that maybe five million votes will be cast. That would make this the most expensive election ever, at about $700 per vote.
My cheque, labelled the "Ontario Taxpayer Rebate," arrived today. It will be pinned to the wall and never cashed so that I will not be responsible for having added to the debt that Ford is using his existing strong mandate to create. Goodness knows how much more debt he can create with a "very" strong mandate. Curiously, the blurb attached to the cheque says, "to help with the costs of the federal carbon tax and interest rates, the Ontario government is putting money back into peoples' pockets by providing a tax-free, $200 rebate ... "
Say, wasn't it Ford who, during his first strong mandate, scrapped the cap-and-trade plan that existed when he took office and thus forced the federal carbon tax on the people of Ontario? Can we afford any more mandates for Ford?
Graeme Elliott, Toronto
Pride flag is not anti-Catholic
'This is a culture war': GTA school board upholds Pride flag policy amid fierce debate, Jan. 22of
School boards such as the publicly funded Dufferin-Peel Catholic District School Board should be more than ashamed to deny any their pupils not only their rights but also their dignity. Displaying a Pride flag does not mean endorsing conduct contrary to Catholic teachings. All schools must offer lessons of tolerance and acceptance. Stereotypes underlie hate. I have a right to be different and to be accepted as different. I am happy to be Roman Catholic - I just want to be myself. To quote Article 1 of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights, "all human beings are born free and equal in dignity and rights."
Marvin Zuker, Toronto
Giving seniors a choice of homes
Smaller nursing homes are cropping up across Canada. While experts say they are better for residents and staff, why is Ontario going another route? Jan. 24
The National Institute on Ageing report on small homes misses the mark in not going far enough to advocate for non-profit, neighbourhood-based homes small enough (no more than six people) to safely deliver emotion-focused care. These kinds of residential options are available to every other disability group in the province - except seniors, the only group besides convicted felons that's still mass-institutionalized.
Older adults should be consulted on their residential care preferences, and these need to be reflected in research. Seniors are not interested in ending up in 14-bed "small homes" that are not small, are located on long-term-care grounds, or are in age-segregated "neighbourhoods." What we want is to not have to leave our own communities to receive care.
Patricia Spindel, Ajax

More cracks showing in
Canada's response to Donald Trump's tariff threat, Jan. 22
In the face of U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff threats, Canada is struggling to formulate a unified and compelling response. Yet there's been a recent upsurge in patriotism in this country, as well as an overwhelming rejection of Trump's idea that Canada become a U.S. state. How can individual Canadians make a difference? By buying Canadian products instead of American ones, wherever the choice to do so exists.
How can we make this easy on consumers? Why not stick a maple leaf on products that are made in Canada? We should ask or require Canadian producers and manufacturers to put such an icon on all packaging materials, just the way there used to be a Good Housekeeping Seal of Approval.
Jim Thomas, Toronto

Elephants are not people under the law, the Colorado Supreme Court says. But they still deserve rights, Menon, Jan. 22
My cat would like to express her appreciation for Vinay Menon's column advocating for the rights of animals. But she also says she is quite distressed that no politician ever seems to mention the global mass extinction currently underway at the hands of humans. What will become of us? she asks.
Raphael Vigod, Toronto

Document 480 of 500

Trump, Ford put stumbling blocks in Poilievre's path to power

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trump-ford-put-stumbling-blocks-poilievres-path/docview/3159970607/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trump%2C+Ford+put+stumbling+blocks+in+Poilievre%27s+path+to+power&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's starting to become clear why Pierre Poilievre was so keen to have an election last fall.
Not only is Donald Trump raining on the Conservative leader's parade, but now Premier Doug Ford will be beating Poilievre to the campaign trail with the Feb. 27 Ontario election to be called within days.
Poilievre is going to be hoping that Ontarians are in such an anti- Liberal mood that they will be happy to vote conservative twice this year. A federal election campaign is widely expected to be launched not long after Parliament resumes at the end of March.
Ford has long signalled that he wanted a provincial election before that federal vote - and Poilievre is one of the reasons, my Queen's Park colleagues have reported.
Back last June, when the Star first reported that a 2025 election was on the horizon for Ontario, the reasons cited included the damage that the federal Conservatives could do to Ford and their provincial counterparts.
Ford and his team were worried that a Poilievre victory would usher in slashed spending, reduced transfer payments to the provinces and a scrapping of the electric vehicle strategy that Ford has been aggressively pursuing in concert with Justin Trudeau and his Liberals.
Trump is wreaking chaos on that last score with his own rollback of electric vehicle incentives in the U.S., but the more Ontarians absorb what that's doing to the huge auto industry in the province, the less they may like a federal Conservative leader headed down the same path.
There isn't a strict overlap between federal Conservatives and Ontario's Progressive Conservatives. Indeed, many of the same people who have voted for Ford have also voted for Trudeau's Liberals.
The lines get a little more direct when it comes to volunteers and resources. Enthusiastic partisans, willing to sacrifice time and energy for a political cause, are precious to all political parties.
Ford will be tapping into the same network that Poilievre is hoping to mobilize whenever Canadians do go to the polls.
Maybe it will be good practice for the conservative troops on the ground in Ontario. They'll get a chance to see what's working - and what isn't - at the doorstep. The same will be true, too, for Ontario Liberals and New Democrats, whose supporters and resources also are shared with their federal counterparts.
Ford has been asked - as recently as last week, again - whether he's trying to get around this quirk of the federation, in which Ontarians seem to be content with having one party in power federally and the opposite provincially.
That phenomenon, known as the "Underhill balance theory," has been the reality for 70 of the past 81 years.
The premier says that's not it; he's just looking for a mandate to take on Trump.
Trump, though, is another problem for Poilievre. It's early, but a new poll from Ekos suggests the Liberals here in Canada may be benefiting from what is happening south of the border. In what Ekos is calling a "dramatic recovery," its latest polling shows the Liberals back in the realm of 30 per cent popularity, for the first time since 2022.
Abacus Data CEO David Coletto has also been thinking that Poilievre may have a Trump problem. The U.S. president is not gaining any friends in Canada with all his belligerent talk and threats toward this country, but any Conservative leader here has to tread a fine line.
"He cannot appear too accommodating of Trump's threats, lest he alienate voters who view Trump as a destabilizing force, yet he cannot seem overly belligerent toward the new administration, given the need to safeguard cross-border economic ties and satisfy a sizable portion of his own base who likes Trump," Coletto wrote.
The more that Trump dominates, even frames, the next federal election debate only highlights this conundrum for Poilievre.
Meanwhile, the two campaigns that Poilievre intended to wage last fall, against the "carbon tax" and Justin Trudeau, are fizzling.
The two leading contenders for the Liberal leadership, Chrystia Freeland and Mark Carney, have already said they are backing off on the consumer price on carbon. Even Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault, who is endorsing Carney, said he was forced to admit that policy is dead.
So much for the "carbon tax election" - a phrase that was uttered more than 800 times in the Commons last year alone, according to the OpenParliament.ca website.
The anti-Trudeau campaign now lacks a villain, given Trudeau's looming resignation. In politics, a week is a long time. By the time Poilievre goes to the polls, Trudeau will have been gone for many weeks.
Poilievre was definitely in majority territory if an election had been held last fall. Thanks to Trump, Trudeau and not least of all Ford, the fates of 2025 don't seem to be aligning on the Conservative leader's side.
CREDIT: susan delacourt OPINION

Subject: Provincial elections; Conservatism; Political campaigns; Leadership; Carbon; Electric vehicles; Environmental tax; Tax elections

People: Trudeau, Justin

Document 481 of 500

Why the path to replace Justin Trudeau should run right through cities like Toronto

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/why-path-replace-justin-trudeau-should-run-right/docview/3159792546/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+the+path+to+replace+Justin+Trudeau+should+run+right+through+cities+like+Toronto&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If you're lost in the political wilderness, find your way back to the city.
Federal Liberal leadership candidates should take this approach to heart: don't be afraid to talk about cities during the race to replace Justin Trudeau. In fact, do it a lot and tell Canadians how you'll make theirs better to live in. Sounds simple but it's actually quite novel.
Federal politicians in Canada tend to ignore cities. Entire campaigns and leadership debates go by with barely a city mention despite around 80 per cent of Canadians living in them.
Leadership races are interesting, even fun, because they can be about ideas, about what a party wants to be. Right now Liberals are thinking about that.
There is nothing wrong with living in small towns or the countryside, of course. They are good, important and should never be ignored. Skilled and honest politicians speak to both. Often, however, politics pits city against country. While it may be successful electorally, it's bad for the country, national unity and solving actual problems.
Though cities in Canada are largely a provincial matter, the feds play a big role in funding projects and the Minister of Housing, Infrastructure and Communities is essentially the federal municipal minister. Perhaps the name should be changed to reflect that. Minister Sean Fraser recently showed how much effect the office can have by tying housing funding to municipalities allowing more housing of various kinds.
Some will quibble that the oft-mentioned 80 per cent statistic groups together people in downtowns and those in deeper suburbia with different lifestyles. True, there are differences, even tension. The lifestyle in, say, The Annex is different than in Willowdale, but that's normal and OK. The differences have been exploited though - hyped and turned into wedges by politicians who gain from increased division, at the expense of things in common.
In Toronto this happens with issues like transit, where car-oriented lifestyles are pitted against, well, everyone else. The Ford brothers, whether in the mayor or premier's office, have been adept at conjuring a "war against the car." When the focus is on the wedge there's a void on the other side, an absence of talking about shared values and challenges, like just trying to get to where we need to be, whatever the mode of transportation.
On the national stage transit troubles in Toronto can be connected to those in Calgary, Halifax or elsewhere. Suddenly there's a national constituency with common cause on this and other city issues. Only a federal politician can do that, and other parties would have to respond.
Another reason Liberals should talk about cities is an untapped voter block. In pitting city against country, epithets like "downtown elites" are thrown around, latte sippers different from "real" people living outside cities who drive trucks, grow crops or do things with their hands. Nonsense, but also politically successfully sometimes. Pierre Poilievre, despite an entire career employed as a politician, enjoys holding two-by-fours and using tools as cameras record. That works, it seems.
However, despite years of deindustrialization in cities like Toronto, a lot of manufacturing still takes place here. That working class exists, but so does an army of service workers who may not fit the traditional image of the working class. Gig and contract workers are here too, often disparaged as the "laptop class," young people who have no pensions and hustle for work constantly, who lead lives as precarious as any boom-bust factory worker.
Housing, transportation, cost of living and other issues intersect here for these voters who have been abandoning Liberals for Conservative messages. An overt city framing could gather these concerns together, give it shape and place, and pull those voters back.
Some urban hay could also be made right now talking about Canadian cities as havens for a possible American brain drain as U.S. research budgets and institutions are threatened by Trump.
To really think big, Liberals should start talking about constitutional reform, giving cities more standing and autonomy, a discussion that may happen soon in Toronto itself. Next week Mayor Olivia Chow's executive committee is going to debate a motion that would create a citizens advisory board to discuss the power imbalance between Toronto and other levels of government, particularly Ontario - essentially, how Toronto can stand up for itself.
Canada's municipal power dynamics were forged in 1867 when most Canadians lived rurally. Cities weren't very important then, but they're critical now. Toronto's discussion could mean becoming a special status "Charter City" or something else.
Imagine if Toronto and other municipalities were talking about this at the same time a federal party is. It would be foolish for Chow's executive committee not to open this discussion formally, just as cities provide an opportunity wilderness wandering Liberals shouldn't squander.
CAPTION: Entire campaigns and leadership debates go by with barely a city mention despite around 80 per cent of Canadians living in them. The Liberal leadership race is a chance to change that.

Subject: Canadians; Working class; Voters; Politics; Politicians; Cities

Location: Canada

People: Trudeau, Justin
