Document 482 of 500

Doug Ford only makes things worse when Pierre Poilievre doesn't have Justin Trudeau to kick around anymore

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-only-makes-things-worse-when-pierre/docview/3159778159/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+only+makes+things+worse+when+Pierre+Poilievre+doesn%27t+have+Justin+Trudeau+to+kick+around+anymore&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's starting to become clear why Pierre Poilievre was so keen to have an election last fall.
Not only is Donald Trump raining on the Conservative leader's parade, but now Premier Doug Ford will be beating Poilievre to the campaign trail with the Feb. 27 Ontario election to be called within days.
Poilievre is going to be hoping that Ontarians are in such an anti-Liberal mood that they will be happy to vote conservative twice this spring. A federal election campaign is widely expected to be launched not long after Parliament resumes at the end of March.
Ford has long signalled that he wanted a provincial election before that federal vote - and Poilievre is one of the reasons, my Queen's Park colleagues have reported.
Back last June, when the Star first reported that a 2025 election was on the horizon for Ontario, the reasons cited included the damage that the federal Conservatives could do to Ford and their provincial counterparts.
Ford and his team were worried that a Poilievre victory would usher in slashed spending, reduced transfer payments to the provinces and a scrapping of the electric vehicle strategy that Ford has been aggressively pursuing in concert with Justin Trudeau and his Liberals.
Trump is wreaking chaos on that last score with his own rollback of electric vehicle incentives in the U.S., but the more Ontarians absorb what that's doing to the huge auto industry in the province, the less they may like a federal Conservative leader headed down the same path.
There isn't a strict overlap between federal Conservatives and Ontario's Progressive Conservatives. Indeed, many of the same people who have voted for Ford have also voted for Trudeau's Liberals.
The lines get a little more direct when it comes to volunteers and resources. Enthusiastic partisans, willing to sacrifice time and energy for a political cause, are precious to all political parties.
Ford will be tapping into the same network that Poilievre is hoping to mobilize whenever Canadians do go to the polls.
Maybe it will be good practice for the conservative troops on the ground in Ontario. They'll get a chance to see what's working - and what isn't - at the doorstep. The same will be true for Ontario Liberals and New Democrats too, whose supporters and resources also are shared with their federal counterparts.
Ford has been asked - as recently as week, again - whether he's trying to get around this quirk of the federation, in which Ontarians seem to be content with having one party in power federally and the opposite provincially. That phenomenon, known as the "Underhill balance theory," has been the reality for 70 of the past 81 years.
The premier says that's not it; he's just looking for a mandate to take on Trump.
Trump, though, is another problem for Poilievre. It's early, but a new poll from EKOS suggests the Liberals here in Canada may be benefiting from what is happening south of the border. In what EKOS is calling a "dramatic recovery," its latest polling shows the Liberals back in the realm of 30 per cent popularity, for the first time since 2022.
Abacus Data CEO David Coletto has also been thinking that Poilievre may have a Trump problem. The U.S. president is not gaining any friends in Canada with all his belligerent talk and threats toward this country, but any Conservative leader here has to tread a fine line.
"He cannot appear too accommodating of Trump's threats, lest he alienate voters who view Trump as a destabilizing force, yet he cannot seem overly belligerent toward the new administration, given the need to safeguard cross-border economic ties and satisfy a sizable portion of his own base who likes Trump," Coletto wrote.
The more that Trump dominates, even frames, the next federal election debate only highlights this conundrum for Poilievre.
Meanwhile, the two campaigns that Poilievre intended to wage last fall, against the "carbon tax" and Justin Trudeau, are fizzling.
The two leading contenders for the Liberal leadership, Chrystia Freeland and Mark Carney, have already said they are backing off on the consumer price on carbon. Even Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault, who is endorsing Carney, said he was forced to admit this week that policy is dead.
So much for the "carbon tax election" - a phrase that was uttered more than 800 times in the Commons last year alone, according to the openparliament.ca website.
The anti-Trudeau campaign now lacks a villain, given Trudeau's looming resignation. In politics, a week is a long time. By the time Poilievre goes to the polls, Trudeau will have been gone for many weeks.
Poilievre was definitely in majority territory if an election had been held last fall. Thanks to Trump, Trudeau and not least of all Ford, the fates of 2025 don't seem to be aligning on the Conservative leader's side.
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre speaks during a news conference in Delta, B.C. on Jan.16, 2025.

Document 483 of 500

Ford courts disaster with election call

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-courts-disaster-with-election-call/docview/3159778130/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+courts+disaster+with+election+call&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford has made his intentions clear: He will call a provincial election on Wednesday, two-and-a-half years into the mandate Ontarians gave him in June 2022 with 41 per cent of the popular vote and an 83-seat majority.
Ford is courting disaster. He is poised on the precipice of repeating the greatest blunder in Ontario political history: foisting an unnecessary and unwanted early election on an unconvinced and skeptical electorate. He is at dire risk of Ontario emerging from his folly in a weakened position and turning off many of his own supporters.
Ford has been frogmarched to this ledge by two forces: the self-interested opportunists in his caucus and cabinet greedy for four more years of job security and power, and the geniuses in his backroom, who can't get past the shallow topline of their polling data and insist the time will never be better to lock in another mandate.
Ford has displayed solid instincts in his political career, but by calling this election now, he is defying history, common sense - and probably his own gut.
The "reason for going" Ford spelled out for Tory donors in Markham on Tuesday night has two pillars, both anchored in sand. He proposes to hit the campaign trail claiming he needs a new, strengthened mandate to "send a message" to U.S. President Donald Trump that Ontarians back robust tariff countermeasures, and to obtain public support for tens of billions of dollars in economic relief spending.
It's a far-fetched rationale light on the ring of truth, unlikely to stand voters' sniff test and tailor-made for unintended consequences.
Trump has zero interest in whether Ford's government holds its current 79 or all 124 of Ontario's seats. But he will be sure to take note if Ford's early election dice-roll yields an even slightly weakened majority, or worse.
Can even the smartest of political geniuses predict exactly how the four-week election campaign will unfold? Ask David Peterson, whose 50 per cent lead in the polls in 1990 convinced him to call an early election "to protect the province in the event of a national crisis" - an election that ended up being won by Bob Rae's NDP.
Has Ford looked in the mirror and asked himself if calling this election is really worth that risk? What if he emerges from this adventure with a weakened majority? A minority? Or what if his gambit results in - gasp! - a change of government? What message will that send south about public support for tariff countermeasures and spending big to protect Ontarians?
These risks are real.
The Tory backroom knows that its base believes it gave Ford a strong mandate through June 2026. Will notoriously waste-averse Conservatives warm to dropping up to $175 million on an early election to confirm what they've already decided? Justifying to his own faithful the cost of this trip to the polls will be Ford's first hurdle in this race - but not his highest.
Far more deeply flawed is Ford's claim that he needs a new mandate to support spending tens of billions of dollars to insulate the province from Trump's America-first protectionism - because that's not really what he'll be seeking from voters. To spend tens of billions, Ford first needs to raise tens of billions.
Unless Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy has discretely installed a money-printing press in the basement of the Frost Building, Ford will need to ask voters, including tax-hating Ford Nation, to support new "revenue tools" - aka, taxes, or massive deficit financing - to fill and refill the war chest that will enable Ontario to weather the gathering economic storm.
Does Ford genuinely believe he can persuade his parsimonious base that tens of billions in new taxes or deficit is a good thing? Can he convince fiscal conservatives to fund a massive new social and corporate welfare program that may help their neighbours, but may not help them - and to show their support by actively getting out and voting for it?
Odds are that, like unenthusiastic supporters of the U.S. Democratic ticket in November, unconvinced Ontario Tories will show their disquiet not by taking their support elsewhere, but simply by staying home on voting day.
Equally perilous for Ford are the issues of timing and a perceived power gap. When Trump levies his threatened 25 per cent tariff on Saturday, Ford will suddenly find himself torn between slogging along the campaign trail and donning the Captain Canada cape he clearly loves. Can he do the former effectively and the latter legitimately when by definition he's seeking a mandate? Or will his performance in both roles suffer, along with the results he delivers?
Ford, his caucus and cabinet, and his backroom brain trust are convinced their reported 46 per cent polling number and roughly 25 per cent lead over both Bonnie Crombie's Liberals and Marit Stiles' NDP provides plenty of cushion to work with in this race. But right out of the gate, history and human nature will deliver Ford a strong dose of unwelcome reality: when the words election, early, unwanted and unnecessary commingle, voters push back.
Within a week, the Tories' vaunted support among decided voters will shrink toward the 30s. And in the most classic of unintended consequences, by going to the polls Ford will instantly elevate and legitimize the two opposition leaders, whose anonymity and near-invisibility to date has been the key driver of their poor polling performance.
Should one or both of Crombie and Stiles gain serious traction, all bets are off.
These are the perils Ford weighed for weeks as he perched with his toes on the edge, staring into the electoral abyss. It's now too late for him to step back, strap in and resign himself to working for the next 18 months with the mandate he has in hand. He is taking the plunge - and the election Ford believes is gift-wrapped for him may prove to be a booby-trapped package that blows up in his face.
Dan Rath is co-author of "Not Without Cause: David Peterson's Fall from Grace," the story of the 1990 Ontario election.
CREDIT: Dan Rath Contributor Dan Rath is co-author of "Not Without Cause: David Peterson's Fall from Grace," the story of the 1990 Ontario election.;

Document 484 of 500

Weaponization of 'woke': Word born from Black empowerment movements has become Poilievre's tool for discrediting rivals

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/weaponization-woke/docview/3159778024/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Weaponization+of+%27woke%27%3A+Word+born+from+Black+empowerment+movements+has+become+Poilievre%27s+tool+for+discrediting+rivals&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Wasteful. Weird. Wacko. Woke.
It may seem strange how four letters strung together, nestled among a series of alliterative buzzwords, bearing a modern definition so amorphous so as to be nearly meaningless, can evoke such clear feelings of something bad, of something reaching into places it shouldn't.
But the word, lifted from the oeuvres of Black political activists, singers and writers, has became one of the terms Pierre Poilievre has relied on most to discredit his political rivals in his relentless bid to end almost a decade of Liberal rule.
In "woke," the Conservative leader has found the perfect word, a term right-wing movements and politicians around the world have successfully captured to brand progressives as performative activists bent on government overreach.
The word is so pervasive that U.S. President Donald Trump didn't even need to say it when he promised during his inaugural address this week to scrap any policy "trying to socially engineer race and gender into every aspect of public and private life," something he said would restore America "and the revolution of common sense."
Maybe that sounds a bit familiar.
Newly published research has shown that Poilievre's Conservatives have excelled at using "woke" to try and delegitimize progressive parties, people and policies and frame them as a threat to Canada's democratic values.
To chart how Poilievre arrived at this moment, however, you have to start in the early and mid-20th century, when the term was first used to call for awareness and vigilance about systemic injustices in a profoundly prejudiced America.
One of its earliest recorded uses is tied to a 1938 song by American folk and blues singer Huddie Ledbetter, who went by the stage name Lead Belly. The song is called "Scottsboro Boys," and tells the story of a landmark set of race cases that saw nine Black youths falsely accused of raping two white women near Scottsboro, Ala., in 1931. In a spoken recording after the song concludes, Lead Belly warns those listening to be "a little careful" when they pass through the area, and to "stay woke, keep their eyes open."
Decades later, the phrase "stay woke" would re-enter the musical mainstream as a refrain in Erykah Badu's 2008 track "Master Teacher," and surface again during the protests and racial reckonings that followed the police killings of Michael Brown in Missouri in 2014 and George Floyd in Minnesota in 2020.
The use of the word "woke," with that original meaning intact, has only shown up once in Canada's parliamentary record.
Several years before he became the first Black Speaker of the House of Commons, Liberal MP Greg Fergus used it to mark the start of Black History Month in 2019.
"Whereas we once divided ourselves into narrow interests based on where we or our ancestors came from, more and more we are a woke community that is working together to achieve a common interest: to be equal and to be treated as equal, equal in business, studies, law, culture, innovation, politics, government and citizenship," Fergus told his fellow MPs.
"That was really the only instance," said Patrick McCurdy, a communications professor at the University of Ottawa who traced, with two other researchers, how Conservative MPs have wielded the word in parliamentary debates.
"Then we really see the entrance of this culture war."
McCurdy and his colleagues analyzed the use of the word "woke" in all House of Commons and committee records from 2001 until June 2023. They found 122 instances of the word and its variations being used in a "non 'wake up' context" from 2019 onwards, with most of those uses delivered by Conservative MPs - and mostly only after Poilievre took the party's helm in 2022.
The data shows that Poilievre first used the word once in 2021 and again in 2022 - the year when the term's use climbed to 20 instances, 19 of which were uttered by Conservative MPs. In the first six months of 2023, the word was used 88 times: 63 of those stemming from Conservatives, 33 of which were attributed to Poilievre.
A Star search of the parliamentary record showed that after McCurdy's data collection period, Poilievre only said "woke" on seven occasions, though other Conservative MPs used it more than 70 times. It's also a word the Conservative leader has used frequently on social media and in media interviews over the past year, most recently in a lengthy conversation with media personality Jordan Peterson, in which he said Canada had been stricken with "horrendous utopian wokeism."
Conservatives have used "woke" to malign a peculiar jumble of issues: race and gender inclusivity in school curriculums and workplace hiring practices, attempts to modernize Canada's military, animal rights activism, the country's telecommunications regulator, the bail system, drug supply policies, reusable grocery bags, pro-Palestinian protests and the federal carbon pricing scheme.
The party did not respond to the Star's requests for comment on this story.
"When you have a sort of vague and shifty term like this, it means that when you get criticized, you can always say, 'No, no, that's not what I meant by this,' and shift to another meaning. I think it can give rise to a kind of slipperiness that can be very useful in the cut and thrust of public debate," said Jennifer Saul, a chair in social and political philosophy of language at the University of Waterloo.
That kind of ambiguity is the hallmark of political dog whistles, said Tari Ajadi, a political science professor at McGill University.
"If you replace the word 'woke' with a skin colour, if you replace the term 'woke' with a sexual orientation, if you replace the term 'woke' with a gender ... you get a very different response. It wouldn't be allowed to be said," Ajadi said.
The myriad uses of the word prompted McCurdy and his colleagues to sort the examples they collected into seven different definitions of "woke." The "authoritarian" definition meant the word was used to characterize something as controlling or intolerant, for example, and the "ineffective" definition was applied when it was used to paint a policy as actively harmful.
The researchers concluded that MPs, largely Conservative ones, had succeeded in shifting the term from its social justice roots and into an understanding associated with control, positioning Poilievre "as the only political defender of freedom against this looming totalitarian threat."
The Conservative leader has previously said as much. "It has plenty of pretexts but only one purpose: control. It is designed to divide people by race, gender, ethnicity, religion, vaccine status and any other way one can divide people into groups. Why? It is because then one can justify having a government to control all those groups," Poilievre told the Commons in 2023 when asked to define the word. "No more woke. We need freedom."
How Poilievre arrived at that definition begins not with the right, but with the left.
Ajadi said the word "woke" was first recast from "a very in-group thing that's talking about being socially conscious and politically conscious ... to talking generically about social justice, which it was never intended to be."
He said the term was seized by the upper-middle class, corporations, universities, and politicians who claimed it as a trendy term that came to represent a "poorly defined set of values."
"This is how this kind of appropriation of language emerges and unfolds, and then very quickly gets appropriated once more by the right to mock what some might call performative activism," Ajadi said.
Indeed, the word eventually became synonymous with inauthentic virtue signalling from progressives (think Justin Trudeau, who had publicly condemned racism before admitting he had previously worn blackface), and left-leaning policies that earnestly sought to correct historic inequities, but were spun by the right as infringements on their freedoms.
The result? Laws like Florida Gov. Ron DeSantis's so-called Stop WOKE Act - or Stop Wrongs to our Kids and Employees. (The 2022 state law, which banned education and workplace initiatives that suggest people are oppressed or privileged based on their race, gender or nation of origin, has faced legal challenges.)
Such heavily politicized uses of "woke" left progressive politicians in Canada floundering to respond, McCurdy said.
His research showed that Liberal, NDP and Bloc Québécois MPs tried to "challenge" Conservative uses of "woke" on 22 occasions, and found that they largely wound up legitimizing the word's use as a pejorative in the process.
In one case, then-Liberal Housing Minister Ahmed Hussen repeatedly characterized the term as something offensive after Poilievre referred to mayors whose housing policies he opposed as "woke."
"He stood up in this House and insulted and denigrated three of the leading mayors of Canada's largest cities, calling them 'woke,' " Hussen said during a 2023 debate in the Commons.
"The idea (behind the word) has become sullied, and no one's prepared to try and defend it," McCurdy said.
The word has also been effective for Poilievre because regular people also use it to convey dissatisfaction, said Mitch Heimpel, director of policy at Enterprise Canada and a former Conservative staffer.
Heimpel said he's heard people in his neighbourhood of young families use "woke" to refer to topics like what children are learning in schools and news stories about someone released on bail committing another crime. "It's a reflection of a change to a system that in the mind of the person listening, is causing it to not work," Heimpel said. "So it becomes a good shorthand in that way."
Both Heimpel and Saul said it is not cause for alarm that words evolve, taking on new meanings over time. "If you try to hold language in place, you're fighting a losing game," Saul said.
But for Ajadi, the movement of political language is deliberate, something done with a motive in mind. "Language evolves, but I think that removes agency from the equation," Ajadi said. "People make language evolve."
CAPTION: In "woke," Pierre Poilievre has found the perfect word, a term right-wing movements and politicians around the world have successfully captured to brand progressives as performative activists bent on government overreach, writes Raisa Patel. One of the earliest uses of "woke" is linked to singer Huddie (Lead Belly) Ledbetter's 1938 song about the Scottsboro Boys - nine Black youths falsely accused of rape in Alabama.In a spoken recording after the song concludes, he warns listeners to "stay woke, keep their eyes open."Nathan Denette THE CANADIAN PRESS file photoHulton ArchiveGetty Images
CREDIT: Raisa Patel Toronto Star

Document 485 of 500

The road to victory runs through cities

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/road-victory-runs-through-cities/docview/3159778010/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+road+to+victory+runs+through+cities&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If you're lost in the political wilderness, find your way back to the city.
Federal Liberal leadership candidates should take this approach to heart: don't be afraid to talk about cities during the race to replace Justin Trudeau. In fact, do it a lot and tell Canadians how you'll make theirs better to live in. Sounds simple but it's actually quite novel.
Federal politicians in Canada tend to ignore cities. Entire campaigns and leadership debates go by with barely a city mention, despite around 80 per cent of Canadians living in them.
Leadership races are interesting, even fun, because they can be about ideas, about what a party wants to be. Right now Liberals are thinking about that.
There is nothing wrong with living in small towns or the countryside, of course. They are good, important and should never be ignored. Skilled and honest politicians speak to both. Often, however, politics pits city against country. While it may be successful electorally, it's bad for the country, national unity and solving actual problems.
Although cities in Canada are largely a provincial matter, the feds play a big role in funding projects and the Minister of Housing, Infrastructure and Communities is essentially the federal municipal minister. Perhaps the name should be changed to reflect that. Minister Sean Fraser recently showed how much effect the office can have by tying housing funding to municipalities allowing more housing of various kinds.
Some will quibble that the oft-mentioned 80 per cent statistic groups together people in downtowns and those in deeper suburbia with different lifestyles. True, there are differences, even tension. The lifestyle in, say, The Annex is different than in Willowdale, but that's normal and OK. The differences have been exploited though - hyped and turned into wedges by politicians who gain from increased division, at the expense of things in common.
In Toronto this happens with issues like transit, where car-oriented lifestyles are pitted against, well, everyone else. The Ford brothers, whether in the mayor or premier's office, have been adept at conjuring a "war against the car." When the focus is on the wedge there's a void on the other side, an absence of talking about shared values and challenges, like just trying to get to where we need to be, whatever the mode of transportation.
On the national stage, transit troubles in Toronto can be connected to those in Calgary, Halifax or elsewhere. Suddenly there's a national constituency with a common cause on this and other city issues. Only a federal politician can do that, and other parties would have to respond.
Another reason Liberals should talk about cities is an untapped voter block. In pitting city against country, epithets like "downtown elites" are thrown around, latte sippers different from "real" people living outside cities who drive trucks, grow crops or do things with their hands. Nonsense, but also politically successful sometimes. Pierre Poilievre, despite an entire career employed as a politician, enjoys holding two-by-fours and using tools as cameras record. That works, it seems.
However, despite years of deindustrialization in cities like Toronto, a lot of manufacturing still takes place here. That working class exists, but so does an army of service workers who may not fit the traditional image of the working class. Gig and contract workers are here, too, often disparaged as the "laptop class," young people who have no pensions and hustle for work constantly, who lead lives as precarious as any boom-bust factory worker.
Housing, transportation, cost of living and other issues intersect here for these voters who have been abandoning Liberals for Conservative messages. An overt city framing could gather these concerns together, give it shape and place, and pull those voters back.
Some urban hay could also be made right now talking about Canadian cities as havens for a possible American brain drain, as U.S. research budgets and institutions are threatened by Trump.
To really think big, Liberals should start talking about constitutional reform, giving cities more standing and autonomy, a discussion that may happen soon in Toronto itself. Next week, Mayor Olivia Chow's executive committee is going to debate a motion that would create a citizens advisory board to discuss the power imbalance between Toronto and other levels of government, particularly Ontario - essentially, how Toronto can stand up for itself.
Canada's municipal power dynamics were forged in 1867 when most Canadians lived rurally. Cities weren't very important then, but they're critical now. Toronto's discussion could mean becoming a special status "Charter City" or something else.
Imagine if Toronto and other municipalities were talking about this at the same time a federal party is. It would be foolish for Chow's executive committee not to open this discussion formally, just as cities provide an opportunity wilderness wandering Liberals shouldn't squander.
CAPTION: Entire election campaigns go by with barely a city mention, despite about 80 per cent of Canadians living in them, Shawn Micallef writes. The Liberal leadership race is a chance to change that.Steve Russell Toronto Star file photo
CREDIT: Shawn Micallef OPINION

Document 486 of 500

Doug Ford is courting disaster with an early election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-is-courting-disaster-with-early/docview/3159777965/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+is+courting+disaster+with+an+early+election&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford has made his intentions clear: he will call a provincial election on Wednesday, two-and-a-half years into the mandate Ontarians gave him in June 2022 with 41 per cent of the popular vote and an 83-seat majority.
Ford is courting disaster. He is poised on the precipice of repeating the greatest blunder in Ontario political history: foisting an unnecessary and unwanted early election on an unconvinced and skeptical electorate. He is at dire risk of Ontario emerging from his folly in a weakened position and turning off many of his own supporters.
Ford has been frogmarched to this ledge by two forces: the self-interested opportunists in his caucus and cabinet greedy for four more years of job security and power, and the geniuses in his backroom, who can't get past the shallow topline of their polling data and insist the time will never be better to lock in another mandate.
Ford has displayed some solid instincts in his political career, but by calling this election now, he is defying history, common sense - and probably his own gut.
The "reason for going" Ford spelled out for Tory donors in Markham on Tuesday night has two pillars, both anchored in sand. He proposes to hit the campaign trail claiming he needs a new, strengthened mandate to "send a message" to U.S. President Donald Trump that Ontarians back robust tariff countermeasures, and to obtain public support for tens of billions of dollars in economic relief spending.
It's a far-fetched rationale light on the ring of truth, unlikely to stand voters' sniff test and tailor-made for unintended consequences.
Trump has zero interest in whether Doug Ford's government holds its current 79 or all 124 of Ontario's seats. But he will be sure to take note if Ford's early-election dice-roll yields an even slightly weakened majority, or worse.
Can even the smartest of political geniuses predict exactly how the four-week election campaign will unfold? Ask David Peterson, whose 50 per cent lead in the polls in 1990 convinced him to call an early election "to protect the province in the event of a national crisis" - an election that ended up being won by Bob Rae's NDP.
Has Ford looked in the mirror and asked himself if calling this election is really worth that risk? What if he emerges from this adventure with a weakened majority? A minority? Or what if his gambit results in - gasp! - a change of government? What message will that send south about public support for tariff countermeasures and spending big to protect Ontarians? These risks are real.
The Tory backroom knows full well that its base believes it already gave Ford a strong mandate through June 2026. Will notoriously waste-averse Conservatives warm to dropping up to $175 million on an early election to confirm what they've already decided? Justifying to his own faithful the cost of this trip to the polls will be Ford's first hurdle in this race - but not his highest.
Far more deeply flawed is Ford's claim that he needs a new mandate to support spending tens of billions of dollars to insulate the province from Trump's America-first protectionism - because that's not really what he'll be seeking from voters. To spend tens of billions, Ford first needs to raise tens of billions.
Unless Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy has discretely installed a money-printing press in the basement of the Frost Building, Ford will need to ask voters, including tax-hating Ford Nation, to support new "revenue tools" - aka, taxes, or massive deficit financing - to fill and refill the war chest that will enable Ontario to weather the gathering economic storm.
Does Ford genuinely believe he can persuade his parsimonious base that tens of billions in new taxes or deficit is a good thing? Can he convince fiscal conservatives to fund a massive new social and corporate welfare program that may help their neighbours, but may not help them - and to show their support by actively getting out and voting for it?
Odds are that, like unenthusiastic supporters of the U.S. Democratic ticket in November, unconvinced Ontario Tories will show their disquiet not by taking their support elsewhere, but simply by staying home on voting day.
Equally perilous for Ford are the issues of timing and a perceived power gap. When Trump levies his threatened 25 per cent tariff on Saturday, Ford will suddenly find himself torn between slogging along the campaign trail and donning the Captain Canada cape he clearly loves. Can he do the former effectively and the latter legitimately when by definition he's seeking a mandate? Or will his performance in both roles suffer, along with the results he delivers?
Ford, his caucus and cabinet, and his backroom brain trust are convinced their reported 46 per cent polling number and roughly 25 per cent lead over both Bonnie Crombie's Liberals and Marit Stiles' NDP provides plenty of cushion to work with in this race. But right out of the gate, history and human nature will deliver Ford a strong dose of unwelcome reality: when the words election, early, unwanted and unnecessary commingle, voters push back.
Within a week, the Tories' vaunted support among decided voters will shrink toward the 30s. And in the most classic of unintended consequences, by going to the polls Ford will instantly elevate and legitimize the two opposition leaders, whose anonymity and near-invisibility to date has been the key driver of their poor polling performance. Should one or both of Crombie and Stiles gain serious traction, all bets are off.
These are the perils Ford weighed for weeks as he perched with his toes on the edge, staring into the electoral abyss. It's now too late for him to step back, strap in and resign himself to working for the next 18 months with the mandate he has in hand. He is taking the plunge - and the election Ford believes is gift-wrapped for him may prove to be a booby-trapped package that blows up in his face.
CAPTION: "(Premier Doug Ford)is poised on the precipice of repeating the greatest blunder in Ontario political history: foisting an unnecessary and unwanted early election on an unconvinced and skeptical electorate," writes Dan Rath.
CREDIT: Dan Rath Contributor

Subject: Provincial elections; Political campaigns; Election results; Voters; Political history; Protectionism; Tariffs

Document 487 of 500

How Pierre Poilievre successfully weaponized the word 'woke'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/how-pierre-poilievre-successfully-weaponized-word/docview/3159685934/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+Pierre+Poilievre+successfully+weaponized+the+word+%27woke%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Wasteful. Weird. Wacko. Woke.
It may seem strange how four letters strung together, nestled among a series of alliterative buzzwords, bearing a modern definition so amorphous so as to be nearly meaningless, can evoke such clear feelings of something bad, of something reaching into places it shouldn't.
But the word, lifted from the oeuvres of Black political activists, singers and writers, has became one of the terms Pierre Poilievre has relied on most to discredit his political rivals in his relentless bid to end almost a decade of Liberal rule.
In "woke," the Conservative leader has found the perfect word, a term right-wing movements and politicians around the world have successfully captured to brand progressives as performative activists bent on government overreach.
The word is so pervasive that U.S. President Donald Trump didn't even need to say it when he promised during his inaugural address this week to scrap any policy "trying to socially engineer race and gender into every aspect of public and private life," something he said would restore America "and the revolution of common sense."
Maybe that sounds a bit familiar.
Newly-published research has shown that Poilievre's Conservatives have excelled at using "woke" to try and delegitimize progressive parties, people and policies and frame them as a threat to Canada's democratic values.
To chart how Poilievre arrived at this moment, however, you have to start in the early and mid-20th century, when the term was first used to call for awareness and vigilance about systemic injustices in a profoundly prejudiced America.
One of its earliest recorded uses is tied to a 1938 song by American folk and blues singer Huddie Ledbetter, who went by the stage name Lead Belly. The song is called Scottsboro Boys, and tells the story of a landmark set of race cases that saw nine Black youths falsely accused of raping two white women near Scottsboro, Alabama in 1931. In a spoken recording after the song concludes, Lead Belly warns those listening to be "a little careful" when they pass through the area, and to "stay woke, keep their eyes open."
Decades later, the phrase "stay woke" would re-enter the musical mainstream as a refrain in Erykah Badu's 2008 track Master Teacher, and surface again during the protests and racial reckonings that followed the police killings of Michael Brown in Missouri in 2014 and George Floyd in Minnesota in 2020.
The use of the word "woke," with that original meaning intact, has only shown up once in Canada's parliamentary record.
Several years before he became the first Black Speaker of the House of Commons, Liberal MP Greg Fergus used it to mark the start of Black History Month in 2019.
"Whereas we once divided ourselves into narrow interests based on where we or our ancestors came from, more and more we are a woke community that is working together to achieve a common interest: to be equal and to be treated as equal, equal in business, studies, law, culture, innovation, politics, government and citizenship," Fergus told his fellow MPs.
"That was really the only instance," said Patrick McCurdy, a communications professor at the University of Ottawa who traced, with two other researchers, how Conservative MPs have wielded the word in parliamentary debates.
"Then we really see the entrance of this culture war."
McCurdy and his colleagues analyzed the use of the word "woke" in all House of Commons and committee records from 2001 until June 2023. They found 122 instances of the word and its variations being used in a "non 'wake up' context" from 2019 onwards, with most of those uses delivered by Conservative MPs - and mostly only after Poilievre took the party's helm in 2022.
The data shows that Poilievre first used the word once in 2021 and again in 2022 - the year when the term's use climbed to 20 instances, 19 of which were uttered by Conservative MPs. In the first six months of 2023, the word was used 88 times: 63 of those stemming from Conservatives, 33 of which were attributed to Poilievre.
A Star search of the parliamentary record showed that after McCurdy's data collection period, Poilievre only said "woke" on seven occasions, though other Conservative MPs used it more than 70 times. It's also a word the Conservative leader has used frequently on social media and in media interviews over the past year, most recently in a lengthy conversation with media personality Jordan Peterson, in which he said Canada had been stricken with "horrendous utopian wokeism."
Conservatives have used "woke" to malign a peculiar jumble of issues: race and gender inclusivity in school curriculums and workplace hiring practices, attempts to modernize Canada's military, animal rights activism, the country's telecommunications regulator, the bail system, drug supply policies, reusable grocery bags, pro-Palestinian protests, and the federal carbon pricing scheme.
The party did not respond to the Star's requests for comment on this story.
"When you have a sort of vague and shifty term like this, it means that when you get criticized, you can always say, 'No, no, that's not what I meant by this,' and shift to another meaning. I think it can give rise to a kind of slipperiness that can be very useful in the cut and thrust of public debate," said Jennifer Saul, a chair in social and political philosophy of language at the University of Waterloo.
That kind of ambiguity is the hallmark of political dog whistles, said Tari Ajadi, a political science professor at McGill University.
"If you replace the word 'woke' with a skin colour, if you replace the term 'woke' with a sexual orientation, if you replace the term 'woke' with a gender ... you get a very different response. It wouldn't be allowed to be said," Ajadi said.
The myriad uses of the word prompted McCurdy and his colleagues to sort the examples they collected into seven different definitions of "woke." The "authoritarian" definition meant the word was used to characterize something as controlling or intolerant, for example, and the "ineffective" definition was applied when it was used to paint a policy as actively harmful.
The researchers concluded that MPs, largely Conservative ones, had succeeded in shifting the term from its social justice roots and into an understanding associated with control, positioning Poilievre "as the only political defender of freedom against this looming totalitarian threat."
The Conservative leader has previously said as much.
"It has plenty of pretexts but only one purpose: control. It is designed to divide people by race, gender, ethnicity, religion, vaccine status and any other way one can divide people into groups. Why? It is because then one can justify having a government to control all those groups," Poilievre told the Commons in 2023 when asked to define the word.
"No more woke. We need freedom."
How Poilievre arrived at that definition begins not with the right, but with the left.
Ajadi said the word "woke" was first recast from "a very in-group thing that's talking about being socially conscious and politically conscious ... to talking generically about social justice, which it was never intended to be."
He said the term was seized by the upper-middle class, corporations, universities, and politicians who claimed it as a trendy term that came to represent a "poorly-defined set of values."
"This is how this kind of appropriation of language emerges and unfolds, and then very quickly gets appropriated once more by the right to mock what some might call performative activism," Ajadi said.
Indeed, the word eventually became synonymous with inauthentic virtue signalling from progressives (think Justin Trudeau, who had publicly condemned racism before admitting he had previously worn blackface), and left-leaning policies that earnestly sought to correct historic inequities, but were spun by the right as infringements on their freedoms.
The result? Laws like Florida Gov. Ron DeSantis' so-called Stop WOKE Act - or Stop Wrongs to our Kids and Employees. (The 2022 state law, which banned education and workplace initiatives that suggest people are oppressed or privileged based on their race, gender or nation of origin, has faced legal challenges.)
Such heavily politicized uses of "woke" left progressive politicians in Canada floundering to respond, McCurdy said.
His research showed that Liberal, NDP and Bloc Quebecois MPs tried to "challenge" Conservative uses of "woke" on 22 occasions, and found that they largely wound up legitimizing the word's use as a pejorative in the process.
In one case, then-Liberal Housing Minister Ahmed Hussen repeatedly characterized the term as something offensive after Poilievre referred to mayors whose housing policies he opposed as "woke."
"He stood up in this House and insulted and denigrated three of the leading mayors of Canada's largest cities, calling them 'woke,'" Hussen said during a 2023 debate in the Commons.
"The idea (behind the word) has become sullied, and no one's prepared to try and defend it," McCurdy said.
The word has also been effective for Poilievre because regular people also use it to convey dissatisfaction, said Mitch Heimpel, director of policy at Enterprise Canada and a former Conservative staffer.
Heimpel said he's heard people in his neighbourhood of young families use "woke" to refer to topics like what children are learning in schools and news stories about someone released on bail committing another crime.
"It's a reflection of a change to a system that in the mind of the person listening, is causing it to not work," Heimpel said. "So it becomes a good shorthand in that way."
Both Heimpel and Saul said it is not cause for alarm that words evolve, taking on new meanings over time.
"If you try to hold language in place, you're fighting a losing game," Saul said.
But for Ajadi, the movement of political language is deliberate, something done with a motive in mind.
"Language evolves, but I think that removes agency from the equation," Ajadi said. "People make language evolve."
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre speaks during a news conference in Delta B.C., on Jan.16, 2025.

Subject: Conservatism; Social justice; Political activism; Race; Politicians; Blues music; Gender

Location: Canada

People: Poilievre, Pierre

Document 488 of 500

Waiting for your $200 Ontario 'tax rebate'? Here's what to do if it hasn't arrived

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/waiting-your-200-ontario-tax-rebate-heres-what-do/docview/3159685911/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Waiting+for+your+%24200+Ontario+%27tax+rebate%27%3F+Here%27s+what+to+do+if+it+hasn%27t+arrived&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Waiting for a cheque in the mail can be anxiety-provoking, especially when people are still financially recovering from extravagant holiday spending.
Ontario finance minister Peter Bethlenfalvy recently announced that the province has started to issue the $200 "tax rebate" cheques for 15 million Ontario adults and children.
On social media, people are sharing various plans for their incoming cheque, with some saying they'll use the money for groceries, while others plan to donate it to charity or even to the opposition party.
However, not everyone has seen their cheque land in the mail yet.
How can you track your rebate status? What should you do if it's missing? Here's what you need to know.
How much money will I receive?
The payouts - rolling out days before Doug Ford's plan to call an early Ontario election - will cost the treasury $3 billion, despite a $6.6-billion budget deficit.
The money will be put into the pockets of 12.5 million adults in Ontario and 2.5 million children, the Ministry of Finance estimates.
All Ontario taxpayers 18 and over who filed their 2023 income tax are eligible for a $200 cheque and families who qualify for the federal Canada Child Benefit payment could receive an additional $200 for every child under 18.
That means a family of four would receive a total of $800.
Those who were bankrupt or incarcerated last year do not qualify for a payment.
What should I do if I haven't received my cheque?
The cheque may still be on its way.
The payments are by cheque only, so check your mailbox if you haven't opened it in a while.
The finance minister began issuing the cheques on Jan. 17 and they are set to reach families over the coming weeks, said Colin Blachar, the spokesperson for the minister.
Ontarians can verify the cheque amounts for adults and children, the mailing address of the cheque and whether or not it has been mailed out on the Service Ontario website, Blachar added.
The system will require an account login, the last four digits of an individual's Social Insurance Number, their date of birth and the postal code of the mailing address used in the Canada Revenue Agency tax return.
A person can only check their status a maximum of three times a day, the website reads.
Those who have concerns about the cheque being missing or stolen can visit Ontario.ca/TaxpayerRebate, or call Service Ontario at 1-833-351-0409 or 1-888-821-9056, Blachar said.
CAPTION: Ontario finance minister Peter Bethlenfalvy announced Jan. 17 that the province has started to issue the $200 "tax rebate" chequesfor 15 million Ontario adults and children.

Document 489 of 500

After a year in a homeless shelter, ex-MPP has a new home: 'I can finally breathe': Friends, political leaders rallied to help former colleague after Star story

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/after-year-homeless-shelter-ex-mpp-has-new-home-i/docview/3159624156/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=After+a+year+in+a+homeless+shelter%2C+ex-MPP+has+a+new+home%3A+%27I+can+finally+breathe%27%3A+Friends%2C+political+leaders+rallied+to+help+former+colleague+after+Star+story&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Tonight, Lorenzo Berardinetti will enjoy the serenity of an apartment all to himself - quiet, safe, peaceful. When day breaks he'll watch the lake waves see-saw from his driveway.
"Yesterday, I opened up the fridge, and I realized - I haven't opened up a fridge in 14 months," said the 63-year-old former politician. "It felt weird. I opened the door and I thought, 'This is my food. I get to keep it. It won't get stolen. It won't get taken away from me.' "
For the past year, Berardinetti - who had held elected office for 30 years as a four-term Scarborough councillor and then as a four-term Liberal MPP - slept in a homeless shelter in Ajax.
Since 2018, Berardinetti had lost his job to a New Democrat, his house in a divorce, and his health to a seizure disorder that started in his 60s.
Unable to work or pay rent, he moved in with his brother but ended up leaving, with nowhere to go, following a disagreement.
After the Star published an article about Berardinetti last month, politicians from all three major parties raised nearly $47,000 to help him get back on his feet.
"I've been so proud to be associated with this bipartisan group," said Justin Van Dette, architect of the fundraiser, a veteran political staffer who now works for Tridel. "Political rivals, political enemies - they don't agree much on policy but, boy, they've all shown their big hearts."
Van Dette and former deputy premier George Smitherman are managing the money raised for Berardinetti. Together, they found him his apartment, which Smitherman and his husband have been helping to furnish. They've pledged to keep supporting Berardinetti while he re-establishes his independence and restarts his legal career.
"I'm in touch with him on a daily basis," said Smitherman. "I think the experience he had was quite isolating. So it's important that we build networks of support for him and remain dedicated to helping him in the long term. That's the kind of thing you have to do when you're part of a community."
The community is preparing for its next crisis. A portion of the fund for Berardinetti is meant to be reserved for other politicians in need. Base pay for MPPs has been frozen at $116,000 since 2009 and they do not get a pension.
"In bringing together people for Lorenzo, we heard quite a few other sobering stories about MPPs," said Smitherman. "I anticipate that over time what can emerge is a capacity to support former MPPs who are in a struggle."
More than 30 current and former politicians have sponsored the fundraiser for Berardinetti, including former Toronto mayors David Miller and John Tory, and former premiers Dalton McGuinty and Kathleen Wynne.
Homeless advocates say they might have better served him by building stronger social safety nets.
"Nobody deserves to be homeless, and this includes MPP Berardinetti," said Toronto community worker Diana Chan McNally. "I'm very glad to hear that he's found a new apartment. He deserves it. But, it has to be said, there is a real hypocrisy that underscores his particular situation."
Homelessness, said Chan McNally, is caused by bad policy decisions. And so government officials are both responsible for the crisis of homelessness and empowered to fix it, if they find the will.
"How they've bolstered this campaign shows no regard for homelessness as a broad crisis," she said. "Frankly, it's gross that they've chosen to very publicly support the one person with whom they can personally identify."
Berardinetti may have a home now, but about 80,000 people in Ontario remain without. The fundraiser for him did not make any allowances to support them, nor the shelter that took him in.
That's fine with Marla Walters, executive director of Doors of Compassion, where Berardinetti lived for a year.
"I'm happy that the community rallied around him and am hopeful that this will help them with decision-making regarding vulnerable populations," she said. "A win for one is a win for all. I don't like the crab-in-the-bucket mentality that people often carry when seeing others succeed."
Walters said Berardinetti sharing his story made people confront the precarity of their own financial situations - regardless of how well off they feel at the moment - and the ubiquity of the housing crisis.
Berardinetti said that was his intent - and to give hope to the people who need it.
"Sometimes, people who are down and out, they give up," he said. "But you should never give up. Keep pushing for help."
For Berardinetti, the last year felt like a prison sentence. Every night, he had his bag searched and he had to take his shoes off to have them inspected for weapons and drugs.
He lived on guard, without privacy, sleeping in a room with 23 other people - his rest disturbed by arguments that lasted into the night. Belongings were never safe. His boots were stolen once. So too was his friend's phone.
"I can finally breathe," he said. "It's hard to describe the feeling. It's a huge relief, emotionally. It's like leaving a prison."
While the circumstances at Doors of Compassion were difficult, the staff and volunteers there were nothing but kind, Berardinetti said. He was touched when, last month, they invited him to a holiday party, where he read a poem about the virtue of generosity. Staff wanted it printed and framed. Berardinetti joked he'd become the "poet laureate of the homeless."
Soon, Berardinetti said, he will return to them with cards and flowers.
"I can't say enough good things about them," he said. "They're so kind. They treated me with respect. They treated me really, really well. I'm grateful they were so accommodating."
CREDIT: Ben Cohen Toronto Star

Document 490 of 500

Is funding really a done deal?: City urges Ottawa to release the cash for new subway cars

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/is-funding-really-done-deal/docview/3159623959/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+funding+really+a+done+deal%3F%3A+City+urges+Ottawa+to+release+the+cash+for+new+subway+cars&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.18&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With a spring federal election looming, advocates and city politicians are voicing concern about what a change in government might mean for recently promised funding for Toronto's much-needed Line 2 subway cars.
Inquiries made to all levels of government by the Star suggest that the promised funding is not yet set in stone, raising the spectre of changing political fortunes somehow pulling the plug on the transit project - as history has shown can happen.
Despite the federal government's public pledge in November to help buy the cars, the dollars are not officially committed until the city's request is signed. While officials said they see no obstacle to that, with no dollars set to flow until 2026 they acknowledge there are no guarantees should there be a change in government, something current polls suggest is likely.
Mayor Olivia Chow has urged the Liberal government to get the money flowing as soon as possible. The government isn't budging on the timeline, however, saying Toronto and other cities promised funds have been told they won't "be made available for disbursement starting in fiscal year 2026-27. The Government of Canada has made this timeline clear to all of its partners throughout the application process."
A need for new cars
The 26-kilometre Line 2, which opened in 1966 and is today responsible for more than 400,000 daily boardings, has been in dire need of modernization, with the current cars reaching the end of their 30-year service life in 2026.
The TTC said it needed full funding for the new trains by March 2025, and expects the first of batch of cars to be delivered starting in 2030.
The $2.3-billion purchase of a new fleet of cars for Line 2 was seen as a collaborative effort, with funding from Ottawa and Queen's Park each covering a third of the cost and the city setting aside the remaining third.
As part of its "new deal" with Toronto announced in November 2023, Doug Ford's government promised more than $1 billion in funding for public transit in Toronto - including the $758 million that was its share of the new Line 2 cars. But that money was contingent on Justin Trudeau's government kicking in its share.
A funding announcement
In late November last year, then-finance minister Chrystia Freeland finally announced Ottawa's portion of the funding.
"We know that Toronto is growing and we need to have public transit that grows with the needs of our growing city," Freeland said at the press conference.
Freeland quit her post just weeks after the Line 2 announcement, which led to an upheaval within the federal government that ultimately ended with Trudeau's resignation.
Though the November press conference made it sound like the federal government had committed to the $758 million in Line 2 funding, the press secretary for Nate Erskine-Smith, the new federal infrastructure minister, now says the government is "considering" the TTC's funding application.
"Once approved and finalized by the minister and all partners," spokesperson Sofia Ouslis said last week, federal funding will begin to flow in the 2026 fiscal year.
In the meantime, the city has already started the process of choosing a supplier, the mayor's office said, even though federal funding remains under consideration. In early December, the TTC issued a request for proposal for 55 new subway cars, as well as a new automatic train control system.
"The funding is committed from both the federal and provincial governments," Zeus Eden, a spokesperson for Mayor Olivia Chow, said last week. "The (request for proposals) has been issued and we look forward to seeing riders on the new cars."
Transit questions unanswered
While the Trudeau government has more than doubled its transit funding for Toronto, Pierre Poilievre and his Conservatives, who are polling well ahead of any opposition, have been opaque on their approach to transit funding.
Poilievre, in a post in French on X in June 2023, took a stance against a proposed Quebec tramway project, accusing the Trudeau government of waging a "war on cars" and ignoring suburban commuters. He has also proposed tying funding for transit projects to high-density housing construction.
The Star repeatedly reached out to Poilievre for comment on whether he would alter the federal government support for the Line 2 subway cars should he be elected in the spring. He did not respond.
The risks of keeping trains running past the end of their service life was underscored when the Scarborough RT derailed in 2023. In June of last year, then-TTC CEO Rick Leary warned there could be similar problems on Line 2 if its trains weren't replaced.
Once the proposals to build the subway cars are in, it will take months before a vendor is selected, and then another several years before trains are produced and go onto the tracks, TTC staff explained.
"Given the political uncertainty in Ottawa, if the promised cheque isn't delivered now, not only could subway delays become more common, Line 2 could eventually shutdown completely," Coun, Josh Matlow, who sits on the TTC board, warned.
"This would disrupt the lives of millions, and the economy of Canada's largest city"
A lesson from the past
Though the Liberal government appears to be safe from opposition efforts to trigger a non-confidence vote and snap election until at least March 24, if it is defeated in a spring election, a new government could alter its commitment, or pull funding altogether.
That happened in 1995, when then-premier Mike Harris unilaterally pulled funding from the Eglinton West subway, filling in tunnels that had just been dug a year prior. And the same occurred in 2010, when several planned light-rail lines were cancelled by former mayor Rob Ford once he took office, with the approval of the McGuinty provincial government.
TTCriders, the transit advocacy group, had tried to get Toronto MPs to commit to supporting federal funding for the TTC. They received pledges from MPs of all stripes, including Erskine-Smith - but not from any Conservatives.
"Transit riders are urging Minister Erskine-Smith to find a way to accelerate the Canada Public Transit Fund, especially the urgently needed new subway trains," said Pizey-Allen, executive director of TTCriders, in an email.
"Until federal money flows and contracts are signed, it is hard to feel confident that the new subway trains are secure."
CREDIT: David Rider Senior Politics Reporter Andy Takagi Toronto Star

Subject: Press conferences; Federal government; Light rail transportation; Public transportation; Federal funding; Monitoring systems

Document 491 of 500

Does Ford really need a new mandate?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/does-ford-really-need-new-mandate/docview/3159623951/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Does+Ford+really+need+a+new+mandate%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford says a 'clear mandate' from Ontario voters is the best way to deal with Donald Trump, Jan. 20
Premier Doug Ford does not need a new mandate from Ontario voters. He has a majority government, and the leaders of both the Liberal and New Democratic parties have confirmed that they will support any expenditures necessary to address the tariffs that President Donald Trump has threatened to introduce. This is simply Ford's attempt to exploit the recent media exposure he's been getting to extend his hold on power at a cost of millions of dollars.
Greg Sheehan, Mississauga
Ford is showing himself to be the ultimate political opportunist. The premier already has a strong mandate to do practically whatever he wants. We have seen him in action: think Ontario Place, the Ontario Science Centre, interference in municipal affairs. It would be despicable of Ford to use the spectre of Trump as an excuse to call an expensive and unwanted election. And it's opportunistic of him to do so just as Ontarians are receiving their $200 rebate cheques - not to mention before the RCMP's Greenbelt investigation is complete.
The premier already has a mandate to improve health care and education. He has a mandate to deal with the housing crisis. Take off the baseball cap, Premier Ford, and do your job. Complete your term.
Peggy Stevens, Newmarket
Ford does not need to waste millions and millions of dollars seeking a new mandate when his current one has a year and a half remaining. He should use this time, and any election money saved, to defend Ontario residents and businesses. For him to claim not to be able to do that without a new mandate from the people suggests to me that perhaps he is not the best person for the job of premier. But we can just worry about that in 2026. For now, let's focus on the present.
Gary Fish, Toronto
When one nation is attacked by another, that nation's leader has a responsibility to defend its citizens and its territory to the hilt - without hesitation, without compromise.
For Ford to suggest that he must call an election in order to get Ontarians' say-so to defend the province against Trump's declaration of economic warfare is utterly ridiculous. Trump clearly intends to wreack havoc on our economy and bring us to our knees. His statements to that effect have been unequivocal.
Our premier needs to keep his attention and activities 100 per cent focused on the existential threat confronting us. He enjoys a commanding majority in the Ontario legislature. To call an election call now would not only be unnecessary, but also unconscionable.
Penny Gill, Dundas
Kensington is one of a kind
Toronto says it wants to protect Kensington Market. But city laws mean we'll never have another place like it, Jan. 19
Unfortunately, Shawn Micallef's poignant article about Kensington Market ends with a question that is sadly too easy to answer: Why can't we have any more Kensington Markets?
The beauty of Kensington Market lies in the simple fact that it doesn't feel like Toronto. It's old and beautiful, adjectives that run contrary to the visions of developers and the politicians who enable them.
Gary Kostin, Mississauga
Daycare costs unsustainable
Most provinces have dropped daycare costs to $10/day. Why are some Toronto parents still paying full fees? Jan. 19
My wife and I raised our daughter in Quebec, which does not participate in the Canada-wide Early Learning and Child Care program. Instead, for more than 30 years, Quebec has had its own daycare subsidy program. And thank goodness: it was hard enough to pay for daycare for our daughter back then. I can't imagine what it would be like now.
Actually, I can imagine it - and so, presumably, can Premier Doug Ford and his education minister, Jill Dunlop. If Toronto and Ontario want to keep hard-working, tax-paying people here to raise families and buy homes, then subsidizing daycare has to be part of the package.
The woman featured in Brendan Kennedy's story last weekend leaves her preschooler in a daycare that charges market rate. That's $2,090. The woman also has an infant, whom she was hoping would be starting in the same daycare. The market rate for that child is $2,565. That's almost $4,700 a month, as Kennedy's story points out. Let's assume the couple have a $1 million house with a 25-year amortization period and a 5 per cent fixed-rate mortgage. That's more than $5,000 a month for the house. Add to that insurance, utilities, and the ever-rising cost of groceries and transportation, and it's clear you'd have to be a few French fries short of a Happy Meal to think this is sustainable.
Raymond Beauchemin, Hamilton
A carbon-tax alternative
After all that, it will be the Liberals themselves who will actually 'axe the tax,' Jan. 21
So the Liberal leadership contenders are going to drop the carbon tax - but they'll probably just replace it with a similar policy under a different name.
How about instead of another tax, we reach out to Canadians to do their part to reduce emissions. Small businesses could voluntarily go to four-day work weeks. We could schedule more errands in a single driving trip. We could do the old "turn off lights and electronics when not in the room" routine. We could plant more trees and gardens to filter the air. We could turn down the thermostat a notch and put on a sweater.
Coming at Canadians with a do-the-best-you-can challenge would go a lot further than any oppressive tax. We should still put pressure on big businesses to cut down on industrial pollution, and we should also pressure countries such as China and India to cut their emissions, too.
Mark Yates, Elmira
It's never too late
As an aging boomer who was never really an activist, I have decided that the time to act is now. In all my 70-plus years, I have been a very proud Canadian, but what have I done to keep my country safe? Our prime minister has resigned, our government has been dissolved, our beloved Canada is being threatened by a bully, and there is infighting among our leaders. So what to do? I believe the only thing I can do is make my voice heard and, as a result, I have joined the federal Liberal Party so that my "voice" can help decide who will lead it into the next election. The other thing I can do is volunteer. Because I believe so strongly in the party's values, I will commit to knocking on doors, making phone calls, and doing other tasks that the party thinks necessary. Of course, the party needs money, too - but there is so much else that can be done. If you believe in forging a positive future, then hopefully I have encouraged you to take action as well. It is a very unnerving time out there, and it's time to act resolutely.
Marilyn Herbert, Toronto
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford, wearing a 'Canada Is Not For Sale' hat, speaks as he arrives for a first ministers meeting in Ottawa on Wednesday, Jan. 15, 2025. THE CANADIAN PRESS/Justin Tang

Subject: Leadership; Voters

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

Location: Canada; Quebec Canada

People: Trump, Donald J

Document 492 of 500

Think your Ontario rebate cheque could go to better use? Here's how to make it count

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/think-your-ontario-rebate-cheque-could-go-better/docview/3159623607/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Think+your+Ontario+rebate+cheque+could+go+to+better+use%3F+Here%27s+how+to+make+it+count&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Last week, the Ontario government started sending out $200 cheques to nearly all the province's residents. While this comes as a necessary financial boost for many, others may feel that they don't obviously need $200. If you're one of those people, you face an important question: What should you do? You could consider paying it forward - but how?
The concept of "effective altruism" offers a compelling answer: use evidence and reason to determine how you can help others as much as possible with your donations.
Surprisingly, most donors don't research where their money goes. A 2019 report from the BBB Wise Giving Alliance suggests that only about 20 per cent of donors conduct thorough research before contributing. Many of us are taught to view charity as an act that doesn't require scrutiny: it's simply good to do good. Yet while it is good to give, research shows that charities can vary dramatically in terms of their impact per dollar. This underscores the importance of looking beyond local or merely emotionally compelling causes to focus on interventions that can create evidence-based change. It's not just about giving - rather, it's about giving strategically.
For example, Ontario is fortunate enough to have a relatively robust health-care system. In contrast, less-resourced countries face widespread, devastating health challenges that can be addressed at shockingly low costs. The idea of a child dying from diarrhea is almost unthinkable in Ontario, but globally, diarrhea is the third leading cause of death in children under five - and the overwhelming majority of those deaths occur in low- and middle-income countries. The good news is that diarrhea can be effectively treated with oral rehydration therapy, which costs around US$3 per child annually.
Every human deserves the opportunity to thrive, regardless of where they were born. This isn't about diminishing the importance of local community support but recognizing our shared humanity. A life saved in Uganda is just as meaningful as a life saved in Ontario, but an individual donor can save many more lives in Uganda for the same amount of money.
So how do you separate the programs that really work from those that are simply well-marketed? Being thoughtful about your charitable gifts can be difficult, and poring over academic literature or interviewing charities is more work than most donors have time for. Fortunately, if you're interested in effective altruism, you can turn to charity evaluators and funds such as GiveWell or Giving What We Can. That way, you don't have to do the research yourself. Instead, you can rely on experts to find the best opportunities for you.
GiveWell is known for rigorously analyzing charities to identify the most cost-effective global-health interventions. Their approach is focused on people: How many lives can be saved? How dramatically can lives be improved? And at what cost? The results are striking. GiveWell's top-rated charities can save a life for remarkably little (about US$3000, to be specific), with funds going toward malaria prevention, essential medical treatments, critical nutrition programs and more. Giving What We Can, meanwhile, provides a carefully vetted and continually updated list of high-impact charities and funds based on research from charity evaluators.
During my time at the Centre for Effective Altruism, I've seen how small but thoughtful contributions can have an outsize impact. This $200 rebate represents more than a government support measure: it's also an opportunity to reconsider what generosity can look like. In a world facing complex challenges, we have an unprecedented ability to make a meaningful difference.
So when that $200 cheque arrives, take a moment to scrutinize how you plan to spend it and ask yourself: How can I make it matter most?
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford, wearing a 'Canada Is Not For Sale' hat, speaks as he arrives for a first ministers meeting in Ottawa on Wednesday, Jan. 15, 2025. THE CANADIAN PRESS/Justin Tang
CREDIT: Frances Lorenz

Subject: Altruism; Charities; Diarrhea; Community support

Location: United States--US; Uganda

Company / organization: Name: GiveWell; NAICS: 813211

Title: Think your Ontario rebate cheque could go to better use? Here's how to make it count

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2025

Publication date: Jan 25, 2025

Section: opinion/contributors

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto

Country of publication: Canada
